entry_text: Goth af ‘from, away from’, ON af id., OE æf, of id., OFris af, of id., OS af id., OHG aba id. (with secondary -a). Identical with Skt ápa ‘away, off’, Av ap¡ id., Gk ἀπό, ἄπο ‘from’, Alb pa ‘without’, Lat ab ‘from’. Bartholomae 72; Torp-Falk 15; Holthausen AEEW 9; Walde-Hofmann I 1; Feist 3; Mayrhofer I 37; Pokorny I 53; Vries ANEW 2; Frisk I 122; Onions 624; Lehmann GED 2; Lloyd-Springer I 5–8; Kluge-Seebold 2–3; Orel AED 307; Boutkan OFED s.v.
ON efsa ‘to cut off’, OE efesian ‘to cut, to round, to shear’. Probably derived from *aba. Vries ANEW 94.

ON efja ‘mud, ooze’, OE ebba ‘ebb’ (masc.), OFris ebba id., MDu ebbe id. Closely related to Gk ἄπιος ‘far away’. Derived from *aba. Torp-Falk 15; Holthausen AEEW 87; Pokorny I 54; Vries ANEW 94; Frisk I 121–122; Onions 299; Kluge-Seebold 203.

ON efni ‘material, stuff’, OE and-efn ‘equality, proportion, measure, amount’. Related to Skt ápna- ‘possession, work’, Gk ἄφνος ‘riches, wealth’ (with irregular -f-). Torp-Falk 15–16; Holthausen AEEW 88; Mayrhofer I 40; Vries ANEW 94.
ON efna ‘to perform, to prepare, to make arrangements’, OE efnan ‘to perform, to execute, to labor’, OHG ebanōn id. Derived from *abnjan. Torp-Falk 15– 16; Holthausen AEEW 88; Vries ANEW 94.

Goth aba ‘husband, man’, ON afi‘grandfather, man’, OHG prop. Abo. Probably, a Lallwort (Uhlenbeck PBB XXII 188, XXVII 113–114: nursery word for ‘father’). Torp-Falk 15; Bezzenberger BB XXI 296 (to *abraz and Lith úošvis ‘father-in-law’); Lidén Studien 73–74 (to IE *op- ‘to be able, to bring about’); Förstemann AND I 11; Zimmermann BB XXIX 272 (to Etr apa ‘father, husband’); Schulze KZ LII 311 (same as Bezzenberger); Meringer IF XVII 129, XVIII 208–209 (follows Lidén); Zachrisson Festschr. Kock 405 (to OE Aba, Abba, Afa); Feist 1; Pokorny I 2; Vries ANEW 2 (to *afalan; separates ON afi ‘man’ from afi‘grandfather’, the latter to Goth awo ‘grandmother’); Zalizniak I 152; Jucquois LBd LX 199–203; Benveniste IEL 181; Lehmann GED 1 (against comparison of Goth with ON).

Goth abrs ‘great, severe, extreme’, ON afr-endr at afli ‘very strong, valiant’. Related to MIr abor- ‘very’, W afr- id. (Loth RC XLII 64), Thrac prop. Ἀβρο-ζελμις. Meyer Goth. 64 (to Skt ábhva- ‘monstrous’); Kluge KZ XXV (to Skt āprá- ‘active, eager (?)’); Johansson IF III 239–241 (to Skt ámbha- ‘power’, Lat omnis ‘all’); Bezzenberger BB XXI 296 (to *abōn); Fick KZ XLV 56–57 (same as Johansson); Uhlenbeck Btrg. Gesch. XXX 253; Meillet Etudes 183 (to Slav *obrъ ‘Avar’); Torp-Falk 16; Feist 1–2; Stender-Petersen Slav.-germ. 184–185 (Slav *obrъ from Gothic); Mezger KZ LXV 122–123 (follows Bezzenberger); Vaillant RESl XXIX 122 (against Stender-Petersen); Jucquois LBd LX 199–203; Wagner KZ LXXV 58–75 (to Sum áb 2 ‘to procreate’, Akk abāru ‘to be strong’); Jóhannesson IEW 86; Pokorny I 2; Vries ANEW 2–3; Vendryes A6–7; Lehmann GED 1; Bammesberger Nom. 248; Heidermanns 93 (to Skt ápas- ‘work, action’, Lat opus ‘work’).
ON au-viräi ‘worthless wretch, damage’, MHG abe ‘from’. A variant of *aba. Cf. Gk Arc-Cypr ἀπύ for ἀπό. Pokorny I 55; Frisk I 122.

ON ǫfugr ‘bent back, inverted’, OS abuh ‘evil’, OHG abuh ‘bad, wrong’. Derived from *aba. Cf. similar but not identical derivatives in Skt ápāka- ‘aside, back’, Slav *opakъ ‘back, back to front’. Schmidt Plur. 392; Kretschmer KZ XXXI 389; Torp-Falk 15; Weisweiler IF XLI 345–350; Holthausen AEEW 2; Pokorny I54; Vries ANEW 685; Vasmer-Trubachev III 142; Lloyd-Springer I 33–35; Kluge- Seebold 6; Heidermanns 93–94.
OSwed ko-adel ‘cow’s urine’, OE adela ‘filth’, MLG adel(e) ‘unpleasant dampness’. Of unknown origin. Torp-Falk 10 (to Gk ὄνθος ‘dung’); Holthausen AEEW 2; Onions 12.

ON afl‘strength’, OE afol ‘power’, OS abal id. The same root is preserved in Gk ἀν-απελάσας: ἀναρρωσθείς, Hes., Ion εὐπελής ‘strong’, Hom ὀλιγηπελίη ‘weakness’, Ion ἀνηπελίη: ἀσθένεια (Hes.). Torp-Falk 16; Holthausen AEEW 2; Porzig Gliederung 130; Pokorny I 52; Vries ANEW 2 (OE < ON); Lehmann 1 (to *abōn).
ON afla ‘to acquire, to cause, to contrive’, OHG part. avalōnti ‘farmer’. Derived from *afalan. Torp- Falk 16; Vries ANEW 2, 94.

Goth afar ‘after’, ON afar- ‘very’ (in compounds), OHG avur ‘again’. Related to Skt ápara- ‘posterior, later’, Av aparō id. Cf. *aba. Bartholomae 76–77; Torp-Falk 15; Feist 3–4; Mayrhofer I 38; Pokorny I 54; Chemodanov SGGJa I 107; Vries ANEW 2; Lehmann GED 2–3.

Goth afara ‘descendant’, OE eafora ‘heir, offspring, successor, son’, OS avaro ‘descendant’. Derived from *afarē (Feist 4). Torp-Falk 15; Holthausen AEEW 83; Thyagaraju JEGP XLVII 180 (to *abōn); Vries ANEW 2 (to ON afr ‘buttermilk’); Schmidt Studien 164; Lehmann GED 3.

Goth aftana ‘from behind’, ON aptan ‘afterwards, again’, OE æftan ‘behind’, OFris efta id., OS aftan ‘eventually’, OHG aftan ‘behind, from behind’. Derived from *aftē. Holthausen AEEW 9; Feist 11; Pokorny I 54; Vries ANEW 11; Onions 18; Lehmann GED 7; Boutkan OFED s.v.

ON aptann, eptann ‘evening’, OE æfen id., OFris ēvend, iound id., OS āband id., OHG āband id. Derived from *aftanē (Johanisson MASO V 50–75) with taboo transformations. Brugmann IF V 376 (to IE *epi ~ *opi); Wiedemann BB XXVIII 73 (same as Brugmann); Holthausen AEEW 9; Pokorny I 324; Onions 331; Vries ANEW 11; Lloyd-Springer I 9–13; Beekes KZ CIX 231–232 (non-Indo- European); Kluge-Seebold 3–4.

Goth aftaro ‘in back of, from behind’ (with analogical -o), ON eptir ‘after’, OE æfter id., OFris efter id., OS aftar id., OHG after id. Modification of a comparative degree of *aba influenced by *afta. Close to Skt apataram ‘farther off’, OPers apataram ‘away from, far away’, Gk ἀπωτέρω ‘farther’ (Brugmann Grundriß II/1 324–326). Torp- Falk 15; Feist 11–12; Schulze KZ XL 414; Pokorny I 53; Schmidt Studien 262 (to IE *opi); Mayrhofer I 37; Vries ANEW 11; Onions 18; Peeters SL XXIX, 59–60; Lehmann GED 8; Kluge- Seebold 18; Boutkan OFED s.v.

Goth afta ‘behind’, ON eft ‘after’ (with e- from eftir), OE æft ‘behind, again’, OFris eft ‘later, afterwards’, OS eft, eht ‘after’. Derived from *aba (Schmidt Studien 88). Holthausen AEEW 88; Feist 11; Pokorny I 53–54 (to Gk ὄπιθεν ‘behind’). Streitberg Bibel I 484 (Goth afta as an erroneous reading of aftra); Lehmann GED 7–8; Boutkan OFED s.v.
Goth aftra ‘back, again’, ON aptr id. See *aft(e)raz. Torp-Falk 15; Feist 12; Pokorny I 53; Schmidt Studien 262 (to IE *opi); Vries ANEW 11; Lehmann GED 8.
Goth aftuma ‘aftermost, hindmost’. Superlative degree of *aba. Cf. *aftumistaz. Feist 12; Pokorny I 53; Lehmann GED 8; Bammesberger Nom. 234.
Goth aftumists ‘endmost, final’, OE æftemest ‘last’. Secondary superlative based on *aftumōn. Holthausen AEEW 9; Feist 12; Pokorny I 53; Lehmann GED 8; Bammesberger Nom. 234.

Goth ag-laitei ‘licentiousness, debauchery’ (irregular -t- < *-k-), OE az-lác ‘misery, grief, trouble’. Compound of *aza (see *azanan) and *laikan (Mezger Word II 66–71). Torp-Falk 9; Holthausen AEEW 3; Aufderhaar Lehnw. 28–31 (OHG agaleizi ‘diligence, zeal’ < Goth); Feist 14 (to *azluz); Scardigli Goten 227–228 (follows Aufderhaar); Lehmann GED 10.

Goth agan ‘to be afraid of’ part. un-agands ‘fearless’. Related to Gk ἄχομαι ‘to be sad, to mourn’, OIr ad- ágathar ‘to fear’. Torp-Falk 9; Feist 13, 380–381 (on Goth og ‘to be afraid of’); Pokorny I 7; Vries ANEW 3; Frisk I 202–203; Seebold 362; Peeters KZ XC 9–10; Lehmann GED 9.

Goth ahana ‘chaff’, ON ǫgn id. (pl. agnir < fem. *azaniz), OE ezenu ‘little round heap’ (< *azinō; pl. from fem. *azanōn), OS agana ‘chaff’, OHG agana id. Derived from *azaz. Derivationally close to Skt aśáni- ‘thunderbolt, tip of a missile’, Gk ἄκαινα ‘spike, prick’, ἄκανος ‘pine-thistle’, Lat agna ‘ear (of grain)’ < *a˚nā, Lith ašnīs ‘edge, blade’, Latv asns ‘new shoot’. Aufrecht KZ I 353 (to Lat acus ‘needle, pin’); Zupitza Gutt. 129; Torp-Falk 7; Holthausen AEEW 89; Walde-Hofmann I 22–23; Feist 16; Mayrhofer I 59; Pokorny I 19–20; Chemodanov SGGJa I 63; Fraenkel 18; Frisk I 49, 62; Vries ANEW 685; Zalizniak II 194; Onions 66; Lehmann GED 11.
Swed ag ‘swamp grass’, MLG ag ‘perch’. For the etymology see *azanō ~ *axanō. Pokorny I 18.

OE ezeäe, ezäe ‘rake, harrow’, OFris eide id. OS egitha id., OHG egida id. Related to OW ocet id. < *okitā, OPrus aketes id., Lith akî‘ios, ekî‘ios id. Torp- Falk 8; Hirt IF XXXVII 230 (also to Lat occa ‘harrow’; Holthausen AEEW 88; Pokorny I 22; Chemodanov SGGJa I 62; Fraenkel 119; Kluge-Seebold 205.

Goth agis ‘awe, fear’, OE masc. eze ‘fear, horror, dread’. An es-stem derivationally identical with Gk neut. ἄχος id. (Aufrecht KZ I 355). Further connected with *azanan. Already in Goth oblique cases are based on *azesan. Cf. masc. *azesōn id. > OE ezesa id., OS egisa id., OHG egiso id. Zupitza Gutt. 218; Torp-Falk 9; Holthausen AEEW 89; Feist 14; Pokorny I 7; Chemodanov SGGJa I 110; Frisk I 202–203; Zalizniak II 223; Seebold 362; Lehmann GED 10; Plassmann Karg-Gasterstädt Festschr. 93– 135 (to *azwi- ‘snake’ in *azwi-þaxsjōn); Bammesberger Nom. 211; Kluge-Seebold 902.
Goth un-agei ‘fearlessness’, OHG egī ‘fear’. Derived from *azanan. Feist 13; Pokorny I 7; Vries ANEW 3; Zalizniak II 223; Onions 65; Lehmann GED 10; Heidermanns 94.
ON egg-steinn ‘sharp stone’, MLG egge-stēn ‘corner-stone’, MHG ecke-stein id. Compound of *azjō and *stainaz. Carr Nom. comp. 83 (parallel formations).

Burg *agja ‘edge’, ON egg id., OE ecz ‘edge, angle, blade, sword’, OFris egg, ig ‘edge, sword’, OS eggia id., MHG ecke ‘sharp point, side’. Related to Gk ἀκίς ‘sharp point’, Lat aciēs ‘sharp edge’ and other continuants of IE *a˚- ‘sharp’. Torp-Falk 8; Holthausen AEEW 87; Walde-Hofmann I 8; Pokorny I 19; Vries ANEW 94; Zalizniak I 148; Frisk I 52; Onions 301; Bammesberger Nom. 113; Köbler 708; Kluge-Seebold 204; Boutkan OFED s.v.
ON eggja ‘to incite, to goad’, OE eczan ‘to sharpen’, OFris eggia ‘to drive, to fence’, MLG eggen ‘to sharpen’. Derived from *azjō. Holthausen AEEW 87; Vries ANEW 95.

Goth agljan ‘to harm’, OE ezlan ‘to trouble’, MLG egelen ‘to annoy’, OHG egilen ‘to torment’. Derived from *azluz. Holthausen AEEW 89; Feist 15; Pokorny I 8; Onions 21; Lehmann GED 10; Heidermanns 95.

Goth aglus ‘difficult, toilsome’, OE ezle ‘troublesome, hateful’. Connected with Skt aghá- ‘bad’, Av agō id. Schweizer KZ I 151; Wiedemann BB XXVIII 50 (to Gk ἀχλύς ‘fog’); Bartholomae 47–48; Torp-Falk 9 (to Gk ὄχλος ‘crowd, throng’); Feist 15; Holthausen AEEW 89; Mezger Word II 67; Mayrhofer I 19; Pokorny I 8; Lehmann GED 10; Bammesberger Nom. 249; Heidermanns 95.
ON agn ‘bait’. From *a˚nó-. Related to *axjanan. Structurally close to Skt neut. áśana- ‘meal’. Torp-Falk 8; Pokorny I 18; Chemodanov SGGJa I 106; Vries ANEW 3.

ON ǫgr ‘kind of fish, carp’. Derivationally close to OIr aicher ‘sharp’, Lith ešer‹s, dial. ašer‹s ‘perch’, Latv asars, asaris id. (Möller KZ XXIV 466). Further related to IE *a˚- ‘sharp’. Torp- Falk 23; Trautmann BSW 14; Pokorny I 20; Fraenkel 125.

OE áäexe ‘lizard’, OS egithassa, ewithissa id., OHG egidehsa id. Compound consisting of *azwi- (from *og⁄hi-, cf. Skt áhi- ‘snake’, Gk ὄφις id., Kluge apud Zupitza Gutt. 99) and *þaxsjōn derived from *þexsanan. Torp- Falk 9; Holthausen AEEW 8; Mayrhofer I 68; Pokorny I 44; Frisk II 453.
ON ey-land ‘island’, OE éz-land id., OFris a-lond id., MLG ei-lant, eyg-lant id. Compound of *azwjō ~ *axwjō and *lanđan. Onions 486–487; Kluge-Seebold 209; Boutkan OFED s.v.

ON ey ‘island’, OE íz id., OFris a, ey id., OHG ouwa id. Pln. Oium (Jordanes 60.15) < Goth *aujan and -avia in Scandinavia belong here. Derived from *axwō (Bugge KZ XI 403–404). Torp-Falk 9; Holthausen AEEW 186; Feist 18; Pokorny I 23; Chemodanov SSGJa I 54; Zalizniak I 148; Vries ANEW 106; Onions 486; Darms Vṛddhi 25; Lehmann GED 13; Lloyd-Springer I 99–103; Bammesberger Nom. 113; Kluge-Seebold 61; Boutkan OFED s.v.

OE eánian ‘to yean, to bring forth (of ewe)’, WFris eandje id., Du dial. oonen id. Based on the unattested *azwnaz identical with Lat agnus ‘lamb’, Gk ἀμνός id., OIr úan id., Slav *agnę id. Specifically close to Slav *agniti sę ‘to bring forth (of ewe)’. Meillet BSL XXIV 183–184 (isolates Gk and Lat); Walde-Hofmann I 23; Chemodanov SGGJa I 59; Pokorny I 9; Frisk I 93; Onions 1019; Trubachev Zhiv. 71–73, ESSJa I 54–55; Dybo BSA 123–124.

Goth ahs ‘ear of grain’, ON ax id., OE ear id., OFris ār id., OS ahar id., OHG ehir id. Related to Toch B āk ‘ear of grain (?)’, Gk ἄχνη ‘foam, chaff’ < *a˚snā, Lat acus id. (s-stem), Slav *ostь ‘spike of corn, sharp point’, *osera ‘ear, spike of corn’. Zupitza Gutt. 129; Torp-Falk 7–8; Holthausen AEEW 85; Walde-Hofmann I 11; Feist 17–18; Pokorny I 22; Chemodanov SGGJa I 63; Vries ANEW 21; Frisk I 202; Zalizniak II 223; Onions 297; Vasmer-Trubachev III 167; Trubachev Etim. 1968 60 (OHG ahir < *a˚ero- = Slav *osera, Lith ašer‹s ‘perch’); Lehmann GED 12; Lloyd- Springer I 95–98; Bammesberger Nom. 212; Kluge-Seebold 21; Adams TB 35–37.
Dan ær ‘maple’, Germ dial. Acher id. Related to Lat acer id. Walde-Hofmann I 6–7; Pokorny I 20.
ON æja ‘to bait, to rest’. Probably derivationally close to Skt āśayati ‘to make eat’ (to Skt aśnāti ‘to eat, to consume’). Torp-Falk 8; Mayrhofer I 60; Pokorny I 18.
Swed dial. àm ‘swamp grass’. Derivationally similar to Gk ἀκμή ‘point, edge’ < *a˚-m- ‘sharp point’. Pokorny I 19; Frisk I 153–54.

ON ǫxl ‘shoulder-joint’ (from fem. *axsliz), OE eaxel ‘shoulder’, eaxle id. (ōn-stem), OFris axle, axele id., OS ahsla id., OHG ahsla id. (ō- and ōn-stem). Derived from *axsō. Derivationally similar to Lat āla id. < *a·slā. Zupitza Gutt. 187; Reichelt Wu S XII 112–114; Torp-Falk 8; Holthausen AEEW 87; Walde-Hofmann I 25; Pokorny I 6; Chemodanov SGGJa I 89; Vries ANEW 689; Zalizniak II 195; Lloyd-Springer I 114–116; Kluge-Seebold 12.

OE eax ‘axis, axle’, OFris axe id., MLG asse id., OHG ahsa id. Related to Skt ákṣa- ‘axle’, Gk ἄξων id., Lat axis id., OIr aiss id., Lith ašīs id., Latv ass id., Slav *osь. Torp-Falk 8; Meillet BSL XXV 144; Trautmann BSW 14–15; Reichelt Wu S XII 112–114; Holthausen AEEW 87; Benveniste Origines 7; Walde- Hofmann I 89; Mayrhofer I 16; Pokorny I 6; Chemodanov SGGJa I 108; Fraenkel 19; Frisk I 116; Zalizniak I 146; Vasmer-Trubachev III 167–168; Lloyd-Springer I 113–114; Kluge-Seebold 11–12.
ON ǫxull ‘axle’. Derived from *axsō. Derivationally close to W echel id., Bret ahel id. < *aksilā. Torp-Falk 8; Pokorny I 185; Kluge-Seebold 11.

Goth ahtau ‘eight’, ON átta id., OE eahta id., OFris achta id., OS ahto id., OHG ahto id. Continues the Indo- European word for ‘eight’: Toch A okät, B okt-, Skt aṣṭáu, aṣṭā, Av ašta, Gk ὀκτώ, Arm ut', Lat octō, OIr ocht n-, Lith aštuonī. Zupitza Gutt. 188; Bartholomae 260; Torp-Falk 8; Muller IF XLIV 137– 138; Pedersen Kelt. Gr. I 103, 392; Walde-Hofmann II 199–200; Feist 18; Mayrhofer I 63; Pokorny I 22, 775; Fraenkel 19–20; Vries ANEW 17; Frisk II 374–375; Onions 304; Lehmann GED 12; Lloyd-Springer I 121–124; Ross- Berns Numerals 588–589; Kluge-Seebold 12; Adams TB 110; Boutkan OFED s.v.

Goth ahtuda ‘eighth’, ON átte id., OHG ahtodo id. Other forms, ON áttandi, OFris achtunda, MLG achtende and the like, were restructured under the influence of *sebunđan ~ *sebunþan. Torp-Falk 8; Feist 18; Vries ANEW 17; Ross-Berns Numerals 629–630; Boutkan OFED s.v.

OS ahorn ‘maple’, OHG ahorn id. Derivationally close to Gk ἀκαρνα: δάφνη (Hes.), Lat acernus ‘of maple-tree’. Connected with *axiraz ~ *axuraz. Walde-Hofmann I 6–7; Pokorny I 20; Lloyd-Springer I 110– 113; Kluge-Seebold 20–21.
ODan à-dokke ‘waterdock’, OE éa-docce id. Compound of *axwō and *dukkōn. Onions 280.

ON soä-áll ‘flesh-hook’ (= Lat fuscinula), OE neut. awel ‘awl’, OHG ahil ‘point of the ear (of corn)’. Derivationally similar to Lat aculeus ‘spine’, W ebil ‘drill’ < *a˚⁄īlƒo-. Connected with IE *a˚- ‘sharp’, see *azanō ~ *axanō. Torp-Falk 8; Holthausen AEEW 8; Walde-Hofmann I 11; Pokorny I 19; Chemodanov SGGJa I 63, 107; Vries ANEW 6.

Goth aƕa ‘flowing body of water, river’, ON á ‘river’, OE eá ‘running water, stream’ (root stem), OFris ā, ē id., OS aha id., OHG aha id. Identical with Lat aqua id. Zupitza Gutt. 60; Torp-Falk 9; Bonfante IF LII 225; Holthausen AEEW 82; Walde- Hofmann I 60; Feist 18–19; Porzig Gliederung 100–101, 205–207 (on the relation with *ap- ‘river’); Wagner KZ LXXV 67 (to *e˚⁄os); Jungandreas Zd Ph LX 113 (ON to Gmc theon. ON Yngvi, OE Inz, OS Ing); Pokorny I 23; Chemodanov SSGJa I 54; Vries ANEW 1; Zalizniak II 205; Onions 487; Lehmann GED 12–13; Bammesberger Nom. 199; Lloyd-Springer I 99–103; Kluge- Seebold 11; Boutkan OFED s.v.

Crim. Goth sched-iit ‘light’, OE ád ‘funeral pile’, OS ēd ‘firebrand’, OHG eit id. Identical with Skt édha- ‘fuel’, Gk αἶθος ‘firebrand’, OIr áed ‘fire’. Pedersen Kelt. Gr. I 57; Torp-Falk 2; Wood JEGP XIII 504; Holthausen AEEW 2; Feist 414; Mayrhofer I 128; Pokorny I 11; Frisk I 37–38; Lehmann GED 298.
ON eisa ‘glowing embers’, MLG ēse ‘hearth’. Derived from *aiđaz. Reflects an original s-stem *aidhes-. Torp-Falk 3; Vries ANEW 97–98; Pokorny I 12.

Goth aigin ‘belongings, possessions, property’, ON eigin ‘one’s own, of property’, OE ázen ‘property’, OFris ēgin, ein id., OS ēgan id., OHG eigan id. Substantivized *aizanaz ~ *aizenaz. Holthausen AEEW 10; Vries ANEW 95; Seebold 69–70.
ON eiginn ‘own’, OE ázen id., OFris ein, egen id., OS ēgan id., OHG eigan id. Past participle of *aixa. Torp-Falk 1; Holthausen AEEW 10; Vries ANEW 96; Onions 638; Boutkan OFED s.v.

ON eigin ‘seed’, MLG īne ‘shoot, sprout’. Related to Lith áiža ‘crack, rift’, áižëti ‘to be peeled’, éižëti ‘to jump up, to start up’. Fick III 2 (to *ai˚- ‘spear’); Torp-Falk 2; Bezzenberger BB XXVII 166; Pokorny I 15; Fraenkel 4; Vries ANEW 95–96.

Goth ga-aiginon ‘to appropriate’, ON eigna ‘to dedicate, to attribute’. Cf. also *aizenēnan in OE áznian ‘to possess, to own, to appropriate’, MLG ēgenen id., OHG eigenēn ‘to take possession of’. Derived from *aizanaz ~ *aizenaz. Holthausen AEEW 3; Feist 20; Vries ANEW 96; Lehmann GED 14; Kluge- Seebold 209.
ON eiga ‘ownership, property’, OE áze ‘property’. Derived from *aixa. Holthausen AEEW 3; Vries ANEW 95; Seebold 69.

Goth un-aiwisks ‘blameless’, OE æwisc ‘disgraced, ashamed, abashed’, MLG eisch ‘hateful’. Related and structurally similar to Gk αἶσχος ‘shame’ < *aig⁄hsko- (Fick I 345–346), cf. *aizwjanan > OE æwan ‘to despise, to scorn’ (Wood MLN XVI 309). Torp- Falk 2 (to Gk αἶσχος ‘shame’); Holthausen AEEW 14; Feist 30; Pokorny I 14; Frisk I 146–47; Mezger KZ LXXIX 38–41 (to *aiwaz ~ *aiwiz); Beck Gedenkschr. Güntert 53, 55; Puhvel IF LXXXIII 138 (agrees with Mezger); Seebold KZ LXXXI 123; Lehmann GED 21; Kluge- Seebold 214.

Goth aih ‘to have, to own’, ON á id., OE áh id., OFris aga, hāga id., OS pl. ēgun id., OHG pl. eigun id. Related to Toch B aik- ‘to know, to recognize’, Skt īśe, īṣṭe ‘to possess, to own’, Av aēs- ‘to dominate’ (Pedersen Gr. 31). Schweizer KZ I 153; Bartholomae 26; Torp-Falk 1; Feist 20; Holthausen AEEW 10; Jóhannesson IEW 46; Mayrhofer I 96; Pokorny I 299; Zalizniak I 150; Vries ANEW 95; Onions 638; Seebold 69–72; Lehmann GED 14; Kluge-Seebold 208–209; Adams TB 101–102; Boutkan OFED s.v.

Goth aihts ‘belongings, possessions, property’, ON ætt, átt ‘family, race’, OE æht ‘possessions, property, power’, OHG ēht ‘property, power’. Derivative in *-ti- of *aixa. Close to Av īšti- ‘ability, power, property’. Bartholomae 376–377; Torp-Falk 1; Holthausen AEEW 10; Feist 20; Mezger WZUG VI 218–220; Pokorny I 298–299; Zalizniak I 150; Seebold 70; Lehmann GED 14; Bammesberger Nom. 143.

Goth af-aikan ‘to deny, to abjure’, OHG in-eihhan ‘to claim’ (gloss, = in-sagēn). Related to MIr éigid ‘to shout’. Further cf. *aixtrōjanan > Goth aihtron ‘to beg, to pray’, a denominative based on the unattested *aixtra- = Gk οἰκτρός ‘pitiable’. Bezzenberger Zd Ph V 229–230 (from *ai·- ‘to spring’); Sütterlin IF IV 101 (to Gk εἶπον ‘to speak, to say’); Grienberger Goten 4 (to Gk ἠμί ‘to say’); Torp-Falk 1; Uhlenbeck PBB XXVII 114 (to Slav *izъ ‘out, from’); Feist 3; Foes JEGP XLII 270 (to Gk οἶκτος ‘waiting’, MIr éigid ‘to cry’), Goth. 2–3; Bailey BSOAS XXI 536–538 (to Toch en- ‘to instruct’, Gk ἀναίνομαι ‘to refuse, to renounce, to spurn’); Pokorny I 298; Frisk II 361– 362; Seebold 72–73; Lehmann GED 2.
Burg *aikins, ON eikinn ‘oaken’, OE æcen, ácen id., OFris ēzen, ētzen id., MLG ēken id., OHG eihhīn id. Derived from *aikz. Holthausen AEEW 8; Vries ANEW 96; Köbler 708.

ON íkorni ‘squirrel’, OE ácwern id. (neut.), MLG ēkeren, ēkhorn id., OHG eihhurno id. A taboo transformation of the name attested in Lat uīuerra ‘weasel’, Bret gwiber ‘squirrel’, Lith vëverīs, vaiverīs id., Slav *vîverica id., probably influenced by *aikz and formations in *-ern-, cf. *þezwernō(n). Torp-Falk 27 (to Skt éjati ‘to stir, to move’); Much ZDADL XLII 166 (the first part to ON eikinn ‘savage’); Schrader BB XV 134 (the first component to Skt ejati ‘to stir, to move’); Holthausen AEEW 2; Specht KZ LXII 253–254 (Lat uīuerra < *uī-uer-sa); Walde-Hofmann II 808; Pokorny I 14 (compound of *aik- connected with Skt éjati ‘to move’ and *wernrelated to Lith vaiverīs ‘squirrel’, Slav *vîverica id.); Fraenkel 1233; Vries ANEW 284; Kluge-Seebold 207 (reconstructs *aikurnan).

Burg *aiks ‘oak’, ON eik id., OE ác id., OFris ēk id., OS ēk id. (i-stem), OHG eih id. (i-stem). Compared with Gk αἰγίλωψ ‘kind of oak’, with the second component identified with λώψ: χλαμύς, Hes., λοπός ‘shell, bark’, Lat aesculus ‘kind of oak’ (Kretschmer Glotta III 335). Schrader KZ X 461–462 (to Gk αἰγανέη ‘hunting spear’); Kuhn KZ LXXI 151 (to *akranan); Torp-Falk 1; Holthausen AEEW 2; Pokorny I 13; Vries ANEW 96; Frisk I 31; Zalizniak II 205; Pisani St. balt. X 52 (to OPrus ansonis ‘oak’); Onions 619; Bammesberger Nom. 195; Griepentrog Wurzelnomina 21–32; Köbler 708; Kluge-Seebold 207.

OE ál ‘flame’. Despite phonetic difficulties, probably from *aiđlan connected with *aiđaz (Sievers IF IV 339). Derivationally close to MIr áel ‘lime’ < *aidh-lo-. Fick KZ I (NF) 3–4 (to Lat ad-oleō ‘to burn’); Johansson Zd Ph XXXI 285–288 (influenced by IE *alēto: Skt neut. alāta- ‘fire-brand, coal’); Wood JEGP XIII 500 (to OE ile ‘hard skin’); Holthausen AEEW 3; Pokorny I 12; Vries ANEW 99–100.
ON eldr ‘fire’, OE æled ‘fire, conflagration’, OS ēld ‘fire’. Derived from *ailjanan. Torp-Falk 3–4 (to W ailwyd ‘hearth’ < *ailētā); Holthausen AEEW 10; Pokorny I 12; Vries ANEW 99–100; Onions 38.
OE ælan ‘to kindle, to set on fire, to burn’. Derived from *ailan. Torp-Falk 3; Holthausen AEEW 10; Pokorny I 12.

ON eimr ‘reek, vapor’, OE ám ‘reed, slay of a weaver’s loom’, E dial. oam ‘warm wind’. Continues *ai-mo- or, better, *aidh-smo-, eventually related to *aidh- ‘to burn’, cf. in particular Av aēsma- ‘firewood’, Lith íesmë id. Bartholomae 26–27; Holthausen AEEW 3; Pokorny I 11–12; Vries ANEW 96; Onions 308.

ON eimyrja ‘embers’, OE æmyrie, æmerze id., MLG ēmere id., OHG eimuria ‘pyre, hot ash’. Derived from *aimaz (Kluge KZ XXVI 84). Torp- Falk 32; Holthausen AEEW 11; Vries ANEW 96; Onions 308; Kluge-Seebold 34 (compound of *aimaz and *usjōn > ON poet. ysja ‘fire’).
ON ein-eygr ‘one-eyed’, OE án-eáze id., MLG ein-ōge id., OHG ein-ougi id. Compound of *ainaz and *auzjaz. Carr Nom. comp. 64.
ON ein-borinn ‘only begotten’, OE án-boren id., OHG ein-boran ‘unique, sole’. Compound of *ainaz and participle of *beranan. Carr Nom. comp. 92 (calque of Lat unigenitus).

ON ein-dagi ‘term for pay’, OE án-daza ‘fixed day, time appointed, day appointed for hearing a cause’, OS ēn-dago ‘day of death’. Compound of *ainaz and *đazaz. Krogmann Eng S LXX 40–43 (compares *ain- with Skt inóti ‘to advance upon, to press upon’; cf. *ferinō); Torp-Falk 199; Holthausen AEEW 4; Carr Nom. comp. 58; Pokorny I 241–242; Lehmann GED 86.

Goth ain-falþs ‘in one piece, simple’, ON ein-faldr id., OE ánfeald ‘simple, single, one, alone’, OFris enfald ‘singular, single’, OS ēn-fald id., OHG ein-falt ‘pure, single’. Compound of *ainaz and *falđaz. Feist 23; Carr Nom. comp. 64–65; Lehmann GED 16; Heidermanns 187–188; Kluge-Seebold 211; Boutkan OFED s.v.
ON ein-fœttr ‘onelegged’, OE án-féte id. Compound of *ainaz and *fōtjaz (see *fōtz ~ *fōtuz). Carr Nom. comp. 65.

Goth ainaha ‘only, sole’ (< *ainaxaz), ON einigr, eingi ‘none, any’, OE æniz ‘any, anyone’, OFris ēnich ‘only’, OS ēnig id., OHG sbst. einago ‘hermit, monk’. Derived from *ainaz. Cf. a similar derivation in Lat ūnicus id. (Solmsen PBB XXVII 356). Torp-Falk 3, 557 (to Slav *inokъ ‘monk’ but *-okъ < *ok⁄o-); Holthausen AEEW 11; Walde-Hofmann II 821; Feist 22; Pokorny I 286; Vries ANEW 97; Onions 41; Lehmann GED 16; Ross-Berns Numerals 656–657; Kluge-Seebold 211– 212; Boutkan OFED s.v.
ON ein-hendr ‘singlehanded’, OE án-hende id., OHG ein-henti id. Compound of *ainaz and *xanđjaz (see *xanđuz). Carr Nom. comp. 65.
ON ein-harär ‘courageous, constant’, OS ēn-hard ‘hostile, evil’, OHG ein-harti ‘courageous, constant’. Compound of *ainaz and *xarđuz. Carr Nom. comp. 63.

ON ein-hleypr ‘single, unmarried’, OE án-hlíepe id., late OS ēnlōpe ‘alone, single’. Derived from *ainaz and *xlaupan. Holthausen AEEW 163; Carr Nom. comp. 62; Seebold 260; Matzel Festschr. Schröder 259–261, KZ CV 129; Heidermanns 293.
ON einka- ‘only, special’ (in compounds), Du ēnec ‘alone’. Derived from *ainaz. Cf. a similar pattern in Slav *jьnogъ ‘single’ and Cypr ǫggia. Vries ANEW 99; Trubachev ESSJa VIII 231; Lehmann GED 16.

Goth ainakls ‘alone, standing alone’, ON name of a sea-king Ekkill, OSwed sbst. ænkill ‘widower’, MLG enkel ‘single’. Derived from *ain(a)kai. Grienberger Got. 14 (from *ain-ak-l-az, cf. *akanan); Feist 22–23; Vries ANEW 99; Lehmann GED 16; Ross-Berns Numerals 658.

Goth ainlif ‘eleven’, ON ellifu id., OE endleofan id., OFris andlova, elleva id., OS elleban id., OHG einlif id. Most of WGmc forms have a secondary -(a)n. Compound of *ainaz and *lif- ‘left’ (see *laibjanan). A similar structure is found in Lith vienuólika id. in which -lika is identical with Gmc *lif- (Grimm Germania I 19, DG II 946–947). Meillet MSL XV 259; Zupitza Gutt. 12; Torp-Falk 3; Bezzenberger KZ XLIV 133–135; Holthausen AEEW 91; Feist 23; Porzig Gliederung 146; Pokorny I 670; Chemodanov SGGJa I 74–75; Fraenkel 1239–1240; Vries ANEW 101; Onions 306; Lehmann GED 17; Ross-Berns Numerals 593–596 (*aini- in Goth); Kluge-Seebold 217; Boutkan OFED s.v.
ON ellepti ‘eleventh’, OE ællyfta, endlefta id., OFris andlofta id., OS ellifto id., OHG einlifto id. Derived from *aina-lifa ~ *aina-liba. Ross-Berns Numerals 633–634; Boutkan OFED s.v.
OSwed ēnlīker ‘alone’, OE ænlíc ‘singular, incomparable, excellent’. Derived from *ainaz. Ross-Berns Numerals 663.
ON ein-nættr ‘one night old’, OE adv. án-nihte id., MHG einnehte id. Compound of *ainaz and *naxtjaz (see *naxtz). Carr Nom. comp. 65.
ON ein-ráär ‘self-willed’, OE án-ræd ‘one-minded, unanimous, agreed’, OHG ein-rāti ‘secret’. Compound of *ainaz and *rēđaz. Carr Nom. comp. 94–95 (parallel formations).
ON ein-stapi ‘a kind of fern, Pteris aquilina’, OE án-stapa ‘lone wanderer’. Compound of *ainaz and *stapan. Carr Nom. comp. 87 (parallel formations).
ON ein-valdi ‘monarch’, OE án-wealda ‘sovereign, governor’. Compound of *ainaz and *walđōn. Carr Nom. comp. 87 (calque of Lat monarchus in OE).
ON ein-vígi ‘single combat’, OE án-wíz id., OHG ein-wīgi id. Compound of *ainaz and *wīzjan (see *wīzan). Carr Nom. comp. 58.

Goth ains ‘one’, ON einn id., OE án id., OFris ān, ēn, on id., OS ēn id., OHG ein id. Identical with Lat ūnus id., OIr óen id., OPrus ains id. further related to Skt éka- id., Av aēvō id., Gk οἶος id. Bartholomae 22–24; Pedersen Kelt. Gr. I 57, 447; Torp-Falk 3; Holthausen AEEW 4; Walde-Hofmann II 821; Feist 24; Mayrhofer I 126; Pokorny I 281–286; Vries ANEW 97; Frisk II 367; Onions 627; Toporov PJa I 63–64; Lehmann GED 17; Bammesberger Nom. 227; Ross-Berns Numerals 559–561; Kluge-Seebold 210; Boutkan OFED s.v.

Goth airus ‘emissary, messenger’, ON árr, ǫrr ‘messenger’, OE ár ‘messenger, herald’, OS ēr ‘messenger’ (< *airiz). Probably, a derivative of *airi. Grienberger Goten 15; Torp- Falk 3; Wiedemann BB XXVIII 46; Wood MLN XIII 82 (from *ei- ‘to go’); Bugge PBB XXIV 430–433 (to *airō); Meringer IF XVIII 249–251 (to *arjanan); Holthausen PBB LXVI 267 (to ON írask ‘to be rumored abroad’), AEEW 6; Feist 26–27; Vries ANEW 14; Zalizniak II 198; Lehmann GED 19; Bammesberger Nom. 66.

Goth air ‘early’, ON ár id., OE ær ‘previously, before’, OS ēr id., OHG ēr id. Continues loc. *aƒ(e)ri. Related to Av ayar 6 ‘day’, Gk ἠέριος ‘early’. Cf. comparative *airiz > Goth airis ‘before, earlier’, ON ær id., OE ær ‘early, former’, OFris ēr ‘before, earlier’, OS ēr id., OHG ēr id. and superlative *airistaz > OE ærest ‘first’, OS ērist id., OHG adv. ērist id. Fick KZ XXII 95–96; Bartholomae 157; Torp-Falk 3; Holthausen AEEW 12; Feist 24–25; Schulze Kl. Schr. 825, 833; Pokorny I 12; Vries ANEW 12, 681; Frisk I 624; Onions 323–324; Lehmann GED 18; Kluge-Seebold 205–206; Boutkan OFED s.v.
ON pl. ár-dagar ‘days of yore’, OE pl. ær-dazas id., OS pl. ēr-dagos id. Cf. also OHG adj. ēr-tagīg ‘of yesterday, before the dawn’. Compound of *airi and *đazaz. Carr Nom. comp. 58.
Goth airinon ‘to be an emissary, to be a messenger’, ON árna ‘to intercede, to pray’. Derived from *airaz ~ *airuz. Feist 25; Vries ANEW 14.
ON ár-vakr ‘early awake’, OHG ēr-wackar id. Compound of *airi and *wakraz. Carr Nom. comp. 63.
ON ár ‘oar’, OE ár id. Of unknown origin. Bugge PBB XXIV 429 (to *rōanan); Torp-Falk 3; Holthausen AEEW 7; Pokorny I 298 (to Gk oǫaj ‘handle of rudder’); Vries ANEW 12; Onions 619; Lehmann GED 19.

OE ascian ‘to inquire, to ask’, OFris āskia ‘to demand, to claim’, OS ēscon ‘to promote, to further’, OHG eiscōn ‘to search, to look for’. Related to Skt éṣati ‘to seek’, iccháti, ‘to seek, to wish’ < *is-s˚e-, Av aēš- id., Arm hayc'em ‘to beg’, Lith íeškau, ieškóti ‘to seek’, Slav *iskati id. (Fick BB XVI 170–171). Bugge KZ III 40–41; Bartholomae 28–31; Torp-Falk 4; Trautmann BSW 67; Holthausen AEEW 7; Mayrhofer I 85; Pokorny I 16; Fraenkel 182; Onions 54; Trubachev ESSJa VIII 238–239; Kluge-Seebold 366; Boutkan OFED s.v.

ON eiskra ‘to roar, to rage’. A denominative based on the unattested *aiskran. Related to Lith áiškus ‘clear’ and derivationally close to Slav *jьskra ‘sparkle’ (< neut.). Persson Beiträge 950; Johansson IF XIX 112–114 (to Lat aestus ‘heat’); Fraenkel 3; Pokorny I 16–17; Trubachev ESSJa VIII 239–240.

OHG eiz ‘abscess’. Related to Arm aitnum ‘to swell’, Gk οφιδάω id. (Fick KZ I 5–6). Structurally close to Gk neut. οἶδος ‘swelling, tumor’. Torp- Falk 2; Pokorny I 774; Frisk II 357– 358; Kluge-Seebold 214.
ON sbst. eitill ‘nodule in stone’, EFris eitel ‘fast, raging’, OHG sbst. eizzala ‘gallnut’. Derived from *aitaz. Bezzenberger BB XXVII 172; Vries ANEW 98; Kluge-Seebold 214.

OE æte ‘oats’, OS eriwit ‘pea’ (< *arwa-aitō), OHG araweiz id. (< *arwaaitō). May be related to *aitaz (Wood JEGP XIII 500). For the semantic development cf. Gk oǫdaj ‘unripe fig’. Wiedemann BB XXVIII 46, 50 (reconstructs *araw-aitwith suff. *-ait-); Holthausen AEEW 7; Pokorny I 774; Onions 619; Kluge-Seebold 228.

ON eitr ‘poison’, OE ætor id., OFris āter ‘pus’, ātter id., OS ēttor id., OHG eitar id. Derived from *aitaz. If originally from *‘swelling’, can be directly compared with Slav *îdro ‘womb, lap’. Torp-Falk 2; Holthausen AEEW 8; Pokorny I 774; Vries ANEW 98; Zalizniak I 138; Kluge-Seebold 214 (to Slav *îdъ ‘poison’). *aiþa-stabaz m: ON eiä-stafr ‘wording of an oath’, OE áä-stæf ‘oath’, OS ēd-staf id., MHG eit-stap id. Compound of *aiþaz and *stabaz. Carr Nom. comp. 46.

Goth aiþs ‘oath’, ON eiär id. (gen. sg. reflects *aiþiz), OE áä id., OFris ēth, ēd id., OS ēth id., OHG eid id. Identical with OIr óeth id. and, probably, with Gk οἶτος ‘fate’, OPhryg oito- id. (Meringer IF XVIII 295; Grienberger Goten 16). These forms could be (but not necessarily are) derived from *ei- ‘to walk’ (Brugmann IF XXXVII 241) or related to Hitt ḫai- ‘to believe, to trust’ (Puhvel III 9–10). Arbois de Jubainville MSL VII 292 (from Celtic, cf. OIr oeth ‘oath’); Osthoff BB XXIV 208–209 (to Gk αἶνος ‘consent’); Torp-Falk 2; Bartholomae IF XII 30 (compares οἶτος with Av aēta- ‘punishment’); Meringer IF XVIII 295 (to *ei- ‘to go’); Pedersen Kelt. Gr. I 58 (to Lat ūtor ‘to need’); Holthausen AEEW 8; Feist 29; Tucker Notes 36 (to Gk ǫthlow ‘abiding’); Lane Language IX 246 (from Celtic); Krahe Spr. Vorz. 134; Porzig Gliederung 121; Pokorny I 11, 295; Chemodanov SGGJa I 96; Vries ANEW 95; Zalizniak II 185; Onions 619; Frisk II 370–371; Benveniste 393, 433; Chantraine 788; Scardigli Goten 53–54 (against the Celtic etymology); Polomé JIES XI 281–298; Lehmann GED 20; Beekes Laryngeals 128; Bammesberger Nom. 78; Orel Phrygian 449; Kluge-Seebold 207–208; Boutkan OFED s.v.

Goth aiþei ‘mother’, OHG fuotar-eidī ‘nanny’. Cf. also *aiþōn ‘mother’ > ON eiäa. Derived from *aiþaz (Mezger KZ LXXVI 85–86: a legally acknowledged mother as opposed to a concubine-mother). Uhlenbeck Btrg. Gesch. XXVII 115 (to Basque aita ‘father’); Grienberger Goten 16 (to Gk fut. ὀίσω ‘to carry’); Torp-Falk 2; Feist 28, PBB LIII 397–400 (from non-existent Illyr OHYH ‘mater Dea’); Krahe PBB LVII 426–428 (against Feist); Vries ANEW 95; Zalizniak I 157; Lehmann GED 20.

Goth aiws ‘age, eternity’, OE æw, á ‘law’, OFris a- ‘law’ (in cmpn.), OS ēo id., OHG fem. ēwa id. Cf. also ON ævi ‘time, eternity’ < *aiwīn. Acc. sg. *aiwin is used as an adverb ‘ever’: Goth aiw, ON æ, OE á, OS ēo, OHG io. Related to Skt āyu- ‘vital power’, Av āyū ‘lifespan’, Gk αἰών ‘vital power’, Lat aeuus, aeuum ‘age’. Vogt Btrg. Gesch. LVIII 1–66; Zupitza Gutt. 74 (to *ei- ‘to go’); Bartholomae 333; Torp-Falk 4; Holthausen AEEW 8; Walde-Hofmann I 21; Benveniste BSL XXXVIII 103–112; Feist 30–31; Tucker Notes 36 (to Lat iūs ‘right, law’); Mayrhofer I 77; Pokorny I 17; Frisk I 49; Zalizniak II 214; Onions 66; Lehmann GED 22; Beck Gedenkschr. Güntert 47–48, 53–54; Kluge- Seebold 238, 410; Boutkan OFED s.v.
ON lang-æä ‘long lasting’, OHG ēwida ‘eternity’. Derived from *aiwaz ~ *aiwiz. Vries ANEW 680.

Goth aiz ‘copper’, ON eir ‘brass’, OE ár ‘ore, brass, copper’, OS ēr ‘ore’, OHG ēr ‘ore, copper’. Related to Skt áyas- ‘metal, iron’, Av ayah- id., Lat aes ‘ore’ continuing IE *aƒos-. Bartholomae 159; Torp-Falk 4; Holthausen AEEW 7; Walde-Hofmann I 19–20; Walde-Hofmann I 19–20; Feist 31; Mayrhofer I 46; Pokorny I 15; Chemodanov SGGJa I 90; Vries ANEW 97; Zalizniak I 138; Lindeman Verschärfung 11.4; Gamkrelidze-Ivanov 709–710; Lehmann GED 22; Bammesberger Nom. 212; Kluge-Seebold 206.

ON eir ‘peace, clemency’ (< *aizjō), OE ár ‘honor, glory, kindness’, OFris ēre ‘honor’, OS ēr, ēra ‘honor, help’, OHG ēra ‘honor’. Related to Osk abl. pl. aisusis ‘sacrifice’, Marruc dat. pl. aisos ‘gods’, Umbr esono- ‘divine, sacred’ and probably to Gk aǫdomai ‘to respect’ < *aiz-d- (Bezzenberger BB IV 313). Perhaps, further connected with *aiskōjan. Torp-Falk 4; Holthausen AEEW 7; Feist 27–28; Pokorny I 16; Vries ANEW 97; Frisk I 34–35; Zalizniak II 191; Peeters IF XCIV 295; Kluge-Seebold 206; Boutkan OFED s.v.

Crim. Goth nom. pl. ada ‘egg’, ON egg id., OE æz id., OS ei id., OHG ei id. Related (without traces of *⁄ and with Verschärfung) to OPers xāya id., Gk ᾠόν id., Arm ju id., W wy id., Slav *ajьce, *aje id. Molz Btrg. Gesch. XXXI 324–325; Torp-Falk 1; Holthausen AEEW 9–10; Feist 2; Pokorny I 783–784; Vries ANEW 94–95; Frisk II 1150; Zalizniak II 223; Schindler Sprache XV 144–167; Lindeman SL XIV/2 10–11, Verschärfung 10.2; Onions 303; Trubachev ESSJa I 61–62; Lehmann Phon. 36–46, GED 2; Kluge- Seebold 206.

Goth ajuk-duþs ‘eternity’, OE éce ‘eternal, perpetual’. Derived from *aiwaz ~ *aiwiz. Probably derivationally similar to Lat iūgis ‘permanent, perpetual’ (Seebold Etym. 93–94). Torp-Falk 4; Bréal MSL IV 140–141 (Goth suffix < Lat); Feist 32; Holthausen AEEW 8; Walde-Hofmann I 727; Pokorny I 17; Bammesberger AE 12, Nom. 262; Lehmann GED 23; Heidermanns 97.

ON aka ‘to drive (in a vehicle)’, OE acan ‘to ache, to pain’. Despite the semantic discrepancy, these forms are historically identical (Seebold 74–75). Cf. the meanings of Lat agitō ‘to put in motion, to drive, to rouse up, to disturb’. Related to Toch B āk- ‘to lead, to guide, to drive’, Skt ájati ‘to drive’, Av azaiti id., Arm acem ‘to lead, to bring’, Gk ἄγω ‘to lead’, Lat agō ‘to drive’, OW agit ‘to go’. Zupitza Gutt. 195; Wood AJPhil XXVII 59 (OE to Skt āgas- ‘transgression, fault’ Gk ἄγος ‘pollution, guilt, expiation’); Loewe KZ XLVIII 99–100 M (same as Wood); Bartholomae 223–224; Torp-Falk 7; Holthausen AEEW 2; Walde-Hofmann I 23–24; Porzig Gliederung 120; Mayrhofer I 23; Pokorny I 4; Vries ANEW 3; Frisk I 18; Onions 8–9; Adams TB 36.
Run prop. aka R, Identical with Gk ἄγός ‘leader, chief’. Further related to *akanan. Frisk I 18; Bammesberger Nom. 51.

Goth ak ‘but, however’, OE ac id., OS ak id., OHG oh id. Related to Gk ἄγε ‘now, then’, Lat age id. (Holthausen IF XVII 458–459). Brugmann KVG 616 (from *aþa/*aþi and particle *k(e) as in Goth mik); Holthausen AEEW 1; Feist 32; Sturtevant GR XXVI 57 (to IE *a˚- ‘sharp’ followed by a particle); Lehmann GED 23.
ON akr-karl ‘plowman, reaper’, OE æcer-ceorl id. Compound of *akraz and *karlaz. Carr Nom. comp. 80 (parallel formations).
ON akr-maär ‘plowman, farmer’, OE æcer-man id., MLG acker-man id., OHG ackar-man id. Compound of *akraz and *mannz. Carr Nom. comp. 71 (parallel formations).

Goth akran ‘fruit’, ON akarn ‘acorn’, OE æcern id., MLG eckeren id. Derived from *akraz (Torp-Falk 16). Zimmerapud Zupitza Gutt. 213 (derivationally close to Celt *agrīnƒā ‘sloe’: Ir áirne, W aeron ‘fruit’ MBret irin, and other continuants of IE *ōg-); Lidén IF XVIII 503–506 (to Lith úoga ‘berry’ Slav *agoda id.); Uhlenbeck TNTL XXV 249; Pedersen Kelt. Gr. I 103, 143; Holthausen AEEW 9; Feist 32–33; Pokorny I 773, KZ L 46–48; Vries ANEW 4; Zalizniak I 139; Lehmann GED 24; Bammesberger Nom. 83; Kluge-Seebold 204.
ON akr-verk ‘fieldwork’, OE æcer-weorc id. MLG acker-werk id. Compound of *akraz and *werkan. Carr Nom. comp. 71 (parallel formations).

Goth akrs ‘field’, ON akr id., OE æcer id., OFris ekker id., OS akkar id., OHG ackar id. Related to Skt ájra- ‘plain, field’, Gk ἀγρός ‘field’, Arm art id., Lat ager id. continuing IE *a·ros, a derivative of *a·- ‘to drive’ (see *akanan). Torp- Falk 7; Brugmann IF XVIII 132; Pedersen KZ XXXIX 352; Krause KZ LV 312–313; Holthausen AEEW 8; Walde- Hofmann I 22; Feist 33; Trier PBB LXVII 126; Mayrhofer I 23; Pokorny I 6; Vries ANEW 4; Frisk I 16; Zalizniak I 129; Seebold 74; Lehmann GED 24; Bammesberger Nom. 74; Lloyd- Springer I 40–42; Kluge-Seebold 13; Boutkan OFED s.v.

OS akus ‘axe’, OHG ackus id. In dat. sg. WGmc keeps traces of a root stem. Probably related to Gk ἀξίνη id., Lat ascia ‘axe of carpenters and masons’ < *acsiā (Janzén Bu Z 20: reconstructs IE *ag- parallel to *a˚-). Zupitza Gutt. 89; Kretschmer Einleitung 164; Walde- Hofmann I 71–72; Pokorny I 9; Frisk I 115–116; Zalizniak II 205; Onions 66; Lloyd-Springer I 44; Kluge-Seebold 70 (*akwesjō).

Goth aqizi ‘axe’, ON øx id. (*akusjō), OE æcs, æx id., OFris axa id. Derived from *akusiz. Zupitza Gutt. 89; Torp-Falk 7; Holthausen AEEW 9; Feist 54; Porzig Gliederung 133; Pokorny I 9; Vries ANEW 683; Zalizniak II 205; Lehmann GED 40; Kluge-Seebold 70.
ON al-hvítr ‘quite white’, OE eal(l)-hwít id., OS ala-hwīt id. Compound of *alaz and *xwītaz. Carr Nom. comp. 62.
ON al-máttigr ‘almighty’, OE eall-mihtiz id., OFris elmechtich id., OS ala-mahtīg id., OHG ala-mahtīg id. Compound of *alaz and *maxtizaz. Carr Nom. comp. 91–92 (calque of Lat omnipotens).

Goth ala-mans ‘(all) mankind’, ON al-manna- ‘general, common’ (in compounds), MLG al-man ‘mankind’, OHG ethn. Ala-man. Cf. Ἀλα-μανοί, Ala-manni = OHG Ala-man. Compound of *alaz and *mannz. Carr Nom. comp. 56–57; Schönfeld PVN 6–8; Feist 34; Lehmann GED 25.

Goth alan ‘to grow on, to feed on’, ON ala ‘to beget, to bear’, OE alan ‘to nourish, to grow, to produce’. Related to Lat alō ‘to nourish’, OIr alim id. Schweizer KZ I 558 (to Lat part. altus); Torp-Falk 20; Holthausen AEEW 3; Walde-Hofmann I 31–32; Feist 34; Jóhannesson IEW 37–38; Pokorny I 26; Vries ANEW 4–5; Seebold 75–77; Lehmann GED 25.
ON al-þjóä ‘the commons’, OS ala-thioda ‘mankind’. Compound of *alaz and *þeuđō. Carr Nom. comp. 59.
ON al-valdr ‘sovereign king’, OE eal-wealda id., OS ala-waldo id., MHG al-walte id. Compound of *alaz and *walđan. Lehmann GED 29 (calque of Gk παντοκράτωρ or Lat omnipotens).

Goth ala-mans ‘mankind’, ON al-vitr ‘all-wise’, OE eal-mihtig ‘almighty’, OFris al-evna ‘in a similar way’, OS alahwīt ‘quite white’, OHG ala-wāri ‘friendly, benign’. Usually compared with Toch B āl ‘entire, uncastrated (?)’ and to Lat ollus ‘ille’, OIr oll ‘amplus’. However, more probably, immediately derived from *alanan with a semantic development similar to that of Lat tōtus ‘all’ (to *teu- ‘to swell’) and Lith vīsas ‘all’, Slav *vьsь id. (to Lith veĩsti ‘to breed’), see Mikkola St. Balt. III 132; Pisani Paideia VIII/4–5 292. Torp-Falk 20; Holthausen AEEW 10; Walde-Hofmann II 206–207; Feist 33; Pokorny I 24–25; Seebold 75–76; Lehmann GED 24; Adams TB 53–54.

ON ǫlpt ‘swan’ (traces of a root stem), OHG elbiz id. Cf. also OE ilfetu id. < *albet(j)ō. Derivative of *albh- ‘white’ similar to Slav *olbǫdь ~ *elbędь ‘swan’. Pictet KZ IV 124–126; Osthoff IF VIII 65–66; Torp- Falk 22 (adduce Gk ἐλεφιτίς ‘a kind of fish’); Holthausen AEEW 186; Porzig Gliederung 144; Pokorny I 30; Chemodanov SGGJa I 80; Vries ANEW 101; Zalizniak II 205; Stang LS 31; Trubachev ESSJa VI 19.

Burg *alfs ‘elf’, ON alfr ‘nightmare, elf’, OE ælf ‘elf, genius, incubus’, MLG alf ‘evil spirit’, OHG alb id. Of uncertain origin. Kuhn KZ IV 110 (to Skt ṛbhú- ‘clever, skilful’); Wadstein Festschr. Bugge 152–155 (to *albh- ‘white’); Torp-Falk 21; Saussureapud Mastrelli St G XIII 5–13 (to the name of the Alps); Holthausen AEEW 186; Pokorny I 30; Vries ANEW 5–6; Köbler 708; Kluge-Seebold 24–25.

ON rvn. Elfr, Swed dial. älv ‘deep river-bed’, OE rvn. Ielf, MLG elve ‘river-bed’, MHG rvn. Elbe. Usually connected with Lat albus ‘white’, Gk ἀλφός ‘dull-white leprosy’ (Schulze Kl. Schr. 120). However, the meaning in Swed and MLG makes this etymology not very reliable and implies the existence of a Gmc appellative *albiz ‘river, river-bed’ hardly derived from a color adjective. Torp- Falk 21; Ekblom SSUF 1939/13 1–15 (to Swed dial. älv and Lat Alpes); Walde- Hofmann I 26–27; Rozwadowski Nazwy 174 (on hydronyms including that of the Elbe); Pokorny I 30–31; Vries ANEW 100; Frisk I 81–82.

Goth aldomo ‘old age’, OE eald-dóm ‘age’, OHG alt-tuom id. Derived from *alđaz (Johansson Festschr. Noreen 457); for the derivational pattern cf. *wīsa-đomaz. Grienberger Goten 18 (reconstructs superl. *aldumin, to *alanan); Endzelin KZ LII 124; Feist 34–35; Kluge Nom. Stamm. 81 (suffix *-ōman identical with Lat -āmen); Lehmann GED 25.

Crim.-Goth alt ‘old’, OE eald id., OFris ald id., OS ald id., OHG alt id. Derived from *alanan. Derivationally similar to Gk ἄναλτος ‘sateless’, Lat altus ‘high’ (Brugmann PBB XLIII 310–324). Brugmann PBB XLIII 310–324; Torp- Falk 20; Walde-Hofmann I 31–32; Feist 40; Holthausen AEEW 83–84; Hofmann IF XXXVIII 179–183 (semantic parallel in Lat decem annos natus); Walde-Hofmann I 31–32; Porzig Festschr. Debrunner 343–349; Pokorny I 26; Frisk I 102; Onions 625; Lloyd- Springer I 171–173; Seebold 76; Lloyd-Springer I 171–173; Bammesberger Nom. 250–251; Heidermanns 97–98; Kluge-Seebold 30–31; Boutkan OFED s.v.

Goth alds ‘generation, age, life-time’, ON ǫld ‘age, time’, OE pl. ilde ‘men’, OS pl. eldi ‘people’. Related to *alđaz, *alđran. Pokorny I 26–27; Brugmann Btrg. Gesch. XLIII 310–324; Torp-Falk 20; Holthausen AEEW 186; Feist 35; Vogt Btrg. Gesch. LVIII 11–18; Vries ANEW 686; Zalizniak I 150; Seebold 76; Lehmann GED 26; Bammesberger Nom. 144.

ON elli ‘old age’, OE ildu ‘age, life’, OFris elde id., OS eldī id., OHG eltī id. Derived from *alđaz. Torp-Falk 20; Holthausen AEEW 186; Pokorny I 27; Vries ANEW 100; Zalizniak I 157; Heidermanns 98.
ON eldask ‘to grow old’, OE ildan ‘to delay, to tarry, to defer’, MHG elten ‘to make old’. Derived from *alđaz. Holthausen AEEW 186; Vries ANEW 99; Heidermanns 98.

ON alda ‘wave’, Norw dial. olda ‘trough’, Swed dial. àlla ‘long and narrow hollow’. Related to Slav *oldьja ‘boat’, Lith aldijā, eldijā ‘river boat’ (Lidén BSB 3–4, 9–10). Torp-Falk 21; Meillet RES VII 7–8; Trautmann BSW 6; Pokorny I 31–32; Fraenkel 120; Vasmer-Trubachev II 510.
ON pl. aldr-dagar ‘everlasting life’, OE ealdor-dæz ‘day of life’. Compound of *alđran and *đazaz. Carr Nom. comp. 80 (parallel formations).
ON aldr-lag ‘death, destruction’, OE ealdorlæz id., OS aldarlagu id. Compound of *alđran and *lazan. Carr Nom. comp. 45.

Goth adj. fram-aldrs ‘aged, advanced in age’, ON aldr ‘age, life, old age’ (< *alđraz), OE ealdor ‘age, life’, OS aldar id., OHG altar id. Related to *alđaz. Continues IE *altrom, cf. OIr altram ‘nourishment’ (cf. OIr altru ‘foster-father’ W athraw ‘teacher’), or *altros as in ἀλτρός: μισθός, Hes. (Brugmann PBB XLIII 317). Further connected with IE *al- ‘to grow’. Pedersen Kelt. Gr. I 137, II 45; Torp- Falk 20; Holthausen AEEW 84; Feist 164; Vogt PBB LVIII 11–18; Pokorny I 26–27; Vries ANEW 5; Zalizniak II 165; Lehmann GED 124; Lloyd-Springer I 173–174; Bammesberger Nom. 86; Kluge-Seebold 31.

ON elgr ‘elk’, OE eolh id., OHG elaho id. Accentual variants continuing IE *ol˚is ~ *el˚is (Gk ἄλκη id., Lat alcēs id., Slav *olsь id.) (Much ZDADL XXXIX 26; Osthoff Etym. I 318). Kirchhoff KZ I 39 (to IE *ṛ˚to- ‘bear’); Försterman KZ I 493 (same as Kirchhoff); Torp-Falk 26; Holthausen AEEW 92; Walde-Hofmann I 28; Pokorny I 303; Vries ANEW 100; Frisk I 75; Onions 306; Stang LS 33–34; Kluge-Seebold 215.

Goth alhs ‘temple’, ON -áll (in cmpn.), OE ealh ‘residence, temple’, OS alah ‘temple’. The consonantal stem appears to be a secondary modification of *alxaz. Identical with Lith afikas, alkā ‘holy grove’ (Mikkola BB XXII 241 Būga II 148–149). Cf. also Gk ἀλκή ‘strength, defence’, ἄλσος ‘gorve, sacred grove’ (Hoffmann BB XXV 106)? Presumably further connected with Skt ρáκἑατι ‘to protect, to guard’, Gk ἀλέξω ‘to ward off, to defend’. Kirchhoff KZ I 39; Thumb KZ XVI 188–190; Meringer Wu S IX 108–109 (to Lat ulciscor ‘to avenge, to punish’); Rooth Wortst. 10–11 (derived from *alanan); Zupitza Gutt. 128; Torp- Falk 21 (to Gk ἀλκή); Holthausen AEEW 84; Feist 36–37; Porzig Gliederung 97; Mayrhofer III 30; Pokorny I 32; Vries ANEW 6–7; Frisk I 75; Fraenkel 7; Zalizniak II 205; Stang LS 13; Lehmann GED 27; Bammesberger Nom. 195; Griepentrog Wurzelnomina 33–40.
ON ǫln-bogi ‘elbow’, OE eln-boza id., MLG ellen-boge id., OHG elin-bogo id. Derived from *alinō and *buzōn. Torp-Falk 273; Carr Nom. comp. 46; Vries ANEW 686; Onions 305; Kluge-Seebold 218.

Goth aleina ‘cubit’ (with *-ī-), ON ǫln ‘cubit, forearm’ (partly *aliniz), OE eln ‘ell’, OFris ielne id., OS elina id., OHG elina id. Related to Gk ὠλένη id., Lat ulna id., OIr uilenn ‘angle’, W elin ‘elbow’, OCorn elin id., Bret ilin id. (< *olīnā, cf. the Gothic form), Alb llërë ~ llanë id. < *olenā. Kluge KZ XXVI 101 (on -ei- in Goth); Roethe SAW [1919] 793 (reconstructs *-ī-); Pedersen Kelt. Gr. II 59, 111; Torp-Falk 21; Holthausen AEEW 90; Walde-Hofmann II 812; Feist 35; Pokorny I 307-308; Dybo VSJa 25 (pretonic shortening of *ō- in Gmc); Vries ANEW 686; Zalizniak II 192; Frisk II 1146; Onions 306; Orel AED 238; Lehmann GED 26; Schrijver LL 352 (follows Dybo); Kluge-Seebold 217–218.

Goth *alisa ‘alder’ (> Sp aliso id.), OS elira id., elis- (in cmpn.), OHG elira, erila id. Cf. also ON elri < *alizjan, alri < *alizan. Identical with Maced ἄλιζα: ἡλεύκη τῶν δένδρων (Hes.), Slav *elьxa ‘alder’ Further connected with Lat alnus ‘alder’. Lith afiksnis id. Persson Beiträge 893–895; Kretschmer Glotta XV 305–307; Torp-Falk 26, 560; Gamillscheg 27 (reconstruction of Goth); Walde-Hofmann I 31; Pokorny I 302–303; Chemodanov SSGJa I 57; Fraenkel 8; Frisk I 73; Vries ANEW 101; Zalizniak II 214; Onions 23; Trubachev ESSJa VI 23–24; Kluge- Seebold 230.

Goth alja-leiko ‘otherwise’, ON el-liga, el-ligar id., OE comp. el-lícor, elcor id., OFris comp. ekkor ‘apart from that, moreover’, OS comp. elcor ‘otherwise’, OHG comp. elihhōr ‘further’. Based on the unattested adj. *alja-līkaz, compound of *aljaz and *līkaz. Holthausen AEEW 89; Feist 37–38; Vries ANEW 101; Lehmann GED 27–28; Boutkan OFED s.v.
Goth aljan ‘to fatten’, Norw dial. elja id. Derived from *alanan. Feist 38; Pokorny I 26.

Goth aljan ‘avidity, eagerness, zeal’, ON fem. eljan ‘endurance’, OE ellen ‘strength, power, vigor’, OS ellean ‘courage’, OHG ellen ‘zeal’. Derived from *aljaz with a semantic development similar to that of Gk δεινός ‘fearful’ ~ δείδω ‘to be afraid’ going back to *d⁄ō ‘two’. Fick Spracheinh. 302 (to Lat alacer ‘lively, excited’); Osthoff Nom. Stamm. I 63–66 (same as Fick); Torp- Falk 20; Johansson Zfdt Ph XXXI 285– 289 (to IE *al- ‘to burn’); Froehde BB XX 185 (to Gk ἐλάω ‘to drive, to drive on’); Collitz SSN VIII 1–13 (to Gk ἀλύω ‘to be active’); Walde-Pokorny I 156 (to Lat alacer ‘lively, brisk, quick’); Holthausen AEEW 89; Feist 38; Pokorny I 25, 28; Vries ANEW 100; Frisk I 357–358; Zalizniak II 173; Lehmann GED 28 (agrees with Johansson); Puhvel III 52 (to Hitt ḫal⁄ammar ‘eagerness, zeal’).
ON elna ‘to wax, to grow’, OE elnian ‘to make strong, to strive with zeal’, OHG ellinōn ‘to strive with zeal’. Derived from *aljanan I. Holthausen AEEW 90; Vries ANEW 101.

ON al-vitr ‘supernatural being, Valkyria’, OE æl-wiht ‘strange creature, monster’. Compound of *aljaz (with analogical influence of *alaz) and *wixtiz (Sievers PBB XII 488). Wessén Festschr. Bugge 175 (non-syncopated form of ON elptr ‘swan’); Ólsen ANF XXXIX 114–115 (ON from *alfvitr); Carr Nom. comp. 32 (ON < OE); Vries ANEW 7.

Goth aljis ‘other’, OFris ili-, eleid. (in compounds). In other Germanic languages gen. sg. (meaning ‘otherwise, else’) is preserved, cf. OSwed äljes, OE elles, OFris elles, MDu els, OHG alles. Related to Arm ail id., Gk ἄλλος id., Lat alius id., Gaul alios id., OIr aile id. Pedersen Kelt. Gr. I 32, 69; Torp-Falk 22; Holthausen AEEW 90; Walde- Hofmann I 30–31; Feist 38–39; Pokorny I 25; Frisk I 76–77; Zalizniak II 173; Onions 307; Schmidt Studien 70; Lehmann GED 28–29; Kluge-Seebold 216–217; Boutkan OFED s.v. *aljōn I prep., adv.: Goth alja ‘but, except’, ON ella ‘otherwise, else’. Derived from *aljaz. Similar with Gk ἀλλά ‘but’, Arm ail ‘but (rather)’. Feist 37; Frisk I 76–77; Lehmann GED 27.
ON elja ‘concubine’, MDu elle ‘rival’, OHG ella ‘concubine’. Derived from *aljaz. Vries ANEW 100.
ON alka ‘sea bird, alca, awk’, OE ealce id. Of unknown origin. Lidén ANF XIII 30–31 (to Lat olor ‘swan’); Torp-Falk 22; Holthausen AEEW 83; Vries ANEW 6 (same as Lidén).
ON all-bjartr ‘very bright’, OE eall-beorht id. Compound of *allaz and *berxtaz. Carr Nom. comp. 63.
ON all-kaldr ‘very cold’, OE eal-ceald id. Compound of *allaz and *kalđaz. Carr Nom. comp. 63.

Goth all-waldands ‘ruler, almighty’, OS ala-waldand id., MHG al-waltende id. Participial form of a compound based on *alaz or *allaz and *walđanan. A calque from Lat omnipotens or Gk παντοκράτωρ cannot be excluded. Feist 40; Carr Nom. comp. 57; Seebold KZ LXXXII 79 (extension of *al(l)awalđaz ~ *al(l)a-walđan); Lehmann GED 29.

ON ǫl-værr ‘friendly, hospitable’, OE adv. eal-wer-líce ‘liberally, freely’, MLG al-wār ‘foolish’, OHG alawāri ‘friendly, hospitable, benign’. Cf. also Goth allawerei ‘simplicity, sincerity, total gareement’ Compound of *alazor, *allaz and *wērjaz. Carr Nom. comp. 62; Vries ANEW 687; Seebold IF LXXVIII 146–162; Kluge-Seebold 25.

Goth alls ‘all’, ON allr id., OE eall id., OFris alle, al id., OS al id., OHG al id. Continues *alnaz derivationally close to Osc allo ‘all’, OIr uile id. (Untermann IF LXIII 241–245). Further connected with *alaz. Kuhn KZ I 516; Meillet RG XXI 38 (from *alos with a secondary geminate); Torp-Falk 21; Holthausen AEEW 84; Walde- Hofmann II 206–207; Feist 40, 579; Martinet Gém. 176; Pokorny I 24–25; Vendryes O-21; Fraenkel 7 (no connection with Lith aliái ‘every, all’); Dzhaukian SG 41 (to Arm amb-o∑ú ‘all’ < *olƒ-); Vries ANEW 7; Vendryes U-17-18; Onions 25; Seebold 76; Lehmann GED 29; Lloyd-Springer I 129–131; Bammesberger Nom. 228, 246; Kluge-Seebold 27; Ivanov Proc. IX UCLA 76; Boutkan OFED s.v.
ON ǫllung-is ‘altogether’, OE eallunza, eallinza id., OS alung id., OHG adj. alang ‘whole’. Derived from *alaz, *allaz (Hellquist ANF XI 348). Holthausen AEEW 84; Vries ANEW 686.
Goth usalþans ‘old, infirm’, ON aldinn ‘old’. Participle of the unattested strong vb. *alþanan (with analogical -þ- in Gothic). See *alþjaz. Feist 529; Seebold 77–78.

Goth alþeis ‘old’, ON comp. ellri ‘older’. Identical with OIr altae ‘adult’ (Marstrander Corr. 47). Further related to *alanan. Schweizer KZ I 558 (to Lat part. altus); Torp-Falk 20; Brugmann PBB XLIII 310–324; Karsten NB II 330–331, 370; Torp-Falk 20; Feist 40; Pokorny I 27; Vries ANEW 101; Mastrelli AGIt XLVI 139–147 (Germanic tradition of killing the aged); Lehmann GED 29–30; Heidermanns 98–99.

ON ǫlunn ‘a kind of fish, mackerel’, OS alund ‘whitefish’, OHG alunt id. Of unclear origin. Derived from *alanan? Loewe PBB LIX 254–255; Torp-Falk 558; Holthausen AWN 357 (to *alziz ~ *elxaz ~ *elxōn); Vries ANEW 686–687 (to *al- ‘white’); Kluge-Seebold 24.

ON ǫl ‘beer, ale’ (wa-stem with traces of a root stem), OE ealu, ealo id., OS alu- (in compounds). Connected with or borrowed to OPrus alu ‘mead’, Lith alūs id., Slav *olъ id. Scyth *alut- (cf. prop. Ἀλούθαγος and Osset æluton ‘beer’) from Gmc, indicative of the early chronology of Germanic contacts with East Iranian (from where NCauc *’Vl Vdw V ‘beer’). Grg ludi, dial. aludi id. is also from Gmc but hardly via Iranian. Kuhn KZ XV 313–314; Sievers PBB X 449; Torp-Falk 21; Hirt PBB XXIII 336; Búga KS 64–66, 70 (Balt and Slav borrowed from Gmc); Kiparsky Lehnw. 78–80; Holthausen AEEW 84; Specht 195–196; Abaev OJa F 338–339; Pokorny I 33–34 (related to Lat alūta ‘a kind of soft leather’); Fraenkel 8–9; Vries ANEW 686; Zalizniak I 221; Onions 24; Stang LS 13; Vasmer- Trubachev III 132–133; Bammesberger Nom. 215; Nikolayev-Starostin NCED 224.
ON ǫl-bekkr ‘alebench’, OE ealu-benc id. Compound of *aluþ and *bankiz. Carr Nom. comp. 55.
ON ǫl-hús ‘ale-house’, OE ealu-hús id. Compound of *aluþ and *xūsan. Carr Nom. comp. 82 (parallel formations).
ON ǫlr ‘alder’, OE alor id. Variant of *alizō ~ *alisō. Holthausen AEEW 3; Pokorny I 302; Vries ANEW 686; Zalizniak II 214.
ON amma ‘grandmother’, MDu amme ‘nanny’, OHG amma id. A Lallwort typical of many other languages cf. Gk ἀμμά ‘mother’. Torp-Falk 16; Vries ANEW 8; Lloyd-Springer I 205–206; Kluge-Seebold 33–34.

ON apr ‘cold, sharp’, MDu amper ‘sour, sharp’. Probably from an earlier *amraz. Related to Skt amlá- ‘sour’ < *om 6 ro- Lat amārus ‘bitter’. Johansson IF III 240; Torp-Falk 16; Karsten NB II 256–257; Walde-Hofmann I 35; Mayrhofer I 46; Pokorny I 778; Vries ANEW 11; Lloyd-Springer I 207–209; Heidermanns 99–100; Kluge-Seebold 35.

Goth ams ‘shoulder’. Related to Hitt an(aš)ša ‘part of the back’, Toch A es, B āntse ‘shoulder’, Skt áásaid., Arm us id., Gk ὠμός id. (with lengthening of the root vowel), Lat umerus id., Slav *ǫsъ ‘mustache’ continuing IE *omsos ~ *omesos (Lachmanapud Aufrecht KZ I 283–4). Torp-Falk 16–17; Schrader- Nehring I 636; Walde-Hofmann II 815; Feist 40–41; Mayrhofer I 14; Pokorny I 778; Vries ANEW 16; Frisk II 1148; Zalizniak I 129; Trubachev Etim. 1970 13–14; Lehmann GED 30; Adams TB 43–44.

OE ósle ‘blackbird’, OHG amsla id. Usually connected with Lat merula id. and Celt *mesalkā or *misalkā > W mwyalch id. (Fick I 515). On the other hand, the Germanic word appears to be a transparent derivative of *amsaz (a bird with a distinctly colored back?). Pedersen Kelt. Gr. I 73; Torp-Falk 16; Suolahti DVN 54–55; Holthausen AEEW 243; Walde-Hofmann II 77–78; Pokorny I 35–36; Chemodanov SGGJa I 101; Onions 636; Hamp IF LXXXVII 77–79; Lloyd-Springer I 212–213; Kluge-Seebold 35.
Goth an ‘so, then’. Similar to Lat particle an ‘or’, Gk ἄν. Torp-Falk 11; Walde-Hofmann I 44; Feist 41; Pokorny I 37; Lehmann GED 30 (unrelated to Gk ἄν).

Goth ana ‘on account of, at’, ON á ‘on, upon’, OE on id., OFris a ‘to, in, at’, OS an id., OHG ana, an id. Related to Av ana ‘through, along’, Gk (Ion-Att) ἄνα ‘on’ (Schmidt Studien 203– 207). Bartholomae 112–114; Torp- Falk 11; Holthausen AEEW 4; Feist 41; Pokorny I 39–40; Vries ANEW 1; Frisk I 100; Onions 627; Lehmann GED 30; Boutkan OFED s.v.

OFris omma ‘breath’. Close to Gk ἄνεμος ‘wind’, Lat animus ‘spirit, soul’, OIr anim id. For further connections see *ananan. Pedersen Kelt. Gr. I 170, 240; Walde-Hofmann I 49–50; Pokorny I 38; Frisk I 105; Lehmann GED 388; Adams TB 40–42 (Toch A āñcäm, B āñme ‘wish, self, soul’ as a conflation of *ētmen and *an 6 mo-); Boutkan OFED s.v.

Goth uz-anan ‘to breathe out, to expire’. Related to Toch B anāsk- ‘to inhale, to breathe’, Skt ániti ‘to breathe’ and further to Toch AB āñm- ‘life, spirit’, Gk ἄνεμος ‘breath’, Lat animus ‘soul’ anima ‘air, wind’, Slav *vonь ‘smell’. The participle in *-to- is reflected in OE neut. or-oä ‘breath, breathing’ < *-anþan identical with Gk ἄνται: ἄνεμοι, ἀντας:πνοιάς, Hes. Torp-Falk 10; Walde-Hofmann I 49–50; Feist 538; Holthausen AEEW 242; Mayrhofer I 33–34; Pokorny I 38–39; Frisk I 105; Seebold 78–79; Vasmer-Trubachev I 349; Lehmann GED 385; Bammesberger Nom. 77; Adams TB 12; Burlak IFTJa 237.

Goth and ‘throughout, from one side to the other’, ON and- ‘opposite, against’, OE and- id., OS and- id., OHG ant- id. Closely related to Gk ἄντα ‘opposite’, Lith αάτ ‘on, in order to’. Cf. comp. Goth andiz-uh ‘either’, ON endr, enn ‘in times of yore, again’, OE énd ‘formerly, of old’, OS endi ‘earlier’, MHG end id. For further connections see *anđjaz. Torp- Falk 13; Lindquist SNF IX/1 6; Holthausen AEEW 5, 90; Feist 46, 50; Pokorny I 49; Fraenkel 11, IF XL 88–90; Vries ANEW 9, 102; Frisk I 112–113; Schmidt Studien 310– 315 (to *an followed by *-ta, *-ti); Lehmann GED 34, 37.

Goth andbahts ‘servant’, ON ambátt ‘bondwoman’ (> *anđbaxtō), OE ambeht ‘servant’, OHG ambaht id. If Goth -nd- is due to analogy with the prefix and-, an alternative reconstruction *ambaxtaz is more acceptable. Borrowed from Celt *amb(i)-aktos: Gaul-Lat ambactus ‘servant’ W amaeth ‘servus arans’ (Arbois de Jubainville MSL VII 289–290). Zeyss Zfcelt Ph III 353, 374 (on the substitution of Celt amb- with Gmc *anđ-); Brugmann IF XIX 390 (on the structure of the Celtic word); Torp-Falk 13; Holthausen AEEW 3; Feist 48–49; Walde- Hofmann I 36–37; Vries ANEW 8; Benveniste IEL 110–111; Lehmann GED 36; Lloyd-Springer I 195–196; Kluge- Seebold 35–36.

Goth andbahti ‘service’, ON embætti id., OE ambiht ‘office, ministry, service’, OS ambaht ‘service’, OHG ambaht id., ambahti id. Derived from *anđbaxtaz. Torp-Falk 13; Holthausen AEEW 3; Feist 48–49; Vries ANEW 102; Zalizniak II 214; Kluge-Seebold 35–36.

ON en, enn ‘and’, OE and id., OFris and, anda id., MDu enn id. A full grade of the conjunction originally reflecting *–te. Cf. Skt ánti ‘before, near’, átha ‘then, now’ (if from *–t-), Gk ἀντί ‘over against’. See *unþi. Torp-Falk 13–14; Holthausen AEEW 5; Mayrhofer I 28 (against the connection with Skt); Pokorny I 50; Vries ANEW 102; Frisk I 113–114; Onions 36; Kluge- Seebold 847; Boutkan OFED s.v.

Goth andi-laus ‘endless’, ON enda-lauss id., OE ende-leás ‘infinite, eternal’, OS endi-lōs ‘endless’, MHG ende-lōs id. Compound of *anđjaz and *lausaz. Feist 50; Carr Nom. comp. 88 (parallel formations); Lehmann GED 229.

Goth andeis ‘end’, ON endir id., OE ende id., OFris enda, ende id., OS endi id., OHG neut. enti id. Based on IE adv. *anti ‘opposite’ (Skt ánti ‘before, near’, Gk ἀντί, see also *anđa). Cf. in particular Skt antya- ‘last’, Gk ἀντίος ‘opposite’. Cf. also *anþjan > ON enni ‘forehead’ with a different stress. Torp- Falk 13; Holthausen AEEW 90; Feist 49; Walde-Hofmann I 54; Mayrhofer I 35–36; Pokorny I 48–50; Vries ANEW 102–103; Frisk I 113–114; Zalizniak II 165; Onions 313; Lehmann GED 36; Bammesberger Nom. 66; Kluge-Seebold 220–221; Boutkan OFED s.v.
ON enda ‘to end’, OE endian id., OFris endia id., OS endion, endōn id., OHG entōn id. Derived from *anđjaz. Holthausen AEEW 91; Vries ANEW 102.

ON ǫnd ‘porch’. Compared with Skt ātā ‘door frame’, Lat antae ‘pillars or pilasters on each side of doors’ (Bugge KZ XIX 401). Yet, a comparison with *anđjaz is not less attractive. Torp- Falk 13; Walde-Hofmann I 52; Mayrhofer I 72; Vries ANEW 687.
ON anda ‘to breathe’, OE óäian ‘to blow strongly’. Derived from *anđōn. Holthausen AEEW 243; Pokorny I 38; Vries ANEW 9.

ON andi ‘breath’, OE anda ‘malice, envy, hatred’, OS ando ‘breath’, OHG anto ‘zeal’. Cf. also ON ǫnd ‘breath’ < *anđō. Derived from *ananan. Structurally similar to Gk ἄνται: ἄνεμοι, Hes. Torp-Falk 10–11; Holthausen AEEW 6; Pokorny I 38; Vries ANEW 687; Frisk I 113; Zalizniak I 152; Seebold 78.

OHG angar ‘corn worm’. May be close to Gk ǫmbhriw: đgxeluw, Μεθυμναῖοι, Hes. < *eng⁄(h)ēri- and Lith υνγυρίσ ‘eel’ (from *angur‹s, cf. Otreębski LP V 26), OPrus angurgis id., Slav *ǫgъrь ‘pimple, maggot, eel’ (Trautmann BB XXIX 307). Bezzenberger BB II 154; Zupitza Gutt. 128; Pokorny I 44; Fraenkel 1163; Frisk I 725; Stang LS 40; Vasmer-Trubachev IV 146–147; Toporov PJa I 88–89.

ON angr ‘grief, sorrow’. Originally, a neut. s-stem identical with Skt áṃhas- ‘anxiety, trouble’, Lat angor ‘strangling, anguish’. Fick III 12; Torp- Falk 12; Unwerth PBB XXXVI 7; Walde-Hofmann I 47; Mayrhofer I 14; Pokorny I 43; Vries ANEW 10.

ON pl. Englar ‘Angles’, OE Engle id., OHG Angil- (in cmpn.). Rendered as Lat Anglii, Gk Ἀγγείλοι. Related to *anzōn II, *anzulaz (Lundahl Festschr. Sahlgren 2–3). Erdmann SVSU I/1 114 (to *anzōn); Vries ANEW 102.
ON enskr ‘Anglian’, OE enzlisc id., OFris engelsk id., MLG engelsch id. Derived from *anz(i)laz. Holthausen AEEW 91; Vries ANEW 103.
ON eng ‘meadow’, MDu eng ‘farmland’. Related to *anzraz. Vries ANEW 102; Kluge-Seebold 39.
ON angi ‘sweet odor’. Close to Arm anjn ‘soul’ < *an·hen- (Lidén Arm. St. 38–39). Lagercrantz KZ XXXV 274 (to Gk ὀμφά: ὀσμή Λάκωνες); Torp-Falk 557; Pokorny I 43; Vries ANEW 10.

ON angi ‘spine, prickle’, OE anza ‘thorn, sharp point’, OHG ango ‘hook, hinge’. Related to Skt áñcati ‘to bend, to curve’, aṅká- ‘curve, hook’, Lat uncus ‘bent; hook’, Gk ἀγκών ‘bend of the arm, elbow’. Torp-Falk 12; Holthausen AEEW 6; Walde-Hofmann II 816; Mayrhofer I 19, 24; Pokorny I 45–46; Vries ANEW 10; Frisk I 11; Zalizniak I 152.

ON angr ‘bay’, OS angar ‘meadow’, OHG angar id. Derivationally close to Lat ancrae ‘convalles vel arborum intervalla’. Further connected with *anzōn II. Sütterlin IF XLV 307 (to Gk νάπη ‘woodland vale’); Torp-Falk 12; Walde- Hofmann I 61; Pokorny I 46; Vries ANEW 10; Lloyd-Springer I 247–249; Kluge-Seebold 39.

ON ǫngull ‘angle, hook’, OE anzel ‘hook, fishing-hook’, MLG angel ‘hinge’, OHG angul ‘hook, hinge’. Identical with Gk ἀγκύλος ‘crooked’. Further connections see under *anzōn II. Zupitza Gutt. 128; Torp-Falk 12; Holthausen AEEW 6; Pokorny I 46; Vries ANEW 687; Frisk I 11.

OFris masc. angst, ongost ‘fear’, MLG angest id., OHG angust id. Derived from *anzuz ~ *anzwjaz. Close to Slav *ǫzostь ‘narrowness’ < *an·hosti-. Cf. also Lat angustus ‘narrow’ < *an·hosto-. For further connections see *anzaz. Schleicher KZ I 143; Torp-Falk 12; Walde-Hofmann I 49; Pokorny I 43; Lloyd-Springer I 253– 255; Kluge-Seebold 40; Boutkan OFED s.v.

Goth aggwus ‘narrow’, ON ǫngr id. (wa-stem), OE enge id., OS engi id., OHG engi id. Related to Skt aṃhú- id., Gk ἄγχι ‘near’, Arm anjuk ‘narrow’, Lat *angu- (in angiportus), Slav *ǫzъkъ id. (Feist Btrg. Gesch. XV 545). Karsten NB II 407–408; Zupitza Gutt. 98; Torp-Falk 12; Feist 13–14; Holthausen AEEW 91; Walde-Hofmann I 47; Mayrhofer I 14; Pokorny I 42; Vries ANEW 687; Frisk I 17–18; Heidermanns KZ XCIX 279–280; Vasmer- Trubachev IV 154; Lehmann GED 9; Bammesberger Nom. 262; Heidermanns 100–101; Kluge-Seebold 221.
Goth aggwiþa ‘trouble, affliction’, OS engitha ‘straits’, OHG engida ‘oppression, fear’. Derived from *anzuz ~ *anzwjaz. Feist 13; Vries ANEW 682; Bammesberger Nom. 117; Heidermanns 100.
Goth ga-aggwjan ‘to oppress, to constrain’, OHG engen ‘to scare, to press’. Derived from *anzuz ~ *anzwjaz. Torp-Falk 12; Feist 174; Vries ANEW 682; Lehmann GED 9; Heidermanns 100.

Goth bals-agga ‘neck, narrow of the neck’ (leg. hals-agga), OHG anka ‘occiput’. Related to *anzuz ~ *anzwjaz. Cf. a similar formation in Gk ἄμφην ‘neck, throat’. Schrader KZ X 461 (to Gk ἄγγος ‘pitcher, bucket, vessel’); Zupitza Gutt. 89; Feist 242; Pokorny 42–43; Lehmann GED 175.

OE óht ‘fear, terror, hostile pursuit’, OFris acht, achte ‘hostile pursuit’, OHG āhta ‘persecution’. Close to Ir écht ‘manslaughter’ < *anktu-. Further connected with *anzuz ~ *anzwjaz. Torp- Falk 558; Holthausen AEEW 241; Pokorny I 45 (further to Gk ἀνάγκη ‘force, constraint, necessity’); Lloyd- Springer I 118–120; Kluge-Seebold 12–13.

ON áll ‘seed, sprout’. An accentual variant of *anzulaz. Derivationally close to Skt aṅkurá- ‘sprout, shoot’, Gk ἄγκυλος ‘crooked, curved’ (Falk ANF VI 115). See *anxulō. Torp-Falk 12; Pokorny I 46; Vries ANEW 6; Frisk I 11.
ON ál ‘strap, bridle’. Despite a different stress pattern, identical with Gk ἀγκύλη id. Further related to *anzulaz, *anxulaz. Torp-Falk 12; Pokorny I 46; Vries ANEW 4; Frisk I 11.

ON ǫkkla ‘ankle’, OFris onkel id., MDu ankel id., OHG ankala id. Cf. also MLG enkel id., OHG enkil id. < *ankilaz. Derived from *ankōn. Structurally similar to Lat angulus ‘angle’ and, Slav *ǫglъ id. Torp- Falk 11; Walde-Hofmann I 48–49; Pokorny I 47; Vries ANEW 686; Onions 38; Vasmer-Trubachev IV 145; Lloyd- Springer I 258–261; Kluge-Seebold 222; Boutkan OFED s.v.

MHG anke ‘scruff, thigh, shank’. Related to Skt áṅga- ‘limb’. A further connection with Gk διθύρ-ambow ‘dithyramb’ (Fröhde BB XXI 195) is probable and would imply *ang⁄o- as a source. Mayrhofer I 21; Pokorny I 46–47; Vries ANEW 99; Frisk I 8.

OHG anko ‘butter’. Close to Lat unguen ‘fatty substance, ointment’, Umbr umen ‘fat’, OIr imb ‘butter’ < *–g⁄en- (Szemerényi Scripta 872–874). Cf. also OPrus anctan ‘butter’ < *ong⁄t-om. Further connected with Skt anákti ‘to anoint’, Lat unguō id. Torp-Falk 11; Trautmann BSW 9–10; Walde- Hofmann II 819–820; Pokorny I 779; Chemodanov SGGJa I 102; Toporov PJa I 91; Bammesberger Nom. 175; Lloyd- Springer I 263–265; Kluge-Seebold 41.

OHG ana ‘grandmother’. Cf. also OHG ano ‘grandfather’. A descriptive word similar with Hitt anna- ‘mother’, ḫanna- ‘grandmother’, Arm han id., Gk ἀννίς: μητρός ἢ πατρός μήτηρ, Hes., Lat anna ‘foster-mother’, Messap ana ‘πότνια’, OPrus ane ‘old woman’. Torp-Falk 11; Walde-Hofmann I 50; Abaev I 148; Pokorny I 36; Frisk I 112; Benveniste 190; Puhvel III 84–86; Lloyd-Springer I 215–217; Kluge-Seebold 20.

Goth ans ‘beam’, ON áss ‘pole, rafter’, MHG ans-boum ‘beam of a bridge’. Related to Lat ānsa ‘handle’, MIr pl. éisi ‘rein’, OPrus ansis ‘hook’, Lith [sā ‘handle, loop’ (Grienberger Got. 27). Hoffmann BB XXV 108 (to Skt ána- ‘cart’, Lat onus ‘burden’); Torp-Falk 14 (to OIr asna ‘rib’); Uhlenbeck PBB XXX 260–261 (same as Hoffmann); Meringer IF XVIII 26 (to Gk νέομαι ‘to go back, to return’), XXI 302–303, Wu S IX 115 (to *ansiz ~ *ansuz); Torp-Falk 14 (to Lat asser ‘beam’); Wood JEGP II 213 (to *–sis ‘sword’); Walde-Hofmann I 51–52; Feist 52; Pokorny I 48; Toporov PJa I 92; Lehmann GED 38.

Goth-Lat pl. anses ‘(pagan) god’, ON áss id., OE ós id., OS ās, ōs in prop. (a-stem), OHG ansi- (in cmpn.). Identical with Skt ásu- ‘life, vital strength’, Av aŋhu- id., Venet ahsu- ‘cult image’ (Bradke ZDMG XL 347). Grimm DM 22 (to *ansaz, in view of worship pillars erected to Germanic gods); Försterman KZ I 243; Bartholomae 283; Torp-Falk 14; Grienberger ZDADL XXXVI 313; Meringer IF XVII 159–160, Wu S IX 115 (to *ansaz); Güntert Weltkönig 102 (follows Bradke); Holthausen AEEW 242; Feist 52–53; Mayrhofer I 65–66; Vries ANEW 16; PoloméEG VIII 36–44; Pokorny I 48; Zalizniak II 198; Lehmann GED 38–39.

ON æs ‘outer border, edge (of a shoe)’, MLG öse ‘loop, sling’. Derived from *ansaz. Identical with Gk ἡνία ‘bridle, reins’ (Persson Beiträge 5; Falk NVA 1919 131). Torp-Falk 14; Walde Festschr. Streitberg 153–154; Walde- Hofmann I 51–52; Pokorny I 48; Fraenkel 18; Vries ANEW 681; Frisk I 637.

Goth ansts ‘beneficence, graciousness, grace’, ON ást ‘love, affection’, OE ést ‘will, consent, favor, grace’, OFris enst ‘favor, grace’, OS anst id., OHG anst id. Derived from *unnanan. Schweizer KZ I 143; Torp-Falk 14; Feist 53; Pokorny I 47; Vries ANEW 16; Zalizniak II 228; Seebold 79; Lehmann GED 39; Bammesberger Nom. 144.
ON æsta ‘to demand, to ask’, MHG ensten ‘to be kind’. Derived from *anstiz. Vries ANEW 681.
Goth adv. anþar-leiko ‘differently, otherwise’, OS ōäar-līk id., OE adv. óäer-líce. Compound of *anþeraz and *līkaz. Feist 54; Lehmann GED 40.

Goth anþar ‘other’, ON annarr id., OE óäer id., OFris ōther id., OS ōäar id., OHG ander id. Identical with Skt ántara- ‘other’, Av antara- id., Osc anter id., OPrus antars ‘other’, Lith añtras, añtaras id., Slav *vъtorъ ‘second’. Meillet Etudes 407; Torp-Falk 13; Schulz ZDWf X 157; Holthausen AEEW 243; Feist 53; Mayrhofer I 35; Pokorny I 37; Fraenkel 12; Vries ANEW 10; Lehmann GED 39; Lloyd-Springer I 241–242; Ross-Berns Numerals 625; Kluge- Seebold 38; Boutkan OFED s.v.

ON ǫnd ‘duck’ (traces of a root stem), OE ened id., OS anud, anad id., OHG anut, enita id. Related to Skt ātí- ‘aquatic bird’, Gk νῆσσα ‘duck’, Lat anas id., Lith ántis id., Slav *ǫty id. Schmidt KZ XXIII 268–270; Suolahti DVN 420; Torp-Falk 11; Meillet MSL VIII 236; Holthausen AEEW 91; Walde-Hofmann I 44–45; Mayrhofer I 72–73; Pokorny I 41–42; Chemodanov SGGJa I 59; Fraenkel 11–12; Vries ANEW 687; Frisk II 317–318; Zalizniak II 205; Onions 288; Vasmer-Trubachev IV 174; Hamp KZ CII 29–31; Bammesberger Nom. 202–203 (reconstructs *anþz); Kluge-Seebold 222–223.

ON apaldr ‘apple-tree’ OE neut. (?) apulder, apuldor id. OS apuldra id., MHG apfalter id. Derived from *ap(u)laz with suffix *-dhro-. Torp-Falk 14–15; Holthausen AEEW 6; Pokorny I 2; Zalizniak II 182; Vries ANEW 11; Kluge-Seebold 17–18.

ON api ‘ape’, OE apa id., OS apo id., OHG affo id. Borrowed from Celtic, cf., ἀβρωνας [sc. ἀββωνας]: Κελτοί τούς κερκοπιθήκους, Hes. Försterman KZ I 496 (to Skt kapí- ‘monkey’, Gk κῆπος ‘long-tailed monkey’); Torp-Falk 14 (to Slav *opica ‘ape’ which, however, is from Gmc); Holthausen AEEW 6; Pokorny I 2–3; Vries ANEW 11; Zalizniak I 153; Onions 42; Lloyd-Springer I 58–59; Kluge-Seebold 17.

Crim. Goth apel ‘apple’, ON apal-grár ‘dapple-gray’, OE æppel ‘apple’, OFris appel id., OS appul id., OHG apful id. (< *ap(u)liz). Related to Gaul avallo ‘pomma’, OIr ubull ‘apple’ < *ablu-, Lith obuol‹s id., Slav *ablъko id. (Fick BB II 195). Torp-Falk 14; Feist 54, 579; Hoops Waldbäume 477–479; Holthausen AEEW 11; Lane Language IX 251 (on Celtic words); Pokorny I 1–2; Chemodanov SGGJa I 63–64; Vries ANEW 103; Büga I 433; Zalizniak II 214; Fraenkel I 515; Onions 44; Hamp Zfcelt Ph XXXVII 164 (from non-IE *àblu); Adams IF XL 79–82 (against Hamp); Trubachev ESSJa I 44–47; Ivanov-Gamkrelidze 637–641 (to Hitt šamlu⁄anza id.); Lloyd-Springer I 60–63, 298–301; Lehmann GED 40; Kluge- Seebold 46–47.

Goth arbaiþs ‘work, labor’, OFris arbē(i)d id., OS arbēth id., OHG arbeit id. An unusual derivative of *arbaz. Probably, a compound of *arbaz and *iđiz. Kirchhoff KZ I 39; Grienberger Goten 27–28 (to *arjanan); Bezzenberger BB XXVII 150; Pedersen KZ XXXVI 94; Brugmann IF XIX 384 (deverbative from the unattested *arbējō); Torp-Falk 19; Uhlenbeck PBB XVI 562; Meringer IF XVII 128 (compound of *arbho- ‘plow-animal’ and *i-ti- ‘walk, tread’); Feist 55; Kronasser HS 170 (to Lith dárbas ‘work’); Pokorny I 782; Zalizniak II 214; Stang LS 44; Lehmann GED 40–41; Lloyd-Springer I 313–318; Kluge-Seebold 50.

ON erfiäi ‘toil, labor’, OE earfoä ‘hardship, trouble’, OS arbēthi ‘toil, labor’, OHG fem. arbeiti id. Derived from *arbaiđiz. Holthausen AEEW 86; Pokorny I 782; Vries ANEW 103–104; Zalizniak II 214; Lehmann GED 40–41.
ON erfiäa ‘to toil, to labor’, OE earfoäian ‘to to trouble’, OHG arbeitōn ‘to torment’. Derived from *arbaiđiz. Torp-Falk 19.

ON arfr ‘inheritance’. Related to OIr orb(b)e id. Further connected with Skt árbha- ‘little, weak, young’, Gk ὄρφανός ‘orphaned’, Lat orbus ‘bereaved, parentless’, Slav *orbъ ‘slave’. Kirchhoff KZ I 39; Sievers PBB XII 176–177; Torp-Falk 19; Meringer IF XVII 128; Wood MLN XXI 39 (to Skt arpáyati ‘to put into, to fix’); Peterson PBB XL 97–101; Walde-Hofmann II 219–220; Feist 56; Porzig Gliederung 121–122; Pokorny I 781; Vries ANEW 13; Frisk II 431; Zalizniak II 214; Benveniste 68–70; Vasmer-Trubachev III 453; Grønvik Inheritance passim; Lehmann GED 41; Bammesberger Nom. 53.

Goth arbi ‘inheritance, heritage’, ON erfi‘wake, funeral feast’, OE erfe ‘inheritance’, OFris erve id., OS erbi id., OHG erbi id. Close to OIr orb(b)e id. Derived from *arbaz. Arbois de Jubainville MSL VII 286–288 (from Celtic); Torp-Falk 19; Meringer IF XVII 128 (to *arjanan); Holthausen AEEW 186; Feist 56; Porzig Gliederung 121–122 (from Celtic); Pokorny I 781; Chemodanov SGGJa I 96; Vries ANEW 12–13, 103; Zalizniak II 214; Benveniste IEL 68–70; Lehmann GED 41; Bammesberger Nom. 53, 66; Kluge-Seebold 227; Boutkan OFED s.v.

ON erfa ‘to honor with a funeral feast’, OE irfan ‘to inherit’, OFris ervia id., OS giervan id., OHG ir-erben id. Derived from *arbjan. Structurally close to Slav *orbiti ‘to work’. Holthausen AEEW 186; Vries ANEW 103; Vasmer-Trubachev III 487; Boutkan OFED s.v.
ON erfi-vǫrär, arfvǫrär ‘heir’, OE irfe-weard ‘guardian of an inheritance, heir’, OS erbi-ward ‘heir’. Compound of *arbjan and *warđaz. Carr Nom. comp. 47.

Goth arbja ‘heir, inheritor’, ON arfiid. (a-stem), OE ierfa id., OFris erva id., MLG erve id., OHG erbo id. Related to *arbjan. Structurally close to Gaul PN Orbius (Vendryes 0–27). Torp-Falk 19; Holthausen AEEW 186; Feist 56; Porzig Gliederung 121; Pokorny I 781; Vries ANEW 12–13; Zalizniak II 214; Lehmann GED 41–42; Szemerényi Festschr. Meid 359–368; Kluge-Seebold 227–228; Boutkan OFED s.v.

ON ein-arär ‘firm, single’, OE sbst. eard ‘state, condition’, MDu sbst. aert ‘kind, situation’, MHG sbst. art ‘kind’. Related to Skt ṛtá- ‘right, true’, Av aša- ‘truth, right’, Arm ard ‘structure’, Gk ēμαρτῆ‘together, at once’, Lat artus ‘fitted, close, strait, narrow’. Bartholomae 229–238; Torp-Falk 17; Walde- Hofmann I 70; Mayrhofer I 122; Pokorny I 57; Frisk I 87; Kluge- Seebold 54.

ON ǫrä ‘crop, produce’, OE irä ‘plowing, tilling, crop, produce’, OFris raef-erd ‘plowing off boundaries’, OHG art ‘plowed land, yield’. Derivative of *arjanan. Torp-Falk 18; Holthausen AEEW 187; Pokorny I 62; Vries ANEW 688; Zalizniak I 150; Seebold 82; Lloyd-Springer I 347–349; Bammesberger Nom. 144; Kluge-Seebold 54–55.

Burg *args ‘cowardly, mean’, ON argr ‘emasculate, effeminate’, OE earz ‘inert, weak, timid, cowardly’, OFris erch ‘evil, wrong’, MLG arch ‘angry, evil’, OHG arg ‘greedy, cowardly, worthless’. Related to Lith aržūs, eržūs ‘salacious, lecherous’ (Būga Aist. st. 119), further to Hitt ark- ‘to climb, to cover’, Gk ὀρχέομαι ‘to dance, to jump’. Karsten NB I 49; Weisweiler IF XLI 16–18; Sütterlin IF XLV 307 (to Skt ṛhánt- ‘small’); Zupitza Gutt. 208; Torp-Falk 19; Holthausen AEEW 85; Loewenthal PBB LIV 157 (to Gk ἀρχός ‘rectum, anus’); Pokorny I 339; Vries ANEW 13 (to Toch AB erkāt ‘depreciatory’); Fraenkel 123–124; Frisk II 433; Krause SUR 58; Lloyd-Springer I 321–324; Puhvel Festschr. Risch 154–155; Heidermanns 102–103; Kluge-Seebold 51 (to Gk ὄρχις ‘scrotum’); Boutkan OFED s.v.
ON ergi ‘lewdness, lust’, OHG argī ‘negligence, greed’. Derived from *arzaz. Vries ANEW 104; Heidermanns 102.
ON ergjask ‘to become a coward’, MHG gi-ergen ‘to spoil, to destroy’. Derived from *arzaz. Heidermanns 103.

ON ǫr ‘arrow’, OE earh id., Cf. also Goth arƕaz-na id. based on an old s-stem. Close to Lat arcus ‘arch, bow’. Torp-Falk 18–19; Holthausen AEEW 85; Walde-Hofmann I 64; Feist 56; Pokorny I 67; Chemodanov SGGJa I 87; Vries ANEW 688; Lehmann GED 42; Bammesberger Nom. 115.

OS erida id. Continues *ar 6-to- morphologically different from the usual *ar 6-tro- id.: Arm araur, Gk ἄροτρον, Lat arātrum (Kuhn KZ LXXI 141). See *arjanan. Meringer IF XVII 121– 122; Walde-Hofmann I 62; Pokorny I 62; Vries ANEW 12; Frisk I 147.

Goth arjan ‘to plow’, ON erja id., OE erian id., OFris era id., OS erian id., OHG erren id. Identical with MIr airim id., Lith ariū, árti id., Slav *orjǫ, *orati id. Further connected with Gk ἀρόω id., Lat arō id. Persson Beiträge 659, 669; Torp-Falk 18; Holthausen AEEW 94; Walde-Hofmann I 69; Feist 56–57; Specht KZ LVI 29; Belardi RL II 193–195; Jóhannesson IEW 34–35; Pokorny I 62–63; Chemodanov SGGJa I 62; Fraenkel 17; Vries ANEW 104; Frisk I 147–148; Onions 297; Seebold 81–83; Vasmer-Trubachev III 148–149; Lehmann GED 42; Matzel Festschr. Rosenfeld 455–468.

Run nom. pl. superl. arjoste R ‘noble’. Related to Skt aryá- ‘lord, master of the house’, Av airyō ‘Aryan’, Ir aire, airech ‘swain, suitor’ (Krause FF X 217– 218; Run. 539). Bartholomae 198; Mayrhofer I 52; Pokorny I 67; Vries ANEW 13; Palmer Gedenkschr. Güntert 11–19; Heidermanns 103– 104.
ON arm-baugr ‘armlet’, OE earm-béaz id., MLG arm-bōg id., OHG arm-boug id. Compound of *armaz and *bauzaz. Carr Nom. comp. 46.
ON alm-bogi ‘elbow’, Swed arm-bàge id., OFris arm-boga. Compound of *armaz and *buzōn. Probably, influenced by *alina-buzōn. Carr Nom. comp. 46.

Goth arma-hairts ‘merciful, compassionate’, OE earm-heort id., OHG arm-herz id. Calque of Lat misericors id. (Raumer Einwirk. 345). Compound of *armaz II and *xertōn. Kluge Btrg. Gesch. XXXV 148–149, 159; Holthausen AEEW 86; Feist 57; Carr NC 3–4 (OHG < Goth); Beck Zd Ph XCVIII 109–129; Lehmann GED 42–43; Kluge- Seebold 82.
Goth arma-hairtiþa ‘mercy, charity’, OHG arm-herzida id. Calque of Lat misericordia id. (Raumer Einwirk. 345). Cf. *arma-xertaz. Feist 57; Lehmann GED 43.
Goth arma-hairtei ‘mercy, compassion’, OHG arm-herzī id. Calque of Lat misericordia id. (Raumer Einwirk. 345). Cf. *arma-xertaz. Feist 57; Lehmann GED 43.
ON arm-ligr ‘pitiful’, OE earm-líc ‘miserable, wretched’, OS arm-līk id., OHG armi-līh id. Derived from *armaz. Heidermanns 104.

Goth arms ‘arm’ (from *armiz), ON armr id., OE earm id., OFris erm, arm id., OS arm id., OHG arm id. Related to Skt īrmá- id., Av ar 6 mō id., Lat armus ‘upper part of the arm’, Gaul aramō ‘fork’, OPrus irmo ‘arm’ and in particular, with the same ablaut grade, Slav *ormê ‘shoulder’ (Kirchhoff KZ I 39). Būga Aist. (adds Lith pl. armaĩ ‘bars holding the front shaft of a cart’ which is, however, from dial. German); Bartholomae 197; Torp-Falk 18; Holthausen AEEW 86; Walde-Hofmann I 69; Feist 58; Mayrhofer I 96; Pokorny I 58; Fraenkel 16; Vries ANEW 14; Zalizniak II 185; Onions 50; Vasmer-Trubachev III 440–441; Hamp JIES X 187–189; Lehmann GED 43; Lloyd-Springer I 331–333; Kluge-Seebold 52; Boutkan OFED s.v.

Goth arms ‘poor, pitiable, miserable’, ON armr id., OE earm id., OFris erm ‘poor’, OS arm id., OHG arm ‘poor, powerless’. Probably continues *arbmaz derived from *arbaz (Johansson PBB XV 223–224; Wood MLN XXI 39). On the other hand, cf. Skt armaka- ‘ruins’ based on the unattested adj. *arma- (Humbach Festschr. Knobloch 189–193; Beck- Strunk Festschr. Eggers 40), Hitt arman- ‘sickness’ (Ivanov Etimologija 1979 131). Fick III 24 (to Gk ἐρῆμος ‘desolate, lonely’); Meillet MSL X 280 (to Arm o∑-orm ‘pity’); Torp-Falk 19; Feist 57–58; Holthausen AEEW 86; Weisweiler IF XLI 304–329; Mezger KZ LXXVI 178– 180 (to Hitt araḫza- ‘outside, all around’); Specht 264 (same as Johansson); Vries ANEW 14; Solta 427–428 (follows Meillet); Beck-Strunk Festschr. Eggers 18–41 (to Lith īrti ‘to fall apart, to dissolve’); Reiter Zf Balk XIII 133–138; Puhvel 157–160; Lehmann GED 43; Lloyd-Springer I 333–335; Bammesberger Nom. 71 (to Gk ἄρθρον ‘joint’), 244; Kluge-Seebold 52–53; Heidermanns 104–105; Boutkan OFED s.v.
