Goth arman ‘to have mercy, to pity’, OS part. gi-armod ‘poor, needy’, OHG armēn ‘to become poor’. Derived from *armaz ‘poor’, probably a calque of Lat miserēri (Kluge Btrg. Gesch. XXXV 159). Feist 57; Lehmann GED 43; Heidermanns 104.
ON erma ‘to keep unhappy’, OE irman ‘to make miserable, to afflict’. Derived from *armaz II. Holthausen AEEW 186; Vries ANEW 104; Heidermanns 104.

Goth adv. arniba ‘certainly’, ON ern ‘brisk, vigorous’. Related to Skt árṇa- ‘undulating, surging’ and further to ṛṇóti ‘to arise, to move’, Gk ὄρνυμι ‘to set into motion’ (Bartholomae Zd Wf VI 355). Torp-Falk 17; Brugmann IF XIX 384; Feist 58; Pokorny I 328; Vries ANEW 105; Frisk II 422–424; Onions 297; Lehmann GED 43–44; Heidermanns 105.

ON ǫrn ‘eagle’, OE earn id., OS arn id. (in prop.), OHG arn id. (i-stem). Based on *arōn. Structurally similar to Gk ὄρνις ‘bird’, ὄρνεον id. Holthausen AEEW 86; Pokorny I 326; Frisk II 421–422; Vries ANEW 688; Zalizniak II 220; Onions 324.

Goth ara ‘eagle’, ON ari id., OS aro id., OHG aro id. Related to Hitt ḫara(n)- id., Gk ὄρνις ‘bird’, Bret er ‘eagle’, cf. also Lith er‚lis, ar‚lis id., Slav *orьlъ id. Meillet Etudes 418; Torp- Falk 17; Suolahti DVN 345–352; Trautmann BSW 13; Feist 54–55; Pokorny I 325–326; Chemodanov SGGJa I 111; Fraenkel 122, KZ LVIII 285–287; Vries ANEW 13; Frisk II 421–422; Zalizniak II 220; Onions 324; Vasmer- Trubachev III 150–151; Lehmann GED 40; Bammesberger Nom. 176; Puhvel III 137–139; Lloyd-Springer I 341–344; Kluge-Seebold 2.

ON ars ‘behind, backside’, OE ears id., OS ars id., OHG ars id. Related to Gk ὄρρος id., Arm ofi, pl. ofik', continuing IE *orsos, cf. also OIr err ‘tail’ < *ersā. Pedersen Kelt. Gr. II 101; Torp-Falk 20; Holthausen AEEW 86; Pokorny I 340; Vries ANEW 14; Frisk II 427; Zalizniak I 129; Lloyd-Springer I 345–346; Bammesberger Nom. 54; Kluge-Seebold 54.

ON erta ‘to taunt, to tease’. Identical with Skt ardáyati ‘to make flow’ (causative of árdati, ṛdáti ‘to flow, to dissolve’), Lith ardÿti ‘to untie, to unstitch’. Torp-Falk 19; Marstrander apud Vries ANEW 105 (to *arzaz); Mayrhofer I 51; Pokorny I 63 (to OIr aird ‘point, top’), 329–330; Fraenkel 15–16; Vries ANEW 105.

ON arta ‘teal, Anas querquedula’, OE earte ‘wagtail’. Probably connected with *artjanan. Similarity with Lat ardea ‘heron’, Gk ἐρωδιός, =ωδιός id. may be deceptive. Torp-Falk 19; Holthausen AEEW 86; Walde-Hofmann I 64; Pokorny I 68; Chemodanov SGGJa I 59; Vries ANEW 15; Frisk I 572–573.
ON arär ‘plow’, Close to Gk ἄροτρον id., OIr arathar id., Lat arātrum id. Further connected with *arjanan. Torp-Falk 18; Walde-Hofmann I 62; Frisk I 147; Bammesberger Nom. 86.

OS arut ‘piece of ore’, OHG aruz ‘ore, piece of ore, lump of clay’. OHG meanings indicate an original word for marsh iron and contradict the popular hypothesis of an old borrowing going back to Sum urudu ‘copper’ (despite Karsten Germanen 196). Probably related to *erwōn, cf. Slav *želîzo ‘(marsh) iron’ ~ *žel(e)za ‘gland’. Torp-Falk 18; Ipsen IF XLI 174–175; Lloyd-Springer I 355–358; Kluge-Seebold 232; Trubachev VJa XXXIII/5 7–8.

ON arfi‘chickweed’, OE earfe ‘tare’, EFris arf, arve ‘vetch’. Borrowed from an unknown source as well as Lat eruum ‘vetch, pea’, Gk ὄροβος ‘bitter vetch’ (Hoops Waldbäume 463). Walde- Hofmann I 419– 420; Vries ANEW 13.

ON ǫrr ‘swift, ready’, OS aru ‘ready for harvesting’. Identical with Toch A ārwar, B ārwer ‘ready’, Av aurva- ‘quick, brave’. Bugge KZ XI (1870) 403 (to Skt árvant- ‘courser, horse’); Bartholomae 200; Torp-Falk 17; Holthausen AEEW 86; Karsten NB II 194, 412–414; Fraenkel Zfslav Ph XXI 138–140; Pokorny I 331; Vries ANEW 688; Lloyd- Springer I 311–312; Sanders Festschr. Kolb 558–568; Bammesberger Nom. 243; Heidermanns 106.
ON {rr ‘scar’, MLG are id. Close to Skt neut. áruṣ- ‘wound’. (Torp-Falk 19). Pokorny I 338; Chemodanov SGGJa I 107.

ON pl. ertr ‘pea’, OS erit id., MLG erwete id., OHG arawiz id. See *arfōn? Torp-Falk 19–20 (non-IE); Binz Zd Ph XXXVIII 371 (OHG araweiz < *arwa-aita-); Ipsen Festschr. Streitberg 230–232; Walde-Hofmann I 419–420; Vries ANEW 105; Frisk II 424.

Goth asilus ‘donkey, ass’, OE eosul id., OS esil id., OHG esil id. An early borrowing (with *-l- < -n-?) from Lat asinus id. (Kluge Urgerm. 68) or a later loan from Rom *áselus < Lat asellus id. (Luft ZDADL XLI 241–242). Forsterman KZ I 495; Schwentner PBB XLIV 497–498; Holthausen AEEW 93; Feist 59–60; Schulze Kl. Schr. 507 (follows Luft); Lehmann GED 45; Kluge-Seebold 233.
ON esja ‘a kind of clay’, OHG essa ‘chimney’. Related to *azenaz ~ *azenan. Torp-Falk 22; Pokorny I 68; Vries ANEW 105–106; Kluge- Seebold 234.

Burg *asks ‘ash-tree’, ON askr id., OE æsc id., OS ask id. (< *askiz), OHG asc id. (< *askiz). Close to Arm hac'i id., Alb ah ‘beech’ < PAlb *aska, cf. also Gk ὀξύα ‘beech’. Hoops Waldbäume 121; Zupitza Gutt. 159; Torp-Falk 23; Holthausen AEEW 12; Pokorny I 782; Vries ANEW 15; Frisk II 400; Zalizniak I 185; Onions 54; Normier Sprache XXVII 22–29 (Indo-Iranian parallels); Lloyd-Springer I 360–363; Kluge- Seebold 233; Orel AED 2.
ON eski ‘ashen box’, MDu esch id., esche ‘ash’. Derived from *askaz. Schulze KZ LXI 136; Vries ANEW 106.

Goth azgo ‘ashes’ (with voiced inlaut), ON aska id., OE æsce id., MLG asche id., OHG asca id. Connected with Arm a‘iun id. < *azg-. Cf., on the other hand, *ās- in Hitt ḫašša- ‘ashes, hearth’, Skt āsa- ‘ashes, dust’ and *azd- in Gk ἄζα ‘dry dirt’ (Walde KZ XXXI 290). Meillet Esquisse 29; Osthoff PBB XIII 396–399 (from *ast-k-ōn, to Gk ἄζω ‘to dry up, to parch’); Zupitza Gutt. 96; Grienberger Goten 41 (Goth suffix *-zvs. other Gmc *-k-); Torp-Falk 22; Kretschmer KZ XXXI 452; Holthausen AEEW 12; Feist 72; Specht 69; Mayrhofer I 83; Pokorny I 69; Chemodanov SGGJa I 110; Vries ANEW 15; Frisk I 25–26; Zalizniak I 155; Onions 54; Lehmann GED 54; Kluge- Seebold 56.
Goth asneis ‘day-laborer, hired man’, OE esne ‘servant’, OHG asni, esni id. Derived from *az(a)niz ~ *asaniz. Holthausen AEEW 94; Feist 60; Onions 297; Lehmann GED 45; Bammesberger Nom. 66–67.

ON ǫsp ‘asp’, OE æsp id., æspe id., OHG aspa id., Cf. also MLG espe id. < *aspjōn. Connected with Latv apse id., OPrus abse id., Slav *osa id. Torp-Falk 23; Trautmann BSW 11–13; Holthausen AEEW 13; Thieme KZ LXIX 175 (on Skt sphyá- id. < *aspi-); Pokorny I 55; Fraenkel 14; Vries ANEW 688; Zalizniak II 214; Janert KZ LXXIX 89–91, XCVII 202–203; Onions 54; Stang LS 13; Vasmer-Trubachev III 156; Lloyd- Springer I 370–372; Kluge-Seebold 234.

Goth asts ‘branch, bough’, OS ast id., OHG ast id. Note a derivative with a long grade *ōstaz ‘knot, knob’: OE óst, MLG ōst. Identical with Gk ὄζος ‘branch’, Arm ost ‘twig’. Further cf. Hitt ḫašt⁄er- ‘twigs, stick’. Bloomfield Language III 213–214 (*ozdo- < *o-s(e)do-); Sturtevant Language IV 163; Torp-Falk 559 (to Gk ὄσχος ‘shoot’); Holthausen AEEW 243; Feist 60; Pokorny I 280; Chemodanov SGGJa I 110; Frisk II 353; Darms Vṛddhi 236–238; Lehmann GED 45; Knobloch IF XCII 29–32; Puhvel III 339–340; Lloyd-Springer I 373–375; Kluge-Seebold 58.

OE ast ‘kiln’, MDu ast id. Identical with Gk ἄζα ‘dryness, heat’, Slav *ozdъ ‘malt-kiln, oast’. Frisk I 25–26; Pokorny I 68–69; Chemodanov SGGJa I 88 (to Lat aestus ‘heat’); Onions 619; Dybo BSA 229–230.

Goth at ‘from, at, with’, ON at ‘at, to’, OE æt id., OFris et id., OS at id., OHG az id. Related to Phryg ad-, Lat ad id., Umbr aṛ-, Osc az id., OIr ad-. Torp-Falk 9–10; Holthausen AEEW 13; Walde-Hofmann I 11–12; Feist 60–61; Pokorny I 3; Vries ANEW 16; Onions 58; Lehmann GED 45; Boutkan OFED s.v.

Goth atisk ‘standing grain, grainfield’ (masc. or neut.), MLG esch id., OHG ezzisca ‘seed’. Derived from *atjanan (Diefenbach VW I 78; Grienberger Got. 31). Grimm DG I 47; Lottner KZ VII 180 (from *ades-ko-, to Lat ador ‘spelt’); Fick I 351 (to Gk ἀθήρ ‘awn’); Torp- Falk 24; Schrader-Nehring II 648 (follow Lottner); Feist 61; Lane Language XIV 25 (to Toch A āti, B acc. atiyai ‘grass’); Pedersen Toch. 64 (against Lane); Pokorny I 3 (follows Lottner); Ramat Gedenkschr. Güntert 77–78 (follows Grienberger); Lehmann GED 46; Kluge- Seebold 233.

Goth fra-atjan ‘to give away (to be eaten)’, ON etja ‘to fodder’, OE ettan id., OFris etta id., MLG etten id., OHG ezzen id. Causative of *etanan. Holthausen AEEW 94; Feist 61; Vries ANEW 106; Seebold 180; Lehmann GED 208; Kluge-Seebold 61.

Goth atta ‘father’, ON atti id., OFris aththa id., OHG atto ‘ancestor’. A nursery word (Meillet BSL XXXIV/ 101 1–2), cf. Gk ἄττα id., Lat atta id. and the like. Torp-Falk 10; Friedrich ZDMG I (NF) 159 (from one of the languages of Asia Minor), Glotta XXIII 207–210; Feist 62; Martinet Gém. 56, 64–65, 161–162; Porzig Gliederung 129; Pokorny I 70; Vries ANEW 17; Zalizniak I 153; Benveniste IEL 169, 370; Lühr Expr. 254–255; Lloyd- Springer I 385–388; Lehmann GED 46; Kluge-Seebold 60–61.

Crim.-Goth atochta ‘bad’ (leg. atocha?). Identical with Hitt ḫatug(a)i- ‘horrible, frightening’ < *oduko- (Tischler KZ XCII 108–111), further to Lat ōdī ‘to hate’, Gk ὀδύσσομαι id., Arm ateam id. (Grienberger Zd Ph XXX 127; Tischler KZ XLII 108–111). Walde- Hofmann II 202–203; Feist 61; Pokorny I 773; Lehmann GED 46; Puhvel III 375–376; Heidermanns 106–107.

ON atall ‘fierce’, OE atol ‘dire, terrific, terrible’, MDu atel ‘terrible’. Cf. also ON etja ‘to fight, to contend’ < *atjanan. Related to Lat ōdi ‘to hate’, Gk ὀδύσσομαι id., Arm ateam id. (Fick BB I 334, II 195). See *atuzaz. Torp-Falk 10; Feist 61; Holthausen AEEW 8; Karsten NB II 274, 283; Walde- Hofmann II 202–203; Pokorny I 773; Vries ANEW 17; Frisk II 351; Heidermanns 107.

OSwed aäal ‘nature, disposition’, OHG adal ‘descent, lineage’. Cf. also OFris edila ‘grandfather’. Identical with Toch A ātäl ‘man’. Cf. also Gk ἀταλός ‘tender, delicate’. Grienberger Got. 104 (prefix *a-/*ō-); Torp-Falk 10; Holthausen AEEW 13; Neckel PBB XLI 385–436; Leumann Glotta XV 154 (against comparison with ἀταλός); Karsten NB II 274– 275, 370; Szemerényi Word VIII 42–50 (to Lat indoles ‘inborn quality, nature’); Pokorny I 71 (to *attōn); Mezger KZ LXXII 114; Vries ANEW 1–2; Frisk I 176; Zalizniak II 214; Benveniste 370; Van Windekens 171; Darms Vṛddhi 192–207; Palmer Denkschr. Güntert 15; Lloyd-Springer I 44–46; Heidermanns 109; Kluge-Seebold 14; Boutkan OFED s.v.

Burg *aþals ‘noble’, OESc aþul, aþal- ‘marital’, OE æäele ‘noble, eminent’, OFris ethele ‘having free citizenship’, OS ethili ‘of good origin’, OHG adal ‘noble’, edili ‘noble, distinguished’. Derived from *aþalan. Holthausen AEEW 13; Köbler 708; Heidermanns 107–109; Kluge-Seebold 14, 204; Boutkan OFED s.v.

Burg *aþalings ‘nobleman’, ON ǫälingr ‘prince’, OE æäelinz ‘king’s son, man’, OFris etheling ‘free citizen’, MLG edelinc ‘nobleman’, OHG ediling id. Derived from *aþalan. Holthausen AEEW 13; Vries ANEW 685; Köbler 708; Heidermanns 108; Boutkan OFED s.v.

ON øäli, eäli ‘patrimony, origin’, OE æäelo ‘nobility’, OS adali ‘noble origin’, OHG edili ‘clan, nobility’. Derived from *aþalan. Torp-Falk 10; Holthausen AEEW 13; Pokorny I 71; Vries ANEW 93–94; Zalizniak II 214; Heidermanns 108.
ON ǫälask ‘to gain, to win’, OE ze-æäelian ‘to render celebrated’. Derived from *aþalan. Heidermanns 108.

Goth aþ-þan ‘but then, but’. Related to Toch A atas, B ate ‘away’, Skt áti ‘beyond, very’, Av aiti id., OIr aith- ‘against’, Slav *otъ ‘from’. Bartholomae 54; Torp-Falk 10; Feist 63; Pokorny I 70; Vasmer-Trubachev III 168; Adams TB 10.

Goth aþn ‘year’. Identical with Lat annus id. < *atnos, Osc akeneí ‘in the year’, Umbr acc. pl. acnu ‘year’. Meyer KZ XXVIII 164 (further to Skt átati ‘to go, to walk’); Froehde BB XVI 196–197; Torp-Falk 10; Strachan BB XX 8; Brugmann IF XVII 492; Walde- Hofmann I 51; Feist 62–63; Szemerényi TPS 172–175 (Lat annus < *asnos); Pokorny I 69; Chemodanov SGGJa I 90; Lehmann GED 47.
Goth at-aþni ‘year’. Structurally close to Lat bi-ennium. Derived from *aþnaz. Walde-Hofmann I 51.
ON auä-fyndr ‘easily perceived, clear’, OE eáä-fynde ‘easy to be found’. Compound of *auđaz ~ *auđan and *funđiz II. Carr Nom. comp. 92 (parallel formations); Matzel KZ CV 105.

Goth audags ‘blessed, fortunate’, ON auäigr ‘rich, opulent’, OE eádiz ‘happy, blessed, prosperous’, OS ōdag ‘rich, opulent’, OHG ōtag id. Derived from *auđaz ~ *auđan. Zimmermann BB XXIII 275–276 (to Lat autumnus ‘autumn’); Torp-Falk 6; Holthausen AEEW 83; Feist 63; Porzig Gliederung 130; Vries ANEW 18; Lehmann GED 47.
ON auä-zœtt ‘easily got’, OE eáä-zete ‘easily got, prepared’. Compound of *auđaz ~ *auđan and a derivative of *zetanan. Carr Nom. comp. 92 (parallel formations).

ON auäinn ‘lent’, OE eáden ‘given’, OS ōdan ‘given as a gift’. Participle of the unattested verb *auđanan. See *auđaz ~ *auđan. Torp- Falk 6 (to Lith áusti ‘to weave’); Holthausen AEEW 83; Lindquist Festschr. Pipping 354; Jóhannesson IEW 7–8; Krahe Su V 105; Pokorny I 76; Vries ANEW 18; Seebold 83–84.

Burg *aud(s) ‘wealth’, ON auär ‘fate, destiny, wealth’ (partly < *auđiz), OE eád ‘possession, riches, prosperity, happiness’, OS ōd ‘happiness’. Cf. Goth auda-hafts ‘fortunate, blessed’, MHG klein-ōt ‘treasure’. Probably, connected with W udd < *audos ‘lord, master’, Bret ozac’h < *udakkos ‘home owner’. Also compared with Lat ūber ‘rich, fertile’ < *oudh-ero- (Szemerényi IBK XV 207). Loth RC XLI 234; Torp SHS 172 (to Lith áudžu, áusti ‘to weave’); Fowkes Goth. 5–6 (to IE *au(6)- ‘to increase’); Torp-Falk 6 (same as Torp); Holthausen AEEW 83; Walde- Hofmann II 738–739; Porzig Gliederung 130; Pokorny I 62–76; Chemodanov SGGJa I 72; Vries ANEW 18 (separates ON auär ‘riches, wealth’ from auär ‘fate, destiny’); Zalizniak I 185; Mezger KZ LXXXII 288–297 (to Gk αὐτός ‘self’); Seebold 83; Lehmann GED 47; Köbler 708; Bammesberger Nom. 54.
Goth auga-dauro ‘window’, OE eáz-dúru id., OHG ouga-tora id. Compound of *auzōn and *đuran, *đurō. Carr Nom. comp. 44.
Norw dial. öygna ‘to show’, MLG t-ōnen id., OHG ougenen id. Derived from *auzōn. Torp-Falk 5.
late ON eygär ‘having eyes’, OE súr-eázede ‘blear-eyed’. Derived from *auzjanan. Torp-Falk 5; Vries ANEW 107.

Goth augjan ‘to show’, ON eygja ‘to furnish with an eye’, OE eáwan ‘to show, to manifest’, OS ōgan ‘to show’, OHG ougen id. Derived from *auzōn. Osthoff PBB VIII 261–262; Torp-Falk 5; Holthausen AEEW 87; Feist 64; Vries ANEW 107; Lehmann GED 48.
ON eygr ‘having eyes’, OE súr-íze ‘blear-eyed’, OHG sūr-ougi ‘with watery eyes’. Derived from *auzōn. Torp-Falk 5; Vries ANEW 107.

Goth augo ‘eye’, ON auga id., OE eáze id., OFris āge id., OS ōga id., OHG ouga id. From *az(w)ōn influenced by *auzōn. Further to IE *ok⁄- id.: Toch A ak, B ek, Skt ákṣi, Av dual aši, Gk ὄσσε, Alb sy, Lith akīs, Slav *oko (Schmidt KZ II 381–382). A consonantal stem *azwz and an i-stem *azwiz can be reconstructed on the basis of compounds (Schmidt Plur. 106–108, 388–405). Goth aha ‘mind, understanding’ hardly belongs here (despite Fick I 13; Zupitza Gutt. 72; Osthoff IF XXVII 175). Meillet LHLG I 289–290 (taboo); Zupitza Gutt. 73–74 (to Slav *aviti ‘to show’); Torp- Falk 5; Stokes KZ XXXV 151–152 (to MIr uag ‘den, cave’); Holthausen AEEW 83; Specht KZ LXII 211; Feist 64–65; Mayrhofer I 16; Pokorny I 775–777; Fraenkel 5; Vries ANEW 19; Frisk II 436; Zalizniak I 157; Onions 340; Vasmer-Trubachev III 128–129; Lehmann GED 48; Bammesberger Nom. 173–174; Kluge-Seebold 65; Huld Festschr. Puhvel I 85; Adams TB 74–75; Orel AED 405; Boutkan OFED s.v.

Run auja ‘good fortune’. Related to Skt ávati ‘to be pleased, to strive, to promote’, Av avaiti ‘to help’, Gk ἀΐτης ‘friend, beloved’. Bartholomae 162; Mayrhofer I 57; Pokorny I 683; Vries ANEW 19; Frisk I 47; Krause Run. I 241–244, 261–263; Lehmann GED 52.

Goth aukan ‘to increase, to augment’, ON auka id., OFris āka id., OHG auhhan id. Connected with Toch B auk- ‘to grow, to increase’, Lat augeō id., Lith áugu, áugti ‘to grow’, Latv aªgt id. Zupitza Gutt. 160; Torp-Falk 5; Walde- Hofmann I 82–83; Feist 67; Jóhannesson IEW 10; Pokorny I 85; Fraenkel 24; Vries ANEW 19; Anttila Schw. 91; Onions 304; Seebold 84–85; Lehmann GED 50; Adams TB 130; Boutkan OFED s.v.

Goth auk ‘but, also, furthermore’, ON auk ‘also’, OE eác ‘also, however’, OFris āk ‘also’, OS ōk id., OHG ouh id. Identical with Gk αὖ-ge ‘again’. A compound of *au (Gk αὖ ‘again’) and particle *ἵε. Cf. also Toch A -ok, B -uk < *u-·e (Van Windekens I 329–330). Torp-Falk 5; Holthausen AEEW 82; Feist 67; Pokorny I 74; Vries ANEW 19; Frisk I 183; Onions 304; Seebold 84 (to *aukan); Lehmann GED 50; Kluge- Seebold 61–62; Adams TB 109; Boutkan OFED s.v.
ON auka ‘to increase’ (weak forms, cf. *aukanan), OE ókian id., MLG ōken id., OHG ouhhōn id. Derived from *aukōn. Pokorny I 85; Vries ANEW 19; Seebold 84.
ON auki ‘increase’, OE eáca ‘addition, increase, advantage’, OFris āka ‘increase’, MLG ōke id. Derived from *aukanan. Holthausen AEEW 83; Vries ANEW 19; Seebold 84.

Norw dial. aul ‘pipe’. Identical with Gk αὐλός id., Lith aªlas ‘bootleg’. Further related to Toch B pl. auloñ ‘blood vessels’, Lith aul‹s ‘beehive’, Slav *ulь(jь) id. Torp-Falk 6; Pokorny I 88–89; Frisk I 186–187; Adams TB 134.

ON aumr ‘poorly, miserable’. Identical with Toch B aume ‘misery, poverty’ (Krause GGA 1958/1–3 54; Strunk Festschr. Eggers 31–35). Karsten NB II 244, 247; Vries ANEW 19; Heidermanns 109–110; Adams TB 132; Burlak IFTJa 240.

ON aurr ‘wet clay, mud, wet soil’, OE ear ‘sea, ocean’. Related to Gk ἄναυρος ‘without water’, Thrac rvn. Αὔρας (Kretschmer Glotta X 51, XIV 93), Latv rvn. Aure. See *ūran. Gering Komm. I 24 (reconstructs *auza- = Lat aurum ‘gold’); Torp-Falk 6 (separate ON aurr ‘clay, soil’ to be compared with *ūran); Persson IF XXXV 199–200; Endzelin KZ LII 116–117; Janzén NB XXIV 247–253; Feist 67–68; Holthausen AEEW 85; Detschew Thr. 39; Pokorny I 80; Vries ANEW 20; Frisk I 103; Vendryes W–26 (to OIr úr ‘soil, earth’); Lehmann GED 50; Dybo BSA 33.

ON ausa ‘to sprinkle’, MLG ōsen ‘to scoop’, MHG ōsen id. Identical with Gk αὔω ‘to get a light, to light a fire’ (with a secondary semantic development, see Fick BB II 187), cf. further Lat hauriō ‘to draw’ < earlier *auriō. Torp-Falk 6; Walde-Hofmann I 637; Vries ANEW 21; Schwyzer I 644; Schulze Kl. Schr. 190–191; Pokorny I 90; Frisk I 193; Seebold 85.
ON ausa ‘ladle’, OE ease ‘glass, tumbler’. Derived from *ausanan. Holthausen AEEW 86; Vries ANEW 21.
ON austan ‘from the East’, OE eástan id., OS ōstana id., OHG ōstana ‘eastward’. Derived from *austaz. Torp- Falk 6; Holthausen AEEW 86; Vries ANEW 21; Onions 298; Kluge-Seebold 605.

OE eást ‘East’, late MHG ost id. Related to Skt uṣā ‘morning light’, Gk Hom ἠώς ‘dawn’, Lat aurōra ‘daybreak, dawn’, Lith aušrā id. Torp-Falk 6; Holthausen AEEW 86; Walde- Hofmann I 86; Pokorny I 86–87; Fraenkel 27; Frisk I 605–606; Onions 298; Kluge-Seebold 605.
ON austr ‘drawing water’. Derived from *ausanan. Structurally close to Gk ἐξαυστήρ: κρεάγρα, αὐστήρ: μέτρου ὄνομα. Pokorny I 90; Frisk I 529.

Burg adj. *austr ‘Northern’, ON austr ‘East’, OE eáster, eástre ‘Easter’, OHG pl. ōstara id. (fem.). Related to *austaz. Continues *ausro-, cf., Lat auster ‘South wind’ (< *aus-tero-), Lith aušrā ‘dawn’, Latv austrā id., austra vējš ‘Eastern wind’. Torp- Falk 6; Holthausen AEEW 87; Walde- Hofmann I 87; Pokorny I 87; Vries ANEW 21; Onions 298; Lehmann GED 270 (on Goth ostro- in Ostrogothae); Köbler 708; Kluge-Seebold 605–606.
ON austrœnn ‘eastern’, OE eástern id. OS ōstrōni id. OHG sundōstrōni ‘south-eastern’. Derived from *austraz ~ *austran. Holthausen AEEW 87; Vries ANEW 21.

ON auä-fengr ‘easy to get’, OE íäe ‘easy, pleasant’, OS ōthi, ōtho ‘light, easy’, OHG ōdi, ōdo id. Close to Lat ōtium ‘leisure’, further cf. Skt ávati ‘to be pleased, to strive, to promote’. See *aujan. Torp-Falk 5; Falk-Torp 1407; Holthausen AEEW 87, 187; Karsten NB II 390–391; Walde-Hofmann II 228–229; Pokorny I 77; Vries ANEW 18; Mezger KZ LXXXII 288–297; Heidermanns 110.
ON eyäa ‘to waste, to destroy’, OE íäan ‘to lay waste, to destroy’, OHG fir-ōden ‘to waste, to desert’. Derived from *auþjaz. Torp-Falk 5; Mezger KZ LXXXII 288–297; Heidermanns 111.

Goth auþeis ‘barren, desolate, deserted’, ON auär ‘desert, empty’, OE íeäe ‘desert, forlorn’, OHG ōdi ‘desert, empty’. Identical with Gk αὔσιος ‘empty, vain’ (Schulze Kl. Schr. 71), thus historically may be related to *auþaz ~ *auþōn. Thurneysen OI 41 (to OIr úathad ‘small number’); Torp-Falk 4–5; Feist 69–70; Holthausen AEEW 187; Karsten NB II 387–389; Mezger Word II 229, KZ LXXXII 288 (to Gk αὐτός); Pokorny I 73; Vries ANEW 18; Frisk I 191; Lehmann GED 52 (identical with *auþaz ~ *auþōn); Heidermanns 111; Kluge- Seebold 597.

Goth auso ‘ear’ (*-s-), ON eyra id., OE eáre id., OFris āre id., OS ōra id., OHG ōra id. Related to Av uš- id., Arm unkn id., Gk Dor ὦς id., Alb vesh id., Lat auris id., OIr áu id., Lith ausīs id., Slav *uxo id. Kuhn I 377; Schmidt Plur. 109, 406–407; Bartholomae 414; Torp-Falk 7; Meringer SBAW CXXV/2 12–13 (to *ōus- ‘mouth’); Holthausen AEEW 85; Walde-Hofmann I 85–86; Feist 69; Pokorny I 785; Fraenkel 26; Vries ANEW 107–108; Zalizniak I 157; Frisk II 448–449; Onions 297; Vasmer- Trubachev IV 179; Lehmann GED 51; Bammesberger Nom. 174; Orel AED 501; Kluge-Seebold 599; Boutkan OFED s.v.

Goth aweþi ‘herd of sheep’ (-þ- < *-đ-, irreg. -e-), OE eówd(e) id., OHG ouwiti id. Derived from *awiz. Bremer Btrg. Gesch. XI 32 (reconstructs Goth *aweiþi); Holthausen AEEW 93; Feist 70; Onions 332; Benveniste 48; Lehmann GED 52.

Goth awistr ‘sheepfold’, OE éwestre id. (masc.). Derived from *awiz. Osthoff KZ XXIII 316 (from *awi-wistraz); Meillet MSL XII 218–219 (from *o⁄i-sth(e)-ro-); Bezzenberger KZ XXII 267–269; Kluge Nom. Stamm. 51–52; Holthausen AEEW 93; Feist 70–71; Onions 332; Lindeman Verschärfung 16.4; Lehmann GED 53; Bammesberger Nom. 88.

ON ær ‘ewe’ (root stem), OE eówu id. (< *awjō), OFris ei id., OS ewi ‘lamb’, OHG ouwi, ou ‘sheep’. Identical with Hitt ḫa⁄i- ‘sheep’, Toch B ā(u)w id., Skt ávi- id., Gk ὄϊς id., Lat ovis id., OIr ói id., Lith avīs id., Slav *ovь-ca id. (Forsterman KZ I 493). Torp-Falk 22; Trautmann BSW 20–21; Mikkola Festschr. Streitberg 269; Holthausen AEEW 93; Walde-Hofmann II 229; Mayrhofer I 59; Pokorny I 784; Fraenkel 28; Frisk II 367–368; Vries ANEW 681; Zalizniak II 206; Onions 332; Trubachev Zhiv. 69–71; Vasmer-Trubachev III 116; Puhvel III 379–380; Adams TB 35.

ON ái ‘great-grandfather’. Cf. also Goth awo ‘grandmother’. Related to Hitt ḫuḫḫa- ‘grandfather’, Toch āwe id., Arm hav id., Lat auus id., OPrus awis ‘uncle’, Slav *ujь id. Uhlenbeck PBB XXX 263 (Lallwort); Torp-Falk 22; Walde-Hofmann I 88–89; Feist 71; Pokorny I 89; Benveniste IEL 181; Toporov PJa I 179–180; Adams TB 56.

Goth asans ‘harvest, harvest time’, ON ǫnn ‘working season’, OE masc. ern ‘harvest’, OFris ern id., OS aran-fimba ‘mow, rick’, asna ‘tax, levy’ (< *asnō), OHG arn ‘harvest’. Related to OPrus assanis ‘harvest’, Slav *esenь ‘autumn’. Cf. verbal derivatives in OE earnian ‘to earn’, MLG arnen ‘to reap’, OHG arnōn id. Weinhold KZ I 247; Froehde BB I 32 (to Lat annōna ‘annual yield’ < *ann-osnā); Bezzenberger BB V 176 (to Skt neut. vasná- ‘price, value’); Torp-Falk 18, 22; Pedersen IF V 44; Schulze Qu. 475 (to Gk ὥρα ‘spring’); Grienberger Got. 30 (to *askōn); Holthausen AEEW 86, 94; Feist 58–59; Walde-Hofmann I 50; Pokorny I 343; Vries ANEW 687–688; Zalizniak II 192; Martynov Slav.-germ. 133 (from Slavic); Onions 297; Stang LS 25; Trubachev ESSJa VI 28–29; Ramat Gedenkschr. Güntert 71; Lehmann GED 44; Lloyd- Springer I 304–307; Wagner KZ CV 273–275; Kluge-Seebold 231.

ON arinn ‘hearth’, OHG erin ‘floor, floorboard’. Related to Hitt loc. ḫašši ‘herd’, Toch AB as- ‘to dry’, Skt āsa- ‘ashes, dust’, Lat āra ‘altar’, Osc loc. aasaí id. Torp-Falk 18 (to Lat ārea ‘ground, vacant space, threshing floor’); Schröder Festschr. Streitberg 340–343 (to Skt íriṇa- ‘hole in the ground’); Reichelt KZ XLVI 315–316; Holthausen KZ LXXI 55 (to Gk ἀραρίσκω ‘to fit together, to equip’); Walde-Hofmann I 61; Mayrhofer I 83; Pokorny I 68; Chemodanov SGGJa I 66; Vries ANEW 13; Knobloch Festschr. Polomé 347–352 (to Lat ārea).

Goth badi ‘bed’, ON beär id. (< *bađjaz ~ *bađiz), OE bedd id., OFris bed id., OS bed, beddi id., OHG betti id. Probably connected with W bedd, Corn bedh, Bret béz ‘grave’ (Meringer IF XIX 448; further see Posch). Dubious. Fick I, 491; Posch Wu S XVI 4–16 (to *bhedh- ‘to dig, to pierce’); Braune PBB XXIII 250; Bugge PBB XIII 176 (to Slav *podъ ‘base, bottom support’); Torp-Falk 258; Holthausen AEEW 18; Feist 73; Pokorny I 114; Vries ANEW 29 (to *baþan); Zalizniak II 212; Onions 84; Lehmann GED 55; Lloyd-Springer I 572–574; Bammesberger Nom. 65; Kluge- Seebold 104; Boutkan OFED s.v.
ON beäja ‘wife, bedfellow’, OE ze-bedda id., OFris bedda ‘husband’, OS gi-beddio id., MHG ge-bette ‘wife, bed-fellow’. Derived from *bađjan. Vries ANEW 29.

ON bǫä ‘battle’, OE beadu ‘battle, war’, OS badu- (in cmpn.), OHG batu- (in cmpn.). Cf. Burg *badus id. Related to OIr bodb ‘crow, image of the battle goddess’. Further cf. a semantically distant but formally similar Slav *body, gen. *bodъve ‘fish-spear, harpoon’. Grimm KZ I 437; Torp-Falk 256; Torp-Falk 256; Holthausen AEEW 17; Pokorny I 114; Chemodanov SGGJa I 96; Vries ANEW 69; Zalizniak I 149; Trubachev ESSJa II 155; Köbler 708.

Goth bagms ‘tree’, ON baämr, barmr id. (with assimilation), OSwed bagn id. An irregular phonetic transformation of *baumaz (Johansson PBB XV 224–225; Uhlenbeck PBB XXX 263–264). Schweizer KZ I 159; Meringer IF XVI 158 (to *baþan, isolates *bazmaz from *baumaz); Grienberger Got. 42 (to Skt bāhú- ‘arm’); Torp-Falk 257–258; Feist 73–74; Pokorny I 149; Vries ANEW 22; Zalizniak II 226; Peeters KZ LXXXVIII 129–133; Lehmann GED 55–56.

Goth baidjan ‘to compel, to force’, ON beiäa ‘to request, to ask’, OE bædan ‘to constrain, to compel’, OS bēdian ‘to compel’, OHG beiten ‘to compel, to urge’. Causative of *bīđanan (Wood MP IV 489–490). Structurally similar to Lith baidÿti ‘to frighten’ and Slav *bîditi ‘to constrain, to persuade, to cause evil’ (Vasmer-Trubachev I 142) unless the latter is a denominative. Torp-Falk 270–271; Zimmer KZ XXX 43–45; Sommer IF XI 79 (to Lat boiae ‘collar’); Holthausen AEEW 14; Feist 74; Pokorny I 117; Vries ANEW 30; Stang LS 14–15; Benveniste IEL 95, 99; Peeters KZ XCIV 206–207; Seebold 95; Trubachev ESSJa II 56–57 (Slavic verb as denominative); Lehmann GED 56.

ON bein ‘bone’, OE bán id., OFris bēn id., OS bēn id., OHG bein id. Continues *bhoino- based on IE *bhei- ‘to strike’ (Bugge PBB XXIV 459; Wood MLN XXIX 69). For the semantic development cf. Slav *bedro ‘hip’ < *bhedh- ‘to strike, to prick’. Doederlein LS VI 125 (to Lat femur ‘hip’ < *bhedh-); Hellquist ANF VII 7 (to ON beinn ‘straight’); Wadstein Zd Ph XXVIII 529 (same as Hellquist); Breal MSL V 158; Torp-Falk 257 (same as Doederlein); Hirt Idg. Gr. I 312 (from *bhonƒo-); Holthausen AEEW 16; Pokorny I 117–118; Vries ANEW 30; Zalizniak I 139; Onions 106; Lloyd- Springer I 515–516; Bammesberger KZ CIII 264–268 (to Lat fīnis ‘boundary’); Heidermanns 113; Kluge-Seebold 94; Boutkan OFED s.v.

ON beit ‘ship’, OE bát ‘boat, ship, vessel’, OFris bāt ‘boat’. Probably related to *bītanan (Lidén Festschr. Bugge 85–86, HVSU VI/1 34). Torp-Falk 270; Holthausen AEEW 16; Vries ANEW 30 (to *baitjanan); Onions 103; Seebold 97; Kluge- Seebold 125–126.
Norw beitel ‘chisel’, MLG beitel, bētel id., MHG beizel ‘stylus, prick’. Derived from *bītanan. Torp-Falk 270.

ON beita ‘to yoke’, OE bætan ‘to bridle, to rein in, to restrain’, OS und-bētian ‘to dismount’, OHG beizen ‘to spurn on’. Causative of *bītanan. Torp-Falk 270; Holthausen AEEW 15; Pokorny I 116; Vries ANEW 30–31; Seebold 97.
ON beiti ‘pasturage, alum’, OE ze-bæte ‘bit (of a bridle)’, MLG bēte ‘bit’, MHG ge-beize ‘falconry’. Cf. OHG beiza ‘alum’ < *baitō. Derived from *bītanan. Vries ANEW 31; Seebold 97.

Goth baitrs ‘bitter’, ON prop. Beitr. An ablaut variant of *bitraz. Derived from *bītanan. Niedermann IF XXXVII 145–147 (to Slav *bridъkъ ‘sharp’); Wood MLN XX 41–42; Torp- Falk 270; Feist 77; Vries ANEW 31; Onions 97; Seebold 97; Lehmann GED 58–59; Heidermanns 115.

Burg *bairs ‘boar’, OE bár id., OS bēr-swīn id., OHG bēr id. Identical with Lith baĩsas ‘terrible apparition’, Slav *bîsъ ‘demon’ < *bhoidh-s-o- (Kluge- Seebold 80). Torp-Falk 257 (reconstruct *bairaz); Wissman Postv. 78; Holthausen AEEW 16; Onions 103; Köbler 708; Lloyd-Springer I 542.

ON bak ‘back’, OE bæc id., OFris bek id., OS bak id., OHG bahho id. (<*bakōn). Of unknown origin. The similarity with Slav *bokъ ‘side’ (Mladenov 40), itself unclear, is semantically attractive but phonetically difficult. Zupitza Gutt. 160; Torp-Falk 259; Holthausen AEEW 14; Vries ANEW 23; Zalizniak II 227; Onions 68; Kluge-Seebold 72; Boutkan OFED s.v.

OE bacan ‘to bake’, MLG backen id., OHG backan, bahhan id. (*-kk-). With its pret. *bōka, related to Gk φώγω ‘to roast’. Persson Wurzelerw. 190; Zupitza Gutt. 213; Torp-Falk 257; Holthausen AEEW 14; Pokorny I 113; Frisk II 1057; Onions 69; Seebold 87– 88; Lloyd-Springer I 419–421; Kluge- Seebold 72 (to Skt bhrjáti ‘to roast’); Peeters IF LXXXI 30–31.

ON bekkr ‘brook, rivulet’ (< *bakjaz), OE bece id., OFris -bitze (in compounds), OS beki id., OHG bah id. Of uncertain origin. Cf. *bankōn? Zupitza Gutt. 160; Mikkola BB XXI 218 (to Slav *bagno ‘marsh’); van Wijk IF XXIV 233 (to MIr búal ‘flowing water’ < *bhogloand further to *bheg ⁄- ‘to run’, or to Slav *bagno ‘marsh’); Torp-Falk 257; Holthausen AEEW 18; Pokorny I 161; Chemodanov SSGJa I 54; Vries ANEW 31; Zalizniak II 212; Onions 84; Peeters IF 212–214; Lloyd-Springer I 427–429; Kluge-Seebold 71.
ON baka ‘to bake’, OHG bachōn ‘to bake’. Derived from *bakanan ‘to bake’. Seebold 87.

ON mann-baldr ‘great man’, theon. Baldr, OE bealdor ‘prince, hero’. Derived from *balþaz. Grimm DM I 202; Schröder ZDADL XXXV 241 (to Lith báltas ‘white’); Meringer IF XVIII 282–285 (to *bulaz ~ *bulō); Kuhn Festschr. Helm 37–45; Schröder GRM XXXIV 168; Holthausen AEEW 17; Pokorny I 121; Vries ANEW 24; Lehmann GED 60.

Goth balgs ‘bag made of skin, wine-skin’, ON belgr ‘skin, bellows’, OE belz ‘bulge, bag, purse’, NFris balge ‘bag’, MLG balch id., OHG balg id. Related to Ir bolg ‘sack’, OPrus balsinis ‘pillow’, Slav *bolzina ‘pillow, beam’ continuing *bhel·h- ‘to swell’. See *belzanan. Pedersen Kelt. Gr. I 105 (to Lat follis ‘hose’ < *bhol·hnis); Zupitza Gutt. 26, 204; Torp-Falk 268; Feist 78; Lane Language IX 255; Vendryes BSL XLI 134–139; Pokorny I 125–126; Vries ANEW 32; Zalizniak I 149; Onions 87–88; Stang LS 14; Seebold 99; Trubachev ESSJa II 183–184; Van Windekens I 371 (to Toch B pelke ‘solemn but joyous utterance’); Lehmann GED 59; Lloyd-Springer I 438–440; Bammesberger Nom. 133 (to OE belzan ‘to make oneself angry, to swell with anger’); Kluge-Seebold 75; Adams TB 399 (against Van Windekens).
ON belgja ‘to inflate’, OE a-belzan ‘to anger, to irritate’, OS ar-belgid ‘incensed’, OHG gi-belgen ‘to irritate’. Derived from *belzanan. Seebold 99.

OHG belihha ‘coot’. Close to Lat fulica id. < *bholikā (Sommer HL 66; Vani‘ek Lat. 181). Suolahti VDN 302 (to *bhel- ‘white’ because of a white spot on the bird’s forehead); Walde-Hofmann I 559–560; Pokorny I 119; Lloyd- Springer I 431–434; Kluge-Seebold 96.

ON bjálki ‘beam’, OE balca ‘beam, bank, ridge’, OFris balka ‘beam’, MLG balke id., OHG balco id. Related to Gk φάλαγξ ‘line of battle, phalanx, beam’. Torp-Falk 267–268; Pokorny I 123; Vries ANEW 70; Frisk II 985–986; Onions 70.

ON bǫllr ‘ball’, OHG bal id. Related to Lat follis ‘bellows, ball’, Gk φαλλός ‘penis’, Thrac ethn. Tri-βαλλοί, OIr ball ‘membrum’. Torp-Falk 267; Kerényi Glotta XXII 41; Walde- Hofmann I 524–525; Schulze Kl. Schr. 308; Detschew Thr. 523–526; Pokorny I 120; Vries ANEW 70; Frisk II 987–988; Onions 70; Lerchner 54; Lloyd- Springer I 430–431; Kluge-Seebold 76.

Goth adv. balþaba ‘boldly, openly’, ON ballr ‘hard, stubborn’, OE beald ‘bold, brave, confident’, OS bald ‘valiant, bold’, OHG bald ‘brave, courageous’. The old comparison with Lith báltas ‘white’ based on the semantic development into ‘strong’ or ‘shining’ (Grimm DM I 202) could be tenable. Schweizer KZ I 479; Uhlenbeck PBB XXXV 161–180; Torp-Falk 267; Osthoff PBB XVIII 255–256 (prefix b-, -alþaz to *aljanan II); Feist 78–79; Holthausen AEEW 17; Pokorny I 121; Vries ANEW 24 (to *bhel 6- ‘to swell’, cf. Norw bala ‘to rut’); Onions 105; Lehmann GED 60 (same as Vries); Lloyd-Springer I 434–436; Matzel KZ CV 121 (< *bhol 6-to-, to *bhel 6- ‘to swell’); Heidermanns 115–116; Kluge-Seebold 75.

Goth balþjan ‘to be bold, to dare’, ON bella ‘to deal with’, OE byldan ‘to make bold, to encourage’, OS beldian ‘to make valiant’, MHG balden ‘to hurry’. Derived from *balþaz. Holthausen AEEW 22; Feist 78–79; Vries ANEW 32; Lehmann GED 60.
Goth þrasa-balþei ‘quarrelsomeness’, OE beldo ‘boldness, rashness’, OHG baldī ‘courage, boldness’. Derived from *balþaz. Holthausen AEEW 22; Feist 78; Onions 94; Heidermanns 115.

Goth balwa-wesei ‘malice, wickedness’, ON bǫl ‘misfortune, bale’, OE bealu ‘woe, harm, wickedness, depravity’, OFris balu-mon ‘unfaithful guardian’, OS balu ‘wickedness, misfortune’, OHG balo ‘disease, sickness’ (masc.). Connected with OCorn bal ‘illness’ < *bal⁄- (Pictet KZ V 351: also adds Slav *bol’ь ‘pain, illness’). Kuhn KZ I 516 (to Gk φαῦλος ‘weak, little’); Berneker I 71 (against the comparison with Slav *bol’ь reflecting no traces of *-⁄-); Torp-Falk 268–269; Schmidt KZ XXXII 342; Meringer IF XXI 296 (to Slav *bъlvanъ ‘idol’); Schrader KZ XXX 466 (to Lat fallō ‘to deceive’); Kretschmer KZ XXXI 449 (same as Kuhn); Schmidt KZ XXXIV 342; Bugge PBB XIII 182 (prefix b-); Osthoff PBB XVIII 256 (same as Bugge, -alwan to Gk Hom ὀλοός ‘destructive, deadly’); Feist 79; Holthausen AEEW 17; Weisweiler IF XLI 70– 77; Karsten NB II 195–196; Pokorny I 125; Chemodanov SGGJa I 80; Vries ANEW 70; Zalizniak II 165; Onions 70; Stang LS 15; Seebold Lu E 482–483 (from *·h⁄el- ‘to exchange’); Lehmann GED 60; Lloyd-Springer I 444–445; Bammesberger Nom. 243; Trubachev ESSJa II 188 (agrees with Berneker); Boutkan OFED s.v.

ON bǫl-víss ‘detestable’, OS sbst. bala-wīso ‘malice’ (masc. n-stem). Cf. also Goth balwa-wesei ‘malice, wickedness’ for which *balwa-wīsīn may be conjectured (Schubert Erw. 69). Compound of *balwan and *wīsaz. Feist 79; Lehmann GED 60.

ON band ‘bond, band’, OFris band id., OS hōbid-band ‘crown’, OHG bant ‘priest’s band, band’. Identical with Skt bandhá- ‘bond, fetter’, Av bandaid. Cf. also Toch B pānto ‘support, aid’. Derived from *benđanan. Bartholomae 926–927; Torp-Falk 259; Mayrhofer II 407; Pokorny I 127; Zalizniak II 216; Vries ANEW 25; Onions 72; Seebold 103; Kluge-Seebold 77; Adams TB 365.
ON bendill ‘small cord, string’, ME bendel id., MLG bendel id., OHG bentil ‘band’. Derived from *banđjō. Vries ANEW 32; Seebold 103; Kluge- Seebold 97.
ON benzl ‘bow in a bent state’, MLG bendsel ‘association’. Derived from *banđjō. Vries ANEW 32.
ON bendi ‘cord’, MLG bende id., MHG bende id. Derived from *benđanan. Vries ANEW 32.
ON benda ‘to bend (a bow), to join’, OE bendan ‘to bend’, MHG benden id. Derived from *benđanan. Torp-Falk 259; Holthausen AEEW 19; Vries ANEW 32; Onions 88; Seebold 103.

Goth bandi ‘bond, fetter’. Identical with MIr buinne ‘band’ < *bhondhƒā. Derived from *benđanan. Cf. WGmc *banđiz: OE bend ‘band, bond, ribbon’, OFris bend ‘bond, tie’, bende id., OS bendi id. Torp-Falk 259; Holthausen AEEW 19; Feist 79; Pokorny I 127; Zalizniak II 216; Seebold 103; Lehmann GED 60–61; Bammesberger Nom. 134.

ON báss ‘stall in a cowhouse’, LG banes ‘interior of a barn’ (fem.), G Banse ‘bay in a barn’. Cf. also OE bós-iz ‘winter cow-stall’. Derived from *benđanan (Johansson IF XIX 116). Goriaev 9 (to Russ baz ‘cowshed’); Torp- Falk 259; Uhlenbeck PBB XXX 264; Holthausen AEEW 31; Feist 80–81; Vries ANEW 28; Seebold 103; Lehmann GED 61–62; Hessmann Festschr. de Smet 185–193; Bammesberger Nom. 77.

Goth bansts ‘barn’, OFris bōste ‘marital tie’. Derived from *banđan (Diefenbach VW 274) or *banđsaz. Grienberger Got. 43; Torp-Falk 259; Uhlenbeck PBB XXX 264; Schrader KZ XXX 483–484 (to Gk συ-φεός ‘pigsty’); Johansson IF XIX 114–116; Wiedemann BB XXVIII 61; Feist 80–81, PBB XV 546; Seebold 103; Lehmann GED 61–63; Kluge-Seebold 79.
Goth bandwjan ‘to mark, to make a sign’, ON benda ‘to give a sign, to beckon’. Derived from *banđwō. Torp-Falk 256; Feist 79; Pokorny I 104; Vries ANEW 32; Lehmann GED 61.

Goth bandwa ‘sign’. Probably related to the isolated Lith bandÿti ‘to try’, OPrus per-bandan ‘trial’. Meyer Goth. 59 (to IE *bhā- ‘to shine’); Diefenbach VW 296–297 (to *bendanan); Wood MP II 471 (same as Diefenbach; semantically *‘knotted cloth’); Torp-Falk 256; Feist 79–80; Pokorny I 104; Fraenkel 34; Lehmann GED 61 (follows Meyer).

Icel banga ‘to hammer’, E bang, G dial. bangen ‘to knock’. If not of imitative origin, probably related to Skt bhanákti ‘to break’, Arm bek ‘broken’, Lith beñgti ‘to finish’ (Vries ANEW 25). Torp-Falk 256 (to *banōn); Trautmann BSW 26; Mayrhofer II 469; Pokorny I 114–115; Fraenkel 34.
ON bend ‘wound’, OFris benethe id., OS baneäi id. Related to *banjō. Vries ANEW 32.

Goth banja ‘wound’, ON ben id., OE ben id., OS beni-wunda id. Derived from *banōn. Grimm KZ I 82; Schade AW I 39 (to IE *g ⁄hen- ‘to strike’); Torp- Falk 256 (to *bađwō); Scheftelowitz BB XXIX 37 (to Arm banam ‘to open’); Torp-Falk 256; Holthausen AEEW 19; Feist 80–81 (to Av banta- ‘sick’); Pokorny I 126; Vries ANEW 32; Zalizniak II 173; Onions 72; Seebold KZ LXXXI 113 (follows Schade); Vendryes B-32–34 (to OIr benaid ‘to beat, to strike’); Lehmann GED 61; Bammesberger Nom. 112.
ON benja ‘to wound mortally’, OE bennian ‘to wound’. Derived from *banjō. Holthausen AEEW 19; Vries ANEW 32.
ON bekk-þili ‘wainscotted walls of a hall’, OE benc-äel ‘bench-floor’. Compound of *bankiz and *þeljan. Carr Nom. comp. 80 (parallel formations).

Burg *banki ‘bench’, ON bekkr id., OE benc id., OFris benk, bank id. (fem.), OS bank id. (fem.), OHG banc id. (masc., fem.). See *bankōn. Uhlenbeck PBB XXXV 171; Torp-Falk 259; Holthausen AEEW 19; Vries ANEW 31; Zalizniak II 176; Onions 88; Köbler 709; Lloyd-Springer I 456–458; Kluge- Seebold 78 (from *branka-).

ON bakki ‘bank, ridge’, OE banca ‘lair, bed’, LG bank ‘bank, shore’. Of uncertain origin. Wood MLN XV 95 (to Skt bhanákti ‘to break, to shatter’); Torp-Falk 259 (to Lith bangā ‘wave’); Holthausen AEEW 16; Vries ANEW 23.

ON bann ‘prohibition, ban’, OE ze-bann id., OFris ban(n) ‘order’, OS ban(n) id. (masc.), OHG ban ‘order, ban’ (masc.). Related to *bannanan. Craigie ANF X 158 (from OIr); Torp- Falk 256; Holthausen AEEW 16; Wiessner Tu B 19–28; Pokorny I 106; Seebold 89; Lloyd-Springer I 454–456; Kluge-Seebold 79.

OE bannan ‘to summon’, OFris banna id., OS bannan ‘to precharge’, OHG bannan ‘to precharge, to order’. Cf. Skt bhánati ‘to speak, to sound’, and further probably to Gk φημί ‘to say’, Lat fārī ‘to speak’, Arm ban ‘word’ < IE *bhā- ‘to speak’. Torp- Falk 256; Holthausen AEEW 16; Walde-Hofmann I 525–526; Mayrhofer II 469–470; Pokorny I 106; Frisk II 1009–1010; Onions 71–72; Seebold 88– 90; Kluge-Seebold 79.
ON banna ‘to hinder, to forbid’, OHG bannōn ‘to call upon’. Derived from *bannan. Seebold 89.
ON bana ‘to kill’, OFris bonia id. Derived from *banōn. Vries ANEW 25.

ON bani ‘death, bane’, OE bana ‘killer, murderer’, OFris bona id., OS bano id., MHG bane ‘death’. Of uncertain origin. Persson UUÅ 1891 73 (to *bađwō); Zupitza Gutt. 30; Torp-Falk 256; Holthausen AEEW 16; Pokorny I 126 (to Av bānay 6 n ‘they make ill’); Vries ANEW 25; Zalizniak I 153; Onions 72; Meid Germ. Rek. 109 (to IE *g ⁄hen- ‘to kill’); Bammesberger Nom. 176.

Crim. Goth baar ‘boy’, MHG bar ‘son, man’. Derived from *beranan (Høst ZDADL CXIV 44–45). Schröder NGWG [1910] 15 (mistaken for *barn); Feist 73; Seebold 105 (from Goth *baur); Hamp JEGP LXXII 60–61 (continues Goth *barn); Lehmann GED 55.

ON barr ‘barley’, OE bere id., OFris ber- id. (in cmpn.). Related to Lat far ‘spelt’, Osc far id., Umbr far id., Alb bar ‘grass’. Holthausen AEEW 21; Walde-Hofmann I 455–456; Feist 81; Porzig Gliederung 109; Pokorny I 111; Lehmann GED 62; Bammesberger Nom. 211 (old s-stem); Orel AED 16–17.

Crim. Goth bars ‘beard’, OE beard id., OFris berd id., MDu baert id., OHG bart id. Related to Lat barba id., Lith barzdā id. (with a secondary -zd-), Slav *borda id. (Solmsen KZ XXXIV 21). Pedersen IF V 73 (Slav, Lat < Gmc); Torp-Falk 262; Holthausen AEEW 17; Walde-Hofmann I 96; Feist 82; Pokorny I 111; Chemodanov SGGJa I 61; Trier Venus 188–191; Fraenkel 36; Vries ANEW 26 (ON barä id. < MLG bard); Zalizniak II 166; Onions 83; Lehmann GED 62–63; Lloyd-Springer I 488–490; Trubachev ESSJa II 197–198; Kluge-Seebold 83.
Burg *bardo ‘axe’, ON baräa ‘a kind of axe’, OHG barta id. Derived from *barđaz. Cf. ON skegg-ǫx id. with a similar semantic motivation. Torp- Falk 262; Köbler 709.

ON bergja ‘to taste’, OE berzan ‘to taste’. Probably derived from *berzanan. On the other hand, the comparison with Lat fragrō ‘to smell’ cannot be completely ruled out. Holthausen IF XXXII 340; Zupitza Gutt. 208; Holthausen AEEW 23; Vries ANEW 33 (to Gk φέρβω ‘to feed, to nourish’).

Goth barizeins ‘of barley’. Structurally close to Lat farīna ‘flour’ < *farsīna, cf. also Slav *boršьno ‘dish, rye flour’ < *bharsinom. Derived from *baraz ~ *bariz. Hoops Waldbäume 362–363; Walde-Hofmann I 455; Feist 81; Porzig Gliederung 109; Chemodanov SGGJa I 63; Trubachev ESSJa II 212–213; Lehmann GED 62.

ON berja ‘to strike’, OE berian ‘to take away, to withdraw’, OHG berren ‘to treat cruelly’. Identical with Slav *boriti ‘to fight’, *borjǫ, *borti id. and further with Alb bie ‘to fall, to hit’ < PAlb *berja, Lat feriō ‘to bore, to drill’, Lith barū, bárti ‘to scold’. Torp-Falk 261; Holthausen AEEW 21; Walde- Hofmann I 481–482; Porzig Gliederung 204; Pokorny I 134; Lerchner 22; Fraenkel 35–36; Vries ANEW 33; Trubachev ESSJa II 203–204, 213–214; Orel AED 24.

Swed bark ‘stubborn and unfriendly man’. Identical with Arm bark ‘harsh, sharp’, OIr borb ‘foolish, silly’, Latv bargs ‘stern, grim’. Bezzenberger BB XXVI 167 (to OE beorcan ‘to make a sharp explosive sound, to bark’); Pokorny I 138–139.
ON bǫrkr ‘bark’, MLG borke id. (fem.). Related to *berkō. Petersson IF XXIII 403 (to *bher- ‘to cut’); Torp- Falk 263; Pokorny I 166; Vries ANEW 70; Kluge-Seebold 126–127.

Goth barms ‘lap, bosom’ (i-stem), ON barmr ‘brim’, OE bearm ‘bosom, lap’, WFris berm ‘latus sinuatum viae, aggeris’, OS barm ‘bosom, lap’, OHG barm id. Identical with Skt bhárman- ‘support, nourishment’, Gk φορμός ‘basket for carrying’ (Hirt IF XXXII 287). Wood MP XI 326; Güntert Wu S XI 139; Torp-Falk 260–262; Holthausen AEEW 18; Feist 81–82; Detschew Thr. 53 (to Thrac mtn. Βέρμιον ὄρος); Mayrhofer II 481; Pokorny I 131, 137, 142 (to *bruman); Vries ANEW 27; Zalizniak II 185; Frisk II 1037; Onions 75; Seebold 105; Anttila Schw. 91; Lehmann GED 62; Bammesberger Nom. 71.
ON barn-lauss ‘childless’, OE bearn-leás id., OFris bern-lās id. Compound of *barnan and *lausaz. Carr Nom. comp. 60.

Goth barn ‘child’, ON barn id., OE bearn id., OFris bern id., OS barn id., OHG barn id. Related to Lith bérnas ‘boy’, Latv b=rns ‘child’, Alb barrë ‘foetus’ < *barnā (Bezzenberger BB XVII 21). A participle in *-no- of *beranan (Meillet MSL XXI 47) in its meaning ‘to give birth’. Torp-Falk 260; Holthausen AEEW 18; Feist 82; Pokorny I 131; Trubachev Rod. 44; Fraenkel 40; Vries ANEW 27; Zalizniak I 139; Onions 69; Seebold 105; Martynov BSI 12 (adds dubious Slav fem. *berna ‘pregnant’); Lehmann GED 62; Bammesberger Nom. 73; Orel AED 19.
Goth barnisks ‘childish, childlike’, ON bernskr ‘childish’. Derivationally identical with Lith bérniškas ‘of servant’. See *barnan. Feist 82; Vries ANEW 34; Lehmann GED 62.

E bar ‘beam, barrier’, MHG bar id. Related to *barjanan. Derivationally close to Lat forus ‘open space, public place’, forum id., Lith bãras ‘swath, row of mowed corn’ (Fick I 191). Torp-Falk 261; Walde-Hofmann I 537; Pokorny I 134.

ON bǫrgr ‘barrow-hog’, OE bearz id., OFris barg id., OS barug id., OHG barug id. Derivationally identical with Alb barukë ‘fleece’ < *bhoru-k-o-, based on IE *bhoru- attested in another derivative as *bhor⁄os in Alb berr ‘sheep, goat’, Slav *borvъ ‘sheep, barrow’ (Orel Linguistica XXVI 172–173). Weinhold KZ I 247; Försterman KZ I 497 (related to Lat porcus ‘hog, pig’); Torp-Falk 263; Matzenauer LF XIV 81 (to Slav *porzъ ‘bull, hog’); Trautmann BSW 27 (on singulative -k- as in Av pasu- ‘cattle’ ~ pasuka- ‘(one) animal’); Kiparsky Lehnw. 61 (Slav < Gmc); Holthausen AEEW 17; Trier Holz 87–88; Machek KZ LXIV 263 (further to Skt bhárvati ‘to chew’); Pokorny I 135; Vries ANEW 70; Zalizniak I 129; Onions 76; Stang LS 15; Trubachev ESSJa II 214–215; Lloyd-Springer I 493–495; Kluge- Seebold 81; Orel AEW 18, 21–22.

ON bǫrr ‘a kind of tree’, OE bearu ‘wood, grove’. Related to Slav u-stem *borъ ‘pine, pine forest’ (Löwe PBB LX 162; Schrader BB XV 287– 288). Schrader BB XV 287; Hirt PBB XXIII 331 (Slav < Gmc); Meillet Etudes II 234 (same as Hirt); Persson Beiträge 22 (against Hirt); Berneker I 76; Torp- Falk 260; Holthausen AEEW 18; Porzig Gliederung 144; Pokorny I 109; Chemodanov SGGJa I 79; Vries ANEW 70; Zalizniak I 138; Martynov Slavgerm. 108 (from Slavic); Stang LS 15; Trubachev ESSJa III 216–217.

ON pl. barar ‘hand-bier’, OE bearwe ‘basket, bier’, EFris barwe ‘hand-barrow’. Cf. also WGmc *bērō > OE bær ‘bier’, OS bāra id., OHG bāra id. Derived from *beranan. Cf. also *bērō. Torp-Falk 260; Holthausen AEEW 15, 18; Vries ANEW 25; Pokorny I 131; Zalizniak II 219; Onions 94; Lloyd- Springer I 469–470; Kluge-Seebold 74.

ON barr ‘needles of a fir-tree’, OE bears ‘coniferous tree, foliage, tree’, OHG bars id. Closely related to OIr barr ‘mop of hair, top’. W bar id., Corn bar id. Substantivized *barzaz. Torp-Falk 265–266; Holthausen AEEW 18; Pokorny I 109; Vries ANEW 27; Heidermanns 117.

ON barr ‘ready, strong, vigorous’, OHG bar ‘upright’. Identical with MIr borr ‘thick, large, strong’ < *bhorso-. Torp-Falk 265–266; Holthausen AEEW 18; Pokorny I 108–109; Lloyd-Springer I 466–467; Heidermanns 117; Kluge-Seebold 83.

ON bast ‘inner bark of a tree’, OE bæst id., MLG bast id. (masc.), OHG bast id. (masc.). Continues *bh 6-sto- or *bh 6 s-to- related to *bhā- ‘to shine’, *bhās-: Skt bhāti ‘to shine’, bhās- ‘light’, Gk φαίνω ‘to appear’, φώσκει: διαφάνει and the like. Semantically similar to Slav *lyko ‘bast’ < IE *leuk- ‘to shine’ (Trubachev Rem. 165– 166). Johansson IF XIX 121 (from *bhad-stu-); Abegg IF XLVI 267; Jacobsohn ZDADL LXVI 238–240 (from Scythian, cf. Av basta ‘bundle, bunch’, Osset bast id.); Torp-Falk 269; Holthausen AEEW 15; Mayrhofer II 493–494, 499; Pokorny I 104–105; Szemerényi KZ LXXI 211–213 (from Illyrian *bast- < *bh–dh-t-); Vries ANEW 28; Zalizniak II 166; Frisk II 982–984; Onions 78; Darms Vṛddhi 257–264; Koivulehto Festschr. Schmitt 246–258 (to bábhasti ‘to chew, to masticate, to devour’); Lloyd-Springer I 500–502; Kluge-Seebold 84.

ON betr ‘better’, OE bet id., OFris bet id., OS bet, bat id., OHG baz id. (< *bataz). See *batizōn. Cf. superl. Goth batista ‘best’, ON beztr id., OE betst id., OFris betst id., OHG bezzisto id. Grimm DG II 43 (reconstructs *batanan ‘vigere, valere’); Osthoff Morph. Unt. VI 286–288; Torp-Falk 258; Holthausen AEEW 21–22; Feist 83; Pokorny I 106; Vries ANEW 34–35; Onions 90–91; Lehmann GED 63; Bammesberger Nom. 235; Kluge-Seebold 84, 102.

Goth batiza ‘better’, ON betri id., OE betre id., OFris betere id., OS betaro id., OHG bezziro id. May be related to the isolated Skt bhadrá- ‘blessed, delightful, fortunate’, Av hu-badra ‘happy, lucky’ (Bopp Glossarium 269) if those are not from *bh–dró-. Bartholomae 1828; Torp-Falk 258; Stokes KZ XL 245 (to MIr baid ‘durable’); Feist 83; Holthausen AEEW 21–22; Wissmann NP 14–16; Mayrhofer II 467–468; Pokorny I 106; Chemodanov SGGJa I 107; Vries ANEW 34; Onions 91; Lehmann GED 63; Hamp IIJ XXX 175 (together with MW bodd ‘good will’ from *bhod-); Lloyd-Springer I 503–505, 577–578; Bammesberger Nom. 235; Kluge-Seebold 102.
Goth ga-batnan ‘to benefit, to profit’, ON batna ‘to improve, to get better’. Related to *batōn. Feist 174; Pokorny I 106; Vries ANEW 28; Heidermanns 118.
ON bati ‘improvement, advantage’, OFris bata ‘advantage, gain’, MLG bate ‘improvement’. See *batiz. Torp-Falk 258; Pokorny I 106; Vries ANEW 28; Lehmann GED 63; Heidermanns 119.
ON baä-hús ‘bathinghouse’, OE bæä-hús id., OHG bada-hūs id. Compound of *baþan and *xūsan. Carr Nom. comp. 71 (parallel formations).

ON baä ‘bath, bathing’, OE bæä ‘bath, font’, OFris be(i)th ‘bath’, OS bath id., OHG bad id. Derived from *bējanan. Torp-Falk 256; Holthausen AEEW 15; Pokorny I 113; Vries ANEW 22; Zalizniak I 139; Onions 79; Kluge- Seebold 72.
ON baä-stofa ‘bathroom’, MLG bade-stove ‘bathroom’, OHG bada-stuba id. Compound of *baþan and *stufōn. Carr Nom. comp. 83 (parallel formations).
ON rare baäast ‘to bathe’, OE baäian ‘to wash, to bathe’, Du baden ‘to bathe’, OHG badōn id. Derived from *baþan. Holthausen AEEW 17; Vries ANEW 22; Onions 79.

ON baugr ‘ring’, OE beáz ‘ring, crown’, OFris bāg ‘ring’, OS bōggebo ‘one presenting a ring’, MLG bōch ‘ring’, OHG boug id. Derived from *beuzanan. Together with Skt bhogá- id., continues a noun *bhougho- derived from *bheugh- ‘to bend’. Reflected in Slav rvn. *Bugъ (West Bug, right tributary of Wisla) also known in the XII century as Armilla (Tomaschek ZÖG XXVI 523; Brückner 47). Torp-Falk 274; Holthausen AEEW 17; Mayrhofer II 504; Pokorny I 152–153; Vries ANEW 29; Zalizniak I 129; Benveniste 111; Seebold 110; Trubachev ESSJa III 78 (OCS bougъ ‘armillar’ as a parallel to rather than a borrowing from Gmc); Lehmann GED 73; Bammesberger Nom. 52.

Goth us-baugjan ‘to sweep out’, ON beygja ‘to bow, to bend’, OE bízan id., OFris beia id., OS bōgian id., OHG bougen id. Causative of *beuzanan. Grienberger Got. 230–231; Torp-Falk 273; Holthausen AEEW 22; Feist 529; Pokorny I 153 (to Av bunúainti ‘to liberate’); Vries ANEW 34; Seebold 111; Lehmann GED 73, 380; Kluge-Seebold 105.

ON bákn ‘beacon’, OE beácen id., OFris bāken id., OS bōkan id., OHG bouhhan id. Related to *bukjanan. Torp-Falk 257; Holthausen AEEW 17; Pokorny I 105 (to IE *bhā- ‘to shine’); Zalizniak I 139; Onions 82; Kluge- Seebold 74.
ON bauka ‘to dig’, MLG boken ‘to knock, to beat’, MHG buchen id. Of imitative origin. Pokorny I 97–98; Vries ANEW 29.

OE beám ‘tree’, OFris bām id., OS bōm id., OHG boum id. Continues *bhou-mo- derived from IE *bheu- ‘to grow’, see *bewwanan. Cf. *bheumen- in Gk φῦμα ‘growth’, Skt bhūman- ‘being, world, earth’ (Johansson Btrg. Gesch. XV 224–225). Torp-Falk 258; Holthausen AEEW 17; Specht 54 (reconstructs *baum-na-); Mayrhofer II 512–513; Pokorny I 149; Zalizniak II 226; Frisk II 1052–1053; Onions 82–83; Peeters KZ LXXXVIII 129–133 (secondary form of *bazmaz); Lehmann GED 55–56; Bammesberger Nom. 71; Kluge- Seebold 86; Boutkan OFED s.v.

ON baun ‘bean’ (i-stem), OE beán id., OS bōna id., OHG bōna id. (ōand ōn-stem). Of uncertain origin. May be close to Slav *buna ‘swelling, tuber’ < *bhounā, further related to *bewwanan. Petersson IF XXIII 390 (to *bheu- ‘to swell’); Torp-Falk 257 (to Lat faba id., Slav *bobъ id.); Holthausen AEEW 17; Pokorny I 106 (from *babnō related to OPrus babo id., Slav *bobъ id.); Vries ANEW 29; Zalizniak II 192; Onions 83; Trubachev ESSJa III 95–96; Kluge- Seebold 123.

Burg *bausiz ‘wicked’, Norw baus ‘proud, bumptious’, OFris bāsa-feng, bās-feng ‘obscene touch’, MLG bōse, bōs ‘unclean’, OHG bōsi ‘worthless’. Probably, continues *bauđ-s-az, to *beuđanan (Heidermanns 120–121). Torp-Falk 276; Köbler 709.

ON bauta ‘to beat, to hunt’ (secondary wk.), OE beátan ‘to beat’, MHG bōzen id. (some forms are wk.). Of unknown origin. Torp-Falk 274 (to Lat con-fūtō ‘to repress, to diminish, to put to silence’); Feist 74; Holthausen AEEW 18; Jóhannesson IEW 596–597; Pokorny I 112 (to Lat fūstis ‘knobbed stick, cudgel’); Vries ANEW 29; Onions 83; Seebold 90–91.

ON beytill ‘Equisetum hiemale’, OE bíetel ‘hammer’, MLG bπtel ‘beating implement’, OHG stein-bōzil ‘cudgel’. Derived from *bautanan. Holthausen AEEW 23; Pokorny I 112; Vries ANEW 35; Onions 85; Seebold 90; Kluge-Seebold 95.

Goth bauþs ‘deaf, dumb’. Probably related to Slav sbst. *buta ‘stupid person; haughtiness, pride’. Grienberger Got. 45; Pedersen Kelt. Gr. I 111 (to OIr bodar ‘deaf’); Uhlenbeck PBB XXX 265–266; Petersson IF XXIII 395 (to *beuđaz); Meillet MSL X 282 (to Lith bukūs ‘blunt, dull’); Feist 86; Fraenkel 63; Trubachev ESSJa III 101–102; Lehmann GED 65.

ON berr ‘naked, bare’, OE bær id., OFris ber-fōt ‘barefoot’, OS bar ‘naked, bare’, OHG bar ‘uncovered, naked, bare’. Identical with Arm bok' ‘barefoot’, Lith bãsas id., Slav *bosъ id. Verner KZ XXIII 113; Pedersen IF V 46; Hübschmann AG 430; Karsten NB I 42–43; Persson UUÅ 1891 115 (to *bhes- ‘to scrape’); Kretschmer KZ XXXI 414; Torp-Falk 269; Trautmann BSW 28; Holthausen AEEW 15; Fraenkel 36, Glotta XXXV 87; Pokorny I 163; Vries ANEW 34; Onions 75; Trubachev ESSJa II 223–224; Lloyd-Springer I 465– 466; Bammesberger Nom. 239; Kluge- Seebold 79; Heidermanns 121.

Goth weina-basi ‘wine-berry, grape’ (< *basjan), ON ber ‘berry’, OE berize ‘berry, grape’ (fem.), OS beri ‘berry’, OHG beri id. Connected with Skt bábhasti ‘to chew, to masticate, to devour’. For the semantic development cf. Slav *gruša ‘pear’ < *grušiti ‘to break, to destroy’, Lat pirum ‘pear’ < *pisom, to *peis- ‘to stick, to pound’. Bugge PBB XXI 421 (to Norw dial. bas, base ‘shrub’); Lidén IF XVIII 415–416 (to OE basu ‘crimson, purple’); Torp-Falk 269; Holthausen AEEW 21; Walde-Hofmann II 309–310; Feist 559; Mayrhofer II 409; Pokorny I 105 (to Skt bhās- ‘light, splendour’), 145; Vries ANEW 32–33; Onions 90; Zalizniak II 182; Lehmann GED 400; Lloyd- Springer I 560–561; Kluge-Seebold 89 (to Lith úoga ‘berry’ < *ōg ⁄hā).
ON bera ‘to make bare’, OFris baria ‘to reveal’, OS baron id., OHG gi-barōn ‘to lay bare’. Derived from *bazaz. Vries ANEW 33; Heidermanns 121.

ON bjórr ‘beaver, beaver’s skin’, OE beofor ‘beaver’, MLG bever id., OHG bibar id. Originally, a color adjective. Related to Skt babhrú- ‘reddishbrown, brown’, Av bawra-, bawri- ‘beaver’, Lat fiber id., Lith b‚bras id., OPrus bebrus id., Slav *bebrъ id. ON bjórr ‘triangular cut offpiece of meat’ is a metaphoric usage of the above and has nothing to do with Lat fibra ‘fiber’ < *g ⁄hisrā (despite Noreen Abriß 228). Försterman KZ I 497 (related to Lat fibra ‘fibre, filament’); Bartholomae 925; Torp-Falk 264; Trautmann BSl Wb 28–29; Holthausen AEEW 20; Walde- Hofmann I 490–492; Mayrhofer II 409–410; Pokorny I 136; Fraenkel 38; Vries ANEW 40; Zalizniak II 227; Onions 83; Hamp IF LXX 162 (to *beranan); Trubachev ESSJa I 174–175; Kluge-Seebold 107.

ON bikkja ‘to plunge into water’, MHG bicken ‘to punch, to kick’. Probably related to Gk φέβομαι ‘to be put to flight, to flee’, Lith bėgu, bėgti ‘to run’, Slav *bîgti id. Pokorny I 116; Vries ANEW 36.
ON bikkja ‘bitch’, OE bicce id. Unclear. Perhaps based on *bekwjanan. Zupitza Gutt. 96; Torp-Falk 259 (to *bakan ~ *bakaz); Vries ANEW 36 (to Skt bhaga- ‘vulva’).

ON part. bolginn ‘to swell’, OE belzan ‘to swell with anger’, OFris part. ovir-bulgen ‘to incense’, OS belgan id., OHG belgan ‘to get angry’. Related to *balziz. Zupitza Gutt. 26, 204; Torp-Falk 268; Holthausen AEEW 19; Jóhannesson IEW 629–630; Pokorny I 126; Vries ANEW 49; Seebold 99–101; Kluge-Seebold 640.
OHG widar-bellan ‘to rebound’. Probably connected with Slav *bel’ati ‘to swing, to limp’. Holthausen AEEW 19; Jóhannesson IEW 629; Trubachev ESSJa I 185; Seebold 101.

OE bellan ‘to bellow, to roar’, MHG bellen ‘to bay, to bark’. Related to Toch AB päl-, pāl- ‘to praise’, OPrus billit ‘to say, to speak’, Lith bīlstu, bīlti ‘to start speaking’. Torp-Falk 266; Pokorny I 123–124.
ON bjalla ‘bell’, OE belle id., MLG belle id. Derived from *bellanan II. Torp-Falk 266; Holthausen AEEW 19; Pokorny I 123 (to *bullōjanan); Lerchner 24–26.

OE belone, belene ‘hen-bane’, MLG billen id. Close to Slav *belnъ ~ *belna id. Further cf. Gaul βελένιον id., W bela id. Pictet KZ IV 361–362 (to OIr béal ‘sun’); Kretschmer Glotta XIV 96–97 (a Wanderwort); Torp-Falk 267 (reconstruct *belunōn < Celtic, cf. Gaul bilinuntia id.); Trubachev ESSJa I 185– 187 (to *bhel- ‘to speak’); Kluge-Seebold 111.

Goth bindan ‘to bind’, ON binda id., OE bindan id., OFris binda id., OS bindan id., OHG bintan id. Related to Skt badhnāti id., Av bandayeiti id. Bartholomae 926; Torp-Falk 259; Meillet MSL XVII 194; Feist 93; Holthausen AEEW 23; Jóhannesson IEW 611–612; Mayrhofer II 406; Pokorny I 127; Vries ANEW 37; Mayrhofer II 406, 408; Onions 95; Seebold 102– 104; Lehmann GED 71; Kluge-Seebold 111–112.

ON bingr ‘bolster, bed, a heap of corn’, MLG binge ‘kettle-shaped deepening’. Related to Skt bahú- ‘much, abundant, large’, Gk παχύς ‘thick’, Latv bīezs id. Zupitza Gutt. 177; Mayrhofer II 424–425; Pokorny I 127–128; Vries ANEW 37; Frisk II 484–485.

Norw dial. bunt ‘Aira caespitosa’ (< *bunut-), OE bionot ‘dry blade of grass’, OS binet ‘bulrush’, OHG binuz id. If originally stood for *‘dead grass’, could be compared with *banōn. Torp- Falk 271; Onions 89.

Goth bairan ‘to bear, to carry; to give birth’, ON bera id., OE beran id., OFris bera id, OS beran id, OHG beran id. Cf. also *za-beranan ‘to give birth’: Goth ga-bairan, OE ze-beran, OS giberan, OHG gi-beran. Continues IE *bher- ‘to bear’: Toch AB pär-, Skt bhárati, Gk φέρω, Arm berem, Lat ferō, OIr berid merged with *bher- ‘to give birth’. Prefixed *at-beranan > Goth at-bairan ‘to bring, to offer’ is identical with Phryg impf. abberet, Lat adferō ‘to bring’. Torp-Falk 260–261; Holthausen AEEW 21; Walde-Hofmann I 483–485; Feist 75; Jóhannesson IEW 612–613; Mayrhofer II 473–476; Pokorny I 128–131; Vries ANEW 33; Frisk II 1003–1004; Illich-Svitych Opyt I 195 (on the contamination of IE *bher- ‘to bear’ and *bher- ‘child; to give birth’); Onions 83; Seebold 104–106; Lehmann GED 57; Kluge-Seebold 303; Orel Phrygian 409; Adams TB 371; Burlak IFTJa 248.

ON bjarg ‘rock’, OE beorz ‘hill, mountain’, OFris berch id., OS berg id., OHG berg id. Closely related to Av bar 6 zah- ‘height’, Arm barjr ‘high’, Thrac pln. Βέργα, OIr brí ‘hill’, W bera ‘heap’, Slav *bergъ ‘shore, mountain’. Further connected with IE *bher·h- ‘high’. Zupitza Gutt. 204; Bartholomae 950; Torp-Falk 265; Holthausen AEEW 20; Feist 75–76 (on Goth bairgahei ‘hill country, mountainous region’); Detschew Thr. 51–52; Pokorny I 140–141; Vries ANEW 39; Zalizniak II 166; Onions 76; Trubachev ESSJa 191–193; Lehmann GED 57–58; Bammesberger Nom. 60; Lloyd-Springer I 553–554; Kluge-Seebold 98–99.

Goth bairgan ‘to preserve, to keep’, ON bjarga ‘to save, to help’, OE beorgan ‘to save, to protect, to defend’, WFris bergje ‘to keep’, OS bergan id., OHG bergan id. Identical with Slav *bergti id., cf. also Lith bīrginti ‘not to spend much’, Osset æm-bærzyn ‘to cover’. Kirchhoff KZ I 39; Zupitza Gutt. 177; Persson Beiträge 927; Meringer IF XVIII 262; Torp-Falk 265; Trautmann BSW 31; Holthausen AEEW 20; Feist 76; Jóhannesson IEW 624; Pokorny I 145; Chemodanov SGGJa I 81; Fraenkel 44; Abaev AION-L IV 33; Vries ANEW 39; Onions 108; Benveniste 152; Seebold 106–107; Lehmann GED 58; Trubachev ESSJa I 189–191 (follows Mikuckij in comparing these forms with Lith geıbti ‘to praise’); Lloyd-Springer I 554–556; Kluge- Seebold 99.

ON bjǫrg ‘help, deliverance’, OE heáfod-beorz ‘head-shelter, helmet’, OFris here-berge ‘inn’, OS heri-berga id., OHG her-berga id. Derived from *berzanan. Holthausen AEEW 20; Vries ANEW 41; Seebold 107; Bammesberger Nom. 109.
ON bjart-ligr ‘clear’, OE beorht-líc ‘bright, light, clear’, OS berht-līk ‘bright’. Derived from *berxtaz. Heidermanns 124.

Goth bairhts ‘bright, clear, manifest’, ON bjartr ‘bright’, OE beorht ‘bright, light, clear’, OS berht id., OHG beraht id. Identical with W berth ‘beautiful’ < *bher·-t-, Slav sbst. *berstъ ‘elm’. Further related to Skt bhrājate ‘to shine, to beam’, Av brāzaiti id., Alb bardhë ‘white’. Försterman KZ I 104; Zupitza Gutt. 188; Bartholomae 972; Torp-Falk 264–265; Feist 76–77; Holthausen AEEW 20; Karsten NB II 324; Mayrhofer II 529–530; Pokorny I 139; Vries ANEW 39; Onions 118; Lehmann GED 58; Lloyd-Springer I 545–546; Bammesberger Nom. 256; Heidermanns 123– 124; Orel AED 17.
Goth bairhtei ‘brightness, clarity’, ON birti id., OE birhtu id., OHG berahtī ‘clarity’. Derived from *berxtaz. Holthausen AEEW 23; Feist 76; Vries ANEW 37; Lehmann GED 58; Heidermanns 124.

Goth bairhtjan ‘to exhibit, to show, to disclose’, ON birta ‘to illuminate, to brighten’, OE ze-birhtan ‘to make bright’, OHG part. gi-beraht ‘brightened’. Derived from *berxtaz. Torp- Falk 263–264; Holthausen AEEW 23; Feist 76; Vries ANEW 37; Lehmann GED 58; Heidermanns 124.

Burg *berils ‘bearer’, ON berill ‘barrel (for fluids)’, OS biril ‘basket’, OHG biril ‘big pot’. Derived from *beranan (Schulze KZ LXX 258). Vries ANEW 33, 68 (alternatively, from MLat barillus ‘keg’); Seebold 105; Köbler 709.
ON birkinn ‘related to birch’, OE bircen, beorcen id., OHG birkīn id. Derived from *berkō. Vries ANEW 37.
ON coll. birki ‘birch’, OE birce ‘birch’, OS birka id., OHG birca id. Derived from *berkō. Zupitza Gutt. 196; Torp-Falk 263; Holthausen AEEW 23; Pokorny I 139; Vries ANEW 37; Zalizniak II 216.

ON bjǫrk ‘birch’, OE beorc id., OS berka id. Related to Skt bhūrjá- ‘kind of birch’, Lat frāxinus ‘ash’, and especially to Lith béržas ‘birch’, OPrus berse id., Slav *berza id. Cf. also Dac pln. Bers-ovia. Wiedemann IF I 512; Hirt IF X 53; Torp-Falk 263; Kretschmer Festschr. Bezzenberger 94; Holthausen AEEW 20; Walde-Hofmann I 544; Feist 87 (on Goth bercna ‘b-rune of the Gothic Runic alphabet’ < *berknō); Pokorny I 139; Fraenkel 40–41; Vries ANEW 41; Zalizniak II 216; Onions 96; Trubachev ESSJa I 201–203; Lehmann GED 66; Kluge-Seebold 112.
ON birna ‘she-bear’, OE byrene id., OHG birin id. Derived directly from *berōn I or from *bernuz. Holthausen AEEW 24; Vries ANEW 37.
ON bjǫrn ‘bear’, OE beorn ‘man, prince, nobleman’. Derived from *berōn I. Torp-Falk 263; Holthausen AEEW 21; Specht 120; Pokorny I 136; Vries ANEW 41; Zalizniak II 220.

Burg *bers ‘bear’, OSwed prop. Biari, OE bera ‘bear’, OS bero id. (in prop.), OHG bero id. Close to Lith bėras ‘brown’ (with a long grade), Toch A parno, B perne ‘radiant, luminous’. Försterman KZ I 497; Zupitza Gutt. 31; Torp-Falk 263; Gottlieb Anim. 39–41; Holthausen AEEW 21; Pokorny I 136; Fraenkel 39; Zalizniak II 220; Onions 83; Stang LS 15; Lloyd-Springer I 563–565; Bammesberger Nom. 176 (to Gk θήρ ‘animal’, Lith ἤωερīσ id., Slav *zvîrь id.); Köbler 709; Kluge-Seebold 79–80.
ON á-beri ‘accuser’, OE -bera ‘porter, bearer’, OFris -bera id., OS -bero id., OHG -bero id. Derived from *beranan. Holthausen AEEW 21; Vries ANEW 33.
OE beoräor ‘child birth’. Close to Gk φέρετρον ‘stretcher’, see *beranan (Bammesberger Nom. 85). Holthausen AEEW 21; Pokorny I 129; Frisk II 1003–1004.

Goth ana-biudan ‘to bid, to command’, ON bjóäa ‘to offer’, OE beódan ‘to command, to order, to announce’, OFris biāda id., OS biodan id., OHG biotan id. Related to Skt bódhati ‘to wake, to be awake’, Av baod 6 nt- ‘becoming aware’, Gk πεύθομαι ‘to give notice’, Slav *bl’udǫ, *bl’usti ‘to observe, to keep, to protect’, and, with nasalization, OIr ad-bond- ‘to announce’ and the like (Meyer Goth. 61). Bugge KZ XIX (1870) 441; Bartholomae 917–918; Torp-Falk 274; Holthausen AEEW 19–20; Lane Language XIV 27 (adds Toch A pot-, B paut- ‘to honor, to flatter’); Feist 41; Jóhannesson IEW 607–608; Porzig Gliederung 122; Mayrhofer II 449–450; Pokorny I 150–151; Fraenkel 62; Frisk II 625–626, GHÅ XLIV 21–23; Vries ANEW 40; Onions 93; Seebold 108–110; Trubachev ESSJa II 136–137; Lehmann GED 30; Kluge- Seebold 109.

Goth biuþs ‘table’, ON bjóär id., OE beód id., OS biod id., OHG biet id. Derived from *beuđanan (Uhlenbeck PBB XXX 268). Grienberger Got. 50 (to *buđmaz ~ *butmaz); Brugmann Grundriß II/1 155; Petersson IF XXIII 395 (to *bauþaz); Stroh DWG 15; Torp-Falk 275; Holthausen AEEW 19; Feist 97; Pokorny I 151; Vries ANEW 40; Zalizniak I 129; Seebold 109 (against Uhlenbeck); Lehmann GED 74; Bammesberger Nom. 60; Kluge-Seebold 105.

Goth biugan ‘to bow, to bend’, ON pret. pl. bugu id., OE búzan id., OS pret. bōg id., pres. būgan, OHG biogan id. Related to Skt bhujáti id., Ir fid-bocc ‘wooden bow’ (Uhlenbeck PBB XXX 268; Thurneysen KZ XLVIII 65), Lith būgti ‘to frighten’, Slav *bъgati ‘to bend’ (Il’inskii RFV LXII 253–259). Zupitza Gutt. 218; Torp- Falk 273; Berneker I 366 (Slav *bъgati may be a metathesis of *gъbati id.); Wood MP V 270 (from IE *bheuk-); Trautmann BSW 39; Holthausen AEEW 38; Feist 96; Jóhannesson IEW 606–607; Mayrhofer II 504–506; Pokorny I 152–153; Fraenkel 37; Vries ANEW 40; Onions 111; Seebold 110–111; Benveniste IEL 111; Trubachev ESSJa 115; Lehmann GED 73; Kluge-Seebold 108.
ON bjúgr ‘bowed, bent’, OHG sbst. biogo ‘bend, curve’. Derived from *beuzanan ~ *būzanan. Torp-Falk 273; Pokorny I 151; Vries ANEW 41.
ON bjór-salr ‘beerhall’, OE beór-sele id. Compound of *beuraz ~ *beuran and *saliz. Carr Nom. comp. 80 (parallel formations).

ON bjórr ‘beer’ (masc.), OE beór id., OFris biār id., MLG bēr id., OHG bior id. Borrowed from Rom *biw(e)r < *biber ‘wine, drink’ ~ LLat biber id. (Lindquist DK 29–30). Zupitza Gutt. 15; Kluge PBB XXXV 570 (ON < OE); Torp-Falk 276 (from *beuzan); Holthausen IF LX 280 (< *breu-ra-); AEEW 20; Vries ANEW 40; Zalizniak II 166; Onions 85; Kluge-Seebold 108 (to

OE 1 sg. beó, bíom ‘to be’, OFris 1 sg. bim id., OS 1 sg. bium id., OHG 1 sg. bim id. Related to Skt bhávati ‘to become, to be’, Av bavaiti id., Gk φύω ‘to bring forth, to produce, to beget’, Lat perf. fuī ‘I was’, OLat fūī id., Lith būti ‘to be’, Slav *byti id. OE beó ‘(I) am’ is a relic of an old paradigm also reflected in Lat fīō < *fīō, pass. of faciō, OIr -bíu, both from *bh⁄iƒō (Osthoff Perf. 426). Bartholomae 927–933; Torp-Falk 272; Holthausen AEEW 20; Walde-Hofmann I 504–505, 557–558; Mayrhofer II 485–487; Pokorny I 149; Fraenkel 68; Frisk II 1052–1054; Seebold 112–115; Trubachev ESSJa III 155.
ON bygg ‘barley’, OE béow id., OS bewō ‘seed’. Related to *bōw- (w)anan. Torp-Falk 273; Holthausen AEEW 21; Vries ANEW 66; Pokorny I 149.
OHG bāgan ‘to scold, to have a row’. Close to OIr bágaid ‘to fight’ (Zimmer KZ XXXVI 447–454). Torp- Falk 257; Seebold 93–94.

ON bágr ‘contest, strife’, OE báz ‘bragging, boasting’, OS bāg ‘praise’, OHG bāg ‘quarrel’. Derived from *bēzanan. Torp-Falk 257; Jóhannesson IEW 598–599; Pokorny I 115; Chemodanov SGGJa I 96; Vries ANEW 22–23, Seebold 93–94.
ON bága ‘to quarrel’, OFris bāga ‘to boast’, OHG bāgēn ‘to quarrel’. Derived from *bēzaz. Torp- Falk 257; Vries ANEW 69; Seebold 94.
OHG bāen ‘to warm (with a compress)’. A parallel derivational variant of *bakanan? Torp-Falk 256; Pokorny I 113; Kluge-Seebold 73 (to Slav *grîti ‘to warm’, supposedly from

ON bál ’flame, fire’, OE bæl id. Related to *bhel- ‘white’: Gk φαλ- [l]ός: λευκός (Hes.), Lith βάλασ, cf. in particular Skt bhāla- ‘light, shine, forehead’, Slav *bîlъ ‘white’. Persson Beiträge 28–30; Torp-Falk 267; Holthausen AEEW 15; Mayrhofer II 496–497; Pokorny I 119; Fraenkel 32; Vries ANEW 23; Frisk II 988; Onions 70; Trubachev ESSJa III 79–81.

ON bærr ‘due, entitled to’, OE wæstm-bære ‘fruitful, fertile’, OFris ā-bær ‘obvious’, MDu open-baer id., OHG dancbāri ‘grateful’. Derived from *beranan. Karsten NB I 94–95; Torp-Falk 260– 261; Pokorny I 131; Vries ANEW 69; Seebold 106; Matzel Festschr. Schröder 100, 117, KZ CV 108; Heidermanns 124–125.
ON bára ‘wave’, ME báre id., MLG bāre id. Derived from *beranan. Torp-Falk 260; Vries ANEW 25; Pokorny I 131.

Goth bi ‘by, about, over’, OE be, bí ‘by’, OFris be, bī id., OS be, bī id., OHG bī id. Secondary lengthening of the stressed auslaut vowel in WGmc. Probably connected with *umbi. Brugmann Grundriß II/2 820–821; Torp-Falk 270; Holthausen AEEW 22; Feist 88; Pokorny I 34; Schmidt Studien 225–227 (from IE *pi); Onions 131; Lehmann GED 67; Griepentrog KZ CIV 122; Kluge- Seebold 92.

ON bifask ‘to shake’, OE beofian ‘to tremble, to quake’, OFris beva ‘to shake’, OS bibōn id., OHG bibēn id. Reduplication related to Skt bháyate ‘to fear, to be afraid’, Skt bibhéti id., Av ni-wyeiti id., OPrus biātwei id., Slav *bojati sę id. (Meillet MSL XIV 346). Kluge KZ XXVI 85–86; Wackernagel KZ XLI 305–309 (against the above etymology); Bartholomae 927; Torp-Falk 271; Holthausen AEEW 23; Mezger KZ LXXII 127; Mayrhofer II 431, 471–472; Pokorny I 162; Vries ANEW 35; Trubachev ESSJa II 163–164; Kluge-Seebold 88.
ON biä ‘biding, waiting, delay’, OE bíd ‘delay, abiding’. Derived from *bīđanan. Holthausen AEEW 22; Bammesberger Nom. 56.
ON biäill ‘wooer, suitor’, OE adj. bédul ‘prayerful, suppliant’ (< *biđulaz), OHG bitil ‘swain, suitor, solicitor’. Derived from *biđjanan. Holthausen AEEW 9; Vries ANEW 35; Seebold 92.

Goth bidjan ‘to ask, to pray’, ON biäja ‘to beg, to pray’, OE biddan ‘to ask, to pray’, OFris bidda id., OS biddian id., OHG bitten id. Derived from *bīđanan (Osthoff PBB VIII 140–143). Schweizer KZ I 561 (to Skt bādhate ‘to press, to force’); Cuny REA 1910 10–13 (from Celtic); Bezzenberger BB XVI 252 (with doubts, to * g ⁄hedh-); Zupitza Gutt. 31 (same as Bezzenberger); Osten- Sacken IF XXIII 377; Kern TNTL I 32–37 (to IE *bhedh- ‘to bow’); Meillet BSL XXIV 23–24 (to IE * g ⁄hedh-); Torp- Falk 258; Holthausen AEEW 22; Feist 89; Pokorny I 114; Vries ANEW 35; Seebold 91–93; Onions 93; Benveniste IEL 500; Meid Germ. Rek. 109; Lehmann GED 67–68; Kluge-Seebold 114.

Goth bida ‘request, prayer, bead’, OE béd ‘prayer, supplication’ (neut.), OFris bede id., OS beda id., OHG beta id. Derived from *biđjanan or directly from *bīđanan. Holthausen AEEW 19; Feist 88–89 (to Skt bādhate ‘to press, to force’); Pokorny I 114; Seebold 93; Benveniste IEL 501; Lehmann GED 67; Bammesberger Nom. 110; Kluge- Seebold 104; Boutkan OFED s.v.
ON biäa ‘to bide a bit’, OE on-bidian ‘to wait’, OFris bidia id., OS bidōn ‘to remain’. Derived from *bīđanan. Holthausen AEEW 22; Vries ANEW 35; Seebold 95.

ON bil ‘moment’, OHG bil ‘a moment when the hunted game stands on guard’. If derived from *bhei- ‘to cut, to strike’ (Lidén KZ LXI 13), identical with Slav *bilo ‘beater’. Noreen Abriß 227 (to Lith bailūs ‘fearful’); Torp Festschr. Unger 172–172 (to *bhi as in Gk ἀμφί ‘about, on both sides of ’); Vries ANEW 36.

OE bile-wit ‘merciful, mild, gentle’, MHG bil-wiz ‘cobold’. Close to MIr bil ‘good’ < *bhili- and probably Gk φίλος ‘beloved, dear’. Meringer IF XVIII 284–286; Holthausen AEEW 23; Pokorny I 153–154; Frisk II 1018–1020.
ON bit ‘bite’, OHG gi-biz ‘rein, bit’. Derived from *bītanan. Vries ANEW 38; Seebold 97.
ON bita ‘to divide with cross-beams, to cut into bits’, OE grist-bitian ‘to gnash’, OHG bizzōn ‘to crunch, to gnash’. Derived from *bitōn. Holthausen AEEW 25; Vries ANEW 38; Seebold 97.

ON biti ‘bit, cross-beam, girder’, OE bita id., OFris kor-bita ‘consecrated piece’, MDu bēte ‘morsel, bit’, OHG bizzo ‘bit, wedge’. Derived from *bītanan. Torp-Falk 270; Holthausen AEEW 24; Pokorny I 116; Vries ANEW 38; Onions 97; Seebold 97.

ON bitr ‘biting, sharp’, OE biter ‘bitter, sharp, severe’, OS bittar ‘bitter’, OHG bittar ‘sharp, bitter’. Derived from *bītanan. Marstrander NTS III 312; Karsten NB II 254; Niedermann IF XXXVII 145–147 (to Slav *bridъkъ ‘sharp, bitter’); Torp-Falk 270; Holthausen AEEW 25; Pokorny I 116; Vries ANEW 38; Onions 97; Seebold 97; Lehmann GED 58–59; Bammesberger Nom. 247; Heidermanns 126–127; Kluge- Seebold 114.
ON bitull ‘bit’, OE bitol, bytol ‘rein’, MHG bizzel ‘little bit’. Derived from *bitan. Holthausen AEEW 25; Vries ANEW 38; Seebold 97.
Dan bille ‘beetle’, OE bítel ‘beetle, biting’. Derived from *bitan or directly from *bītanan. Cf. *bitulaz. Torp-Falk 270.

Goth beidan ‘to wait’, ON bíäa id., OE bídan ‘to bide, to abide, to continue, to wait’, OFris bīda ‘to wait’ (usually wk.), OS bīdan id., OHG bītan id. Related to Gk πείθομαι ‘to be persuaded, to obey’, Lat fīdō ‘to trust’. Wood MP IV 489–490; Torp-Falk 270; Walde- Hofmann I 493–494; Feist 86–87; Holthausen AEEW 22; Trier Zd Ph LXX 341–342 (to Lat fiscus ‘basket, chest’, Icel biäa ‘milk tub’ and the like); Jóhannesson IEW 604; Pokorny I 117; Vries ANEW 35; Frisk II 487–488; Benveniste IEL 95–98 (the original meaning in Germanic: ‘attendre avec confiance’); Chantraine 868–869; Onions 93; Seebold 94–96 (to Latv gāidît ‘to wait’); Lehmann GED 56, 65–66.

ON bÿ ‘bee’, OE beō id., OFris bē id., OS bīa id., OHG bīa id., cf. bīan id. (masc.). Cf. WGmc *binjan ~ *binō > OS bini- (in compounds), bina id., OHG bini id., bīna id. Somehow related to Lat fūcus ‘drone’ < *bhoi-ko-, OIr bech id. < *bhi-ko-, W bydaf ‘beehive’, Lith bītë id., Slav *bь‘ela ‘bee’ (Fick I 490; Meillet MSL XIV 476–478). Pedersen Kelt. Gr. I 367, 537; Kluge Glotta III 280 (reconstructs *bhouk ⁄o- for Gmc and Lat); Torp-Falk 271; Johansson IF III 225 (to Lat fūcus ‘drone’ < *bhoiko-), KZ XXXVI 358; Walde-Hofmann I 555– 556; Holthausen AEEW 18, 24; Pokorny I 116, 163; Fraenkel 45; Vries ANEW 66; Zalizniak II 220; Onions 84; Trubachev ESSJa III 91, 104–105; Kluge- Seebold 108.
Goth anda-beit ‘reproach, rebuke, ἐπιτιμία’, ON hæl-bítr ‘shadower’, OE and-bita ‘unleavened bread’. Derived from *bītanan. Feist 46; Seebold 97.

Goth beitan ‘to bite’, ON bíta id., OE bítan id., OFris bīta id., OS bītan id., OHG bīzan id. Related to Skt bhinátti ‘to split, to cleave, to pierce’, Gk φείδομαι ‘to spare’, Lat findō ‘to split’. Torp-Falk 270; Feist 87; Holthausen 24; Walde-Hofmann I 500–501; Jóhannesson IEW 602–604; Mayrhofer II 500; Pokorny I 116–117; Vries ANEW 38; Frisk II 999–1000; Seebold 96–99; Onions 97; Lehmann GED 66; Kluge-Seebold 94.

ON bíldr ‘axe, an instrument for bleeding’ (masc.), MLG bīl ‘axe’, OHG bīhal ‘axe’. Cf. also WGmc *biđlan > OE bill ‘crooked double-edged sword, bill, falchion’, OS bill ‘sword’, MHG bil ‘pick’. Continues IE *bh(e)i-tlo- (Holthausen AEEW 23) also reflected in Slav *bidlo ‘rake, pole, plectrum’ (Trubachev Etim. 46). Torp-Falk 269; Pokorny I 118; Vries ANEW 36; Onions 94; Trubachev Rem. 130–132 (on Slav *-dlo < IE

Burg *blaþ ‘blade, edge’, ON blaä ‘leaf, blade’, OE blæd id., OFris -bled id., OS blad id., OHG blat id. Identical with Toch A pält, B pilta id. and related to *blōanan (Hirt Urgerm. II 41). Torp-Falk 283; Holthausen AEEW 25; Pokorny I 122; Vries ANEW 41; Zalizniak I 139; Onions 98; Lehmann GED 76; Kluge-Seebold 116; Adams TB 388.

ON blaära ‘bladder’, OE blædre ‘blister, pimple, bladder’, OS blādara ‘bladder’, OHG blātara ‘pock-mark’. Derived from *blēanan. Torp-Falk 283; Holthausen AEEW 25; Pokorny I 121; Vries ANEW 41; Zalizniak I 155; Onions 98.

ODan blaa ‘oakum’, OHG blaha ‘rough linen cloth’. Related to Latv blaks ‘even, smooth’, Lith blãkas ‘equal’. Petersson Glotta IV 296–297 (to Lat floccus ‘lock, flock’); Torp-Falk 285 (same as Petersson); Pokorny I 161; Fraenkel 47; Vries ANEW 46.

ON bleikr ‘pale, wan’, OE blác ‘shining, white’, OS blēk id., OHG bleih ‘yellowish, pale’. Derived from *blīk(j)anan. Karsten NB I 58; Zupitza Gutt. 196; Torp-Falk 286; Holthausen AEEW 25; Pokorny I 156; Vries ANEW 43; Onions 99; Seebold 118; Bammesberger Nom. 237; Heidermanns 127– 128; Kluge-Seebold 118.
ON bleikja ‘to bleach linen’, OE blæcan id., MLG blēken ‘to bleached’, OHG bleihhen ‘to bleach, to make pale’. Derived from *blaikaz. Holthausen AEEW 25; Vries ANEW 43; Onions 99; Heidermanns 127.
ON bleikja ‘chalk paint’, OHG bleihha ‘make-up’. Derived from *blaikaz. Torp-Falk 286; Onions 99; Heidermanns 127.

OE blát ‘pale, livid’. Identical with Slav *blîdъ id. Further may be connected with *blaikaz. Schmidt Voc. II 71; Torp-Falk 286; Holthausen AEEW 26; Otrébski LP I 122 (adds Lith blaĩvas ‘pale’ ?< *blaidvas); Pokorny I 160; Stang LS 15; Trubachev ESSJa II 111–112; Lehmann GED 75; Heidermanns 128.

E bloat ‘to bloat’. Derivationally close to Gk φλοιδέω ‘to seethe’. Further related to Gk φλιδάω ‘to overflow with moisture, to be ready to burst’ (Fick III 286), Latv blîdu, blîst ‘to increase quickly’. Mühlenbach-Endzelin I 316; Pokorny I 156; Frisk II 1027– 1028.

ON blaka ‘to slap, to flutter’, MDu blaken ‘to shiver’. Probably connected with Lat flagrum ‘whip, scourge, lash’ (Zupitza Gutt. 213). Trautmann BB XXX 328; Torp-Falk 284; Walde- Hofmann I 511–512; Pokorny I 154.

Norw blakra ‘to flash’. Identical with Lat flagrō ‘to flame, to blaze, to burn’ and further with Gk φλέγω ‘to burn’. See *blankaz. Torp- Falk 284; Walde-Hofmann I 510–511; Pokorny I 124–125; Frisk II 1022–1024; Lerchner 34–35.
ON í bland ‘among’, OE ze-blond ‘mixture’. Derived from *blanđanan. Seebold 116.

Goth blandan ‘to mix, to mingle, to associate with’, ON blanda ‘to mix’, OE blandan id., MDu blanden id., OHG blantan id. Related to Lith blendžiū, bl.sti ‘to sleep, to mix food with flour’, Slav *blędǫ, *blęsti ‘to err’. Further connected with *blenđaz. Brugmann IF XXXII 179–181; Lidén Studien 77; Torp- Falk 285; Trautmann BSW 34; Holthausen AEEW 26; Feist 98–99; Jóhannesson IEW 646; Pokorny I 157–158; Fraenkel 47–48; Vries ANEW 42; Onions 99; Seebold 115–117; Trubachev ESSJa II 115; Lehmann GED 74–75; Kluge-Seebold 118.

OE blendan ‘to blind’, OFris blenda, blinda id., MLG blenden, blinden id., OHG blenten id. Factitive of *blenđaz. Structurally identical with Lith blandÿti ‘to wander, to lower eyes’, Slav *blǫditi ‘to wander, to fornicate’. Torp-Falk 285; Fraenkel 47; Kluge-Seebold 118.

ON blakkr ‘pale, wan’, poet. ‘horse’, OE blanc ‘white, grey’, MLG blanc ‘light’, OHG blanc ‘bright, white’. Probably, a deverbative. The corresponding str. vb. *blenkanan, however, is only marginally attested (MLG blinken) and may be secondary. Connected with WGerm *blakjanan ‘to be visible’ (OFris blesza, MLG blecken ‘to bare’, OHG blecken ‘to shine’) and further with *blakrōjanan. Related to Gk φλέγω ‘to burn’, Lat flagrō id. Karsten NB I 52–53; Falk ANF XLI 118; Torp-Falk 284; Holthausen AEEW 86, 418; Walde-Hofmann I 510–511; Pokorny I 124– 125; Vries ANEW 42; Frisk II 1023–1024; Heidermanns 129 (to OE blæc ‘black’); Kluge-Seebold 115.
ON blekkja ‘to impose upon’, OE blencan ‘to deceive, to cheat’. Derived from *blankaz. Holthausen AEEW 27; Vries ANEW 43; Onions 99; Heidermanns 129.

ON sbst. blesi ‘blaze, a white star on a horse’s forehead’, OHG blas ‘pale, white’. Hardly can be separated from *bhel- ‘white’ (Gk φαλός and the like). Matzenauer LF XIII 170 (to Slav *plîšь ‘baldness’); Torp-Falk 285; Holthausen AEEW 26; Pokorny I 158; Vries ANEW 43; Onions 99; Kluge-Seebold 116.

Burg *blauts ‘bare’, ON blautr ‘soft, faint’, OE bleát ‘miserable, wretched’, OFris blāt ‘poor’, MLG bloot ‘uncovered’, MHG blōz id. Probably connected with φλαῦρος ‘petty, paltry, indifferent’, φλυδαρός ‘soft’. Karsten NB I 62; Torp-Falk 287; Muller AIW 180 (to Lat flacceō ‘to be flabby’); Holthausen AEEW 26; Pokorny I 159; Vries ANEW 43 (to φλυδάω ‘to have an excess of moisture’); Onions 101; Lehmann GED 75 (with pre-Gmc *d, related to *blauþaz); Lühr Expr. 267–268; Heidermanns 130– 131; Kluge-Seebold 12.

ON blauär ‘weak, efeminate’, OE bleáä ‘gentle, timid, peaceful’, OS blōth ‘fearful, despondent’, OHG blōdi ‘apathetic, tedious’. Related to *blautaz. Wood MLN XV 326 (to Skt mlāyati ‘to wither, to fade’); Torp-Falk 287; Holthausen AEEW 26; Pokorny I 159; Vries ANEW 43; Peeters IF XCIV 295–296; Lehmann GED 75; Heidermanns 131–132; Kluge-Seebold 120.
ON bleyäi ‘cowardice’, OS blōthī id., OHG blōdī ‘apathy’. Derived from *blauþaz. Vries ANEW 44; Heidermanns 131.

Goth blauþjan ‘to make powerless, to bring to naught’, ON bleyäa ‘to lose heart’, OS part. gi-blōthid ‘despondent’, OHG blōden ‘to invalidate, to fear’. Derived from *blauþaz. Torp- Falk 287; Feist 99; Holthausen AEEW 26; Karsten NB II 391–392; Vries ANEW 44; Lehmann GED 75; Lühr Expr. 267–268; Heidermanns 131.

Goth blinds ‘blind’, ON blindr id., OE blind id., OFris blind id., OS blind id., OHG blint id. Related to Lith blandūs ‘unclean’, bl.sti ‘to sleep, to mix food with flour’, Slav *blęsti ‘to err’. Cf. *blanđanan.
