Torp-Falk 285; Feist 100; Holthausen AEEW 27; Wissmann NP 82; Karsten NB I 27; Fraenkel 196–197; Stang LS 15; Pokorny I 157; Chemodanov SGGJa I 85; Fraenkel 47–48; Vries ANEW 44; Onions 100; Seebold 116; Lehmann GED 75–76; Bammesberger Nom. 240–241, 256; Heidermanns 133–134; Kluge-Seebold 119.
ON blindi ‘blindness’, OS blindi id., OHG blintī id. Derived from *blenđaz. Heidermanns 134.
Goth ga-blindjan ‘to blind, to make blind’, OFris blinda ‘to blindfold’. Derived from *blenđaz. Feist 100; Vries ANEW 44; Heidermanns 134.
ON blinda ‘to blind’, OFris blindia id. Derived from *blenđaz. Heidermanns 134.
ON bljúgr ‘bashful, shy’, MHG bliuc id. See *blautaz? Torp-Falk 287–288 (to Lith blúkštu ‘to become slack’); Pokorny I 159; Vries ANEW 44.
ON blys ‘torch’, OE blysa id. Connected with *bhleus- in Gk peri-πεφλευσμένος πυρί ‘burned by fire all around’. Holthausen AEEW 28; Pokorny I 159.

Goth bliggwan ‘to beat, to strike’, ME bléwe id., MLG blouwen id., OHG bliuwan id. Of unknown origin. Osthoff KZ III (NF) 84–85 (to Lat flīgō ‘to strike, to strike down’); Wood MLN XV 327–328; Torp-Falk 287; Feist 100; Gāters KZ LXXV 80–86; Pokorny I 125 (to *balwan); Onions 102; Seebold 120–122; Lindeman Verschärfung 15.17, 20.4; Lehmann GED 75; Kluge- Seebold 119.

OE bláwan ‘to blow’, OFris pret. sg. on-blē id., OHG part. -blāen id. Related to Lat flō id. Bremer PBB XI 278; Torp-Falk 283; Holthausen AEEW 26; Walde-Hofmann I 517; Jóhannesson IEW 627–628; Pokorny I 121; Onions 101–102; Seebold 117–118.

MHG blæjen ‘to bleat’. Identical with Lat fleō ‘to weep, to neigh’, Latv blēju, blēt ‘to bleat’, Slav *blîjǫ id. (Holthausen KZ XLVII 310). Torp- Falk 284; Walde-Hofmann I 515–516; Pokorny I 154–155; Stang LS 15.

Goth uf-blesan ‘to blow up, to boast’, ON blása ‘to blow’, MLG blasen id., OHG blāsan id. Emphatic derivative of *blēanan. Torp-Falk 283; Holthausen 26; Feist 511–512; Jóhannesson IEW 627–628; Pokorny I 121; Vries ANEW 42; Onions 99; Seebold 120; Lehmann GED 373; Kluge- Seebold 115.
ON indecl. blæsma ‘of a ewe in heat’, OLFrank sbst. blasmo ‘torch’. Derived from *blēsanan. Vries ANEW 46.

ON blástr ‘blast, breath’, OE blæst ‘blast’, OHG blāst ‘breath’. Derived from *blēsanan. Torp-Falk 283; Holthausen AEEW 26; Pokorny I 121–122; Vries ANEW 42–43; Zalizniak II 199; Onions 98; Lehmann GED 373.

ON blár ‘blueish, dark blue’, OE blæ-h(æ)wen ‘blueish, violet’, OFris blāu ‘blue’, OS blāo ‘blueish’, OHG blāo ‘blue, purple’. Related to Lat flāvus ‘golden yellow’, OIr blár ‘yellow’, W blawr ‘grey, blue grey’ (Persson Beiträge 31). Torp-Falk 285; Schwentner FB 69–74; Hirt Etym. 193 (from *mlē⁄o-, to Gk μέλας ‘black’); Holthausen AEEW 25; Walde-Hofmann I 513–514; Karsten NB II 191–192; Pokorny I 160; Vries ANEW 42; Lehmann Festschr. Starck 57–58; Polomé RBPH XLIV 110–112; Onions 102; Bammesberger Nom. 244; Heidermanns 135; Kluge-Seebold 116.
ON blik ‘gleam’, OS blek ‘metal leaf’, OHG bleh id. Related to *blīk(j)anan. Torp-Falk 286; Pokorny I 156; Vries ANEW 44; Zalizniak II 185; Seebold 119; Kluge-Seebold 117.
ON blika ‘to shine’, OE blician id. Derived from *blikan. Holthausen AEEW 27; Vries ANEW 44; Seebold 119.
ON blika ‘flash, lightning’, OE ofer-blice ‘surface’. Derived from *blikan. Seebold 119.

ON blíkja ‘to gleam’, OE blícan ‘to shine’, OFris blīka id., OS blīkan id., OHG bi-blīhhan id. A suffixal formation related to *blīþjaz. Cf. also Lith bl‹kšti ‘to pale’, blyškėti ‘to sparkle, to shine’, Slav *blьstati ‘to shine’, *bliskati id. Zupitza Gutt. 196; Torp-Falk 286–287; Holthausen AEEW 27; Jóhannesson IEW 644; Pokorny I 156; Chemodanov SGGJa I 84; Vries ANEW 44; Fraenkel 46; Onions 99; Seebold 118–120; Gołąb Origins 127; Kluge-Seebold 118.
Goth bleiþei ‘benevolence, mercy’, OHG herz-blīdī ‘joy’. Derived from *blīþ(j)az. Feist 99; Heidermanns 132.
Goth bleiþjan ‘to take pity, to be merciful’, OHG blīden ‘to rejoice’. Derived from *blīþ(j)az. Feist 99; Vries ANEW 44; Heidermanns 132.

Goth bleiþs ‘kind-hearted, merciful’, ON blíär ‘gentle, mild’, OE blíäe ‘joyful, glad, merry’, OS blīthi ‘shining, light’, OHG blīdi ‘merry, glad’. Continues *mlīto- ‘sweet’, cf. Hitt miliddu- ~ maliddu-, Luw *mlitu- (Heidermanns 133). Johansson PBB XV 226–227 (to Skt mlāyati ‘to fade, to wither’); Torp-Falk 286; Bugge PBB XIII 181–182 (separates a prefix *b-, further to Lith pa-líeti ‘to pour in’); Johansson PBB XV 226–227; Feist 99; Holthausen AEEW 27; Karsten NB II 443–444; Pokorny I 155; Vries ANEW 44; Zalizniak I 146; Lehmann GED 75; Heidermanns 132–133.

ON blÿ ‘lead’, OFris blī id., OS blī id., OHG blīo id. Identical with Lith blÿvas ‘violet blue’. Further related to *blīþjaz and WGmc *blīwōn ‘color’ > OE blío, OFris blīen, OS blī, *blajjōn > OE blæze ‘blay (fish), gudgeon’, MLG bleie id., G Blei(h)e id. Persson UUÅ 1891 109, 173; Much ZDADL XLII 163 (from Celtic); Senn JEGP XXXII 509; Torp- Falk 287 (together with Gk μόλυβδος, μόλιβος ‘lead’ and Lat plumbum id., from an unknown source); Holthausen AEEW 25, 27; Pokorny I 155; Vries ANEW 45–46; Zalizniak I 146; Chemodanov SGGJa I 81; Onions 99; Lehmann GED 75; Kluge-Seebold 117–118.

OE blówan ‘to blow, to bloom, to blossom’. Corresponds to wk. *blōjanan elsewhere: OFris blōia, OS blōjan, OHG bluoen. Related to Lat flō ‘to blow’, flōs ‘bloom, flower’, MIr bláth id. See *blēanan. Torp-Falk 283– 284; Holthausen AEEW 28; Walde- Hofmann I 518–519; Jóhannesson IEW 626–627; Pokorny I 122; Chemodanov SGGJa I 101; Onions 102; Seebold 122; Lehmann GED 76; Kluge-Seebold 121.
ON blóäigr ‘bloody’, OE blódiz id., OFris blōdich id., OS blōdag id., OHG bluotag, bluotīg id. Derived from *blōđan. Vries ANEW 45.
ON blóäga ‘to make bleed’, OHG bluotagōn ‘to make bloody’. Derived from *blōđazaz ~ *blōđizaz. Vries ANEW 44–45.

Goth bloþ ‘blood’ (with *-þ-), ON blóä id., OE blód id., OFris blōd id., OS blōd id., OHG bluot id. Derivationally identical to MIr bláth ‘blossom’, W blawd, OCorn blodon < IE *bhlō-to- and further connected with *blōanan. Grienberger Got. 52 (to Lat flāvus ‘golden yellow’); Hirt Ablaut 90 (to Gk μέλας ‘black’); Uhlenbeck PBB XXX 269–270; Torp-Falk 283; Holthausen AEEW 27; Feist 101; Pokorny I 122; Vries ANEW 44–45; Zalizniak I 139; Onions 101; Lehmann GED 76–77; Kluge-Seebold 121 (to *bhel- ‘to swell’).
ON blóä-rauär ‘blood-red’, OE blód-reád id., early G blutrot id. Compound of *blōđan and *rauđaz. Carr Nom. comp. 88 (parallel formations).

Goth bloþa-rinnands ‘blood-running, bleeding’, OE blódyrnende ‘having an issue of blood’, OFris blōd-rennand id. Compound of *blōđan and participle of *rennanan. Carr Nom. comp. 88 (parallel formations).
ON indecl. blóä-risa ‘bruised and bloody’, LG dial. Blut-rise id. Cf. also OFris blōd-risne ‘bleeding wound’, MLG blōtrisene id. Compound of *blōđan and *risaz derived from *rīsanan. Vries ANEW 45.
ON blœäa ‘to bleed’, OE blédan id., OFris blēda id., MLG blöden id., OHG part. bluotenti ‘bloody’. Derived from *blōđan. Holthausen AEEW 27; Vries ANEW 46; Onions 99.
ON blómask ‘to bloom’, OHG bluomōn id. Derived from *blōmōn. Vries ANEW 45.

Goth bloma ‘flower’, ON blómi ‘bloom, blossom’, OFris blōma id., OS blōmo id., OHG bluomo id. Derived from *blōanan. Hirt Ablaut 90 (to Gk βλώσκω ‘to go, to come, to pass’); Meillet Etudes 178; Petersson KZ XLVII 290; Torp-Falk 284; Feist 100; Pokorny I 122; Zalizniak I 153; Onions 101; Seebold 122; Lehmann GED 76; Kluge-Seebold 121.
ODan blōstr ‘blossom’, MHG bluost id. Derived from *blōanan. Petr BB XXI 210 (to Slav *bl’uš‘ь ‘ivy’); Lehmann GED 76.
OHG bluostar ‘sacrifice’. A similar form can be reconstructed for Gothic on the basis of guþ-blostreis ‘worshiper of God’. Derived from *blōtanan. Feist 228; Seebold 123; Bammesberger Nom. 87.
ON blót-hús ‘heathen house of worship’, OHG bluoz-hūz ‘temple’. Compound of *blōtan and *xūsan. Carr Nom. comp. 83 (parallel formations).
ON blót ‘sacrifice, worship’, OE blót id. Related to *blōtanan. Torp- Falk 287; Holthausen AEEW 28; Vries ANEW 45; Seebold 123.

Goth blotan ‘to serve (God), to honor (through sacrifice)’, ON blóta ‘to worship’, OE blótan ‘to sacrifice’, OHG bluozan id. Probably related to *blōanan. Bugge BB III 98 (to Lat flāmen ‘sacrificial priest’ < *bhlād-men-); Grienberger Got. 51–52 (to OHG blōz ‘superbus’); Osthoff BB XXIV 142 (same as Bugge); Loewenthal ANF XXXV 231 (to *bhel- ‘to swell’); Meringer ZÖG LIV 398–399 (to Skt mlāyati ‘to wither, to fade’); Loewenthal PBB XLV 258–259 (to *blōđan); Torp-Falk 287; Holthausen AEEW 28; Feist 100–101; Walde- Hofmann 512–513; Jóhannesson IEW 643; Krause GGA CCXII 54 (to Toch A plāc, B plāce ‘speech’); Pokorny I 154; Chemodanov SGGJa I 87; Vries ANEW 45; Seebold 122–123; Hamp IF LXXIX 157 (to W blawdd ‘to boast, to brag’); Puhvel KZ LXXIX 5 (follows Walde- Horfmann); Lehmann GED 76.

Goth -ba ‘if, even if’. Related to Av bā ‘truly’, Gk φῆ‘as’, Lith bā ‘so, yes’, bē ‘and’, Slav *bo ‘for, because’ (Bader BSL LXVIII 39, 53). Grienberger Got. 41 (syncopated form of *ebō); Bartholomae 912; Joseph IF LXXXVII 166–169 (to Gk -sfa in μέσφα ‘until’); Trautmann BSW 22–24; Feist 72; Pokorny I 113; Fraenkel I 28, Gnomon XXVIII 238; Frisk II 1007–1008; Trubachev ESSJa II 141–142; Lehmann GED 55.

ON bófi‘knave, rogue’, OE prop. Boba, Bofa, OS prop. Bōvo, MLG bōve ‘scam, scoundrel’, Alam prop. Boabo, MHG buobe ‘boy, servant’. Originally, a nursery word. Pokorny I 164 (short form of *brōþar); Persson SVSU X 253 (to Lat faba ‘bean’); Wood MP XI 324 (same as Persson); Torp-Falk 272; Jóhannesson IEW 583 (to Icel babba ‘to say, to talk, to chat’); Vries ANEW 47.

ON bógr ‘shoulder (of an animal)’, OE boz ‘shoulder, arm’, OFris bōg ‘bow, branch’, MLG bōch ‘joint, shoulder, hip’, OHG buog ‘joint, shoulder, hip’ (< *bōziz). Identical with Toch A poke, B poko ‘arm’, Skt bāhú- ‘arm, fore-arm’, Av bāzu- ‘arm’, Gk πῆχυς ‘elbow’. Zupitza Gutt. 205; Bartholomae 955–956; Torp-Falk 272; Holthausen AEEW 29; Mayrhofer II 429; Pokorny I 108; Vries ANEW 47; Zalizniak II 199; Frisk II 531–532; Onions 109–110; Rix Sprache XXXII 311–323; Bammesberger Nom. 156; Kluge-Seebold 143; Adams TB 403.

OE bóian ‘to boast’. Identical with Lat 1 sg. *fōr ‘to speak’ < *fā-ƒ-ōr, Slav *bajǫ, *bajati ‘to tell, to narrate’ (Holthausen KZ XLVIII 238–239; Brugmann Grundriß II/3 100, 197). Further related to IE *bhā- ‘to speak’: Skt bhánati, Gk φημί. Meillet BSL XX 31, 161; Holthausen AEEW 29; Walde- Hofmann I 525–526; Mayrhofer II 469–470; Pokorny I 106; Frisk II 1009–1010; Trubachev ESSJa I 138– 139.
ON bók-list ‘book learning’, OHG pl. masc. buoh-liste id. Compound of *bōkz and *listiz. Carr Nom. comp. 83 (parallel formations).

ON bók-stafr ‘letter, character’ (if not from OE), OE bóc-stæf id., OS bōk-staf id., OHG buoh-stab id. Compound of *bōks and *stabaz. Krogmann IF XLVIII 268; Torp-Falk 272; Vries ANEW 48; Ebbinghaus GL XXI 194–197; Lehmann GED 77; Kluge- Seebold 142 (ON < OE).
ON bæki ‘beech’, OE béce id., OS bōkia id. Derived from *bōkz. Holthausen AEEW 28; Pokorny I 107; Onions 84–85.

Goth boka ‘letter, character’, OS bōka ‘beech’, OHG buohha id. (ō- and ōn-stem). See *bōkz. Forsterman KZ I 237; Torp-Falk 271–272; Feist 102– 103; Pokorny I 107; Zalizniak II 206; Lehmann GED 77; Kluge-Seebold 141.
ON bóka ‘to affirm by oath on the book’, OE bócian ‘to give by charter, to charter’, OFris bōkia id., MLG bōken id. Derived from *bōkz. Holthausen AEEW 28; Vries ANEW 48.

ON bók ‘beech; book’, OE bóc ‘beech’ (partly neut.), OFris bōk ‘book’, OS bōk ‘beech’ (also neut.), OHG buoh ‘beech’ (also neut. and masc.). Related to Gk φηγός ‘oak’, Lat fāgus ‘beech’, Gaul bāgo- in compounds, Slav *bъzъ ‘elder, Sambucus’ (Hoops Waldbäume 126). Wissmann Buche passim; Osthoff BB XXIX 249–251 (from *bhāu·-); Zupitza Gutt. 214; Bartholomae IF IX 271–272 (on Kurd būz ‘elm’ < Iran *būz-); Torp- Falk 271–272; Holthausen AEEW 28; Walde-Hofmann I 445–446; Feist 102–103; Paßler Fu S 155–160; Pokorny I 107; Chemodanov SGGJa I 90; Vries ANEW 47–48; Zalizniak II 206; Frisk II 1008; Onions 107; Lane KZ LXXXI 197–212; Lehmann GED 77; Bammesberger Nom. 197–198; Griepentrog Wurzelnomina 41–45; Kluge-Seebold 141.

ON bœn ‘prayer, request’, OE bén ‘prayer, petition’. A derivative in *-niof *bōjanan. Cf. in particular Arm ban ‘word, speech’ < *bhā-ni-, and also Gk φωνή ‘sound, tone’. Torp-Falk 256; Holthausen AEEW 29; Walde-Hofmann I 525–526; Pokorny I 105–106; Vries ANEW 69; Frisk II 1009–1010; Szemerényi KZ XLIII 122–123 (to

OE bósum ‘bosom’, OFris bōsem, bōsm id., OS bōsom id., OHG buosum id. Continues *bhōs-mo- related to Skt bábhasti ‘to blow’, Gk ψύχω id. Torp- Falk 272 (from *bōz-sma- related to *bōzuz ~ *bōzaz); Holthausen AEEW 31; Pokorny I 101 (to IE *bheu- ‘to swell’), 146; Onions 108; Kluge-Seebold 147.
ON bóta-lauss ‘getting no redress’, OFris bōt-lās id. Compound of *bōtō and *lausaz. Carr Nom. comp. 91 (parallel formations).

Goth bōtjan ‘to do good, to be of use’, ON bœta ‘to better, to improve’, OE bétan id., OFris bēta id., OS bōtian id., OHG buozen ‘to help, to exculpate’. Derived from *bōtō. Torp-Falk 258; Holthausen AEEW 29; Feist 103; Vries ANEW 69; Lehmann GED 78; Kluge-Seebold 147.

Goth bota ‘advantage, benefit, good, usefulness, profit’, ON bót ‘cure, remedy, compensation’ (traces of root stem), OE bót ‘help’, OFris bōte ‘improvement’, OS bōta id., OHG buoza id. Connected with *batiz. Wood MP XI 323 (to Skt bhūtí- ‘well-being, prosperity, power’); Torp-Falk 258; Holthausen AEEW 31; Feist 103; Pokorny I 106; Vries ANEW 51; Zalizniak II 206; Onions 107; Lehmann GED 78; Kluge- Seebold 147.
OSwed bōþ ‘hut’, MLG bōde id., MHG buode id. Related to *bōw(w)anan. Pokorny I 149.

Goth pl. bai ‘both’, ON bá-äir id., OE bezen (< *bō-jenō), bá id., OFris bēthe id., OS bē-thia id., OHG bē-de, bei-de id. Related to the second element of Toch A āmpi, B ant-api id., Skt u-bháu id., Gk ἄμφω id., Lat ambō id., Lith abū id., Slav *oba id. Sievers PBB X 495 (on OE); Schmidt KZ XXVI 384; Meringer KZ XXVIII 235–237 (Goth -ai under the influence of twai ‘two’); Torp-Falk 255 (Goth -oþ- in bajoþs id. = Lat -āt- in nostrātes ‘ours’); Holthausen AEEW 29; Walde-Hofmann I 37; Feist 74, 77; Mayrhofer I 107; Pokorny I 34–35; Fraenkel 1; Frisk I 100; Vries ANEW 22; Onions 109; Lindeman Verschärfung 11.6; Lehmann GED 56; Jasanoff BSL LXXI 123–131 (Toch < *Hent-bho-); Lloyd-Springer I 513–515; Kluge- Seebold 93; Ross-Berns Numerals 571– 573; Adams TB 14; Burlak IFTJa 238; Boutkan OFED s.v.

Goth bauan ‘to dwell, to inhabit’ (partly wk.), ON búa ‘to dwell, to live’, OE búan ‘to dwell’ (partly wk.), OFris buwa, bowa id., OS būan id. Related to Skt bhávati ‘to become, to be’, Av bavaiti id., Gk φύω ‘to grow’, Alb buj ~ bûj ‘to state overnight’, Lat perf. fuī ‘to be’, Lith būti id., Slav *byti id. See *bewwanan. Schmidt KZ XXVI 1–4 (Goth -au- < *-ōw-); Kluge PBB VI 381–383; Bartholomae 927–933; Torp- Falk 272; Holthausen AEEW 38; Walde-Hofmann I 557–558; Feist 83–84; Jóhannesson IEW 604–605; Mayrhofer II 485–487; Pokorny I 146–149; Fraenkel 68; Frisk II 1052– 1054; Seebold 124–128 (specifically to Skt bhāvayati ‘to bring into being’ and Slav *baviti ‘to be slow, to stay’); Trubachev ESSJa I 168–170, III 155; Lehmann GED 63–64; Kluge-Seebold 86; Orel AED 39.
ON bœr ‘town, village, farm’, OFris half be ‘half of the house’. Derived from *bōw(w)anan. Seebold 126.
ON byggja ‘to settle, to inhabit’, OE bÿan ‘to inhabit, to stay’, OFris būwa id., OS būan id., OHG būen, būwen id. Derived from *bōw(w)anan. Holthausen AEEW 39; Vries ANEW 67; Seebold 125.

ON bragä ‘moment, quick movement’, OE brægd ‘deceit, fraud’, OFris breud ‘pulling’. Derived from *brezđanan. Holthausen AEEW 32; Vries ANEW 52; Van Windekens I 387 (to Toch A praṅk, B prentse ‘moment’).

OE bræzen ‘brain’, OFris brein id., MLG bragen, bragen, bregen id. A difficult word. Perhaps, originally an adjective *braznan (mazzan) ‘upper (marrow)’ derived from IE *bher 6·h- ‘high’ and opposed to *mazziz ~ *mazzan proper. On the other hand, the original meaning of *braznan could be *‘head’. For the formation and meaning cf. Av bar 6šnu- ‘height, head’. Torp-Falk 279; Holthausen AEEW 32 (to Gk βρεχμός ‘top of the head’); Onions 113.

Norw dial. brasma ‘bream (a kind of fish)’, OS bressemo id. (masc.), OHG brahsmo id. (masc.), brahsma id. Probably derived from *brexanan ~ *brexōjanan (Torp-Falk 278). Pokorny I 142 (to *bher 6˚- ‘to shine’).

Goth braiþs ‘broad’, ON breiär id., OE brád id., OFris brēd id., OS brēd id., OHG breit id. Probably from *bhroi-to- based on IE *bhrei- ‘to cut’ (Wiedemann BB XXVIII 35). Bezzenberger BB III 81 (to Lith beıti ‘to pour out, to empty’); Petersson IF XXIII 392 (same as Bezzenberger, adds Lat frōns ‘forehead’); Grienberger Got. 53 (to Lat brūtus ‘heavy, difficult’ < *bhroito-); Johansson KZ XXX 451 (to Gk βρ—θύς ‘heavy’); Bugge PBB XXIV 453–454 (reconstructs *bhor 6 do-); Karsten NB I 58; Siebs KZ XXXVII 306 (to *sprīđanan and Lith brÿdoti ‘to stand at arrest while wading’); Loewenthal Wu S X 166–167 (from *bhr-oitos ‘wall-eyed’); Torp-Falk 277; Feist 104; Holthausen AEEW 31; Trier Lehm 43; Vries ANEW 55; Onions 119; Lehmann GED 79; Bammesberger Nom. 252; Heidermanns 136–137 (to
ON breizl ‘cover’, OE masc. (?) brædels ‘anything spread, carpet, covering’. Derived from *braiđaz. Holthausen AEEW 32; Vries ANEW 55.
Goth braidei ‘breadth’, OE brædo id., OFris brēde id., OHG breitī id. Derived from *braiđaz. Holthausen AEEW 32; Feist 104; Onions 115; Heidermanns 137.

Goth us-braidjan ‘to spread out’, ON breiäa ‘to spread’, OE brædan ‘to make broad’, OS brēdian ‘to spread’, OHG breiten id. Derived from *braiđaz. Holthausen AEEW 32; Feist 529; Vries ANEW 55; Lehmann GED 79; Heidermanns 137.
ON breiäa ‘drift, flock’, OE bræde ‘breadth’, MLG brēde id., OHG hant-breita ‘palm’. Derived from *braiđaz. Holthausen AEEW 32; Vries ANEW 55; Heidermanns 136.

ON brak ‘creaking noise’, OE bræc ‘breaking, flowing, rheum’, MLG brak ‘burst, fracture’, MHG brach ‘noise’. Derived from *brekanan. Similar to Lat fragor ‘fragility’. Osthoff Morph. Unt. V 100 (adds OIr braigim ‘to fart’ < *bhragƒō); Torp-Falk 277; Feist 104 (separates *brakan from *brekanan); Pokorny I 165; Vries ANEW 53; Seebold 133; Lehmann GED 79 (to Lat fragor ‘crash, noise’).
ON braka ‘to creak’, OS brakōn ‘to crash’, MHG braken id. Derived from *brakan. Vries ANEW 53; Seebold 133.
Norw brake ‘juniper bush’, E brake ‘bush’, MLG brake ‘branch’. Derived from *brekanan. Torp-Falk 277; Pokorny I 165.
ON brand-reiä ‘grate’, OE brand-rád ‘branding rod’, OS brande-rēda ‘grate’, OHG brante-reita id. Compound of *branđaz II and *raiđō. Carr Nom. comp. 46.

ON brandr ‘firebrand’, OE brand id., OFris brand id., OS brand id., OHG brant id. Derived from *brennanan (Charpentier KZ XL 462). Torp- Falk 263; Holthausen AEEW 32; Jóhannesson IEW 619–620; Pokorny I 144–145; Vries ANEW 53–54; Zalizniak I 129 (identifies *branđaz I and II); Onions 114; Seebold 138; Kluge-Seebold 130.

ON brandr ‘blade of a sword’, OE brand ‘sword’ (if not from ON), MHG brant id. Related to Lat frōns ‘forehead’ (Petersson KZ XXXIX 392). It can also be just a metaphoric usage of *branđaz I. Petersson IF XXIV 40–42 (to *burđan); Holthausen AEEW 32; Walde-Hofmann I 551; Pokorny I 167; Vries ANEW 53; Zalizniak I 129; Onions 114 (identical with *branđaz I).
ON branga ‘quarrel (?)’ (hap. leg.), MLG brank ‘fight’. Unclear. Falk Festschr. Bugge Kr. 13–14 (to

Goth ga-brannjan ‘to burn (trans.)’, ON brenna id., OE bærnan ‘to kindle, to light, to burn’, OFris barna ‘to burn (trans.)’, OS gi-brennian id., OHG brennen id. Causative of *brennanan. Torp-Falk 263; Holthausen AEEW 15; Feist 176; Vries ANEW 56; Onions 128; Seebold 137; Lehmann GED 80; Kluge- Seebold 134; Boutkan OFED s.v.
ON brenna ‘fire’, OHG brenne-friscing ‘burning offering’. Derived from *brannjanan. Seebold 138.

ON brattr ‘steep’, OE brant ‘high, deep, steep’. Probably related to Latv bruôds ‘roof ridge’ (Persson KZ XXXIII 292: Latv bruõdinš id.). Torp- Falk 279; Holthausen AEEW 32; Karsten NB II 229–230; Pokorny I 167; Chemodanov SGGJa I 77; Vries ANEW 54 (to *branđaz II); Heidermanns 137.
ON brasta ‘to bluster’, OHG brastōn ‘to pelt down, to patter’. Derived from *brestanan. Chemodanov SGGJa I 75; Seebold 139.

Crim. Goth broe ‘bread’, ON brauä id., OE breád ‘bit, morsel’, OFris brād ‘bread’, OS brōd id., OHG brōt id. Continues IE *bhrou-tó- related to *brewwanan (Falk ANF XLI 117–118). Another accentual and apophonic type of the same derivative in *-to- is represented by *bruþan. Persson SVSU X 784–785; Schröder NGWG [1910] 13–15 (Goth broe < *broet); Torp-Falk 281; Holthausen IF XLVII 329 (reconstructs Goth *broc = OHG brocko ‘hunk, chunk’), AEEW 33; Wood MLN XXV 72 (to Skt bhárvati ‘to chew, to devour’); Feist 106; Pokorny I 145; Vries ANEW 54; Zalizniak I 139; Stearns CG 132 (follows Schröder); Onions 115; Seebold 143; Lehmann GED 80–81; Bammesberger Nom. 79; Kluge-Seebold 137.

ON breyskr ‘brittle’, MLG brōsch id., G dial. brausch ‘brittle, dry’. Despite morphological difficulties, derived from *breutanan. Holthausen KZ XLVII 311; Torp-Falk 282; Pokorny I 169; Vries ANEW 56 (reconstructs
ON breyta ‘to alter, to change’, OE brytan ‘to break’. Causative of *breutanan. Holthausen AEEW 37; Vries ANEW 56; Seebold 141.
Norw dial. bradd ‘shore, bank’, OE breord ‘brim, margin, rim’, OHG brart ‘edge, prow of a ship’. Related to *bruzđaz. Torp-Falk 266.
ON brigä ‘change, breach’ (also fem.), OE brizd ‘change, variety’. Derived from *brezđanan. Holthausen AEEW 34; Vries ANEW 56.

ON bregäa ‘to move swiftly’, OE brezdan ‘to move to and fro, to vibrate’, OFris breida ‘to pull, to tug’, OS part. brugdun ‘knitted, tied’, OHG brettan ‘to pull, to tug’. Derived from *brekanan. However, if ‘moving swiftly’ or ‘vibrating’ are older meanings, one could derive this verb from *brexanan ~ *brexōjanan. Torp-Falk 278; Holthausen AEEW 33; Feist 103–104; Jóhannesson IEW 621–623; Pokorny I 142 (to *bher 6˚- ‘to shine’); Vries ANEW 55 (to *bher- ‘fence’); Onions 113; Seebold 129–132; Lehmann GED 79 (to *brēzwō ~ *brēxwō); Heidermanns 140.

ON brjá, brá ‘to flicker’ (wk.), MHG brehen ‘to light up’ (str. and wk.). Connected with Skt bhrāśate ‘to shine, to glitter’, Gk φορκόν: λευκόν, πολιόν, =υσόν, Hes. Torp-Falk 278 (to Lith mérkti ‘to shut (eyes)’, Slav *mьrknǫti ‘to become dark’, Gk ἀμαρύσσω ‘to sparkle, to twinkle’); Mayrhofer II 532 (the comparison with Skt “subjective Auswahl bleiben muß”); Pokorny I 141–142; Frisk II 1036.
ON brek ‘fraudulent purchase of land’, OE brec ‘breaking, crash, noise’, MLG brek ‘affliction, shortage’. Derived from *brekanan. Holthausen AEEW 33; Vries ANEW 55; Seebold 132–133.

Goth brikan ‘to break’, OE brecan id., OFris breka id., OS brekan id., OHG brehhan id. Related to Lat frangō id., Lith bróžti ‘to whisk’ (Curtius Gr. Et. 542). Cf. WGmc derivative *brōkaz ~ *brōkan > OE bróc ‘brook’, MLG brōk ‘marsh’, OHG bruoh ‘swamp’. Zupitza Gutt. 196, 214; Torp-Falk 277–278 (*brōkaz ~ *brōkan to Gk βραγός: ἑλος, Hes.); Holthausen AEEW 33, 35; Walde- Hofmann I 541; Feist 105–106; Pokorny I 165; Fraenkel 35; Onions 115, 120; Seebold 132–135; Lehmann GED 80; Kluge-Seebold 133.
ON breki ‘breaker’, OE wiäer-breca ‘adversary’, OHG hūs-brehho ‘burglar’. Derived from *brekanan. Holthausen AEEW 33; Vries ANEW 55; Seebold 133.

OE bremman ‘to roar, to rage’, OHG pret. bram ‘rugiebam’ (*-m-), MHG brimmen ‘to roar’. Identical with Lat fremō ‘to roar, to resound’, W brefu ‘to bleat’ (Schulze KZ XLV 55; Fick II 184). Persson Beiträge 36 (on Gk βρέμω ‘to roar’ with initial *b-); Osthoff Morph. Unt. V 93–94 (from *mrem-); Torp-Falk 279; Holthausen AEEW 33–34; Walde-Hofmann I 544– 545; Pokorny I 143; Seebold 135–136.

Swed brind, brinde ‘elk’, Norw bringe id. Close to Messap βρένδον: đlafon (Hes.), br°nτιον: ἡ κεφαλὴ τοῦ ἐλάφου (Strabo), brunda ‘caput cervi’ (isid.). Cf. Lith bríedis ‘deer’, OPrus braydis ‘mooze’ and Alb bri ~ brî ‘horn’. Bezzenberger BB XXIII 299–300; Löwenthal Wu S XI 60; Torp- Falk 279; Mayer KZ LXVI 75–77, 87–89 (on onomastics containing this stem); Pokorny I 168–169; Fraenkel 57; Toporov PJa I 244–245; Orel AED 36–37.

Goth briggan ‘to bring’, OE brinzan id., OFris bringa id., OS bringan id., OHG bringan id. Related to IE *bhrenk- in W he-brwng ‘to bring, to lead’, Toch B prāṅk- ‘to take away’. Brugmann IF XII 155–158 (from *bher- ‘to carry’ and *ene˚- ‘to reach’); Gauthiot Mélanges Saussure 119–121 (follows Brugmann); Johansson PBB XV 227–228 (prefix *b-; related to Gk ῥίμφα ‘quick, fast’); Zupitza Gutt. 209; Torp-Falk 279; Pedersen KZ XXXIX 354 (to Arm bafinam ‘to exalt’ < *bhor·h-n-); Holthausen AEEW 34; Feist 105; Pokorny I 168; Chemodanov SGGJa I 111; Onions 118; Seebold 136–137; Lehmann GED 79–80; Kluge-Seebold 136.
ON bringa ‘breast, chest’, OE brinz-ádl ‘epilepsy’. Derived from *brenzanan. Torp-Falk 279 (to Icel bringr ‘hill’, and further to Lith brīnkti ‘to soak’).

ON fem. brekka ‘slope’, ME neut. brinke ‘bank, shore’, MLG brink ‘edge of a field, hill’. Close to Toch B prenke ‘island’ (Van Windekens I 387). Zupitza Gutt. 197–198 (from IE *mrengand further to *markan); Torp-Falk 277 (to *brekanan); Pokorny I 167; Vries ANEW 55; Onions 118; Lerchner 50–51; Kluge-Seebold 136 (to *barmaz II).

Goth brinnan ‘to burn (intr.)’, ON brinna, brenna id., OE beornan id., OFris burna id., OS brinnan id., OHG brinnan id. Of unknown origin. It is not clear whether ON brimi ‘fire’, ME brim ‘embers’ belong here. Kuhn KZ I 201; Bezzenberger BB XII 77 (to Lat furnus ‘stove’); Strachan BB XX 12 (to MIr doe-prinn ‘affluit’); Abrahamson NTU VIII 15–21; Pedersen Kelt. Gr. II 477–478; Persson Beiträge 784 (from *bhren-⁄-ō); Torp-Falk 263; Holthausen AEEW 24; Feist 106; Jóhannesson IEW 619–621; Pokorny I 144–145 (to IE *bhreu- ‘to move violently’); Vries ANEW 56–57; Onions 128; Seebold 137–138 (to * g ⁄her- ‘to burn’); Lehmann GED 80; Kluge- Seebold 134.
Goth brinno ‘fever’, OHG brinna ‘burning, pain’. Derived from *brennanan. Feist 106; Seebold 137.

ON bresta ‘to burst’, OE berstan id., OFris bersta id., OS brestan id., OHG brestan id. Related to OIr brissim ‘to break’. Torp-Falk 280; Holthausen AEEW 21; Jóhannesson IEW 640–641; Pokorny I 169 (to MIr brosc ‘noise’); Vries ANEW 56; Onions 129; Seebold 139; Kluge-Seebold 100 (to
ON brestr ‘outburst, crash’, OE berst ‘loss’, OHG bresto ‘destruction, damage’. Derived from *brestanan. Torp-Falk 280; Holthausen AEEW 21; Vries ANEW 56.
ON brjósk ‘gristle’, MLG brūsche ‘bump’. Probably related to *brustz. Torp-Falk 281–282; Pokorny I 171.
ON brjóst-bjǫrg ‘breastplate’, OE bréost-beorz id. (masc.). Compound of *breustan and *berzō. Carr Nom. comp. 80 (parallel formations).

ON brjóst ‘breast’, OE bréost id. (also fem. and masc.), OFris briast id., OS pl. briost id. An ablaut variant of *brustz. Structurally close to Slav *br’ustъ ‘calf (anat.)’, *br’ustъvь id. (Trubachev ESSJa III 34–35). Grimm- Grimm II 443 (influenced by *breutanan); Kluge Btrg. Gesch. VIII 510; Torp-Falk 283; Holthausen AEEW 34; Pokorny I 170–171; Chemodanov SGGJa I 61; Vries ANEW 57–58; Zalizniak II 206; Onions 116; Lehmann GED 82; Bammesberger Nom. 200; Kluge-Seebold 140.

ON brjóta ‘to break’, OE breótan ‘to bruise, to break’, MHG briezen ‘to bud’. Of unclear origin. Torp-Falk 281–282 (to Lith briáutis ‘to penetrate’); Holthausen AEEW 34; Wood MP V 270–271 (to IE *bhreu- ‘to cut off’); Persson UUÅ 1891 125 (to *barjanan); Trier Holz 81; Jóhannesson IEW 635–636; Pokorny I 169; Vries ANEW 58; Stang NTS XX 239–242, LS 16; Seebold 141–142; Lehmann GED 81; Köbler 715.

ON part. brugginn ‘to brew (beer)’, OE breówan id., OFris past part. browen id., OFris brouwa id., OS part. gebreuuan id., MLG brouwen id., MHG briuwen, brūwen id. Related to Lith briáuti ‘to push’, Slav *brujati ‘to buzz, to flow’. See *bruþan. Torp-Falk 281; Holthausen AEEW 34; Jóhannesson IEW 619–621; Pokorny I 145 (to *brennanan); Vries ANEW 60; Onions 117; Seebold 143–144; Lindeman Verschärfung 17.6; Kluge-Seebold 132.

OSwed part. brādhin ‘melted’, OE brædan ‘to roast, to broil’, OFris brēda id., MLG braden id., OHG brātan id. Can be connected with *brējanan. Torp-Falk 263–264; Holthausen AEEW 32 (to *brēđaz); Feist 104–105; Jóhannesson IEW 613–614; Pokorny I 132–133; Vries ANEW 62; Seebold 128–129; Lehmann GED 79; Kluge- Seebold 131 (to IE * g ⁄her- ‘to burn’).

ON bráär ‘hasty, sudden’, OE sbst. bræä ‘odor, scent, breath’. Derived from *brējanan. Perhaps, further related to Lat fretum, fretus ‘raging, swelling, heat, violence’. Torp-Falk 263; Holthausen AEEW 32; Walde-Hofmann I 546–547; Pokorny I 132– 133 (to IE *bher- ‘to boil up’); Vries ANEW 52; Onions 116.
ON bræäa ‘to melt’, OE brædan ‘to roast’. Derived from *brēđanan. Torp-Falk 263; Holthausen AEEW 32; Seebold 129.
ON bráä ‘raw meat’, OE bræde ‘roasted meat’, OS brādo ‘ham’, OHG brāto ‘meat, roasted meat’. Related to *brēđanan. Torp-Falk 263; Holthausen AEEW 32; Vries ANEW 52; Kluge-Seebold 131.

Goth braƕ ‘twinkling (of an eye)’ (only in the context of in braƕa augins §n =ιπαι ὀφθαλμοῦ), ON brá ‘eyelid’, OFris āg-brē id., OS slegi-brāwa, brāha ‘eyelid, brow’, OHG brāwa ‘eyelid, brow’. Cf. also OE masc. bræw ‘eyelid’ < *brēzwiz ~ *brēxwiz. Derived from *brexanan ~ *brexōjanan. Kuhn KZ I 134 (to IE *bhreu- ‘brow’); Fick KZ XX (1872) 178; Spitzer I 350, 463 (follows Kuhn); Johansson KZ XXX 445–448 (to Lith mérkti ‘to motion’); Noreen SVSU V/3 7; Torp-Falk 278–279; Holthausen AEEW 32; Feist 103–104; Specht 83, 162 (to Lat frōns ‘forehead’); Pokorny I 142; Vries ANEW 51–52 (to *brezđanan); Zalizniak II 192; Onions 112; Lehmann GED 78–79 (to IE *bhrek- ‘to gleam’); Kluge-Seebold 131– 132; Adams TB 418–419 (to Toch B preke ‘time, occasion’).

Crim. Goth breen ‘to roast’, MDu bræyen id., MHG bræjen ‘to smell’. A distant connection with Skt bhuráti ‘to move’, Gk porf,rv ‘to heave, to rise’ is possible. Fick I 696 (to Lat fretum, in its metaphoric meaning: fretus ipse anni permiscet frigus et aestum, Lucr. 6, 364); Grienberger Zd Ph XXX 133; Bremer PBB XI 279; Holthausen PBB LXVI 268; Feist 104–105; Pokorny I 132–133; Lehmann GED 79 (to IE *bher- ‘to boil up’).
ON brim-leiä ‘sea’, OE brim-lád ‘sea-way’. Compound of *briman and *laiđō. Carr Nom. comp. 54.

Burg *brim ‘flood’, ON brim ‘surf’, ME brim ‘surf, sea’. Related to Gk φριμάω ‘to snort and leap (of animals)’ (Froehde BB XVII 310). Charpentier IF XXIX 376 (to Skt bhrámati ‘to move around’); Pokorny I 133 (to *brēđaz); Vries ANEW 56–57; Frisk II 1043; Köbler 709.

ON brók ‘tartan cloth, breeches’, OE bróc ‘breeches’, OFris brōk id., MLG brōk id., OHG līn-bruoh ‘stocking’. The relations with Gaulish brāca ‘breeches’, MIr bróc id., OPrus broakay id. are not clear. The latter is probably from Gmc. Much ZDADL XLII 170 (to *brekanan); Stokes IF II 168 (to Lat frāgrō ‘to emit a smell, to smell’); Zimmer KZ XXX 87–88 (MIr bróc ‘trousers’ < Gmc); Jaberg Wu S IX 148–151 (from Celtic); Torp-Falk 280; Holthausen AEEW 35; Jacobsohn ZDADL LXVI 244–246 (same as Jaberg); Pokorny I 165; Vries ANEW 58; Zalizniak I 157; Onions 116; Birkhan Gu K 402; Toporov PJa I 253–254; Bammesberger Nom. 198; Griepentrog Wurzelnomina 62–90; Kluge- Seebold 138.

Goth broþar ‘brother’, ON bróäir id., OE bróäor id., OFris brōther id., OS brōthar id., OHG bruodar id. Continues IE *bhrāter- id.: Toch A pracar, B procer, Skt bhrātar-, Av brātar-, Gk Ion φρήτηρ: ἀδελφος (Hes.), Arm e∑bayr, Lat frāter, OIr bráth(a)ir, OPrus brāti, Slav *bratrъ. Kieckers HG 95; Bartholomae 971–972; Torp-Falk 280– 281; Holthausen AEEW 36; Walde- Hofmann I 541–542; Feist 106–107; Mayrhofer II 530–531; Pokorny I 163–164; Zalizniak I 157; Vries ANEW 58; Frisk II 1039–1040; Onions 121; Benveniste IEL 172; Szemerényi Kinship 25–28; Trubachev ESSJa II 238; Lehmann GED 81; Hamp KZ CIII 102–103; Bammesberger Nom. 208; Kluge- Seebold 138–139 Adams TB 421–422; Burlak IFTJa 250.
ON bróäur-bani ‘fratricide’, OE bróäor-bana id. Compound of *brōþēr and *banōn. Carr Nom. comp. 54.

ON bryggja ‘landing-stage, pier’, OE brycz ‘bridge’, OFris bregge, brigge id., OS bruggia id., OHG brugga id. A guttural derivative connected with Gaul brīva ‘bridge’ < *bhrē⁄ā, Slav *brьvь id. Cf. also ON brú ‘bridge’ < *brōwō. Kuhn KZ I 134 (to Skt bhrū- ‘brow, eyebrow’, Gk ὀφρύς id.); Meringer Wu S I 189; Torp-Falk 281; Lane Language IX 253–254; Holthausen AEEW 37; Pokorny I 173; Vries ANEW 59, 61–62; Zalizniak II 179; Seebold IF LXXXVII 189–191; Trubachev ESSJa III 71–72; Kluge-Seebold 138.
Norw dial. brogn, brogne ‘twig’, OE brozn(e) id. Closely related to W brwyn-en ‘bulrush’, OCorn brunnen ‘juncus, scirpus’, Bret broenn-enn < *bhrugh-no-. Holthausen AEEW 36; Pokorny I 174.

Goth ga-bruka ‘broken piece, crumb’, OE ze-broc ‘broken piece, fragment’ (neut.), OHG brocko ‘a kind of biscuit’. Comparison with *brekanan (Curtius Gr. Et. 542; Güntert Ablaut 54, 79–86) is impeded by the vocalism of *brukōn (but cf. *brunjaz ~ *brunōn). Torp-Falk 277; Hirt Idg. Gr. II 82; Holthausen AEEW 36; Feist 176; Walde-Hofmann I 541; Pokorny I 165; Lehmann GED 134.

ON brum ‘bud’, OHG brom id. Probably from *bruzman, related to *bruznō. Bugge BB III 99 (to Lat frōns ‘foliage, branch, bow’); Lidén Studien 96 (same as Bugge); Torp-Falk 105 (follow Bugge); Pokorny I 142; Vries ANEW 60 (to OE bróm ‘shrub from which besoms are made, broom’).
Swed brànad ‘conflagration’, OE brúnéäa ‘a kind of disease’, OHG bronado ‘scab, rash’. Derived from *brunjaz ~ *brunōn. Torp-Falk 263.
ON bruni ‘burned lava-field’, OE bryne ‘burning, flame, fire’. Related to *brennanan. Torp-Falk 263; Holthausen AEEW 37; Vries ANEW 61.

Goth brunjo ‘armor, breastplate’, ON brynja ‘coat of mail’, OE byrne ‘corslet, coat of mail’, OS brunnia id., OHG brunna, brunnī id. Borrowed from Celtic, cf. OIr bruinne ‘breast’, OW bronn id., Bret bronn id. (Stokes Urkelt. 184). Grienberger Got. 53 (to *brūwō); Wiedemann BB XXVII 235 (to Alb mbrej ‘to harness’, brinjë ‘rib’); Wood MLN XIII 82 (to *bher- ‘to carry’); Petersson IF XXIII 390–392 (to Lat frōns ‘forehead’); Torp-Falk 282; Holthausen AEEW 41; Feist 107–108; Szadrowsky GRM XXXI 273; Pokorny I 170; Vendryes B-104–105; Vries ANEW 62; Zalizniak I 155; Lehmann GED 81–82; Kluge- Seebold 139 (to *brustz).

Goth brunna ‘fountain, source’, ON brunnr, bruär ‘spring, well’ (from *brunnaz), OE burna ‘stream’, OFris burna ‘spring, well’, OS brunno, burno id., OHG brunno id. Based on an r/l-stem reflected in Arm a∑biwr ‘fount’, Gk φρέ`ρ ‘well, reservoir’. Johansson BB XVIII 37; Stokes Urkelt. 172 (to MIr bruinnid ‘to make spout’); Osten-Sacken IF XXIII 379 (to Slav *brujati ‘to stream’); Walde KZ XXXIV 517; Torp-Falk 264; Holthausen AEEW 39; Feist 108; Pokorny I 144; Vries ANEW 61; Zalizniak II 178; Frisk II 1040–1041; Onions 128; Lehmann GED 82; Kluge-Seebold 139–140.
Goth ala-brunsts ‘burnt offering’, OHG brunst ‘fire, burning, inflammation’. Derived from *brennanan. Torp-Falk 263; Feist 33–34; Seebold 138; Lehmann GED 24–25; Kluge- Seebold 140.
Norw brusk ‘bush’, LG brüsch ‘hair-lock’. Close to Lith pl. br~zgai ‘scrub, copse, bush’, brūzgas ‘rod, twig’. Fick III 282; Torp-Falk 282; Pokorny I 172; Fraenkel 61.

Goth brusts ‘breast’, OFris brust id., MLG borst id., OHG brust id. (also an i-stem). Identical with Slav *brъstъ, *brъstь ‘bud’. Grimm DW II 443 (to *breutanan); Zupitza Gutt. 129; Bugge PBB XIII 320–322 (to Slav pl. *pьrsi ‘breast’); Grienberger Got. 53–54 (to *brūwō); Uhlenbeck PBB XXX 271; Johansson IF XIX 117–118 (to Slav *bьrdo ‘hill, elevation’); Petersson IF XXIII 391 (to IE *bhreus- ‘to swell’); Torp-Falk 283; Feist 108–109; Pokorny I 170–171 (to OIr brú ‘belly’); Chemodanov SGGJa I 61; Vries ANEW 57; Zalizniak II 206; Trubachev ESSJa III 57–58; Lehmann GED 82; Bammesberger Nom. 200; Griepentrog Wurzelnomina 463–471; Kluge-Seebold 139–140 (to Slav *grǫdь ‘breast’).
ON brot ‘broken piece, fragment’, OE ze-brot ‘fragment’. Derived from *breutanan. Holthausen AEEW 36; Vries ANEW 59; Seebold 141; Bammesberger Nom. 56.
ON brytja ‘to chop’, OE bryttian ‘to divide into fragments’. Related to *brutjōn. Holthausen AEEW 38; Pokorny I 169; Vries ANEW 62.

ON bryti ‘steward, bailiff’, OE brytta ‘bestower, dispenser, distributor’. Derived from *breutanan. Torp- Falk 282; Holthausen AEEW 37; Pokorny I 169; Vries ANEW 62; Seebold 141; Bammesberger Nom. 182.

ON broä ‘broth’, OE broä id., OHG brod id. Continues IE *bhrútoidentical with OIr bruth ‘heat’, Thrac βρῦτος ‘barley beer’, βροῦτος: τό §k κριθῶν πόμα, Hes. (Tomaschek Thr. II/1 7) further belonging to IE *bhreu- ‘to move strongly’, see *brewwanan. Torp- Falk 281; Holthausen AEEW 36; Detschew Thr. 93; Pokorny I 145; Georgiev Issledov. 119; Vries ANEW 58; Zalizniak I 139; Onions 121; Bammesberger Nom. 79.

ON broddr ‘spike, shaft’, OE brord ‘prick, sharp point’, OHG brort ‘edge, stem’. Continues *bhruzdhos < *bhrus-dh-. Related to Lith bruzdūklis ‘bridle’, Slav *brъzda id. Pedersen IF V 73; Meillet Etudes I 156; Torp-Falk 266; Trautmann BSW 39; Holthausen AEEW 36; Pokorny I 110; Fraenkel I 60; Vries ANEW 58; Zalizniak I 129; Onions 112; Trubachev ESSJa III 62.
ON brydda ‘to prick, to point’, OE bryrdan ‘to prick, to goad’. Derived from *bruzđaz. Holthausen AEEW 37; Vries ANEW 61.

ON brúä-gumi ‘bridegroom’, OE brÿd-guma id., OFris breid-goma id., OS brūdi-gomo id., OHG brūti-gomo id. Compound of *brūđiz and *zumōn. Torp-Falk 282; Schmidt ZDADL LI 28–287; Carr Nom. comp. 46; Peeters KZ XC 8–9; Kluge-Seebold 132–133.

ON brúä-laup ‘bride’s journey’, OFris brō-lop id. Cf. also OS brūd-loht, MHG brūt-louft. Compound of *brūđiz and *xlaupan. Schröder ZDADL LXI 17–34 (reconstructs the original meaning as ‘bride’s dance’); Krogmann Wu S XVI 80–90; Torp-Falk 282; Carr Nom. comp. 46; Vries ANEW 59; Kluge-Seebold 133.

Goth bruþs ‘bride, young wife’ (< *brūþiz), ON brúär ‘bride’, OE brÿd ‘bride, wife’, OFris breid ‘newly married’, OS brūd ‘bride, married woman’, OHG brūt ‘newly married woman, young woman, daughter-in-law’. Of unknown origin. Uhlenbeck PBB XXII 188 (< *mrūti- ‘promised, engaged’, to Lith martī ‘bride, daughter-in-law’, Skt brávīti ‘to speak, to say’); Wood MLN XV 96 (follows Uhlenbeck); Wiedemann BB XXVII 205 (same as Wood); Bugge PBB XIII 184–185 (from *per-ūdhi-, based on *⁄edh- ‘to lead’); Torp Festschr. Unger 174 (same as Uhlenbeck); Braune PBB XXXII 58 (to Lat Frutis, epithet of Venus); Kluge PBB XXXIV 561–562 (follows Braune); Helten PBB XXXV 306–310 (to *brauđan, for the meaning cf. OE hlæf-dize ‘bread-kneader, mistress, lady’); van Helten Zd Ph XLII 446–447 (to Slav *brakъ ‘marriage’); Langenhove LS II 48–64 (from IE *bhṛ-H⁄-e H-ti- ‘lifegiving force bearer’); Krogmann Wu S XVI 80–90 (to *breutanan); Hammarström Glotta XI 216–217 (to Gk Ἀφροδίτη); Torp-Falk 282; Holthausen AEEW 37; Feist 103, 110–111; Trier ZSSR LXV 254–255 (to IE *bhrāter- ‘brother’); Pokorny I 299; Vries ANEW 59–60; Zalizniak I 150; Onions 117; Lehmann GED 83–84; Kluge-Seebold 132.

OE brúcan ‘to use’, OFris brūka id., OS brūkan id., OHG brūhhan id. Cf. Goth brukjan id. < *brūkjanan. Related to Lith braukiū, bra Ukti ‘to smear, to paint’, Slav *brusiti ‘to whet’. Tomaschek Thr. I/30 (to Thrac ethn. Βρύγες); Solmsen KZ XXXIV 70 (same as Tomaschek); Torp-Falk 281; Woods MP V 270–271 (to *breutanan); Holthausen AEEW 37; Feist 107; Holthausen AEEW 36; Pokorny I 170, 173 (to Lat frūx ‘fruits of the earth, produce of the fields, pulse’, fruor ‘to enjoy, to delight in’); Chemodanov SGGJa I 91; Onions 120; Seebold 140–141; Kluge-Seebold 131.

Goth bruks ‘useful’, OE brÿce id., OHG brūhhi id. Derived from *brūkanan. Wood MP V 270–271 (to *breutanan); Zupitza Gutt. 96; Feist 107; Walde-Hofmann I 552–553 (to Lat frūgī ‘having merit’, dat. of frūx ‘fruit’); Holthausen AEEW 36; Pokorny I 173; Matzel Festschr. Schröder 97, KZ CV 134–135; Lehmann GED 81; Bammesberger Nom. 260; Heidermanns 142.

ON brúnn ‘brown’, OE brún ‘dark, brown’, OFris brūn ‘brown’, OS brūn-rōd ‘sparkling red’, OHG brūn ‘reddish-brown, blackish’. Structurally identical with Gk φρῦνος ‘frog’ < *‘brown’ (Kuhn KZ I 200). Further related to IE *bhreu-, cf. Skt babhrú- ‘reddish-brown, brown’. Torp-Falk 264; Schwentner FB 56–59; Holthausen AEEW 36 (adds Slav *brunîti ‘to shine’); Krogmann Zd Ph LXVII 1–10; Karsten NB II 204–205; Mayrhofer II 409–410; Pokorny I 136; Dybo VSJa 26; Vries ANEW 61; Frisk II 1047; Onions 121; Heidermanns 143; Kluge-Seebold 132.

ON sbst. brún ‘edge’, OE brún ‘shining, flashing’. Related to OIr brú ‘border, edge, shore’, Lith briaunā ‘edge’ (Persson Beiträge 17–19, 750–752: further to *brūwō). Holthausen AEEW 36; Pokorny I 170–173; Fraenkel 57; Vries ANEW 60; Zalizniak II 220; Heidermanns 143 (identifies *brūnaz I and II); Adams TB 374 (identical with Toch A pärwāá, B pärwāne ‘brows’).
Swed brūsa ‘to roar, to thunder’, MLG brūsen id., MHG brūsen id. Of imitative origin. Persson UUÅ 1891 164 (to Gk φρυάσσομαι ‘to neigh, to be wanton’); Pokorny I 171–172; Vries ANEW 61.

ON brún ‘eyebrow’ (generalized pl. stem), OE brú ‘eyebrow’. Related to Toch A dual pärwān-, B pärwāne id., Skt bhrū- id., Gk ὀφρῦς id., Maced ἀβροῦτες (leg. ἀβρούWες): ὀφρῦς. Makeδόνες (Hes.), MIr brúad, Lith bruvīs id., Slav *bry, gen. -ъve id. Kretschmer Einleitung 287 (on Macedonian); Hoffmann Mak. 51 (conjecture for Macedonian); Torp-Falk 281; Holthausen AEEW 36; Mayrhofer II 534–536; Pokorny I 172–173; Fraenkel 57; Zalizniak II 220; Frisk II 454; Onions 121; Trubachev ESSJa III 63–64; Gindin Etim. 1984 41 (Maced ἀβροῦτες = Slav pl. *brudi ‘whiskers’).

ON boä ‘bid, commandment’, OE bod ‘command, commandment’, OFris bod id, OS gi-bod id, OHG bot ‘authoritative pronouncement’. Derived from *beuđanan. Torp-Falk 274; Holthausen AEEW 28; Pokorny I 151; Seebold 109; Bammesberger Nom. 56.
ON prop. Boddi, Norw budda ‘newborn domestic animal’, ME budde ‘bud’, LG budde ‘louse’. Of descriptive origin. Holthausen AEEW 38; Vries ANEW 47; Kluge-Seebold 105–106.

ON botn ‘bottom’ (*-t-), OE bodan id. (also botm with *-t-), OFris bodem id., OS bodom id., OHG bodam id. (<*-þ-). Unexplained fluctuations in the intervocalic dental. Close to Gk πυθμήν id. and further to Skt budhná- ‘bottom, ground’, Av buna- id. (?< *bhundhno-), Lat fundus ‘bottom’. Petersson SVSL I 17–18 (alternation *d: *dh explained from the paradigm nom. *bhudh-, gen. *bhu(n)dnes); Bartholomae 968–969; Torp-Falk 275 (add OE byden ‘bushel, barrel’, MLG bodene ‘cask’, OHG butin(a) id. which are from MLat butina ‘bottle, vessel’); Porzig Wu S XV 112–133; Nordmeyer GR XIII 127– 129; Holthausen AEEW 31; Walde- Hofmann I 564–565; Mayrhofer II 438; Pokorny I 174; Vries ANEW 51; Zalizniak II 226; Frisk II 620–621; Onions 109; Lühr Expr. 340–341; Kluge- Seebold 122–123.
ON boäa ‘to announce’, OE bodian ‘to tell, to announce’, OFris bodia ‘to summon’, MLG boden id., MHG boten id. Derived from *beuđanan. Holthausen AEEW 28; Vries ANEW 46–47.

Burg *buda ‘messenger’, ON boäi id., OE boda id., OFris boda id, OS bodo id, OHG boto id Derived from *beuđanan. Torp-Falk 275; Holthausen AEEW 28; Pokorny I 151; Vries ANEW 47; Zalizniak I 139; Onions 104; Seebold 109; Bammesberger Nom. 174; Köbler 709; Kluge-Seebold 128.

Crim. Goth boga ‘bow’, ON bogi id., OE boza id., OFris boga id., OS swi-bogo ‘vault’, OHG bogo ‘arch, bow’. Derived from *beuzanan. Torp-Falk 273; Holthausen AEEW 29; Feist 102; Pokorny I 152–153; Vries ANEW 47; Zalizniak I 153; Onions 111; Seebold 111; Lehmann GED 73, 77; Bammesberger Nom. 174; Kluge-Seebold 123.
ON bygill ‘stirrup’, MLG bogel ‘hanger’, G Bügel id. Derived from *beuzanan. Vries ANEW 67; Kluge- Seebold 143.
ON bugr ‘bowing, winding’, OE bÿze ‘bowing, bending, angle’, MDu boge ‘bend’. Derived from *beuzanan. Holthausen AEEW 39; Vries ANEW 63; Seebold 111.

Goth bugjan ‘to buy’, ON byggja ‘to let out, to lend’, OE byczan ‘to buy’, OS buggian id. Derived from *beuzanan (Grienberger Got. 54). For the semantic development cf. Gk πωλέω ‘to sell’ ~ πέλω ‘to turn, to spin’. Osthoff IF V 293–295; Uhlenbeck Btrg. Gesch. XXX 2777; Loewenthal ANW XXXII 282 (to Gk φεύγω ‘to fly’); Torp-Falk 274; Holthausen AEEW 39; Feist 111; Pokorny I 153; Vries ANEW 67; Benveniste 110–111 (to Av baog- ‘to save, to redeem, to liberate’); Seebold 111; Benveniste IEL 108–111; Lindeman Verschärfung 15.3; Lehmann GED 84.
ON bót ‘patch’, OE byht ‘bending, corner’ (masc.), MLG bucht ‘bay’, G Bucht id. Derived from *beuzanan. Holthausen AEEW 39; Vries ANEW 51; Kluge-Seebold 142.
OFris buckia ‘to bend’, MLG bucken id., MHG bücken id. Based on the unattested *beukanan to be compared with Skt bhujáti id., Ir fid-bocc ‘wood bow’. Mayrhofer II 504–506; Pokorny I 153.

ON bukkr, bokkr ‘buck’, OE bucc ‘roebuck’, MLG buck ‘goat, ram’, OHG boc, buc id. Borrowed from the Celtic word for ‘he-goat’, cf. MIr bocc, W bwch, Corn boch, Bret bouc’h (Pedersen Litteris VII 23–24). Meillet MSL XV 356 (hypocoristic gemination); Zupitza Gutt. 196; Torp-Falk 273 (related to Celtic and other Indo-European forms continuing *bhu·os ~ *bhukkos); Lane Language VII 282 (to *beuzanan); Martinet Gém. 87, 138–139; Porzig Gliederung 115; Pokorny I 174; Vries ANEW 64; Zalizniak II 178; Onions 122; Kluge- Seebold 122.
ON bokki ‘he-goat’, OE bucca id. See *bukkaz. Holthausen AEEW 38; Martinet Gém. 113, 139, 178; Onions 122.
ON bolr, bulr ‘trunk’, MLG bole ‘plank’, MHG bole id. Related to *būljō(n). Torp-Falk 266 (to Slav *bъlvanъ ‘idol’); Vries ANEW 49 (to
ON bylgja ‘billow’, MLG bulge ‘rising wave’, MHG bulge ‘storm wave’. Derived from *belzanan. Torp- Falk 268; Pokorny I 126; Vries ANEW 67; Kluge-Seebold 144.
ON bylja ‘to roar’, OE bylzan ‘to offend, to anger’, MDu bullen ‘to race, to rage’, MHG büllen ‘to bark’. Connected with *bellanan I. Holthausen AEEW 40; Pokorny I 123; Vries ANEW 67.

ON bolli ‘bowl’, OE bolla ‘round vessel, cup, pot’, OFris strot-bolla ‘larynx’, OS bollo ‘cup’, OHG bolla ‘bud, round pod, globular vessel’ (fem.). Related to *būl(j)ōn. Torp-Falk 267 (to Lat follis ‘purse, bag’); Holthausen AEEW 30; Martinet Gém. 194; Pokorny I 121 (to IE *bhel- ‘to swell, to blow); Vries ANEW 49; Onions 111.
Icel-Norw bulla ‘to babble’, OHG bullōn ‘to howl, to bark’. Related to *bellanan II. Martinet Gém. 142; Pokorny I 123.
ON bola ‘to fell trees’, OHG bolōn ‘to roll’. Derived from *bulaz ~ *bulō. Vries ANEW 48.

ON boli ‘bull’, OE bula ‘young bull’, MDu bul, bolle ‘bull’. WGmc words reflect *bull- < *buln- extracted from oblique cases of *bulōn. Perhaps connected with Thrac βόλινθος ‘wild bull’ < *bhul-ento- (Tomaschek Thr. II/1 5). Torp-Falk 266; Detschew Thr. 74; Pokorny I 121 (to IE *bhel- ‘to swell’); Georgiev Issledov. 119; Vries ANEW 49; Lerchner 55–56; Kluge-Seebold 144.

ON bólstr ‘cushion’, OE bolster ‘bolster, pillow’, MDu bolster id., OHG bolstar id. Probably from *bulxstraz, to *belzanan. Torp-Falk 268; Holthausen AEEW 30; Vries ANEW 49; Onions 105; Seebold 100; Bammesberger Nom. 87; Kluge-Seebold 639–640.

ON boltr ‘bolt’, OE bolt id., MLG bolte id., OHG bolz ‘catapult’. Probably related to Lat fullō ‘to trample’, Lith béldžiu, bélsti ‘to knock, to rap’, bīldu, bildėti ‘to crash, to thud’. Brüch ZDADL LXXIII 75–86; Torp-Falk 268; Walde- Hofmann I 560–561; Holthausen AEEW 30; Pokorny I 124; Fraenkel 39; Vries ANEW 49; Onions 105; Kluge- Seebold 124.

ON bunga ‘elevation, convexity’, MLG bunge ‘drum’, OHG bungo ‘tuber’. Related to Skt bahú- ‘much, abundant, large’, Gk παχύς ‘thick’, Latv bīezs id., Lith búožë ‘knob’. Zupitza Gutt. 177; Mann Language XXVI 387 (to Alb bung ‘a kind of oak’); Pokorny I 128; Fraenkel 64; Vries ANEW 65.
OSwed bunka ‘to strike, to hit’, MLG bunken id., G bunken id. Denominative of *bunkōn. Pokorny I 115 (to IE *bheg- ‘to break, to strike’); Frisk II 484–485.

ON bunki ‘heap, pile’, Du bonk ‘clump, clot’. To be compared with Alb bung ‘a kind of oak, Quercus sessiflora’ < PAlb *bunga, further to *bheu- ‘to grow’ (see *bewwanan). Torp-Falk 259 (to *bankiz); Vries ANEW 65 (to Icel buna ‘ox bone’); Lerchner 41–44; Orel AED 41–42.
Goth ga-baur ‘tribute, contribution, tax, λογεία, φόρος’, OHG gi-bor ‘progeny, offspring’. Derived from *beranan. Kluge KZ XXVI 71; Feist 174; Seebold 105; Bammesberger Nom. 58.
ON borr ‘borer’, OE bór id., MLG bor id. Connected with *burōjanan. Holthausen AEEW 30; Vries ANEW 51.
ON borä-þak ‘covering of planks’, OE bord-äaca ‘board thatch, a cover of a house, snare’. Compound of *burđan and *þakan. Carr Nom. comp. 80 (parallel formations).

Goth fotu-baurd ‘footstool’, ON borä ‘board, plank’, OE bord id., OFris bord id., OS bord id., OHG bort ‘deck’. A secondary ablaut variant is *barđan > ON barä ‘edge, brim’ sometimes explained from *barđaz (Torp-Falk 262; Zalizniak II 166) or compared with Lith bartā ‘board’ (Vries ANEW 26). Together with Umbr furf-ant ‘(they) put upon the board’ and Slav *bьrdo ‘reed (in loom), mountain ridge’ continues IE *bhṛdho- (Diefenbach KZ XVI 221). Uhlenbeck Afsl Ph XV 483 (Slav *bьrdo from Germanic); Brückner Afsl Ph XLII 127 (against Uhlenbeck); Torp-Falk 262–263; Holthausen AEEW 30; Feist 159; Trier Holz 86–87; Pokorny I 138; Vries ANEW 50 (separates *burđan ‘board’ from *burđan ‘edge, board of a ship’); Zalizniak I 139; Onions 103; Trubachev Rem. 26–31, 131, ESSJa III 164–166; Anttila Schw. 91; Lehmann GED 121; Kluge-Seebold 126.
ON boräi ‘border’, OE borda ‘hem, trimming’, OS borda id., OHG borto id. A morphological variant of *burđan. Holthausen AEEW 30; Vries ANEW 50.
ON byräingr ‘merchant ship’, MLG bordinc ‘small vehicle’. Derived from *burđan (Falk Wu S IV 111–112). Vries ANEW 67.

Goth ga-baurþs ‘birth’, ON masc. burär id., OE ze-byrd id., OFris berd, berde id., OS gi-burd id. (masc.), OHG giburt id. Continues IE *bhṛtí-, cf. OIr breth, brith id. < *bhṛtā. Further related to *beranan. Torp-Falk 261; Holthausen AEEW 40; Feist 175; Pokorny I 131; Zalizniak II 176; Vries ANEW 68; Onions 96; Seebold 105; Trubachev ESSJa III 97–98; Lehmann GED 134; Bammesberger Nom. 141; Kluge- Seebold 304.
ON byräa ‘to embroider’, OE byrdan id., OS burdian id. Derived from *burđan. Holthausen AEEW 40; Vries ANEW 67.

ON borga ‘to bail, to pay’, OE borzian ‘to borrow’, OFris borgia id., MLG borgen id., OHG borgēn id., ir-borgōn ‘to promise’. Derived from *berzanan. Torp-Falk 265; Holthausen AEEW 31; Vries ANEW 50; Onions 108; Kluge-Seebold 126.
ON byrgi ‘enclosure, fence’, OE eorä-byrz ‘earth mound’ (fem.). Derived from *burzz. Holthausen AEEW 41; Vries ANEW 68.
ON byrgja ‘to close, to shut’, OE byrzan ‘to bury’. Cf. also OHG borgēn id. Derived from *berzanan. Holthausen AEEW 41; Vries ANEW 68; Seebold 107.
ON á-byrgja ‘to answer for, to take care of’, OE on-byrzan ‘to vouch for’, MLG börgen id., OHG unter-burgen ‘to fake’. Related to *burzēnan ~ *burzōjanan. Holthausen AEEW 41; Vries ANEW 68.

Goth baurgs ‘castle, citadel, city’, ON borg ‘hillock, wall, castle, city’ (istem), OE burz ‘fortified place, castle, city’, OFris burch, burich id., OS burg id. (also an i-stem), OHG burg id. (also an i-stem). Corresponds to Av b 6 r 6 z- ‘high’, Arm barjr id. < *bhṛ·hu-, OIr brí ‘hill’. Other parallels see s.v. *berzanan. Bréal MSL VII 135–136 (to Gk πύργος ‘tower, fortification, castle’); Much ZDADL XLI 113, Wu S XII 353 (partly from Lat burgus ‘castle, fort, fortress’ < Gk πύργος); Zupitza Gutt. 204; Jacobsohn KZ XLVIII 139–140 (to Gk φύρκος: τεῖχος, Hes.); Kretschmer Glotta XXII 100–102 (from the same North Balkan source as Gk πύργος); Gutenbrunner ZDADL LXXII 173–175; Bartholomae 949–950; Torp- Falk 265; Holthausen AEEW 38; Feist 85–86; Porzig Gliederung 173; Pokorny I 140–141; Vries ANEW 50 (to Gk φράσσω ‘to fence in, to fortify’, φύρκος ‘wall’); Zalizniak II 206; Onions 108; Motz IF LXXXI 204–220; Lehmann GED 64–65 (influenced by Lat burgus); Bammesberger Nom. 195–196; Griepentrog Wurzelnomina 91–116; Kluge- Seebold 145–146.
ON byrli ‘cup-bearer’, OE byrele id. Derived from *burilaz, see *berilaz. Holthausen AEEW 41; Vries ANEW 68.

Goth baur ‘he who is born, γενητός’, ON burr ‘son’, OE byre id. Derived from *beranan. Torp-Falk 261; Holthausen AEEW 40; Feist 84–85; Vries ANEW 65; Pokorny I 131; Seebold 105; Lehmann GED 64; Bammesberger Nom. 132.

ON byrr ‘fair wind’, OE byre ‘event, opportunity’, WFris bur ‘wind’, MLG bore-lōs ‘without fair wind’. Related to Slav *bur’a ‘storm’ (Uhlenbeck PBB XXX 278; Lewy IF XXXII 158–159). Torp-Falk 261; Holthausen AEEW 40; Pokorny I 131; Vries ANEW 68; Trubachev ESSJa III 97–98.

ON byrja ‘to begin’, OE bÿrian ‘to happen’, OFris bera id., OS gi-burian ‘to take care of, to befit’, OHG purren ‘to happen, to be due’. Derived from *buriz II. Torp- Falk 261; Holthausen AEEW 41; Trier Holz 88; Pokorny I 131 (to *beranan); Vries ANEW 68; Kluge-Seebold 304.

Goth adv. ga-baurjaba ‘gladly, with pleasure’, OE am-byr ‘favourable’. Related to *buriz II. Feist 175; Holthausen AEEW 3, 5; Matzel KZ CIV 240–250, CV 135; Bammesberger ESts LXIV 97–101 (reconstructs *buruz from

ON bora ‘to bore’, OE bórian id., OS borōn id., OHG borōn id. Related to *barjanan. Torp-Falk 261; Endzelin KZ LII 124 (to Latv bīrze ‘furrow in the field of corn’); Holthausen AEEW 31; Pokorny I 135; Vries ANEW 49–50; Onions 108; Kluge-Seebold 124.
ON bora ‘bore-hole’, late OHG bora ‘auger’. Related to *burōjanan. Onions 108.

ON burst ‘bristle’, OE byrst id. (neut.), OS bursta id. (ō-stem), OHG burst id. (neut.), bursta id. (ō-stem). Identical with Skt bhṛṣṭí- ‘sharp point’. Cf. also Slav *bъrš‘ь ‘kind of plant, Heracleum’ (Petr BB XXI 211). Torp-Falk 266; Holthausen AEEW 41; Pokorny I 109; Vries ANEW 65; Zalizniak II 195; Onions 119; Trubachev ESSJa III 131–132; Kluge-Seebold 127.

Goth baurþei ‘burden, load’, OFris berde id., OHG burdī id. Based on *bhṛti- attested in Skt bhṛtí- ‘load, burden’, cf. also bhṛtyā ‘care, food’. For further connections see *beranan. Torp-Falk 261; Feist 86; Pokorny I 131; Vries ANEW 67–68; Zalizniak II 220; Onions 127; Lehmann GED 65; Kluge-Seebold 145.

Norw busk ‘bush’, ME busch, busche id., MLG busch id., OHG busc id. Derived from *bōw(w)anan. Torp-Falk 276–277; Vries ANEW 66 (from late Lat boscus ‘a forest where wood gathering is permitted’); Kluge-Seebold 147 (to *bruskaz).
ON bútr ‘log’, LG adj. butt ‘blunt, plump’, MHG butze ‘cut out piece’. Probably related to *bautanan. Torp-Falk 274; Vries ANEW 66.

ON ból ‘farm, abode’, OE bold ‘building, dwelling, house’, OFris bodel ‘movable property’, OS bodal ‘land property’. Derived from *bōw(w)anan. Structurally similar to Lith būklā ‘residence, camp’ < *bhū-tlo-. Torp-Falk 272–273; Holthausen AEEW 30; Fraenkel 68; Vries ANEW 48; Onions 124; Seebold 126; Kluge-Seebold 143 (ON to MLG boule ‘sleeping place’, MHG buole id. < *bōlōn further compared with Lith guõlis ‘resting-place’).

ON búkr ‘trunk, body’, OE būc ‘belly, stomach’, OFris būk ‘trunk, body’, MLG būk id., OHG būh ‘stomach, womb’. A descriptive formation. Osthoff BB XXIX 255 (to OE búc ‘bottle, jug’); Torp-Falk 273 (to *beuzanan); Holthausen AWN 29 (to Latv baugas ‘intestines’); Vries ANEW 64 (same as Torp-Falk); Pokorny I 100; Zalizniak I 130; Onions 123, 125; Kluge-Seebold 85.

ON bóla ‘blister’, OE bÿl (masc.), bÿle ‘bile, blotch, sore’, OFris bēl, bēle id., OS būla id., OHG bulla ‘blister, pimple, swelling’. Cf. Skt buli- ‘buttocks, vulva’, buri- ‘vulva’ (reflecting unaspirated *b), Lith bulīs id. Osthoff Suppl. 66–67 (to IE *bheu- ‘to grow); Zupitza Gutt. 72; Persson Beiträge 30; Torp-Falk 274–276; Holthausen AEEW 39; Feist 511; Mayrhofer II 439–440; Pokorny I 98–102; Vries ANEW 48; Fraenkel 63–64; Onions 104; Lehmann GED 371; Kluge- Seebold 105, 143.

ON búr ‘chamber, pantry’, OE búr ‘cottage, dwelling, room’, OS būr ‘dwelling, room’ (masc.), OHG būr ‘house’ (masc.). Close to Messap βύριον: oǫkhma, Hes. (Krahe IF XLVII 326, Spr. Vorz. 104). Further to *bōw(w)anan. Torp-Falk 273; Holthausen AEEW 38; Porzig Gliederung 129; Pokorny I 146, 149; Vries ANEW 65; Zalizniak II 167; Onions 111; Seebold 126; Lehmann GED 64; Bammesberger Nom. 75; Kluge- Seebold 86.

Goth ana-busns ‘command, commandment’, ON bÿsn ‘wonder’, OE bÿsen ‘example, pattern’, OS am-būsan ‘order’. Derivative in *-sni- based on *beuđanan. Brugmann Grundriß I 705 (compensatory length in *-ū- < *-us-); Torp-Falk 275; Holthausen AEEW 41; Feist 41–42; Vries ANEW 68; Seebold 109; Lehmann GED 31; Bammesberger Nom. 148.
Norw būsa ‘to dart, to make a dash’, EFris būsen ‘to be turbulent, to storm’, MDu būsen ‘to tipple’. Related to Slav *bystrъ ‘quick’. Torp- Falk 276; Vries ANEW 68; Trubachev ESSJa III 153–154.

ON búä ‘abode, booth’, MLG bōde ‘hut’ (*-ō-), MHG buode id. (-ō-). Derived from *bōw(w)anan. Structurally close to OIr both ‘hut’ < *bhutā, Lith būtas ‘house’, OPrus buttan id., Slav *byto ‘stay, possessions’. Torp- Falk 272; Trautmann BSW 40–42; Fraenkel 68; Vries ANEW 63; Trubachev ESSJa III 155; Lehmann GED 64; Kluge-Seebold 142.
ON bú-land ‘homeland’, OS bū-land ‘cultivated land’. Compound of *būwan and *lanđan. Carr Nom. comp. 83 (parallel formations).
ON bú ‘house, estate’, OE bú ‘dwelling’, OS bū id. Derived from *bōw(w)anan. Torp-Falk 272; Holthausen AEEW 38; Pokorny I 149; Vries ANEW 63, 67; Seebold 125; Lehmann GED 64.
ON bÿr ‘town, village, farm’. Identical with Lith b~vis ‘residence’. Further derived from *bōw(w)anan. Torp-Falk 272; Chemodanov SGGJa I 73; Fraenkel 68.
ON búi ‘dweller’, OHG erd-būwo ‘inhabitant of the earth’. Derived from *bōw(w)anan. Vries ANEW 63. ## đ

Goth ga-daban ‘to happen, to be suitable’, OE part. ze-dafen ‘becoming, fit, suitable’. Related to Arm darbin ‘smith’ (< *dhabhro-), Lat faber ‘forger, smith, artificer, carpenter, joiner’, Lith dabā ‘nature, way’ (if not from Slavic), dabaı ‘now’, Slav *doba ‘time’, *dobiti ‘to form, to decorate’. Torp- Falk 200–201; Holthausen AEEW 69; Walde-Hofmann I 436–437; Feist 176–177; Pokorny I 233–234; Fraenkel 79; Seebold 146; Trubachev ESSJa V 38–39; Lehmann GED 134–135; Kluge- Seebold 166.

Norw dial. dabba ‘to tap’, E dab ‘to tap lightly’, EFris dafen ‘to strike’, MDu dabben ‘to patter’, G tappen id. An imitative verb. However, a connection with *đabanan cannot be ruled out—in this case cf. Slav *dobiti ‘to form, to decorate’. Vries ANEW 71 (to *đabjaz); Trubachev ESSJa V 38–39.

Norw dial. dab, dabbe ‘small fat person’, MHG tappe ‘clumsy man’. Identical with Slav *dobь ‘valiant, noble’. Loewenthal ANF XXXV 240 (to Skt dabhnóti ‘to hurt, to injure’); Persson IF XXXV 202 (to Latv depis ‘fool’); Pokorny I 233 (to Gk τάφος ‘funeral rites; raised platform’); Holthausen AWN 33 (to SCr depati ‘to strike’); Vries ANEW 71.
ON dafna ‘to thrive well’, OE ze-dafenian ‘to be becoming, to fit’. Connected with *dabanan. Torp- Falk 200; Holthausen AEEW 69; Vries ANEW 71; Lehmann GED 134–135.
Norw dial. dadra ‘to shake, to tremble’, G dial. tattern id. Of imitative origin. Torp-Falk 202.
ON dag-langr ‘all day long’, OE dæz-lanz ‘lasting a day’, MHG tage-lanc id. Compound of *đazaz and *lanzaz. Carr Nom. comp. 88 (parallel formations).
ON dag-verk ‘day’s work’, OE dæz-weorc id., OFris dei-werch id., OS dag-werk id., OHG taga-werk id. Compound of *đazaz and *werkan. Carr Nom. comp. 52.

Goth dags ‘day’, ON dagr id., OE dæz id., OFris dei id., OS dag id., OHG tag id. Identical with Skt ni-dāghá- ‘heat, summer’, MIr daig ‘fire’, Lith dãgas ‘summer heat’. Continues *dhog ⁄hos, derivative of IE *dheg ⁄h- ‘to burn’. Schulze Kl. Schr. 833, 844–847; Zupitza Gutt. 101, 205; Torp-Falk 199; Holthausen AEEW 69; Feist 113–114; Walde- Hofmann I 466–467 (contamination with IE *¡·h-r- ‘day’); Lane JEGP XXXV 25 (phonetically difficult *-g ⁄h- > *-z-); Mayrhofer II 39; Pokorny I 7 (related to Skt áhar- ‘day’ with secondary *đ-), 240–242; Fraenkel 85–86; Vries ANEW 71–72; Zalizniak I 130; Seebold KZ LXXXI 125; Onions 246; Lehmann GED 86; Bammesberger Nom. 51; Kluge- Seebold 812.
ON daga ‘to dawn’, OE dazian id., MLG dagen id., OHG tagēn id. Derived from *đazaz. Torp- Falk 199; Holthausen AEEW 70; Vries ANEW 71.

Goth daigs ‘dough, soft mass’, ON deigr id., OE neut. dáz id., OFris deeg id., MLG dēch id., OHG teig id. Continues *dhoi·hos derived from IE *dhei·h- ‘to knead’. Close to Toch A tseke ‘molded figure’, Skt deha- ‘body’, Av uzdaēza- ‘wall’, Arm dez ‘heap’, Gk τοῖχος ‘wall’, Slav *δîἤα ‘kneading trough, vessel’ < *δοιἵηωā. In the Gmc perspective, substantivated *đaizaz II. See *đīzanan. Bartholomae 411–412; Torp-Falk 206; Holthausen AEEW 69–70; Feist 114; Mayrhofer II 65–66; Pokorny I 244–245; Vries ANEW 74–75; Zalizniak I 130; Frisk II 865–866; Onions 286; Seebold 151; Trubachev ESSJa V 23– 24; Lehmann GED 87; Bammesberger Nom. 52; Heidermanns 145; Kluge- Seebold 819.

ON deigr ‘soft’, MLG dēch ‘pasty’, MHG teic ‘soft’. Derived from *đīzanan. Karsten NB I 58–59; Torp- Falk 206; Vries ANEW 75; Seebold 151; Matzel Festschr. Schröder 94, KZ CV 96; Heidermanns 145; Kluge-Seebold 819–820.

ON deigja ‘dairy-maid’, OE dæze ‘female baker’. Derived from *đīzanan or directly from *đaizaz I. Formally close to Slav *dîža ‘kneading trough, vessel’ < *doi·hƒā. Sievers PBB L 16; Holthausen AEEW 69; Vries ANEW 75; Onions 242; Seebold 151; Trubachev ESSJa V 23–24; Bammesberger Nom. 180–181.
ON deild ‘deal, share’, OHG teilida ‘division’. Derived from *đailjanan. Torp-Falk 198; Vries ANEW 75.

Goth fem. dails ‘part, portion, share’, late ON deill ‘deal, share’, OE dæl ‘part’, OFris dēl id., OS dēl id., OHG teil id. Identical with Slav *dîlь ‘part, division’. Further related to *dāi-: *dī- ‘to divide’ with an irregular development of the anlaut. Boppapud Schweizer KZ I 478 (to Skt dṛṇāti ‘to burst’); Wood MLN XXI 39 (to Gk δήλεομαι ‘to hurt, to damage’); Pedersen KZ XXXIX 372 (from Slav *dîlъ ‘portion’); Torp- Falk 198; Holthausen AEEW 69; Feist 114; Pokorny I 176, 195; Chemodanov SGGJa I 84; Zalizniak II 185; Onions 247; Martynov Jazyk 17–18 (follows Pedersen); Trubachev ESSJa V 9–10; Lehmann GED 87; Kluge-Seebold 820; Boutkan OFED s.v.

Goth ga-dailjan ‘to divide, to allot’, ON deila ‘to divide, to deal’, OE dælan ‘to divide, to separate’, OFris dēla id., OS dēlian id., OHG teilen id. Derived from *đailiz. Structurally close to Lith dailÿti ‘to divide’, Slav *dîliti id. Būga RFV LXX 103; Berneker I 195; Torp-Falk 198; Holthausen AEEW 69; Feist 114; Pokorny I 176; Fraenkel 80; Vries ANEW 75; Martynov Slav.-germ. 112 (Gmc < Slav); Benveniste IEL 156; Trubachev ESSJa IV 233–234; Lehmann GED 87.
ON deila ‘disagreement, contest’, MHG teile ‘division’. Related to *đailiz. Vries ANEW 75.

Goth daddjan ‘to suckle’, OSw dæggia id. Structurally identical with Skt dháyati ‘to suck, to drink’, Osset dæjūn ‘to suck’, Slav *dojǫ, *dojiti ‘to suckle, to milk’. Further related to *đējanan, *đīōjanan. Torp-Falk 205; Feist 112–113; Mayrhofer II 93; Pokorny I 241–242; Vries ANEW 76; Lindeman Verschärfung 10.4; Trubachev ESSJa V 53–54; Lehmann GED 85.

Goth dal(s) ‘ravine, pit, valley’, ON dalr ‘dale’, OE dæl ‘dale, den, gulf ’, OFris del ‘valley’, OS dal id., OHG tal. Identical with W dol ‘valley’, Slav *dolъ id. (Hellquist ANF VII 171; Meillet Etudes 242–243). Cf. probably Gk θόλος ‘round building with a conical roof ’. Schweizer KZ I 478; Sperber Wu S VI 40; Loth RC XLII 86; Torp-Falk 204; Holthausen AEEW 69; Feist 114–115; Huisman KZ LXXI 103; Pokorny I 245; Vries ANEW 72; Frisk I 677; Zalizniak II 186; Onions 242; Trubachev ESSJa V 64–65; Bammesberger Nom. 54; Kluge-Seebold 813.
Norw dial. dalga ‘to strike’, LG dalgen id. Related to *đulzan. Torp-Falk 210; Pokorny I 247.

Goth ib-dalja ‘declivity, slope’ (masc. or neut.), OE ofdæle ‘downward slope’, dell ‘deep hollow, valley’, OFris dele id. MLG delle id., late MHG telle id. Derived from *đalaz ~ *đalan. Torp-Falk 204; Holthausen AEEW 71; Feist 114, 287; Onions 253; Lehmann GED 202; Kluge-Seebold 169.

ON dammr ‘dam’, OFris damm, domm id., MLG dam id., MHG tam, tamm id. Continues *dh 6-mno, based on *dhē-men-, cf. Skt dhāman- ‘statute, law’ (Grienberger Got. 66). Schröder ZDADL XLII 66 (to IE *dheb- ‘thick’); Torp-Falk 201–202 (same as Grienberger); van Wijk IF XXIV 31–32 (from *đambna-, to Gk θάπτω ‘to bury’, Arm damban ‘grave’); Wood MP XVIII 84 (from *dh 6 m-⁄o-, cf. Gk θαμύς ‘thick’); Güntert Labyrinth 30 (pre-IE term); Walde-Pokorny I 850 (follow Schröder); Pokorny I 238; Vries ANEW 72–73; Onions 242; Lehmann GED 111; Kluge-Seebold 161 (back formation from *đammjanan).

Goth faur-dammjan ‘to dam up, to hinder’, OE for-demman ‘to dam up’, OFris damma ‘to fence in’, MHG ver-temmen ‘to fence in’. Derived from *đammaz ~ *đamman. Torp-Falk 201; Holthausen AEEW 71; Feist 146; Vries ANEW 75; Onions 242; Lehmann GED 111; Kluge-Seebold 161.

MLG damp ‘steam’, OHG dampf id. Based on str. vb. *đempanan > MHG dimpfen ‘to steam, to smoke’ which can be related to Lith dumpiū, dūmpti ‘to blow’. Pokorny I 247–248 (to Skt dhámati ‘to blow’, MIr dem ‘black, dark’); Onions 243.

Goth prop. Danus, ON pl. Danir ‘Dane’, OE pl. Dene id., OHG pl. Teni id. Of uncertain origin. Zachrisson Festschr. Kock 404–408; Lindquist NB 106–110 (to *đentanan); Holthausen AEEW 71; Vries ANEW 73.

ON dengja ‘to hammer’, OE denczan ‘to knock, to ding’, MHG tengen ‘to strike’. Causative of *đenzwanan. Identical with Slav *dǫžiti ‘to rush’, cf. further Lith dangīnti ‘to move, to resettle’. Torp-Falk 200; Vries ANEW 75; Trubachev ESSJa V 100; Kluge- Seebold 170.
ON Dan-mǫrk ‘Denmark’, OE Dena-mearc id., OHG Denemarca id. Compound of *đaniz and *markō. Vries ANEW 73.
ON dǫkk ‘pit, pool’. Could be formally identical with Latv danga ‘vault, angle’, Slav *dǫga ‘arc’ but the latter seem to reflect *-gh-. Torp-Falk 200; Vries ANEW 93; Trubachev ESSJa V 98–99.

ON dǫkkr ‘dark’, OFris diunk id. Identical with Hitt dankui- ‘black, dark’ (Kammenhuber KZ LXXVII 62). Torp-Falk 201; Schwentner PBB XLV 457; Karsten NB II 429–431; Pokorny I 247–248 (to MIr dem ‘black’); Vries ANEW 92; Heidermanns 146, 152, KZ XCIX 297; Kluge-Seebold 199.
OS danna ‘pine’, OHG tanna ‘fir wood’. If related to Gk θάμνος ‘thicket’ (Fick I 464), *đamnōn should be reconstructed. Torp-Falk 200 (to Skt neut. dhanva- ‘bow’); Frisk I 652.

ON dapr ‘sad, downcast’, MLG dapper ‘heavy’, OHG tapfar id. Compared with Toch A tpär, B tapre ‘high’ (Wiedemann BB XXX 216: also to Slav *dobrъ ‘good’, *debelъ ‘strong, thick’). Wood MLN XXI 227 (to Skt dabhrá- ‘little, small’); Torp-Falk 200; Karsten NB II 257; Pokorny I 239; Chemodanov SGGJa I 111; Vries ANEW 73; Onions 244; Trubachev ESSJa V 45–46; Heidermanns 146–147; Kluge-Seebold 815; Adams TB 280 (compares Toch with *đeupaz); Burlak IFTJa 246 (same as Adams).
Burg *dards ‘spear’, ON darraär ‘dart’, OE daroä id., OHG tart id. Derived from *đarran. Holthausen AEEW 70; Vries ANEW 74; Onions 244; Köbler 709.
ON darr ‘dart’. Borrowed from Iran *dāru-: Av dāuru- ‘trunk, wooden weapon’. Martinet Gém. 199; Vries ANEW 74 (to Skt dhārā ‘edge, blade’).

Goth ga-dars ‘to venture, to dare’, OE dear ‘to dare’, OFris dūr id., OS dar, darr id., OHG -tar id. Related to Skt dhṛṣṇú- ‘bold, courageous’, Av darši- ‘bold, strong’, Gk θρασύς ‘bold, rash’, θαρσέω ‘to be courageous’, Lith dr Asūs ‘brave’ and probably Slav *dьrzъ ‘daring, audacious’ (with unexplained *-z-). Bartholomae 699; Torp-Falk 203; Meillet IF V 333; Feist 177–178; Holthausen AEEW 80; Mayrhofer II 112–113; Pokorny I 259; Fraenkel 101–102; Frisk I 654–655; Onions 244; Seebold 147; Trubachev ESSJa V 228–229; Lehmann GED 135.

ON dasi ‘lazy fellow’, MDu daes ‘fool’. Related to Lat fēriae, OLat fēsiae ‘days of rest, holidays’, fēstus ‘of the holidays, festive, solemn’, Umbr fesnaf-e ‘in fanum’ < *dhēs-. Walde-Hofmann I 481, 489; Pokorny I 259; Vries ANEW 74 (to MLG dasen ‘to mock’).

Goth daufs ‘dull, numb, deaf, πεπωρωμένος’, ON daufr ‘deaf’, OE deáf id., OFris dāf id., MLG dōf id., OHG toub id. Probably related to Gk τυφλός ‘blind’, τῦφος ‘smoke, conceit’ (Persson UUÅ 1891 55). On the other hand, cf. Slav *dubъ ‘upright, straight’. Karsten NB I 62; Torp-Falk 209–210; Lewy KZ XL 420 (to Latv stubs ‘stump’); Feist 115–116; Holthausen AEEW 70; Wissmann NP 88; Jóhannesson IEW 503–506; Pokorny I 264; Vries ANEW 74; Frisk II 949–950; Chantraine 1147–1148; Onions 246; Seebold 155; Trubachev ESSJa V 145; Lehmann GED 88; Bammesberger Nom. 237; Heidermanns 148; Kluge-Seebold 817.
Goth daubiþa ‘dullness, numbness, stubborness’, ON deyfä ‘deafness’. Derived from *đaubjanan I. Feist 115; Vries ANEW 76; Heidermanns 148.
Goth ga-daubjan ‘to make insensitive, to be numb, to harden’, ON deyfa ‘to make blunt’, OHG gi-touben ‘to weaken’. Derived from *đaubaz. Feist 115; Vries ANEW 76; Lehmann GED 88; Heidermanns 148.
ON deyfa ‘to dive’, OE á-diefan id., OS bi-dōbian id. Derived from *đūbanan. Holthausen AEEW 73; Vries ANEW 76.

Goth dauþs ‘dead’ (-þunder the influence of *đauþaz ~ *đauþuz), ON dauär id., OE deád id., OFris dād id., OS dōd id., OHG tōt id. Derived from *đewanan. Feist 118; Torp-Falk 199; Holthausen AEEW 70; Karsten NB II 333–334; Høst NTS XV 410–411; Pokorny I 260; Vries ANEW 74; Onions 246; Seebold 148; Lehmann GED 89–90; Bammesberger Nom. 251; Heidermanns 149; Kluge-Seebold 829; Boutkan OFED s.v.
Goth dauþjan ‘to kill’, ON deyäa id., OS bi-dōdian id., OHG pret. gi-dōtta id. Derived from *đauđaz. Feist 118; Vries ANEW 76; Lehmann GED 90; Heidermanns 149.

Goth daug ‘to be good, to be suitable’, OE deáh ‘to be of use, to be good’, OFris dāch, dāech ‘to be suitable for’, OS dōg id., OHG toug id. Related to Gk τεύχω ‘to produce, to make, to build’, τυγχάνω ‘to meet, to attain a goal’, Lith da Ug ‘much, many’, Slav *dugъ ‘strength, power’, *δυἤь ‘strong, large’ (Wood MP XI 333). Zupitza Gutt. 178; Grienberger Got. 54–55; Torp-Falk 207; Holthausen AEEW 79; Feist 127–128; Johansson KZ XXX 422 50; Jóhannesson IEW 508; Pokorny I 271; Fraenkel 84, IF LIX 161; Vries ANEW 86; Frisk II 888; Onions 286; Trubachev ESSJa V 150, 167–168; Seebold 149– 151; Lehmann GED 96–97; Kluge- Seebold 818.

Goth dauns ‘odor, smell’, ON daunn id. Related to IE *dheu- ‘to fly about, to whirl’: Skt dhūnóti ‘to shake, to agitate’, Gk θύω ‘to sacrifice’. Schweizer KZ I 159; Torp-Falk 206–207; Feist 116–117; Mayrhofer II 107–108; Pokorny 259–260; Vries ANEW 74; Frisk I 698–699; Lehmann GED 88–89.

Goth daupjan ‘to immerse, to baptize’, ON deypa ‘to dip’, OFris dēpa ‘to immerse, to baptize’, OS dōpian id., OHG toufen id. Derived from *đeupaz. Kluge Btrg. Gesch. XXXV 131–132 (Gothic influence on WGmc meanings); Torp-Falk 209; Feist 117; Vries ANEW 76; Lehmann GED 89, 92; Heidermanns 154; Kluge-Seebold 817–818.
ON pl. dauäa-bǫnd ‘death bond’, MHG tōt-bant id. (hap. leg.). Compound of *đauþaz ~ *đauþuz and *banđan. Carr Nom. comp. 83 (OHG is a calque of Lat uincula mortis).
ON dauäa-dagr ‘death day’, OE deáä-dæz id. Compound of *đauþaz ~ *đauþuz and *đazaz. Carr Nom. comp. 80 (parallel formations).

Goth dauþus ‘death’, ON dauär id., dauäi id. (n-stem), OE déaä id., OFris dāth id., OS dōth id., OHG tōd id. Substantivized *đauđaz. Torp-Falk 199; Holthausen AEEW 71; Feist 118; Pokorny I 260; Vries ANEW 74; Zalizniak II 199; Lehmann GED 90; Bammesberger Nom. 159; Kluge- Seebold 826; Boutkan OFED s.v. *đawjanan str./wk.vb.: ON deyja ‘to die’, OS dōian id., OHG douwen ‘to set, to go down’. Derived from *đewanan. Torp-Falk 198–199; Jóhannesson IEW 502–503; Pokorny I 260–261; Vries ANEW 76; Onions 266; Seebold 147; Kluge-Seebold 826.
ON dǫggva ‘to bedew’, OFris dawa ‘to melt’, MHG touwen id. Derived from *đawwō ~ *đawwan. Torp-Falk 204; Vries ANEW 92–93.

ON dǫgg ‘dew’, OE masc. deáw id., OFris dāw id., OS masc. mili-dou ‘mildew’, OHG tou ‘dew’. Connected with Toch A twe, B tweye ‘dust’, Lat suf-fiō ‘to fumigate, to perfume, to scent’ < *-dh⁄iƒō. Torp- Falk 204; Holthausen AEEW 71; Walde-Hofmann II 625; Pokorny I 260–261; Vries ANEW 92; Zalizniak II 173; Onions 263; Lehmann GED 89; Kluge-Seebold 817; Boutkan OFED s.v.

Sw dial. del ‘teat’, OE delu id., OHG tila. Identical with Alb djalë ‘boy, youth’, cf. also Latv d=ls ‘son’, Lat fīlius id. with a long vowel. Derivative of IE *dhēi- ‘to suck’, see *đajjanan. Torp-Falk 205; Holthausen AEEW 71; Walde-Hofmann I 496–497; Pokorny I 242; Dybo VSJa 25 (pretonic shortening of *ē in Gmc); Lehmann GED 85; Schrijver LL 352 (follows Dybo); Orel AED 67.

OE delfan ‘to dig’, OFris delva id., OS bi-delban id., OHG bitelpan id. Related to Slav *dьlbti ‘to gouge, to thump’, rare *delbti id. Torp-Falk 204; Holthausen AEEW 71; Pokorny I 246; Chemodanov SGGJa I 82; Onions 254; Stang LS 17; Seebold 153; Trubachev ESSJa IV 205, V 206.
ON steindelfr ‘wagtail, Motacilla’, MLG delf ‘ditch’. Derived from *đelbanan. Cf. a structurally close Slav *dьlbъ ‘chisel, pit’. Vries ANEW 75; Trubachev ESSJa V 206– 207.
Swed dill ‘dill’, OE dile id., OS dilli id., OHG tilli id. Related to OIr deil ‘rod’ < *dheli-. Torp-Falk 203; Trier Holz 56; Kluge-Seebold 181 (to OHG toldo ‘foliage’).

ON dimmr ‘dark, dim’, OE dim id., OFris comp. dimme id. Related to OIr dem ‘black, dark’. Torp-Falk 201; Holthausen AEEW 73; Schwentner PBB XLV 454–455; Karsten NB II 220–221; Pokorny I 247–248; Vries ANEW 77; Onions 268; Heidermanns 151–152.
ON dimma ‘to become dim’, OE part. á-dimmad ‘to dim, to darken, to obscure’. Derived from *đemmaz. Vries ANEW 77; Heidermanns 151.
OSwed diunga ‘to knock, to rap’, ME dinzen, denzen ‘to beat’. See *đanzwjanan. Torp-Falk 200; Vries ANEW 75; Trubachev ESSJa V 100; Kluge-Seebold 170.

OHG tenar ‘flat hand, palm’. Related to Hitt dannar-eššar ‘armpit’, Gk θέναρ ‘palm, footsole’. Thematization of the original *dhenṛ (Lühr Wortschatz 68). No connection with WGmc *đenjan > OE denn ‘den’, MLG fem. denne ‘low ground’, OHG tenni ‘threshing-floor’ (despite Kluge-Seebold 821) which is to be compared with Skt dhánuß- ‘dry land’, dhánu- ‘sandbank, shore’. Wood MLN XXI 41–42 (*đenjan to Skt pl. dhānā ‘grain’); Torp-Falk 199; Holthausen AEEW 72; Pokorny I 249; Frisk I 661–662; Onions 255–256; Bammesberger Nom. 74; Lühr Wortschatz 67–70; Kluge-Seebold 821.

ON detta ‘to fall, to drop’. Related to Alb gdhend ‘to plane’ < PAlb *ka-denta (Jokl Studien 21–22). Fick I 463 (to Lat de-fendō ‘to fend, to repel, to avert’); Torp-Falk 199–200; Jóhannesson IEW 512–514; Pokorny I 250; Vries ANEW 76; Seebold 154; Bammesberger Nom. 131; Orel AED 112.

OE deorfan ‘to labor’, OFris far-derva id., MLG vor-derven ‘to step down’, MHG ver-derben ‘to die, to pass away’. Related to Lith dīrbu, dīrbti ‘to work’, dárbas ‘work’. Torp-Falk 567; Holthausen AEEW 72; Pokorny I 257; Chemodanov SGGJa I 71; Vries ANEW 77–78; Fraenkel 82; Seebold 154–155.

ON djarfr ‘bold, daring’, OE deorf ‘valiant’, OFris derve ‘daring, audacious’, OS derbi ‘hostile, evil’. Derived from *đerbanan. Torp Festschr. Unger 176–179 (to Gk τρέφω ‘to feed’); Torp- Falk 202; Holthausen AEEW 70; Karsten NB I 32; Walde-Pokorny I 863 (to Lith dárbas ‘work’); Pokorny I 257–258; Vries ANEW 77–78; Seebold 154; Heidermanns 152–153.

OE deorc ‘dark, obscure’. Identical with OIr derg ‘red’ < *dhergoand further connected with Lith dérgiu, dérgti ‘to become dirty’. Cf. also Toch A tärkär, B tarkär ‘cloud’ < *dhṛg-ru-. Zupitza Gutt. 160; Pedersen BB XIX 299–300 (to Lat furuus ‘ark, dusky, gloomy’ < *dhorg ⁄o-); Reichelt KZ XLVI 323 (same as Pedersen); Torp-Falk 202; Schwentner PBB XLV 457–458; Holthausen AEEW 72; Pokorny I 251–252; Fraenkel 103; Heidermanns 153; Adams TB 21.
ON djúp-ligr ‘deep’, OE deóp-líc id. Derived from *deupaz. Heidermanns 154.

Goth diups ‘deep’, ON djúpr id., OE deóp id., OFris comp. diāpe id., OS diop id., OHG tiof, tiufiid. Related to OIr domain id., Lith dubūs id., Latv duôbs id. Zupitza Gutt. 37; Torp-Falk 209; Feist 121; Holthausen AEEW 72; Karsten NB I 29; Pokorny I 267; Fraenkel 108–109; Vries ANEW 78; Onions 250; Lehmann GED 92; Bammesberger Nom. 240; Heidermanns 153– 154; Kluge-Seebold 824; Adams TB 280 (to Toch A tpär, B tapre ‘high’).

Goth diupiþa ‘depth’, ON dÿpt id., ME depthe id., OLG diopitha id., MLG dēpede id., MHG tūfede id. Derived from *đeupaz. Identical with Alb det ‘sea’ < PAlb *deubeta (Jokl Studien 14–15). Torp-Falk 209; Feist 121; Vries ANEW 90; Onions 258; Bammesberger Nom. 117; Heidermanns 154; Orel AED 61–62.
Goth diupei ‘depth’, OS diupi id., OHG tiufī id. Derived from *đeupaz. Torp-Falk 209; Feist 121; Onions 258; Lehmann GED 92; Heidermanns 153.
ON dÿr-ligr ‘glorious’, OS diur-līk ‘valuable, excellent’, OHG tiur-līh id. Derived from *đeurjaz. Heidermanns 154.
ON dÿrä ‘glory, fame’, OS diuritha id., OHG tiurida id. Derived from *đeurjaz. Torp-Falk 199; Vries ANEW 90; Onions 247; Heidermanns 154.

ON dÿrr ‘dear, precious’, OE deóre ‘dear, expensive’, OFris diure id., OS diuri ‘valuable, expensive’, OHG tiuri id. Related to Skt adv. dhurā ‘violently’, Gk é-y-rv ‘to play’. Torp-Falk 199; Holthausen AEEW 73; Kluge 129, 727; Karsten NB II 384–385; Pokorny I 266–267; Vries ANEW 91 (to *dheu- ‘to weave’); Onions 247; Heidermanns 154– 155 (to Slav *durь ‘stupidity, madness’); Kluge-Seebold 822–823 (from *dheg ⁄hro- to *dheg ⁄h- ‘to burn’).

Goth dius ‘beast, animal’, ON dÿr id., OE deór id., OFris diār id., OS meri-dior ‘sea animal’, OHG tior ‘animal’. Continues IE *dheuso- closely related to *dhouso- > Alb dash ‘ram’, Lith pl. da Usos ‘paradise’, Slav *duxъ ‘breath, spirit’ (Burda KZ II 190–191; Saussure Mémoire 81). Persson UUÅ 1891 81–83; Grienberger Got. 58 (to Gk θέω ‘to run’); Torp-Falk 217; Wood AJPhil XLI 345– 346 (to Gk θύω ‘to rage’); Feist 121–122; Holthausen AEEW 72; Pokorny I 244–245; Chemodanov SGGJa I 83; Fraenkel 115; Vries ANEW 90; Zalizniak I 139; Onions 250; Trubachev ESSJa V 153–154; Lehmann GED 92–93; Bammesberger Nom. 96; Heidermanns 155–156; Kluge-Seebold 825; Orel AED 57.

Goth diwan ‘to die’. Connected with Av 1 dual. pres. med. dvaidī ‘to press’, Phryg δαός: [...] ÍpÚ Φρυγῶν λύκος, Hes., Slav *daviti ‘to press, to squeeze’. Grienberger Got. 58 (to Gk θέω ‘to run’); Torp-Falk 198–199; Feist 122; Lidén Arm. St. 40–41 (to Arm di ‘corpse’); Wood MLN XXI 226–227 (to Lat fatigō ‘to grow weary’); Pokorny I 235; Seebold 147–149; Lindeman Verschärfung 16.5; Lehmann GED 3, 93.

Goth ga-deþs ‘act, deed’, ON dáä id., OE dæd id., OFris dēde id., OS dād id., OHG tāt id. Formally identical with Av ni-dāiti- ‘laying down’, Lith dėtis ‘load’ derived from IE *dhē- ‘to put, to set’, see *đōnan. Bartholomae 1082; Torp- Falk 198; Holthausen AEEW 68; Feist 178; Trier Lehm 104–109; Pokorny I 237; Fraenkel 91–92; Vries ANEW 71; Zalizniak I 150; Onions 250; Seebold 157; Lehmann GED 136; Bammesberger Nom. 146; Kluge-Seebold 816; Boutkan OFED s.v.
OHG tāha ‘jackdaw’. Probably telated to OPrus doacke ‘starling’ < *dāk- (Pott BVS VI 113). Nesselmann Thesaurus 31; Toporov PJa I 354; Kluge-Seebold 187.

OHG tāen ‘to suckle’. Related to Skt dháyati ‘to suck, to drink’, Gk aor. θήσατο id., Arm diem id., OIr dínim id., Latv δἄξυ id., Slav *dojiti ‘to milk’. Cf. zero grade in OSw dīa ‘to suckle’, MLG tīen id. See *đajjanan. Torp-Falk 205; Mayrhofer II 93; Pokorny I 241–242; Frisk I 673–674; Lindeman NTS XXII 72–73; Lehmann GED 85.

ON digr ‘stout, big’, OFris adv. diger ‘careful’, MDu deger ‘complete’, MHG adv. tiger, tigere ‘complete, total’. Derived from *đīzanan. Karsten NB II 254; Torp-Falk 205; Feist 119; Van Windekens I 533 (to Toch A tsiṅk ‘hardly, strongly’); Pokorny I 244–245; Vries ANEW 76; Seebold 151; Lehmann GED 90 (on Goth digrei ‘abundance’); Heidermanns 156–157.

Goth irreg. d(e)igan ‘to knead’, Norw digen ‘to milk’. Related to Toch A tsek-, B tsaik- ‘to shape, to form’, Skt degdhi ‘to smear, to anoint’, Av caus. daēzayeiti ‘to wall about’, Gk θιγγάνω ‘to touch, to take hold of’, Lat fingō ‘to form’, Lith δíεἤτι ‘to skin, to flay’. Bartholomae 673–674; Torp-Falk 205–206; Holthausen AEEW 69–70; Walde- Hofmann I 501–502; Feist 118–119; Jóhannesson IEW 501–502; Mayrhofer II 62; Pokorny I 244–245; Fraenkel 98; Vries ANEW 76; Frisk I 674–675; Onions 242; Seebold 151–152; Lehmann GED 90; Burlak IFTJa 263.

ON díki ‘ditch, dike’, OE díc id. (fem.), OFris dīk id., OS dīk id., OHG tīh id. Of unknown origin. Fick KZ XXII 103–104 (to Lat fīgō ‘to fix, to fasten, to thrust in, to attach’, Lith dÿgti ‘to grow, to crop’); Torp-Falk 205 (follow Fick); Holthausen AEEW 72; Pokorny I 244; Vries ANEW 76; Zalizniak II 182; Onions 278; Lehmann GED 90; Kluge-Seebold 167.
