OSwed dīa ‘to suck’, MHG dīen ‘to suck, to suckle’. Related to *đējanan. Torp-Falk 205; Pokorny I 241–242; Frisk I 673–674.

ON dís ‘goddess, sister’. Unclear prefixal formations in WGmc: OE ides ‘woman’, OS idis ‘woman, wife’, OHG itis ‘woman’. Unclear. Related to *đajjanan? Or to Arm pl. di-k' ‘gods’ if the latter is to be separated from Gk θεός ‘god’? Torp-Falk 206; Frisk I 662–663.
Goth ga-dofs ‘appropriate, suitable’, OE ze-défe ‘becoming, fit, proper’. Derived from *đabanan. Torp- Falk 200; Feist 179; Seebold 146.

Goth fidur-dogs ‘four days long’, ON døgr ‘day or night’. Derived from *đazaz. Torp-Falk 199; Schulze Kl. Schr. 844–845; Holthausen AEEW 74; Feist 149; Pokorny I 7, 241–242; Vries ANEW 92; Lehmann GED 86.
ON dœl ‘little dale’, LG döle ‘hole’, OHG tuola ‘hole, pit’ (ō- and ōnstem). Related to *đalaz ~ *đalan. Torp-Falk 204; Vries ANEW 92; Lehmann GED 87.
ON dóm-hús ‘courthouse’, OE dóm-hús id. Compound of *đōmaz and *xūsan. Carr Nom. comp. 80 (parallel formations).
ON dóm-stóll ‘judgment seat’, OHG tuom-stuol ‘tribunal’. Compound of *đōmaz and *stōlaz. Carr Nom. comp. 85 (parallel formations).

Goth doms ‘discernment, distinction’, ON dómr ‘court of judgment, judgment’, OE dóm ‘judgment, ruling’, OFris dōm id., OS dōm id., OHG tuom id. (also neut.). Close to Gk θωμός ‘heap’, Phryg duma, dumos ‘religious assembly’, further derived from *dhē- ‘to put, to set’. See *đōnan. Torp-Falk 198; Holthausen AEEW 74; Feist 122–123; Pokorny I 238; Dybo VSJa 27; Fraenkel 92 (Lith dom E ‘attention’ is secondarily derived from domėtis ‘to watch’); Frisk I 700; Vries ANEW 79; Zalizniak II 167; Onions 24; Seebold 158; Lehmann GED 93; Bammesberger Nom. 72; Orel Phrygian 425.

Goth domjan ‘to deem, to judge’, ON dœma ‘to judge, to talk’, OE déman ‘to think, to judge’, OFris dēma id., OS ā-dōmian ‘to judge’, OHG tuomen id. Derived from *đōmaz. Torp-Falk 198; Holthausen AEEW 74; Feist 122; Vries ANEW 92; Onions 250; Lehmann GED 93.

OE dón ‘to do, to make’, OFris dwa id., OS dōan id., OHG tuon id. Related to IE *dhē- ‘to put’: Hitt dāi- ‘to put’, Toch AB tā- id., Skt dádhāti id., Av dadaiti ‘to bring’, Gk τίθημι ‘to put’, Lat faciō ‘to do, to make’, Lith dėti ‘to put’, Slav *dîti ‘to put’. Bartholomae 678; Torp-Falk 197–198; Holthausen AEEW 75; Walde-Hofmann I 440–442; Mayrhofer II 14–15; Pokorny I 235–239; Fraenkel 91–92; Frisk II 897–898; Onions 279–280; Seebold 157–160; Kluge-Seebold 841; Burlak IFTJa 245.

Goth part. af-dauiþs ‘troubled, annoyed’. Similar to Slav *daviti ‘to strangle, to press, to squeeze’. Cf. also ON dá ‘catalepsy’ < *đawan close to Phryg δάος:... ÍpÚ Φρυγῶν λύκος (Hes.). Causative of *đewanan. Kretschmer Einleitung 211; Wood MLN XXI 226–227 (to ON dasask ‘to become weary’); Fick BB XXIX 236; Torp-Falk 198; Feist 4; Detschew Thr. 117; Pokorny I 235; Fraenkel 100 (Lith dõvyti ‘to torment’ < Slav); Vries ANEW 71; Stang LS 18; Trubachev ESSJa IV 198– 199; Lehmann GED 3; Orel Phrygian 422.

ON draf ‘draff, husks’, ME masc. draf ‘yeast’, MLG draf ‘draff, husks, dregs’, OHG pl. trebir ‘husks’. See *đrōb(j)az. Further to be compared with OIr drab ‘dregs, yeast’, Slav *drobъ ‘bit, intestine’ (Fraenkel 102–103). Torp- Falk 202–203; Feist 126–127, 179; Pokorny I 204, 252; Vries ANEW 79–80 (to Gk τρέφω ‘to fatten, to nourish’); Chantraine 1135 (follows Vries); Onions 287; Trubachev ESSJa V 119– 120; Lehmann GED 96, 136; Kluge- Seebold 834.

ON drafli ‘curdled milk’. Cf. also neut. drafl‘tattle’ < *đrablan. Structurally close to Gk τροφαλίς ‘fresh cheese, sour milk’, further to Gk τρέφω ‘to feed’ (Petersson SVSL I 125). Vries ANEW 80; Frisk II 925–927.
ON drag ‘rim, lining’, OE ze-dræz ‘dragging, band, multitude’, MLG ge-drach ‘yield’. Derived from *đrazanan. Holthausen AEEW 75; Vries ANEW 80.

Goth dragan ‘to draw, to accumulate, to take on’, ON draga ‘to draw’, OE drazan ‘to draw, to drag’, OFris draga, drega ‘to carry’, OS dragan id., OHG tragan id. Identical with Alb dredh ‘to turn, to rotate’ < PAlb *dradza, cf. also Arm dafinam ‘to turn’ (Lidén Arm. St. 101–102). Walde IF XIX 106 (to Lat trahō ‘to pull’ if from *dhraghō); Zupitza Gutt. 177; Berneker 212–213 (to Slav *dorga ‘road’); Trautmann BB XX 330 (to Gk τρέχω); Torp-Falk 211; Feist 123; Holthausen AEEW 76; Walde-Pokorny I 859 (to Skt dṛhyati ‘to be strong’); Jóhannesson IEW 519–521; Pokorny I 257; Vries ANEW 80; Onions 288; Seebold 160–162; Lehmann GED 93–94 (to IE *dher- ‘to hold’); Mottausch KZ CVI 160–172; Kluge-Seebold 830–831; Orel AED 73.
Burg *dragils ‘carrier’, ON dregill ‘ribbon’, OHG tregil ‘bearer’. Derived from *đrazan, *đrazanan. Seebold 161; Köbler 709.

ON dregg ‘dregs, lees’. Related to OPrus dragios ‘yeast’ (if not from Germanic), Lith pl. drãgës id. (if not from Old Prussian), Lett dial. dradzi ‘sediment in melted butter’, Slav *droždža ‘yeast’ < *dhroghƒō, Alb dra ~ drâ ‘oil-cake, melted butter’ < PAlb *draga (Förstemann KZ XIX (1870) 355). Fick KZ XXI 4; Froehde BB XIII 455 (adds Lat fracēs ‘grounds, dregs of oil’); Persson Beiträge 929 (follows Froehde); Torp Festschr. Unger 178; Zupitza Gutt. 31, 178; Torp-Falk 211; Reichelt KZ XLVI 322; Pokorny I 251; Fraenkel 100; Vries ANEW 82; Onions 289; Trubachev ESSJa V 128–129; Kluge-Seebold 835; Orel AED 71; Dybo BSA 13.
ON pl. drǫgur ‘timber carried on horseback’, OE dræze ‘dragnet’ (neut.?), OHG traga ‘barrow, nurse, nanny’. Derived from *đrazanan. Holthausen AEEW 75; Vries ANEW 80; Seebold 161.

ON dráttr ‘draught, pulling’, MLG dracht id. Structurally close to Lat part. tractus, of trahō ‘to drag’. Further related to *đrazanan. Torp-Falk 211; Walde-Hofmann II 698–699; Vries ANEW 81; Kluge- Seebold 830.

Goth draibjan ‘to drive, to propel’, ON dreifa ‘to scatter, to disperse’, OE dræfan ‘to drive’, MHG durch-treiben id. Derived from *đrībanan. Holthausen AEEW 75; Feist 123; Vries ANEW 82; Seebold 163.
ON dramb ‘roll of fat on the neck’. Close to Gk θρόμβος ‘coagulated mass’. Pokorny I 257–258; Frisk I 685–686 (further to τρέφω ‘to thicken, to curdle’); Chantraine 442; Lehmann GED 96.

ON drangr ‘lonely upstanding rock’. Identical with Alb drang ‘barge-pole, punting-pole; young creature, cub’, Lith drañgas ‘thick pole’, dránga ‘perch, pole’, Slav *drǫgъ ‘club, stick’ (Zupitza Gutt. 177). Johansson KZ XXXVI 374; Torp-Falk 211; Trautmann BSW 59; Stang LS 19; Pokorny I 254; Fraenkel 101; Vries ANEW 81; Mann Language XXVI 386; Trubachev ESSJa V 129–130; Orel Zf Balk XXIII/1 73, AED 72.
Goth dragk ‘drink, potion’, OHG tranc id. (masc., neut.). Derived from *đrenkanan. Feist 123; Onions 289; Seebold 165; Bammesberger Nom. 53; Kluge-Seebold 832.

Goth dragkjan ‘to make drink’, ON drekkja ‘to drown, to swamp’, OE drencan ‘to give to drink’, OFris drentza id., OS or-drenkian id., MHG trenken id. Causative of *đrenkanan. Torp-Falk 211–212; Holthausen AEEW 76; Feist 123; Vries ANEW 82; Onions 289; Seebold 165; Kluge- Seebold 832.

ON draugr ‘ghost, spectre’ (unless = draugr ‘dry log’), OS gi-drōg ‘mirage’, OHG gi-trog ‘deception’ (neut.). Identical with Av draoga- ‘lie, deceit’. See *đreuzanan. Bartholomae 768; Torp- Falk 213; Pokorny I 276; Vries ANEW 81; Lehmann GED 95; Crozier ANF CII 1–12 (on ON draugr ‘companion’).

ON draumr ‘dream’, OE dreám ‘joy, pleasure’, OFris drām ‘dream’, OS drōm id., OHG troum id. Derived from *đreusanan ‘to fall’, semantically similar to the development of IE *s⁄ep- ‘to sleep’ based on *s⁄ep- ‘to fall’, presumably, with an intermediate stage ‘to become weak, to drowse’ (cf. E drowse also connected with *đreusanan). Note that *đraumaz is formally close to Gk θραῦμα, θραῦσμα ‘debris, wound’ based on θραύω ‘to break’ < IE *dhreus-. Schröder GRM XVI 164 (from *đrauzmaz, to *drauzaz); Schulze Kl. Schr. 862; Torp-Falk 213 (separate OS and OE); Holthausen AEEW 76; Porzig Gliederung 180; Pokorny I 275; Vries ANEW 82; Zalizniak I 130; Frisk I 680–681; Onions 289; Chantraine I 439; Bammesberger Nom. 70; Kluge- Seebold 833–834.
ON dreyma ‘to dream’, Du dromen id., OHG troumen id. Derived from *đraumaz. Vries ANEW 83.
ON dreypa ‘to drop, to dip’, OE dreópan id., MLG dröpen id., OHG troufen id. Causative of *đreupanan. Torp-Falk 214; Holthausen AEEW 77; Vries ANEW 83; Seebold 169.
Goth ga-drausjan ‘to cast down, to overthrow’, ON dreyra ‘to bleed, to ooze’, OHG trōren ‘to rain’. Causative of *đreusanan. Torp-Falk 214; Feist 5; Vries ANEW 83; Seebold 171; Lehmann GED 95.
ON dreyri ‘gore, blood’, OE dreór ‘blood’, OS drōr id., OHG trōr id. Derived from *đreusanan. Torp-Falk 215; Holthausen AEEW 77; Vries ANEW 83; Onions 289; Seebold 171; Lehmann GED 95.

Goth drigkan ‘to drink’, ON drekka id., OE drincan id., OFris drinka id., OS drinkan id., OHG trincan id. Of unknown origin. It is plausible that *đrenkanan is a back formation based on *đrankjanan, the latter originally meaning ‘to milk’ and related to Slav *dręgati ‘to pull’. Wood MLN XVIII 15–16 (to *đrazjō); Zupitza Gutt. 161 (to Skt dhrájati ‘to glide, to move’); Torp-Falk 211–212 (to Lith nérti ‘to dive’); Cahen Etudes 23– 25 (from pre-Gmc); Zupitza Gutt. 161 (to Skt dhrájati ‘to glide, to move’); Schulze KZ XXVII 606 (to Skt adv. hap. leg. drahyát- ‘firmly’); Feist 125; Holthausen AEEW 77; Kluge 791–792; Jóhannesson IEW 529–530; Pokorny I 273 (to *dhre·- ‘to pull’); Vries ANEW 82; Onions 290; Seebold 165–166; Lehmann GED 94–95 (to Lith drėgti ‘to become damp’); Kluge- Seebold 836.
ON drekka ‘drink, beverage’, OE drince id. Derived from *đrenkanan. Holthausen AEEW 77; Vries ANEW 82; Seebold 164.
ON drep ‘blow’, OE ze-drep ‘stroke’. Derived from *đrepanan. Torp- Falk 212; Holthausen AEEW 77; Seebold 166; Bammesberger Nom. 59.

ON drepa ‘to strike’, OE part. drepen id., OS ofar-drepan ‘to outdo, to surpass’, OHG treffan ‘to strike’. Related to Slav *drebiti ‘to crush’. Torp-Falk 212; Holthausen AEEW 77; Jóhannesson IEW 530; Pokorny I 272– 273; Vries ANEW 83 (to Goth ga-draban ‘to chip, to hew out’); Seebold 166–167; Trubachev ESSJa V 106; Bammesberger Nom. 59, 62; Kluge-Seebold 834.

Goth driugan ‘to wage war, to lead a military life’, OE dreózan ‘to do, to work, to perform, to bear’. Cf. also ON drÿgja ‘to commit’ < *đreuzjanan. Historically identical with WGmc *đreuzanan > OE be-dróz ‘seduced’, OFris bidriāga ‘to seduce’, OS bi-driogan id., OHG triogan ‘to deceive’ (Ambrosini ASNSP XXVII 229). Probably related to Lith dra Ugas ‘fellow traveller’, OPr drūktai ‘hard’, Slav *drugъ ‘friend, other, second’ (Lane Language IX 247; Stang LS 19–20). Kuhn KZ I 181 (connected with *đrauzaz); Persson Wurzelerw. 25, 60 (*đreuzanan ‘to seduce’ to Lat fraus ‘deceit, fraud’); Torp- Falk 213–214; Zupitza Gutt. 102, 177; Feist 125–126; Holthausen AEEW 77; Porzig Gliederung 196; Jóhannesson IEW 516–517; Pokorny I 255; Fraenkel 102; Zalizniak I 150; Onions 289; Benveniste IEL 89; Seebold 167–168; Trubachev ESSJa V 131–132; Lehmann GED 95 (to IE *dher- ‘to hold’); Kluge- Seebold 839.

ON drjúgr ‘rich, solid, substantial’, OE dréoz ‘studious, earnest’, NFris driech ‘extensive’. See *dreuzanan. Torp-Falk 213–214; Zupitza Gutt. 129; Holthausen AEEW 76–77; Karsten NB I 40; Pokorny I 254–255; Vries ANEW 84; Heidermanns 159–160.

ON drjúpa ‘to drip’, OE dreópan id., OFris driāpa ‘to drip, to trickle’, OS driopan id., OHG triofan ‘to drip’. A secondary formation parallel to *đreusanan. Torp-Falk 214; Holthausen AEEW 77; Jóhannesson IEW 527– 529; Pokorny I 274–275; Vries ANEW 84 (to OIr drucht ‘dew’); Seebold 169– 170; Kluge-Seebold 836.

Goth driusan ‘to fall’, OE dreósan ‘to rush, to fall, to perish’, OS driosan ‘to fall’. Etymologically identical with Gk θραύω ‘to break, to grind’ (Johansson KZ XXX 422), cf. also Slav *drusati ‘to shake, to throw up, to swing’ with *-s- < *-˚-. Torp-Falk 214–215; Feist 126; Holthausen AEEW 77; van Windekens KZ LXXIII 26–27 (to Gk δρόσος ‘dew’); Jóhannesson IEW 527– 529; Pokorny I 274; Frisk I 680–681; Onions 289–290; Seebold 170–171; Trubachev ESSJa V 133; Lehmann GED 95.

ON drák ‘streak’. Related to Skt dhrájati ‘to glide, to move, to fly’, dhrāj- ‘tractive force’. Zupitza Gutt. 161; Mayrhofer II 115; Pokorny I 273; Vries ANEW 80; Dybo BSA 128 (with a length of an original root noun).

OE drán ‘drone’, OS drān id. Cf. also OHG tren id. with short vocalism. Identical with Gk ἀν-θρήνη ‘wild bee’, θρῆνος ‘dirge’. Torp-Falk 211; Holthausen AEEW 76; Pokorny I 255; Dybo VSJa 25–26 (pretonic shortening in OHG); Frisk I 681– 682; Onions 290; Kluge-Seebold 195.
ON drif ‘driven snow’, OE ze-dríf ‘driving, movement’, OHG thanatrib ‘divorce’. Derived from *đrībanan. Torp-Falk 212; Holthausen AEEW 77; Vries ANEW 83; Seebold 163; Bammesberger Nom. 56.
ON hring-drifi ‘prince’, OHG uz-tribo ‘exorcist’. Derived from *đrībanan. Seebold 163.

ON drift, dript ‘snow-drift’, ME drift ‘current, flow’, OFris ur-drift ‘driving out’, MLG drift id., OHG anatrift ‘influence’. Derived from *đrībanan. Vries ANEW 83; Seebold 163; Lehmann GED 94; Kluge-Seebold 836.

Goth dreiban ‘to drive’, ON drífa id., OE drīfan id., OFris drīva id., OS drīban id., OHG trīban id. Related to Lith drimbū, drībti ‘to fall down in flakes’, cf. ON drífr snær ‘snow drifts’ ~ Lith sni‚gas dri Mba ‘the snow is falling thickly’ (Grienberger Got. 59–60). Meringer IF XVIII 235–236 (follows Grienberger); Persson Beiträge 75, 309; Uhlenbeck PBB XXVII 117–118; Torp-Falk 212; Feist 124–125; Holthausen AEEW 77; Jóhannesson IEW 526–527; Pokorny I 274; Fraenkel 104; Vries ANEW 83; Onions 290–291; Seebold 162–163; Lehmann GED 94; Levickij IF CIII 224 (to *đrōbjanan); Kluge-Seebold 834.
ON drífa ‘fall of snow, sleet’, MLG drīve ‘gutter’, MHG trībe ‘diarrhea’. Cf. also OE drif ‘fever’. Derived from *đrībanan. Holthausen AEEW 77.

ON dríta ‘cacare’, ME driten id., OFris part. be-drīt id., MDu drīten id., OHG gloss drizo ‘megio’. Similar to but phonetically not agreeing with Lith tríedžiu, tríesti ‘to have diarrhea’ < *treidh-. Torp-Falk 212; Holthausen AEEW 78; Jóhannesson IEW 518–519; Pokorny I 256; Fraenkel 1122; Vries ANEW 84; Onions 271; Stang LS 20 (to Slav *dristati ‘cacare’); Seebold 164–165.

OE dróf ‘draffy, draggy, dirty’, OS drōbi ‘murky, cloudy’, OHG truobi id. Related to *đraban and eventually to Gk τρέφω ‘to become fat’ (Kluge 742). Torp-Falk 203; Feist 126–127; Holthausen AEEW 78; Pokorny I 252; Frisk II 925–927; Lehmann GED 96; Matzel KZ CV 121; Heidermanns 160– 161; Kluge-Seebold 839.

Goth drobjan ‘to stir up, to excite’, OE dréfan ‘to disturb, to agitate’, OS drōbian ‘to become sad’, OHG truoben ‘to entangle’. Derived from *đrōb(j)az. Zupitza Gutt. 31; Torp-Falk 203; Holthausen AEEW 78; Feist 126–127; Lehmann GED 96; Heidermanns 160.

Goth drauhti-witoþ ‘campaign’, ON drótt ‘people, bodyguard’, OE dryht ‘people, multitude, army’, OFris dracht, drecht ‘people’, OS druht-folk ‘suite’, OHG truht ‘crowd’. Derived from *đreuzanan. Kuhn KZ I 181; Grienberger Got. 80; Zupitza Gutt. 178; Torp-Falk 214; Lane Language IX 247; Holthausen AEEW 79; Feist 124; Porzig Gliederung 196; Pokorny I 255; Chemodanov SGGJa I 66; Vries ANEW 84–85; Zalizniak I 150; Seebold 167; Benveniste IEL 89, 93; Lehmann GED 94; Bammesberger Nom. 140; Kluge-Seebold 839.

ON dróttinn ‘lord, master’, OE dryhten ‘ruler, lord, prince’, OFris drohten id., OS drohtīn id., OHG truhtīn id. Cf. Goth drauhtinon ‘to campaign, to serve in the army’ < *đruxtīnōjanan. Derived from *đruxtiz. Kuhn KZ I 181; Torp-Falk 214; Holthausen AEEW 79; Feist 124; Porzig Gliederung 196; Vries ANEW 85; Seebold 167; Benveniste IEL 89; Lehmann GED 94; Bammesberger Nom. 83.
Norw drynja ‘to rumble’, LG drönen id. Derived from *đrunjuz. Torp-Falk 211; Onions 290 (to *đrēnō ~

Goth drunjus ‘sound, call’, Norw dryn id. A verbal abstract (Brugmann Grundriß II/1 224–225) based on an unattested verb, cf. Skt dhráṇati ‘to sound’. Torp-Falk 211; Feist 127; Mayrhofer II 115–116 (onomatopoeia in Skt); Mayrhofer II 115–116; Pokorny I 255–256 (to Tokh A träṅk-, B treṅk- ‘to speak’); Vries ANEW 85–86; Lehmann GED 96.
ON drukkinn ‘drunken’, OE druncen id., MLG drunken id., OHG win-truncan id. Derived from *đrenkanan. Torp-Falk 212; Holthausen AEEW 78; Vries ANEW 85; Lehmann GED 95; Kluge-Seebold 839.
Goth drugkanei ‘drunkenness’, OHG truncanī id. Derived from *đrunkanaz ~ *đrunkenaz. Feist 127; Lehmann GED 94–95.

ON drukna ‘to be drowned’, OE druncnian ‘to sink, to drown, to be drunk’, OHG trunkanēn, trunkanōn ‘to sink, to drown’. Derived from *đrunkanaz ~ *đrunkenaz. Holthausen AEEW 78; Vries ANEW 85; Onions 291.

ON drykkr ‘drink, beverage’, OE drync id., MLG drunk id., OHG trunc id. Derived from *đrenkanan. Torp- Falk 212; Holthausen AEEW 79; Vries ANEW 85; Bahder Verbalabstr. 15–17; Seebold 165; Lehmann GED 4, 95; Lühr Expr. 243, 255; Bammesberger Nom. 53, 131.

ON dropi ‘drop’, OE dropa id., ME droppe id., OS dropo id., OHG tropfo id. Derived from *đreupanan. Torp-Falk 214; Holthausen AEEW 78; Pokorny I 275; Vries ANEW 84; Zalizniak I 153; Onions 291; Seebold 169; Kluge-Seebold 838.
Swed dial. drusa ‘to fall down hard’, MLG drusen ‘to fall with noise’. Derived from *đreusanan. Vries ANEW 85.
Goth drus ‘fall’, ON drør ‘phlegm, rheum’, OE dryre ‘fall, decline’. Derived from *đreusanan (Lidén MASO I 109). Holthausen AEEW 79; Feist 127; Vries ANEW 86; Seebold 171.

OHG trūt ‘friend, beloved’. A substantivized adjective identical with OIr drúth ‘salacious, lecherous’ < *dhrū-to-. Marstrander NTS I 245 (to Lat Frutis, epithet of Venus); Torp- Falk 214 (to Skt dhruvá- ‘firm, fixed’); Pokorny I 215; Meid KZ LXXIX 291; Heidermanns 161–162.
ON drúpa ‘to droop’, OE drupian ‘to drop’. Derived from *đreupanan. Holthausen AEEW 78; Vries ANEW 85; Seebold 170.

Swed dodra ‘marsh marigold’, ME doder ‘dodder, devil’s guts, Caltha palustris’, Du dodder id., OHG totoro ‘cuscuta, epithymus’. Identical with Skt dudhra- ‘violent’ further related to Skt dódhat- ‘violent, shocking’, Gk Hom θυσσανόεις ‘tasseled, fringed’ (based on *θυθωα). Torp-Falk 208; Pokorny I 264–265; Frisk I 697.
ON dupt, duft ‘powder’, MHG tuft ‘fog, dew’. Related to *đaubaz. Torp-Falk 209; Pokorny I 264; Vries ANEW 87 (to *steubanan ~ stūbanan); Lehmann GED 328.

ON dygä ‘virtue’, OE duguä ‘manhood, multitude, majesty, glory’, OFris dugethe ‘virtue’, MLG doget, dogent id., OHG tugund id. Derived from *đauza. Holthausen AEEW 79; Vries ANEW 89; Kluge-Seebold 840 (to ON dyggr ‘faithful’).

Goth dauhtar ‘daughter’, ON dóttir id., OE dóhtor id., OFris dochter id., OS dohtar id., OHG tohter id. Related to Toch A ckācar, B tkācer id., Skt duhitár- id., Av dug 6 dar- id., Gk θυγάτηρ id., Arm dustr id., Osc fūtír id., Lith dukt E id., Slav *dъkti id. Delbrück KZ XIX (1870) 240–247; Bartholomae 748; Torp-Falk 207; Holthausen AEEW 74; Feist 116; Mayrhofer II 56; Pokorny I 277; Deroy II Facht. IGAS 160–162 (to *đauza); Vries ANEW 79; Zalizniak I 157; Fraenkel 110; Frisk I 690; Szemerényi Kinship 22 (to *đuxtiz ~ *đuxtō), Hermes CV 387; Onions 245; Trubachev ESSJa V 178–179; Peeters IF LXXXIV 206–207; Hamp IF LXXXVIII 94–95; Lehmann GED 88; Kluge-Seebold 826; Adams TB 312.

Goth dauhts ‘feast’, MHG duht ‘doughtiness’. Derived from *đauza (Diefenbach VW II 616). Grimm DG II 208; Grienberger Got. 79; Holthausen An ZDA XXIV 33 (to Gk τεύχω ‘to make, to build’); Feist 116; Lehmann GED 88.

ODan -dokke ‘dock, coarse weedy herb’ (in compounds), OE docce id., OS dokka ‘doll’, OHG tocka id. Probably connected with Latv duga ‘slime on the surface of water’. Holthausen AEEW 74; Onions 280; Lühr Expr. 226–227; Kluge-Seebold 187.

ON dolg ‘enmity’, OE dolz ‘wound, scar’, OFris dolg id., MLG dolk id., OHG tolg id. Based on WGmc *đelzanan > OFris delga ‘to wound’, MHG telken ‘to beat’ related to Gk θέλγω ‘to enchant, to transform using a charm’ (Ehrismann PBB XX 60; Havers KZ XLIII 231, IF XXVIII 190–192). The original meaning of *đulzan must have been ‘mark (of blood)’ while the Greek verb denoted the act of making such a mark. For the semantic development cf. Fr blesser ‘to wound’ < WGmc *blōđsjanan (OE blédsian ‘to bless, to consecrate’) based on *blōđan. Saussure MSL VIII 443 (Gk θέλγω to Lith ἤωελγιū ‘to look, to peer’); Torp-Falk 210; Holthausen AEEW 74; Dauzat DELF 93; Walde-Pokorny I 866 (to Lat falx ‘sickle, reaping-hook, scythe’); Pokorny I 247; Vries ANEW 78–79; Zalizniak I 139; Frisk I 658–659; Onions 100; Chantraine I 427; Seebold 153.

Goth dulgs ‘debt’. Related to OIr dliged ‘duty, law’, dligim ‘to earn’, Slav *dъlgъ (Kuhn KZ I 205; Berneker I 244). Grimm KZ I 82; Lottner KZ XI 174 (Gmc < Slav); Zupitza Gutt. 178; Torp-Falk 210; Feist 128–129; Chemodanov SGGJa I 96; Scardigli Goten 54 (from Celtic); Lehmann GED 97.

Goth dulþs ‘festival’, OHG tuld id. Derivative in *-ti- related to OE deall ‘proud, exulting, eminent’, MHG ge-telle beautiful, nice’ and further to MIr dell-rad ‘shine’. Dietrich ZDADL V 224–225 (to ON dvala ‘to delay’); Delbrück Zd Ph I 9 (to Skt dhṛ†i- ‘firmness, resolution’); Grienberger Got. 60 (derived from *đwelanan); Torp-Falk 204; Senn JEGP XXXII 513, 528; Holthausen AEEW 70; Feist 129; Trier Holz 55 (follows Delbrück⁾; Pokorny I 246; Zalizniak I 150; Mayrhofer II 111–112 (against Delbrück); Lehmann GED 97; Kluge-Seebold 198 (from

Goth dumbs ‘dumb’, ON dumbr id., OE dumb id., OFris dumb id., OS dumb ‘stupid, simple’, OHG tumb id. An expressive form related to *đaubaz (Holthausen GEW 24. Torp Festschr. Unger 179 (to ON dumba ‘mist’); Torp- Falk 201; Lewy KZ XL 420 (to Slav *dǫbъ ‘oak’); Feist 129; Holthausen AEEW 80; Karsten NB I 15; Pokorny I 264; Vries ANEW 87; Onions 293; Lehmann GED 97–98 (to IE *dheubh- ‘darkened’); Lühr Expr. 101–103; Heidermanns 166; Kluge-Seebold 199.

OE dunz ‘dung’, OFris dung id., OS dung id., OHG tunga ‘manuring, fertilizer’. Cf. also ON dyngja ‘heap’ < *đunzjōn. Related to Lith dengiū, deñgti ‘to cover’, OIr dinged ‘to suppress’. Zupitza Gutt. 99; Torp-Falk 208; Holthausen AEEW 80; Lindquist NB XV 97–132 (to *đenzwanan); Pokorny I 249; Fraenkel 88–89; Vries ANEW 90; Onions 294; Kluge-Seebold 199.
ON dyngja ‘dungeon, lady’s bower’, OE dyncze ‘plowed land, fallow land’. Derived from *đunzaz ~ *đunzō. Holthausen AEEW 82; Vries ANEW 90.

ON dynr ‘din’, OE dyne ‘din, noise’. Related to Skt dhvánati ‘to sound’, Gk θύνω ‘to rush, to dart along’. Torp- Falk 208; Holthausen AEEW 81; Mayrhofer II 118; Pokorny I 277; Vries ANEW 90; Frisk I 697–698; Onions 269.
ON dynja ‘to gush, to shower’, OE dynian ‘to make a noise, to resound’, OS dunian ‘to roar, to rumble’. Connected with *đuniz. Torp-Falk 208; Holthausen AEEW 82; Vries ANEW 90; Onions 269.

ON dust ‘dust’, OE dust id., OFris dūst ‘heap’, MLG dust ‘dust’, dunst ‘smoke, haze’, OHG dunst ‘wind, storm’. Related to Latv dvans ‘smoke, haze’. Any connection with *đuniz? Torp-Falk 216–217; Holthausen AEEW 80; Pokorny I 266; Vries ANEW 88; Onions 295.
ON dyttr, dyntr ‘dint’, OE dynt ‘stroke, blow’. Derived from *đentanan. Holthausen AEEW 82; Vries ANEW 90–91; Seebold 154; Bammesberger Nom. 131.

Goth daur ‘door, gate’, OE dór ‘large door’, dúru ‘door’, OFris dure id., OS dor ‘gate’, duru ‘door’, OHG tor ‘gate’. A morphological variant of *đurz. Meringer IF XVI 125 (on masc. *đurōn > Goth auga-dauro ‘window’); Torp-Falk 210; Holthausen AEEW 75. 80; Feist 117–118; Pokorny I 278–279; Zalizniak II 206; Onions 284; Lehmann GED 89; Kluge-Seebold 828.
ON dura-stoä ‘doorpost’, OE dúru-stod. Compound of *đurz and *stuđō. Carr Nom. comp. 52.
Goth daura-wards ‘door-keeper’, OE dúru-weard id., OHG tor-warto id. (n-stem). Compound of *đurz and *warđaz. Feist 117; Carr Nom. comp. 69 (parallel formations); Lehmann GED 89.
OE dyrst ‘tribulation’, OHG ki-turst ‘boldness’. Derived from *đarsa. Identical with Skt dhṛṣṭí- id. derived from dhṛṣnóti ‘to be audacious’. Torp-Falk 203; Pokorny I 259.

ON pl. dyrr ‘door’, ODu dori id. (i-stem), OHG turi id. (i-stem with traces of a root stem). Related to Toch B twere id., Skt nom. pl. dvār- id., Av acc. sg. dvar 6 m id., Arm pl. dur-k' id., Gk θύρ` ‘door’, Alb derë id., Lat foris, forēs id., W dor ‘door’, Lith dūrys id., Slav *dvьrь id. Brugmann Grundriß II/1 133; Bartholomae 766; Torp-Falk 210; Walde- Hofmann I 529–530; Feist 117–118; Mayrhofer II 83–84; Pokorny I 278–279; Fraenkel 112–113; Vries ANEW 91; Zalizniak II 206; Frisk I 695–696; Onions 284; Trubachev ESSJa V 171–172; Lehmann GED 89; Bammesberger Nom. 197; Griepentrog Wurzelnomina 117–152; Kluge-Seebold 841; Orel AED 60; Adams TB 323– 324.

MHG türre, dürre ‘valiant’. Derived from *đarsa. Structurally identical with Skt dhṛṣú- ‘bold, courageous’, Gk θρασύς ‘brave, valiant’ and probably Slav *dьrzъ-kъ ‘daring’ (with unclear *-z-). Mayrhofer II 112–113; Pokorny I 259; Frisk I 654–655; Seebold 147; Matzel Festschr. Schröder 98, 109, 113, KZ CV 104; Georgiev Lu E 199 (on Slav *dьrzъkъ); Heidermanns 167–168.

OE dox, dosc ‘dark, swarthy’. Identical with Lat fuscus ‘dark, blackish’ < *dhusko- (Zupitza KZ XXXVII 388). Cf. *đusnaz. Torp-Falk 216; Schwentner PBB XLIX 428–429; Holthausen AEEW 75; Walde-Hofmann I 572–573; Pokorny I 270; Onions 295; Heidermanns 168.

OE dosen ‘chestnut brown’, OS dosan ‘maroon, ash-colored’, OHG tusin ‘ash-colored’. Identical with MIr donn ‘dark brown’, MW dwnn ‘dark red’ < *dhusno- ~ *dh⁄osno-. Cf. *đuskaz. Förster Festschr. Liebermann 137; Schwentner FB 61–62, PBB XLIX 423–427; Torp- Falk 216; Holthausen AEEW 80; Pokorny I 270–271; Vries ANEW 87; Lockwood KZ LXXIX 294–300; Heidermanns 168–169.
ON dytta ‘to meddle’, OE dyttan ‘to close, to shut up’. Derived from *đuttaz. Holthausen AEEW 82; Vries ANEW 91.

Norw dott ‘tuft, wisp’, OE dott ‘dot, small spot’, LG dott ‘tuft, bunch’. Derived from *dheu- ‘to blow’: Skt dhūnØti ‘to shake, to move’, Gk y-v ‘to offer by burning’, Lat suf-fiō ‘to fumigate, to perfume, to scent’. Torp-Falk 208 (to Gk θύσανος ‘tassel, fringe’); Holthausen AEEW 75; Pokorny I 262–263; Vries ANEW 91; Onions 285.

ON dúfa ‘to dive’ (wk.), OE dúfan ‘to sink’, MLG be-duven ‘to be covered’. Eventually related to *đeupaz. Zupitza Gutt. 37; Torp-Falk 209; Holthausen AEEW 79; Jóhannesson IEW 508–509; Pokorny I 267–268; Vries ANEW 86; Lerchner 61; Seebold 155–156.
ON dÿfa ‘to dip’, OE dÿfan id. Derived from *đūbanan. Holthausen AEEW 81; Vries ANEW 89; Onions 278; Seebold 156; Lehmann GED 92.

Goth hraiwa-dubo ‘turtledove’, ON dúfa ‘dove’, OE dúfe-doppa ‘pelican’, OFris dūve ‘dove’, OS dūba id., OHG tūba id. Originally, a dark-colored bird (cf. Gk πέλεια ‘wild dove’ ~ πελιός ‘dark, black and blue, livid’)? If so, connected with OIr dub ‘black’, OW dub id. and Gk τυφλός ‘dark, blind’ (Petersson Aufs. 53–54), cf. for example Slav *golǫbь ‘dove’ ~ *golǫbъ ‘blue’. Suolahti DVN 206–212; Torp-Falk 209; Holthausen AEEW 79; Feist 269–270; Pokorny I 264; Vries ANEW 86; Zalizniak I 155; Frisk II 949–950; Ebbinghaus GL XVI 9–13 (onomatopoeia); Lehmann GED 190–191; Kluge-Seebold 817.

OE dún ‘mountain, hill, down’, OFris dūne ‘hill, dune’, MDu duun id., OHG dūna ‘cape, dune’. Borrowed from Gaul *dūnom, cf. Celtic place names in -dūnum. Torp- Falk 207 (from *dheu- ‘to blow’); Holthausen AEEW 80; Pokorny I 263; Onions 286; Kluge-Seebold 199 (to Gk y,w ‘heap, shore, sand’, Skt δηíἑνιθα- ‘generous’).

ON sbst. dús ‘lull, dead calm’ (< *đūsan), OFris dūs ‘still’. Identical with Slav *dyxъ ‘breath’, for further connections see *đeuzan. Jóhannesson IEW 508; Vries ANEW 88 (to Gk θύω ‘to offer, to sacrifice’); Trubachev ESSJa V 200.
ON dúsa ‘to doze’, MLG dosen, dusen ‘to slumber, to resound’. Derived from *đūsaz. Torp NNEO 79; Holthausen PBB XLI 476; Mohr ZDADL LXXVI 179 (adduces MLG dūschen ‘to rush’); Vries ANEW 88.

Goth dwals ‘foolish, stupid’, MLG dwal ‘insane’. Compared with Gk θολόω ‘to make turbid, to confound’ (Pott KZ XXVI 189), OIr dall ‘blind, dark’. The connection with *đwelanan seems indisputable. Torp-Falk 215; Karsten NB II 241, 297; Feist 130; Pokorny I 265–266 (OIr dall < *dh⁄¬no-); Vries ANEW 88 (to Gk θόλος ‘dirt’); Seebold 172–173; Lehmann GED 98; Bammesberger Nom. 238; Heidermanns 169; Kluge-Seebold 827.
Goth dwaliþa ‘folly, foolishness’, OE dwild ‘error, heresy’. Derived from *đwalaz. Feist 130; Heidermanns 169.

ON dvelja ‘to delay’, OE dwellan ‘to lead into error, to deceive’, OFris dwelia ‘to be mistaken’, OS bi-dwellian ‘to hinder’, MDu dwellen ‘to stun, to perplex’, OHG twellen ‘to stop, to halt’. Derived from *đwelanan. Torp-Falk 215; Holthausen AEEW 81; Vries ANEW 88–89 (to *đwalaz); Onions 296; Seebold 172.
OE dwolema ‘darkness, chaos’, NFris dwelm ‘stupidity’, OS dwalm ‘fascination’, OHG twalm ‘confusion’. Derived from *đwalaz. Torp-Falk 216; Holthausen AEEW 81; Lehmann GED 98.
ON dylma ‘to be careless’, OE for-dwilman ‘to confuse, to perplex’. Derived from *đwalmaz ~ *đwalmōn. Torp-Falk 216; Holthausen AEEW 81; Vries ANEW 89.
ON dvala ‘to delay’, OE dwalian ‘to err’, OFris dwalia id., OHG ga-twalōn ‘to delay, to postpone’. Derived from *đwalō(n). Holthausen AEEW 80; Seebold 172.

ON dvǫl ‘short stay, stop’, dvala ‘hesitation’, OE dwala ‘error, doubt’, MDu dwale id., OHG twāl ‘procrastination’, twāla id. Derived from *đwalaz. Holthausen AEEW 80; Vries ANEW 88–89; Lehmann GED 98.

Icel dulinn ‘swellheaded’, OE part. ze-dwolen ‘erroneous, wrong, perverse’, OS far-dwolon ‘missed, lost’, OHG twelan ‘to remain, to be slow, to lag’. Probably related to Lat fallō ‘to deceive’, Lith dūlti ‘to rot’ and derivationally identical with Lith dvélti ‘pedere’. Torp-Falk 215–216; Holthausen AEEW 80; Walde-Hofmann I 447–448 (Lat fallō < *·h⁄elnō); Jóhannesson IEW 503–506; Pokorny I 261–267; Fraenkel 109; Seebold 172.

ON dvergr ‘dwarf’, OE dweorz id., MLG dwerch id., OHG twerg id. (neut.). Of unknown etymology. Bartholomae IF XII 131 (to Av drva- ‘dwarfish, bent’); Zupitza Gutt. 99; Torp- Falk 215; Holthausen PBB XI 548, AEEW 81; Krogmann KZ LXII 143 (to Latv drugt ‘to subside, to fall’); Pokorny I 278; Vries ANEW 89 (to *þweraz); Zalizniak I 130; Onions 296 (to Gk σέρφος ‘midge’); Kluge-Seebold 919.

ON dvína ‘to dwindle’ (wk.), OE dwínan ‘to pine, to fade, to dwindle’, MDu dwīnen ‘to fade’. Based on *đewanan. Persson SVSU X 744; Torp- Falk 217; Holthausen AEEW 81; Jóhannesson IEW 502–503; Pokorny I 260–261; Vries ANEW 89 (to *đawjanan); Lerchner 70–71; Seebold 171 (to OIr díth ‘loss’).

ON sbst. dul ‘self-conceit, pride’ (< *đwulō), OE dol ‘foolish, erring’, OFris dol id., OS dol id., OHG tol id. Related to *đwalaz. Torp-Falk 215; Holthausen AEEW 74; Wissmann NP 104; Pokorny I 266; Vries ANEW 87; Onions 293; Heidermanns 170; Kluge- Seebold 827.

Goth ibai, iba ‘whether, possibly’, ON ef ‘if’, OS ef id., OHG ibu id. A sequence of two Indo-European particles *e (like in Gk ἐ-κει ‘beside’) and *bhe ~ *bho: Av bā ‘indeed’, Lith ba id. (Brugmann Dem. 115–117, Grundriß II/2 323–324). See *ba. Grienberger Got. 125 (from IE *epi ‘after, upon’); Bartholomae 912; Uhlenbeck TNTL XXV 281 (same as Brugmann); Kluge Urgerm. (a case form in *-bh-); Feist 286–287; Pokorny I 113, 280–281; Fraenkel 28– 29; Vries ANEW 94; Frisk I 475–476; Lehmann GED 202; Kluge-Seebold 595.

Goth ibna-leiks ‘of equal substance, of similar form’, OSwed jæmliker ‘homogeneous’, OE efn-líc ‘equal’, OHG eban-līh id. Compound of *ebnaz and *līkaz. Feist 287; Lehmann GED 142, 202; Heidermanns 171.

Goth ibns ‘flat, even’, ON jafn, jamn ‘even, equal, same’, OE efen ‘even, equal’, OFris adv. even ‘same’, OS efni ‘flat, even’, OHG eban, ebano ‘equal, same’. Identical with MBret effen ‘like’, W iawn ‘just’ < *epno- (Pedersen Kelt. Gr. I 92). Johansson PBB XV 229–230 (to Lat aemulus ‘imitating’, imāgo ‘image’); Sommer IF XXXI 361–362 (from *epnó-, to IE *epi); Brugmann IF XXXVII 161 (same as Johansson); Torp-Falk 28; Feist 287–288; Holthausen AEEW 88; Thurneysen apud Feist 287 (to MIr emain ‘twins’); Karsten NB II 211; Pokorny I 505; Vries ANEW 289; Mezger KZ LXXIX 45 (from *i-bh- ‘here’); Onions 331; Lehmann GED 202; Bammesberger Nom. 246; Heidermanns 171–172; Kluge-Seebold 203.
ON jafna ‘to make equal, to compare’, OFris evenia ‘to smooth (the ground)’, OHG ebanōn ‘to make equal, to make the same’. Derived from *ebnaz. Vries ANEW 289; Lehmann GED 202; Heidermanns 171.

ON jǫfurr ‘wild boar, king, warrior’, OE eofor ‘boar’, MLG ever id., OHG ebur id. Close to Lat aper id., Umbr acc. sg. abrunu id. (with a secondary a- in Italic), Thrac ἑβρος: τράγος, βάτης: καί ποταμός Θράκης, Hes. (Försterman KZ I 497). Cf. also Slav *veprь, Latv vepris ‘castrated boar’ with an unexpected initial *⁄- (Pedersen KZ XXXVIII 311). Torp-Falk 25; Holthausen AEEW 92; Walde-Hofmann I 56, II 754–755; Detschew Thr. 163; Pokorny I 323; Chemodanov SSGJa I 57; Vries ANEW 294; Zalizniak I 130; Vasmer- Trubachev I 292.

ON jaäarr ‘edge’, OE eodor ‘hedge, fence’, OS edor, eder ‘woven fence’, OHG etar ‘fence’. Probably related to Slav *odrъ ‘wooden platform, bed’ (Uhlenbeck PBB XXVI 295) or to Slav *îdro ‘net, cloth’ (Trubachev Etim. 31– 32). Torp-Falk 24, 559 (to Arm ezer ‘border, edge’); Meringer IF XVIII 256– 258; Holthausen AEEW 91; Pokorny I 290; Chemodanov SGGJa I 81; Vries ANEW 289; Gołąb Origins 145; Kluge- Seebold 236.

Goth iþ ‘but, however, if’, ON pl. iä-gjǫld ‘recompense’, OE edcerr ‘return’, OS ed- id., OHG it-lōnōn ‘to remunerate’. Continues IE *eti: Skt áti ‘beyond, very’, Av aiti id., Gk đti ‘furthermore, still’, OPhryg eti ‘and, also’, Lat et ‘and’, Gaul eti ‘also’, OPrus et-, at- (Jacobsohn KZ XLIX 194). Bartholomae 54; Torp-Falk 24; Holthausen AEEW 88; Walde-Hofmann I 421–422; Feist 297; Mayrhofer I 27; Pokorny I 344–345; Vries ANEW 283; Lehmann GED 208; Orel Phrygian 429.

Dan igle ‘kind of worm, leech’, Norw igle id., MLG egel, īle ‘leech’ (fem.), OHG egala id. (fem.). Related to Gk ἐχῖνος id., Gk ἔχις ‘snake’, Arm ιἤιδ., Lith ež‹s ‘hedgehog’, Slav *ežь id. Fick BB XXIX 237; Torp-Falk 23; Trautmann BSW 73; Pokorny I 44; Fraenkel 330; Frisk I 602; Vasmer-Trubachev II 10.
ON jó-reiä ‘horsemen(?)’, OE eó-red ‘cavalry, legion’, OLG eo-rid id. Compound of *exwaz and *raiđō. Structurally close to OIr fem. coll. echrad ‘horses’. Carr Nom. comp. 46–47.

Goth aiƕa-tundi ‘thornbush, bramble’, ON jór ‘stallion’, OE eoh ‘warhorse’, OS ehu-skalk ‘groom, ostler’. From IE *e˚⁄os ‘horse’: Toch A yuk, B yakwe, Skt áśva-, Gk ἵππος, Lat equus, OIr ech (Försterman KZ I 493). Kock ANF XXXII 189; Torp-Falk 23; Holthausen AEEW 92; Walde-Hofmann I 412–413; Feist 21; Mayrhofer I 62; Pokorny I 301; Vries ANEW 293; Zalizniak I 138; Frisk I 733–735; Lehmann GED 15; Bammesberger Nom. 69; Adams TB 482.

Goth pret. iddja ‘went’, OE pret. eóde id. Suppletive preterite of *zanzanan identical with Skt áyāt and further continuing IE *ei- ‘to go’: Hitt pa-izi, Toch AB y-, Skt éti, Av aēiti, Gk εἶμι, Lat eō, OIr pass. pret. etha, Lith eimī, εϊτι, Slav *iti. Bartholomae 147–154; Torp- Falk 27; Kluge Beiträge 124–127 (from an augmented aorist); Holthausen IF XIV 342 (to Lat vadere ‘to go’); Collitz Prät. 142–148 (from perfect middle); Meillet MSL XXIII 257 (from an unaugmented imperfect); Feist 288–289; Holthausen AEEW 91; Cowgill Language XXXVI 483–501; Walde- Hofmann I 406–409; Mayrhofer I 128; Pokorny I 293–297; Fraenkel 119; Frisk I 462–463; Trubachev ESSJa VIII 247–248; Seebold 174–176; Lindeman Verschärfung 17.2; Burlak IFTJa 252.

Goth ik ‘I’, ON ek id., OE ic id., OFris ik id., OS ic id., OHG ih id. Continues IE *e·ō id.: Gk ἐγώ, Arm es, Lat egō, OLith eš. Other Indo-European forms differ from *e·ō in phonetic and derivational details (Skt ahám, Av az 6 m, Slav *azъ). Cf. also emphatic OHG ihha. Acc. sg. *meke (Goth mik, ON mek, mik, OE mec, mic, OS mik, OHG mih) continues IE *(e)me ge, cf. Gk ἐμέγε id. Dat. sg. *mez: Goth mis, ON mér, OE mé, OFris mi, OS mī, OHG mir. Gen. sg. *mīna: Goth meina, ON mín, OE mín, OFris mīn, OS mīn, OHG mīn. Bartholomae 225– 227; Torp-Falk 23, 300; Holthausen AEEW 185; Walde-Hofmann I 395–396; Feist 291–292; Porzig Gliedrung 128; Mayrhofer I 68; Pokorny I 418, 702; Fraenkel 18; Vries ANEW 98–99, 384, 386–387; Frisk I 441; Schmidt Pron. 21–48, 54–55; Trubachev ESSJa I 100–103; Lehmann GED 204–205, 254; Kluge-Seebold 392; Boutkan OFED s.v.

ON il ‘footsole’, OE ile ‘footsole, hard skin’, OFris ili id., MLG ele, elde, elt id. Derived from IE *el-: Gk (rare) ἐλάω ‘to drive’, ἐλαύνω id. Torp-Falk 28 (to Lat pl. īlia ‘flanks, entrails’); Persson UUÅ 1891 78 (to OHG īlen ‘to hurry’); Holthausen AEEW 187; Pokorny I 306–307; Vries ANEW 284; Frisk I 482–483; Boutkan OFED s.v.

ON almr ‘elmtree’, OE elm id., OHG elm id. ON reflects an old o-grade. Related to Lat ulmus id. (< *¬mos), MIr lem id. (< *lemos) and Slav *jьlьmъ id. < *(u)lmos with some irregularities in vocalism (Pedersen KZ XXXVIII 313–314). Uhlenbeck Afsl Ph XV 487 (Slav < Gmc); Torp-Falk 26; Holthausen AEEW 90; Walde- Hofmann II 811–812; Pokorny I 303; Chemodanov SSGJa I 57; Vries ANEW 7; Zalizniak I 129; Onions 307; Vasmer- Trubachev II 126–127; Trubachev ESSJa VIII 222–223.

ON def. art. (h)inn, OHG enēr id. Related to Lith anās ‘that one’, Slav *onъ < *ono-. Cf. also Skt instr. anéna ‘through this’, Gk đnh ‘third day’ < *‘yonder day’. Mayrhofer I 32; Pokorny I 320; Fraenkel 10; Vries ANEW 286; Frisk I 514; Lehmann GED 210; Kluge-Seebold 410.

Goth inna ‘within, inside’, ON inni ‘indoors’, OE inne ‘in, within, inside’, OFris inna ‘inside’, OS inna id., OHG inna- id. Locative of *enđē (Feist 293). Brugmann IF XXXIII 304 (from *eni-nō); Holthausen AEEW 188; Feist 293; Vries ANEW 287; Lehmann GED 206; Kluge-Seebold 401.

Goth innana ‘from within, within, inside’, ON innan ‘from within’, OE innan id., OFris inna id., OS innan id., OHG innan, innana ‘inside’. Derived from *enđē. Holthausen AEEW 188; Feist 294; Vries ANEW 286; Kluge-Seebold 401.
ON innan-verär ‘inward’, OE innan-weard, in-weard id., MDu inne-wert, in-wert id., OHG in-wart ‘inside’. Compound of *enđē and *warđaz. Onions 484.

Goth inn ‘in’, ON inn id., OE inn ‘in, within’, OFris in ‘in’, OS īn id., OHG īn id. (with secondary lengthening). Derived from *eni. Close to Gk đndon ‘within’ (Collitz MLN XXXVII 215– 218), Hitt anda(n) ‘in’, Alb ndë. Cf. also comp. *enđizōn > ON innri, iäri ‘inmost’, OE innera, innerra ‘inner, inward’, OFris inra, innere ‘inside, interior’, OHG innaro, inner id. Torp-Falk 25 (extension of *eni by means of a particle *ne); Holthausen AEEW 188; Feist 293; Pokorny I 311– 314, 774; Frisk I 511–512; Vries ANEW 286–287; Onions 466, 476; Tischler HEG I 33–34; Lehmann GED 206; Orel AED 284.

Goth in ‘in’, ON í id., OE in ‘in, within’, OFris in id., OS in id., OHG in id. Identical with Gk ἐν, ἐνί id., Lat in id., OIr in- id., OPrus en id., Lith ⁄ id., Slav *vъn id. Torp-Falk 25; Holthausen AEEW 188; Walde-Hofmann I 687–688; Feist 292; Pokorny I 311–312; Fraenkel 181; Vries ANEW 282; Frisk I 508–509; Onions 466; Lehmann GED 205; Kluge-Seebold 397.

ON ekki ‘convulsive sobbing’, OE inca ‘doubt, question, cause of complaint’. Related to Lith éngti ‘to strangle, to squeeze, to torment’, Slav *ęga ~ *ęza ‘witch, illness, wrath’ (Pogodin RFV XXXIII 328; Lidén Studien 70–71). Torp-Falk 28; Trautmann BSW 70; Holthausen IF XVII 295, AEEW 188; Pokorny I 13; Fraenkel 10, 1163–1164; Vries ANEW 99; Stang LS 25; Trubachev ESSJa VI 68–69; Dybo BSA 201.
Goth igqar ‘your (dual)’, ON ykkar id., OE incer id., OS inca id. Derived from *enkwara (see *jutwō ~ *jitwō). Holthausen AEEW 188; Feist 291; Lehmann GED 204.
ON økkr ‘glans, glandula’. Related to Gk ἀδήν ‘gland’ < *–g⁄ēn, Lat inguen ‘groin, abdomen’ (Bugge BB III 115). Saussure MSL VI 53; Torp-Falk 25; Walde-Hofmann I 701; Pokorny I 319; Frisk I 20.

ON ístr ‘fat of the paunch’, MLG inster, inser ‘intestines of a slaughtered animal’. Close to Latv îstri ‘kidneys’, OPrus instran ‘fat’ < *–stro- (Förstemann KZ XIX 355). Further to Gk ἐντός ‘within, inside’, Lat intus id. < *entos. See *enþran. Torp-Falk 25; Endzelin KZ LII 121; Walde-Hofmann I 713; Trautmann BSW 69–70; Pokorny I 182, 314; Vries ANEW 287; Fraenkel 188; Frisk I 525; Stang LS 25; Toporov PJa III 54–55.

ON pl. iär, innr ‘bowels, entrails’. Identical with IE *entro- > Skt neut. antrá- ‘intestine’, Gk ἔντερα id., Arm pl. 6 nder-k' id., Slav *ętro ‘liver, intestines’. Torp-Falk 25; Pokorny I 313; Mayrhofer I 36; Vries ANEW 283; Frisk I 524–525; Trubachev ESSJa VI 72–73.
ON jarkna-steinn ‘gem’, OE eorcnan-stán ‘precious stone, pearl’. Compound of *erknaz and *stainaz. Holthausen AEEW 92; Pokorny I 65.

Goth un-airkns ‘impure’, OHG ercan ‘holy, sublime’. Related to Toch A yärk, B yarke ‘adoration, reverence’ but to be separated from the family of Skt árcati ‘to shine, to adore’. Osthoff Morph. Unt. VI 33 (to Gk ἀργός ‘white, quick’); Torp-Falk 26; Feist 25; Holthausen AEEW 92; Pokorny I 65 (“Kreuzung” of *ar·- ‘white’ und *erk⁄- ‘to light, to shine’); Meid IF LXIX 245; Lacy JIES VII 287–296 (from IE *er-·nos ‘having a good birth’); Lehmann GED 18; Lühr Expr. 339; Bammesberger Nom. 246; Heidermanns 174–175.

ON jarl ‘earl, leader, nobleman’, OE eorl ‘nobleman of high rank’, OS erl id., OHG erl- id. (in cmpn.). Related to *erōn. Probably can be directly compared with Thrac prn. Ὀλόρος, Oroles (Tomaschek Thr. II/2 29; Kretschmer Einleitung 214, 221) unless the latter is to Slav *orьlъ ‘eagle’. Torp-Falk 25; Holthausen AEEW 92; Detschew Thr. 341; Pokorny I 326 (to *arōn), 328; Vries ANEW 290; Zalizniak I 130; Onions 297; Lehmann GED 40. *ermena-zrunđiz ~ *ermuna-zrunđan sb.f./n.: ON jǫrmun-grund ‘Earth’, OE eormen-grund id. Based on *ermenaz ~ *ermunaz and *zrunđuz ~ *zrunþuz. Carr Nom. comp. 58.

Goth prop. Ermeni-ricus, ON jǫrmun-gandr ‘great monster’, Jǫrmunr (name of Odin), OE eormencyn ‘mankind, human race’, OS irmin-man ‘man’, OHG irmin-sūl ‘tall column’. Of uncertain origin. Probably related to Gk ὄρμενος ‘high’, Slav *ormînъ ‘strong, large’. Brückner KZ XLV 107–108 (to Gk ὄρνυμι ‘to urge, to incite’); Torp- Falk 18; Feist 132; Holthausen AEEW 92; Walde-Pokorny I 183–184 (to Lat armentum ‘cattle for plowing, herd’); Pokorny I 58; Beeler Festschr. Wolff 9–21 (to *erōn); Vries ANEW 295 (to Skt aryaman- ‘companion, host, god of hospitality’); Vasmer-Trubachev III 441; Hilmarsson TIES III 65–75 (to Toch A yärm, B yarm ‘size, measure’); Lehmann GED 100; Heidermanns 175–175.

ON jara ‘fight, battle’. Connected with Skt ṛṇóti ‘to arise, to move, to reach, to attack’, Av 6 r 6 naoiti ‘to move’, Gk ὄρνυμι ‘to urge, to incite’, Lat orior ‘to rise, to get up’. Cf. especially Skt sam-ará- ‘fight, battle’. Bartholomae 183–184; Walde-Hofmann II 222–223; Mayrhofer I 122; Pokorny I 332; Frisk II 422–424.

Burg *erps ‘brown’, ON jarpr id., OE eorp ‘dark, dusky, brown’, OS prop. Erp-hund, OHG erpf ‘dark brown’. Unclear. Hardly to Gk ὀρφνός ‘dark brown’ (~ Arm arún id. < *org ⁄hen-). Osthoff Etym. I 78–79 (< *ereb(h)-, cf. Lith jérbë ‘hazel-grouse’); Torp-Falk 332; Holthausen AEEW 93; Karsten NB II 220; Pokorny I 334; Vries ANEW 291; Frisk II 431–432; Köbler 709; Heidermanns 176–177.
Swed jord-bær ‘strawberry’, OE fem. eorä-berize id., MDu erdbere id., OHG erd-beri id. Compound of *erþō and *bazjan. Froehde BB XVII 310 (to Lat arbutus ‘wild strawberry-tree’); Carr Nom. comp. 47.
ON jarä-borg ‘earth stronghold’, OE eorä-burh ‘earth mound’, OHG erd-burg ‘earth stronghold’. Compound of *erþō and *burzz. Carr Nom. comp. 47.
ON jarä-fall ‘earthslip’, OFris erth-fall ‘fall to the ground’, MLG erd-fal id., OHG erd-fal ‘earth-slip’. Compound of *erþō and *fallaz. Carr Nom. comp. 75 (parallel formations).
ON jarä-fastr ‘fixed in the earth’, OE eorä-fæst id., OFris erth-fest id., MLG erd-fast id., early G erdfest id. Compound of *erþō and *fastaz. Carr Nom. comp. 60.
Swed jord-nöt ‘earthnut’, OE eorä-hnutu id., MDu erd-note id., OHG erd-nuz id. Compound of *erþō and *xnutz. Carr Nom. comp. 47.
ON jarä-hús ‘cave, underground home’, OE eorä-hús ‘earthhouse, den’, OS erth-hūs ‘cave, underground home’, OHG erd-hūs id. Compound of *erþō and *xūsan. Carr Nom. comp. 47.
Goth airþa-kunds ‘of earthly birth’, OE eorä-cund ‘earthly, terrestrial’. Compound of *erþō and *kunđaz. Carr Nom. comp. 88 (calque of Lat terrigenus).
ON jarä-vegr ‘earth’, OE eorä-wez ‘earth, earth-way’. Compound of *erþō and *wezaz. Carr Nom. comp. 54.

Goth airþa ‘earth’, ON jǫrä id. (partly < *erþiz), OE eoräe id. (< *erþōn), eorä id., OFris erthe id., OS ertha id., OHG erda id. Related to or derived from *erwōn (Bremer ZDADL XXXI 205–206). Torp-Falk 26, 560; Holthausen AEEW 93; Feist 25–26; Pokorny I 332; Vries ANEW 295; Zalizniak II 195; Onions 298; Lehmann GED 18; Bammesberger Nom. 116; Kluge-Seebold 228–229.

ON jǫrfi‘gravel’, OHG ero ‘earth’. Close to Toch B yare ‘gravel’, W erw ‘field’, Corn erw id., OBret eru ‘furrow’, Gk đr` id. and probably to Phryg dat. sg. eiroi ‘earth, earthly (?)’ (Orel Phrygian 71). Schwyzer I 424; Torp- Falk 26; Pokorny I 332; Vries ANEW 295; Frisk I 546–547; Chantraine 363; Lehmann GED 18.
Goth airziþa ‘deception, delusion’, OHG irrida ‘heresy’. Derived from *erzjaz. Feist 27; Heidermanns 177.
Goth airzjan ‘to mislead, to delude’, OS irrian ‘to dissolve’, OHG irren ‘to entangle’. Derived from *erzjaz. Feist 27; Lehmann GED 19; Heidermanns 177.

Goth airzeis ‘deluded, erring, misled’, OE eorre ‘angry, enraged, fierce’, OFris īre ‘furious’, OS irri id., OHG irri ‘stray’. Related to Lat errō ‘to go astray’, Arm efiam ‘to be restless’ < *ersāƒō (Kretschmer KZ XXXI 184). Torp-Falk 26; Walde-Hofmann I 416–417; Feist 27; Holthausen AEEW 186–187; Pokorny I 336–337; Lehmann GED 19–20; Heidermanns 177–178; Kluge-Seebold 406. *esti athem.: Goth ist ‘is’, ON es id., OE is id., OFris is, ist id., OS is, ist id., OHG ist id. Continues IE *es-ti id.: Hitt ēšzi, Skt ásti, Av asti, Gk ἐστί, Lat est, OIr is, Lith ‚sti, Slav *estь, Alb është. See *wesanan I. Bartholomae 266–279; Torp-Falk 27; Feist 292; Holthausen AEEW 92; Walde-Hofmann II 628–629; Kluge 700–701; Lane Language XXX 113; Jóhannesson IEW 78–79; Mayrhofer I 67; Pokorny I 340–342; Fraenkel 124; Vries ANEW 101, 105; Frisk I 463–464; Seebold 176–178; Trubachev ESSJa VI 32; Lehmann GED 205; Puhvel II 285–291; Orel AED 156.

Goth itan ‘to eat’, ON eta id., OE etan id., OFris eta id., OS etan id., OHG ezzan id. Related to Hitt e-id-mi id., Skt átti id., Gk đdv id., Arm utem id., Lat edō id., OIr ith id., Lith ėmi id., Slav *îdmь id. (Balto-Slavic lengthening due to Winter’s law) Torp-Falk 24, 232; Brugmann Grundriß II/3 96; Trautmann BSW 66; Feist 163, 296–297; Holthausen AEEW 94, 116; Walde-Hofmann I 392–393; Jóhannesson IEW 53–54; Mayrhofer I 28; Pokorny I 287–289; Fraenkel 124–125; Vries ANEW 106; Frisk I 444–445; Onions 298, 376; Chantraine 312–313; Seebold 179–180; Trubachev ESSJa VI 53–54; Lehmann GED 208; Puhvel II 315–320; Kluge- Seebold 235, 285.
ON etall ‘consuming’, OE etol ‘voracious, gluttonous’, OHG filu-ezzal ‘greedy’. Derived from *etanan. Holthausen AEEW 94; Seebold 179–180; Heidermanns 178–179.

ON jǫtunn ‘giant’, OE eóten id. The meanings of *etulaz make a tentative connection with *etanan probable. Schütte IF XV 266 (to the ethnonym Etiones); Torp-Falk 24; Holthausen AEEW 93; Vries ANEW 295–296; Bammesberger Nom. 82.

Goth aiþþau ‘else, or’, ON eäa id., OE eääa, oääa id., OS ettho id., OHG edo id. An unclear compound, probably continuing *ef þau (Grienberger Got. 17) or *ex þau (Cercignani JIES XII 315–344). Schmidt Studien 85–95 (reconstructs *eft-þau); Johansson BB XIII 120–122; Holthausen AEEW 94; Feist 29; Pokorny I 344; Vries ANEW 93 (*eþ- to Lat et); Lehmann GED 21; Kluge-Seebold 597. ## ē
ON júgr ‘udder’, OFris jāder id., OS geder id. A secondary ablaut variant of *ūđaran. Torp-Falk 29;

OE æäm ‘vapor, breath, smell’, OFris ēthma ‘breath’ (n-stem), OS āđum id., OHG ātum ‘spirit, life breath’. Continues IE *ēt-mo- close to *ēt-men- reflected in Skt ātmán- ‘breath, soul, self’. Torp-Falk 24; Holthausen AEEW 13; Mayrhofer I 73; Pokorny I 39, 345; Chemodanov SGGJa I 106; Zalizniak I 130; Schindler Sprache XIII 202; Lloyd-Springer I 391–393; Bammesberger Nom. 71–72; Kluge-Seebold 59; Boutkan OFED s.v.

ON adv. áär ‘ere, already, soon’, OE adv. ædre ‘quickly, promptly, at once’, OS adv. ādro ‘early’, OHG ātar ‘swift, eager’. Related to Lith otrūs ‘lively’, Latv ãtrs ‘swift’. Torp-Falk 559–560; Holthausen AEEW 9; Schneider KZ LXVI 249–251 (to Toch A atär ‘hero’); Pokorny I 345; Chemodanov SGGJa I 77; Fraenkel 518–519; Vries ANEW 2; Lloyd-Springer I 379–381; Heidermanns 173.

OE ædre, ædr ‘artery, vein, sinew’, MLG ādere, āder ‘sinew’, OHG ādra ‘blood vessel, sinew’. Related to Gk Hom ἦτορ ‘heart’, OIr inathar ‘intestines’ < *en-ōtro- (Fick I 366). Cf. also ON fem. æär ‘vein’ < *ēđiz. Schmidt Plur. 198; Torp-Falk 24–25, 560; Holthausen AEEW 9; Pokorny I 344; Vries ANEW 680; Zalizniak I 221; Frisk I 645; Lloyd-Springer I 54–57; Kluge-Seebold 15.

ON ægir ‘sea, ocean’, OE æz ‘water, island’, æze ‘island’. Long grade derivative of *axwō. Pokorny I 23; Vries ANEW 681; Szemerényi VJa 1967/4 19 (to Slav Noreen SVSU V/3 39–43; Pokorny I 347; Vries ANEW 294; Zalizniak II 184; Onions 954; Kluge-Seebold 237–238.

ON áll ‘colored stripe along the horse’s back’. Identical with Arm il ‘spindle’ < *ēlo-. Lidén SHVS Uppsala VI 82 (to Skt āra- ‘cavity’); Pokorny I 308–310 (to Skt āli- ‘row, line’ but the latter is from *āḍi-); Vries ANEW 6; Zalizniak I 130.

ON áll ‘eel’, OE æl id., OFris ēl id., OS āl id., OHG āl id. Historically identical with *ēlaz I (Uhlenbeck PBB XXXV 162). Schröder ZDADL XLII 63 (from *ēdlo- ‘eater’); Loewenthal Wu S X 144 (to Skt ālam ‘discharge from a venomous animal’); Torp-Falk 26; Holthausen AEEW 10; Pokorny I 309–310; Vries ANEW 6; Zalizniak I 130; Onions 302; Lloyd-Springer I 133–135; Kluge-Seebold 1 (to *alinō).

ON alr ‘awl’ (< *alaz), OE æl id., OS āl id., OHG āla id. (< *ēlō). Identical with Skt ārā id. Torp-Falk 26; Holthausen AEEW 10; Mayrhofer I 78; Pokorny I 16; Vries ANEW 7; Dybo VSJa 26; Onions 66; Zalizniak II 191; Lloyd-Springer I 135–136; Kluge- Seebold 20.

ON ámr ‘black, loathsome (?)’, OE sbst. óm ‘rust’, G dial. sbst. ahm, ohm ‘blast (in corn), erysipelas’. Of unknown origin. Wood Language VIII 213 (to Lat āreō ‘to be dry’); Holthausen AEEW 241; Vries ANEW 8.

Goth inu ‘without’, ON án id., OFris ōne id., OS āno id., OHG ānu id. Related to Skt ánu- ‘after, along’, Av anu ‘along’, Gk ἄνευ ‘without, away from’. Brugmann Grundriß II/2 837 (to Osset änä ‘without’); Bartholomae 127; Torp-

ON át ‘act of eating’, OE æt ‘food’ (masc., fem.), OFris ēt id., OS āt id., OHG āz ‘carrion’. Together with Lith ėda id., Latv pl. êdas id., Slav *îda id. continues IE *ēdom ~ *ēdā. For further connections see *etanan. Torp-Falk 24; Trautmann BSW 66; Holthausen AEEW 13; Pokorny I 288; Fraenkel 125; Vries ANEW 17; Zalizniak I 139; Seebold 180; Lehmann GED 4, 208, 385; Trubaf

Goth voc. fadar ‘father’ (hap. leg.), ON faäir id., OE fæder id., OFris feder, fader id., OS fadar id., OHG fater id. Identical with Toch A pācar, B pācer id., Skt pitar- id., Gk πατήρ id., Lat pater id., Arm hayr id., OIr athir id. Torp-Falk 227; Holthausen AEEW 95; Walde- Hofmann II 262–264; Feist 133; Mayrhofer II 277–278; Pokorny I 829; Vries ANEW 109; Frisk II 481–482; Onions 347; Benveniste IEL 169–170; Lehmann GED 101; Bammesberger Nom. 207–208; Kluge-Seebold 853; Adams TB 365.

Goth bruþ-faþs ‘bridegroom’. Identical with Toch A pats, B pets ‘husband’, Skt páti- ‘master, owner, husband’, Av paiti- ‘master, spouse’, Gk πόσις ‘husband’, Lat potis ‘capable’, Lith pāts ‘married man’ (Meillet Wu S XII 17–20). Bartholomae 821; Schrader II 337– 340; Torp-Falk 227; Feist 109–110; Mayrhofer II 200–201; Pokorny I 842; Fraenkel 551–552; Frisk II 584; Lehmann GED 82–83. chev ESSJa VI 38; Bammesberger Nom. 62–63.
ON ætr ‘edible’, OE ofer-æte ‘gluttonous’. Derived from *etanan. Holthausen AEEW 13; Matzel Festschr. Schröder 103, 117, KZ CV 112; Heidermanns 178.

Goth af-etja ‘glutton’, OE selfæta ‘cannibal’. Structurally similar to Skt ādyá- ‘edible’, Lith ėdžia ‘fodder, glutton’, Slav *îdja ‘food’. Derived from *etanan or directly from *ētan. Holthausen AEEW 13; Feist 5; Mayrhofer I 74; Fraenkel 125; Seebold 180; Lehmann GED 4.
Goth uz-eta ‘manger, crib’, MLG or-ēte, or-āte ‘remainder of animal feed, straw’. Derived from *ētan. Feist 538; Lehmann GED 385.
ON pl. feägar ‘father and son’. Identical with Gk πατρικός ‘fatherly’. Derived from *fađēr. Pokorny I 829; Vries ANEW 114; Frisk II 481; Lehmann GED 101.

OE fædera ‘father’s brother’, OFris federia id., OHG fatureo, fetiro id. Identical with Skt pitṛvya- ‘paternal uncle’, Av tūiryō id., Lat pātruus ‘father’s brother, paternal uncle’ < *p 6 tṛ⁄ƒo-. See *fađēr. Bartholomae 657; Holthausen AEEW 95; Walde-Hofmann II 263; Mayrhofer II 278–279; Pokorny I 829; Lehmann GED 101; Kluge-Seebold 863.

ON feginn ‘glad, joyful’, OE fæzen ‘glad, joyful’, OS fagan, fagin ‘glad’, OHG gi-fagan ‘contented’. Participle of *fexanan. Gutmacher Btrg. Gesch. XXXIX 69–71; Torp-Falk 225; Feist 134; Holthausen AEEW 95; Wissmann NP 14; Pokorny I 796–797; Vries ANEW 115; Onions 342; Seebold 189; Lehmann GED 102; Heidermanns 180–181.

Goth faginon ‘to feel happy, to rejoice’, ON fagna ‘to be fain, to rejoice’, OE fæzenian ‘to rejoice’, OS faganōn id., OHG feginōn id. Derived from *fazanaz ~ *fazenaz. Zupitza Gutt. 188; Torp-Falk 225; Holthausen AEEW 95; Feist 134; Vries ANEW 109; Onions 348; Seebold 189; Lehmann GED 102.

Goth fagrs ‘proper, suitable’, ON fagr ‘fair, bright’, OE fæzer ‘fair, beautiful’, OS fagar id., OHG fagar ‘beautiful, bright, splendid’. Identical with Toch A pākär, B pākri ‘manifest, apparent’, Skt pajrá- ‘firm, solid’, Umbr pacer ‘bent’ (Brugmann IF IX 349). See *fanxanan, *fexanan. Schweizer KZ I 155; Braune PBB XLIII 369; Zupitza Gutt. 188; Karsten NB II 252; Torp-Falk 224; Feist 134; Holthausen AEEW 95; Wissmann NP 14, 109, 123; Mayrhofer II 186; Pokorny I 796–797; Vries ANEW 109 (to *fanxanan or *fēzōjanan); Mayrhofer II 186; Onions 342; Lehmann GED 101; Bammesberger Nom. 248; Heidermanns 181–182.
ON fegrä ‘beauty, fairness’, OS gi-fagiritha ‘ornament’. Derived from *fazraz or *fazrjanan ~ *faxrjanan. Heidermanns 182.
ON fegri ‘beauty’, OHG fagarī ‘splendor’. Derived from *fazraz. Heidermanns 181.
Goth ga-fahrjan ‘to put in proper condition, to make suitable’, ON fegra ‘to adorn’. Derived from *fazraz. Feist 180; Vries ANEW 115; Heidermanns 182.
ON fær ‘sheep’. Close to Gk πόκος ‘wool, fleece’ (Fick BB I 60) further related to *fexu. Schmidt Plur. 149; Torp-Falk 225; Pokorny I 797; Vries ANEW 149; Frisk II 492–493.

ON fax ‘mane’, OE feax ‘hair’, OFris fax id., OS fahs id., OHG fahs id. Based on an original s-stem as in Gk πέκος ‘fleece’. Derived from *faxez. Zupitza Gutt. 189; Torp-Falk 225; Holthausen AEEW 100; Pokorny I 797; Vries ANEW 114; Zalizniak II 167; Frisk II 492–493; Kluge-Seebold 254.

OSwed fæt ‘wool, fleece’, OE feht, fieht ‘sheepskin with wool’, OFris fecht ‘wool, fleece’, Du vacht id. Related to *faxsan. Cf. also *fextanan. Torp-Falk 225; Holthausen AEEW 103; Pokorny I 797; Vries ANEW 149.

ON feigr ‘death-bound’, OE fæze ‘fated, doomed’, OS fēgi ‘death-bound’, OHG feigi id. Related to *faixaz II (Bezzenberger BB XXVII 176; Wiedemann BB XXVIII 36–38). Osthoff KZ III (NF) 427–428 (to Skt pakvá- ‘cooked, baked, ripe’); Schütt Zd Wf XI 274–275; Zupitza Gutt. 189; Torp-Falk 223 (to Lith paĩkas ‘stupid’); Holthausen AEEW 95, 420; Kluge 207–208; Karsten NB II 444; Pokorny I 795; Vries ANEW 115; Onions 353; Lehmann GED 68; Huldapud Jones- Bley Festschr. Puhvel I 214–215 (to *faixaz I); Heidermanns 182–183; Kluge- Seebold 256.
ON feigä ‘approaching death’, OE fæzä ‘hostility’. Derived from *faizaz. Cf. *faixiþō. Holthausen AEEW 95; Vries ANEW 115; Heidermanns 183.

Goth filu-faihs ‘multicolored, manifold’, ON blá-fár ‘in blue speckles’, OE fáh, fáz ‘colored’, OS fēh id., OHG fēh id. Identical with Skt péśas- ‘shape, form, color’, Av paēs- ‘to color, to ornament’, Gk ποικίλος ‘colorful’, Toch A peke ‘tablet, painting’ < *poi˚o-. Zupitza Gutt. 132; Bartholomae 817; Torp-Falk 241; Feist 153–154; Holthausen AEEW 97; Karsten NB I 45–46; Porzig Gliederung 136 (short form of the original *filu-faixaz ‘with many stains’); Mayrhofer II 342; Pokorny I 794–795; Vries ANEW 112; Frisk II 572–573; Lehmann GED 116; Bammesberger Nom. 237; Heidermanns 183–184; Kluge-Seebold 255.

OE fáh ‘guilty, criminal, hostile, inimical’, OFris fāch ‘criminal’, MDu vee ‘hostile, inimical’, OHG gi-fēh ‘hostile’. Identical with Lith paĩkas ‘stupid’ (Zupitza KZ XXXVII 401). Any connection with *faixaz I? Zupitza Gutt. 189 (to *faixaz I); Torp-Falk 240–241; Holthausen AEEW 97; Wissmann Postv. 79– 81; Pokorny I 795; Fraenkel 525; Lehmann GED 68; Heidermanns 184–185; Kluge-Seebold 255; Boutkan OFED s.v.
ON fá ‘to draw, to paint’, OHG fēhen ‘to decorate’. Derived from *faixaz I. Torp-Falk 241; Vries ANEW 108; Lehmann GED 116; Heidermanns 183.

ON feikn ‘fright, misfortune’, OE fácen ‘deceit, fraud’, OS fēkan ‘guile, malice’, OHG feihhan id. Substantivized *faiknaz. Zupitza Gutt. 161; Torp-Falk 241; Holthausen AEEW 94; Pokorny I 795; Vries ANEW 115 (to *faixaz II); Zalizniak I 139; Heidermanns 185.
ON pl. feikn-stafir ‘bane, evil’, OE fácen-stæf ‘deceitful deed’. Compound of *faiknan and *stabaz. Carr Nom. comp. 54.

ON feikn ‘perishable’, OE fæcne ‘deceitful, fraudulent, wicked’, OS fēkni ‘guileful, malicious’, OHG feihhan ‘insidious’. Related to Lat impers. piget ‘to annoy’ (Curtius Gr. Et. 164). Karsten NB II 449; Torp-Falk 241; Holthausen AEEW 94–95; Walde-Hofmann II 300– 301; Feist 89–90; Pokorny I 795; Vries ANEW 115; Krause Run. 122; Lühr Expr. 337–338 (to *faixaz II); Heidermanns 184–185.

Norw dial. feim ‘foam’, OE fám id., OHG masc. feim id. Related to other phonetically diverging words for ‘foam’: Skt phéna- ‘foam, froth’, Sogd pym’kh id., Lat spūma id., pūmex ‘pumice’, Slav *pîna ‘foam’, Lith spaĩnë id. Weinhold KZ I 247; Persson Beiträge 690; Pedersen IF V 80; Meillet Etudes 445; Torp-Falk 224; Trautmann BSW 227– 228; Holthausen AEEW 98; Walde- Hofmann II 388–389; Mayrhofer II 399; Pokorny I 1001 (reconstructs *spoimno-); Fraenkel 858; Onions 366; Vasmer-Trubachev III 231; Kluge- Seebold 256.

ON feima ‘bashful girl, young lass’, OE fæmne ‘virgin, maid, woman’, OFris fāmne ‘young woman’, OS fēmea id. Fem. of unattested *faimōn ‘shepherd, herder’ related to Gk ποιμήν id., Lith piemuõ id. Cf. in particular Lith piem‚në ‘female shepherd’. Torp-Falk 240 (to Av paēman- ‘milk’); Fraenkel 585; Frisk II 573.

ON feitr ‘fat’, MLG veet id., MHG veiz id. Related to the IE adjective reflected in Skt pīvan- ‘swelling, fat, strong’, Gk πίων id. Torp-Falk 241; Holthausen AEEW 97; Karsten NB I 59; Mayrhofer II 297–298; Pokorny I 794 (to Gk πιδύω ‘to gush forth’); Vries ANEW 115–116; Frisk II 532; Onions 346–347; Heidermanns 186–187; Kluge- Seebold 257, 261.
ON feiti ‘fatness’, MHG veize id. Derived from *faitaz. Heidermanns 186.
ON feita ‘to fatten’, OE part. fæt id., OHG feizen id. Derived from *faitaz. Holthausen AEEW 97; Vries ANEW 116; Onions 346–347; Heidermanns 186.

ON falr ‘for sale’, OHG fali ‘saleable’. Related to Gk πωλέω ‘to sell’, Lith pefinas ‘profit, yield’. Torp-Falk 237; Kartsen NB I 66; Pokorny I 804; Fraenkel 567–568; Vries ANEW 110– 111; Frisk II 633; Matzel KZ CV 110; Heidermanns 187; Kluge-Seebold 256.
ON marg-faldligr ‘manifold’, OHG ein-faltlīh ‘simple’. Derived from *falđaz. Heidermanns 188.

Goth -falþs ‘-fold’, ON -faldr id., OE -feald id., OFris -fald id., OS -fald id., OHG -falt, -falto id. Appears only as the second element of adjectival compounds (see *aina-falđaz). Derived from *falþanan. Torp-Falk 238; Feist 23; Holthausen AEEW 99; Wissmann NP 33; Onions 366–367; Heidermanns 187– 188; Kluge-Seebold 247–248; Boutkan OFED s.v.

ON feldr ‘cloak’, OE fyld ‘fold, volume’. May be related to Gk πέλτη ‘small shield’ and probably (with phonetic difficulties) to Skt pa†a- ‘woven cloth, garment, blanket’. In any case, further related to *falþanan. Mayrhofer II 190; Vries ANEW 117; Frisk II 501; Seebold 184.
ON marg-falda ‘to multiply’, OE ze-meniz-fealdan id., OHG manag-faltōn id. Derived from *falđaz. Vries ANEW 110; Seebold 184; Heidermanns 188.
ON falr ‘socket of a spear’s head in which the handle is put’, OE fealh ‘harrow’ (fem.). Derived from *felxanan (Lidén apud Vries). Zupitza Gutt. 132; Holthausen AEEW 99; Vries ANEW 110.
ON fálki ‘falcon’, MLG falke id., OHG falco id. Reflects *pol-·-, a variant of *pol-˚- in Lith pálšas ‘pale’, Latv pālss id. See *falwaz (Torp-Falk 239). Pokorny I 805.

ON fall ‘fall’, OE fem. (?) feal ‘trap, pitfall’, OFris fal id., OS fal id., OHG fal ‘fall’. Directly derived from *fallanan ‘to fall’ or substantivized *fallaz. Torp-Falk 239; Holthausen AEEW 99; Vries ANEW 110; Zalizniak II 186; Bammesberger Nom. 54.

ON falla ‘to fall’, OE feallan id., OFris falla id., OS fallan id., OHG fallan id. Reflects *pol-n- or *pol-. Connected with Arm p'lanim ‘to fall’, Lith púolu, pūlti id., Latv pult id., OPrus au-pallai ‘to find’. Wood GR I 309 (to Gk πίπτω ‘to fall’); Bugge KZ XXXII 28– 30; Torp-Falk 238–239; Holthausen AEEW 99; Jóhannesson IEW 582–583; Pokorny I 851; Fraenkel 666; Vries ANEW 110; Onions 343; Seebold 181– 182; Toporov PJa I 159–160; Kluge- Seebold 247; Boutkan OFED s.v.
ON vá-fallr ‘making a dangerous fall, tottering’, OFris pl. gers-falle ‘cut off, fallen’. Derived from *fallanan ‘to fall’. Karsten NB I 79–80; Heidermanns 188–189.
ON hug-fellr ‘pleasing’, OFris gars-felle ‘falling into the grass, hewn off’. Derived from *fallanan. Karsten NB I 111; Matzel Festschr. Schröder 106, KZ CV 127; Heidermanns 189; Boutkan OFED s.v.

ON fella ‘to fell, to make fall’, OE fyllan ‘to fell, to cut down’, OFris fella, falla ‘to fell’, OS fellian id., OHG fellen id. Causative of *fallanan. Torp-Falk 238; Holthausen AEEW 119; Vries ANEW 118; Onions 350; Kluge-Seebold 247; Boutkan OFED s.v.

Goth falþan ‘to fold’, ON falda ‘to cover one’s head’, OE fealdan ‘to fold up’, MLG volden id. (wk., with str. part.), OHG faldan id. Continues *pel-t- further connected with Gk ē-πλόος ‘simple’, Lat sim-plus id. (Brugmann IF XXXVIII 128–130). Torp-Falk 238; Feist 141–142; Holthausen AEEW 99; Walde-Hofmann II 540; Kluge 182– 183; Jóhannesson IEW 557; Pokorny I 802–803, 834–835; Vries ANEW 110; Frisk I 121–122; Onions 366; Seebold 183–184; Peeters IF XCIV 295; Lehmann GED 107–108; Kluge-Seebold 248.

Dan fald ‘herd of sheep’, OE falod, feald ‘fold, enclosure for animals, fold’ (neut.), OS faled id., OHG fald, falt ‘fold’. Derivationally close to Slav *polъtъ ‘side, layer’ < *pol-uti- further related to alb palë ‘pair, fold’, slav *polъ ‘half’. Torp-Falk 236; Marstrander NTS VII 347–349 (to Mir aile, gen. ailed ‘fence’); Holthausen AEEW 97; Onions 366; Vasmer-Trubachev III 316; Lehmann GED 107; Orel AED 309.

burg *falus ‘fallow’, on fǫlr ‘wan, pale’, OE fealu ‘fallow, yellow’, OS falu ‘pale yellow’, OHG falo ‘pale, reddish yellow’. Identical with Lith pafivas ‘wan, pale’, Slav *polvъ ‘yellow, whitish’ (Kuhn KZ I 516). Further connected with Gk πολιός ‘gray’ < *poli Wός, Arm alik' ‘white hair’ and other continuants of IE *pel-. Karsten NB II 194–195; Persson SVSU X 32, Beiträge 808; Schwentner FB 83–86; Torp-Falk 239; Schwyzer ZDADL LXVI 95–96; Trautmann BSW 213; Holthausen AEEW 99; Porzig Gliederung 204; Pokorny I 804; Chemodanov SGGJa I 68; Fraenkel 534; Vries ANEW 150; Frisk II 575–576; Onions 343; Vasmer-Trubachev III 313; Bammesberger Nom. 243; Köbler 710; Heidermanns 189–190; Kluge-Seebold 244–245.
ON fǫlski ‘ash over burning ambers’, OHG falawisca ‘hot ashes’. Derived from *falwaz. Torp- Falk 239; Vries ANEW 150.

Goth ga-fah ‘catching, catch’, ON fang ‘catching fish’, OE fanz ‘booty’ (masc.), OHG gi-fang ‘clothing’. Related to *fanxanan. Holthausen AEEW 98; Feist 179–180; Porzig Gliederung 104, 127; Vries ANEW 110, 117; Onions 344; Seebold 185; Lehmann GED 102; Bammesberger Nom. 134.

ON fengr ‘gain, booty’, OE fenz ‘grasp, span’, OFris basa-fang ‘obscene grip’, OS ana-fang ‘touch’, OHG ant-fang ‘reception, acceptance’. Substantivized *fanziz II. Torp-Falk 224; Holthausen AEEW 100; Seebold 185– 186; Boutkan OFED s.v.

ON bráä-fengr ‘hot, hasty’, OE and-fenze ‘acceptable, approved, fit’, OFris fensze ‘suitable’, OHG ant-fengi ‘acceptable, pleasant’. Derived from *fanxanan. Karsten NB I 111–112; Holthausen AEEW 100; Vries ANEW 118; Seebold 186; Matzel Festschr. Schröder 106, KZ CV 127; Heidermanns 190.

Goth fahan ‘to catch, to capture’, ON fá ‘to fetch, to catch’, OE fón ‘to grasp, to catch, to seize’, OFris fā id., OS fāhan id., OHG fāhan id. Related to Lat pangō ‘to fortify’, Gk πήγνυμι ‘to stick, to fix in’ (Froehde BB XVI 189). Further connected with *fazraz. Zupitza Gutt. 6, 189; Brugmann Grundriß II/3 291; Torp-Falk 224; Feist 134– 135; Holthausen AEEW 112; Walde- Hofmann II 245–246; Jóhannesson IEW 531–532; Pokorny I 788; Vries ANEW 108; Frisk II 525–526; Onions 344; Seebold 185–186; Lehmann GED 102– 103; Kluge-Seebold 249; Boutkan OFED s.v.

Goth fani ‘clay, mud’, ON fen ‘fen, quagmire’, OE fen ‘fen, marsh, mud, dirt’ (masc. and neut.), OFris fenne ‘wet meadow’ (masc., fem.), OS feni, fenni ‘swamp, fen’, OHG fenni id. Etymologically connected with Thrac pln. Πάνιον, Gaul anam ‘paludem’, MIr en ‘water’, an id., enach ‘swamp’, OPrus pannean ‘quagmire’ (Bopp Glossarium 224; Lidén BB XXI 93; Būga Roczn. Sław. VI 37). Schulze Kl. Schr. 716 (to *funōn, as ‘fire-colored’); Kuhn WF XII 7; Endzelin KZ LII 116; Torp-Falk 228; Holthausen AEEW 101; Feist 142; Pokorny I 807–808; Vries ANEW 118; Zalizniak II 174; Stang LS 40–41; Duridanov Trakite 44; Lehmann GED 108; Kluge- Seebold 258.

Goth fana ‘piece of cloth, rag’, ON -fani ‘flag’ (in compounds), OE fana ‘flag’, OFris fana, fona ‘flag’, OS fano ‘cloth’, OHG fano ‘flag’. Related to Gk πῆνος: ὕφασμα Hes., πήνη ‘cloth’, Lat pannus id. (Curtius Gr. Et. 275–276; Osten-Sacken IF XXXIII 238). Torp- Falk 227; Holthausen AEEW 98; Walde-Hofmann II 247–248; Feist 142; Specht Festschr. Voretzsch 40 (same as Curtius and Osten-Sacken); Trier Lehm 27–28 (to *fanjan); Pokorny I 788; Vries ANEW 111; Zalizniak I 153; Frisk II 529–530; Lehmann GED 108; Kluge- Seebold 245.

ON fattr ‘bowed backward’. Identical with Lat pandus ‘bent, crooked, curved’ (Bugge KZ XIX 437–438; Vani‘ek 333). Torp-Falk 228 (to *fenþanan); Holthausen IF XLVIII 255 (to Πάνδαρος in Asia Minor); Walde- Hofmann II 245; Pokorny I 788; Vries ANEW 114; Heidermanns 190–191.
ON far-ald ‘journey’, OE fær-eld ‘way, going, motion, journey’. Compound of *faran and *alđan (see *alđaz). Torp-Falk 229; Holthausen AEEW 96; Vries ANEW 112.

ON far ‘travel, ship’, OE fær ‘journey, vehicle, ship’, OHG far ‘haven’. Derived from *faranan. Torp-Falk 229; Holthausen AEEW 96; Pokorny I 816; Vries ANEW 112; Zalizniak I 140; Onions 345; Seebold 187.

Goth faran ‘to pass over, to wander, to travel’, ON fara ‘to travel’, OFris fara id., OE faran id., OFris fara id., OS faran id., OHG faran id. Related to Skt píparti ‘to bring over, to save, to surpass’, Av par- ‘to bring over’, Gk περάω ‘to penetrate’, πείρω ‘to pierce, to run through’, Slav *pьrǫ, *perti ‘to move, to fly’. Brugmann IF XXXII 173–175 (*-a- from *farjanan); Bartholomae 851; Torp-Falk 229; Meillet MSL XIX 135; Trautmann BSW 215–216; Feist 142– 143; Holthausen AEEW 98; Jóhannesson IEW 548–551; Benveniste BSL LI 36–41; Mayrhofer II 284; Pokorny I 816–818; Vries ANEW 112; Frisk II 491–492; Onions 345; Seebold 186–188; Vasmer-Trubachev III 240; Lehmann GED 108–109; Kluge-Seebold 245.

Burg *fards ‘journey, way’, ON ferä ‘travel, journey’, OE fyrd ‘army, expedition’, OFris ferd ‘military expedition’, OS fard ‘way’, OHG fart id. Derived from *faranan. Torp-Falk 229; Pokorny I 817; Vries ANEW 119; Zalizniak I 150; Seebold 187; Lehmann GED 136–137, 381; Bammesberger Nom. 144–145; Köbler 710; Kluge-Seebold 245.
ON fram-feräi ‘procedure, conduct’, MLG ze-verde ‘ride, way’, OHG gi-ferti ‘way’. Derived from *farđiz. Vries ANEW 118.

OE fearh ‘little pig, farrow’, OHG neut. farah, farh id. Identical with Lat porcus ‘pig’, MIr orc ‘young pig’, Lith paıšas ‘gelded pig’, Slav *porsę ‘piglet’ (Kirchhoff KZ I 39). Zupitza Gutt. 190; Pedersen Kelt. Gr. I 91; Torp-Falk 234; Trautmann BSW 207; Holthausen AEEW 99; Walde-Hofmann II 341; Chemodanov SGGJa I 58; Pokorny I 841; Trubachev Zhiv. 63; Fraenkel 542; Vries ANEW 113; Trubachev Zhiv. 63; Onions 345; Vasmer-Trubachev III 332; Bammesberger Nom. 52; Kluge-Seebold 259.

OHG faro ‘colored’. Related to Skt pṛśni- ‘spotted, variegated’, Gk πορκνός ‘dark’, MIr erc ‘spotted, dark red’ (Hamp IF LXXVIII 142–143). Cf. also *furxnō. Torp-Falk 234; Mayrhofer II 336; Pokorny I 820–821; Szemerényi Language XLVIII 5–9 (with metathesis, to Lat corpus ‘body’); Lehmann GED 109; Bammesberger Nom. 49–50.

Goth masc. or neut. farwa ‘form, appearance’, OFris ferwe id., MDu verwe id., OHG farawa id. Substantivized *farxwaz (Meid Wortbild. 75). Torp- Falk 234; Szemerényi Language XLVIII 5–9 (metathesis of *k⁄erp-: Skt kṛp- ‘shape, beautiful appearance’, Lat corpus ‘body’); Lehmann GED 109 (against Szemerényi); Kluge-Seebold 249.

Goth farjan ‘to travel, to go by sea’, ON ferja ‘to ferry, to transport’, OE ferian ‘to carry, to bear, to lead’, OFris feria ‘to lead, to ferry across’, OS ferian ‘to travel by ship’, OHG ferren ‘to sail’. Causative/iterative of *faranan. Torp-Falk 229; Holthausen AEEW 102; Feist 142–143; Vries ANEW 118; Onions 352; Seebold 187; Lehmann GED 108; Kluge-Seebold 245, 259.
ON ferja ‘ferry’, MLG vere id., MHG vere id. Derived from *faranan. Torp-Falk 229; Vries ANEW 118; Onions 352.

ON farmr ‘freight, cargo’, OE fearm id., OS farm ‘stampede’, OHG farm ‘fast ship’. Identical with Slav *pormъ ‘ferry’ (Fick I 475; Persson Beiträge 641). Derived from *faranan. Hirt RFV XXIII 336 (Slav < Gmc); Torp-Falk 229; Trautmann BSW 216; Holthausen AEEW 99; Pokorny I 816–817; Chemodanov SGGJa I 80; Vries ANEW 112; Zalizniak I 131; Stang LS 43; Vasmer- Trubachev III 331–332; Seebold 187.

OE fearn ‘fern’, MLG farn id., OHG farn id. Related to phonetically diverging IE words for ‘fern’: Gk πτέρις id., Alb fier id. < PAlb *sper(n)a, OIr raith id. (< *prati-), Lith papártis id., Slav *paportь id. Further connected with and exactly corresponding formally to Skt παρὠá- ‘wing’, Av par 6 na- id. Bartholomae 869–870; Pedersen Kelt. Gr. I 91; Torp-Falk 234; Holthausen AEEW 99; Pokorny I 826; Fraenkel 538; Frisk II 611; Onions 351; Kluge-Seebold 249–250; Orel AED 96.
ON fǫr ‘journey’, OE faru id., OFries fera id., MHG far id. Derived from *faranan. Torp-Falk 229; Holthausen AEEW 98; Vries ANEW 151; Onions 345; Seebold 187; Bammesberger Nom. 107.

ON -fari ‘sea-farer’, OE zefara ‘companion’, OFris rum-fara ‘pilgrim, traveler to Rome’, OHG -faro ‘traveler’. Derived from *faranan. Identical with Toch B akwam-pere ‘sprout and stalk’ < *-porom. Holthausen AEEW 98; Vries ANEW 112; Seebold 187; Bammesberger Nom. 176; Adams TB 5.

ON farri ‘bullock’, OE fearr ‘bull, ox’ (from *farzaz), MLG varre id., OHG farro id. Deverbative based on IE *pers-: Skt pṛßan ‘spotted’, Tokh AB pärs- ‘to sprinkle’. For the semantic development cf. Latv vepris ‘boar’, Slav *veprь id. ~ Skt vápati ‘to throw (out), to eject, to excrete’. Paul Wu S XX 38; Osthoff PBB XX 90–91 (to Skt pṛthuka- ‘boy, young of an animal’, Arm ort' ‘calf ’); Torp-Falk 235; Holthausen AEEW 99; Martinet Gém. 178; Mayrhofer II 336; Pokorny I 818, 823; Trubachev Zhiv. 42–43 (to Slav *porzъ ‘bull, hog’ < *porso-); Vries ANEW 112; Zalizniak II 195; Bammesberger Nom. 50; Kluge- Seebold 250.

ON fǫsull ‘brood’, OE fæsl ‘offspring, progeny’ (masc. or neut.?), MLG vasel ‘mature bull’, OHG fasal ‘offspring, progeny, kin’ (neut.). Connected with Skt neut. pásas- ‘membrum virile’, Gk πέος id., Lat pēnis id. < *pesni-s (Grimmapud Aufrecht KZ I 288). Torp-Falk 239; Holthausen AEEW 96; Walde-Hofmann II 281; Mayrhofer II 241; Pokorny I 824; Zalizniak II 167; Frisk II 507; Kluge-Seebold 250–251.
ON fast-haldr ‘fastholding’, OE fæst-heald ‘firmly fixed’. Compound of *fastaz and *xalđaz (see *xalđanan). Carr Nom. comp. 63.
ON fast-ligr ‘strong, fast’, OE fæst-líc ‘firm’, OHG fast-līh ‘solid, firm, safe’. Derived from *fastaz. Heidermanns 192.

Burg *fastis ‘fast, firm’, ON fastr id., OE fæst id., OFris fest id., OS fast id., OHG fasto id. identical with Arm hast ‘firm’ (Hübschmann ASt I 38; Solta Arm. 439–440), probably from *po-st H-o- ‘standing firm’ based on *stā- ‘to stand, to set’. Karsten NB II 373–374; Uhlenbeck PBB XX 328 (to Skt pastya- ‘dwelling, abode’); Torp-Falk 239; Feist 143–144; Holthausen AEEW 96; Pokorny I 789; Chemodanov SGGJa I 107; Vries ANEW 113; Onions 346; de Lamberterie Sprache XXVI 133–144 (to Hitt paḫ“- ‘to protect’, Slav *pasti ‘to pasture’); Lehmann GED 109–110; Seebold Festschr. Rosenfeld 499–503; Köbler 710; Heidermanns 192–193; Kluge-Seebold 260–261.

Goth fastan ‘to hold fast, to fast’, ON fasta ‘to fast’, OE fæstian ‘to entrust, to commit’, OFris festia ‘to fast’, OS festian ‘to strengthen’, OHG fastēn ‘to fast’. Derived from *fastaz. The semantic development from ‘keeping, observing’ to ‘fasting’ is explained by calquing Lat (iēiūnium) observāre (Wessén ANF XLIV 104–105: ON < OE; Velten JEGP XXIX 502–503). Torp-Falk 239; Feist 143–144; Falk ANF XLI 116; Höffler ANF XLVII 285 (ON < MLG vasten); Pisani AGIt LVI 19–28 (the cultic meaning of *fastēnan is based on Lat statiō); Lamberterie Sprache XXVI 139–144 (from IE *pā-); Vries ANEW 113; Onions 346; Mazzuoli Porru AGI LVI 19– 28; Solari RIL CIX 433–436 (calque of Gk φυλάσσω); Lehmann GED 109– 110; Seebold Festschr. Rosenfeld 493–505 (from *p⁄os-to-, to Lat pūrus ‘clean’ < *p⁄6 s-); Heidermanns 193; Kluge- Seebold 251.
ON fastna ‘to pledge’, OE fæstnian ‘to fasten’, OFris festna id., OS fastnōn id., OHG festinōn, fastinōn ‘to strengthen’. Derived from *fastaz. Holthausen AEEW 97; Vries ANEW 113; Onions 346.
ON staä-festi ‘steadfastness’, ODu festi ‘solidness’, OHG festī ‘strength, might’. Derived from *fastaz. Vries ANEW 118; Heidermanns 192.
OSwed fæste ‘fortress’, OFris feste ‘contract, treaty’. Derived from *fastaz. Heidermanns 192.

ON festa ‘to fasten’, OE fæstan id., OFris bi-festa ‘to entrust’, OS part. gi-festid ‘fastened’, OHG festen ‘to strengthen, to fasten’. Derived from *fastaz. Torp-Falk 239; Holthausen AEEW 96; Vries ANEW 118; Heidermanns 192.
ON fasta ‘fasting’, OS fasta id., OHG fasta ‘fast, parsimony’. Connected with *fastēnan. Vries ANEW 113; Lehmann GED 109.
Goth fastubni ‘fasting, observance, vigilance’, OS fastunnia ‘fasting’. Derived from *fastēnan or directly from *fastaz. Feist 144; Lehmann GED 109–110.

ON fat ‘vat’, OE fæt ‘vessel’, OFris fet id., OS fat id., OHG faz id. ON fat ‘clothes, dress’ is a historically different word (see *fatan II). Identical with púodas ‘pot’ (an original neuter with a lengthening due to Winter’s law). Probably further related to Lith pėdas ‘sheaf’, Latv pêda ‘bunch’. Torp-Falk 226; Holthausen AEEW 97; Trier ZDPh LXX 353–355; Pokorny I 790; Fraenkel 563; Vries ANEW 113; Zalizniak I 140; Onions 971; Lehmann GED 113 (to *ped- ‘foot’); Kluge-Seebold 251; Dybo BSA 130– 131.

ON fat ‘clothes, dress’. An isolated form. Probably an old Oriental loanword, cf. NCauc *Hp>d V: Avar hebét ‘linen’, Lak pat:a ‘old clothes’, Rutul badu ‘trousers’. Vries ANEW 113; Nikolayev-Starostin NCED 600.

ON fetill ‘strap’, OE fetel ‘girdle, belt’, MDu vetel ‘fetter’, OHG fezzil ‘girdle, band’ (also neut.). Derived from *fatan II. Hardly related to *feturaz ~ *feturō(n). Torp-Falk 226; Holthausen AEEW 102; Vries ANEW 118–119; Onions 352; Kluge-Seebold 260.
OFris fatia ‘to grasp’, OE fetian ‘to fetch, to marry’. Derived from *fetanan. Torp-Falk 226; Pokorny I 790–791; Onions 352.

ON fata ‘to step’, OE fatian ‘to fetch’, OHG sih fazzōn ‘to rise, to exalt’. Derived from *fetanan. Torp- Falk 226; Holthausen AEEW 98; Vries ANEW 114; Seebold 196; Lehmann GED 113; Kluge-Seebold 251.

ON faämr ‘fathom’, OE fæäm ‘embracing arms, lap, cubit, fathom’, OFris fethm ‘fathom’, OS pl. fathmōs id., OHG fadam ‘yarn, fathom’. Etymologically related to W etem, NW edau ‘thread’ < *petimā and, with certain difficulties, to Slav *pasmę ‘thread bundle’ (< *pōt-s-mā). Further see *faþō (Meringer Festschr. Heinzel 174; Lane Language IX 249). Torp-Falk 226; Holthausen AEEW 97; Pokorny I 824–825; Chemodanov SGGJa I 97; Vries ANEW 109; Zalizniak I 131; Onions 347; Trubachev Rem. 102–103; Vasmer-Trubachev III 212; Lehmann GED 110; Bammesberger Nom. 71; Kluge-Seebold 244.
ON feäma ‘to span’, MLG vedemen ‘to measure with outspread arms’. Derived from *faþmaz. Vries ANEW 114.
ON faäma ‘to embrace’, OE fæämian ‘to fathom, to embrace’, MDu vademen ‘to thread’, OHG fadamōn ‘to spin, to sew’. Derived from *faþmaz. Vries ANEW 109.

Goth faþa ‘hedge’, ON pln. Fǫä, MHG vade ‘hedge’. Related to Gk πετάννυμι ‘to spread out’, Lat pateō ‘to stand open’ (Grienberger Got. 65). Uhlenbeck PBB XXX 274–275 (to IE *pā- ~ *pō- ‘to protect’); Grienberger Got. 65; Olsen ANF XXIII 90–94 (adds ONorw theon. Fǫä, originally—‘border’); Torp-Falk 226; Walde-Hofmann II 262; Feist 144; Trier ZSSR LXV 242–243 (follows Uhlenbeck); Pokorny I 824–825; Vries ANEW 150; Frisk II 520–521; Lehmann GED 110.

Goth pl. fawai ‘few’, ON fár id., OE feáwa id., OS fā, fao id., OHG fō ‘rare, few’. Related to Lat paucus id., and further to Lat paullus id., Gk παῦρος ‘small’ (Kuhn KZ I 515). Bugge KZ XXXII 31 (adds Arm p'ok'r ‘small’); Torp-Falk 239; Walde- Hofmann II 265–266; Feist 147; Pokorny I 842–843; Vries ANEW 112; Frisk II 482–483; Onions 353; Lindeman Verschärfung 16.6; Lehmann GED 112; Bammesberger Nom. 239.
Norw dial føyr ‘rotten’, Du voos ‘spongy’. Derived from *fūanan. Torp-Falk 242–243; Vries ANEW 114; Pokorny I 790; Vasmer- Trubachev III 414.

Goth fidur- ‘four’ (in cmpn.), ON masc. fjórir, neut. fjǫgur id., OE feäer-fóte ‘foor-footed’. Continues *k⁄et⁄ṛ-, cf. Skt catúr-aṅga- ‘four-membered’, Gk tetra-, Myc qe-to-ro-, Lat quadru-. See *feđwōrez. Torp-Falk 227; Holthausen AEEW 103; Walde- Hofmann II 706; Feist 149; Mayrhofer I 372; Pokorny I 642–644; Frisk II 883–884; Szemerényi Numerals 79–85; Lehmann GED 113; Ross-Berns Numerals 579–582.
ON fer-skeyttr ‘four-cornered, square’, OE feówer-scÿte id., OHG fiorscōzi id. Compound of *feđurez, *feđwōrez and *skautjaz (see *skautaz). Carr Nom. comp. 65.

ON fjóräi ‘fourth’, OE feóräa, feóweräa id., OFris fiārda id., OS fioräo id., OHG fiordo id. Derived from *feđwōrez, *feđurez. In WGmc *feđurþōn > *feurþōn. Identical with Toch A śtärt, B ≤tarte id., Gk τέταρτος id., Skt caturthá- id., Lat quārtus id., Lith κετωιὁτασ id., Slav *‘etvьrtъ id. Torp-Falk 227; Trautmann BSW 132; Meillet MSL XIV 382, BSL XXIX 34, 36–37; Walde-Hofmann II 399; Mayrhofer I 371; Fraenkel 247; Vries ANEW 124; Frisk II 883–884; Onions 373; Trubachev ESSJa IV 95; Ross-Berns Numerals 627; Adams TB 641; Burlak IFTJa 258.

Goth fidwor ‘four’, OE feówer id., OFris fiūwer, fiōwer id., OS fiwar, fiuwar id., OHG fior id. Phonetically transformed by analogy with *fenfe (Zupitza Gutt. 7). Related to IE *k⁄et⁄ōr id.: Toch śtwar, B ≤twer, Skt catvāra-, Gk τέσσαρες, Alb katër, OIr cethir, Lat quattuor, Lith keturī, Slav *‘etyri. Meillet MSL VII 162, IX 158–159; Scheftelowitz BB XXVIII 301; Torp-Falk 227; Trautmann BSW 131; Holthausen AEEW 102; Walde-Hofmann II 400–401; Feist 149–150; Mayrhofer I 371–372; Pokorny I 642–644; Fraenkel 247–248; Hamp MGS II 1; Vries ANEW 124; Frisk II 883–884; Onions 373; Lehmann GED 113–114; Trubachev ESSJa IV 97–98; Ross-Berns Numerals 582–584; Kluge- Seebold 864; Orel AED 173–174; Adams TB 641–642; Burlak IFTJa 258.

OE ze-feón ‘to be glad, to rejoice’, OHG gi-fehan id. Probably related to *fanxanan. Fick I 471 (to Lat pāx ‘peace’); Zupitza Gutt. 188–189 (same as Fick); Torp-Falk 224–225; Wood JEGP XIII 504 (to *fextanan); Holthausen AEEW 101; Pokorny I 796–797; Seebold 189.

OE feohtan ‘to fight’, OFris fiuchta id., OS fehtan id., OHG fehtan id. Identical with Lat pectō ‘to comb’ (Zupitza Gutt. 189; Specht KZ LXVIII 205–207), further to Gk πέκω id. (note πεκτέω ‘to shear, to clip’), Lith pešū, pēšti ‘to pluck’. Osthoff Etym. 369–371 (to Lat pugnus ‘fist’); Torp-Falk 225; Holthausen AEEW 101; Walde-Hofmann II 269–270; Specht KZ LXVIII 507, 613 (on the influence of *flextanan); Pokorny I 797; Fraenkel 580–582; Ader Festschr.
