Trier KG 146–159; Frisk II 492–493; Onions 355; Seebold 190–191; Kluge- Seebold 254.

Goth faihu ‘money, movable goods’, ON fé ‘cattle, property’, OE feoh id. (< *fexan), OFris fiā id. (masc., neut.), OS fehu id., OHG fihu id. Identical with Skt paśú- ‘cattle, animal’, Av pasu- ‘cattle’, Lat pecū id., Umbr pl. pequo ‘pecua’, Lith p‚kus ‘cattle’, OPrus pecku id. Osthoff Etym. I 214, 256–257 (adds Slav *pьsъ ‘dog’ as *‘cattle guard’); Zupitza Gutt. 190; Schulze KZ XL 412; Bartholomae 879–880; Torp-Falk 225; Fränkel Zfslav Ph XI 49–51; Holthausen AEEW 101; Walde-Hofmann II 270–271; Feist 135–136; Mayrhofer II 239– 240; Pokorny I 797; Fraenkel 564– 565; Vries ANEW 114; Zalizniak II 199; Benveniste IEL 39, 47–48, 155; Lehmann GED 102–103; Bammesberger Nom. 156; Kluge-Seebold 864.
Goth faihu-friks ‘covetous, greedy’, ON fé-frekr ‘greedy for money’. Compound of *fexu and *frekaz. Carr Nom. comp. 67.
Goth faihu-gairns ‘covetous, avaricious’, ON fé-gjarn ‘greedy’, OE feoh-zeorn ‘avariciuos, covetous’. Compound of *fexu and *zernaz. Carr Nom. comp. 59.
ON fé-hús ‘stall’, OE feoh-hús ‘treasury’, MLG vē-hūs ‘stall’. Compound of *fexu and *xūsan. Carr Nom. comp. 71 (parallel formations).

Goth filhan ‘to bury, to conceal’, ON fela ‘to hide, to conceal’, OE féolan ‘to cleave, to stick, to reach’, OFris bi-fella ‘to conceal, to commit’, OS bi-felhan ‘to commit’, OHG felahan ‘to transmit, to entrust, to bury’. Of unknown origin. Kirchhoff KZ I 39; Ebel KZ VI 217 (to OHG felga ‘bend, rim’); Uhlenbeck PBB XXVII 118 (to Gk πάλκος: πηλός, Hes.); Grienberger Got. 68 (to Slav *pъlkъ ‘warfare, troop’); Zupitza Gutt. 130; Torp-Falk 237; Heinertz ESt. 29–46; Feist 151–152; Holthausen AEEW 101; Trier Lehm 24–30; Jóhannesson IEW 557–558; Pokorny I 803–804; Vries ANEW 117 (to IE *pel- ‘cover, skin’); Seebold 191–193; Benveniste IEL 128–129; Lehmann GED 115; Kluge-Seebold 90 (to Lat appellō ‘to drive to, to move toward’, Latv pefit ‘to vilify’).

Goth þruts-fill ‘leprosy’, ON fjall ‘skin’, OE fell id., OFris fell id., OS fell id., OHG fel id. Identical with Gk acc. pl. πέλλας id., Lat pellis id. < *pel-n- (Bugge KZ XIX (1870) 409–410). Cf. also WGmc *feltaz > OE felt ‘felt’, MLG filt id., OHG filz id. and *speltaz ~ *speltō > OE masc. (?) spelt ‘spelt’, OS spelta ‘spelt’, OHG spelza ‘spelt’. Curtius Gr. Et. 276 (*feltaz to Lat pilleus ‘felt cap’); Schrader KZ X 479–481; Hoops Waldbäume 420 (*speltaz ~ *speltō to *spalđanan); Schrader-Nehring II 648 (*speltaz ~ *speltō to Lat pollen ‘fine flour, fine powder’ < *(s)p¬dēn); Torp-Falk 236– 238; Holthausen AEEW 100; Walde- Hofmann II 275–276; Feist 503–504; Pokorny I 801–803 (*feltaz to Lat pellō ‘to beat, to push’); Vries ANEW 123; Zalizniak I 140; Frisk II 499; Onions 349–350, 852; Lehmann GED 114–115; Kluge-Seebold 258, 265, 776.
Goth filleins ‘leathern’, OE fellen ‘made of skin’, OHG fellīn id. Derived from *fellan. Structurally close to Lat pellīnus id. Holthausen AEEW 105; Walde-Hofmann II 275; Feist 152; Lehmann GED 114.
OE film ‘skin, husk’. Derivationally close to Gk πέλμα ‘sole of foot’. Further related to *fellan. Torp- Falk 235; Holthausen AEEW 100; Pokorny I 803; Frisk II 499–500; Onions 356.

OE feld ‘field’, OFris feld id., OS feld id., OHG neut. feld id. Continues *peltu-, a secondary transformation of the original zero grade *p¬tu-: Skt pṛthú- ‘broad, wide’, Av p 6 r 6 yuid., Gk πλατύς ‘flat’. Related to *fulđō(n). Bartholomae 892–893; Torp-Falk 237; Holthausen AEEW 100; Mayrhofer II 333; Pokorny I 806 (to *pel 6-: *plā- ‘wide’), 833; Vries ANEW 137; Zalizniak II 199; Frisk II 553–554; Onions 354; Bammesberger Nom. 155; Kluge- Seebold 257.

Goth filu ‘much, very’, ON fjǫl-auäigr ‘very rich’, OE fela, feala, feola ‘many, much’, OFris felo, ful id. (< *fulu), OS filu id., OHG filu, filo id. Identical with Skt purú- ‘much, many, abundant’, Av pauru- ‘much, many’, Gk πολύ id. Osthoff PBB XIII 444–445; Bartholomae 854–855; Torp-Falk 235; Holthausen AEEW 100; Feist 152–153; Benveniste Origines 54; Mayrhofer II 311; Pokorny 799–800; Vries ANEW 125; Frisk II 577–578; Lehmann GED 116; Bammesberger Nom. 262–263; Kluge-Seebold 864.

ON fjall ‘rock, mountain’, OS felis id., OHG felis ‘rock, cliff’. Closely related to Gk πέλλα: λίθος (Hes.) < *pelso-, OIr all ‘cliff, stone’ < *p¬so- (Vendryes RC XXXVIII 184). Pokorny I 807; Vries ANEW 123; Frisk II 499; Kluge-Seebold 258.

Goth fimf ‘five’, ON fimm id., OE fíf id., OFris fīf id., OS fīf id., OHG fimf id. From *fenxwe identical with Toch A päñ, B pi≤id., Skt páñca id., Av pan‘a id., Arm hing id., Gk πέντε id., Arm hing id., Alb pesë ~ pêsë id., Lat quīnque id., OIr cóic id., Lith penkī id., Slav *pętь id. < *penk⁄ti-. Sabler KZ XXXI 280 (to *fenzraz); Pedersen KZ XXXII 272, Kelt. Gr. I 37; Zupitza Gutt. 7–8; Helten IF XVIII 84; Bartholomae 844–845; Lane JEGP XXXVIII 192–193; Torp-Falk 228; Trautmann BSW 213; Holthausen AEEW 104, AWN 61; Walde-Hofmann II 407–408; Feist 154; Mayrhofer II 187; Pokorny I 808; Fraenkel 570; Vries ANEW 120; Zalizniak II 206; Frisk II 506–507; Onions 358; Vasmer- Trubachev III 426; Lehmann GED 117; Ross-Berns Numerals 584–585; Kluge-Seebold 290–291; Orel AED 316; Adams TB 388; Burlak IFTJa 248.

ON fífrildi ‘butterfly’, OE fíf(f)ealde id., OS fifaldra, fīfoldara id., MDu viveltere id., OHG fīfaltra id. Compound based on *fenfe and *falđaz, *‘(creature) of five folds’ (cf. OE fíf-feald ‘five-fold’), tabooed and modified in various ways in different Gmc languages. Hardly a descriptive stem similar to Lat pāpiliō ‘butterfly’. Bezzenberger BB VII 75 (to Lat pāpiliō ‘butterfly, moth’); Noreen Abriß 228 (same as Bezzenberger); Torp- Falk 238; Holthausen AEEW 104; Walde-Hofmann II 249–250; Pokorny I 801; Vries ANEW 119; Zalizniak I 222; Kluge-Seebold 248 (reconstructs *fifaldrōn).

Goth fimfta- ‘fifth’ (in cmpn.), ON fimti, fimmti id. (for *fífti), OE fífta id., OFris fīfta id., OS fīfto id., OHG fimfto id. Close to Toch A pänt, B piṅkte id., Av puxda- id., Gk πέμπτος id., Lat quīntus id., OIr cóiced id., Lith peñktas id., Slav *p=tъ id. Further see *fenfe. Bartholomae 908–909; Torp-Falk 228–229; Trautmann BSW 213; Holthausen AEEW 104; Walde-Hofmann II 408; Feist 154–155; Pokorny I 808; Fraenkel 570; Vries ANEW 120; Szemerényi Numerals 27–44, 71–76; Frisk II 506–507; Onions 358; Vasmer- Trubachev III 426; Lehmann GED 117; Ross-Berns Numerals 628; Adams TB 385; Burlak IFTJa 248.
ON fimt ‘number of five’. Identical with Skt paṅktí- ‘group of five’, Alb pesë ~ pêsë ‘five’, Slav *pętь id. Torp-Falk 229; Mayrhofer II 185; Vries ANEW 120; Vasmer-Trubachev III 426; Orel AED 316.
Goth figgra-gulþs ‘finger-ring’, ON fingr-gull id. Compound of *fenzraz and *gulþan (Carr Nom. comp. 67, 276). Feist 150; Carr Nom. comp. 67; Lehmann GED 161.

Goth figgrs ‘finger’, ON fingr id. (contamination of u-stem and root stem), OE finzer id., OFris finger id., OS fingar id., OHG fingar id. Together with OIr cóicer ‘set of five persons’ and Arm hinger- (in hinger-ord ‘fifth’), continues *penk⁄rós (Meillet MSL IX 151, BSL XXIX 36). Further connected with *fenfe (Stewart BB XXX 238). Grimm DG II 60 (to *fanxanan); Bugge BB XIV 79 (from *penk⁄res); Pedersen KZ XXXII 272; Grienberger Got. 67 (with comparative suffix *-ero-); Loewenthal ANF XXXV 229 (to *fimiz); Zupitza Gutt. 73; Torp-Falk 228; Holthausen AEEW 105; Feist 150; Schulze Kl. Schr. 193 (follows Grimm); Pokorny I 808; Vries ANEW 120; Zalizniak II 206; Onions 357; Lehmann GED 114; Bammesberger Nom. 75; Ross-Berns Numerals 661; Kluge-Seebold 266.
Goth-Lat fenea (= Goth *finja) ‘pearl barley, barley dish’. Related to Lat penus ‘store of food’, Lith p‚nas ‘nourishment’. Walde-Hofmann II 283; Pokorny I 807; Fraenkel 569; Lehmann GED 112.

Goth finþan ‘to find out, to recognize, to learn’, ON finna ‘to find’, OE findan id., OFris finda id., OS findan id., OHG findan id. A secondary verb derived from *pont Ho-: Skt pánthā- ‘path’, Gk πόντος ‘sea’, Arm hun ‘ford’, Lat pōns ‘bridge’, OPrus pintis ‘way’, Slav *pǫtь id. (Bailey-Ross TPS 1961 138). Cf. also *p–t Ho- ‘one pertaining to the way’ in Toch B amäkß-pänte ‘wagon-master (?)’ (Adams TB 19). The original meaning was better preserved in WGmc *fanþjōn > OE féäa ‘troop, band, company’, OHG fendo ‘soldier, pedestrian’ reflecting an underlying verbum movendi. Sütterlin Btrg. Gesch. XVIII 261; Torp-Falk 228; Bartholomae 732 (to Gk παπταίνω ‘to peer’); Pedersen KZ XXXIX 366; Trautmann BSW 205–206; Holthausen AEEW 105; Walde-Hofmann II 336– 337; Feist 155; Benveniste Word X 257; Jóhannesson IEW 543; Pokorny I 808–809; Mayrhofer II 210–211; Vries ANEW 120; Hamp Lingua XXXIV 229– 234; Frisk II 578–579; Onions 356; Seebold 193–194 (to *pet- ‘to fall’); Vasmer-Trubachev III 413; Lehmann GED 117; Kluge-Seebold 266.

Goth fairra ‘far’, ON fjarri id., OE feorr id., OFris fēr id., OS ferr, ferro id., OHG ferro id. Derivative of IE *per- (Skt párā ‘away, off’, Av par¡ id.) similar to IE *up-ero- (Brugmann IF XXXIII 300–301). Grienberger Got. 64 (comparative *ferizōt); Bartholomae 852–853; Torp-Falk 231 (reconstruct *-ai); Kluge Urgerm. 247; Holthausen AEEW 102; Feist 141; Mayrhofer II 215; Pokorny I 811; Vries ANEW 123; Onions 345; Lehmann GED 107; Kluge-Seebold 259; Adams TB 359 (identical with Toch A pärne, B parna ‘outside’ < *pereno- < *perero- with dissimilation).
ON firra ‘to deprive, to save’, OE a-firran ‘to remove, to take away’, OFris fira id., OS firrian id., OHG firren id. Derived from *fer(e)rai ~ *fer(e)rōt. Torp-Falk 231; Vries ANEW 121.

Goth fairguni ‘mountain, mountain range’, ON fjǫrgyn ‘mother-earth’, OE firgen ‘mountain, mountain-woodland’, OHG mtn. Fergunna. Identical with (Much ZDADL XXXII 454–458) or borrowed from (Kossinna IF VII 284–285) Celt ἙΕρκύνιος δρυμός, Hercynia silva, cf. further Lith perkūnas ‘thunder god; thunder’, OPrus Percunis ‘thunder’ (Grimm DM I 156–157). Derived from *ferxwuz. Schweizer KZ I 155–157; Hirt IF I 479–483 (same as Much); Zupitza Gutt. 130; Zimmer ZDADL XIX 164–166 (to Skt Parjánya- ‘raincloud, god of rain’); Kretschmer Einleitung 81 (Celt < Gmc); Wiedemann BB XXVIII 8–9 (to Slav *porgъ ‘threshold, rapids’ and mnt. *pergyni); Muller PBB XXVI 282 (same as Kossinna); Feist 137–138; Karsten GFL 75–76 (Lith perkūnas < EGmc); Müllenhoff II 241–242 (originally, the name of the Alps); Torp-Falk 234; Jóhannesson IEW 557 (to *ferxwan); Feist 137–139; Jakobson Word XI 616 (to Slav *pergyni ‘wooded hill’); Porzig Gliederung 195; Pokorny I 822 (from pre-Celtic); Fraenkel 575; Vries ANEW 126; Zalizniak II 216; Friedrich Trees 133–140; Vasmer- Trubachev III 246–247; Hamp JL III 83–90 (follows Karsten); Lehmann GED 104–105.

ON fjǫr-gjafi‘one who saves another’s life’, OE feorh-zifa ‘giver of life’. Compound of *ferxwan and *zebōn. Carr Nom. comp. 80 (parallel formations; OE is a calque of Lat largitor vitae). *ferxwa-rēđan ~ *ferxwa-rēđiz sb.n./ m.: ON fjǫr-ráä ‘plotting against one’s life’, OE feorh-ræd ‘life-benefit’. Compound of *ferxwan and *rēđan ~ *rēđiz. Carr Nom. comp. 80 (parallel formations).
ON fjǫr-sjúkr ‘sick unto death’, OE feorh-seóc ‘mortally wounded’. Compound of *ferxwan and *seukaz. Carr Nom. comp. 61.

ON fjǫr ‘life’, pl. masc. fjǫrvar ‘men’, OE feorh ‘life, soul’, OFris ferch id., OS ferah id., OHG ferah id. Related to *ferxwuz (Grimm-Grimm III 1527). Kirchhoff KZ I 39; Wiedemann BB XXVIII 14–15 (to Skt párśu- ‘rib; sickle’); Zupitza Gutt. 63; Torp-Falk 234; Holthausen AEEW 101; Feist 139; Johannson apud Vries(to Gk πραπίδες ‘midriff; understanding, mind’); Torp- Falk 234 (same as Wiedemann); Specht KZ LXVIII 200 (follows Grimm); Vendryes RC XLIV 313–315 (follows Grimm); Pokorny I 822; Vries ANEW 125; Zalizniak II 199; Friedrich Trees 137 (same as Grimm); Meid Germ. Rek. 98– 100; Lehmann GED 105–106; Bammesberger Nom. 155.

Crim. Goth fers ‘man’, ON pl. firar id., OE pl. firas ‘living beings, men’, OS firihos ‘people’, OHG gen. pl. u[i]rho, dat. pl. mit firahim ‘people’. Derived from *ferxwan (Diefenbach VW I 361). Grienberger ZDPh XXX 128 (Goth fers compared with OE ferhä ‘soul, spirit, mind’); Wiedemann BB XXVIII 1; Zupitza Gutt. 64; Kisch KVSL XLVIII 2 (Crim. Goth < Hung férj ‘man’); Feist 148; Vries ANEW 121; Meid Germ. Rek. 98–100; Lehmann GED 105–106, 113.

Goth fairƕus ‘world’, OHG fereh-eih ‘oak’. Identical with Lat quercus id. (Hirt IF I 479). Wiedemann BB XXVIII 3; Torp-Falk 234; Walde- Hofmann II 402–403; Pokorny I 822– 823; Meid Festschr. Oberhuber 159–170.
ON firin-verk ‘lechery’, OE firen-weorc ‘wicked work’, OS firin-werc ‘act of violence’. Compound of *ferinō and *werkan. Carr Nom. comp. 47; Vries ANEW 121.

Goth fairina ‘fault, offence, reproach’, ON firn ‘abomination’ (neut. < *ferinu-), OE firen ‘wicked deed, sin, crime’, OFris firne id., OS firina id., OHG firina id. Connected with *fēran ~ *fērō. Uhlenbeck Got. Wb. 41 (prefixal form related to Skt inóti ‘to advance upon, to press upon’, iná- ‘strong, mighty’); Grienberger Got. 63 (to Gk πεῖρα ‘attempt, undertaking’); Weisweiler IF XLI 29–30 (same as Uhlenbeck); Torp-Falk 231; Meillet BSL XXII 224 (to Iran par- ‘to go through’); Schneider KZ LXVI 253 (to Toch A pare, B peri ‘debt’); Feist 139– 140; Holthausen AEEW 105, AWN 62 (to Toch A pare, B peri ‘guilt’); Vries 121 (to *fer(e)rai ~ *fer(e)rōt); Pokorny I 10, 818; Zalizniak I 174; Lehmann GED 106.

Goth fairinon ‘to blame, to accuse, to reproach’, ON firna ‘to blame’, OE firenian ‘to sin’, OHG firinōn ‘to stain (by crime)’. Derived from *ferinō. Holthausen AEEW 105; Feist 139; Vries ANEW 121; Lehmann GED 106.

Goth fairns ‘previous (of year)’ (af fairnin jera = ἀπό πέρυσι), OS fernun gēre id., MHG verne id. Identical with Umbr perne ‘before’ < *pernai, Lith pl. pérnai id., Latv p.rns id. Probably to be explained from *per- ‘previous’ and zero grade of *eno- ‘year’. Meillet BSL XXIX 229–230, MSL XXIII/IV 274– 275; Torp-Falk 231; Feist 140–141; Schulze Kl. Schr. 537–540 (from *per-no-, with suffix *-no-); Karsten NB II 210– 211; Chemodanov SGGJa I 77; Fraenkel 576; Pokorny I 810–811; Stang LS 42; Lehmann GED 106–107; Bammesberger Nom. 246; Kluge-Seebold 267.

Goth fairneis ‘old’, OE fyrn ‘ancient, old’, OHG firni ‘old, erstwhile, former’. Derived from *fernaz. Torp- Falk 231; Feist 140; Vries ANEW 123 (to *fer(e)rai ~ *fer(e)rōt); Lehmann GED 106; Heidermanns 196; Kluge-Seebold 267.

Goth fairzna ‘heel’, OE fiersn id. (< *fersniz), OS fersna id., OHG fersana, fersna id. Connected with Hitt paršina ‘upper thigh’, Toch B porsno ‘ankle (?)’ < *pērsn-, Skt pārṣṇi- ‘heel’, Av pāšna- id., Gk πτέρνη ‘heel’, Lat perna ‘haunch’ < *pērsnā (Fick I 81–82). Bartholomae 891; Torp-Falk 234– 235; Holthausen AEEW 104; Walde- Hofmann II 289–290; Feist 141; Kieckers HG 27 (*ē > *e before sonorant followed by obstruent); Pokorny I 823; Chemodanov SGGJa I 106; Zalizniak II 193; Frisk II 611–612; Lehmann GED 107; Bammesberger Nom. 147; Kluge- Seebold 260; Adams TB 404.

ON freta ‘to break wind’ (with metathesis), MLG verten id., OHG ferzan id. Cf. OE feortinz ‘pedatio’. Related to Skt párdate id., Gk πέρδομαι id., Alb pjerdh, Lith pérdžiu, pérsti id., Slav *pьrdîti id. Torp-Falk 234; Trautmann BSW 220; Holthausen AEEW 102; Jóhannesson IEW 554; Mayrhofer II 225; Pokorny I 819; Vries ANEW 142; Fraenkel 577; Frisk II 511–512; Onions 346; Seebold 194–195; Vasmer- Trubachev III 235–236; Kluge-Seebold 250; Orel AED 330.
ON fretr ‘fart’ (with metathesis), MLG vort id., OHG firz, furz id. Derived from *fertanan. Torp-Falk 234; Vries ANEW 142, 147; Kluge-Seebold 292.

ON í fjǫrä ‘in the previous year’, MHG vert ‘previous year’. Identical with Skt parút ‘last year’, Arm heru id., Gk Att πέρυσι id., OIr ónn-urid ‘ab anno priore’. (Brugmann Grundriß II/2 708). Torp-Falk 231; Holthausen IF XXV 148; Mayrhofer II 219; Pokorny I 810–811; Vries ANEW 124; Frisk II 518–519; Bammesberger Nom. 199–200; Griepentrog Wurzelnomina 444–445.

MLG vist ‘foist, fart’, MHG vist id. Identical with Gk πέζις ‘bullfist, Lycoperdon Bovista’, Lat pēdis ‘louse’, further to Gk βδέω ‘to break wind’, Lat pēdō id., Lith bezdū, bezdėti ‘to break wind’, Slav *pьzdîti id. Fick BB VII 270; Schmidt KZ XXVII 320; Trautmann BSW 221; Walde-Hofmann II 272–273; Pokorny I 829; Frisk I 230; Vasmer- Trubachev I 163; Kluge-Seebold 268.

ON fet ‘step, pace’. Continues IE *pedó-, cf. Hitt pedan ‘place’, Toch A päts, B patsa ‘bottom’, Skt padám ‘step, footstep’, Av pad 6 m id., Gk πέδον ‘ground, earth’, Arm het ‘foot trace’, Lat peda id., OIr ed ‘period’, Lith pëdā ‘foot, sole’. Thematization of IE *ped- ‘foot’. See *fōtz. Bartholomae 842; Torp- Falk 225; Trautmann BSW 209–210; Walde-Hofmann II 293–294; Mayrhofer II 204–205; Pokorny I 790–791; Fraenkel 561–563; Frisk II 485–486; Bammesberger Nom. 59; Adams TB 362; Dybo BSA 134 (lengthening in Baltic due to Winter’s law).

ON feta ‘to step’, OE pret. ze-fæt ‘to fall’, OHG pret. faz id. Related to IE *ped- id.: Skt padyate ‘to fall’, Slav *padǫ, *pasti id. (Meillet MSL XIV 336). Torp-Falk 225; Holthausen AEEW 102; Jóhannesson IEW 541– 542; Mayrhofer II 206–207; Pokorny I 790–792; Vries ANEW 118; Seebold 195–196; Vasmer-Trubachev III 184; Kluge-Seebold 251.

ON fit ‘webbed foot of waterbirds’, OE fitt ‘song, poem’, OS fittea ‘section’, OHG fizza ‘hem, skein of yarn’. Structurally close to Toch B paiyye ‘foot’ < *podƒo-, Gk πέζα ‘foot, edge’, Skt padya- ‘related to foot’ (Bugge BB III 116). Further connected with *fetan. Torp- Falk 226; Holthausen AEEW 106; Pokorny I 791; Vries ANEW 122; Frisk II 486; Van Windekens 82; Kluge- Seebold 268.
ON fitja ‘to web, to knit’, MDu vitten ‘to be suitable’, OHG fizzōn ‘to surround’. Derived from *fetjō. Vries ANEW 122.

ON fjǫturr ‘fetter, shackle’, OE fetor, feter id., OS pl. feteros id., OHG fezzara id. Derived from *fōtz (Richter KZ XXXVI 118). Torp-Falk 226; Holthausen AEEW 103; Trier Zd Ph LXX 354 (reconstructs the original meaning as ‘forked piece of firewood’); Pokorny I 792; Vries ANEW 127; Zalizniak I 174; Onions 352.
ON fjǫtra ‘to fetter’, OE ze-feterian ‘to fetter, to bind’, OFris fiteria ‘to bind’, MDu veteren id., OHG fezzarōn id. Derived from *feturaz ~ *feturō(n). Holthausen AEEW 102; Vries ANEW 127.
ON fjaär-hamr ‘winged haunch’, OE feäerhama ‘wings, plumage’, OS fedar-hamo id. Compound of *feþrō and *xamaz, *xamōn. Carr Nom. comp. 47.
ON fiäri ‘feathers, plumage’, OE fiäere ‘wing’, MDu ge-vedere ‘plumage’, OHG gi-federi id. Derived from *feþrō. Torp-Falk 227; Holthausen AEEW 106; Vries ANEW 119.
ON fiära ‘to feather’, OE ze-fiäerian ‘to give wings’, G be-fiedern ‘to feather’. Derived from *feþrjan, feþrō. Torp-Falk 227.

ON fjǫär ‘feather’, OE feäer id., OFris fethere id., OS feäara id., OHG federa id. Related to Hitt pattar ‘wing’, Skt patará- ‘flying’, Gk πτερόν ‘feather, wing’, OW peterin ‘bird’ (Pedersen KZ XXXII 245), further connected with *pet- ‘to fly’. Saussure Recueil 420 (from *pet-tro-); Torp-Falk 226–227; Holthausen AEEW 103; Mayrhofer II 199; Pokorny I 826; Vries ANEW 124–125; Zalizniak I 146; Frisk II 612–613; Onions 348; Bammesberger Nom. 118; Kluge-Seebold 254.

ON fága ‘to polish, to clean’, OFris fēgia ‘to clean’, MDu vāgen id., OS fegōn id., OHG fegōn ‘to sweep’. Despite phonetic difficulties, related to *fanxanan. Vries ANEW 109 (to Lith púošiu, puõšti ‘to decorate’); Pokorny I 797; Kluge-Seebold 255.
ON fár-ligr ‘disastrous’, OE fær-líc ‘sudden, unexpected, quick’, MHG vær-lic id. Derived from *fēran ~ *fēraz. Onions 351.

ON fár ‘harm, evil passion, bale’, OE fær ‘fear, danger, peril’, OS fār ‘ambush, lurking danger’ (fem.), OHG fār ‘ambush’, fem. fāra ‘ambush’. Compared with Arm p'orj ‘attempt’, Gk πεῖρα id. (Fick BB II 210). Meillet BSL XXXVI 110; Torp- Falk 230; Holthausen AEEW 96; Pokorny I 818; Vries ANEW 112; Zalizniak II 174; Frisk II 489–490; Solta Arm. 342–343 (isolates Arm p'orj); Onions 348; Lehmann GED 112; Kluge- Seebold 305.

Goth fera ‘side, limb’, OHG fera ‘ham’. Probably derived from IE *(s)pēi-, cf. Skt sphāyate ‘to grow fat, to increase’, Lith spėti ‘to be fast enough’, Slav *spîti ‘to flourish, to ripen’ (Uhlenbeck PBB XXX 275; Schulze Kl. Schr. 54). Structurally close to Lith spėrus ‘rapid, energetic’. Grienberger Got. 66 (to Gk πέρας ‘exit, end’); Stokes KZ XL 248 (to OIr íriu ‘land’); Güntert Wu S XI 139 (to Gk πήρα ‘satchel’); Torp-Falk 567; Feist 148; Mayrhofer III 541–542; Pokorny 983–984; Fraenkel 865–866; Vasmer-Trubachev III 734; Lehmann GED 112.
ON færa ‘to slight’, OE færan ‘to terrify, to frighten’, MHG færen ‘to readjust’. Derived from *fēran ~ *fērō. Torp-Falk 230; Holthausen AEEW 96; Vries ANEW 149; Onions 348; Lehmann GED 112.

Goth fetjan ‘to adorn’, ON fæta ‘to manage, to deal’, OE fætan ‘to equip’. Long grade variant of *fatōjanan. The same ablaut grade is found in Lith pėdas ‘sheaf’, Latv pêda ‘bunch’. Torp- Falk 226; Persson Beiträge 224–225 (to Skt palla- ‘large granary, barn’); Bezzenberger BB XXVII 175 (to Latv pēda ‘armful, bunch’); Holthausen AEEW 97; Feist 148–149; Pokorny I 790; Fraenkel 563; Vries ANEW 149; Lehmann GED 113; Kluge-Seebold 305.
Goth fijaþwa ‘enmity’, OE féozaä ‘hatred’. Derived from *fījēnan. Holthausen AEEW 101; Feist 151; Lehmann GED 114.
ON fimr ‘nimble, agile’. Close to OIr éim, ém ‘quick, fast’ < *pe(i)mi- (Lidén BB XXI 95). Torp-Falk 228; Chemodanov SGGJa I 100; Vries ANEW 120.

OE feól ‘file (instrument)’, MLG fīle id., MHG vīle id. Related to Skt pimśati ‘to hew out, to carve’, Gk πείκω ‘to scratch’, Lith piešiū, pi‚šti ‘to draw lines with coal’, Slav *pьsati ‘to write’. Zupitza Gutt. 64; Torp-Falk 241; Holthausen AEEW 105; Mayrhofer II 267–268; Fraenkel 587; Vries ANEW 117; Onions 355; Kluge-Seebold 256.
OE finc ‘finch’, MDu vinke id., OHG finc, finco id. Related to Gk σπίγγος id., W pinc id. Suolahti DVN 109–110; Torp-Falk 241–242; Holthausen AEEW 105; Onions 356; Kluge-Seebold 266.

ON Finnr ‘Finn’, OE pl. Finnas id., G Finne id. Attested as Gk Φίννοι, Lat Fennī. Unclear. Karsten GFL 230 (to *fenþanan); Lindroth NB V 45–50 (to G mtn. Finne and, eventually, to Celt *penn- ‘hill’); Zachrisson Festschr. A. Kock 312 (to OIr cenn ‘head’); Holthausen AEEW 105; Vries ANEW 120– 121; Onions 357.

OSwed fina ‘fin’, OE finn ‘fin’, MLG finne id. Probably related to Toch A spin- ‘hook’, Skt sphyá- ‘splinter, staff’. Torp-Falk 240 (to Lat pinna ‘feather’, OIr ind ‘point, tip’ < *pinno-); Holthausen AEEW 105; Pokorny I 981; Onions 356; Kluge- Seebold 266.

Goth fisks ‘fish’, ON fiskr id., OE fisc id., OFris fisk id., OS fisk id., OHG fisc id. Etymologically related to Lat piscis id., OIr íasc id. < *peiskos (Wood MLN XV 95). Mikkola RFV XLVIII- XLIX 279–280 (to Slav *piskar’ь ~ *piskor’ь ‘a kind of river fish, gudgeon’); Torp-Falk 242; Krogmann KZ LXII 267; Holthausen AEEW 105; Walde- Hofmann II 310; Feist 155; Porzig Gliederung 110, 193; Pokorny I 796; Chemodanov SGGJa I 59; Vries ANEW 121–122; Zalizniak I 131; Onions 358; Lehmann GED 118; Kluge-Seebold 267–268.

Goth fiskon ‘to fish’, ON fiska id., OE fiscian id., OFris fiskia id., OS fiskōn id., OHG gi-fiskōn id. Denominative based on *fiskaz. Derivationally similar to Lat piscor ‘to fish’. Torp-Falk 242; Feist 155; Walde-Hofmann II 310; Porzig Gliederung 110; Pokorny I 796; Vries ANEW 121–122; Lehmann GED 118.
ON fit ‘meadow land’, EFris fit ‘muddy pool’. Related to Gk πῖδαξ ‘spring, fountain’, OIr iath ‘meadow’ < *peito-. Torp-Falk 241; Vries ANEW 122; Frisk II 533.

Goth fijan ‘to hate’, ON fjá id., OE fión, feó(z)an id., OHG fīēn id. Related to Skt pīyati ‘to revile, to blame’ (Schulze KZ XXVII 426). Hoffmann BB XVIII 149–151 (from *k⁄eƒō, to Lat piō ‘to punish’, Gk τείω ‘to respect’); Torp-Falk 240; Holthausen AEEW 105; Feist 150–151; Mayrhofer II 294–295; Pokorny I 792–793; Vries ANEW 122; Onions 354; Lehmann GED 114; Kluge-Seebold 257.

Goth fijands ‘enemy, fiend’, ON fjándi id., OE feónd id., OFris fīand id., OS fīond id., OHG fīant id. Present participle of *fījēnan. Holthausen AEEW 105; Feist 150–151; Vries ANEW 123; Onions 354; Lindeman Verschärfung 1.4; Lehmann GED 114; Bammesberger Nom. 215; Kluge-Seebold 257.
ON físa ‘to fart’, MHG vīsen ‘to fart’. An imitative stem. Persson Wurzelerw. 199 (to Lat spīrō ‘to breathe’); Torp-Falk 242; Pokorny I 796 (to Slav *piskati ‘to squeak’); Vries ANEW 121.
ON flag ‘spot where a turf has been cut out’, LG flag, flagg ‘flat land’. Related to *flaxz. Vries ANEW 128 (to
Norw dial. flag ‘open sea’, E flaw ‘gust of wind’, MLG vlage ‘procella’. Derived from *flaxanan. Torp-Falk 250; Pokorny I 832; Vries ANEW 128 (to
ON flaga ‘slab’, MLG vlage ‘layer of earth’. Derived from *flazan. Zupitza Gutt. 130; Torp-Falk 250; Vries ANEW 128 (to *flaxz or *flaxanan).

ON flá ‘to flay, to strip offthe skin’, OE fleán id., MDu vlaen id. Related to Lith plėšiu, plėšti ‘to tear’, Latv plêst id. (Zupitza Gutt. 190). Fick I 487 (to Lat plēctō ‘to beat, to punish’); Persson Beiträge 232–234; Torp-Falk 250; Holthausen AEEW 107; Jóhannesson IEW 580–581; Pokorny I 835; Chemodanov SGGJa I 71; Fraenkel 619; Vries ANEW 127; Onions 361; Stang LS 42–43; Seebold 198.

OE fleax ‘flax’, OFris flax id., MLG flas id., OHG flahs id. (masc.). Identical with Alb plaf ‘wool blanket, rug’ < PAlb *plaxa < *plo˚so-. Further see *flextanan. Torp-Falk 251; Pokorny I 834–835; Frisk II 557–558; Onions 361; Kluge-Seebold 269; Orel AED 331.

Goth flahta ‘braid’, ON flátta ‘mat’. Derived from *flextanan. In vocalism close to Slav *plotъ ‘fence’ but structurally reminding of Gk πλεκτή ‘coil, twisted rope’. Torp-Falk 251; Feist 156; Vries ANEW 129; Seebold 199; Lehmann GED 118; Vasmer-Trubachev III 285.

ON flá ‘strip of meadow land’. Structurally close to Gk πλάξ ‘flat land, flat stone’. Further to Lat placeō ‘to please, to be pleasing’, Latv plakt ‘to become flat’ (Zupitza Gutt. 130). Torp-Falk 250; Walde-Hofmann II 313–314; Pokorny I 835; Vries ANEW 127; Frisk II 550– 551.

Goth ga-þlaihan ‘to comfort, to admonish’ (str.), MLG vlēn ‘to ask’, OHG flēhen ‘to fondle, to caress’. Related to *flaixaz. Osthoff PBB XIII 399 (to Lat lēna ‘bawd’ < *tlaic-snā); Torp-Falk 195 (to Gk λαικάς ‘whore’).

ON flár ‘false, insidious’, OE fláh ‘insidious, artful, deceitful’. Of unknown origin. Osthoff PBB XIII 399 (to Lat lēna ‘bawd, procuress’); van Wijk IF XXIV 236–238 (from *flēwaz); Zupitza Gutt. 210; Torp-Falk 195; Holthausen AEEW 106; Karsten NB I 59; Nordmeyer Language XI 219 (to Gk ἀπειλέω ‘to promise, to threaten’); Vries ANEW 129; Heidermanns 198.

ON fleinn ‘bayonet-like pike, shaft, dart’, OE flán ‘dart, arrow’ (fem., masc.). Derived from *flīnanan. Torp-Falk 252; Trautmann APSpr. 400 (to OPrus playnis ‘barn, stable’, Lith pli‚nas id.); Holthausen AEEW 106; Vries ANEW 129–130 (to *(s)pel- ‘to split’).

ON flesk ‘pork’, OE flæsc ‘meat’ (i-stem), OFris flāsk id., OS flēsk id., OHG fleisc id. Of uncertain origin. Cf. also OE flæc id. < *flaik- suggesting *flaiskaz < *flaik-sk-. Osthoff PBB XIII 401–403 (reconstructs *þlaiskiz, to Lat lāridum ‘fat of bacon, lard’); Torp-Falk 252 (to *flikkjan); Holthausen AEEW 106; Pokorny I 835 (to *flīkō); Vries ANEW 130 (to ON flís ‘splinter’); Zalizniak II 216; Onions 362.
ON fleiri ‘more’. Related to Skt prāyaъ ‘more’, Gk πλείων id., Lat plūs id. (a contamination of *plei-s- and *pleu-s-). Torp-Falk 235; Walde-Hofmann II 327–328; Pokorny I 800; Frisk II 556.

Goth þlaqus ‘supple, tender’, MLG vlak ‘flat’, OHG flah id. See *flakōn. Zupitza Gutt. 214; Torp- Falk 249; Walde-Hofmann II 314; Feist 499; Pokorny I 832; Onions 364; Lehmann GED 363; Heidermanns 199; Kluge-Seebold 269, 271.

ON flakka ‘to rove about’, MDu vlacken ‘to blaze’, G dial. flacken id. Related to Gk πλάζω ‘to turn aside, to turn away’ < *πλαγγωω, with an unclear geminate, Lat plangō ‘to strike, to beat’. Walde-Hofmann II 315–316; Martinet Gém. 170; Pokorny I 832; Vries ANEW 128 (šintensive gemination”); Frisk II 548–549.

ON flaki, fleki ‘hurdle, shield wicker-work’, ME flake, fleke ‘sticking point, hurdle’, EFris flake ‘floe’, OS flaka ‘foot sole’ (fem.). Related to Gk Herakl πλάγος ‘side’, Lat plaga ‘region, quarter, tract’. Torp-Falk 249; Walde- Hofmann II 314; Pokorny I 832; Vries ANEW 128 (to *felxanan).

Icel e-m veräur flökurt ‘to feel sick’, OE flacor ‘flickering’. Related to *flōkanan. Berneker IF IX 364 (to Lat flagrō ‘to flame, to blaze’); Torp-Falk 249; Holthausen AEEW 106; Jóhannesson IEW 572; Heidermanns 200 (secondary derivation from *flakurōjan).
ON flǫkra ‘to flutter’, ME flakeren ‘to tremble’, MDu flackeren ‘to flutter’, MHG vlackern ‘to flicker’. Derived from *flakuraz. Heidermanns 200; Kluge-Seebold 269.

ON flaska ‘flask’, OE flaxe id., OHG flasca id. Close to Lith plókš‘ias ‘flat’, Slav *ploskъ id. or directly derived from *flataz. Schröder An ZDA XXIII 157 (to IE *plē- ‘full, to fill’); Pokorny I 831; Pisani KZ XC 18–19; Kluge- Seebold 270 (from *flaxt-ska-).

ON flatr ‘flat’, OS flat id., OHG flaz id. An irregular continuant of IE *plet(6)- ‘wide’ in the full grade, cf. Skt práthas- ‘width’, Av frayah- id., Gk πλάτος id., W lled id. See *flaþō(n). Bartholomae 983; Karsten NB II 372–373; Torp-Falk 251; Mayrhofer II 364; Pokorny I 834; Vries ANEW 129 (to Latv plade ‘placenta’); Frisk II 553–554; Onions 360; Heidermanns 200.

ON flet ‘set of benches or rooms’, OE flet ‘floor, dwelling’, OFris flett ‘building, house’, OS flet, fletti ‘quarters, chamber’, OHG flezzi ‘threshing-floor, hall’. Derived from *flataz. Torp-Falk 251; Holthausen AEEW 108; Pokorny I 834; Vries ANEW 130–131; Zalizniak I 145; Onions 360; Heidermanns 200; Kluge-Seebold 275.
ON fletja ‘to cut open’, WGmc *flatjan > Fr flatir id. Derived from *flataz. Gamillscheg Rom. Germ. I 158; Vries ANEW 131.
Norw flade ‘small valley, flat field’, OHG flada ‘a kind of thin and wide cake’, masc. flado id. A regular reflex of IE *plet(6)- ‘wide’, cf. *flataz. Torp-Falk 251; Pokorny I 834.
ON flaära ‘to fawn’, MHG vladern ‘to flutter’, OHG fledirōn ‘to be careless, mindless’. Of imitative origin. Torp- Falk 251; Vries ANEW 127 (to MDu vleder, a crossing of *feprō and *fleuzan).
ON fleygr ‘able to fly’, OHG wint-flougi ‘flying, soaring’. Derived from *fleuzanan. Torp-Falk 254; Matzel Festschr. Schröder 96, KZ CV 98; Heidermanns 200–201.

ON fleygja ‘to cause to fly, to throw’, OFris flāgia ‘to flee’. Probably also Goth us-flaugjan ‘to carry about’ if this reading is to be preferred to us-walugjan (see *walzjanan ~ *walzōjanan). Causative of *fleuzanan. Braune LGRP XXIX 328 (on the reading of the Gothic word); Torp-Falk 254; Feist 530; Vries ANEW 131; Seebold 201, 518; Lehmann GED 381.

ON fló ‘flea’, OE fleá id., MLG vlo id., OHG flōh id. Somehow distantly related to Skt plúßi- id., Arm lu id., Alb plesht id. < *pleu-s-tā. The influence of *fleuxanan is possible. Meillet MSL XXII 142; Torp-Falk 195–196 (to *fleuxanan); Holthausen AEEW 107; Mayrhofer II 386–387; Pokorny I 102; Vries ANEW 132; Zalizniak II 207; Onions 361; Lehmann GED 363; Kluge- Seebold 274; Orel AED 333.

ON flaumr ‘eddy, crowd’, OHG weralt-floum ‘transitoriness of the world’. Derived from *flōwanan. Structurally similar with Gk πλῦμα ‘water in which something has been washed’. Zupitza Gutt. 74; Torp-Falk 253; Pokorny I 836; Vries ANEW 129 (reconstructs *flauzmaz); Seebold 204.

Goth flauts ‘boastful, conceited’, OHG adv. flōz-līhho ‘effusive’. Derived from *fleutanan (Grienberger Got. 70). Holthausen Anz ZDA XXIV 34 (to Lat plaudō ‘to slap, to beat’); Endzelin KZ LI 258 (to Latv plaudis ‘bream, Cyprinus brama’); Feist 156; Pokorny I 837; Lehmann GED 118; Heidermanns 201.
ON fleyta ‘to float, to lift’, MLG flōten ‘to drive’, OHG flōzzen ‘to flow’. Causative of *fleutanan. Torp- Falk 255; Vries ANEW 131; Seebold 203.
ON pl. flautir ‘a kind of whipped milk’, OE fliéte ‘cream, curds’. Derived from *fleutanan. Torp- Falk 255; Holthausen AEEW 108; Vries ANEW 129.

ON fley ‘a kind of ship’. Identical with Toch B plewe ‘ferry, ship’, Gk πλοῖον ‘ship’. For further connections see *flōđuz. Torp-Falk 253; Porzig Gliederung 186; Pokorny I 836; Vries ANEW 131; Frisk II 559–560; Lehmann GED 119; Burlak IFTJa 250.

OHG flewen ‘to rinse’. Close to Skt plāváyati ‘to cause to swim, to bathe, to submerge’, Slav *plujǫ, *ploviti ‘to swim’. See *flōđuz. Torp-Falk 253– 254; Mayrhofer II 384–385; Pokorny I 835–837; Vasmer-Trubachev III 271– 272.

OE flohten-fóte ‘webfooted’, OS flehtan ‘to braid’, OHG flehtan id. Related to Lat plectō ‘to plait, to braid’, Slav *pletǫ, *plesti ‘to weave’. Further connected with Gk πλέκω ‘to weave, to braid’, Lat plicō ‘to fold together’ (extracted from prefixal verbs, cf. ex-plicō). Persson Beiträge 346; Zupitza Gutt. 190; Torp-Falk 250–251; Trautmann BSW 224; Holthausen AEEW 107; Walde-Hofmann II 321; Jóhannesson IEW 580; Pokorny I 834–835; Vries ANEW 131; Frisk II 557–558; Seebold 198–199; Vasmer-Trubachev III 280; Lehmann GED 118; Kluge-Seebold 271.
ON flétta ‘braid’, OE fleohta ‘hurdle’, OHG flehta ‘braid’. Derived from *flextanan. Torp-Falk 251.

ON flekkr ‘spot, fleck’, MLG vleke ‘plot of land’ (fem.), OHG flec ‘stain, fleck’. Probably related to *flakaz ~ *flakuz. Torp-Falk 250; Martinet Gém. 197; Vries ANEW 130 (to *felxanan); Sommer Schr. 15 (to *flīkō); Lühr Expr. 216–218; Kluge-Seebold 271.
ON flekka ‘to fleck, to stain’, MDu vlecken id., OHG gi-fleckōn id. Derived from *flekkaz. Vries ANEW 130.

ON fljúga ‘to fly’, OE fleózan id., OFris fliāga id., MLG vlegen id., OHG fliogan id. Derivationally close to Lith plaukiū, plaªkti ‘to swim’ (Fraenkel IF L 229). Further related to Toch A plu- ‘to fly’, Skt plávate ‘to float, to swim’, Gk πλέω ‘to swim (on a ship)’, Lith pláuju, pláuti ‘to rinse, to wash’, Slav *plovǫ, *pluti id. Zupitza Gutt. 130; Torp-Falk 254; Trautmann BSW 223–224; Feist 530– 531; Holthausen AEEW 107; Jóhannesson IEW 575–578; Mayrhofer II 383– 384; Pokorny I 837; Chemodanov SGGJa I 76; Fraenkel 608; Vries ANEW 132; Frisk II 559–560; Onions 365; Stang LS 42; Seebold 201–202; Vasmer- Trubachev III 288–289; Lehmann GED 130; Kluge-Seebold 272.

ON fluga ‘fly, moth’, OE fleóze ‘fly’, MLG vlēge id., OHG flioga id. Derived from *fleuzanan. Zupitza Gutt. 130; Torp-Falk 254; Holthausen AEEW 107; Pokorny I 837; Vries ANEW 134; Zalizniak I 155; Onions 365.

Goth þliuhan ‘to flee’, ON pret. fló id., OE fleón id., OFris flīa id., OS fliohan id., OHG fliohan id. Cf. also ON wk. flÿja id. < *fleuxjanan. The comparison with *fleuzanan (Zupitza Gutt. 131) of which *fleuxanan is an early lexicalized variant is decisive in postulating the Gothic change *fl- > þl- in unknown phonological conditions. Osthoff PBB XIII 412–415 (to Lat lōcusta ‘grasshopper, shellfish, lobster’); Loewenthal ANF XXXV 238–239 (to Slav *tъlpa ‘crowd’), PBB XLIX 419; Torp-Falk 195–196; Feist 499–500; Holthausen AEEW 107 (same as Zupitza); Jóhannesson IEW 578; Pokorny I 837; Fraenkel 608; Vries ANEW 134; Onions 361; Seebold 517–518; Lehmann GED 363; Kluge- Seebold 273.

ON fljót ‘river’ (mainly in rvn.), OE fleót ‘bay, gulf, river; raft, ship’ (masc.), MLG flēt ‘river’ (masc., neut.). Derived from *fleutanan. Substantivized *fleutaz? Torp-Falk 255; Holthausen AEEW 107; Vries ANEW 132; Onions 361; Lerchner 253.

ON fljóta ‘to float’, OE fleótan id., OFris fliāta id., OS fliotan id., OHG fliozan id. Related to Lith plūstu, plūsti ‘to flow, to stream’, Latv plûstu, plûst ‘to pour out, to flow’ continuing *pleud-, an extension of IE *pleu- (Aufrecht KZ I 119), cf. also OIr luaidid ‘to move’. See *flōwanan. Torp-Falk 255; Holthausen AEEW 107; Jóhannesson IEW 575–578; Pokorny I 837; Fraenkel 634; Vries ANEW 132; Onions 361; Seebold 202– 204; Lehmann GED 119; Kluge-Seebold 273.

ON fljótr ‘fleet, swift’, E fleet ‘quick, fast’, MDu adv. vliet, vliete ‘swift, hurried’. Derived from *fleutanan. Structurally close to OIr lúath id. (Windisch KZ XXI 431). Torp-Falk 255; Karsten NB II 401–402; Vries ANEW 132; Heidermanns 202.
ON flikki ‘flitch (of bacon)’, OE flicce id., MLG vlicke id. Related to *flīkō. Torp-Falk 252; Holthausen AEEW 108; Vries ANEW 131; Onions 363.

OE flint id., MLG vlint-stēn ‘flintstone’. Cf. also MLG vlins id., OHG flins id. (Kuhn KZ I 380). Borrowed from the same unknown source as Gk πλίνθος ‘brick, tile’. Torp-Falk 253; Güntert Labyrinth 22; Kretschmer Glotta XXIII 12; Holthausen AEEW 108; Vries ANEW 131; Frisk II 562–563; Onions 362.

ON flík ‘tatter, rag’. Derivationally close to Lith plyšā ‘tear, break, split’, further connected to Lith plÿšti ‘to burst, to tear’. Torp-Falk 252 (to Lith pléikëti ‘to become rough (of a face)’); Pokorny I 835; Fraenkel 625; Vries ANEW 131.
Norw dial. flīna ‘to whine’. Related to Lith plÿnas ‘even, bald’ (Torp-Falk 252). The meaning in Gmc is secondary as attested by the derivative *flainaz. Pokorny I 834.
ON flóä ‘tide, flood’, OE flód ‘flow, wave, tide’, OFris flōd id. Derived from *flōwanan. Torp-Falk 254; Holthausen AEEW 109; Seebold 204.
ON flœär ‘flood-tide, high water’, OS flōd ‘tide, torrent’, OHG fluot id. See *flōđuz. Torp-Falk 254; Seebold 204.
ON flœäa ‘to flood over’, OE flédan id., MDu vloeden id., MHG vlüeten id. Derived from *flōđuz. Holthausen AEEW 109; Vries ANEW 135.

Goth flodus ‘stream’, OE flód ‘flow, wave, tide’, OS flōd id., OHG fluot id. Close to Gk πλωτός ‘floating’. Further derived from *flōwanan. Torp-Falk 254; Holthausen AEEW 109; Feist 156–157; Pokorny I 836–837; Vries ANEW 133; Zalizniak II 216; Onions 363; Frisk II 565–566; Seebold 204; Lehmann GED 118; Bammesberger Nom. 159–160; Kluge-Seebold 277.
ON fló ‘layer’, OE flóh ‘fragment, piece’, OHG fluoh ‘rock, cliff’. Related to *flaxanan. Zupitza Gutt. 130; Holthausen AEEW 109; Vries ANEW 132; Kluge-Seebold 276.

Goth flokan ‘to bewail, to mourn’, ON flókinn ‘bewildered, unsettled’, OE flocan ‘to clap, to strike’, OS flōkan ‘to curse’, OHG widar-fluohhan ‘to revile’. Related to Gk πλάζω ‘to make roam, to mislead’, Lat plangō ‘to bewail, to strike’. See *flakkōjanan. Torp-Falk 250; Feist 157; Holthausen AEEW 109; Walde-Hofmann II 315–316; Jóhannesson IEW 573; Pokorny I 832–833; Frisk II 548–549; Seebold 205–206; Lehmann GED 119; Kluge-Seebold 275.
ON flókinn ‘clotted, entangled’, OFris ur-flōkin, OS far-flōcan ‘cursed’. Derived from *flōkanan. Lehmann GED 119.
ON flóki ‘felt, hair’, OE flóc id. Related to *flakaz ~ flakuz. Zupitza Gutt. 214; Holthausen AEEW 109; Vries ANEW 133; Onions 364.

ON flór ‘floor, pavement’, OE flór ‘floor’, MDu vloer id., MHG vluor ‘field’. Related to OIr lár ‘floor’, W llawr (Fick KZ I 366–367: further to Lat plānus ‘flat’, Lith plóti ‘to flatten’). Cf. also Lat ex-plōrō ‘to search out, to examine’ based on *plōro- ‘ground’. Kuhn WF XII 8; Torp-Falk 249; Holthausen AEEW 109; Walde-Hofmann I 430–431; Porzig 119; Pokorny I 806; Chemodanov SGGJa I 98; Vries ANEW 133; Onions 363; Kluge-Seebold 276.

OE flówan ‘to flow’. Cf. also ON wk. flóa ‘to overflow’ < *flōwēnan. Long grade of IE *pleu-, see *flōđuz. Cf. in particular Gk Ion πλώω ‘to sail’, Slav *plavati ‘to swim’. Torp-Falk 254; Trautmann BSW 223; Holthausen AEEW 110; Jóhannesson IEW 575–578; Pokorny I 835–837; Vries ANEW 132; Frisk II 565–566; Onions 364; Seebold 204–205; Vasmer-Trubachev III 271; Lehmann GED 119.
ON flói ‘marshy moor’. Derivationally close to OIr ló ‘water’ < *plō⁄o-. Further see *flōwanan. Saxén SNF I/3 60; Torp-Falk 254; Vries ANEW 133.
ON flog ‘flight’, OE ze-floz ‘infectious disease’, MLG vloch ‘flight’. Derived from *fleuzanan. Torp-Falk 254; Holthausen AEEW 109; Vries ANEW 133.

ON flugr ‘flight’, OE flyze id., OS flugi id., OHG flug id. Derived from *fleuzanan. Torp-Falk 254; Holthausen AEEW 110; Pokorny I 837; Vries ANEW 134; Zalizniak II 186; Seebold 201; Lehmann GED 363; Bammesberger Nom. 130.
ON flygi ‘flying insect’, E fledge id., MHG adj. vlücke ‘fledged, flying’. Cf. also *fluzōn > ON fluga ‘fly’. Derived from *fleuzanan. Pokorny I 837; Vries ANEW 134; Zalizniak I 155.
ON flogall ‘volatile’, OE fluzol ‘apt to fly, flying swiftly, swift’. Derived from *fleuzanan. Karsten NB II 276, 286; Holthausen AEEW 110; Heidermanns 204.
ON flótti ‘flight’, OE flyht id., OFris flecht id., OS fluht id., OHG fem. fluht id. Derived from *fleuzanan. Holthausen AEEW 110; Vries ANEW 134; Onions 362; Lehmann GED 363.

ON flokkr ‘troop, band, crowd’, OE flocc ‘flock, band, company’, MLG vlocke ‘flock’. Historically identical with WGmc *flukkōn ‘snowflake’: MLG vlocke, OHG flocko (Torp-Falk 254) further compared with Lith pláukas ‘hair’, Latv pl. plauki ‘snowflakes’. Matzenauer LF LI 125–126; Holthausen AEEW 109; Martinet Gém. 197; Pokorny I 837; Vries ANEW 133 (to

ON flyära ‘flounder’, MLG vlundere ‘thin board’. Somehow connected with Lat pluteus ‘penthouse, shed’, Lith plaªtas ‘porch of the beehive’, Latv plauts ‘shelf’. Torp- Falk 251 (to *flaþō(n)); Pokorny I 838; Vries ANEW 134.

ON á flot ‘afloat’, OE flot ‘water deep enough for sustaining a ship, sea’, MDu vlot ‘flow’, OHG fluz ‘current, flow’ (masc.). Derived from *fleutanan. Torp-Falk 255; Holthausen AEEW 109; Vries ANEW 134; Seebold 203.
ON flota ‘to float’, OE flotian id., OS flotōn id. Derived from *flutan. Holthausen AEEW 110; Vries ANEW 134; Onions 363.
ON floti ‘fleet, float’, OE flota ‘ship, fleet’, MLG vlote id. Derived from *fleutanan. Torp-Falk 255; Holthausen AEEW 109; Vries ANEW 134; Seebold 203; Kluge-Seebold 275.

ON flúä ‘low skerries flooded by the sea’. Derivationally close to Skt pluta- ‘floated, swimming in’, Latv pluts ‘raft’. Further related to *fleutanan. Torp-Falk 253; Holthausen AEEW 107; Mayrhofer II 386; Pokorny I 835–836; Vries ANEW 134.
Norw fnas ‘awn (of corn)’, OE fnæs ‘fringe’. Of unknown origin. Torp-Falk 245 (to ON fnasa ‘to sneeze, to snort’).

ON fnÿsa ‘to sneeze’, OE fnésan ‘to sneeze’, MDu fniesen id., MHG pfnūsen id. Probably related to Gk πνέω ‘to blow’. Torp-Falk 245; Holthausen AEEW 110; Pokorny I 839; Chemodanov SGGJa I 109; Vries ANEW 136; Frisk II 566–567.
ON fœzla ‘food’, OE fédesl ‘feeder’, MDu voetsel ‘food’, OHG neut. fuotisal ‘fodder, grazing’. Derived from *fōđjanan. Holthausen AEEW 111; Vries ANEW 150.

Goth fodjan ‘to feed, to nourish’, ON fœäa id., OE fédan id., OFris fōdia id., OS fōdian id., OHG fuoten id. Derived from *fōđōn. Torp-Falk 223; Feist 157; Holthausen AEEW 111; Pokorny I 787; Vries ANEW 149; Onions 349; Lehmann GED 119; Kluge- Seebold 293.

OE fóda ‘food, nourishment’. An archaism based on IE *pā-: Hitt paḫ“- ‘to defend, to guard’, Toch A pās-, B pāsk- ‘to guard’, Lat pāscō ‘to feed, to pasture’. Holthausen AEEW 110; Walde-Hofmann II 260; Pokorny I 787; Onions 368; Lehmann GED 119; Burlak IFTJa 248.

Goth fodr ‘casing, sheath’, ON fóär ‘lining’ (*-þ-), MLG fōder ‘casing, sheath’, OHG fuotar id. Identical with Hitt pattar ‘basket’, Toch B pātro- ‘vessel for alms’, Skt neut. pātra- ‘receptacle, vessel’. Torp-Falk 223; Feist 157–158; Mayrhofer II 252–253; Pokorny I 839; Vries ANEW 136 (identical with *fōđran II); Zalizniak I 140; Onions 370; Lehmann GED 120; Bammesberger Nom. 86; Kluge-Seebold 293.

ON fóär ‘fodder’ (*-þ-), OE fódder id., MLG voder id., OHG fuotar id. From IE *pā-tro- also attested in Arm hawran ‘herd’ and belonging to IE *pā- ‘to graze’. See *fōđōn. Holthausen AEEW 110; Pokorny I 787; Vries ANEW 136; Zalizniak I 140; Onions 366; Kluge- Seebold 293. fōzjanan wk.vb.: OE fézan ‘to join, to bind’, OFris fōga id., OS fōgian id., OHG fuogen id. Derivationally close to Skt pāśáyati ‘to bind’. Further cf. Gk πήγνυμι ‘to stick, to fix’, Lat pangō ‘to fasten, to fix’, Lith puõšti ‘to adorn’. See *fanxanan. Holthausen AEEW 111; Walde- Hofmann II 245–246; Pokorny I 797; Fraenkel 669; Frisk II 525–526; Lehmann GED 102; Kluge-Seebold 289– 290.

OE félan ‘to feel’, OFris fēla id., OS gi-fōlian id., OHG fuolen id. Derivative of *fallanan semantically similar to such phrases as E to fall in love. Cf. in particular the meanings in Baltic: Lith púolu, pūlti ‘to fall’, Latv pult id. as opposed to OPrus au-pallai ‘to find’. Holthausen AEEW 111; Onions 349 (to *fulmō ‘palm’); Kluge-Seebold 290 (to Lat palpō ‘to stroke, to touch, to pat’).

ON fœrr ‘able, capable’, OE fere ‘passable’, OFris fēre ‘conducive’, OHG gi-fuori ‘appropriate, suitable’. Derived from *faranan. Karsten NB I 112–113; Torp-Falk 230; Holthausen AEEW 111; Vries ANEW 150; Seebold 188; Matzel Festschr. Schröder 104, KZ CV 119; Heidermanns 205–206.

ON tor-fœri ‘difficult passage’, OFris fēre ‘utility’, OS gi-fōri id., OHG gi-fuori ‘favorable condition, utility, house’. Derived from *fōriz. Torp-Falk 230; Vries ANEW 150; Seebold 188; Heidermanns 206.

ON fœra ‘to bring’, OE féran ‘to go, to travel, to march’, OFris fēra ‘to bring’, OS fōrian ‘to lead, to convey’, OHG fuoren id. Causative of *faranan. Cf. Skt pāráyati, causative of píparti ‘to bring over’, Slav *pariti ‘to soar’. Torp-Falk 230; Holthausen AEEW 111; Vries ANEW 150; Seebold 188; Lehmann GED 108; Vasmer-Trubachev III 207; Kluge-Seebold 290.
ON ó-fœra ‘dangerous situation’, OHG un-gi-fuora ‘unfavorable condition’. Derived from *fōriz. Heidermanns 206.
ON fóstr-bróäir ‘foster-brother’, OE fóster-bróäor id. Compound of *fōstran and *brōþēr. Carr Nom. comp. 54.
ON fóstr-faäir ‘fosterfather’, OE fóster-fæder id. Compound of *fōstran and *fađēr. Carr Nom. comp. 54.
ON fóstr-land ‘native land’, OE fóster-land ‘land assigned for the procuring of provisions’. Compound of *fōstran and *lanđan. Carr Nom. comp. 80 (parallel formations).
ON fóstr-laun ‘reward for fostering’, OE fóster-leán ‘remuneration for rearing a foster-child’. Compound of *fōstran and *launan. Carr Nom. comp. 81 (parallel formations).
ON fóstr-móäir ‘fostermother’, OE fóster-módor id., OLG fōstar-muoder id. Compound of *fōstran and *mōđēr. Carr Nom. comp. 47–48.

ON fóstr ‘fostering’, OE fóster id., MLG fōster id. Related to *fōđōn. Goes back to *pāt-tro-, cf. Gk πατέομαι ‘to eat and drink’, Lat pāscō ‘to feed, to pasture’, MIr ás ‘growth’ < *pāt-to-. Torp-Falk 223; Holthausen AEEW 113; Walde-Hofmann II 260; Pokorny I 787; Vries ANEW 139; Frisk II 480; Lehmann GED 119; Bammesberger Nom. 87.
ON fóstr-systir ‘fostersister’, OE fóster-sweostor id. Compound of *fōstran and *swestēr. Carr Nom. comp. 54.
ON -fœttr ‘with feet’, OE -féted id., MLG -vœted id. Derived from *fōtz ~ *fōtuz. Holthausen AEEW 111; Vries ANEW 150.
Goth fotu-baurd ‘foot-stool’, ON fót-borä id. compound of *fōtz ~ *fōtuz and *burđan. Carr Nom. comp. 67.
ON fót-spor ‘footprint’, OE fót-spor ‘foot-track, foot-trace’, MLG vōt-spor id., OHG fuoz-spor id. Compound of *fōtz ~ *fōtuz and *spuran. Carr Nom. comp. 72 (parallel formations).
ON fót-verkr ‘gout’, OE masc. fót-wærc ‘pain in the foot’. Compound of *fōtz ~ *fōtuz and *werkaz. Carr Nom. comp. 81 (parallel formations).

Goth fotus ‘foot’, ON fótr id., OE fót id., OFris fōt id., OS fōt id. (sg. i-stem), OHG fuoz id. (sg. i-stem). Related to Hitt pata- id., Toch A peá, B paiyye id., Skt pad- id., Av pad- id., Arm pl. ot-k' id., Gk πούς, Dor πώς id., Lat pēs id. Bartholomae 842; Torp- Falk 225–226; Holthausen AEEW 113; Walde-Hofmann II 293–294; Feist 159–160; Specht 82; Mayrhofer II 249; Pokorny I 790–791; Vries ANEW 139; Zalizniak II 207; Frisk II 587–588; Onions 368; Sommer Schr. 13–15; Lehmann GED 121; Bammesberger Nom. 198; Griepentrog Wurzelnomina 153– 170; Kluge-Seebold 293; Adams TB 401–402.

Goth fragjan ‘to ask, to test’. Alternatively can be reconstructed as str. *frazanan. Identical with Lith prašÿti ‘to ask, to request’, Slav *prositi id. See *frexnanan ~ *freznanan. Grienberger Got. 72; Feist 160; Pokorny I 821–822; Fraenkel 647; Lehmann GED 121.

Goth fraisan ‘to put to the test, to tempt’. Cf. WGmc *fraisō: OFris frāse ‘danger, hazard’, OS frēsa ‘damage’, OHG freisa ‘setting, downfall, destruction’. Derived from *fraiþaz. Bugge PBB XXIV 435–436 (from *perei-); Brugmann Grundriß I 925 (compound *fra-isanan, to ON eisa ‘to shower down (embers)’); Hoffmann Festschr. Fick 38 (compound related to *aiskōjanan); Torp- Falk 245; Holthausen AEEW 115; Feist 162–163; Seebold 207 (to Gk πεῖρα ‘trial, attempt’); Lehmann GED 123.

OS frēthi ‘renegade, runaway’, OHG freidi ‘run-away, heretic’. Identical with Skt préta- ‘dead’ < *pro-ito-, cf. further OPhryg proitavos (a title). Hirt IF XXXVII 235–236; Mayrhofer II 353; Pokorny I 295; Heidermanns 207–208 (secondary ja-stems).

Goth fraiw ‘seed’, ON fræ, frjó id. Of unknown etymology. Meyer KZ VIII 248 (to Gk σπείρω ‘to strew’); Diefenbach VW I 398; Wiedemann BB XXVIII 43–44 (to Lat praegnans ‘pregnant’); Osthoff PBB XX 95–96 (< IE *pro-i⁄om, to *ei- ‘to go’); Bugge PBB XXIV 457 (to Lat pariō ‘to bring forth’); Wiedemann BB XXVIII 43–44 (reconstructs *fraizwan, to Lat praegnans ‘pregnant’); Petersson Aufs. 50 (from *fraizwan, to Gk πέρκη ‘bass’, with the original meaning ‘sprayed out’); Torp- Falk 245; Feist 163; Vries ANEW 145; Onions 379; Lehmann GED 123; Bammesberger Nom. 70.
ON frjór ‘fertile’. Derived from *fraiwan. Wiedemann BB XXVIII 43 (from *fraizwjaz, to Lat praegnans ‘pregnant’); Torp-Falk 245; Onions 379.
ONorw frakkr ‘brave’, OE fræc ‘voracious, greedy’, MLG vrak id. An ablaut variant of *frekaz. Förster Eng S XXXIX 327–339; Holthausen AEEW 114; Vries ANEW 139; Lehmann GED 103; Heidermanns 208–209.

Goth prep. fram ‘forward; from’, ON frá, fram id., OE fram ‘from’, OS fram ‘forward; from’, OHG fram id. See *framaz. Torp-Falk 232; Holthausen AEEW 114; Feist 164; Onions 378; Pokorny I 814–815; Vries ANEW 139–140; Lehmann GED 124.
Goth framaþjan ‘to estrange’, OE á-fremäan ‘to alienate’, MLG ent-vrömeden id., OHG fremiden id. Derived from *framaþjaz. Feist 164; Lehmann GED 124.

Goth framaþeis ‘strange, foreign’, OE fremäe id., OFris fremethe id., OS fremithi id., OHG fremidi id. Based on unattested *framaþaz, further connected with *framaz. Holthausen AEEW 115; Feist 164; Lehmann GED 124; Kluge- Seebold 285.

Goth comp. adv. framis ‘farther, further’, ON framr, comp. fremr ‘forward’, OE fram ‘valiant, stout, firm’, MHG fram ‘forward’. Related to Gk πρόμος ‘foremost man, chief’, Umbr promom ‘primum’. Maybe, further connected with *frumaz ~ *frumōn. Stokes BB XXIII 321 (to OIr riam ‘beforehand’); Torp-Falk 232–233; Feist 164; Holthausen AEEW 114; Karsten NB II 442; Schulze Kl. Schr. 310 (derives πρόμος from πρόμαχος); Pokorny I 814–815; Chemodanov SGGJa I 109; Vries ANEW 140–141; Frisk II 600; Onions 378; Chantraine 941; Lehmann GED 124; Bammesberger Nom. 245; Heidermanns 209; Kluge-Seebold 287.
ON fremja ‘to further, to promote’, OE framian ‘to avail, to profit’, OFris frema, fremma ‘to promote’, OS fremmian id., OHG fremmen id. Derived from *framaz. Holthausen AEEW 115; Vries ANEW 141.
ON frakka ‘javelin, dart’, OE franca ‘javelin, lance’. Unclear. Cf. also ON furkr ‘spear’. Torp-Falk 246 (to Lat-Gmc framea ‘spear, javelin’).

Goth frasts ‘child, offspring’. Together with Slav *prostъ ‘simple, free’ continues IE *pro-stā- (Uhlenbeck PBB XXVII 119), a prefixal derivative of *stā- ‘to stand’ (cf. Törnqvist SN XIII 305–307). For the semantic connection between Gmc and Slav cf. Lat līberī ‘children’ (Benveniste IEL 262–272). Grimm-Grimm IV/1/1 64 (to Goth fraþjan ‘to perceive, to understand’—semantically plausible in view of Slav *otrokъ); Kluge KZ XXV 313 (to Lat prōlēs ‘descendant’); Kögel ZDADL XXXVII 218 (to Lat pariō ‘to give birth’); Charpentier KZ XL 456 (from *prod-ti-); Osthoff PBB XX 89–91 (to *sēi- ‘to sow’, from *pro-s(6)tis, or to Lat prōsāpia ‘stock, family’); Meillet MSL XII 219 (reconstructs *pro-s 6–ti-, to *sēi- ‘to sow’); Charpentier KZ XL 456 (to Av frād- ‘to cultivate’); Petersson Aufs. 13 (to Slav *proso ‘millet’); Torp-Falk 232; Feist 165; Mezger Language XIX 262–263 (from *pro-s-tƒo-); Vasmer-Trubachev III 380; Lehmann GED 125–126 (a nursery word or cognate of *fraw(j)ōn).

Goth us-fratwjan ‘to make wise, to instruct’, OE frætwian ‘to adorn, to deck, to embroider’. Derived from *fratwō. Holthausen AEEW 114; Feist 531; Sturtevant JEGP XXXVI 181–182 (on the semantic development in Gothic); Lehmann GED 381– 382.

OE pl. frætwe ‘ornament, adornment, decoration’. Unclear. Probably continues IE *pro dō-. Grienberger Got. 231 (prefixed form related to *tēwō); Hollander ZDWf VII 301 (to *tawjanan); Holthausen AEEW 114; Feist 474–475; Lehmann GED 381–382.
OHG frad ‘strong, able’. Cf. also Goth fraþi ‘understanding’ < *fraþjan. Related to *frōđaz. Trautmann BSW 230; Feist 165–166; Pokorny I 845; Lehmann GED 126.
Goth fraþjan ‘to perceive, to understand’. Derived from *fraþaz. Fick KZ XX 449; Torp-Falk 246; Feist 165–166; Seebold 208; Bammesberger Nom. 65.
ON frauä ‘froth’, ME froäe id. Derived from *frawaz. Torp- Falk 248.
ON frá-ligr ‘swift, quick’, OHG frawa-līh ‘happy, merry’. Derived from *frawaz. Heidermanns 211.

ON frár ‘swift’, ME adv. frow ‘hurriedly’, OFris frē ‘glad, happy’, OS frā id., OHG frō id. Probably related to Av fravi- ‘mobile (?)’, Slav *pravъ ‘right, straight’ < *prō⁄os. Osthoff Etym. 336– 340 (to Skt pravate ‘to spring up’ < *pleu-); Torp-Falk 233; Pokorny I 845; Vries ANEW 140; Onions 378; Vasmer- Trubachev III 352; Abaev VJa XXXI/2 22 (on Av); Heidermanns 211–212; Kluge-Seebold 287.

Goth frauja ‘lord, master’, ON theon. Freyr, OE freá ‘lord, master’, OFris frā id., OS frōho, frāho id., frōio id., OHG frō id. Derived from IE *pro ‘forward’ > Gmc *fra- (Streitberg PBB XIV 186). Budenz KZ VIII 289 (to Lat prōuincia ‘province’); Schmidt KZ XXVI 15, Voc. I 107 (same as Budenz); Osthoff IF VIII 53 (follows Streitberg); Mikkola Festschr. Streitberg 270 (to Slav *pravo ‘right, law’); Marstrander NTS III 123–124, 153 (compound close to Skt pra-avati ‘to be merciful’); Torp-Falk 233 (from *frawaz); Holthausen AEEW 115– 116; Feist 166; Langenhove LS II 56–59 (compound of *per- ‘to possess’ and *He⁄- ‘vital force’); Pokorny I 813–815 (related to *p‘⁄o- ‘first’); Vries ANEW 142; Zalizniak II 216; Lindeman Verschärfung 16.8; Benveniste IEL 274; Lehmann GED 126–127; Bammesberger Nom. 183; Kluge-Seebold 283.

ON freyja ‘lady’, Freyja (name of goddess), OS frūa id., OHG frouwa id. Cf. also *frōwōn > OS frūa ‘lady, mistress’. Derived from *fraw(j)ōn. Pokorny I 815; Vries ANEW 142; Zalizniak II 216; Kluge-Seebold 283.

Goth fraihnan ‘to question, to ask’, ON fregna ‘to ask’, OE freznan ‘to inquire’, OS pret. fragn id. Continues IE *pre˚-: Toch AB pärk- ‘to ask’, Skt pṛccháti ‘to ask, to question’, Av p 6 r 6 saite id., Arm harc'anem id., Lat poscō id., precor id., MIr arco id., Lith peršū, piıšti ‘to arrange a marriage’, Slav *prositi ‘to ask’. Further related to *frazjanan and *furskō. Schweizer KZ I 150; Zupitza Gutt. 190; Torp-Falk 245–246; Meillet MSL XV 98–100 (nāpresent); Marstrander NTS II 103–106 (innovation built on the basis of preterite *fraxa); Bartholomae 997–1000; Pedersen Kelt. Gr. I 44; Trautmann BSW 216; Holthausen AEEW 115; Walde- Hofmann II 346–347; Feist 161–162; Jóhannesson IEW 551–552; Mayrhofer II 329; Pokorny I 821; Fraenkel 598– 599; Vries ANEW 140–141; Seebold 208–210; Vasmer-Trubachev III 377– 378; Lehmann GED 122–123; Kluge- Seebold 282; Adams TB 372.
ON frétt ‘enquiry’, OE freht, fryht ‘prediction’. Related to *freznanan ~ *frexnanan. Torp-Falk 246; Holthausen AEEW 114; Vries ANEW 142; Seebold 209.

Goth faihu-friks ‘covetous, avaricious’, ON frekr ‘greedy’, OE frec ‘desirous, greedy, gluttonous, audacious’, MDu vrec ‘mean’, OHG freh ‘greedy’. Related to Slav *pragnǫti ‘to desire’. See *frak(k)az. Kauffmann PBB XII 514 (to Lat precāri ‘to request’); Grienberger Got. 62 (to Lith piıkti ‘to buy’); Förster Eng S XXXIX 327–339; Torp Festschr. Unger 183–185 (to Gk σπαργάω ‘to swell, to be full’); Hirt PBB XXIII 352; Torp-Falk 245; Feist 136; Holthausen AEEW 115; Karsten NB I 27; Lane Language IX 258 (to W rhewydd ‘lascivious’); Pokorny I 845; Vries ANEW 141; Lehmann GED 103; Heidermanns 212–213; Kluge- Seebold 283.
Goth faihu-frikei ‘avarice, cupidity’, OE scyld-frecu ‘guilty greed’, OHG frehhī ‘avarice’. Derived from *frekaz. Feist 13; Heidermanns 213.
ON poet. freki ‘wolf’, OE freca ‘bold man, warrior’. Derived from *frekaz. Holthausen AEEW 115; Vries ANEW 141; Heidermanns 213.

ON frest ‘delay’, OE fyrst, first ‘space of time, time’ (< *frestiz), OFris frist ‘respite’, OS first id., frist id. An old compound *pres-sto- structurally similar to Skt puraъ-sthātár- ‘standing on the top’ < *peros-st 6 to- and OIr arsaid ‘old, ancient’ < *peros-st 6 ti- (Brugmann IF XIII 164). Heinertz apud Vries (to *friþuz); Torp-Falk 233–234; Holthausen AEEW 117; Trier NGWG III (NF)/4 128–129 (to G First ‘fence, ridge’); Mayrhofer II 309; Pokorny I 813; Vries ANEW 141–142; Zalizniak II 186; Lehmann GED 129.

ON frjósa ‘to freeze’, OE freósan id., MDu vriesen id., OHG friosan id. Related to Skt prußvā ‘rime, ice’, Lat pruīna ‘frost’ (Bugge KZ XIX 440). Schmidt KZ XXVII 328; Woods MP V 272–273; Torp-Falk 248; Holthausen AEEW 116; Walde-Hofmann II 378– 379; Jóhannesson IEW 569; Mayrhofer II 381–382; Pokorny I 846; Vries ANEW 143; Vendryes R-24–25 (to OIr réud ‘cold’); Seebold 210–211 (to *preus- ‘to sprinkle’); Hamp JIES I 215–223; Lehmann GED 129; Kluge-Seebold 286.

ON frægr ‘famous’, OE zefræze ‘known, renowned, famous’, OS gifrāgi ‘known’. Related to *frexnanan ~ *freznanan. Torp-Falk 246; Holthausen AEEW 114; Vries ANEW 145; Lehmann GED 122; Matzel Festschr. Schröder 103, KZ CV 112; Heidermanns 212.

Goth fri(j)aþwa ‘love’, OE freóäo ‘peace’. Identical with late Skt priyatva- ‘being dear, being beloved’. Further see *frijaz. Torp-Falk 247; Holthausen AEEW 116; Feist 168 (OE fríod equivalent to Skt priyátā ‘love’); Mayrhofer II 379 (against the comparison with Skt); Benveniste IEL 266; Lehmann GED 128; Bammesberger Nom. 118–119.
ON frjáls-gjafi ‘freed-man’, OE freóls-zefa ‘giver of freedom’. Compound of *frija-xalsaz and *zebōn. Carr Nom. comp. 81 (parallel formations).

Goth sbst. freihals ‘freedom’, ON frjáls ‘free’, OE fríols ‘free, freedom’, OFris sbst. frī-hals ‘freedom’, OHG frīhals ‘free’. Originally, *‘having a free neck, neck unhampered by a yoke’. A compound of *frijaz and *xalsaz. Neckel PBB XLI 405 (‘Schonhals’); Torp-Falk 247–248; Holthausen AEEW 117; Feist 167; Carr Nom. comp. 65–66; Porzig Gliederung 119; Pokorny I 844, 1021; Vries ANEW 143; Onions 375; Lehmann GED 127–128; Heidermanns 215.
ON frelsi ‘freedom’, OFris frīhelse id., OHG frīhalsī id. Derived from *frija-xalsaz. Torp-Falk 247–248; Vries ANEW 141.

Goth freis ‘free’, OE freó id., OFris frī id., MLG vrī id., OHG frī id. Etymologically close to Skt priyá- ‘own, dear, beloved’, Av fryō id., Lat pro-prius ‘to own’, W rhydd ‘free’, Slav *prijati ‘to be disposed, to favor’. Froehde BB IX 110 (to Lat prīuus ‘single, each, private’); Arbois de Jubainville MSL VII 291 (Celtic loanword); Bartholomae 1026; Karsten NB I 90; Wood MLN XVI 310 (from *pro-ei- ‘to go forward’); Torp-Falk 247–248 (follow Froehde); Hirt Zd Ph XXIX 301; Neckel PBB XLI 403–405; Trautmann BSW 231; Feist 167– 168; Holthausen AEEW 116; Walde- Hofmann II 373–374; Scheller 20–23, 90–91 (semantics); Porzig Gliederung 119; Mayrhofer II 378–379; Pokorny I 844; Onions 375; Vasmer-Trubachev III 369–370; Lindeman Verschärfung 124–125; Mezger KZ LXXIX 32–38; Benveniste IEL 266; Lehmann GED 128; Bammesberger Nom. 242; Heidermanns 215– 216; Kluge-Seebold 284.

ON theon. Frigg, OHG theon. Frija. Substantivized feminine of *frijaz with Verscharfung. Identical with Skt fem. priyā ‘own, dear, beloved’, Av fem. fryā id. Mikkola Festschr. Streitberg 268; Bartholomae 1026; Torp-Falk 247; Vries ANEW 143; Lindeman Verschärfung 9.5, 17.9; Onions 377.

ON friäill ‘lover, gallant’, OS friuthil ‘loved one’, MLG vridel id., OHG friudil id. Corresponds to Slav *prijatelь id. At the same time may be identical with Dac πριάδιλα, πριάδηλα Ἑβρυωνία μέλαινα’. Further see *frijōjan. Torp-Falk 247; Detschew Thr. 556 (Dac πριά-dila as a compound of *priƒoas in *frijaz, and *dhal- ‘to bloom’); Vries ANEW 142.
ON friäla ‘loved one, mistress’, OHG friudila id. Derived from *frijōđelaz. Torp-Falk 247; Vries ANEW 142.

Goth frijon ‘to love’, ON fría ‘to deliver’, OE freózan ‘to free, to respect, to love’, OFris friaia ‘to love’, MDu vrien ‘to marry’, MHG vrien id. Derived from *frijaz. Structurally identical with Skt priyāyáte ‘to make friends with’, Slav *prijati ‘to favor’. Torp-Falk 246; Holthausen AEEW 117; Feist 168; Mayrhofer II 379; Pokorny I 844; Vries ANEW 143; Mezger LZ LXXIX 32–38; Onions 377; Benveniste IEL 266–267; Lehmann GED 128–129; Vasmer-Trubachev III 369–370; Heidermanns 215–216; Kluge-Seebold 284.
ON frí ‘lover’, OHG frīo ‘free man’. Derived from *frijaz. Heidermanns 215–216.
ON frænd-hollr ‘faithful to one’s kinsmen’, OHG friunthold ‘friendly’. Compound of *frijōnđz and *xulþaz. Carr Nom. comp. 90 (parallel formations).

Goth frijonds ‘friend’, ON frændi ‘relative, friend’, OE freónd ‘friend’, OFris friōnd id., OS friund id., OHG friunt id. Participle of *frijōjan. Torp-Falk 246; Holthausen AEEW 117; Feist 168; Pokorny I 844; Vries ANEW 145; Onions 377; Benveniste IEL 266; Metzger KZ LXXIX 32–38 (nonparticipial formation from *frijō); Lehmann GED 128; Bammesberger Nom. 215; Kluge-Seebold 285–286.

ON ferskr ‘fresh’, OE fersc id., MLG vrisch id., OHG frisc id. Related to *frijaz. Torp-Falk 248; Feist 163; Holthausen AEEW 102; Būga II 302 (against the comparison with Lith prėskas ‘unleavened’, Slav *prîsnъ id.); Mentz KZ LXV 263–265 (to Lat prīscus ‘previous, former’); Vries ANEW 118; Heidermanns 216–217 (reconstructs *pr-isko- vs. Lat prīscus < *pri-isko- vs. Balto-Slav *proisko-); Kluge-Seebold 287.

Goth ga-friþon ‘to reconcile, to establish peace’, ON friäa ‘to restore peace’, OE friäian ‘to keep the peace, to protect’, OFris frethia id., OS frithōn id., MHG vriden ‘to bring to peace’. Derived from *friþuz. Torp-Falk 247; Holthausen AEEW 117; Feist 180–181; Vries ANEW 142; Lehmann GED 137.

ON friä-lauss ‘outlawed’, OE friä-leás ‘not included in the treaty of peace’, OFris fretho-lās ‘outlawed’, MLG vrede-lōs id., MHG vride-lōs id. Compound of *friþuz and *lausaz. Carr Nom. comp. 89 (parallel formations).
ON pl. friä-menn ‘men of peace’, OHG fridu-man ‘peaceful man’. Compound of *friþuz and *mannz. Carr Nom. comp. 83 (parallel formations).

Goth prop. Fredu-mirus, ON friär ‘peace’, OE friä id. (masc., neut.), OFris fretho id., OS frithu id., OHG fridu id. Derived from *frijaz. Torp-Falk 247; Holthausen AEEW 117; Feist 169; Pokorny I 844; Vries ANEW 142–143; Zalizniak I 151; Lehmann GED 129, 153; Bammesberger Nom. 159; Kluge- Seebold 286.

ON fríär ‘fair, beautiful’, OE fríd-hengest ‘stately horse’. Related to *frijaz. Structurally identical with Skt prītá- ‘satisfied’, Av friya- ‘glad, happy’. Bartholomae 1025; Karsten NB II 302–303; Torp-Falk 247; Feist 167; Holthausen AEEW 116; Mayrhofer II 380; Pokorny I 844; Vries ANEW 143; Lehmann GED 127; Heidermanns 214–215.

Goth freidjan ‘to keep safe, to take care’, ON fríäa ‘to adorn’, OHG frīten ‘to take care of, to pet’. Derived from *frīđaz. Torp-Falk 247; Feist 167; Vries ANEW 142; Lehmann GED 127; Heidermanns 214; Kluge- Seebold 286.

ON pl. Frísir ‘Frisian’, OE pl. Frísan id. (n-stem), OFris pl. Frīsa, Frīsa id., MDu pl. Friesen, Vresen id., OHG pl. Frieson id. Related to *frijaz? Erdmann SVSU I/1 83–86 (to Fris frīsle ‘frizz’ or to Lat prīmus ‘first’); Holthausen AEEW 116; Vries ANEW 143.

Goth froþs ‘wise, intelligent’, ON fróär ‘knowing, learned’, OE fród ‘wise, prudent, skilful’, OFris frōd ‘clever, wise’, OS frōd ‘learned, wise’, OHG fruot ‘skillful, long-lasting, beloved’. Derivationally close to Lith prõtas ‘understanding’, Latv prāts id. Further related to Toch A pratim, B pratiá ‘decision’, Lith prantū, prāsti ‘to get used, to become accustomed to, to understand’, Latv prast ‘to understand’ (Fraenkel IF L 229). Försterman KZ I 237; Curtius KZ IV 237 (to Lat interpres ‘negotiator’); Torp- Falk 246; Feist 169; Holthausen AEEW 117; Karsten NB I 65; Trier DW 71– 73; Jóhannesson IEW 567; Pokorny I 845; Chemodanov SGGJa I 75–76; Fraenkel 646; Vries ANEW 144; Seebold 208; Piirainen 11–82; Lehmann GED 126; Heidermanns 217–218.
Goth frodei ‘intelligence’, ON frœäi ‘knowledge’, OHG fruotī ‘maturity, prudence’. Derived from *frōđaz. Torp- Falk 246; Feist 169; Heidermanns 218.
ON frœäi ‘record, lore’, MDu vroede ‘intelligence’. Derived from *frōđaz. Vries ANEW 145.
Burg *frodjan ‘to be wise’, ON frœäa ‘to teach’, OHG fruoten ‘to teach, to make wise’. Derived from *frōđaz. Vries ANEW 145; Köbler 710; Heidermanns 218.

ON frœkn ‘valiant, stout’, OE frécne ‘horrible, savage, audacious’, OS frōkni ‘valiant, daring’, OHG fruohhan- (in prop.). Related to *frekaz. Karsten NB II 449; Förster Eng S XXXIX 328–332; Torp-Falk 245; Feist 136; Holthausen AEEW 117; Pokorny I 845; Vries ANEW 145; Lehmann GED 103; Lühr Expr. 331; Heidermanns 218–219.
MLG adv. vrō ‘early’, OHG adv. fruo id., adj. fruoi id. Identical with Gk adj. πρώϊος id. Torp-Falk 233; Pokorny I 814; Frisk II 607–608.

Goth fruma ‘first, former’, ON frum-burär ‘firstborn’, OE frum ‘original, primitive, first’, OFris forma ‘first one’, OS formo id. Related to Lith pīrmas ‘first’, OPrus pirmas id. further connected with IE *pṛH⁄o- id. Cf. also, with the same suffix, Gk πρόμος ‘foremost man, chief’, Lat prīmus ‘first’ < *prīsmo-. Cf. superlative *frumistaz > Goth frumists ‘first’, OE fyrmest id., OFris formest id. Torp-Falk 233; Holthausen AEEW 118, 120; Feist 169–170; Walde- Hofmann II 362–363; Pokorny I 814– 815; Chemodanov SGGJa I 74; Fraenkel 597–598; Vries ANEW 144; Frisk II 600; Lehmann GED 129–130; Bammesberger Nom. 234; Ross-Berns Numerals 624.
Goth frumei ‘beginning’, OS frumī ‘usefulness’. Derived from *frumaz ~ *frumōn. Feist 169; Lehmann GED 129– 130.

ON froskr ‘frog’, OE frox id., MLG vorsch id., OHG frosc id. OE frozza, frocza id. is a pet-form of *fruskaz similar to docza ‘dog’. Cf. also ON fraukr, frauki ‘frog’. Derived from *frawaz. Marstrander Mindeskr. Bugge 242 (to *frauþō(n)); Uhlenbeck PBB XXII 197; Torp-Falk 248; Sturtevant MP XXVI 473 (ON frauki from *frauđkōn); Holthausen AEEW 113; Porzig IF XLV 165 (to Slav *prygati ‘to jump’); Pokorny I 846; Vries ANEW 140, 144; Zalizniak I 131; Onions 378; Kluge-Seebold 288; Adams TB 416–417 (to Toch AB pruk- ‘to make a leap’).

ON frost ‘frost’, OE frost, forst id., OFris forst, frost id., OS frost id., OHG frost id. Derived from *freusanan ‘to freeze’. Torp-Falk 248; Holthausen AEEW 113; Pokorny I 846; Vries ANEW 144; Zalizniak II 167; Onions 379; Seebold 211; Lehmann GED 129; Bammesberger Nom. 77; Kluge-Seebold 288.
ON frygä ‘blossoming’, Du vreugde id. Derived from unattested *fruwaz, cf. *frawaz. Torp-Falk 233.

ON frer ‘frost’, OHG fror id. Cf. Goth frius ‘coldness, frost’ < *freusan. Derived from *freusanan. Zupitza Gutt. 9; Torp-Falk 248; Feist 169; Vries ANEW 145; Seebold 211; Bammesberger Nom. 56; Lehmann GED 129.

ON fuä-flogi ‘runaway from his bride’, E dial. fud ‘vulva’, MHG vut ‘cunnus’. Related to Skt pūti- ‘putrid, stinking’, Av pūiti- ‘putrefaction, rottenness’, Lat pūteō ‘to stink, to be rotten’ (van Helten Zd Wf X 195). Johansson KZ XXXVI 352–353; Bartholomae 909; Walde-Hofmann II 392; Mayrhofer II 321; Pokorny I 849; Vries ANEW 145–146; Kluge-Seebold 281 (to Skt dual putau ‘buttocks’).

Goth fugls ‘bird’, ON fugl id., OE fuzol id., OFris fugel id., OS fugal id., OHG fogal id. From a dissimilated *fluzlaz derived from *fleuzanan (Graff III 434; Meyer Goth. 556), especially in view of OE fluzol ‘apt to fly, flying swiftly’ (see *fluzulaz). Bopp Glossarium 224 (to Lith paªkštis ‘bird’); Grienberger Got. 76; Bezzenberger Zd Wf VII 78; Berneker IF IX 361–362 (to Skt phuka- ‘bird’); Uhlenbeck PBB XXVI 310; Solmsen IF XIV 482–485 (to *fulōn); Zupitza Gutt. 132; Torp-Falk 243; Holthausen IF XXV 152 (to Gk πευκεδανός ‘pinecovered’), AEEW 118; Feist 170 (to Lat plūma ‘feather’); Solmsen IF XXXI 482– 484 (to *pōu- ‘small, little’); Feist 170; Pokorny I 837; Chemodanov SGGJa I 74; Vries ANEW 146 (follows Bopp); Zalizniak I 131; Onions 374; Seebold 201 (follows Bopp); Szemerényi Scripta 1074; Trubachev VJa XXIX/3 10 (to Slav *puga ‘blunt end of an egg’); Lehmann GED 130; Bammesberger Nom. 75; Kluge-Seebold 866.

Goth fauho ‘vixen, she-fox’, ON fóa id., MLG vohe, vō id., OHG foha id. Related to Toch B pako ‘tail’, Skt púccha- ‘tail’, Alb push ‘down, fluff’ < PAlb *puksja, Lith paustīs ‘hair (of animals)’, Slav *puxъ ‘down, fluff’ (Uhlenbeck Btrg. Gesch. XXII 538–539). Försterman KZ I 498 (related to élī-pek-); Meillet BSL XXII 243 (questions the IE source); ZubatŸ KZ XXXI 13 (to Slav *pyskъ ‘muzzle’); Schrader BB XV 135–136 (to Gk Lac φοῦαι: ἀλώπεκες, Hes.); Wood AJPhil XLI 349 (to Gk πευκεδανός ‘sharp’); Lidén NB XIX 87–88; Torp- Falk 243; Feist 144–145; Mayrhofer II 298–299; Pokorny I 837; Fraenkel 554; Vries ANEW 136; Zalizniak II 227; Onions 374; Vasmer-Trubachev III 414; Kutzelnigg Muttersprache XC 185–188 (against Uhlenbeck); Lehmann GED 110; Bammesberger Nom. 174; Toporov PJa IV 84–85 (similar semantic development in Lith lãpë ‘fox’ ~ lãpas ‘leaf ’); Kluge- Seebold 244; Orel AED 351; Adams TB 352–353.

ON fox ‘fraud’ (neut.), OSwed fux ‘male fox’, OE fox id., OS fohs id., OHG fuhs id. Derivative in *-s- connected with *fuxōn. Holthausen AEEW 113; Pokorny I 849; Vries ANEW 139; Zalizniak II 227; Onions 374; Lehmann GED 110; Bammesberger Nom. 174; Kluge-Seebold 289.
ON fold-vegr ‘Earth’, OE fold-wez ‘earth-way, earth’. Compound of *fulđō(n) and *wezaz. Carr Nom. comp. 54.

Burg *fulþ ‘field’, ON fold ‘field, earth’, OE folde ‘earth’, OS folda id. Related to Skt pṛthá- ‘palm of the hand’, Gk πλάτη ‘blade of an oar, winnowing fan’ and the like (Persson UUÅ 1891 10). See *felþuz ~ *felþan. Hellquist ANF VII 8 (to Gk πλάτος ‘breadth, width’); Lindquist Festschr. Olson 238–240 (same as Hellquist); Torp-Falk 244; Holthausen AEEW 111; Trier Lehm 24– 30; Mayrhofer II 332; Pokorny I 806 (to *pel 6-: *plā-), 833; Vries ANEW 137; Zalizniak II 199; Frisk II 553–554; Bammesberger Nom. 175; Köbler 710.

ON fylgja ‘to follow’, OE folzian id., OFris folgia, folia id., OS folgon id., OHG folgēn id. Identical with Slav *pьlzîti ‘to crawl’ (Anikin Etim. 1984 29) derived from *pьlzti id. Torp-Falk 237; Holthausen AEEW 111; Pokorny I 850; Vries ANEW 148; Onions 367; Vasmer-Trubachev III 309; Anikin Etim. 1980 41–49, Etim. 1981 131–140; Kluge-Seebold 277–278.
ON fylgä ‘following, backing up’, OE folgoä ‘train, retinue, service’, OHG folgida ‘sect’. Derived from *fulzēnan. Holthausen AEEW 112; Vries ANEW 147.
ON fyl ‘foal’, OHG fuli id. Derived from *fulōn. Vries ANEW 147.
ON fólk-drótt ‘band of warriors’, OE folc-dryht ‘multitude of people’. Compound of *fulkan and *đruxtiz. Carr Nom. comp. 80 (parallel formations).
ON fólk-land ‘land occupied by an army, district’, OE folcland ‘land held by freemen’. Compound of *fulkan and *lanđan. Carr Nom. comp. 80 (parallel formations).

ON fólk ‘crowd, army’, OE folc ‘folk, people’, OFris folk id., OS folk id., OHG folc id. Etymologically related to OBret ol- ‘to follow’, Bret heulia id., OBret a olguo ‘by seeking’, W ol ‘trace’, Corn ol id. continuing *pol·-. Bezzenberger BB V 171 (to Lat uulgus ‘great mass, multitude, people’); Zupitza Gutt. 9; Torp-Falk 235 (to Lat populus ‘people’); Jokl Studien 71; Holthausen AEEW 111; Pokorny I 799 (to IE *pel 6- ‘to fill, to pour’); Vries ANEW 137; Zalizniak II 167; Onions 367; Fleuriot Gloses 276; Kluge-Seebold 866.
ON fólk-víg ‘warfare’, OFris fol-wīch id., MLG volk-wīch id., OHG folk-wīg. Compound of *fulkan and *wīzan. Carr Nom. comp. 47.
ON fylki ‘country, shire’, OE ze-fylce ‘army, troop, band of warriors’. Derived from *fulkan. Vries ANEW 148.
ON full ‘goblet’, OE full ‘cup’. Derived from *fullaz. Torp-Falk 235; Holthausen IF XXV 152 (to Lat peluis ‘basin’, Gk πέλλα ‘wooden bowl’), AEEW 118; Vries ANEW 146 (follows Holthausen).
Goth fulla-weis ‘mature, thoroughly instructed, τέλειος’, ON fullvíss ‘quite certain’. Compound of *fullaz and *wīsaz. Carr Nom. comp. 67.

Goth fulls ‘full’, ON fullr id., OE ful(l) id., OFris fol, ful id., OS ful id., OHG fol id. Continues IE *p¬Hnos id., an adjective in *-no- of *pel H- ‘to fill’: Skt pūrṇá-, OIr lán, Lith pīlnas, Slav *pьlnъ (Hirt Idg. Gr. II 130). Torp-Falk 235; Pedersen Kelt. Gr. I 52, 178; Trautmann BSW 218; Feist 172; Holthausen AEEW 118; Karsten NB II 206; Martinet Gém. 25, 71; Mayrhofer II 324; Pokorny I 798–800; Fraenkel 591; Vries ANEW 146; Onions 380; Vasmer-Trubachev III 312; Lehmann GED 131; Heidermanns 220–221; Kluge-Seebold 866.

Goth ufar-fullei ‘superabundance’, OESc eþa-fylli ‘group of men necessary for an oath’, OE fyllu ‘fulness’, OHG fullī id. Derived from *fullaz. Holthausen AEEW 119; Feist 509–510; Onions 355; Lehmann GED 131; Heidermanns 220; Kluge-Seebold 290.

Goth fulljan ‘to fill’, ON fylla id., OE fyllan id., OFris fella id., OS fullian id., OHG fullen id. Derived from *fullaz. Cf. a similar derivation in Slav *pьlniti id. Torp-Falk 235; Holthausen AEEW 119; Feist 172; Vries ANEW 148; Lehmann GED 131; Heidermanns 220; Kluge-Seebold 290.
Goth fullnan ‘to be full, to become full’, ON fullna ‘to fulfil’. Derived from *fullaz. Feist 172; Heidermanns 220.
OESc fulla ‘to fulfil’, OE fullian id., OFris fulla, fullia id., OS fullon id., OHG follōn id. Derived from *fullaz. Holthausen AEEW 119; Heidermanns 220.
Goth fullo ‘fullness’, ON theon. Fulla, OHG folla ‘plenitude’. Derived from *fullaz. Structurally close to Av p 6 r 6 nā ‘plenitude’. Bartholomae 850; Feist 172; Heidermanns 220.

OE folm ‘palm’, OS pl. folmōs id., OHG folma id. Close to Lat palma ‘palm (of the hand)’, OIr lám ‘hand’, Gk παλάμη ‘palm of the hand’ (Kretschmer KZ XXXI 398). Torp-Falk 236; Holthausen AEEW 112; Walde- Hofmann II 240–241; Pokorny I 806; Frisk II 466; Bammesberger Nom. 115.

Goth fula ‘foal’, ON foli id., OE fola id., OFris fola id., OS folo id., OHG folo id. A zero grade form related to Arm ul ‘she-goat’, Gk πῶλος ‘foal’, Alb pelë ‘mare’ < *pō(u)lo-, *pō(u)lā (Försterman KZ I 495; Mladenow KZ L 54–55). Hirt Ablaut 39 (to *pōu- ‘small’); Torp- Falk 244; Holthausen AEEW 111; Feist 170–171; Pokorny I 842–843; Vries ANEW 137; Zalizniak I 153; Frisk II 634; Onions 366; Lehmann GED 130; Kluge-Seebold 277; Orel AED 314.
ON fundr ‘a thing found’, MLG vunt ‘find’, MHG vunt id. Derived from *fenþanan. Vries ANEW 147.
ON fyndr ‘find’, OFris fynd id. Substantivized *funđiz II. Vries ANEW 148; Seebold 194.
ON ein-fyndr ‘as finder entitled to the whole’, OE eáä-fynde ‘easy to find’. Derived from *fenþanan. Holthausen AEEW 120; Seebold 194; Matzel Festschr. Schröder 99, KZ CV 105; Heidermanns 221.

OE fÿst ‘fist’, OFris fēst id., OS fūst id., OHG fūst id. Related to Lith kúmstë id. (with metathesis), Slav *pęstь id < *p–k⁄-sti- (Saussure MSL VII 93). Further connected with *fenfe. Torp-Falk 243; Trautmann BSW 218– 219; Holthausen AEEW 121; Pokorny I 839; Pokorny I 30; Chemodanov SGGJa I 80; Fraenkel 309–310; Onions 358; Stang LS 42; Lehmann GED 114; Ross- Berns Numerals 662; Kluge-Seebold 253.

Goth fon ‘fire’ (< *fwōn), gen. sg. funins (with traces of a root stem), ON funi id. Reflects the old oblique forms in *-n- of r/n-stem as in OPrus panno id. (Fick KZ XVIII 416), cf. *fuwer ~ *fūr. From *funōn WGmc *funkōn (ME fonke ‘spark’ MLG vunke id., OHG funco id.) is derived. Weinhold KZ I 248; Brugmann IF XXXIII 308–310; Zupitza Gutt. 162 (*funkōn to Skt pājas- ‘surface, face’); Torp-Falk 227, 243; Feist 158–159; Pokorny I 828, 898–990 (*funkōn to Gk φέγγος ‘light’, Lith spīngiu, spingėti ‘to shine’); Vries ANEW 147; Zalizniak I 223; Lehmann GED 120; Bammesberger Nom. 205–206.

Goth prop. Ala-fonsus, ON fúss ‘willing, eager’, OE fús ‘ready, prepared, prompt’, OS fūs ‘ready’, OHG funs ‘ready, expectant’. Perhaps, related to Gk πένομαι ‘to toil, to work’, Arm hanum ‘to weave’, Lith pīnti ‘to braid’, Slav *pьnǫ, *pęti ‘to spin’, cf. the same zero grade in *p–to-: πάτος: đnduma τῆς ἭΗρας, Hes. Torp-Falk 228 (to *fenþanan); Holthausen AEEW 119; Wissmann NP 59 (to *fenþanan, from *p–tso-); Stern Eng S LXVIII 161–173; Karsten NB II 343; Schröbler ZDADL LXXXII 245–247; Pokorny I 808–809; Vries ANEW 147; Frisk II 504–506; Seebold 194; Bammesberger Sprache XVII 46–49 (to *fenþanan, from *p–t-to-); Lehmann GED 131–132; Bammesberger Sprache XVII 46–49, Nom. 250; Heidermanns 222–223.
ON fÿsa ‘to exhort’, OE fÿsan ‘to hasten, to make haste, to incite’, OS part. a-fūsid ‘to strive’. Derived from *funsaz. Torp-Falk 228; Holthausen AEEW 121; Vries ANEW 149; Heidermanns 222.

Goth faur ‘in front of, before’, ON for- id., OE fór id., OFris fora id., OS fora, for id., OHG fora id. Cf. also ON fyr id., OS furi id., OHG furi id. Related to Gk πάρ ‘along’, Lat por- (Güntert IF XX 37–40). Cf. *furān. Torp-Falk 231; Holthausen AEEW 112; Walde-Hofmann II 343; Feist 145; Pokorny I 813; Vries ANEW 148; Frisk II 472–473; Onions 368; Lehmann GED 110; Kluge-Seebold 291, 867.

Goth faura ‘ahead, before, in front of’, OE fóre ‘before, aforetime’, OFris fora ‘in front of’, OS fora id., OHG fora id. Close to Skt purā ‘formerly, before’, Av par¡ id., Gk παρά ‘near’. Collitz BB XVII 17 (to Gk παραί ‘near’); Bartholomae 852; Solmsen KZ XLIV 165 (follows Collitz); Holthausen AEEW 112; Feist 145; Mayrhofer II 310; Pokorny I 810–818; Frisk II 472–473; Onions 368–369; Lehmann GED 110–111.

OE ford ‘ford’, OFris forda id. (n-stem), OS -ford (in cmpn.), OHG furt id. Cf. also *ferþuz > ON fjǫrär ‘firth, bay’. Related to Av p 6 r 6 tu- ‘ford, bridge’, OW rit ‘ford’, Lat portus ‘harbor, door’. Bartholomae 892; Torp-Falk 230; Holthausen AEEW 112; Walde-Hofmann II 343– 344; Pokorny I 817; Vries ANEW 126; Onions 369; Seebold 188; Lehmann GED 109, 136; Bammesberger Nom. 158; Kluge-Seebold 292.

ON forr ‘forward, haughty’. Identical with Gk πρόκα ‘right now, right away’, Lat recī-procus ‘turning back the same way, returning, receding’, Slav *prokъ ‘spare, remaining’. Persson Beiträge 960 (to Lat pro-sperus ‘hurried’); Karsten NB I 16–17 (to IE *pers- ‘to spray’); Torp-Falk 232; Walde-Hofmann II 424; Pokorny I 813; Vries ANEW 139; Frisk II 599; Heidermanns 223.
OE forn ‘trout’, OHG forhana id. Close to Skt pṛśni- ‘spotted, variegated’, Gk πορκνός ‘dark’. Torp-Falk 244; Holthausen AEEW 113; Pokorny I 821; Lehmann GED 109.

ON for ‘drain, sewer’, OE furh ‘furrow’ (root stem), OFris furch id., MLG vore id., OHG furh, furuh id. (< *furxiz). Identical with Lat porca id., W rh#ch id. (Fick III 244; Zupitza KZ XXXV 256–257). Zupitza Gutt. 132; Pedersen Kelt. Gr. I 122; Torp-Falk 244; Holthausen AEEW 119; Walde-Hofmann II 340–341; Porzig Gliederung 125; Pokorny I 821; Chemodanov SGGJa I 102; Vries ANEW 137; Zalizniak II 207; Onions 382; Bammesberger Nom. 196 (root stem); Kluge-Seebold 291.

Goth faurhts ‘fearful’, OE forht id., OS forht, foraht ‘afraid, frightened’, OHG got-forht ‘fearful of God’. Derivative in *-to- related to Toch AB pärsk- ‘to fear’, A praski ‘fear’, B prosko, proskye id. (Holthausen IF XXXIX 65). Kirchhoff KZ I 39; Torp-Falk 244 (to Arm erk‘'nim ‘to be afraid’); Zupitza Gutt. 5–6, 190; Sütterlin IF XXV 65 (to Gk φρίσσω ‘to be rough’); Lewy IF XXXII 159 (to Lith peršėti ‘to hurt, to pain’); Feist 146–147; Holthausen AEEW 112; Pokorny I 820; Chemodanov SGGJa I 111; Onions 377; Van Windekens I 366; Lehmann GED 111; Bammesberger Nom. 250, JIES XIX 354 (to *frexnanan ~

Goth faurhtei ‘fright, fear’, OE fyrhtu id. Cf. also OHG forahta id. Derived from *furxtaz. Torp-Falk 244; Holthausen AEEW 120; Feist 146; Pokorny I 820; Zalizniak II 179; Onions 377; Lehmann GED 111; Heidermanns 224; Kluge-Seebold 291– 292.

Goth faurhtjan (sik) ‘to be frightened’, OE fyrhtan ‘to terrify, to frighten’, OFris unt-fruchta ‘to beware’, OS forhtian ‘to fear’, OHG for(a)hten ‘to fear, to loathe’. Derived from *furxtaz. Torp-Falk 244; Holthausen AEEW 120; Feist 146; Pokorny I 820; Lehmann GED 111; Heidermanns 224.

ON fura, fúra ‘fir-tree’, OE furh-wudu ‘fir-tree’, OS furia ‘pine’ (jōstem), OHG foraha ‘pine, fir-tree’. Related to Lat quercus ‘oak’, W perth ‘bush’ < *k⁄erk⁄-t-. Cf. *ferxwuz. Loewe PBB LX 160–163 (to Gk πέρκη ‘perch’ rather than to Lat quercus); Hoops Waldbäume 118; Zupitza Gutt. 190; Torp-Falk 234; Holthausen AEEW 119; Walde- Hofmann II 402–403; Porzig Gliederung 147; Pokorny I 822; Vries ANEW 147 (to Gk mnt.
