Πυρραῖον, Skt parka†ī ‘Ficus infectoria’); Zalizniak II 216; Onions 357; Griepentrog Wurzelnomina 443; Kluge-Seebold 277.

ON fyrstr ‘first’, OE fyrest, fyrst id., OFris ferost id., OS furist, OHG furisto ‘first’. Superlative formation based on *fur(a). Torp-Falk 232; Holthausen AEEW 120; Pokorny I 812; Vries ANEW 149; Onions 357; Ross-Berns Numerals 625; Kluge-Seebold 292.

Goth ga-faurs ‘temperate, restrained, well-behaved’, MHG ge-vüere ‘fitting, useful’. Derived from *faranan with an unaccountable divergence in vocalism (Grienberger Got. 81). Wood MLN XVI 310 (to Gk παῦρος ‘small’); Uhlenbeck PBB XXX 279 (to Lat pūrus ‘pure’); Feist 180; Lehmann GED 137.
ON forn-maär ‘man of the olden time’, OE fyrn-mann ‘man of yore. Compound of *furnaz and *mannz. Carr Nom. comp. 58.
ON forn ‘old’, OS adv. forn ‘previously’, OHG adv. forn ‘previously’. Zero grade of *fernaz. Torp-Falk 231; Vries ANEW 138; Lehmann GED 106.

ON fors ‘waterfall, stream’. Close to Slav *porxъ ‘dust’ (Meillet Etudes 285) further related to Skt párßati ‘to sprinkle’, Toch AB pärs- ‘to sprinkle’. Pedersen IF V 54; Torp-Falk 244; Mayrhofer II 230; Pokorny I 823; Vries ANEW 139; Vasmer-Trubachev III 332–333; Adams TB 375.
OHG forsca ‘question’. An archaic zero-grade noun identical with Class. Skt pṛcchā id., Arm harc' id. See *frexnanan ~ *freznanan. Torp-Falk 246; Porzig IF XLV 156–157; Mayrhofer II

OE forä ‘forth, forwards’, OFris forth id., OS forth id., MHG vort id. Cf. also Goth faurþis ‘earlier, beforehand’. Derived from *fur(a). Torp-Falk 232; Holthausen AEEW 113; Feist 147; Pokorny I 810–818; Onions 372.
ON foräa ‘to help oneself forth, to save one’s life’, OE foräian ‘to further, to advance’. Derived from *furþa. Holthausen AEEW 113; Vries ANEW 138.

ON fÿrr ‘fire’ (masc. i-stem), fúrr (< *fūraz), OE fÿr id., OFris fiūr, fiōr id., OS fiur id., OHG fiur, fuir id. Related to Hitt paḫḫur id., Toch A por, B pūwar id., Arm hur id., Gk πῦρ id., Umbr pir id. Cf. *funōn. Brugmann IF XXXIII 308–313; Torp-Falk 243; Holthausen AEEW 120; Feist 158– 159; Pokorny I 828; Vries ANEW 149; Zalizniak I 223; Frisk II 627–628; Onions 357; Bammesberger Nom. 205; Kluge-Seebold 262; Adams TB 392– 393.
ON part. fúinn ‘rotten’. Related to Skt pūyati ‘to become putrid, z

Goth gabigs ‘rich’, ON gǫfugr ‘worshipful, noble’, OE ziefiz ‘rich’, OHG kepic id. Cf. OHG gebigī ‘opulence’. Derived from *zebanan. Schmid ZDADL XLIX 520; Holthausen AEEW 128; Feist 175; Vries ANEW 198; Lehmann GED 134.

Goth gabei ‘riches, opulence’, OHG kepī id. Derived from *zebanan. Much Festschr. Jellinek 84 (from adj. *zabaz, further—to *zebanan); Bugge PBB XII 416–417 (from *za-ab-, to Lat cōpia ‘crowd’); Kluge Zd Wf VIII 28 (to Lat habeō ‘to have’); Feist 175–176; to stink’, Gk p-yomai ‘to rot’, Lat pūteō ‘to stink, to be rotten’, Lith pūvū, púti ‘to rot’. Torp-Falk 242–243; Walde- Hofmann II 392; Jóhannesson IEW 565–566; Mayrhofer II 322; Pokorny I 848–849; Fraenkel 680–681; Frisk II 621–622; Seebold 196–197.

Goth fuls ‘fetid, foul’, ON fúll ‘rotten, stinking’, OE fúl ‘foul, dirty, rotten’, OFris fūl ‘stinking’, MLG vūl ‘rotten, spoiled’, OHG fūl ‘foul, putrid’. Derived from *fūanan. Structurally close to Lith pl. pūliai ‘pus’ < *pūlo-. Torp- Falk 242; Feist 172; Holthausen AEEW 118; Karsten NB II 263–264; Pokorny I 848–849; Dybo VSJa 26; Chemodanov SGGJa I 78; Fraenkel 680; Vries ANEW 146; Onions 373; Seebold 197; Lehmann GED 131; Bammesberger Nom. 249; Heidermanns 219; Kluge- Seebold 252.

ON fúi ‘rottenness’. Close to Gk πῦον ‘discharge from a sore’, Arm hu ‘blood from an ulcer’, Lat pūs ‘pus, white matter’ < *pu⁄os and further to *fūanan ‘to rot’. Torp-Falk 242; Pokorny I 849; Vries ANEW 146; Frisk II 621–622; Seebold 196. Benveniste IEL 48; Lehmann GED 134.

OE pl. zaflas ‘fork, prop’, OS gabala id., OHG gabala id. Related to or borrowed from Celt-Lat gabalus ‘a kind of gallows’, OIr gabul ‘gable, fork’. Torp-Falk 125; Walde- Hofmann I 575; Trier ZDADL LXXVI 15–19; Chemodanov SGGJa I 98; Kluge- Seebold 294.
OSwed gadder ‘lattice’, MLG gaddere id., OHG gataro id. Derived from *zađōjanan (see *zađilinzaz). Torp- Falk 123.

Goth gadiliggs ‘cousin’, OE zædelinz ‘companion’, OS gaduling ‘relative’, OHG gatiling id. Cf. also *zađōjanan: OFris gadia ‘to unite’, MLG gaden ‘to suit, to please’, OHG bi-gatōn ‘to forgather, to get together’. Derived from IE *ghadh-: Skt gádhya- ‘fitting, suiting’, Lith goda ‘honor’, Slav *godъ ‘time, year’ (Grassmann KZ XII 128–129). Froehde BB VIII 165 (to Lat habeō ‘to have’); Fick III 123; Bezzenberger BB XVI 243 (same as Froehde); Wood KZ XLV 69; Brugmann IF XXI 316– 318; Prellwitz 2 (to Gk ἀγαθός ‘good’); Torp-Falk 123; Holthausen AEEW 121; Feist 178–179; Mayrhofer I 320–321; Pokorny I 423–424; Fraenkel 159–160; Trubachev ESSJa VI 191– 192; Lehmann GED 136; Kluge-Seebold 301.

ON gagl ‘wild goose’. Closely related to OPrus gegalis ‘a kind of duck’, Slav *gogolь ‘male duck’ (Bezzenberger BB I 253). Of imitative origin. Zupitza Gutt. 172; Trautmann BSW 74; Vries ANEW 152; Toporov PJa II 187– 189; Vasmer-Trubachev I 425.
ON gegn ‘against’, OE zezn id., OFris ien id., OS gegin id., OHG gegin, gagan id. Derived from *zazniz. Torp-Falk 119–120; Holthausen AEEW 126; Vries ANEW 160; Kluge- Seebold 306.

ON gegn ‘short (of way), ready’, OE zén ‘direct, short (of a road)’. Related to *zanzanan? Lewy PBB XXXII 140 (to Skt jaghána ‘hinder part, buttock, pudenda’, Gk κοχώνη ‘perineum’); Torp-Falk 119; Holthausen AEEW 126; Mayrhofer I 411; Vries ANEW 160; Onions 18; Kluge-Seebold 306.
ON gegna ‘to go against, to meet, to suit’, OE zieznan ‘to turn, to drive’, OHG gaganen, geginen ‘to meet’. Derived from *zazniz. Torp- Falk 120; Holthausen AEEW 128; Vries ANEW 160.
ON gaga ‘to throw the neck back’, MHG gāgen ‘to cackle’. Close to Lith gagū, gagėti id. (Mann Language XXVIII 35–36). Of imitative origin. Fraenkel 127–128; Pokorny I 407; Vries ANEW 152.

Goth gaidw ‘shortage, lack’, OE zád ‘lack, want, desire’, OFris gād ‘need’. Etymologically close to Gk χατέω ‘to lack’ further connected with Skt jáhāti ‘to leave, to abandon’ and the like. Probably, to be reconstructed as *·h 6 i-t⁄ó- (Schulze KZ XXVII 425, Kl. Schr. 53). Zupitza Gutt. 173; Torp-Falk 133; Feist 185; Holthausen AEEW 121; Mayrhofer I 426; Pokorny I 418–419; Frisk II 1077–1078; Meid Wortbild. 163 (to Skt hetú- ‘instigation, inducement’); Lehmann GED 139; Bammesberger Nom. 84; Heidermanns 227.
ON geigr ‘scathe, serious hurt’. Derived from *zīzēnan ~ *zinzēnan. Torp-Falk 134; Lehmann GED 137.

ON geiga ‘to take a wrong direction’, OE forzæzan ‘to transgress, to prevaricate’, OFris gēja ‘to pay penance’, G geigen ‘to move back and forth’. Derived from *zaizaz. Zupitza Gutt. 97; Torp-Falk 134; Holthausen AEEW 121; Pokorny I 421 (to Lith žiógauti ‘to yawn’); Vries ANEW 160–161; Lehmann GED 137.
OESc gælskaper ‘fornication’, OE zálscipe ‘luxury, lastfulness’. Derived from *zailaz. Heidermanns 226.

Burg *gails ‘merry’, OESc gælmaþer ‘bedfellow’, OE zál ‘light, pleasant, wanton’, OS gēl ‘merry’, OHG geil ‘highspirited’. Identical with Lith gailūs ‘piteous; biting, nippy, corrosive’, Latv gails ‘lecherous, lascivous’, Slav *zîlъ ‘strong’ (Zupitza Gutt. 171). Torp-Falk 120; Trautmann BSW 75; Feist 185; Holthausen AEEW 123; Pokorny I 452; Fraenkel 128–129; Stang LS 21; Vasmer-Trubachev II 92; Piirainen 56– 58; Vasmer-Trubachev II 92; Lehmann GED 140; Köbler 710; Heidermanns 226; Kluge-Seebold 307–308.
Goth gailjan ‘to gladden’, OHG ir-geilēn ‘to raise (one’s eyes)’. Derived from *zailaz. Zupitza Gutt. 171; Torp-Falk 120; Feist 185; Lehmann GED 139–140; Heidermanns 226.
ON geisl ‘staff’, masc. geisli id., MLG geisel ‘whip’, OHG geisila id. Derived from *zaizaz. Torp-Falk 121; Vries ANEW 162.
Goth us-gaisjan ‘to frighten’. Based on an unattested noun *zaisaz identical with Av zaēša- ‘horrible’. Bartholomae 1651; Torp-Falk 135; Feist 531–532; Pokorny I 427; Lehmann GED 382.

ON geispa ‘to yawn’, ME záspen ‘to gasp’. An emphatic derivative of *zaisjanan secondarily influenced by *zipōjanan. Torp-Falk 134 (reconstruct *zaipsōjanan, to *zipōjanan); Vries ANEW 162 (to ON geisa ‘to chafe, to rage’ or to *zipōjanan).

OE zæst ‘soul, spirit, mind’, OFris jēst id., OS gēst id., OHG geist id. Related to *zaisjanan. Cf. also a structurally similar but semantically diverging Lith žaizdā ‘wound’. Zupitza Gutt. 202; Torp-Falk 135; Holthausen AEEW 122; Pokorny I 427; Fraenkel 1285; Zalizniak I 131; Fraenkel 1285; Onions 396; Lehmann GED 382; Bammesberger Nom. 76; Kluge-Seebold 308.

Goth sbst. gaitein ‘kid’, OE zæten ‘pertaining to goats’, OHG geizīn id. Diminutive of *zaitz. Structurally identical with Lat haedīnus ‘of a kid’ (Porzig 114). Torp-Falk 120; Holthausen AEEW 122; Walde-Hofmann I 632; Feist 186; Pokorny I 409; Lehmann GED 140.

Goth gaits ‘she-goat’ (i-stem), ON geit id., OE zát id., OS gēt id., OHG geiz id. (i-stem). Related to Lat haedus ‘buck’ (Försterman KZ I 497). Prellwitz 500 (to Skt jáhāti ‘to leave, to abandon’); Fick I 433 (to Lith žáidžiu, žáisti ‘to play’); Zupitza Gutt. 200–201; Charpentier KZ XL 472; Torp-Falk 120; Hirt Urgerm. II 51 (on the consonantal stem in Gmc); Holthausen AEEW 124; Walde-Hofmann I 632; Feist 186; Krahe Spr. Vorz. 76; Porzig Gliederung 114; Trubachev VJa VIII/1 22–23 (adds Slav *ži(d)-mъlstь ‘honey-suckle’ < *‘goat’s throat’); Pokorny I 409–410; Chemodanov SSGJa I 58; Vries ANEW 162; Zalizniak II 207; Onions 404; Lehmann GED 140–141; Tollenaere KZ XCVI 141–144; Peeters KZ CI 167; Bammesberger Nom. 195; Griepentrog Wurzelnomina 443; Kluge-Seebold 308.
ON geir-laukr ‘garlic’, OE neut. zár-leác id. Compound of *zaizaz and *laukaz. Hoops Waldbäume 644 (ON < OE); Carr Nom. comp. 32 (follows Hoops); Vries ANEW 161.

ON geiri ‘goar, triangular strip’, OE zára ‘angular point of land, promontory’, OFris gāra ‘triangular piece (of land)’, MLG gēre ‘wedge-shaped piece’, OHG gēro ‘angular strip of land’. Derived from *zaizaz (after the triangular shape of the spear-head). Torp-Falk 121; Holthausen AEEW 124; Onions 406; Kluge-Seebold 307.

Goth prop. Gais-walds, ON geirr ‘spear’, OE zár ‘dart, javelin, spear’, OFris gēr id., OS gēr id., OHG gēr id. Cf. also *zaiđō > OE zád ‘point, sting, prick, goad’. Related to Lat-Gaul gaesum, γαῖσον ‘heavy iron spear’, OIr gaë ‘spear’, W gwayw id., MBret goaffid., Corn gew id. The same Indo-European word is attested as a loanword from an unknown (Western) source in Gk χαῖον, χαῖος ‘shepherd’s stick’. Cf. also Thrac prop. Boulo-γαίσης. Zupitza Gutt. 202; Torp- Falk 120–121; Falk SVAO 1914/6 82– 83; Schrader-Nehring II 425 (Gmc < Celt); Walde-Hofmann I 575–576; Holthausen AEEW 124; Detschew Thr. 79; Pokorny I 410 (to Skt heßa- ‘weapon’); Vries ANEW 161–162; Zalizniak I 131; Onions 403; Frisk 1061– 1062; Chantraine 1240; Bammesberger Nom. 54 (to Gk χαῖος); Kluge-Seebold 315; Boutkan OFED s.v.

ON gala ‘to crow, to sing’, OE zalan ‘to sing’, OHG galan ‘to incantate’. Originally, of imitative origin. Brugmann IF XXXII 190 (secondary present vocalism based on preterital *zōl-); Torp-Falk 130; Berneker I 293 (to Slav *galiti ‘to triumph, to laugh’ < *ghōl-eƒo-); Holthausen AEEW 123; Schwarz Festschr. Trier 442–444 (reconstructs the original meaning ‘to praise’); Jóhannesson IEW 382–385; Pokorny I 428; Vries ANEW 153; Onions 1019; Seebold 212–213; Lehmann GED 157– 158 (to Gk χελιδών ‘swallow’).
ON geldingr ‘wether’, G Gelling-hanf ‘male hemp’. Derived from *zalđiz ~ *zalđjaz. Vries ANEW 162; Heidermanns 228.

Goth pln. Galtis (< *galþeis), ON geldr ‘barren, yielding no milk’, OE zielde ‘fruitless’, MLG gelt id., OHG galt, gialt id. Related to Slav *goldъ ‘hunger’. Karsten NB II 376; Lessiak ZDADL 146–147 (participle of *zalanan); Olsen IF XXXVIII 168 (to Phryg pl. Γάλλοι ‘castrated servants of Cybela’); Torp-Falk 132 (to Lith žalā ‘harm, damage’); Holthausen AEEW 129; Feist 189; Schwarz Festschr. Trier 448–449 (to IE *ghel- ‘to peel, to cut’); Pokorny I 434; Vries ANEW 196, 198–199; Trubachev ESSJa VI 199–200; Lehmann GED 143; Heidermanns 228 (to IE *·hel- ‘to cut’); Kluge-Seebold 310.

ON gelda ‘to geld’, MDu gelten id., G gelzen id. Derived from *zalđiz ~ *zalđjaz. Structurally close to Slav *golditi ‘to cause hunger, to starve’. Jóhannesson IEW 385 (to *·hel- ‘to cut’); Schwarz Festschr. Trier 448–449; Vries ANEW 162–163; Trubachev ESSJa VI 199; Heidermanns 228.

ON galdr ‘song, witchcraft’, OE zealdor ‘incantation, charm’, OHG galtar id. Derived from *zalanan. Torp-Falk 130; Holthausen AEEW 124; Vries ANEW 153; Seebold 212; Lehmann GED 157, 193; Bammesberger Nom. 86.
ON gálga-tré ‘gallows’, OE zealz-tréow id. Compound of *zalzōn and *trewan. Carr Nom. comp. 54.

Goth galga ‘stake, gallows, cross’, ON gálgi ‘gallows’, OE zealza ‘gallows, gibbet, cross’, OFris galga ‘gallows’, OS galgo id., OHG galgo id. Related to Lith žalgā ‘long pole’ (Müller WZKM V 68) and, probably, to Arm ja∑k ‘branch’. Pedersen KZ XXXIX 361; Zupitza Gutt. 178, 201; Torp-Falk 131; Trautmann BSW 364; Holthausen AEEW 125; Feist 189; Pokorny I 411; Fraenkel 1286–1287; Vries ANEW 153; Zalizniak I 153; Onions 387; Solta Arm. 314–315; Stang LS 65; Lehmann GED 142; Kluge-Seebold 295.

ON gall ‘gall, bile’, OS galla id., OHG galla id. Continues *·hol-n-. Connected with Av zāra- id., Gk χόλος id., χολή id., Lat fel id. (n-stem) (Kuhn KZ I 516). Believed to be further related to the color adjective *ἵηελ-: Skt hári- ‘pale, yellowish, greenish’, Av zairi- ‘yellow, yellowish’, Lat heluus ‘light bay’, Lith žãlias ‘green’, žefivas ‘greenish’. Zupitza Gutt. 171; Persson Beiträge 794; Bartholomae 1690; Torp- Falk 131; Holthausen AWN 99, AEEW 125; Walde-Hofmann I 473–474, 639; Porzig Gliederung 110, 207; Mayrhofer III 581; Pokorny I 429–430; Chemodanov SGGJa I 89; Fraenkel 1287, 1297; Vries ANEW 153; Zalizniak II 182; Frisk II 1109–1110; Onions 386; Kluge-Seebold 296.

ON galli ‘fault, flaw’, OE zealla ‘fretted place on the skin’, MLG galle ‘wounded spot on the skin’, MHG galle ‘swelling’. Related to Lith žalā ‘harm, damage’, Slav *zъlъ ‘bad, angry’ (Holthausen AWN 79). Homonymic OE zealla ‘gall, bile’ belongs to *zallan ~ *zallōn. Zupitza Gutt. 201 (to OIr galar ‘sickness’); Torp-Falk 130; Holthausen AEEW 125; Endzelin KZ XLIV 66; Pokorny I 490; Fraenkel 1286; Vries ANEW 154; Onions 386 (to *zallan ~
ON galmr ‘sword’ (þula), OS galm ‘tone’, OHG galm ‘tone, sound’. Derived from *zalanan. Torp-Falk 130; Vries ANEW 154.
Swed dial. galpa ‘to cry (of birds)’, OS galpōn ‘to cry loudly’. Derived from *zelpanan. Torp-Falk 132.

ON gǫltr ‘boar, hog’, OE zealt-borz ‘pig’. Probably related to Lith acc. núo-galdā ‘dock in a stable’, ūž-galda id., Latv gafids ‘splinter, board, plank’. Suolahti ANT 158; Hirt BB XXIV 246 (to Skt huḍa- ‘ram’); Torp- Falk 131; Holthausen AEEW 125; Fraenkel 131; Vries ANEW 198–199; Kluge-Seebold 311.

ON gamall ‘old’, OE zamol, zomel id., MDu gamel id. Probably related to Lat humilis ‘low’, Gk χθαμαλός ‘near the ground, low’ (Szemerényi Word VIII 50). Kluge KZ XXVI 70 (from *zamēlaz, to *mēlan I); Wadstein IF V 12 (ON gamall < *za-xamalaz, to *xamalōjanan); Torp-Falk 127 (to *zōmz); Holthausen AEEW 123; Rooth Wortst. 50 (to ON gambra ‘to brag, to bluster’); Walde-Hofmann I 664–665; Pokorny I 414–416; Vries ANEW 154; Frisk II 1098–1099; Kluge-Seebold 856.

ON gaman ‘game, sport, amusement’, OE zamen ‘joy, game, pleasure’, OFris game, gome id., OS gaman id., OHG gaman ‘joke, joy’. Identical with Slav *gomonъ ‘noise’ (Il’inskii RFV LXIII 341) derived from *gomъ id. Torp-Falk 127; Berneker I 326–327 (Slav < Gmc); Pokorny I 490 (to *zampaz); Vasmer- Trubachev I 436.
ON gamna ‘to amuse, to divert’, OE zamnian ‘to joke, to play’. Derived from *zamanan. Vries ANEW 155; Onions 388.
ON gammi ‘Finnish dwelling’, G dial. Gamm ‘heap of drying bricks’. Related to *zōmz. Lidén Arm. St. 14 (to Arm gom ‘barn, pen’); Torp-Falk 127; Chemodanov SGGJa I 110.
Norw gamp ‘big, fat man, fat horse’, MHG gampf ‘shaking’. Of unknown origin. Torp-Falk 127.
ON gan ‘frenzy, frantic gestures’. Identical with Gk χάνος ‘yawning, gaping’. See *zanōjanan. Pokorny I 411; Frisk II 1076–1077.

Goth gaggan ‘to go’, ON ganga id., OE zanzan id., OFris gunga id., OS gangan id., OHG gangan id. Connected with *zanzaz. May be a back formation based on *zanzjanan (Porzig 147). Cf. also Lith žeñgti ‘to stride, to march’, Latv zīet ‘to trespass, to sin’ (Stang Sc Sl VIII 162–164). Schweizer KZ I 150; Holthausen AEEW 123; Zupitza Gutt. 178, 200; Torp-Falk 124; Feist 181–182, 288–289; Porzig Gliederung 147; Jóhannesson IEW 343– 344; Pokorny I 438; Chemodanov SGGJa I 76; Vries ANEW 156; Seebold 213–216; Mottausch KZ CXI 134–142; Lehmann Language XIX 318–319, GED 137–138.

Goth neut. gagg ‘street, road’, ON gangr ‘going, walking’, OE zanz ‘going, journey, way’, OFris gang, gong id., OS gang id., OHG gang id. See *zanzanan. Torp-Falk 124; Holthausen AEEW 123; Feist 181; Pokorny I 438; Vries ANEW 156; Zalizniak II 186; Onions 388; Seebold 214; Lehmann GED 138; Mottausch KZ CIX 76–109; Kluge-Seebold 297.
OESc eþ-ganger ‘legitimate’, OE acc. sg. fela-zeonze ‘having travelled much’, MLG gange ‘passable’, G gang und gäbe. See *zanzanan. Heidermanns 229–230.
ON gengil-beina ‘woman with crooked legs’, OE æfter-zenzel ‘successor’, OHG fuoz-gengil ‘pedestrian’. Derived from *zanziz. Holthausen AEEW 127; Seebold 214.

ON gengr ‘able to walk’, OE zenze ‘going, current, prevalent’, OFris gendze, ganse ‘current’, MDu genge id., OHG gengi id. Derived from *zanzanan. Karsten NB I 106–107; Torp-Falk 124; Holthausen AEEW 127; Vries ANEW 163; Seebold 214; Matzel Festschr. Schröder 106; KZ CV 127; Heidermanns 230; Kluge-Seebold 297; Boutkan OFED s.v.

Goth faura-gaggi ‘stewardship, management, administration’, ON gengi ‘help, support, success’, OE zenze ‘gang, flock, company’ (fem.), OS gi-gengi ‘row’, OHG ana-gengi ‘beginning’. Derived from *zanzanan or *zanziz. Holthausen AEEW 127; Feist 182; Vries ANEW 163; Seebold 214.
Goth gangjan ‘to go, to go on a journey’, OE zenzan ‘to go, to pass’, MHG gengen ‘to make go’. Derived from *zanzanan, *zanzaz. Holthausen AEEW 127; Feist 182; Pokorny I 438; Seebold 214.

Goth faura-gaggja ‘steward, manager’, ON for-ingi ‘leader’, OE ánzenza ‘lone-goer, loner’, OFris brād-genza ‘beggar’, OHG nāh-gengo ‘servant’. Derived from *zanzanan. Holthausen AEEW 127; Feist 182; Porzig Gliederung 147; Vries ANEW 138; Seebold 214.

ON ganga ‘walking’, OHG miti-ganga ‘servant, maid’. Derived from *zanzanan. Cf. in particular structurally similar Skt jáṅghā ‘lower leg’, Lith pra- žangā ‘violation, breach’. Zupitza Gutt. 197; Trautmann BSW 370; Fraenkel 1300; Seebold 214.
ON gǫngull ‘strolling’, OE wíd-zanzol ‘rambling, roving’. Derived from *zanzanan. Karsten NB II 286; Vries ANEW 199; Seebold 214; Heidermanns 230–231.

Goth fram-gahts ‘progress’, ON gátt ‘rabbet of a door-sill’, OE bed-zíht ‘evening’, OHG betti-gāht ‘going to bed’. Derived from *zanzanan. Torp-Falk 124; Holthausen AEEW 130; Feist 164; Vries ANEW 158; Seebold 214; Lehmann GED 124–125.

ON gana ‘to rush, to glare’, OE zánian ‘to yawn, to gape’, MHG jānen ‘to gape, to glare’. Derived from *zanaz. Related to Gk χαίνω id. Holthausen AEEW 123–124; Pokorny I 411, 419; Vries ANEW 155; Frisk II 1076–1077.
ON gás-haukr ‘goshawk’, OE zós-hafoc id. Compound of *zansz and *xabukaz. Carr Nom. comp. 48; Onions 407.

ON gás ‘goose’, OE zós id., OFris gōs id., MLG gōs id. (i-stem), OHG gans id. (i-stem). Span ganso id. is from Goth *gansus. Related to Skt haṃsá- id., Av zā id., Gk χήν id., Lat ānser id., OIr géiss ‘swan’, Lith žāsīs ‘goose’, Slav *gǫsь id. Zupitza Gutt. 201; Suolahti DVN 410–415; Berneker I 342 (Slav < Gmc); Torp-Falk 125; Holthausen AEEW 135; Walde-Hofmann I 52; Mayrhofer III 571; Pokorny I 412; Fraenkel 1292; Vries ANEW 157; Zalizniak II 207; Frisk II 1094–1095; Onions 406; Trubachev ESSJa VII 88–89; Bammesberger Nom. 196; Griepentrog Wurzelnomina 201–232; Kluge-Seebold 298.

OHG ganz ‘healthy, unharmed, whole’. Identical with Lith n‚gandas ‘dissatisfaction’, OLith adv. comp. gandžiaus ‘rather’. Torp-Falk 125; Fay IF XXXII 331–332 (to Alb gjithë ‘all’); Fraenkel 133, KZ LXXII 185–190 (Baltic forms to Skt ghaná- ‘compact, firm, hard, dense’); Heidermanns 233; Kluge- Seebold 298.

ON gapa ‘to gape’, OE ofer-zapian ‘to neglect, to disregard’, MLG gapen ‘to gape’, MHG gaffen id. Of imitative origin. Zupitza Gutt. 18, 217; Torp-Falk 125; Holthausen AEEW 124; Vries ANEW 156 (to *·hē- ‘to yawn’); Onions 389; Kluge-Seebold 294.

Goth gards ‘house, family, court’ (< *zarđiz), ON garär ‘yard’, OE zeard ‘enclosure, yard, dwelling, land’, OS gard ‘land, field’, pl. ‘yard’, OHG boumgart ‘garden, orchard’. Related to Toch B kerccī ‘palace’, Skt gṛhá- ‘house’, Av g 6 r 6 da- ‘dev’s cave’, Alb gardh ‘fence’, Lith gaıdas ‘pen, hurdle’, Slav *gordъ ‘fence, town’ (Lottner KZ XI 192). Meringer IF XVI 140; Grienberger Got. 90; Torp- Falk 129; Holthausen AEEW 125; Feist 197–198; Porzig Gliederung 171; Mayrhofer I 344; Pokorny I 444; Fraenkel 135–136; Vries ANEW 156; Zalizniak II 195; Onions 1018; Benveniste IEL 274; Trubachev ESSJa VII 37–38; Lehmann GED 147–148; Kluge-Seebold 300; Orel AED 110; Adams TB 196.
ON geräi ‘fenced field’. Derived from *zarđaz. Derivationally identical with Toch B kerccī ‘palace’, Phryg pln. Γόρδιον (Lidén Toch. 21–22). Adams TB 196,

Goth garda ‘enclosed yard, stockyard’, OFris garda ‘land property’, OS gardo ‘garden’, OHG garto id. A morphological variant of *zarđaz. Torp- Falk 129; Feist 197; Pokorny I 444; Zalizniak II 195; Kluge-Seebold 300.

ON garn ‘yarn’, OE zearn id., MLG garn id., OHG garn id. See *zarnō. Zupitza Gutt. 201; Torp-Falk 129; Holthausen AEEW 125–126; Pokorny I 443; Vries ANEW 157; Zalizniak I 140; Fraenkel II 1291; Onions 1018; Kluge-Seebold 299– 300.
ON garn-vinda ‘skein of yarn’, OE zearn-winde ‘reel’ (masc.?), OFris jern-wine id., MLG garnwinde id., OHG garn-winta id. Compound of *zarnō and *wenđōn. Carr Nom. comp. 72 (parallel formations).

ON gǫrn ‘gut’. Cf. WGmc neut. *zarnjan > OE myc-zern ‘fat about the kidneys’, OS mid-garni ‘internal fat’, OHG mitti-garni id. Identical with Lith žarnā ‘gut’, Latv zarna ‘intestine’. Further cf. Alb zorrë id. < PAlb *džārnā with a long *ā < *ē. Torp-Falk 129; Trautmann BSW 367; Pokorny I 787; Fraenkel 1291; Vries ANEW 199; Stang LS 65; Kluge-Seebold 299; Orel AED 525–526.

ON gerstr ‘sour, dismal’, MLG garst ‘rancid’, MHG garst id. Identical with MIr goirt ‘bitter’ < *ghorsti-. Torp-Falk 130; Pokorny I 445 (to Lith grastīs ‘menace, threat’, grasūs ‘causing weariness’ based on gresiū, gr Esti ‘to menace, to threaten’); Fraenkel 166– 167; Heidermanns 233; Kluge-Seebold 300.

ON gǫrr ‘ready for something’, OE zearu ‘ready, prepared’, OS garu id., OHG garo id. Related to *zeraz. Kaufmann PBB XX 530 (from *za-arwaz, see *arwaz); Karsten NB II 17, 414–416; Torp-Falk 128; Holthausen AEEW 126; Pokorny I 494; Vries ANEW 163–164 (to IE *g ⁄her- ‘to burn’), 199; Onions 1018; Seebold Lu E 458–460 (against the connection with *g ⁄her- ‘to burn’); Kabell KZ LXXXVII 26–31; Sanders Festschr. Kolb 558–568 (follows Kaufmann); Bammesberger Nom. 244; Heidermanns 233–234; Kluge-Seebold 298–299.
ON gerä ‘gear, harness, armor’, OHG garawida ‘garment, gear’. Derived from *zarwaz. Torp-Falk 128; Vries ANEW 164; Heidermanns 234.
ON gǫrvi ‘gear, apparel’, OE zearwe ‘clothing, attire, gear’, OHG garawī ‘equipment, attire, jewelry’. Derived from *zarwaz. Holthausen AEEW 126; Vries ANEW 165; Onions 392; Heidermanns 234.

ON gørva ‘to get ready, to make ready’, OE zierwan ‘to make ready, to prepare’, OS garwian, gerwian id., OHG garawen id. Derived from *zarwaz. Torp-Falk 128; Holthausen AEEW 130; Vries ANEW 163–164; Onions 389; Heidermanns 234; Kluge- Seebold 315–316.
ON gest-gjafi‘host’, MHG gast-gebe id. Compound of *zastiz and *zebōn. Carr Nom. comp. 83 (parallel formations).

Goth gasts ‘stranger, guest’, ON gestr ‘guest’, OE zæst, ziest ‘stranger, guest, enemy’, OFris jest ‘guest’, OS gast ‘enemy, guest’, OHG gast ‘guest, stranger’. Etymologically identical with Lat hostis ‘stranger’, Slav *gostь ‘guest’. Brugmann IF I 172–174; Zupitza Gutt. 172; Meillet MSL IX 374; Torp-Falk 133; Holthausen AEEW 130; Walde- Hofmann I 662–664; Feist 202; Mezger Language XXIV 154–155; Porzig Gliederung 207; Pokorny I 453; Chemodanov SGGJa I 65; Vries ANEW 165; Zalizniak I 149; Onions 418; Lindeman Verschärfung 11.2; Benveniste IEL 75, 78, 294, 301; Trubachev ESSJa VII 67–68; Lehmann GED 149; Kluge-Seebold 300.

ON gat ‘hole’, OE zeat ‘gate, door’, OFris gat, jet ‘hole, opening’, OS gat ‘eye of a needle, hole, opening’. Of uncertain origin. Probably connected with ancient Balkanic (Phrygian?) ζέτνα ‘door’ for which a conjecture *ζέταν is required. Holthausen PBB XI 553 (to Skt hádati ‘cacare’, Gk χέζω id.), AEEW 126; Zupitza Gutt. 201; Torp-Falk 123; Pokorny I 423; Georgiev Issledov. 139; Vries ANEW 157; Onions 391; Kluge- Seebold 301.

Goth gatwo ‘thoroughfare, street’, ON gata ‘thoroughfare’, MDu gate ‘passage’, OHG gazza ‘quarter’. Derived from *zatan (Persson Beiträge I 599). Bremer PBB XI 272 (to *·hēi- ‘to go’); Grienberger Got. 94 (to Gk χάσκω ‘to yawn, to gape’); Wiedemann BB XXX 214 (to Lith gendū, gēsti ‘to spoil’); Torp- Falk 123; Holthausen PBB XI 565 (to Skt hádati ‘to defecate’); Pedersen Kelt. Gr. I 63 (to W gwddw ‘neck’); Feist 205–206; Pokorny I 423; Vries ANEW 158; Zalizniak II 228; Onions 391; Lehmann GED 151; Kluge-Seebold 300.
ON gauä ‘barking’, OE zeáä ‘foolishness, luxury, mockery’. Derived from *zaujanan. Torp-Falk 121; Holthausen AEEW 126; Vries ANEW 158.

Goth gawi ‘region, country, environment’, OE -zé ‘land, district’ (in cmpn.), OFris gā ‘district’, OS -gō, -gā id. (in cmpn.), OHG gouwi, gewi ‘county, land’. Etymologically close to Gk χάος ‘emptiness’ < *xa Wow (Grienberger Got. 96). Legerlotz KZ VIII 119 (to Gk γαῖα ‘earth’); Burk Zd Wf II 341–342 (from Gmc *za-a(x)wjan ‘land among the waters’); Scheftelowitz BB XXVIII 310 (to Arm gavar ‘area’); Uhlenbeck PBB XXX 282 (to *·heu- ‘to call’); Torp-Falk 119 (follow Burk); Holthausen AEEW 124; Feist 210–211 (to Gk oǫh ‘village’), PBB XV 547–548 (from *za-wix-, to IE *wei˚- ‘dwelling’); Pokorny I 449; Frisk II 1072–1073; Lehmann GED 153; Kluge-Seebold 301; Boutkan OFED s.v.

ON geyja ‘to bark’. Cf. OE zóian ‘to lament’ < *zōwjanan. Related to Skt hávate ‘to call’, Lith žavėti ‘to charm’, Slav *zovǫ, *zъvati ‘to call’. Kock ANF XI 344–345 (from *za-waujan, to *waujan ‘to ring out, to resound’); Wood MLN XVI 19 (to IE *·heu- ‘to pour’); Torp-Falk 121; Holthausen AEEW 134; Jóhannesson IEW 318–320; Pokorny I 449 (to *·heu- ‘to yawn’); Chemodanov SGGJa I 75; Vries ANEW 165 (to Lith ga Usti ‘to rumble, to hum’); Vasmer-Trubachev II 85; Seebold 216.

ON gaukr ‘cuckoo’, OE zeác id., OFris gāk id., MLG gōk id., OHG gouh id. Of imitative origin. Uhlenbeck ANF XV 151–153 (< *za-aukaz, to *aukanan); Grienberger SBAW CXLII/8 95 (to *zaujanan); Meillet MSL XII 213–215 (to Lith geguz E ‘cuckoo’, Slav *žegъzica id.); Torp-Falk 122; Holthausen AEEW 124; Vries ANEW 158; Onions 408; Zalizniak I 131; Kluge- Seebold 301.
ON rare geymd ‘heed, attention’, OHG bi-goumida ‘care, worry’. Derived from *zaumjanan. Vries ANEW 166.

Goth gaumjan ‘to pay attention, to see, to observe’, ON geyma ‘to keep, to watch’, OE zÿman ‘to care for, to heed, to watch, to observe’, OS gōmian ‘to guard’, OHG goumen ‘to heed, to watch’. Derived from *zaum(j)ō(n). Zupitza Gutt. 172; Torp-Falk 121; Holthausen AEEW 129; Feist 207; Pokorny I 453; Vries ANEW 165–166; Lehmann GED 151; Kluge-Seebold 302.

ON gaum ‘attention, heed’, masc. gaumr id., OE zÿme ‘care’, OFris gāme id., NFris gumi ‘christening celebration’, OS gōma ‘entertainment’, OHG gouma ‘meal, banquet’. Derived from *zawēnan (Torp SHS 186). Torp- Falk 121; Johansson Festschr. Noreen 461, BB XV 228 (from *za-aumō, to Slav *umъ ‘mind, intelligence’); Pedersen IF V 68 (from *za-usm-, to *auzōn); Slotty IF XLVI 366–372; Grienberger Got. 95; Meillet MSL VIII 280 (to Slav *govîti ‘to worship, to fast’); Holthausen AEEW 129; Feist 207; Ramtoft GHÅ LVI/3 263–265; Pokorny I 453; Vries ANEW 158; Zalizniak II 216; Lehmann GED 151; Boutkan OFED s.v.

OE zeap ‘crooked, bent, curved’. Identical with Slav *gubъ ‘fold’. Further related to Lith gaubiū, ga Ubti ‘to cover from top to bottom’, Slav *gъbnǫti ‘to bend’. Pokorny I 450; Trubachev ESSJa VII 164–165.

ON gaupn ‘both hands held together’, OHG goufana ‘handful, empty hand, palm’. Derived from *zaupaz. Zupitza Gutt. 18–19, 202; Torp-Falk 137 (to *gheub(h)- ‘to bend’); Fraenkel IF LV 81 (to Lith žiūpsnis ‘handful’); Pokorny I 449; Chemodanov SGGJa I 73; Vries ANEW 159; Heidermanns 234–235; Kluge-Seebold 302.

ON gaupa ‘lynx’, OE earn-zeáp ‘a kind of falcon’. Derived from *zaupaz (Kralik GGA CLXXVI 158–160). Holthausen AEEW 125; Pokorny I 449; Vries ANEW 158. *zauraz adj: Goth gaurs ‘sad, gloomy’, ON sbst. gaurr ‘ruffian, rough’ (masc.). Identical with Skt ghorá- ‘dreadful, horrible’ (Bopp Glossarium 113; Zupitza Gutt. 172: adds Slav *žuriti ‘to perturb’). Grienberger Got. 96 (against Slavic parallels); Torp-Falk 122; Uhlenbeck PBB XXX 282; Feist 208; Petersson IF XXIV 253–254; Mayrhofer I 362–363; Pokorny I 453–454; Vries ANEW 159; Lehmann GED 152; Vasmer-Trubachev II 68 (add Slav *gurati ‘to push’); Heidermanns 235 (to *zaujanan).

Goth gansjan ‘to cause’ (leg. *gausjan), ON geysa ‘to rush, to gush’. Causative of *zeusanan (Bezzenberger BB III 81). Grienberger Got. 89–90 (to *zantaz, OE zin ‘wide, spacious’); Johansson PBB XV 228–229 (to *(za)nesanan); Wiedemann BB XXVII 204–205 (to *zennanan); Torp-Falk 137; Endzelin KZ LI 258 (to Latv i‚-gansts ‘cause’); Feist 196; Lerchner 91; Lehmann GED 147.

Lat-Goth prop. Gauts, ON pl. Gautar ‘inhabitants of Western Sweden’, OE pl. zéatas id., OS -gōt id. (in cmpn.), OHG -gōz id. (in cmpn.). In Latin and Greek sources appears as Gauti, Γαυτοί. Derived from *zeutanan. Collitz JEGP I 222–238 (an ablaut variant of *zutaz ~ *zutōn); Wadstein IF V 9 (to Lat audeō ‘to venture, to dare’); Streitberg GE 6–7; Schönfeld PVN 103–104; Holthausen AEEW 126; Kuhn Festschr. Trier 417–433; Vries ANEW 159; Lehmann GED 164.

ON gauta ‘to prate, to brag’, G dial. gauzen, gäuzen ‘to bark’. Related to Lith gaudžiū, ga Usti ‘to buzz, to rumble’. Maybe connected with *zaujanan. Zupitza Gutt. 172; Pokorny I 443, 449; Fraenkel 140–141;Vries ANEW 159.

ON gá ‘to heed, to mark’. Related to Lat faueō ‘to be favorable, to be well disposed, to favor, to promote’, Slav *govîti ‘to fast, to respect’ (Meillet MSL VIII 280). Danielsson- Noreen ANF III 17 (from *za-a(n)xanan, to Lat anculus ‘servant’); Torp-Falk 121; Walde-Hofmann I 464–466; Pokorny I 453; Trubachev ESSJa VII 72–73.

Goth gazds ‘sting, goad’, ON gaddr ‘goad’, OS gard id., OHG gart id. Compared with MIr gat ‘willow rod’ < *ghazdhā. There exists a similar word with an unvoiced inlaut cluster: Lat hasta ‘stick, pole’, MIr gass ‘shoot’ < *ghasto- (Fick I 108; Zupitza Gutt. 173; Walde KZ XXXIV 488–489). Uhlenbeck PBB XIX 520 (to Slav *žьrdь ‘pole, stake’); Meillet Etudes 261 (on Slav *gvozdь ‘nail’); Grienberger Got. 97; Torp-Falk 133; Schrader-Nehring II 424 (semantic differences of Gmc and Celt); Walde- Hofmann I 636; Feist 213; Szemerényi Ar L V 1–21 (to Umbr hostatu ‘armed with a spear’); Pokorny I 412–413; Vries ANEW 151; Zalizniak I 131; Onions 384; Benveniste IEL 324; Lehmann GED 154–155; Kluge-Seebold 317.

Goth giban ‘to give’, ON gefa id., OE zifan id., OFris ieva id., OS geban id., OHG geban id. Related to Lat habeō ‘to hold, to have’, OIr gaibid ‘to take’ continuing IE *ghabh- (Bezzenberger BB XXVII 181). In Germanic, *zebanan substitutes IE *dō-. The original vocalism of *ghabh- was adapted to the Gmc strong verb system. Lottner KZ V 398 (to Lith gabénti ‘to carry’); Meyer IF XXXV 234–235; Zupitza Gutt. 173; Torp-Falk 125–126; Kretschmer Glotta XIX 208 (*-e- in *zebanan influenced by *nemanan); Feist 214; Holthausen AEEW 128; Walde-Hofmann I 630–631; Jóhannesson IEW 186–189; Pokorny I 408; Vries ANEW 160; Zalizniak I 150; Onions 399; Seebold 217–219; Lehmann GED 155; Kluge- Seebold 303.
ON gjaf-stóll ‘throne’, OE zif-stól id. Compound of *zebō and *stōlaz. Carr Nom. comp. 54.

Goth gibla ‘gable’, MLG gevel id., OHG gibil id. Cf. also OS gibilla ‘skull, gable’, OHG gibilla id. and ON gafl ‘gable-end, gable’. Related to Toch A śpāl ‘head’, Gk κεφαλή id., Maced γαβαλάν: ἐγκέφαλον ἢκεφαλήν, Hes., Thrac theon. Γεβελέϊζις (Schulze Kl. Schr. 261). See *zablaz ~ *zablō. Kuhn KZ I 136 (to Lat gabalus); Zupitza Gutt. 217; Torp-Falk 126; Feist 214; Trier ZDADL LXXVI 13–44, Zd Ph LXX 356–357; Schulze Kl. Schr. 252, 261; Detschew Thr. 100; Porzig Gliederung 186; Pokorny I 423; Vendryes RC XL 436; Chemodanov SGGJa I 110; Vries ANEW 152; Frisk I 835; Benveniste Word X 255–256; Lehmann GED 155; Kluge-Seebold 323–324; Adams TB 642–643.

Goth giba ‘gift’, ON gjǫf id. (partly < *zebiz), OE zifu id., OFris jeve id., OS geba id., OHG geba id. Derived from *zebanan. Torp-Falk 126; Holthausen AEEW 128; Feist 213; Porzig Gliederung 172; Pokorny I 408; Vries ANEW 171; Zalizniak II 193; Seebold 218; Lehmann GED 155; Bammesberger Nom. 110.
ON þrif-gjafi‘bounteous giver’, OE beáh-zifa ‘ring-giver’, OFris rēd-jeva ‘judge’, OS bōg-gebo ‘one giving a ring’. Derived from *zebanan. Torp- Falk 126; Seebold 218.
ON gjǫfull ‘munificent’, OE zifol ‘generous, bountiful’. Derived from *zebanan. Holthausen AEEW 130; Vries ANEW 171; Seebold 218; Heidermanns 237.

Goth fra-gifts ‘gift, betrothal’, ON gipt, gift ‘gift of nature, endowment’, OE zift ‘gift, marriage gift’, OFris jeft ‘gift, privilege’, OS sundar-gift ‘privilege’, MDu gift ‘gift’, OHG gift ‘gift, donation’. Derived from *zebanan. Schulze Kl. Schr. 61; Torp-Falk 126; Feist 214; Vries ANEW 168; Zalizniak I 150; Onions 397; Seebold 218; Lehmann GED 121–122; Bammesberger Nom. 142; Kluge-Seebold 324; Boutkan OFED s.v.
ON gipta ‘to give a woman in marriage’, MLG giften ‘to give’, OHG fir-giften ‘to sell, to transfer’. Derived from *zeftiz. Torp-Falk 126.

Goth gild ‘tribute, tax’, ON gjald ‘tribute, payment’, OE zild ‘money, tribute, compensation’, OFris jeld ‘price’, OS geld ‘payment, recompense’, OHG gelt ‘payment’. Related to *zelđanan. Torp-Falk 132; Feist 214–215; Pokorny I 436; Vries ANEW 169; Zalizniak I 140; Seebold 222; Benveniste IEL 58–60; Lehmann GED 155; Kluge-Seebold 309.

Goth fra-gildan ‘to restore, to recompense’, ON gjalda ‘to pay, to yield, to repay’, OE zildan id., OFris jelda ‘to count’, OS geldan id., OHG geltan ‘to pay for’. Related to Slav *želdǫ, *želsti ‘to redeem’ (Trautmann BSW 82; Knutsson Zfslav Ph XV 140) despite the difference in suffixes—Gmc *-đ- < IE *-t- as it follows from OSwed gjalla ‘to repay’ < *zélþanan. Bezzenberger BB XVI 253 (to Gk τέλθος: χρέος); Fick BB XVII 322; Osthoff IF IV 269 (against Bezzenberger); Torp-Falk 131–132; Feist 161, 214; Holthausen AEEW 128; Kiparsky AASF XXXII/2 190–191; Stender-Petersen Slav.-germ. 325 (Slav < Gmc); Machek Slavia XXI 252–253; Jóhannesson IEW 386–387; Pokorny I 436; Vries ANEW 169; Onions 1020; Vasmer II 42; Benveniste IEL 57, 59–60; Seebold 221–222; Vasmer- Trubachev II 42; Lehmann GED 122, 155; Kluge-Seebold 310.
ON gildi ‘payment, tribute’, OFris jelde, jolde ‘guild’, MLG gilde id. Substantivized *zelđjaz. Vries ANEW 166; Onions 419; Lehmann GED 155.

ON gildr ‘of full worth, full’, OE un-zilde ‘not entitled to wergild’. Derived from *zelđanan. Karsten NB I 92–93; Vries ANEW 167; Seebold 222 (reconstructs *zelđiz); Matzel Festschr. Schröder 98, KZ CV 102, 105; Heidermanns 230.
ON gildi ‘guild member’, OE zilda id., OFrank gildo id. Derived from *zelđan. Holthausen AEEW 131; Vries ANEW 166; Kluge-Seebold 324.

ON gjalfr ‘noise, sea’, MLG gelve ‘wave’. Related to Skt jálpati ‘to murmur, to speak’, Lith želpúoti ‘to chatter, to babble’. Kuhn KZ I 140 (to *zelpanan); Torp-Falk 132; Būga Aist. 191; Fraenkel 1296; Vries ANEW 169.
ON gilja ‘to beguile’, OHG gilōn ‘to howl’. Probably derived from *zellanan. Pokorny I 489 (to Gk θέλω ‘to be willing’); Vries ANEW 167 (to *zelan).

ON gjalla ‘to yell’, OE ziellan id., MLG gellen id., OHG gellan id. Related to *zalanan. Siebs KZ XXXVII 299–300 (to *skellanan); Torp-Falk 130; Holthausen AEEW 129; Jóhannesson IEW 382–385; Pokorny I 428 (to Gk χέλιδων ‘swallow’); Vries ANEW 169– 170; Onions 1019; Seebold 222–223; Lehmann GED 157; Kluge-Seebold 310.
ON gjallr ‘ringing’, MLG gel id., MHG gel ‘loud, clear (of sounds)’. Derived from *zellanan. Karsten NB I 24; Heidermanns 239.

ON gjalp ‘boasting, bragging’, OE zielp ‘glory, pride, boasting’ (masc.), OS gelp ‘defiance, fleer’, OHG gelpf ‘chatting’ (masc.). Derived from *zelpanan. Torp-Falk 132; Holthausen AEEW 129; Vries ANEW 170 (to Lith gufibis ‘swan’ and further to *zellanan); Seebold 223.

OE zielpan ‘to boast, to glory’, MHG gelpfen ‘to shout, to sing’. Probably an emphatic form derived from *zellanan. Zupitza Gutt. 21; Matzenauer IF VIII 195 (to Lith gufibis ‘swan’); Torp-Falk 132; Holthausen AEEW 129; Pokorny I 428 (to Lith gufibinti ‘to praise’); Seebold 223–224.

Goth gilstr ‘tax’, OHG gelstar ‘toll, tribute’. Derivative in *-stro- from *zelđan, *zelđanan. Osthoff KZ XXIII 315 (from *zelđtra-); Feist 215; Lehmann GED 156; Seebold 222; Benveniste IEL 57–59; Bammesberger Nom. 87.

ON pl. gjǫlnar ‘gills (of a fish)’. Identical with Arm je∑un ‘palate’, Gk χέλνη ‘lip, jaw’ (Osthoff IF IV 276). Cf. also *zeliz > Sw gäl, gel ‘gill’, Dan gjælle id. Solmsen KZ XXIX 352 (to Gk χεῖλος ‘lip’); Zupitza Gutt. 217; Torp-Falk 131; Pokorny I 436; Chemodanov SGGJa I 108; Vries ANEW 171; Frisk II 1086; Onions 397.

OE zeolu ‘yellow’, OS gelu id., OHG gelo id. Related to Lat heluus ‘dun-colored’, Lith žefivas ‘greenish’. Cf. also *zallan ~ *zallōn. Kuhn KZ I 516 (to Skt gaurá- ‘whitish, yellowish’, Lat giluus ‘pale yellow’); Curtius Gr. Et. 202 (to Lat fuluus ‘bright yellow’); Persson Beiträge 794; Schwentner FB 66–68; Torp-Falk 131; Holthausen AEEW 127; Walde-Hofmann I 639; Porzig Gliederung 207; Pisani IF LIII 38; Pokorny I 429–430; Fraenkel 1297; Vries ANEW 194; Onions 1019; Bammesberger Nom. 243; Heidermanns 240; Kluge-Seebold 309.

Goth du-ginnan ‘to begin’, OE be-zinnan id., OFris bi-ginna id., OS be-ginnan id., OHG bi-ginnan id. Despite the anlaut with no traces of *zw-, to be compared with Skt hánti ‘to strike’, Gk θείνω ‘to kill’, Slav *gъnati ‘to drive’. Wiedemann BB XXVII 193–196 (to Alb zë ‘to seize’); Bugge PBB XII 405 (to Lat re-cēns ‘fresh, young, recent’); Torp-Falk 125 (to Slav *‘ęti ‘to begin’ or from *gen-⁄-); Holthausen IF XXX 47 (to Gk χανδάνω ‘to seize’), AEEW 131; Feist 128; Pokorny I 437–438, 491–494; Onions 85–86; Seebold 224–225 (to MLG ent-ginnen ‘to cut’, OHG an-ginnan id.); Lehmann GED 97; Kluge-Seebold 91 (to Lat pre-hendō ‘to lay hold of, to grasp, to snatch’).

Burg *girs ‘greedy’, ON gerr id., OHG ger, giri id. Related to Toch A kar(y)-, B ker(y)- ‘to laugh’, Skt háryati ‘to yearn for, to like’, Av zara- ‘striving (?)’, Gk χάρις ‘favor’, Lat horior ‘to admonish’. Karsten NB I 24; Zupitza Gutt. 200; Bartholomae 1670; Torp-Falk 128; Walde-Hofmann I 657–658; Feist 186; Kluge 68; Mayrhofer III 583; Pokorny I 440–441; Vries ANEW 164; Frisk II 1062–1064; Onions 1019; Lehmann GED 140; Köbler 710; Heidermanns 241– 242; Adams TB 197.

Goth gairda ‘belt, girdle’, ON gjǫrä id. (partly *zerđiz), MDu gherde ‘girdle’. Connected with *zurđjanan. Torp-Falk 129; Feist 185–186; Porzig Gliederung 137; Jóhannesson IEW 363– 364; Pokorny I 444; Vries ANEW 164, 171; Zalizniak II 193; Onions 399; Seebold 225; Lehmann GED 140; Bammesberger Nom. 110.

OESc giri ‘greed’, Norw dial. gīr id., OS fehu-giri ‘rapacity’, OHG girī ‘rapacity, greed’. Derived from *zeraz. Cf. also Goth faihu-geiro ‘greed’ with an unexpected *-ī- probably explained by the influence of *ziēnan. Feist 16–17; Lehmann GED 103–104; Heidermanns 241.

Goth faihu-gairns ‘covetous, avaricious’, ON gjarn ‘eager, willing’, OE zeorn ‘eager, ardent, careful’, OFris adv. gerne ‘gladly’, OS gern ‘desiring’, OHG gern ‘eager’. Derived from *zeraz. Torp- Falk 128; Feist 186; Holthausen AEEW 127; Karsten NB II 43; Pokorny I 440– 441; Vries ANEW 170; Onions 1019; Benveniste IEL 161; Bammesberger Nom. 246; Heidermanns 242; Kluge- Seebold 316.

Goth gairnei ‘desire, wish, longing’, ON á-girni ‘ambition, cupidity’, OHG gernī ‘zeal, care’. Derived from *zernaz. Hirt PBB XXIII 291; Feist 186; Vries ANEW 168; Benveniste IEL 161; Lehmann GED 140; Heidermanns 242.

Goth gairnjan ‘to lust, to desire, to wish’, ON girnask id., OE ziernan ‘to yearn’, OS girnian id. Derived from *zernaz. Zupitza Gutt. 200; Torp- Falk 128; Holthausen AEEW 129; Feist 186; Vries ANEW 168; Onions 1019; Benveniste IEL 161; Heidermanns 242.

OS gersta ‘barley’, OHG gersta id. If the full grade in Gmc is secondary, it is particularly close to Gk κρῖ, κριθή id., Alb drithë id. < *ghṛzdhā. Cf. also Lat hordeum id. (probably also with *-ṛ-). Further cf. *zrōanan. Hoops Waldbäume 364–371; Torp-Falk 130; Jokl Festschr. Kretschmer 89–91 (adds Gk ἄχερδος ‘wild pear’); Walde-Hofmann I 656–657; Pokorny I 446; Chemodanov SGGJa I 88; Frisk II 18–19; Hamp KZ XCVIII 11–12; Kluge-Seebold 316– 317; Orel AED 75.

Goth gistra-dagis ‘tomorrow’, OE adj. zeostra ‘of yesterday’, OHG gestre id. Related to the Indo-European word for ‘yesterday’: Skt hyás, Gk χθές, Alb dje, Lat heri. IE *ἵηωεσ is directly attested in ON adv. gær id. < *zēz. A direct equivalent of *zestraz is W neithiwyr ‘last night’ < *nok ⁄ti-·hestro- (Fick II 195). Cf. also Lat hesternus < *hestrinos, a derivative of heri with the same suffix *-stro-. Kretschmer KZ XXXI 436–437; Torp-Falk 133; Walde-Hofmann I 642–643; Feist 215–216; Specht KZ LXVIII 201–205; Mayrhofer III 614; Pokorny I 416; Chemodanov SGGJa I 86; Vries ANEW 197; Frisk II 1097–1098; Chantraine 1258; Otrébski KZ LXXXIV 85–87; Schindler Sprache XXIII 32–34; Lehmann GED 156; Puhvel Festschr. Hoenigswald 315–318 (IE *·hƒes as loc. of a form continued in *dazaz); Kluge-Seebold 320; Orel AED 68.
ON sann-getall ‘guessing true’, OE ofer-zitol ‘forgetful’, MLG vor-getel id., OHG ā-gezzal id. Derived from *zetanan. Karsten NB II 278; Seebold 226; Heidermanns 243– 244.

Goth bi-gitan ‘to find, to discover’, ON geta ‘to get’, OE bi-zitan ‘to gain, to obtain, to seize’, OS bi-getan id., OHG bi-gezzan id. Related to Gk χανδάνω ‘to hold’, Lat pre-hendō ‘to lay hold of’. Zupitza Gutt. 173 (to Lith goda ‘honor’); Rooth Wortst. 63; Torp-Falk 123; Feist 90; Holthausen AEEW 130; Walde-Hofmann II 359; Jóhannesson IEW 344–345; Pokorny I 437–438; Vries ANEW 165; Frisk II 1071–1072; Onions 85, 396; Seebold 226–227 (to Slav *gadati ‘to guess, to tell fortunes’); Lehmann GED 69; Kluge-Seebold 856.
ON geta ‘guess’, OE and-ziet ‘understanding, knowledge’, MLG vorget ‘forgetfulness’, OHG ā-gez id. Derived from *zetanan. Holthausen AEEW 130; Vries ANEW 165; Seebold 226; Bammesberger Nom. 59.

ON gjósa ‘to gush, to break out’. A morphological variant of *zeutanan. On the other hand, cf. Toch B kus- ‘to shoot upward, to gush’, MIr gus ‘strength, wildness’, W gwst ‘strength’ < *ghus-tu- similar to ON gustr ‘gust, blast’ < *ghus-ti-. Torp-Falk 137; Jóhannesson IEW 315–318; Pokorny I 447–448; Vries ANEW 170–171; Lerchner 91; Seebold 227–228; Bammesberger Nom. 65; Adams TB 186.

Goth giutan ‘to pour’, ON gjóta ‘to drop, to throw’, OE zeótan ‘to pour’, OFris bi-jāta ‘to water’, OS giotan ‘to pour’, OHG giozan id. Close to Toch AB kutk- ‘to cast in a mould’, Lat fundō ‘to pour’, perf. fūdī. Probably, an extension of *·heu- id.: Toch AB ku- ‘to pour’, Skt juhóti ‘to pour in the fire’, Gk χέω ‘to pour’ (Aufrecht KZ I 119). Zupitza Gutt. 203; Torp-Falk 136; Holthausen AEEW 128; Walde-Hofmann I 563–564; Feist 216; Jóhannesson IEW 315–318; Pokorny I 447–448; Chemodanov SGGJa I 91; Vries ANEW 170; Onions 373; Benveniste IEL 476, 482; Seebold 228–229; Lehmann GED 156– 157; Kluge-Seebold 324; Adams TB 179–180, 183.

ON gjóär ‘Falco haliaetus’, OE earn-zeát ‘goateagle, vulture’, OHG erin-grioz ‘a kind of eagle’ (for *-zioz?). Of uncertain origin. Johansson BB XIII 117–118 (*‘fisher’, related to Gk φιχθύς ‘fish’); Vries ANEW 170 (to *zaujanan).

ON gæfr ‘meek, quiet’, OFris jēve ‘passable’, MLG gēve ‘available’, MHG gæbe ‘pleasant’. Derived from *zebanan. Rooth Wortst. 22–23; Karsten NB I 98; Torp-Falk 126; Vries ANEW 197; Seebold 217–219; Matzel Festschr. Schröder 102, KZ CV 112; Heidermanns 236–237; Kluge-Seebold 294.

Burg *giba ‘gift’, late ON gáfa id., MHG gābe id. Derived from *zebanan. Torp-Falk 126; Köbler 710. *zēnan athem.: Crim Goth geen ‘to go’, OSwed gá id., OE zán id., OFris gān id., OS gān id., MHG gān, gēn id. Connected with Skt jáhāti ‘to leave, to abandon’, Av zazāiti ‘to return, to give birth (?)’. Osthoff Suppl. 57–58; Grienberger Got. 82–83; Helten PBB XXXV 285–287; Meillet MSL XX 104; Bartholomae 1687; Torp-Falk 120; Feist 181–182, 213; Holthausen AEEW 123; Mayrhofer I 426; Pokorny I 418–419; Onions 403; Seebold 216–217 (to Gk κιχάνω ‘to reach, to hit’, Skt med. jíhīte); Lehmann GED 138; Kluge-Seebold 307.

ON á-gætr ‘famous, excellent’, OE or-zíete ‘plausible’. Derived fom *zetanan. Karsten NB I 100; Holthausen AEEW 130; Vries ANEW 198; Seebold 227; Matzel Festschr. Schröder 102, KZ CV 112; Heidermanns 243.

ON gæta ‘to watch, to tend’, OFris gēta, gāta ‘to observe’. Derived from *zētiz. Noreen ANF III 16 (from *za-axtjan); Kock ANF XIII 164 (follows Noreen); Falk-Torp ND 321 (directly from *zetanan); Torp-Falk 121 (reconstruct *zawatjanan, to *zawēnan); Vries ANEW 197–198.

OHG giēn ‘to strive, to aspire’. Related to Toch B kāy- ‘to open wide’, Lat hiō ‘to open, to be open, to gape’, Lith žióju, žióti ‘to open mouth’, Slav *zîjǫ, *zîjati id. Persson Beiträge 318 (OE ziscian ‘to sob’ compared to Lat hīscō ‘to open, to gape, to yawn’); Torp-Falk 133; Trautmann BSW 368; Walde-Hofmann I 647–648; Pokorny I 419–422; Fraenkel 1312; Seebold 220; Vasmer-Trubachev II 98; Lehmann GED 104; Adams TB 153.
ON gil ‘deep narrow glen with a stream at bottom’, MDu giel ‘open mouth’, OHG gil ‘hernia’. Derived from *ziēnan. Zupitza Gutt. 203; Vries ANEW 166.

ON gymbr ‘ewe lamb of a year old’, OFrank in-gimus ‘one winter old animal’. From the zero grade of the word for ‘winter’, cf. Skt himá- ‘winter, cold’, Gk δύσ-χιμος ‘stormy, wintery’, Lat bīmus ‘two years old’ < *bi-himus. See *zōmz. Zetterholm apud Vries (secondary ON -y-); Torp-Falk 127; Walde- Hofmann I 106; Pokorny I 425; Vries ANEW 196.

ON gin ‘mouth (of beasts)’, OE zin ‘gap, opening, abyss’, OHG witum ginun ‘vasto ore’. Derived from *zīnanan. Zupitza Gutt. 203; Holthausen AEEW 131; Vries ANEW 167; Seebold 219; Bammesberger Nom. 73.
Norw dial. gipa ‘to gape’, OE part. zypizend ‘yawning’. Related to *ziēnan? Zupitza Gutt. 203; Torp-Falk 134; Vries ANEW 161 (to

OHG gīt ‘voracity’. Continues IE *gheidh-: Lith geidžiū, geĩsti ‘to desire’, Slav *žьdati ‘to wait, to expect’ (Zupitza Gutt. 173). Derivationally similar to Lith gaĩdas ‘violent desire’. Further related to *ziēnan. Schmidt Vok. I 73; Meillet MSL XIV 349; Torp-Falk 134; Trautmann BSW 82; Pedersen KZ XXXVIII 390; Pokorny I 426–428; Vasmer-Trubachev II 39; Lehmann GED 104.

ON hap. leg. gífr ‘greedy’, OE zífre ‘greedy, covetous’. An alternative reconstruction is *zībraz. Derived from *ziēnan. Cf. *zīraz. Zupitza Gutt. 203; Torp-Falk 134; Holthausen AEEW 130; Wissmann NP 41–42; Pokorny I 421; Vries ANEW 166 (to Lat hippitāre ‘ōscitāre’); Heidermanns 237–238 (based on *zīfez-).

Goth gageigan ‘to gain, to acquire’, OHG gingēn, gingōn ‘to yearn’. A reduplicated derivative of *ziēnan (Grienberger Got. 82) similar to *zanzanan < *zēnan. Uhlenbeck PBB XXX 283 (reduplication); Zupitza Gutt. 173, 203 (same as Grienberger); Torp-Falk 134, 565; Wissmann NP 156; Feist 181; Pokorny I 424 (to *·hēi- ‘to yawn’); Lehmann GED 137.
Norw dial. gīl ‘beer in a keg’, Du gijl id. Of unknown origin. Torp- Falk 120 (to *zailaz); Pokorny I 452; Vries ANEW 166.
ON gíma ‘vast opening’, G dial. gīm id. Derived from *ziēnan. Zupitza Gutt. 203; Pokorny I 420; Vries ANEW 167.

ON gína ‘to yawn, to gape’, OE zínan ‘to yawn’, OS gīnan ‘to yawn’ (mainly wk.), OHG ginanto ‘hianter’. Derived from *ziēnan. Zupitza Gutt. 203; Torp-Falk 133; Holthausen AEEW 123–124, 131; Jóhannesson IEW 300–305; Pokorny I 419–420; Vries ANEW 167; Onions 1018; Seebold 219– 221; Kluge-Seebold 295.
Norw dial. gīr ‘lust, passion’, OHG gīr ‘vulture’. Derived from *ziēnan. Torp-Falk 133; Pokorny I 420.

Goth prop. Gisla-mundus, ON gísl ‘hostage’, OE zísel ‘pledge, hostage’, OFris jēsel, gīsel id., OS gīsal id., OHG gisal id. Continues IE *gheidh-tlo-s together with Gaul -geistlus (in prop.), OIr gell id., W gwystl id., OCorn guistel id., Bret goestl ‘gage, caution’ or *gheis- (o)lo- together with OIr gíall ‘hostage’. Zupitza Gutt. 217; Torp-Falk 135; Holthausen AEEW 131; Pokorny I 426; Chemodanov SGGJa I 97; Vries ANEW 168; Zalizniak I 131; Bammesberger Nom. 88; Kluge-Seebold 308.
Icel gill ‘parhelion, mock-sun’, Langob gīsil ‘arrow’. Related to *zaizaz. Torp-Falk 121.
ON gísla ‘to give as hostage’, OE zíslian id., MDu giselen ‘to take hostage’, MHG ver-gīseln ‘to give as a hostage’. Derived from *zīslaz. Vries ANEW 168.
ON glaä-ligr ‘glad, bright, cheerful’, OE zlæd-líc ‘bright, pleasant, kind’. Derived from *zlađaz. Heidermanns 244.

Burg *glads ‘glad, shining’, ON glaär ‘glad, bright’, OE zlæd ‘shining, bright’, OFris gled ‘smooth’, MLG glat ‘shining’, OHG glat ‘clear, light’. Related to Lat glaber ‘smooth’, Lith glodūs ‘tender’, Slav *gladъkъ ‘smooth’. Diefenbach KZ XVI 222; Karsten NB I 57; Zupitza Gutt. 174; Walde IF XIX 104 (to Lat laetus ‘luxuriant’); Ernout MSL XIII 339; Torp-Falk 147; Holthausen AEEW 132; Walde-Hofmann I 603; Schwarz Festschr. Trier 445–449; Pokorny I 432; Fraenkel 158; Vries ANEW 171; Meid KZ LXXIX 292–293; Onions 399; Trubachev ESSJa VI 115–116; Köbler 710; Heidermanns 244–245 (to *zlōanan); Kluge-Seebold 326.
ON glaäa ‘to gladden’, OE zladian ‘to be glad, to make glad’. Derived from *zlađaz. Holthausen AEEW 132; Vries ANEW 171; Heidermanns 244.
OE zlæm ‘brightness, splendor, radiance’, OHG gleimo ‘glow-worm’. Derived from *zlīmanan. Close to Latv glaima ‘fun, joke’. Torp-Falk 148; Holthausen AEEW 132; Onions 400.
ON gleipa ‘to mock’, OHG part. gi-gleifit ‘oblique, crooked’. Derived from *zlīpanan. Wood MP IV 492–493; Vries ANEW 172.
Norw glan ‘skylight’, MHG glan ‘shine’. Substantivized *zlanaz II. Heidermanns 246.
OHG glan ‘soft, sweet’. Identical with OIr glan ‘shining, clean’, W glan id. Pokorny I 429; Heidermanns 246 (reconstructs OHG superl. *glanista; to *zlōanan).

ON gler ‘glass’, OE zlæs id., zlær ‘amber’, OFris gles ‘glass’, OS glas id., MLG glar ‘resin’, OHG glas ‘glass, amber’. Substantivized *zlasōn. Borrowed to Lat glēsum, glaesum ‘amber’. Zupitza KZ XXXVII 398 (to Slav *glazъ ‘stone, pebble’); Torp- Falk 147–148; Walde-Hofmann I 604; Holthausen AEEW 132; Pokorny I 432; Vries ANEW 172; Zalizniak I 140; Onions 400; Kluge-Seebold 326.
ON verä-glasi ‘shining from lightnings’, OS glasa ‘grey-blue’. Further connected with *zlađaz and *zlastaz. Heidermanns 247.
MHG glast ‘shine’. Identical with OIr glas ‘light green, light blue’ < *ghl 6 s-to- (Meid KZ LXXIX 292– 293). Further connected with *zlasōn. Zupitza Gutt. 174; Heidermanns 247.

ON glaumr ‘merry noise’, OE zleám ‘joyous noise, jubilation’. Identical with Slav *glumъ ‘joke’ (Berneker I 308). Zupitza Gutt. 174; Torp-Falk 149; Holthausen AEEW 132; Pokorny I 451; Vries ANEW 171; Stang LS 22–23; Trubachev ESSJa VI 147–148.

Goth adv. glaggwaba, glaggwuba ‘meticulously, diligently’, ON glǫggr ‘clear-sighted, clear, clever’, OE zléaw ‘clear-sighted, wise, prudent’, OS glau ‘clever, intelligent’, MHG glau ‘attentive, intelligent’. If the original meaning is ‘clear’, to be compared with Gk χλόος ‘greenish-yellow’. Uhlenbeck Got. Wb. 65 (to *zlōanan); Persson Beiträge 794–795 (close to Uhlenbeck); Brugmann IF XVI 500 (to Lith žvefigti ‘to look’); Torp-Falk 149; Feist 216; Holthausen AEEW 132; Pokorny I 433; Trubachev Slav. jaz. V 172 (to Slav *gluzdъ ‘mind, brain’); Vries ANEW 177; Henry KZ LXXVII 140– 145; Lindeman Verschärfung 150; Seebold IF LXXVI 328; Lehmann GED 157 (follows Uhlenbeck); Heidermanns 247– 248.

Norw dial. gletta ‘to look’, ME glenten ‘to appear, to look’. Identical with Latv glendêt ‘to look, to look for’, Slav *ględîti, *ględati ‘to look’ (Schmidt Vok. I 57). Further cf. OIr inglennat ‘investigant’ (Pedersen Kelt. Gr. II 539). Zupitza KZ XXXVI 66; Torp-Falk 147; Trautmann BSW 92; Pokorny I 431; Vasmer-Trubachev I 418.
ON glæsa ‘to make shining, to embellish’, MLG glāren ‘to shine’. Derived from *zlasōn. Vries ANEW 177.
ON gleäa ‘kite, Milvus regalis’, OE zlida id., MLG glede id. Derived from *zlīđanan. Charpentier KZ XL 434 (to Gk χελιδών ‘swallow’); Holthausen AEEW 132; Vries ANEW 172; Onions 400.
Swed glimma ‘to shine’, Du glimmen id. Of imitative origin. Vries ANEW 175.
Norw dial. glisa ‘to glimmer’, OE zlisian ‘to shine, to glisten’, OFris glisa id., MLG glisen ‘to shine, to glisten’. Cf. *zlimmōjanan. Torp-Falk 149; Pokorny I 433.
ON glitra ‘to glitter’, MHG glitzern id. Derived from *zlitiz. Torp-Falk 148; Vries ANEW 174.

OSwed glīda ‘to glide’ (if not from WGmc), OE zlídan id., OFris glīda id., OS glīdan ‘to slip’, OHG glītan ‘to glide’. A voiced variant of the root attested in *klīþōn. Torp-Falk 148; Holthausen AEEW 133; Jóhannesson IEW 375–382; Pokorny I 433 (to *zlītanan); Onions 401; Seebold 230–231 (to Latv glaidît ‘to stroke’ or IE *·hlei- ‘to shine’); Kluge-Seebold 327.
Norw glīma ‘to smolder’, MHG glīmen ‘to shine’. Related to *zlīōjanan. Torp-Falk 148.
OS glīmo ‘brightness’, OHG glīm, glīmo ‘glow-worm’. Related to *zlīmanan. Torp-Falk 148; Onions 400.
ON gljá ‘to glitter’, OFris glīa id. Related to Gk χλίω ‘to luxuriate, to revel’ (Persson Beiträge II 793– 794). Torp-Falk 148; Pokorny I 432; Vries ANEW 175; Frisk II 1103–1104.
Norw dial. glīpa ‘to stay open’, MHG glīfen ‘to slant’. An emphatic derivative of *zlīōjanan. Torp- Falk 148–149.

OS glītan ‘to shine’, OHG glīzan id. Zero grade is attested in ON wk. glita ‘to shine, to glitter’ < *zlitōjan. Maybe related to Gk χλιδή ‘voluptuousness, luxury’. On the other hand, cf. *zlīōjan. Torp-Falk 148; Feist 216–217; Jóhannesson IEW 375–382; Pokorny I 433; Frisk II 1103–1104; Onions 401; Seebold 231–232 (to MIr glé ‘clear’); Lehmann GED 157; Kluge- Seebold 327.
ON glit ‘glitter’, OHG glīz ‘brightness’. Related to *zlītanan. Vries ANEW 174; Seebold 231; Lehmann GED 157.

ON glÿ ‘glee, gladness’, OE zlíw ‘glee, joy’. Cf. OIr glé ‘shining, bright’ < *glei⁄o-. Further related to *zlīōjanan. Fick I 419, III 149 (to Gk χλεύη ‘joke, jest’); Torp-Falk 148; Holthausen AEEW 133; Pokorny I 432, 451; Vries ANEW 176; Onions 400.

OE zlówan ‘to glow’. Continues *·hlō(u)- distantly related to *·hel- ‘to shine’, cf. *zlawwaz. Torp- Falk 149; Holthausen AEEW 133; Jóhannesson IEW 375–376; Pokorny I 430, 433; Vries ANEW 175; Onions 402; Seebold 233.

ON glóä ‘red-hot embers’ (root stem), OE zléd ‘burning coal, ember, fire, flame’, OFris glēd ‘ember, hot coal’, MLG glōd id., OHG gluot id. Derived from *zlōanan. Stolz Lat. (to Lat flamma ‘flame’ < *fladma); Zupitza Gutt. 174; Torp-Falk 149; Holthausen AEEW 133; Pokorny I 430, 433; Vries ANEW 175; Zalizniak II 207; Onions 400; Seebold 233; Kluge-Seebold 329.
ON glœäa ‘to sparkle’, OE ze-zlédan ‘to kindle’. Derived from *zlōđiz. Vries ANEW 177.
ON glófi‘glove’, OE zlóf id. Of unknown origin. Wadstein IF V 23 (from *za-lōf-, to *lōfōn); Torp-Falk 119; Holthausen AEEW 133; Vries ANEW 175; Onions 402.
Norw glōm ‘transparent membrane’, OE zlóm ‘twilight, gloom’. Derived from *zlōanan. Torp-Falk 149.
ON glóa ‘to shine, to glitter’, OFris part. gliand ‘to glow’, OS glōian id., OHG gluoen ‘to glow, to burn’. Derived from *zlōanan. Torp-Falk 149; Seebold 233; Kluge-Seebold 329.
EFris sbst. glor ‘bright embers’. Identical with Gk χλωρός ‘greenish-yellow’. Torp-Falk 149; Pokorny I 430; Frisk II 1109–1110; Vries ANEW 175 (adds ON fagr-glór ‘shining’ but -glóz < *zlō(w)az).
Norw glora ‘to stare’, ME zlóren id., OFris glōren ‘to glow’. Derived from *zlōraz. Torp-Falk 149; Vries ANEW 175.
Norw gluma ‘to become dark’, ME gloume, gloumbe ‘to look sullen’. Derived from *zlūmaz. Pokorny I 433; Onions 401; Vries ANEW 176.
ON glúpna ‘to look downcast’, ME zlopnen ‘to be surprised’. Related to *zlūpanan. Torp-Falk 150.

Icel gloppa ‘split’, EFris glopp id., Du glop ‘narrow passage’. Maybe related to Slav *glybь ‘depth’ unless the latter is a secondary variant of *glǫbь id. Torp- Falk 150; Vries ANEW 175; Vasmer- Trubachev I 417–418.
ON sbst. glúmr ‘bear’, EFris glūm ‘dark look’, LG glūm id. Probably from *·hlū-mo- related to Gk χλόος, χλοῦς ‘greenish-yellow’, cf. *zlawwaz. Pokorny I 433; Vries ANEW 176.
Norw dial. glyma ‘to stare sullenly’, EFris glūmen ‘to look secretly’. Derived from *zlūmaz. Pokorny I 433.
Norw dial. glūp ‘gullet’, EFris glūpe ‘rift, crevice’. See *zluppō(n) ~ *zluppan. Torp-Falk 150.

Norw dial. glūpa ‘to gape’, OFris glūpa ‘to look’ (wk.), MLG glūpen ‘to cast a secret look with halfopened eyes’ (wk.). Originally, a strong verb. Related to Slav *glupъ ‘late (of night), stupid, dumb’, despite the irregular correspondence of labials. Torp- Falk 150 (to *zlūpan).
Norw glyra ‘to cast a pierce side glance’, MHG glūren ‘to lurk’. Cf. *zlūmjanan. Torp-Falk 149–150; Vries ANEW 176 (to ON glys ‘finery’).
ON gnadd ‘grumbling, muttering’. Of imitative origin. Vries ANEW 177.
Norw gnadra ‘grumble’, G gnättern id. Derived from *znađđan. Vries ANEW 177.

ON gnaga ‘to gnaw’, OE znazan id., MLG gnagen id., OHG gnagan id. Related to Toch B nāsk- ‘to eat, to gnaw’, Av aiwi-gnixta ‘gnawed’ (Persson Wurzelerw. 136; Siebs KZ XXXVII 321). Bartholomae 89; Torp- Falk 137; Holthausen AEEW 133; Jóhannesson IEW 337–341; Pokorny I 436–437; Vries ANEW 177–178 (to IE *ghen- ‘to gnaw’); Onions 403; Seebold 233–234; Kluge-Seebold 581; Adams TB 334.

ON gneisti ‘spark’, OE znást id. (< *znaistaz), OHG gneista id. Closely related to OPrus knaistis ‘fire’ (Johansson PBB XIV 355). Fick KZ XXI 1 (from *za-xnaista-); Zupitza Gutt. 120; Torp-Falk 96 (follow Fick); Johansson IF XIX 136 (from *zan-aista-, to *ghen- ‘to grate, to rub’ and *aidh- ‘to burn’); Holthausen AEEW 133; Vries ANEW 178.
ON gneggja ‘to neigh’, OE hnæzan id., OS hnēgian id., MHG negen, nyhen id. Of imitative origin (Walde-Pokorny I 394). Siebs KZ XXXVII 321 (to *znazanan); Holthausen AEEW 165; Vries ANEW 178.
Norw gnarra ‘to blow (of cold wind)’, E dial. gnarr ‘to growl’, OFris gnarren ‘to crunch, to gnash’, LG gnarren id. Of imitative origin. Vries ANEW 179 (to ON gnarr ‘sea’).
ON gnesta ‘to crack’, G dial. gnasten ‘to pelt down, to patter’. Of imitative origin. Noreen Gr. § 91; Vries ANEW 178.
ON gnǫtra ‘to clatter’, E dial. gnatter ‘to growl’, EFris gnatern id., LG gnetern ‘to make a noise’. Of imitative origin. Vries ANEW 181.
Swed dial. gnatt ‘mote’, OE znæt ‘gnat’, LG gnatte id., G dial. Gnatze id. Connected with *znitō? Torp- Falk 137; Holthausen AEEW 133; Pokorny I 436; Onions 403.

ON gnit ‘nit’. Closely related to Slav *gnida id., Lith glīnda id. (< *gnīnda), Latv gnĩda id. (Persson Beiträge 94–96, 202). Can be further connected to such phonetically diverging forms as Gk κονίς id., Alb thëri ~ thëni < PAlb *tsanidā. See *xnitz. Zupitza Gutt. 120; Wadstein IF V 16 (from *za-xnit-); Wood IF XVIII 24–26; Meillet MSL XXII 143; Pokorny I 437; Fraenkel 157–158; Vries ANEW 179; Frisk I 912–913; Orel AED 476.

Swed gnida ‘to rub’, OE znídan ‘to rub, to break’, MLG gniden ‘to rub’, OHG gnītan id. Continues *ghnei-tor *ghnei-dh- related to Toch B nitt- ‘to break, to crush’, Gk χνίει: ψακάζει, θρύπτει, Slav gniti ‘to rot’. Persson Beiträge II 811; Torp-Falk 138; Holthausen AEEW 133; Jóhannesson IEW 337–341; Pokorny I 436–437; Vries ANEW 179; Frisk II 1106; Seebold 234; Trubachev ESSJa VI 176–177; Adams TB 337–338.
ON jafn-góä-ligr ‘equally costly’ (uncertain reading), OE zód-líc ‘good’, OS gōd-līk id., OHG guot-līh ‘glorious’. Derived from *zōđaz. Heidermanns 251.

Goth goþs ‘good’, ON góär id., OE zód id., OFris gōd id., OS gōd id., OHG guot id. Related to Lith guõdas ‘honor’, goda id., Latv gūods id., Slav *goditi ‘to please, to fit’, *godьnъ ‘fitting’. Legerlotz KZ VIII 416 (to Gk ἀγαθός ‘good’); Grassmann KZ XII 129; Schmidt KZ XXV 150 (hesitantly follows Legerlotz); Torp-Falk 124; Feist 218; Holthausen AEEW 134; Bartoli AGIt XXXII 97–114; Pokorny I 423–424; Fraenkel 159–160; Vries ANEW 181; Onions 405; Stang LS 23–24; Lehmann GED 158; Trubachev ESSJa VI 188– 190; Heidermanns 250–252; Beekes KZ CIX 227–230 (non-IE loan); Kluge- Seebold 343.
Goth godei ‘virtue, goodness’, OS gōdī ‘goodness’, OHG guotī ‘goodness, virtue’. Derived from *zōđaz. Feist 217; Heidermanns 251.

ON gœäa ‘to endow, to enrich’, MDu ver-goeden ‘to reimburse’, MHG güeten ‘to expiate, to make up for’. Derived from *zōđaz. Structurally close to Slav *gaditi ‘to indulge, to gratify’, *gadjati ‘to hit’. Vries ANEW 198; Trubachev ESSJa VI 80; Heidermanns 251.

Goth goljan ‘to greet’, ON gœla ‘to comfort, to soothe’. Connected with *zalanan. Structurally close to the isolated Slav *galiti ‘to jump, to triumph, to laugh’ (Berneker I 293). Zupitza Gutt. 172; Torp-Falk 130; Feist 217; Vries ANEW 198 (to *zellanan); Seebold 212; Lehmann GED 157; Trubachev ESSJa VI 92–94 (Slav *galiti to Gk βάλλω ‘to throw’).

ON gómr ‘palate’, OE zóma id., MHG guome id. Related to Lith gomur‹s ‘throat, gullet’ (Schulze KZ XXVII 29). Zupitza Gutt. 175; Torp-Falk 135 (to Gk χήμη ‘yawning, gaping’); Trautmann BSW 77; Holthausen AEEW 134; Pokorny I 449 (to *zaujanan); Chemodanov SGGJa I 73; Vries ANEW 182; Zalizniak I 153; Onions 419; Kluge-Seebold 302.

Norw dial. gō ‘period of February–March’. Cf. also ON fem. gói ‘month from the middle of February to the middle of March’. Continues nom. *·hƒōm. Related to Skt himá- ‘cold, winter’, héman- ‘winter’, Gk χιών ‘snow’, Arm jiwn id., Lat hiems ‘winter’, OIr gaim id., Lith žiemā id., Slav *zima id. Pedersen Kelt. Gr. I 66; Trautmann BSW 367–368; Walde-Hofmann I 645–646; Specht KZ LXII 115; Pokorny I 425; Fraenkel 1306; Frisk II 1079–1081.

Goth graban ‘to dig’, ON grafa id., OE zrafan id., OFris grēva, griova id., OS gravan id., OHG graban id. Related to Lith grėbiu, grėbti ‘to rake’, Slav *grebǫ, *grebti ‘to rake, to dig’ (Schleicher KZ I 142), Alb kreh ~ kref ‘to comb’ < PAlb *grebska. Zupitza Gutt. 175; Torp- Falk 140–141; Feist 218–219; Holthausen AEEW 136; Pokorny I 456; Fraenkel 165–166; Vries ANEW 184; Onions 411; Stang LS 24; Seebold 235– 237; Trubachev ESSJa VII 109–110; Lehmann GED 158–159; Orel AED 195.
ON grefill ‘little hoe’, OHG grebil ‘peg’. Derived from *zrabanan. Structurally similar to Slav *greb(ъ)lo ‘rake, spade, shovel’. Vries ANEW 185; Trubachev ESSJa VII 110–111.

ON grefr ‘hoe’, OE zræfe ‘graving instrument’. Derived from *zrabanan. Identical with Slav *grobja ‘heap of stones, pit, mountain ridge’ (from old neut.?). Torp-Falk 141; Vries ANEW 185; Seebold 236; Trubachev ESSJa VII 132.

Goth graba ‘trench, ditch’, ON grǫf ‘hole, pit’ (partly < *zrabiz), OE zræf ‘grave, trench’, OS graf ‘grave’, OHG grab id. Derived from *zrabanan. Structurally close to Slav *grobъ ‘grave’. Torp-Falk 141; Holthausen AEEW 135; Feist 218–219; Pokorny I 456; Vries ANEW 193; Zalizniak II 196; Onions 411; Seebold 235; Trubachev ESSJa VII 133–134; Lehmann GED 158–159.

ON grǫptr ‘digging, burial’, OE zræft ‘carving’, OFris greft ‘digging’, MLG graft ‘pit, tomb’, OHG zraft ‘chiseling, anaglyph’. Derived from *zrabanan. Torp-Falk 141; Holthausen AEEW 135; Pokorny I 456; Vries ANEW 193; Zalizniak II 199; Seebold 236.
ON harä-greipr ‘hardclutched’, OE æt-zræpe ‘grasping, seizing’. Derived from *zrīpanan. Holthausen AEEW 135; Seebold 237; Matzel Festschr. Schröder 94–95, KZ CV 96; Heidermanns 253.

ON greip ‘space between the thumb and the other fingers’ (traces of a root stem in pl.), OE zráp ‘grasp, clutch’, MDu grepe ‘fist’, OHG greifa ‘fork, trident’. Derived from *zrīpanan. Torp- Falk 144; Holthausen AEEW 136; Pokorny I 457–458; Vries ANEW 186–187; Zalizniak II 208; Seebold 237; Lehmann GED 160; Bammesberger Nom. 106.

ON gramr ‘furious’, OE zram id., OS gram ‘hostile’, OHG gremi ‘grim, wild’ (ja-stem). Related to Av gen. pl. gram 6 ntam ‘angry’, and further to Gk χρόμος ‘noise, neighing’, Slav *gromъ ‘thunder’, a deverbative formation based on IE *ghrem-. Torp-Falk 142; Wood IF XXII 163; Holthausen AEEW 136; Karsten NB II 452; Pokorny I 458–459; Vries ANEW 184; Frisk II 1116–1117; Onions 414; Trubachev ESSJa VII 138; Lehmann GED 159; Heidermanns 253– 254; Kluge-Seebold 333.

Norw grymta ‘to grunt’, OE zremettan, zrymettan ‘to roar, to rage’. Cf. also OHG gremizzōn ‘to grumble’ < *zramitōjanan. Close to Gk χρεμετίζω ‘to neigh, to whinny’, pres. *χρεμίζω. Further cf. *zramjanan. Torp-Falk 142; Pokorny I 458; Frisk II 1116–1117.
ON gremi ‘anger, wrath’, OS gremi id., OHG gremī id Derived from *zramjanan. Torp-Falk 142; Vries ANEW 187; Heidermanns 254.

Goth gramjan ‘to anger, to provoke’, ON gremja ‘to anger’, OE zremian ‘to provoke, to irritate’, OHG gremmen ‘to anger, to irritate’. Derived from *zramaz. Structurally identical with Slav *gromiti ‘to thunder, to destroy’. Zupitza Gutt. 175 (to Gk χρεμίζω ‘to neigh’, Lat fremō ‘to roar, to resound’, but see *brem(m)anan); Torp-Falk 142; Holthausen AEEW 136; Feist 219; Pokorny I 458; Vries ANEW 187; Onions 414; Trubachev ESSJa VII 137; Lehmann GED 159; Heidermanns 254.

ON grandi ‘isthmus’, LG grand ‘rough sand’. Derived from *zrenđanan. Structurally close to Slav *grǫdъ ~ *grǫda ‘heap, lump’. Torp- Falk 140 (to Gk χόνδρος ‘granule, lump’ < ? *χρόνδρος); Pokorny I 459; Vries ANEW 184; Trubachev ESSJa VII 146–148.

ON grenja ‘to howl, to bellow’, OE zrennian ‘to grin’, OHG grennan ‘to show the teeth’, OHG grennōn ‘to whimper’. A descriptive stem, cf. OE zránian ‘to groan, to lament’ < *zrainōjanan, OHG grinnen ‘to show the teeth’ < *zrennanan. Torp-Falk 140; Holthausen AEEW 137; Sturtevant JEGP XXXV 216 (from *za-xranjanan); Vries ANEW 187 (to Skt hṛṇīté ‘to be angry’); Onions 414; Seebold 240–241.
ON grannr ‘thin, slim, slender’, LG grann ‘mean, sharp’. Related to *zranō. Karsten NB II 320–321, 335– 336 (reconstructs *zranþaz); Torp-Falk 139; Vries ANEW 185; Heidermanns 254–255.

Goth-Lat acc. pl. granos ‘pigtail’, ON grǫn ‘moustache’ (pl. granir reflects *zraniz), OE zranu id., OHG grana, gran id. Related to Slav *grono ~ *grona ‘grape’, *granь, *grana ‘branch, edge, tip’. Zupitza Gutt. 203; Torp- Falk 138–139 (to Gk χραίνω ‘to touch lightly, to smear, to paint’); Holthausen AEEW 136; Feist 220; Pokorny I 440; Dybo VSJa 25 (pretonic shortening in Gmc); Chemodanov SGGJa I 99; Vries ANEW 193; Zalizniak II 193; Trubachev ESSJa VII 104–106, 139–140; Lehmann GED 159; Kluge-Seebold 334 (to MIr grend ‘beard’).
ON gras-grœnn ‘grassgreen’, OE zærs-zréne id., MDu gras-groene id., MHG grase-grüene id. Compound of *zrasan and *zrōniz. Carr Nom. comp. 60.

Goth gras ‘grass’, ON gras id., OE zræs, zærs id., OFris gers, gres id., OS gras id., OHG gras id. Related to Lat grāmen ‘grass, stalk’ < *ghr 6 s-men (Brugmann Morph. Unt. I 50–51) and Slav *grozdъ ‘cluster, bunch (of grapes)’ (Berneker I 355) from IE *ghras-dho-. Schmidt KZ XXV 133; Zupitza Gutt. 203; Wood KZ XLV 63; Holthausen IF XVII 294–295, AEEW 135; Torp-Falk 143; Walde-Hofmann I 616–617; Feist 220; Krahe Su V 75; Pokorny I 454; Chemodanov SGGJa I 89; Vries ANEW 185; Zalizniak I 140; Onions 410–411; Trubachev ESSJa VII 142; Lehmann GED 159–160; Kluge-Seebold 335 (to
ON arin-greypr ‘encompassing the hearth’. Derived from str. *zreupanan > Swed dial. griopa ‘to cut out’. The latter is of uncertain origin. Pokorny I 462; Heidermanns 256.
ON greypa ‘to groove’, MLG grōpen ‘to hollow up’. Derived from *zraupiz. Torp-Falk 146; Vries ANEW 188 (to *zrēpōjanan); Heidermanns 256.

ON sbst. grautr ‘porridge’, OE zreát ‘great, large, thick’, OFris grāt ‘large, heavy’, OS grōt ‘large, large-scale’, OHG grōz ‘great, large’. Derived from *zreutanan. Structurally close to Lith graudūs ‘sprittle, brittle’, Latv grauds ‘corn, grain’, Slav *gruda ‘heap’. Schmidt Voc. I 170 (to Lat grandis ‘great, large’); Bersu Gutt. 130 (same as Schmidt); Torp-Falk 145; Holthausen AEEW 136; Pokorny I 461; Chemodanov SGGJa I 77; Fraenkel 170; Vries ANEW 185; Onions 412 (to *zrūtan); Seebold 242 (to OHG gi-grozzan broth ‘panis cretinus’, cf. *zreutanan); Bammesberger Nom. 237; Heidermanns 256; Hoad WGE 124–131; Kluge- Seebold 340.
ON grey ‘greyhound’, OE zríz-hund id. Of unknown origin. Holthausen AEEW 137; Vries ANEW 188 (to
ON grimm-ligr ‘fierce, fearful’, OE zrim-líc ‘grim, fierce’, OS adv. grim-līko ‘grimly’, OHG adv. grim-līhho id. Derived from *zremmaz. Heidermanns 258.

Burg *grimms ‘grim’, ON grimmr ‘grim, stern’, OE zrim ‘sharp, bitter, fierce, grim’, OFris grim ‘grim, stern’, OS grim id., OHG grim id. Connected with *zramaz. Zupitza Gutt. 175; Torp- Falk 142; Holthausen AEEW 137; Pokorny I 458–459; Vries ANEW 189; Onions 414; Lehmann GED 159; Köbler 710; Heidermanns 258–259; Kluge- Seebold 338.
ON grimd ‘grimness, fierceness’, OHG grimmida ‘cruelty’. Derived from *zremmaz. Heidermanns 258.
ON grimmask ‘to chafe, to be furious, OE zrimman ‘to run with haste, to rage’, OS grimman ‘to rage’, OHG grimmen id. Derived from *zremmaz. Holthausen AEEW 137; Heidermanns 258.
Goth grinda-fraþjis ‘fainthearted, ὀλιγόψυχος’, OE ze-zrind ‘grinding, rubbing together, noise, commotion’, OHG grint ‘impetigo, scab’ (masc.). Derived from *zrenđanan. Feist 222; Seebold 240.

OE zrindan ‘to grind’. Identical with Lat frendō ‘to rub away, to gnash’ (Froehde KZ XVIII 313), Lith gréndžiu, grësti ‘to rub, to scrape’ (Zupitza Gutt. 177). Persson Wurzelerw. 72–73 (follows Froehde); Torp-Falk 140; Sommer HL 187; Trautmann BSW 96–97; Holthausen AEEW 137–138; Walde-Hofmann I 545–546; Pokorny I 459; Chemodanov SGGJa I 71; Fraenkel 167; Onions 414; Seebold 240; Lehmann GED 160.

ON grind ‘lattice-door, gate’. Close to Slav *gręda ‘beam’, cf. also Lith grindā ‘floor board, plank’ (Lagerkrantz KZ XXXVII 182–184; Walde IF XIX 99). Zupitza Gutt. 176; Pedersen KZ XXXVIII 394; Torp-Falk 140; Trautmann BSW 98; Trier NGWG NF III/4 93 (to *zrunđaz); Pokorny I 459; Fraenkel 170–171; Vries ANEW 189; Trubachev ESSJa VII 120–122.

ON grjón ‘groats’, OE zríen ‘sand’, MLG grēn ‘grain of sand, sea sand’, MHG grien ‘sand’. Probably related to Gk aor. ἐν-εχραύε ‘to scrape’, W gro ‘sand’. Torp-Falk 145; Holthausen AEEW 137; Pokorny I 460; Vries ANEW 190; Frisk II 1115–1116.

ON grjót ‘stone, gravel’, OE zreót ‘sand, dust’, OFris grēt ‘sand, gravel’, OS griot id. (masc.), OHG grioz id. (masc.). Derived from *zreutanan. Related to Latv grauds ‘grain’, Lith grúodas ‘frost, frozen street dirt’, Slav *gruda ~ *grudъ ‘lump, heap’ reflecting IE *ghroud(h)o-. Cf. also *zrautaz. Zeuss Deutsch. 407 (derived the tribal name Greotingi from here); Streitberg GE 7 (follows Zeuss); Zupitza Gutt. 176; Torp-Falk 145; Būga RFV LXXI 468; Feist 221; Holthausen AEEW 137; Pokorny I 461; Fraenkel 173; Vries ANEW 190; Zalizniak II 168; Onions 415; Stang LS 24–25; Seebold 242; Trubachev ESSJa VII 146–148; Lehmann GED 160; Kluge-Seebold 338; Dybo BSA 25.
OHG part. griozan ‘κρίθινος’. Related to Lith grūdžiu, grūsti ‘to grind barley to make groats’. See *zreutan. Pokorny I 461; Fraenkel 173– 174; Dybo BSA 25.

ON prop. Grÿtingr, OE pl. Greotinzas ‘Greuting, East Gothic tribal name’, OHG Griuzing id. Cf. also Lat-Goth ethn. Greotingi. Derived from *zreutan (Zeuss Deutsch. 407). Feist 221; Svensson NB V 128–132; Schwarz Germ. 87, 206 (preserved in OPrus *Graudingis > G Graudenz); Vries ANEW 192.

Goth gredags ‘hungry’, ON gráäugr ‘greedy’, OE zrædiz id., OS grādag id., OHG grātag id. Derived from *zrēđuz ~ *zrēđaz. Torp- Falk 140; Holthausen AEEW 135; Feist 220; Vries ANEW 184; Onions 412; Lehmann GED 160.

Goth gredus ‘hunger’, ON gráär ‘gluttony, greed’, OE zræd ‘greed, rapacity’. Continues *·hrē-tú- ~ *·hrē-tó-, a derivative of *·her-: Skt háryati ‘to yearn for, to like’, Gk χαίρω ‘to rejoice’ (Bremer PBB XI 280). Structurally similar to Gk χαρτός ‘causing delight, welcome’. Hirt PBB XXIII 291; Grienberger Got. 99; Zupitza Gutt. 176 (to Skt gṛdhyati ‘to desire, to be eager’); Grassmann KZ XII 130; Persson Beiträge 728; Torp-Falk 140; Holthausen AEEW 135; Feist 220; Pokorny I 441 (reconstructs *·hrē-dh-); Vries ANEW 183–184 (to Slav *goldъ ‘hunger’); Zalizniak II 199; Frisk II 1062–1064; Lehmann GED 160.

ON grápa ‘to pilfer’, OE zrápian ‘to grope, to touch’, MDu grapen ‘to grope’, MHG grāpen id. Related to Lith grėbti ‘to rake, to grasp’, Slav *grebǫ, *grebti ‘to rake, to dig’ (Zupitza Gutt. 171). The unvoiced *-p- is difficult to explain. See *zrabanan. Holthausen AEEW 135; Pokorny I 456; Fraenkel 165–166; Vries ANEW 185.

Goth gretan ‘to weep’, ON gráta id., OE zrætan, zrétan ‘to bewail’ (wk. part.), OS pret. sg. griat ‘to weep’. Of unknown origin. Meillet MSL X 280 (to Skt hrādate ‘to sound’); Zupitza Gutt. 204; Torp-Falk 139; Holthausen KZ XLVIII 239, AEEW 135; Feist 221; Jóhannesson IEW 403; Pokorny I 439 (to Slav *gъrkati ‘to coo’); Vries ANEW 185; Onions 413; Seebold 241; Lehmann GED 160–161.
Goth grets ‘weeping’, ON grátr id. Derived from *zrētanan. Feist 221–222; Vries ANEW 185; Seebold 241.

ON grár ‘grey’, OE zræz id., OFris grē id., MDu grau id., OHG grāo id. Identical with Lat rāvus ‘grey’ (Vani‘ek 98). Uhlenbeck PBB XXVI 298 (to Lith žeriū, žerėti ‘to shine’); Schwentner FB 76–79; Horn Eng S LIV 74–75; Torp- Falk 142–143; Holthausen AEEW 135; Karsten NB II 419–421; Pokorny I 5441–442; Chemodanov SGGJa I 91; Vries ANEW 185; Onions 413; Bammesberger Nom. 244; Heidermanns 259; Kluge-Seebold 336.

Goth griþs ‘step, pace, standing’, MHG grit ‘step, stride’. Related to Lith grīsti ‘to carry, to bring’, grīdyti ‘to go’ (Siebs KZ XXXVII 321). Persson Beiträge 850; Torp-Falk 143–144 (to *zrīnan); Būga KS 219 (same as Siebs); Feist 222; Pokorny I 456–457; Fraenkel 171 (follows Siebs); Vries ANEW 503; Lehmann GED 161.
ON grip ‘grip, grasp’, OE ze-zrip ‘gripe, seizing’, OFris grip ‘grip’, MHG grif id. Derived from *zrīpanan. Holthausen AEEW 138; Vries ANEW 189.

ON gripr ‘property, value’, OE zripe ‘grip, possession’, OFris bi-grip ‘statute, rule’, OHG ubar-grif ‘breaking, violation’. Derived from *zrīpanan. Torp-Falk 144; Holthausen AEEW 138; Vries ANEW 189; Onions 414; Seebold 238; Lehmann GED 160.

ON gríä ‘frantic eagerness’, MHG grīt ‘desire’. Cannot be separated from *zrēđuz ~ *zrēđaz but the vocalism presents difficulties. Falk-Torp 144 (to *zriđiz); Wadstein IF V 18 (from *zaxrīđō); Wood MP I 240 (to Lith greĩtas ‘quick, nimble’).

ON gríma ‘hood, poitrail’, OE zríma ‘mask, visor, helmet’, EFris grīme id. (fem.), OS grīma ‘mask’ (fem.), OHG grīmo id. Close to Gk χρῖμα ‘unguent, oil’, Lith γρεϊμασ ‘cream, slimy sediment in water’, further connected with Lith griejū, gri‚ti ‘to skim offmilk’, Gk χρίω ‘to smear’. Zupitza Gutt. 204; Torp-Falk 143–144; Holthausen AEEW 137; Pokorny I 457; Fraenkel 168; Dybo VSJa 26; Vries ANEW 188; Frisk II 1120; Lerchner 89–91.
ON grína ‘to pout, to whine’, MLG grīnen ‘to grin’, OHG grīnan ‘to bark’. Of imitative origin. Torp- Falk 143.

Goth greipan ‘to seize, to grasp’, ON grípa id., OE zrípan id., OFris grīpa id., OS grīpan ’to touch, to take hold of’, OHG grīfan id. Related to Lith griebiū, gri‚bti ‘to seize’. Kuhn KZ I 139 (to Skt gṛbhnāti ‘to seize’), 142 (to Slav *grabiti ‘to rob’); Reichelt KZ XXXIX 21; Zupitza Gutt. 176; Torp-Falk 144; Feist 220– 221; Holthausen AEEW 138; Jóhannesson IEW 392; Pokorny I 457–458; Chemodanov SGGJa I 72; Fraenkel 168; Vries ANEW 189; Onions 414; Stang LS 24; Seebold 237–239; Lehmann GED 160 (follows Kuhn); Kluge- Seebold 336–337.

OE zrísan ‘to shudder, to be frightened’, MDu grisen ‘to shudder’. Identical with Gk xr,v ‘to rub, to anoint, to graze’. Wood MP V 265; Torp-Falk 144; Holthausen AEEW 138 (to Skt jihréti ‘to be ashamed’); Pokorny I 457; Frisk II 1120; Onions 415; Seebold 239.
ON sbst. gríss ‘young pig’ (? < *‘grey’), OFris grīs ‘grey’, OS grīs id., OHG grīs id. Related to *zrīsanan. Torp-Falk 144; Pokorny I 457; Frisk II 1120–1121.

ON gróa ‘to grow’, OE zrówan id. Of unknown origin. Torp- Falk 144; Holthausen AEEW 139; Jóhannesson IEW 390–391; Pokorny I 454; Vries ANEW 190 (to *zrasan); Onions 417; Seebold 242–243 (phonetic variant of *krē- > Lat crēscō ‘to grow’).

Goth groba ‘dugout, hole’, ON gróf ‘pit’, OHG gruoba id. Derived from *zrabanan. Zupitza Gutt. 175; Torp-Falk 141; Feist 222; Pokorny I 456; Vries ANEW 190; Zalizniak I 146; Onions 415; Seebold 236; Kluge- Seebold 340.
ON gróäi, gróär ‘growth’, OFris grēd ‘meadow’, MLG grōde ‘growth’. Derived from *zrōanan. Torp-Falk 144; Vries ANEW 190.
ON grómr ‘grime, dirt’, EFris grum ‘sediment’, MDu groom ‘dirt’. Derived from *zrōanan. Vries ANEW 190 (to Lith grémžiu, grémžti ‘to scrape’).

Burg *gronis ‘green’, ON grœnn id., OE zréne id., OFris grēne id., OS grōni id., OHG gruoni id. Cf. also *en-zrōniz: ON sbst. ígrœn ‘bright green’ (fem.), MDu ingroene id., MHG ingrüene id. Derived from *zrōanan. Structurally close with Slav *granь ‘branch’ < *ghrōni-. Schwentner FB 62–66; Zupitza Gutt. 203; Torp-Falk 144; Sommer Krit. 61 (to Lat grāmen ‘grass, stalk’, frōns ‘foliage’); Johanisson MASO V 41 (directly from a corresponding verb); Holthausen AEEW 138; Karsten NB II 182; Pokorny I 454; Vries ANEW 192, 284; Onions 413; Seebold 242; Trubachev ESSJa VII 104–106; Bammesberger OE 18–19; Heidermanns 260–261; Kluge-Seebold 341.
ON grœna ‘to paint green’, OHG gruonen ‘to green’. Derived from *zrōniz. Structurally identical with Slav *graniti ‘to grow branches, to demarcate’. Trubachev ESSJa VII 107; Heidermanns 260.

ON grœta ‘to make weep’, OE zrétan ‘to bewail, to deplore’, OFris grēta ‘to accuse’, OS grōtian ‘to excite’, OHG gruozen ‘to excite’. Causative of *zrētanan. Torp-Falk 139; Holthausen AEEW 138; Vries ANEW 192; Onions 413; Seebold 241; Lehmann GED 160; Kluge-Seebold 342 (semantic influence of Lat grātus ‘beloved, dear, acceptable, grateful’ in OHG).

MHG grob ‘strong, fat, big’. Close to Lith grubūs ‘rough, stiff, rigid’, Slav *grǫbъ ~ *grubъ ‘crude, coarse’ (with nasalization). Torp-Falk 146; Pokorny I 461–462; Fraenkel 172–173; Stang LS 24; Trubachev ESSJa VII 145–146.

ON grufla ‘to grovel on all fours’, OHG grubilōn ‘to dig through’. A denominative closely related to Lith grūblas ‘unevenness, mound’, Latv grubulis ‘shard, fragment’. Cf. also Lith grumbū, grūbti ‘to stiffen’, grubinėti ‘to stumble’. Further see *zrubaz. Torp-Falk 141 (to *zrabanan); Fraenkel 172; Vries ANEW 191.

ON grunr ‘suspicion’. Goes back to a root stem *g ⁄hr–- close to Gk φρήν ‘midriff, heart’ (Fick BB XVIII 142). Fick I 417 (adds Lat rēn ‘kidney’); Wadstein IF V 28 (from *za-runaz); Wiedemann BB XXVII 239 (to *zrēwaz); Zupitza Gutt. 97; Torp-Falk 146; Pokorny I 496; Chemodanov SGGJa I 109; Vries ANEW 191; Frisk II 1041– 1043.
ON grunn-fastr ‘fast aground (of a ship)’, MLG grunt-vast ‘fixed in the ground’. Compound of *zrunđuz ~ *zrunþuz and *fastaz. Carr Nom. comp. 90 (parallel formations).
ON grunn-lauss ‘boundless’, OE grund-leás ‘groundless, bottomless’, MLG grunt-los ‘boundless’. Compound of *zrunđuz ~ *zrunþuz and *lausaz. Carr Nom. comp. 89 (parallel formations).

Goth grundu-waddjus ‘foundation-wall’, ON grunnr ‘bottom’, gruär (< *zrunþaz), grund ‘field’ (< fem. *zrunđiz), grunn ‘shallow, shoal’ (< *grunþan), OE zrund ‘ground, bottom’, OFris grund id., OS grund id., OHG grunt id. Continues *ghr–-tu- based on *ghren-: Gk χραίνω ‘to touch slightly, to smear’, cf. in particular Lith pã-grindas ‘lake bottom’.
