Siebs KZ XXXVII 322 (to Lith grindis ‘floor-board’); Hübner KZ LI 18; Uhlenbeck PBB XXVII 121 (to Gk χέρας ‘gravel’); Torp-Falk 146 (to Lith gri Msti ‘to sink’); Holthausen AEEW 139; Feist 222–223; Pokorny I 459; Vries ANEW 191 (to *zrenđiz); Zalizniak II 200; Frisk II 1115; Onions 416; Lehmann GED 161 (to *zrenđanan); Bammesberger Nom. 158; Kluge-Seebold 341.

OE zrost ‘gristle’. Close to Lith grauzdūs ‘huge and shapeless’. Cf. WGmc derivative *zrustilaz ~ *zrustilō > OE zristel ‘gristle’, OFris gristel id., MLG gristel id., OHG grustila id. Holthausen AEEW 137, 139; Pokorny I 461; Fraenkel 170; Onions 415.
ON grúfa ‘to grovel, to crouch’, G dial. grūben ‘to crouch of cold’. Related to *zrubaz, *zrubilōjanan. Vries ANEW 191 (to Gk γρυπός ‘hook-nosed’).
Norw dial. grūt ‘sediment’, OE zrút ‘fine meal’, EFris grūt ‘sediment’, MLG grūt ‘a kind of ferment’. Related to *zrautaz. Torp-Falk 145; Holthausen AEEW 139; Vries ANEW 185.

Goth guda-faurhts ‘God-fearing’, OE god-fyrht id., OS godforoht id. Cf. MHG gote-vorhtec id. Compound of *zuđz ~ *zuđaz and *furxtaz. Feist 223; Carr Nom. comp. 88 (parallel formations); Lehmann GED 111, 165.

Goth gud-hus ‘temple’, ON goäa-hús id. If not a calque of Lat domus Dei (Kretschmer KZ XXXIX 542), a compound of *zuđz ~ *zuđaz and *xūsan. Mikkola MSN VII 271; Streitberg IF XXIX 181, XXVII 156–157; Feist 223; Carr Nom. comp. 69 (parallel formations); Lehmann GED 161.

Goth guda-laus ‘godless’, ON guä-lauss id. Compound of *zuđz ~ *zuđaz and *lausaz. Probably, a calque of Gk ἄθεος. Feist 223; Carr Nom. comp. 95 (parallel formations); Lehmann GED 165, 229. *zuđ(a)-wabjaz ~ *zuđ(a)-wabjan sb.m./n.; Goth *godawabi ‘silk’ (reconstructed from Slav *godovabjь ‘silk’), ON guä-vefr ‘good weaving’, OE zod-web ‘precious fabric’, OS godo-webbi id., OHG gota-webbi id. Compound of *zuđz ~ *zuđaz and *wabjaz ~ *wabjan. Štrekelj apud Peisker 88; Berneker I 316; Carr Nom. comp. 48; Vasmer-Trubachev I 426.

Goth af-guþs ‘godless’, Norw dial. av-gud ‘godless person’, OFris sbst. af-god ‘idol’, OS sbst. af-god ‘idol’, OHG sbst. ab-got ‘idol’. Derivative of *zuđz ~ *zuđaz (Lehmann GED 4). Uhlenbeck BB XXX 253–254; Feist 5; Lloyd- Springer I 24; Kluge-Seebold 5.
Goth gudja ‘priest’, ON goäi id. Derived from *zuđz ~ *zuđaz. Osthoff BB XXIV 197–200 (semantic analysis); Torp-Falk 136; FEIST 224, ANF XXXV 264–265; Vries ANEW 181; Lehmann GED 161.

Goth gup ‘God’, ON goä, guä id. (traces of s-stem in pl.), OE zod id., OFris god id., OS god id., OHG masc. got id. (traces of s-stem in pl.). Masc. forms are secondary. From *·hu-t(ó)- ‘libated one’ derived from *·heu- ‘to pour’ (Pictet Origines 659–661; Bury BB VII 79). The alternative connection with *·heu- ‘to call, to invoke’ (Osthoff Morph. Unt. IV 84–85, BB XXIV 191– 194) and derivation from *·hut(ó)- (< *·hūt(ó)- according to Dybo’s law) might be supported by the comparison with Toch A ñkät, B ñakte ‘god’ < *ni-·hūto- ‘one called down’ (Normier KZ XCIV 267–269). Cf. further OIr guth ‘voice’ < *·hutu-. Raumer Einwirk. 338 (to *zōđaz or to Gk κεύθω ‘to hide’ or from Pers xudāy); Schweizer KZ I 157; Ebel KZ V 236 (to κεύθω); Meyer KZ VII 12–18 (to Skt jyuti- ‘light’); Lidén BB XXI 97; Aufrecht BB XX 256 (to *zeutanan); Wood MLN XVI 310 (to Slav *govîti ‘to worship, to fast’); Meringer IF XVI 158; Schrader-Krahe Idg. 126 (follows Osthoff); Schröder PBB LI 30; Torp- Falk 136; Holthausen AEEW 134; Feist 227–228; Pokorny I 413; Vries ANEW 181, 193; Zalizniak II 186; Onions 404; Watkins Gedenkschr. Güntert 102 (follows Pictet); Lehmann GED 165–166; Bammesberger Nom. 79; Kluge-Seebold 332; Adams TB 264.

ON gólf ‘floor’, EFris gulf ‘section in a barn’. May be related to Slav *želbъ ‘gutter, trough’ (Zupitza Gutt. 175). Wadstein IF V 17 (from *za-xulfan); Hirt BB XXIV 258 (to Gk δελφύς ‘womb’); Torp-Falk 137; Walde-Pokorny I 630 (to OIr gulban ‘prick’); Mladenov 167; Porzig Gliederung 119; Vries ANEW 181; Vasmer- Trubachev II 61.

ON gyltr ‘young sow’, OE adj. zylte id. Cf. also MLG gelte ‘castrated pig’, OHG galza, gelza id. <*zaltjōn. Derived from *zaltaz ~ zaltuz. Torp-Falk 131; Holthausen AEEW 129; Vries ANEW 196; Kluge-Seebold 310–311.
ON gull-band ‘golden head-band’, MDu goud-bant ‘diadem’, OHG gold-bant ‘golden head-band’. Compound of *zulþan and *banđan. Carr Nom. comp. 83 (parallel formations).
ON gull-bjartr ‘bright as gold’, OE gold-beorht ‘bright with gold’. Compound of *zulþan and *berxtaz. Carr Nom. comp. 61.
ON goll-roäinn ‘ornamented with gold’ (< *goll-hroäinn), OE gold-hroden id. Compound of *zulþan and part. *xruđenaz (see *xreuđanan). Carr Nom. comp. 61.

Goth gulþ ‘gold’, ON gull, goll id., OE zold id., OFris gold id., OS gold id., OHG gold id. Closely related to Thrac pln. Ζάλδ-apa, ELith žefitas ‘golden’, Latv zēlts ‘gold; golden’, Slav *zolto ‘gold’ with different vowel grades. Further connected with Indo-European color adjectives belonging to IE *·hel-. Zupitza Gutt. 204; Torp-Falk 131; Trautmann BSW 368; Holthausen AEEW 134; Feist 224–225; Detschew Thr. 173; Porzig Gliederung 142; Pokorny I 430; Chemodanov SGGJa I 84; Fraenkel 1296–1297; Vries ANEW 194; Zalizniak I 140; Onions 405; Vasmer- Trubachev II 103–104; Stang LS 64; Lehmann GED 162; Bammesberger Nom. 77; Kluge-Seebold 331.
ON gull-smiär ‘goldsmith’, OE gold-smiä id., OFris gold-smith id., MLG golt-smit id., OHG gold-smid. Compound of *zulþan and *smiþaz. Carr Nom. comp. 72 (parallel formations).

Goth gulþeins ‘golden’, ON gullinn id., OE zylden id., OFris gulden, golden, gelden id., OS guldīn id., OHG guldīn id. Derived from *zulþan. Holthausen AEEW 140; Feist 225; Vries ANEW 194; Boutkan OFED s.v.
ON gylla ‘to guild’, OE zyldan id., MDu ver-gouden, ver-gulden id., OHG ubar-gulden id. Derived from *zulþan. Holthausen AEEW 140; Vries ANEW 196.

Goth guma ‘man’, ON gumi id., OE zuma id., OFris breid-gomo ‘groom’, OS gumo ‘man’, OHG gomo ‘hero, a famous man’. Etymologically related to Toch B śaumo id., Lat homō id., Lith žmuõ, žmogūs id. describing ‘man’ as ‘earthly’, further—to IE *dhe·hom ‘earth’ (Osthoff Etym. 220–221; Brugmann Grundriß II/1 135). Zupitza Gutt. 204; Torp-Falk 126; Holthausen AEEW 140; Walde-Hofmann I 654–655; Feist 225–226; Benveniste BSL XXXVIII 143; Porzig Gliederung 80, 208; Pokorny I 414–415; Chemodanov SGGJa I 60– 61; Fraenkel 1320; Vries ANEW 194; Zalizniak I 153; Lehmann GED 162; Peeters KZ LXXXIX 8–9; Bammesberger Nom. 184.
ON gumpr ‘buttock, podex’. Identical with Lith gu Mbas ‘bump, excrescence’, cf. further Slav *gǫba ‘lip, sponge’. Torp-Falk 137; Fraenkel 176; Vries ANEW 194–195; Trubachev ESSJa VII 78–80.

Goth gund ‘gangrene’, Norw dial. gund ‘scurf’, OE zund ‘matter, corruption’, OS gund ‘pus’, OHG gunt id. Probably connected with *zunþz. Torp-Falk 136; Holthausen KZ XXVIII 282 (to Gk κανθύλη ‘abscess, tumor’), AEEW 140; Trautmann Zd Wf VII 268 (to Av gunda- ‘small, round bread’); Scheftelowitz ZDMG LIX 695–696 (to NPers gundeh ‘round object’); Feist 116; Pokorny I 438; Lehmann GED 163.
ON gunn-borä ‘shield’, OE zúä-bord id. Compound of *zunþz and *burđan. Carr Nom. comp. 54.
ON gunn-fani ‘war banner’, OE zúä-fana id., OS gūd-fano id., OHG gund-fano id. Compound of *zunþz and *fanōn. Carr Nom. comp. 48; Vries ANEW 111, 195.
ON gunn-hvatr ‘fierce in battle’, OE zúä-hwæt ‘active, vigorous in war’. Compound of *zunþz and *xwataz. Carr Nom. comp. 61.

Burg *gunþs ‘fight’, ON gunnr, guär ‘war, battle’, OE zúä id. Cf. also OS gūthea id. Related to Hitt kuen- ‘to smite, to raze, to kill’, Skt hánti ‘to strike, to kill’, Av úainti ‘to strike’, Gk θείνω ‘to kill’, Lat dēfendō ‘to defend’, OIr gonim ‘to wound, to kill’. Zupitza Gutt. 97; Bartholomae 490–493; Torp-Falk 124; Holthausen AEEW 140; Walde-Hofmann I 332– 333; Mayrhofer III 575–576; Pokorny I 437; Vries ANEW 195; Frisk I 657– 658; Köbler 710; Puhvel IV 206–212.

ON gor ‘cud’, OE zor ‘dung, dirt’, MLG gor ‘filth’, OHG gor ‘dung, dirt’. Related to OIr gur ‘matter, pus’. Froehde BB XXI 327 (to Lat foria ‘flux, a disease of swine’); Torp-Falk 129; Pokorny I 494 (to *g ⁄her- ‘to burn’); Wood JEGP XIII 502 (to Gk χυλός ‘juice’); Holthausen AEEW 134; Vries ANEW 183; Zalizniak II 168; Onions 406.
OHG gurt ‘girdle’. Zero
ON gyräill ‘girdle’, OE zyrdel id., OFris gerdel id., MDu gurdel id., OHG gurtil id. Derived from *zurđjanan or *zurđaz. Torp-Falk 129; Holthausen AEEW 140; Vries ANEW 197; Onions 399; Seebold 225.

ON gyräa ‘to begird’, OE zyrdan id., OFris un-e-gert ‘unbegirded’, OHG gurten ‘to begird’. Derived from *zurđaz. Torp-Falk 129; Holthausen AEEW 140; Vries ANEW 197; Onions 399; Seebold 225; Lehmann GED 68; Kluge-Seebold 343.
OE zorst ‘gorse, furze, bramble, Ulex europæus’. Continues *·hṛst- close to Alb drithë ‘grain’, Lat hordeum ‘barley’ < *·hṛzd- (Lehmann KZ XLI 391). See *zerstōn. Schmidt Plur. x

ON haf ‘sea’, OE heaf ‘sea, water’, OFris hef ‘sea’, MLG haf id., MHG hab ‘sea, haven’. Derived from *xafjanan. Historically identical with *xaban II. Strachan BB XX 13 (to OIr cúan ‘haven, sea’ < *kopno-); Torp-Falk 72; Holthausen AEEW 144; Pokorny I 528; Vries ANEW 201; Zalizniak I 140.
ON upp-haf ‘beginning’, OHG ur-hab ‘cause, reason’. Derived from *xafjanan. Seebold 244.
ON hand-hafi‘having in hand’, OHG un-gi-hab ‘poor, insignificant’. Derived from *xabēnan. Holthausen AEEW 147; Kluge 69; Heidermanns 263.

ON hǫfn ‘haven’, OE hæfen id., MLG havene id., MHG habene id. Based on *xaban I. Derivationally close 117, 362; Holthausen AEEW 135; Walde-Hofmann I 656–657; Frisk II 18–19; Onions 407; Bammesberger Nom. 77; Orel AED 75.

Goth Gut-þiuda ‘Gothic people’, ON Goti ‘Goth’, OSwed pl. Gutar, Gotar ‘people’, OE pl. Gotan ‘Goths’. Probably, n-stem reflected also in gen. pl. gutani of the Pietroassa inscription. Cf. also Germ-Lat Gutones, Gotones. Borrowed to Lith gūdas ‘Bielorussian’ and OPrus *gud- (in pln.) before the Lautverschiebung (Karsten ZDADL LXVII 253–255). Further connections are unclear, see *zautaz ~ *zautōn. Grienberger Got. 101–103 (from Goth *gut ‘sea, bay’, to *zeutanan); Lindroth NB II 75–77 (to the name of Gotland); Holthausen AEEW 135; Feist 226–227; Krause Run. I 227–235 (adduces the ON parallel), HG 5 (to *zeutanan); Fraenkel 174 (-d- in Baltic influenced by OPrus gudde ‘bush’). to OIr cúan ‘curve, bend, recess, bay’ < *kopno-. Torp-Falk 72; Chemodanov SGGJa I 98; Vries ANEW 279; Onions 431; Kluge-Seebold 347.

Goth haban ‘to have’, ON hafa id., OE habban id., OFris hebba, habba id., OS hebbian id., OHG habēn id. Durative of *xafjanan (Lottner KZ XI 192). Collitz Prät. 86–88 (amalgamation of *ghabh- and *kap-); Meyer IF XXXV 224–237; Vendryes MSL XVIII 310; Prokosch MP XXVI 464; Zupitza Gutt. 206; Torp-Falk 72; Holthausen AEEW 143; Feist 229; Porzig Gliederung 107–108 (on IE *kap-: *ghabh-), 115; Pokorny I 528; Vries ANEW 201; Onions 431; Seebold 244; Lehmann GED 167; Kluge-Seebold 345.
ON hǫfugr, hǫfigr ‘heavy’, OE hefiz id., OS hebig id., MHG hebec id. Based on *xabiz > OE hefe ‘weight’ derived from *xafjanan. Torp- Falk 72; Holthausen AEEW 153–154; Vries ANEW 280; Onions 434.
ON hefä ‘loss or gain of claim’, MHG hebede ‘property’. Derived from *xabēnan. Falk-Torp ND 450; Vries ANEW 215.

ON hafr ‘he-goat’, OE hæfer id. Etymologically related to Gk κάπρος ‘boar’, Lat caper ‘he-goat’. Further may be connected with Skt kápṛth- ‘penis’. Scheftelowitz IF XXXIII 142 (oriental Wanderwort); Petersson PBB XL 105– 107; Zupitza Gutt. 114; Torp-Falk 73; Holthausen AEEW 144; Walde- Hofmann I 157–158; Mayrhofer I 157; Porzig Gliederung 114; Pokorny I 529; Chemodanov SGGJa I 90; Vries ANEW 201; Frisk I 782–783; Zalizniak I 131; Kluge-Seebold 345.

ON pl. hafrar ‘oats’, OS havoro id., OHG habaro id. Derived from *xabraz (Falk-Torp ND 387), cf. Slav *ovьsъ ‘oats’ < *a⁄i˚o-, to *a⁄i- ‘ewe’. Charpentier KZ XL 436 (to Skt neut. śáṣpa- ‘young grass’); Petersson LUÅ 1918/31 19 (to Norw hagr ‘tail hair of the horse’); Wood MP XVII 568 (to Slav *koprъ ‘dill’); Zupitza Gutt. 31; Torp- Falk 73; Specht 27 (to *xōbō); Pokorny I 529; Vries ANEW 202; Zalizniak I 153; Onions 619; Kluge-Seebold 347.
ON hǫfuä-bein ‘skull, head-bone’, OE heáfod-bán id. Compound of *xabuđan and *bainan. Carr Nom. comp. 81 (parallel formations).
ON hǫfuä-baugr ‘chief ring in the wergild’, OE heáfodbeáz ‘crown’. Compound of *xabuđan and *bauzaz. Carr Nom. comp. 81 (parallel formations).
ON hǫfuä-lauss ‘headless’, OE heáfodleás id., OFris hāved-lās id., MLG hōvet-lōs id., MHG houbet-lōs id. Compound of *xabuđan, *xaubuđan and *lausaz. Carr Nom. comp. 89 (parallel formations).

ON hǫfuä ‘head’, OE heáfod id. Identical with Lat caput ‘head’ and, probably, Skt *kaput- in neut. kapúcchala- ‘tuft of hair on the back of the head’ < *kaput-≤ala- (Kuhn KZ I 136). Torp-Falk 73; Holthausen AEEW 150; Walde-Hofmann I 163–164; Mayrhofer I 156–157; Pokorny I 529–530; Vries ANEW 279; Zalizniak I 141; Mayrhofer I 156–157; Lehmann GED 178–179; Beekes KZ CIX 218–220 (from non-IE).
ON hǫfuä-svími ‘dizziness’, OE heáfod-swíma id. Compound of *xabuđan and *swimōn. Carr Nom. comp. 81 (parallel formations).
ON hǫfuä-vǫrär ‘body-guard’ (u-stem), OE heáfod-weard ‘chief guardian’. Compound of *xabuđan and *warđaz II. Carr Nom. comp. 54.

ON haukr ‘hawk’, OE hafoc id., OFris havek id., OS havuk id., OHG habuh, habech id. Identical with Slav *kobъzъ ~ *kobuzъ ‘hawk, kite’ (Zupitza Gutt. 15, 102), from IE *kobu·o-. Further connections are not clear. Pogodin RFV XXXIII 328–329; Berneker I 535; Suolahti DVN 360 (to Lat capys, capus < Etr); Torp-Falk 73; Petersson PBB XL 96; Holthausen AEEW 147; Suolahti ANT 360 (from *xafjanan; Slav < Gmc); Pokorny I 528; Vries ANEW 214; Zalizniak I 131; Onions 431; Trubachev ESSJa X 92–93; Kluge-Seebold 345.

ON héäinn ‘jacket of fur or skin’, OE heden ‘hood’. A substantivized adjective close to Slav *kotьnъ ‘pregnant (of animals)’ further derived from *kotiti sę ‘to give birth (of animals)’, cf. also Lat catulus ‘cub’ (Osthoff Etym. I 250). Zupitza Gutt. 207; Torp-Falk 90 (from Thrac *kitōn, cf. Gk χιτών ‘tunic, garment worn next to skin’, Ion κίθων id.); Loewenthal PBB XLIX 72; Jónsson ANO 1926 190; Holthausen AEEW 153; Vries ANEW 215.

ON haäna ‘young she-goat’. Cf. also MHG hatele ‘she-goat’ close to Lat catulus ‘cub’. See *xađinaz. Zupitza Gutt. 206; Torp-Falk 69; Walde-Hofmann I 183; Pokorny I 534; Chemodanov SGGJa I 58; Vries ANEW 200; Trubachev ESSJa XI 204–205, 219–220.
ON hefill ‘clew-lines of a sail’, OHG lefi-hefil ‘yeast’. Derived from *xafjanan. Torp-Falk 72; Vries ANEW 215; Seebold 244.

Goth hafjan ‘to hold up, to bear up, to lift’, ON hefja id., OE hebban id., OFris heva id., OS hebbian id., OHG heffen id. Related to *xabēnan. Etymologically close to Lat capiō ‘to seize’, Gk κάπτω ‘to gulp down’, Lat capiō ‘to take’, Alb kap ‘to seize, to grasp’, Latv kāmpju, kāmpt ‘to seize’, and other continuants of IE *kap-. Meyer IF XXXV 224–237; Zupitza Gutt. 103; Torp-Falk 71; Feist 229–230; Holthausen AEEW 153; Walde-Hofmann I 159–160; Porzig Gliederung 127; Jóhannesson IEW 186–189; Pokorny I 528; Vries ANEW 215; Frisk I 783; Onions 433; Seebold 244–245; Lehmann GED 167; Orel AED 169; Kluge-Seebold 362.
Goth ufar-hafnan ‘to be (excessively) exalted’, ON hafna ‘to forsake, to abandon’. Derived from *xafjanan. Feist 230; Vries ANEW 201 (to
ON hapt, haft ‘bond’, OE hæft id. See *xaftaz II. Holthausen AEEW 144; Vries ANEW 209; Seebold 245; Heidermanns 263; Kluge-Seebold 347.
ON haptr, haftr ‘captive’, OE hæft id., MHG haft id. Substantivized *xaftaz II. Holthausen AEEW 144; Porzig Gliederung 126; Heidermanns 263; Kluge-Seebold 347.

Goth hafts ‘bound, joined’, OS haft id., OHG haft id. Identical with Lat captus ‘captured’, W caeth ‘slave’, OCorn caid ‘captured’ (Lane Language IX 246). From *kap-to-, a participial form of *kap- (see *xafjanan). Torp-Falk 72; Meillet RG XXI 40 (from Celtic); Krahe Su V 80; Mohr ZDADL LXXVI 169; Holthausen AEEW 144; Walde- Hofmann I 159; Feist 230; Karsten NB II 306; Pokorny I 527–528; Chemodanov SGGJa I 102; Vries ANEW 209; Seebold 245; Lehmann GED 168; Bammesberger Nom. 251; Heidermanns 263–264; Kluge-Seebold 347.
Goth anda-hafts ‘answer’, MHG haft ‘detention’. Structurally similar with Lat captiō ‘deceit, harm’. Derived from *xafjanan. Walde-Hofmann I 159; Feist 46; Lehmann GED 34; Kluge- Seebold 347.
ON hepti ‘haft’, OE hæft, hæfte id., MLG hechte id., OHG hefti id. Derived from *xaftjanan. Torp-Falk 72; Holthausen AEEW 144; Onions 423; Kluge-Seebold 363.

Goth haftjan ‘to hold fast to, to adhere’, ON hepta, hefta ‘to bind, to fetter’, OE hæftan ‘to seize, to bind’, OFris hefta ‘to fortify, to secure’, OS heftian id., OHG heften ‘to fortify, to bind’. Derived from *xaftaz, *xafjanan. Torp-Falk 72; Holthausen AEEW 144; Feist 230; Vries ANEW 222; Lehmann GED 167–168; Kluge-Seebold 363.
Goth ga-haftnan ‘to stick, to adhere’, OE hæftnian ‘to seize, to capture’. Derived from *xaftaz, *xaftjanan. Feist 183; Heidermanns 264.

OHG hagan ‘to feed’. Related to Skt ≤aknóti ‘to be able’, Av sak- ‘to agree on smth.’. See *xazaz, *xazōn. Bartholomae 1552–1553; Wissmann NP 72; Torp-Falk 67–68; Holthausen AEEW 147; Mayrhofer III 288–289; Pokorny I 522; Vries ANEW 202; Seebold 245–246.

ON hag-þorn ‘hawthorn’, OE hæz-äorn id., MLG hage-dorn id., OHG hagan-dorn id. Compound of *xazōn and *þurnuz ~ *þurnaz. See *xaz(i)naz. Torp-Falk 68; Holthausen AEEW 146–147; Carr Nom. comp. 48; Onions 431; Kluge-Seebold 348.

ON hagr ‘handy, skilful’, MHG be-hac ‘of pleasure’. Related to Skt ≤aknóti ‘to be able’, Av sak- ‘to agree on smth.’. See *xazanan. WGmc words for ‘witch’ belong here: OE hæztesse, hæztes, MDu haghetisse. Bartholomae 1552– 1553; Torp-Falk 67–68; Karsten NB I 10; Holthausen AEEW 144; Mayrhofer III 288–289; Pokorny I 522; Vries ANEW 203; Onions 423; Heidermanns 264–265.
Norw hegn ‘fencing, fence’, OS hagin ‘fence’, OHG hagan ‘hawthorn’. Derived from *xazōn. Vries ANEW 216.
ON hegna ‘to hedge, to fence’, MLG hegenen id., MHG heinen ‘to surround with a hedge’. Derived from *xaz(i)naz. Vries ANEW 216.

ON heggr ‘bird-cherry’, OE heze ‘hedge’. Cf. also EFris hegge id., MDu hegge id., OHG hegga id. Identical with W cae ‘enclosure’ < *kagƒos. See *xazōn. Torp-Falk 68; Holthausen AEEW 153; Onions 434; Vries ANEW 215–216; Kluge-Seebold 362.
ON hagl-steinn ‘hailstone’, OE hazol-stán id., MLG hagel-stēn id., MHG hagel-stein id. Compound of *xazlan ~ *xazlaz and *stainaz. Carr Nom. comp. 48.

ON hagl ‘hail’, OE hæz(e)l, hazol id., OFris heil id., OS hagal id., OHG hagal id. From IE *˚o˚ló-, reduplication of *˚el- ‘cold’ similar to (but not identical with) Skt ≤í≤ira- ‘cool, cold’. Cf. also *xexlōn. Uhlenbeck PBB XXVI 208 (to Gk κάχληξ ‘pebble’); Zupitza Gutt. 207–208 (same as Uhlenbeck); Torp-Falk 68; Holthausen AEEW 147; Mayrhofer III 345–346; Pokorny I 551; Frisk I 805; Chantraine I 507 (Gk κάχληξ to καχλάζω ‘to splash, to bubble’); Pokorny I 518, 551; Chemodanov SGGJa I 108; Vries ANEW 202–203; Zalizniak II 168; Onions 423; Lehmann GED 167; Kluge-Seebold 348.

ON haga ‘to manage, to arrange’, OE on-hazian ‘to be within smb.’s powers’, OFris hagia ‘to please’, OHG bi-hagon ‘to make comfortable’. Derived from *xazanan (Wissmann NP 72). Torp-Falk 67; Holthausen AEEW 147; Vries ANEW 202; Seebold 245; Kluge- Seebold 91–92.

ON hagi ‘pasture’, OE haza ‘enclosure’, OS hago ‘pasture’, OHG hag, hac ‘fence, enclosure’ (< *xazaz). Derived from *xazanan. Zupitza Gutt. 111; Uhlenbeck PBB XXIX 332–333 (to Slav *košь ‘basket’); Torp-Falk 68; Herbig Philologus LXXIII 452 (to Lat caulae ‘opening, hole, enclosure’, dim. of *cauā < *kaghā); Holthausen AEEW 147; Pokorny I 518; Chemodanov SGGJa I 101; Vries ANEW 202; Onions 432; Kluge-Seebold 348.

ON hauk-staldr ‘young man’ (with an irreg. hauk- instead of *hǫg-), OE hazo-steald ‘bachelor, young man, young warrior’, OS hagu-stald id., OHG hagu-stalt ‘hired worker’. Compound of *xazuz (see *xazaz) and *stalđaz (see *stalđan). Torp-Falk 489; Holthausen AEEW 147; Falk Festschr. Bugge Kr. 16 (on ON hauk-); Carr Nom. comp. 48; Vries ANF LVIII 93–104, ANEW 214; Seebold 462; Lehmann GED 37; Kluge-Seebold 348.

MHG hahse ‘back of knee’. Identical with Skt kákṣa- ‘armpit’, OIr coss ‘foot’, Lat coxa ‘hip’. Meringer Wu S III 47; Torp-Falk 67; Walde-Hofmann I 283; Pokorny I 611; Adams TB 136–137 (adds Toch B kakse ‘body part’).
ON háttr ‘mode, way’. Closely related to OIr cucht ‘colour’ (Zupitza Gutt. 207). Hirt BB XXIV 231 (to ON hagr ‘state, condition’); Sperber Wu S VI 18 (same as Hirt); Vries ANEW 213.

ON heiär ‘bright, cloudless’. Cf. also sbst. heiä ‘brightness of the sky’. Identical with Skt keta- ‘mark, sign’, cf. also ketú- ‘brightness, light’. Further related to *xaiđuz ~ *xaiđiz. Torp-Falk 64; Karsten NB II 259, 449; Mayrhofer I 265; Pokorny I 916–917; Vries ANEW 217; Heidermanns 265.

OE hádor ‘clear, bright, serene’, OS hēdar ‘clear, light’, OHG heitar id. Derived from *xaiđaz. Structurally similar with Skt citrá- ‘excellent, bright’, Av ‘iyrō ‘visible, clear’. Cf. also Lith skaidrūs ‘light, clear’. Zupitza Gutt. 105; Bartholomae 586–587; Torp-Falk 64; Holthausen AEEW 143; Karsten NB II 259, 449; Mayrhofer I 387; Pokorny I 916–917; Fraenkel 791; Vries ANEW 217; Onions 443; Bammesberger Nom. 247; Heidermanns 265– 266; Kluge-Seebold 367.

Goth haidus ‘way, manner’, ON heiär ‘honor’, OE hád ‘person, degree, rank’, OFris -hēd, OS hēd id., OHG heit id. Identical with Skt ketú- ‘brightness, form’ < *k 6 i-tu- (Güntert Kalypso 49), further related to *(s)kāi- ‘to be bright, to shine’, see *xaiđaz. Helten PBB XVII 288; Zupitza Gutt. 105; Torp- Falk 64; Holthausen AEEW 143; Feist 231; Mayrhofer I 265; Pokorny I 916; Zalizniak II 200; Lehmann GED 168; Bammesberger Nom. 159; Kluge- Seebold 367.

Goth haifsts ‘conflict, competition’, OE hæst ‘violence, fury’, OFris hāst ‘haste’, MLG heist ‘vehemence’. Cf. also WGmc *xaifstaz ‘violent’: OE hæst(e), OFris hāst, OHG heisti ‘loud’. Related to Lith kaipstū, kaĩpti ‘to be dizzy, to ail, to be sickly’. Zupitza Gutt. 182; Grienberger Got. 104 (to Lith šaipa Us, šaipÿtis ‘to mock, to show one’s teeth’); Uhlenbeck PBB XXX 286 (against Grienberger); Torp-Falk 65; Holthausen AEEW 146, 427; Feist 231; Pokorny I 542–543; Öhmann Zd Wf XVI 166–167; Fraenkel 203; Vries ANEW 218; Lehmann GED 169; Kluge-Seebold 359.

Goth haihs ‘one-eyed’, ON theon. Hárr ‘one-eyed god (Odin)’. Identical with Lat caecus ‘blind’, OIr caech ‘one-eyed’. Further cf. Skt kekara- ‘squinteyed’ and probably Dac koiko-λίδα ‘nightshade’. Saussure Festschr. Thomsen 202; Zupitza Gutt. 206, 209; Torp-Falk 64; Walde-Hofmann I 129; Feist 232; Detschew Thr. 551; Mayrhofer I 264; Pokorny I 519–520; Chemodanov SGGJa I 100; Vries ANEW 210; Lehmann GED 169; Heidermanns 267.

Goth hailags ‘holy’ (on the Petroassa ring), ON heilagr id., OE háliz id., OFris hēlich id., OS hēlag id., OHG heilag id. Derived from *xailaz II. Ochs PBB XLV 102–112; Zupitza Gutt. 105; Torp-Falk 65; Holthausen AEEW 148; Feist 232; Pokorny I 520; Vries ANEW 218; Onions 445; Scardigli Goten 219– 223; Benveniste IEL 452; Lehmann GED 169; Heidermanns 268; Kluge-Seebold 365.
ON helga ‘to make holy’, OE hálgian id., OFris hēligia id., OS hēlagōn id., OHG heilīgōn id. Derived from *xailazaz. Holthausen AEEW 148; Vries ANEW 221; Onions 424.

ON sbst. heil-indi ‘health’, OE hál-wende ‘conducive to health, healing’, OHG sbst. heil-ant ‘savior’. Compound of *xailaz II and *wanđjaz (see *wanđjanan). Holthausen AEEW 148; Carr Nom. comp. 60; Vries ANEW 218.

Crim.-Goth iel ‘good, health’, ON heill ‘good luck, omen’, OE hæl ‘omen, auspice’, OS heil ‘health, luck’, OHG heil id. Substantivized *xailaz II. Close to W coel ‘sign, omen’. Zupitza Gutt. 105; Holthausen AEEW 144; Feist 290; Pokorny I 520; Zalizniak II 223; Vries ANEW 218; Lehmann GED 169; Heidermanns 267.

Goth hails ‘healthy’, ON heill ‘whole, healed’, OE hál ‘whole, well, sound’, OFris hēl ‘unhurt, whole, unharmed’, OS hēl ‘unhurt, unharmed’, OHG heil ‘whole, healthy, unscathed’. Related to W coel ‘sign, omen’, OPrus kails ‘healthy’, Slav *cîlъ ‘whole’. The words for ‘health’ derived from here (OE hælu id., OS hēli id., OHG heila, heilī id.) probably explain the further derivation of the noun for ‘brain’: ON heili ‘brain’, OFris hēli, hāl, heila id. Pictet KZ V 37–38; Zupitza Gutt. 105; Karsten NB II 266; Gray BB XXVII (to Osc kaíla ‘temple’); Torp-Falk 65; Wood MLN XXIX 71 (ON heili to Skt ≤íkhā ‘tuft of hair’); Specht KZ LXIV 21; Marstrander NTS XI 288; Feist 232; Holthausen AEEW 145, 148; Baetke HG 68–79; Porzig Gliederung 141–142; Pokorny I 520; Chemodanov SGGJa I 68; Vries ANEW 218 (ON heili to MIr coelán ‘intestine’); Zalizniak II 223; Onions 1005; Beck Festschr. Eggers 21–25; Stang LS 25, 81; Benveniste IEL 451–452, 468; Toporov PJa III 136–143; Lehmann GED 169– 170; Trubachev ESSJa III 179–180; Bammesberger Nom. 249; Heidermanns 267–268.

ON heilsa ‘to greet’, OE hælsian ‘to foretell’, OHG heilisōn id. Cf. also ON heilla ‘to bewitch’ < *xailzōjanan. Derived from *xailesō based on *xailaz. Zupitza Gutt. 105; Holthausen AEEW 145; Jóhannesson IEW 176; Pokorny I 520; Vries ANEW 218; Benveniste IEL 451; Lehmann GED 170.
OGutn haildir ‘damages’, OHG heilida ‘health’. Derived from *xailjanan or *xailaz. Heidermanns 268.

Goth hailjan ‘to heal’, OSwed hela id., OE hælan id., OFris hēla id., OS hēlian id., OHG heilen id. Derived from *xailaz. Structurally close to Slav *cîliti id. Holthausen AEEW 144; Feist 232; Porzig Gliederung 141; Vries ANEW 218; Onions 432; Trubachev ESSJa III 178; Lehmann GED 169– 170; Heidermanns 268; Kluge-Seebold 365.
Goth ga-hailnan ‘to be healed, to recover’, OGutn hailna ‘to heal’. Derived from *xailaz. Feist 183; Heidermanns 268.
ON heim-fǫr ‘return home’, OE hám-faru ‘forcible entry into a house’. Compound of *xaimaz ~ *xaimō and *farō. Carr Nom. comp. 81 (parallel formations).
ON heima ‘at home, home’, OS hēme id., OHG heim id. Derived from *xaimaz ~ *xaimiz. Vries ANEW 218–219; Lehmann GED 170; Kluge-Seebold 365.
ON heiman ‘from home’, OHG heimina id. Derived from *xaimaz ~ *xaimiz. Vries ANEW 219.

Goth haimoþli ‘patrimonial land’. Cf. also OHG heimōdilis ‘laribus’. Compound of *xaimaz ~ *xaimiz and *ōþljan. ON heimild, heimold ‘title, right’ does not belong here (despite Schwarz GNA 266) and is better to be explained from *xaimiliþō (Hesselman apud Vries). Feist 233; Carr Nom. comp. 44; Vries ANEW 219; Lehmann GED 170; Kluge-Seebold 365.
ON heimta ‘to get back, to bring home the sheep’, OE hámettan ‘to provide with a home’. Derived from *xaimaz ~ *xaimiz. Holthausen AEEW 148; Vries ANEW 220; Onions 430.

Goth haims ‘village’ (pl. < *xaimō), ON heimr ‘abode, land, village’, OE hám ‘home, house, dwelling’, OFris hām, hēm id., OS hēm id., OHG heima id. Related to Gk κώμη ‘village’, Lith káimas ‘village’, šeimā ‘family’, Slav *sîmьja id. May also be connected with OIr cóim ‘kind, good’. Zupitza Gutt. 49, 185; Torp-Falk 87–88; Trautmann BSW 300–301; Holthausen AEEW 148; Feist 233–234; Pokorny I 540; Chemodanov SGGJa I 65; Fraenkel 251, 970; Trubachev Rod. 165; Vries ANEW 219; Zalizniak II 196; Frisk II 61–62; Onions 445–446; Vasmer- Trubachev III 600; Lehmann GED 170; Bammesberger Nom. 72; Kluge-Seebold 365; Vine Festschr. Watkins 685–702 (rejects comparison with Gk κώμη ‘village’).
ON heimskr ‘foolish, silly’, OHG heimisc ‘native’. Derived from *xaimaz ~ *xaimiz. Vries ANEW 219.

ON heima ‘to take one in’, OE hæman ‘to have intercourse with, to marry’, MHG heimen ‘to drive home’. Derived from *xaimaz ~ *xaimiz. Torp-Falk 88; Holthausen AEEW 145; Vries ANEW 218; Frisk I 809 (derived from κεῖμαι ‘to lie’ which is itself related to *xaimaz).

ON hein ‘hone, whetstone’, OE hán ‘boundary stone’. Close to Av saēni- ‘top’. Cf. further Gk κῶνος ‘pinecone’ < *˚ō(i)-no- (Hirt BB XXIV 234). Zupitza Gutt. 184; Torp-Falk 64; Holthausen AEEW 149; Pokorny I 542; Vries ANEW 220; Onions 446.

ON hárr ‘hoary’, OE hár ‘hoary, grey, old’, OFris hēr ‘high, sublime’, OS hēr ‘high, noble’, OHG hēr ‘great, sublime’. Identical with Slav *sîrъ ‘grey’, further cf. MIr cíar ‘dark’ < *˚eiro-. Schwentner FB 80–82; Meillet Etudes 321–322, 403 (Slav < Gmc); Pedersen KZ XXXVIII 392–393, XL 176–177; Torp-Falk 65, 88; Holthausen AEEW 149; Karsten NB II 258–259; Porzig Gliederung 166; Pokorny I 519, 540; Chemodanov SGGJa I 68; Vries ANEW 212–213; Onions 442–443; Vasmer-Trubachev III 611– 612; Bammesberger Nom. 248; Heidermanns 269–270; Kluge-Seebold 363.

OE hás ‘hoarse’, OFris hās id., MDu hees id., OHG heis id. Of uncertain origin. Cf. *xairaz. Torp-Falk 65; Holthausen AEEW 149; Pokorny I 519 (to *xajaz); Vries ANEW 213; Onions 443; Heidermanns 270; Kluge-Seebold 366.

ON háss ‘hoarse’, ME hoors id., MLG hēsch id., OHG heisar ‘rough, hoarse’. Derivative of *xaisaz or a compromise form combining *xaisaz and *xairaz. Zupitza Gutt. 52; Torp-Falk 65; Karsten NB II 205, 381–382; Pokorny I 519; Vries ANEW 213; Heidermanns 270–271.

Goth anda-hait ‘confession, acknowledgment’, ON heit ‘solemn promise, vow’, OE hát ‘vow, promise’, OS masc. bi-hēt ‘calling’, OHG masc. bi-heiz ‘promise’. Derived from *xaitanan. Holthausen AEEW 149; Feist 46; Seebold 246–247; Lehmann GED 172.

Goth haitan ‘to call (by name), to address’, ON heita id., OE hátan ‘to bid, to call, to name’, OFris hēta ‘to call (by name), OS hētan id., OHG heizan id. If the original meaning is ‘to name’ < *‘to identify’ rather than ‘to call, to summon’, probably based on neut. *xit (see *xi). Brugmann IF VI 89–90 (to Gk κίω ‘to go, to drive’); Wood MLN XVI 310 (same as Brugmann); Hoffmann Festschr. Fick 40–41 (to Gk κ—νέω ‘to set in motion’); Zupitza Gutt. 105 (to Slav *cîditi ‘to strain, to filter’); Petersson PBB XLIV 173 (to Osset sīdyn ~ sedun ‘to call’); Pedersen Kelt. Gr. II 490–491 (to Ir cisse ‘carried in’); Torp-Falk 64; Holthausen AEEW 150; Feist 236–237; Prokosch CGG 176–178 (from *kēi-d-); Jóhannesson IEW 198; Pokorny I 538; Vries ANEW 220; Onions 440; Seebold 246–248; Abaev III 105–106 (same as Petersson); Lehmann GED 171–172; Kluge-Seebold 367.

ON heitr ‘hot’, OE hát id., OFris hēt id., OS hēt id., OHG heiz id. Related to *xajaz. Zupitza Gutt. 112; Zupitza Gutt. 112; Torp-Falk 89; Holthausen AEEW 149–150; Karsten NB I 18, 59; Pokorny I 519; Vries ANEW 220; Onions 449; Stang LS 25; Heidermanns 271–272; Kluge-Seebold 366–367.
ON heita ‘to brew, to heat’, OE hætan ‘to heat’, OS hētian id., OHG heizen ‘to light up’. Derived from *xaitaz. Holthausen AEEW 146; Vries ANEW 220; Onions 433; Heidermanns 271; Kluge-Seebold 367.
Goth bi-haitja ‘boaster’, OE scyld-hete ‘enemy, woe’, OS scult-hētio ‘bailiff’. Derived from *xaitanan. Feist 90; Seebold 247.
ON heiäin-dómr ‘heathendom’, OE hæäen-dóm id., MLG heiden-dom id., OHG heidan-tuom id. Derived from *xaiþinaz. Carr Nom. comp. 73 (parallel formations).

Goth haiþns ‘heathen’ (with syncope), ON heiäinn id., OE hæäen id., OFris hēthin id., OS hēthin id., OHG heidan id. Derived from *xaiþiz with the original meaning ‘living on the heath, wild’. In individual Germanic languages, the word might have been influenced by Lat pāgānus (Grienberger Got. 106, but see Vries ANEW 216–217). Attested as Χαιδεινοί (Ptol.), people of Western Scandinavia. Simon Zd Ph LIII 46–49 (ON and WGmc from Goth); Jellinek GGS 165, 190, ZDADL LXVI 134 (against Simon); Torp-Falk 64; Holthausen IF XXX 48 (to *skaiđanan ~ *skaiþanan), AEEW 146; Wessén ANF XLIV 86–91; Schulze Kl. Schr. 519–527 (Goth haiþns as an adaptation of Gk đynh); Feist 237–238; Vries ANEW 216; Onions 433; Lehmann GED 172–173; Kluge-Seebold 364.

Goth haiþi ‘field’, ON heiär ‘heath’, OE masc., neut. hæä id., OS hētha, heitha id. (in pln.), OHG heida id. Related to OW coit ‘woods’, OCorn cuit id., Bret coet id. (Fick KZ I 368– 369). Fick II 76 (to Lat bū-cētum ‘pasture for cattle’); Solmsen KZ XXXIV 14 (follows Fick); Braune PBB XLIII 430–431; Torp-Falk 64; Kluge ZDW XI 26; Holthausen AEEW 146; Feist 237; Pokorny I 521; Vries ANEW 217; Zalizniak II 213; Schulze Kl. Schr. 521– 524; Onions 433; Lehmann GED 172; Kluge-Seebold 363.
Goth hais ‘torch’, OHG hei ‘heat’. Probably related to Lith kaistū, kaĩsti ‘to become hot’ < *kai-t-. Zupitza Gutt. 112; Feist 235–236; Pokorny I 519; Fraenkel 204; Lehmann GED 171; Heidermanns 272.

ON haki ‘hook’, OE haca ‘bolt’, OS hako ‘bolt, hook’, OHG fem. hacka id. Identical with Hitt kaka- ‘tooth’ (Puhvel IV 14–15). From here WGmc *xakuđaz ~ *xakiđaz ‘pike’ > OE hacod, OS hakth, OHG hehhit derivationally similar to Slav *kogъtь ‘claw’ (Brugmann Grundriß II/1 427). Cf. also *xōkaz > OE hóc ‘hook’, OFris hōk id., MLG hōk ‘corner, angle’. Zupitza Gutt. 111; Torp-Falk 66; Lewy IF XXXII 160; Trautmann BSW 112; Holthausen AEEW 143, 167; Pokorny I 537–538; Vries ANEW 203 (to Latv kegis ‘crutch’); Onions 447; Stang LS 30; Vasmer- Trubachev II 275; Kluge-Seebold 349–350.

Goth hakuls ‘cloak, mantle’, ON hǫkull ‘priest’s cope’, OE hacele ‘cloak, mantle’, hæcla id., OFris hezil id., OHG hahhul id. Derived from *xakōn, lit. a cloak buttoned with fibulae (Diefenbach VW II 513). Hirt BB XXIV 277 (from *xōkō ‘goat’ related to Slav *koza id.); Grienberger Got. 107 (to ON haka ‘chin’); Meillet Etudes 172 (agrees with Hirt); Uhlenbeck PBB XXX 287 (follows Diefenbach); Sperber Wu S VI 20; Trautmann Zd Wf VII 171 (to Slovene kozól ‘woven vessel’); Wiedemann PBB XXIX 314 (to Latv sagša ‘cover’); Zupitza Gutt. 106; Torp- Falk 67; Holthausen AEEW 143; Feist 238–239; Pokorny I 517; Chemodanov SGGJa I 80; Vries ANEW 280; Zalizniak II 182; Lehmann GED 173; Gołąb Origins 143; Kluge-Seebold 385–386. *xalba-brunnanaz ~ *xalba-brunnenaz adj.: ON hálf-brunnin ‘half-burnt’, OHG halb-brunnan id. Compound of *xalbaz and participle of *brennanan. Carr Nom. comp. 92 (parallel formations).
ON hálf-dauär ‘half-dead’, OE healf-deád id., OHG halb-tōt id. Compound of *xalbaz and *đauđaz. Carr Nom. comp. 92 (parallel formations).

Goth halbs ‘half’, ON hálfr id., OE healf id., OFris half id., OS half id., OHG halb id. A difficult word. Probably related to Skt kálpate ‘to succeed, to fit, to be partaken by’. Grienberger Got. 107–108 (to Lith šalīs ‘side’); Güntert Wu S XI 137–138 (to Gk κόλπος ‘pudenda’); Uhlenbeck PBB XXX 287 (< IE *skel-p- ‘to cut’); Pedersen KZ XXXVIII 373 (to Slav *xolpъ ‘slave, serf’); Zupitza Gutt. 106; Torp-Falk 85 (to OE helfe ‘handle’, MLG helve id., OHG halb id.); Feist 239; Holthausen AEEW 151; Karsten NB I 48; Mayrhofer I 183–184; Pokorny I 926; Vries ANEW 204; Lehmann GED 173; Heidermanns 272–273; Kluge-Seebold 350.
ON helft ‘half’, OFris helfte, halfte id., MLG helfte id. Derived from *xalbaz. Torp-Falk 85; Vries ANEW 221; Kluge-Seebold 350.

Goth halba ‘half’, OE healf id., OS halba id., OHG halb id. Related to *xalbaz. Torp-Falk 85; Holthausen AEEW 151; Feist 239; Vries ANEW 204; Zalizniak II 180; Onions 424; Lehmann GED 173; Heidermanns 273; Kluge- Seebold 350.
ON hálfa ‘region, quarter’, OFris halve ‘side’, OS halba id., OHG halba id. Derived from *xalbaz. Torp- Falk 85; Heidermanns 273.
ON hald ‘hold, fastening, support, custody’, OE heald ‘guardianship, protection’, OHG masc. burg-halto ‘city advocate’. Derived from *xalđanan. Holthausen AEEW 150–151; Vries ANEW 204; Seebold 248.

Goth haldan ‘to tend, to herd, to graze cattle’, ON halda ‘to hold’, OE healdan id., OFris halda ‘to capture’, OS haldan ‘to hold’, OHG haltan id. Related to Toch B käl(t)s- ‘to goad, to drive’, Lat prae-cellō ‘to surpass, to excel’ < *keld-, celsus ‘soaring, high’. Meringer IF XVIII 236; Zupitza Gutt. 106 (to Gk bou-κόλος ‘shepherd’), BB XXV 99 (to Lat aus-cultō ‘to listen’); Brugmann IF XXXII 181 (on the vocalism); Torp-Falk 84; Walde-Hofmann I 197; Holthausen AEEW 151; Feist 239–240; Jóhannesson IEW 246–247; Pokorny I 548; Onions 444; Vries ANEW 204; Seebold 248–249; Lehmann Language XVIII 129 (to *kel- ‘to drive’, cf. Skt kaláyati ‘to impel, to bear, to carry, to do’), GED 173–174; Kluge-Seebold 351–352; Adams TB 174.

Goth comp. ni þe haldis ‘none, by no means’, ON adv. heldr ‘rather, more’, OS comp. than hald ni ‘just as’, OHG comp. halt ‘sooner’, adv. halto ‘quickly, swiftly’. A variant of *xalþaz. Diefenbach VW II 517–518; Fick III 85; Grienberger Got. 108–109 (directly to *xalđanan ‘to hold’); Kroes GRM XXXVI 345–347; Torp-Falk 85; Uhlenbeck PBB XXX 287; Feist 240; Pokorny I 552; Vries ANEW 221; Lehmann GED 174; Heidermanns 274; Kluge-Seebold 351.

ON hǫlär ‘a kind of higher yeoman’, halr ‘man’, OSwed hälith ‘man, soldier’, OE hæle(ä) ‘man, hero’ (root stem *xalēþs), OS helith ‘soldier, hero’, OHG helid id. Probably related to Skt kalya- ‘healthy, prepared for, clever’, Gk καλός ‘beautiful’, kalli- (in compounds) (Specht KZ LXII 257– 258). Zupitza Gutt. 107; Johansson WZKM XIX 237 (to Gk κέλης ‘courser, riding horse’ < *kelēt-); Meillet MSL XVII 114 (same as Johansson); Torp- Falk 84 (follow Johansson); Holthausen AEEW 144; Specht 128, 195 (directly to Gk καλός ‘beautiful’); Jóhansson SF 307 (from *‘man from Halland’); Mayrhofer I 184–185; Pokorny I 524; Vries ANEW 206; Frisk I 766–767; Zalizniak II 208; Frisk I 766–767 (against Specht on semantic grounds); Bammesberger Nom. 215 (to IE *˚el- ‘to raise’); Kluge- Seebold 368; Adams TB 155 (to Toch B kāly-≤ke ‘boy, youth’).

Goth adv. halis-aiw ‘hardly ever’, MHG hel ‘weak’. Probably, historically connected with *xaliþz ~ *xaluþz. On the other hand, cf. Latv kālst ‘to dry’ and probably Slav *kolîti ‘to freeze’ (Zubaty Afsl Ph XVI 395). Holthausen IF XIV 340 (to Gk κόλος ‘mutilated’), XXXIX 70; Feist 240; Lehmann GED 174.
Goth-Lat pl. haliurunnae ‘witches’ (Jord.), OE helle-rúne ‘sorceress, necromancer’, OHG helli-rūna ‘magic’. Compound of *xaljō and *rūnō. Carr Nom. comp. 44.
ON hel-víti ‘hell’, OE helle-wíte ‘hell-torment, hell’, OS helliwīti ‘hell’, MHG fem. helle-wīze id. Compound of *xaljō and *wītjan. Vries ANEW 222.

Goth halja ‘hell’, ON hel ‘abode of the dead’, theon. Hel, the goddess of death, OE hell ‘hell’, OFris helle id., OS hellia id., OHG hella id. Derived from *xelanan thus probably repeating the semantic motivation of Gk ἭΑιδης < *–- ⁄id-. Note MIr luid ar cel ‘went to hell’ (Güntert Kalypso 34–35), cel ‘death’. Szadrowsky PBB LXXII 221–223 (to *xalluz ~ *xallaz); Zupitza Gutt. 185; Torp-Falk 80; Holthausen AEEW 1454; Feist 240–241; Trier Lehm 52– 61 (‘hell’ < ‘lattice work’); Pokorny I 554; Vries ANEW 220–221; Frisk I 33–34; Zalizniak II 213; Onions 435; Lehmann GED 174, 194; Bammesberger Nom. 112; Kluge-Seebold 380–381.

ON sbst. hall-æri ‘bad season’, NFris hall ‘dry’, MHG hel ‘weak’. Cf. also MDu hael ‘dried up’ < *xēlaz (Matzel KZ CV 110). Related to Lat caleō ‘to be warm’, W clyd ‘warm’ < *˚¬to-, Lith šylū, šīlti ‘to become warm’. Torp-Falk 83; Walde-Hofmann I 137; Pokorny I 551–552; Heidermanns 275.

ON hǫll ‘hall’ (partly from *xalliz), OE heall id., OS halla id., OHG halla id. From *xal-n-ō based on *xelanan. Torp-Falk 80; Holthausen AEEW 151; Pokorny I 554; Vries ANEW 280–281; Zalizniak II 193; Onions 424; Kluge- Seebold 350.

Goth hallus ‘stone slab, rock’, ON hallr ‘big stone, boulder’, OE heall ‘rock’. Related to MIr coll ‘head, leader’, Lat collis ‘hill’ < *kolnis (or *k¬nis), Lith kálnas ‘mountain’, Latv kafins id. Zupitza Gutt. 47, 106; Torp-Falk 86; Bugge BB XXXII 24 (to Skt ≤ilā ‘stone, rock, crag’); Hirt BB XXIV 278 (to OPrus kalso ‘flat cake’); Brugmann IF XVII 487, XXIX 233; Holthausen AEEW 151; Walde- Hofmann I 245; Feist 241; Pokorny I 544, 925; Fraenkel 209–210; Vries ANEW 205; Zalizniak II 200; Lerchner 112; Wagner KZ XCVII 281–283 (to Lat callum ‘callous’, OIr calath ‘hard’); Hamp IF LXXXI 36–38; Lehmann GED 174–175.

ON hálmr ‘straw’, OE neut. healm ‘stalk, straw’, MLG halm id., OHG halm id. Identical with Hitt kalam(m)a- ‘broom, rake’, Gk κάλαμος ‘reed’, Lat culmus ‘stalk’, OPrus salme ‘straw’, Latv safims id., Slav *solma id. Zupitza Gutt. 182; Torp-Falk 85; Trautmann BSW 298; Holthausen AEEW 151, ANW 104; Walde-Hofmann I 303–304; Pokorny I 612; Chemodanov SGGJa I 62–63; Frisk I 760–761; Vries ANEW 206; Zalizniak II 227; Onions 430; Vasmer-Trubachev III 713; Puhvel IV 18; Kluge-Seebold 351.

OFris halia ‘to call’, OS halōn id., OHG halōn id. Identical with Lat calō ‘to call’, further related to Hitt kaleš- id., Gk καλέω id. Torp- Falk 83; Walde-Hofmann I 141–142; Pokorny I 548–549; Frisk I 763–764; Onions 424; Seebold 253–254; Kluge- Seebold 380.
ON hali ‘tail’. Closely related to Skt ≤alá- ‘staff’, MIr cail ‘spear’. Zupitza Gutt. 182; Torp-Falk 82; Mayrhofer III 313–314; Pokorny I 552; Vries ANEW 204.
ON fem. háls-beina ‘neckbone’, MLG hals-bēn id., OHG halsbein id. Compound of *xalsaz and *bainan. Carr Nom. comp. 83 (parallel formations).

ON háls-fang ‘embracing’, OE heals-fanz ‘compensation paid by one sentenced to the pillory’, OFris hals-fang ‘rape’. Compound of *xalsaz and *fanzan ~ *fanxan. Carr Nom. comp. 72 (parallel formations).
ON háls-men ‘necklace’, OE masc. heals-mene id., OLG hals-meni id. Compound of *xalsaz and *manjan. Carr Nom. comp. 48.

Goth hals ‘neck’, ON háls id., OE heals id., OFris hals id., OS hals id., OHG hals id. Together with Lat collus, collum id. and MIr coll ‘head, chief’ continues IE *k⁄olso- (Falk ANF XLI 120). No traces of the initial *k⁄- in Gmc (Noreen IF IV 320–321). Solmsen KZ XXXIV 547 (follows Noreen); Stokes IF XII 187 (on the Irish form); Torbjörnsson Festschr. Noreen 257; Zupitza Gutt. 50, 106; Torp-Falk 82; Holthausen AEEW 151; Walde-Hofmann I 245; Feist 241–242; Krahe Su V 75; Porzig Gliederung 113; Pokorny I 639– 640; Chemodanov SGGJa I 88; Vries ANEW 206; Zalizniak I 132; Lehmann GED 175; Kluge-Seebold 351.
ON helsi ‘collar’, MHG helse id. Derived from *xalsaz. Vries ANEW 221.
ON hálsa ‘to embrace’, OS helsian id., OHG halsōn, halsēn id. Derived from *xalsaz. Vries ANEW 206.

Goth halts ‘crippled, lame’, ON haltr id., OE healt id., OFris halt id., OS halt id., OHG halz id. Related to Arm ka∑‘lame’, Gk κελλόν: στρεβλόν, πλάγιον (Hes.), OIr coll ‘destruction, ruin’. Zupitza Gutt. 107; Torp-Falk 84; Feist 242–243; Holthausen AEEW 152; Karsten NB I 41; Sütterlin IF XXV 67 (to Lat claudus ‘lame’); Walde- Hoffmann I 231 (to Skt khoḍa- ‘limping, lame’, Lat claudus); Specht 130 (to Russ koldyka ‘lame person’); Trier Holz 49 (to *xultan); Pokorny I 547; Chemodanov SGGJa I 85; Vries ANEW 206; Onions 425; Lehmann GED 175 (from *kel-, *klād- ‘to strike’; original meaning in Gmc: *‘broken bones’); Heidermanns 275–276.
ON helti ‘lameness’, OFris strump-helte ‘lameness cause by mutilation’, OS spuri-helti ‘limping’, OHG sērhalzī ‘disability caused by injury’. Derived from *xaltaz. Heidermanns 276.
ON heltask ‘to become halt’, OHG ir-helzen ‘to lame’. Derived from *xaltaz. Heidermanns 276.

ON hallr ‘sloping, leaning to one side’, OE heald ‘bent, inclined’, OFris north-hald ‘directed northwards’, OS hold ‘inclined, merciful’, OHG hald ‘bent down’. Related to *xalđanan (cf. also *xalþanan > OSwed halla ‘to hold’). Torp- Falk 82–83; Holthausen AWN 104 (to Skt ká†aka- ‘bracelet, ring’, Lith at-kalt E ‘back’), AEEW 151; Pokorny I 552 (to Lith šalīs ‘side’); Vries ANEW 205; Onions 434; Lehmann GED 403; Heidermanns 276–277; Kluge-Seebold 350; Boutkan OFED s.v.
Goth wilja-halþei ‘partisanship, partiality’, OHG uo-haldī ‘steep place’. Derived from *xalþaz. Feist 563; Vries ANEW 205; Onions 434; Heidermanns 276.

ON hella ‘to pour out’, OE hyldan ‘to incline, to bend’, OS af-heldian ‘to cease’, OHG gi-helden ‘to incline, to tilt’. Derived from *xalþaz. Zupitza Gutt. 113; Torp-Falk 83; Holthausen AEEW 158; Vries ANEW 221; Heidermanns 277.
ON halla ‘to lean sideways’, OHG haldōn ‘to be tilted, to bend’. Derived from *xalþaz. Torp-Falk 82–83; Heidermanns 277.

ON prop. Hamall, MLG hamel ‘wether’, MHG hamal id. Substantivized *xamalaz II. Torp-Falk 73; Holthausen AWN 105 (to Skt neut. ≤ámala- ‘stain, spot, fault’), AEEW 152; Pokorny I 929; Vries ANEW 206–207 (to IE *skep- ‘to cut’); Heidermanns 277; Kluge-Seebold 353. *xamalaz II adj.: ON hamal-kyrni ‘a kind of seed’, OHG hamal ‘mutilated’. Identical with Slav *komolъ ‘hornless’ (Schulze KZ XL 259), further connected with *komъ ‘lump’. Karsten NB II 233; Berneker I 554 (against Schulze); Torp- Falk 73; Meillet MSL XIV 376; Wissmann AWNP 97–98 (secondary derivative of *xamalōjan); Pokorny I 929; Trubachev ESSJa X 174–175; Heidermanns 277; Kluge-Seebold 353.
ON hamla ‘to mutilate’, OE hamelian id., OFris homelia id., OHG bi-hamalōn ‘to cut, to strike’. Derived from *xamalaz II. Torp-Falk 73; Holthausen AEEW 148; Heidermanns 277; Kluge-Seebold 353.

ON hamla ‘oar-loop’, OHG hamila ‘satyrion, orchis’. Related to Lith kambl‹s ‘leafless stock’, Slav *komьlь ‘butt-end (of a tree)’. Further cf. *xamalaz II. Fraenkel 213; Vries ANEW 207 (to Gk κάμαξ id.).

ON hamarr ‘hammer’, OE hamor, hamer id., OFris hamer, homer id., OS hamar, hamur id., OHG hamar id. With a metathesis of the anlaut similar to that of slav *kamy, continues a thematic derivative of IE *a˚-men- ‘stone’: *a˚moros identical with Skt a≤mará- ‘stony’ (Streitberg IF II 419). Thus, the original meaning of the Germanic word was ‘stone hammer’. Pedersen KZ XXXII 247; Detter ZDADL XLII 57 (to *xamalaz II, cf. Lat saxum ‘large stone, rock’ ~ secō ‘to cut’); Zupitza Gutt. 108; Torp-Falk 74; Güntert Wu S XI 140 (to *xamalaz II with a secondary attraction to *a˚-men-), Labyrinth 21 (to pre-Gk καμάρα ‘covered carriage, vaulted chamber’); Holthausen AEEW 149; Fraenkel KZ LXIII 183–184; Mayrhofer I 60; Pokorny I 19; Vries ANEW 207; Zalizniak I 132; Onions 425; Stang LS 25; Schelesniker Festschr. Ölberg 23–26; Maher JIES I 441–442; Kluge-Seebold 352–353.

ON hamr ‘skin, shape’, OE ham ‘covering, garment, shirt’, hama ‘covering’, MLG ham id., OHG hamo ‘fishingnet’. Probably related to Skt ≤amī ‘Prosopis spicigera’ (Zupitza BB XXIV 182) and further to *˚em- ‘to cover’. Zupitza Gutt. 182; Torp-Falk 74; Holthausen AEEW 148; Mayrhofer III 299; Pokorny I 556; Vries ANEW 208.

OS hamustra ‘curculio, corn-weevil’, OHG hamustra ‘hamster’. Probably borrowed from Lat comestor ‘glutton’ (Murianov Etim. 1983 105). However, the obvious connection with Slav *xomîstorъ ‘hamster’ remains unclear. Fick BB II 266 (to Lat cumera ‘chest, box’ < *komeso-); Berneker I 395 (< Slav); Fraenkel 896; Onions 425.

Goth hamfs ‘mutilated, maimed’, OS hāf ‘with crippled hands’, OHG hamf ‘mutilated’. Related to OIr camm ‘crooked, bent’ and further to Gk καμπή ‘bend’, Lat campus ‘field’ < *‘bending’, Lith ka Mpas ‘corner, edge’, Slav *kǫpъ ‘hill, island’ (Saussure Recueil 598). Diefenbach VW II 529–531 (to Gk κόπτω ‘to strike’); Schröder Idg. Anz. XXVI 59 (to ON hnafa ‘to cut off’); Aufderhaar Lehnw. 21–22 (WGmc < Goth); Zupitza Gutt. 108; Torp-Falk 74– 75; Walde-Hofmann I 148–149; Feist 243; Pokorny I 525; Fraenkel 213–214; Frisk I 774–775; Trubachev ESSJa XII 56–57; Lehmann GED 175–176; Puhvel IV 61–63 (adds Hitt kappi- ‘small, little’ <? *kampi-); Heidermanns 278.

ON hemingr ‘skin of the hide shanks’, prop. Hemingr, OE hemminz ‘a kind of shoe’, OFris hemminge ‘leather wear, shoe’, OHG prop. Haming. Derived from *xam(m)ō. Falk NVA 1919, 133; Holthausen AEEW 155; Vries ANEW 222.

ON hemja ‘to restrain, to hold back’, OE hemman id., OFris hemma ‘to mutilate’, MDu hemmen ‘to slow down’, OHG gi-hemmen ‘to humiliate’. Derived from *xam(m)ō. Zupitza Gutt. 108; Löwenthal KZ XLVII 146 (to Gk κήμος ‘a kind of plant’, Slav *konь ‘horse’); Torp-Falk 74; Holthausen AEEW 155; Pokorny I 555; Vries ANEW 222; Kluge-Seebold 369.

ON hǫm ‘ham’, OE ham id., MLG ham(m)e id., MHG hamme id. From *xanmō < *kon 6 mā, an ablaut variant of *knāmā: Gk κνήμη ‘leg, shank’, OIr cnáim ‘bone’ (Fick KZ I 368–369). Torp-Falk 74; Holthausen AEEW 148; Pokorny I 613–614; Frisk I 883; Vries ANEW 279; Zalizniak II 225; Onions 425.
Norw dial. humul ‘bar’, MHG hamel ‘bar, rod’. Related to *xam(a)lōn. Torp-Falk 74 (to Gk κάμαξ ‘vine-pole, pole, shaft’).

ON hampr ‘hemp’, OE hænep id., OS hanup id., OHG hanaf, hanif id. An early borrowing from Lat cannabis id. < Gk κάνναβις id. Hoops Waldbäume 472–473 (from Scythian or Thracian); Lewy IF LIII 122; Torp-Falk 70; Holthausen AEEW 145; Vries ANEW 208; Zalizniak II 187; Onions 436 (related to Lat cannabis); Kluge-Seebold 354.

Goth handugs ‘wise’, Norw hendig ‘deft, adroit, quick’, OE list-hendiz ‘having skilful hands’, OHG hantag, hantīg ‘wise’. Derived from *xanđuz (Meillet MSL IX 49). Kern TNTL XX 245–246; Grienberger Got. 110 (to Lith kandūs ‘snappy, acrimonious’); Blankenstein IF XXI 115; Zupitza Gutt. 206; Zupitza Gutt. 206; Torp-Falk 71; Feist 244; Holthausen AEEW 155; Kluge Nom. Stamm 100 (to Gk κεντέω ‘to prick’); Feist 244; Pokorny I 567; Meid Wortbild. 192; Lehmann GED 176 (follows Kluge); Heidermanns 278–279.
Goth handugei ‘wisdom’, OHG hantagī ‘sharpness, agony, pain’. Derived from *xanđazaz ~ *xanđizaz ~ *xanđuzaz. Feist 244; Heidermanns 279.
ON henda ‘to catch, to pick up’, OE ze-hendan ‘to hold’, OFris henda, handa ‘to catch’. Derived from *xanđuz. Holthausen AEEW 155; Vries ANEW 222; Seebold 255.
OE handle ‘handle’, MLG hantel id., OHG hantilla ‘towel’. Derived from *xanđuz. Holthausen AEEW 149; Onions 426.

ON hǫndla ‘to handle, to manage’, OE handlian ‘to feel, to handle’, OFris handelia, hondelia ‘to handle’, OS handlon ‘to act, to deal’, OHG hantalōn ‘to handle, to feel’. Derived from *xanđlan ~ *xanđlō. Holthausen AEEW 149; Vries ANEW 208; Onions 426; Kluge-Seebold 353.
ON hand-bani ‘homicide’, OE hand-bana id., OS handbano id. Compound of *xanđuz and *banōn. Carr Nom. comp. 48.
ON hand-lami ‘with a lame hand’ (n-stem), OHG hant-lam id. Compound of *xanđuz and *lamaz. Carr Nom. comp. 90 (parallel formations).
ON hand-lauss ‘without hands’, OFris hand-lās id., MHG hande-lōs id. Compound of *xanđuz and *lausaz. Carr Nom. comp. 89 (parallel formations).
ON hand-megin ‘strength of hand, strength’, OE handmæzen id., OS hand-magan id. Compound of *xanđuz and *mazenan. Carr Nom. comp. 48.
ON hand-sax ‘short sword’, OE hand-seax id., OHG *hand-sahs id. (> Fr hansart). Carr Nom. comp. 49.
ON hand-verk ‘handiwork’, OE hand-weorc id., OS handwerk id., OHG hant-werc id. Compound of *xanđuz and *werkan. Carr Nom. comp. 72 (parallel formations).

Goth handu-waurhts ‘handmade’, OE hand-worht id. Compound of *xanđuz and *wurxtaz. Feist 245; Carr Nom. comp. 88 (calque of Gk χειροποιητός in Goth and of Lat manufactum in OE); Velten JEGP XXIX 349 (calque on Greek); Lehmann GED 177.

Goth handus ‘hand’, ON hǫnd id. (root stem), OE hand id., OFris hand, hond id., OS hand ‘side’, OHG hant ‘hand’. Derived from *xenþanan (Grimm DG 35), cf. Lith rankā ‘hand’ ~ renkū, riñkti ‘to gather’. Loewenthal ANF XXXIII 123–124 (to Slav *‘êti ‘to begin’); Zupitza Gutt. 183; Torp-Falk 71; Holthausen AEEW 149; Feist 244–245; Fraenkel 736; Vries ANEW 281; Zalizniak II 208; Onions 425; Wagner Zfcelt Ph XXXII 76–77; Seebold 255; Markey JIES XII 261–292; Huld 120 (to Alb thua ~ thue ‘nail’); Lehmann GED 176–177; Bammesberger Nom. 155–156; Kluge-Seebold 353.
ON hengja ‘to hang up, to suspend’, OFris hingia id., OHG ir-henken id. Causative of *xanxanan ~ *xanzanan. Vries ANEW 222; Seebold 250; Lehmann GED 168.
Goth faura-hah ‘curtain’, OHG bruoh-hāh ‘belt, girdle’. Derived from *xanxanan ~ *xanzanan. Feist 145; Seebold 250.

Goth hahan ‘to hang, to make hang’, ON hanga id., OE hón id., OFris huā id., OS hāhan id., OHG hāhan id. Related to Hitt kanka-, kankiya- id. (Sturtevant Language VII 172). Probably further connected with Skt ≤áṅkate ‘to doubt, to fear’, Lat cūnctor ‘to hesitate’, cf. with a similar metaphoric usage Goth saiwala unsare hahis (John 10: 24). Meillet MSL XV 351–352 (phonetics of Goth); Torp-Falk 70; Feist 230–231; Holthausen AEEW 169; Walde-Hofmann I 307; Jóhannesson IEW 207–208; Mayrhofer III 290; Pokorny I 566; Vries ANEW 208 (to *xakōn); Kammenhuber KZ LXXVII 63 (on Hitt); Onions 426; Seebold 249–250; Lehmann GED 168; Puhvel IV 48–51; Kluge-Seebold 354–355.

ON há-sin ‘Achilles’ tendon, hamstring’, OE hóh-sinu id., OFris hōxene id., MDu haes-sene id., OHG hāh-sina. Compound of *xanxaz II and *sin(a)wō. Torp-Falk 67; Holthausen AEEW 168; Carr Nom. comp. 49; Vries ANEW 213; Lehmann GED 168; Kluge-Seebold 346. *xanxaz I sb.m.: Run dat. sg. hahai ‘courser’, OHG hāh-, hang- (in prop.). Related to Gk κηκίω ‘to gush, to bubble forth’, Lith šóku, šókti ‘to spring, to dance’ < *(s)˚ek-. Or same as *xanxaz II? Pokorny I 522; Fraenkel 1021–1022; Frisk I 838.

ON há-mót ‘ankle-joint’, OE hóh ‘heel’. Related to Lith kīnka ‘leg, thigh, knee-cap’ (Zupitza Gutt. 115). Torp-Falk 67 (to Lat coxa ‘hip’); Petersson IF XXXV 271–272; Holthausen AEEW 168; Pokorny I 566; Fraenkel 239; Vries ANEW 199; Onions 434; Stang LS 27–28; Kluge- Seebold 346; Dybo BSA 20.

Goth hahan ‘to hang’, ON hanga id. (part of forms), OE hanzian id., OFris hangia id., OS hangon id., OHG hangēn id. Derived from *xanxanan ~ *xanzanan. ON há ‘to wear (by sickness or work), to make depend’ < *xanxēnan belongs here rather than to Lith kankā ‘pain’ (despite Zupitza Gutt. 111, 132). Zupitza Gutt. 133; Torp-Falk 70; Holthausen AEEW 149; Feist 230–231; Vries ANEW 200; Seebold 250.

ON hæll ‘heel’, OE héla id., OFris hēla id., MDu hiel, hiele id. Derived from *xanxaz II. Cf. similar forms in Skt kaṅkāla- ‘scrag, shell’, Lith kenkl E ‘knee-cap’. Meringer IF XVII 121 (ON hæll to OHG huohilī ‘small plow’); Zupitza Gutt. 115; Torp- Falk 67, 70; Holthausen AEEW 167; Mayrhofer I 137; Fraenkel 239; Vries ANEW 209–210, 276; Onions 434; Lehmann GED 189; Kluge-Seebold 346; Dybo BSA 20.

ON hestr ‘stallion’, OE henzest ‘horse, steed’, OFris hanxt, hengst, hingst id., MDu henxt id., OHG hengist ‘gelding’. Cf. Celt *kankstikā ‘mare’: W caseg, Bret pl. kezeg, OCorn cassec (from Gmc?). Superlative of *xanxaz I. Sperber Wu S VI 21 (to MHG hagen ‘draught animal’); Schrader- Nehring I 626 (to Lat cant(h)ērius ‘gelding, ass, mule’ < *canctērius); Torp-Falk 70; Holthausen AEEW 155; Pokorny I 522 (to Lith šankūs ‘quick, fast’); Vries ANEW 226; Zalizniak II 227; Onions 436; Rasmussen APILKU IV 140; Kluge- Seebold 369.

ON hár ‘thole’. Identical with Skt ≤aṅkú- ‘peg, spike’, cf. also Slav *sǫkъ ‘branch’. Zupitza Gutt. 798; Torp-Falk 70; Sperber Wu S VI 20 (to ON hagr ‘handy, skilful’); Zupitza Gutt. 133, 184; Mayrhofer III 290; Pokorny I 523; Vries ANEW 209 (to *xōxōn); Vasmer-Trubachev III 798; Lehmann GED 189.
ON hǫnk ‘hank, coil’, masc. hanki ‘hasp, clasp’, MLG hank ‘handle’. See *xakōn. Torp-Falk 67 (to Lith kéngë ‘hook’); Fraenkel 255 (Lith kéngë < Gmc).

Goth hana ‘cock, rooster’, ON hani id., OE hana id., OFris hana, hona id., OS hano id., OHG hano id. Cf. also WGmc *xan(n)jō > OE hæn, hen ‘hen’, OFris henne id., MLG henne id., OHG henna id. Originally, a ‘singer’, cf. Gk ἠικανός ‘rooster’. Further connected with Lat canō ‘to sing’, OIr canim id. Unwerth PBB XXXVI 23; Marstrander NTS III 247, VII 335– 337; Zupitza Gutt. 108; Brugmann IF XXXVII 249–253; Suolahti DVN 228– 242; Torp-Falk 69; Holthausen AEEW 149, 155; Walde-Hofmann I 154–155; Feist 243–244; Porzig Gliederung 200; Pokorny I 525–526; Frisk I 626; Vries ANEW 208; Zalizniak I 153; Onions 436; Lehmann GED 176; Bammesberger Nom. 176–177; Kluge-Seebold 348.

Goth hansa ‘troop, cohort, crowd’, OE hós ‘company, band’, MLG hanse ‘company’, OHG hansa ‘cohort, company’. May be compared with Skt ≤áásati ‘to praise, to declare, to vow’, Lat cēnseō ‘to tax, to assess’ (Zupitza Gutt. 109). Bugge PBB XII 418–419 (from IE *˚om-sed- ‘to sit together’); Osthoff PBB XIII 425–426 (from *˚on-d-tā, to *dō- ‘to give’); Wiedemann BB XXVII 212 (to *xenþanan); Zupitza Gutt. 109; Torp-Falk 71; Meissner apud Feist(to *xunslan); Helm PBB XXIX 194–196; Kaufmann Zd Ph XXXVIII 238–240; Schnetz apud Lehmann GED 177 (to Lat scandula ‘shingle’, from IE *(s)kend- ‘to split up, to strew’); Rooth Wortst. 168 (follows Meissner); Holthausen AEEW 171; Walde-Hofmann I 198–200; Feist 245–246; Mayrhofer III 284–285 (separates Skt from Lat); Pokorny I 566; Zalizniak I 147; Onions 427; Benveniste IEL 63–65; Lehmann GED 177; Bammesberger Nom. 116 (to *xenþanan); Kluge-Seebold 355.
ON henta ‘to fit’, OE hentan ‘to pursue, to follow’. Unclear. Holthausen AEEW 155; Vries ANEW 222; Onions 441, 453 (to *xenþanan).

ON happ ‘good luck’. Cf. a derivative in OE hæp-líc ‘equal’. Compared with OIr cob ‘victory’, Lith kabūs ‘tenacious, prehensile’, Slav *kobь ‘omen, fortune telling’, *koba ‘premonition’ (Schachmatov Afsl Ph XXXIII 90). Pedersen Kelt. Gr. I 116; Torp-Falk 74 (reconstruct *xampan); Pokorny I 610; Fraenkel 200–201; Vries ANEW 542; Fraenkel 200; Onions 427; Trubachev ESSJa X 101. *xarbistu-mēnōþz ~ *xarbustumēnōþz sb.m.: ON haust-mánuär ‘autumn month, September’, OE hærfestmónaä id., OHG herbist-mānōd id. Compound of *xarbistuz ~ *xarbustuz and *mēnōþz. Carr Nom. comp. 72 (parallel formations).

ON haust ‘autumn’ (neut.), haustar-tími ‘autumn period’ (reflects old masc.), OE hærfest ‘harvest, autumn’, OFris herfst id., OHG herbist id. Derived from IE *karp- ~ *kerp-: Hitt karp-, karap- ‘to pick, to pluck’ (Sturtevant Language VI 155–156), Lat carpō id., Gk καρπός ‘fruit’ (Hirt IF XXXVII 233–234). Cf. *xarfaz from which *xarbistaz can be formally derived. Brugmann IF XXVIII 373 (to Lat castrō ‘to emasculate, to prune’ < *carp(i)strō); Zupitza Gutt. 114; Torp-Falk 79; Holthausen AEEW 145; Walde-Hofmann I 172–173; Pokorny I 944; Vries ANEW 214; Frisk I 792–793; Zalizniak II 168; Onions 429; Puhvel IV 91–98; Kluge- Seebold 370.
ON heräi ‘hardihood’, OHG hertī ‘rigidity’. Derived from *xarđuz. Heidermanns 281.

ON pl. heräar ‘shoulder’, OHG hartī id. Derived from *xarđuz (Hellquist ANF VII 29) and historically identical with *xarđīn. Schmidt Vok. II 25 (to Russ pl. *kortyški ‘shoulders’—but this form does not exist); Hirt PBB XXIII 351 (to Lat cartilagō ‘cartilage’); Zupitza Gutt. 115 (follows Schmidt); Torp-Falk 78 (follow Schmidt); Lane JEGP XXXII 293 (to IE

Goth ga-hardjan ‘to harden’, ON heräa ‘to temper (of iron), to clench, to fasten’, OE hyrdan ‘to make hard’, OFris herda ‘to strengthen’, OS herdian id., OHG herten ‘to harden’. Derived from *xarđuz. Holthausen AEEW 159; Feist 246; Vries ANEW 223; Onions 427; Lehmann GED 177; Heidermanns 281.
ON haräa ‘very’, OE hearde ‘severely, very much’, OFris herde ‘very’, MLG herde id., OHG harto id. Derived from *xarđuz. Lehmann GED 177.
Goth deriv. harduhairtei ‘hard-heartedness’, OE heard-heort id. Compound of *xarđuz and *xertōn. Feist 246; Lehmann GED 177.
ON harä-ligr ‘hard, severe’, OE heard-líc id., OHG hart-līh ‘tenacious’. Derived from *xarđuz. Heidermanns 281.
ON harä-ráär ‘hard in counsel, tyrannical’, OE heard-ræd ‘steadfast, firm’. Compound of *xarđuz and *rēđaz. Carr Nom. comp. 95 (parallel formations).

Goth hardus ‘hard’, ON harär id., OE heard ‘hard, austere, stern, firm’, OFris adv. herde ‘firmly, very’, OS hard ‘hard’, OHG hart id. Related to Lith kartūs ‘bitter’, Slav *kort-ъkъ ‘short’. Karsten NB II 361–362; Zupitza Gutt. 109; Meillet Etudes II 325; Torp- Falk 78; Feist 246–247; Holthausen AEEW 152; Walde-Pokorny I 354; Pokorny I 531; Chemodanov SGGJa I 109; Fraenkel 225; Frisk II 9–10; Vries ANEW 210–211; Onions 427; Benveniste IEL 366–367; Strunk MSS XXXIV 169–170 (on the comparison with Gk κρατύς ‘strong’); Trubachev ESSJa XI 104; Lehmann GED 177; Bammesberger Nom. 262; Heidermanns 280–282; Kluge-Seebold 358.

ON prop. Harfr, Swed harv ‘harrow’. Related to Gk καρπός ‘fruit’, κρώπιον ‘sickle’, Lat carpō ‘to pick, to pluck’, MIr corrán ‘sickle’. Torp-Falk 79; Walde-Hofmann I 172–173; Trier Holz 70; Pokorny I 944; Vries ANEW 211; Frisk I 792–793.
ON her-ferä ‘military expedition’, OFris here-ferd id., OS herevart id., OHG heri-fart id. Compound of *xariz ~ *xarjaz and *farđiz. Carr Nom. comp. 73 (parallel formations).
ON her-fólk ‘men of war’, OE here-folc ‘people forming an army’, OFris hiri-folk ‘men of war’. Compound of *xariz ~ *xarjaz and *fulkan. Carr Nom. comp. 49.
ON her-horn ‘trumpet’, OHG heri-horn id. Compound of *xariz ~ *xarjaz and *xurnan. Carr Nom. comp. 83 (parallel formations).
ON her-maär ‘warrior’, OE here-mann ‘soldier’, MDu herman id., OHG heri-man id. Compound of *xariz ~ *xarjaz and *mannz. Carr Nom. comp. 49.

ON her-skjǫldr ‘war-shield’ (if not from MLG), MLG her-schilt id., MHG herschilt ‘shield as a sign of a levy’. Compound of *xariz ~ *xarjaz and *skelđuz ~ *skelđiz. Carr Nom. comp. 73 (parallel formations).
ON her-skip ‘warship’, OHG heri-skif id. Compound of *xariz ~ *xarjaz and *skipan. Carr Nom. comp. 83 (parallel formations).
ON her-vegr ‘war path’, OE here-wez ‘highway’, OFris herewei ‘war path’, MLG her-wech id., MHG her-wec id. Compound of *xariz ~ *xarjaz and *wezaz. Carr Nom. comp. 49.
ON pl. her-váäir ‘armor’, OE here-wæd id. Cf. also MLG neut. here-wāde id. Compound of *xariz ~ *xarjaz and *wēđiz. Carr Nom. comp. 73 (parallel formations).
ON her-vápn ‘weapon’, OE here-wæpen id. Compound of *xariz ~ *xarjaz and *wēpnan. Carr Nom. comp. 81 (parallel formations).
ON her-óp ‘war-cry’, OE here-wóp ‘shout raised by an army’. Compound of *xariz ~ *xarjaz and *wōpan ~ *wōpaz. Carr Nom. comp. 81 (parallel formations).

Goth harjis ‘army’, ON herr ‘host, people, army’, OE here ‘army’, OFris here id., OS heri id., OHG neut. heri id. Related to MIr cuire ‘crowd’, Lith kãrias ‘army, war’, OPrus kargis ‘army’. Neckel KZ LX 284 (*Hari- as a deity in ancient prop. dat. Hari-gasti); Zupitza Gutt. 109; Torp-Falk 76; Specht KZ LX 130–138; Holthausen AEEW 157; Feist 247; Pokorny I 615–616; Būga III 950–951; Fraenkel 220; Vries ANEW 224–225; Zalizniak II 213; Onions 429; Benveniste IEL 91, 93, 247; Toporov PJa III 221; Lehmann GED 177–178; Kluge-Seebold 362–363.

ON Herjann (Odin’s epithet). Identical with Gk κοίρανος ‘king, head of the army’ (Osthoff IF V 275–277; Bugge PBB XXI 422), Phryg kuryan-eyon ‘chief, king’ (Lubotsky Kadmos XXVII/1 23). Further connected with *xariz ~ *xarjaz. Torp-Falk 76; Specht KZ LX 130; Pokorny I 615; Vries ANEW 223–224; Frisk I 894–895; Lehmann GED 178; Orel Phrygian 438.

ON herja ‘to harry, to despoil, to waste’, OE herzian ‘to pillage, to plunder’, OFris ur-heria ‘to make raids’, OS heriōn, herrōn id., OHG heriōn, herrōn ‘to plunder, to raid’. Derived from *xariz ~ *xarjaz. Torp-Falk 76; Holthausen AEEW 157; Vries ANEW 223; Onions 429.

ON harmr ‘sorrow, grief’, OE hearm ‘harm, injury, grief’, OFris herm ‘sorrow, grief’, OS harm id., OHG harm id. Identical with fšar 6 ma- ‘shame’, Slav *sormъ id. (Meillet Etudes 428) and, further, with Lith šarmā ‘frost’ (Larin Vestnik LGU XIV 150–153 with other examples of this semantic evolution). Kartsen NB II 245–246; Zupitza Gutt. 183–184; Meillet Etudes 428; Torp-Falk 79; Trautmann BSW 303; Holthausen AEEW 152; Pokorny I 615; Fraenkel 965; Vries ANEW 212; Zalizniak I 132; Onions 428; Vasmer-Trubachev III 724; Koivulehto Festschr. Alinei 133–147; Kluge-Seebold 357.
ODan harm ‘angry’, OE hearm ‘causing harm, grievous’, OS harm ‘grievous’. See *xarmaz I. Heidermanns 282.
ON hermd ‘vexation, anger’, OHG harmida ‘calamity’. Derived from *xarmaz I. Vries ANEW 224.
ON harma ‘to bewail’, OE hearmian ‘to harm, to hurt, to injure’, OHG harmēn ‘to sadden’. Derived from *xarmaz I. Vries ANEW 212.
OE hearma ‘shrewmouse (?)’, OS harmo ‘weasel, shrewmouse’, OHG harmo id. Identical with Lith šarmuõ, šermuõ id. Torp-Falk 79; Trautmann BSW 300; Pokorny I 574; Fraenkel 965; Kluge-Seebold 371.
ON hǫrpu-slagr ‘striking the harp’, OE hearp-sleze id. Compound of *xarpōn and *slaziz. A secondary composition vowel in ON. Carr Nom. comp. 81 (parallel formations).
ON hǫrpu-strengr ‘harp string’ (secondary -u-), OE hearpestrenz id. Compound of *xarpōn and *strenzaz. Carr Nom. comp. 81 (parallel formations).

ON harpa ‘harp’, OE hearpe id., OS harpa id., OHG harpfa id. Cf. also ON munn-harpa ‘cramp in the mouth from cold’. Related to Lat corbis ‘basket’, Lith kaıbas id., Slav *korbъ ~ *korba id. Sperber Wu S III 68–77 (to *xarfaz); Zupitza Gutt. 114, KZ XXXVI 65 (ON munn-harpa to Gk κράμβος ‘dry’); Torp- Falk 78; Trautmann BSW 117– 118; Holthausen AEEW 152; Walde- Hofmann I 272–273; Pokorny I 948 (to *(s)kerb- ‘to turn, to bend’); Fraenkel 220; Vries ANEW 212 (ON munn-harpa to G dial. Harpf ‘woman’); Zalizniak I 155; Onions 428; Trubachev ESSJa XI 52–54; Lühr Expr. 264–265; Kluge- Seebold 356–357.

Norw dial. harsk ‘with a hard voice’, MLG harsch ‘harsh, rough’. Derived from or related to *xarđuz (Holthausen AWN 107). Karsten NB II 450; Torp-Falk 80; Pokorny I 532–533; Vries ANEW 212–213 (to Lith karšiū, kaıšti ‘to comb’); Puhvel IV 109 (to Hitt karši- ‘harsh’); Heidermanns 282–283.

ON hǫrgr ‘heathen altar of stones’, OE hearz ‘temple, idol’, OHG harug ‘holy grove, holy stone’. Borrowed from continental Celt *karrikā ‘stone’ (OW carrecc, OBret carrec) or, rather, from its non-IE source (Hubschmidt Pyren. 31–32, 65–66). Noreen Abriß 87, 829 (to Lat carcer ‘prison’); Meringer Wu S X 188 (same as Noreen); Torp-Falk 77; Specht KZ LXIV 12; Ekwall MASO III 41 (to Swed dial. har ‘stony ground’); Holthausen AEEW 152; Pokorny I 532 (to IE *kar- ‘hard’); Vries ANEW 281; Zalizniak I 132; Vendryes C-31; Friedrich Trees 150 (to OPrus karige ‘rowan-tree’); Bammesberger Nom. 156 (< *xarzuz).

ON pl. Hǫräar ‘Hords, inhabitants of the Hardangerfjord in Norway’, OE pl. Hæredas id. Also attested as Lat Harudes, Gk Χαροῦδες. Probably further related to WGmc *xaruþaz ‘forest’ (OE haraä, MHG hart). (Grienberger ZDADL XLVI 166). Torp-Falk 76–77; Holthausen AEEW 145; Pokorny I 532 (to OIr caur ‘hero’ < *karuts); Vries ANEW 281.
Swed hassel-nöt ‘hazelnut’, OE hæsel-hnutu id., MLG hasel-note id., OHG hasal-nuz id. Compound of *xaslaz and *xnutz. Carr Nom. comp. 49.

ON hasl ‘hazel’, OE hæsel id., MDu hasel id., OHG hasal id. Identical with Lat corulus ‘hazel-shrub, filbert-shrub’ < *kosulo-, OIr coll ‘hazel’, OW coll id. < *koslo-. (Zupitza Gutt. 206). Torp-Falk 76; Holthausen AEEW 146; Walde-Hofmann I 280; Krahe Su V 80; Porzig Gliederung 194; Pokorny I 616; Vries ANEW 213; Zalizniak II 174; Onions 432; Kluge-Seebold 359.

OHG hasan ‘polished, fine’. Identical with Lat cānus ‘grey’ < *˚asno- (Weise BB II 289). Cf. also Hitt kaši- ‘shade of white’. Schwentner FB 80; Vasmer Zfsl Ph II 57–58 (adds Slav *sosna ‘pine’ < *‘grey tree’); Walde-Hofmann I 156; Pokorny I 533; Puhvel IV 119–120; Heidermanns 283–284.

ON heri ‘hare’, OE hara id., OFris hasa id., MLG hase id., OHG haso id. Related to W ceinach id. < *kasi-n-ako-, OPrus sasins, cf. also Skt ≤a≤áid. < *≤asá- (Försterman KZ I 498) and Hitt gagastiƒa- ‘hare (?)’ (Puhvel IV 17). A taboo word based on the color adjective preserved in *xasnaz, *xaswaz. Zupitza Gutt. 183; Schwentner FB 79–80; Hendriksen IF LVI 27; Torp-Falk 76; Holthausen AEEW 149; Porzig Gliederung 197; Mayrhofer III 316–317, Sprache VII 181; Pokorny I 533; Chemodanov SSGJa I 58; Vries ANEW 223; Zalizniak I 153; Onions 427; Kluge- Seebold 358–359.

ON hespa ‘hasp, fastening’, OE hæpse ‘hasp, clasp’, MLG haspe id. Unclear. Loewenthal ANF XXXV 233 (to Slav *kosa); Petersson Afsl Ph XXXVI 137–138; Torp-Falk 71 (reconstruct *xaps-, to Lith kab E ‘small hook’); Holthausen AEEW 156; Vries ANEW 225–226; Onions 430; Kluge-Seebold 359.

ON hǫss ‘grey’, OE hasu id., MHG heswe ‘pale, dull’. Related to *xasnaz. Schwentner FB 80; Torp- Falk 87; Holthausen AEEW 149; Pokorny I 533; Vries ANEW 282; Zalizniak I 153; Bammesberger Nom. 244; Heidermanns 284; Hilmarsson Mat. 156 (to Toch B kāswo ‘eruption, inflammation of the skin’).

OESc of-hatul ‘extremely pleasant’, OE hatol, hetol ‘hostile, evil’, OS hatulo ‘hate-filled’, OHG hazzal ‘guileful, malicious’. Derived from *xataz ~ *xatez, *xatjanan. Holthausen AEEW 150; Heidermanns 284–285.

Goth hatis ‘hatred, anger’, ON hatr id., OE hete id., OFris hat id., OS heti id., OHG haz id. Related to Toch A kat ‘destruction’, Av sādra- ‘grief, sorrow’, Osc cadeis ‘enmity’, Gk κῆδος ‘sorrow’, MIr caiss ‘hatred’. Zupitza Gutt. 184; Bartholomae 1570– 1571; Torp-Falk 68–69; Holthausen AEEW 157; Feist 247–248; Ernout- Meillet 698 (to Lat ōdī ‘to hate’ with a špreformant” *k-); Pokorny I 517; Chemodanov SGGJa I 87; Frisk I 836–837; Zalizniak II 223; Onions 430; Lehmann GED 178; Bammesberger Nom. 212; Kluge-Seebold 359.
Goth hatjan ‘to hate’, OE hettan ‘to hate’, MHG hetzen ‘to pursue’. Derived from *xataz ~ *xatez. Torp-Falk 68; Holthausen AEEW 158; Feist 248; Peeters KZ CI 168; Lehmann GED 178; Kluge-Seebold 372.

Goth hatan ‘to hate’, ON hata id., OE hatian id., OFris hatia id., OS haton id., OHG hazzōn id. Derived from *xataz ~ *xatez. Kuhn KZ I 95; Torp-Falk 68; Holthausen AEEW 150; Feist 247; Vries ANEW 213; Onions 430; Lehmann GED 178.

ON hǫttr ‘hood’, OE hætt ‘hat’, OFries hath ‘hood’. Cf. also WGmc *xōđaz > OE hód ‘coat with a hood’, OE hód ‘hood’, OFris hōd id., OS hōd id., OHG huot id. Of unknown origin. Zupitza Gutt. 207; Torp-Falk 69 (to Lat cassis ‘helmet’); Holthausen AEEW 146, 167 (*xōđaz to Lat cassis ‘helm’); Pokorny I 516; Vries ANEW 282; Onions 430, 447; Kluge-Seebold 389–390.

OHG hadara ‘lump’, early G Hader ‘quarrel’. Derived from *xaþuz. Identical with Slav *kotera ~ *kotora ‘quarrel’ (Bugge KZ XXXII 49). Skt ≤atru- ‘enemy’ is derivationally less close. Zupitza KZ XXXVII 400; Uhlenbeck PBB XXVI 287 (to Arm kotor ‘scrap, fragment’); Torp-Falk 69; Mayrhofer III 294; Trubachev ESSJa XI 200–201; Kluge-Seebold 346 (separates OHG from this etymon).

Burg *haþus ‘fight’, ON hǫä ‘war, slaughter’, theon. Hǫär, OE heaäu-deór ‘brave, stout in war’, OFris -had (in prop.), OS hathu- id. (in prop.), OHG prop. Hadu-wīg id. Identical with Hitt kattu- ‘spiteful’, OIr cath id., Thrac theon. Κότυς (Fick Spracheinh. 422). Torp-Falk 69; Holthausen AEEW 153; Detschew Thr. 258–259; Pokorny I 534; Vries ANEW 278–279; Frisk I 932; Köbler 711; Puhvel IV 138–140; Kluge- Seebold 346; Boutkan OFED s.v.

Goth haubiþ ‘head’, ON haufuä id., OE heáfod id., OFris hāved, hād id., OS hōbid id., OHG houbit id. Secondary (taboo?) variants of *xabuđan (Noreen ANF VI 310). Marstrander Kl. 25 (the first syllable influenced by the following -u-); Zupitza Gutt. 103; Torp-Falk 73; Holthausen AEEW 150; Feist 248; Pokorny I 530 (analogical influence of *xūbōn); Zalizniak I 141; Onions 432; Lehmann GED 178–179; Bammesberger Nom. 215–216; Kluge-Seebold 360.

ON haugr ‘mound, cairn’, OHG houg ‘hill’. A variant of *xauxaz with a different stress pattern. Close to Lith ka Ukas ‘swelling, ulcer’, Slav *kuka ‘hook’ (Wood IF XVIII 35). Zupitza Gutt. 110; Torp-Falk 91; Trautmann BSW 121–122; Fraenkel 229–230; Vries ANEW 213–214; Trubachev ESSJa XIII 86–87; Heidermanns 286; Kluge-Seebold 386.
ON há-bjarg ‘high rock’, OE héah-beorz ‘high mountain’. Compound of *xauxaz and *berzan ~ *berzaz. Carr Nom. comp. 86 (parallel formations).
Goth hauh-hairts ‘haughty, proud’, OE heáh-heort id. Compound of *xauxaz and *xertaz (see *xertōn). Feist 249; Carr Nom. comp. 94 (parallel formations); Lehmann GED 171, 179.
ON há-ligr ‘high, sublime’, OE heá-lic ‘high’, OHG hōh-līh id. Derived from *xauxaz. Heidermanns 286.
ON pl. há-salir ‘high hall’, OE heáh-sele id. Compound of *xauxaz and *saliz ~ *salaz. Carr Nom. comp. 58.
ON há-staär ‘high place’, OE heáh-stede id. Compound of *xauxaz and *stađiz. Carr Nom. comp. 58.

Goth hauhs ‘high’, ON hár id., OE heáh id., OFris hāch id., OS hōh id., OHG hōh id. Closely related to Toch A koc, B kauc ‘high’. Further cf. Skt koca- ‘desiccation’, kucati ‘to curve’, NPers kōž ‘bent, crooked’, Lith ka Ukas ‘bump, swelling’, Slav *kuka ‘hook’ (Wood IF XVIII 35), OIr cúar ‘crooked’ < *kukro-. Kuhn KZ I 137; Schweizer KZ I 558, 560; Karsten NB I 62; Zupitza Gutt. 110, 132; Torp-Falk 91; Feist 249; Holthausen AEEW 150; Mayrhofer I 269; Pokorny I 589; Fraenkel 229–230; Vries ANEW 210; Onions 440; Stang LS 27; Trubachev ESSJa XIII 86–87; Lehmann GED 179; Bammesberger Nom. 237; Heidermanns 285–286; Kluge- Seebold 378; Adams TB 208; Boutkan OFED s.v.

Goth hauhiþa ‘height’, ON hæä id., OE híhäo id., MDu hogede id., OHG hōhida id. Derived from *xauxaz. Pipping SNF XII/1 68 (ON < *xaxwiþō); Torp-Falk 91; Holthausen AEEW 158; Feist 249; Vries ANEW 276; Onions 435; Lehmann GED 179; Bammesberger Nom. 117; Heidermanns 286.
Goth hauhei ‘height’, OS hōhī id., OHG hōhī id. Derived from *xauxaz. Feist 249; Lehmann GED 179; Heidermanns 286.

Goth hauhjan ‘to make high’, OESc höghia ‘to raise’, OE héan id., OFris hēia id., OLG ir-hōen id., OHG gi-hōhen id. Derived from *xauxaz. Structurally close to Slav *ku‘iti ‘to touch, to gather’. Holthausen AEEW 158; Feist 249; Trubachev ESSJa XIII 81; Lehmann GED 179; Heidermanns 286.

ON haull ‘rupture, hernia’, OE heála ‘rupture’, OHG hōla id. Related to Gk κήλη ‘hump, hernia’ < *kā⁄elā, Lith kūla ‘swelling’, Slav *kyla ‘hernia’ (Solmsen KZ XXXI 471). Zupitza Gutt. 110; Būga RFV LXXI 54; Torp-Falk 66; Kretschmer KZ XXXI 471–473 (on Gk κήλη); Trautmann BSW 144; Holthausen AEEW 150; Pokorny I 536–537; Chemodanov SGGJa I 109; Fraenkel 306; Frisk I 839–840; Vries ANEW 214; Trubachev ESSJa XIII 262–263.

Goth hauns ‘low, humble’, OE heán ‘low, mean, abject’, OFris hāna ‘low, humble’, OHG hōni ‘shameful’. Close to Gk καυνός: κακός, σκληρός, κλῆρος (Hes.), Latv kāuns ‘ignominy, shame’. Zupitza Gutt. 110 (to MHG hūren ‘to cower’); Torp-Falk 66; Feist 249–250; Holthausen AEEW 152; Pisani RIL LXXVI/2 30 (to Toch A çāñi ‘shame’); Pokorny I 535; Frisk I 803; Chantraine 506; Stang LS 27; Lehmann GED 179; Bammesberger Nom. 246 (from *xawwanan); Heidermanns 286–287; Kluge-Seebold 380.

Goth hauniþa ‘silence, humility’, OE hÿnä, hÿnäu ‘abasement, humiliation’, OFris hēnde ‘injury’, OS hōnitha ‘ignominy, shame’, OHG hōnida ‘disgrace’. Derived from *xaunaz. Holthausen AEEW 159; Feist 249; Heidermanns 287.

Goth haunjan ‘to abase, to humiliate’, OE hÿnan ‘to abuse, to humiliate’, OFris hēna ‘to get over’, OS gi-hōnian ‘to abase, to humiliate’, OHG hōnen id. Derived from *xaunaz. Holthausen AEEW 159; Feist 249; Heidermanns 287.

OE héap ‘heap, pile, crowd’, OFris hāp id., OS hōp id., OHG houf id. A phonetically irregular counterpart of IE *koupo- (with Gmc *-p- < IE *-p-?): Av kaofa- ‘hill’, Lith ka Upas id., Slav *kupa ~ *kupъ ‘heap, group (of trees)’. Zupitza Gutt. 22, 115; Bartholomae 431; Torp-Falk 94–95 (< *koupn-); Holthausen AEEW 152; Pokorny I 590–591 (thinks of a separate IE *keub-), 591; Fraenkel 231; Zalizniak I 132; Onions 432; Stang LS 27 (to Lith ka Ubras ‘hill’); Trubachev ESSJa XIII 107–108; Kluge-Seebold 360.

ON hauss ‘skull’. Identical with Skt kóßa- ‘cupboard, treasury’, Lith káušas ‘skull, laddle, scoop’ (Hirt BB XXIV 278). Johansson IF XIX 125– 133; Zupitza Gutt. 111; Mayrhofer I 273; Pokorny I 953; Fraenkel 231–232; Vries ANEW 214.

Goth hausjan ‘to hear’, ON heyra id., OE hÿran id., OFris hēra id., OS hōrian id., OHG hōren id. Related to Gk κοέω ‘to note’, ἀκούω ‘to hear’ with unclear é- (Delbrück KZ XVI 271), Lat caueō ‘to take heed’. See *skawwōjanan. Grienberger Got. 111 (preposition *x- and main stem *ausjanan); Zupitza Gutt. 206; Torp-Falk 66; Holthausen AEEW 159; Walde-Hofmann I 186–187; Feist 251– 252; Pokorny I 587–588; Frisk I 890– 891; Vries ANEW 226; Onions 433; Lehmann GED 180–181; Kluge-Seebold 383.

ON heyja ‘to execute, to perform’, OE híezan ‘to perform’. Probably connected with *xawwanan. Zupitza Gutt. 122 (to Gk σκεῦος ‘vessel, implement’, Slav *kutiti); Torp-Falk 66; Holthausen AEEW 158; Vries ANEW 226; Van Windekens I 237–238 (to Toch A ktsets ‘finished, perfect’, B ktsaitse ‘old’).
ON hǫgg ‘stroke, blow’, OE ze-heáw ‘striking together, gnashing’. Derived from *xawwanan. Holthausen AEEW 153; Vries ANEW 280; Seebold 251.

ON hǫggva ‘to strike, to smite’, OE heáwan ‘to cut, to hew’, OFris hāwa id., OS pret. ga-hio id., OHG houwan id. Related to Toch A ko-, B kau- ‘to kill’, Lat cūdō ‘to strike’, Lith káuju, káuti id., Slav *kujǫ, *kovati ‘to forge’ (Bugge KZ XIX (1870) 414). Hirt BB XXIV 276; Zupitza Gutt. 122; Meillet MSL XIV 355; Torp-Falk 65–66; Trautmann BSW 123; Holthausen AEEW 153; Walde-Hofmann I 300–301; Jóhannesson IEW 177; Pokorny I 535; Fraenkel 232; Vries ANEW 280; Onions 439; Seebold 251; Lindeman Verschärfung 15.7; Lehmann GED 181; Kluge-Seebold 360; Adams TB 208

Goth hawi ‘hay’, ON hey id., OE hez id., OFries hā, hai, hē id., OS hōi id., OHG hewi, hou id. Derived from *xawwanan (Pott apud Feist). The original meaning was ‘cut grass’. Lidén Festschr. Bugge 94 (reconstructs *xazwjan); Uhlenbeck PBB XXII 191 (to Slav *kovylь ‘feather-grass, Stipa pennata’); Zupitza Gutt. 52, 75, 122; Torp-Falk 66; Holthausen AEEW 158; Feist 252; Pokorny I 535; Vries ANEW 226; Zalizniak I 145; Onions 431; Seebold 251; Lindeman Verschärfung 16.10; Lehmann GED 181; Bammesberger Nom. 65–66; Kluge-Seebold 372.

ON haddr ‘woman’s hair’. Cf. OE heordan ‘hards of flax’, MDu hēde ‘flax fiber, oakum’, OFris hēde ‘oakum’. Related to OIr cass ‘curl’ < *kastos, Lith kasā ‘plait’, OPrus kexti ‘plaited hair’, Slav *kosa id. Zupitza Gutt. 103; Torp-Falk 76; Trautmann BSW 120; Holthausen AEEW 156; Pokorny I 585; Chemodanov SGGJa I 61–62; Fraenkel 226; Vries ANEW 200; Trubachev ESSJa XI 131–133; Kluge-Seebold 362.

Goth hazjan ‘to praise’, OE herian id. Unclear. Fick I 42, 420 (to Skt ≤āsti ‘to order, to direct’, Av sāsti ‘to call, to learn’); Vani‘ek Lat. 68 (to Lat carmen ‘verse, song’); Zupitza Gutt. 184; Torp-Falk 76; Holthausen AEEW 157; Feist 252–253; Pokorny I 530– 531 (reconstructs *kar- ‘to praise’), 533; Vries ANEW 224 (to Skt carkarti ‘to make mention of’ < *ker-); Lehmann GED 181 (against comparisons with *˚ens-).

ON hǫrr ‘flax’, OFris her, har id., OHG har, haro id. Substantivized *xaswaz? Alternatively, a derivative of IE *kes- ‘to comb’: Hitt kišāi- ‘to comb’, Slav *‘esati id. (Kluge-Seebold 345). Hirt BB XXIV 275; Torp-Falk 79 (reconstruct *xarwaz < *xarzwaz); Vries ANEW 281–282.

ON héla ‘hoar frost’. Close to Skt ≤í≤ira- ‘cool, cold’, Lith šešėlis ‘shade, shadow’ reduplicated from IE *˚el- ‘cold’. Cf. *xazlan ~ *xazlaz. Zupitza Gutt. 184; Torp-Falk 84; Mayrhofer III 345–346; Pokorny I 551; Fraenkel 975–976; Fraenkel 976.

OE helan ‘to conceal, to hide’, OFris hela id., OS helan id., OHG helan id. Related to Lat celō ‘to hide, to conceal’, OIr celid ‘to conceal’ (Güntert Kalypso 34–35). Zupitza Gutt. 185; Torp- Falk 80–81; Holthausen AEEW 154; Walde-Hofmann I 198–199; Jóhannesson IEW 242–244; Pokorny I 553; Seebold 252–253; Lehmann GED 174, 193; Kluge-Seebold 363.

ON hildr ‘battle’ (nom. sg. < *xelđiz), OE hild id., OS hildi id., hild id., OHG hilt(i)a id. Related to OIr coll ‘destruction’, MIr ceallach ‘war’, W coll ‘destruction’, Bret koll id. (Stokes BB IX 88). Försterman KZ I 104; Zupitza Gutt. 107; Torp-Falk 82; Holthausen AEEW 160; Lane Language IX 247; Trier PBB LXVI 241; Pokorny I 547; Chemodanov SGGJa I 97; Vries ANEW 226–227; Zalizniak II 213.

Goth hilms ‘helmet’, ON hjálmr id., OE helm id., OFris helm id., OS heliä-helm id., OHG helm id. Close to Skt neut. ≤árman- ‘protection, shelter, cover’ (Bugge BB III 118), Thrac ζαλμός ‘hide’ (Tomaschek Thr. II/1 10). See *xelanan. Marstrander NVA 1925/1 32 (to Lat culmen ‘point, summit, ridge’); Torp-Falk 80; Holthausen AEEW 154; Feist 255; Detschew Thr. 175; Mayrhofer III 310–311; Pokorny I 553; Vries ANEW 230; Zalizniak I 132; Onions 435; Seebold 252; Lehmann GED 183; Kluge- Seebold 369.

OE helma ‘helm, rudder’, MLG helm ‘oar shaft’, OHG joh-halmo ‘yoke rudder’. Related to Lith kélmas ‘tree stump’, OPrus kalmus ‘cane’. Zupitza Gutt. 113, 152; Persson Beiträge 147–149; Torp-Falk 82; Trautmann BSW 126; Holthausen AEEW 154; Pokorny I 546, 924–925; Fraenkel 237; Vries ANEW 230–231 (from *skel-b-); Onions 435.
ON hjálp-lauss ‘helpless’, ME help-less id., OFris helpe-lās id., MHG helfe-lōs id. Compound of *xelpō and *lausaz. Carr Nom. comp. 89 (parallel formations).

Goth hilpan ‘to help’, ON hjálpa id., OE helpan id., OFris helpa id., OS helpan id., OHG helfan id. With unclear differences in consonantism, connected with Lith šelpiū, šefipti id. Cf. also OLith šelbtis (Meillet BSL XXI 126, XXIX 31) which, however, seems to have a secondary -b- (Fraenkel 971).
