Zupitza Gutt. 185; Torp-Falk 85; Trautmann BSW 302; Feist 255–256; Holthausen AEEW 154; Jóhannesson IEW 250; Trier Lehm 56; Pokorny I 554; Chemodanov SGGJa I 76; Vries ANEW 231; Fraenkel 971–972; Zalizniak II 196; Onions 436; Seebold 254–255; Lehmann GED 183; Kluge-Seebold 368.

ON hjǫlp, hjálp ‘help’, OE help id., OFris helpe id., OS helpa id., OHG helfa id. Cf. Burg *hilps id. Derived from *xelpanan. Torp-Falk 85; Holthausen AEEW 154; Pokorny I 554; Vries ANEW 233; Zalizniak II 196; Onions 436; Seebold 254; Bammesberger Nom. 110; Köbler 711; Kluge- Seebold 374.

ON hjalt ‘knob at the end of a sword’s hilt’, OE hilt ‘handle’, OS helta id. (fem.), OHG helza id. (fem.). Connected with *xultan. Torp-Falk 84; Falk NVA 1914/6 24; Holthausen AEEW 160; Pokorny I 547; Vries ANEW 231; Zalizniak II 174; Onions 441.
ON hilla ‘shelf’, MLG hilde ‘manger’. Probably derived from *xelanan. Torp-Falk 81 (to Lith keliū, kélti ‘to lift’); Pokorny I 553–554; Vries ANEW 227.
Goth himina-kunds ‘heavenly’, OE heofon-cund id. Compound of *xemenaz and *kunđaz. Feist 256; Carr Nom. comp. 88 (parallel formations); Lehmann GED 183.
ON himinljómi ‘heavenly light’, OE heofon-leóma id. Compound of *xemenaz and *leuxmōn. Carr Nom. comp. 81 (parallel formations).

ON himin-tungl ‘luminary, heavenly body’, OE heofontunzol id., OS heban-tungal id. Cf. also OHG himil-zungal id. Compound of *xemenaz and *tunzlan. Carr Nom. comp. 50; Zalizniak I 144; Lehmann GED 348.
ON himin-vangr ‘heaven, heavenly field’, OS heban-wang id. Compound of *xemenaz and *wanzaz. Carr Nom. comp. 56.

Goth himins ‘sky, heaven’, ON himinn id., OE heofon id., OS heban id. Cf. also heteroclytic l-forms: OFris himel, himul id., OS himil id., OHG himil id. With a metathesis of the anlaut and modifications of vocalism, continues a thematic derivative of IE *a˚-men- ‘vault of stone, sky’: Skt á≤man- ‘stone, rock, heaven’, Av asman- ‘stone, heaven’, Gk ἄκμων ‘(meteoric) stone, anvil’, ἄκμων: οὐρανός (Hes.), Lith akmuõ ‘stone’, Slav *kamy id. (Reichelt KZ XLVI 343, IF XXXII 23–57). Bartholomae 207– 208; Torp-Falk 73; Lane Language XI 193 (to Latv kamiêsis ‘shoulder’); Fraenkel KZ LXIII 183–184; Holthausen AEEW 156; Feist 256; Prokosch CGG 87 (dissimilation of sonorants in *xemelaz < *xemenaz); Zonderreger An ZDA LXXI 152 (variation of l- and n-suffixes); Benveniste Origines 17 (to Gk καμάρα ‘covered carriage, vaulted chamber’); Porzig Gliederung 75, 190; Mayrhofer I 60–61; Pokorny I 19, 557 (from IE *˚em- ‘to cover’); Fraenkel 5; Vries ANEW 227 (against Reichelt); Zalizniak I 132; Frisk I 54; Onions 433; Schindler Kratylos XV 152; Hamp JL III 83–90; Maher JIES I 441–462 (from the original meaning *‘sharp thing’ > ‘axe’ > ‘thunder’ > ‘sky’); Lehmann GED 183– 184 (related to *xamaz); Kluge-Seebold 374–375.

OHG hemera ‘hellebore’. Identical with Lith pl. kemeraĩ ‘Eupatorium cannabinum L.’, Slav *‘emerъ ‘hellebore’ (Zupitza KZ XXXVII 399). Loewenthal Wu S X 185; Torp-Falk 74; Pokorny I 558; Fraenkel 251–252; Stang LS 16; Trubachev ESSJa IV 52–53.

ON hind ‘hind, roe’ (root stem), OE hind ‘hind’, OS hindi- (in cmpn.), OHG hinta id. From *ken-t- related to Skt kanīna- ‘young’, Gk καινός ‘new’, Lat re-cēns ‘fresh, young, new’. Zupitza Gutt. 207; Torp-Falk 71 (to Gk κεμάς ‘young deer’); Holthausen AEEW 160; Walde-Hofmann I 423–424; Mayrhofer I 151; Pokorny I 556 (to Skt ≤áma- ‘hornless’), 563–564; Vries ANEW 228; Frisk I 754–755; Zalizniak II 208; Onions 441; Kluge-Seebold 375.
ON hinka ‘to limp, to hobble’, OE hincian id. Cf. a secondary strong verb in MLG hinken id., OHG hincan id. Related to *xakōn? Kluge- Seebold 375 (to Gk σκάζω id.).

Goth fra-hinþan ‘to take captive, to capture’, OSwed hinna ‘to gain’. Any connection with Gk κεντέω ‘to prick, to goad’? Torp-Falk 70–71; Holthausen IF XX 326 (to Lat scandō ‘to climb, to rise’); Feist 161; Seebold 255– 256; Lehmann GED 176.

ON hinna ‘film, membrane’, OE hion ‘bone of the head (?)’. Probably derived from *xenþanan. Lidén BB XXI 107 (to OIr ceinn ‘scale’); Zupitza Gutt. 116; Torp-Falk 71; Holthausen AEEW 160; Pokorny I 929; Chemodanov SGGJa I 99; Vries ANEW 228.

Goth hairdeis ‘herdsman, shepherd’, ON hiräir id., OE hierde id., OS hirdi id., OHG hirti id. Derived from *xerđō. Structurally close to Lith keıdžius ‘shepherd’. Trautmann BSW 127–128; Holthausen AEEW 159; Feist 234; Pokorny I 579; Chemodanov SGGJa I 72; Fraenkel 242; Vries ANEW 229; Zalizniak I 137; Onions 437; Benveniste 48; Lehmann GED 170–171; Bammesberger Nom. 67; Kluge-Seebold 377.

Goth hairda ‘herd, flock’, ON hjǫrä id. (partly *xerđiz), OE heord id., OS herda ‘sequence, shift’, OHG herta ‘herd’. Connected with Skt ≤árdha- ‘crowd, might’, Av sar 6 da- ‘type’, Gk κόρθυς ‘heap’, MW cordd ‘crowd’, OPrus kērdan ‘time’, Slav *‘erda ‘series, row, herd’ (Fick KZ XX 167–168). WGmc *xerđōn > OE heoräa ‘deerskin’, OHG herdo ‘fleece’ is derived from *xerđō. Meillet MSL VIII 297, Etudes II 256–257; Zupitza Gutt. 105, 115; Bartholomae 1566–1567; Torp-Falk 78; Trautmann BSW 127–128; Holthausen AEEW 156–157; Feist 234; Lehmann Language XVIII 130 (to *˚er- ‘to grow’); Mayrhofer III 309–310; Pokorny I 579, 941; Vries ANEW 233 (to *ker- ‘to braid’), 255 (to ON hreär ‘scrotum’, Lat cortex ‘bark’); Frisk I 921–922; Zalizniak II 193; Onions 437; Benveniste IEL 48; Trubachev ESSJa IV 60–63; Lehmann GED 170–171; Kluge-Seebold 370.
ON hirsi ‘millet’, OHG hirsi id. Any relation to *xersnōn ~ *xersnan? Torp-Falk 80 (to *˚er- ‘to feed, to grow’); Pokorny I 577.
ON hjarn-skál ‘skull’, OHG hirni-scala id. Compound of *xersnōn ~ *xersnan and *skēlō. Carr Nom. comp. 83 (parallel formations).

ON hjarni ‘brain’, ME herne id., MDu hersene id. Cf. also OHG hirni id. < neut. *xerznjan. Based on a poorly attested masc. *xersōn ‘crown of the head’: ON hjarsi. Closely related to the stem of oblique cases in Skt ≤īrṣṇ- (of ≤íras- ‘head’), cf. also Gk κόρση ‘temple’. Further connected with Lat cerebrum ‘brain’, Gk κέρας ‘head’. Zupitza Gutt. 185; Torp-Falk 76; Walde-Hofmann I 203–204; Mayrhofer III 341; Pokorny I 574–576; Vries ANEW 231–232; Frisk I 826–827, 923 (κόρση from κείρω ‘to cut’); Bammesberger Nom. 67–68; Kluge-Seebold 376–377.

Goth hairto ‘heart’, ON hjarta id., OE heorte id. (fem.), OFris herte, hirte id., MLG herte id., OHG herza id. Related to Hitt kir-, kart- id., Toch A kri ‘will’, B pl. käryañ ‘hearts’, Arm sirt ‘heart’, Gk καρδία id., Lat cor id., OIr cride id., Lith širdīs id., Slav *sьrdьce id., *serda ‘middle’. Zupitza Gutt. 182; Torp-Falk 77; Trautmann BSW 302; Holthausen AEEW 157; Walde-Hofmann I 271–272; Feist 234–235; Pokorny I 579–580; Vries ANEW 232; Frisk I 787–788; Zalizniak II 177; Onions 433; Fraenkel 986–987; Vasmer-Trubachev III 605– 606; Benveniste IEL 143; Szemerényi Scripta 1290–1292; Lehmann GED 171; Bammesberger Nom. 175; Puhvel IV 189–191; Kluge-Seebold 372; Adams TB 165.

OE heorä ‘hearth’, OFris herth, herd id., OS herth id., OHG herd id. Related to Skt kartá- ‘cavity, hole’, Slav *‘ьrta ‘line, edge’ and other derivatives of *(s)ker-t- ‘to cut’ denoting a (boundary) line. The delimiting function of the hearth, or of a log in it, is well attested in other traditions: SCr badnjak and Alb buzëm (Toporov Etim. 1974 3–15; Orel AED 43–44). Torp-Falk 75; Holthausen AEEW 157 (to Lat carbō ‘coal’, cremāre ‘to burn’); Mühlenbach-Endzelin I 375 (to Latv cęri ‘hot stones in a sauna’, Slav *‘ernъ ‘hearth’); Mayrhofer I 173; Pokorny I 941–942; Onions 433; Trubachev ESSJa IV 161, XI 58–60 (to Slav *korda ~ *krada ‘pile of logs’; phonetically impossible); Lehmann GED 180 (to *xurjan ~ *xurjaz).

Goth pl. hairþra ‘intestines, bosom, heart’, OE hreäer ‘breast, bosom’ (masc.?), OHG smala-herdar ‘small intestine, gut’. May be connected with ON hreär ‘scrotum’. Derived from *xerþaz. Saussure MSL VI 246–248 (to *xertōn, with suffix *-tro-); Jóhansson PBB XV 229 (same as Saussure); Meillet Etudes 167 (to *kert- ‘to weave’); Zupitza Gutt. 111; Torp-Falk 77, 102; Mayrhofer I 257; Holthausen AEEW 173; Feist 235; Lehmann GED 171 (from *(s)ker- ‘to cut’).

ON hjǫrtr ‘stag, hart’, OE heorot id., OFris hert id., OS hirot id., OHG hiruz id. Derivationally identical with Gk κορυδός ‘crested lark’ < *˚orudos. Further related to *˚er(u)- ‘horn’ (Försterman KZ I 497), see *xurnan. Zupitza Gutt. 116; Torp-Falk 76; Holthausen AEEW 156; Pokorny I 576; Chemodanov SSGJa I 58; Vries ANEW 234; Frisk I 924; Zalizniak II 200; Onions 429; Kluge-Seebold 377.

Goth hairus ‘sword’, ON hjǫrr id., OE heoru id., OS heru- (in cmpn.). Related to MIr coire id. Luft KZ XXXVI 145 (from *(s)ker- ‘to cut’); Zupitza Gutt. 182; Torp-Falk 75; Holthausen AEEW 157; Feist 235; Pokorny I 939–940 (to Lith kiıvis ‘axe’); Vries ANEW 234; Zalizniak I 152; Lehmann GED 171; Bammesberger Nom. 156.

ON hjarn ‘hard frozen snow’. Close to Arm safin ‘ice’, Slav *sernъ ‘layer of ice, hard snow’, Lith šeıkšnas ‘hoar-frost’, Latv sērns, sērksnis, sę¯rsna id. (Schulze Kl. Schr. 112–114). Zupitza Gutt. 185; Torp-Falk 78; Trautmann BSW 303; Krogmann KZ LXIV 133– 134; Holthausen ANW 116; Pokorny I 573; Fraenkel 973–974; Vries ANEW 231; Vasmer-Trubachev III 608.

ON hjarri ‘hinge’, OE heorra ‘bar, hinge’. Might be related to Lith skeısas ‘across’, Slav *‘erzъ ‘through, across’ (secondary development of *‘ersъ) < *(s)kert-s-o. Zupitza Gutt. 113; Torp- Falk 80; Falk MM 1910 12 (to Lat cardō ‘door-hinge’); Trautmann BSW 129–130; Holthausen AEEW 156; Trier Holz 73–74; Jóhannesson IEW 830; Vries ANEW 232; Vasmer-Trubachev IV 337–338.

Goth hiufan ‘to lament, to wail’, OS pres. hioban id., OHG pres. hiufit, hiufit id. Of unknown origin. Weinhold KZ I 251; Grienberger Got. 113; Zupitza Gutt. 117; Torp-Falk 95; Bezzenberger BB XVI 120 (to Lith šaukiū, ša Ukti ‘to shout, to call’); Torp- Falk 95; Feist 258 (to *keu- ‘to howl’); Holthausen AEEW 155; Jóhannesson IEW 177–178; Pokorny I 535–536; Seebold 256 (to Skt káuti ‘to cry’, Gk κωκύω id.); Lehmann GED 185.
ON hjúfra ‘to pant, to heave’, G dial. hīvern ‘to lament’. Derived from *xeufanan. Mohr ZDADL LXXV 252 (ON < WGmc); Vries ANEW 233.

Norw dial. hjūpa ‘briar’, OE héopa ‘briar’, OS hiopo ‘briar’, OHG hiufo id. Related to *xaupaz (Torp-Falk 95) repeating the semantic ratio between OPrus kāaubri ‘thorn’ and Lith kaubr E ‘hill’ (Schmalstieg 330). Zupitza Gutt. 185 (to Slav *šipъ ‘thorn’); Trautmann BSW 349 (to OPrus kaāubri ‘thorn’); Holthausen AEEW 156; Pokorny I 595; Onions 441; Stang LS 27.

ON hÿr-ligr ‘smiling, sweet’, OE un-hír-líc ‘fierce, savage, dismal’, OS sbst. un-hiur-līk ‘monster’, OHG sbst. un-hiur-līh id. Derived from *xeurjaz. Torp-Falk 88; Feist 517; Holthausen AEEW 159; Kluge 252 (from *xīw-ra-); Pokorny I 539–540; Heidermanns 291.

ON hÿrr ‘sweet, smiling, mild’, OE hÿre ‘pleasant, gentle, mild’, MDu on-ghehiere ‘terrible’, OS un-hiuri ‘eery, uncanny’, OHG hiuri ‘simple’. Connected with *xīwan. Torp-Falk 88; Holthausen AEEW 159; Vries ANEW 276; Lehmann GED 376; Heidermanns 290–291; Kluge-Seebold 307.

Goth hiwi ‘form, appearance’, ON hÿ ‘down (of plants or hair)’, OE hiw ‘shape, form, appearance’. Probably derived from *xawwanan. Grienberger Got. 114 (to Skt ≤yāvá- ‘brown, dark-brown’, MIr céo ‘fog’); ZubatŸ KZ XXXI 12 (to Skt skunāti ‘to cover’); Zupitza Gutt. 207; Torp-Falk 88–89 (same as Grienberger); Holthausen AEEW 159; Feist 259; Pipping Xenia 165; Vries ANEW 274 (to Skt ≤yāvá- ‘brown, dark-brown’, Slav *sivъ ‘grey’); Onions 451; Lindeman Verschärfung 16.11; Lehmann GED 185; Hilmarsson Mat. 207– 208 (to Toch B kwele ‘black’ < *kiwolo-).
ON háfr ‘pock-net for herring fishing’, Du haaf ‘net’. Derived from *xafjanan. Vries ANEW 201.

ON hæfr ‘fit, proper’, OE be-héfe ‘necessary’. Derived from *xafjanan. Holthausen AEEW 167–168; Vries ANEW 276; Seebold 245; Matzel Festschr. Schröder 104–105, KZ CV 115–116, 119; Heidermanns 287–288.

ON há ‘second hay-crop, aftermath’. Identical with Skt neut. ≤āka- ‘pot-herb, vegetable’, Lith šėkas ‘freshly mown hay’. Torp-Falk 68; Trautmann BSW 301; Mayrhofer III 320–321; Pokorny I 544; Fraenkel 970–971; Vries ANEW 199.

ON háll ‘slippery’, OE hálstán ‘crystal’, OHG hāli ‘smooth’. Probably related to Lith šālū, šálti ‘to freeze’, Skt ≤í≤ira- ‘cool, cold’. Karsten NB II 380; Zupitza Gutt. 184; Torp-Falk 83–84; Holthausen AEEW 145; Mayrhofer III 345–346; Pokorny I 551; Fraenkel 960–961; Vries ANEW 205; Lerchner 94; Matzel KC CV 110; Heidermanns 289.
ON háll ‘cunning’, MLG hāl ‘hidden, secret’. Derived from *xelanan (unless historically identical with *xēlaz I). Torp-Falk 81 (to Lat celō ‘to hide, to conceal’); Vries ANEW 205 (to *xaljō).

Goth her ‘here’, ON hér id., OE hér id., OFris hīr id., OS hēr id., OHG hiar id. Continues *˚ēi-r- (Jellinek PBB XV 298–300) further related to *xi. Sievers PBB XVI 238–240, XVIII 403; Torp- Falk 63, 87; Brugmann IF VI 89–90; Holthausen AEEW 157; Kluge Urgerm. 123–124; Feist 254; Pokorny I 609; Vries ANEW 222–223; Onions 437; Ringe Sprache XXX 138–150 (lowering of *ī to *ē); Lehmann Phon. 72–73, GED 182; Bammesberger Nom. 259; Kluge- Seebold 374.

ON hár ‘hair’, OE hær id., OFris hēr id., OS hār id., OHG hār id. Related to Lith šer‹s ‘bristle’, Latv sars id. with a different vowel grade. Detter ZDADL XLII 55 (to IE *sker- ‘to cut’); Torp-Falk 75; Holthausen AEEW 145; Pokorny I 583; Fraenkel 973; Vries ANEW 210; Zalizniak I 141; Onions 423; Adams JIES XVI 69–93; Bammesberger Nom. 96; Kluge-Seebold 345.
ON hæra ‘hair, grey hair’, OE hære ‘hair-cloth’. Derived from *xēran. Holthausen AEEW 145; Vries ANEW 277.

Goth dat. himma ‘this’, OE hé ‘he’, OS he, hē id., OHG hē id. Note neut. forms: Goth acc. hita ‘it’, ON hitt, OE hit id., OFris hit, het id., MDu het id. Continues IE demonstrative *˚ei-, cf. Alb si-vjet ‘this year’, Lith šīs ‘this’, Slav *sь id. Further cf. Hitt ka- ‘this (one)’. Torp- Falk 63 (< *xe), 87; Trautmann BSW 304; Holthausen AEEW 150, 161; Feist 255, 258; Pokorny I 539–540, 609–610; Fraenkel 990; Onions 488; Vasmer- Trubachev III 591–592; Lehmann GED 181–182; Puhvel IV 3–12; Orel AED 397.

Goth hidre ‘hither’, ON héära ‘here, hither’, OE hider ‘hither’. Derived from *xi. Structurally similar to Lat cītrā ‘on this side’, (ultro et) cītrō ‘(here and) there’. Torp-Falk 87; Walde- Hofmann I 222 (no historical connection between Gmc and Lat); Holthausen AEEW 158; Feist 255; Vries ANEW 215; Onions 442.
ON skjór ‘magpie’, OE hizere id. (< *xizurō), OHG hehara ‘jay’. Of imitative origin. Torp-Falk 89 (to Gk κίσσα ‘jay, Garrulus glandarius’).

Goth hindana ‘beyond’, OE hindan ‘from behind’, OS hindan ‘from beyond’, OHG hintana ‘behind’. A secondary locative. Based on *xinđ- derived from *xi (Streitberg GE 132; Brugmann Dem. 144). Kögel PBB XVI 514; Wiedemann BB XXVII 199 (to Skt kanīna- ‘young’); Zupitza Gutt. 116; Holthausen AEEW 160; Feist 256; Tischler HEG I 539–542 (to Hitt katta(n) ‘down, under’); Vries ANEW 228; Onions 441; Lehmann GED 184; Kluge- Seebold 375.

Goth hindar ‘behind, beyond, over to, the other side of’, ON compar. hindri ‘hinder, hindmost’, OE hinder ‘back, behind, down’, OFris hindera ‘beyond’, OS hindiro id., OHG hintar ‘behind’, adj. hintaro. An old comparative of *xin-, a derivative of *xi. Torp-Falk 87; Holthausen AEEW 160; Feist 256–257 (comparative form of *xinđ-); Pokorny I 609; Vries ANEW 228; Onions 441; Lehmann GED 184; Kluge- Seebold 375–376.

ON hindra ‘to hinder, to keep behind’, OE hindrian id., OFris hinderia id., MLG hinderen id., OHG hintarōn id. Derived from *xinđr(ōn). Torp-Falk 87; Holthausen AEEW 160; Vries ANEW 228; Onions 441; Kluge- Seebold 375.

Goth heito ‘fever’, ON hita ‘heating’, OE hitt id., OHG hizza id. Connected with *xaitaz. van Helten PBB XXX 241 (to Slav *sьjati ‘to shine’); Zupitza Gutt. 112; Torp-Falk 89; Holthausen AEEW 161; Feist 253; Pokorny I 519; Vries ANEW 229; Zalizniak II 216; Onions 433; Lehmann GED 181; Kluge-Seebold 377.

Goth heiwa-frauja ‘master of the family’, ON pl. hjón, hjún ‘man and wife’, OE pl. híwan ‘members of a household’, OFris pl. hīuna id., OS hīwa ‘spouse, family member’, OHG pl. hīwun id. Close to Skt ≤éva- ‘dear, intimate’, ≤ivá- ‘kind, friendly, dear’, Lat cīuis ‘citizen’, Latv si‚va ‘wife’. Tucker Notes 37; Torp-Falk 88; Holthausen AEEW 161; Walde-Hofmann I 224–225; Feist 253– 254; Mayrhofer III 344, 376; Pokorny I 540; Chemodanov SGGJa I 65; Vries ANEW 233; Zalizniak II 177; Schulze Kl. Schr. 201; Onions 440; Benveniste IEL 273–275; Lehmann GED 181–182; Bammesberger Nom. 69; Kluge-Seebold 366.
ON hÿski ‘family, household’, OE híwisc id., OS hīwiski ‘family’, OHG hīwisci id. Derived from *xīwan. Torp-Falk 88; Holthausen AEEW 161; Vries ANEW 276.
ON hlaä ‘pile, stack, barn’, OE hlæd ‘heap, pile’. Derived from *xlaþanan. Torp-Falk 110; Holthausen AEEW 161; Vries ANEW 234; Seebold 259.

Goth hlahjan ‘to laugh’, ON hlæja id., OE hlehhan id., OS pret. hlōg id., OHG hloc ‘adrisit’. A descriptive stem formally close to Slav *klekotъ ‘laughter, noise’, MIr clocc ‘bell’, Lat glōciō ‘to cluck’. Cf. in particular Gk κλώσσω id. Zupitza Gutt. 117, 191; Torp-Falk 110; Feist 259; Holthausen AEEW 163; Walde-Hofmann I 606; Jóhannesson IEW 277–278; Pokorny I 600; Vries ANEW 241; Frisk I 879; Onions 518; Seebold 257–258; Trubachev ESSJa IX 191–192; Lehmann GED 185 (to IE *kel- ‘to shout, to call’); Kluge-Seebold 498.
ON hlátr ‘laughter’, OE hleahtor id., OHG neut. hlahtar id. Derived from *xlaxjanan. Torp-Falk 110; Holthausen AEEW 162; Pokorny I 600; Vries ANEW 235; Zalizniak II 200; Seebold 258.

Goth hlaifs ‘bread’, ON hleifr ‘loaf’, OE hláf id., OFris hlēf id., OHG leib, hleib id. Derived from *xlībjanan (Kern TNTL V 55). Zupitza BB XXV 94 (to OIr cliab ‘basket’); Schrader Festschr. Sievers 5–11 (to Gk κλίβανος ‘covered earthen vessel’); Pedersen IF V 50, KZ XXXVIII 393–394 (related to Slav *xlîbъ ‘bread’ which, however, is from Gmc); Persson Beiträge 303 (to Lat lībō ‘to take out as a sample, to take a little of’, Gk λοιβή ‘pouring, drink-offering’); Torp-Falk 109; Paschall JEGP VIII 18–24 (same as Zupitza); Schrader- Nehring II 245; Mohl MSL VII 403 (from Uralic); Kluyver TNTL VIII 254– 257 (from an unknown source); Kozlovskij Afsl Ph XI 386 (same as Pedersen); Grienberger Got. 114 (same as Schrader); Pedersen IF V 50 (to Lat lībum ‘flan, cake’); Lidén PBB XV 514–515 (same as Pedersen); Stender-Pedersen Slav.-germ. 297–299 (Slav < Gmc); Holthausen AEEW 162; Feist 260; Trier Lehm 59 (to IE *˚lei- ‘to lean, to bend’); Vries ANEW 236; Zalizniak I 132; Onions 533; Lehmann GED 185–186; Bammesberger Nom. 52; Kluge-Seebold 499–500.
Goth ga-hlaiba ‘companion’, OHG gi-leibo id. Derived from *xlaibaz. Calqued by Rom *com-pāniō id. (Brüch 81). Velten JEGP XXIX 345 (calque from Rom); Feist 183; Lehmann GED 139.

OE hlæder ‘ladder’, OFris hlēdere, hladder id., MLG ledder id., OHG leitara, leitar id. Derived from IE *˚lei-, cf. *xlinōjanan. Structurally close to MIr clithar ‘hedge’ and similar other derivatives (Zupitza KZ XXXV 259, Gutt. 186), see *xlīþrō. Torp-Falk 112; Holthausen AEEW 161; Porzig Gliederung 124; Pokorny I 601; Onions 511; Kluge-Seebold 514.

Goth masc. or neut. hlain(s) ‘hill’, Norw dial. fem. hlein ‘slope’. Etymologically close to OIr clóen ‘crooked, unjust’, Lith šlainūs ‘crooked’. Further related to *xlinōjanan. Zupitza Gutt. 186; Torp-Falk 111; Feist 260–261; Pokorny I 601; Fraenkel 997; Lehmann GED 186.

OE hlæne ‘lean, meagre’, LG leen id., early G lehne ‘gently sloping’. Derived from *xlinōjanan. Structurally close to Lith šlainūs ‘sloping, oblique’, Latv slains ‘swaying’, OIr clóen ‘crooked, unjust’. Holthausen AEEW 161; Onions 520; Stang LS 29; Pokorny I 601; Fraenkel 997; Heidermanns 291–292.

Goth hlaiw ‘tomb, grave’, Run hlaiwa id., OE hláw, hlæw ‘rising ground, a funeral mound’ (< *xlaiwiz), OS hlēo ‘grave, burial mound’ (masc.), OHG (h)lēo id. (masc.). Closely connected with Lat clīuus ‘mound’ < *˚lei⁄os, Lith šleĩvas ‘with crooked legs, crooked’ (Solmsen KZ XXXVIII 453). Further see *xlinōjanan. Sütterlin IF XXV 72; Zupitza Gutt. 186; Torp-Falk 112; Holthausen AEEW 162; Walde-Hofmann I 237; Feist 261; Mladenov 669 (to Slav *xlîvъ ‘sty, pen’); Krahe Su V 75; Trier Lehm 54; Porzig Gliederung 109; Pokorny I 601; Chemodanov SGGJa I 90; Fraenkel 1001–1002; Vries ANEW 234; Zalizniak II 187; Onions 519; Lehmann GED 186; Bammesberger Nom. 69.

ON hlakka ‘to cry, to scream’, OFris hlakkia ‘to laugh’. Of imitative origin. Formally close to Gk κλάζω ‘to scream, to barek’ < *klaggjv. Cf. also *xlaxjanan. Zupitza Gutt. 118, 162; Torp-Falk 110 (reconstruct *xlank-, cf. Lat clangō ‘to clang, to sound’); Pokorny I 599–600; Vries ANEW 235; Frisk I 863–864; Lehmann GED 185.
ON hlemmr ‘lid, cover’, OE úht-hlem ‘din made in the early morning’, OFris hlem ‘blow’. Derived from *xlemmanan. Torp-Falk 111; Krahe Su V 78; Vries ANEW 237; Seebold 264; Lehmann GED 186.
ON hlamma ‘to give a dull, heavy sound’, OS hlamōn ‘to rush’. Derived from *xlammiz. Martinet Gém. 142, 144; Chemodanov SGGJa I 91 (to Lat clāmō ‘to shout’); Seebold 264.
ON hland ‘urine’, OE hland id. Unclear. Zupitza Gutt. 118; Torp-Falk 111 (to Lith klãnas ‘puddle’); Walde-Pokorny I 500; Holthausen AEEW 162; Chemodanov SGGJa I 77; Vries ANEW 235.
ON hlekkr ‘link, chain of links’, OE hlenca ‘link’, hlence id., OS hlanka ‘hip, thigh’, OHG hlanca ‘hip’. Related to *xlankjanan. Torp-Falk 110; Holthausen AEEW 162; Onions 530; Kluge-Seebold 310.

ON hlekkjask ‘to get impeded, to suffer miscarriage’, OE ze-hlencan ‘to roll, to wrap’, MLG lenken ‘to bend’, MHG lenken id., OHG ir-lenken ‘to dislocate (a limb)’, ūz-lenken ‘to unbuckle’. Implies an unattested str. vb. *xlenkanan related to Lat clingō ‘to shut’, Slav *klęknǫti ‘to squat, to kneel’ (Johansson PBB XIV 298). Holthausen AEEW 162; Walde-Hofmann I 233–234; Pokorny I 603; Vries ANEW 237; Trubachev ESSJa X 33–34; Kluge- Seebold 310 (to Lith lenkiū, leñkti ‘to bend’); Adams TB 222 (to Toch AB klänk- ‘to doubt’).

Goth af-hlaþan ‘to burden’, ON hlaäa ‘to load’, OE hladan ‘to heap, to pile up’, OFris part. hleden ‘to load’, OS pres. hladan id., OHG hladan id. Related to Lith klóti ‘to spread out’ and Slav *kladǫ, *klasti ‘to put’ (Lottner KZ XI 179; Scherer GBS 21). Torp-Falk 110; Trautmann BSW 135–136; Feist 6; Holthausen AEEW 161; Jóhannesson IEW 273–274; Pokorny I 599; Vries ANEW 234; Fraenkel 274–275; Onions 511; Stang LS 29–30; Seebold 258–259; Trubachev ESSJa IX 187–188; Lehmann GED 5; Kluge-Seebold 499.
ON hlaäa ‘store-house, barn’, MDu lade ‘footlocker’, MHG lade id. Derived from *xlaþanan. Kluge- Seebold 498.

ON hlaunn ‘buttock, haunch’. Identical with Skt ≤roṇi- ‘hip, loin, buttock’, Av sraoni- ‘buttock’, Lith šlaunīs ‘hip’. Cf. also Lat clūnis ‘buttock’, W clun id. Petersson IF XXXV 269– 270; Zupitza Gutt. 186; Bartholomae 1633; Pedersen Kelt. Gr. I 121; Trautmann BSW 306; Torp-Falk 109; Walde- Hofmann I 239; Mayrhofer III 395; Pokorny I 607; Fraenkel 1000; Vries ANEW 235.
ON hlaup ‘leap’, MLG lōp ‘jump’, OHG louf ‘run’. Derived from *xlaupanan. Torp- Falk 110; Seebold 260.

Goth us-hlaupan ‘to leap forth, to leap up’, ON hlaupa ‘to leap, to jump’, OE hleápan id., OFris hlāpa ‘to run, to hasten’, OS pret. uz-hliopun id., OHG hloufan id. Related to Lith šlumbū, šlūbti ‘to become lame’, klumbū, klūbti ‘to stumble’ (with depalatalized *˚-), Latv slubît ‘to limp’ (Hoffmann Festschr. Fick 51). Zupitza Gutt. 118119, 145; Torp- Falk 109–110; Feist 532–533, PBB XXXII 452–453; Holthausen AEEW 162; Specht 130–131; Trier Lehm 58; Jóhannesson IEW 262–263; Pokorny I 630; Mattausch KZ LXXVII 137–139; Vries ANEW 235; Fraenkel 276, 1009; Onions 520; Mottausch KZ LXXVII 129–139; Seebold 259–261 (to Gk κέλευθος ‘way, course’, Lith keliáuti ‘to travel, to wander’); Knobloch Wu SBez. 50–51 (to Lith klūpti); Lehmann GED 382; Kluge-Seebold 506.

Goth hlauts ‘lot, inheritance’, ON hlautr ‘lot’, OS hlōt ‘lot, property’, OHG lōz, hlōz id. Cf. *xlutiz. Derived from *xleutanan. Zupitza Gutt. 119; Torp-Falk 113; Holthausen AEEW 163; Feist 262; Kluge 445–446; Pokorny I 605; Vries ANEW 235; Zalizniak II 200; Seebold 265; Lehmann GED 187; Bammesberger Nom. 52; Kluge-Seebold 525.

Goth hlifan ‘to steal’. Close to Toch B kälyp- id., Gk κλέπτω id., Lat clepō id., OPrus au-klipts ‘concealed’ (Schulze KZ XLIX 252). Zupitza Gutt. 118; Torp-Falk 111; Walde-Hofmann I 232; Feist 263–264; Pokorny I 604; Frisk I 870–871; Seebold 261; Lehmann GED 187; Adams TB 175–176.

ON pret. hlam ‘to clink’, OE hlimman ‘to sound, to roar’, OHG pres. limmit ‘infremuit’. Related to Lat clamō ‘to call’, Lith šlamū, šlamėti ‘to chirp’. Zupitza Gutt. 118; Torp-Falk 111; Berneker I 526 (to Slav *klьnǫ, *klêti ‘to damn’); Holthausen AEEW 162–164; Walde-Hofmann I 227; Jóhannesson IEW 247–249; Pokorny I 548–550; Fraenkel 998–999; Vries ANEW 238; Seebold 263–264.

Goth hliuma ‘hearing’, ON hljómr ‘sound, tune, voice’. Identical with Av sraoman ‘hearing’ < *˚leu-men- further related to IE *˚leu- ‘to hear’. Försterman KZ I 8; Knoblauch KZ I 573; Zupitza Gutt. 186; Bartholomae 1634; Torp-Falk 112; Pokorny I 605–607; Lehmann GED 188; Kluge-Seebold 516.

OHG hliumunt ‘testimony, glory’. Structurally close to Toch A klyom, B klyomo ‘noble’ < *˚leumōnt- and Skt ≤romata- ‘good reputation’. Further see *xleumōn ~ *xleumaz. Müller KZ XIX 47 (to Lat crīmen ‘verdict, judgment’); Torp-Falk 112; Mayrhofer III 373; Pokorny I 605–607; Vries ANEW 238; Kluge-Seebold 516; Adams TB 231.

ON hljóta ‘to get by lot, to get, to undergo, to suffer’, OE hleótan ‘to cast lots’, OS hliotan id., OHG hliozan id. Related to Lith kliuda U, kliudÿti ‘to make hang on, to push’, kliauda U, klaudÿti ‘to prevent, to be in the way’. Further connected with Lith kliūvū, kliūti ‘to hang on’, Latv Îkl Ut ‘to become’, Îklūtas ‘fate’. Schrader KZ XXX 495 (to Gk κλάδος ‘branch’); Wood MP V 274 (same as Schrader); Zupitza Gutt. 119; Grienberger Got. 115; Torp-Falk 113; Holthausen AEEW 162; Feist 262; Jóhannesson IEW 275–276; Pokorny I 604–605; Fraenkel 274; Vries ANEW 238–239; Onions 537; Seebold 264– 265; Stang LS 29; Lehmann GED 186; Kluge-Seebold 525.

Goth hliuþ ‘hearing (?), silence (?)’ (hap. leg.), ON hljóä ‘hearing’. Continues a derivative in *-to- of IE *˚leu- ‘to hear’. Structurally close to Av sraota- ‘hearing’. Bartholomae 1633; Torp-Falk 112; Feist 264; Petersen Language XV 89–90 (on Toch A klots, B klautso ‘ear’); Porzig Gliederung 180; Pokorny I 605–606; Vries ANEW 238; Lehmann GED 180; Bammesberger Nom. 79–80; Heidermanns 294.

Icel hljóäur ‘tone’, OE hleóäor ‘hearing, noise, sound’, OHG hliodar-sāz ‘magic’. Identical with Skt neut. ≤rotra- ‘ear’, Av sraoyra- ‘hearing’ < *˚leutro- further related to *˚leu- ‘to hear’. See *xleuþan. Bartholomae 1633; Torp- Falk 112; Holthausen AEEW 162; Mayrhofer III 373; Pokorny I 605– 607; Vries ANEW 238; Lehmann GED 188; Bammesberger Nom. 86.

ON hlÿr ‘cheek’, OE hleór id., OS hleor id. Close to Av sraoša- ‘hearing’, OIr clúas ‘ear’, Slav *sluxъ ‘hearing’. See *xleuþan. Torp-Falk 113; Holthausen AEEW 162; Bugge ANF II 216; Pokorny I 606; Chemodanov SGGJa I 99; Vries ANEW 241.

Run prop. hlewa-gastiz. Related to the Indo-European word for ‘glory’: Toch A klyu, B kälywe ‘glory’, Skt ≤rávas- ‘glory, praise, renown’, Av sravah- ‘word’, Gk κλέος ‘glory’, OIr clú id., Slav *slovo ‘word’. Bartholomae 1643– 1644; Mayrhofer III 389; Frisk I 869–870; Lindeman Verschärfung 16.14; Lehmann GED 188.

ON hléär ‘famous’. Identical with Gk κλειτός id. < *˚le⁄eto-. It is possible to compare this word with Slav sbst. *kleveta ‘calumny’ without assibilation of *˚-. Further see *xlewaz. Falk Festschr. Bugge Kr. 17; Pokorny I 605–606; Vries ANEW 236; Frisk I 877–878; Trubachev ESSJa X 14–17; Heidermanns 296.
ON hlána ‘to thaw’, G dial. läunen id. Derived from *xlēwaz. Torp-Falk 109; Vries ANEW 235 (to *xliwan).

ON hlær ‘warm, mild’ (secondary ja-stem), MDu laeu, lau id., OHG lāo id. Probably to Gk κλύζω ‘to rinse, to cleanse’. See *xlūtraz. Schmidt Vok. II 454; Karsten NB II 421–422; Torp- Falk 109 (to Lat caleō ‘to be warm’); Walde-Hofmann I 239–240; Pokorny I 552, 607; Vries ANEW 241; Frisk I 876–877; Onions 538; Darms Vṛddhi 54–60; Heidermanns 294–295 (to *xliwan); Kluge-Seebold 505.

ON hliä ‘gate, gateway’, OE hlid ‘lid, cover’, OFris hlid id., OS hlid id., OHG ubar-lit id. Derived from *xlīđanan. Identical with Skt ≤ritá- ‘adhering to smth., situated’, Gk ἄ-κλίτος ‘stable’, Lith πά-šλιτασ ‘curved’ continuing derivatives in *-to- based on IE *˚lei- ‘to lean’. Zupitza Gutt. 119; Torp-Falk 111; Holthausen AEEW 163; Mayrhofer III 388–389; Pokorny I 601–602; Vries ANEW 237–238; Frisk I 873–875; Zalizniak I 141; Onions 526–527; Seebold 262; Bammesberger Nom. 56; Kluge-Seebold 518.
OHG hlina ‘back (of a chair), lattice’. Close to Gk κλίνη ‘couch, bier’. Further related to *xlinōjanan. Pokorny I 601; Frisk I 876–877; Kluge-Seebold 510.

Norw dial. lina ‘to lean’, OE hlinian id., OFris lena id., OS hlinōn id., OHG hlinēn id. See *xlinō. Derivationally close to Gk klÃinv ‘to bend’, Lat clīnō in clīnātus ‘inclined, bent, sunk’, Lith šlienū, šli‚ti ‘to lean’. Persson Beiträge 915; Torp-Falk 111; Trautmann BSW 308; Walde-Hofmann I 234–236; Pokorny I 601; Fraenkel 1004; Frisk I 873–875; Onions 520; Lehmann GED 186; Kluge-Seebold 510–511; Adams TB 223–224 (to Toch AB klin- ‘to be necessary’).

Goth masc. hlija ‘booth, tent’ (leg. *hliwa), ON hlé ‘lee’, OE hléow ‘shelter, protection’, OFris hlī id., OS hleo ‘shelter from bad weather’ (masc.). Continues IE *˚lei-, cf. *xlīþrō. Zupitza Gutt. 119; Grienberger Got. 117; Uhlenbeck PBB XXX 291 (to *˚lei- ‘to bend’); Torp- Falk 109 (to *xlēwaz); Holthausen AEEW 163; Feist 263–264; Vries ANEW 235–236 (to W clyd ‘warm’); Onions 522; Lehmann GED 187–188; Kluge-Seebold 509.
ON hlÿäa ‘shed, a part of the ship’, OE hlíwä ‘protection, shelter’. Derived from *xliwan, *xliwjanan. Holthausen AEEW 163; Vries ANEW 240.
ON hlÿja ‘to cover, to shelter’, OE hlÿwan ‘to make warm, to protect’. Derived from *xliwan. Torp- Falk 109; Holthausen AEEW 163; Vries ANEW 240.
OHG līban ‘to protect, to care’. Continues IE *˚lei- ‘to bend’, cf. *xliwan, *xlīþrō. Torp-Falk 112; Seebold 261–262.

Goth hleibjan ‘to shield, to protect’, ON hlífa ‘to give cover or shelter’, OHG līben ‘to protect, to care’. Derived from *xlībanan. Aufrecht KZ I 311 (to Lat clipeus ‘schield’); Froehde BB XXI 329 (follows Aufrecht); Uhlenbeck PBB XXX 291 (to Slav *slîpъ ‘blind’); Torp-Falk 112; Feist 262; Trier Lehm 56; Jóhannesson IEW 242–244; Vries ANEW 238; Seebold 261; Lehmann GED 187.
ON hlíf ‘cover, shelter, protection’, OHG lība ‘calming’. Derived from *xlībanan. Zupitza BB XXV 94 (to MIr cliab ‘basket’); Loewenthal Wu S IX 176; Trier Lehm 56; Vries ANEW 238; Seebold 262.

OE tó-hlídan ‘to yawn, to gape, to open’, OFris hlīda ‘to cover’, OS bi-hlīdan ‘to lock, to cover’. See *xlībanan. Zupitza Gutt. 119; Holthausen AEEW 163; Pokorny I 600–602; Seebold 262–263; Kluge-Seebold 518.

ON hlíä ‘slope’, OE neut. hlíä id., MLG līt, līte id., OHG līta id. To *xlīđanan. Structurally close to MIr clíath ‘hurdle’. Torp-Falk 111; Holthausen AEEW 164; Vries ANEW 237; Lehmann GED 187; Kluge-Seebold 514.

Goth hleiþra ‘tent’. Identical with Umbr kletram ‘litter’ < *klītrā, Lat dim. clītellae ‘pack-saddle’ based on *clītra. Cf. also MIr clithar ‘fence’. Meringer IF XVI 210 (on semantic development); Zupitza KZ XXXV 259; Torp-Falk 112; Feist 263; Porzig Gliederung 124; Pokorny I 601; Chemodanov SGGJa I 102; Lehmann GED 187; Kluge-Seebold 514.

ON hlóa ‘to bellow, to roar’, OE hlówan id., OLFrank luoian ‘to low, to moo’, OHG hluoen ‘to roar’. Probably distantly related to Lat clāmō ‘to shout’. Zupitza Gutt. 118; Torp-Falk 83; Holthausen AEEW 164; Walde- Hofmann I 227; Onions 538.

Norw dial. and-løg ‘inclined to laugh’, OHG ant-hluogi ‘merry’. Back formation of *xlōzjanan ~ *xlōxjanan. Further derived from *xlaxjanan. Karsten NB I 117; Matzel Kratylos XXXIV 137, KZ CV 122–123; Heidermanns 296.
Goth uf-hlohjan ‘to make laugh’, ON hlœgja id. Causative of *xlaxjanan. Torp-Falk 110; Feist 512; Vries ANEW 241; Seebold 258.

OHG prop. Hlud-wīg. From IE *˚lutó- ‘famous’: Skt ≤rutá- ‘heard, famous’, Av sruta- ‘heard’, Gk κλυτός, Arm lu ‘known’. Lat in-clutus ‘famous’. Bartholomae 1648; Walde-Hofmann I 690–691; Mayrhofer III 372–374; Pokorny I 605–607; Frisk I 877–878.

ON hlynr ‘maple tree’, OE hlyn id., LG löne, läne id. Probably connected with Maced κλινό-troxon ‘a kind of maple’ (Theothrastes), Slav *klenъ ~ *klьnъ ‘maple’, W kelyn id. Schröder IF XVII 316; Pedersen Kelt. Gr. I 378; Vasmer NB XXI 126 (to Gk Λοῦνα ὕλη ‘small Carpathian mountains’); Torp- Falk 113; Holthausen AEEW 165; Stang LS 28–29; Specht 60 (to OHG līn-boum ‘maple’); Pokorny I 603; Vries ANEW 240–241; Trubachev ESSJa IX 194–195.

ON hlust ‘ear, the inner part of the ear’, OE hlyst ‘hearing’ (fem., masc.), OS hlust ‘hearing, ear’. Structurally similar to Skt ≤rußtí- ‘obedience’, OIr cluas ‘ear’ < *˚loustā. Derived from *xluzan. Zupitza Gutt. 186; Torp-Falk 113; Holthausen AEEW 165; Mayrhofer III 394; Pokorny I 606; Vries ANEW 240; Zalizniak II 228; Onions 531; Bammesberger KZ LXXXII 298– 303; Kluge-Seebold 526.

ON hlutr ‘lot, share’ (partly < *xlutuz), OE hlyt ‘share’, OFris neut. hlot ‘lot’, OHG hluz id. Cf. also *xlutōn ~ *xlutan. Derived from *xleutanan. Schrader KZ X 475 (to Gk κλάδος ‘branch’); Torp-Falk 113; Holthausen AEEW 165; Pokorny I 605; Vries ANEW 240; Zalizniak II 200; Onions 537; Seebold 265; Bammesberger Nom. 133.
ON hluta ‘to draw lots’, MLG loten, lotten id. Derived from *xlutiz. Vries ANEW 240.
ON hluti ‘part, share’, OE hlot ‘lot’, OFris hlot id., MLG lot id., OHG hluz id. Derived from *xleutanan. Holthausen AEEW 164; Seebold 265.

ON hler ‘shutter, door’, masc. hleri id. The phrase standa á hleri ‘to stand eavesdropping’ may preserve the original meaning of the word rather than be a metaphor. Related to IE *˚leu-s-: Tokh A klyoß-, B klyauß- ‘to hear’, Av sraoša- ‘hearing’, OIr sluas ‘ear’. Bartholomae 1634–1636; Pokorny I 606.

OE hlúd ‘loud’, OFris hlūd id., OS hlūd id., OHG lūt id. Stands close to adj. *˚lutós derived from *˚leu- ‘to hear’, cf. Skt ≤rutá- ‘heard, famous’, Gk κλυτός ‘famous’, W clywed ‘heard’, Arm lu ‘known’. See *xluđaz. Torp-Falk 112– 113; Holthausen AEEW 164; Mayrhofer III 373; Pokorny I 605–606; Dybo VSJa 33; Vries ANEW 238; Frisk I 877–878; Onions 538; Heidermanns 296–297; Kluge-Seebold 507.

ON hlÿäa ‘to hearken, to listen’, OE hlÿdan ‘to sound, to make a loud noise’, OFris hlēda ‘to ring’, OS ā-hlūdian ‘to pronounce’, OHG hlūten ‘to sound’. Related to *xlūđaz. Holthausen AEEW 165; Vries ANEW 240.

Goth hlutrs ‘clean, pure’, OE hlutor ‘clear, pure’, OS hluttar ‘clear, pure’, OHG hlūtar ‘clear, pure, light’. Related to Gk κλύζω ‘to wash off’ < *κλυδωō, and further to Lat cluō ‘to cleanse’, Lith šlúoju, šlúoti ‘to sweep, to clean up’. See *xlēwaz. Zupitza Gutt. 119 (to Slav *kl’uditi ‘to cleanse’ rather than to Gk κλύζω); Torp- Falk 113; Berneker I 527 (against Zupitza); Trautmann BSW 307; Walde- Hofmann I 239–240; Feist 264–265; Holthausen AEEW 164; Pokorny I 607; Fraenkel 1010–1011; Frisk I 876–877; Lehmann GED 188; Heidermanns 297– 298; Kluge-Seebold 507.
Goth hlutrei ‘purity’, OHG hlutarī ‘sincerity, clarity’. Derived from *xlūtraz. Feist 264; Lehmann GED 188; Heidermanns 298.
Goth hnaiws ‘low’, OE hnáh ‘bent, low’. Derived from *xnīzwanan. Holthausen AEEW 166; Feist 265; Seebold 266; Lehmann GED 188; Heidermanns 298.

Goth hnaiwjan ‘to make low, to humble’, ON hneigja ‘to bow, to bend, to incline’, OE hnæzan ‘to cause to bow, to bring low’, OHG hneigen ‘to bow’. Derived from *xnaizwaz or *xnīzwanan. Torp-Falk 98; Holthausen AEEW 165; Feist 265; Vries ANEW 242; Lehmann GED 188; Seebold 266–267; Heidermanns 298.

Icel hneikja ‘to strike, to kick’, OE hnæcan ‘to kill’, OHG neihhen ‘to sacrifice’. Implies unattested str. *xnīkjanan of unknown etymology. Cf. *xnaitjanan. Holthausen AEEW 165; Vries ANEW 243 (to Gk épo-κναίω ‘to scrape, to rub off’).
ON hneisa ‘to disgrace’, MHG nesen ‘to torment’. Related to *xnaisōn > ON hneisa ‘disgrace, shame’. Cf. *xnaitjanan. Sturtevant Language III 170 (to *naitjanan); Vries ANEW 242.
ON hneita ‘to cut, to wound (?)’, MLG neten ‘to strike’, OHG neizen ‘to oppress, to exhaust, to destroy’. Causative of *xnītanan. On the other hand, cf. *naitjanan. Vries ANEW 242– 243.

ON hnakki ‘occiput, nape’, MLG nacke, necke id., OHG nac, nacko id. Related to Toch A kñuk ‘neck, nape’. Another ablaut grade is probably represented in Skt ≤aṅkhá ‘conch’, Gk κόγχος id., κόγχη id. < *˚onkho-. Zupitza Gutt. 207; Torp-Falk 97 (to OIr cnoc ‘hill’); Holthausen AEEW 166; Martinet Gém. 137, 188; Mayrhofer III 290–291; Pokorny I 558 (to IE *ken- ‘to press, to squeeze’), 614; Vries ANEW 242; Frisk I 889–890; Chantraine I 550–551; Onions 605 (to OIr cnocc ‘hill’ < Celt *knukkos); Zalizniak II 178; Kluge-Seebold 580.

ON hnappr ‘button’, OE hnæpp ‘cup, bowl’, OS hnapp ‘scoop’, OHG napf ‘bowl’. Related to Gk κνάπτω ‘to card or to comb wool’, Lith knabóti ‘to pluck out’. Torp-Falk 97; Holthausen AEEW 165; Martinet Gém. 194; Vries ANEW 242; Zalizniak I 132; Levickij IF CIII 223–224 (to *xneupanan); Kluge- Seebold 582.

ON hneppa ‘to button’, OE hnæppan ‘to strike (?)’, G dial. nepfen ‘to limp’. Derived from *xnappaz. Zupitza Gutt. 120; Torp-Falk 97 (from *knabh-n,-, to Gk κνάπτω); Holthausen AEEW 165; Martinet Gém. 170, 175; Pokorny I 562; Fraenkel 277; Vries ANEW 243; Frisk I 881–882; Heidermanns 299.

Goth hnasqus ‘soft, delicate’, OE hnesce id., WFris nesk id., MDu nesch, nisch ‘soft’. Of unknown origin. Karsten NB II 408; Grienberger Got. 117–118 (to Gk κνάω ‘to scratch’); Zupitza Gutt. 99, 120; Torp-Falk 97–98; Wood PBB XXIV 530 (to Skt khādati ‘to chew, to bite, to eat’ < *k–6 d-); Torp- Falk 97; Feist 265 (to *knes- in Gk κνέωρος ‘nettle’); Holthausen AEEW 166; Pokorny I 561; Bammesberger Sprache XXI 188; Lehmann GED 188–189 (from IE *ken- ‘to rub, to scrape’); Heidermanns 299–300.

ON hnǫggr ‘niggardly, stingy’, OE hneáw ‘stingy, near, niggardly’, WFris nau ‘narrow’, MLG nouwe ‘narrow’, MHG ge-nou ‘careful, thorough’. Derived from *xnewwanan. Karsten NB II 189, 378–380; Torp-Falk 99; Holthausen AEEW 166; Pokorny I 562; Lindeman Verschärfung 143; Vries ANEW 246; Onions 610; Kluge-Seebold 312.

ON pret. sg. hnauä ‘to strike, to punch’, MLG neden ‘to rivet’, OHG bi-hniutit ‘munit’. Based on *xnewwanan. Probably related to Gk κνύζα ‘itch’, Latv knudēt ‘to itch’. Torp- Falk 100; Jóhannesson IEW 213–214; Pokorny I 559–563; Vries ANEW 244; Frisk I 886; Seebold 268; Kluge- Seebold 589.

Goth dis-hniupan ‘to rend, to break apart, to tear’, OE pret. sg. a-hneop ‘to pluck, to tear’. Cf. also OSwed niupa ‘to pinch’. Related to Lith kniáubtis ‘to cover the face with hands and to incline one’s head’, kniumbū, kniūbti ‘to kneel and to touch the ground with one’s face’. Torp-Falk 100; Grienberger Got. 56–57 (to Latv kṇubt ‘to turn, to bend’); Persson Beiträge 882 (to Gk κνύω ‘to scratch’); Torp-Falk 100; Feist 119– 120; Holthausen AEEW 166; Pokorny I 91 (to IE *ken- ‘to scratch, to rub’); Fraenkel 278; Seebold 269; Lehmann GED 91.

ON hnjósa ‘to sneeze’, MLG nēsen id., OHG niosan id. Of imitative origin (Vries IF LXII 136– 150). Torp-Falk 100–101; Porzig Gliederung 212; Jóhannesson IEW 213–214; Pokorny I 953 (to Skt kßáuti ‘to sneeze’); Vries ANEW 244; Onions 606; Seebold 269; Kluge-Seebold 588.

ON hnǫggva ‘to humble, to bring down’, OHG hniuwan ‘to punch, to crush’. Related to Gk κνύω ‘to scratch’. Meringer Wu S I 22–23; Torp-Falk 99; Jóhannesson IEW 213– 214; Pokorny I 559–563; Vries ANEW 246; Frisk I 887; Seebold 270.

ON hnippa ‘to poke in the side’, ME nippen ‘to pinch’, LG nippen ‘to sip’, G dial. nipfen id. Cf. Lith knībti ‘to fall, to bend’. Zupitza Gutt. 120; Torp-Falk 99 (to Gk κνίπω ‘to scratch’); Pokorny I 562; Chemodanov SGGJa I 76; Vries ANEW 243.

Icel hnissa ‘strong smell’. Close to Gk κνῖσα, κνίση ‘steam and odour of fat, smell or savor of a burnt sacrifice’ < *κνιδ-σ-ωā. Further cf. Lat nīdor ‘vapor, steam, smell’ (Johansson KZ XXX 416–417). Torp-Falk 98; Walde-Hofmann II 166–167; Pokorny I 561–562.

OE hnitu id. (traces of cons. stem), MLG nēte, nit id., OHG niz id. Related to Gk κόνις id., Arm anic ‘louse’ < *˚(o)nid-, Alb thëri ~ thëni id. < *˚onidand probably MIr sned ‘nit’ < *s(˚)nidā (Fick II 316; Pedersen KZ XXXIX 343, 387). Cf. *znitō. Persson SVSU X 94–96; Meillet MSL XXII 143; Torp- Falk 98–99; Holthausen AEEW 166; Pokorny I 608–609; Frisk I 912–913; Onions 611; Griepentrog Wurzelnomina 474–476; Orel AED 476; Kluge- Seebold 589.

Goth hneiwan ‘to decline’, ON hníga ‘to bow dow, to sink’, OE hnízan ‘to bend, to bow’, OFris hnīga id., OS hnīgan id., OHG nīgan id. Close to Lat cōnīueō ‘to shut the eyes, to overlook’ < *˚om-kneig ⁄h- (Osthoff PBB VIII 274; Walde-Hoffmann I 261). Meillet MSL XV 101–103 (development of *-zw-); Uhlenbeck PBB XXX 291 (to Slav *niknǫti ‘to lower, to plummet’); Zupitza Gutt. 100; Torp-Falk 98; Feist 265– 266; Holthausen AEEW 166; Walde- Hofmann I 261; Krahe Su V 78; Jóhannesson IEW 264; Pokorny I 608; Chemodanov SGGJa I 91; Vries ANEW 243; Seebold 266–267 (to *xnīpanan); Lehmann GED 189; Kluge-Seebold 585.
ON part. hnipinn ‘drooping, desponding’. Related to Lith kneibiū, kneĩbti ‘to pluck out’. Torp-Falk 99; Jóhannesson IEW 264; Pokorny I 608; Fraenkel 277–278; Vries ANEW 243; Seebold 267.
ON hnípa ‘to be downcast, to droop’, OE hnipian ‘to bow the head’. Derived from *xnīpanan. Zupitza Gutt. 120; Torp-Falk 99; Holthausen AEEW 166; Pokorny I 608; Vries ANEW 243; Seebold 267.

ON hníta ‘to strike, to wound to the death’, OE hnítan ‘to strike’, OS hnītan id. Related to Gk κνίζω ‘to scratch, to pound’, Latv knidēt ‘to itch’, kniedēt ‘to rivet’ (if not a loanword). Zupitza Gutt. 120; Torp-Falk 98; Holthausen AEEW 166; Jóhannesson IEW 213–224; Pokorny I 562; Vries IF LXII 142–143, ANEW 244; Frisk I 884–885; Seebold 267–268.
OHG nuoen ‘to plane, to shave’. Close to Gk κνάω ‘to rub, to scratch’, MIr cnáim ‘to consume’. Pokorny I 560–561; Frisk I 880–881; Dybo BSA 94.

Icel hnokki ‘small iron hook’, OE hnocc ‘hook, penis’, MDu nocke ‘cut on the arrowhead’. Related to *xnutz. Related to Dac σικουπνούξ ‘plant ἠρύγγιον’ (< *-knouj), MIr cnú ‘nut’, Lat nux id. Torp-Falk 99–100 (to Gk κνυζόν: ἀέρα ἐπινέφελον, Hes.); Holthausen AEEW 166–167; Vries ANEW 245 (to *ken- ‘to squeeze, to press’).
ON hnykill ‘clew (of yarn)’, LG nückel ‘hill’. Derived from *xnukkaz ~ *xnukkōn. Vries ANEW 245.
ON hnykkja ‘to pull violently’, LG nucken ‘to kick, to punch’. Related to *xnukkaz ~ *xnukkōn. Martinet Gém. 77; Vries ANEW 245.
ON hnykkr ‘a kind of wrestler’s movement’, MLG nuck, nucke ‘flip, hitch’. Derived from *xnukkaz ~ *xnukkōn. Vries ANEW 245.

ON hnot ‘nut’, OE hnutu id., MLG not, note id. (i-stem), OHG nuz, hnuz id. (i-stem). Related to Dac sikou-πνούξ ‘plant ἠρύγγιον’, MIr cnú ‘nut’, Lat nux id. See *xnukkaz ~ *xnukkōn. Lohmann Zfcelt Ph XIX 62–64; Zupitza Gutt. 207; Torp-Falk 100; Vendryes MSL XXI 41 (on the dissimilation in Latin); Holthausen AEEW 167; Walde-Hofmann II 191–192; Detschew Thr. 559–560; Pokorny I 558; Chemodanov SGGJa I 101; Vries ANEW 244–245; Zalizniak II 208; Onions 618; Griepentrog Wurzelnomina 471–473; Kluge-Seebold 594; Adams TB 202 (adds Toch B kontac(e) ‘nut (?)’).
ON hnǫri ‘sneeze’, OE hnora id. Derived from *xneusanan. Holthausen AEEW 167; Vries ANEW 246.

Goth hnupo ‘pointed stake, spike’, ON hnúär ‘knob, ball’. Probably derived from *xnewwanan. Any connection with *knūtaz? Petersson Afsl Ph XXXVI 150–151 (to Russ sny‘ ‘catch (of a lock)’, Czech snît ‘bough’); Torp NTF XVI 144 (to Latv knute, knutele ‘long stick’, itself from Slav *knutъ); Torp-Falk 100; Thumb KZ XXXVI 190–192 (to Gk κεντέω ‘to prick’); Feist 266; Chemodanov SGGJa I 733; Vries ANEW 245 (follows Torp); Lehmann GED 189 (to *ken- ‘to scrape, to scratch’).
ON ó-hœfä ‘indecency’, MLG he-hōfte ‘need’. Derived from *xēbiz ~ *xōbiz. Heidermanns 288.
OS hōba ‘plot of land’, OHG huoba, huob id. Close to Gk κῆπος ‘garden’ (Meyer KZ VII 28). Schrader KZ X 473; Zupitza Gutt. 103; Chemodanov SGGJa I 107; Frisk I 842; Bammesberger Nom. 111.

ON hóf ‘measure, moderation’, OFris bi-hōf id., MLG be-hōf id., MHG be-huof ‘profit, advantage’. Closely related to Gk κώπη ‘handle’, Lith kúopa ‘crowd, flock, band, penalty for damage caused by cattle’. Torp-Falk 73; Holthausen AEEW 168; Trier Zd Ph LXX 344; Pokorny I 528; Vries ANEW 246–247; Frisk II 63; Seebold 245 (to

ON hófr ‘hoof’, OE hóf id., OFris hōf id., OS hōf id., OHG huof id. Related to Skt ≤aphá- id., Av safa- id. Zupitza Gutt. 186; Bartholomae 1557– 1558; Torp-Falk 90; Holthausen AEEW 168; Mayrhofer III 297; Pokorny I 530; Chemodanov SGGJa I 106; Vries ANEW 247; Zalizniak I 132; Onions 447; Kluge-Seebold 386.
ON hœfa ‘to hit, to aim’, OE be-hófian ‘to need, to require’, OFris bi-hōvia ‘to aim’, MLG be-hōven id. Derived from *xōfaz. Torp-Falk 73; Holthausen AEEW 168; Vries ANEW 277.

Goth hoha ‘plow’. Cf. also OHG huohilī ‘small plow’ (Grimm Gesch. 40). Related to Skt ≤ākhā ‘branch’, Alb thekë ‘fringe’, Lith šakā id., Slav *soxa ‘plow’ (Bartholomae BB X 290; Meillet BSL XXXVI/107 111, Etudes 158, 174). Hehn Kult. 565; Hoops Waldbäume 499–501; Pedersen KZ XXXVI 332, XXXVIII 391; Zupitza Gutt. 138; Meringer IF XVII 121; Schrader- Nehring II 208–209; Trautmann BSW 297; Feist 266–267; Mayrhofer III 321–322; Pokorny I 523; Fraenkel 957–958; Vasmer-Trubachev III 729– 730; Lehmann GED 189; Orel AED 473.

ON hól ‘praise, flattery’, OE hól ‘vain speech, calumny, slander’. Derived from *xelanan (Grienberger Got. 6–7). Uhlenbeck PBB XXX 291 (to Lat calumnia ‘trickery, artifice, false accusation’, Slav *xvala ‘praise’); Torp-Falk 90 (to Lat caluor ‘to deceive, to devise tricks’, calumnia); Uhlenbeck PBB XXX 291–292 (to Lat calō ‘to call’); Holthausen AEEW 168; Vries ANEW 247; Lehmann GED 189 (to IE *kēl- ‘to bewitch, to deceive’).
ON hœla ‘to praise, to flatter’, OE hélan ‘to slander’. Derived from *xōlan. Holthausen AEEW 168; Vries ANEW 278 (to Gk κηλέω ‘to charm, to bewitch’); Lehmann GED 189.
Goth holon ‘to slander’, OE hólian id. Derived from *xōlan. Torp-Falk 90; Holthausen AEEW 169; FEIST 267; LEHMANN GED 189.

ON pl. hœns ‘hens, fowl’, OS hōn ‘hen’, OHG huon id. (residual sstem). Long grade variant of *xanōn. Cf. the same ablaut grade in Slav *kan’a ‘hawk’ < *kōnƒā. Vani‘ek Lat. 48 (to Lat cicōnia ‘stork’); Brugmann IF XXXVII 249; Zupitza Gutt. 108; Meillet MSL XII 213; Holthausen IF XXXIX 67; Torp-Falk 69–70; Vries ANEW 278 (to Lat cōnia ‘stork’); Lehmann GED 176; Kluge-Seebold 386.

ON hóp ‘small landlocked bay’, OE fen-hóp ‘fen-heap’, OFris hōp ‘circle’, Du hoep id. Of unknown origin. Torp-Falk 71 (to Gk κόμβος ‘roll, band, girth’); Holthausen AEEW 169; Vries ANEW 248; Onions 447; Toporov PJa III 108 (to *xappan).
ON hór-dómr ‘adultery’, OFris hōr-dōm ‘whoredom’. Derived from *xōrōn. Onions 1005.
ON hór-kona ‘adulteress’, OE hór-cwene id. Compound of *xōran and *kwenōn. Carr Nom. comp. 81 (parallel formations).

ON hór ‘adultery, fornication’, OE hór ‘adultery’, OFris hōr ‘adultery, fornication’, OS hōr-hūs ‘brothel’, OHG huor ‘adultery, fornication’. See *xōraz. Zupitza Gutt. 64; Torp-Falk 90; Holthausen AEEW 170; Vries ANEW 249; Lehmann GED 190.

Goth hors ‘fornicator, adulterer’, ON hórr id. Related to Lat cārus ‘dear’, OIr caraim ‘to love’, Latv kãrs ‘lusty, greedy’. Zupitza Gutt. 122; Torp-Falk 90; Walde-Hofmann I 175; Feist 267–268; Pokorny I 515; Dybo VSJa 27; Vries ANEW 249; Onions 1005; Van Windekens I 147 (adds Toch B an-kāre ‘immorality (?)’); Lehmann GED 190; Bammesberger Nom. 75, 97; Kluge- Seebold 389.
ON hóra ‘to commit adultery’, MLG hōren id., OHG huorōn id. Derived from *xōraz. Torp-Falk 90; Vries ANEW 249.

ON hóra ‘whore, harlot’, OE hóre id., MLG hōre id., OHG neut. huor id. Derived from *xōraz. Torp-Falk 90; Machek Slavia XXI/2 259–261 (from Slav *kurъva id.); Holthausen AEEW 170; Pokorny I 515; Vries ANEW 249; Martynov Slav-germ. 297–210 (follows Machek); Zalizniak I 155; Onions 1005; Kluge-Seebold 389.

Burg *hramns ‘raven’, ON hrafn id., OE hræfn id., OS naht-hraban ‘nocticorax’, OHG hraban, raban ‘raven’. Derived from *xrabōn. Zupitza Gutt. 23, 124; Suolahti DVN 174–179; Torp- Falk 103; Holthausen KZ XXVII 623 (to Lat crepō ‘to rattle, to crack’), AEEW 171; Pokorny I 569; Vries ANEW 250; Zalizniak II 220; Onions 741; Lehmann GED 192; Köbler 711; Kluge-Seebold 662.

MLG rave ‘raven’, OHG rabo id. Of imitative origin. Uhlenbeck PBB XXVI 305 (to Lat crepō ‘to rattle, to crack’, Latv krepēt ‘to be dirty’); Suolahti DVN 174–179; Torp-Falk 103; Pokorny I 569; Zalizniak II 220; Onions 741; Kluge-Seebold 662.

OE hreddan ‘to rid, to save’, OFris hredda id., OHG ir-retten id. Identical with Skt ≤ratháyati ‘to loosen’ (further related to ≤rathnāti ‘to become loose’) and Slav *krotiti ‘to tame’ (Machek Slavia XVI 187). Vasmer-Trubachev II 383; Kluge-Seebold 683.

ON hræll ‘weaver’s rod’, OE hræzel ‘garment’, OFris hreil id., OHG hregil id. Cf. also OE hreól ‘reel’ (Torp ANF XXIV 95). Of unknown origin. Bezzenberger BB XXVII 170 (to Slav *krosno ‘loom’); Torp-Falk 101 (to Gk κερκίς ‘weaver’s shuttle’); Holthausen AEEW 171; Pokorny I 618 (to Gk κρέκω ‘to weave, to strike’); Vries ANEW 263; Onions 736.

ON hreifa ‘to swing’. Close to Lith kraipa U, kraipÿti ‘to turn’, cf. also kreĩpti id. (Zupitza Gutt. 125; Pedersen IF V 57). Persson Beiträge 949; Trautmann BSW 140; Pokorny I 937; Fraenkel 292; Vries ANEW 252.

ON hegri ‘heron’ (with dissimilation), OE hrázra id., OS regera id. (fem.), MLG reiger id., OHG reigaro, reigar id. Probably, results from a dissimilation of *xranzrōn based on *xrenzanan. Torp-Falk 104 (to Slav *krikъ ‘shout’ or Gk κρίζω ‘to creak’); Holthausen AEEW 172; Chemodanov SGGJa I 59; Vries ANEW 216 (to Gk aor. κρίκε ‘to creek, to screech’); Onions 438.
ON hreinn ‘reindeer’, OE hrán id. From *xrainiz. (Johansson KZ XXX 339) (to*xurnan); Holthausen AEEW 172; Vries ANEW 252; Onions 752; Kluge-Seebold 679–680.
ON hreinsa ‘to cleanse’, OHG reinisōn id. Derived from *xrainiz. Vries ANEW 252–253; Lehmann GED 193.
Goth un-hrainiþa ‘impurity’, OHG hreinida ‘purification, cleansing’. Derived from *xrainjanan. Feist 520; Lehmann GED 190; Heidermanns 303.

Goth hrains ‘clean’, ON hreinn id., OFris hrēne id., OS hrēni id., OHG reini id. From *˚roi-ni- probably related to Skt ≤rī- ‘beauty’, Av srī- id., Gk Hom κρείων ‘noble’. Karsten NB II 181–182; Zupitza Gutt. 217; Torp-Falk 104; Feist 268–269; Kluge PBB VIII 525 (adjective in *-ni- from IE *krei- ‘to cut’, further related to *(s)ker-); Pokorny I 618, 946; Vries ANEW 252; Lehmann GED 190; Heidermanns 302–303; Kluge- Seebold 677.
Goth hrainei ‘cleanliness’, OHG reinī id. Derived from *xrainiz. Feist 268; Lehmann GED 190; Heidermanns 303.
Goth hrainja-hairts ‘pure in heart’, OHG rein-herzi id. Compound of *xrainiz and *xertōn. Feist 268; Lehmann GED 190.
Goth hrainjan ‘to cleanse’, OS hrēnian id., OHG reinen id. Derived from *xrainiz. Feist 268; Lehmann GED 190; Heidermanns 303.

ON hræ ‘corpse, carrion’, OE hráw ‘body, corpse’, hræw id., OFris hrē-rāf ‘corpse-robbery’, OS hrēu ‘corpse’, OHG hrēo, rēo id. (s-stem in pl.). Cf. also Goth hraiwa- in hraiwa-dūbo ‘turtle-dove’ < *‘dove of death’, *‘dove of corpses’ (Schrader II 141–142; Wiedemann BB XXVIII 32). Substantivized adjective related to Gk κροιός ‘sickly, weak’, Lith κρεϊωασ ‘crooked’, Slav *krivъ id. (Solmsen IF XXXI 466–467). Stokes Urkelt. 97 (to OIr crí ‘body’); Bugge PBB XXIV 427–428 (to *xrawaz); Zupitza Gutt. 208; Grienberger Got. 119; Unwerth PBB XXXVI 3 (traces of s-stem); Torp-Falk 101 (to Latv krija ‘bark’); Wiedemann BB XXVIII 30–32 (to Slav *‘ervo ‘womb’); Torp NNEO 558 (to Lett krijat ‘to flay’); Wood MLN XVIII 15 (to *skrei- ‘to turn’); Neckel ZDADL LXXI 187–188; Holthausen AEEW 172; Feist 269; Pokorny I 936; Fraenkel 203; Vries ANEW 263; Zalizniak II 187; Frisk II 22; Ebbinghaus GL XVI 9–13 (Goth to Gk κόραξ ‘raven’, Lat corvus id.); Lehmann GED 190–191.

ON hrekja ‘to worry, to vex’, OFris hreka ‘to tear’. Related to Lith krangū, krāgti ‘to tower, to overhang’. Zupitza Gutt. 125; Torp-Falk 101 (to Skt kárjati ‘to pain, to torment’); Scheftelowitz Zf Ind II 279; Chemodanov SGGJa I 107; Fraenkel 286; Vries ANEW 254.
MLG raem ‘frame, rack’, OHG ram id. Identical with Slav *kromъ ‘loom, edge’ (Zupitza Gutt. 122). Feist 270; Stang LS 31; Pokorny I 945; Trubachev ESSJa XII 185–186.

ON hrammr ‘paw, palm’, OE hramma ‘cramp, spasm’, MLG ram, ramme id. An emphatic form of *xrampaz. Torp-Falk 104; Holthausen AEEW 172; Martinet Gém. 190; Pokorny I 948–949 (to Gk κάρφος ‘dry stalk, chip’); Vries ANEW 251.

Goth hramjan ‘to crucify’, ON hremma ‘to clutch’, OE hremman ‘to obstruct, to hinder’, EFris remmen ‘to lace, to tie’, MDu remmen ‘to brake, to check (wheels)’. Denominative of *xrammaz ~ *xrammōn (Feist 270). Cf. a similar formation in Slav *kromiti ‘to divide, to break’. Zupitza Gutt. 114, 122; Torp- Falk 103; Holthausen AEEW 173; Chemodanov SGGJa I 80; Vries ANEW 251, 254; Lehmann GED 191 (to IE *kre-m-, from *(s)ker- ‘to cut’); Trubachev ESSJa XIII 5; Van Windekens I 234 (to Toch B krämp- ‘to disturb, to check’).
Norw dial. ramp ‘thin man’, MLG ramp ‘cramp, spasm’, MHG rampf id. Probably related to Gk κράμβος ‘blight in grapes, when they shrivel before they are ripe’. Torp-Falk 103; Frisk II 5–6.

Norw rams ‘Allium ursinum’, OE pl. hramsan ‘broad-leaved garlic’, MLG ramese ‘wild garlic’. Probably related to Gk κρόμυον ‘onion’ < *kromuso-, κρέμυον id., MIr crim ‘garlic’ and further to Lith kermūšë ‘wild garlic’, Slav *‘ermъša id., *‘ermъxa ‘bird cherry’. The IE word was borrowed from NCauc *kärmu≤V ‘kind of fruit tree, quince, plum’. Bugge KZ XIX 419–420; Schmidt KZ XXXII 346; Pedersen IF V 33; Torp-Falk 103; Holthausen AEEW 172; Pokorny I 580–581; Fraenkel 243; Frisk II 23–24; Onions 738; Trubachev ESSJa IV 68; Starostin DV 121 (IE and NCauc words genetically related); Nikolayev-Starostin NCED 700; Kluge-Seebold 665.
ON hrapa ‘to fall, to tumble down’, ME rapen ‘to hurry’. Derived from *xrappaz. Torp-Falk 102 (to *xratōjann); Adams TB 154 (to Toch AB kārp- ‘to descend’ < *korb-).
ON prop. Hrappr, MLG rapp ‘swift’. Related to Slav *krîpъkъ ‘firm’. Torp-Falk 102 (to *xrapōjanan); Pokorny I 620; Vasmer-Trubachev II 372.

ON hreppa ‘to catch, to obtain’, OE hreppan ‘to touch, to treat’, OFris reppa ‘to touch’, MLG reppen ‘to move slightly’. Derived from *xrappaz. Zupitza Gutt. 207; Torp-Falk 102–103 (to Lith krapštÿti ‘to scrape, to rub, to scratch’); Holthausen AEEW 173; Martinet Gém. 169; Vries ANEW 254.

ON hrata ‘to fall, to stagger, to tumble’, OE hratian ‘to hurry’, MHG razzen ‘to rage’. Related to Skt kūrdati ‘to leap, to jump’, Gk κραδάω ‘to swing, to wave’. On the other hand, cf. *xrappjanan. Zupitza Gutt. 122; Torp- Falk 101; Holthausen AEEW 172; Mayrhofer I 254–255; Vries ANEW 252; Frisk II 1–2.

ON hraär ‘swift’, OE hræä, hræd id., MLG rat ‘swift, quick’, OHG hrad id. Structurally close to Lith adv. apikratai ‘quick, fast’. Further related to MIr crothaim ‘to shake’, Lith kre‘iū, kr Esti id. (Fick II 99; Schwentner PBB XLVIII 79–85). Karsten NB I 57–58, II 294; Torp-Falk 102; Holthausen AEEW 171; Pokorny I 620; Fraenkel 295; Vries ANEW 250; Onions 741; Heidermanns 304–305.
ON hrauä ‘mail-coat, cuirass’, OE scild-hreóäa ‘shield, buckler’. Derived from *xreuđanan. Holthausen AEEW 173; Vries ANEW 252 (to ON hrjóäa ‘to strip, to disable’).

ON hraukr ‘small stack’, OE hreác ‘heap, stack’, MDu rooc id. Related to MIr crúach ‘heap’ (Kluge PBB X 444). Bugge KZ XIX (1870) 420– 421 (to Lith kráuti ‘to put in a heap’); Zupitza Gutt. 123, 215; Torp-Falk 107; Holthausen AEEW 172; Chemodanov SGGJa I 98; Vries ANEW 252; Onions 766.
ON hraumi ‘noisy fellow’, OE hreám ‘cry’. Probably continues *xraut-mōn ~ *xrautmaz derived from *xreutanan ~ *xrūtanan I. Torp-Falk 573 (to LG schrauen ‘to cry, to shout’).

ON hraustr ‘strong, valiant’, G dial. rπst ‘sprightly, active’. Probably derived from *xreusanan. Karsten NB II 339–340; Schwentner PBB XLVIII 85; Zupitza Gutt. 123; Torp-Falk 108; Vries ANEW 252; Pokorny I 623; Heidermanns 305–306.

ON hrár ‘raw, fresh’, OE hreáw ‘raw’, OS hrao id., OHG *rao id. Related to Skt kraví- ‘raw flesh’, Gk κρέας ‘flesh, meat’, Lat cruor ‘blood’, MIr crú id., Lith kra Ujas id., Slav *kry id. Zupitza Gutt. 124; Torp-Falk 106; Holthausen AEEW 172; Walde-Hofmann I 294– 295; Mayrhofer I 277; Pokorny I 621; Fraenkel 290; Vries ANEW 251; Frisk II 11–12; Onions 742; Trubachev ESSJa XIII 67–70; Hamp IF LXXXII 75–76; Bammesberger Nom. 239; Heidermanns 306; Kluge-Seebold 690.

ON hrǫnn ‘wave’, OE hærn ‘waves, tide’. Identical with Gk κρήνη ‘spring, fountain’ (Saussure MSL 1889 119), Alb krua ~ krue id. < PAlb *kresnā (Camarda I 50). Petersson IF XXIV 46 (to *xreusanan); Kuhn NB XXIX 113; Torp-Falk 104; Holthausen AEEW 146; Vries ANEW 264 (to *xrōzjanan); Frisk II 16; Orel AED 198.

OE hrif ‘womb, belly’, OFris hrif, href id., OHG href id. Related to Skt kṛp ‘shape, beautiful appearance’, Lat corpus ‘body’, MIr crí ‘body’ < *kṛpes. Cf. an interesting derivative ON hriflingr ‘a kind of shoe’ < *xrefalinzaz with a semantic development similar to Slav *‘ervikъ ‘shoe’ < *‘ervo ‘belly’ (Sobolevskii RFV LXXI 444). van Wijk IF XXVIII 123– 124 (to Lith kreĩpti ‘to turn’); Zupitza Gutt. 53, 125 (ON to Lat carpisculus ‘a kind of shoe’, OPrus kurpe ‘shoe’); Torp-Falk 103 (ON to Gk κρηπίς ‘high boot, halfboot’); Holthausen AEEW 174; Walde- Hofmann I 277–278; Mayrhofer I 260; Pokorny I 620; Vries ANEW 255 (ON to Lith kūrpë ‘shoe’); Onions 575.

ON hrinda ‘to kick, to push, to throw’, OE pret. sg. rand id. Probably related to Lith krañtas ‘steep bank’, krentū, krīsti ‘to fall’, Slav *krǫtъ ‘steep’ (Zupitza Gutt. 123). Torp-Falk 102 (to Gk κρότος ‘clapping’); Holthausen AEEW 174; Jóhannesson IEW 272; Pokorny I 621; Vries ANEW 256; Vasmer-Trubachev II 387 (against Zupitza); Seebold 273 (to *xrewwanan); Trubachev Etim. 1978 6–7.

Crim. Goth. rinck ‘ring, circle’, ON hringr id., OE hrinz id., OFris hring id., OS hring id., OHG hring id. Related to Slav *kręgъ ‘mountain ridge’ < *krengho-, *krǫgъ ‘circle’ < *krongho-, *kręžь ‘stump, mountain ridge’ < *krenghƒo- (Miklosich 142, Zeyss KZ XX 132). Wood IF XVIII 11; Zupitza Gutt. 125, 178; Torp-Falk 102; Holthausen AEEW 174; Feist 398; Schrijnen KZ XLII 103; Trier Holz 80; Pokorny I 936; Vries ANEW 256; Zalizniak I 132; Onions 768; Stang LS 30–31; Lehmann GEDE 285 (from *(s)ker- ‘to turn, to bend’); Trubachev ESSJa XII 142, 148– 149, XIII 25–27; Kluge-Seebold 687.

ON hringja ‘to ring’, OE hrinzan id. (secondary str. forms). Derived from *xrenzaz? Zupitza Gutt. 124; Torp-Falk 102; Berneker I 612 (to Lith krankiū, krañkti ‘to croak’, Slav *krêkati ‘to quack’); Holthausen AEEW 174; Vries ANEW 256; Onions 768.
ON hringja ‘buckle’, OE hrinze id., OS hringa id., OHG ringa id. Derived from *xrenzaz. Holthausen AEEW 175; Vries ANEW 256; Kluge- Seebold 688.

ON hrip ‘basket (carried on the horse-back)’, OHG ref id. Related to Lith kr‚pšas ‘basket’. Torp-Falk 103 (to Lat corbis ‘basket’); Būga RFV LXVII 241; Pokorny I 937 (to Latv kribas ‘woven floor of the sleigh’); Chemodanov SGGJa I 77; Vries ANEW 257; Kluge- Seebold 673–674.
OHG redan ‘to sieve’. Related to Lith kre‘iū, kr Esti ‘to shake’, for the semantic development cf. especially Lith kreš‘iuóti ‘to sieve’. Pokorny I 620; Fraenkel 295; Seebold 274–275.

OE hreód ‘reed’, OFris hriād id., OS hriod id., OHG hriot ‘place where reed grows’. Identical with Slav *kr’udъ ‘whip’ (Trubachev ESSJa XII 178). Torp-Falk 108; Holthausen AEEW 173; Pokorny I 623; Onions 749; Kluge-Seebold 687 (to Toch A kru, B kärwats ‘reed’).

ON part. hrođinn ‘covered with metal’, OE pret. sg. hreád ‘to cover’. Related to Lith kráuju, kráuti ‘to put on top of each other’, Slav *kryti ‘to cover’ (Wood MP V 276). Sievers PBB XXXVI 404; Torp-Falk 108; Holthausen AEEW 173; Jóhannesson IEW 268–269; Pokorny I 616–617; Fraenkel 291; Vries ANEW 258; Seebold 275.

ON hrjúfr ‘rough, scabby’, OE hreóf ‘rough, scabby, leprous’, OHG riob ‘mangy, leprous’. Related to Lith kraupūs ‘uneven, rough’. Further cf. Alb kripë ~ krypë ‘salt’, Slav *krupa ‘groats’ (Meyer Alb. St. III 4, 31, 71). Karsten NB I 23; Zupitza Gutt. 126; Torp-Falk 108; Trautmann BSW 143; Holthausen AEEW 173; Kluge 279, Nom. Stamm. 60, 88; Pokorny I 623; Chemodanov SGGJa I 74; Fraenkel 290; Stang LS 30; Vries ANEW 258; Heidermanns 306– 307; Orel AED 197; Kluge-Seebold 339, 695.

OE hreósan ‘to fall’, OHG hriusu ‘reor’ (leg. ruor). Etymologically related to Gk κρούω ‘to strike’, Lith krušū, krūšti ‘to trample’, OPrus krūt ‘to fall’, Slav *krušiti ‘to destroy’. Zupitza Gutt. 125, 208; Torp-Falk 107; Holthausen AEEW 173; Pokorny I 622; Fraenkel 302–303; Vries ANEW 258 (to Gk κρύος ‘icy cold, frost’); Frisk II 27–28; Seebold 276–277.

ON hrjóta ‘to snore’, OE hrútan ‘to make a noise, to snore’, OFris hrūta ‘to wheeze’, OS pres. hrūtan ‘to snore’, OHG rūzan id. Of imitative origin. Torp-Falk 107; Holthausen AEEW 176; Jóhannesson IEW 230–236; Pokorny I 567–571; Vries ANEW 258; Seebold 277–278.
ON hrjóta ‘to rebound, to fall, to fly’, MHG rūzen ‘to move quickly’. Related to *xreusanan. Torp-Falk 107 (to OPrus krūt ‘to fall’); Pokorny I 622.
ON hrygg-ligr ‘mournful, sad’, OE hréow-líc ‘grievous, miserable’. Derived from *xrewwaz. Heidermanns 308.

OE hreówan ‘to make sorry, to grieve’, OFris hriouwa, riouwa ‘to regret’, OS hreuwan ‘to hurt, to pain’, OHG riuwan id. Cf. also ON hryggja, hryggva ‘to distress, to grieve’ < *xrewwjanan. Of unknown origin. Torp-Falk 106– 107 (to *kreu- ‘blood’); Holthausen AEEW 173; Pokorny I 622 (to *kreu- ‘to strike’); Vries ANEW 263; Seebold 278– 279 (to Gk κρούω ‘to strike, to smite’, Lith krušū, krūšti ‘to pound’); Kluge- Seebold 683.

ON hryggr ‘afflicted, grieved, distressed’, OE hréowe ‘sad, grieved’, OS hriuwi ‘worried’. Derived from *xrewwanan. Karsten NB II 444– 445; Uhlenbeck PBB XXVI 306 (to *xrawaz); Persson SVSU X 178 (to Skt karúṇa- ‘lamentable, compassionate’); Torp-Falk 107; Holthausen AEEW 173; Jóhannesson IEW 267; Pokorny I 622 (to *kreu- ‘to strike’); Vries ANEW 263; Lindeman Verschäfrung 150–151; Seebold 278–279; Matzel Festschr. Schröder 95, KZ CV 98; Heidermanns 308.
ON hrækja ‘to spit’, OE hræcan ‘to clear the throat, to spit’. Derived from *xrēkōn. Holthausen AEEW 171; Vries ANEW 263; Onions 743.

ON hráki ‘spittle’, OE hráca ‘expectoration, spittle’. Related to Gk κρώζω ‘to croak’, Lith kregėti ‘to speak loudly, to grunt’. Zupitza Gutt. 124, 162; Torp-Falk 101; Holthausen AEEW 171; Pokorny I 569; Vries ANEW 251; Frisk II 31; Onions 743.
OFris rāta ‘honeycomb’, MLG rāte id., OHG rāza id. An ablaut variant of *xrōtan. van Wijk IF XXVIII 121–123 (to Slav *krada ‘pile, stack’); Pokorny I 617.
Goth af-hrisjan ‘to shake off’, OE hrissian id., OS hrisian ‘to shake oneself, to tremble’. Related to *xristjanan. Torp-Falk 105; Holthausen AEEW 175; Feist 7; Pokorny I 19; Lehmann GED 5.
Burg *hrispa ‘thicket’, MHG rispe ‘bush’. Close to Lat crispus ‘curly, twisted’, W crych ‘vibrant, curly’ (Lehmann GED 5). Walde-Hofmann I 293; Pokorny I 938; Köbler 711.

ON hrista ‘to shake’, MLG risten ‘to braid’. Related to *xrīsan and *xrisjanan ~ *xrissōjanan. Etymologically close to MIr cressaim id. (Marstrander Zfcelt Ph VII 412). Torp-Falk 105; Pokorny I 937; Vries ANEW 257.

ON hrífa ‘to catch, to grapple’, EFris rīfen ‘to rake’, MDu riven id. Of unknown origin. A modification of *zrīpanan or *xrīnanan? Zupitza Gutt. 126; Torp-Falk 105 (to Latv skrīpāt ‘to scratch’); Schrijnen KZ XLII 101; Vries ANEW 255 (to ON hreifi‘wrist’).
ON hrífa ‘rake’, OFris hrīve id., MDu rive id. Related to *xrībanan. Torp-Falk 105; Vries ANEW 255.

OE hridder ‘sieve’, OS hrīdra ‘sieve’, OHG rītera id. Continues *krei-dhro- (as in Lat crībrum ‘sieve’) or *krei-tro- (as in OIr crīathar id.). Zupitza Gutt. 125 (to *xrisjanan ~ *xrissōjanan); Torp-Falk 104; Holthausen AEEW 174; Pokorny I 946; Chemodanov SGGJa I 88; Onions 767; Lehmann GED 190; Kluge-Seebold 678.
ON hrím-kaldr ‘icy cold’, OE hrím-ceald id. Compound of *xrīman ~ *xrīmaz and *kalđaz. Carr Nom. comp. 61.

ON hrím ‘hoar frost’, OE hrím id., Du rijm id. Probably from *xrīp-m- in view of WGmc masc. *xrīpōn id.: OS hrīpo, OHG hrīfo. Unclear. Zupitza Gutt. 126; Torp- Falk 104 (to Lith krënā ‘foam on the cooking dish’); Holthausen AEEW 174 (to Latv krēims ‘cream’); Pokorny I 618 (to *xrīnanan); Vries ANEW 256; Onions 768; Kluge-Seebold 676.

ON hrína ‘to cleave, to stick’, OE hrínan ‘to touch’, OS hrīnan id., OHG hrīnan id. Of unknown etymology. Zupitza Gutt. 124, 187; Torp-Falk 104 (to Latv krīt ‘to skim’); Holthausen AEEW 174; Jóhannesson IEW 266; Pokorny I 618; Chemodanov SGGJa I 75 (to Latv krina ‘pig’); Vries ANEW 256 (to *krei- ‘to touch’); Seebold 271; Bammesberger Nom. 56.
ON hrísla ‘twig’, OE hrisel ‘weaving shuttle’ (masc.?). Derived from *xrīsan. Holthausen AEEW 175; Vries ANEW 257.

ON hrís ‘shrubs, brushwood’, OE hrís ‘twig, branch’, OFris hrīs ‘switch, rod’, MLG rīs ‘twig, branch’, OHG hrīs, rīs id. Etymologically connected with OPrus craysi, crays ‘straw’, Lat crīnis ‘hair’ (< *crisnis) and related to *xristjanan (Lehmann GED 5). Zupitza Gutt. 126; Holthausen AEEW 175; Walde-Hofmann I 292–293; Trier Holz 58–62; Pokorny I 937; Vries ANEW 257; Zalizniak I 141; Kluge-Seebold 677.

ON hríä ‘tempest, storm’, OE hríä id. Related to OIr crith ‘trembling, fever’, W cryd ‘fever’. Lidén PBB XV 511 (to Gk κρίσις ‘separation, decision, judgement’); Torp-Falk 105; Holthausen AEEW 175; Falk ANF XLI 119; Pokorny I 937; Vries ANEW 255.

ON hróf ‘boat-shed’, OE hróf ‘roof, top, summit’, OFris hrōf ‘roof’, MLG rōf id. Continues *krō-po-, cf. *xrōtan < *krō-do-. On the other hand cf. OIr cró ‘enslosure, barn’ < *krāpo-. Zupitza Gutt. 11, 208, IF XIII 51 (to Slav *stropъ ‘roof’ < IE *˚rapo-); Pedersen Kelt. Gr. I 92; Torp- Falk 106; Holthausen AEEW 176; Pokorny I 616; Vries ANEW 259; Zalizniak II 168; Onions 772.

ON hrókr ‘rook’, OE hróc id., MLG rōk id., OHG hruoh id. Related to *xrēkōn. Zupitza Gutt. 23, 124; Holthausen AEEW 175; Suolahti ANT 183; Torp-Falk 105; Pokorny I 569; Vries ANEW 259; Frisk II 31; Onions 772; Lehmann GED 192.

ON hróp ‘crying, screaming’, OE hróp ‘crying, clamor’. Derived from *xrōpanan ‘to shout, to call’. Torp-Falk 106; Holthausen AEEW 176; Jóhannesson IEW 192; Vries ANEW 260; Seebold 279; Kluge-Seebold 695.

OE hrópan ‘to cry out, to clamor’, OFris hrōpa ‘to shout, to call’, OS hrōpan id., OHG ruofan id. Of unknown origin. Cf. *xrōþaz. Noreen Abriß 206 (to Lith skrebū, skrebėti ‘to rustle’); Bloomfield PBB XXXVII 251 (rhyming word with *wōpjanan); Torp- Falk 106; Feist 270; Holthausen AEEW 176; Trier Holz 70 (from IE *ker- ‘to cut’); Pokorny I 531; Seebold 279–280; Lehmann GED 191 (to IE *kar- ‘praise’); Kluge-Seebold 695.
Goth hrops ‘clamor, outcry’, OHG ruof ‘shout, rumor’. Derived from *xrōpanan. Feist 270; Vries ANEW 260; Seebold 279; Lehmann GED 191; Bammesberger Nom. 136; Kluge-Seebold 695.
Goth hropjan ‘to shout’, ON hrœpa ‘to libel, to defame’, OHG ruofen ‘to shout, to call’. Derived from *xrōpan ~ *xrōpaz. Zupitza Gutt. 23; Torp-Falk 106; Vries ANEW 260; Seebold 279; Lehmann GED 191.

Goth hrot ‘roof, house’, ON hrót ‘roof’. Related to *xrētō. Close to Slav *krada ‘pile of logs’ (Bezzenberger BB XXVII 170; Uhlenbeck PBB XXX 302) and Alb krodhë ‘beehive’ < PAlb *krādā. As it follows from the comparison with *xrōfan ~ *xrōfaz, the original structure of this word is *krō-do-, a derivational variant of *krō-po-. Lidén Festschr. Noreen 432 (to Pers sarā(y) ‘palace’ < Iran *srāda-); Zupitza Gutt. 127; Grienberger Got. 119–120 (to Gk κράδη ‘tree-top’); Wiedemann IF I 194 (to Slav *kryti ‘to cover’); Uhlenbeck PBB XXX 292 (same as Lidén); Torp-Falk 106; Hirt Ablaut 77 (to Gk κέρας ‘head’); Berneker 605 (against Bezzenberger); Van Wijk IF XXVIII 121 (compares Slav *krada with Gmc *xrētō); Trautmann BSW 140 (to Lith kra Utë ‘attic’); Heinertz ESt. 432–435 (against Bezzenberger; reconstructs *krōu-d-, eventually, to IE *ker- ‘to weave’); Feist 270–271; Trier Holz 70 (to IE *(s)ker- ‘to cut’); Pokorny I 617; Vries ANEW 261; Zalizniak I 141; Lehmann GED 191–192; Trubachev ESSJa XI 58–60 (Slav *korda rather than

Burg *hroþs ‘glory, victory’, ON hróär ‘praise’, OE hréä ‘fame, glory’, hróäor ‘joy’, OS hrōä ‘glory’, OHG prn. Ruod-beraht. Any connection with *xrađjanan? Hirt PBB XXIII 292 (to *kar- ‘to praise’: Gk κήρυξ ‘herold’, Skt carkarti ‘to make mention of, to praise’); Torp-Falk 105; Holthausen AEEW 175; Feist 271; Pokorny I 530–531; Vries ANEW 259; Lehmann GED 192 (to IE *kar- ~ *krā- ‘to praise’); Köbler 711.
Goth hroþeigs ‘victorious, triumphant’, ON hróäugr id., OE hréäiz id. Derived from *xrōþaz. Feist 271; Lehmann GED 192.

Run prop. hro Ra R, OE hrór ‘stirring, active’, OS hrōr ‘lively’. Identical with Slav *krasъ ‘beautiful, colored’, *krasa ‘beauty’. Torp-Falk 106; Holthausen AEEW 176; Pokorny I 572; Vries ANEW 264; Matzel KZ CV 123; Trubachev ESSJa XII 95–97, 105; Heidermanns 309.

ON hrœra ‘to move, to stir’, OE hréran ‘to shake, to move’, OFris hrēra ‘to move’, OS hrōrian id., OHG hruoren ‘to push, to move’. Derived from *xrōzaz. Structurally close to Slav *krasiti ‘to ornate’. Zupitza Gutt. 187; Torp- Falk 106; Holthausen AEEW 175; Vries ANEW 264; Trubachev ESSJa XII 99–100; Heidermanns 309; Kluge- Seebold 696.
ON hrufa ‘crust, rough surface (of a stone)’, OFris rofe, rafe ‘crust (on a wound)’, MLG rove id., OHG ruf ‘scab’. Cf. also Gep *hrufs ‘scurf’. Related to *xreufaz. Vries ANEW 261; Köbler 715.

ON hryggr ‘back, spine’ (traces of i-stem), OE hrycz id., OFris hreg id., OS hruggi id., OHG hruggi id. Connected with Lat crux ‘cross, tree or wooden frame for execution’, Ir crúach ‘heap’. Zupitza Gutt. 127, 187; Trautmann KZ XLII 374 (to Slav *kъr‘iti ‘to bend, to ply’); Torp-Falk 107; Holthausen AEEW 177; Walde-Hofmann I 296–297; Pokorny I 938; Vries ANEW 262; Zalizniak II 213; Onions 767; Lindeman Verschärfung 15.17; Kluge- Seebold 694.
ON hrogn ‘fish roe’, ME rowne id., MLG rogen id., OHG masc. rogan id. Derived from *xruzōn. Zupitza Gutt. 11, 126; Torp-Falk 107; Pokorny I 619; Vries ANEW 259; Kluge-Seebold 690.

ME rowe ‘fish roe’, MDu roge id., OHG hrogo id. Related to *xrawaz. Bezzenberger BB XXI 315 (to Lith kurkulaĩ ‘frog-spawn’); Zupitza Gutt. 126; Torp-Falk 107; Pokorny I 619; Vries ANEW 259; Kluge-Seebold 690.
ON hroki ‘heap above the brim of a full vessel’, OE corn-hrycce ‘corn-rick’. Related to *xraukaz. Vries ANEW 259.
Norw dial. rump ‘flat hill top, buttocks’, ME rumpe ‘podex’ (fem.), MLG rump ‘trunk’. Of unknown origin. Torp-Falk 103 (to *xrampaz).

Goth hrugga ‘staff’, OE hrunz ‘rung, staff, rod’, MLG runge ‘pole, stake’, OHG runga id. Related to *xrenzaz (Torp-Falk 108). Charpentier BB XXX 159; Schröder PBB XXIX 499 (reconstructs the original meaning as *‘round pole’: to Skt krúñcati ‘to curve, to move crookedly’); Uhlenbeck TNTL XXV 279–280 (against Schröder); Holthausen AEEW 176; Feist 271– 272; Pokorny I 936; Zalizniak I 147; Onions 777; Lehmann GED 192; Kluge- Seebold 696.

OE hruse ‘earth, ground’, OHG (h)rosa ‘crust, ice’. Derived from *xreusanan. Identical with Lith krušā ‘hail, floe’, Slav *krъxa ‘bit’. Torp-Falk 108; Walde-Hofmann I 295 (to Gk κρύος ‘frost, cold’, Lat crusta ‘crust’); Fraenkel 302; Vasmer-Trubachev II 384.

ON hross ‘horse’, OE hors id., OFris hors, hars id., OS hros id., OHG hros, ros id. Borrowed from East Iranian, cf. Osset (v)urs ‘stallion’ (Brøndal APS III 1–3; Benveniste BSL LVIII 41–43). Derivation from *xreusanan is less probable. Cf. also *xruzan. Kögel PBB VII 176 (to Gk κραδάω ‘to shake’); Froehde BB XIV 105 (to Lat currō ‘to run’, OIr carr ‘vehicle’); Zupitza Gutt. 123; Torp- Falk 79; Holthausen AEEW 170 (to Skt kūrdati ‘to leap, to jump’); Jóhannesson IEW 239 (to *xurskaz); Brøndal APS III 1–31 (from East Iranian); Pokorny I 583; Vries ANEW 260; Zalizniak I 141; Onions 448; Kluge-Seebold 692; Adams TB 234 (to Toch A kursär, B kwarsär ‘league, course’).
ON hrǫr ‘corpse’, OE ze-hror ‘fall, ruin, death’. Derived from *xreusanan. Holthausen AEEW 176; Jóhannesson IEW 268; Vries ANEW 264; Seebold 276.
OE hrúm ‘soot’. Identical with Av xrūma- ‘terrible, horrible’, Gk kr:υμός ‘icy cold, frost’. Further cf. *xrūþaz ~ *xrūþōn. Torp-Falk 106; Frisk II 28.

ON hrúär ‘crust, scab’, OE fem. hrúäe ‘mange, scabies’, OS hrūtho ‘scab’, OHG rūdo id. Related to *xrawaz (Torp NNEO 550). Cf. Lat crusta ‘crust’ < *˚reu- ‘blood’ (Bücheler Kl. Schr. II 270). Torp-Falk 106; Walde-Hofmann I 295 (against Bücheler); Holthausen AEEW 177; Pokorny I 621; Vries ANEW 261 (related to Lat kreve ‘scab’); Kluge-Seebold 669.

ON hof ‘pagan temple’, OE hof ‘dwelling, house, hall’, OFris hof ‘yard’, OS hof id., OHG hof id. Related to *xaupaz (with irregular -p-). Torp-Falk 94; Holthausen AEEW 168; Pokorny I 591; Vries ANEW 246; Zalizniak II 168; Kluge- Seebold 379.
ON hof-staär ‘sanctuary’, OS hof-stedi ‘court’. Compound of *xufan ~ *xufaz and *stađiz. Carr Nom. comp. 83 (parallel formations).

OE hofer ‘hump, swelling’, MLG hover id., MHG hover id. Close to Lith kuprā id. Cf. also *xufan ~ *xufaz. Torp-Falk 94; Holthausen AEEW 168; Pokorny I 591; Chemodanov SGGJa I 73; Fraenkel 314; Vries ANEW 274; Stang LS 31.

Goth ga-hugds ‘perception, mind’, ON -úä (in cmpn.), OE ze-hyzd ‘thought, cogitation’, OS gi-hugd id., OHG gi-hugt ‘memory’. Derivative in IE *-ti- from *xuzjanan. Kluge PBB IX 152–153; Collitz Prät. 13–14, 74–77 (on

entry_headword_and_label: *-ti- > *-đi- assimilated to root final *-z-); Feist 184; Lehmann GED 139. *xuzi-rūnō sb.f.
entry_text: ON pl. hug-rúnar ‘magical runes’, OE hyze-rún ‘secret of the mind’. Compound of *xuziz ~ *xuzuz and *rūnō. Carr Nom. comp. 81 (parallel formations).
ON hug-þokki ‘mind, disposition, judgment’, OE hyze-äanc ‘thought’. Compound of *xuziz ~ *xuzuz and *þankaz. Carr Nom. comp. 81 (parallel formations).

Goth hugs ‘understanding, mind’, ON hugr ‘mind, mood’, OE hyze ‘mind, heart, soul’, OFris hei ‘mind’, OS hugi id., OHG hugu ‘mind, sense, spirit’. See *xuzjanan. Bopp Glossarium 380 (to Skt ≤añkate ‘to sway’); Fick BB XVII 320 (to Lat cupiō ‘to desire’); Uhlenbeck PBB XXII 541 (to Gk κυκάω ‘to mix, to stir up’), XXX 293 (to *ku·h- ‘to hide’); Charpentier BB XXX 153– 154; Torp-Falk 91; Mikkola BB XXII 239 (to Lith ka Ukas ‘pixie, goblin’); Hirt Ablaut 110 (from *˚ukí-, cf., Skt ≤úci- ‘bright, pure’, further to Skt ≤ócati ‘to shine, to glow, to burn’, Av part. sao‘int- ‘burning’); Wood MP XVII 572 (to Czech ‘ihati ‘to lie in wait’); Johansson PBB XVIII 37 (follows Hirt); Holthausen AEEW 183; Feist 273; Pokorny I 596; Vries ANEW 265; Zalizniak II 201; Lehmann GED 192–193 (to Skt kaví- ‘seer, sage, poet’); Bammesberger Nom. 130.

Goth hugjan ‘to think’, ON hyggja ‘to think, to mean, to believe’, OE hyczan ‘to think, to be midful’, OFris hugia ‘to think’, OS huggian id., OHG huggen ‘to remember, to hope’. Related to Hitt kunk- ‘to weigh (?)’, Lith k’štū, kūšti ‘to start to move’ (Woodhouse IF CIV 211–213). For the semantic development cf. Lat pendō ‘to weigh, to consider’, G wägen ‘to determine the weigh, to consider’. Fick BB XVII 320 (to Lat cupiō ‘to long, to desire’); Torp-Falk 91; Holthausen AEEW 183; Feist 272–273; Fraenkel 321–322; Vries ANEW 274; Lehmann GED 192–193; Makovskii VJa XXXV/5 143 (to *xawwanan); Kluge- Seebold 379.
ON huga ‘to consider, to think’, OE hozian ‘to mind, to think’, OFris hugia ‘to think’, OS huggean id., OHG huggen id. See *xuzjanan. Holthausen AEEW 168; Vries ANEW 265.
ON hugi ‘mind, mood’, OE hoza ‘care’, hozu ‘care, worry’. Related to *xuziz. Holthausen AEEW 168; Vries ANEW 265.

ON hol ‘cavity, hollow’, OE hol ‘covering’, OFris hol ‘den’, OHG hol id. Substantivized *xulaz. Zupitza Gutt. 185; Holthausen AEEW 168; Feist 274–275; Vries ANEW 247; Onions 444; Lehmann GED 194, 383.

ON holr ‘hollow’, OE hol id., OFris hol id., OS hol id., OHG hol id. Derived from *xelanan (Brugmann IF XXXIII 305–307). Karsten NB I 2; Zupitza Gutt. 185; Meyer NGWG 1906 187 (against Brugmann; to Skt kulyā ‘brook, ditch, channel’, Gk καυλός ‘stem, shaft’, Lat caulis ‘stem, penis’); Torp- Falk 81, 563; Holthausen AEEW 168; Porzig Gliederung 177; Pokorny I 537; Vries ANEW 248; Onions 444; Lehmann GED 383; Heidermanns 310–311; Kluge- Seebold 379–380.

ON hold ‘flesh, meat’, OE hold ‘carcass, body’. Continues a participle *(s)k¬to- of *(s)kel- ‘to cut’. On the other hand, probably related to MIr colinn ‘meat’. Zupitza Gutt. 107; Torp- Falk 96 (to *kel- ‘to strike’); Holthausen AEEW 169; Pokorny I 925; Dybo VSJa 33 (to Lith “˚elt ‘to split’); Chemodanov SGGJa I 99; Vries ANEW 247.
ON hylda ‘to slash’, OE hyldan ‘to flay, to skin’, holdian ‘to cut open’. Derived from *xulđan. Holthausen AEEW 183; Vries ANEW 274.

ON hulda ‘hiding, secrecy’, E hull, OHG hulla ‘cover, veil’. Cf. also ON prop. Huld < *xulđō. Derived from *xelanan, cf. *xuljanan. Zupitza Gutt. 185; Torp-Falk 81; Holthausen AEEW 177; Onions 451; Vries ANEW 266; Lehmann GED 193.

Goth hulistr ‘cover, veil’, OE heolstor ‘darkness, veil, covering’, Du holster ‘holster’. Derivative in *-str-o- of *xuljanan (Kluge Nom. Stamm. 49–50). Cf. also *xulisō ‘husk, pod’: MLG hulse, OHG hulsa. Torp-Falk 81; Feist 274; Holthausen AEEW 156; Lehmann GED 193.

Goth huljan ‘to cover’, ON hylja ‘to hide, to cover’, OFris bi-hella ‘to cover’, OS bi-hullean id., OHG hullen id. Derived from an unattested aorist present based on *xelanan (Meillet BSL XX 25). Torp-Falk 81; Feist 274; Pokorny I 553; Vries ANEW 274; Seebold 252; Lehmann GED 193.
ON hola ‘hole’, OHG holī id. Derived from *xulaz. Holthausen AEEW 184; Vries ANEW 247 (to Gk καλιά ‘hut, barn’).

ON hólkr ‘metal tube’, OE holc ‘hollow, cavity’. Of unknown origin. To be separated from OE hulc ‘light ship’, MLG hulk, holke ‘ship of burden’, OHG holco id. (despite Heinertz PBB XLI 194) borrowed from Lat holcas id. < Gk ὅλκας id. Falk Wu S IV 88; Holthausen AEEW 169, 177; Vries ANEW 247 (to *xulaz); Onions 451.

ON hólmr ‘islet’, OE holm ‘mound, hill’, OS holm ‘hill’. Related to *xalluz ~ *xallaz. Reflects *k¬mo-, cf. Lat columen ‘point, top, pillar’ < *kelom– (Curtius Gr. Et. 153). Zupitza Gutt. 106; Torp-Falk 82; Holthausen AEEW 169; Walde-Hofmann I 249–250; Pokorny I 544; Vries ANEW 248; Lerchner 112, 117; Kluge-Seebold 381.
ON hylma ‘to hide, to conceal’, OE for-hylman ‘to cover over, to conceal’. Related to *xelmaz, *xelanan. Holthausen AEEW 184; Vries ANEW 275.

OE hyll ‘hill’, Fris hel id., MDu hille id. Close to Lat collis ‘hill’ if the latter continues *k¬nis rather than *kolnis. See *xalluz ~ *xallaz. Zupitza Gutt. 106; Torp-Falk 82; Holthausen AEEW 183; Walde-Hofmann I 245; Pokorny I 544; Chemodanov SSGJa I 55; Onions 441.
Goth us-hulon ‘to hollow out’, ON hola ‘to make hollow’, OHG holōn id. Derived from *xulaz. Feist 533; Vries ANEW 247; Lehmann GED 383; Heidermanns 310.

ON holt ‘wood, grove’, OE holt id., OFris holt id., OS holt id., OHG holz ‘wood’. Related to Gk κλάδος ‘branch’, MIr caill ‘forest’, W celli id., Corn celli ‘nemus’ < Celt *kaldī and Slav *kolda ‘log’ (Bezzenberger BB XVI 240). Zupitza Gutt. 121; Torp-Falk 84; Holthausen AEEW 169; Trier Holz 43–51; Borck Festschr. Trier 456– 476; Pokorny I 546–547; Vries ANEW 248; Zalizniak I 141; Onions 445; Trubachev ESSJa X 122–123; Kluge- Seebold 381.

Goth hulþs ‘kind, clement’, ON hollr ‘gracious, faithful’, OE hold ‘kind, friendly, pleasant’, OFris hold ‘fair, faithful’, OS hold ‘kind, dear, devoted’, OHG hold ‘true, devoted’. Probably derived from *xalđanan (Mezger KZ LXXXIII 150–152). Karsten NB II 314–315; Wood AJPh XXIII 195–1956 (to Skt ≤rāmyati ‘to become weary’); Fortunatov BB VI 219 (to Skt ká†a- ‘twist’); Zupitza Gutt. 107; Torp-Falk 83; Feist 274; Güntert Kalypso 91 (to *xuljanan); Royen Festschr. Schrijnen 713–716; Holthausen AEEW 169; Trier Lehm 55–56; Pokorny I 552; Vries ANEW 247–248; Scardigli Goten 70–71, 105– 106; Mezger KZ LXXXIII 150–152 (to *xalþaz); Lehmann GED 193–194; Heidermanns 311–312; Kluge-Seebold 380.
