Burg *hulþi ‘favor’, ON hylli ‘grace, favor’, OE hyldu ‘kindness, favor’, OFris helde ‘goodwill, benevolence, favor’, OS huldi id., OHG huldī id. Derived from *xulþaz. Torp-Falk 83; Holthausen AEEW 183; Vries ANEW 274–275; Lehmann GED 193; Köbler 711; Heidermanns 311; Kluge-Seebold 386–387.
ON hylla ‘to court one’s friendship’, OHG hulden ‘to calm, to comfort’. Derived from *xulþaz. Torp- Falk 83; Heidermanns 312.

Goth un-hulþa ‘devil’, OE un-holda ‘fiend’, OFris holda ‘friend’, OS un-holdo ‘devil’, OHG holdo ‘friend’. Derived from *xulþaz. Feist 520–521; Lehmann GED 193; Bammesberger Nom. 179; Kluge-Seebold 848; Heidermanns 311.
Goth un-hulþo ‘fiend, she-devil’, OHG un-holda ‘monster’. See *xulþōn I. Feist 520–521; Lehmann GED 193; Kluge-Seebold 848; Heidermanns 311.
ON humarr ‘lobster’, LG hummer id. Structurally close to Gk κάμαρος ‘lobster, aconite’. Zupitza Gutt. 208; Torp-Falk 95; Pokorny I 558; Vries ANEW 266; Frisk I 773.

ON humall ‘hop-plant’, OE hymele id., MLG homele id. Together or via Slav *xъmelь id. borrowed from the East (Kuhn KZ XXXV 313–314), cf. Chuv x 6 mla id. < *qumlag. The latter goes back to Iranian: Osset xumællæg id. < *xaum-ala-ka- derived from *xauma- ‘sacred drink, soma’ (Trubachev ESSJa VIII 143–144). Late Lat humulus id. is probably from Gmc. Hirt PBB XXIII 338; Uhlenbeck Afsl Ph XV 485 (Slav < Gmc); Holthausen AEEW 184; Vries ANEW 266; Onions 447 (from Slav *xъmelь id.); Vasmer- Trubachev IV 249–250.

Norw dial. humla ‘bumble-bee’, MLG hummel id., OHG humbala id. Cannot be separated from Slav *‘ьmelь id. but the phonetic details are not clear. Further cf. Lith kaman E id., OPrus camus id. Zupitza Gutt. 127; Meillet MSL XIV 367; Torp-Falk 95; Lewy IF XXXII 162 (on OHG -b-); Vries ANEW 266; Fraenkel 212; Vasmer-Trubachev IV 459; Trubachev ESSJa IV 145–146; Toporov PJa III 200–202; Kluge-Seebold 387.

ON huppr ‘hip’, E hump, LG fem. humpe ‘limp’. Related to Skt kúmba- ‘thick end of a bone’, Gk κύμβη ‘hollow of a vessel’, κυμβός id., MIr comm ‘vessel’. Torp-Falk 94; Pokorny I 592; Vries ANEW 267; Frisk II 48.

ON hunang ‘honey’ (secondary -n-), OE huniz id., OFris hunig id., OS honig, OHG honag id. A taboo-motivated innovation based on a color adjective ‘yellow’, cf. Skt neut. kāñcaná- ‘gold, money, wealth’, Gk κνηκός ‘pale yellow’, OPrus cucan ‘brown’. Torp-Falk 93; Holthausen AEEW 178; Mayrhofer I 195; Pokorny I 564–565; Vries ANEW 266; Frisk II 882–883; Zalizniak I 141; Onions 446; Kluge-Seebold 382; Hilmarsson Mat. 181–182 (adds Toch B kro(n)k≤e ‘bee’).
ON hunang-sœtr ‘sweet as honey’, OE huniz-swéte id., MDu honich-soet id., MHG honec-süeze id. Compound of *xunazan and *swōtuz. Carr Nom. comp. 89 (parallel formations).

Goth hunda-faþs ‘centurion’. Identical with Skt ≤atá-pati- ‘captain’. Compound of *xunđan and *fađiz. Feist 276; Benveniste BSL LVIII 45 (independent late formation); Chemodanov SGGJa I 107; Lehmann GED 195.

Goth pl. hunda ‘hundred’, OE hund id., OFris hund id., OS hund id., OHG hunt id. Identical with Lyc sñta, Toch A känt, B kante, Skt neut. ≤atá- id., Av sat 6 m id., Gk ἵκατόν id., Lat centum id., OIr cét, Lith ši Mtas id., Slav *sъto id. Zupitza Gutt. 187; Bartholomae 1555; Torp-Falk 93; Trautmann BSW 305; Holthausen AEEW 178; Walde- Hofmann I 200–201; Feist 275–276; Mayrhofer III 293; Pokorny I 192; Fraenkel 984; Frisk I 475; Onions 452– 453; Vasmer-Trubachev III 761–762; Lehmann GED 194–195; Melchert St. Cowgill 202–203; Ross-Berns Numerals 620; Kluge-Seebold 388; Adams TB 139.

ON hundraä ‘hundred’, OE hundred id., OFris hundred id., OS hundrod, hunderod id., MHG hundert id. Compound of *xunđan and otherwise unattested *rađan identical with Lat ratum, part. of reor ‘to reckon, to believe’. See *raþjanan ~ *raþōjanan. Torp-Falk 93, 336; Walde-Hofmann II 429; Holthausen AEEW 178; Pokorny I 59, 192; Risch IF LXVII 129–141; Vries ANEW 267; Onions 452–453; Peeters KZ XCII 27–28; Bengston Diachronica IV 257–262; Ross-Berns Numerals 620 (difficult -o- in OS); Kluge-Seebold 388.
OSwed hundari ‘company (of one hundred)’, OHG huntari id. Derived from *xunđan. Derivationally cf. Lat centuria ‘division of troops, company’. Torp-Falk 93; Walde-Hofmann I 201; Lehmann GED 195.

Goth hunds ‘dog’, ON hundr id., OE hund id., OFris hund id., OS hund id., OHG hunt id. Continues *˚⁄–-toand is derivationally close to Latv deprec. sūnt-ene ‘big dog’ (Osthoff Etym. I 240–241; Persson Beiträge 585). Further related to the Indo-European word for ‘dog’: Toch AB ku, Skt ≤ván-, Av spā, Gk κύων, Lat canis, OIr cú, Lith šuõ (Kuhn KZ I 380; Förstermann KZ I 493). Hirt PBB XXII 231–232; Osthoff Etym. 240–241; Bugge KZ XXXII 54 (to Arm skund ‘little dog’ < *˚⁄on-tā); Zupitza Gutt. 187; Bartholomae 1610–1612; Torp- Falk 93; Holthausen AEEW 178; Walde-Hofmann I 152–153; Feist 276– 277; Specht 422; Mayrhofer III 402; Pokorny I 632–633; Fraenkel 1033– 1035; Vries ANEW 267; Frisk II 58–59; Zalizniak I 133; Onions 449; Hamp FLH IV 137–138; Lehmann GED 195; Melchert MSS L 97–101; Kluge-Seebold 388; Adams TB 179.

Goth huggrjan ‘to make to hunger’, ON hungra id., OE hynzran, hynzrian id., OFris hungera id., OS gi-hungrian id., OHG hungaren id. Derived from *xunzruz ~ *xunxruz. Holthausen AEEW 184; Feist 272; Onions 453; Lehmann GED 193.

Goth huhrus ‘hunger’, ON hungr id., OE hunzor id. (a-stem), OFris hunger id., OS hungar id. (a-stem), OHG hungar id. (a-stem). Derivationally close to Hitt tangarant- ‘not having eaten’ (with assimilation) and Lith keñkras ‘lean, emaciated’. Further to Skt kāṅkßati ‘to wish, to desire’, Gk κέγκει: peinō, Hom πολυκαγκής ‘parching, very dry’, κάγκανος ‘dry’, Lith kankā ‘pain, torture’ (Bezzenberger BB IV 357). Zupitza Gutt. 111, 132; Torp-Falk 70; Holthausen AEEW 178; Feist 273; Schulze Kl. Schr. 329; Mayrhofer I 194; Pokorny I 565; Fraenkel 240; Vries ANEW 267; Frisk I 750–751; Zalizniak II 201; Onions 453; Lehmann GED 193; Bammesberger Nom. 161–162; Puhvel IV 48; Kluge-Seebold 388.

Goth hunsl ‘sacrifice’, ON húsl ‘housel, eucharist’ (from OE?), OE húsel id. Probably continues *˚⁄–s-lo-, a noun in *-lo- derivationally close to Av spanah- ‘holiness’ < *˚⁄enes-. Further connected with Av sp 6 nta- ‘holy, saint’, Lith šveñtas id., Slav *svętъ id. (Fick I 767). Detter ZDADL XLII 55 (to *xunđan, cf. Gk ἵκατόμβη); Zupitza Gutt. 187; Grienberger Got. 122 (to Lat canō ‘to sing’); Bartholomae 1612, 1619– 1621; Torp-Falk 93; Bezzenberger BB XXVII 151 (to Lith šūsti ‘to stew’); Osthoff Festschr. Fick 252–253 (to *xenþanan); Mekler Festschr. Fick 263 (to Gk καίνω ‘to kill’); Kock ANF XV 326; Braune PBB XLIII 417; Rooth PBB XLIX 114–118 (follows Osthoff), Wortst. 108; Zupitza Gutt. 50, 187; Torp-Falk 93; Trautmann BSW 311; Holthausen AEEW 179; Feist 277; Trier PBB LXVI 234 (to *xansō); Porzig Gliederung 167; Pokorny I 630; Fraenkel 1041–1042; Vries ANEW 268 (ON not from OE; follows Trier); Zalizniak I 141; Vasmer- Trubachev III 585 (against Fick); Lehmann GED 195–196.
Goth hunþs ‘captivity, capture’, OE húäe ‘prey, booty’, OHG heri-hunda id. Derived from *xenþanan. Torp-Falk 70; Holthausen AEEW 179; Feist 277; Lehmann GED 196; Bammesberger Nom. 58.

Goth hups ‘hip’, OE hype id., MLG hup id., OHG fem. huf id. Close to Gk κύβος ‘hollow above the hips on cattle’, W gogof ‘hollow, den’ (< *upo-kubā). Zupitza Gutt. 208; Torp-Falk 93–94; Holthausen AEEW 184; Feist 277–278; Pokorny I 589–590; Frisk II 39–40; Zalizniak II 176; Onions 441; Lehmann GED 196; Bammesberger Nom. 130 (to Lat cubāre ‘to lie’); Kluge-Seebold 386.

ON hoppa ‘to hop, to skip’, OE hoppian ‘to hop, to leap’, MLG huppen id., MHG hupfen, hopfen id. Derived from *xupiz. Pedersen IF II 304; Zupitza Gutt. 121; Torp-Falk 94 (to Gk κυβιστάω ‘to tumble head foremost’); Holthausen AEEW 170; Vries ANEW 248–249; Onions 447; Sanders Festschr. de Smet 411–417 (*xupō(n) > OE hopa ‘hope’, OFris hope id., MLG hope id. is from *xuppōjanan); Kluge-Seebold 389.

Goth haurds ‘door’, ON hurä id., OE hyrd id., OS hurth ‘netting’, OHG hurd, hurt ‘lattice, fence’. Etymologically identical with Lat crātis ‘wicker-work’ and related to Skt kṛṇátti ‘to twist, to spin’, OPrus corto ‘fence’ and the like. Zupitza Gutt. 111; Torp-Falk 77; Schrader-Nehring II 557; Holthausen AEEW 184; Feist 250; Walde-Hofmann I 285–286; Mayrhofer I 257; Pokorny I 584; Vries ANEW 267–268; Zalizniak I 151; Onions 453; Lehmann GED 179– 180; Kluge-Seebold 389.

ON horr ‘mucus’, OE horh, horz ‘phlegm, rheum’, OS horu ‘excrements, dirt’, OHG horo id. Of imitative origin. Much ZDADL XLII 169 (to OIr corcach ‘swamp’); Lidén SVSU VI/1 50 (to Skt kalká- ‘paste, dirt, sin’); Zupitza Gutt. 72, 109; Torp-Falk 95; Holthausen AEEW 170; Schulze Kl. Schr. 113 (to Lith šiıvas ‘grey’); Krogmann KZ LXIV 130–136 (to Gk κέρχνος ‘rough excrescence’ and its cognates); Specht KZ LXIV 136– 137; Porzig Gliederung 111; Pokorny I 573; Vries ANEW 249; Zalizniak II 168.

ON horr ‘starvation’. Identical with Skt kṛ≤a- ‘emaciated, lean, weak’, Av k 6 r 6 sa- ‘lean, meager’ (in cmpn.) (Uhlenbeck PBB XXXV 172). Further to Lith kárštu, káršti ‘to reach old age, to grow decrepit’. Zupitza Gutt. 109; Bartholomae 469; Mayrhofer I 262; Pokorny I 581; Fraenkel 223–224, BS 60; Vries ANEW 249.

Goth hauri ‘coal, ember’, ON hyrr ‘embers of fire’ (partly *xuriz). Related to Lith kuriū, kūrti ‘to heat’, Slav *kuriti ‘to smoke’ (Scheftelwoitz BB XXVIII 305; Petersson Aufs. 40–41). Bugge KZ XXXII 51 (to Arm krak ‘fire’); Uhlenbeck Altind. Wb. 62 (to Gk καίω ‘to burn’); Zupitza Gutt. 114; Torp-Falk 75; Feist 250; Pokorny I 571–572; Fraenkel 319; Vries ANEW 275–276; Zalizniak II 213; Fraenkel 319; Lehmann GED 180; Trubachev ESSJa XIII 123–125; Dybo BSA 16.
ON horn-bogi ‘hornbow’, OE horn-boza id., MHG horn-boge id. Compound of *xurnan and *buzōn. Carr Nom. comp. 73 (parallel formations).
ON horn-fiskr ‘garfish’, OE horn-fisc id. Compound of *xurnan and *fiskaz. Carr Nom. comp. 74 (parallel formations).

Goth haurn ‘horn’, ON horn id., OE horn id. (masc.), OFris horn id., OS horn id., OHG horn id. Closely related to Lat cornū id., Skt neut. ≤ṛṅga- id., probably built on the unattested nominative in -g of the original *˚ṛn-. Belongs to IE *˚er- ‘head, horn’. Petersson SVSL I 5–7; Zupitza Gutt. 186; Torp-Falk 75–76; Holthausen AEEW 170; Walde- Hofmann I 276; Feist 251; Pokorny I 576; Mayrhofer III 369–370; Vries ANEW 249; Zalizniak I 141; Onions 448; Lehmann GED 180; Bammesberger Nom. 73; Nussbaum Head passim; Kluge- Seebold 383.

ON hornungr ‘bastard’, OE hornunz-sunu id., OFris horning id., MLG horninc id., OHG hornung ‘February’. Derived from *xurnan (Uhlenbeck KZ XL 555). Zupitza Gutt. 207 (to Gk κύρνος ‘bastard’, secondarily associated with *xurnan); Holthausen AEEW 170; Vries ANEW 249.
Goth haurnjan ‘to blow a horn’, OHG hurnen ‘to equip with horns’. Derived from *xurnjan. Feist 251; Lehmann GED 180.

ON hyrna ‘one of the horns of an axe-head’, OE hyrne ‘angle’, OFris herne id. Derived from *xurnan. For the semantic development cf. Slav *rogъ ‘horn, corner’. Torp-Falk 76; Holthausen AEEW 184; Vries ANEW 275.
ON horsk-ligr ‘brave, wise, noble’, OHG adv. horsc-līhho ‘lively, resolutely, quickly’. Derived from *xurskaz. Heidermanns 313.

ON horskr ‘wise’, OE horsc ‘quick, ready, active’, OS horsk ‘rapid, eager’, OHG horsc ‘swift, fast, lively’. Of unknown origin. Froehde BB XIV 105 (to Lat currō ‘to run’); Karsten NB II 294–295; Zupitza Gutt. 125 (to Lat scrūtāri ‘to investigate’); Torp-Falk 79; Schwentner PBB XLVIII 80–81; Feist 49; Holthausen AEEW 170; Pokorny I 579 (to Gk κέρδος ‘gain, advantage’, W cerdd ‘art’); Jóhannesson Suff. 29 (from *xurþ-skaz); Vries ANEW 249–250; Lehmann GED 37; Heidermanns 313.

Norw dial. rust ‘grove, wood’, OE hyrst ‘copse, wood’, MLG horst, hurst ‘bush’, OHG hurst id. Related to W prys ‘grove’ < *k ⁄ṛsto-, Slav *xvorstъ ‘bush, oak, firewood’ (with an unclear *x-). Torp-Falk 78; Pokorny I 633.

ON hosa ‘hose, gaiter’, OE hose id., OS hosa ‘trousers’, OHG hosa ‘hose, gaiter’. Of unknown origin. Zupitza Gutt. 128; Torp-Falk 96; Holthausen AEEW 171; Pokorny I 953 (to Gk κύστις ‘bladder’); Vries ANEW 250 (to Skt κóἑα- ‘cupboard’); Zalizniak II 177; Onions 448; Kluge-Seebold 385.

Goth huzd ‘treasure’, ON fem. hodd id., OE hord id., OS hord, horth id., OHG hort id. Of uncertain origin. Brugmann IF VI 103–104 (together with Gk κύσθος ‘female genitals’, from *kudh-to-); Johansson IF XIX 129–130; Schmidt KZ XXV 166–167; Zupitza Gutt. 96, 128; Torp-Falk 96; Debrunner IF LII 228; Holthausen AEEW 170; Feist 278–279; Pokorny I 953 (to *xūđjanan); Vries ANEW 246; Zalizniak II 174; Onions 443; Lehmann GED 196; Kluge-Seebold 384 (to Gk κεῦθος ‘depth, inside’).
Goth huzdjan ‘to treasure up, to store up, to gather’, OE hordian ‘to hoard, to store’, MHG gi-hurten id. Derived from *xuzđan. Feist 278; Lehmann GED 196.

ON húfa ‘cap, hood’, OE húfe ‘covering for the head’, OFris hūve, houwe ‘cap, hood’, OS hūva id., OHG hūba id. Related to Skt kakúbh- ‘peak, summit’, Gk k:υφός ‘bent, crooked’, Slav *kubъ ‘vessel, pot’ or to Skt kūpa- ‘hole, cave, well’, Gk κύπη: τρώγλη (Hes.), Lat cūpa ‘barrel’. Zupitza Gutt. 104, 128; Torp-Falk 94; Holthausen AEEW 177, 183; Walde- Hofmann I 310–311; Mayrhofer I 135, 253; Pokorny I 590–591; Vries ANEW 264–265; Onions 442; Frisk II 52–53; Zalizniak II 180; Trubachev ESSJa XIII 77–78; Kluge-Seebold 360.

ON húä ‘hide, skin’, OE hÿd id., OFris hēde, hud id., OS hūd id., OHG hūt id. Related to Gk κύτος ‘cover, hide’, Lat cutis ‘hide’, W cwd ‘bag, testicle’, Lith κυτίσ ‘bag’ (Siebs KZ XXXVII 282). Cf. also WGmc *xuþan ‘testicle’: OFris hotha, MDu hode, OHG hodo. Torp-Falk 92; Holthausen AEEW 183; Walde- Hofmann I 320; Pokorny I 952; Fraenkel 323; Vries ANEW 264; Frisk II 57; Zalizniak I 151; Onions 440; Lehmann GED 196; Kluge-Seebold 361; Dybo VSJa 12, BSA 35–36 (with shortening in the oxytonic stem).

OE hÿdan ‘to hide’, OFris hēda id., MLG hūden id. Related to Gk κεύθω ‘to cover, to hide’, κεκύθω ‘to conceal’ (Nowicki KZ XCII 187). Torp-Falk 92; Holthausen AEEW 183; Pokorny I 952; Frisk I 834; Onions 440; Lehmann GED 310.

ON húka ‘to sit on one’s hams’ (but part. hokinn indicates a str.vb.), MLG hūken id., MHG hūchen id. Probably based on *xawwanan. Torp-Falk 91; Kluge-Seebold 378; Adams TB 180 (to Toch B kuk- ‘to bow down’).

ON pl. Húnar, Hÿnir ‘Huns’, OE pl. Húnas id., OHG pl. Hūni id. Attested in Lat Hunnī, Chunnī, Gk Ο[unclear]ννοι. Adaptation of Chinese Hiung-nu. Much Wu S I 45 (secondarily influenced by *xūnaz ~ *xūnō); Schrader-Nehring I 517; Vries ANEW 266.

ON húnn ‘knob, bear-cub’, MDu hune ‘crow’s nest’. Identical with Lith kūnas ‘body, meat’, Latv kūnis ‘body, chrysalis’. Zupitza Gutt. 208; Torp-Falk 92–93 (to Skt ≤ūná- ‘swollen’); Pokorny I 592; Fraenkel 310; Vries ANEW 267.
Icel hús-laukr ‘houseleek’, late ME house-leek id., MLG hūs-lōk id., OHG hūs-louh id. Compound of *xūsan and *laukaz. Carr Nom. comp. 74 (Icel < OHG).
ON hús-lauss ‘without a house’, OFris hūs-lās id., MHG hūs-lōs id. Compound of *xūsan and *lausaz. Carr Nom. comp. 89 (parallel formations).

Goth gud-hus ‘temple’, Crim. Goth hus ‘house’, ON hús id., OE hús id., OFris hūs id., OS hūs id., OHG hūs id. Borrowed from a phonetically advanced East Iranian *xuz ~ *xud < Iran *kata-, cf. Av kata- ‘room, cellar’ (also borrowed to ESlav *xata ‘house’, probably via OHung). Note a similarity of *xūsan and Yns *xús ‘tent of skin and bark’, probably explained by the Iranian origin of the latter. Iran *kata- may be also in some obscure relation with Gmc *xēþjōn > Goth heþjo ‘chamber’ (Bartholomae Zd Wf VI 355). Grimm DM I 922 (from *kūdh-s-o, cf. Gk κεῦθος ‘hiding-place’); Schmidt KZ XXV 166 (same as Grimm); Bezzenberger BB XXVGII 146 (to Lith kūtīs ‘stable’); Patrubány IF XIII 163; Zupitza Gutt. 128; Grienberger Got. 112 (reconstructs Goth heþjo < *heiþjo); Bartholomae 432; Torp-Falk 92 (to *xūđjanan); Holthausen AEEW 178 (to Skt kóßa- ‘cupboard’); Feist 223–224 (to Hitt kuttaš ‘wall’), 254–255 (Goth heþjo to OFris hōde ‘supervision, protection’, MLG hōde id., OHG huot id.); Pokorny I 586–587, 953 (related to *xusōn); Vries ANEW 268; Zalizniak I 141; Onions 450; Lehmann GED 161 (to *ke⁄H- ‘to cover, to surround’), 182; Starostin KS 155; Trubachev ESSJa VIII 21–22; Kluge-Seebold 360; Adams TB 234 (to Toch B kwaßo ‘village’).
ON hÿsa ‘to house, to harbor’, OFris hūsia id., MLG husen id. Derived from *xūsan. Vries ANEW 276.
ON húsa ‘to build houses’, OE húsian ‘to house’, MDu husen, huysen id., MHG hūsen ‘to build a house’. Derived from *xūsan. Vries ANEW 268.
Goth ƕadre ‘whereto, whither’, OE hwæder ‘whither’. Derived from the pronominal stem *xwa- (see *xwat). Holthausen AEEW 179; Feist 279; Lehmann GED 197.

Swed dial. hven ‘low and swampy field’. Close to Lat caenum, coenum ‘dirt’ < *k ⁄oino- (Zupitza Gutt. 53). Torp-Falk 118; Walde-Hofmann I 131– 132; Pokorny I 628; Chemodanov SSGJa I 55; Vries ANEW 270.
ON hveiti-korn ‘grain of wheat’, OE hwæte-corn id. Compound of *xwaitjaz and *kurnan. Carr Nom. comp. 74 (parallel formations).
ON hveiti-mjǫl ‘flour’, OE hwæte-melu id. Compound of *xwaitjaz and *melwan. Carr Nom. comp. 74 (parallel formations).

Goth ƕaiteis ‘wheat’, ON neut. hveiti id., OE hwæte id., OFris hwēte id., OS hwēti id., OHG (h)weizi id. Connected with *xwītaz, cf. Bret gwiniz ‘wheat’ ~ gwenn ‘white’ (Hoops Waldbäume 356). Wiedemann BB XXVII 213 (to Slav *cvьtǫ ‘to blossom’); Meyer Alb. St. III 51 (to Gk σῖτος ‘grain’ borrowed from a sat 6 m-language); Zupitza Gutt. 54; Torp-Falk 118; Holthausen AEEW 179; Feist 280–281; Pokorny I 629; Vries ANEW 270; Zalizniak II 168; Onions 1001; Lehmann GED 197; Ramat Gedenkschr. Güntert 71; Bammesberger Nom. 67; Kluge-Seebold 884.

OE hwæz ‘whey’, WFris waei id., MDu wey id. Identical with Skt káya- ‘body’ further connected with cinóti ‘to put in layers, to thread, to string’, Gk ποίεω ‘to make, to produce’. Holthausen AEEW 179 (to Arm ši‘uk id.); Pokorny I 637–638; Onions 1002.
ON hval-fiskr ‘whale’, MLG wal-visch id., OHG wal-fisc id. Compound of *xwalaz and *fiskaz. Carr Nom. comp. 84 (parallel formations).

ON hvalr ‘whale’ (partly *xwaliz), OE hwæl id., OS hwal id., OHG hwal, wal id. Of unknown origin. Lidén Festschr. Bugge 91 (to Lat squalus ‘big sea fish’), GHÅ XXVI/2 91 (to OPrus kalis ‘sheat-fish’); Petersson SVSL I 80 (from *˚⁄ob-lo-); Zupitza Gutt. 55; Torp-Falk 117; Holthausen AEEW 179; Pokorny I 958; Vries ANEW 268–269; Zalizniak II 187; Onions 1000–1001 (to OPrus kalis); Stang LS 26; Sevilla Rodriguez JIES XVII 177–180 (to Av kara-, Gk ἄσπαλος); Kluge-Seebold 872.

ON hválf ‘concavity’, OE fem. hwealf ‘arched covering’, OS hwolvo ‘hollow brick’, OHG walbo id. Related to Gk κόλπος ‘bosom, hollow’. Derived from *xwelbanan, *xwalbaz. Zupitza Gutt. 54; Torp-Falk 117; Holthausen AEEW 180; Pokorny I 630; Vries ANEW 268; Frisk I 904–905; Onions 1001; Seebold 281; Lehmann GED 200; Kluge-Seebold 873.
Icel í-hvolfr ‘something vaulted’, OE hwealf ‘arched, vaulted’. Derived from *xwelbanan. Karsten NB I 41–42; Wissmann NP 25; Holthausen AEEW 180; Heidermanns 314.

ON hvelfa ‘to arch, to vault’ (strong part. holfinn), OE be-hwylfan ‘to cover’, OS bi-hwelbian ‘to hide’, OHG bi-welben ‘to surround, to enclose’. Derived from *xwelbanan. Torp-Falk 117; Holthausen AEEW 182; Vries ANEW 271; Onions 1001; Seebold 281; Lehmann GED 200; Kluge-Seebold 896.
ON hválfa ‘to turn upside down’, OHG zisamane-gi-walbōn ‘to come together, to gather’. Derived from *xwelbanan, *xwalbaz. Seebold 281.

Goth ƕan ‘when’, OE hwænne, hwanne id., OFris hwenne id., OS hwan id., OHG hwanne, wanne, wenne id. Derived from *xwa- (see *xwat). Cf. OIr can, W pan id. and probably OPrus kan ‘when’. Schmidt KZ XXXII 402 (instr. in *-n- ~ Av -nā); Paul PBB IV 385–388 (to Av k 6 m ‘how’, OLat quom ‘when’); Dal Pr. 39–40 (WGmc loc. in *-n-); Torp- Falk 114; Holthausen AEEW 180; Feist 281; Pokorny I 644–645; Onions 1001; Lehmann GED 198; Kluge-Seebold 874.

ON hvap ‘dropsical flesh’. Originally, *‘steam, vapor’. Despite the irregular dvelopment of *-p-, related to Gk κάπος: ψυχή, πνεῦμα (Hes.), Lith κωάπασ ‘breath’ further connected with IE *k⁄ep- ‘to breathe’. Curtius Gr. Et. 142 (to Lat uappa ‘flat wine’); Feist 7; Pokorny I 596; Fraenkel 325–326; Frisk I 781–782; Lehmann GED 5.
Goth af-ƕapjan ‘to choke, to extinguish’, MHG ver-wepfen ‘to become fusty (of wine)’. Derived from *xwapan. Torp-Falk 115; Feist 7; Lehmann GED 5.

Goth ƕar ‘where’, ON hvar id., OE hwær id., OFris hwēr id., OS hwār id., OHG hwar, wār id. Derived from the pronominal stem *xwa- (see *xwat). The suffix *-r- appears in Arm ur ‘where’, Lat cūr, OLat quōr ‘why’, Lith kuı ‘where’, Alb kur ‘when’. Goth ƕarjis ‘which’, ON hverr ‘who’ continue *xwar jaz with the second component from *jaz < *ƒos ‘who, which’: Skt yáъ, Av yō, Gk ὅς (Schmidt KZ XXXII 401, Plur. 43). A similar structure is reconstructed for Alb kush ‘who’ < *k ⁄u-so- (Meillet Idg. Jb. I 13; Orel LB XXX/1 57–58). Bartholomae 1199–1227; Torp-Falk 114; Holthausen AEEW 179; Walde- Hofmann I 313; Feist 281–282; Mayrhofer III 14; Pokorny I 644–645; Fraenkel 314; Vries ANEW 269, 272; Onions 1002; Lehmann GED 198; Kluge-Seebold 895; Orel AED 206– 207.

ON hvarf ‘disappearance, circle’, OE hwearf ‘turn, space, change, corwd’, OFris hwarf ‘time’, OS hwarf ‘crowd’, OHG fem. warba ‘turn’. Derived from *xwerbanan. Torp-Falk 116; Holthausen AEEW 180; Pokorny I 631; Vries ANEW 269; Zalizniak II 169.

ON hverfa ‘to make to turn’, OE hwirfan ‘to turn, to revolve’, OS gi-hwervian id., OHG werben id. Derived from *xwerbanan. Torp-Falk 116; Holthausen AEEW 182; Vries ANEW 271; Seebold 283; Lehmann GED 197.

Goth ƕarbon ‘to walk about, to wander’, ON hvarfa ‘to turn round, to wander’, OE hwearfian ‘to turn, to change’, OS hwarbōn ‘to walk about’, OHG warbōn id. Derived from *xwarban ~ *xwarbaz. Torp-Falk 116; Holthausen AEEW 180; Feist 282; Vries ANEW 269; Seebold 283; Lehmann GED 197.

Goth adv. ƕassaba ‘sharply’, ON hvass ‘pointed, sharp’, OE hwæs ‘sharp, keen’, OHG hwas ‘sharp’. Derived from *xwataz, *xwētanan. Aufrecht KZ I 472–474 (to Lat catus ‘sharp’); Karsten NB II 344; Zupitza Gutt. 56; Torp-Falk 115; Feist 282; Holthausen AEEW 179; Pokorny I 636; Vries ANEW 269; Seebold 284; Bammesberger Nom. 251–252; Heidermanns 315–316; Kluge-Seebold 888.
Goth ƕassei ‘sharpness’, OS hwessi id., OHG wassī id. Derived from *xwassaz. Feist 282; Heidermanns 315.

Goth ƕa ‘what’ (loss of -t in an unaccented position?), ON hvat id., OE hwæt id., OFris hwet id., OS hwat id., OHG hwaz id. Identical with IE *k ⁄od id.: Skt kad, Lat quod. The loc. of the same stem (identical with Lith kaĩ ‘how’) appears in *xwai wē > Goth ƕaiwa ‘how’, OHG hwio id. where *wē goes back to IE *⁄ē ‘or’: Skt vā, Av vā, cf. also a variant with short *e in Gk ἠ-ϝέ, Lat -ue. Möller Idg. Anz. XXXIII 58 (derives Goth ƕa from *k ⁄om); 79; Schulze Lat. 5 (Goth ƕ-aiwa to Skt éva- ‘walk’); Torp- Falk 114; Bartholomae 1306–1310; Hujer IF XXIV 71 (*xwai wē < *k ⁄o-oi⁄os, to Gk ποῖος ‘of what kind’); Walde- Hofmann II 411, 740; Feist 279, 281; Mezger JEGP XLI 508–509 (reconstructs *xwīxwē); Mayrhofer I 192, III 180; Pokorny I 75, 644–646; Vries ANEW 269; Onions 1001; Hollifield Sprache XXV 55 (reconstructs *xwai wō); Lehmann GED 197–199; Kluge-Seebold 876, 885, 889.

ON hvatr ‘bold, active, vigorous’, OE hwæt ‘quick, active, vigorous’, OS mēn-hwāt ‘nefarious’. Derived from *xwētanan. Karsten NB I 56; Jurmann KZ XI 399 (to Lat triquētrus ‘threecornered’); Zupitza Gutt. 56; Torp-Falk 115; Holthausen AEEW 179; Endzelin KZ LI 290 (to Latv skadrs ‘sharp’); Feist 184–185; Pokorny I 636; Vries ANEW 270; Onions 1002; Seebold 284; Lehmann GED 139; Heidermanns 316; Kluge- Seebold 887–888.

Goth ga-ƕotjan ‘to whet, to sharpen’ (leg. ga-ƕatjan), ON hvetja ‘to whet, to sharpen, to encourage’, OE hwettan id., MLG wetten ‘to sharpen’, OHG hwezzen, wezzen id. Derived from *xwataz. Torp-Falk 115; Feist 184; Vries ANEW 272; Onions 1002; Seebold 284; Lehmann GED 139; Heidermanns 316; Kluge-Seebold 887–888.

Goth ƕaþar ‘which of two’, ON hvaäarr id., OE hwæäer, hweäer id., OFris hweder, hwedder id., OS hwethar id., OHG wedar id. Comparative in *-ter- based on *xwa- (see *xwat). Identical with Skt katará- id., Av katārō id., Gk πότερος id., Osc pútúrúspíd ‘utrique’, Lith katrās ‘which of two’, Slav *kotorъ ‘which’. Bartholomae 433– 434; Torp-Falk 114; Trautmann BSW 120–121; Holthausen AEEW 179–180; Feist 283; Mayrhofer I 148; Pokorny I 645–646; Fraenkel 229; Vries ANEW 268; Frisk II 586; Onions 1002; Lehmann GED 199; Kluge-Seebold 878.

Goth ƕaþ ‘where to, whither’. From IE *k ⁄o-te similar to Hitt kuwatta(n) ‘where’ and, probably, Gk πόσε ‘where to’ (Sturtevant AJPh L 364). See *xwat. Meillet MSL XIX 93 (on the suffix *-te); Feist 282–283; Chantraine 921–922; Tischler HEG 698–700; Lehmann GED 199.
Goth ƕaþo ‘foam’, Swed dial. hwā id. Connected with Skt kváthati ‘to boil’. Zupitza Gutt. 56 (to Lat quatiō ‘to shake’); Feist 283; Mayrhofer I 283; Pokorny I 627–628; Lehmann GED 199.

Goth ƕas ‘who’, ON hverr id., OSwed hvā, hvār id., OE hwá id., OFris hwā id., OS hwē id., OHG hwer id. Continues IE *k ⁄os id.: Skt káъ, Av kō, Lith kās. See *xwat. Bartholomae 422–427; Torp-Falk 114; Holthausen AEEW 179; Feist 282; Porzig Gliederung 198; Mayrhofer I 192; Pokorny I 644– 645; Fraenkel 199; Vries ANEW 271; Onions 1004; Lehmann GED 198–199; Kluge-Seebold 885.

ON hjól, hvel ‘wheel’, OE hweól, hweozul, hweowol id., OFris fial id., MLG wēl id. Continues IE *k ⁄ek ⁄lo- id.: Toch A kukäl, B kokale ‘cart, wagon’, Skt cakrá- ‘wheel’, Av ‘axrō id., Gk κύκλος ‘circle’ (Reichelt IF XL 58), Phryg κίκλην: τὴν ἄρκτον τό ἄστρον. Φρύγες, Hes. (Fick BB XXIX 239). Olson ANF XXXI 14; Zupitza Gutt. 65; Bartholomae 576; Torp-Falk 116–117; Holthausen AEEW 181; Mayrhofer I 366; Pokorny I 639–640; Vries ANEW 232, 270; Frisk II 44–45; Onions 1001; Adams TB 200; Burlak IFTJa 241–242.

ON hvika ‘to quail, to shrink, to waver’, MDu wicken ‘to move back and forth’. Of descriptive origin. Hirt BB XXIV 253 (to Slav *‘eznǫti ‘to disappear’); Zupitza Gutt. 58 (to Lat conquinīscō ‘to bend, to squat’), 197; Torp-Falk 114–115; Vries ANEW 272.

ON part. holfinn ‘to curve’, OFris bi-wolven ‘obrutus’ (hap. leg.), MHG pret. sg. walb ‘to expand’. See *xwalban ~ *xwalbōn. Torp-Falk 117; Jóhannesson IEW 263; Pokorny I 630; Vries ANEW 247; Seebold 281.
Icel hvella ‘to ring out’, OE hwelan ‘to roar, to bellow’. Probably related to Toch B kul ‘bell’ (Adams TB 185). Torp-Falk 116; Holthausen AEEW 180; Vries ANEW 271 (to *xwelpaz); Heidermanns 318.
ON hvellr ‘shrilling, thrilling’, OHG hwel ‘importunate’. Derived from *xwellanan. Karsten NB II 219; Zupitza Gutt. 49, 57; Torp-Falk 116; Heidermanns 318.

ON hvelpr ‘whelp’, OE hwelp id., OS hwelp id., OHG neut. welf id. A phonetically irregular development of IE *g ⁄elbh-: Skt gárbha- ‘womb’, Av gar 6 va- id., Gk δελφύς id. The Germanic form appears to reflect a consonantally shifted *k ⁄elb-. Suolahti ANT 17–18; Zupitza Gutt. 57; Schröder Zd Ph XXXVII 393–394; Torp-Falk 117 (to Gk σκύλαξ ‘cub, puppy’); Schrader- Nehring II 336 (to Lat uolpēs ‘fox’ < *g ⁄olpi-); Holthausen AEEW 181; Mayrhofer I 329; Pokorny I 473; Vries ANEW 271 (to *xwellanan); Frisk I 363; Zalizniak I 133; Onions 1001; Kluge- Seebold 884.

ON hverr ‘cauldron’, OE hwer ‘kettle, pot’, OHG hwer, wer ‘cauldron’. Related to Toch B kerū ‘drum’, Skt carú- ‘vessel, pot’, OIr coire ‘kettle’, W pair id., Corn pêr id. Zupitza Gutt. 57; Torp-Falk 116; Holthausen AEEW 181; Mayrhofer I 377; Pokorny I 642; Vries ANEW 272; Zalizniak I 133; Lehmann GED 197; Van Windekens I 216; Adams TB 195.

ON hverfull ‘shifty, changeable’, OE hwurful ‘changeable, fickle’, OHG sina-werbal ‘rounded’. Derived from *xwerbanan. Karsten NB II 286; Holthausen AEEW 183; Bammesberger Festschr. Derolez 26– 29; Heidermanns 319–320.

Goth ƕairban ‘to go around, to walk, to live, περιπατεῖν’, ON hverfa ‘to turn round’, OE hweorfan ‘to turn, to change’, OFris hwerva ‘to turn’, OS hwerban ‘to go back and forth’, OHG werban ‘to turn’. Related to Toch B kurp- ‘to be concerned with’ (Adams TB 184– 185). Kirchhoff KZ I 39; Schrader KZ X 473 (to Gk καρπός ‘wrist’); Zupitza Gutt. 57 (to Skt neut. ≤ūrpa- ‘winnowing basket’); Hirt BB XXIV 253 (to Slav *svьrdlo ‘drill’ < *svьrb-dlo); Fick BB XXIX 239 (to Phryg Κορύβαντες); Torp- Falk 116; Feist 279–280; Holthausen AEEW 181; Jóhannesson IEW 261–262; Pokorny I 631; Vries ANEW 271; Seebold 282–284; Lehmann GED 197; Kluge-Seebold 885.

Goth ƕeila-ƕairbs ‘temporary, transient’, ON hverfr ‘shifty’, OE hweorf ‘turning about, shifting’, OHG sina-werf ‘smooth, polished’. Derived from *xwerbanan. Karsten NB I 24–25; Holthausen AEEW 181; Feist 284; Seebold 282; Bammesberger Nom. 241; Heidermanns 318–319.
ON hvirfill ‘circle, ring, crown of the head’, OHG wirbil ‘plectrum’. Derived from *xwerbanan. Torp- Falk 116; Vries ANEW 273; Onions 1003; Seebold 282; Lehmann GED 197; Kluge-Seebold 893.
ON hvirfla ‘to whirl’, MDu wervelen, worvelen id. Derived from *xwerbilaz. Vries ANEW 273.

Goth ƕairnei ‘skull’, ON hverna ‘pan, basin’. Close to MIr cern ‘bowl’ < IE *k ⁄ernā and further connected with *xweraz. Hirt IF XVII 390 (to Gk κέρνος ‘bowl for offerings’); Zupitza Gutt. 58; Torp-Falk 116; Feist 280; Pokorny I 642; Chemodanov SGGJa I 98; Vries ANEW 271; Lehmann GED 197.

Goth ƕe ‘to whom, wherewith’, ON hvé ‘how’. Old instrumental *k ⁄ē, cf. Gk πήποκα ‘ever yet’ (Schmidt KZ XXVII 292, XXXII 403). Feist 284; Pipping GHÅ XI/3 33 (to *xwai wē, see *xwat); Lindquist Festschr. A. Kock 359– 367 (ON hvé < *xwena); Pokorny I 644– 645; Vries ANEW 270; Lehmann GED 199.
ON hvæsa ‘to hiss’ (wk.), OE hwésan ‘to wheeze’. Related to Skt ≤vásiti ‘to breathe’, Lat queror ‘to complain, to lament’. Torp-Falk 117; Walde-Hofmann II 403–404; Pokorny I 631.

ON hváta ‘to hasten’, OE pret. sg. á-hwét ‘to drive out’, OS pres. far-hwātan ‘to punch, to kick’, OHG wāzan ‘to drive away’. Related to Latv kûdît ‘to drive’, Slav *kydati ‘to throw’. Cf. *xwataz, *xwatjanan. Zupitza Gutt. 56; Feist 286; Holthausen AEEW 179; Jóhannesson IEW 281–282; Pokorny I 636; Vries ANEW 270; Seebold 284– 285.

ON hviäa ‘fit’, OE hweoäu ‘air, breeze’. Close to Av šyāta- ‘glad, merry’, Lat quiēs ‘rest, lying still’. Bartholomae 1716; Torp-Falk 118; Holthausen AEEW 172; Walde-Hofmann II 406; Pokorny I 638; Vries ANEW 272 (to *xwataz).
Goth ƕi-leiks ‘what sort of, which’, ON hví-líkr id., OE hwelc, hwilc id., OFris hwe-lik, hwelk id., OS hwilīk id., OHG hwelīh id. Derived from

Gk τίς ‘who’, Lat quis id. Åkerlund Festschr. Kock 414–428 (original short vowel in ON); Torp-Falk 114; Holthausen AEEW 180–181; Walde-Hofmann II 410; Feist 285; Pokorny I 644–648; Vries ANEW 272 (secondary length in ON hví-); Onions 1002; Frisk II 903–904; Lehmann GED 200; Kluge-Seebold 884 (< *xwe-līkaz).
ON hvísla ‘to whisper’, OE hwistlian ‘to hiss, to whistle’. Of imitative nature. Holthausen AEEW 182; Onions 1004.
ON hví ‘why’, OE hwÿ id., OS hwī id. From *k ⁄ei, loc. of *k ⁄o-, see *xwat. Cf. Lat quī ‘who, which’, Slav *‘i ‘how, whether’. Holthausen AEEW 181; Walde-Hofmann II 405; Vries ANEW 272; Onions 1005.

Goth ƕeilan ‘to pause, to cease’, ON hvíla ‘to rest’, OFris hwīla id., MLG wīlen ‘to stay’, OHG wīlēn ‘to remain in a place’. Derived from *xwīlō. Zupitza Gutt. 49; Torp-Falk 117; Feist 284; Vries ANEW 272; Lehmann GED 199; Kluge-Seebold 882.

Goth ƕeila ‘while, time, hour’, ON hvíla ‘bed’ (*xwilōn), OE hwíl ‘while, time’, OFris hwīle id., OS hwīla id., OHG hwīla, wīla id. Derivationally close to Slav *‘ilъ ‘lively, strong’. Further based on IE *k ⁄eƒH- ‘to rest, to be quiet’. Torp-Falk 117; Feist 284; Holthausen AEEW 182; Wissmann NP 72; Pokorny I 638; Vries ANEW 272; Zalizniak II 180; Onions 1002; Nyman IF LXXXIV 132–156; Trubachev ESSJa IV 112; Lehmann GED 199–200; Heidermanns 319; Kluge-Seebold 882.

ON hvína ‘to whizz, to whistle’, OE pres. hwínan id. Further cf. *xwīōjan > ON hvía ‘to squeal’, *xwajōjan > OHG hweiōn id. Of imitative origin. Torp-Falk 118; Holthausen AEEW 182; Jóhannesson IEW 258–259; Pokorny I 628; Vries ANEW 273; Onions 1003; Seebold 280.

Goth ƕeits ‘white’, ON hvítr id., OE hwít id., OFris hwīt id., OS hwīt id., OHG hwīz id. Corresponds (with a different suffix or an irregular dental) to IE *˚wei-t- in other languages: Skt ≤vetá- ‘white, bright’, Av spaēta- id., Lith švie‘iū, švi‚sti ‘to gleam’, Slav *svîtъ ‘light’. Bartholomae 1609; Karsten NB II 205–206; Zupitza Gutt. 54; Torp-Falk 118; Schwentner FB 24–37; Trautmann BSW 310–311; Feist 284–285; Holthausen AEEW 182; Mayrhofer III 406; Pokorny I 629; Fraenkel 1043; Vries ANEW 273–274; Onions 1004; Vasmer-Trubachev III 575–576; Lühr Expr. 263–264; Lehmann GED 200; Heidermanns 316–317; Kluge-Seebold 883.
ON hvítill ‘white bedcover’, OE hwítel ‘cloak, mantle’. Derived from *xwītaz. Holthausen AEEW 182; Vries ANEW 273.
ON hvítingr ‘a kind of whale’, E whiting, MLG wītink id. Derived from *xwītaz. Vries ANEW 273.
ON hvíti ‘whiteness’, OHG wīzī ‘white color, white spot’. Derived from *xwītaz. Heidermanns 317.
Swed dial. hwīte ‘wheat’, ME whīte id. An ablaut variant of *xwaitjaz. Cf. also G dial. wi 6 te id. < *xwitjō (Holthausen IF XXXII 338). Lehmann GED 197.
Goth ga-hweitjan ‘to whiten’, OE hwítan ‘to make white, to polish’, OHG wīzen ‘to paint white’. Derived from *xwītaz. Feist 284; Onions 1004; Heidermanns 317.

Goth ƕopan ‘to boast’, OE hwópan ‘to threaten’. Of unknown origin. Grienberger Got. 125 (to *xaupaz); Bloomfield PBB XXXVII 251 (formed after *wōpan ~ *wōpaz, *wōpjanan); Thumb KZ XVI 193–196 (to Gk κοβαλός ‘impudent rogue’); Uhlenbeck PBB XXX 294 (to Lat cupiō ‘to desire’); Petersson PBB XXXVIII 321 (to Skt kváṇati ‘to sound’); Feist 286; Holthausen AEEW 182; Kroes Neophilologus XXXI 14–16 (to Lith kvãpas ‘vapor’); Pokorny I 535–536 (from *ka⁄-); Seebold 285 (to *xwētanan); Lehmann GED 200–201.

OE hwósan ‘to wheeze’. Identical with Skt kāsate ‘to cough’, Lith kósiu, kósëti id. Further cf. Toch B kosi ‘cough’, Alb kollë id. (< *k ⁄āslā), MIr cassacht id., Lith kosul‹s id., Slav *kašlь id. Torp-Falk 118; Holthausen AEEW 182; Mayrhofer I 206; Pokorny I 649; Fraenkel 283–284; Seebold 285; Orel AED 189; Adams TB 207.
ON hósta ‘to cough’, OE hwóstan id., MDu hoesten id., OHG huostōn id. Derived from *xwōstōn. Torp-Falk 118; Vries ANEW 250.

ON hósti ‘cough’, OE hwósta id., MLG hōste id., OHG huosto id. Structurally close to W pas id. < *k ⁄6 s-t-. Derived from *xwōsanan. Zupitza Gutt. 58; Torp-Falk 118; Holthausen AEEW 182; Pokorny I 649; Vries ANEW 250; Zalizniak I 154; Seebold 285; Trubachev ESSJa IX 160–161; Bammesberger Nom. 185; Kluge-Seebold 389.

Goth ƕota ‘threat, reprimand’, ON neut. pl. hót id. Derived from *xwētanan, *xwatjanan. Aufrecht KZ I 471; Bloomfield PBB XXXVII 250 (rhyme word); Feist 286; Mayrhofer I 400–401 (to Skt códati ‘to incite, to animate’); Pokorny I 636 (to Lat tri-quetrus ‘three-cornered’); Vries ANEW 250; Seebold 284; Lehmann GED 201.
Goth ƕotjan ‘to threaten, to reprimand’, ON hœta ‘to threaten’. Derived from *xwōtō. Aufrecht KZ I 471; Torp-Falk 115; Feist 286; Vries ANEW 250, 278; Seebold 284; Lehmann GED 201.
ON iä ‘deed, doing’. Derivationally close to Skt ití- ‘going, walking’, further to *ei- ‘to go’. Torp-Falk 27; Pokorny I 293–295.

ON iäur-mæltr ‘of a meter repeating the same syllable’, MDu ederkauwen ‘to repeat over and over’. Identical with Lat iterum ‘again’ derived from *i-tero-: Skt ítara- ‘other’. Torp-Falk 27; Walde- Hofmann I 723–724; Pokorny I 284.

ON ígull ‘hedgehog, ruff’ (with secondary lengthening), OE izil ‘hedgehog’, OS igil id., OHG igil id. The name of a prickly animal or fish is etymologically identical with the isolated Slav *jьgla ‘needle’ < *ighulā and, probably, OPrus ayculo < *eighulā. On the other hand, it could be influenced by the continuants of IE *e·hi- ‘hedgehog’. Försterman KZ I 498 (to *ezalaz); Pictet KZ VI 185–186; Wrede Ostg. 144 (adds Goth PN Igila); Fick BB XXIX 237; ī
Goth ei ‘in that case, then, thereby’. Identical with Slav *i ‘and’ < *ei (Feist 130). Sütterlin IF IV 93–95 (to IE *ƒo-); Pokorny I 281–284; Lehmann GED 99.

OE ídel ‘vain, empty, void’, OFris īdel ‘foolish, vain, empty’, OS īdal ‘vain, empty’, OHG ītal ‘empty’. Of unknown origin. Karsten NB II 275–276; Wood MLN XVII 6, XXV 213; Torp- Falk 3 (to IE *aidh- ‘to burn’); Van Wijk IF XXXV 266; Sturtevant Language VI 25 (to Hitt idalu- ‘bad, evil’); Holthausen AEEW 185; Pokorny I 12 (to Gk αἰθάλη ‘soot’); Onions 460; Bonfante BSL LXIX 69–71 (same as Brüch Festschr. Kretschmer 10–11; Torp- Falk 23; Trautmann APSpr. 296, BSW 3; Holthausen AEEW 187; Specht KZ LXVI 56–57 (taboo term); Porzig Gliederung 202; Pokorny I 292; Zalizniak I 133; Toporov PJa I 59; Trubachev ESSJa VIII 213–214; Lehmann GED 204; Kluge-Seebold 394.

Goth is ‘he’, ON es id., OFris er id., OS es, is id., OHG er id. Related to Skt yá- ‘who, which’, Av yō id., OPhryg yos id., Lat is ‘this’, Lith jīs ‘he’, Slav *jь ‘this’. Neut. *it (Goth ita ‘it’, OS it id., OHG iz id.) is identical with Lat neut. id ‘this’. Bartholomae 1199–1227; Torp-Falk 27; Walde-Pokorny I 96– 98; Walde-Hofmann I 720–721; Feist 296; Mayrhofer III 8; Pokorny I 283; Fraenkel 194; Vries ANEW 105; Trubachev ESSJa VIII 204–205; Lehmann GED 207–208; Orel Phrygian 469–470; Kluge-Seebold 227 (from IE *eis). Sturtevant); Heidermanns 321 (segments *ī- as in Lith ypatūs ‘special’, Skt īdṛk ‘such-like, of such a kind’); Kluge- Seebold 214.

ON ÿr ‘yew-tree’, OE eóh id., OS īh id., OHG fem. īwa id. Other WGmc forms have the stress on the suffix: OE íw < *īzwaz, OHG īgo < *īzwōn. Continues IE *eik-⁄o-. Despite formal difficulties, cannot be separated (as a loanword to Celtic?) from OIr éo id., W ywen id., OCorn hiuin id., Bret ivin id. On the other hand, Celtic forms hardly can be isolated from Lith ievā ‘bird cherry’, Slav *iva ‘willow; band, edge’. A difficult word. Hoops Waldbäume 126–127, 239–
ON ís-kaldr ‘ice-cold’, OE ís-ceald id., MLG īs-kolt id., MHG īskalt id. Compound of *īsaz ~ *īsan and *kalđaz. Carr Nom. comp. 89 (parallel formations).

ON íss ‘ice’, OE ís id., OFris īs id., OS īs id., OHG īs id. Related to Av aēxa- ‘cold’, isu- ‘icy’, Osset yex ‘ice’ (Specht 18). Bartholomae 11; Torp-Falk 28; Sievers PBB XXXVIII 324–329; Holthausen AEEW 189; Vries ANEW 287; Pokorny I 301; Zalizniak II 169; Onions 458; Kluge-Seebold 213.
ON járn-smiär ‘blacksmith’, OE ísen-smiä id., OS īsarnsmith id., OHG īsarn-smid id. Compound of *īsarnan and *smiþaz. Carr Nom. comp. 75 (parallel formations).
Goth eisarn ‘iron’, ON ísarn j

Goth ja, jai ‘yes’, ON já id. (with irregular lengthening), OE zeá id., OFris jē id., OS ja id., OHG jā id. Cf. also Goth jah ‘and, indeed’ (< *jō xwe = Skt yácca ‘and though’), Run ja(h) id., OE ze id., OS ja id., OHG jā id. Fem. of the pronominal stem *ƒo- or *ī- (Brugmann Grundriß II/2 328). Grimm DG III 270; Torp-Falk 327–328; Holthausen AEEW 141; Feist 299–300; Pokorny I 283; Vries ANEW 289; Onions 1018; Lehmann GED 210; Kluge-Seebold 408.

Goth jabai ‘if ’, OE id., OE ísern, ísen id., OFris īsern, īrsen id., OS īsarn id., OHG īsarn, īsan id. Borrowed from Celtic: Gaul isarno-dori ‘ferrei ostii’, OIr íarn, W haiarn, OCorn hoern, Bret houarn (Thurneysen apud Feist). Johansson BB XVIII 17 (to *aizan); Noreen ANF IV 110; Torp-Falk 28; Holthausen AEEW 189; Feist 131; Pokorny I 300, KZ XLVI 292 (from Illyrian); Vries ANEW 287; Zalizniak I 141; Onions 485; Lehmann GED 99; Kluge-Seebold 213.
ON járn-nagli ‘iron nail’, OHG īsarn-nagal id. Compound of *īsarnan and *nazlaz. Carr Nom. comp. 84 (parallel formations).

ON ítr ‘excellent, glorious’, OE prop. Iter-mann. Derived from IE *ƒet-: Toch A yat- ‘to reach, to achieve’, Skt yátate ‘to place oneself (in the right position)’, W add-iad ‘yearning’. Karsten NB II 458; Holthausen AEEW 423; Jónsson ANF XLVI 334–335; Pokorny I 506–507; Vries ANEW 288; Heidermanns 322 (segments *ī- as in *īđalaz).

ON járn ‘iron’, OE íren id. An accentual variant of *īsarnan. Holthausen AEEW 189; Pokorny I 300. zif id., OFris jef id., OS ef id., OHG ibu id. Pronominal stem *ƒo- followed by particle *bhe ~ *bho. However, the root vocalism does not match in individual languages. Cf. Slav *i bo ‘for, because’. Aufrecht KZ I 287; Brugmann KVG 669 (derived from *ja); Torp-Falk 328; Holthausen AEEW 142; Feist 299–300; Trubachev ESSJa VIII 167– 168; Lehmann GED 210; Boutkan OFED s.v.

Goth jains ‘that (one)’, OE zeon id., OFris jen id., MLG jene id., OHG jenēr id. Not quite clear. It is possible that *jainaz and *jenaz go back to two different sources. While *jainaz reflects demonstrative particle *j- (cf. Lith jīs ‘he’) followed by *ainaz (Brugmann Grundriß II/2 335–339) and may be derivationally close to Slav *jьnъ ‘other’ < *ƒ-(o)inos (Berneker I 432, Lang ČMF I 97–99 but see differently Trubachev VJa XXIX/1 143: Slav *jьnъ = Phryg ιος νι), *jenaz is a combination of the same *j- and *enaz. The situation is further complicated by ON hann ‘he’ < *jainaz with a secondary h- by analogy with *xi. Cf. also a similar formation in Lith víenas ‘one’ < *⁄-ēinos. Hoffmann- Krayer KZ XIV 144–158; Grienberger Got. 135–136; Torp-Falk 328; Holthausen PBB XIII 372, 590 (separates *jai- < loc. *ƒoi), AEEW 142; Kock ANF XXIV 186–189; Kieckers HG 145 (follows Brugmann); Feist 300; Pokorny I 319–321; Fraenkel 1239–1240; Vries ANEW 209; Trubachev ESSJa VIII 233–234; Lehmann GED 210.
Goth jaind ‘thither’, OE zeond ‘yond, yonder’. Derived from *jainaz ~ *jenaz. Torp-Falk 328; Holthausen AEEW 142; Feist 300; Lehmann GED 210.
ON einir ‘juniper’. Identical with MIr áin ‘bulrush’ < *ƒoini-, Lat iūniperus ‘juniper-tree’ (Fick II 223; Lidén IF XVIII 507). Torp-Falk 328; Walde- Hofmann I 731; Pokorny I 513.

ON eykr ‘beast of draught’, eyk-hestr ‘cart-horse’. Close to Skt yógyaid., further to *jukan. Karsten NB I 105–106; Torp-Falk 330; Schulze KZ LXI 189; Mayrhofer III 20; Pokorny I 510; Matzel Festschr. Schröder 96, KZ CV 99; Vries ANEW 107; Heidermanns 322.

ON part. já ‘to assent’, OFris jā ‘to confess’, OS gehan ‘to articulate, to pronounce’, OHG jehan ‘to say, to speak, to admit’. Related to Skt yācati ‘to ask, to solicite, to entreat’, MIr ieith ‘language’, W iaith id. (Lidén ANF III 328). Torp-Falk 328; Hirt Ph Wschr. XIV 609 (to Lat iaciō ‘to throw, to utter’); Jóhannesson IEW 96–98; Mayrhofer III 14–15; Pokorny I 503–504; Vries ANEW 289; Seebold 286–287; Boutkan OFED s.v.
OFris jecht ‘confession’, OHG jiht id. Derived from *jexanan. Derivationally close to W iaith ‘language’ < *ƒekti-. Torp-Falk 328; Pokorny I 503; Seebold 286.
ON játta ‘to acknowledge, to assent’, OFris iechta ‘to confess’. Derived from *jextiz. Torp-Falk 328; Vries ANEW 291.
ON jóla-dagr ‘Yuleday’, OE zeóhhel-dæz id. Compound of *jexwlan and *đazaz. Carr Nom. comp. 81 (parallel formations).
ON jól-mánuär ‘the Yule month’, OE zéol-mónaä id. Compound of *jexwlan and *mēnōþz. Carr Nom. comp. 81 (parallel formations).

ON pl. jól ‘feast of Yule (later — Christmas)’, OE zeóhhol, zeól id. Of unknown origin. Grimm DM II 664 (to *xwezw(u)lan ~ *xwexwlan); Bugge ANF IV 135 (to Lat iocus ‘game’ and *jexanan); Grienberger SBAW Wien CXLII/8 137 (to Lith jenkū, jēkti ‘to become blind’); Meringer Wu S V 184 (to Av yācñā ‘to ask’); Loewenthal PBB XLV 265 (compound with elements related to Skt īßā ‘pole, shaft of a carriage’ and IE *k⁄el- ‘wheel’); Fay JEGP XVII 424–425 (a taboo modification of *xwezw(u)lan ~ *xwexwlan); Karsten IF XXII 298; Rooth Wortst. 43 (follows Bugge); Krogmann KZ LX 114 (same as Bugge); Torp- Falk 328–329; Holthausen AEEW 141; Feist 301–303, KZ LI 143–144 (influenced by *jēran); Walde-Hoffmann I 716 (to Gk ἵψία ‘game, amusement’); Vries ANEW 292; Onions 1021; Lehmann GED 211 (against Fay).

Goth jiuleis ‘Yule-month, December’, ON ÿlir ‘mid-November to mid-December’, OE zíuli ‘December and January’. Derived from *jexwlan. Torp- Falk 328–329; Holthausen AEEW 142; Feist 301–302; Onions 1021; Lehmann GED 211.
ON jǫkull ‘icicle’, OE zicel id., OFris itsil ‘spur’, OS ichilla ‘stiria’. Derived from *jekōn. Fick KZ I 11 (to Lith yžiā ‘floe’); Torp-Falk 328; Holthausen AEEW 142; Vries ANEW 294; Onions 459.

ON jaki ‘icicle’, LG īs-jack id., G dial. jäch ‘hoar-frost’. Related to Hitt eka- ‘cold, frost, ice’, MIr aig ‘ice’ < *ƒagi-. Torp-Falk 328; Pokorny I 503, Celtica V 236 (from Uralic, cf. Finn jää ‘ice’, Hung jég id.); Vries ANEW 289; Kammenhuber KZ LXXVII 67 (follows Pokorny); Puhvel II 257–259.

OHG jesan ‘to ferment, to brew’. Related to Toch AB yäs- ‘to excite sexually’, Skt yásyati ‘to boil, to become hot’, Av yah- ‘to boil’, Gk ζέω ‘to boil, to seethe’. Bartholomae 1281; Torp-Falk 329; Mayrhofer III 13; Pokorny I 506; Frisk I 612; Onions 1019; Seebold 287; Kluge-Seebold 299; Adams TB 500–501; Burlak IFTJa 253.
ON jǫstr ‘yeast’, OE zist id., MLG gest ‘dregs, dirt’, MHG gest, jest ‘yeast’. Derived from *jesanan. Torp- Falk 329; Holthausen AEEW 142; Vries ANEW 295; Onions 1019.

OE zeoht ‘yoke’. Derived from *jeukan. Structurally identical with Skt neut. yuktá- ‘harnessed, yoked’. (Holthausen IF XLVIII 266). Holthausen AEEW 142; Mayrhofer III 20; Pokorny I 508–509; Lehmann GED 212.
MHG jiuch ‘yoke’. Close to Gk ζεῦγος ‘yoke of beasts, pair of mules, oxen or horses’. Further see *jukan. Torp-Falk 330; Frisk I 610.

Goth jiukan ‘to fight, to conquer’, ga-jiukan ‘to overcome’, MHG jouchen ‘to chase, to drive’. Cf. also OE zeócor ‘strong, fierce, harsh’. Related to Toch AB yuk- ‘to conquer’, Av yaozaiti ‘to be agitated, to be excited’ < *ƒeu·-. See *jeukan. Wood MP II 471; Bartholomae 1231–1232; Holthausen AEEW 141; Feist 301; Pokorny I 512; Lehmann GED 211 (further to *ƒeu-dh- ‘to move, to fight’).

OE zeómor ‘sad, sorrowful’, OFris sbst. jāmer ‘ruefulness, crying shame’, OS sbst. jāmar ‘misery, misfortune’, OHG sbst. jāmar id. Identical with Gk ἥμερος ‘tame, cultivated’ (Solmsen KZ XXXII 147). Further related to Skt yámati ‘to subjugate’. Torp-Falk 329; Pokorny I 505; Dybo VSJa 26; Frisk I 635–636.
Swed dial. àn ‘reaper’s progress, a stroke of the scythe’, MHG jān ‘row, line’. Identical with Skt yāna- ‘going, riding’, derived from *ƒē-: *ƒā- ‘to go’. Torp-Falk 329; Pokorny I 296.
ON ár-mánaär ‘month of the year’, OHG jār-mānōd ‘January’. Compound of *jēran and *mēnōþz. Carr Nom. comp. 83 (parallel formations).

Goth jer ‘year’, ON ár id., OE zeár id., OFris jēr id., OS jār, gēr id., OHG jār id. Related to Av yār- id., Gk ὥρ` ‘season’, Slav *jaro ‘heat, spring’ and the like. Bartholomae 1287; Torp-Falk 329; Schulze Kl. Schr. 831; Holthausen AEEW 141; Feist 301; Porzig Gliederung 115; Pokorny I 297; Dybo VSJa 26; Vries ANEW 12; Zalizniak I 141; Frisk II 1150–1151; Chantraine 1303–1304; Onions 1019; Trubachev ESSJa VIII 175–176; Lehmann GED 210–211; Kluge- Seebold 408–409.
ON ár-tal ‘reckoning by years’, OFris ier-tel ‘one year’, MDu jaertal ‘number of years’. Compound of *jēran and *talan. Carr Nom. comp. 74–75 (parallel formations).
ON játa ‘to say šyes”’, OHG gi-jāzen ‘to agree’. Derived from *ja. But cf. *jextōjanan. Torp-Falk 328; Vries ANEW 291.
ON ér ‘you’, OE zé id., OFris gī id., OS gī id., OHG ir id. Analogical modifications of *jūz. Torp- Falk 330; Holthausen AEEW 141; Pokorny I 513–514; Vries ANEW 103; Onions 1018; Kluge-Seebold 395.

Goth ju ‘just now, already’, OE zeó ‘formerly, before’, OS ju ‘already’, OHG jū id. Identical with Lith ja U id., Latv jāu id., Slav *ju id. Kretschmer KZ XXXI 466 (to IE *ƒe⁄en- ‘young’); Meillet MSL IX 52; Trautmann BSW 106; Torp-Falk 328; Holthausen AEEW 141; Feist 303; Pokorny I 285; Fraenkel 190; Stang LS 25; Trubachev ESSJa VIII 190–191; Lehmann GED 212.

OE zeózuä ‘youth’, OFris jogethe id., OS juguth id., OHG jugund id. Identical with Skt yuvatí- ‘young woman’ < *ƒu⁄–ti-, with a taboo change of *-w- > *-z-. Related to *junzaz. Brugmann Grundriß II/1 439 (*-w- > *-z- by analogy with *đuzunþiz > OE duguä, OHG tugund); Torp-Falk 331; Holthausen AEEW 141; Mayrhofer III 23; Lehmann Phon. 48 (*z < *H⁄), GED 213; Kluge- Seebold 412–413.

Goth juk ‘yoke’, ON ok id., OE zeoc id., OS juk id., OHG joh, juh id. Etymologically related to Hitt i-ú-ga-an id., Skt yugá- id., Gk ζυγόν id., Arm luc id. < *ƒugƒo-, Lat iugum id., W iau id., Lith jūngas id. (with *-n- from the nasal infix of the verbal present), Slav *jьgo id. Pedersen Kelt. Gr. I 64, 98; Meillet MSL VII 57; Torp-Falk 330; Trautmann BSW 109; Holthausen AEEW 141; Walde-Hofmann I 728–729; Feist 304; Pokorny I 508; Vries ANEW 417; Mayrhofer III 19; Fraenkel 196; Zalizniak I 142; Frisk I 615–616; Onions 1020; Puhvel II 495–496; Trubachev ESSJa VIII 206–208; Lehmann GED 212; Kluge-Seebold 411.
OE ziccan ‘to itch’, OS jukkian id., OHG jucken id. Related to *jeukēnan. Bartholomae 1231–1232; Holthausen AEEW 142; Pokorny I 512; Onions 488.
ON oka ‘to yoke’, OE zeocian ‘to preserve, to save’. Derived from *jukan. Cf. a structural similarity in Lat iugō id. Walde-Hofmann I 728; Vries ANEW 418.

Goth ga-juka ‘companion, yoke-fellow’, ON oki ‘cross-beam’. Identical with Skt sam-yúj- ‘joined through friendship or relationship’, Lat con-iux ‘spouse’. Derived from *jukan. Walde- Hofmann I 261; Feist 186–187; Vries ANEW 418; Lehmann GED 141.

Goth jukuzi ‘yoke’ (with an unclear -u-), OE zÿcer ‘acre’. Fem. *(ƒe)- ƒug-usī of a perfect participle from IE *ƒeug- (Bammesberger Language XLI 416–419, Nom. 213). Noreen IF IV 325 (Goth -uz- < IE *-⁄es-); Streitberg IF XIV 494; Unwerth PBB XXXVI 20 (sstem, cf. Gk ζεῦγος ‘team’, Lat iūgera ‘acre’, Slav *jьgo ‘yoke’); Holthausen AEEW 142; Feist 304; Lehmann GED 213.

Goth junda ‘youth’. Identical with Lat iuuenta id. < *ƒu⁄–tā (Brugmann Grundriß II/1 418). Torp-Falk 331; Walde-Hofmann I 736; Feist 304–305; Chemodanov SGGJa I 91; Lehmann GED 213; Bammesberger Nom. 148.
ON ynglingr ‘young person’, OE zeonzlinz id., OFris jungeling id., OS jungling id., MHG jungeling id. Derived from *junzaz. Onions 1021; Kluge-Seebold 413.
ON ung-ligr ‘youthful’, OHG jung-līh id. Derived from *junzaz. Heidermanns 326.

Goth juggs ‘young’, ON ungr id., OE zeonz id., OFris jung id., OS jung id., OHG jung id. Related to Skt yuva≤á- ‘youthful’, Lat iuuencus ‘youth’, OIr óac ‘young’, W ieuanc id. from *ƒu⁄–-˚ó- further connected with *ƒeu- ‘young’. Cf. also Khwar ’yw’nk ‘young’ < Iran *aiwānak (Teubner Gedenkschr. Güntert 302). Torp-Falk 331; Walde-Hofmann I 735; Feist 303–304; Holthausen AEEW 142; Kluge 342–343; Mayrhofer III 23–24; Porzig Festschr. Debrunner 343–349; Hoffmann MSS VI 39–40; Pokorny I 510–511; Vries ANEW 635; Eggers Festschr. Starck 62–81; Onions 1021; Hamp KZ LXXXIV 1; Lehmann GED 212; Bammesberger Nom. 253;
ON ungi ‘young of a bird’, OHG jungo ‘animal cub’. Derived from *junzaz. Heidermanns 325.

ON it ‘you two’, OE zit id., OFris iat id., OS git id. The vocalism has been influenced by *wetwō. Identical with Lith jūdu id. Originally, a compound of *ƒu ‘you’ and *d⁄ō ‘two’ (Meillet MSL XIII 208). For the oblique cases cf. gen. *enkwara > Goth igqara, ON ykkar, OE incer. Streitberg UGG 265; k

ON kefsir ‘slave (?)’, OE ciefes ‘concubine’, OS kevis ‘concubine’, OHG kebis, kebisa id. Probably related to Lith žãbas ‘branch, bough’, žabóju, žabóti ‘to bridle’, žebóju, žebóti id. The semantic development in Gmc may be connected with that of the Baltic verb or similar to that of Slav *kopylъ ‘shoot, branch’ ~ ‘bastard, boy, servant’. Lidén SSUF 1894 63–64 (to OIr bé ‘woman’); Torp-Falk 34; Zupitza KZ XXXVII 391 (to Lat captīuus ‘captive, captured’), Gutt. 183; Schroeder Zd Ph XXXVIII 523 (to OHG keva ‘shell; cavea’); Trautmann BSW 364; Holthausen AEEW 47; Pokorny I 408 (from *gabh- eventually related to *zebanan); Fraenkel 1282–1283; Vries ANEW 304; Pisani TC XII 71 (to Gk ἀγαπάω); Menges Zfsl Ph XLIII 400–406 (together with the unclear Pol kobieta ‘woman’ from Uralic); Kluge-Seebold 435.

ON kafli ‘cut offpiece’, MDu cavele ‘piece of wood used to throw lots’ (fem.). Probably related to Lith žãbas ‘branch’. See *kabisjaz ~ *kabisjō. Zupitza Gutt. 183; Torp-Falk 34; Pokorny I 353; Fraenkel 1282–1283; Vries ANEW 296–297; Kluge-Seebold 415. Lotspeich JEGP XXX 150; Petersen JEGP XXXIII 66; Torp-Falk 329–330; Feist 291; Holthausen AEEW 142; Pokorny I 513–514; Fraenkel 199; Vries ANEW 288, 677; Lehmann GED 204.

Goth jus ‘you’. Related to Av yūš id., Lith jūs id. Further to Skt yūyám id., Av yūž6 m id., Gk ὕμμες id. See *jēz ~ *jīz. Bartholomae 1303–1305; Torp- Falk 330; Feist 305; Mayrhofer III 26; Pokorny I 513–514; Fraenkel 199; Onions 1018; Lehmann GED 213; Kluge-Seebold 395.
Norw dial. kage ‘low bush’, E dial. cag ‘stump’, G dial. Kag id. Of unknown origin. Torp-Falk 33.
ON ákafr ‘vehement, fiery’, OE cáf ‘quick, sharp, nimble’. Derived from *kībōjanan. Torp-Falk 43; Holthausen AEEW 42; Heidermanns 327.
Norw keila ‘channel’, MLG kēl ‘narrow bay’. Related to *kīlaz. Torp-Falk 43; Vries ANEW 304.

ON keipr ‘rowlock’, MLG kēp ‘cut, notch’. Related to Lat gibbus ‘hunch, hump’, Latv gībstu, gībt ‘to bend’ (Holthausen PBB LXVI 272). Torp-Falk 44; Jóhannesson IEW 298 (to Icel keipa ‘to move the fishing line up and down’); Vries ANEW 305.
ON kaka ‘cake’, ME cake id. Probably related to Lith žãgas ‘conical stack’. Pokorny I 349 (to Lith gúogë ‘cabbage-head’); Fraenkel 1284; Kluge- Seebold 490.

ON kala ‘to freeze’, OE calan ‘to be cold, to become cold’. Related to Lat gelū ‘cold, frost, ice’, Slav *zolь ‘bad weather’. Zupitza Gutt. 143; Torp-Falk 40; Holthausen AEEW 42; Walde-Hofmann I 585–586; Jóhannesson IEW 373–375; Pokorny I 365; Trubachev Slav. jaz. V 179; Vries ANEW 297; Seebold 288–289; Lehmann GED 214.

ON kálfr ‘calf ’, OE cealf id., OFris calf id., OS kalf id., OHG kalb id. (i-stem). Despite the difference in the anlaut (*g- as opposed to *g⁄-), related to Skt gárbha- ‘womb’, Av gar 6 wa- id., Gk δελφύς ‘womb’, δέλφαξ ‘piglet’, δολφός: ἡμήτρα, Hes. Zupitza Gutt. 77; Persson Beiträge 74–75 (to *kalbōn I); Torp- Falk 42; Holthausen AEEW 45; Mayrhofer I 329; Pokorny I 359 (to *kalbōn I), 473; Vries ANEW 298; Zalizniak II 223; Frisk I 363; Onions 136; Lehmann GED 214 (to Gaul galba ‘very fat man’, Lat globus ‘globe, heap’); Bammesberger Nom. 211 (old s-stem); Kluge-Seebold 418.
ON kálfi‘calf (anat.)’, E calf id., G dial. Kalb ‘muscle’. Of uncertain origin. A descriptive stem? Cf. *klabōn. Persson Beiträge 74–75 (to *kalbaz); Pokorny I 359.
Goth kalbo ‘heifer’, OHG kalba id. Derived from *kalbaz. Torp- Falk 42; Zupitza Gutt. 77, 143; Feist 305–306; Pokorny I 359; Zalizniak II 223; Lehmann GED 214.

Goth kalds ‘cold’, ON kaldr id., OE ceald id., OFris kald id., OS kald id., OHG kalt id. Derivative of *kalanan (in *-to-?). Somehow related to Slav sb. *xoldъ ‘cold’, despite the phonetic difficulties (Zupitza KZ XXXVII 390). Karsten NB II 332; Bezzenberger BB XVI 242 (to Slav *želdica ‘black ice’); Zupitza Gutt. 143; Torp-Falk 40; Holthausen AEEW 45; Feist 306; Pokorny I 366; Chemodanov SSGJa I 56; Vries ANEW 298; Onions 190; Seebold 288; Trubachev ESSJa VIII 57–58; Lehmann GED 214; Bammesberger Nom. 251; Heidermanns 328; Kluge-Seebold 420; Boutkan OFED s.v.
ON kalda ‘to become cold’, OE cealdian id., OHG kaltēn id. Derived from *kalđaz. Structurally close to Slav *xoldîti ‘to become cold’. Vries ANEW 298; Trubachev ESSJa VIII 56; Heidermanns 328.
ON kall ‘call’. Derivationally close to Slav *golsъ ‘voice’. Further related to W galw ‘to call’ < *gal⁄-, Slav *golgolъ ‘speech’. Torp-Falk 42; Pokorny I 350; Chemodanov SGGJa I 84.
ON kalla ‘to call, to cry’, OFris kella ‘to name’, MLG kolsen ‘to call’, OHG kallōn ‘to talk’. Derived from *kalzan. Zupitza Gutt. 143; Torp-Falk 42; Pokorny I 350; Vries ANEW 298–299; Onions 137.

OE calu ‘bald’, MDu kalu id., OHG kalo id. Closely connected with Slav *golъ ‘bare, naked’. Torp-Falk 42; Zupitza Gutt. 144; Torp-Falk 42; Holthausen AEEW 43; Senn JEGP XXXII 521; Kluge 346–347; Pokorny I 349– 350; Stang LS 24; Trubachev ESSJa VI 204–205; Heidermanns 329; Kluge- Seebold 417.

ON kambr ‘comb’, OE camb id., OS kamb id., OHG kamb id. Identical with Toch A kam ‘tooth’, B keme id., Skt jámbha- ‘tooth, tusk’, Gk γόμφος ‘peg, nail’, Alb dhëmb ~ dhamb ‘tooth’, Lith ἤα Mbas ‘sharp edge’, Latv zūobs ‘tooth’, Slav *zǫbъ id. Kuhn KZ I 131; Zupitza Gutt. 192; Torp-Falk 37–38; Trautmann BSW 369; Holthausen AEEW 43; Specht 86; Mayrhofer I 419; Pokorny I 369; Fraenkel 1288– 1289; Frisk I 319–320; Vries ANEW 299; Zalizniak I 133; Onions 193; Vasmer-Trubachev II 106; Bammesberger Nom. 53; Kluge-Seebold 420; Orel AED 82.
ON kemba ‘to comb’, OE cemban id., OS kembian id., OHG kemben id. Derived from *kambaz. Torp-Falk 38; Holthausen AEEW 46; Vries ANEW 306.

ON kampr ‘beard, moustache’, OE cenep ‘moustache’, OFris kenep, kanep id., MDu canef-been ‘cheek bone’. Of unknown origin. Holthausen AEEW 46; Trier Holz 86 (to *kenuz); Vries ANEW 299 (to *knabōn, see *knappaz I); Kluge-Seebold 455.

Goth kann ‘to get to know’, ON kann ‘to know’, OE can id., OFris kan id., OS can id., OHG kann id. Related to IE *·en 6- ‘to know’: Toch A knā-, Skt jānāti, Gk Ep perf. γέγωνα ‘to shout, to be made heard’, Alb njoh ~ njof ‘to know’, Lat (g)nōscō ‘to know, to acknowledge’, Lith žinóti ‘to know’, Slav *znati id. Zupitza Gutt. 193; Torp-Falk 35–36; Trautmann BSW 371; Feist 316–317; Holthausen AEEW 63; Walde-Hofmann II 176–177; Kluge 319–320; Jóhannesson IEW 331–332; Mayrhofer I 429; Pokorny I 376–378; Fraenkel 1310; Vries ANEW 334; Frisk I 293; Onions 139–140; Seebold 289– 290, KZ LXXX 273–283 (*-nn- < *-n H-); Vasmer-Trubachev II 100–101; Lehmann GED 141, 222; Kluge-Seebold 471–472; Orel AED 305; Burlak IFTJa 243; Boutkan OFED s.v.
ON kenning ‘doctrine, teaching’, MLG kenninge ‘feeling, recognition’, MHG kennunge id. Derived from

Goth kannjan ‘to make known’, ON kenna ‘to teach’, OE cennan ‘to choose, to prove, to declare’, OFris kenna ‘to know’, OS ant-kennian ‘to perceive’, OHG in-kennen ‘to know’. Causative of *kanna. Zupitza Gutt. 193; Torp-Falk 36; Holthausen AEEW 46; Feist 307; Vries ANEW 306; Onions 503; Seebold 289; Lehmann GED 215; Kluge-Seebold 437.

ON kenna (barn) ‘to father a child’, OE cennan ‘to beget, to conceive’. Related to *kunjan. At a deeper level of reconstruction identical with *kannjanan I (Schröder PBB XLIII 495; Güntert Wu S XI 128–130). Torp- Falk 34; Holthausen AEEW 46; Vries ANEW 306.

ON kanna ‘can, tankard, jug’, OE canne ‘can, cup’, OS kanna id., OHG kanna ‘vessel’. Related to *kanōn (Lidén BB XXI 109). Hardly borrowed from late Lat canna ‘mug, jug’ (Frings apud Vries) which is from Gmc. Torp- Falk 35; Lidén BB XXI 109 (to MIr gann ‘vessel’ < *gandh-n-); Holthausen AEEW 43; Pokorny I 351; Vries ANEW 300; Onions 139; Kluge-Seebold 422.

ON kani ‘bowl, dish’, MLG kane ‘boat’. Of unknown origin. Torp- Falk 35; Lidén BB XXI 109 (to *kannōn); Falk Wu S IV 89 (to Norw kana ‘to crane one’s neck’); Loewenthal Wu S X 184 (to Gk γένυς ‘chin’); Pokorny I 351 (to MIr gann ‘vessel’ < *gandh(n)-); Vries ANEW 300; Kluge-Seebold 417.
ON karl-fólk ‘male folk’, OE ceorl-folc ‘common people, public’. Compound of *karlaz ~ *kerlaz and *fulkan. Carr Nom. comp. 81 (parallel formations).

ON karl ‘man’, OE carl ‘churl, rustic’, ceorl ‘freeman of the lowest class, man, husband’, OFris zerl ‘adult man’, MLG kerle id., OHG karl ‘man, spouse’. Related to *karskaz and further derived from IE *·er(6)- ‘to ripen, to grow old, to become frail’, cf. Skt járant- ‘old’, Arm cer id., Gk γέρων ‘old man’ and the like. Torp-Falk 38; Holthausen AEEW 46–47; Porzig Festschr. Debrunner 348 (separates Gmc from this root); Pokorny I 391; Vries ANEW 301; Zalizniak I 133; Frisk I 301–302; Onions 146, 175; Kluge- Seebold 438.

OE cirm ‘noise, shout, clamor’, OS karm ‘shout, cry’. Derivationally identical with OIr gairm ‘cry, call’, W garm id., Corn garm id., Bret garm id. Further related to *karō. Zupitza Gutt. 78, 193; Holthausen AEEW 45, 47; Pokorny I 566.

Goth kara ‘care’, ON kǫr ‘bed in which one lies bedridden’, OE caru, cearu ‘care, sorrow, grief ’, OFris kar-festere ‘fasting penitent’, OS kara ‘care, sorrow, mourning’, OHG kara ‘mourning’. Related to Lat garriō ‘to prattle’, OIr gáir- ‘clamor’, W gair ‘word’ < *·arƒo- and the like. Zupitza Gutt. 78, 193; Torp-Falk 39; Holthausen AEEW 44; Walde- Hofmann I 583; Feist 307–308; Pokorny I 352; Zalizniak I 147; Onions 146; Lehmann GED 215; Bammesberger Nom. 107–108; Kluge-Seebold 427.

Goth karon ‘to care about’, OE carian ‘to take care, to regard, to heed’, OS karōn ‘to mourn’, OHG karōn ‘to bewail’. Derived from *karō. Torp- Falk 39; Holthausen AEEW 44; Feist 307; Onions 146; Lehmann GED 215.

ON karskr ‘brisk, bold’, MLG karsch ‘lively, fresh’, MHG karsch id. Derived from IE *·er(6)- ‘to ripen, to grow old, to become frail’, cf. Skt járati ‘to make old, to grow old, to decay’, Slav *zьrîti ‘to ripen’. Karsten NB II 449; Zupitza Gutt. 144; Torp-Falk 39; Mayrhofer I 420; Pokorny I 390; Vries ANEW 302 (to Gk ἐγείρω ‘to wake’); Vasmer-Trubachev II 106; Wissmann AWNP 36; Heidermanns 330.

Goth katils ‘kettle’, ON ketill id., OE cytel id., MLG ketel id., OHG kezzil id. An old borrowing from Lat catillus ‘small dish, deep vessel for cooking’, dem. of catīnus (Luft ZDADL XLI 242). Schwentner PBB XLIV 498; Brüch Festschr. Kretschmer 6–12; Jellinek ZDADL LXVI 129; Holthausen AEEW 48; Feist 308; Vries ANEW 307; Onions 504; Lehmann GED 215–216; Kluge-Seebold 439.
ON kati ‘small ship’, LG kat ‘small vehicle’. Of unknown origin. Falk Wu S IV 88; Brüch Festschr. Kretschmer 12–14 (from OPic cat ‘man of war’); Vries ANEW 303.

ON kaun ‘sore’, MDu coon ‘jaw’. Derived from *kewwanan. Derivationally close to Lith žiáuna ‘gill’, Latv ža Unas ‘gills, jaw’, Slav *žuna ‘lip, mouth’ < *·eunā. Bugge KZ XIX (1870) 431–432 (to Skt gavīnī ‘groins’); Torp-Falk 45; Zupitza Gutt. 78; Pokorny I 397; Fraenkel 1302– 1303; Vries ANEW 303 (to ON ká ‘to harass’); Lehmann GED 222.
ON kaup-maär ‘merchant’, OE ceáp-man id., OFris kāpmon id., MLG kōp-man id., OHG kouf-man id. Compound of *kaupaz and *mannz. Carr Nom. comp. 75 (parallel formations).
ON kaup-skip ‘merchant ship’, OE ceáp-scip id., MLG kōpschip id., OHG kouf-skif id. Compound of *kaupaz and *skipan. Carr Nom. comp. 75 (parallel formations).
ON kaup-staär ‘market-town’, OS kōp-stedi ‘marketplace’, OHG fem. kouf-stat id. Compound of *kaupaz and *stađiz. Carr Nom. comp. 75 (parallel formations).

ON -kaup ‘purchase’ (in cmpn.), OE ceáp ‘cattle, price, sale’, OFris kāp ‘sale’, OS kōp ‘bargain, purchase’, OHG kouf id. A modification of Lat caupō ‘small tradesman’ (Brüch ZDADL LXXXIII 92–103). Holthausen AEEW 45; Vries ANEW 303; Onions 166; Bammesberger Nom. 97; Boutkan OFED s.v.

Goth kaupon ‘to trade, to do business’, ON kaupa ‘to buy’ (irreg.), OE cÿpan ‘to sell’, OFris kapia ‘to buy, to sell’, OS kōpian ‘to buy, to trade’, OHG koufen id. Derived from *kaupaz, cf. Lat caupōnāri ‘to haggle’ (Jellinek GGS 185). Feist 309; Holthausen AEEW 48; Vries ANEW 308; Onions 166; Benveniste IEL 110–112; Lehmann GED 216; Kluge- Seebold 434; Boutkan OFED s.v.
Norw kaure ‘frizzy curl’, cf. also neut. kaur ‘wool’. Close to MIr gúaire ‘hair’, Lith pl. gauraĩ id., Latv gauḫi ‘pubic hair’ (Lidén IF XIX 341). Pokorny I 397; Fraenkel 140; Vries ANEW 302.

Goth kausjan ‘to sample, to experience, to taste’, OFris kēra ‘to choose’. Structurally identical with Skt joßáyate ‘to caress, to take delight in’. Iterative-causative of *keusanan. Feist 310; Mayrhofer I 445; Pokorny I 399–400; Seebold 294.
Dan kaa ‘daw, jackdaw’, OE ceó ‘a kind of crow, jay’, MLG kā ‘daw, jackdaw’, OHG kā ‘crow’. Of imitative origin, cf. Slav *kavъka ‘daw, gull’. Torp- Falk 45.

Goth kas ‘jar, vessel’, ON ker id., MLG kar id., OHG kar id. Cultural loanword, probably (indirectly) from Sem *ka’su- ‘beaker’ > Akk kāsu- (Güntert Festschr. Panzer 12). Curtius Gr. Et. 476 (to Lat uās ‘vessel, dish’); Osthoff BB XIX 321 (to Lat gerō ‘to carry, to lead’); Zupitza Gutt. 82, 193; Torp-Falk 42; Sütterlin IF XXIX 125 (to Gk γαυλός ‘bucket’); Feist 308; Vries ANEW 306; Lehmann GED 215; Kluge-Seebold 426.
MHG kiver ‘jaw, jawbone’ (neut. or masc.). Identical with Av zafar- ‘mouth’. Bartholomae 1657; Torp- Falk 34 (reconstruct *kefru); Pokorny I 382; Kluge-Seebold 440.
ON kjalki ‘jowl’, OHG kelh, kelah ‘goitre, swelling’. Related to *kulkaz. Torp-Falk 41; Vries ANEW 310–311.
ON kelpa ‘otter trap’, MLG kelp ‘low fellow’. Probably related to Lat galba ‘fat paunch, big belly’ (from Celtic). Vries ANEW 306 (to ON kilpr ‘handle of a vessel’).

OE cild ‘child’. From here Goth kilþei ‘womb’ < *kelþīn is derived. Of uncertain origin. Fortunatov BB VI 217 (to Skt ja†hára- ‘belly, stomach, womb’); Lidén IF XIX 335; Zupitza Gutt. 77, 211; Torp-Falk 42; Mikkola 5 BB XXI 225 (to Swed kolla, kulla ‘girl’ < *kulþ-); Holthausen AEEW 48; Feist 311; Pokorny I 358; Onions 169; Lehmann GED 218 (from IE *gel- ‘rounded’); Bammesberger Nom. 211.

ON kjǫlr ‘keel’, OE ceole ‘throat’, MLG kele id. Related to OIr gelid ‘to swallow, to eat’, gaile ‘stomach’. Torp-Falk 41; Holthausen AEEW 46; Pokorny I 365; Vries ANEW 312–313 (to Skt giráti ‘to swallow, to eat’).

Swed dial. kimb ‘ends of a stave protruding over the ground’, MLG kimme ‘rim’. Probably related to *kambaz. Cf. also Lith gémbë ‘wooden hook’. Torp-Falk 37–38; Persson SVSU X 80; Holthausen AEEW 49; Pokorny I 368; Fraenkel 146; Vries ANEW 309 (to Gk γέμω ‘to be full’).
ON kimbill ‘bundle’, MLG kimmel ‘gag, toggle’, OHG kembil ‘fetter, shackle’. Derived from *kembaz ~ *kembō. Torp-Falk 37; Vries ANEW 309.

ON kind ‘kind, variety’. Derivationally close to Lat gēns ‘race, clan’, OW -gint (in compounds). Further related to *kenþan. Persson Beiträge 686 (on Av fra-zaintiš ‘progeny’); Brugmann Grundriß II/1 434; Torp-Falk 35; Walde-Hofmann I 592; Pokorny I 373– 374.
ON kinn-bein ‘cheekbone’, OE cin-bán id., MLG kinne-bēn id., OHG kinni-bein id. Compound of *kennuz and *bainan. Carr Nom. comp. 50.
ON kinn-bjǫrg ‘cheekpiece (of a helmet)’, OE cin-berz id. Compound of *kennuz and *berzō. Carr Nom. comp. 81 (parallel formations).

Goth kinnus ‘cheek’, ON kinn id. (root stem), OE cin ‘chin’, OFris kin id. The geminate is believed to reflect the generalized *-n⁄- of gen. sg. in *·enus, *·en⁄os (Hirt Urgerm. II 47). Related to Toch A dual. ≤anwem ‘jaw’, Skt hánu- id. (with irregular h-), Av dual zanva id., Gk γένυς ‘chin’, Lat gena ‘cheek’, OIr gin, giun ‘mouth’. Wood IF XVIII 32 (identical with *·enu- ‘knee’); Back IF XL 162–167 (to *·en 6- ‘to give birth’); Zupitza Gutt. 193; Torp-Falk 37; Holthausen AEEW 49; Walde-Hofmann I 589–590; Feist 312; Martinet Gém. 69; Porzig Gliederung 126; Mayrhofer III 574–575; Pokorny I 381; Vries ANEW 309; Zalizniak II 208; Frisk I 298; Onions 170; Lehmann GED 218–219; Bammesberger Nom. 161; Kluge-Seebold 442.

Norw dial. kind ‘child’, OS kind id., OHG kind id. Identical with Lat part. genitus, further connected with *·en 6- ‘to give birth’. Zupitza Gutt. 193; Persson Beiträge 686; Torp-Falk 35; Hermann IF LIII 102; Walde-Hofmann I 592; Pokorny I 373–375; Vries ANEW 309; Humbach MSS VII 55 (to Lat Genita (Mana), OSc Genetaí, W geneth ‘girl’); Lehmann GED 218; Hamp IF LXXXVII 77; Bammesberger IF LXXIII 134 (OFris kind, OS kind < OHG), Nom. 94–95; Kluge-Seebold 442.

ON kjappi ‘he-goat’, G dial. kippe ‘newly born calf ’. Cf. Alb c(j)ap, sqap ‘goat’ < PAlb *tsapa, Slav *capъ id., Rum flap id., Ital zappo id. continuing an oriental Wanderwort of Iranian or Altaic origin, cf. NPers ‘apiš ‘one year old goat’, Osset cæw ‘goat’, OTurk ‘äbiš ‘six-months old kid’. Martinet Gém. 116, 139; Vries ANEW 311.

OE ceorfan ‘to carve, to cut, to hew’, OFris kerva id., MDu kerven id., MHG kerben ‘to notch’. Related to Gk γράφω ‘to scratch, to write’. Zupitza Gutt. 144; Torp-Falk 40; Holthausen AEEW 46; Jóhannesson IEW 362–363; Pokorny I 392; Onions 149; Frisk I 324–326; Jegers KZ LXXX 63–66 (on the connection with OPrus gīrbin ‘number’, Slav *žerbьjь ‘lot’); Seebold 292– 293; Kluge-Seebold 438.
ON kirnu-askr ‘churn-pail’, OE ciern ‘butter tub’ (ō-stem), MLG kerne id., early G kerne id. Probably related to *kernōn. Torp-Falk 38; Holthausen AEEW 46; Vries ANEW 310; Kluge- Seebold 428.

ON kjarni ‘kernel, nucleus’, MLG kerne id., OHG kerno, kern id. An ablaut variant of *kurnan. Zupitza Gutt. 193; Torp-Falk 38; Porzig Gliederung 195; Pokorny I 391; Vries ANEW 312; Zalizniak II 227; Lehmann GED 217; Kluge-Seebold 438.

OE pret. pl. curran ‘to creak’, OHG kerran id. An onomatopoeic form that might be close or similar to Skt járate ‘to crackle, to roar’. Zupitza Gutt. 78, 193; Holthausen AEEW 47; Mayrhofer I 421; Pokorny I 383–385; Seebold 293.
ON kjarr ‘copsewood’. Identical with Gk γέρρον ‘wicker-work, oblong shield, wattled screens’ < *γέρσον (Lidén SVSU VI/1 7–9). Torp-Falk 40; Pokorny I 392; Vries ANEW 312; Frisk I 300–301.

ON kjóll ‘keel, barge, ship’, OE ceól ‘keel, ship’, OS kiol ‘ship’, OHG kiol ‘big ship’. May be close to Gk γαυλός ‘round vessel’, γαῦλος ‘kind of ship’ (Pedersen KZ XXXIX 459). Both Gmc and Gk, however, can go back to the same Semitic source, cf.... ka¤ tà Φοινικικà πλοῖα γαῦλοι καλοῦνται, Hes. (Lewy Fremdw. 151, 210). Falk Wu S IV 88–89 (ON < OE); Zupitza Gutt. 145; Torp-Falk 46, 561; Holthausen AEEW 46; Pokorny I 397 (to Skt gola- ‘globe, ball’, Gk γύαλον ‘cavity of the cuirass’); Vries ANEW 312; Zalizniak I 133; Frisk I 291–292; Onions 502; Chantraine 239–240; Kluge-Seebold 440.

Goth kiusan ‘to test’, ON kjósa ‘to elect, to choose’, OE ceósan id., OFris kiāsa id., OS kiosan id., OHG kiosan id. Identical with Skt jußáte, jóßati ‘to be fond of, to like, to enjoy’, Av zaoš- ‘to be pleased with’, Gk γεύομαι ‘to taste’, Alb dua ~ due ‘to love’ < *ἵēυσ-ν-ō, OIr asa-gú ‘to wish’. Zupitza Gutt. 194; Bartholomae 1656; Torp-Falk 48; Feist 312–313; Holthausen AEEW 47; Jóhannesson IEW 322–323; Mayrhofer I 441; Pokorny I 399–400; Vries ANEW 312; Frisk I 302; Onions 172; Seebold 293–294; Lehmann GED 219; Kluge- Seebold 441; Orel AED 77–78.

OE ceówan ‘to chew’, MLG keuwen id., OHG kiuwan id. Cf. also ON tyggja, tyggva id. with abnormal t- (? < *kjujja-). Related to Slav *žuti id., Toch AB śuwā- ‘to eat’. Zupitza Gutt. 145; Torp-Falk 44; Holthausen AEEW 47; Jóhannesson IEW 390; Pokorny I 400; Vries ANEW 602; Onions 168; Seebold 294–295; Dybo BSJa O 16–18 (Gmc gemination in a mobile paradigm); Kluge-Seebold 434; Adams TB 631– 632; Burlak IFTJa 259.

Norw dial. kaam ‘dark, clouded’, MLG sbst. kām ‘mould on liquids’, MHG sbst. kām id. Related to or, at least, similar with IE *˚ƒēmo-: Skt ≤yāmá- ‘dark gray, black’, Lith šėmas ‘gray and blue, blue’. Torp-Falk 37; Pokorny I 541.
ON kiä ‘kid’, OHG kizzi id. Borrowed from an unknown IE source continuing *ghaido- (see *zaitz ~ *zaitiz). Torp-Falk 44 (to Alb kedh ‘kid’); Pokorny I 409; Onions 505; Kluge-Seebold 444.

ON keppr ‘cudgel, club’, OE cipp ‘log, coulter, plowshare’, OS kip ‘post’. Cf. also OHG kipfa ‘axle, stave’ (fem.). Related to *kaipaz ~ *kaipō. Torp-Falk 44 (to Lat scipiō ‘walking staff’); Holthausen AEEW 49; Martinet Gém. 195; Vries ANEW 306; Onions 170.

ON kippa ‘to pull, to snatch’, MLG kippen ‘to hatch’, G kippen ‘to knock over’. Derived from *kip(p)az. Falk-Torp ND 511 (to Oir scibiud ‘to move’); Torp-Falk 43 (to Slav *cîpiti ‘to clutch’); Vries ANEW 310.
ON kippa ‘bundle’, MLG kip ‘bundle (of furs, fish)’. Derived from *kip(p)az. Martinet Gém. 197; Vries ANEW 310.

ON kitla ‘to tickle’, OE citelian id., OS kitilōn id., OHG kizzilōn id. A descriptive stem. Scheftelowitz BB XXIX 30 (to Arm kcem ‘to itch, to tickle’ < *gidƒō); Torp-Falk 44; Holthausen AEEW 50; Vries ANEW 310; Onions 507; Kluge-Seebold 444.

ON kífa ‘to strive, to quarrel’, OFris zīvia id., MLG kiven id., MHG kīben id. Retains elements of a strong paradigm. Probably related to Lith geibstū, geĩbti ‘to weaken’. Persson Beiträge 84–85; Torp-Falk 43; Holthausen AEEW 42; Pokorny I 354; Vries ANEW 308; Heidermanns 327; Kluge- Seebold 436.

Norw dial. kīkja ‘to gasp, to pant’, MHG kīchen id. A reduplicated form similar to Toch AB kāk- ‘to call’ further related to Skt gāti, gāyati ‘to sing’, Slav *gajati ‘to shout’. Torp-Falk 44; Pokorny I 355.
Norw kīle ‘wedge’, MLG kīl id., OHG kīl id. Related to *kīnanan. Torp-Falk 43; Pokorny I 355 (reconstructs *kīđlaz).
Norw dial. kīme ‘strip, piece’, OS kīmo ‘germ’, OHG chīmo ‘sprout’. Related to *kīnanan. Torp- Falk 43; Pokorny I 355.

Goth keinan ‘to sprout, to bud’ (wk. pret.), OE cínan ‘to gape, to break into chinks’, OS kīnan ‘to germinate’, OHG kīnan ‘to sprout, to germinate’. Related to Latv z‚iju, ziêt ‘to blossom’ and, further, to Lith žÿdžiu, žydėti id. (Zupitza Gutt. 79, 193). Pedersen KZ XXXIX 402 (to Arm ce∑, ciw∑‘stalk’); Torp-Falk 43; Feist 310; Holthausen AEEW 49; Pokorny I 355; Onions 170; Seebold 290–291; Lehmann GED 217 (to IE *skei- ‘to cut, to separate’); Kluge- Seebold 437.

ON klafi‘a kind of fork put on the neck of cattle’, MLG klave ‘yoke’. Related to Lith glėbiu, glėbti ‘to embrace’, glóbiu, glóbti id., Slav *globa ‘kind of pole’. Zupitza Gutt. 146; Torp-Falk 57; Pokorny I 359; Fraenkel 156; Vries ANEW 313; Stang LS 22; Trubachev ESSJa VI 131–132.
ON kleima ‘to daub, to smear’, OE clæman ‘to smear, to anoint’, MDu klēmen id., OHG part. bikleimenti. Derived from *klaimōn ~ *klaimaz. Holthausen AEEW 50; Vries ANEW 315; Onions 179.

ON kleima ‘blot, dab’, OE clam ‘mud, clay’, MDu kleem ‘glue’, OHG kleim ‘clay’. Related to Lith gléima ‘slime’, Latv glaĩma ‘flattery’. Torp-Falk 57 (to Lith gl‚mës ‘slime’); Holthausen AEEW 50; Pokorny I 364; Fraenkel 157; Vries ANEW 315; Onions 179, 181; Kluge-Seebold 448.

Burg *klaini ‘tender, shining’, OE clæne ‘clean’, OFris klēne ‘narrow, thin’, OS klēni ‘narrow, slim, delicate’, OHG kleini, klein ‘delicate, fine, small’. Identical with Slav *glînь ‘clay, dregs’ < *gloini-. Cf. *klajjaz. Torp-Falk 57; Holthausen AEEW 50; Trier Lehm 30–33; Pokorny I 366–367; Onions 180; Trubachev ESSJa VI 120–121; Köbler 711; Heidermanns 332–333; Kluge- Seebold 448.

OS klē ‘clover’, OHG klēo, klē id. Cf. also OE clæfre id., MLG klāver id. Identical with Gk γλοιός ‘glue, tar’, Slav *glîvъ ‘slime’. Cf. *klenanan. Torp- Falk 58; Holthausen AEEW 50 (to Slav *glьbiti ‘to stick fast’); Pokorny I 364; Frisk I 312–313; Onions 183; Trubachev ESSJa VI 121–122; Kluge- Seebold 447.

OE clæz ‘clay’, OFris klāy id., MLG klei id. Related to Gk γλοιός ‘glutinous substance, gum’ and particularly close semantically to Slav *glьjь ‘clay, glue’. See *klaiwaz. Torp-Falk 57 (add ON kleggi ‘cleg, horsefly’ < *klajjōn); Holthausen AEEW 50; Pokorny I 362–363; Frisk I 312–313; Onions 180; Trubachev ESSJa VI 162; Dybo BSA 40.

ON klakkr ‘peg’, MDu klak ‘stain’, G Kleck ‘stain, fleck’. Related to Slav *glazъ ‘stone, pebble’ (Persson Beiträge 792). Zupitza Gutt. 211; Torp- Falk 55; Holthausen AWN 154 (to Slav *glezna ‘ankle’); Persson SVSU X 70 (to E dial. clink ‘boulder’); Jóhannesson IEW 366 (to *klanzjanan); Pokorny I 357–358; Vries ANEW 313–314.
ON klembra ‘to jam, to pinch in a smith’s vice’, MHG klemberen ‘to cotter’. Derived from *klambrō. Vries ANEW 316 (to IE
ON klǫmbr ‘smith’s vice’, E clammer ‘clip, bracket’, MHG klammer id. Derived from *klembanan. Torp-Falk 56; Vries ANEW 318.
ON klǫpp ‘pier-like rock, stepping stones over a stream’, MLG klampe ‘hook, footbridge’. Related to *klembanan? Torp-Falk 57; Pokorny I 360.
ON klengjask ‘to pick up a quarrel’, OE clenzan ‘to exhilarate’. Causative of *klenzanan. Holthausen AEEW 51; Vries ANEW 316; Seebold 300.
ON klapp ‘clapping the hands’, MHG klapf ‘crash’. Of imitative origin. Torp-Falk 56; Holthausen AEEW 50; Onions 179.

ON klappa ‘to pack, to clap’, OE clappan ‘to move, to palpitate’, OFris klappia id., MLG klappen id., MHG klaffen ‘to sound’. Of imitative origin. Cf. *klappan ~ *klappaz. Torp- Falk 56; Wissmann NP 175; Holthausen AEEW 51; Martinet Gém. 168, 170; Vries ANEW 314; Onions 179.
Norw dial. klatra ‘to beat, to knock’, LG klateren ‘to rattle, to clatter’. Cf. also OE clatrunz ‘drum, rattle’. Of imitative origin. Torp-Falk 55.

ME cleat ‘wedge’, MDu cloot ‘ball, sphere’, OHG klōz ‘clod, lump, wedge’ (masc., neut.). Close to Slav *gluda ‘lump’. Berneker I 307–308; Onions 180; Trubachev Slav. jaz. V 171, ESSJa VI 144–145; Kluge-Seebold 452.

ON kláäi ‘itch’, OE cleweäa ‘clawing, scratching’, OHG klāwido ‘scabies’. Derived from *klawjanan. Holthausen AEEW 51; Pokorny I 361; Vries ANEW 313. *klawjanan str./wk.vb.: ON klá ‘to scratch’ (secondary *klaxanan), kleyja id., OE clawan ‘to claw’, OHG klāwen ‘to scratch’. Of unknown origin. Torp- Falk 58; Holthausen AEEW 51; Jóhannesson IEW 371; Pokorny I 361; Vries ANEW 313; Onions 180; Seebold 295–296.

OE climban ‘to climb’, MLG klimmen id., OHG klimban id. Probably related to Lith glėbiu, glėbti ‘to grasp’, Slav *globiti ‘to fix together, to assemble’. See *klabōn. Torp-Falk 56; Trautmann BSW 91; Holthausen AEEW 52; Pokorny I 360; Onions 182 (nasalized form of *klībanan); Vasmer- Trubachev I 413; Trubachev ESSJa VI 131–132.
ON kleppr ‘plummet, lump’, LG klimp ‘small cliff’. Connected with *klampō. Zupitza Gutt. 146; Torp-Falk 57; Vries ANEW 316 (to IE *glembh-).
OHG klenan ‘to glue, to stick, to smear’. Transformation of the original *klinanan identical with OIr glenaid ‘to adhere’ < *gli-nā- (Braune AHDG 307). Cf. *klaiwaz. Pokorny I 32–363; Dybo BSA 39.

OE clinzan ‘to cling, to stick close’, MDu clingen id., OHG pres. clinget ‘crispat’. If assimilated from *klemzanan, probably related to Lith gle Mžti ‘to sweep, to snatch up’. Torp- Falk 56; Holthausen AEEW 52; Fraenkel 156; Onions 182; Seebold 300; Pokorny I 361; Kluge-Seebold 450.

ON klǫkkr ‘bending, soft’, LG klinker ‘weak’. Further connected with ON klǫkkva ‘to soften’ < *klenkwanan related to Lith glęžtū, glēžti ‘to become flabby’ < *glen·-. Torp-Falk 56; Jóhannesson IEW 293–294; Pokorny I 401 (to Lith gl‚žnas ‘weak’); Chemodanov SGGJa I 84; Vries ANEW 318; Fraenkel 157; Seebold 300–301.

ON kljúfa ‘to cleave, to split’, OE cleófan id., OS pret. sg. klōf id., OHG klioban id. Related to Gk γλύφω ‘to hollow out’ and identical with Lat glūbō ‘to deprive of the bark, to bark, to peel’ < *gleubhō (Zupitza Gutt. 211). Torp-Falk 59; Holthausen AEEW 51; Walde-Hofmann I 610–611; Jóhannesson IEW 404–405; Pokorny I 401–402; Vries ANEW 317; Frisk I 315; Onions 180; Seebold 301–302; Kluge-Seebold 449.
ON klé ‘one of the stones to keep the warp straight in the old upright loom’, OE cleowe ‘ball of thread’. Related to *klaujanan. Torp-Falk 58.
ON klifa ‘to repeat’, OE clifian ‘to cleave, to adhere’, OS klibon ‘to adhere’, OHG klebēn ‘to glue, to stick’. Derived from *klībanan. Holthausen AEEW 52; Seebold 297; Kluge-Seebold 447.
ON klifra ‘to climb’, OE clifrian ‘to scratch, to claw’. Related to *klībanan. Holthausen AEEW 52; Vries ANEW 317.
ON klif ‘cliff’, OE clif id., OS klif id., OHG kleb id. Related to *klībanan. Torp-Falk 58; Holthausen AEEW 52; Vries ANEW 316–317; Onions 182.

ON klífa ‘to climb’, OE clífan ‘to cleave, to adhere’, OFris klīva ‘to hang’, OS bi-klīban ‘to take root’, OHG az-klīban ‘to stick fast’. Related to Slav *glibati ‘to stick, to get stuck’. Zupitza Gutt. 147; Torp-Falk 58; Holthausen AEEW 52; Jóhannesson IEW 365–373; Pokorny I 363–364; Vries ANEW 316– 317; Onions 181; Stang LS 22 (to Slav *glьbîti ‘to stick fast’); Trubachev ESSJa VI 124; Seebold 296–297.

OE clíäa ‘plaster, salve, poultice’. Close to Gk γλίττον: γλοιόν (Hes.), Lith glitūs ‘smooth, sticky’, Latv glîst ‘to be slimy’. Torp-Falk 58; Holthausen AEEW 51; Pokorny I 364; Frisk I 313; Onions 183.

ON kló ‘claw’, OE clawu ‘nail, claw, hoof’, OFris klawe, klē ‘claw’, MLG kla, klouwe id., OHG klāwa id., klōa id. Connected with *klawjanan. May be further related to Toch B kolyi ‘clawed paw (?)’ (Adams TB 205). Kock ANF XXXII 198; Zupitza Gutt. 146; Torp-Falk 58; Holthausen AEEW 51; Pokorny I 361 (to Skt gláu- ‘round lump’); Vries ANEW 317; Zalizniak II 209; Onions 180; Kluge-Seebold 447.
ON klof ‘cleft between the legs’, OE clufu ‘ear of corn, clove of garlic’. See *klubōn. Holthausen AEEW 53; Vries ANEW 317.
ON klyf ‘trunk on a pack-horse’, OHG klubba ‘tongs, a split piece of wood used as tongs’. Derived from *klubōn. Torp- Falk 59.
