ON klofi‘cleft, rift’, OE zeclofa ‘copy of a destroyed document’, OFris klova ‘chasm’, OS fugal-klovo ‘perch (of the bird-catcher)’, OHG klobo ‘snare, trap’. Derived from *kleubanan. Zupitza Gutt. 211; Torp-Falk 59; Holthausen AEEW 53; Vries ANEW 317; Seebold 302; Kluge-Seebold 451.
Norw klundr ‘ball, lump’, LG klunder ‘bush’. Of unknown origin. Vries ANEW 318 (to ON klettr ‘rock, cliff’).
ON klungr ‘bramble’, OHG klunga ‘ball’. Derived from *klenzanan. Vries ANEW 318 (to
ON klunna ‘to cling’, OE clynian ‘to wrap’. Related to *klewōn. Holthausen AEEW 53; Pokorny I 361; Vries ANEW 318 (related to *klōwō ~

OE clyppan ‘to embrace, to clasp’, OFris kleppa id. Related to Toch B klup- ‘to squeeze’ (Adams TB 226). Zupitza Gutt. 146; Holthausen AEEW 53; Pokorny I 362 (to Slav *glyba ‘clod, lump’); Onions 182 (to Lith glėbti ‘to embrace’).
OE clott ‘clot’, MHG klotz ‘lump’. With *-tt- < *-dh- close to Lith gludūs ‘smooth, soft’ (related to gla Usti ‘to cling’). Holthausen AEEW 53; Pokorny I 362; Fraenkel 155–156; Onions 183.
ON klútr ‘kerchief’, OE clút ‘small piece of cloth, patch’, MLG klūt, klūte ‘patch’. See *kluttaz. Torp-Falk 59; Holthausen AEEW 53; Onions 183.
ON knefill ‘post, pole’, OS knevil ‘short crossbar’, OHG knebil ‘columbar, horse’s collar’. Derived from *knabōn (see *knappaz I). Zupitza Gutt. 147; Torp-Falk 49; Vries ANEW 321 (to
Swed knagg ‘knag, gnarl’, ME knagge id., MLG knagge id. A phonetic variant of *knakkaz ~ *knakkō. Torp- Falk 49.
Icel knakkr ‘fracture, crash’, ME cnak id., G Knack id. Historically identical with *knakkaz ~ *knakkō. Kluge-Seebold 453.

ON knakkr ‘little chair, high stool’, LG knacke ‘peg’. Cf. verbal derivatives in MLG knacken ‘to crash’, early G knacken id. Of unknown origin. Can be the source of WGmc *knextaz > OE cniht ‘boy, youth, servant, knight’, OFris knecht, kniucht ‘servant, soldier’, OS knecht ‘lad, youth’, OHG kneht ‘youth, servant’ (for the semantics see *knappaz I). Zupitza Gutt. 194; Torp-Falk 35 (*knextaz to *kenþan), 48–49; Holthausen KZ XLVII 307 (*knextaz to *knazzaz), AEEW 54; Martinet Gém. 137, 196; Pokorny I 370; Vries ANEW 319, 321; Onions 508; Kluge-Seebold 453.

ON knappr ‘knob, stud’, OE cnæpp ‘top, cop, knop’, OFris knapp ‘knob’, MLG knapp ‘hill’. Of unknown origin. WGmc words for ‘boy’ reflecting *knap(p)an and *knabōn probably belong here (OE cnapa, cnafa, ODu cnapo, OHG knapo, knappo, knabo). For the semantics cf. Slav *kopylъ ‘shoot, branch’ ~ ‘bastard, boy, servant’. Torp-Falk 49; Holthausen AEEW 54; Martinet Gém. 117, 196; Vries ANEW 320 (to Gk γόμφος ‘bolt, dowel’, γνάμπτω ‘to bend, to curve’); Onions 507; Lühr Expr. 274– 275; Kluge-Seebold 453.

ON knappr ‘scanty’, MLG knap ‘scanty, scarce, rare, spare’. Probably connected with *knappaz I. Karsten NB II 228–229, 455; Torp- Falk 49; Pokorny I 370; Vries ANEW 320 (to Gk γνάπτω, κνάπτω ‘to bend, to curve’); Heidermanns 337.
ON knǫrr ‘merchant ship’, ME knarre ‘knag, gnarl’, Du knar ‘tree stump’. Similar to *knazzaz and *knappaz I. Falk Wu S IV 109; Vries ANEW 323.
Norw knatra ‘to crackle’, MDu cnateren ‘to growl, to grumble’, G knattern ‘to crackle’. Somehow connected with *knakkaz. Vries ANEW 321.
ON knauss ‘knoll, crag’, G dial. knaus ‘button-like topping on the bread loaf’. Derived from *knusjanan ~ *knusōjanan. Vries IF LXXII 136–150 (to Norw knust ‘knotty log’), ANEW 320.

OSwed pres. knodha ‘to knead’ (< *knuđa-), OE cnedan id., OS part. gi-knedan id., OHG knetan id. Compared with Slav *gnetǫ, *gnesti ‘to press’ (Lottner KZ XI 193). Zupitza Gutt. 147; Torp-Falk 49; Holthausen AEEW 54; Jóhannesson IEW 332–337; Pokorny I 370–373; Chemodanov SGGJa I 72; Vries ANEW 322; Onions 507; Seebold 303–304; Stang LS 23; Trubachev ESSJa VI 165–166; Kluge- Seebold 455.

Goth kniu ‘knee’, ON kné id., OE cneó id., OFris knī, knē id., OS knio id., OHG knio id. From *·ne⁄o-, a thematic derivative of IE *·enu- id.: Hitt genu-, Toch A dual. kanweá, B keni, Skt jānu, Arm cunr, Gk γόνυ, Lat genū and the like (Kuhn KZ I 129). Petersson SVSL I 7; Zupitza Gutt. 194; Torp-Falk 49; Lohmann Genus 13; Holthausen AEEW 54; Walde-Hofmann I 592–593; Feist 313; Mayrhofer I 429; Pokorny I 381; Vries ANEW 320; Zalizniak I 146; Frisk I 321; Onions 507; Lindeman Verschärfung 16.18; Lehmann GED 220; Puhvel IV 146–151; Kluge-Seebold 456; Adams TB 193; Burlak IFTJa 240.

ON kná ‘to be able’, OE cnáwan ‘to know’. An archaic morphonological variant of IE *·en 6- ‘to know’: Skt pass. jñāyáte ‘to be known’, Toch A knā-, Gk γιγνώσκω ‘to come to know, to perceive’, Lat (g)nōscō ‘to know’, Lith ἤινóτι, Slav *znati. See *kanna. Zupitza Gutt. 193; Torp-Falk 36; Holthausen AEEW 54; Walde-Hofmann II 176–177; Jóhannesson IEW 331–332; Mayrhofer I 447; Pokorny I 376–378; Fraenkel 1310; Vries ANEW 319; Frisk I 308–309; Onions 508; Vasmer-Trubachev II 100–101; Seebold 302–303; Burlak IFTJa 243.

Crim. Goth acc. sg. knauen ‘good’, ON knár ‘pithy, viogorous’, OE on-cnæwe ‘known, recognized’. Related to MIr gnóe ‘excellent, beautiful’ (Much Idg. Anz. IX 198), Lat gnāuus ‘busy, diligent’. See *knēanan. Karsten NB II 193–194; Bezzenberger BB III 81; Torp-Falk 36; Feist 313; Pokorny I 378; Vries ANEW 320; Seebold 303; Hamp IF LXXXI 39; Hamp IF LXXXI 39 (Celto-Germanic parallel); Lehmann GED 219; Heidermanns 337– 338 (reconstructs *knawaz and *knēwiz).

ON knífr ‘knife’, OE cníf id., OFris knīf id., MLG knīf id., G dial. Kneif ‘shoemaker’s knife’. Related to *knīpanan despite the difference in the inlaut consonantism. Zupitza Gutt. 148; Torp- Falk 50; Falk ANF XLI 118; Pokorny I 370–371; Vries ANEW 321; Onions 508.
Norw dial. knīpa ‘to pinch’, MLG knīpen id. Identical with Lith gnÿbti id., žnÿbti id. Torp-Falk 50; Trautmann BSW 93; Pokorny I 370– 371; Fraenkel 159; Stang LS 23.

Goth knoþs ‘race, stock’, OHG knōt, knuot ‘origin, nature’. Continues *·nōtís close to Skt jñatí- ‘kinsman’, Gk γνωτός ‘related by blood, brother’. Derived from *·en 6- ‘to know’. Förstemann KZ I 238; Torp-Falk 35; Feist 313–314; Mayrhofer I 446; Pokorny I 373–375; Frisk I 306; Lehmann GED 220; Bammesberger Nom. 146.
ON knoka ‘to knock’, OE cnucian id., MHG knochen id. An imitative stem. Hellquist GHÅ XIV/2 12; Zupitza Gutt. 148; Torp-Falk 50; Vries ANEW 322; Onions 508; Kluge-Seebold 453 (to *knakkaz).

MLG knoke ‘bone’, MHG knoche id. Probably related to *knewan. No direct connection with Gk γνύξ ‘on the knees’, where -j is analogical (Schwyzer I 620). Torp-Falk 50; Pokorny I 381; Frisk I 317; Lühr Expr. 219–220; Kluge-Seebold 457.
Norw dial. knupp ‘bud’, MLG knuppe id., OHG knopf ‘knot’. Identical with OIr gnobh ‘knot, knag’. Torp-Falk 50; Pokorny I 371.

ON knosa ‘to bruise, to beat’, OE cnyssan ‘to press, to strike, to punch’, MDu cnosen ‘to pinch’, OHG knussen ‘to strike, to strike together’. An onomatopoeic formation, cf. *knīpanan. Torp-Falk 51; Holthausen AEEW 55; Pokorny I 372; Vries ANEW 322. *knut(t)janan ~
ON knytja ‘to knit, to truss’, OE cnyttan ‘to tie, to bind, to knit’, MHG knützen ‘to squeeze’. Derived from *knuttōn. Holthausen AEEW 55; Vries ANEW 323 (to *knūtaz).
OE cnotta ‘knot, fastening’, MLG knotte ‘knot, knob’, MHG knotze id. Cf. *knūtaz and *knuppaz. Holthausen AEEW 55; Pokorny I 372–373 (to Lith gniutū, gniūsti ‘to press, to push’); Onions 508.

ON knylla ‘to beat with a blunt weapon’, OE cnyllan ‘to knell, to sound a bell’, MHG knüllen ‘to punch, to kick’. Of imitative origin. Torp-Falk 51; Holthausen AEEW 55; Pokorny I 372 (from *knusjanan ~ *knusōjanan); Vries ANEW 323.
ON knÿfill ‘short horn’, late OHG knobel ‘small bone’. Of unknown origin. Cf. *knukōn? Pokorny I 371–372 (to Lith gniáubti ‘to embrace’; Vries ANEW 322–323; Kluge-Seebold 457 (OHG < *knuwilaz).

ON sbst. knútr ‘knot’, MHG knūz ‘bold, pert, overconfident’. Related to *knūwjanan. Zupitza Gutt. 148; Torp- Falk 50; Wissmann NP 42–43; Pokorny I 371–373; Lerchner 152; Lühr Expr. 281–282; Heidermanns 338–339; Kluge- Seebold 458.

ON knÿja ‘to knock, to press’, OE cnuwian ‘to knock, to pound’, EFris knōjen ‘to press’. Distantly related to Toch B nusk- ‘to squeeze’ < *·nu-sk- and Lith žniungū, žniūgti ‘to fall’, žniáugu, žniáugti ‘to choke, to gag’ < *·neu-g(h)-. Torp-Falk 50; Holthausen AEEW 54; Pokorny I 371; Fraenkel 1321; Vries ANEW 323; Heidermanns 339; Adams TB 341.
OE cól ‘cool’, MLG kōl id., OHG kuoli id. Derived from *kalanan. Zupitza Gutt. 143; Torp-Falk 40–41; Holthausen AEEW 56; Onions 213; Seebold 288; Heidermanns 339.

ON kœla ‘to cool’, OE célan ‘to be cold, to become cold’, OFris kēla ‘to relieve’, OHG kuolen ‘to cool’. Derived from *kōlaz ~ *kōliz. Torp- Falk 40–41; Holthausen AEEW 56; Vries ANEW 342; Onions 502; Seebold 288; Heidermanns 339; Boutkan OFED s.v.

Burg *konis ‘bold’, ON kœnn ‘wise, skilful’, OE céne ‘keen, fierce, bold’, MLG kōne ‘valiant, brazen’, OHG kuoni ‘valiant, brave, strong’. Derived from *kanna. Karsten NB I 113; Torp-Falk 36; Holthausen AEEW 56; Pokorny I 378; Vries ANEW 342; Onions 502; Seebold 290; Piirainen 49–50; Matzel Festschr. Schröder 98, 109–110, KZ CV 136; Lühr Expr. 537–538 (from *knō-niz); Bammesberger Nom. 243 (follows Lühr); Köbler 711; Heidermanns 339–340; Kluge-Seebold 491.
ON lǫg-kœni ‘skill in the law’, OHG kuonī ‘courage, strength’. Derived from *kōniz. Heidermanns 340.

ON kópa ‘to stare, to gape’, OE cépan ‘to observe, to keep, to regard’. Related to Lith žëbiū, žEbti ‘to eat unwillingly’, Slav *zobati ~ *zobiti ‘to peck, to care’. The original meaning seems to be preserved in ON (Zupitza Gutt. 194). Torp-Falk 34; Trautmann BSW 364; Holthausen AEEW 56; Pokorny I 349; Vries ANEW 326; Vasmer-Trubachev II 102 (against Zupitza).

ON kÿr ‘cow’, OE cú id., OFris kū id., OS kō id. (< *kōwiz), OHG kuo id. (i-stem with traces of a root stem in pl.). Related to Toch A acc. sg. ko, B keu id. Skt gáu- ‘bull, cow’, Av gēuš id., Arm kov ‘cow’, Gk βοῦς ‘bullock, bull’, Lat bōs ‘ox, bull, cow’, OIr bó ‘cow’, Latv gūovs id., Slav *gov-ędo ‘cattle’ (Försterman KZ I 493). Kern KZ I 237–240; Bartholomae 505–509; Torp- Falk 45; Holthausen AEEW 62; Walde-Hofmann I 112; Mayrhofer I 351; Pokorny I 482–483; Vries ANEW 340–341; Zalizniak II 209; Frisk I 260–261; Onions 223; Trubachev ESSJa VII 74–75; Peeters KZ LXXXVIII 134–136 (reconstructs *kwōz); Bammesberger Nom. 200; Griepentrog Wurzelnomina 233–256; Kluge-Seebold 491; Adams TB 189.
ON krafla ‘to paw, to scrabble with the hands’, MLG krabbeln ‘to crawl’. Probably derived from *krabbōn. Vries ANEW 328.

ON krabbi ‘crab’, OE crabba id., MLG krabbe id. Borrowed from an unknown Mediterranean source, cf. Gk kärabow ‘horned beetle, crayfish’ > Lat cārabus ‘a kind of sea-crab’ of the same origin. Cohen BSL XXVII 100 (Ethio-Sem parallels); Torp-Falk 52; Walde-Hofmann I 164; Holthausen AEEW 58–59; Martinet Gém. 87; Cohen BSL XXVII 100; Vries ANEW 328 (*‘scratching animal’, to Gk γράφω ‘to scratch, to write’); Onions 223; Lühr Expr. 296; Kluge-Seebold 481.
ON krefja ‘to demand’, OE crafian ‘to ask, to implore’. Of uncertain origin. Zupitza Gutt. 212; Holthausen AEEW 59; Pokorny I 388; Vries ANEW 329.

ON kraptr, krǫptr ‘might, strength, power’, OE cræft ‘power, might, art, skill’, OFris kreft ‘might, strength, power’, OS kraft id. (also fem.), OHG kraft id. (fem.). Derived from *krafjanan. Zupitza Gutt. 149; Torp- Falk 52 (to Slav *krîpъkъ ‘strong’); Holthausen AEEW 59; Pokorny I 388; Vries ANEW 329 (to *krappaz); Zalizniak II 201; Onions 224.
ON kraki ‘pale, stake’, OHG kracko, krahho ‘hook, harpune’. Of unknown origin. Torp-Falk 51; Pokorny I 385 (to Gk γυργαθός ‘wickerbasket’); Vries ANEW 328; Lerchner 161.
ON kremja ‘to squeeze, to bruise’, OE crammian ‘to cram, to stuff’, MLG krammen ‘to clutch with claws’. Derived from *kremmanan. Zupitza Gutt. 149–150; Torp-Falk 52; Holthausen AEEW 59; Onions 224.
ON kreppa ‘to clench’, OHG part. widar-gi-chrampft ‘to rise’. Derived from *krempanan. Torp- Falk 53; Seebold 308.
ON krangr ‘weak, crank’, MLG krangh ‘weak, worn out, ill’, OHG kranc ‘weak’. Derived from *krenzanan. Karsten NB I 69; Torp-Falk 52; Seebold 309; Heidermanns 341.
OM krá ‘angle’, OE cróz, cróh ‘corner’, MLG krōch ‘fenced land’. Connected with *krenzaz despite the unvoiced *-x-? Holthausen AEEW 61; Pokorny I 385; Vries ANEW 327 (to

OESc kranker ‘bad, wicked’, OFris kronk ‘weak, ill’, MLG krank id., OHG kranc ‘weak, invalid’. A phonetic variant of *kranzaz. Torp-Falk 52; Seebold 308–309; Toporov PJa II 289–290 (to Lith gr.žti ‘to drill, to bore’); Heidermanns 341–342; Kluge-Seebold 483.
OESc krænkas ‘to get worse’, OFris krenza ‘to damage’, OHG bi-kranken ‘to make ill’. Derived from *krankaz. Heidermanns 342.

ON trani ‘crane’ (with irregular t-), OE cran id. (< *kranaz), OS krano id., OHG krano id. Etymologically and derivationally close to Gk γέρανος id., Gaul (tri)garanos id., W garan id., Corn garan id., Bret garan id., Lith γαρνίσ ‘heron, stork’. Probably further related to *kerranan. Zupitza Gutt. 149; Suolahti DVN 292; Torp-Falk 39; Holthausen AEEW 59; Pokorny I 384; Fraenkel 137–138; Vries ANEW 596; Zalizniak II 179; Frisk I 299; Onions 225; Kluge- Seebold 483.

OE cranoc, cornuc ‘crane’, MLG kranek id., OHG kranuh, kranih id. Close to Arm kfiunk id. < *gerungo- (Meillet BSL XXXVI 121–123). On the other hand, may be a later derivative from *kranōn. Zupitza Gutt. 149; Holthausen AEEW 59; Pokorny I 384; Kluge-Seebold 483.

ON krappr ‘narrow, straight’, MLG krap ‘meagre, moderate’, G dial. krapf ‘unprepossessing’. Of unknown origin. Karsten NB I 51; Torp-Falk 52; Martinet Gém. 175; Pokorny I 387–388; Vries ANEW 328 (to Slav *grabъ ‘hornbeam’); Lühr Expr. 269, 288; Heidermanns 342–343; Kluge-Seebold 482 (reconstructs *krampaz > OHG krampf ‘crooked’, ON krappr).

ON kartr ‘cart’ (with metathesis), OE cræt ‘cart, chariot’, MDu crat ‘hamper’, OHG kratto ‘basket’. Of unknown origin. Bugge Indf. 258; Torp-Falk 51; Holthausen AEEW 59; Pokorny I 386 (to Lith grandīs ‘bracelet’); Vries ANEW 302–303.
ON krauna ‘to splash, to patter’, MDu cronen ‘to moan, to groan’, OHG krōnen ‘to chat’. Of imitative origin. Pokorny I 384; Vries ANEW 329 (to Lat grūs).

OE crimman ‘to crumble’, OHG krimman ‘to bite, to devour’. Probably related to Lat gremium ‘lap, bosom’, Lith grumiúos, grūmtis ‘to struggle, to wrestle’. Torp-Falk 52; Walde-Hofmann I 621; Pokorny I 383.

ON part. kroppinn ‘to cripple’, MLG krimpen ‘to slave away, to grab’, OHG krimpfan ‘to rub’. Probably related to Toch B krämp- ‘to disturb, to check’ (Hilmarsson Mat. 174). Torp- Falk 53; Jóhannesson IEW 347–355; Pokorny I 385–390; Vries ANEW 331 (to Lith grūbti ‘to congeal’, Slav *grǫbъ ‘rough’); Seebold 308 (to Latv grumbt ‘to get wrinkled’); Adams TB 216.

OE crinzan ‘to yield, to fall, to perish’, OFris pret. pl. krungon ‘to receive’. Related to Lith gręžiū, gr.žti ‘to turn’. Torp-Falk 52; Holthausen AEEW 60; Jóhannesson IEW 350; Pokorny I 385–390; Fraenkel 167–168; Vries ANEW 328; Seebold 308–309.

Gep *krings ‘arena’, ON adv. kring ‘round’, MLG krink ‘circle, ring’, MHG krinc id. Derived from *krenzanan. The relation to *xrenzaz is not clear. Karsten NB I 33, II 272; Torp- Falk 52; Pokorny I 385–386; Vries ANEW 330; Köbler 715; Heidermanns 344; Kluge-Seebold 487.
ON kringla ‘circle, disk’, MLG kringel id. Derived from *krenzaz. Zupitza Gutt. 82, 205; Vries ANEW 330.

ON krjúpa ‘to creep, to crouch’, OE creópan ‘to creep, to crawl’, OFris krūpa, kriāpa id., MLG krepen id. Related to Lith grūbti ‘to stiffen’, Slav *grubъ ~ *grǫbъ ‘rough’ (for the meaning cf. *kruppaz I). Zupitza Gutt. 212; Torp- Falk 54; Holthausen AEEW 60; Jóhannesson IEW 347–355; Pokorny I 389 (from *greu-b-); Fraenkel 172–173; Vries ANEW 331; Onions 227; Seebold 310; Trubachev ESSJa VII 145–146; Trubachev ESSJa VII 145–146.

Goth kriustan ‘to gnash teeth’, OSwed krÿsta id. Close to Latv skraustêt ‘to crackle’, Slav *xrustîti id. < *skrou-st-. Meyer Goth. 3 (to Gk γρύζω ‘to grunt’); Zupitza Gutt. 82; Torp-Falk 54 (to Gk βρύχω ‘to gnash, to devour’); Feist 315 (to Lith γρáυἤτι ‘to gnaw, to bite off’); Pokorny I 405–406 (to Lith grukšėti ‘to crash’); Trubachev ESSJa VIII 104; Lehmann GED 221.

Goth Kreks ‘Greek’, OE pl. Crécas id., OFris Crēc-land ‘Greece’, OHG pl. Kriehhi ‘Greek’. Despite phonetic difficulties, an early (indirect?) borrowing from Lat Graecus id. (Kretschmer Festschr. Much 279–280). Thumb Zd Wf VII 264; Jacobsohn ZDADL LXVI 223–225; Holthausen AEEW 60; Feist 314–315; Onions 412–413; Lehmann GED 220–221.
ON krás ‘dainty’, OE créas ‘delicacy, food’. Of unknown origin. Walde IF XIX 101 (to Skt grásati ‘to swallow, to consume’); Zupitza Gutt. 150; Torp-Falk 53; Pokorny I 474–476 (from

Crim. Goth criten ‘to cry’, MLG kriten ‘to scream’, MHG krīzen ‘to cry loudly’. Eventually connected with the group continuing IE *ger-: Skt járate ‘to sough, to sound’, Lith gūrti ‘to sound loudly’. Torp-Falk 53; Feist 112; Pokorny I 383–384; Lehmann GED 85.

Goth ga-kroton ‘to crush, to smash’, OSwed refl. krootas ‘to break into pieces’. Cf. also *krattōjanan > MLG kratsen ‘to scratch’, OHG krazzōn id. Of imitative origin. Holthausen IF XXXIX 71; Feist 187; Pokorny I 391; Onions 801; Lehmann GED 141 (to *·er- ‘to ripen’).
ON krubba ‘crib’, OE cryb id., MLG krübbe id. Of unknown origin. See *kruppaz? Holthausen AEEW 62; Vries ANEW 332 (to ON krof ‘carcass of an animal’).
Norw dial. kryda ‘to swarm, to abound’, MLG kroden, kruden ‘to pester, to trouble’, MHG kroten id. Related to Lith grūdžiu, grūsti ‘to stomp, to bruise’. Torp-Falk 54; Pokorny I 461; Fraenkel 173.

Icel krumr ‘intestines’, OE cruma ‘crumb, fragment’, MLG krome, krōme id. Related to Gk γρῦμέα ‘bag for old clothes’, Lat grūmus ‘little heap, hillock’ (Osthoff Morph. Unt. IV 124; Persson Wurzelerw. 124). Torp-Falk 54; Walde-Hofmann I 623; Pokorny I 388–389; Frisk I 329.

ON kroppr ‘bump, bunch’, OE crop ‘sprout, bunch, cluster’, MLG krop ‘body, shoot’. Based on *kreupanan, with an unaccountable gemination of -p- > -pp-. Zupitza Gutt. 77; Holthausen AEEW 61; Martinet Gém. 187; Torp-Falk 55 (to Gk γρῦπός ‘hooknosed, hooked, curved’); Pokorny I 389 (reconstructs *greu-b-); Vries ANEW 331 (< *krubnaz); Zalizniak I 133; Frisk I 329–330.
ON pln. Kroppr, OE crop ‘crop, craw’, MLG krop ‘crop’, OHG kropf id. Identical with *kruppaz I. Holthausen AEEW 61; Pokorny I 389; Onions 230; Kluge-Seebold 489 (to IE
ON kryppill ‘cripple’, OE crypel id., MLG kroppel id., dial. G Krüpel id. Derived from *kruppaz I. Torp-Falk 55; Kluge-Seebold 490.
ON krytja ‘to murmur’. Identical with Gk γρύζω ‘to say, to grumble’. Zupitza Gutt. 212; Pokorny I 406; Vries ANEW 332.
Norw dial. kryl ‘hump, hunch’, MLG krouwel ‘a kind of gable’, MHG krouwel ‘crooked fork’. Of unknown origin. Torp-Falk 53; Pokorny I 388.
Icel kubbi ‘block, log’, ME cob ‘head, lump’. See *kubōn. Pokorny I 395–396; Vries ANEW 333 (to *kuppaz I).

ON kofi‘hut, shed’, OE cófa ‘cove, cave, room, chamber’, MLG cove ‘stable’, MHG kobe id. Related to Gk γύπη: κοίλωμα γῆς, θαλάμη, γωνία (Johansson IF II 50). Looks like an old loanword, cf. Sem *kūbu- > Akk kūbu ‘building’. Zupitza Gutt. 148; Torp- Falk 46; Holthausen AEEW 56; Pokorny I 395–396; Frisk I 335; Onions 222; Kluge-Seebold 459.

ON koddi ‘pillow’, OE codd ‘bag, sack, husk’. Of unknown origin. Petersson SVSU X 943 (to *kuđran); Zupitza Gutt. 81; Torp-Falk 46; Holthausen AEEW 56; Martinet Gém. 163; Pokorny I 394; Vries ANEW 323; Onions 187.
MLG koder ‘double chin’, early G Koder id. Identical with Hitt kuttar ‘neck, upper arm’, Lat guttur ‘gullet, throat’ (Persson Beiträge 109). Walde- Hofmann I 629; Pokorny I 394; Kluge- Seebold 460.

Goth kukjan ‘to kiss’, EFris kükken id., Du dial. kukken id. A descriptive stem, hardly connected with *kussjanan. Feist Festschr. Hildebrand 25– 25 (reduplicated *·eu-); Grienberger Got. 142–143 (to *kewwanan); Schrader- Nehring I 670 (to *kussjanan); Feist 315–316; Pokorny I 626; Onions 506; Lehmann GED 221; Kluge-Seebold 496.
ON kokkr ‘cock, rooster’, OE coc id. Of imitative origin. Zupitza Gutt. 197; Holthausen AEEW 55 (from Rom *coccus id.); Martinet Gém. 197; Onions 186.

ON kol ‘coal’, OE cól id., OFris kole id., MLG kol, kole id., OHG kol id. Related to Toch B ≤oliye ‘hearth’, OIr gúal id. Torp-Falk 48; Holthausen AEEW 56; Schulze KZ LVI 141 (to Skt jvalati ‘to burn’); Pokorny I 399; Vries ANEW 324; Zalizniak II 183; Onions 185; Kluge-Seebold 461; Adams TB 635.

ON kolfr ‘bolt, pole’, MLG kolve ‘club, mace’, OHG kolbo id., kolb ‘staff’. Any connection with *kelpōn ~ *kelpaz? Sütterlin IF IV 105; Persson Beiträge 64–65 (to Lat globus ‘ball, sphere, globe’); Zupitza Gutt. 211; Torp- Falk 57 (to *klembanan); Pokorny I 359; Vries ANEW 324.
ON kuldi ‘cold’, MLG külde id., G dial. kulde id. Related to *kalđaz. Vries ANEW 333; Lehmann GED 214.
ON kylr ‘gust of cold air’, OE cíle ‘cold’. Derived from *kalanan. Holthausen AEEW 47; Vries ANEW 340.

Swed dial. kolk, kulk ‘swallow, mouthful’, OE colc ‘hole, cask’, OFris kolk ‘hole full of water’, MLG kolk, kulk id. Related to Slav *glъgati ‘to swallow’. Further cf. *kelkaz ~ *kelkōn. Torp-Falk 41; Holthausen AEEW 56; Vries ANEW 311; Trubachev ESSJa VI 157.

ON kollr ‘summit, top, head’, MLG kol ‘head’. Related to Skt gulī ‘globe, pill’. Bersu Gutt. 185 (to Lith galvā ‘head’, Lat galea ‘leather helm’); Zupitza Gutt. 148; Torp-Falk 46; Mayrhofer I 341; Pokorny I 357; Vries ANEW 325.

Norw dial. kult ‘plump, thick figure; hill top’, OE colt ‘foal’. May be connected with Lith guldÿti ‘to castrate’. Torp-Falk 41–42 (to *kalbaz and Skt gaḍi- ‘young bull’ < *goldi-); Pokorny I 358; Fraenkel 175.

ON kumbl ‘mark, sign, badge’, OE cumbol ‘sign, image, ensign’, OS kumbal ‘sign’, OHG kumbal-boro ‘standard-bearer’. A difficult word. Fischer apud Vries (from Lat cumulus ‘heap, pile’); Meringer IF XIX 445 (to ON kumbr ‘chopping, cutting’); Holthausen AEEW 63; Vries ANEW 333–334.

Goth kuna-wida ‘cord, fetter’, OE cynewiääe ‘royal wreath, diadem’, OHG kuna-wid ‘chain’. Compound of *kuna- related to *knewan and *wiþjōn (Meringer IF XVI 178). Grienberger Got. 143 (compares with Lith kūnas ‘body’); Schrader-Nehring I 677 (same as Meringer); Meissner Festgabe Bezold 132–136 (*kuna- to *kunjan); Torp- Falk 406; Feist 315–316; Carr Nom. comp. 44; Pokorny I 397 (to *kaunan ~ *kaunō); Onions 1010; Lehmann GED 221–222 (supports Meissner).

Goth airþa-kunds ‘of earthly origin’, ON ein-kunn ‘sign, mark’, OE æäel-kund ‘of noble kind’, OS god-kund ‘of divine kind’, OHG gomman-kund ‘male, masculine’. Related to *kenđiz. Structurally identical with Skt jātá- ‘born’, Gk -γνητός, Lat (g)nātus. Karsten NB II 316; Zupitza Gutt. 193; Torp-Falk 35; Walde-Hofmann I 597–599; Feist 26; Holthausen AEEW 63; Schulze Kl. Schr. 70; Mayrhofer I 427; Pokorny I 373–374; Meid Wortbild. 227–228; Vries ANEW 334; Frisk I 306–308; Heidermanns 347.

Goth uf gakunþai ‘érxÒmenow’, ON troll-kyndr ‘descented from trolls’, OE cÿääu ‘native country, home’, MHG kunde ‘knowledge’. Derived from *kanna. Feist 187; Holthausen AEEW 68; Feist 187; Vries ANEW 334; Lehmann GED 141; Seebold 290; Bammesberger Nom. 141.
ON kynda ‘to light, to kindle’, MHG künten, künden ‘to ignite’. Derived from *kunđiz. Vries ANEW 340.
ON kyndi ‘kind, kin’, OE cynd ‘nature, kind’. Related to *kunjan. Holthausen AEEW 67; Vries ANEW 340.
ON konung-dómr ‘kingdom’, OE cyninz-dóm ‘kingly power, kingdom’, OS kuning-dōm id. Derived from *kuninzaz ~ *kununzaz. Carr Nom. comp. 75 (parallel formations).
ON konung-ríki ‘kingdom’, OE cyninz-ríce id., OS kuning-rīki id., OHG kuning-rīhhi id. Compound of *kuninzaz ~ *kununzaz and *rīkjan. Carr Nom. comp. 75 (parallel formations).

ON konungr ‘king’, OE cyninz id., OFris kining, koning, kening id., OS kuning id., OHG kuning id. Derived from *kuniz. Torp-Falk 35; von Friesen apud Vries (to *kwenōn); Holthausen AEEW 67; Vries ANEW 326; Zalizniak I 133; Onions 506; Kluge-Seebold 470.
ON konr ‘nobleman’, OE cyne-helm ‘crown, diadem’. See *kunjan. Torp-Falk 35.
ON kyn-borinn ‘of noble kin’, OE cyne-boren ‘of royal birth’. Compound of *kunjan and *burenaz, participle of *beranan. Carr Nom. comp. 61.

Goth kuni ‘clan, tribe, race, generation’, ON kyn ‘kin, kind’, OE cyn id., OFris kinn, kenn id., OS kunni id., OHG kunni id. Continues IE *·–ƒo- related to Skt jána- ‘race’, Av zana- ‘people’ (in cmpn.), Gk γένος id., Lat genus ‘birth, descent’. Cf. in particular Lat genius ‘tutelar deity’ < *·enƒos. Zupitza Gutt. 193; Bartholomae 1660; Torp-Falk 35; Holthausen AEEW 67; Walde- Hofmann I 597–599; Feist 316; Pokorny I 375; Vries ANEW 340; Zalizniak I 145; Frisk I 306–308; Onions 505; Benveniste IEL 368; Lehmann GED 222; Bammesberger Nom. 65.

Goth ga-kunnan ‘to get to know’, OE cunnian ‘to prove, to try, to inquire’, OS gi-kunnon ‘to recognize’, OHG kunnēn ‘to learn, to try’. Derived from *kanna. Holthausen AEEW 63; Feist 317; Seebold 289.

Goth kunþs ‘known’, ON kunnr, kuär id., OE cúä id., OFris kūth id., OS kūth id., OHG kund id. Continues *·–6-tó-, cf. Skt jñātá- id., Gk γνωτός id., Lat (g)nōtus id., OIr gnáth id., Lith pa- ἤīντασ ‘known’. Derived from *kanna. Negative *un-kunþaz > Goth un-kunþs ‘unknown’, ON ú-kúär id., OE un-cúä id., MDu on-cont id., OHG un-kund id. is structurally close to Toch A āknats, B aknātsa id., Skt ájñāta- id., Arm an-canaut' id., Gk ἄγνωτος id., Lat īgnōtus id., OIr ingnad. Torp-Falk 36; Holthausen AEEW 64; Walde-Hofmann I 677; Feist 317–318; Mayrhofer I 13; Pokorny I 373–374; Chemodanov SGGJa I 77; Fraenkel 1310; Vries ANEW 334; Frisk I 308–309; Onions 958; Seebold 290; Lehmann GED 223; Bammesberger Nom. 251; Kluge-Seebold 493; Burlak IFTJa 243.

Goth kunþi ‘knowledge’, ON kynni ‘acquaintance’. Cf. also OHG ana-kundī ‘accusation’ < *-kunþīn. Structurally close to Lith pa-žintīs ‘acquaintance’. Derived from *kunþaz. Rosell UUÅ 1942/7 109; Feist 317; Fraenkel 1310; Vries ANEW 340; Lehmann GED 223.

Goth ga-swi-kunþjan ‘to make manifest, to make known’, ON kynna id., OE cÿäan id., OFris kētha id., OS kūđian, cūäean id., OHG kunden id. Derived from *kunþaz. Torp-Falk 36; Holthausen AEEW 68; Feist 317; Vries ANEW 340; Onions 507; Lehmann GED 223.

ON koppr ‘cup, small vessel’, OE cop ‘top, cop, summit’, MLG kop ‘cup, bowl’, OHG kopf ‘cup, skull, head’. Related to Latv gubt ‘to bend’, Slav *gъbnǫti id. There are no reasons to reconstruct *gheubh- rather than *geub- for Balto-Slavic forms insofar as their Gmc parallels (Pokorny I 450) are not reliable. Torp-Falk 46; Holthausen AEEW 57; Martinet Gém. 193; Pokorny I 395–396 (to Gk γύπη: κοίλωμα γῆς, θαλάμη, γωνία, Hes.); Fraenkel 140; Zalizniak I 133; Trubachev ESSJa VII 188–189; Lühr Expr. 275–276; Kluge-Seebold 477 (from Lat cūpa ‘tub, cask’).
ON kurfr ‘chip, a cut-off piece’, OE cyrf ‘cutting off’ (fem.?), OFris kerf ‘cut’. Derived from *kerbanan. Torp- Falk 40; Holthausen AEEW 68; Seebold 292; Bammesberger Nom. 131.
ON þver-kyrfa ‘to carve, to cut clean across’, OE corfian ‘to cut up small, to mince’. Derived from *kerbanan. Holthausen AEEW 58; Seebold 292.
ON korn-hús ‘granary, barn’, OE corn-hús id., OS korn-hūs id., OHG korn-hūs id. Compound of *kurnan and *xūsan. Carr Nom. comp. 75 (parallel formations).

Goth kaurn ‘grain, wheat’, ON korn ‘corn, grain’, OE corn id., OFris korn id., OS korn id., OHG korn id. Identical with Skt jūrṇá-, jīrṇá- ‘old, decayed, withered’, Lat grānum ‘corn’, OIr grán id., Lith žīrnis id., Slav *zьrno id. Hirt Idg. Gr. II 130–131; Meillet BSL XXIV/74 109; Zupitza Gutt. 193; Pedersen Kelt. Gr. I 52; Torp-Falk 38; Trautmann BSW 372; Holthausen AEEW 58; Walde-Hofmann I 618–619; Feist 309; Porzig Gliederung 195; Mayrhofer I 439; Pokorny I 390–391; Dybo VSJa 14–15, BSA 15; Chemodanov SGGJa I 62; Fraenkel 1314; Zalizniak II 227; Vries ANEW 326; Onions 215; Vasmer-Trubachev II 95–96; Lehmann GED 216–217; Kluge-Seebold 478.
ON korn-vist ‘store of corn’, OHG korn-wist ‘corn meal’. Compound of *kurnan and *westiz II. Carr Nom. comp. 84 (parallel formations).
ON hamal-kyrni ‘a kind of seed’, OS hrēn-korni ‘wheat’, OHG folcurni ‘einkorn’. Derived from *kurnan. Lehmann GED 217.

ON kyrtill ‘tunic, gown’, OE cyrtel ‘vest, garment, frock’. Goes back to Iranian (Av k 6 r 6 ti- ‘a kind of clothes’, Sogd qwrty ‘shirt’, Osset k w 6 ræt id.) from where, probably, numerous North Caucasian and Kartvelian forms. Note the resemblance with *zurđilaz. Holthausen AEEW 68; Abaev I 609; Onions 506 (derived from Lat curtus ‘short’); Nikolayev-Starostin NCED 449.

Goth kaurus ‘heavy, weighty’. From earlier *kwuruz. Related to Indo- European adjectives for ‘heavy’: Skt gurú-, Gk βαρύς < *g ⁄6 ru- (Feist 310). The development of the labiovelar in Germanic is not clear. Zupitza Gutt. 81; Torp- Falk 48; Feist 310; Mayrhofer I 340; Pokorny I 476–477; Frisk I 221–222; Peeters IF LXXIX 33–34 (< *g ⁄ṛ-u-); Lehmann GED 217; Heidermanns 355.
Goth un-ga-kusans ‘unqualified, unsuccessful’, OE un-ze-coren ‘unchosen, evil’. Past participle based on *keusanan. Feist 519; Lehmann GED 219.
Norw kusma ‘paroritis’, Du kossem ‘paunch, belly’. Of unknown origin. Torp-Falk 60 (from *kwiþsmōn, to

ON kyssa ‘to kiss’, OE cyssan id., OFris kessa id., OS kussian id., OHG kussen id. Derived from *kussuz. Torp-Falk 48 (to Lat bāsium ‘kiss’); Holthausen AEEW 68; Vries ANEW 341; Onions 506; Kluge-Seebold 496.
ON kussi ‘calf, bullock’, Du kuis id., G dial. kusse ‘old sheep’. An affective derivative of *kōwz ~ *kūz (Trautmann apud Vries). Vries ANEW 335.
ON kussa ‘cow’, MHG kuose ‘female calf’. See *kussōn I. Vries ANEW 335.

ON koss ‘kiss’, OE cos id., OFris koss id., OS kus id., OHG kus id. Derived from *keusanan. Zupitza Gutt. 81 (to OIr bus ‘mouth’, Lat bāsium ‘kiss’); Torp-Falk 48; Holthausen AEEW 58; Kluge PBB XLI 180 (to *kweþanan); Feist 315; Pokorny I 399–400, 626 (to the descriptive stem *kus-); Vries ANEW 327; Zalizniak I 133; Kluge-Seebold 496; Hemrajani Festschr. Shevoroshkin 124.

Goth ga-kusts ‘approvedness, test’, ON kostr ‘choice, condition’ (traces of u-stem), OE cyst ‘choice, election’, OFris kest ‘choice’, OS kust ‘choice, decision’, OHG kust id. Structurally identical with Skt júṣṭi- ‘love, favor’. Derived from *keusanan. Torp-Falk 48; Holthausen AEEW 68; Feist 187; Porzig Gliederung 122; Mayrhofer I 442; Pokorny I 400; Vries ANEW 327; Zalizniak II 201; Seebold 294; Lehmann GED 219; Bammesberger Nom. 157.

ON kosta ‘to try, to tempt’, OE costian ‘to tempt, to try, to prove’, OS koston ‘to try, to attempt’, OHG kostōn ‘to try’. Derived from *kustiz, *kustuz. Structurally similar to Lat gustāre ‘to taste’. Torp-Falk 48; Holthausen AEEW 58; Walde-Hofmann I 628–629; Vries ANEW 327; Seebold 294; Kluge- Seebold 480.

Goth kustus ‘test, trial’, ON kostr ‘choice’ (traces of u-stem in acc. pl. kostu), MHG fem. kust ‘test’. Identical with Lat gustus ‘tasting’, OIr guss ‘excellence’ (Loth RC XLV 193). Derived from *keusanan. Torp-Falk 48; Holthausen AEEW 58; Walde-Hofmann I 628– 629; Feist 318; Porzig Gliederung 122; Pokorny I 400; Vries ANEW 327; Zalizniak II 201; Seebold 294; Lehmann GED 219; Bammesberger Nom. 140, 157.

ON kot ‘cottage, hut’, OE cot ‘cot, cottage, house’, MLG kot id. Borrowed from dial. Iran *kuta-, cf. Av kata- ‘chamber’ (Brøndal APS III 1–31). See *xūsan. Persson SVSU X 109 (to Skt gudá- ‘intestine, anus’); Bartholomae 432; Stender-Petersen Slav.-germ. 232– 234 (from Slav *kot-ьcь ‘hut’); Torp-Falk 46–47; Holthausen AEEW 58; Brøndal APh S III 8–9 (to Fr cote ‘hut’ as a Wanderwort); Pokorny I 393 (to *geu- ‘to bend’); Vries ANEW 327; Onions 219; Kluge- Seebold 432.
ON hús-kytja ‘cottage’, OE cyte id. Derived from *kutan. Holthausen AEEW 48; Vries ANEW 341.
ON kør ‘choice’, OE ze-cor ‘making a decision’, OFris kor-birta ‘consecrated morsel’. Derived from *keusanan. Vries ANEW 341; Seebold 294; Kluge- Seebold 494.
ON kúfr ‘heap above the brim of a vessel’. Close to Gk γύπη: κοίλωμα γῆς, θαλάμη, γωνία (Hes.). Torp-Falk 46; Holthausen AEEW 66 (< Rom *cupia); Pokorny I 395; Vries ANEW 333.
ON kúla ‘ball, knob’, MLG kūle ‘club’, MHG kūle ‘ball, globe, club’. Related to Skt gola- ‘globe, ball’. Zupitza Gutt. 145; Torp-Falk 46; Mayrhofer I 349; Pokorny I 396–397; Vries ANEW 333.

Icel kúra ‘to duck, to crouch’, MLG kūren ‘to crouch’, MHG kūren id. Related to Gk g-row ‘round’, Lith g~rinti ‘to mince while stooping’, Slav *guriti (sę) ‘to tighten’. Falk Festschr. Bugge 18; Pokorny I 397–398; Fraenkel 177; Vries ANEW 335; Frisk I 335–336.

ON kvefja ‘to quench, to extinguish’, OHG ir-queban ‘to suffocate’. Related to Gk βάπτω ‘to dip’ (Fick BB II 189). Torp-Falk 60–61; Jóhannesson IEW 412; Pokorny I 465–466; Frisk I 218–219; Vries ANEW 336; Seebold 311–312.

ON kveäja ‘to call on, to address’, OE cweddian ‘to greet’, OS queddian id., OHG quetten ‘to greet, to call out’. Causative of *kweþanan. Holthausen AEEW 64; Vries ANEW 336; Seebold 319; Lehmann GED 278.

Goth qainon ‘to mourn, to wail’, late ON kveina ‘to lament, to complain’, OE cwánian ‘to bewail, to deplore’. Of unknown origin. Cf. *wainōjanan. Fick BB VIII 331 (to Gk ὠδίνω ‘to have birth pains’); Torp- Falk 63; Thumb KZ XXXVI 196–198 (to Lith gíestu, giedóti ‘to begin to sing’); Mohr ZDADL LXXVI 167 (ON < WGmc); Zupitza Gutt. 84; Holthausen AEEW 64; Feist 385–386; Pokorny I 467; Vries ANEW 336; Lehmann GED 275 (to
ON kveisa ‘whitlow, boil’, MLG quēse ‘hematocyst’. Of unknown origin. Falk-Torp ND 604 (to *kaunan ~ *kaunō); Pokorny I 469 (to Gk δεῖσα ‘slime, filth’); Vries ANEW 337.
ON kvaka ‘to twitter’, E quack, MLG quaken ‘to quack, to croak’, G quaken id. Of imitative origin. Torp-Falk 59; Vries ANEW 335.
ON kvelja ‘to torment’, OE cwellan ‘to kill’, OS quellian id., OHG quellen id. Derived from *kwelanan. Torp-Falk 62; Holthausen AEEW 64; Pokorny I 471; Vries ANEW 337; Onions 731; Seebold 313.

Goth masc. or neut. ana-qal ‘rest, quiet’, ON kvǫl ‘torment, torture’, OE cwalu ‘quelling with weapons, torment, violent death’, OS quāla ‘ordeal’, OHG quāla id. Derived from *kwelanan. Derivationally close to Lith gëlā ‘fierce pain’, Slav *žalь ‘sorrow’. Torp-Falk 62; Holthausen AEEW 64; Feist 43–44; Pokorny I 471; Fraenkel 145–146; Zalizniak I 147; Onions 731; Seebold 313–314; Lehmann GED 32; Bammesberger Nom. 108; Kluge- Seebold 659.

ON kvǫstr ‘bathing scrub’, MLG quest, quast id., MHG quast id. Related to Alb gjeth ‘foliage’ < *g⁄ozdo-, W both ‘wheel-nave’, Slav *gvozdъ ‘wood, nail’ (Brückner Afslav Ph XXXIX 10; Jokl IF XXX 199–200). Torp-Falk 62–63; Pokorny I 485; Georgiev Zf Balk III 104–105 (on ethn. Costoboci < Gmc *kwasta-bōk-); Kluge-Seebold 660; Orel AED 133– 134.

ON kviär ‘saying, speech’ (traces of u-stem), OE cwide ‘sentence, saying’, OS quidi ‘word’, OHG quiti ‘sentence, saying’. Derived from *kweþanan. Holthausen AEEW 65; Vries ANEW 338; Seebold 319; Lehmann GED 278; Bammesberger Nom. 136.

ON kváäa ‘resin’, OE cwidu ‘cud’. Cf. also OHG quiti, kuti ‘glue’ (masc.). The Scandinavian variant (attested also in ODan kvade, Norw dial. kwæde ‘birch sap’) with a long vowel seems to be secondary. Related to Skt játu ‘varnish, gum’, MIr beithe ‘birch’, W bedw id. Zupitza Gutt. 85; Torp-Falk 60; Holthausen AEEW 65; Mayrhofer I 415; Pokorny I 480; Vries ANEW 335; Zalizniak II 219; Onions 233; Bammesberger Nom. 156; Kluge-Seebold 444.

OE cwelan ‘to die’, OS quelan ‘to be subject to torture, to die’, OHG quelan ‘to suffer’. Related to Arm ke∑em ‘to torture’, OIr at-baill ‘to die’, W ballu id., Lith gëlā ‘strong pain’, OPrus gallan ‘death’, Slav *žalь ‘pity’. Meillet Etudes 265, MSL VIII 165; Zupitza Gutt. 85; Pedersen Kelt. Gr. II 460; Torp- Falk 62; Trautmann BSW 83; Holthausen AEEW 64; Pokorny I 471; Fraenkel 145–146; Onions 731; Seebold 313–314; Vasmer-Trubachev II 34–35; Stang LS 21.

ON kveld ‘evening’, OE cwild-tíd ‘dead time’, cwield ‘death’, OHG quilti-werc ‘work till night’. Historically, participle of *kwelanan identical with Latv part. dzelts ‘stung’. Torp-Falk 62; Dybo VSJa 33; Kluge-Seebold 442.

OS quellan ‘to gush, to stream’, OHG quellan id. Related to Skt galati ‘to drop, to fall down’. Torp-Falk 62; Holthausen AEEW 57 (on OE collenferhä ‘fierce-minded, bold’); Mayrhofer I 329; Pokorny I 471–472; Seebold 314–315.

Goth qiman ‘to come’, ON koma id., OE cuman id., OFris kuma id., OS kuman id., OHG queman id. Related to Toch A kum-, B käm- ‘to come’, Skt gámati ‘to go’, Av jamaiti id., Gk βαίνω id., Lat ueniō ‘to come’. Brugmann Grundriß II/3 89; Meillet BSL XX 25 (originally athematic); Zupitza Gutt. 81, 86; Torp-Falk 61; Feist 387–388; Holthausen AEEW 63; Walde-Hofmann II 747–749; Kluge 389–390; Endzelin KZ XLIV 61 (to Lith gi Mti ‘to be born’); Porzig Gliederung 209; Jóhannesson IEW 413–414; Mayrhofer I 323; Pokorny I 463–465; Vries ANEW 325; Frisk I 208–210; Chantraine 156–158; Onions 84, 193; Seebold 315–317; Lehmann GED 276; Kluge- Seebold 465; Adams TB 160–161.
ON kvenn-maär ‘woman’, OHG quena-man ‘uxorious person’. Compound of *kwenōn and *mannz. Carr Nom. comp. 84 (parallel formations).
Goth qinein ‘female’. Related to *kwenōn and identical with Slav poss. *ženinъ ‘belonging to woman’. Feist 388; Lehmann GED 277.

OE cwincan ‘to disappear, to vanish’, OFris quinka ‘to be extinguished’. Probably, a secondary formation based on the unattested *kwe(s)nanan to be compared with Gk ζείναμεν: σβέννυμεν, Hes. < *z-g⁄es-n-. Further cf. Skt jásate ‘to be exhausted, to languish’, Gk σβέννυμι ‘to extinguish’, Lith gęstū, gēsti id., Slav *gasiti id. Holthausen AEEW 66; Pokorny I 479; Onions 731.

Goth qino ‘woman, female’, ON kona id. (partly from *k(w)unōn), OE cwéne ‘woman, wife, queen, prostitute’, OS quena ‘woman, wife’, OHG quena ‘wife’. Cf. also *kwēniz. Particularly close to Alb grua ~ grue ‘woman’ < *g⁄nōn (with dissimilation) unless the latter continues Gk γραῦς ‘old woman’. Related to Toch A śäṃ, B śana id., Skt gnā ‘goddess, divine female’, Av g 6 nā ‘woman, wife’, Arm kin id., Gk γυνή ‘woman’, OIr ben id., OPrus genno id., Slav *žena id. (Kuhn KZ I 129). Zupitza Gutt. 81, 86; Pedersen Kelt. Gr. I 47; Torp-Falk 60; Trautmann BSW 84; Holthausen AEEW 64; Schulze Kl. Schr. 850; Feist 388; Mayrhofer I 351; Pokorny I 473–474; Trubachev Rod. 105; Vries ANEW 325; Zalizniak II 196; Frisk I 333–335; Onions 731; Peeters IF XCIV 296; Lehmann GED 276–277; Kortlandt TIES II 77–79 (erroneously adds Toch A kuli, B klīye ‘woman’); Bammesberger Nom. 177, 203; Orel AED 125; Adams TB 621.
ON kyrkja, kvirkja ‘to strangle’, OFris querka, quertza id., MLG querken id. Derived from *kwerkō. Vries ANEW 341; Boutkan OFED s.v.

ON kverk ‘throat’, MLG querke, quarke id., OHG querca id. An obscure derivative of IE *g ⁄er H- ‘to swallow’, cf. Gk βάραθρον ‘gulf, pit’, Lith γυρκλίσ ‘throat’, Slav *gъrdlo id., *žьrdlo ‘orifice’. The same structure is found in Lat gurg-itō ‘to engulf’, gurguliō ‘cornworm, weevil’ (Torp-Falk 61). Zupitza Gutt. 86; Trautmann BSW 89–90; Jóhannesson IEW 417; Pokorny I 475; Vries ANEW 337; Vasmer-Trubachev I 441–442.
ON kvern-steinn ‘millstone’, OE cweorn-stán id., OS quirnstēn id., OHG quirn-stein id. Compound of *kwernuz ~ *kwernō and *stainaz. Carr Nom. comp. 51.

Goth asiluqairnus ‘millstone’, ON kvern ‘handmill’ (pl. < *kwerniz), OE cweorn ‘mill, quern’, OFris quern ‘millstone’, OS querna id., OHG quirn, quirna id. Etymologically close to OPrus girnoywis ‘mill’, Lith gīrna ‘millstone’, Slav *žьrny id. Petersson SVSL I 141; Zupitza Gutt. 84; Torp-Falk 61; Trautmann BSW 89; Holthausen AEEW 65; Feist 59; Porzig Gliederung 140; Pokorny I 477; Chemodanov SGGJa I 82; Vries ANEW 337; Zalizniak II 201; Onions 731; Vasmer-Trubachev II 49–50; Lehmann GED 44–45.

OE part. a-cworren ‘drunk’. Related to Skt giráti ‘to swallow, to eat’, Av úaraiti ‘to swallow’, Gk βιβρώσκω ‘to eat, to swallow’, Lat uorō ‘to swallow’, Lith geriū, gérti ‘to drink’, Slav *ἤьρǫ, *ἤερτι ‘to swallow’. Persson Beiträge 534, 755; Bartholomae 512; Torp-Falk 61; Trautmann BSW 89– 91; Holthausen AEEW 65; Walde- Hofmann II 836; Mayrhofer I 335; Pokorny I 474–476; Fraenkel 148–149; Frisk I 235–236; Seebold 318.

Goth qairrus ‘gentle, friendly’, ON kyrr ‘still, quiet’, MLG quere ‘tame’, MHG kirre, kürre id. Related to Hitt kariƒa- ‘to stop, to pause, to rest’, Toch A kur-, B kwär- ‘to become feeble, to age’, Lith gūrti ‘to collapse, to be weak’, Latv guıt ‘to slacken’. Karsten NB II 367; Grienberger Got. 171–172 (to Gk γῆρυς ‘voice’); Johansson KZ XXXII 479 (to Skt guru- ‘heavy’, Gk βαρύς id.); Siebs KZ XXXVII 317 (same as Johansson); Brugmann IF XXXIII 302; Zupitza Gutt. 84; Torp-Falk 61–62 (follow Siebs); Feist 386; Fraenkel KZ LXIX 78 (to Lith gulūs ‘tired, weak’); Pokorny I 476–477; Fraenkel 179; Vries ANEW 341, MSS XXXV 73–92 (to IE *g⁄er H- ‘to praise’, with Gmc *-rr- < *-r H- and secondary u-stem); Lehmann GED 275; Bammesberger Nom. 262; Knobloch KZ CIII 286–288 (u-stem under the influence of *xarđuz; < *g⁄er-ero- ‘driven by a goad’); Heidermanns 350–352; Puhvel IV 82–83; Kluge-Seebold 443; Dybo BSA 22 (follows Siebs).

Goth ga-qiss ‘agreement, arrangement’, OE cwis ‘saying, speaking’. Continues *g ⁄et-ti-, a derivative in *-ti- of *kweþanan. Holthausen AEEW 66; Feist 196; Onions 89; Seebold 319; Lehmann GED 32; Bammesberger Nom. 142.
Goth qistjan ‘to destroy’, Dan dial. quista ‘to press’, MLG quisten ‘to destroy’. Derived from *kwestō. Torp-Falk 63; Feist 388–389; Lehmann GED 277.

MLG quist ‘torment’, MLG quist ‘destruction’, OHG quist ‘torment’. Related to the isolated Slav *žestъ ‘hard’. Fick BB VI 211 (to IE *g ⁄eƒ6- ‘to press, to force’); Hirt PBB XXIII 352 (to Skt jásate ‘to be exhausted’); Brugmann IF VI 103 (to ON kvistr ‘twig, branch’); Zupitza Gutt. 87; Grienberger Got. 172 (to Lith gendū, gēsti ‘to become defective’); Uhlenbeck PBB XXX 304 (contra Brugmann); Wood KZ XLV 64 (same as Fick), MLN XVII 9, XXV 74; Sperber Wu S VI 31–32 (same as Brugmann); Sturtevant Language VI 226 (to Hitt kištari ‘to be wiped out’); Vries ANEW 339; Vasmer- Trubachev II 50; Lehmann GED 277; Puhvel IV 121–123 (to Hitt kašt-, kišt- ‘hunger, starvation’), 168.

Goth qiþan ‘to say’, ON kveäa id., OE cweäan id., OFris quetha id., OS quethan id., OHG quedan id. Probably related to Slav *gatati ‘to speak’, a variant of *gadati id. Wiedemann IF I 513 (to OIr bél ‘lip, mouth’ < *g ⁄etlo-, Arm ko‘em ‘to call’ < *g ⁄otƒ-); Bezzenberger BB XII 239, XVI 245 (to Lat arbiter ‘spectator, witness’); Torp-Falk 59–60; Feist 389–390; Holthausen AEEW 65; Johanisson ANF LXII 196– 199 (development of meaning); Morgenstierne NTS VII 116–120 (Iranian parallels); Jóhannesson IEW 410–411; Pokorny I 480–481; Chemodanov SGGJa I 110; Vries ANEW 336; Onions 734; Seebold 318–320; Lehmann GED 277–278 (from *g ⁄et- > Skt gádati ‘to say, to speak’); Boutkan OFED s.v.

ME cwéd ‘evil, bad’, OFris quād id., MLG quāt id., OHG quāt id. Related to Lith sbst. gėda ‘shame’ (Brugmann IF V 375–376), Slav *gydъkъ ‘disgusting’ and Alb zi ‘black’ < *g ⁄edi-. Hirt IF XXXVII 236 (to Skt gūtha- ‘excrement’); Zupitza Gutt. 86; Torp-Falk 60; Trautmann BSW 81; Holthausen AEEW 64; Pokorny I 484; Fraenkel 142; Stang LS 21; Heidermanns 348–349; Orel AED 524; Kluge-Seebold 480.

Goth un-qeþs ‘unspeakable, unatterable’, ON sam-kvæär ‘concordant’, OE sóä-cwæde ‘veracious’. Derived from *kweþanan. Karsten NB I 98–99; Feist 522; Seebold 319; Bammesberger Nom. 261; Matzel Festschr. Schröder 102, KZ CV 113; Heidermanns 349.

ON kvæmr ‘free to come’, OE ze-cwéme ‘pleasant, grateful, acceptable’, OFris *kēme ‘pretty, beautiful’, MDu be-quame ‘useful’, OHG bi-quāmi ‘suitable’. Derived from *kwemanan. Karsten NB I 96; Torp-Falk 61; Weisweiler IF LIII 55; Vries ANEW 339; Seebold 316; Bammesberger Nom. 260; Matzel Festschr. Schröder 100, 117, KZ CV 108; Heidermanns 350; Kluge-Seebold 98.

Goth qens ‘wife’, ON kván, kvæn id., OE cwén ‘woman, wife, queen’, OS quān ‘woman, wife’. Related to Skt jāni- ‘wife’ (in cmpn.), Av úāni- ‘woman’ (Streitberg IF III 330). Further connected with *kwenōn. Bartholomae 603–604; Torp-Falk 60; Feist 386; Mayrhofer I 429; Pokorny I 473–474; Vries ANEW 339; Zalizniak II 196; Onions 731; Lehmann GED 275–276.
ON kvik-fé ‘livestock’, OFris quik-fia id. Compound of *kwikwaz and *fexu. Carr Nom. comp. 87 (parallel formations).

ON kvikr ‘alive’, OE cwic id., OFris quik ‘living’, OS quik ‘living, alive’, OHG quec ‘alive’. Secondary phonetic or taboo development of *kwiwaz. Torp-Falk 63; Holthausen AEEW 65; Chemodanov SGGJa I 77; Vries ANEW 338; Onions 731; Lerchner 174; Hamp Festschr. Palmer 89; Piirainen 43–44; Lehmann GED 278; Bammesberger GL XXVI 259–263 (inlaut *-kw- from reduplicated verb forms); Klein Kratylos XXXVII 140; Heidermanns 352–353; Kluge-Seebold 435.
ON kveykva, keykva ‘to quicken, to kindle’, MLG quicken ‘to enliven, to animate’, OHG quicken id. Cf. also Goth ga-qiujan ‘to make alive’. Derived from *kwikwaz. Feist 390; Heidermanns 352.
ON kvika ‘flesh under nails or claws’, OE cwice ‘quick-growing grass’, MDu queke ‘flesh under nails or claws’, OHG quecka ‘intiba’. Derived from *kwikwaz. Vries ANEW 338.
ON kvisa ‘to whisper, to gossip’, LG dial. quesen ‘to moan, to grumble’, G dial. queisen ‘to whimper’. Of imitative origin. Vries ANEW 338–339.

Goth qiþus ‘stomach, belly, womb’, ON kviär ‘womb’, OE cwiä ‘womb’, OHG quiti id. (< masc. *kwiþiz). Identical with OIr bith ‘world’, W byd id., Bret bed id. < Celt *bitu- (Pedersen Kelt. Gr. I 41). Further connected with *g⁄eƒ6- ‘to live’. For the semantic development cf. Russ život ‘belly’ < Slav *životъ ‘life’ based on the same root. Schwentner IF LXI 234– 236; Zupitza Gutt. 85; Grienberger Got. 173 (to Lat uitulus ‘calf’); Torp-Falk 60; Berneker I 297 (to Slav pl. *gatje ‘pants’); Wood KZ XLV 60 (follows Grienberger), MP XVIII 303 (to Lat guttus ‘vessel’); Holthausen AEEW 66; Feist 390; Pokorny I 467–469, 481 (to OIr bél ‘lip’); Vries ANEW 338 (to Lat botulus ‘sausage’); Zalizniak II 201; Anttila Schw. 91; Meid Festschr. Knobloch 253–254 (follows Pedersen); Lehmann GED 278; Bammesberger Nom. 159; Meid Festschr. Knobloch 253–254; Kluge-Seebold 496; Adams TB 156 (to Toch A kāts, B kātso ‘belly, stomach’ < *g⁄ōt-ƒōn-).

Goth qius ‘alive’. Identical with IE *g ⁄i⁄os id.: Skt jīvá-, OPers úīva-, Gk βίος ‘life’, Lat uīuus, OIr béu, béo (with short *-i- as in Gmc), Lith gÿvas, Slav *ἤιωъ derived from *g⁄ei- ‘to live’ (Aufrecht KZ I 160). Karsten NB II 409–411, 435; Zupitza Gutt. 87; Pedersen Kelt. Gr. I 62; Torp-Falk 63; Trautmann BSW 76; Walde-Hofmann II 808–810; Feist 390–391; Mayrhofer I 440; Pokorny I 467–469; Dybo VSJa 24 (pretonic shortening in Gmc); Chemodanov SGGJa I 77; Fraenkel 154; Frisk I 237–239; Onions 731; Lindeman Verschärfung 16.27; Lehmann GED 278; Schrijver LL 355 (follows Dybo); Heidermanns 352–353.
ON hug-kœmr ‘ingenious’, OE cwéme ‘pleasant, grateful, acceptable’. Derived from *kwemanan. Karsten NB I 115–116; Matzel Festschr. Schröder 107; Heidermanns 353–354.
Goth ga-qumþs ‘gathering, synagogue, assembly’, ON sam-kund
Goth qums ‘coming, arrival’, OE cyme ‘coming, approach’, OFris on-keme ‘penetration’, OS kumi ‘advent’, l
Norw labb ‘paw’, OHG lappo ‘palmula, oar blade’. An expressive form probably related to *lōfōn (Torp-Falk 370). Holthausen PBB LXVI 266; Lehmann GED 237.

Icel leäja ‘clay’, OHG letto id. Related to MIr laith ‘marsh, swamp’, W llaid ‘mud’ < *latƒo-. Torp- Falk 360 (to *slađan); Mühlenbach- Endzelin II 425 (to Latv rvn. Late); Pokorny I 654–655; Levickij IF CIII 221.

ON lag ‘layer, shape’, OE pl. ze-lazu ‘collection of water’, OS pl. aldarlagu ‘life-time’, OHG ur-laz ‘fate’ (masc.). Derived from *lezjanan. Torp-Falk 358; Holthausen AEEW 242; Pokorny I 658–659; Vries ANEW 683; Onions 518.

Goth lagjan ‘to lay’, ON leggja ‘to lay, to put’, OE leczan ‘to lay, to put, to place’, OFris ledza ‘to lay’, OS leggian ‘to produce’, OHG leggen ‘to lay’. Derived from *lezjanan. Structurally close to Slav *ložiti ‘to put, to lay’ (Collitz Prät. 77–78; Pedersen Kelt. Gr. II 572). Torp-Falk 358; Meillet MSL IX 144; Holthausen AEEW 197; Feist 319; OHG fona-uf-kume ‘arrival’. Derived from *kwemanan. Holthausen AEEW 67; Feist 391; Seebold 316; Lehmann GED 279; Bammesberger Nom. 131.
ON kÿmi-ligr ‘amusing, funny’, OE cym-líc ‘comely, convenient’. Derived from *k(w)ūmjaz. Holthausen AEEW 67; Vries ANEW 340.

OE cyme ‘becoming, suitable, lovely’, OS cūmi ‘weak’, MHG kūme ‘thin, weak’. Derived from *kwemanan. Torp-Falk 45 (to Gk γύαλον ‘hollow’); Holthausen AEEW 67. Pokorny I 659; Onions 519; Seebold 325; Lehmann GED 5, 233; Kluge- Seebold 509–510.
ON laga ‘to shape, to put right, to mend’, OFris lagia ‘to establish’. Derived from *lazan or *lezjanan. Seebold 325; Boutkan OFED s.v.

ON lǫgr ‘sea, lake’, OE lazu ‘sea, water’, OS lagu-strōm ‘sea current’. Identical with Lat lacus ‘lake’, OIr loch id. Further related to Gk λάκκος ‘pit, tank, pond’ < *λάκ Wow, Slav *loky ‘pond, pool’ (Kern KZ II 553–554). Fick I 531 (to *lezjanan); Torp-Falk 358; Pedersen Kelt. Gr. I 149; Trautmann BSW 149; Holthausen AEEW 194; Walde-Hofmann I 748; Pokorny I 653; Chemodanov SSGJa I 54; Frisk II 75–76; Lehmann GED 224; Trubachev ESSJa XVI 10–11; Bammesberger Nom. 156.

ON lær ‘leg’, OE leów ‘thigh, ham’, Langob neut. lagi ‘thigh’. Connected with Lith l Ekti ‘to fly’, Latv lekt id. as a regular deverbative (for the vocalism and semantics cf. Lith lãktë ‘pole on which birds sit’). Miklosich 171 (to Slav *lęga ‘thigh’); Zupitza Gutt. 65, 134 (reconstructs *laz(w)jaz); Lidén BB XXI 94; Torp-Falk 357 (to Lat lacertus ‘lizard’, Lith alkúnë ‘elbow’); Pokorny I 673; Onions 522.

Icel lá ‘to rebuke’, OE leán ‘to blame, to reproach’, OS lahan id., OHG lahan id. Related to OIr locht ‘guilt, mistake’ < *lok-tu-. Zupitza Gutt. 209; Torp-Falk 357; Holthausen AEEW 197; Pokorny I 673; Seebold 321.
ON poet. lá ‘sea’, OHG lah ‘pool, swamp’. Related to *lazuz. Torp-Falk 358; Pokorny I 653; Chemodanov SSGJa I 54.

ON lax ‘salmon’, OE leax id., OS lahs id., OHG lahs id. Related to Toch B laks ‘fish’ (from Toch A?), OPrus lasasso ‘salmon’, Lith lãšis id., Latv lasis id., Slav lososь id. < IE *la˚so-. Löwenthal KZ LII 98 (to Lith lãšas ‘drop’), Wu S X 140; Zupitza Gutt. 191; Torp- Falk 357; Schrader-Nehring II 2; Trautmann BSW 150; Morgenstierne NTS VII 120 (adds Osset læsæg ‘salmon’); Holthausen AEEW 197; Specht 31; Thieme KZ LXIX 209–216 (to Skt lākßā ‘red varnish’); Krogmann KZ LXXVI 161–178; Porzig Gliederung 184; Mayrhofer ZDMG CV 175–177; Pokorny I 653; Chemodanov SGGJa I 111; Fraenkel 341–342; Zalizniak I 134; Onions 519; Van Windekens ZDMG CXX 305–307 (on Toch B laks); Abaev II 32 (Osset læsæg from a European IE dialect); Adams IF XC 918–919; Diebold Evol. passim; Toporov PJa IV 101–110; Trubachev ESSJa XVI 88–90; Kluge- Seebold 498; Adams TB 544.
OFris laster ‘slander, damage’, OS lastar ‘disgrace’, OHG lastar id. Derived from *laxstuz. Torp-Falk 357; Pokorny I 673; Zalizniak II 226; Boutkan OFED s.v.

ON lǫstr ‘fault, flaw, blunder’. Cf. also OE leahter ‘moral defect, crime’ < *laxtraz. Derived from *laxanan. Torp-Falk 357; Holthausen AEEW 196; Pokorny I 673; Zalizniak II 226; Bammesberger Nom. 86.

Goth bi-laibjan ‘to leave behind’, ON leifa ‘to leave a heritage, to relinquish’, OE læfan ‘to leave, to remain’, OFris lēva ‘to bequeath’, OS lēvian ‘to leave’, OHG leiben id. Causative of *lībanan. Torp-Falk 369; Holthausen AEEW 191; Feist 319; Pokorny I 670; Onions 521; Seebold 326; Lehmann GED 69.

Goth laiba ‘remains, remainder’, ON pl. leifar ‘leavings, remnants’, OE láf ‘remnant, remains’, OFris lāva id., OS lēba id., OHG leiba id. Derived from *lībanan. Torp-Falk 369; Holthausen AEEW 194; Feist 319; Pokorny I 670; Zalizniak I 147; Seebold 326; Lehmann GED 225–226; Bammesberger Nom. 106.

ON leiäa ‘to lead, to conduct’, OE lædan id., OFris lēda id., OS lēdian ‘to bring’, OHG leiten ‘to lead’. Causative of *līþanan. Torp-Falk 368; Holthausen AEEW 191; Pokorny I 672; Onions 520 (from *laiđō); Seebold 329; Kluge-Seebold 514.

ON leiä ‘way, road, course’ (partly < *laiđiz), OE lád ‘course, way, watercourse’, OFris lēde ‘way, direction’, OHG leita id. Derived from *līþanan. Torp-Falk 368; Holthausen AEEW 190; Pokorny I 672; Zalizniak II 193; Onions 533; Seebold 329; Bammesberger Nom. 107.

ON lán, lén ‘fief ’, OE læn ‘loan, grant, fief’ (fem.), OFris lēn id., OS lēhan id., OHG lēhan ‘loan’. Derived from *līxwanan. Structurally similar with Skt rékṇas- ‘inherited possession, property’, Av raēx 6 nah- ‘inheritance’. Meillet MSL XV 254–256; Bartholomae 1480; Torp-Falk 367; Holthausen AEEW 191; Mayrhofer III 72; Pokorny I 669; Onions 533; Benveniste 150–157; Seebold 327; Lehmann GED 230; Bammesberger Nom. 72, 147; Heidermanns 356; Kluge-Seebold 510.

Goth laikan ‘to jump, to hop’, ON leika ‘to play, to sport’, OE lácan ‘to swing, to play’, MHG part. geleichen ‘cheated’. Related to Skt réjate ‘to tremble’, Gk λιγύς ‘clear, shrill (of sound)’, λιγαίνω ‘to shout, to sing, to dance’, Lith láigyti ‘to run about wildly’ (Diefenbach VW II 768). Schweizer KZ I 559; Bugge KZ XX (1872) 11–12 (to Lat lūdere ‘to play’); Zupitza Gutt. 90; Torp-Falk 354–355; Feist 319–320; Holthausen IF XXV 148, AEEW 190; Mikkola BB XXV 75; Jóhannesson IEW 734–735; Mayrhofer III 72; Pokorny I 667–668; Fraenkel 330–331; Frisk II 121–122; Onions 515; Seebold 321–322; Lehmann GED 225.

Goth laiks ‘frolic, dance’, ON leikr ‘game, play, sport’, OE lác ‘battle, struggle, sacrifice’ (neut., fem.), OHG leih ‘melody, tune’. Derived from *laikanan. Zupitza Gutt. 163; Torp-Falk 355; Holthausen AEEW 190; Feist 319; Pokorny I 667; Zalizniak II 187; Beck Festschr. Höfler I 1–16; Seebold 322; Lehmann GED 225; Kluge-Seebold 511.

OE neut. lám ‘clay, mud, earth’, OS lēmo ‘clay’, OHG leim id., leimo id. Connected with Gk λειμών ‘moist place, meadow’, Lat līmus ‘silt’ < *loimos (Solmsen KZ XXXVIII 453). Further see *līman ~ *līmaz. Schulze KZ XLV 55; Torp-Falk 365; Holthausen AEEW 194; Walde-Hofmann I 804–805; Trier Lehm 10–16; Pokorny I 662; Chemodanov SSGJa I 55; Kluge-Seebold 510.

Goth lais ‘to know’. Of uncertain origin. Presumably connected with *laisōn (Pedersen IF V 40; Fick KZ XVIII 412–413). Meillet IF XXVI 200–203 (innovation based on *waita); Marstrander NTS II 99–103 (to OIr lenaid ‘to follow’); Torp-Falk 370; Feist 320; Benveniste EGS I 1–5 (against the reconstruction of *leis-); Pokorny I 671; Seebold 322–323; Lehmann GED 225 (Gothic innovation).

Goth laisjan ‘to teach’, OE læran id., OFris lēra id., OS lērian id., OHG lēren id. Causative of *laisa. Torp-Falk 369–370; Feist 320; Holthausen AEEW 192; Pokorny I 671; Seebold 323; Lehmann GED 225; Kluge-Seebold 511.

OS wagan-lēsa ‘wagon track’, OHG wagan-leisa id., MHG leise, leis ‘track’. If the connection with *laisa is accepted, cf. also WGmc *laizō > OE lár ‘teaching, learning’, OFris lāre id., OS lēra id., OHG lēra id. Etymologically identical with Slav *lîxa ‘land strip, track, furrow’ < *loisā (Pedersen IF V 40). Cf. also Lat līra ‘furrow’ < *leisā, Lith lÿsë ‘gardenbed’. Torp-Falk 369–370; Holthausen AEEW 195; Walde-Hofmann I 812–813; Pokorny I 671; Chemodanov SGGJa I 67–68; Fraenkel 376; Onions 537; Trubachev ESSJa XVI 184–186; Bammesberger Nom. 116 (from IE *loitt-ā); Kluge-Seebold 310, 511.

Goth laists ‘track, trace’, ON leistr ‘foot below the ankle, short sock’, OE læst ‘step, footstep, sole’, lást id., MLG lēste ‘last’, OHG leist ‘foot trace’. Derived from *laisōn. Torp- Falk 369; Holthausen AEEW 192; Feist 321; Pokorny I 671; Zalizniak II 187; Onions 516; Lehmann GED 226; Kluge- Seebold 514.

Goth laistjan ‘to follow’, OE læstan ‘to follow, to accompany, to do, to perform’, OFris lāsta, lēsta ‘to fulfill’, OS lēstian ‘to pursue, to carry out’, OHG leisten ‘to follow, to carry out’. Derived from *laistiz ~ *laistaz. Torp- Falk 369; Holthausen AEEW 192; Feist 321; Pokorny I 671; Onions 516; Lehmann GED 226; Kluge-Seebold 514; Boutkan OFED s.v.
ON leiä-indr ‘loathsome, tiresome’, OE láä-wende ‘evil, hostile’. Compound of *laiþaz and *wanđjaz (see *wanđjanan). Carr Nom. comp. 62.

Burg *laiþs ‘tired, hated’, ON leiär ‘loathed, disliked’, OE láä ‘causing hate, evil, hated’, OFris lēth id., OS lēth ‘hostile’, OHG leid ‘hated, spoiled’. Related to Gk ἀλιταίνω ‘to transgress’, ἀλείτης ‘sinner’ (Saussure Mémoire 75), OIr liuss ‘loathing’ < *lit-tu-. Grienberger Got. 192–193 (to *slīþijaz); Torp-Falk 355 (derived from ON læ ‘harm, damage’ < *laiwan); Holthausen AEEW 195–196; Maurer passim; Pokorny I 672; Frisk I 66; Onions 533; Lehmann GED 315; Bammesberger Nom. 237 (to *līþanan); Köbler 712; Heidermanns 357–358; Kluge-Seebold 512.
ON leiäa ‘to make smb. loathe’, OS a-lēthian ‘to spoil, to make hated’, OHG leiden ‘to feel distasted, to hate’. Derived from *laiþaz. Torp-Falk 355; Onions 533; Heidermanns 357.

Sweb *lawerka ‘lark’, OSwed lœrikia id., ON lævirki id., OE láwerce id., NFris lāsk (< *laiwas(i)kōn), OS lēwerka id., OHG lērihha id. A difficult word, probably an old compound. Torp-Falk 355; Kluge Nom. Stamm 32–33; Vasmer Zfslav Ph XII 103 (reconstructs Gmc *laiwō ‘lark’ > Slvn léva id.); Holthausen KZ L 141–142 (*laiw- to Gk λαίειν: φθέγεσθαι), AEEW 193; Onions 515; Köbler 715; Kluge-Seebold 515.

ON leir ‘clay, earth, mud’. Identical with OPrus layso ‘earth, clay’. See *laimōn. Torp-Falk 365; Porzig Gliederung 146; Pokorny I 662–663; Chemodanov SSGJa I 55; Stang LS 31; Merlingen IF LXXXIII 58–59; Toporov PJa IV 41.

ON lakan ‘bed-sheet’, OFris leken ‘piece of fabric, linen’, OS lakan ‘cloth’, OHG lahhan id. Identical with Gk λάγανον ‘thin broad cake’ further related to λαγαίω ‘to release’. Derived from *lakaz. Torp-Falk 356; Pokorny I 959–960; Frisk II 68; Kluge- Seebold 500.

ON lakr ‘lacking, defective, lacking in quality’, MLG lak ‘slack, loose’. Cf. *slakaz. Probably related to Gk λήγω ‘to stop, to end’, Lat langueō ‘to be slack’. Torp-Falk 356; Walde-Hofmann I 758–759; Pokorny I 959–960; Vries ANEW 344; Frisk II 113–114; Onions 511; Heidermanns 359; Kluge-Seebold 498.

OE læccan ‘to take, to seize, to grasp’. Identical with Gk λάζομαι ‘to seize, to grasp’ < *λαγ⁄-ω-, Hom aor. đllabe. Torp-Falk 356; Wood Postcons. 117 (to Lat laciō ‘to entice, to allure’); Holthausen AEEW 190; Pokorny I 958; Frisk II 71; Onions 516.

ON lami ‘lame’, OFris lom id., OS lam ‘withered’, OHG lam ‘lame, paralyzed’. Related to IE *lem- ‘to break’. Structurally close to Lith ãplamas ‘awkward, clumsy’, Slav *lomъ ‘breaking, something broken’. Karsten NB II 452–453; Stokes KZ XXXVII 258 (to OIr laime, láime ‘axe’); Torp-Falk 363; Holthausen AEEW 194, 423; Pokorny I 674; Fraenkel 13; Vries ANEW 345; Onions 513; Stang LS 33; Trubachev ESSJa XVI 25–27; Heidermanns 359– 360; Kluge-Seebold 499.

Goth lamb ‘lamb’, ON lamb id., OE lamb id., Northumb. lemb id. (< *lambez), OFris lamb id., OS lamb id., OHG lamb (pl. lembir). Of unknown origin. The derivationally unusual NCauc *∑Vmbag V ‘sheep’ may be somehow connected. Fick I 188 (to Skt rámbhate ‘to roar’); Osthoff Etym. I 308; Bugge PBB XIII 315–116 (to *el-en- ‘deer’); Mikkola BB XXI 219–220 (to Alb lopë ‘cow’, Latv luõps ‘cattle’); Hirt Idg. Gr. II 155; Petersson Heter. 22–23 (secondary nasalization); Torp-Falk 363–364 (to Gk ἔλαφος ‘deer’ < *ελἢβηο-); Holthausen AEEW 194; Feist 321; Schulze Kl. Schr. 254; Pokorny I 304; Zalizniak II 223; Onions 512; Benveniste IEL 48; Lehmann GED 226; Kluge-Seebold 500– 501; Nikolayev-Starostin NCED 757.
ON lemd ‘lameness (from a blow)’, OFris lemethe ‘paralysis’, MLG lemede id., MHG lemede, lemde id. Derived from *lamaz ~ *lamōn. Heidermanns 360.

ON lemja ‘to thrash, to flog, to lame’, OE lemian ‘to lame, to cripple’, OFris lema, lemma ‘to paralize’, OS part. bi-lemid ‘paralyzed’, OHG lemmen ‘to paralyze, to lame’. Derived from *lamaz ~ *lamōn. Structurally close to Lith lãmyti ‘to engulf’, Slav *lomiti ‘to break’. Torp- Falk 363; Būga RFV LXVII 242; Trautmann BSW 162; Holthausen AEEW 198; Pokorny I 674; Fraenkel 338; Stang LS 33; Trubachev ESSJa XVI 16–19; Heidermanns 360.
ON láss ‘lock, latch’, MLG lōs id. Probably related to *lamaz ~ *lamōn. Pedersen KZ XXXII 252; Torp-Falk 363.
ON land-búi ‘inhabitant’, OHG lant-būwo id. Compound of *lanđan and *būwōn. Carr Nom. comp. 84 (parallel formations).
ON land-kaup ‘purchase of land’, OFris lond-kāp id., MLG lant-kōp id. Compound of *lanđan and *kaupan ~ *kaupaz. Carr Nom. comp. 76 (parallel formations).

Goth land ‘land’, ON land id., OE land id., OFris land, lond id., OS land id., OHG lant id. Related to OIr land ‘open place’, OPrus lindan ‘valley’, Slav *lędo ‘waste ground’. Schweizer KZ I 559; Much ZDADL XLII 170 (on ON lundr ‘grove’); Lewy PBB XXXII 138; Torp-Falk 362; Trautmann BSW 157; Holthausen AEEW 194; Feist 321–322; Pokorny I 675; Chemodanov SSGJa I 54–55; Zalizniak I 142; Onions 513; Scardigli Goten 47 (non-IE origin following from comparison with Basque landa ‘field’); Stang LS 33; Mažiulis PKP II 138 n. 221 (to Balt *lind- ‘to fall down’); Trubachev ESSJa XV 44–48; Toporov PJa IV 263–269; Lehmann GED 226– 227; Kluge-Seebold 501.
ON land-siär ‘custom of the land’, OE land-sidu id., OS landsidu id., MHG lant-site id. Compound of *lanđan and *seđuz. Carr Nom. comp. 76 (parallel formations).
ON land-skapr ‘region’, OHG lant-scaf ‘land’. Derived from *lanđan. Carr Nom. comp. 76 (parallel formations).
ON land-skyld ‘rent of land’, OS land-skuld id., MHG lantschult id. Compound of *lanđan and *skulđiz. Carr Nom. comp. 84 (parallel formations).

ON lend ‘loin’, OE pl. lendenu ‘loins, reins’, OFris lenden id., OS pl. lendin id., OHG lentī id. Related to Lat lumbus ‘thigh’ < *londh⁄os (Kuhn KZ XVII 233; Osthoff Perf. 534), Slav *lędvьje id. (Schmidt Plur. 6). Torp-Falk 362; Trautmann BSW 158; Holthausen AEEW 198; Walde-Hofmann I 832; Pokorny I 674; Chemodanov SGGJa I 61; Zalizniak II 220; Trubachev ESSJa XV 48–50; Kluge-Seebold 515.
ON lenda ‘to land’, OHG lenten id. Derived from *lanđan. Kluge-Seebold 501.
ON landi ‘countryman’, OE ze-londa id., MDu ge-lande id., OHG gi-lanto id. Derived from *lanđan. Vries ANEW 345.

ON lang-ær ‘long-lasting’. Compound of *lanzaz I and *aiwiz. Might be identical with Lat longaevus ‘long-lived’ (Bugge BB III 104) if the latter is not a translation of Gk μακραίων id. (Walde-Hofmann I 820). Pokorny I 17; Vries ANEW 681; Lehmann GED 22.
ON lang-lífr ‘longlived’, OE lanz-lífe id., OHG lang-līb id. Compound of *lanzaz I and *līb(j)az (see *lībanan). Carr Nom. comp. 66.
OESc lang-liker ‘stopping for a long time’, OHG lang-līh ‘long’. Derived from *lanzaz I. Heidermanns 361.
Goth lagga-modei ‘patience’, OHG lang-muotī id. Compound of *lanzaz I and *mōđaz. Probably, a calque of Gk μακροθυμία id. Feist 318; Lehmann GED 224.
ON lang-rækr ‘having a long memory, brooding long over past wrongs’, MHG lanc-ræche ‘vindictive’. Compound of *lanzaz I and *wrēkiz. Torp-Falk 361.

Goth laggs ‘long’, ON langr id., OE lanz id., OFris long id., OS lang id., OHG lang id. Identical with Lat longus id. further related to other similar adjectives with irregular phonetic correspondences: Skt dīrgha-, Av dar 6 ga-, Gk δολιχός, Hitt dalugai-. Schweizer KZ I 558; Bartholomae 693–694; Karsten NB I 44; Zupitza Gutt. 218; Torp-Falk 360–361; Walde-Hofmann I 820–821; Feist 318–319; Holthausen AEEW 195; Porzig Gliederung 123; Mayrhofer II 47; Pokorny I 196–197; Chemodanov SGGJa I 103; Frisk I 406–407; Onions 536; Strung Glotta XLVII 3–4; Lehmann GED 224; Lühr Expr. 161; Heidermanns 360–361; Kluge-Seebold 502.

ON end-langr ‘from one end to another’, OE and-lanz ‘continuous, all-along, throughout’, OFris adv. ondlenghe ‘stretching’, OS ant-lang ‘continuous’, OHG gi-lang ‘related’. Related to Lith lenkiū, leñkti ‘to incline, to tilt’ and identical with Lith lañkas ‘curve, circle’, Slav *lǫkъ ‘curve, arc’. The meanings of *lanzaz II must have been influenced by *lanzaz I. Persson Beiträge 340, 345; Trautmann BSW 159–161; Holthausen AEEW 195; Wissmann NP 24; Pokorny I 676–677; Fraenkel 356–357; Vries ANEW 9; Trubachev ESSJa XVI 148– 149; Heidermanns 361–362.
ON lengd ‘length’, OE lenzä id., MLG lengde id. Derived from *lanzaz I. Torp-Falk 361; Holthausen AEEW 199; Onions 523.
Goth laggei ‘length’, OE lenzu id., OFris lentze id., OHG lengī id. Derived from *lanzaz I. Holthausen AEEW 199; Feist 318; Heidermanns 361.

ON lengja ‘to lengthen’, OE lenzan id., OHG lengen id. Derived from *lanzaz I. Derivationally similar to Slav *lǫ‘iti ‘to join, to connect’. Torp-Falk 361; Holthausen AEEW 198; Onions 530; Heidermanns 361.
ON lengja ‘oblong piece’, MLG lenge ‘long rope’. Related to *lanzaz I. Torp-Falk 360 (to *lanxaz); Chemodanov SGGJa I 82; Gołąb Origins 132.

ON langa ‘to long for, to yearn’, OE lanzian ‘to cause longing’, OS langōn ‘to long for, to yearn’, OHG gi-langōn ‘to gain, to attain’. Derived from *lanzaz II. Structurally identical with Slav *lǫkati ‘to separate, to peel’. Torp-Falk 361; Holthausen AEEW 195; Onions 536; Trubachev ESSJa XVI 142–143; Heidermanns 362; Kluge-Seebold 858.
OE lóh ‘strap’. Related to *lanzaz II. Identical with Slav *lǫkъ ‘curve, arc’. Torp-Falk 360; Holthausen AEEW 206; Gołąb Origins 132; Trubachev ESSJa XVI 148–149.

ON lǫn ‘small oblong hayrick, row of houses’, OE lane ‘lane, street’, OFris lan, lone ‘way’, Du laan ‘alley’. Could be compared with the etymologically obscure Slav *lono ‘lap’. Torp-Falk 354 (to Gk ἐλαύνω ‘to drive’); Holthausen AEEW 194; Onions 514; Vasmer- Trubachev II 517 (Slav *lono < *logh-snoin parallel to *λοἤεσνο id. < *logh-esno-).
OHG laffan ‘to slurp’. Related to Lat lambō ‘to lick’. Torp- Falk 362; Walde-Hofmann I 754; Pokorny I 651; Seebold 323–324.
Icel lepja ‘to lick’, OHG gi-lepphen ‘to ladle, to scoop’. Derived from *lapanan. Torp-Falk 362; Onions 514; Seebold 323; Kluge-Seebold 523 (to Slav *lobъzati ‘to kiss’).

OE læppa ‘skirt, ear-lobe’, OFris lappa ‘cloth, rag’, OS lappo id., OHG *lapfo id. Cf. also ON leppr ‘rag, tatter’ < *lappjaz. Related to Gk λοβός ‘lobe’, Alb labë ‘bark’. Noreen Abriß 121 (to Lat labō ‘to totter, to be ready to fall’); Torp-Falk 363; Holthausen AEEW 192; Pokorny I 655–656; Frisk II 131– 132; Onions 514; Kluge-Seebold 503.

ON las-meyrr ‘mellow, decrepit’, MLG sbst. las ‘rag’. Cf. also WGmc *lēziz > E dial. leer ‘empty’, OS lāri id., MHG lære id. Related to Lith lúosas ‘lame’, Slav *lošь ‘bad’ (Schmidt Verw. 39). Uhlenbeck PBB XXX 298; Torp-Falk 364; Trautmann BSW 150; Pokorny I 680; Onions 522; Fraenkel 359–360; Stang LS 34 (against Schmidt); Vasmer-Trubachev II 526; Trubachev ESSJa XVI 92–94.

Goth lasiws ‘weak’, OE lysu ‘depraved, corrupt, evil’. Cf. also MHG er-leswen ‘to become weak’. Derived from *lasaz. Identical with Toch B laswi ‘attacks of weakness’ < *losi⁄o- (Van Windekens Etim. 1972 145). Pott EF II/1 839 (to Lat sublestus ‘feeble, faint’); Froehde BB I 192 (same as Pott); Torp- Falk 364; Fraenkel KZ LXIX 77–78.

ON lǫskr ‘weak, idle’, WFris lask ‘light, thin’, MLG lasch ‘tired, weak’. Related to Lat lascīuus ‘wanton, petulant’, cf. further Slav *laska ‘caress’ (with *-ō-). Cf. also *lataz. Karsten NB II 295–296; Torp-Falk 364; Walde- Hofmann I 766–767; Feist 322–323; Kluge 428–429; Pokorny I 666; Chemodanov SGGJa I 100; Vries ANEW 374; Meid Wortbild. 195; Heidermanns 363; Kluge-Seebold 503–504.

Goth lats ‘sluggish, lazy’, ON latr ‘slow, lazy’, OE læt ‘late, slow, sluggish’, OFris comp. lettera ‘later’, OS lat ‘sluggish, casual’, OHG laz ‘sluggish, lazy’. Derived from *lētanan (Sütterlin IF IV 99; Persson Beiträge 710). Schweizer KZ I 561; Karsten NB I 57; Torp-Falk 359; Feist 323; Holthausen AEEW 192; Porzig Gliederung 104; Pokorny I 666; Onions 516 (to Lat lassus ‘weary’ < *ladtos); Seebold 334; Lehmann GED 227; Bammesberger Nom. 239; Heidermanns 363–364; Kluge-Seebold 504.
Goth latei ‘sluggishness’, ON leti ‘laziness, sloth’, OHG lazzī ‘slowness’. Derived from *lataz. Feist 322; Lehmann GED 227; Heidermanns 364.

Goth latjan ‘to retard, to delay’, ON letja ‘to hold back, to dissuade’, OE lettan ‘to let, to hinder, to impede’, OFris letta ‘to hinder, to stop, to check’, OS lettian ‘to slow, to become tired’, OHG lezzen ‘to hinder’. Derived from *lataz. Osthoff Perf. 546; Torp- Falk 359; Holthausen AEEW 200; Feist 322–323; Onions 524; Seebold 334; Lehmann GED 227; Heidermanns 364; Kluge-Seebold 858.
ON lata ‘to slow, to slacken, to abate’, OHG lazōn ‘to be slow, sluggish’. Derived from *lataz. Torp- Falk 359; Heidermanns 364.
ON lati ‘late one’, OE hildlata ‘coward’. Derived from *lataz. Heidermanns 364.

ON lǫä ‘bidding, invitation’, OE freónd-laäu ‘friendly invitation’. Probably connected with Slav *lotiti ‘to drive, to grasp’. Osthoff IF V 311– 312; Meringer IF XVI 114–117 (as *‘invitation sent on a special tablet’ connected with MHG lade ‘lath, stake, piece of wood’); Neckel IF XVII 175– 176 (follows Meringer); Torp-Falk 359; Prellwitz 268 (to OIr air-le ‘zeal, regulation’); Holthausen AEEW 196; Feist 323; Puhvel Laryngeals 88 (to Hitt ḫalzai- ‘to call out, to cry’); Lehmann GED 227; Trubachev ESSJa XVI 96.

Goth laþon ‘to invite, to call’, ON laäa ‘to bid, to invite’, OE laäian id., OFris lathia, ladia ‘to invite, to summon’, OS lathian, ladoian ‘to call, to summon’, OHG ladōn id. Derived from *laþō. Torp-Falk 359; Holthausen AEEW 196; Feist 323; Lehmann GED 227; Kluge-Seebold 499.

Goth masc. laufs ‘leaf, foliage’, ON lauf ‘leaf, foliage’, OE leáf id., OFris lāf id., OS lōf id., OHG loub id. Identical with Slav *lubъ ‘bast’ < *loubho-. Further related to Lat liber ‘bast’, OIr luib ‘grass’, Lith lúoba ‘bark’, Latv luõbs ‘shell’. Torp-Falk 377; Holthausen AEEW 196; Walde-Hofmann I 790– 791; Feist 323–324; Pokorny I 690; Fraenkel 343; Zalizniak II 169; Onions 520; Beekes Orbis XX 132–137 (from substratum); Lehmann GED 227–228; Trubachev ESSJa XVI 156–158 (compares Slav *lubъ with Gmc *laupaz); Kluge-Seebold 505; Boutkan OFED s.v.

Goth ga-laufs ‘valuable, expensive’, OE un-ze-leáf ‘unbelieving, incredulous’, OHG gi-loub ‘instilling trust, agreeable’. Derived from *leubaz. Feist 188; Holthausen AEEW 196; Kluge 186, 268; Wissmann Postv. 39–40 (exocentric denominative of *lauba-); Johannisson VPV 4 (derived from *laubjanan I); Lehmann GED 142; Heidermanns 365; Kluge-Seebold 326.
Goth ga-laubjan ‘to believe’, OE ze-lífan id., OFris ge-lēva id., OS gi-lōbian id., OHG gi-louben id. Derivative of *laubaz. Feist 187–188; Onions 87; Lehmann GED 141–142; Kluge-Seebold 326.

Goth us-laubjan ‘to allow’, ON leyfa ‘to permit, to allow’, OE lífan ‘to give leave, to allow, to permit’, OS gi-lōbian ‘to believe’, OHG ir-louben ‘to allow’. Derived from *laubō. Torp- Falk 376; Holthausen AEEW 201; Pokorny I 684; Onions 521; Lehmann GED 141–142; Kluge-Seebold 230 (to Lith liáuti ‘to cease, to stop’).

OE leáf ‘leave, permission’, MHG loube id. Derivationally close to Skt lobha- ‘wish’ and further connected with *leubh- ‘to like, to wish’: Skt lúbhyati ‘to desire ardently’, Lat lubet, libet ‘please’, Slav *l’ubiti ‘to love’. Cf. *laubaz, *leubaz. Kluge Zd Wf VII 169 (semantic development of derivatives); Holthausen AEEW 196; Walde-Hofmann I 793–794; Mayrhofer III 107–108; Pokorny I 684; Onions 521.

OE leád ‘lead’, OFris lād ‘lead (as a measure of weight)’, MLG lōd ‘lead’, MHG lōt ‘plummet, solder’. May be related to Lith liūdë ‘plumb, plummet’ and MIr lúaide ‘lead’ < *loudƒā (if not from *ploudƒā) or borrowed from Celtic. On the other hand, may simply represent a deverbative of *leuđanan (cf. also *lauđaz). Torp-Falk 355; Holthausen AEEW 196; Fraenkel 378 (Lith < MLG); Onions 520; Kluge-Seebold 526; Boutkan OFED s.v.
Goth swa-lauþs ‘so large, so great’, MHG lōte ‘qualified, conditioned’. Derived from *leuđanan. Torp-Falk 375.

Burg *laugs ‘flame’, ON leygr ‘flame, fire’, OE líz ‘flame, lightning’ (also neut.), OFris loga ‘flame, fire’, OHG loug id. Related to Skt rocíß- ‘light’, Lat lūx id., Slav *lu‘ь ‘ray’ further connected with *leuk- ‘light, white’. Zupitza Gutt. 134; Torp-Falk 373; Holthausen AEEW 201; Walde- Hofmann I 823–824; Mayrhofer III 75; Pokorny I 687–689; Zalizniak II 221; Lehmann GED 228, 236; Köbler 712; Trubachev ESSJa XVI 163–164; Kluge-Seebold 524.

Goth analaugns ‘concealed, secret, hidden’, OE unlízne ‘incontrovertible’. Derived from *leuzanan. Karsten NB II 180–181; Torp-Falk 374; Feist 42; Holthausen AEEW 201; Lehmann GED 31; Heidermanns 365–366.

Goth laugnjan ‘to declare untrue, to deny’, OE líznan ‘to lie’, OFris leina ‘to refuse to take an oath’, OS lōgnian ‘to lie’, OHG lougnen ‘to deny’. Derived from *lauznō ~ *lauznaz. Torp- Falk 374; Holthausen AEEW 201; Feist 324; Seebold 337; Lehmann GED 228; Kluge-Seebold 516.
ON laun ‘secrecy’, OHG lougan ‘lie’. Derived from *leuzanan. Torp-Falk 374; Pokorny I 687; Benveniste 134–136; Seebold 336.

ON laug ‘hot spring, bath’, OE léaz ‘lye’, MLG lōge id., OHG louga id. A suffixal formation (< *loukā) related to IE *lou(H)- ‘to wash’: Gk λούω, Lat lauō and the like (Pott WW 1307). Bugge KZ XX (1872) 12–13; Torp-Falk 371; Holthausen AEEW 196; Walde- Hofmann I 773–775; Pokorny I 692; Frisk II 138–139; Zalizniak I 147; Onions 542; Tollenaere KZ XCVI 144–145; Peeters KZ CI 168; Kluge- Seebold 506.

ON ló ‘clearing (in the woods), meadow’, OE léah ‘meadow, open space’, MLG lo ‘bush’, OHG lōh ‘grove, bush’. Identical with Skt loká- ‘open space, place, world’, Lat lūcus ‘holy grove’, Lith la Ukas ‘field’. Connected with *leuk- ‘light, white’. Torp-Falk 372; Holthausen AEEW 196; Walde- Hofmann I 828; Mayrhofer III 113; Pokorny I 687–689; Fraenkel 343–344; Onions 520; Bammesberger Nom. 51; Kluge-Seebold 524.

ON laukr ‘leek’, OE neut. leác id., OS lōk id., OHG louh id. The etymology remains unknown. Any relation to Dac lãj ‘purslain, Portulaca oleracea’ < *lauk- with *au > *a as in other Dacian words? Zupitza Gutt. 164; Torp-Falk 355 (to Gk λύγος ‘willow-like tree’); Holthausen AEEW 196; Detschew Thr. 553–554 (Dac < Lat lax = fraus ‘deceit, fraud’); Pokorny I 681; Zalizniak I 134; Onions 522; Bammesberger Nom. 53 (to *lūkanan); Kluge- Seebold 505.

Goth laun ‘reward’, ON pl. laun id., OE léan id., OFris lān id., OS lōn id., OHG lōn ‘reward, price’ (neut., masc.). Continues *l 6 u-no- related to Skt lótam ‘booty’, Gk Ion ληΐη id. < *λā⁄ωā, Lith λάωθτι ‘to exercise’, Slav *lovъ ‘hunting’. Petr BB XXV 141; Torp-Falk 371; Trautmann BSW 153; Holthausen AEEW 196–197; Feist 325; Mayrhofer III 114; Pokorny I 655; Zalizniak II 169; Frisk II 96; Benveniste IEL 134–136; Vasmer-Trubachev II 508; Lehmann GED 228–229; Bammesberger Nom. 73 (to *leusanan); Kluge-Seebold 524.
ON laupr ‘basket, box’, OE leápp ‘basket’, MLG lōp ‘wooden vessel’. Somehow connected with *leubhreflected in *lauƕan. Torp-Falk 377; Holthausen AEEW 197; Pokorny I 690; Onions 520; Lehmann GED 228.
ON laus-yräi ‘breech of one’s word’, OS lōs-waurd ‘slander’. Cf. also Goth lausa-waurdei ‘empty talk’. Compound of *lausaz and *wurđan. Feist 326; Lehmann GED 229.
Goth lausa-waurds ‘talking idly’, ON laus-orär ‘unreal in one’s words’. Compound of *lausaz and *wurđaz (see *wurđan). Feist 326; Carr Nom. comp. 94 (parallel formations); Lehmann GED 229.

Goth laus ‘empty, void’, ON lauss ‘loose, free’, OE leás id., OFris lās ‘free, deprived’, OS lōs ‘free’, OHG lōs ‘lightminded, wicked, evil’. Derived from *leusanan. Karsten NB I 63; van Heltens Zd Wf XI 56 (from *leuđanan); Torp-Falk 377–378; van Wijk IF XXXV 265–268; Holthausen AEEW 197; Feist 325; Onions 521, 536; Pokorny I 681–682; Seebold 339; Lehmann GED 124, 229; Bammesberger Nom. 237–238; Heidermanns 366– 367; Kluge-Seebold 525; Boutkan OFED s.v.
ON hug-leysi ‘timidity’, OHG lōsī ‘carelessness’. Derived from *lausaz. Heidermanns 366.
Goth us-lauseins ‘release, salvation’, ON ór-lausn ‘solution of a difficulty’. Derived from *lausaz. Feist 533; Lehmann GED 229.

Goth lausjan ‘to make loose, to free’, ON leysa ‘to loosen, to free’, OE lísan id., OFris lēsa id., OS ālōsian id., OHG ir-lōsen id. Derived from *lausaz. Torp-Falk 378; Feist 326; Holthausen AEEW 202; Seebold 339; Lehmann GED 229; Heidermanns 367.
ON staä-lausa ‘absurdity’, OFris hūs-lāse ‘homelessness’. Derived from *lausaz. Heidermanns 366.
ON lausung ‘lying, falsehood’, OE leásunz ‘leasing, lying, fiction’. Derived from *lausaz. Heidermanns 366– 367.
ON laut ‘hollow place’, ESwed löyto ‘little hole’. Identical with Toch A lot ‘hole’, Toch B laute ‘moment, opportunity’ (Hilmarsson apud Adams TB 563) and Hitt luttai ‘window’. Tischler HEG II 79–80.

ON lauär ‘froth, foam, soap’, OE leáäor ‘nitre used for soap, lather’. Identical with Gk λουτρόν ‘bath’, Gaul lautro ‘balneo’, OIr lóathar ‘bowl’, lóthur ‘bath tub’ (Bugge KZ XX 12–13) derived from *lou(H)-: Gk λούω ‘to wash’, Lat lauō id. Torp-Falk 371; Walde- Hofmann I 773–774; Holthausen AEEW 197; Pokorny I 692; Frisk II 138–139; Onions 517.

Goth ligan ‘to lie’ (no jforms in pres.), ON liggja id., OE liczan id., OFris lidza id., OS liggian id., OHG liggen id. Related to *legh- ‘to lie’: Toch B lyäk-, Gk λέξομαι, MIr laigim, Slav *lęgǫ, *legti (Ahrens apud Schweizer KZ I 562). Zupitza Gutt. 178; Meillet MSL XIV 339; Torp-Falk 357–358; Trautmann BSW 158; Feist 330–331; Holthausen AEEW 201; Kluge 440–441; Jóhannesson IEW 750–751; Pokorny I 658–659; Vries ANEW 355; Frisk II 94–96; Onions 527; Seebold 324–326; Lehmann GED 233; Trubachev ESSJa XIV 99– 100; Kluge-Seebold 519; Adams TB 566; Burlak IFTJa 255.

Goth ligrs ‘bed’ (masc.), ON legr ‘bed, grave’, OE lezer ‘lying, couch, lair’, OFris legor ‘camp’, OS legar ‘illness’, OHG legar ‘lair, camp’. Derived from *lezjanan. Meillet BSL XXIV 110 (dissimilated from *lezlan); Schröder ZDADL LXXIV 48; Torp-Falk 358; Holthausen AEEW 198; Feist 331; Pokorny I 658–659; Zalizniak II 169; Onions 512; Seebold 325; Lehmann GED 233; Bammesberger Nom. 74; Kluge-Seebold 499.

ON leka ‘to drip, to leak’, OFris bi-leka ‘to dry out’, OHG lecken ‘to moisten’. Related to OIr legaim ‘to be dissolved, to melt’. Zupitza Gutt. 215; Torp-Falk 356; Holthausen AEEW 197; Jóhannesson IEW 749–750; Pokorny I 657; Vries ANEW 352; Seebold 330; Kluge-Seebold 508– 509.

ON lekr ‘leaky’, OE hlec ‘having cracks’ (with irregular anlaut), MDu lec ‘leaky’, G dial. lech id. Derived from *lekanan. Karsten NB I 27–28; Torp-Falk 356; Holthausen AEEW 197; Onions 520; Seebold 330 (on OE anlaut); Heidermanns 372–373; Kluge- Seebold 508–509.

OE limpan ‘to befall, to happen’, OHG gi-limpfan ‘to fit’. Related to Toch AB läm- ‘to sit, to remain’, Skt rámbate ‘to hang down’. Torp-Falk 363; Holthausen AEEW 203; Mayrhofer III 44; Pokorny I 656–657; Onions 529; Seebold 330–331 (to Lith lémti ‘to join’); Adams TB 654–655.

ON lind ‘lime-tree’, OE lind(e) id., OS lindia id., OHG linta id. Identical with Lith lentā ‘board’, Alb lëndë ~ landë ‘wood, timber, material’ < *lentā (Bezzenberger-Fick BB VI 240; Uhlenbeck PBB XVII 435–440). Cf. also Slav *lǫtъ ‘bast’ (Berneker 740–741). Fick III 631; Torp-Falk 361; Böke IF LIV 119–121; Schmidt KZ LVII 16 (on Alb); Holthausen AEEW 203; Pokorny I 677; Fraenkel 357–358; Zalizniak II 217; Onions 529; Stang LS 34–35; Trubachev ESSJa XVI 150–151; Heidermanns 375 (to adj. *lenþaz); Kluge- Seebold 520; Orel AED 223.
Swed dial. linda ‘fallow’. related to *lanđan. Derivationally close to Slav *lęda ‘hearth, field’. Torp-Falk 362; Trubachev ESSJa XV 44–47.
ON lindi ‘belt, girdle’, MLG lint ‘band’. Related to *lenđ(j)ō. Torp-Falk 361.
OHG lingan ‘to hither with, to flourish’. Identical with Skt ráṃhate ‘to hurry, to spring’, Lith léngstu, léngti ‘to check, to inhibit’. Torp-Falk 360; Pokorny I 660; Fraenkel 356.
ON létt-ligr ‘lightly, light’, OS līht-līk ‘light, bad’, OHG līht-līh ‘insignificant, vain’. Derived from *lenxwtaz. Heidermanns 374.

Goth leihts ‘light’, ON léttr id., OE leóht id., OFris līchte id., OS comp. līhto id., OHG līhti id. Continues *leng⁄h-to- attested also in Alb lehtë id. (unless this is a later derivation of leh id., Orel AED 217) and further connected with Toch B lankutse ‘light’, Skt raghú- ‘hastening, fleet, rapid’, Av ragu- ‘nimble’, Gk ἐλαχύς ‘small’, Lat leuis ‘light’, OIr comp. laigiu ‘smaller’, Lith leñgvas ‘light’, Slav *lьgъkъ id. See *lenz(w)anan. Schweizer KZ I 559; Karsten NB II 445; Zupitza Gutt. 134; Bartholomae 1497; Pedersen Kelt. Gr.
