I 97, 339; Torp- Falk 360; Feist 326–327; Holthausen AEEW 203; Walde-Hofmann I 788– 789; Mayrhofer III 31–32; Pokorny I 660–661; Fraenkel 355–356; Vries ANEW 353; Frisk I 484–485; Onions 527; Lehmann GED 229–230; Bammesberger Nom. 256–257; Trubachev ESSJa XVII 75–79; Heidermanns 373–374; Kluge-Seebold 512; Adams TB 544.
ON létta ‘to lighten, to lift’, OE líhtan ‘to make light, to make easy’, OHG līhten ‘to make lighter’. Derived from *lenxwtaz. Torp-Falk 360; Holthausen AEEW 201; Heidermanns 374.
ON linn-ormr ‘dragon, serpent’, MLG linde-worm ‘snake’, OHG lind-wurm id. Compound of *lenþaz I and *wurmaz ~ *wurmiz. Carr Nom. comp. 84 (parallel formations); Kluge- Seebold 520–521.
ON linnr ‘serpent’, MHG lint id. Substantivized *lenþaz II (Noreen Abriß 138). Vries ANEW 358; Heidermanns 375; Kluge-Seebold 520–521.

OE líä ‘lithe, soft, gentle’, OS līthi ‘mild, merciful’, OHG lindi ‘gentle, mild’. Identical with Lat lentus ‘flexible, tough’, Lith leñtas ‘still, quiet’ (Bezzenberger-Fick BB VI 240; Trautmann Festschr. Bezzenberger 167). Torp- Falk 361; Būga KS 83 (Lith < Pol lenty ‘tardy, slow’ < Lat lentus); Holthausen AEEW 204; Walde-Hofmann I 784–785; Pokorny I 677; Fraenkel 358; Heidermanns 375; Kluge-Seebold 520 (reconstructs *lenþjaz).

OSwed læpi, lippe, lippa ‘lip’, OE lippa id., OFris lippa id., MDu lippe id. Cf. also OHG leffur, lefs id. Related to Lat labium id. (Holthausen KZ L 143). Torp-Falk 362; Holthausen AEEW 204; Walde-Hofmann I 738–739; Pokorny I 656; Chemodanov SGGJa I 89; Zalizniak II 224; Onions 530; Wagner KZ CX 161–165; Kluge-Seebold 521.

Goth lisan ‘to collect’, ON lesa ‘to glean, to gather, to grasp’, OE lesan ‘to gather, to collect’, OFris lesa ‘to gather, to read’, OS lesan ‘to pick, to collect, to gather’, OHG lesan ‘to gather, to read’. Related to Hitt leššāi- ‘to collect, to pick up’, Lith lesū, lēsti ‘to pick up’. Pedersen IF V 47 (adds Slav *lasъ ‘greedy’); Torp-Falk 364; Feist 331; Holthausen AEEW 200; Porzig Gliederung 191; Jóhannesson IEW 758–759; Pokorny I 680; Chemodanov SGGJa I 71; Fraenkel 359; Vries ANEW 353; Onions 520; Stang LS 31; Seebold 332– 333; Lehmann GED 233; Tischler HEG II 64; Kluge-Seebold 515–516.
ON leär-hosa ‘leather hose, gaiters’, OE leäer-hose id., OHG ledar-hosa id. Compound of *leþran and *xusōn. Carr Nom. comp. 50.

ON leär ‘leather’, OE leäer ‘hide, skin, leather’, OFris leither, leder id., MLG leder id., OHG ledar id. Borrowed from Celtic: OIr lethar id., W lledr id., Bret lezr id. Torp-Falk 360; Holthausen AEEW 200; Pokorny I 685; Chemodanov SGGJa I 98; Zalizniak I 142; Onions 521; Kluge-Seebold 509.
Goth liuba-leiks ‘lovely, charming’, OE leóf-líc ‘lovely, beautiful’, OS liof-līk ‘charming’, OHG liub-līh ‘beautiful, charming’. Derived from *leubaz. Feist 332; Lehmann GED 235; Heidermanns 377.

Goth liufs ‘beloved’, ON ljúfr ‘dear, beloved’, OE leóf ‘desirable, pleasant, beloved’, OFris liāf ‘dear, beloved’, OS liof id., OHG liob ‘beloved’. Identical with Slav *l’ubъ id. For further parallels see *laubō. Schleicher KZ I 141; Karsten NB I 23; Torp-Falk 376; Feist 333; Holthausen AEEW 199; Pokorny I 684; Vries ANEW 361; Onions 527; Lehmann GED 235; Bammesberger Nom. 240; Trubachev ESSJa XV 181–182; Heidermanns 376–377; Kluge-Seebold 518.

Goth liudan ‘to grow’, ON part. loäinn ‘shaggy, thick’, OE leódan ‘to grow’, OS liodan id., OHG liotan ‘to spear, to pierce’. Related to Skt ródhati id., Av raodaiti id., Alb lej ‘to give birth’ < PAlb *laudnja. Bartholomae 1492– 1493; Torp-Falk 375; Feist 332–333; Holthausen AEEW 199; Jóhannesson IEW 746; Mayrhofer III 77–78; Pokorny I 684–685; Benveniste 263–264; Vries ANEW 363; Seebold 335–336; Lehmann GED 234–235; Kluge-Seebold 523; Orel AED 217.

Burg *leuds ‘man’, ON lÿär, ljóär ‘people’ (pl. lÿäir, rarely lÿäar), OE leód ‘man’, OFris liude, liode ‘people’, OS liud, liudi id., OHG liut id. Close to Lith liáudis ‘(simple) people’, Slav *l’udь id. Derived from *leuđanan. Hirt PBB XXIII 355 (Slav < Gmc); Brugmann PBB XLIII 316 (doubts the semantic connection); Torp-Falk 375; Trautmann BSW 160–162; Devoto St. Balt. III 75–76; Holthausen AEEW 199; Trier Holz 113–114; Pokorny I 684–685; Chemodanov SGGJa I 81; Fraenkel 360– 361; Vries ANEW 361–362; Zalizniak II 193; Benveniste 262–265; Stang LS 32; Seebold 335; Szemerényi Kinship 116; Lehmann GED 234; Bammesberger Nom. 136; Köbler 712; Trubachev ESSJa XV 194–200; Kluge-Seebold 517.

Goth liugan ‘to lie’, ON ljúga id., OE leózan id., OFris liāga id., OS liogan id., OHG liogan id. Related to Lith lūgóti ‘to request’, Slav *lъgati ‘to lie’ (Schleicher KZ I 141; Endzelin KZ LII 114–116). Goth liuga ‘wedding, marriage’ < *leuzō is derived from *leuzanan in its original meaning *‘to request’ (Grimm DG 88: identifies Goth liugan ‘to marry’ and ‘to lie’). Lottner KZ XI 179; Güntert Weltkönig 69 (Goth liuga to OFris or-loch ‘war’, OHG ur-liugi id.); Zupitza Gutt. 179, 218; Bezzenberger BB XVI 244; Torp-Falk 373–374; Trautmann BSW 163; Sturtevant JAOS LII 8 (to Hitt ḫaluka- ‘message, announcement’); Feist 333–334; Holthausen AEEW 199; Jóhannesson IEW 744–745; Pokorny I 686–687 (Goth liuga from Celt *lugjom > OIr luige ‘oath’); Chemodanov SGGJa I 68, 96; Fraenkel 389; Vries ANEW 361 (to OIr fol-lugaim ‘to conceal’); Onions 527; Stang LS 35; Peeters IF XCIV 296; Seebold 336–337; Lehmann GED 235–236; Trubachev ESSJa XVI 233– 237; Kluge-Seebold 527.

ON ljómi ‘beam, ray, radiance’, OE leóma ‘light, radiance’, OS liomo id. Continues *leuk-men- (Osthoff PBB VIII 277), cf. Lat lūmen ‘light’ < *leuk-s-men. Related to *leuxtan. Zupitza Gutt. 74; Torp-Falk 373; Holthausen AEEW 199; Walde-Hofmann I 832–833; Pokorny I 689; Lerchner 185; Lehmann GED 228, 236; Bammesberger Nom. 183.
ON ljóss ‘light, bright, shining’. For further connections see *leuxtan. Karsten NB I 30–31; Torp-Falk 373; Mayrhofer III 75; Pokorny I 687–689; Meid Wortbild. 135; Lehmann GED 236; Heidermanns 378.
ON lÿsa ‘to shed light, to light up’, OE líxan ‘to shine, to glitter’. Derived from *leuxsaz. Zupitza Gutt. 134; Torp-Falk 373; Holthausen AEEW 202; Lehmann GED 236; Heidermanns 378.

OHG liohsan ‘brilliant’. Close to or identical with Av raoxšna- ‘light, brilliant’. Cf. also Lat lūna ‘moon’, OPruss lauxnos ‘stars’, Slav *luna ‘moon’ < *louk-sn-. Further see *leuxtan, *leuxsaz. Fick KZ XVIII 416; Bartholomae 1488; Bugge KZ XX 13–14; Torp-Falk 373; Trautmann BSW 152; Pokorny I 687–689; Meid IF LXIX 244 (secondary

OE leóht ‘light’, OFris liacht id., OS adj. lioht id., OHG lioht id. Cf. also Goth liuhaþ id. < *leuxađan. Cf. a possible structural parallel in Hitt lukkat- ‘morning’ (Laroche RHA XXVIII 34– 35). Derived from IE *leuk-: Skt rócate ‘to shine, to be bright’, Gk λευκός ‘light, white’, Lat lūx ‘light’ and the like. Grimmapud Kuhn KZ I 380 (to *leuxsaz with an alteration t: s); Zupitza Gutt. 134; Torp-Falk 372–373; Feist 334– 335; Holthausen AEEW 199; Walde- Hofmann I 823–824; Mayrhofer III 75–76; Pokorny I 688; Zalizniak I 142; Frisk II 108–109; Onions 527; Hoffmann KZ LXXXII 215; Lehmann GED 236; Tischler HEG II 65–69; Heidermanns 379–380; Kluge-Seebold 518.

Goth liuhtjan ‘to light’, OE líhtan ‘to shine, to lighten’, OS liohtian ‘to light’, OHG liuhten id. Derived from *leuxtan. Torp-Falk 373; Holthausen AEEW 201; Feist 335; Heidermanns 379; Kluge-Seebold 516.

Goth fra-liusan ‘to lose’, OE for-leósan id., OFris ur-liāsa id., OS far-liosan id., OHG fir-liosan ‘to lose, to destroy’. Related to *leu-: Skt lunāti ‘to cut’, Gk λύω ‘to lose’, Last luō ‘to pay’. Torp-Falk 377–378; Hirt PBB XXIII 316 (*-s- of aoristic origin); Feist 163; Holthausen AEEW 199–200; Jóhannesson IEW 739–741; Mayrhofer III 106– 107; Pokorny I 681–682; Vries ANEW 348; Frisk II 149–150; Seebold 339– 340; Lehmann GED 124; Kluge-Seebold 858.

OSwed liuske ‘softness’, ME léske id., MLG lēsche id. Related to Lith pl. lūskos ‘rags’, Latv lāuska ‘fragment, shred’, Slav *luska ~ *luzga ‘shell, skin’. On the other hand, cf. *leusanan. Torp-Falk 355–356; Iljinskij Afsl Ph XXIX 492 (reconstructs Slav *l’uska ‘fish scales’ that would be identical with Gmc); Būga RFV LXV 318, LXXI 470– 471; Trautmann BSW 152; Vasmer- Trubachev II 530, 535; Trubachev ESSJa XVI 188–190, 213–214.

Goth liuts ‘deceitful, fraudulent’, ON ljótr ‘ugly, hideous, misshapen’, OFris liāt ‘mendacious’. Derived from *lūtanan. Schweizer KZ I 564; Grienberger Got. 150–151 (to Lat lūdus ‘play’); Karsten NB II 453; Torp-Falk 374 (to Slav *ludъ ‘stupid’); Weisweiler IF XLI 63–70; Feist 335–336; Schulze Kl. Schr. 573; Krogmann IF LIII 44–48; Pokorny I 684; Vries ANEW 361; Seebold 341; Stang LS 34; Lehmann GED 236; Heidermanns 380–381.
Goth liutei ‘deceit, hypocrisy’, ON lÿti ‘fault, flaw, deformity’. Derived from *leutaz. Feist 335; Heidermanns 380.

Goth awi-liuþ ‘prayer of gratitude’ (-þ- < *-đ-), ON ljóä ‘song, ditty, lay’, OE leóä ‘song, poem’, OS winilioth ‘folk song’, OHG liod ‘song’. Perhaps, distantly related to Lat laus ‘praise’, laudāre ‘to praise’. Wood AJPh XXIII 200 (to Gk λύσσα ‘frenzy, rage’, Slav *l’utъ ‘wild’); Uhlenbeck PBB XXX 299; Torp-Falk 355; Holthausen AEEW 200; Walde-Hofmann I 776; Feist 336; Pokorny I 683; Zalizniak I 142; Benveniste 160; Lehmann GED 53; Kluge-Seebold 519.
ON ljóä-sǫngr ‘song’, OE leóä-sanz ‘song, poem’. Compound of *leuþan and *sanzwaz. Carr Nom. comp. 82 (parallel formations).

Goth liuþon ‘to sing praise’, ON ljóäa ‘to sing, to make verses’, OE leóäian ‘to sing, to sound’, OHG liudōn id. Derived from *leuþan. Schweizer KZ I 563; Torp-Falk 355; Holthausen AEEW 200; Feist 336; Lehmann GED 237.

ON lé ‘scythe’, MLG lē id. Related to Skt laví- ‘sickle’, lava- ‘cutting’, Gk acc. λαῖον ‘plowshare (?)’. Based on *leu-: Skt lunóti ‘to cut’, Gk λύω ‘to loosen, to unbind’. See *leusanan. Torp-Falk 370; Pokorny I 681; Frisk II 73, 149– 150.

OE léf ‘weak, injured, infirm’, OFris lēf ‘weak, ill’, OS lēf ‘weak, fragile’. Identical with Lith líebas ‘with thin legs’, cf. also láibas ‘slim, thin’ (Holthausen IF XX 323–324), Slav *libъ ‘thin, weak’. Torp-Falk 363, 571; Trautmann BSW 154; Holthausen AEEW 197; Pokorny I 661–662; Fraenkel 329–330; Vasmer- Trubachev II 492; Knapp Btrg. Gesch. XCVI 209–213 (to *lībanan); Bammesberger Nom. 258 (same as Knapp); Heidermanns 367–368; Trubachev ESSJa XV 74–75.

ON láä ‘land’, OE læä id. Continues *lē-tódistantly related to *lētanan (Grienberger Got. 227). Torp- Falk 354; Holthausen AEEW 193; Vries ANEW 343; Frisk II 89–90 (to Gk λάτρον ‘payment’); Chantraine 622–623 (against Frisk); Onions 517; Lehmann GED 377.
Goth un-leþs ‘poor’, OE un-læd ‘poor, miserable, unhappy’. Derived from *lēđan? Torp-Falk 354; Feist 521; Onions 517; Lehmann GED 377.

ON lágr ‘low’, OFris lēch id., MLG lēch, lēge id., MHG læge id. Derived from *lezjanan. Karsten NB II 380, 435; Torp-Falk 358; Charpentier Afsl Ph XXIX 5 (to Slav *lazъ ‘path’); Holthausen AEEW 191; Pokorny I 660; Chemodanov SGGJa I 84; Vries ANEW 344; Heidermanns 368–369 (to Lith l Ekšnas ‘flat’); Kluge-Seebold 510.
ON lægä ‘hollow, low place’, MLG lēgede ‘low place’. Derived from *lēzaz. Heidermanns 368.

ON lægr in eiga leigt ‘to have a right to be buried’, OHG aba-lāgi ‘unmilitant, powerless’. Derived from *lezjanan. Vries ANEW 371; Onions 538; Matzel Festschr. Schröder 103, KZ CV 113; Lloyd- Springer I 9; Heidermanns 369–370.
ON lág ‘felled tree, log’, OFris lēge ‘lying, position, site’, OS wider-lāga ‘repayment, retribution’, OHG lāga ‘ambush’. Substantivized fem. of *lēzaz. Torp-Falk 358.
Goth laian ‘to berate, to revile’. Related to Gk λαίεν: φθέγγεσθαι, Hes., OIr líim ‘to accuse’. Torp-Falk 354.
OHG lāhhin ‘cure, remedy’. Derived from *lēkjaz. Torp-Falk 357; Lehmann GED 232.
Goth lekinon ‘to heal, to cure’, ON lækna id., OE lácnian id., OS lāknōn id., OHG lāhhinōn id. Derived from *lēkinan. Torp-Falk 356– 357; Holthausen AEEW 190; Feist 329; Lehmann GED 232.

Goth lekeis ‘physician’, OSwed läkir id., OE læce id., OFris letza id., OS lāki id., OHG lāhhi id. Borrowed from Celt *lēgi- < *lēpagi- as in OIr liaig ‘doctor’ (Arbois de Jubainville Prem. II 366–367). Stokes KZ XXXV 595; Luft ZDADL XLI 237 (to Gk λέγω ‘to pick up, to tell’, Lat legō ‘to gather, to collect’); Torp-Falk 356; Holthausen IF XXXIX 71 (same as Luft), AEEW 190; Feist 329; Pokorny I 658; Onions 522; Lehmann GED 232.

Crim. Goth. lista ‘too little’, OE læst ‘smallest, least’, OFris lēst id. Based on *lēziz (see *lasaz). Bezzenberger BB III 81 (Goth lista — to Slav *lišiti ‘to devoid’); Tomaschek Taurien (Goth lista from Osset listæg ‘narrow’); Loewe Schw. Meer 136 (follows Tomaschek); Holthausen Einz. ZDADL XXIV 34, AEEW 192; Grienberger Zd Ph XXX 128–129 (Goth lista < *leitista-, cf. WFris lijts ‘small’); Loewe IF XIII 11; Feist 331; Lehmann GED 233–234; Bammesberger Nom. 235 (reconstructs *laiz-ista-).

OE læs, gen. læswe ‘pasture’. Close to Slav *lîsъ ‘wood, forest’ (Lidén GHÅ (1904) apud Berneker I 713) and, in particular, to pre-Greek pln. Λέσβος < *lēs⁄o- otherwise called ἼΙσσα < *⁄idh⁄ā (Trubachev VJa XXXIII/2 26). On the other hand, cf. *lēđan. Torp-Falk 364; Holthausen AEEW 192; Vaillan RES XIV 224; Stang LS 32; Trubachev ESSJa XIV 249–252.
Goth af-let ‘indulgence, forgiveness’, ON lát ‘loss’, OE wæter-ze-læt ‘water-course, aqueduct’. Derived from *lētanan. Torp-Falk 359; Feist 330; Seebold 334.

Goth letan ‘to let’, ON láta id., OE lætan id., OFris lēta id., OS lātan id., OHG lāzan id. Related to Gk ληδεῖν: κοπιᾶν Hes.), Alb lodh ‘to tire, to weary’ < PAlb *lāda, Lat lassus ‘weak’ < *l 6 d-to- (Persson Beiträge 710). Brugmann IF VI 89–100 (to Lith léidžiu, léisti ‘to let’ and further to IE *lē(i)- ‘to leave’); Torp-Falk 359; Mikkola UG III 24 (to Slav *ladъ ‘order’); Feist 329–330; Holthausen AEEW 193; Walde- Hofmann I 767–768; Jóhannesson IEW 732; Pokorny I 666; Vries ANEW 346; Frisk II 114 (the status of Gk is dubious in view of ἀηδῆσαι: κοπιάσαι, καμεῖν suggesting l- might be a mistaken reading of é-); Onions 524; Benveniste 152; Peeters IF LXXXI 32–33; Seebold 333–335; Lehmann GED 232; Kluge- Seebold 504; Orel AED 230; Dybo BSA 96 (on preterital forms in their connection to Lith léisti).
Goth fra-lets ‘freed man’, OFris lēt id., OHG ab-lāz ‘indulgence’. Derived from *lētanan. Torp-Falk 359; Feist 330; Seebold 334.

Goth lew ‘opportunity, cause’. See *lēwjanan. Persson BB XIII 279–280 (to Lith liáuju, liáuti ‘to cease’); Berneker IF X 159 (to Gk λύω ‘to remove’); Torp-Falk 364; Feist 330; Pokorny I 682–683; Lehmann GED 232.

Goth lewjan ‘to betray’, OE læwan id., OHG gi-lāwen id. Derived from *lēwan. Structurally close to and etymologically connected with Slav *lîviti ‘to give way, to yield, to lose time’, further to Litv liáutis ‘to stop’, OPrus aulāut ‘to die’ (Persson BB XIX 279– 280). Torp-Falk 364; Trautmann BSW 161; Holthausen AEEW 193; Feist 330; Fraenkel I 362–363; Stang LS 32; Lehmann GED 232; Toporov PJa I 154–156; Trubachev ESSJa XV 28.

Goth liban ‘to live’, ON lifa id., OE lifian id., OFris libba, liva id., OS libbian id., OHG lebēn id. Connected with *lībanan (cf. in particular such contexts as ON þá er þriäjungr lifir dags šwhen a third of the day ‘lives’ = is left”). Related to Hitt lip- ‘to lick’, Toch AB lip- ‘to remain’, Skt limpáti ‘to smear’ (Meillet JA XVIII 633), Lith limpū, līpti ‘to stick, to adhere’, Slav *lьpnǫ, *lьpnǫti id. For the semantic reconstruction note Blr lipets’ ‘to live with difficulty’ < Slav *lьpîti ‘to stick’ (Trubachev ESSJa XVII 92). Schweizer KZ I 562; Berneker I 754–755; Collitz Prät. 80–84 (from *leibhas in Lat cae-lebs ‘celibate’); Sturtevant Language VI/1 27; Torp- Falk 368; Trautmann BSW 161–162; Holthausen AEEW 200; Feist 330; Mayrhofer III 60–61; Pokorny I 670; Fraenkel 375–376; Onions 532; Seebold 326; Van Windekens I 263; Lehmann GED 232; Tischler HEG II 62–63; Trubachev ESSJa XVII 91–93; Kluge-Seebold 508.
Swed lefver-ört ‘hepatica, blue anemone’, OE neut. liferwyrt ‘liverwort’, MLG lever-wort id. Compound of *lib(a)rō and *wurtiz. Carr Nom. comp. 50.

ON lifr ‘liver’, OE lifer id., OFris livere id., MLG lever id., OHG lebara id. Etymologically identical with Gk λιπαρός ‘fat, greasy’ further derived from *leip- ‘to smear’ (Zupitza Gutt. 12–14). Hardly connected with but probably influenced by the Indo-European word for ‘liver’—*ƒek⁄ṛ-. Meillet RS IX 72 (to *libēnan); Schmidt KZ XXV 23 (to *ƒek⁄ṛ-); Torp-Falk 368; Holthausen AEEW 202; Pokorny I 504, 670; Zalizniak I 147; Frisk II 126–127; Onions 532; Illich-Svitych Opyt II 17 (continues Nostr *lep'A ‘spleen’); Kluge- Seebold 508.

ON liä ‘folk, people, army’, OE lid ‘vessel, ship’ (< *‘crew’), OFris lid ‘band, troop, retinue’. Derived from *līþanan. Holthausen AEEW 201; Seebold 329; Bammesberger Nom. 56, 173; Pârvulescu IF CII 79–80 (to *liþuz, cf. OIr glún ‘knee, generation’).
Goth af-lifnan ‘to be left, to remain’, ON lifna ‘to be left’. Derived from *lībanan. Structurally close to Slav *lьpnǫ, *lьpnǫti ‘to stick’. Feist 8; Seebold 326; Trubachev ESSJa XVII 91–93.

OE liccian ‘to lick’, OS likkōn id., OHG leckōn id. Related to Skt réḍhi id., Arm lizanem id., Gk λείχω id., Lat lingō id., OIr ligim id., Lith liežiū, li‚žti id., Slav *ližǫ, *lizati id. reflecting a root with aspirate *-·h- (Meillet MSL XVI 239–241). Scheftelowitz BB XXVIII 300; Torp-Falk 367; Trautmann BSW 155; Wissmann NP 160, 183–184; Holthausen AEEW 200; Walde-Hofmann I 806; Mayrhofer III 72–73; Pokorny I 668; Fraenkel 369; Frisk II 102; Onions 526; Trubachev ESSJa XV 162–163; Kluge-Seebold 509.
ON lim-heill ‘soundlimbed’, OE lim-hál id. Compound of *liman and *xailaz. Carr Nom. comp. 61.

ON lim ‘foliage, branch’, OE lim ‘member, limb, branch’. Eventually related to *linaz (Johansson PBB XIV 301–304). Wood MP IV 495 (agrees with Johansson); Torp-Falk 366; Holthausen AEEW 202; Trier Lehm 12 (to *līman ~ *līmaz); Vries ANEW 354–357; Lehmann GED 234.

ON linr ‘gentle, soft’, OHG len ‘pretty, gentle’. Related to *linnanan. Corresponds to Skt līná-, participle of lināti ‘to bend down, to duck’. Torp- Falk 365; Krogmann IF LIII 46; Pokorny I 661; Dybo VSJa 21 (pretonic shortening in Gmc); Vries ANEW 358; Onions 531; Schrijver LL 354; Lehmann GED 6; Heidermanns 383.

Goth af-linnan ‘to leave (off)’, OE linnan ‘to cease, to desist, OHG bi-linnan ‘to stop, to end’. Related to Skt lināti ‘to bend down, to duck, to hide’, Gk λίναμαι: τρέπομαι (Hes.), ἀλίνειν: ἀλείφειν (Hes.) (Osthoff Morph. Unt. IV 46: *linnanan < *lin⁄ō), Slav *lin’ati ‘to shed hair, to moult’. See *linaz. Torp- Falk 365; Feist 8; Holthausen AEEW 203; Trier Lehm 10–16 (to IE *lei- ‘slimy, to glide’); Mayrhofer III 102; Pokorny I 661–662; Frisk I 73; Onions 531; Seebold 331–332; Lühr MSS XXXV 79 (*-nn- < *-n H-); Lehmann GED 6; Trubachev ESSJa XV 109–110.
Goth listeigs ‘crafty, cunning’, ON listugr ‘skilled, polite’, G listig ‘crafty, cunning’. Derived from *listiz. Torp-Falk 369.

Goth lists ‘wile, cunning’, ON list ‘art, craft, artifice’ (if not from WGmc), OE list ‘art, skill, craft, artifice’ (also masc.), OFris list id., OS list id., OHG list id. Derived from *laisa. On the other hand, cf. *litō ~ *litaz implying *listiz < *lit-ti-? Schweizer KZ I 564; Torp-Falk 369; Holthausen AEEW 204; Feist 331–332; Pokorny I 671; Zalizniak II 177; Seebold 323; Lehmann GED 234; Bammesberger Nom. 139; Trubachev ESSJa XVII 97–99 (Slav *lьstь ‘flattery’ < Gmc); Kluge-Seebold 522.

Goth lita ‘hypocrisy, insincerity’, OHG liz ‘pretext’. Related to Gk λίζει: παίζει (Hes.), Lat lūdō ‘to play’ < *loidō. Schweizer KZ I 564; Grienberger Got. 149 (to Lith lendū, l⁄sti ‘to creep, to sneak into’); Feist 332; Pokorny I 666; Lehmann GED 234.

ON liäugr ‘ready, willing, free’, OFris lethich ‘single, free’, MLG ledich, leddich ‘free, unoccupied’, MHG lidec, ledic ‘single, free’. Derived from *liþuz. Torp-Falk 366; Torp-Falk 366; Bloomfield Festschr. Collitz 91–94; Pokorny I 309; Vries ANEW 355; Heidermanns 384–385; Kluge-Seebold 509.
ON liä-lauss ‘helpless’, OHG lide-lōs ‘limbless’ (hap. leg.). Compound of *liþuz and *lausaz. Carr Nom. comp. 90 (parallel formations).

Goth liþus ‘limb, joint’, ON liär id., OE liä id. (also neut.), OFris lith id., MLG ge-lit id., OHG lid id. (masc., fem., neut.). Connected with *liman. Schweizer KZ I 563; Persson Wurzelerw. 187 (to Lat lituus ‘crooked staff’); Lidén BB XXI 106); Johansson IF XIX 120 (same as Persson); Torp-Falk 365–366; Holthausen AEEW 204; Feist 332; Pokorny I 309, 672; Zalizniak II 202; Lehmann GED 234; Bammesberger Nom. 159; Kluge-Seebold 327.
OE leornian ‘to learn’, OFris lerna, lirna id., OS līnōn id., OHG lirnēn, lernēn id. Derived from *laisa. Torp-Falk 369; Holthausen AEEW 203; Onions 521.
ON pl. líf-dagar ‘life, days of life’, OE líf-dæz ‘day of life’. Compound of *līban and *đazaz. Carr Nom. comp. 81 (parallel formations).
ON líf-lauss ‘lifeless’, OE líf-leás id., OFris līf-lās id., OS līf-lōs id., MHG lībe-lōs id. Compound of *līban and *lausaz. Carr Nom. comp. 89 (parallel formations).
ON líf-ligr ‘alive, living’, OE líf-líc id., OHG līb-līh id. Derived from *līban. Onions 532.

Burg *lif ‘life’, ON líf ‘life, body, man’, OE líf ‘life’, OFris līf id., OS līf id., OHG līb id. Connected with *libēnan. Torp-Falk 368; Holthausen AEEW 202; Pokorny I 670; Zalizniak II 169; Onions 527; Lehmann GED 232; Köbler 712; Kluge-Seebold 511.

Goth pret. sg. bi-laif ‘to remain’, OE be-lífan id., OFris bi-līva id., OS bi-līban id., OHG bi-līban id. Related to Toch B lip-, lip- ‘to remain’, Skt limpáti ‘to smear’, Lith limpū, līpti ‘to stick’ and other continuants of IE *leip-. Germanic forms were semantically influenced by IE *leik⁄-, see *līxwanan. Osthoff Morph. Unt. IV 4–7; Torp-Falk 368; Feist 91–92; Holthausen AEEW 202; Jóhannesson IEW 737–738; Mayrhofer III 60–61, 104; Pokorny I 670–671; Fraenkel 375; Illich-Svitych Opyt II 18 (on the semantic parallelism between Gmc and Toch); Seebold 326–327; Lehmann GED 70; Kluge-Seebold 118; Adams TB 555.

Goth leiƕan ‘to lend’, ON 1 sg. pres. lé id., OE león id., OFris liā id., OS līhan id., OHG līhan id. Related to Skt riṇákti ‘to leave, to give up, to release’, Gk λείπω ‘to leave’, Lat linquō id., OIr leigim ‘to let’, Lith liekū, līkti ‘to remain’. Torp-Falk 367; Pedersen Kelt. Gr. II 562; Feist 327; Holthausen AEEW 203; Walde-Hofmann I 808–809; Porzig Gliederung 121; Jóhannesson IEW 733; Mayrhofer III 59; Pokorny I 669–670; Vries ANEW 359; Fraenkel 372–373; Frisk II 99–100; Onions 533; Benveniste IEL 150, 157–158; Seebold 327–328; Lehmann GED 230; Kluge- Seebold 513.
ON léa ‘to lend’, OFris līa id. Derived from *līxwanan. Seebold 327.

ON líkami ‘body’, OE líc-hama id., OFris līkoma id., OS līkhamo id., OHG līh-hamo id. Compound of *līkan I and *xamōn, originally ‘bodycovering’, see *xamaz (Güntert Kalypso 64). Torp-Falk 366; Holthausen AEEW 148, 200; Carr Nom. comp. 50; Lehmann GED 4–5, 230.
ON lík-maär ‘gravedigger’, OE líc-mann ‘person having to do with the corpse’. Compound of *līkan I and *mannz. Carr Nom. comp. 82 (parallel formations).

Goth leik ‘body’, ON lík id., OE líc id., OFris līk id., OS līk id., OHG līh id. (also fem. *līkiz). Related to Toch AB lek ‘appearance, body’, Lith lÿgus ‘like’, lÿgti ‘to equal, to match’, Latv lĩgt ‘to agree’ (Van Windekens I 260). Schweizer KZ I 562; Ehrismann PBB XX 53; Uhlenbeck PBB XXX 279 (to Skt liṅgam ‘mark, sign’); Zupitza Gutt. 163; Torp-Falk 366; Holthausen AEEW 200; Feist 327; Pokorny I 667; Fraenkel 370–371; Zalizniak II 187; Onions 526; Lehmann GED 142, 230–231; Kluge- Seebold 512 (to Slav *lice ‘face’).

ON lík ‘leech-line, the borders of a sail’, EFris līk id., MLG līk id. Related to Alb lidh ‘to bind’, Lat ligō id., Slav *ligati id. (Zupitza Gutt. 197). Torp- Falk 366; Walde-Hofmann I 800; Pokorny I 668; Vries ANEW 401; Trubachev ESSJa XV 87; Orel AED 226– 227.

Goth ga-leiks ‘similar’, ON líkr ‘alike’, OE ze-líc ‘like, alike, similar’, OFris ge-līk ‘same’, OS gi-līk id., OHG gilīh ‘same, similar’. Related to *līkan I (Wood MLN XXI 39–40). Torp-Falk 119, 366; Feist 189; Holthausen AEEW 200; Pokorny I 671; Schröder PBB LXXXIII 151–194; Vries ANEW 172; Onions 25; Stang LS 32–33; Lehmann GED 143; Seebold Festschr. Meid 343– 351; Heidermanns 381–383; Kluge- Seebold 326–327.

Goth leikan ‘to please’, ON líka ‘to like’, OE lícian ‘to please’, OFris līkia ‘to like’, OS līkon id., OHG līhhēn id. Derived from *līkaz. Torp- Falk 366; Holthausen AEEW 201; Feist 328; Heidermanns 382.
ON líkna ‘to show mercy to’, OFris a-līknia ‘to compare’, OHG gi-līhhinōn ‘to compete’. Derived from *līkaz. Heidermanns 382.
Goth ga-leiki ‘likeness’, ON líki ‘body, form, shape’. Derived from *līkaz. Feist 188; Heidermanns 382.
ON líkja ‘to be similar’, OHG līhhen ‘to make similar’. Derived from *līkaz. Heidermanns 382.

Goth ga-leikon ‘to liken, to compare’, ON líka ‘to make equal’, OFris līkia ‘to compare’, OHG līhhōn ‘to smooth’. Derived from *līkaz. Feist 328; Pokorny I 671; Chemodanov SGGJa I 76; Lehmann GED 142, 231; Heidermanns 382.
Goth ga-leika ‘σύσσωμος, partner in the same body’, ON líki ‘equal, match’, OE ze-líca ‘equal’, MLG ge-like ‘comrade’. Derived from *līkaz. Feist 188; Vries ANEW 356.

ON lím ‘lime’, OE lím ‘cement, mortar, glue, lime’, MLG līm ‘glue’, OHG līm ‘glue’. Related to *laimōn. Torp-Falk 365; Holthausen AEEW 202; Porzig Gliederung 110; Pokorny I 662; Zalizniak II 169; Onions 528; Kluge-Seebold 513.

Goth lein ‘linen, flax’, ON lín id., OE lín id., OS līn id., MHG masc. līn id. Borrowed from Lat līnum id. As to ON lína ‘line, rope’, OE líne id., OFris līne id., MLG line id., OHG līna id., they go back to Lat līnea ‘linen thread, string, line’ and are secondarily associated with *līnan. Weinhold KZ I 250 (to Slav *lьnъ id.); Rhys RC VII 241 (related to Lat līnum, Gk λίνον); Hehn Kult. 164– 190 (same as Rhys); Schrader-Nehring I 323; Torp-Falk 368; Holthausen AEEW 203; Feist 328; Pokorny I 691; Zalizniak I 155, II 169; Onions 529; Lehmann GED 231; Kluge-Seebold 513.

ON lista ‘edge’, OE líste ‘list, hem, border’, MLG liste ‘edge’, OHG līsta id. Related to *laistiz ~ *laistaz. Froehde BB XVII 312–314 (to Lat lītus ‘sea-shore’); Persson BB XIX 272; Torp- Falk 370 (to Lat lītus ‘sea-shore, strand’); Jokl IF XXX 206 (to Alb ledh ‘mud, alluvium, river-bank’); Holthausen AEEW 204; Pokorny I 672–673; Zalizniak I 156; Onions 531; Kluge-Seebold 514.

Goth leitils ‘small, little’, ON lítill id., OE lytel id., MDu littel, luttel id., OS luttil id., OHG luzzil ‘little, tiny’. Derived from *luttiz ~ *luttjaz. Grienberger Got. 146; Karsten NB II 270–271; Torp-Falk 367, 374; Schmidt KZ LVII 35 (to Lith pa-láidas ‘loose’); Feist 328–329; Holthausen AEEW 209; Krogmann IF LIII 44–48; Specht 125; Pokorny I 661–662; Vries ANEW 358; Matzel Zd Wf XIX 153–158; Onions 532; Lerchner 80; Lehmann GED 231; Lühr Expr. 496–501; Heidermanns 371–372, 390–391; Kluge- Seebold 529.

Goth ga-leiþan ‘to come, to go’, ON líäa ‘to go’, OE líäan id., OFris lītha ‘to suffer’, OS līthan ‘to go’, OHG līdan ‘to leave’. Connected with Toch A lit- ‘to go away, to fall down’, Av raēy- ‘to die’ (Seebold 328–330). Bartholomae 1480–1482; Torp-Falk 367; Feist 8 (to *liþuz); Holthausen AEEW 204; Trier Lehm 15 (to Gk λιτή ‘prayer’); Jóhannesson IEW 736–737; Pokorny I 672; Vries ANEW 354; Seebold 328– 330; Lehmann GED 6; Kluge-Seebold 512.

Goth leiþu ‘cider, strong drink’, ON líä ‘cider, ale’, OE líä ‘strong drink’, OFris līth id., OS līth ‘wine, drink’, OHG līd id. (also masc.). Close to Lith lytūs ‘rain’, Slav *litva ‘shower’ (based on *litu-). Derived from *lei-: MW di-llyd ‘to pour out’, Lith líeju, líeti ‘to pour’, Slav *liti id. Zimmer ZDADL XXXII 284 (to OIr líth ‘festival’); Torp-Falk 364–365 (to Gk ἄλεισον ‘vessel for wine, beaker’ < *ἄλειτ Won); Holthausen AEEW 204; Feist 329; Pokorny I 664–665; Chemodanov SGGJa I 99; Fraenkel 368; Zalizniak II 202; Schulze Kl. Schr. 258–259; Lehmann GED 231 (against comparison with *lei-); Trubachev ESSJa XV 157–160; Kluge- Seebold 513.

Goth lofa ‘palm of hand’, ON lófiid., ME love id. Related to Lith lópa ‘paw’, Slav *lapa id. (Schmidt KZ XIX 272). Stokes Urkelt. 252 (to OIr lue ‘rudder’, W llyw id.); Meringer IF XVII 162 (to Gk λώπη ‘robe, attire’); Torp- Falk 370; Būga RFV LXVI 243–244; Feist 336; Pokorny I 679; Fraenkel 339–340; Onions 402; Stang LS 23; Lehmann GED 237; Trubachev ESSJa XIV 26–27; Kluge-Seebold 529.

ON lóg ‘place’ (in idioms), OE lóz id., OFris lōch id., OHG luog ‘den, pit’. Derived from *lezjanan. Structurally close to Slav *lagъ ‘ravine’. Torp-Falk 358; Holthausen AEEW 206; Pokorny I 659; Zalizniak II 174; Trubachev ESSJa XIV 15.

OE lócian ‘to look’, WFris loaitsje id., OS lōkon id. Cf. also a differently stressed OHG luogēn < *lōzēnan. Probably identical with Dor λωγάω = λέγω ‘to pick up, to tell’ and related to Toch AB läk- ‘to see’ (Adams TB 550). The connection with Bret lagad ‘eye’ is dubious. Zupitza Gutt. 215; Holthausen AEEW 205; Chemodanov SGGJa I 100; Onions 536.
ON lof-gjarn ‘praiseworthy’, OE lof-zeorn ‘desirous of praise’. Cf. also a nominal derivative in OHG loba-gernī. Compound of *luban and *zernaz. Carr Nom. comp. 60.

ON lof ‘praise’, OE lof id. (masc., neut.), OFris lof id., OS lof id., OHG lob id. Derived from *leubaz. Torp-Falk 376; Holthausen AEEW 206; Pokorny I 684; Zalizniak I 142; Bammesberger Nom. 57; Kluge-Seebold 522.

Goth lubja-leis ‘magician, alchemist’, ON lyf ‘healing herb’, OE lybb ‘medicine, drug, poison’, OS lubbi ‘sap, poison’, OHG lubbi ‘poison’. Related to OIr luib ‘plant, herb’, Lat liber ‘bast’. Schrader-Nehring I 61 (to *leubaz like Lat uenēnum ‘poison’ < uenus ‘love’); Güntert Kalypso 203 (from Celtic); Marstrander Corr. 20 (same as Güntert); Torp-Falk 377; Holthausen AEEW 208; Walde-Hofmann I 790; Pokorny I 690; Chemodanov SGGJa I 96; Vries ANEW 346–347, 369; Zalizniak I 145; Lehmann GED 237.
Goth broþru-lubo ‘brotherly love’, OS luva ‘love’, OHG luba id. Related to *laubō, *leubaz. Feist 107; Pokorny I 683–684; Onions 538; Lehmann GED 81, 237; Heidermanns 377.

ON lofa ‘to praise’, OE lofian ‘to praise, to value’, OFris lovia ‘to praise’, OS lobon id., OHG lobōn, lobēn id. Derived from *luban. The variant *lubēnan attested in Goth lubains ‘hope’ is close to Lat lubet, libet ‘to please’. Torp- Falk 376; Walde-Hofmann I 793–794; Holthausen AEEW 206–207; Feist 336–337; Lehmann GED 237; Kluge- Seebold 522.
ON lypta ‘to lift, to raise’, MLG luchten id., MHG lüften id. Derived from *luftuz ~ *luftiz. Onions 527; Kluge-Seebold 527.

Goth luftus ‘air’ (masc. or fem.), ON lopt ‘air, sky, loft, upper room’ (< *luftan), OE lyft ‘air’ (fem., masc., neut.), OS luft id. (fem. and masc.), OHG luft id. (fem. and masc.). Related to Skt lumpáti ‘to break, to injure’, Lith lupū, lūpti ‘to peel’. The original meaning might be ‘porch’ or ‘upper floor’. However, the reverse direction of development is as possible, with ‘air’ defined as ‘section, sector’. Grienberger Got. 152–153 (to Lith luóbas ‘bark’); Torp-Falk 377 (to *lauban); Meringer Mitt. Pal. 57–60 (‘porch’ > ‘airy room’ > ‘air’); Loth RC XLIII 135–137 (to W llwch ‘whirling dust’ < *lup-s-); Torp-Falk 377; Holthausen AEEW 208; Feist 337; Mayrhofer III 108–109; Pokorny I 690–691; Fraenkel 391–392; Zalizniak II 202; Pisani JDE XIV 32–33 (< *luk-tu- ‘upper layer’); Lehmann GED 237; Bammesberger Nom. 157; Kluge-Seebold 527.

Norw lygn ‘lie’, OE lyzen id., OFris leine id., OS lugina id., OHG lugina id. Derived from *leuzanan. Similar to Slav adj. *lъžьnъ, fem. *lъžьna ‘false’. Torp-Falk 373; Holthausen AEEW 208; Pokorny I 687; Zalizniak I 221; Seebold 337; Lehmann GED 236; Trubachev ESSJa XVII 10–11.
ON lygi ‘lie’, OHG lugī id. Derived from *leuzanan. Torp-Falk 373; Pokorny I 686–687; Zalizniak I 221; Seebold 337.
ON þing-logi ‘engagementbreaker’, OE treów-loga ‘one who fails to keep faith’, OS treu-logo ‘traitor’. Derived from *leuzanan. Holthausen AEEW 206; Seebold 337; Bammesberger Nom. 174.

ON logi ‘flame’, OFris loga id., MHG lohe id. Cf. ON log id. (neut.). Related to *leuk- ‘to shine’, see *leuxtan. Torp-Falk 372; Onions 538; Pokorny I 688–689; Zalizniak II 220; Lehmann GED 228, 236; Bammesberger Nom. 175.

Goth lauhatjan ‘to flash (of lightning)’, OHG lohezzen id., ūzlougezzen ‘to spit with fire’ (< *lauzatjan). Structurally similar to Gk λευκάζω ‘to be white’ which, however, seems to be a Greek innovation (Schwyzer Mélanges Pedersen 63, 72). Connected with *lauzaz ~ *lauziz. Torp-Falk 372; Feist 324; Pokorny I 688; Frisk II 108–109; Lehmann GED 228.

OSwed lō ‘lynx’, OE lox id., OS lohs id., OHG luhs id. Related to Arm pl. lusnanunk' id., Gk λύγξ id., MIr lug id. (with irregular -g- < *-k-), Lith lūšis id., Slav *rysь id. (with irregular *r-), see Försterman KZ I 498. Bugge KZ XX 10; Zupitza Gutt. 191; Pedersen Kelt. Gr. I 186, 188; Torp-Falk 373; Holthausen AEEW 207; Pokorny I 690; Fraenkel 392–393; Frisk II 141–142; Zalizniak II 224; Kluge- Seebold 527.

Goth us-luk ‘opening’, ON lok ‘lock’, OE loc ‘lock, bolt, bar’, OFris lok ‘lock’, OHG loh ‘hole, cavity’. Related to *lūkanan. Torp-Falk 372; Holthausen AEEW 205; Feist 533; Pokorny I 686; Zalizniak II 227; Onions 534; Seebold 338; Lehmann GED 143; Bammesberger Nom. 57; Kluge-Seebold 522–523.

ON lokkr ‘hairlock’, OE locc ‘hair, hairlock’, OFris lokk ‘hairlock’, OS lokk id., OHG loc id. Related to IE *leug- ‘to bend’, cf. Gk λύγος ‘withy; screwpress’, Lith lūgnas ‘supple, flexible’. Zupitza Gutt. 164; Torp-Falk 372; Holthausen AEEW 205; Martinet Gém. 85, 92, 188; Pokorny I 685; Fraenkel 388–389; Frisk II 141; Zalizniak I 134; Onions 534; Kluge-Seebold 523.

ON lokka ‘to entice’, OE ze-loccian ‘to stroke gently’, OHG lockōn ‘to entice’. Probably connected with Lith lūgóti ‘to ask, to request’, Slav *lъgati ‘to lie’ (Endzelin KZ LII 114– 116). Cf. *leuzanan. Noreen Abriß 156; Zupitza Gutt. 164; Torp-Falk 374; Holthausen AEEW 205; Martinet Gém. 174; Pokorny I 687; Chemodanov SGGJa I 76; Fraenkel 389; Stang LS 34; Lehmann GED 236; Kluge-Seebold 523 (to *lukkaz).
ON loka ‘cover, lid’, OHG lucka ‘hatch, opening’. Derived from *lūkanan. Seebold 338.

Goth luns ‘ransom’. Finn lunnas id. (Thomsen Einfl. 151) attests the original form (masc. a-stem). Adjective in *-no- of IE *leu-: Skt lūná-, participle of Skt lunāti ‘to cut’, Gk λύω ‘to release’, Lat luō ‘to free, to pay off’. Cf. in particular Gk λύτρον ‘ransom’. Torp-Falk 370; Feist 338; Walde-Hofmann I 834; Mayrhofer III 106–107; Pokorny I 681–682; Dybo VSJa 24 (pretonic shortening in Gmc); Frisk II 149–150; Lehmann GED 238.
ON pl. lundir ‘flesh along the back’, OE lund-laza ‘rein, kidney’, OHG lunda ‘suet’. Related to *lanđīn ~ *lanđjō. Torp-Falk 362; Pokorny I 675.

ON pl. lungu ‘lungs’, OHG lunga ‘lung’. Cf. also *lunzun(j)ō > OE lunzen id., OFris lungen, lungene id., OS lungannia id., OHG lungun id. Related to *lunzwraz. Torp-Falk 360; Holthausen AEEW 207; Pokorny I 661; Zalizniak I 221; Onions 540; Lehmann GED 230; Kluge-Seebold 528.

OE adv. lungre ‘quickly, soon’, OS lungar ‘strong, powerful’, OHG lungar ‘efficient, eager, fast’. Identical with Gk ἐλαφρός ‘light, quick’. For further connections see *lenxtaz. Zupitza Gutt. 179; Torp-Falk 360; Holthausen AEEW 207; Pokorny I 661; Frisk I 484; Lehmann GED 230; Bammesberger Nom. 247; Heidermanns 388–389.
Goth *lunjan ‘to save’ (suggested by us-luneins ‘salvation, redemption’), OE a-lynnan ‘to deliver, to release’. Derived from *lunaz. Holthausen AEEW 209; Feist 338, 534; Lehmann GED 238.

Swed loppa ‘flea’, OE loppe ‘flea, spider(?)’. Derivationally close to Latv lupa ‘fragment, bit, small piece’ further related to Skt lumpáti ‘to break into pieces’, Lith lūpti ‘to peel’, Slav *lupiti id. For the semantic development cf. Slav *molь ‘moth’ from *melti ‘to grind, to break’ (see *maluz ~ *malō). Torp-Falk 375–376 (to MHG lupfen ‘to move quickly, to soar’); Pokorny I 690–691; Fraenkel 391–392.
ON los ‘looseness, breaking up’, OE los ‘loss’, OHG far-los id. Derived from *leusanan. Holthausen AEEW 206; Seebold 339; Onions 537.
Goth fra-lusnan ‘to be lost’, ON losna ‘to get loose, to get free’. Derived from *leusanan. Torp-Falk 377; Feist 163; Seebold 339.
ON losa ‘to loosen’, OE losian ‘to perish, to be lost’, OS lōsian ‘to become free’. Derived from *lusan. Holthausen AEEW 206; Onions 537.
Goth luston ‘to desire’, OE lystan ‘to cause pleasure’, OS lustian, lustean ‘to desire’, OHG lustōn id. Derived from *lustuz ~ *lustaz. Holthausen AEEW 209; Feist 338; Onions 531; Lehmann GED 238.

Goth lustu-sama ‘longed for, desirable’, OS lust-sam ‘desirable, pleasant, charming’, OHG lust-sam id. Derived from *lustuz ~ *lustaz. Cf. also OE lust-sum-líc ‘pleasant, delectable’. Feist 338; Carr Nom. comp. 59; Lehmann GED 238.
Goth fra-lusts ‘destruction, loss’, OS far-lust id., MHG ver-lust ‘waste’. Derived from *leusanan. Feist 163; Seebold 339; Bammesberger Nom. 140.

Goth lustus ‘desire, lust’, late ON lyst id. (fem. *lustiz), OE lust id., OFris lust id., OS lust id., OHG lust id. Probably, from *lub-studerived from *lubō. Schmidt Voc. II 148 (< *¬s-tu-, from *las- ‘to desire’ as in Skt láṣati id. < *-lals-, Gk λάω ‘to look’); Sperber Wu S VI 54–55 (to *lūtanan); Torp-Falk 378; Holthausen AEEW 207–208; Feist 338; Pokorny I 654 (to *las- ‘to be greedy, lascivous’), 683–684; Trier Venus 160–176 (from *leusanan); Zalizniak II 202; Onions 541; Lehmann GED 238; Bammesberger Nom. 157–158; Kluge-Seebold 528–529.
OE lyt ‘little, few’, LG lütt ‘small, little’, OHG luzzi id. Probably derived from *lūtanan. Johanss
ON loäi ‘fur cloak’, OE loäa ‘cloak, upper garment’, OFris lotha id., OS lotho id., OHG lodo, ludo ‘coarse woolen cloth’. Unclear. Torp-Falk 374– 375; Kluge-Seebold 523 (to *leuđanan).

Goth ga-lūkan ‘to lock up, to shut up’, ON lúka ‘to shut’, OE lúcan ‘to close, to conclude, to lock’, OFris lūka ‘to shut’, OS bi-lūkan id., OHG bi-lūhhan id. Connected with Gk λύγος ‘withy; screw-press’, Lith lūgnas ‘flexible’. Cf. *lukkaz. Torp-Falk 371; Feist 189–190; Holthausen AEEW 207; Jóhannesson IEW 743–744; Pokorny I 685–686; Fraenkel 388–389; Vries ANEW 368; Frisk II 141; Seebold 338–339; Lehmann GED 143; Kluge- Seebold 522–523.
OE lúcan ‘to pull up, to weed’, OFris lūka ‘to pull’, MLG lūken ‘to tear’, OHG liohhan ‘to pluck’ (with a secondary diphthong). Related to Skt rujáti ‘to break, to shatter’, m

ON maära ‘madder’, OE mædere id., MLG madre id., OHG matra id. Related to Slav *modrъ ‘purple, blue’ (Berneker II 66; Hellquist apud Vries). Loewenthal ANF XXXII 272 (to Russ matež ‘white spot’); Torp-Falk 306; Holthausen AEEW 210; Machek Ar Or XVII 131 (connects Slav *modrъ with Hitt antara- ‘blue’); Pokorny I 747; Vries ANEW 375 (to WFris miede id., MLG mēde id.); Onions 544; Seebold Lar. 510 (to Hitt antara- ‘blue’ < *ṃdhro-); Trubachev Arm lucanem ‘to break open’, Lith láužiu, láužti ‘to break’. Schweizer KZ I 565; Bezzenberger GGA 1898 556 (to Lat luctō ‘to wrestle, to struggle’, luxor ‘to riot’); Torp-Falk 371–372; Holthausen AEEW 207; Mayrhofer III 64; Pokorny I 686; Fraenkel 347; Seebold 337; Lehmann GED 143; Kluge-Seebold 7.

ON lús ‘louse’, OE lús id., MLG lūs id. (i-stem), OHG lūs id. (istem). Related to W pl. llau ‘lice’, Corn pl. low id., Bret pl. laou id. < *lu⁄ā. Lohmann Zfcelt Ph. XIX 62–64; Torp- Falk 571; Schulze Kl. Schr. 59 (to Slav *vъ“ь id.); Holthausen AEEW 207; Pokorny I 692; Chemodanov SGGJa I 99; Zalizniak II 209; Onions 538; Bammesberger Nom. 195; Griepentrog Wurzelnomina 257–271; Kluge-Seebold 506.

ON lúta ‘to bow down’, OE lútan ‘to lout, to bow, to bend forward’. Of unknown origin. Torp-Falk 374 (to Lith liūstū, li~sti ‘to become sad’, Slav *luditi ‘to deceive’); Holthausen AEEW 208; Jóhannesson IEW 745–746; Pokorny I 684; Vries ANEW 369; Fraenkel 378–379; Onions 538; Seebold 340–341; Lehmann GED 236 (to *leutaz). ESSJa XIX 101–104; Heidermanns 392.

Goth mag ‘can, to be able’, ON má id., OE mæz id., OFris mei id., OS mag id., OHG mag id. Most of the forms belong to the Vth class (Osthoff PBB XV 217–218). Related to Toch AB mokats ‘mighty’, Skt maghá- ‘gift, reward, wealth’, Gk μῆχος ‘means, contrivance’, OIr do-for-maig ‘to extend, to increase’, Lith magėti ‘to please’, Slav *mogǫ, *mogti ‘to be able’ (Schleicher KZ I 141). Curtius Gr. Et. 328 (to IE *me·h- ‘great, big’); Hirt PBB XXIII 35 (Slav *mogǫ, *mogti from Germanic); Wiedemann BB XXVIII 62–66; Meillet MSL XIV 335, XIX 183; Zupitza Gutt. 178; Schmidt KZ XXV 18 (same as Curtius); Torp-Falk 303; Trautmann KZ XLVI 180–182; Feist 338–339; Holthausen AEEW 213; Kluge PBB IX 156 (to Av masyō ‘larger’, Gk μακρός ‘long’); Jóhannesson IEW 650–651; Mayrhofer II 545–546; Pokorny I 695; Fraenkel 395; Frisk II 235; Vries ANEW 374; Zalizniak II 202; Onions 563; Seebold 342–343; Stang LS 37; Lehmann GED 239–240; Trubachev ESSJa XIX 107–111; Kluge- Seebold 565.

Goth magaþs ‘maiden’, OE mæzä ‘maid, virgin, girl, maiden’, OFris megith ‘maiden’, OS magath id. (root stem), OHG magad id. Identical with the first component of Celt *magot-aktā in MIr ingen maccdacht ‘young growing girl’, MW machteith ‘virgo’, OCorn mahtheid id. Further connected with *mazuz. Zupitza Gutt. 66; Torp-Falk 304; Holthausen AEEW 210; Feist 339; Pokorny I 696; Zalizniak II 209; Vendryes M-3; Onions 546; Lehmann GED 240; Bammesberger Nom. 215; Kluge-Seebold 531.

Burg *magin ‘power’, ON megin ‘might, strength’, OE mæzen ‘might, strength’, OS megin id., OHG megin, magan id. Derived from *maza. Torp-Falk 304 (to Gk μηχανή ‘contrivance, machine’); Holthausen AEEW 210; Vries ANEW 375, 381; Onions 546; Seebold 342; Bammesberger Nom. 82; Köbler 712; Heidermanns 392–393.
ON megin-verk ‘mighty deed’, OE mæzen-weorc ‘mighty work’. Compound of *mazenan and *werkan. Carr Nom. comp. 50.
ON meginn ‘strong’, OHG megin id. See *mazenan. Heidermanns 392–393.
ON magna ‘to strengthen’, OE mæzenian ‘to gain strength’, OHG ubar-maganōn, ubar-meginōn id. Derived from *mazenan. Holthausen AEEW 210; Vries ANEW 375, 381.
ON megä ‘doing’, MLG mechte ‘power’. Derived from *maza. Vries ANEW 381.

ON magi ‘stomach’, OE maza id., OFris maga id., MLG mage id., OHG mago id. Connected with W megin ‘bellows’, Lith mãkas ‘purse’. Zupitza Gutt. 134; Torp-Falk 304 (to OIr mén ‘open mouth’ < *makno-); Holthausen AEEW 213; Pokorny I 698; Fraenkel 399; Vries ANEW 375; Zalizniak I 154; Onions 563; Kluge-Seebold 531; Boutkan OFED s.v.

ON magr ‘meager, lean’, OE mæzer id., MLG mager id., OHG magar id. Identical with Lat macer id. and probably Gk μακρός ‘long’. Karsten NB II 251–252; Torp-Falk 304; Holthausen AEEW 210; Walde-Hofmann II 2; Porzig Gliederung 111; Pokorny I 699; Kammenhuber KZ LXXVII 64 (on Hitt makl-ant- ‘thin’); Chemodanov SGGJa I 91; Vries ANEW 375; Frisk II 164– 165; Heidermann 393; Kluge-Seebold 531.
ON megri ‘meagerness’, OHG magarī id. Derived from *mazraz. Torp-Falk 304; Vries ANEW 381; Heidermanns 393.
ON megra ‘to make meager’, OHG magaren id. Derived from *mazraz. Vries ANEW 381; Heidermanns 393.

Goth magus ‘child, boy’, ON mǫgr ‘boy, youth, son, man’, OE mazu ‘child, son, servant’, OS magu ‘servant’, OHG magu-zogo ‘educator, mentor’. Identical with OIr maug, mug ‘slave’, Corn maw ‘youth’, Bret mao id. (Arbois de Jubainville MSL VII 292: Gmc from Celtic) and further related to Av magava- ‘unmarried’ (Fick II 198; Brugmann IF XXXVIII 140–141: further to *maza, cf. Lat uir ‘man’ ~ uīs ‘power’). Schweizer KZ I 559 (to Gk μέγας); Wiedemann BB XXVIII 62–66 (to OIr macc ‘son’); Bartholomae 1111; Berneker IF IX 361 (to Lat homō ‘man’); Zupitza KZ XXVI 237, Gutt. 65; Jokl IF XLIII 57–59 (to Alb makth ‘young hare’); Pedersen Festschr. Jespersen 62–63 (on OIr macc ‘son’); Meillet MSL XXI 46 (from children’s vocabulary); Torp-Falk 304; Holthausen AEEW 214; Feist 339; Pokorny I 696; Chemodanov SGGJa I 96; Vendryes M-70–71; Vries ANEW 400; Zalizniak I 152; Onions 546; Lehmann Zf Mund XXXV 15–19 (non- IE), GED 240; Bammesberger Nom. 156–157.

Goth mawilo ‘little girl’, ON meyla id., OE meówle ‘maid, damsel, virgin’. Derived from *mazwjō. Torp- Falk 304; Hirt Etym. 110 (to Skt mahilā ‘woman, wife’); Holthausen AEEW 219; Feist 352; Chemodanov SGGJa I 106; Vries ANEW 386.
Goth mawi ‘girl’, ON mær id. Derived from *mazuz. Zupitza Gutt. 65; Torp-Falk 304; Feist 352; Vries ANEW 399; Bammesberger Nom. 114.

Goth mahteigs ‘powerful, able, possible’, ON máttigr ‘powerful, mighty’, OE meahtiz id., OFris elle-machtich ‘almighty’, OS mahtig ‘powerful, mighty’, OHG mahtig id. Derived from *maxtiz. Torp-Falk 304; Feist 340; Pokorny I 695; Lehmann GED 240; Boutkan OFED s.v.
ON mátt-lauss ‘powerless, exhausted’, OE meaht-leás ‘powerless’, Du machte-loos id., MHG macht-lōs id. Compound of *maxtiz and *lausaz. Carr Nom. comp. 90 (parallel formations).

Goth mahts ‘power, might’, ON máttr id. (< *maxtuz), OE meaht ‘might’, OFris macht id., OS maht id., OHG maht id. Derived from *maza. Structurally identical with Slav *mogt’ь ‘might, power’. Wiedemann BB XXVI- II 63 (Slav < Gmc); Torp-Falk 304; Holthausen AEEW 216; Feist 340; Benveniste Nom. 108–111; Pokorny I 695; Zalizniak II 202; Onions 575; Stang LS 37; Seebold 342; Lehmann GED 240; Bammesberger Nom. 145; Trubachev ESSJa XIX 111–112; Kluge- Seebold 530.
ON meiär ‘pole, beam’. Related to Skt methí- ‘pillar, post’, Lith mi‚tas ‘pole’. Torp-Falk 301–302; Mayrhofer II 683; Pokorny I 709; Fraenkel 451; Vries ANEW 381.

Goth ga-maiþs ‘crippled’, OE ze-mæd ‘crazy, mad’, OS gi-mēd ‘foolish, stupid’, OHG gi-meit ‘silly, foolish, stupid’. Derived from *maiđjanan (Uhlenbeck PBB XXX 280). Torp-Falk 321; Bugge PBB XXIV 456–457 (to Gk μάτην ‘in vain’); Feist 191; Schwietering ZDADL LVI 125–127; Holthausen AEEW 209; Pokorny I 715; Vries ANEW 144; Onions 544; Lehmann GED 144; Heidermanns 394–395.

Goth maidjan ‘to alter, to falsify’, ON meiäa ‘to hurt, to injure, to maim’, OE part. ze-mæded ‘foolish, maddened’. Causative of *mīþanan (Zupitza BB XXV 98). Torp-Falk 321; Holthausen AEEW 210; Feist 340; Pokorny I 715; Vries ANEW 381; Onions 544; Benveniste IEL 82; Seebold 349; Peeters KZ XCIV 207–208; Lehmann GED 144, 240–241; Heidermanns 394–395.
OE máh ‘wicked, wanton’. Identical with Gk μοιχός ‘adulterer’. Further see *mīzanan. Holthausen AEEW 213; Frisk II 249–250; Bammesberger Nom. 237; Heidermanns 395.

Goth mail ‘wrinkle’, OE mál ‘mole, spot, mark’, MLG mēl ‘mole, spot’, OHG meil id. Identical with OIr mael ‘bald’ < *mailo- (Saussure Recueil 595–596; Wood MLN XXI 40). Persson Beiträge 221 (to Lith pl. mi‚lës ‘yeast’); Hirt Ablaut 151; Grienberger Got. 154 (to Gk μιαρός ‘stained’, μιαίνω ‘to dye’); Bugge PBB XXIV 433 (to Skt mála- ‘dirt, filth’, Gk μέλας ‘black’); Torp-Falk 302; Holthausen AEEW 214; Feist 340–341; Pokorny I 697; Onions 585; Lehmann GED 241.
ON mein-eiär ‘false oath, perjury’, OE mán-áä id., OFris mēn-ēth id., OS mēn-ēth id., OHG meineid id. Compound of *mainaz and *aiþaz. Torp-Falk 319; Carr Nom. comp. 50.
ON mein-fullr ‘noxious’, OE mán-full ‘evil, wicked’, OS mēnful ‘noxious’, OHG mein-fol ‘nefarious, shameful’. Compound of *mainan and *fullaz. Carr Nom. comp. 60.
ON mein-ligr ‘painful, troublesome’, OHG mein-līh ‘nefarious, dastardly’. Derived from *mainaz. Heidermanns 396.

ON mein ‘hurt, harm’, OE mán ‘shameful action, crime, wickedness’, OS mēn id., OHG mein id. Substantivized form of *mainaz. Torp-Falk 319; Holthausen AEEW 214; Pokorny I 710; Vries ANEW 382; Zalizniak II 170; Onions 553; Heidermanns 396.
ON mein-svari ‘perjurer’, OE mán-swara id. Compound of *mainan or *mainaz and *swarōn. Carr Nom. comp. 76 (parallel formations).

ON meinn ‘mean, base’, OE mán ‘wicked, false’, OFris mēn ‘wrong’, MLG mēn id., OHG mein id. Related to Lat mūnus ‘service, office’, OIr móin, máin ‘preciousness’, Lith maĩnas ‘change, exchange’, Slav *mîna id. and other derivatives of *mei- ‘to change, to exchange’. Karsten NB II 225–226, 313; Pedersen Kelt. Gr. I 57, 174; Torp- Falk 319; Feist 190–191; Holthausen AEEW 214; Walde-Hofmann I 255; Pokorny I 710; Chemodanov SGGJa I 86–87; Fraenkel 395–397; Vries ANEW 382; Benveniste 79; Lehmann GED 143–144; Heidermanns 395–396; Trubachev ESSJa XVIII 171–172; Kluge- Seebold 550.

Goth ga-mains ‘common’, OE ze-mæne id., OFris mēne id., OS gi-mēni ‘usual’, OHG gi-meini ‘common’. The form with prefix *za-mainiz is identical with Lat commūnis id. See *mainaz. Torp-Falk 319; Feist 190–191; Holthausen AEEW 211; Walde-Hofmann I 254–256; Pokorny I 710; Onions 565; Peeters KZ XLI 166; Heidermanns 396–397; Kluge-Seebold 311.
Goth ga-mainei ‘community, κοινωνία’, OFris mēne id., OHG gi-meinī id. Derived from *mainiz. Feist 190; Heidermanns 397.

Goth ga-mainjan ‘to demean’, ON meina ‘to harm, to hinder’, MDu ver-menen ‘to damage by a criminal act’, OHG fir-meinen ‘to perjure’. Derived from *mainaz. Structurally identical with Slav *mîniti ‘to change’. Feist 190; Vries ANEW 382; Trubachev ESSJa XVIII 173–174.

OE mænan ‘to mean’, OFris mēna ‘to signify’, OS mēnian ‘to intend, to make known’, OHG meinen ‘to mean’. Related to Slav *mьnîti ~ mьniti ‘to think’, *mîniti ‘to mean, to think’. Torp-Falk 302 (from WGm *mainō ‘meaning, sense’: OFris mēne, OHG meina compared with OIr mían ‘wish’ < *meino-); Holthausen AEEW 211; Pokorny I 714; Onions 565; Meid Sprache XII 109; Stang LS 36; Trubachev ESSJa XVIII 174–175, XXI 113–115; Kluge-Seebold 551.

Goth ga-mainjan ‘to share, to make common’, OE mænan ‘to tell, to relate’, MDu ge-meinen ‘to impart’, OHG gi-meinen ‘to destine, to allot’. Derived from *mainiz. Holthausen AEEW 211; Feist 190; Heidermanns 397.
Goth ga-mainja ‘partaker, participant’, OHG gi-meino ‘comrade, partner’. Derived from *mainiz. Feist 190; Heidermanns 397.

ON landa-mæri ‘borderland’, OE mære ‘boundary, limit’, MDu mēre ‘boundary mark, pole’. Related to Lat mūrus ‘wall’. Torp-Falk 302; Holthausen AEEW 212; Walde-Hofmann II 131–132; Pokorny I 709; Onions 570.

ON meiss ‘basket, wooden box’. Cf. also *maisjō > MLG mēse ‘ton, dry measure’, OHG meisa ‘basket’. Identical with Slav *mîxъ ‘water-skin, fur’, OPrus moasis ‘bellows’, Lith maĩšas ‘bag’ (Bugge KZ XX 1–2; Schrader- Nehring I 629). Uhlenbeck Afsl PH XVI 370; Lidén PBB XV 512 (to *maitanan); Torp-Falk 302; Trautmann BSW 165; Pokorny I 747; Fraenkel 397; Vries ANEW 382; Trubachev ESSJa XVIII 156–159.

Norw dial. meisk ‘mix’, OE máx-wyrt ‘mash-wort, the wort in the mash-tub’, MLG mesche ‘crushed grapes’, MHG meisch ‘crushed grapes’. Identical with Slav *mîzga ‘juice, tar’. Torp-Falk 322 (to *miskjanan ~ *miskōjanan); Holthausen AEEW 216; Onions 559; Trubachev ESSJa XVIII 223–225; Kluge-Seebold 533.

Swed mes ‘tit, titmouse’, OE máse id., OS mēsa id., OHG meisa id. Cf. also ON meisingr id. Derived from *maisaz. Fick II 205 (to W mwyalch ‘blackbird’ < *meisalkā, cf. *amslōn); Wood KZ XLV 70 (to Norw meis ‘weak, frail’); Torp-Falk 302 (to Lar merula ‘blackbird’, W mwyalch id.); Holthausen AEEW 215; Vries ANEW 382; Onions 927; Kluge-Seebold 551.

Goth maists ‘most’, ON mestr id., OE mást id., OFris māst id., OS mēst id., OHG meist id. Superlative related to *maiz. Feist 341; Holthausen AEEW 216; Pokorny I 704; Vries ANEW 385; Onions 592; Lindeman Verschärfung 9.3; Lehmann GED 241; Bammesberger Nom. 236; Kluge- Seebold 551.

Goth maitan ‘to cut, to hew’, OHG meizan ‘to cut off’. Probably related to *mīþanan, *maiđjanan (Solmsen KZ XXXVII 584). Derived from here (with a prefix *ē-) is the WGmc word for ‘ant’ *maitjōn > OE æ-met(t)e ‘ant’, MLG ā-mete, ē-mete id., OHG a-meiza id. For the semantic development cf. Slav *molь ‘moth’ from *melti ‘to grind, to break’. Uhlenbeck PBB XXX 280; Grienberger Got. 154 (same as Hoffmann); Hoffmann Festschr. Fick 45 (to *smītanan); Wood MLN XXI 40 (same as Solmsen); Torp-Falk 320; Noreen Abriß 31, 192 (to Goth mes ‘table’, OE mése id.); Holthausen AEEW 11; Feist 341–342; Pokorny I 697; Vries ANEW 382–383; Onions 39, 582; Seebold 343–344; Lehmann GED 144, 241–242; Lloyd- Springer I 203–205; Kluge-Seebold 33, 551.
ON meitill ‘chisel’, OHG meizil id. Derived from *maitanan. Torp- Falk 320; Vries ANEW 382; Seebold 344; Lehmann GED 242.
ON meita ‘to cut, to trim’, OHG aba-fir-meizan ‘to cut off’ (str.). Derived from *maitanan. Jóhannesson IEW 648; Vries ANEW 382; Seebold 344.
ON prop. Meiti, OHG stein-meizo ‘stone mason’. Derived from *maitanan. Vries ANEW 382.

Goth maiþms ‘gift’, ON pl. meiämar ‘gifts, presents’ (fem.), OE máäum ‘treasure, jewel, ornament’, OS mēdom id., MHG meidem ‘stallion’. Connected with *maiđjanan, *mīþanan. Torp- Falk 320; Feist 342; Holthausen AEEW 216; Pokorny I 715; Vries ANEW 381 (to Lat mūtō ‘to move’); Zalizniak II 175; Benveniste IEL 82; Seebold 349; Lehmann Festschr. Einarsson 165 (metric particularities of the Beowulf form); GED 242; Kienast Gedenkschr. Güntert 57–67; Bammesberger Nom. 70.

ON már, mór ‘mew, gull’, OE mæw id. (< *maiwiz), Fris meau, mieu id., MLG mēve id. (fem.). Derived from *maiwjanan (Suolahti ANT 397). Uhlenbeck PBB XX 328 (from *maixwaz, to Skt mécaka- ‘dark blue, black’); Zupitza Gutt. 66; Pipping SNF VIII/1 5 (to ON mær ‘narrow’); Torp-Falk 301 (follow Uhlenbeck); Holthausen AEEW 213; Pokorny I 751; Vries ANEW 378–379; Zalizniak II 188; Onions 574; Lerchner 198; Kluge-Seebold 572.
ON mjór ‘slim, narrow’. Identical with Toch B maiwe ‘young’. Karsten NB II 445; Torp-Falk 319; Pokorny I 711; Vries ANEW 399; Heidermanns 397–398; Adams TB 472– 473.
ME mawen ‘to shout, to mew’, MDu mauwen id., MHG māwen id. Of imitative origin. Pokorny I 751 (to Latv maût ‘to roar’, Slav *myjati ‘to mew’).

Goth mais ‘more’, ON meir id., OE má id., OFris mār, mā id., OS mē, mēr id., OHG mēr id. Comparative based on *mē- ‘large’ similar to Osc mais ‘more’ (Sommer IF XI 84–90), W mwy id. < *mēis (Osthoff Morph. Unt. VI 219–221). Torp-Falk 300; Meillet BSL XXVII 232–233; Feist 341; Holthausen AEEW 209; Pokorny I 704; Vries ANEW 382; Pisani Glotta L 28–29 (to archaic Aeol *maiw ‘more’); Onions 583; Lehmann GED 241; Boutkan OFED s.v.

Goth maiza ‘larger’, ON meiri ‘bigger, higher’, OE mára ‘larger, more’, OFris māra ‘bigger’, OS mēro ‘bigger, higher’, OHG mēro ‘larger, more’. See *maiz. Torp-Falk 300–301; Feist 341–342; Holthausen AEEW 209; Kluge 470–471; Pokorny I 704; Vries ANEW 382; Onions 589; Lindeman Verschärfung 105; Lehmann GED 242; Bammesberger Nom. 236; Heidermanns 398–399; Kluge-Seebold 550.
ON mak-ligr ‘proper, becoming’, OHG gi-mah-līh ‘comfortable’. Derived from *makaz. Heidermanns 399.
OESc mak ‘condition, state’, OS un-gimak ‘precipitousness’, OHG mah ‘thing’. See *makaz. Heidermanns 399.

ON makr ‘more unsuitable’, OE mæc ‘well-matched, equal, agreeable’, OS un-gimak ‘hostile’, OHG gi-mah ‘appropriate’. Derived from *makōjanan (Spitzer Wu S VI 37). Karsten NB II 453; Torp-Falk 303; Holthausen AEEW 209–210, ANW 190 (ON < MLG); Lidén SSUF 1898 67 (to OIr mám ‘yoke’ < *magmu); Pokorny I 696– 697; Vries ANEW 376; Onions 561; Heidermanns 399–400; Kluge-Seebold 311.

Icel maka ‘to smear’, OE macian ‘to make, to do’, OFris makia id., OS makōn id., OHG mahhōn id. Identical with Slav *mažǫ, *mazati ‘to smear’. Further connected with Gk μάσσω ‘to knead’ (Meringer IF XVII 146–148). Torp-Falk 303; Meillet MSL XIX 122; Endzelin KZ XLIV 66 (on Baltic parallels: Latv muôzêt ‘to torment’ < *‘to smear’); Holthausen AEEW 209; Pokorny I 696–697; Frisk II 180–181; Onions 547; Vasmer-Trubachev II 557–558; Heidermanns 400; Kluge-Seebold 530; Boutkan OFED s.v.
ON maki ‘match, mate’, OE ze-maca ‘mate, equal, companion’, OS gi-mako ‘match’. Derived from *makaz. Torp-Falk 303; Holthausen AWN 190 (ON < OS); Trier Lehm 87–88; Vries ANEW 376.

Goth malan ‘to grind’, ON mala id., OS part. gi-malan id., OHG malan id. Related to Hitt malla- id., Toch A malyw-, B mely- ‘to crush, to grind’, Skt mṛṇāti id., Arm malem id., Lat molō ‘to grind’, OIr melim id., Lith malū, málti id., Slav *meljǫ, *melti id. (Lottner KZ VII 22). Torp-Falk 314–315; Trautmann BSW 168; Walde-Hofmann II 104–106; Feist 342; Holthausen AEEW 218–219; Jóhannesson IEW 672–675; Trier Lehm 67–75; Mayrhofer II 672–673; Pokorny I 716–717; Fraenkel 403–404; Vries ANEW 377; Seebold 344–345; Lehmann GED 242; Tischler HEG II 102–103; Trubachev ESSJa XVIII 90–91; Kluge-Seebold 532–533; Adams TB 456–457; Burlak IFTJa 251.
ON meldr ‘flour, corn in the mill’, OS maldar ‘milling, grinding’, OHG maltar ‘flour’. Derived from *malanan. Torp-Falk 314; Vries ANEW 383.

ON malr ‘knapsack’, ME male id., MLG male id., OHG malaha id. A Wanderwort similar to Gk μάλγος ‘leather sack’. Torp-Falk 316; Feist 524 (to *malskaz); Holthausen ANW 191 (ON < MLG); Vries ANEW 377; Van Windekens I 286 (to Toch AB mälk- ‘to interweave’).

WFris māl ‘foolish, mad’, MLG mall ‘stupid, foolish’. Probably, identical with Skt malvá- ‘thoughtless, foolish, unwise’ (Heidermanns 400). Related to *malanan. Holthausen AEEW 216, 221; Mayrhofer II 602; Pokorny I 716.

Goth malma ‘sand’, ON malmr ‘metal’, OE mealm-stán ‘malmstone’. Cf. a more archaic ablaut grade in *melmaz. Torp-Falk 314; Holthausen AEEW 217; Feist 343; Pokorny I 717; Vries ANEW 377 (eventually, to *malanan); Zalizniak II 179; Onions 549; Seebold 344; Lehmann GED 242; Bammesberger Nom. 71; Kluge-Seebold 535.

Goth un-tila-malsks ‘rash, reckless’, OS malsk ‘proud, high-spirited’. From *malđskaz related to Lat mollis ‘soft’ < *molduis, Slav *moldъ ‘young’ and their cognates (Grienberger Got. 229). Further see *maltaz. Torp-Falk 316; Walde- Hofmann II 103–104; Feist 524; Holthausen AEEW 214; Pokorny I 719 (to Gk μαλακός ‘weak’, MIr malcad ‘rot, decay’); Ebbinghaus GL XVII 20 (to Gk μάλα, μάλλον ‘much, more’); Lehmann GED 378–379 (šmay be non-IE”); Heidermanns 400–401; Trubachev ESSJa XIX 174–179.

ON malt ‘malt’, OE mealt id., OS malt id., OHG malz id. Substantivized *maltaz. Torp-Falk 317; Holthausen AEEW 217; Pokorny I 718; Vries ANEW 377–378; Zalizniak I 142; Onions 549; Martynov Jazyk 20–21 (from Slav *molto); Heidermanns 401; Kluge-Seebold 536.

Icel maltr ‘sharp, bitter (of taste)’, OE mealt ‘cooked, boiled (?)’, OFris malt ‘bad, spoiled’, OHG malz ‘gentle, soft, mild’. Related to Skt mṛdú- ‘soft, tender, mild’, Lat mollis id., W blydd id., Slav *moldъ ‘young’ (with an o-grade as in Gmc), further connected with Skt mṛdnāti ‘to press, to squeeze, to rub’, Gk μέλδω ‘to soften by boiling’. Karsten NB I 42; Torp-Falk 317; Walde- Hofmann II 13–104; Mayrhofer II 676; Pokorny I 718; Frisk II 199–200; Onions 549, 568; Heidermanns 401; Trubachev ESSJa XIX 174–179; Kluge-Seebold 536.
ON melta ‘to malt for brewing’, MLG melten id., MHG malzen id. Derived from *maltan. Vries ANEW 383.

ON melta ‘to melt’, OE miltan id. Goth ga-malteins ‘melting away, dissolution’ is derived from here. Causative of *meltanan. Structurally identical with Slav *molditi ‘to become young, to make young’. Torp-Falk 317; Holthausen AEEW 221; Feist 191–192; Vries ANEW 383; Seebold 351; Trubachev ESSJa XIX 164–166.

OE meld ‘declaration, proclamation’, OS maldia ‘testimony, accusation’, OHG melda id. Despite the phonetic differences, close to Lith maldā ‘plea’. Further related to Hitt mald- ‘to vow’, Arm malt'em ‘to ask, to request’, Lith mefisti ‘to ask, to bid’, Slav *modliti ‘to pray’. Meillet MSL X 227; Torp-Falk 317; Pokorny I 722; Fraenkel 431–434; Tischler HEG II 109–110; Kluge- Seebold 551–552.

Goth malo ‘moth’, ON mǫlr id., OE mæl-sceafa ‘canker’. Close to Slav *molь ‘moth’ (Meillet MSL XIV 373). For further connections see *malanan. Torp-Falk 314; Holthausen AEEW 211; Feist 343; Pokorny I 717; Stang LS 37; Vries ANEW 401; Zalizniak II 203; Lehmann GED 243; Bammesberger Nom. 177; Trubachev ESSJa XIX 203–205; Kluge-Seebold 558.

Goth ga-malwjan ‘to crush, to grind’, ON mǫlva to shiver, to break’. Related to *melwan. Identical with Toch A malyw-, B mely- ‘to crush, to grind’ < *mol⁄ƒ- (Meillet JA XVIII 461). Torp-Falk 314; Lane Language XIV 31; Feist 192; Vries ANEW 383; Lehmann GED 144; Adams TB 470; Burlak IFTJa 251.

Goth man ‘to reckon, to think’, ON man ‘to remember’, mun ‘to become, shall, will’ (aux.), OE man ‘to remember, to be mindful of ’, OS far-man ‘to despise’. Continues IE *men-: Skt mányate ‘to think’, Av mainyete id., Gk μέμονα ‘to think intensively’, Lat meminī ‘to think of, to remember’, Lith menū, μιάτι ‘to remember’, Slav *mьnjǫ, *mьnîti ‘to mean, to think’. Bartholomae 1121– 1124; Torp-Falk 307; Meillet MSL XIII 364–366; Walde-Hofmann II 65–66; Feist 366–367; Holthausen AEEW 227; Jóhannesson IEW 664–665; Mayrhofer II 583–584; Pokorny I 726–728; Fraenkel 455–456; Vries ANEW 395; Frisk II 206–207; Onions 577; Seebold 345–347; Lehmann GED 260; Kluge-Seebold 533.

Goth manag-falþs ‘manifold’, OE maniz-feald id., OFris manich-fald id., OS manag-fald id., OHG manag-falt ‘frequent, multiple’. Compound of *manazaz and *falđaz. Feist 343; Scardigli Goten 223 (WGmc forms influenced by Gothic); Carr Nom. comp. 66; Lehmann GED 243.

Goth manags ‘sufficient, large, many’, late ON mangr ‘many, much’, OE maniz id., OFris manich, monich id., OS manag id., OHG manag id. Related to OIr menic(c) ‘frequent, abundant’, Slav *mъnogъ ‘many; abundant’ (Bugge KZ XIX 444) with variations in phonetic details. Fick I 104, 508 (to *me·h- ‘large’ with a nasal infix); Brugmann Grundriß II 971 (follows Fick); Pedersen Kelt. Gr. I 159; Torp-Falk 309; Trautmann BSW 189; Schulze Kl. Schr. 74–75; Holthausen AEEW 214; Feist 343–344; Pokorny I 712; Vries ANEW 378; Onions 554; Stang LS 39; Lehmann GED 243; Trubachev ESSJa XX 229–231; Kluge-Seebold 536.

Goth managei ‘people, crowd’, ON mengi ‘multitude’ (neut. < *manazjan), OE menizu id., OFris menie id., OS menigi id., OHG managī, menigī id. Derived from *manazaz. Holthausen AEEW 219; Feist 343; Vries ANEW 384; Lehmann GED 243; Kluge-Seebold 553.

Goth ga-man ‘fellow, fellowship, κοινωνία’, ON man ‘bondman’. Derived from *mannz with the original meaning preserved in Gothic. Wadstein IF V 8 (< *za-amanan, to Lat amō ‘to love’); Wiedemann BB XXVII 202 (to Gk θυμός ‘soul, spirit’, Lat fūmus ‘smoke’); Rooth Wortst. 42 (to *zamalaz); Torp- Falk 119; Holthausen AEEW 123; Schulze Kl. Schr. 851–853; Feist 192; Fowkes JEGP XLIV 208–228; Pokorny I 490 (to IE *g⁄hemb-); Vries ANEW 154; Zalizniak I 140; Onions 388.

MHG menz ‘infertile cow’. Close to Alb mëz ~ mâz ‘foal’ < PAlb *mandja, MIr menn ‘young of animals’ < *mendo-, Lat-Celt mannus ‘small Gaulish horse’. Cf. also Messap Iuppiter Menzana (Stier KZ XI 148) < *mendhƒ-. Torp- Falk 570; Krahe Glotta XIX 149; Walde-Hofmann II 29–30; Pokorny I 729; Orel AED 265.

ON mǫndull ‘handle (of a hand-mill)’, G dial. mandel ‘roller, rolling-pin’. Probably derived from *manđjō. Torp-Falk 309 (to Slav *mętǫ ‘to mix, to sway’, Lith mentūrë ‘twirling stick’); Pokorny I 732; Vries ANEW 401; Lerchner 197.

OE manian ‘to remind, to admonish, to exhort’, OFris monia ‘to warn, to advise, to remember’, OS manōn id., OHG fir-manēn, fir-manōn ‘to neglect’. Related to *mana. Structurally close to Lat moneō ‘to remind, to advise’. Torp- Falk 308; Holthausen AEEW 214; Walde-Hofmann II 107; Lehmann GED 261.
ON manga ‘to barter, to chaffer’, OE manzian ‘to traffic, to trade’, OS mangōn id. Based on Lat mangō ‘dealer, trader’. Holthausen AEEW 214; Onions 586.

ON men ‘necklace’, OE mene id. (masc.), OS meni id., OHG menni id. Identical with Skt mányā ‘nape of the neck’, and further related to Lat monīle ‘necklace, collar’, OIr muin-torc ‘necklace’. Torp-Falk 308–309; Holthausen AEEW 219; Walde-Hofmann II 108; Mayrhofer II 584–585, Gedenkschr. Güntert 289–291; Porzig Gliederung 113; Pokorny I 747–748; Vries ANEW 384; Zalizniak II 213; Kluge-Seebold 533.
ON makki ‘upper part of horse’s neck’. Related to OIr mong ‘hair, mane’. See *manjan. Torp-Falk 309; Vries ANEW 376 (further to *manō); Onions 551.
Goth man-auli ‘human image, human figure’, ON mann- œli ‘mannekin, human shape’. Compound of *mannz and *auljan (see *aulaz). Carr Nom. comp. 67.
ON mann-fǫr ‘travelling’, OE mann-faru ‘expedition, a moving band of men’. Compound of *mannz and *farō. Carr Nom. comp. 82 (parallel formations).
ON mann-kyn ‘mankind’, OE mann-cynn id., OS mankunni id., OHG man-kunni id. Compound of *mannz and *kunjan. Rankin JEGP VIII 357–359 (calque of Lat genus humanum); Carr Nom. comp. 50.
ON mann-lauss ‘without men, without a husband’, OE mannleás ‘uninhabited’, MLG man-lōs ‘without men’. Compound of *mannz and *lausaz. Carr Nom. comp. 90 (parallel formations).

Goth man-leika ‘image, likeness’, ON mann-líkan ‘human image, idol’ (neut.), OE mann-líca ‘human form, image of a man, statue’, OHG mana-līhho ‘statue, image’. Compound of *mannz and *līkōn. Carr Nom. comp. 43 (secondary formation in ON); Feist 344; Lehmann GED 230, 244.

Goth mannisks ‘human’, ON mennskr id., OE mennisc id., OFris sbst. manniska ‘man’, OS mannisc, mennisc ‘human’, OHG mennisc id. Derived from *mannz. Torp-Falk 308; Holthausen AEEW 219; Feist 345; Porzig Gliederung 201; Pokorny I 700; Szemerényi IBK XV 193 (close to Lat masculus ‘male, masculine’ < *manu-sk-elos); Vries ANEW 384; Lehmann GED 244; Kluge-Seebold 553.
ON manna ‘to man (a boat)’, OE mannian ‘to supply with men’, MHG mannen ‘to make smb. a man’. Derived from *mannz. Vries ANEW 378.

Goth manna ‘man’ (some forms continue *mannōn), ON maär, mannr id., OE mann id., OFris mann, monn id., OS man id., OHG man id. Cf. also Mannus, ancestor of Germans (Tacit.). Related to Skt mánu-, mánuṣ- id., Av manuš- (in prop.), Slav *mǫžь id. (< *mongƒo-). Bopp Glossarium 286 (to *men- ‘to think’); Schweizer KZ I 158; Berneker IF IX 360–361 (to Lat homō ‘man’); Brugmann IF XXXVII 250; Niedermann Idg. Anz. XXIX 35 (to Lat manus ‘hand’); Uhlenbeck PBB XXX 301 (against Niedermann); Berneker IF IX 360–361 (to Lat homō ‘man’); Bartholomae 1135; Torp-Falk 307–308; Holthausen AEEW 215; Feist 344–345; Vaillant RES XVIII 75 (pre-Slav *-ng- < *-n⁄-); Schulze Kl. Schr. 851–853; Martinet Gém. 64, 69; Mayrhofer II 575–576; Pokorny I 700; Vries ANEW 374–375; Zalizniak I 157; Onions 549–550; Lehmann GED 244; Bammesberger Nom. 201; Trubachev ESSJa XX 158–161; Kluge-Seebold 538.

ON mǫn ‘mane’, OE monu id., OFris mana, mona id., MLG man id., OHG man, mana id. Connected with *manjan. Torp-Falk 308; Holthausen AEEW 215; Pokorny I 748; Vries ANEW 401; Zalizniak I 147; Onions 551; Kluge-Seebold 533.

Goth manwus ‘ready, at hand’. Derived from *manu- ‘hand’ reflected in Lat manus (Uhlenbeck PBB XXX 301). Grienberger Got. 155–156 (to *munēnan); Wood MP XI 32 (to Gk μάνυ: μικρόν, Hes.); Torp-Falk 310; Studerus IF XLVIII 72 (from *manxwuz, to Lith mīnkyti ‘to knead’); Walde- Hofmann II 34–35; Feist 345–346; Pokorny I 740; Lehmann GED 244–245; Heidermanns 403–404 (reconstructs

Goth *marþus ‘marten’ (as attested in Span marta), Crim. Goth. marzus ‘marriage, wedding’, ON mǫrär ‘marten’, OE mearä id., OFris merth id., MHG mart id. Related to Lith martī ‘bride’ (Solmsen KZ XXXV 481–482). For the semantic development ‘bride’ > ‘marten’ cf. Ital donnola ‘marten’ ~ donna ‘woman’, NGk νυμφίτα ‘marder’. Tomaschek Taurien 62 (Goth marzus < *marþ-kus, with *marþ- to Lith martī ‘bride, young woman’ and *-kus to Gmc *keusan); Grienberger Zd Ph XXX 125 (Goth marzus < *marwiþos, to MHG merwen ‘to hold’); Much Idg. Anz. IX 198 (Goth marzus < *marþhus ‘bride house’, to Lith martī ‘bride, young woman’); Torp- Falk 313; Holthausen AEEW 217; Feist 348; Pokorny I 739; Fraenkel 412; Vries ANEW 401 (to *murþan I); Scardigli Goten 260 (Goth marzus < Lat Martius ‘March’, month of weddings); Stearns CG 146; Lehmann GED 246–247; Kluge-Seebold 540.
Goth *marh-skalks ‘horse-groom’ (reconstructed from OItal mariscalco), MLG mar-schalk id., OHG marah-scalc id. Compound of *marxaz and *skalkaz. Gamillscheg RG 12, 66; Carr Nom. comp. 44.

Burg *marh ‘horse’, ON marr ‘steed’, OE mearh id., OHG marahstal ‘stable’. Borrowed from Celtic, cf. Gaul acc. sg. μάρκαν (Paus.), OIr marc, W march (Mansion Rev. Instr. Belg. LVI 196). Zupitza Gutt. 209; Ipsen IF XLIV 347 (an old Wanderwort); Torp-Falk 313; Holthausen AEEW 217; Pokorny I 700; Chemodanov SGGJa I 97; Vries ANEW 380; Zalizniak I 134; Onions 555; Köbler 712; Trubachev Etim. 1988– 1990 18 (Gmc and Celt cognates to Skt márya- ‘stallion’); Kluge-Seebold 533.

ON merr ‘mare’ (partly from *marxiz), OE mere, myre id., OFris merrie id., OS meria id., OHG meriha, merha id. Derived from *marxaz. Torp-Falk 313; Vries ANEW 385; Zalizniak II 180; Onions 555; Kluge-Seebold 533.

ON mǫrr ‘suet’. Cf. NFris marig ‘tallow’. Close to Lith markā ‘steeping (of flax)’, Latv m?rks ‘moisture’, Slav *morky ‘morass’. Further related to Lat marceō ‘to wither, to droop’, Lith merkiū, meıkti ‘to soak (flax)’, mirkstū, miıkti ‘to lie in water’. Torp-Falk 313 (to Gk μίμαρκυς ‘hare-soup, jugged hare’, Skt marcáyati ‘to injure, to hurt’); Walde- Hofmann II 36–37; Pokorny I 739–740; Vries ANEW 402.

OE mersc ‘marsh’, OS mersc id., MLG mersch id. Archaic derivative connected with *mariz and identical with Slav adj. *morьskъ ‘related to sea’ (Trubachev ESSJa Prob. 62). Holthausen AEEW 220; Pokorny I 748; Onions 557.
ON mar-svín ‘a kind of whale’, OE mere-swín ‘porpoise, dolphin’, OS meri-swīn ‘dolphin’, OHG meriswīn id. Compound of *mariz and *swīnan. Carr Nom. comp. 51.

Goth mari-saiws ‘lake, sea’, ON marr id., OE mere ‘sea, lake, pool’ (masc., fem.?), OFris mar ‘pool, ditch’, OHG meri ‘sea, lake’ (neut., masc.). Identical with Lat mare id., OIr muir id., and also related to Lith pl. mãrës ‘sea, bay’, OPrus mary ‘bay’, Slav *mor’e ‘sea’ (Curtius KZ I 33–34). Schmidt Plur. 45–47; Meillet BSL XXVI/3 131; Hoops PBB XXIII 568–569; Torp-Falk 312; Trautmann BSW 169; Holthausen AEEW 220; Walde-Hofmann II 38–39; Feist 347; Schulze Kl. Schr. 117–119; Pokorny I 748; Chemodanov SSGJa I 54; Thieme KZ LXXVIII 240–241; Fraenkel 409–410; Vries ANEW 379– 380; Zalizniak II 197; Onions 570; Lehmann GED 245; Trubachev ESSJa XIX 227–230; Kluge-Seebold 549.

Goth marei ‘sea, lake’, OS merī id., OHG marī, merī id. Morphological variant of *mariz (Sievers PBB V 107). Torp-Falk 312; Feist 346; Pokorny I 748; Zalizniak II 197; Lehmann GED 245; Kluge-Seebold 549.
ON mark-land ‘forest land’, OE mearc-land ‘borderland’. Compound of *markan and *lanđan. Carr Nom. comp. 55.

ON mark ‘mark, landmark’, OFris merke id., MLG mark id., OHG marc id. (fem.). Related to Av mar 6 za- ‘boundary’, Lat margō ‘edge, border’, OIr mruig ‘district’ < *mrogi-. Zupitza Gutt. 164; Uhlenbeck PBB XXX 301; Bartholomae 1153; Torp- Falk 312–313; Holthausen AEEW 217; Walde-Hofmann II 39–40; Feist 347; Pokorny I 738; Vries ANEW 379; Zalizniak II 175; Onions 556; Van Windekens I 290 (adds Toch A märkamin märkam-pal = Skt dharma-); Lehmann GED 246; Griepentrog Wurzelnomina 443; Kluge-Seebold 540–541.
ON merki ‘mark, landmark, banner’, OE ze-mierce ‘sign, mark’, OS gi-merki id., OHG gi-marki ‘boundary’. Derived from *markan. Zupitza Gutt. 164; Holthausen AEEW 221; Vries ANEW 385.

ON merki ‘celery’, OE merece ‘marche (plant)’, OS merca ‘celery’, OHG merc id. A borrowing from NCauc m¤rqw> ‘root’, cf. *murxōn. Holthausen AEEW 220; Pokorny I 748 (to *mariz); Vries ANEW 385 (to *marxaz); Nikolaev-Starostin NCED 827.
ON merkja ‘to mark, to draw’, OHG merken ‘to define, to designate’. Derived from *markan. Torp- Falk 313; Holthausen AEEW 217; Vries ANEW 385; Kluge-Seebold 554.

Goth marka ‘region, border’, ON mǫrk ‘forest’ (i-stem and root stem), OE mearc ‘limit, term, boundary, sign’, OFris merke ‘boundary’, OS marka ‘boundary, district’, OHG marca ‘boundary’. From here – *markō ‘mark, halfpound of silver’. See *markan. Zupitza Gutt. 197; Uhlenbeck PBB XXX 301 (on semantic development); Torp-Falk 312; Holthausen AEEW 217; Feist 347; Pokorny I 738; Vries ANEW 401; Zalizniak II 209; Onions 556; Lehmann GED 246; Kluge-Seebold 540–541.

ON marka ‘to mark, to sign, to fix’, OE mearcian ‘to mark, to make a mark’, OFris merkia ‘to mark’, OS gi-markōn id., OHG marcōn ‘to define, to delimit’. Derived from *markan. Torp- Falk 313; Holthausen AEEW 217; Vries ANEW 379; Onions 556.

ON mara ‘nightmare, ogress’, OE mære ‘nightmare’, MLG mare id., OHG mara id. Related to Slav *mora id. (Berneker II 76–77). Torp-Falk 310–311; Holthausen AEEW 215; Pokorny I 736; Chemodanov SGGJa I 67; Vries ANEW 379; Onions 610; Trubachev ESSJa XIX 211–214; Kluge- Seebold 533.
ODan miør ‘mellow’, OE mearu ‘tender, soft, delicate’, OHG maro ‘ripe, tender, mellow’. Related to OIr meirb ‘soft’ < *mer⁄i-. Torp- Falk 311; Pokorny I 736.

Goth marzjan ‘to anger, to offend’, ON merja ‘to bruise, to crush’, OE mierran ‘to hinder, to obstruct, to waste’, OS merrian ‘to hinder’, OHG merren ‘to hinder’. Causative of unattested *merzanan related to Toch AB märs- ‘to forget’, Skt mṛṣyati ‘to forget’, Lith miıšti id. (Wood MLN XXI 40). Grienberger Got. 156 (to Lat mora ‘delay’); Torp-Falk 310, 313; Holthausen AEEW 222; Feist 347–348 (to Slav *mьrzъkъ ‘disgusting’); Pokorny I 737–738 (to IE *mer-s- ‘to disturb, to irritate’); Vries ANEW 385; Fraenkel 456–457; Onions 554; Lehmann GED 246; Adams TB 455–456.

ON mǫskvi ‘mesh’, OE neut. masc, max ‘mesh, net’, OS maska ‘loop, noose’, OHG masca id. Identical with Lith mãzgas ‘knot’, mezgū, mēgzti ‘to knit, to tie’ (Zubaty Afsl Ph XV 479). Cf. also Slav *mozg-arь ‘spider’ (Trubachev Slav. jaz. V 173). Torp-Falk 318; Walde KZ XXXIV 514; Trautmann BSW 172; Holthausen AEEW 215; Pokorny I 746; Chemodanov SGGJa I 71; Vries ANEW 402; Zalizniak II 219; Stang LS 35; Kluge-Seebold 543.

OE mæst ‘mast, a pole to support a sail’, MLG mast id., OHG mast id. Identical with Lat mālus id. < *mazdo-, further cf. OIr matan ‘club’. Torp-Falk 318; Holthausen AEEW 212; Schrijnen KZ XLVI 377 (Lat < Sabin); Walde- Hofmann II 19; Pokorny I 701; Onions 560.

OE mæst ‘mast, fruit of forest-trees’, OHG mast ‘mast, feed, fodder’. Identical with Gk μαζός, μαστός ‘breast’, Skt médas- ‘fat’ (Schrader KZ X 476). Further see *matjanan. Bradke KZ XXVIII 295; Torp-Falk 318; Holthausen AEEW 212; Pokorny I 694; Frisk II 183; Onions 560 (to *matiz); Kluge-Seebold 544.
ON mǫsurr ‘maple-tree’, OS masur ‘knobbed outgrowth on trees’, OHG masar id. Of unknown origin. Torp-Falk 318; Kluge-Seebold 543.
Goth mati-balgs ‘foodbag, πήρα’, OE mete-bælz ‘bag for food, wallet’ (hap. leg.). Compound of *matiz and *balziz. Carr Nom. comp. 45.
ON mat-lauss ‘without food’, OE mete-leás id. Cf. also a nominal derivative in OS metilōsi. Compound of *matiz and *lausaz. Carr Nom. comp. 60.

Goth mats ‘food’, ON matr ‘meat, food’ (traces of a-stem in pl.), OE mete ‘food’, OFris mete id., OS mat, meti id., OHG maz id. (neut.). Related to Skt mádati ‘to boil, to bubble’, Av madaite ‘to get drunk’, Gk μαδάω ‘to be moist’, Lat madeō ‘to be wet’, OIr maidim ‘to break’ (Grienberger Got. 156–157). Originally, *matiz stood for ‘soft food’ as opposed to ‘hard food’ like in Hamito-Semitic languages. Thurneysen IF XIV 132 (same as Grienberger); Meringer IF XI 211 (to *metan); Karstien Festschr. Behaghel 289–295; Bartholomae 1113; Torp- Falk 305; Holthausen AEEW 220; Walde-Hofmann II 6–8; Feist 348–349; Mayrhofer II 568; Pokorny I 695; Zalizniak II 188; Vries ANEW 380; Frisk II 157–158; Onions 565; Burrow BSOAS XLIV 85–104 (reconstructs IE *mad- ‘common meal’); Lehmann GED 247; Bammesberger Nom. 135; Kluge- Seebold 554.

Goth matjan ‘to eat, to feed’, ON metja ‘to lap (of dogs)’, OE metian ‘to supply with food’. Derived from *matiz. Torp-Falk 305; Holthausen AEEW 220; Feist 348; Pokorny I 695; Vries ANEW 386; Lehmann GED 247.

Goth maþl ‘assembly, market-place’, ON mál ‘speech, suit, case’, OE mæäel ‘assembly, council, speech’, OS mahal ‘law-court, assembly’, mathal id., OHG mahal ‘law-court’. Derivative in *-þla- < *-tlo- of *metanan (Wiedemann IF I 512: to *mōtjanan). Cf. Toch B moliye ‘dispute’ (Hilmarsson apud Adams TB 476). Meringer IF XVIII 211–215; Meyer Goth. 203 (to Skt mántra- ‘speech, sacred text, song of praise’); Bezzenberger BB IX 134 (to Gk μεταλλᾶν ‘to investigate, to ask’); Lidén PBB XV 513 (to Lat macula ‘stain, fleck’); Pedersen KZ XXXIX 411 (to Arm mat‘im ‘to approach’); Torp-Falk 306; Holthausen AEEW 213; Feist 349–350; Pokorny I 705, 746; Zalizniak II 227; Vries ANEW 376; Onions 546; Szemerényi IBK XV 207–211 (close to Lith maldā ‘prayer’, maldÿti ‘to pray’, Slav *modliti id.); Scardigli Goten 32; Klingenschmitt Verbum 71 (follows Wiedemann); Lehmann GED 247–248; Bammesberger Nom. 89 (reconstructs *m 6-tlo- or *mod-tlo-); Hilmarsson Festschr. Rix 213; Kluge-Seebold 311.

Goth maþljan ‘to speak’, ON mæla id., OE mæälan id., OFris mēlia id., OS mahalian id., OHG mahalen ‘to reach an agreement’. Derived from *maþlan. Torp-Falk 306; Holthausen AEEW 216; Feist 350; Pokorny I 746–747; Vries ANEW 399; Szemerényi IBK XV 207–211 (close to Lith maldÿti ‘to pray’, Slav *modliti id.); Martynov Jazyk 21–22 (from Slav *modliti ‘to pray’).
ON mála ‘female acquaintance’, OHG gi-mahala ‘wife’. Derived from *maþlan. Vries ANEW 377.

Goth maþa ‘worm’, OE maäa ‘maggot, worm’, OS matho id., OHG mado id. A diminutive in ON maäkr ‘maggot, worm’. Of unknown origin. Persson Ger. 14, Wurzelerw. 34 (to Lat mandō ‘to chew’); Charpentier KZ XLVI 43–44 (to Skt matkuṇa- ‘bug’); Torp-Falk 306; Holthausen AEEW 216; Feist 349; Pokorny I 700 (to Arm mat'il ‘louse’); Chemodanov SGGJa I 110; Zalizniak I 154; Onions 545; Illich-Svitych Opyt II 77 (against Pokorny: Arm mat'il is from Kartvelian, cf. Georgian mat'il ‘worm’; compares *maþōn with Slav *motylь ‘butterfly’); Lehmann GED 247; Kluge- Seebold 531 (to Slav *motylь ‘butterfly’).

Goth maudjan ‘to remind’. Close to Lith maudžiū, ma Usti ‘to desire, to long for’ and structurally identical with Slav *muditi ‘to procrastinate’. Further to Gk μῦθος ‘speech’, Slav *myslь ‘thought’. Schmidt Voc. I 178 (to OIr smuainim ‘to think’); Pedersen Kelt. Gr. I 113 (adds Lat muttiō ‘to mutter, to mumble’); Trautmann BSW 171–172; Feist 350; Pokorny I 743; Fraenkel 420; Frisk II 264–265.

ON maurr ‘ant’. With a metathesis, from an earlier *marwaz related to Av maoirī- id., OIr moirb id., Slav *morvь ~ *morvъ id. Förstemann KZ III 50; van Wijk IF XXXIII 367; Torp- Falk 302–303; Pokorny I 749; Vries ANEW 380; Lerchner 202; Lehmann GED 253–254; Trubachev ESSJa XIX 246–247.

ON mergr ‘marrow’, OE mearh id. (masc., neut.), OFris merg, merch id. (masc., neut.), OS marg id. (neut.), OHG marg id. (neut.). Related to Toch A mä≤≤unt id., Skt majján- id., Av mazga- ‘marrow, brain’, Lith smãgenës ‘brain, marrow’, Latv smadzenes id., OPrus musgeno ‘marrow’, Slav *mozgъ ‘brain, marrow’ (Walde KZ XXXIV 511–512). Zupitza Gutt. 179; Bartholomae 1159; Torp-Falk Wortschatz 318; Trautmann BSW 172; Holthausen AEEW 217; Porzig 211; Mayrhofer II 549–550; Pokorny I 750; Fraenkel 837; Vries ANEW 385; Zalizniak II 188; Onions 557; Bammesberger Nom. 50; Trubachev ESSJa XX 94–97; Kluge-Seebold 541.

Goth midjungards ‘inhabited world’ (influenced by miduma, see *međum(j)az), OE middan-zeard ‘earth, world’, OS middil-gard id. (with a dissimilation). Cf. also ON miä-garär ‘earth’. Compound of *međjōn (wk. form of *međjaz) and *zarđaz. Grienberg Got. 160 (reconstructs the first part as *međjōnaz); Torp-Falk 307; Holthausen AEEW 221; Feist 357; Carr Nom. comp. 57; Lehmann GED 253.

Goth midjis ‘middle’, ON miär id., OE midd id., OFris midde id., OS middi id., OHG mitti id. Identical with Skt mádhya- id., Av maidya- id., Gk μέσος, μέσσος id., Arm mēú ‘middle’ (noun), Lat medius ‘what is in the middle, intermediary’, Gaul medio- (in prop.), OIr mide ‘middle’, Lith m‚dis ‘wood, tree’, Slav *medja ‘boundary’. Bartholomae 1115– 1116; Torp-Falk 307; Bugge PBB XXI 428; Trautmann BSW 173; Holthausen AEEW 221; Walde-Hofmann II 57–58; Feist 356; Mayrhofer II 572; Pokorny I 706–707; Fraenkel 423–425; Vries ANEW 386; Frisk II 214–215; Onions 575; Lehmann GED 253; Trubachev ESSJa XVIII 45–46; Kluge-Seebold 563.

ON miäja ‘middle’, OE midde id., OS middia id., MHG mitte id. Derived from *međjaz. Structurally close to Slav *medja ‘boundary’. Holthausen AEEW 221; Vries ANEW 386; Trubachev ESSJa XVIII 45–46; Kluge- Seebold 563.

ON adv. meäal, miäil ‘among, between’, OE middel ‘middle’, OFris middel id., OS middil id., OHG mittil-tag ‘noon’. Related to *međjaz. May be structurally close to Lat medulla ‘marrow, pith’. Torp-Falk 307; Holthausen AEEW 221; Walde-Hofmann II 58–59; Vries ANEW 381; Kluge- Seebold 563.
