ON miäla ‘to share’, OE midlian ‘to mediate’, MLG middelen ‘to distribute’, MHG mitteln id. Derived from *međ(a)lan ~ *međilan. Holthausen AEEW 221; Vries ANEW 386.

OE medume ‘middling, moderate, common’, OFris superl. adj. medemest ‘middle’, OHG metamo ‘mediocre’. Superlative identical wih Skt madhyamá- ‘middle’, Av mad 6 maid. Bartholomae 1114–1115; Torp-Falk 307; Holthausen AEEW 218; Feist 357; Szemerényi PBB LXXXII 1–30 (-uma as elative or intensive); Trautmann Adj. 44–45; Mayrhofer II 572; Pokorny I 706–707; Vries ANEW 390; Lehmann GED 253; Bammesberger Nom. 233.

Goth miduma ‘middle’, ON mjǫäm ‘hip’, OE medema ‘step (of a loom)’ (masc.). Derived from *međum(j)az. Torp-Falk 307; Holthausen AEEW 218; Feist 357; Pokorny I 706–707; Vries ANEW 390; Lehmann GED 253; Bammesberger Nom. 233.
ON mjǫärann ‘drinking-hall’, OE medu-ærn ‘banquetinghouse’. Compound of *međuz and *rannan (derived from *rennanan). Carr Nom. comp. 55.
Icel mjaä-urt ‘meadowsweet’, OE medu-wyrt id., MLG medewurt id., OHG metu-wurz id. Compound of *mēđuz and *wurtiz. Carr Nom. comp. 50.

ON mjǫär ‘mead’, OE meodu, medu id. (masc., neut.), OFris mede id., MLG mede id., OHG metu id. Related to Toch B mit ‘honey’, Skt mádhu- ‘honey, mead’, Av madu- ‘berry wine’, Gk μέθυ ‘wine’, OIr mid ‘mead’, Lith medūs ‘honey’, Slav *medъ id. Bartholomae 1114; Pedersen Kelt. Gr. I 37; Torp-Falk 306; Schrader-Nehring I 139; Trautmann BSW 173; Holthausen AEEW 218; Mayrhofer II 570–571; Pokorny I 707; Fraenkel 425; Vries ANEW 390; Zalizniak I 152; Frisk II 191–192; Onions 564; Bammesberger Nom. 157; Trubachev ESSJa XVIII 68–72; Kluge- Seebold 555; Adams TB 461; Burlak IFTJa 252.
ON mikil-ligr ‘grand’, OE micel-líc ‘great, grand’, OHG mihhillīh id. Derived from *mekilaz. Heidermanns 405.

Goth mikils ‘large, many’, ON mikill, mykill ‘large, big’, OE micel ‘great’, OS mikil id., OHG mihhil id. See *mekuz. Structurally close to Gk μεγάλο- ‘great’, with an augmentative (Schulze Kl. Schr. 75–77) or a diminutive suffix (Sieberer Sprache II 113–114). Försterman KZ I 16; Schweizer KZ I 559; Zupitza Gutt. 198; Torp-Falk 303; Thurneysen KZ XLVIII 61 (suffix under the influence of *lītilaz ~ *lūtilaz ~ *luttilaz); Schulze Kl. Schr. 75–79; Feist 358–359; Holthausen AEEW 221; Pokorny I 708; Vries ANEW 386–387; Frisk II 189–191; Onions 574, 594; Benediktsson ANF XCIII 48–62; Lehmann GED 254–255; Heidermanns 405– 406.
Goth mikilei ‘immensity, largeness’, OS mikilī id., OHG mihhilī ‘largeness, greatness’. Derived from *mekilaz. Feist 358; Lehmann GED 254; Heidermanns 405.
Goth mikiljan ‘to make great, to praise’, OS mikilan id. Derived from *mekilaz. Feist 358; Porzig Gliederung 157; Heidermanns 405.
ON mikla ‘to make great, to magnify’, OE miclian ‘to become great, to increase’, OLG ge-mikilon ‘to praise’, OHG mihhilōn id. Derived from *mekilaz. Lehmann GED 254; Heidermanns 405.

ON mjǫk ‘much’. Related to Hitt mekki- ‘much, many’, Toch A māk, B māka ‘many, much’, Skt mahānt- ‘great, large’, Av maz °ānt- id., Gk μέγας ‘great, large’, Phryg meka-, Arm mec ‘big’, Lat magnus ‘great’, OIr maige ‘great, large’, Alb madh ‘big’ (Bopp Alb. 489, 491). Bartholomae 1157; Torp-Falk 303; Walde-Hofmann II 10–12; Schulze Kl. Schr. 75 (u-stem under the influence of *felu); Pokorny I 708–709; Vries ANEW 390; Frisk II 189–190; Onions 594; Mayrhofer II 609–610; Lehmann GED 254; Tischler HEG II 181–185; Bammesberger Nom. 263; Orel AED 240, Phrygian 444; Adams TB 446–447.
Goth mildiþa ‘compassion, sympathy’, OHG miltida ‘mercy, compassion’. Derived from *melđjaz. Feist 359; Heidermanns 406.

Goth friaþwa-milds ‘loving tenderly, affectionate’, ON mildr ‘mild, gentle, graceful’, OE milde ‘mild, gentle, benign’, OFris milde ‘benevolent’, OS mildi ‘munificent, friendly, merciful’, OHG milti ‘merciful, compassionate, mild’. Originally, an oxytonic derivative in -ti-/*tƒo- of *malanan. Lindqvist Festschr. Pipping 361; Karsten NB II 445; Weisweiler IF XLI 352–365; Torp- Falk 317; Feist 359; Holthausen AEEW 222; Pokorny I 719; Vries ANEW 387; Onions 576; Rosengren passim; Lehmann GED 255; Heidermanns 406–407; Kluge-Seebold 559.
ON mildi ‘grace, mercy’, MDu un-mildi ‘mercilessness’, OHG miltī ‘grace, mildness’ Derived from *melđjaz. Vries ANEW 387; Lehmann GED 255; Heidermanns 406.
Norw melde ‘small seed’, OE melde ‘orach (plant)’, MLG melde ‘notch-weed’, OHG melda id. Related to *malanan. Torp-Falk 317; Vries ANEW 383; Kluge-Seebold 551.

ON Mjǫllnir ‘Thor’s hammer’. Despite the differences in vocalism, close to Slav *mъldni ‘lightning’ < *m¬dh-nī (Wiedemann BB XXVIII 11). Further related to OPrus mealde id., Latv milna ‘the hammer of Perkuns’ < *m¬dhnā, W mellen ‘lightning’. ON myln ‘fire’ < *mulđn-j- is identical with Slav *mъldni. Schmidt Voc. II 131 (to Lat malleus ‘hammer’); Loewenthal ANF XXXVIII 281 (ON myln to W melyn ‘yellow’); Mikkola ANF XIX 331; Porzig Gliederung 195; Pokorny I 722; Chemodanov SSGJa I 57; Vries ANEW 390, 397; Goriacheva Etim. 1981 66–67; Trubachev ESSJa XX 220–222.

Sw mjöl-dagg ‘mildew’, OE mil-déaw, mele-déaw ‘honeydew, nectar’, OS mili-dou ‘mildew’, OHG mili-tou id. Compound of *meli- (see *meliþ) and *đawwan ~ *đawwō. Holthausen AEEW 218; Onions 576; Lehmann GED 255.
OE milisc ‘sweet, honeyed’. Derived from IE *meli- ‘honey’, see *meliþ. Torp-Falk 315; Holthausen AEEW 222; Vries ANEW 387.
ON milska ‘mead, honeyed beverage’, OHG milsca ‘wine mixed with honey’. Derived from *meliskaz. Torp-Falk 315; Vries ANEW 387; Lehmann GED 255.

Goth miliþ ‘honey’. Based on IE *meli-t id.: Hitt milit, Arm me∑r, Gk μέλι, Alb mjaltë, Lat mel, OIr mil. Torp- Falk 315; Walde-Hofmann II 61–62; Feist 359–360; Porzig Gliederung 203; Pokorny I 723–724; Frisk II 200–201; Lehmann GED 255–256; Tischler HEG II 207–210; Bammesberger Nom. 215; Orel AED 268.

OE melcan ‘to milk’, OFris pres. melka id., MDu melken id., OHG melcan id. Related to Toch AB mālk- ‘to milk’, Gk ἀμέλγω ‘to draw, to pull’, Lat mulgeō ‘to milk’, MIr bligim id., Alb mjel id., Lith mélžiu, mélžti id., Slav *mьlzǫ, *melzti id. Meyer BB VIII 190; Zupitza Gutt. 198; Pedersen Kelt. Gr. I 43; Torp-Falk 315–316; Meillet MSL XVII/1 60–64; Trautmann BSW 178; Holthausen AEEW 219; Walde- Hofmann II 121; Szemerényi KZ LXXV 170 (adds Skt mṛjáti ‘to rub, to wipe’); Pokorny I 722–723; Fraenkel 434–435; Onions 576; Frisk I 91–92; Seebold 350–351; Lehmann GED 256; Griepentrog Wurzelnomina 282–304; Trubachev ESSJa XVIII 95–96; Kluge-Seebold 552; Orel AED 270; Burlak IFTJa 251.

ON fem. mjólk ‘milch, giving milk’, OE meolc, melc id., MLG melk ‘having a calf, giving milk’, OHG melc ‘giving milk’. Derived from *melkanan. Karsten NB II 329; Torp-Falk 315– 316; Holthausen AEEW 219; Jóhannesson IEW 676–677; Vries ANEW 389; Onions 575; Seebold 350; Bammesberger Nom. 241; Heidermanns 408; Kluge-Seebold 552.

ON mjaltr ‘milch, giving milk’. An archaic formation similar to MIr mlicht ‘milk’ < *m¬·-tu- and W blith id. < *m¬·-ti. Further cf. Slav *melztva ‘milking, milk pot’ based on *mel·tu-. Pokorny I 723; Trubachev ESSJa XVIII 96.

OS melm ‘sand, dust’, OHG melm ‘dust’. An ablaut variant of *malmaz ~ *malmōn. Closely related to Lith melmuõ ‘kidney stone’ < *mel-men- (Schmidt Kritik 104) further connected with IE *mel(H)- ‘to grind’, cf. *malanan. Torp-Falk 314; Pokorny I 717; Fraenkel 431; Zalizniak II 178; Seebold 345; Lehmann GED 243; Kluge-Seebold 535.

Icel part. moltinn ‘weak’, OE meltan ‘to melt’. Related to Skt mṛdnāti ‘to press, to squeeze, to rub’, Gk μέλδομαι ‘to melt’ (Specht KZ LXIV 66). See *maltaz. Cf. also *smeltanan id. Torp-Falk 316–317; Holthausen AEEW 218; Jóhannesson IEW 672–675; Mayrhofer II 676–677; Pokorny I 718; Vries ANEW 383; Frisk II 199–200; Onions 568, 838; Seebold 351; Lehmann GED 144.

ON milti ‘spleen’, OE masc., fem. milte id., OFris fem. milte id., MLG fem. milte id., OHG milzi id. Related to *maltaz (Petersson IF XXIII 158). For the semantics cf. W lleithen ‘spleen’ ~ lleitho ‘to make wet’. Torp-Falk 317; Holthausen AEEW 222; Pokorny I 718; Vries ANEW 387; Zalizniak II 213; Onions 577; Kluge-Seebold 560.
ON mjólka ‘to milk’, OE meolcian id., OFris melka id., MLG melken id. Derived from *melukz. Torp-Falk 315; Holthausen AEEW 219; Vries ANEW 389; Pokorny I 723.

Goth miluks ‘milk’, ON mjólk id., OE meolc, meoluc, milc id., OFris melok id., OS miluk id. (i-stem), OHG miluh id. (i-stem), milih id. (< *melikiz). The origin of the second vowel is not clear (Hirt Idg. Gr. II 118; Persson Beiträge 685). The connection with *melkanan (supposed in Meillet MSL XVII 60–61) is dubious. Meillet MSL XII 219 (to Gk γάλα ‘milk’), XVII 60; Zupitza Gutt. 198; Torp-Falk 315; Hirt IF XXI 173 (< *del 6 k-); Holthausen AEEW 223; Feist 360–361; Porzig Gliederung 132; Mayer KZ LXXIII 235–237; Pokorny I 722–723; Vries ANEW 389; Zalizniak II 209; Onions 576; Szemerényi Scripta 1125 (crossing of *melk- and *mluk-); Hamp KZ XCII 30; Lehmann GED 256; Bammesberger Nom. 196–197; Kluge- Seebold 558–559.

ON mjǫl ‘flour, meal’, OE melu id., OFris dat. mele id., OS melo id., OHG melo id. Identical with Alb miell id. and Slav *melvo ‘grain to be ground’. Further connected with IE *mel- ‘to grind’, cf. *malanan. Wood MLN XV 326 (to Skt malvá- ‘thoughtless, foolish, unwise’, Gk μῶλυς ‘weak’); Torp-Falk 314; Trautmann BSW 168; Holthausen AEEW 218–219; Porzig Gliederung 211; Pokorny I 716–717; Vries ANEW 390; Zalizniak I 146; Onions 564; Seebold 345; Lehmann GED 144; Trubachev ESSJa XVIII 92–93; Bammesberger Nom. 69; Trubachev ESSJa XVIII 92–93; Kluge-Seebold 550; Orel AED 265– 266.

Goth mimz ‘meat’. Related to IE *mēmso- id. with a long vowel: Toch B pl. mīsa ‘meat, flesh’, Skt māṃsám, Arm mis, Alb mish, OPrus mensa, menso, Slav *męso (Schmidt KZ XXVI 16). Torp-Falk 310; Feist 361; Mayrhofer II 615; Pokorny I 725; Scardigli Goten 24, 257; Lehmann GED 256; Enrietti AFLLS XIX 171–173 (from Slav *męso id.); Trubachev ESSJa XIX 7–11; Bammesberger Nom. 95 (Vṛddhi with a long *-ē-); Orel AED 267; Adams TB 464; Burlak IFTJa 252.
Goth ana-minds ‘suspicion, supposition’, ON pl. minjar ‘memorials’. Related to *mana. Cf. *munđiz. Torp- Falk 308; Feist 42–43; Vries ANEW 388; Seebold 346.

ON mél ‘mouth-piece, bit’, OE midl id., OS mindil id., OHG mindil id. Closely related to OIr médal, métal ‘paunch, belly’ < *mentlā. Further related to *munþaz (Lidén Festschr. Bugge 78). Wood MLN XXI 40 (to Gk μίτος ‘thread of the warp’); Torp-Falk 310; Holthausen AEEW 224; Pokorny I 732–733; Vries ANEW 383; Bammesberger Nom. 88.
Goth ga-minþi ‘memory’, ON minni id., OHG fem. minne id. Derived from *mana. Cf. *munđiz. Torp- Falk 308; Feist 193; Vries ANEW 388; Onions 577; Seebold 346; Bammesberger Nom. 95.
ON minna ‘to remind’, OFris minnia ‘to remember’, OS minnian id. See *menþjan. Vries ANEW 388.

ON myrkr ‘dark, murky’, OE mirce id., OS mirki ‘eery, evil’. Related to Lith márgas ‘colorful’, mirgėti ‘to sparkle, to flicker’ (Wiedemann BB XIII 300) and especially close to Slav *mergъ ‘brown, with dark stripes’. Karsten NB II 424–426; Zupitza Gutt. 91; Torp-Falk 313; Feist 350–351; Holthausen AEEW 222; Pokorny I 734; Fraenkel 410; Vries ANEW 398; Onions 597; Stang LS 36–37; Lehmann GED 248 (to *murzinaz); Trubachev ESSJa XVIII 98; Heidermanns 409–410.

ON pl. mjǫt ‘measure’, OE ze-met id., OS gi-met id., OHG mez id. Derived from *metanan. Torp-Falk 305; Holthausen AEEW 220; Pokorny I 705; Vries ANEW 385; Zalizniak I 142; Beveniste 399–406; Seebold 353; Lehmann GED 250, 258.

Goth mitan ‘to measure’, ON meta ‘to tax, to value’, OE metan ‘to measure’, OFris meta id., OS pres. metan ‘to take for’, OHG mezzan ‘to measure, to compare, to evaluate’. Related to Gk μέδομαι ‘to take care of’, Lat meditor ‘to think about’, OIr midiur ‘to think’. Torp-Falk 304; Holthausen AEEW 220; Walde-Hofmann II 55– 56; Feist 363–364; Jóhannesson IEW 662–663; Pokorny I 705; Vries ANEW 385; Frisk II 191; Benveniste IEL 101–102; Seebold 352–354; Lehmann GED 257–258; Kluge-Seebold 554.

ON mjǫtuär ‘God (as a dispenser), weird, bane’, OE metod, meotod ‘fate, destiny’, OS metod id. Derived from *metōjanan. Torp-Falk 305; Holthausen AEEW 220; Kluge Nom. Stamm. 17; Seebold 353; Lehmann GED 258.

Goth miton ‘to plan, to think about, to weigh’, OE zeäanc-metian ‘to deliberate, to consider’, OHG eban-mezzōn ‘to measure’. Derived from *metanan. Torp-Falk 305; Feist 364; Pokorny I 706; Seebold 353; Benveniste IEL 101; Lehmann GED 258.

Goth miþ ‘with, among’, ON meä ‘with’, OE mid, miä id., OFris mith, mithi id., OS mid, midi id., OHG mit, miti id.. Identical with Gk μετά ‘with, among’ (Kuhn KZ I 515). Torp-Falk 306; Holthausen AEEW 221; Feist 364; Pokorny I 702; Vries ANEW 380; Frisk II 216–217; Lehmann GED 258–259; Kluge-Seebold 563.

Crim. Goth. miera ‘ant’, Swed myra id., MLG mīre id. Connected with *mauraz. Probably goes back to *mer⁄ī, cf. Av maoirī- < *mor⁄ī. Further cf. OIr moirb id., Slav *morvja id. < *mor⁄ƒā. Much Idg. Anz. IX 197–198; Torp-Falk 302–303; Feist 357; Pokorny I 253–254; Lehmann GED 253–254; Trubachev ESSJa XIX 244; Kluge-Seebold 557– 558.

OE meós ‘moss’, OHG mios id. Cf. also ON fem. mÿrr ‘moor, bog, swamp’ < *meuziz. An ablaut variant of *musōn ~ *musan. Torp-Falk 327; Holthausen AEEW 219; Pokorny I 742; Chemodanov SSGJa I 55; Vries ANEW 398; Zalizniak II 217; Onions 591; Kluge-Seebold 568.
OE máwan ‘to mow’, OFris pres. mīa id. Related to Gk ἀμάω ‘to cut, to mow’. Torp-Falk 301; Holthausen AEEW 216; Pokorny I 703; Frisk I 88; Onions 594; Seebold 347; Kluge-Seebold 532.

Goth megs ‘son-in-law’, ON mágr ‘male in-law’, OE mæz ‘relative’, OFris mēch, mēg id., OS māg id., OHG māg id. Despite phonetic difficulties, to *mazuz (Uhlenbeck PBB XXX 302). Prellwitz BB XXIV 215–216 (to Gk peri-ημεκτέω ‘to be angry, disgusted’, Lith mėgstu, mėgti ‘to like’); Wiedemann BB XXVIII 65 (same as Prellwitz, cf. for the semantic development Fr beau-fils ‘sonin-law’); Wood MP XI 316 (to *maþlan); Zupitza Gutt. 65; Torp-Falk 304; Holthausen AEEW 210; Feist 352; Pokorny I 707 (to Skt maháyati ‘to animate, to strengthen, to gladden’); Vries ANEW 375; Zalizniak I 134; Fraenkel 425– 426, Lexis II 168–170; Szemerényi Kinship 190–192; Lehmann Zf Mund XXXV 15– 17, GED 249–250; Kluge-Seebold 531.
ON mægä ‘affinity by marriage’, OE mægä ‘family, race’. Derived from *mēzaz. Holthausen AEEW 210; Vries ANEW 398.

OSwed valmoghe ‘poppy’ (< *walxa-mazōn), OS māho id., māho-sāmo id., OHG mago id. Somehow related with (or borrowed from the same source as) Gk μήκων id., Slav *makъ id. Fick I 707 (to Gk μάσσω ‘to knead’); Zupitza Gutt. 135; Pedersen Kelt. Gr. I 159 (adds OIr meccun ‘carrot’); Torp-Falk 303; Trautmann BSW 166; Frisk II 225; Gindin Etim. 69–70 (together with Gk μήκων id. from Thrac *mäkh-); Trubachev ESSJa XVII 149–151; Kluge-Seebold 565.

Goth meki ‘sword’, ON mækir id., OE méce id., OS māki id. Borrowed from NCauc *m¤qc V ‘chisel, hoe, knife’: Avar-And *maq cala, Tsez *miqc, Lezghi *m Vqc(uj). Froehde KZ XIV 454 (to Lat mactō ‘to sacrifice, to kill’); Torp-Falk 303 (to OIr machtaim ‘to slaughter’); Loewenthal ANF XXXIII 113 (to Lith smaĩgas ‘cane, pole’); Holthausen AEEW 210; Feist 352–353; Shevelov Prehist. 169 (from Georgian máxva ‘sharp; knife’, cf. also Avar max: ‘iron’); Szemerényi KZ XCIII 110–118 (from Iran *madyaka ‘attached to the waist’); Vernadsky Saeculum II 368 (Sarmathian loanword); Lehmann GED 250; Menges Slovo 199 (from North Caucasian: Arch, Did ma‘a ‘sword’); Fermeglia ASGM XXI 123–124 (< *mēgƒo-, to IE *mē-); Odintsov Etim. 1982 110 (to Bret mecet ‘to shine’); Nikolayev- Starostin NCED 827.

Goth mel ‘hour, time’, ON mál ‘time, term, mealtime’, OE mæl ‘measure, fixed time’ (neut., masc.?), OFris mēl, māl ‘time, mealtime’, MLG māl ‘period of time’, MHG māl ‘time, moment’. Equivalent of ELith tuo-m El (Fraenkel KZ XLI 417). Derived from IE *mē- ‘to measure’: Toch A me-, B mai-, Skt mímāti, Alb mas id. < PAlb *matja, Lat mētior and the like (Bugge PBB XXIV 434). Grienberger Got. 158 (identical with *mēlan II); Torp- Falk 301; Holthausen AEEW 211; Walde-Hofmann II 81–82; Feist 353; Trier Lehm 72; Mayrhofer II 638; Pokorny I 703–704; Fraenkel 430; Vries ANEW 376; Zalizniak I 142; Onions 565; Stang LS 35–36; Bammesberger Nom. 76; Kluge-Seebold 534– 535; Orel AED 246–247.

Goth mel ‘scripture, writing’, ON mál ‘ornament (on a sword)’, OE mæl ‘mark, sign’ (neut., masc.?), OFris mēl ‘sign’, OS hōbid-māl ‘head portrait’, OHG māl ‘spot, point’. Historically identical with *mēlan I (Loewe KZ XLVII 132; Grienberger Got. 158). Fick I 507 (to Lat macula ‘spot’); Detters ZDADL XLII 57 (to *maþlan); Torp-Falk 315; Hirt Idg. Gr. V 101 (follows Fick and reconstructs *mētló-); Holthausen AEEW 211; Feist 353 (to Skt málam ‘dirt’, Gk μέλας ‘black’, Lith mėlas ‘blue’); Trier Lehm 71; Pokorny I 703–704, 720–721; Vries ANEW 376–377; Zalizniak I 142; Lehmann GED 250–251; Kluge-Seebold 535; Adams TB 469–470 (to Toch A malañ, B meli ‘nose’ < *mēlo-).

OFrank gloss māla ‘cow’, Du maal ‘young cow’. Identical with Gk μῆλον ‘small animals, sheep’, further cf. OIr míl ‘animal’, Arm mal ‘sheep, ram’ (Scheftelowitz BB XXIX 46), Slav *mîlъ ‘small’. See *smalaz. Fick I 519 (to *mē- ‘to bleat’); Pokorny I 724; Dybo VSJa 26; Vendryes M-51; Frisk II 226–227; Chantraine 694–695; Lehmann GED 316; Trubachev ESSJa XVIII 168; Heidermanns 517.

Goth meljan ‘to write’, late ON mæla ‘to paint, to portray’, mála id., OE ze-mælan ‘to spot, to blemish’, OFris mēlia ‘to paint’, OS mālōn ‘to paint, to draw’, OHG mālōn, mālēn id. Derived from *mēlan II. Torp- Falk 315; Holthausen AEEW 211; Feist 353–354; Vries ANEW 399; Lehmann GED 251; Kluge-Seebold 535.
ON mælir ‘measure’, OE mæle, méle ‘cup, bowl’. Derived from *mēlan I. Torp-Falk 301; Holthausen AEEW 211; Vries ANEW 399; Lehmann GED 250.

Goth mena ‘moon’, ON máni id., OE móna id., OFris mōna id., OS māno id., OHG māno id. Derivationally similar to OPrus menins id. A secondary form based on *mēnōþz. Schmidt KZ XXVI 340, 345–346; Torp-Falk 309; Specht KZ LXVI 53; Holthausen AEEW 225; Feist 354; Pokorny I 731–732; Vries ANEW 378; Zalizniak I 154; Onions 588; Lehmann GED 251; Bammesberger Nom. 186.

Goth menoþs ‘month’, ON mánaär, mánuär id., OE mónaä ‘month’, OFris mōnath ‘moon’, OS mānuth id., OHG mānōd id. Related to Toch A mañ id., B meñe id., Skt mās- id., Av māh- id., Arm amis id., Gk Dor μής id., Alb muaj ~ muej id. < PAlb *māsnja, Lat mēnsis id., OIr mí, Lith mėnuo, Slav *mîs=cь id. Schmidt KZ XXVI 340–346, Plur. 193; Bartholomae 1170–1171; Torp-Falk 309; Trautmann BSW 179–181; Holthausen AEEW 225; Walde-Hofmann II 71–72; Feist 354; Mayrhofer II 631–632; Pokorny I 731–732; Fraenkel 438–439; Vries ANEW 378; Zalizniak II 209; Frisk II 227–228; Onions 587; Beekes JIES X 55; Lehmann GED 251; Orel AED 276; Adams TB 468; Burlak IFTJa 251.

Goth meriþa ‘repute, news’, ON mærä ‘praise, song of praise’, OE mæräu, mærä ‘greatness, honor, glory’, OS māritha ‘glory, fame’, OHG mārida id. Derived from *mērjaz. Torp-Falk 300; Holthausen AEEW 212; Feist 355; Vries ANEW 399; Lehmann GED 252; Bammesberger Nom. 117; Heidermanns 408–409.
Goth waila-merei ‘good reputation’, OHG mārī ‘fame’. Derived from *mērjaz. Feist 543–544; Heidermanns 408.

Goth merjan ‘to proclaim’, ON mæra ‘to praise’, OE mæran ‘to make known, to celebrate’, OS mārian ‘to announce, to praise’, OHG mēren id. Derived from *mērjaz. Torp-Falk 300; Holthausen AEEW 211; Feist 355; Vries ANEW 399; Lehmann GED 252; Heidermanns 409; Kluge-Seebold 539.

Goth waila-mereis ‘wellreputed, laudable’, ON mærr ‘famous, glorious, great’, OE mære ‘great, excellent, distinguished’, OS māri ‘brilliant, famous’, OHG māri ‘famous’. Related to OIr már ‘large’, Gk ἐγχεσί-mvrow ‘mighty with the spear’ < *mōros. Fick KZ II 382–383 (to Lat merus ‘pure, unmixed’); Karsten NB II 365–367; Torp-Falk 300; Feist 355; Holthausen AEEW 212; Mittner Wurd 139–141 (contamination of *mē- ‘large’ and *mer- ‘to shine’); Lindow CIH 129–130 (follows Mittner); Pokorny I 704; Vries ANEW 399–400; Frisk I 440; Lehmann GED 252; Heidermanns 408–409; Kluge-Seebold 539–540.

Goth us-met ‘manner of life, behavior, conduct’, ON mát ‘moderation’, OHG māz ‘measure’. Derived from *metanan. Sturtevant JEGP XXXVI 180– 181 (on the meaning in Gothic); Torp- Falk 305; Feist 534; Pokorny I 706; Seebold 353; Lehmann GED 383.

ON mætr ‘valuable, excellent’, OE mæte ‘moderate, mean, small, poor’, OHG un-māzi ‘enormous’. Derived from *metanan. Karsten NB I 100; Torp- Falk 305; Holthausen AEEW 212; Seebold 353; Bammesberger Nom. 261; Matzel Festschr. Schröder 102, KZ CV 113; Heidermanns 410–411; Kluge- Seebold 544.

OE mæä ‘mowing’, OFris de-meth ‘day’s worth of mowing’, MHG māt ‘mowing’. Close to Gk ἄμητος ‘reaping, harvesting’. Derived from *mēanan. Kluge Glotta III 280 (against comparison with Gk); Torp-Falk 301; Benveniste Origines 157; Frisk I 88; Kluge-Seebold 532.
OE mæä ‘measure’. Identical with Skt māti- id., Gk μῆτις id., Alb mot ‘time’. Derived from *mē- ‘to measure’. Torp-Falk 301; Benveniste Nom. 77; Pokorny I 703–704; Frisk II 232–233; Orel AED 274–275.

ON mistr ‘mist’, OE mist ‘mist, dimness’, MLG mist id. Related to Skt meghá- ‘cloud’, Av maēga- id., Arm mēg ‘fog’, Gk ὀμίχλη ‘cloud’, Alb mjegull ‘cloud’, Lith miglā ‘fog’, Slav *mьgla ‘fog, darkness’ (Svedrup IF XXXV 154). Bartholomae 1104–1105; Torp-Falk 320; Holthausen AEEW 223; Benveniste Nom. 107; Mayrhofer II 680; Pokorny I 712; Fraenkel 451–452; Vries ANEW 389; Frisk II 387; Onions 581; Orel AED 269; Trubachev ESSJa XXI 93–94.
ON mistil-teinn ‘mistletoe’, OE mistel-tán id. Compound of *mixstilaz and *tainaz. Torp-Falk 320; Holthausen AEEW 223; Carr Nom. comp. 55; Vries ANEW 389; Onions 582.

ON mistil-teinn ‘mistletoe’, OE mistel ‘basil, mistletoe’, Du mistel ‘mistletoe’, OHG mistil id. Derived from *mixstuz. The mistletoe is the source of the bird glue. Torp-Falk 320; Holthausen AEEW 223; Otrébski St. IE 203 (from IE *em-istlo- ‘taking, seizing’); Pokorny I 713; Vries ANEW 389; Zalizniak I 134; Onions 582; Trubachev ESSJa VI 27 (same as Otrébski); Balles KZ CXII 137–142 (from IE *mizdo- similar to *ni-zdo-); Kluge-Seebold 562–563.
Goth maihstus ‘dung’, OS mist id., OHG mist id. Derived from *mīzanan. Torp-Falk 320; Kluge Nom. Stamm. 70; Feist 340; Pokorny I 713; Seebold 348; Lehmann GED 241; Kluge-Seebold 562.

OE ze-mimor ‘existing in the memory, known’. Identical with Av mimara- ‘mindful’, Lat memor id. Believed to be a reduplication of *(s)mer- ‘to keep in mind’ (Franck KZ XXXVII 128–130; Uhlenbeck PBB XVIII 75). Bartholomae 1186; Walde-Hofmann II 67–68; Pokorny I 969; Vries ANEW 387; Bammesberger MSS XXVIII 5–8.

Goth mins ‘less’, ON minnr, miär ‘less’, OE min ‘small’, OFris min ‘less’, LG min id., OHG min id. Probably with *-nn- < *-nw- (Osthoff Morph. Unt. VI 231). Related to Skt mināti ‘to depreciate’, Gk μινύθω ‘to diminish, to destroy’, Lat minuō ‘to diminish, to become smaller’, minor ‘smaller’, OIr menb ‘small, little’, Slav *mьnьjь ‘smaller’. Further connected with mīyate ‘to grow smaller’, Gk μείων ‘smaller’ < IE *mei-. Torp-Falk 310, 319; Walde- Hofmann II 92–93; Feist 362; Pokorny I 711; Vries ANEW 388; Frisk II 242; Vendryes M-37; Lehmann GED 256– 257; Trubachev ESSJa XXI 119–122.

OE miscian ‘to mix’, MLG mischen id., OHG misken id. From *mix-sk-. Derivationally close to Lat misceō id., OIr mescaim id. Cf. also Toch B mäsk- ‘to exchange’ usually etymologized from *mei- ‘to exchange’ (Melchert KZ XCI 107). Further related to Skt mimikṣati ‘to mix’, Gk μείγνυμι, μίγνυμι id., Lith mi‚šti id., Slav *mîsiti ‘to mix, to knead’. Pedersen IF V 58–59, Kelt. Gr. I 76; Torp-Falk 321; Walde-Hofmann II 95–96; Pokorny I 714; Fraenkel 450– 451; Frisk II 192–193; Adams TB 459.
Goth missa-deþs ‘misdeed, transgression’, OE mis-dæd id., OS mis-dād id., OHG missi-tāt id. Compound of *missaz and *đēđiz. Feist 362; Lehmann GED 257.
ON miss-ari, miss-eri ‘season, half-year’, OE miss-ere id. Compound of *missaz and *jēran. Carr Nom. comp. 58.

Goth missa-leiks ‘diverse, various’, ON mis-líkr id., OE mislíc ‘wanting in likeness, unlike, various’, OFris mis-līk ‘various’, OS mis-līk id., OHG mis-līh, missi-līh id. Compound of *missaz and līkaz. Torp-Falk 321; Lehmann GED 142, 257; Kluge-Seebold 562.

Goth adv. misso ‘each other, mutually’, ON ÿ-miss ‘different, various’, OE mis-byrd ‘abortion’, OFris mis-dēde id., OS adv. mis-līko ‘otherwise, in a different way’, OHG missi ‘different, various’ (hap. leg.). Adjective in *-to- based on IE *meit-: Skt méthati ‘to be angry, to be hostile’, Lat mūtō ‘to change’. See *mīþanan. Kögel PBB VII 173–175 (to Lat mittere ‘to let go, to send’); Karsten NB II 310–312; Torp-Falk 321; Walde- Hofmann II 137–138; Feist 362–363; Mayrhofer II 682–683; Pokorny I 715; Vries ANEW 388; Seebold 349; Lehmann GED 257; Heidermanns 413; Kluge-Seebold 562.

ON missa ‘to miss, to lose’, OE missan ‘to miss’, OFris missa id., MLG missen id., OHG missen id. Based on *missaz. Torp-Falk 321; Holthausen AEEW 223; Onions 581; Lehmann GED 257; Heidermanns 413; Kluge-Seebold 562.
ON missa ‘loss’, OE neut. miss id., MLG misse id., MHG misse id. Related to *missaz. Torp-Falk 321; Holthausen AEEW 223; Vries ANEW 388; Onions 581.

Goth mizdo ‘reward, pay’, OE meord id., méd ‘meed, reward’, OFris mēde ‘rent’, OS mēda ‘payment’, OHG mieta ‘price’. In WGmc a secondary variant is attested. Related to Skt neut. mīḍhá- ‘strife, contest, prize, reward’, Av mižda- ‘reward, gift’, Gk μισθός ‘recompense’, Slav *mьzda ‘reward’. Walde KZ XXXIV 496; Bartholomae 1187–1188; Hirt Urgerm. I 33 (-ē2- compensates the loss of *-z-); Meillet Etudes 248, MSL XXI/3 111; Torp-Falk 322; Trautmann BSW 188; Holthausen AEEW 219; Feist 364–365; Pokorny I 746; Zalizniak II 226; Mayrhofer II 642–643; Frisk II 244; Onions 567; Scardigli Goten 129; Benveniste IEL 131–138; Connolly PBB CI 1–29; Lehmann GED 259; Bammesberger Nom. 179; Trubachev ESSJa XXI 176–178; Kluge-Seebold 558.

ON míga ‘to urinate’, OE mízan id., MLG mīgen id. Tokh mi≤o ‘urine’, Skt méhati ‘to urinate’, Av maēzaiti id., Arm mizem id., Lat meiō id. < *me·hƒō, Lith mêžū, m‹žti id., Slav *mižati id. Zupitza Gutt. 205; Bartholomae 1108; Torp-Falk 319–320; Holthausen AEEW 222; Walde-Hofmann II 60–61; Jóhannesson IEW 658; Mayrhofer II 690; Pokorny I 713; Fraenkel 461–462; Vries ANEW 386; Onions 582; Seebold 347–348; Lehmann GED 241; Trubachev ESSJa XIX 63; Kluge-Seebold 562; Adams TB 463–464.

Goth meins ‘my’, ON mínn id., OE mín id., OFris mīn id., OS mīn id., OHG mīn id. Adjective in *-no- derived from *mei, a secondary stem or locative of IE *me, 1 sg. personal pronoun (Brugmann Grundriß II/1 274, II/2 405). Torp-Falk 300; Holthausen AEEW 223; Feist 352; Pokorny I 702; Vries ANEW 388; Onions 577; Schmidt Pron. 88–92; Lehmann GED 250; Kluge- Seebold 550.

Norw dial. mīt ‘mite’, OE mite id., MLG mīte id., OHG mīza ‘gnat’. Related to Skt mināti ‘to depreciate’, Gk μινύθω id., Lat minor ‘small’. See *minniz ~ *minnaz. Torp- Falk 320 (to *maitanan); Holthausen AEEW 224; Walde-Hofmann II 92–93; Frisk II 242; Onions 582.

OE míäan ‘to conceal’, OFris pres. for-mītha ‘to avoid’, OS mīthan id., OHG mīdan id. Related to Hitt mutāi- ‘to remove’, Skt méthati ‘to be angry, to be hostile’, Lat mūtō ‘to change’, Latv mitêt ‘to change’, Slav *mitî ‘alternately’. Kögel PBB VII 173 (to Lat mittō ‘to cause to go, to send’); Noreen Abriß 190 (same as Kögel); Torp-Falk 320; Holthausen AEEW 224; Lane Language XIV 24 (adds Toch A met-, B mit- ‘to go, to set out’); Walde-Hofmann II 137–138; Mayrhofer II 682–683; Pokorny I 710, 715; Seebold 348–350; Lehmann GED 241; Tischler HEG II 235–236; Trubachev ESSJa XIX 59–60; Kluge- Seebold 550.
Goth modags ‘angry’, ON móäugr ‘moody, gloomy, fierce’, OE módiz ‘highspirited, noble-minded’, OS mōdag, mōdig ‘excited’. Cf. OHG muotigī ‘animosity’. Derived from *mōđaz. Feist 365; Lehmann GED 259.
ON móä-sefi‘mind’, OE mód-sefa ‘mind, spirit, soul’, OS mōdsebo ‘mind’. Compound of *mōđaz and *safjōn. Carr Nom. comp. 51.

Goth moþs ‘anger’ (gen. modis), ON móär ‘wrath’, OE mód ‘mind, spirit, courage’ (neut.), OFris mōd id., OS mōd id. (masc., neut.), OHG muot ‘soul, spirit, courage’ (masc., neut.). Probably continues *mō-tó-, cf. Lat mōtus ‘moved’ < *mo⁄itos and further related to Lat moueō ‘to move’, Lith máuju, máuti ‘to turn down, to touch, to graze’. Fick KZ XXII 377 (to Slav *sъmîti ‘to dare’, Gk μαίομαι ‘to seek’); Prellwitz BB XXVI 310; Torp-Falk 322 (to *mōjanan); Schmidt KZ XXXVII 45 (from IE *mē- ‘to be spiritually aroused’); Meyer Bed. Passim; Holthausen AEEW 224; Walde-Hofmann II 116–117; Feist 365–366; Pokorny I 705 (to Gk μαίομαι); Fraenkel 421; Vries ANEW 391; Zalizniak II 170; Onions 588; Ernout-Meillet 416 (to Lat mōs ‘will, custom’); Chantraine 659, 697 (to Gk μήνις ‘anger’); Lehmann GED 259; Kluge- Seebold 577.

ON móäir ‘mother’, OE módor id., OFris mōdar id., OS mōdar id., OHG muoter id. Related to Toch A mācar, B mācer id., Skt mātár- id., Av mātarid., Gk μήτηρ id., Alb motër ‘sister’, Lat māter ‘mother’, OIr máthir id., Lith mótë id., Slav *mati id. Bartholomae 1167; Torp-Falk 323; Holthausen AEEW 224; Walde-Hofmann II 49–50; Pokorny I 700–701; Mayrhofer II 619; Fraenkel 465–466; Vries ANEW 391; Zalizniak I 157; Frisk II 232; Onions 592; Benveniste 171; Bammesberger Nom. 208; Trubachev ESSJa XVII 254–259; Kluge-Seebold 577; Orel AED 275; Adams TB 447.
ON móäerni ‘mother’s side of lineage, mother’. Derived from *mōđēr and structurally close to Lat māternus ‘of mother’. Walde-Hofmann II 49; Vries ANEW 391.
ON móäa ‘large river’, E dial. mood ‘slime, mucus’, LG mōt, mōd ‘beer foam’. Semantically distant from but historically and formally identical with *mōđaz. Vries ANEW 391.

Goth past part. afmauidai ‘weary’, MLG mōien ‘to toil, to endeavor’, OHG muoen ‘to become tired, to labor’. Identical with Slav *majati ‘to wear out, to labor, to be slow’ (Schmidt KZ XXVI 5). Persson apud Feist (to Gk μαῦρος ‘black’); Bugge PBB XIII 316–317 (to Gk κάμνω ‘to tire’); Grienberger Got. 8 (to Lat moueō ‘to move’); Berneker II 7; Torp-Falk 322; Trautmann BSW 188; Feist 9; Pokorny I 746; Stang LS 35; Lehmann GED 6; Trubachev ESSJa XVII 132–134; Kluge- Seebold 573.
ON móna ‘mammy (of a baby)’, ME móne ‘mother’, MLG mōne id. A word of child language. Vries ANEW 392.

ON mœrr ‘land, border-land, sea’, OE mór ‘moor’, OFris mōr id., OS mōr id., OHG muor ‘marsh, lake’. Secondary form based on *mariz. Holthausen AEEW 226; Pokorny I 748; Vries ANEW 400; Chemodanov SSGJa I 54; Zalizniak II 170; Onions 588; Darms Vṛddhi 158–166; Lehmann GED 245; Kluge-Seebold 568.

ODan mōs ‘porridge’, OE mós ‘food, nourishment’, OFris mōs ‘meal’, OHG muos id. Probably, from *mōđ-s-an related to *mastaz II, *matiz (Uhlenbeck PBB XXX 302). Torp- Falk 305; Holthausen AEEW 226; Darms Vṛddhi 219–231; Lehmann GED 247; Kluge-Seebold 576.

Goth ga-mot ‘to find room’, OE mót ‘must’, OFris mōt id., OS mōt id., OHG muoz ‘can, must’. Derived from *metanan. Delbrück Synt. III 331 (to IE *mē- ‘to measure’); Torp-Falk 323; Feist 193; Holthausen AEEW 226; Pokorny I 705–706; Onions 598; Seebold 354; Beekes KZ LXXXVII 86–98 (on *mēd-: *med-); Lehmann GED 145; Kluge- Seebold 576–577.

ON mót ‘meeting’, ME mót, zemōt id., OS mōt id. (fem.), MHG muoz ‘single combat (of knights)’ (fem.). Derived from *metanan (Wood MP XI 321). Meringer IF XVIII 212–213; Uhlenbeck TNTL XXV 269–270; Torp- Falk 322–323; Pedersen KZ XXXIX 411; Pokorny I 705–706, 746 (to Arm mat‘im ‘to approach’); Vries ANEW 393–394; Onions 588.
ON mót ‘stamp, mark’, EFris mōt ‘spot’, Du moet ‘trace, spot’. Derived from *metanan. Torp-Falk 323; Vries ANEW 393.

Goth ga-motjan ‘to meet’, ON mœta id., OE métan id., OFris mēta id., OS mōtian id. Derived from *mōtan I. Meringer IF XVIII 212–213 (derived from *mōtō); Holthausen AEEW 225; Feist 193; Pokorny I 746; Vries ANEW 400; Onions 567; Lehmann GED 145.

Goth mota ‘tax’, OSwed mōt ‘measure’, OE mót ‘toll, tax’, MHG muoze ‘miller’s pay’. Derived from *mōtan II (Meringer IF XVIII 211–215). Jellinek ZDADL LXVI 129 (from MLat mūta ‘toll’); Much DLZeit. XLI 554 (Lat mūta from Gmc.); Holthausen AEEW 226; Feist 365; Stender-Petersen 322–325 (same as Meringer); Feist 365; Corazza Lat. 64–66; Lehmann GED 259; Kluge- Seebold 548.

ON móär ‘moody, weary’, OE méäe ‘weary, troubled, sad’, OS mōthi ‘moody, weary’, OHG muodi id. Derived from *mōjanan. See *mōđaz. Karsten NB II 392–394; Torp-Falk 322; Feist 9; Holthausen AEEW 225; Pokorny I 746; Vries ANEW 391; Meid Praet. 87; Lehmann GED 6; Matzel KZ CV 132; Heidermanns 414; Kluge-Seebold 572.
ON mœäi ‘weariness, exhaustion’, OHG muodī id. Derived from *mōþaz. Heidermanns 414.
ON mœäa ‘to plague, to exhaust’, OHG muodēn ‘to tire’. Derived from *mōþaz. Torp-Falk 322; Vries ANEW 400.
OFris mōther ‘breast-bandage’, OS mōder id., OHG muodar ‘belly’. Identical with Gk μήτρα ‘womb’. Derived from *mōđēr. Torp-Falk 323; Pokorny I 701; Frisk II 232.
ON muäla ‘to maunder, to munch’, OHG mutilōn ‘to murmur’. Of imitative origin. Torp-Falk 326 (to Lat muttiō ‘to mutter, to mumble’); Vries ANEW 394; Lehmann GED 112.

OSwed mugga ‘mosquito’, OE mycze ‘midge’, OS muggia id., OHG mugga id. Distantly related to *mūsō ~ *mūsīn. Torp-Falk 327 (reconstruct *muwjō); Holthausen AEEW 228; Pokorny I 752; Zalizniak II 226; Onions 575; Kluge-Seebold 572.
ON mold-vegr ‘earthly path’, OE mold-wez ‘way upon earth, earth’. Compound of *mulđō(n) and *wezaz. Carr Nom. comp. 55.
ON mylda ‘to cover with mould’, OE be-myldan id., OHG multen ‘to dig land’. Derived from *mulđō(n). Holthausen AEEW 228; Vries ANEW 397.

Goth mulda ‘dust’, ON mold ‘mould, earth’ (pl. reflects *mulđiz), OE molde id., OFris molde id., OHG molta ‘earth, dust’. Close to OPrus piva-maltan ‘malt’, Lith mīltai ‘flour’. Participle *m¬tóof *mel- ‘to grind’, see *malanan (Solmsen KZ XXXVII 587–589). Försterman KZ I 4; Torp-Falk 314; Trautmann BSW 168; Holthausen AEEW 225; Feist 366; Pokorny I 717; Fraenkel 403–404; Vries ANEW 392; Zalizniak II 197; Onions 593; Seebold 345; Lehmann GED 260.

OE molda, molde ‘top of the head’ (masc., fem.), MDu moude id. Derivationally close to Skt mūrdhán- ‘top, summit, head’, Av ka-m 6 r 6 da- ‘head (of a dev)’, with a pejorative ka-. Bartholomae 440; Holthausen AEEW 225; Mayrhofer II 666; Pokorny I 725; Onions 593.
OSwed molda ‘Chenopodium’, MHG multe ‘notch-weed’. See *melđōn. Torp-Falk 317.
ON mylja ‘to shiver, to crush’, MLG mullen to crush’, OHG mullen ‘to rub’. Related to *malanan. Torp-Falk 315; Vries ANEW 397; Seebold 345; Lehmann GED 260.
ON á-munr ‘eager’, OE ze-mun ‘mindful’. Derived from *mana. Holthausen AEEW 227; Vries ANEW 9; Matzel Festschr. Schröder 99, KZ CV 106; Heidermanns 415.
ON mundr ‘bridal present’, OFris mund ‘guardianship’. Morphological variant of *munđō. Holthausen AEEW 227; Vries ANEW 395; Kluge-Seebold 574.
Goth prop. Mundila, ON prop. Mundill, OFris mundele ‘minor, ward’ (fem.), MHG mündel ‘minor, ward’ (neut.). Derived from *munđō. Vries ANEW 395; Kluge-Seebold 574.

Goth ga-munds ‘remembrance’, ON mynd ‘shape, form, image’, OE ze-mynd ‘mind, memory, remembrance’, OHG gi-munt ‘remembrance’. Continues IE *m–tis: Skt matí- ‘thought, design, devotion’, Av anu-maiti- ‘pondering’, Lith mintīs id., Slav *pa-mêtь ‘memory’, based on *men- ‘to think’. See *mana. Bartholomae 128; Torp-Falk 308; Trautmann BSW 180–182; Holthausen AEEW 228; Feist 194; Mayrhofer II 563–564; Pokorny I 726–728; Fraenkel 455–456; Vries ANEW 398; Onions 577; Seebold 346; Lehmann GED 145; Bammesberger Nom. 141.

ON mund ‘hand’, OE mund id., OFris masc. mund ‘protection, guarding’, OS mund ‘hand’, OHG munt ‘hand, palm as a length measure’. Continues *m–-tā (Danielsson AISt. III 189–190) probably connected with Gk μάρη ‘hand’, Lat manus id., a heteroclytic stem, cf. *manwuz. Torp-Falk 326; Holthausen AEEW 227; Walde-Hofmann II 34–35; Krahe Su V 75; Pokorny I 740–741; Chemodanov SGGJa I 89; Vries ANEW 395; Zalizniak II 194; Frisk II 175; Lehmann GED 244–245; Kluge-Seebold 574.

Goth mundon ‘to pay attention, to observe’, ON munda ‘to point, to aim’. Related to Gk μανθάνω, aor. μαθεῖν ‘to learn’. Torp-Falk 308; Feist 367; Sturtevant MP XXVI 472 (from *mana); Pokorny I 730; Vries ANEW 395; Frisk II 170–171; Lehmann GED 261.

OHG muntar ‘lively, fresh’. Connected with *munđōjanan. Structurally close to Lith mandrūs ‘lively’, Slav *mǫdrъ ‘wise’. Torp-Falk 308; Feist 367–368; Wissmann NP 78; Fraenkel 405–406; Lehmann Language XVIII 128, GED 261; Trubachev ESSJa XX 130–133; Heidermanns 416; Kluge- Seebold 575.
Goth mundrei ‘aim, goal’, OHG muntrī ‘eagerness, diligence’. Derived from *munđraz. Feist 367–368; Lehmann GED 261; Heidermanns 416.
Goth munan ‘to intend, to be about to’, OHG fir-monen ‘to value little’. Derived from *muniz. Feist 367; Seebold 346.

Goth muns ‘thought, intention’, ON munr ‘mind, longing, delight’, OE myne ‘mind, purpose, desire’. Related to *mana. Torp-Falk 308; Holthausen AEEW 228; Feist 368; Vries ANEW 396; Onions 577; Seebold 346; Bammesberger Nom. 132–133.

Goth munþs ‘mouth’, ON munnr, muär id., OE múä id., OFris mund, mond id., OS mūth id., OHG mund id. Etymologically close to Lat mentum ‘chin’, W mant ‘jaw, mouth’ < *m–to-, Alb mat ‘bank, shore’ < *m–to- (Vasmer AW 39–40). Hirt PBB XXII 228 (to Gk στόμα ‘mouth’, from *st(o)ment-); Detter ZDADL XLII 56 (to Skt múkha- ‘mouth’); Kretschmer Glotta XV 195–196 (from *mu-nt- ‘making a mu-sound’ and identical with Lat mundus ‘underground pit’ < *‘mouth of the underworld’); Torp- Falk 310; Holthausen AEEW 227–228; Walde-Hofmann II 72–73; Feist 368; Porzig Gliederung 126, 133; Pokorny I 726, 732–733; Chemodanov SGGJa I 102; Vries ANEW 396; Zalizniak I 134; Onions 594; Lehmann GED 261 (to Gk μασάομαι ‘to chew’); Bammesberger Nom. 77–78; Kluge-Seebold 574; Orel AED 247.
ON munni ‘mouth, opening’, OE múäa ‘mouth (of the river), opening, door’, OFris mūtha id., MLG mūde id. Derived from *munþaz. Holthausen AEEW 228; Vries ANEW 396.
ON morgin-gjǫf ‘bridal gift’, OE morzen-zifu id., MLG morgengave id., OHG morgan-geba id. Compound of *murzinaz and *zebō. Carr Nom. comp. 51; Kluge-Seebold 570.

Goth maurgins ‘morning’, ON morginn, myrginn id., OE morzen id., OFris morn id., OS morgan id., OHG morgan id. Cf. also OE merizen id., MDutch margen, mergen id. < *marzinaz. Identical with Slav *mьr‘ьnъ ‘dark’. Further related to OIr mrecht ‘multicolored’, Lith mérkiu, mérkti ‘to blink’, Slav *mьrknǫti ‘to become dark’. Torp-Falk 326; Trautmann BSW 182–184; Holthausen AEEW 226; Feist 350–351; Pokorny I 733–734; Vries ANEW 393; Fraenkel 441; Zalizniak I 134; Onions 590; Stang LS 36–37; Lehmann GED 248; Trubachev ESSJa XXI 130, 133–135; Kluge-Seebold 569–570.

Goth ga-maurgjan ‘to shorten’, OE mirzan ‘to be happy, to rejoice’. Derived from *murzuz. Torp- Falk 311; Holthausen AEEW 229; Feist 193; Lehmann GED 145; Heidermanns 416; Adams TB 454–455 (adds Toch B märkwace ‘upper leg, thigh’).

OE myr(i)ze ‘pleasant, delightful, sweet’, OHG murg-fāri ‘transitory’, murg ‘short’. Closely related to Av m 6 r 6 zu- ‘short (?)’, Gk βραχύς id., Lat brevis id. (Saussure Recueil 406–407). Bartholomae 1174; Torp-Falk 311; Gauthiot MSL XVIII 343–347; Feist 193; Holthausen AEEW 229; Walde- Hofmann I 115; Pokorny I 750–751; Frisk I 264; Onions 571; Lehmann GED 145; Bammesberger Nom. 262; Heidermanns 416–417.

OE more, moru ‘edible root, carrot, parsnip’, OS morha ‘carrot’, OHG moraha id. Connection with Gk βράκανα: τà ἄγρια λάχανα (Hes.) cannot be supported. Together with Slav *mъrky id., *murxōn was borrowed from a North Caucasian source: NCauc μίρóqς> ‘root’ (in WCauc—‘carrot’). Zupitza Gutt. 135 Torp-Falk 570; Machek LP II 158 (of non-IE origin); Nikolaev-Starostin NCED 827; Trubachev ESSJa XX 247– 249; Kluge-Seebold 565.
OE murnan ‘to mourn, to be sad’. See *mimaraz. Wood MP V 282–283; Torp-Falk 312; Holthausen AEEW 227; Walde-Hofmann II 67–68; Seebold 351–352.

Goth maurnan ‘to worry, to concern oneself’, OS mornian id., OHG mornēn ‘to mourn’. Derived from *murnanan. Wood MP V 282–283 (to Gk μαραίνω ‘to rub off’); Torp-Falk 313; Feist 351; Onions 593 (to Skt smárati ‘to remember, to think of, to long for’, Gk μέριμνα); Lehmann GED 248– 249.
ON murra ‘to murmur’, MLG murren ‘to drone’, G murren ‘to grumble’. Of imitative origin. Torp- Falk 326; Vries ANEW 396.

ON morä ‘murder’, OE morä ‘death, murder’ (neut., masc.), OFris morth ‘murder’, OS morth id., OHG mord id. (neut., masc.). Identical with Skt mṛtá- ‘dead’, Av m 6 r 6 ta- id., Gk βροτός ‘(mortal) man’, μορτός: ἄνθρωπος θνητός (Hes.), Arm mard ‘man’, Lat mortuus ‘dead’, Slav *mьrtvъ id. Derived from *mer- ‘to die’. Meillet MSL XIV/4 378; Bartholomae 1172; Torp-Falk 311; Holthausen AEEW 226; Walde-Hofmann II 112– 113; Mayrhofer II 674; Pokorny I 735; Chemodanov SGGJa I 106; Vries ANEW 392; Zalizniak I 223; Frisk I 270–271; Lehmann GED 249; Bammesberger Nom. 78; Trubachev ESSJa XXI 146–148; Kluge-Seebold 569.

OSwed morþ ‘sand’, EFris murt ‘sand’. Goes back to a participle of *mer- ‘to rub’, *mṛto- also attested in Lat mortārium ‘mortar’. Torp-Falk 311; Walde-Hofmann II 112; Pokorny I 736; Vries ANEW 392–393.
ON morä-verk ‘murder’, OE morä-weorc ‘act which causes death’, OS mord-werk ‘murder’. Compound of *murþan I and *werkan. Carr Nom. comp. 51.
ON myräa ‘to murder’, OFris morthia id., MLG morden id., OHG murden id. Derived from *murþan I. Torp-Falk 313; Vries ANEW 398.

Goth maurþr ‘murder’, OE moräor id. (masc., neut.). Derived from *murþan I. Torp-Falk 311–312; Holthausen AEEW 226; Feist 351; Pokorny I 735; Zalizniak I 223; Onions 597; Lehmann GED 249; Bammesberger Nom. 85–86; Kluge-Seebold 569.
Goth maurþrjan ‘to murder’, OE formyrärian id., OFris morthra id., OHG murdiren id. Derived from *murþran. Torp- Falk 313; Holthausen AEEW 229; Feist 351; Vries ANEW 398.
Goth mana-maurþrja ‘murderer’, OE myrära id., OHG murdreo id. Derived from *murþran. Holthausen AEEW 229; Feist 344; Bammesberger Nom. 182.

MLG more, morwe ‘surly, grouchy’, OHG muruwi ‘delicate, fine’. Close to OIr meirb ‘lifeless’, W merw ‘weak’, Slav *mьrva ‘piece of meat, bit, straw’. Torp-Falk 311; Pokorny I 735– 736; Trubachev ESSJa XXI 151–154; Heidermanns 418; Kluge-Seebold 575.

ON mosi ‘moss’, OE mos ‘moss, marshy place’, MLG mos ‘moss’, OHG mos ‘moss, marsh’. Related to Lith pl. mūsos ‘mould’, Slav *mъxъ ‘moss’. Further cf. Lat muscus id. Bugge KZ XXXII 17; Brugmann II/1 166; Torp-Falk 327; Trautmann BSW 190–191; Holthausen AEEW 226; Walde-Hofmann II 134; Pokorny I 742; Chemodanov SSGJa I 55; Fraenkel 473–474; Vries ANEW 393; Zalizniak II 217; Onions 591; Trubachev ESSJa XX 216–218; Kluge-Seebold 568.

Swed modd ‘dirty wet snow’, E mud, Du mot ‘light rain’, G dial. mott ‘peat area, morass’. Identical with Gk μύδος ‘wetness, rot’, MIr muad ‘noble (?)’ < *moudo-. Torp-Falk 326; Pokorny I 741–742; Frisk II 263.
Norw dial. mutra ‘to mutter’, ME muteren id. Of imitative origin. See *muđ(i)lōjan. Torp-Falk 326.
E mother ‘sediment, yeast’, Du modder ‘mud’, late MHG moder ‘mold, swamp’. Derivationally close to Skt neut. mūtra- ‘urine’, Av mūyra ‘dirt’. Torp- Falk 324; Pokorny I 741.
ON motti ‘moth’, OE moääe id., MLG motte id. An expressive formation based on *mūsō ~ *mūsīn. Torp-Falk 326; Pokorny I 752; Lühr Expr. 252 (from *muþx-, further related to
ON múgi ‘mob, crowd’, OE múza ‘mow, heap, stack of hay’. See *mūxaz. Torp-Falk 325; Zupitza Gutt. 75, 209; Holthausen AEEW 226–227; Vries ANEW 394 (to Gk μύκων ‘stack’); Onions 594.

ON mór ‘moor, heath’, OHG mū-werfo ‘mole’. Identical with Lat mūcus ‘slime’. Torp NNEO 429 (to OIr móin ‘bog, swamp’); Loewenthal ANF XXXIII 106 (from *smōgho- ‘smoke’); Torp-Falk 325; Walde-Hofmann II 133; Pokorny I 744; Vries ANEW 392.
MHG mūhen, muojan ‘to roar, to bellow’. Identical with Gk m!κάομαι id., Lith mūkiū, m~kti id., Slav *my‘ati id. Torp-Falk 324; Pokorny I 751; Frisk II 266.
ON mÿ ‘midge, gnat’. An isolated form distantly connected with *muzjō and *mūsō ~ *mūsīn. Torp-Falk 327; Pokorny I 752; Zalizniak II 226; Kluge-Seebold 572 (reconstructs *muxjan).

Goth muka-modei ‘meekness, gentleness’, Du muik ‘soft, overripe’, G dial. mauch ‘dry and decayed, rotten’. Probably related to OIr mocht ‘soft, tender’, W mwyth ‘weak’ < *mug-to-. Torp- Falk 325; Feist 366; Pokorny I 744; Darms Vṛddhi 402; Heidermanns 415.
Goth faur-muljan ‘to muzzle’, ON mÿla id., LG mūlen ‘to grouse, to moan’. Derived from *mūlōn. Torp- Falk 324; Feist 147; Vries ANEW 397.

ON múli ‘snout, muzzle’, OFris mūla id., MLG mūl id. (neut.), mūle id. (fem.), OHG mūla id. (fem.). Related to Gk μύλλον ‘lip’ (Grienberger Got. 66; Wackernagel SBAB 1918 410–411) but eventually of imitative origin. Cf. also Norw smaul ‘muzzle’ close to Latv smaule id. Kretschmer Glotta XV 195–196 (to *munþaz); Torp-Falk 324; Feist 147; Pokorny I 751–752, 968; Vries ANEW 394; Frisk II 270; Chantraine 721– 722; Lehmann GED 111–112; Kluge- Seebold 546–547.

Gotland mausa ‘mosquito’, Flem meuzie id. (īn-stem). Related to Gk μυῖα ‘fly’, Alb mizë ‘fly’ (derivative of PAlb *mūjā), Lat musca ‘fly’, Lith mus E id., Slav *muxa id. Būga RFV LXXII 192–193; Trautmann BSW 191; Walde-Hofmann II 133; Polomé Language XXV 186; Pokorny I 752; Fraenkel 474; Ernout-Meillet 424; Frisk II 265–266; Trubachev ESSJa XX 170–172; Orel AED 268.

ON mús ‘mouse’, OE mús id., OFris mūs id., OS mūs id., OHG mūs id. Connected with Skt mūṣ- id., Arm mukn id., Gk μῦς id., Alb mi ~ mî id., Lat mūs id., Slav *myšь id. (Försterman KZ I 493). Meyer BB VIII 190; Torp-Falk 327; Holthausen AEEW 227; Walde- Hofmann II 132–133; Mayrhofer II 668; Pokorny I 752–753; Vries ANEW 396; Zalizniak II 209; Frisk II 275– 276; Onions 593; Bammesberger Nom. 195; Trubachev ESSJa XXI 64–67; Griepentrog Wurzelnomina 305–321; Kluge-Seebold 547; Orel AED 265.
ON nafarr ‘auger’, OE nafu-gár id., OS nabu-gēr id. Compound consisting of *nabō and *zaizaz. Torp-Falk 293; Holthausen AEEW 231; Carr Nom. comp. 51; Vries ANEW 403; Onions 62.
ON nef ‘beak, bill, nose’, OE nebb ‘nib, beak, nose’, MLG nebbe ‘beak, bill’. Derived from *nabō. Torp- Falk 292 (to Lith snãpas ‘bill, beak’); Holthausen AEEW 233; Vries ANEW 406; Onions 605.

ON nǫf ‘nave, clasp’ (partly < fem. *nabiz), OE nafu id., OS naba id., OHG naba id. Related to Skt neut. nábhya- ‘nave (of a wheel)’, nābhi- ‘navel, nave’, OPrus nabis id., Latv naba ‘navel’. Torp-Falk 293; Holthausen AEEW 231; Mayrhofer II 135; Pokorny I 315; Vries ANEW 414; Zalizniak II 197; Onions 604; Darms Vṛddhi 152–153; Kluge- Seebold 579.

ON nafli ‘navel’, OE nafela id., OFris navla id., MLG navel id., OHG nabalo, nabulo id. Derived from *nabō. Further etymologically connected with and structurally similar to Gk ὀμφαλός id., Lat umbilīcus id., OIr imbliu id. < *imbilon-. Torp-Falk 293; Holthausen AEEW 231; Walde-Hofmann II 814; Pokorny I 314–315; Vries ANEW 403; Zalizniak I 154; Frisk II 391–392; Onions 604; Anttila Schw. 91; Kluge- Seebold 579.

Goth nadrs ‘adder, viper, snake’, ON naär ‘viper, adder, snake’. Related to Lat natrix ‘water snake’, OIr nathir id., W neidr ‘snake’, Corn nader id., MBret azr id. See also *nēđrōn ~ *nađrōn. Torp-Falk 291–292; Walde-Hofmann II 147; Feist 368; Porzig 110; Pokorny I 767; Chemodanov SGGJa I 101; Vries ANEW 403; Zalizniak II 183; Onions 11–12; Lehmann GED 262; Bammesberger Nom. 86.

ON nagl ‘nail’ (root stem in pl.), OE næzl id., OFris neil id., OS nagal id., OHG nagal, negil id. (a- and i-stem). Related to Skt áṅghri- ‘foot’ < *onghli-, Gk ὄνυξ ‘nail’, Lat unguis id., OIr ingen id., Lith nãgas id., Slav *noga ‘foot’, *nogъtь ‘nail’ (Hirt IF X 49). Zupitza Gutt. 179; Torp-Falk 290; Trautmann BSW 192; Holthausen AEEW 230; Walde- Hofmann II 818–819; Pisani Paideia VIII 112 (to Slav *jьgla ‘needle’); Mayrhofer I 22; Pokorny I 780; Chemodanov SGGJa I 61; Vries ANEW 403–404; Fraenkel 478–479; Zalizniak II 209; Frisk II 398–399; Onions 601; Kluge- Seebold 580–581; Trubachev ESSJa XXV 161–164, 170–173.
Goth ga-nagljan ‘to nail’, ON negla id., OS neglian id., OHG negilen, nagalen id. Derived from *nazlaz. Torp-Falk 291; Feist 194; Vries ANEW 407; Lehmann GED 145–146; Kluge- Seebold 580.

Goth ga-nah ‘to suffice’, OE ze-neah id., OHG gi-nah id. Related to Hitt ḫenk- ‘to bestow, to consign’, Skt náśati ‘to attain’, Gk ἐνεγκεῖν ‘to carry’, Lith nešū, nēšti ‘to carry’, Slav *nesti id. Zupitza Gutt. 191; Torp-Falk 289; Trautmann BSW 198; Feist 92–93; Holthausen AEEW 239; Jóhannesson IEW 58–59; Mayrhofer II 145; Pokorny I 316–318; Fraenkel 497–498, Lexis II 186; Frisk I 512–513; Onions 315; Seebold 355–356; Lehmann GED 70–71; Puhvel III 380–382.
ON nátt-fugl ‘nightbird’, OHG naht-fogal id. Compound of *naxtz and *fuzlaz. Carr Nom. comp. 84 (calque of Lat noctua or nocticorax in OHG).

Goth nahts ‘night’, ON nátt, nótt id., OE niht, neaht id., OFris nacht id., OS naht id., OHG naht id. Related to Hitt nekuz ‘evening’, Skt nák ‘night’, Gk νύξ id., Alb natë id., Lat nox id., W nos id., Lith naktīs id., Slav *nokt’ь id. Brugmann Grundriß II/1 435; Meillet BSL XXV 144, XXVII/1 128; Zupitza Gutt. 136; Pedersen Kelt. Gr. I 123; Torp- Falk 290; Sturtevant Language VI/3 220; Holthausen AEEW 236; Schulze Kl. Schr. 803, 847; Walde-Hofmann II 181–182; Feist 368–369; Benveniste Origines 10; Pokorny I 762–763; Mayrhofer II 121–122; Fraenkel 481–482; Vries ANEW 405; Frisk II 327–328; Zalizniak I 157; Onions 610; Peeters IF LXXIX 31–32 (no traces of labiovelar in Germanic and Greek); Lehmann GED 262; Bammesberger Nom. 201–202; Griepentrog Wurzelnomina 476–479; Trubachev ESSJa XXV 175–177; Kluge-Seebold 580; Orel AED 282–283; Boutkan OFED s.v.

Goth ga-naitjan ‘to treat shamefully, to revile’, OE nætan ‘to trample upon, to crush’, MLG nēten id., OHG neizen id. Related to Skt níndati ‘to blame, to revile, to despise’, Av naēd- ‘to blame, to revile’, Gk ὄνειδος ‘shame’, Lith níedëti ‘to despise’. Bartholomae 1034; Torp-Falk 297; Kuiper Nasalpräsentia 130; Feist 194–195; Mayrhofer II 163–164; Pokorny I 760; Frisk II 394; Fraenkel 501; Lehmann GED 146.

Goth naqaþs ‘naked’, ON nǫkkviär id., OE nacod id., OFris naked id., MLG naket id., OHG nackot id. Identical with Lat nūdus id. < *nog⁄edho-. Further connected with Hitt neku-mant- id., Skt nagná- id., OIr nocht id. < *nog ⁄to-, Lith nuógas id., Slav *nagъ id. Petersson IF XXIV 259; Zupitza Gutt. 92; Torp-Falk 289; Trautmann BSW 201; Holthausen AEEW 229; Walde- Hofmann II 185; Feist 370; Specht 251; Mayrhofer II 126–127; Pokorny I 769; Ernout-Meillet II 798; Vries ANEW 404, 414; Fraenkel 511–512; Onions 601; Hamp Festschr. Seiler 272 (reconstructs *-d- in Lat); Lehmann GED 263; Huld Festschr. Puhvel I 88–89; Trubachev ESSJa XXII 70–72; Heidermanns 419– 420; Kluge-Seebold 580; Dybo BSA 130 (length in Balto-Slavic due to Winter’s law).

ON nǫkkvi ‘small boat’, OE naca ‘boat, bark’, OS nako id., OHG nahho id. Somehow related to IE *nāu- ‘ship’, see *nōwaz (Bugge PBB XIII 515). Maybe borrowed from another Indo- European dialect, with substitution of *-⁄- > *-g ⁄-? Lidén SVS VI/1 34 (to Skt nága- ‘mountain, tree’); Petersson IF XXIII 392 (to Slav *nožь ‘knife’); Torp- Falk 289; Holthausen AEEW 229; Pokorny I 770 (follows Lidén); Chemodanov SGGJa I 106; Vries ANEW 414; Zalizniak II 228; Kluge- Seebold 579.
ON nafn-liga ‘by name’, OFris name-lik id., MDu name-līke id., OHG nama-līhho, nemen-līhho id. Derived from *namōn ~ *nam(n)an. Onions 602.
ON nefnd ‘nomination, levy’, MLG nemede ‘jury trial’, OHG nemnida ‘calling, naming’. Derived from *namnjanan. Vries ANEW 406.

Goth namnjan ‘to name’, ON nefna id., OE nemnan id., OFris namna, nemna id., OS nemnian id., OHG nemnen ‘to call, to name’. Derived from *namnōn. Cf. Gk ὀνομαίνω ‘to name’. Torp-Falk 294; Holthausen AEEW 233–234; Feist 369; Vries ANEW 406; Frisk II 396; Lehmann GED 262; Kluge-Seebold 585.
ON nafni ‘namesake’, MLG ge-nanne id., OHG gi-nanno id. Derived from *namōn ~ *nam(n)an. Vries ANEW 403.

Goth namo ‘name’ (pl. namna), ON nafn id., OE nama id., OFris nama, noma id., OS namo id., OHG namo id. Related to Hitt la-a-man id., Toch A ñem, B ñom id., Skt neut. nāma id., Av nāman- id., Arm anun id., Gk ὄνομα id., Phryg onoman-, Alb emër ~ emën id. < PAlb *enmen-, Lat nōmen id., OIr ainmm n- id., OPrus emnes, emmens id., Slav *jьmę id. (Kuhn KZ I 374). Brugmann Grundriß II/1 237 (secondary masc.); Bartholomae 1062–1063; Torp-Falk 294; Holthausen AEEW 231; Walde- Hofmann II 173–174; Feist 369–370; Mayrhofer II 153; Pokorny I 321; Vries ANEW 403; Zalizniak II 221; Frisk II 396–397; Lejeune BSL LXI 38; Lehmann GED 262–263; Bammesberger Nom. 184; Trubachev ESSJa VIII 227–228; Toporov PJa I 28–30; Kluge- Seebold 581; Orel AED 87, Phrygian 450; Adams TB 270–271; Dybo BSA 14 (identical in vocalism with Skt, with the shortening of the root vowel in an oxytonic stem).

Goth ana-nanþjan ‘to take courage, to dare’, ON nenna ‘to strive, to have a heart for’, OE néäan ‘to have courage, to dare, to venture’, OS nāthian id., OHG nenden id. Factitive/ causative of *nenþanan. Falk ANF 117–118 (prefixal derivative of *anđjaz); Schröder Abl. 9 (similar to Falk); Torp- Falk 292; Feist 43; Vries ANEW 407; Schubert Erw. 69 (denominative); Lehmann GED 31.

ON sbst. prop. Nǫrr, OE nearu ‘narrow’, OS naru id. Structurally identical with Lith naıvas, narvā ‘honeycomb cell’, Slav *po-norvъ ‘worm’. Further related to Lith nar‹s ‘gnarl, loop, sling’, neriū, nérti ‘to thread, to set’. Torp-Falk 294; Trautmann BSW 196–197; Holthausen AEEW 232–233; Pokorny I 976; Fraenkel 495–496; Vries ANEW 405; Onions 603; Vasmer- Trubachev III 325; Heidermanns 421; Kluge-Seebold 582.
ON island name Nǫrva, MLG narwe ‘scar’, OHG narwa, narawa ‘loop, sling, scar’. Derived from *narwaz. Torp-Falk 294; Heidermanns 421.

ON nes ‘cape, promontory’, OE næss id., MLG nes id. Derived from *nasō. Torp-Falk 296; Holthausen AEEW 230; Pokorny I 755; Onions 607; Vries ANEW 408 (to Slav *nosъ ‘promontory’ which, however, is identical with *nosъ ‘nose’).

Goth nasjan ‘to save’, OE nerian id., OFris nera ‘to nourish’, OS nerian ‘to heal, to save’, OHG nerien ‘to nourish, to heal’. Causative of *nesanan. Torp-Falk 296; Holthausen AEEW 234; Feist 370; Pokorny I 766–767; Chantraine 733– 734; Seebold 360; Lehmann GED 146, 263; Kluge-Seebold 581.

Goth nasjands ‘savior’, OE neriend id., OS neriand id. Lexicalized pres. part. of *nasjanan ~ *nazjanan. Kaufmann Zd Ph XLIX 21– 22 (calque of Gk σωτήρ); Feist 371; Trutmann Adj. 20, 41–42; Lehmann GED 263.

ON nǫs ‘nose’ (ō- and i-stem), OE nasu id., OFris nose id., MDu nose id., OHG nasa id. (ō- and n-stem). An old dual. Related to Skt nas-, nāsā ‘nose’, Av nāh- id., OLat nāsum ‘nose’, OPrus nozy id., Lith nósis id., Slav *nosъ id. Bartholomae 1067; Torp-Falk 295; Trautmann BSW 193; Holthausen AEEW 232; Pisani Glotta XXVI 276; Walde-Hofmann II 145–146; Mayrhofer II 146; Pokorny I 755; Fraenkel 508; Vries ANEW 415; Zalizniak II 203; Onions 615; Bammesberger Nom. 195; Trubachev ESSJa XXV 212–216; Kluge-Seebold 582–583; Fritz KZ CIX 14–15.

OE nata- (in cmpn.), MLG nat ‘wet’, OHG naz id. Connected with Skt nadī‘river’ representing fem. of *nadá-. Torp-Falk 291; Weigand apud Heidermanns (from *wnataz, to Skt unátti ‘to spring, to wet, to bathe’); Krahe IF LVIII 209–211; Mayer AIPh VI 133–142; Mayrhofer II 130; Pokorny I 759; Lehmann GED 264; Heidermanns 422; Kluge-Seebold 583.
Norw dial. netla ‘nettle’, OE netele, netel id., MLG nettele id., OHG nezzila id. Derived from *natōn. Torp- Falk 291; Holthausen AEEW 235; Onions 608.

Goth nati ‘net’, ON net id., OE nett id., OFris net, nette id., OS netti id., OHG nezzi id. Related to Lat nōdus ‘knot’ (Persson Beiträge 139, 814), see *nōtō. Other parallels are problematic. Torp- Falk 291 (to Lat nassa ‘wickerbasket with a narrow neck’ < *nadtā); Kluge KZ XXV 313 (Lat nōdus < *nozdo-); Holthausen AEEW 235; Walde-Hofmann II 172–173; Feist 371; Pokorny I 758–759; Vries ANEW 408; Zalizniak I 145; Onions 608; Lehmann GED 263–264 (pre-Gmc); Kluge-Seebold 586.
Goth natjan ‘to make wet, to moisten’, MLG netten id., OHG nezzen id. Derived from *nataz. Feist 371; Lehmann GED 264; Heidermanns 422; Kluge-Seebold 586.
ON netja ‘net-like caul of fat’, OE nette ‘net-like caul’, OFris nette ‘retina’. Derived from *natjan. Holthausen AEEW 235; Vries ANEW 408 (calque of Lat reticulum ‘net, fishing net’).
Norw dial. nata ‘nettle’, OHG nazza id. Close to Skt náda- ‘reed’. Sütterlin IF IV 92 (to Gk ἀδίκη ‘nettle’ < *–dikā); Torp-Falk 291; Pokorny I 759; Vries ANEW 405; Onions 608.
Goth naudi-bandi ‘shackle, manacle, fetter’, OFris nēd-bende ‘violent, forced fettering’, MLG nōd-bende. Compound of *nauđiz and *banđjō. Feist 371; Carr Nom. comp. 45; Lehmann GED 264–265.
ON nauäigr ‘forced, unwilling’, MLG nōdech id., MHG nōtec, nœtec id. Cf. OE neádizness ‘obligation’. Derived from *nauđiz. Vries ANEW 405.
ON nauäga ‘to force, to compel’, MDu nodigen id., MHG nōtegen id. Derived from *nauđizaz. Vries ANEW 405.

ON nauä-syn ‘need, necessity, lawful impediment’, OFris nēdskīne ‘lawful, impediment’, MLG noet-schyn id. Compound of *nauđiz and *sunđjō. WGmc *-sk- is explained by secondary analogies. Carr Nom. comp. 77 (calque of Lat sontica causa).
Swed nöd-tharf ‘pressing need’, OE níd-äearf ‘necessity’. Compound of *nauđiz and *þarbō. Torp- Falk 299.

Goth adj. naudi-þaurfts ‘necessary’, OFris nēd-threft ‘pressing need’, OS nōd-thurft ‘necessity’, OHG nōtdurft id. Compound of *nauđiz and *þurftiz. Torp-Falk 299; Feist 371; Carr Nom. comp. 43; Lehmann GED 264–265; Kluge-Seebold 592.

Goth nauþs ‘need’, ON nauä ‘need, difficulty, distress’, OE níd ‘need, necessity’, OFris nēd, nāth id., OS nōd id., OHG nōt id. Might be related to OPrus acc. sg. nautin id. (Trautmann APSpr. 382). Cf. also Lith naudā ‘need, advantage’, Slav *nuditi ‘to make, to cause’, *nudja ‘need’ reflecting *-d(h)- (Osthoff Etym. I 355). Försterman KZ I 238; Persson Festschr. Bugge 192 (to ON naumr ‘narrow, close’); Meringer IF XVII 152 (to ON gnúa ‘to rub’); Abrahamson NTU VIII 103 (same as Persson); Torp-Falk 298–299; Holthausen AEEW 235; Feist 372; Pokorny I 756; Vries ANEW 405; Fraenkel 513; Zalizniak I 151; Onions 606; Stang LS 39; Lehmann GED 264–265; Kluge-Seebold 592; Trubachev ESSJa XXVI 34–39.

ON naut ‘cattle, ox’, OE neát id., OHG nōz ‘cattle’. Close to Lith naudā ‘use, property’, Latv nāûda ‘money’. Related to *neutanan. Cf., on the other hand, *nauđiz. Torp-Falk 300; Holthausen AEEW 233; Pokorny I 768; Fraenkel 487; Vries ANEW 406; Zalizniak I 142; Onions 605; Stang LS 39; Seebold 361; Lehmann GED 269; Kluge- Seebold 314.
ON nautr ‘mate, fellow’, OE ze-neát ‘companion, associate, vassal’, OFris nāt ‘companion, fellow’, OS ge-nōt id., OHG gi-nōz. Derived from *neutanan. Torp-Falk 300; Vries ANEW 406.
ON neyta ‘to use, to enjoy, to consume’, OFris nēta ‘to utilize’, MDu noten ‘to use’. Causative of *neutanan. Vries ANEW 408; Seebold 361.

Goth nauþjan ‘to compel, to force’, ON neyäa id., OE níedan id., OFris nēda id., OS nōdian ‘to need’, OHG nōten id. Derived from *nauđiz. Torp-Falk 299; Holthausen AEEW 235; Feist 372; Vries ANEW 408; Kluge-Seebold 592.

Goth naus ‘dead man, corpse’, ON nár id., OE né, neó id. Related to Lith nõvë ‘oppression, death’, Latv nâve ‘death’, OPrus nowis ‘body, stump’, Slav *navь ‘corpse’ (Grienberger Got. 164). Kuhn KZ I 137 (to Gk νέκυς ‘corpse’); Pott KZ I 395; Zupitza Gutt. 76; Torp-Falk 295; Trautmann BSW 201; Holthausen AEEW 232; Feist 372; Pokorny I 756; Dybo VSJa 25–26 (pretonic shortening of *ā in Gmc); Chemodanov SGGJa I 67; Fraenkel 509; Vries ANEW 405; Stang LS 39; Lindeman Verschärfung 16.20; Lehmann GED 264; Schrijver LL 352–353 (follows Dybo); Gamkrelidze-Ivanov II 825 (to *nōwaz); Trubachev ESSJa XXIV 49–52.

Goth ni ‘not’, ON né id., OE ne id., OFris ni, ne id., OS ni, ne id., OHG ni, ne id. Identical with Skt ná id., Av na- (with enclitics), Lat ne- (in cmpn.), OIr ni, Lith nē id., Slav *ne id. (Brugmann Grundriß II/3 977). Short variant of *nē. The univerbated *ne ainaz (ON neinn ‘any’, OE nán ‘no one, none’, OFris nēn, nān id., OS nēn ‘not’, OHG nein id.) is structurally close to Lat nōn ‘not’< *neoino-. Another univerbation (Goth niba, nibai ‘if not, but not, unless’, ON nefa ‘if not’, OS neba id., nebo, OHG nibu id.) reminds Slav *ne bo ‘for, because, or’. As to *ne ke (OS nec ‘and not’), it can be identical with Lith nēgi id. (Holthausen KZ XLVII 309). Pedersen Kelt. Gr. I 156; Torp-Falk 288; Trautmann BSW 194–195; Holthausen AEEW 231–232; Walde-Hofmann II 150–151, 157 (Lat *neg(i) reconstructed from negō); Feist 374–375; Mayrhofer II 120; Pokorny I 756; Chemodanov SGGJa I 86; Fraenkel 488–489; Vries ANEW 406–407; Onions 605, 613; Lehmann GED 265–266; Trubachev ESSJa XXIV 91–93; Kluge- Seebold 585.

ON njól ‘night’, OE adj. neowol, nifol ‘prone, prostrate, deep down, profound’, OFris nevil ‘fog, mist’, OS nebal id., OHG nebul id. Together with Gk νεφέλη ‘cloud’, Lat nebula id. derived from IE *nebh-, *nebh-es: Hitt nepiš ‘sky’, Skt nábhas- ‘vapor, cloud, mist’, Av nabah- ‘air, sky’, Gk νέφος ‘cloud, fog’, OIr nem ‘sky’, Lith debesīs ‘cloud’, Slav *nebo ‘sky’. Ploix MSL IV 415, 421; Bartholomae 1040; Pedersen Kelt. Gr. I 255; Torp-Falk 293; Holthausen AEEW 236, PBB LXVI 273; Walde-Hofmann II 151–152; Porzig Gliederung 190; Mayrhofer II 134; Pokorny I 315; Fraenkel 85; Vries ANEW 409–410; Zalizniak II 175; Frisk II 309–310; Chantraine II 748; Trubachev ESSJa XXIV 101–104; Heidermanns 423–424; Kluge-Seebold 584.

ON nefi‘cognate kinsman’, OE nefa ‘grandson, nephew’, OFris neva id., OS nebo id., OHG nevo ‘nephew, relative’. Etymologically close to Skt nápāt ‘offspring, son, grandson’, Av napāt- ‘grandson’, Lat nepōs id., OLith nepuotis id. Bartholomae 1039; Torp-Falk 292; Trautmann BSW 196; Holthausen AEEW 233; Walde-Hofmann II 161–162; Mayrhofer II 132–133; Pokorny I 764; Fraenkel 494; Vries ANEW 406; Zalizniak I 154; Onions 607; Bammesberger Nom. 186–187; Kluge-Seebold 584.

ON nipt ‘female relative, sister’ (contamination with ō-stem), OE nift ‘granddaughter, niece’, OFris nift ‘niece’, MLG nichte id., OHG nift, nifta ‘niece, step-daughter’. Close to Skt naptī- ‘daughter, granddaughter’, Av naptī- ‘granddaughter’, Lat neptis id., OIr necht ‘niece’, OLith nept E ‘granddaughter’ (Grimm KZ I 434). Further connected with *nefōđ(z). Bartholomae 1039; Pedersen Kelt. Gr. I 93; Torp-Falk 292; Būga RFV LXV 329; Trautmann BSW 196; Holthausen AEEW 236; Walde-Hofmann II 161; Mayrhofer II 133; Pokorny I 764; Fraenkel 494; Vries ANEW 410; Zalizniak II 214; Onions 609; Szemerényi Kinship 53; Kluge-Seebold 587.

Goth niman ‘to take’, ON nema id., OE niman id., OFris nema id., OS niman id., OHG neman id. Related to Gk νέμω ‘to deal out, to distribute’. Hirt Idg. Gr. I 328 (to Lat emō ‘to buy’, Lith imū, i Mti ‘to take’); Torp-Falk 293; Meillet BSL XXXIV/103 69–70 (from *ni- and *em- as in Lat emō ‘to take’); Meringer IF XVIII 238–240; Feist 375–376; Holthausen AEEW 236; Jóhannesson IEW 692; Pokorny I 763; Vries ANEW 407; Onions 611; Frisk II 302–304; Benveniste IEL 67–69; Seebold 357–359; Lehmann GED 266–267; Kluge-Seebold 585.
OFrank nimid ‘holy grove’. Related to or borrowed from Celtic: Gaul nemhton ‘sacellum’, OIr nemed ‘holiness’. Torp-Falk 294; Pokorny I 764.
OHG pret. gi-nand ‘to strive’. Related to Toch A nati ‘might, strength’, OIr néit ‘battle’ < *nanti-. Torp- Falk 292; Pokorny I 755; Vendryes N-7; Seebold 359; Lehmann GED 31.

Goth ga-nisan ‘to recover, to be saved’, OE nesan ‘to escape’, OS gi-nesan id., OHG gi-nesan ‘to become healthy’. Related to Toch A nas-, B nes- ‘to be’, Skt násate ‘to approach, to join’, Gk νέομαι ‘to return’ (Meillet JA XVII 456). Torp-Falk 296; Feist 195–196; Holthausen AEEW 234; Jóhannesson IEW 693–694; Mayrhofer II 146; Pokorny I 766–767; Vries ANEW 405; Frisk II 304–306; Seebold 359–360; Lehmann GED 146; Kluge-Seebold 313; Adams TB 344–346.

ON nest ‘provisions, viands’, OE nest ‘provisions, victuals’, OHG weganest ‘provisions for journey’. Derived from *nesanan. Torp-Falk 296; Holthausen AEEW 234; Vries ANEW 408; Seebold 359; Lehmann GED 146; Bammesberger Nom. 78.
Goth ga-nists ‘salvation’, OS gi-nist ‘release, salvation’, OHG gi-nist ‘cure, healing’. Derived from *nesanan. Cf. also *nestan. Feist 195–196; Seebold 359; Lehmann GED 146; Bammesberger Nom. 143.
ON nisti ‘brooch, pin’. Probably, to *natjan. Walde-Hofmann II 172–173; Vries ANEW 408.
ON nesta, nista ‘to pin, to nail fast’, OE nestan ‘to spin’, MHG nesten ‘to tie, to lace up’. Derived from *nestjan. Torp-Falk 296; Holthausen AEEW 234; Vries ANEW 408.

Goth bi-niuhsjan ‘to spy out’, ON nÿsa ‘to pry, to enquire’, OE neós(i)an ‘to search out, to find out’, OS niusian ‘to investigate’, OHG niusen ‘to work on smth.’. Close to Slav *n’uxati ‘to smell’ < *neuks- (Diefenbach VW II 116). Uhlenbeck PBB XXX 267 (same as Diefenbach); Grienberger Got. 48 (to *neujaz); Brugmann IF XIII 153–155 (to Skt návate ‘to go, to move’); Torp-Falk 299; Holthausen KZ LXIX 116–117, AEEW 234; Feist 93–94; Pokorny I 768–769; Chemodanov SGGJa I 81; Vries ANEW 413; Stang LS 40; Lehmann GED 71.

Goth ana-niujan ‘to renew’, OESc ater-nya id., OS niwian id., OHG niuwen id. Derived from *neujaz. Structurally close to Slav *noviti ‘to renew, to make new’. Feist 377; Vries ANEW 412; Heidermanns 426; Trubachev ESSJa XXV 232–233.

Goth niujis ‘new’, ON nÿr id., OE níwe id., OFris nī, nīe id., OS niuwi id., OHG niuwi id. Identical with Skt návya- ‘new, young’, Gk νεῖος ‘new’, OIr núe id., Lith na Ujas id. Further related to IE *ne⁄o-: Hitt ne⁄a- id., Toch A ñu, B ñuwe id., Skt náva- ‘new, fresh, young’, Gk νέος id., Lat nouus id., OLith navas id., Slav *novъ id. (Grimm KZ I 433). Karsten NB II 370; Torp-Falk 298; Trautmann BSW 194; Feist 377–378; Holthausen AEEW 236; Walde- Hofmann II 181; Porzig Festschr. Debrunner 343–349, Gliederung 203; Mayrhofer II 144–145; Pokorny I 769; Būga II 502; Fraenkel 487–488; Vries ANEW 412; Frisk II 297–298, 306–307; Onions 608; Lindeman Verschärfung 16.21; Lehmann GED 267–268; Bammesberger Nom. 255; Heidermanns 425–426; Kluge- Seebold 587; Trubachev ESSJa XXVI 9–13; Adams TB 269; Burlak IFTJa 244.
ON nÿjung ‘novelty, news’, OHG niuwunga ‘renewal’. Derived from *neujaz. Heidermanns 426.

ON nÿra ‘kidney’, ME nére id., MLG nēre id., OHG nioro id. Continues *neg ⁄hro-. Related to Gk pl. νεφροί id., Lat Praenestine pl. nefrōnēs ‘kidneys, testicles’ (Bugge BB III 105; Walde IF XIX 102). Fay Festschr. Bloomfield 140 (to IE *(s)neu- ‘to drip’); Zupitza Gutt. 100; Torp-Falk 289; Walde-Hofmann II 156–157; Pokorny I 319; Vries ANEW 413; Zalizniak II 217; Frisk II 310; Onions 505; Szemerényi IBK XV 193–194 (related to Lat rēn ‘kidney’ < *regno- metathesized from *negro-); Kluge-Seebold 588.

Goth niutan ‘to attain, to enjoy’, ON njóta ‘to use, to enjoy’, OE neótan id., OFris niāta id., OS pres. niotan id., OHG niozan ‘to make use of’. See *nautan. Thurneysen KZ XXX 562 (to Lat nūtriō ‘to suckle, to feed’); Torp-Falk 299–300; Meringer IF XVIII 235 (to Skt nudáti ‘to push, to impel, to remove’); Feist 379; Holthausen AEEW 234; Jóhannesson IEW 688–689; Pokorny I 768; Chemodanov SGGJa I 77; Vries ANEW 410; Onions 605; Seebold 361; Lehmann GED 269; Kluge-Seebold 313–314.

Goth b-nauan ‘to rub’ (secondary -au- < *-ō-), ON núa id., OHG (h)niuwan ‘to pulverize, to grind’. Related to Skt návate ‘to turn’, Gk νεύω ‘to nod, to wave’, Lat nuō id. Grienberger Got. 52 (reconstructs Goth *hnauan = Gk κνύω ‘to scratch’); Torp- Falk 298 (to Skt kṣṇáuti ‘to whet, to sharpen’); Meringer IF XVII 150–151 (to *nōwaz); Kretschmer Glotta II 353; Feist 101–102 (to IE *nāu- ‘to torment’); Pokorny I 767; Vries ANEW 46; Lehmann GED 77.
OHG niuwōn ‘to make new, to renew’. Derived from *neujaz. Structurally identical with Hitt ne⁄aḫḫ- id., Gk νεάω id., Lat (re)nouāre id. Walde-Hofmann II 181; Frisk II 297– 298; Lehmann GED 268.

Goth niun ‘nine’, ON níu id., OE nizon id., OFris nigun id., OS nigun id., OHG niun id. Continues IE *(e)ne⁄– id.: Toch AB ñu, Skt náva, Arm inn, Gk ἐννέα, Lat novem, OIr noí n-, Lith devynī, Slav *devętь. Torp-Falk 295; Holthausen AEEW 236; Schulze Kl. Schr. 496; Walde-Hofmann II 179–180; Feist 378–379; Porzig Gliederung 155; Mayrhofer II 141–142; Pokorny I 318–319; Nehring Sprache VIII 130; Fraenkel 92; Vries ANEW 410; Frisk I 519–520; Onions 611; Lindeman Verschärfung 16.21; Lehmann GED 268–269; Trubachev ESSJa IV 222–223; Ross-Berns Numerals 589–590; Kluge-Seebold 587; Adams TB 268; Burlak IFTJa 244.
ON níund ‘body of nine’. Derived from *newun. Derivationally close to Skt navatí- ‘nine tens’, Slav *devętь ‘nine’. Torp-Falk 295; Pokorny I 318–319.

Goth niunda ‘ninth’, ON níundi id., OE nizoäa id., OFris nigunda id., OS nigundo, niguäo id., OHG niunto id. Derived from *newun. Structurally similar to Toch B ñunte id., Gk đnatow id., Alb nëntë ~ nand id., OPrus newīnts id., Lith deviñtas id., Slav *devętъ id. and the like, from *ne⁄–to. Torp-Falk 295; Holthausen AEEW 236; Sturtevant MLN XL 25–27; Feist 378–379; Pokorny I 318–319; Fraenkel 92; Vries ANEW 410; Frisk I 519–520; Lehmann GED 268; Trubachev ESSJa IV 222; Ross-Berns Numerals 630–631; Orel AED 291–292; Adams TB 269.

Goth ne ‘no, nay’. Related to Skt nā ‘not’, Lat nē ‘not, lest’, OIr ní ‘not’, Slav *nî ‘not’. Walde-Hofmann II 150–151; Feist 373; Mayrhofer II 120 (on “Ved nā ‘not’”); Pokorny I 757; Onions 605; Trubachev ESSJa XXIV 91–93.

ON næfr ‘birch bark’, MLG dēver id. (with assimilation in the anlaut). Probably related to Lith n‚besis ‘weakling’. Torp-Falk 293 (to Skt nábhate ‘to burst, to tear’); Fraenkel 489–490 (Lith n‚besis built on nebe ‘now more’); Adams TB 441 (to Toch B mape ‘ripe’).

ON naära ‘adder, viper, snake’, OE nædre id., OS nādra id., OHG nātara, nātra id. An ablaut variant of *nađraz (Fick I 504). Torp- Falk 291–292; Holthausen AEEW 230; Zalizniak II 183; Onions 11–12; Kluge- Seebold 583.

Goth neƕ ‘near’, ON náligr id., OE neáh id., OFris nēi id., OS nāh id., OHG nāh id. An old compound of *ne- ~ *nō (cf. Lith nuõ ‘from’, Slav *na ‘on’) and *ok ⁄- ~ *6 k ⁄- ‘eye, sight’ (Brugmann Grundriß II/2 799). Cf. in particular Lith pranókti ‘to ferry across’, Latv nākt ‘to get there’ (Zupitza Gutt. 66–67). Wood PBB XXIV 530 (to *nōzaz ~ *nōxaz); Endzelin Latyš. predl. I 127–129, KZ LXII 23–28; Torp-Falk 289–290; Fay IF XXXIII 352 (from *ene˚- ‘nectere’); Trautmann BSW 200; Sturtevant JEGP XXX 157 (on ƕ in Goth); Holthausen AEEW 232; Feist 373; Olson ANF XXXI 10–12; Pokorny I 40; Fraenkel 511; Vries ANEW 402–403; Onions 610; Lehmann GED 265 (to Skt náśati ‘to draw near, to arrive’); Bammesberger Nom. 257–258; Kluge- Seebold 579, 581.
ON ná-frændi ‘near kinsman’, OE neáh-freónd ‘near friend, near relation’. Compound of *nēxw(a) and *frijōnđz. Carr Nom. comp. 87 (parallel formations).
ON ná-ligr ‘near’, OHG nāh-līhho ‘almost’. Derived from *nēxw(a). Vries ANEW 404.
ON ná-mágr ‘near relative by marriage’, OE neáh-mæz ‘near kinsman’, MLG nāmāge id. Compound of *nēxw(a) and *mēzaz. Carr Nom. comp. 86 (parallel formations).
ON ná-vist ‘presence’, OE neáh-west ‘nearness, neighborhood’. Compound of *nēxw(a) and *westiz I. Torp-Falk 290; Carr Nom. comp. 58.
ON ná ‘to reach, to get’, OE ze-næzan ‘to approach’, OHG nāhen id. Derived from *nēxw(a). Zupitza Gutt. 191; Torp-Falk 290; Holthausen AEEW 232; Feist 373; Vries ANEW 402–403; Lehmann GED 265.
ON nánd ‘neighborhood, proximity’, OHG adv. nāhunt ‘recently’. Cf. Goth neƕundja ‘neighbor’. Derived from *nēxw(a). Feist 373; Vries ANEW 405.

MLG neien ‘to sew’, OHG nāien, nā(w)en id. Etymologically related to Gk νέω ‘to spin’, Lat neō id. < *neƒō. Torp-Falk 288; Walde-Hofmann II 159–160; Mayrhofer III 534 (on Dhātup. Skt snāyati ‘to wrap’); Pokorny I 973; Frisk II 311–312; Onions 606.

Goth anda-nem ‘receiving, acceptance’, ON nám ‘learning, study’, OE fem. nám ‘seizure of property’. Long grade derivative of *nemanan. Cf. with the same ablaut grade Toch B ñemek ‘harvest’ < *nēm-oko- (Adams TB 271). Torp- Falk 293; Holthausen AEEW 231; Feist 47; Vries ANEW 404; Seebold 358; Lehmann GED 34–35; Bammesberger Nom. 63.

Goth anda-nems ‘acceptable, pleasant’, ON næmr ‘keen, sharp, quick at learning’, MLG an-nāme ‘acceptable’, OHG nāmi id. Derived from *nemanan. Karsten NB I 96–97; Torp-Falk 293–294; Wissmann NP 113; Feist 47; Pokorny I 763–764; Vries ANEW 413; Seebold 358; Bammesberger Nom. 260– 261; Matzel Festschr. Schröder 99–100, 117, KZ CV 108; Heidermanns 424–425; Kluge-Seebold 39, 312.
ON næma ‘to bereave, to deprive’, OE ze-næman ‘to take away’. Derived from *nēman. Holthausen AEEW 230; Vries ANEW 413.

Goth niþan ‘to help, to support’. Identical with Skt nāthate ‘to ask for help’ (Fick I 96). Fowkes Language XXIII 420–421 (to OIr snád- ‘to protect’); Torp-Falk 291; Feist 376; Mayrhofer II 151–152; Pokorny I 754; Seebold 360 (Goth -i- for -e-); Lehmann GED 267.

Goth neþla ‘needle’, ON nál id., OE nædl id., OFris nēdle id., OS nādla id., OHG nādala id. Derived from *nējanan. Torp-Falk 288; Kluge Nom. Stamm. 52; Holthausen AEEW 229–230; Feist 374; Pokorny I 973; Vries ANEW 404; Zalizniak I 147; Onions 606; Trubachev Etim. 34–36, Rem. 114–115 (close to Slav *snovadlo ‘wooden plank in weaving’, a thematized variant of *(s)neutlo-); Lehmann GED 265; Kluge-Seebold 580.

ON náä ‘rest, quietness, peace’, OFris nāthe ‘mercy, grace’, OS nātha ‘mercy, grace’, OHG gi-nāda id. Related to Skt nātha- ‘refuge, relief, help’. Derived from *nēþanan. Torp-Falk 291; Pokorny I 754.

Goth nidwa ‘rust, decay’. From *ni-t⁄ā, a derivative of IE *ni- ‘down’ (Grienberger Got. 166: ‘rust’ as ‘sediment’, ‘deposit on iron’). Osthoff IF V 299–301 (to Skt nīla- ‘dark blue, black, of a dark color’, Lat niteō ‘to glitter, to shine’); Lidén Studien 60 (same as Osthoff); Falk ANF V 111; Torp-Falk 298; Feist 375; Pokorny I 312–313; Vries ANEW 409; Lehmann GED 266.

ON nykr ‘water goblin, grey water-horse’, OE nicor ‘water monster, hippopotamus’, MLG necker ‘water spirit, water animal’, OHG nihhus ‘crocodile’ (masc., neut.). Related to Skt nénekti ‘to wash, to cleanse’, Gk νίζω ‘to wash’, OIr nigid id. Zupitza Gutt. 92; Torp-Falk 297; Holthausen AEEW 235; Mayrhofer II 179; Pokorny I 761; Zalizniak II 171; Frisk II 319–320; Onions 609; Bammesberger Nom. 213; Kluge-Seebold 590.

OE nest ‘nest’, MLG nest id., OHG nest id. Identical with Toch B lesto ‘nest’, Skt nīḍá- ‘resting-place, abode, nest’, Arm nist ‘place, location, residence’, Lat nīdus ‘nest’, MIr net id., Lith līzdas id. (with irregular l-), Slav *gnîzdo id., with irregular anlaut and *-î- < *-oi- (Schulze KZ XL 415). Pedersen Kelt. Gr. I 88, 419; Torp-Falk 297; Holthausen AEEW 234; Walde-Hofmann II 167– 168; Mayrhofer II 171–172; Pokorny I 887; Fraenkel 383; Onions 607; Van Windekens 261, LP XXV 13–15 (connects Toch B lesto with OPruss lasto ‘bed’ < *les- ‘weak’); Bammesberger Nom. 55; Kluge-Seebold 586.

ON neäan ‘from beneath, from below’, OE neoäan, neoäane ‘down, beneath, below’, OS nithana id., OHG nidana id. Derived from *niþaz. Torp- Falk 297; Holthausen AEEW 237; Vries ANEW 406; Onions 88; Kluge- Seebold 588.
OE superl. niäemest ‘lowest’, OFris bi-nitha ‘beneath’, OS nithe id., OHG nida ‘underneath’. Derived from IE *ni- ‘down’. Torp-Falk 297; Pokorny I 312–313.

Goth niþjis ‘relative, kinsman’, ON niär ‘son, kinsman’, OE pl. niääas ‘men’. Identical with Skt nítya- ‘continual, perpetual, one’s own’ (Schulze KZ XL 412–413). Walde KZ XXXIV 495–496 (from *neptƒo-); Meillet BSL XXX 51, Etudes 393 (same as Walde); Torp-Falk 292; Brugmann IF XXXIII 304 (close to Schulze); Holthausen AEEW 237; Feist 376–377 Fraenkel Zfslav Ph XX 62–63, KZ XLII 260; Mayrhofer II 162–163; Pokorny I 311; Benveniste Hitt. 104 (follows Schulze); Vries ANEW 409; Zalizniak I 138 (from Slav *netijь ‘nephew’); Mezger KZ LXXI 118; Martynov Jazyk 23–24 (same as Zalizniak); Lehmann GED 267; Bammesberger Nom. 84.
ON niä ‘the wane of the moon’, OE niä ‘abyss’. Substantivized *niþaz. Holthausen AEEW 237; Vries ANEW 408.
ON niär ‘down’, OE niäer ‘down, beneath, below’, OFris nither, neder id., OS nithar id., OHG nidar id. See *niþrōn. Torp-Falk 297; Holthausen AEEW 237; Vries ANEW 409; Kluge-Seebold 588.
ON niäri ‘down, beneath’, OE niäere id., OHG nidaro id. Derived from *niþr(a). Torp-Falk 297.

ON neäri ‘lower’, OE neoäera id., OFris nithera id., OS nithiri id., OHG nidari id. Comparative form based on IE *ni- ‘down’, cf. Skt nitarām ‘downwards’. Further see *niþanē. Holthausen AEEW 237; Mayrhofer II 162; Pokorny I 312; Onions 608.
ON niära ‘to put down’, OE niäerian ‘to bring low’, OFris nithria, netheria ‘to abase’, OS nitharōn id. Derived from *niþr(a). Holthausen AEEW 237; Vries ANEW 409.

Goth nei ‘not’, ON ní id. Continues an earlier *ne it related to Skt net, ned ‘absolutely not’, Av nōit id. (Osthoff PBB VIII 312) or to Lat nī ‘not’, OIr ní id., Lith neĩ ‘nor, than’, Slav *ni ‘nor’. Bartholomae 1072–1079; Torp-Falk 288; Zubaty IF IV 471; Walde-Hofmann II 166; Feist 373–374; Mayrhofer II 120; Pokorny I 757; Vries ANEW 408; Fraenkel 491; Trubachev ESSJa XXV 106–107.

Goth neiþ ‘envy, spite’, ON níä ‘libel’, OE níä ‘envy, hatred, enmity’, OFris nīth ‘envy, hatred’, OS nīth ‘enmity, hatred’, OHG nīd ‘wrath, hatred’. Close to W nwyd ‘passion < *nei-to-, OIr níth ‘combat’ < *nī-tu- and Toch B ñyātse ‘danger, plague, distress’ < *nītƒo- (Hilmarsson TIES V 137–139). Further related to *naitjanan. Torp-Falk 297–298; Holthausen AEEW 237; Feist 374; Pokorny I 760; Chemodanov SGGJa I 97; Vries ANEW 409; Zalizniak II 171; Onions 611; Lehmann GED 265; Kluge-Seebold 585; Adams TB 273.
ON níäa ‘to libel, to revile’, OHG nīden ‘to revile’. Derived from *nīþan ~ *nīþaz. Torp-Falk 298; Holthausen AEEW 237; Vries ANEW 409.

ON nœfr ‘clever, skilled’, G dial. nüeber ‘lively, spright’ (von Bahder IF XIV 260). Identical with Arm nurb ‘narrow, thin, sharp’ (Lidén Arm. St. 64– 65). von Bahder IF XIV 260; Torp- Falk 292, 569; Pokorny I 973–974; Solta Arm. 440; Vries ANEW 414; Heidermanns 427–428.
ON g-nóg-ligr ‘abundant’, OFris nōge-lik ‘befitting, adequate’. Derived from *nōzaz ~ *nōxaz. Heidermanns 428.

Goth ga-nohs ‘enough, sufficient’, ON g-nógr id., OE zenóz id., OFris e-nōch id., OS gi-nōg id., OHG gi-nuog id. Related to *naxa. Torp-Falk 289; Feist 196; Vries ANEW 180; Onions 315; Seebold 355; Lehmann GED 70; Matzel KZ CV 118–119; Heidermanns 428–429; Kluge-Seebold 314; Kluge-Seebold 314.
ON nœgä ‘plenty, abundance’, OHG gi-nuogida ‘sufficiency’. Derived from *nōzaz ~ *nōxaz. Vries ANEW 414; Heidermanns 428.

Goth ga-nohjan ‘to satisfy, to satiate’, ON g-nœgja ‘to be enough, to suffice’, OHG gi-nuogen ‘to be present in abundance’. Derived from *nōzaz ~ *nōxaz. Torp-Falk 289; Feist 196; Vries ANEW 181; Heidermanns 428.
ON sbst. nœgi ‘contentment’, OHG gi-nuogi ‘sufficient, rich’. Derived from *nōzaz ~ *nōxaz. Heidermanns 428.

OFris nōmia ‘to name’, MLG nōmen id., MHG be-nuomen id. Derived from *namōn ~ *nam(n)an with the long grade attested outside Germanic in Lat nōmen and Skt nāman- ‘name’, Av nāman- id. Bartholomae 1062–1063; Walde-Hofmann II 173–174; Mayrhofer II 153; Pokorny I 321; Onions 602; Dybo BSA 14 (traces of the barytonic variant without shortening).
Norw nōs ‘muzzle, snout’, OE nose, nosu ‘ness, promontory’. Connected with *nasō. Retains the original long vocalism of nom. sg. *nās-. Torp-Falk 295; Holthausen AEEW 238; Pokorny I 755.

ON nót ‘net’. Etymologically close to Lat nōdus ‘knot’ (Brugmann Tot. 60). See *natjan. Torp-Falk 291; Walde-Hofmann II 172–173; Krahe Su V 76; Pokorny I 535; Chemodanov SGGJa I 88; Vries ANEW 412; Onions 608; Darms Vṛddhi 308–310; Lehmann GED 263; Griepentrog Wurzelnomina 454–457.

ON nór ‘ship’. Related to Skt náu- id., Gk ναῦς, Hom νηῦς id., Lat nāuis id., OIr nau id. Persson UUÅ 1891 142 (to IE *sneu- ‘to flow’); Meringer IF XVII 149 (to *neuwanan ~ *nūwanan); Torp-Falk 288–289; Holthausen AEEW 238; Walde-Hofmann II 148–149; Mayrhofer II 181; Pokorny I 755–756; Szemerényi KZ LXXIII 185– 187; Vries ANEW 411; Zalizniak II 228; Frisk II 292–293.

Goth nu ‘now’, ON nú id., OE nú id., OFris nū id., OS nu id., OHG nu, no id. Related to Hitt nu ‘now’, Toch A nu, B no ‘however, but’, Skt nú, nū ‘now, now then, certainly’, Av nū ‘now’, Gk nu, νῦν ‘now’, OIr nu, nú ‘now’, Lith n~ id., Slav *nъ ‘and, but’.
