Cf. also the univerbation of *nu xwe > Goth nauh ‘still, yet’, OFris noch id., OS noh id., OHG noh id. Schmidt Plur. 219 (on the alternation *u: *ū); Osthoff Morph. Unt. IV 272–274, 351; Kretschmer KZ XXXI 337; Bartholomae 1088–1089; Brugmann IF XXXIII 175; Torp-Falk 298; Trautmann BSW 201; Holthausen AEEW 239; Feist 371, 380; Mayrhofer II 175–176; Pokorny I 770; Fraenkel 509–510; Vries ANEW 412; Frisk II 325; Onions 616; Lühr MSS XXXIV 80–81; Lehmann GED 264, 269; Kluge-Seebold 590, 593–594; Trubachev ESSJa XXVI 50–51; Adams TB 347; Burlak IFTJa 247.
ON g-nótt ‘abundance, plenty’, OE ze-nyht id. (fem., neut.), OHG gi-nuht id. Derived from *naxa (with a secondary analogical vowel). Torp-Falk 289; Vries ANEW 180; Seebold 355; Bammesberger Nom. 142.
Goth arbi-numja ‘heir, inheritor’, OHG nōt-numeo ‘robber’. Derived from *nemanan. Feist 56; Seebold 358; Bammesberger Nom. 182.

ON her-numi ‘captive’, fullnumi ‘practised, experienced’, OE irfe-numa ‘heir’, OFris erf-noma id., OHG sigi-nōmo ‘winner’. Derived from *nemanan. Holthausen AEEW 239; Vries ANEW 412; Seebold 358; Bammesberger Nom. 177.

Goth andanumts ‘acceptance’, OHG numft ‘robbery’. Goes back to *nemanan. Bahder Verbalabstr. 72 (Goth -mt- < *-mft-); Schulze KZ XLII 92; Brugmann Grundriß I 386 (new formation); Feist 47; Seebold 358; Lehmann GED 35 (agrees with Bahder).
ON noräan ‘from the North’, OE noräan id., MLG norden ‘northwards’, OHG nordana id. Derived from *nurþaz. Holthausen AEEW 238; Vries ANEW 411.
ON Nor-egr ‘Norway’, OE norä-wez ‘way to the North’. Cf. also MLat Northwegia. Compound of *nurþaz and *wezaz. Vries ANEW 411.

ON norär ‘North’, OE adv. norä ‘northwards’, OFris adv. north id., OS adv. north id., OHG nord ‘North’ (masc., neut.). Related to Gk νέρ-θε(n) ‘from beneath’, νέρτερος ‘underground’, Umbr nertru ‘sinistro’ (Bugge BB III 105: together with Skt naraka- ‘hell’, Gk đneroi ‘those below, those beneath the earth’). Torp-Falk 295; Holthausen AEEW 238; Huisman KZ LXXI 100; Pokorny I 765–766; Chemodanov SGGJa I 108; Vries ANEW 411; Frisk I 514–515; Onions 614; Kluge-Seebold 591.
ON norär ‘northwards’. ## ō
ON not ‘use, utility’, E to note ‘useful’. Derived from *neutanan. Seebold 361.
ON nyt-samr ‘useful’, MLG nut-sam id., OHG nuzzi-sam id. Compound of *nutiz and *samaz. Carr Nom. comp. 91 (parallel formations).

Goth un-nuts ‘useless, foolish’, ON mál-nytr ‘yielding milk’, OE nytt ‘useful’, OFris nette id., OS nutti id., OHG nuzzi id. Cf. also ON nÿtr ‘fit, usable’ < *neutjaz. Related to *neutanan. Karsten NB I 91; Torp-Falk 299; ō

OE ófer ‘edge, border, margin’, MLG ōver ‘bank, shore’, MHG uover id. Close to Gk ἤπειρος id. < *āperƒos (Lottner KZ VII 180). Torp-Falk 28; Holthausen AEEW 240; Pokorny I 53; Frisk I 640; Kluge-Seebold 845.

ON œfa ‘to exercise’, OS ōbian ‘to celebrate solemnly’, OHG uoben ‘to practice’. Related to Skt āpas- ‘work, religious action’, ápas- ‘work, action’, Av āpah- ‘working’, Lat opus ‘work’. Torp- Falk 16; Walde-Hofmann II 217–218; Mayrhofer I 38, 75; Pokorny I 780; Vries ANEW 684; Heidermanns 431.

ON œfr ‘vehement, angry’, MHG uober ‘active’. Related to *ōbjanan. Torp-Falk 16; Pokorny I 780; Vries ANEW 684; Heidermanns 431. *ōza pret.-pres.: Goth og ‘to fear’. Created on the basis of vestigial *azan. Torp- Falk 9; Feist 380–381; Pokorny I 7; Seebold 362; Lehmann GED 270.

Goth ogjan ‘to frighten, to scare’, ON œgja id., OE on-ézan ‘to Wissmann NP 59–60; Holthausen AEEW 239; Feist 522; Vries ANEW 413; Onions 605; Seebold 361; Bammesberger Nom. 242, 260; Matzel Festschr. Schröder 96, KZ CV 101; Heidermanns 429–430; Kluge-Seebold 594.

ON nyt ‘profit, use’, OE nytt ‘use, advantage, profit’, OHG nuz ‘use’ (masc.). Related to *neutanan. Torp-Falk 299–300; Holthausen AEEW 239; Vries ANEW 413; Seebold 361; Lehmann GED 269; Bammesberger Nom. 112.

ON nytja ‘to milk’, OE nyttian ‘to make use, to enjoy’, OHG nuzzōn ‘to utilize’. Derived from *nutjō. Torp-Falk 299; Holthausen AEEW 239; Vries ANEW 413; Heidermanns 430. fear’. Causative of *ōza, *azanan. Torp- Falk 9; Holthausen AEEW 244; Feist 380; Jóhannesson IEW 17–18; Pokorny I 7; Vries ANEW 684; Seebold 362; Lehmann GED 270.
ON (h)óstr ‘throat’, OE óhsta ‘armpit’. Derived from *axsō. Svedrup IF XXXV 155; Holthausen AEEW 243; Porzig Gliederung 110; Pokorny I 6; Vries ANEW 421.

ON óss ‘river mouth’, OE ór ‘beginning, origin’ (neut.), óra ‘bank’ (masc. n-stem). Related to Skt ās- ‘mouth’, Av āh- id., Lat ōs id., OIr á id. Bartholomae 345; Torp-Falk 29; Holthausen AEEW 241; Walde- Hofmann II 224–225; Mayrhofer I 84; Pokorny I 784; Vries ANEW 421.

Burg *oþil ‘estate’, ON óäal ‘nature, property, hereditary property, inheritance’, OE masc. óäel ‘home, native country’, éäel ‘residence, property, inheritance’ (also masc.), OFris ēthel id., OS ōthil id. (also masc.), OHG uodil ‘property’. Cf. also Goth utal
p

OSwed padda ‘toad’, ME padde id., MLG padde, pedde id. Probably related to Lat badius ‘brown, chestnutcolored’, OIr buide ‘yellow’. Uhlenbeck PBB XXII 199 (to Gk βάτραχος ‘frog’); Walde-Hofmann I 92; Martinet Gém. 186; Pokorny I 92; Vries ANEW 422; Onions 640; Lühr Expr. 299; Kluge- Seebold 607.

Goth paida ‘tunic, shirt’, OE pád ‘coat, cloak’, OS pēda ‘garment’, OHG pfeit ‘shirt’. Borrowed from *baitā, an Oriental word attested in Gk βαίτη ‘coat made of leather’ (Thumb Zd Wf VII 261–263). Bonfante BSL XXXVI 151– 152 (from Illyrian, cf. Alb petk ‘clothes, garment’); Zupitza Gutt. 24; Karsten Germanen 195; Torp-Falk 217; Holthausen AEEW 244; Feist 381–382; Krogmann KZ LXXI 121–123; Pisani Sprache I 138; Pokorny I 92–93 (related to Gk βαίτη); Zalizniak I 147; Frisk I 210–211; Lehmann GED 271; Orel AED 317 (Alb petk derived from petë ‘layer’); Kluge-Seebold 624.
Swed pamp ‘fat person’, G dial. pfampf id. Of imitative origin. Cf. Lith bámba ‘navel’, Latv ba Mba ‘ball’. Pokorny I 94; Vries ANEW 422 (from IE *bemb-).

ON penningr ‘penny’, OE peninz, pendinz id., OFris penning, panning id., OS penning id., OHG pfenning, pfending id. If derived from neut. *panđan ON óäal-torfa ‘patrimonial land’, OE éäelturf ‘native soil’. Compound of *ōþalan ~ *ōþilan and *turban ~ *turbō(n). Carr Nom. comp. 55.

Goth haim-oþli ‘patrimonial land’, ON œäli ‘family estate’. Derived from *ōþalan ~ *ōþilan. Feist 233; Vries ANEW 684. > OFris pand ‘pledge’, OS pant id., OHG pfant id. (Schröder ZDADL XXXVII 124–127), *panđinzaz could be reconstructed. However, this etymology is problematic since forms in -nđ- are not the earliest. Connected with *pannōn > OE panne ‘pan’, OFris panna id., OS panna id., OHG pfanna id.? Holthausen AEEW 244; Onions 659 (*panđan < OFr pan < Lat pannus ‘piece of cloth’), 665; Knobloch Lingua XXVI 311; Kluge- Seebold 624–625 (based on Lat pondus ‘weight’); Boutkan OFED s.v.

OE pæä ‘path’, OFris path id. (neut.), MLG pat id., OHG pfad id. Borrowed from Iran dial. *paþa- id., cf. OPers acc. sg. payim (Brøndal APS III 1–3; Benveniste BSL LVIII 41–43). Torp-Falk 217 (to Lat baetō ‘to go’); Holthausen AEEW 244; Pokorny I 809; Onions 657; Mayrhofer KZ LXXXIV 224–230; Greule KZ XCIV 208–217; Meid Germ. Rek. 110 (from Celt *batos < *g ⁄6 tos); Lanzsweert Festschr. Knobloch 223–230 (to Lith gãtvë ‘path’); Kluge- Seebold 623.

ON pinni ‘pin’, OE pinn id., OS pinn id., OHG pfin ‘nail’. Cf. MIr benn ‘horn, point, tip’ < *b–d-no-. Torp-Falk 218; Holthausen AEEW 246; Pokorny I 96–97; Onions 681 (from Lat pinna, penna ‘wing, feather’); Kluge-Seebold 633.

ON páll ’hoe, spade, pale’, OE pál ‘pale, pole, stake’, OFris pāl id., OS pāl id., OHG pfāl id. Believed to be a borrowing from Lat pālus id. Unclear. Holthausen AEEW 244; Onions 693; Kluge-Seebold 623.

Goth peika-bagms ‘palm tree’, OE píc ‘point, pike’. Usually believed to be a loan from Rom *pīcus (cf. *picāre ‘to prick’). Unclear. Johansson KZ XXXVI 383; Much PBB XVII 33–34 (Goth form from Lat fīcus ‘fig’ via Celtic); Torp-Falk 218 (to Skt neut. bīja- ‘seed’); Holthausen AEEW 246; Feist 383; Onions 680; Lehmann GED 272.
Swed pipa ‘to whistle’, MLG pipen id., MHG phifen id. Of imitative origin. Seebold 363.
ON pípa ‘pipe’, OE pípe id., OFris pīpe id., MLG pīpe id., OHG pfīfa id. Derived from *pīpanan and influenced by Rom *pīpa id. Holthausen AEEW 246; Onions 683.
ON plagg ‘luggage’, MLG plagge ‘shred, rag’. Borrowed from an unknown source, probably continuing *plo˚o-, to IE *ple˚- ‘to tear’, see *flaxanan. Torp-Falk 222; Pokorny I 835.

Goth neut. or masc. plat ‘patch’. Cf. MLG plet ‘rag, piece’ < *platja-. Borrowed from an unknown (Balto- Slavic?) source connected with Skt pṛthú- ‘wide’, Av p 6 r 6 yu- id., Gk πλατύς id., Lith platūs id. Stokes Urkelt. 255 (connected with Ir lonta ‘linen’); Bartholomae 892–893; Torp-Falk 222; Johansson KZ XXXVI 372–373; Feist 383–384 (from Slavic); Pokorny I 833; Fraenkel 606; Frisk II 553–554; Martynov Jazyk 23–24 (from Slav *polta ‘patch’); Lehmann GED 273 (same as Martynov).

OE pleón ‘to risk, to expose to danger’, OS plegan ‘to be responsible for’, OHG pflegan id. Of unknown origin. Cf. also *plextiz > OE pliht ‘danger, damage’, OFris plicht ‘care, protection’, MLG plicht ‘duty, obligation’, OHG pfliht ‘duty, pledge’. Sütterlin BB XVII 166 (to Lat bu-bulcus ‘herdsman’); Lagercrantz KZ XXXVII 178 (contra Sütterlin); Zupitza Gutt. 25; Torp-Falk 221–222; Holthausen AEEW 247; Onions 689.
Norw dial. plitt ‘quarter-deck (of a ship)’, MLG plicht ‘small deck (of a ship)’, OHG pflihta ‘prow’. Probably from *plezanan ~ *plexanan. Torp-Falk 222.
ON plógr ‘produce, gain’, MLG plōg ‘acquisition’, MHG pfluoc ‘trade, sustenance’. If not a metaphoric usage of *plōzuz ~ *plōzaz, may be derived from *plezanan ~ *plexanan. Torp-Falk 222.

ON plógr ‘plow’, OE plóz ‘land that can be plowed by one yoke of oxen in a day’, OFris plōch ‘plough’, MLG plōg id., OHG pfluog id. Of uncertain origin. If continues *blōkócould be compared, rather shakily, to Arm pelem ‘to dig’, W bwlch ‘crack’ < Celt *bolko-. Meringer IF XVII 109–110 (to Lat plaumorati ‘Rhetian plough’); Zupitza Gutt. 25; Uhlenbeck PBB XXXV 176–177; Torp-Falk 222; Holthausen AEEW 248; Trier PBB LXVII 131–136 (to *plezanan ~ *plexanan); Pokorny I 96; Vries ANEW 426; Zalizniak II 203; Martynov Jazyk 24–25 (from Slav *plugъ ‘plough’); Onions 689; Knobloch Slavica VIII 117–120 (from IE substratum); Pisani Iu E 48–50; Kluge-Seebold 626– 627 (to Gk εὐλάκα ‘plough’).
Norw plug ‘peg, small wedge’, MLG plugge ‘wooden nail’, MHG pflock ‘peg’. Despite the anlaut, related to Lith plūkiu, plūkti ‘to strike, to hew’. Torp-Falk 222; Fraenkel 632.

OE pól ‘pool’, OFris pōl id., MLG pōl id., OHG pfuol id. Cf. Norw dial. pøla id. < *pōljōn. Related to Lith balā ‘swamp, marsh’, Latv bala ‘woodless valley’, Slav *balъka ‘ravine’ (Zupitza KZ XXXVII 390), hardly connected with IE *bhel- ‘white’. Uhlenbeck PBB XVII 439–440 (to Lith báltas ‘white’, Slav *bolto ‘marsh’); Torp-Falk 218; Holthausen AEEW 248; Pokorny I 119; Onions 696; Vasmer-Trubachev I 115–116; Kluge- Seebold 627. *pranz(j)anan str.⁄wk.vb.: Goth anapraggan ‘to press upon, to oppress’, Du prangen ‘to pinch’, MHG phrengen ‘to hem in, to drive into a corner’. Related to *pranzō. Fick GGA 1894 32 (to Lat breuis ‘short’); Torp-Falk 221; Feist 43; Pokorny I 103; Onions 646, 715; Seebold 364; Stang LS 15–16; Lehmann GED 32.

OSw prang ‘narrow street’, ME prange ‘narrowing’, MLG prange ‘pinching instrument, pole’, OHG *pfranga ‘pole’. Probably to be compared with Toch AB präṅk- ‘to stay away’ (Krause Toch. 13). Johansson KZ XXXVI 346 (to Gk βρόγχος ‘throat’); Zupitza Gutt. 25–26; Persson Beiträge 870; Grienberger Got. 23 (to Lith brañktas ‘part of harness for a horse’, brangā ‘knee (in a boat)’); Torp-Falk 221 (to Slav *pręgti); Feist 43; Pokorny I 103; Fraenkel 54; Stang LS 15–16 (to Lith brangūs ‘dear, costly’); Lehmann GED 32.

ON prjónn ‘knitting needle’, OE preón ‘pin, brooch, fastening’, MLG prēn ‘puncher, awl’. Related to Lith briaunā ‘edge, keel’ < *breunā. Bugge ANF XXII 128–129; Torp-Falk 220; Holthausen AEEW 249; Pokorny I 170 (reconstructs *bh-); Fraenkel 57 (Lith briaunā to *brūwō); Vries ANEW 428; Hamp KZ XCVI 95–100 (dubious Gmc parallels); Lehmann GED 275.
Icel prik ‘point’, OE pricu id., masc. pricu id. Of unknown origin. Torp-Falk 221 (to Lith brëžīs ‘scratch, crack’).
Icel prika ‘to prick’, OE prician id. Derived from *prikan ~ *prikōn. Torp-Falk 221.

ON poki ‘bag, pouch’, ME pocce id., MDu poke id. Of uncertain origin. Torp-Falk 219; Holthausen AEEW 248; Pokorny I 100 (to IE *beu- ‘to swell’); Vries ANEW 427 (to Lat bucca ‘cheek, mouthful’); Onions 691, 692 (the ME form is from Northern OFr poque, poke).

ON sbst. pollr ‘pool, pond’, EFris pol ‘round, full’, MLG sbst. pol ‘head, tree top’. Despite the anlaut, might be derived from *p¬Hno- ‘full’ or borrowed from an unknown source continuing this IE form. Torp-Falk 220 (from *pulna-, to Lat bulla ‘bubble, boss, knob’); Pokorny I 799–800; Chemodanov SGGJa I 83 (to Lith balā ‘swamp’).

Goth puggs ‘purse, small bag’, ON pungr id., OE punz id., MLG punge id., OHG scaz-pfung ‘money-bag’. Probably connected with *pukōn. Uhlenbeck PBB XX 44 (from Slav *pǫgy ‘tassle’); Grienberger Got. 170–171 (probably from Rom *punga ‘bag’ < MGk); Johansson KZ XXXVI 367 (to Skt baka- ‘a kind of heron (?)’); Torp- Falk 219; Meyer-Lübke REW 567; Holthausen AEEW 250; Feist 385 (from an unknown source); Pokorny I 100; Zalizniak I 134; Corazza Lat. 66, 77 (from MLat punga ‘purse’); Gamillscheg RG 28–29 (MLat punga < Gmc); Lehmann GED 274.

ON pors ‘a kind of onion, Myrica gale’, MLG pors id. Borrowed from an unidentified IE source related to Lat porrum ‘onion’, Gk πράσον id. < *pṛsom. Torp-Falk 220 (to Gk βειρόν, βερρόν: δασύ, Hes., Lat burra ‘shaddy garment’); Walde-Hofmann II 343.

ON posi ‘little bag’, OE posa, pusa ‘bag’, OHG pfoso id. Of uncertain origin. Probably connected with *pukōn, *punzaz, *pūsaz or borrowed from Celtic, cf. MIr búas id. Torp-Falk 220; Johansson KZ XXXVI 357; Holthausen AEEW 248; Pokorny I 101; Vries ANEW 427; Zalizniak I 154.
Icel pota ‘to stick’, OE potian ‘to push, to thrust’. Any connection with *bautanan? Torp-Falk 219.
ON pottr ‘pot’, OE pott id., OFris pot id., MLG pot, put id. Cognate of or, rather, early borrowing from Lat buttis ‘barrel’. Torp-Falk 219; Kluge- Seebold 643.
ON pyttr

ON rafr ‘amber’, NFris reaf id. Derived from *rebanan. Semantically based on the magnetic power of amber, cf. Toporov PJa II 215 (on Balt *gintarto Lith gīnti ‘to defend’, giñti ‘to drive (away)’). Much Zd Wf II 285 (related to *erpaz); Pokorny I 334; Vries ANEW 431.

ON rǫä ‘row’, MLG rat id. Connected with *raþan ‘wheel’. Fick III 337 (to *raþjanan ~ *raþōjanan); Torp- Falk 337 (to Lith rėdas ‘order’); Holthausen AWN 235 (to Lat ratis ‘raft, float’); Pokorny I 59; Vries ANEW 457.

ON rafr ‘fat and fins of a flounder’. Unless a metaphoric usage of *rabaz, related to IE *rep-: Gk ἐρέπτομαι ‘to feed on’, Alb rjep ‘to strip off, to tear off’, Lat rapiō ‘to seize and carry off, to snatch’, Lith rėpiu, rėpti ‘to grasp’ (Bezzenberger GGA 1898 555). Torp- Falk 337; Walde-Hofmann II 417; Pokorny I 865; Frisk I 553; Orel AED 372.
ON refill ‘tapestry, hangings’, EFris rafel ‘fibre’, Du rafel id. Derived from *rafaz. Torp-Falk 337.

ON refsa ‘to punish’, OE ræpsan ‘to prove (?)’, OS respian ‘to punish, to chasten’, OHG refsen ‘to punish, to rebuke’. Based on an s-stem (cf. Skt rápas- ‘defect, injury’) related to Gk ἐρέπτομαι ‘to feed on’, Lat rapiō ‘to seize, to grasp’, Alb rjep ‘to strip off(skin or bark), to tear off’, (< Lat puteus); Pokorny I 99; Vries ANEW 430; Kluge-Seebold 628.

ON púss ‘small bag, purse’, LG pūse id. Probably related to Gk βυνέω < *βυσ-ν-έω ‘to fill up’, Alb mbush ‘to fill’ < PAlb *en-busa. See *pusōn. Torp-Falk 220 (to Skt busa- ‘chaff’); Pokorny I 101. Lith rėpiu, rėpti ‘to grasp’. (Unwerth PBB XXXVI 31). See *rafaz. Fick III 338; Bezzenberger BB III 73; Torp-Falk 338; Holthausen AEEW 256; Walde- Hofmann II 417–418; Pokorny I 865; Fraenkel 721–722; Vries ANEW 436; Frisk I 553.
ON raptr ‘rafter’, OE ræfter id., MLG rafter ‘small beam’. Related to *rēfan ~ *rēfōn. Torp-Falk 338; Holthausen AEEW 253; Vries ANEW 434.

Goth ragin ‘advice, decision’, ON pl. regin ‘gods’ (= *‘advisors’), OE rezn-weard ‘mighty guard’, OS raginō giskapu ‘divine destiny’, OHG prop. Reginfrid. Connected with Toch A rake, B reki ‘word’, Slav *rekti ‘to speak’. Zupitza Gutt. 136; Torp-Falk 335; Sturtevant JEGP XV 251; Holthausen AEEW 256; Feist 392; Pokorny I 863; Vries ANEW 436–437; Vasmer-Trubachev III 478; Lehmann GED 280; Burlak IFTJa 254.
Goth raginon ‘to govern, to counsel’, ON regna ‘to swear’, OE reznian ‘to set in order, to arrange’. Derived from *razinan. Holthausen AEEW 256; Feist 392; Vries ANEW 437; Lehmann GED 280.

ON rá ‘sail-yard, pole’, MLG rā ‘sail-yard’, OHG raha ‘shuttle’. Derived from *rezanan. Can be further compared with Lith rãkas ‘term’, Latv raks ‘aim, boundary’ (Fraenkel 121). Torp- Falk 335; Pokorny I 863; Chemodanov SGGJa I 73; Vries ANEW 430 (to ON regg ‘a kind of ship’); Kluge-Seebold 664.
ON pl. reifar ‘swaddling clothes’. Related to *rifan (Torp-Falk 345). Pokorny I 858.
ON reifa ‘to swaddle, to enrich, to rip up, to disclose’, OE á-ráfian ‘to unrove, to unwind’. Derived from *raibō. Torp-Falk 345; Holthausen AEEW 254; Vries ANEW 437 (to

Goth ga-raiþs ‘arranged, determined’, ON greiär ‘ready, free, straight’, OE ræde ‘ready’, OFris rēd, rēde id., MLG rēde id., OHG gi-reiti id. Derived from *rīđanan (Schubert Erw. 60: from *raiđō). Close to OIr réid ‘level, easy’, W rhwydd ‘easy’ (Vendryes R- 16–17), Lith raidūs ‘upright’, Latv raids ‘ready’ (Persson Beiträge 856–857; Zupitza KZ XXXVI 66). Karsten NB I 107; Torp-Falk 344; Feist 197, 393; Holthausen AEEW 252–253; Pokorny I 60; Fraenkel 687–688; Vries ANEW 186, 437; Lehmann GED 280–281; Lloyd-Springer I 283–285; Heidermanns 433–434; Kluge-Seebold 98; Adams TB 531 (adds Toch AB rätk- ‘to heal’).
Goth ga-raideins ‘regulation, rule’, OE ræden ‘condition, stipulation, rule’. Derived from *raiđjanan. Holthausen AEEW 253; Feist 197; Bammesberger Nom. 148.

Goth raidjan ‘to direct, to mandate’, ON reiäa ‘to bring, to serve, to make ready, to make to ride, to carry’, OE ze-rædan ‘to arrange, to dispose, to direct’, OHG bi-reiten ‘to prepare’. Derived from *raiđaz, *rīđanan. Torp- Falk 344; Holthausen AEEW 252; Feist 393; Pokorny I 60; Chemodanov SGGJa I 76; Vries ANEW 437 (to Latv riest ‘to arrange’); Lehmann GED 280–281 Heidermanns 434.

ON reiä ‘riding, raid, vehicle’ (pl. -ar, -ir reflecting *raiđiz), OE rád ‘riding, road’, OFris rāf-rēd ‘ride on a stolen horse’, OS brande-rēda ‘andiron, fire-dog’, OHG reita ‘wagon’. Derived from *rīđanan. Cf. Gaul rēda ‘wagon’, OIr ríad ‘driving, riding’. Torp-Falk 344; Holthausen AEEW 252; Porzig Gliederung 120; Pokorny I 861; Chemodanov SGGJa I 97; Vries ANEW 437; Zalizniak II 194; Onions 770; Seebold 367; Bammesberger Nom. 107.

ON rá-merki ‘landmark’, OE ræw, ráw ‘row, line’. Cf. also MLG rige ‘row, order’, OHG riga ‘line’ < *rizō. Identical with Skt rekhā ‘stroke, line’. Further to Skt rikháti ‘to scratch’, Gk ἐρείκω ‘to rend, to bruise’, Lith ri‚kti ‘to cut bread’. Torp-Falk 343; Mayrhofer III 58; Pokorny I 862; Fraenkel 729; Vries ANEW 430; Frisk I 551–552; Onions 775.
ON rá-bukkr ‘roebuck’, MLG rē-bok id., OHG rēh-boc id. Compound of *raixōn and *bukkaz. Carr Nom. comp. 84 (parallel formations).

ON rá ‘roe’ (fem., from *raixō), OE rá, ráha id., OS rēho id., OHG rēho id. Of unknown origin. Suolahti ANT 109–112; Petersson PBB XL 101 (to Skt ṛ≤ya- ‘deer’); Torp-Falk 332 (to Ir ríach ‘grey’); Holthausen AEEW 254; Pokorny I 859; Vries ANEW 430 (to Ir ríach ‘grey’, Lith raĩbas ‘multicolored’); Zalizniak II 183; Onions 771; Witczak KZ CVII 141–142 (to Gk ζόρξ ‘roebuck’, W iwrch id.); Kluge-Seebold 675.

OE ræcan ‘to reach’, OFris rēka id., MLG reiken, rēken id., OHG gi-reihhen id. Identical with Lith ráižytis ‘to keep stretching’ further related to OIr rigim ‘to stretch’, Lith réižu, réižti ‘to stretch, to tighten’ (Zupitza Gutt. 198– 200). Wiedemann BB XXVIII 58 (to Lith rėžti ‘to cut’); Wood MLN XXVI 166 (same as Wiedemann); Persson Beiträge 150, 941; Torp-Falk 342; Vendryes MSL XV 363–365 (also to Lat rigeō ‘to be stiff’); Trautmann BSW 242; Holthausen AEEW 252; Pokorny I 862; Fraenkel 715; Vries ANEW 438; Onions 742.

ON rein ‘strip of land’, MLG rēn, rein ‘balk, ridge as a boundary’, OHG rein ‘demarcation line on the ground’. Identical with OIr roen ‘way, mountain chain’, Bret rūn ‘hill’ < *roino- (Fick II 234) and further related or borrowed to Slav *rînь ‘sandbank’. Persson Beiträge 769; Pedersen Kelt. Gr. I 58; Persson Beiträge 773; Torp-Falk 332; Pokorny I 857–858; Vries ANEW 438– 439 (to *raixō); Vasmer-Trubachev III 470.

Goth skaudaraip ‘thong, strap’, ON reip ‘rope’, OE ráp id., OFris rāp id., MLG rēp ‘sling, rope’, OHG reif ‘rope, cord’. Of unknown origin. Zupitza Gutt. 27; Torp- Falk 345; Holthausen AEEW 255; Feist 430; Pokorny I 858; Vries ANEW 439 (to *rainō ~ rainaz); Zalizniak II 172; Onions 773; Lehmann GED 310 (to Gk ἐρίπνη ‘cliff’, Lat rīpa ‘bank’); Kluge- Seebold 676.
ON reipa ‘to fasten with a rope’, OE ræpan ‘to bind with a rope’ MLG rēpen ‘to measure’. Derived from *raipan ~ *raipaz. Holthausen AEEW 253; Vries ANEW 439.

Goth reiran ‘to tremble’, OE rárian ‘to wail, to roar’, MLG rāren, rēren ‘to roar’, OHG rēren id. Based on an old perfect reduplication *rai-rēi- as in Skt lelāyati ‘to move to and fro, to quiver, to tremble’ (Flasdieck Anglia LIX 139, 182; Jasanoff Stative 71–72). Bugge IF V 176 (Goth from Arm ererem ‘to tremble’); Torp-Falk 341 (separate Goth from WGmc); Holthausen AEEW 255; Feist 397; Mayrhofer III 112; Pokorny I 862; Chemodanov SGGJa I 75; Narten Sprache XXVII 10–12 (reconstructs *reir-); Lehmann GED 285–285; Kluge-Seebold 690.

Goth ur-raisjan ‘to raise, to rouse’, ON reisa ‘to raise’, OE ræran ‘to rear, to raise’. Causative of *rīsanan. Torp-Falk 345– 346; Holthausen AEEW 253; Feist 527; Vries ANEW 439; Onions 744; Seebold 372.
late ON reisa ‘journey’, OFris reise id., OHG reisa id. Derived from *rīsanan. Torp-Falk 346; Seebold 372.
ON reita ‘to stir’, OHG reizen ‘to stir, to irritate’. Causative of *rītanan. Torp-Falk 343.

ON pl. rǫk ‘reason, ground, origin, sign’, OE racu ‘explanation, account, narrative’, OS raka ‘situation, talk’, OHG rahha ‘thing, talk’. Substantivized *rakaz. Torp-Falk 333; Holthausen AEEW 251; Pokorny I 856; Vries ANEW 458; Zalizniak II 175; Lehmann GED 280.
ON rak ‘rakings of hay in a field, wick’, OE racu ‘hollow path, bed of a stream’. Derived from *rakaz. Or identical with *rakan ~ *rakō I? Torp-Falk 333; Holthausen AEEW 251; Vries ANEW 432.

ON rakr ‘straight’, MLG adv. rak id. Derived from *rekanan. Cf. *rankaz. Formally can be equated with Lat rogus ‘funeral pile, grave’ < *ro·os (Frisk GHÅ XLIV/1 24). Karsten NB I 71; Zupitza Gutt. 198; Torp-Falk 333; Pokorny I 856; Vries ANEW 432; Heidermanns 436.
ON pl. rekendr ‘chain’, OE racente id., OHG rahhinza id. Derived from *rakaz. Torp- Falk 333 (to *rakkōn II); Holthausen AEEW 251; Vries ANEW 440.
Swed raka ‘rake’, OE raca id. (masc.), racu id., OS raka id. Related to *rekōn II. Torp-Falk 334; Kluge- Seebold 671.

Goth uf-rakjan ‘to stretch’, ON rekja ‘to spread out, to unfold’, OE reccan ‘to stretch’, OFris rētza ‘to reach, to give’, OS rekkian ‘to explain’, OHG recken ‘to reach, to guide, to explain’. Derived from *rekanan (Gonda KZ LXXIII 162–163). Siebs KZ XXXVII 278–279 (to *strakkjanan); Torp-Falk 333; Holthausen AEEW 256; Feist 513; Vries ANEW 440 (to Lat regō ‘to keep straight, to guide, to draw the boundaries’); Onions 735; Seebold 373; Lehmann GED 284; Kluge-Seebold 672.
ON rekkja ‘bed’, MLG recke ‘rack’. Derived from *rakjanan. Vries ANEW 441.
ON raka ‘to rake, to sweep away’, MLG raken id. Derived from *rekanan. Torp-Falk 334; Vries ANEW 432; Lehmann GED 284.
ON rakki ‘dog’, OE ræcc ‘dog that hunts by scent’. Related to *rakjanan. Uhlenbeck PBB XXVI 570; Holthausen AEEW 252; Martinet Gém. 180; Vries ANEW 432.

ON rakki ‘ring by which the sail-yard moves round the mast’, OE racca ‘cord which forms part of the rigging of a ship’. Derived from *rakaz. Holm ANF LXIX 195–209 (to *rakkōn I); Torp-Falk 332; Holthausen AEEW 251; Martinet Gém. 193; Pokorny I 863 (to Skt ra≤anā ‘rope, cord, bridle’); Chemodanov SGGJa I 106; Vries ANEW 432.

Burg *rams ‘strong; ram’, ON rammr ‘strong, mighty’, OE sbst. ramm ‘ram’, MLG sbst. ram id., OHG sbst. ram id. Related to *remiz. Karsten NB I 67, II 230; Torp-Falk 339 (to OIr remor ‘thick, fat’), 340 (*rammaz ‘ram’ to Lith ėras ‘lamb’, Lat ariēs ‘ram’); Holthausen AEEW 254; Martinet Gém. 175; Vries ANEW 432; Onions 737; Köbler 712; Heidermanns 436; Kluge- Seebold 665.

MLG rame ‘stand, frame’, OHG rama, hrama ‘pillar’. Derivationally close to Lith pã-rama ‘prop’. Based on IE *rem-: OIr fo-rim- ‘to put’, Lith remiū, reṃti ‘to support’. See *rammaz. Torp-Falk 339; Heinertz SVSL VII 108–119 (to *xram(m)janan); Pokorny I 864; Fraenkel 718; Vries ANEW 432–433; Zalizniak II 210; Lehmann GED 285.
ON sbst. rani ‘snout’, MHG adj. ran ‘slender, slim’. Derived from *rennanan (Persson SVSU 1912/10 769). Pokorny I 329 (to OIr rind ‘top’); Vries ANEW 433.

Burg *rands ‘edge of a shield’, ON rǫnd ‘border, rim, shield’, OE rand ‘brink, edge, margin, shore, shield’, OFris rād-rond ‘edge, rim, shield’, OS rand id., OHG rant ‘shield buckle, edge’. See *renđō. Torp-Falk 339 (from *ram-đ-, to *ramō); Holthausen AEEW 254; Pokorny I 864; Zalizniak II 210; Vries ANEW 458; Onions 738; Lehmann GED 286; Köbler 712; Kluge-Seebold 665–666.
Norw rand ‘cross-beam’, G dial. rante ‘bar’. Identical with *ranđiz. Torp-Falk 339 (to Lith ra Mtis ‘prop, banister’).

ON rakkr ‘straight, slender’, OE ranc ‘proud, haughty’, MLG rank ‘long and thin’. Related to Skt ṛñjati, ṛjyati ‘to stretch’, Lith rëžti, ràžyti ‘to tighten, to elongate’ (Holthausen IF XXXII 337). Karsten NB I 53–54; Zupitza Gutt. 198; Torp-Falk 334; Szober PF XIV 601– 602 (to WSlav *rǫ‘ь ‘fast’); Trautmann BSW 244; Holthausen AEEW 254; Mayrhofer I 121; Pokorny I 857; Fraenkel 711, 726; Vries ANEW 432; Onions 738–739; Heidermanns 437; Kluge-Seebold 666.
ON rekkja ‘to strain, to stretch out’, OE á-rencan ‘to make proud’. Derived from *rankaz. Holthausen AEEW 257; Vries ANEW 441.

Goth ur-rannjan ‘to make emerge, to make rise’, ON renna ‘to run’, OE ærnan ‘to run’, OS rennian ‘to gather’, OHG gi-rennen ‘to coagulate’. Causative of *rennanan. Similar to Slav *roniti ‘to pour, to drop’. Torp-Falk 17; Trautmann BSW 236–237; Holthausen AEEW 12; Feist 527; Vries ANEW 442; Seebold 376; Vasmer-Trubachev III 501; Lehmann GED 285; Kluge-Seebold 680.

ON rǫskr ‘brave, vigorous’, ME rash id., MLG rasch ‘quick, fast’, OHG adv. rasco id. May be directly related to Thrac prop. ἙΡάσκος (Duridanov Trakite 65). Torp-Falk 341 (to *razđō and further to Skt rasati ‘to roar’); Kluge-Seebold 667 (to *raþaz or

Goth rasta ‘mile’, ON rǫst ‘measure of length between two restplaces’ (pl. i-stem), OE rest ‘rest, quiet’, OS rasta ‘rest, place of rest’, OHG rasta ‘rest, league’. Of uncertain origin. Aufrecht KZ I 360; Fick KZ XXII 375 (to *rōwō); Meringer IF XVIII 237 (to *raznan II); Wood MLN XXV 74 (to Gk ἐραστός ‘charming’); Torp-Falk 340 (to OIr árus ‘dwelling’); Holthausen AEEW 254; Feist 394; Pokorny I 338–339; Vendryes A-90 (to OIr áros ‘residence’); Vries ANEW 458; Zalizniak II 217; Onions 760; Lehmann GED 281 (follows Fick); Bammesberger Nom. 116–117 (from *raþjanan ~ *raþōjanan).

OFris reth ‘wheel’, OS rath id., OHG rad, hrad id. Related to Skt rátha- ‘chariot, car’, Av raya- ‘chariot’, Lat rota ‘wheel’, OIr roth id., Lith rãtas id. Bartholomae 1506; Torp-Falk 336; Walde-Hofmann II 443–444; Mayrhofer III 38–39; Pokorny I 866; Fraenkel 703; Kluge-Seebold 663.

Goth comp. raþizo ‘simpler, easier’, MLG ge-rade ‘immediate, quick’, MHG ge-rat, ge-rade ‘growing straight upwards’. Historically identical with *raþan (Torp-Falk 336). Schwentner PBB XLVIII 83–84; Feist 394 (against Torp-Falk); Holthausen AEEW 252; Mühlenbach-Endzelin III 514 (to Skt ṛtí- ‘row, quarrel’, Gk đriw ‘strife, quarrel’); Pokorny I 866; Vries ANEW 457; Lehmann GED 282; Bammesberger Nom. 238; Heidermanns 437–438.

Goth ga-raþjan ‘to count’, OFris rethia ‘to talk’, OS reäiōn id., OHG redōn id. Back formation based on *raþjō(n) (Kluge- Seebold 673). Torp-Falk 336 (to Lat rēor ‘to reckon, to believe’); Feist 197; Lehmann GED 282.

Goth raþjo ‘number, counting, account, explanation’, OFris rethe ‘speech, word, account’, OS rethia ‘account’, OHG reda ‘speech, account’. Identical with Lat ratiō ‘reckoning, account’ (Feist 394) based on rēor ‘to reckon, to calculate, to believe’. Breal MSL VII 137–138 (from Lat ratiō); Torp- Falk 336 (derived from *raþjanan ~ *raþōjanan); Pokorny I 59 (against Breal); Walde-Hofmann II 419–420; Schulze Kl. Schr. 511; Chemodanov SGGJa I 90; Lehmann GED 281–282 (to *rađan in *xunđa-rađan); Kluge-Seebold 673.

ON reyfa ‘to rob’, OE be-rÿfan ‘to bereave’, OHG roufen ‘to pull, to pluck’. Derived from the unattested str. *reubanan, cf. *raubō ~ *raubaz. Torp-Falk 352; Holthausen AEEW 259; Vries ANEW 442; Seebold 379.

ON rauf ‘hole, rift’, OE neut. réaf ‘spoil, booty’, OFris rāf ‘loot, booty’, OS rōf ‘booty’, OHG roub id. Related to Skt rúpyati ‘to suffer racking pain’, Lith raupiū, ra Upti ‘to dig’, rupūs ‘rough’. Particularly close to Skt neut. ropa- ‘hole, cave’, Slav *rupa ‘pit’. Wadstein IF XIV 402–406; Torp-Falk 352; Trautmann BSW 240; Holthausen AEEW 255; Būga RFV LXXV 141–142; Specht KZ LXVIII 125; Mayrhofer III 68–69; Pokorny I 870; Fraenkel 707–708, 750; Vries ANEW 435; Onions 744; Seebold 379; Lehmann GED 72; Bammesberger Nom. 53; Vasmer-Trubachev III 518; Kluge- Seebold 669; Boutkan OFED s.v.

Goth bi-raubon ‘to rob’, ON raufa ‘to rob, to spoil’, OE réafian ‘to plunder, to spoil, rob’, OFris rāvia id., OS rōbon id., OHG roubōn id. Based on *raubō ~ *raubaz. Torp-Falk 353; Holthausen AEEW 255; Feist 94; Vries ANEW 435; Onions 89–90, 744; Seebold 379; Lehmann GED 71–72; Kluge-Seebold 669; Boutkan OFED s.v.
ON rauä-brúnn ‘red-brown’, OHG rōt-brūn-pfellīn ‘of redbrown fabric’. Compound of *rauđaz and *brūnaz. Carr Nom. comp. 92 (parallel formations).

Goth rauþs ‘red’, ON rauär id., OE reád id., OFris rād id., OS rōd id., OHG rōt id. Derived from *reuđanan. Related to OIr rúad id., Lat rūfus id. (< *roudhos, with dialectal features), Lith ra Udas id., Slav *rudъ id. Bugge KZ XX (1872) 6–7; Karsten NB I 46; Schwentner FB 44–54; Torp-Falk 351; Būga RFV LXXV 141; Trautmann BSW 238; Feist 395–396; Holthausen AEEW 257; Walde-Hofmann II 448; Jóhannesson IEW 714–715; Porzig 194; Pokorny I 872–873; Fraenkel 704–705; Vries ANEW 435; Onions 748; Seebold 378; Vasmer-Trubachev III 513; Risch Festschr. Szemerényi 708–709; Lehmann GED 282–283; Bammesberger Nom. 238; Heidermanns 438–439; Kluge-Seebold 693; Boutkan OFED s.v.

ON rauäi ‘red metal’, OHG rōto ‘red trout’. Derived from *rauđaz. Cf. Slav *ruda ‘ore, blood’ of the same origin. No connection with Sumer urudu ‘copper’ (see *arutaz). Scherer Urh. Idg. 296; Birkhahn Gu K 141; Kilian Urspr. 34; Trubachev VJa XXXIV/5 3–5; Heidermanns 439.

ON reykr ‘smoke, steam’, OE réc ‘smoke’, OFris rēk id., OS rōk id. (<*raukaz), OHG rouh id. (<*raukaz). Derived from *reukanan. Zupitza Gutt. 164; Torp-Falk 349 (to Alb re ‘cloud’ < *rougi-); Richter KZ LV 138–149; Holthausen AEEW 259; Porzig Gliederung 177; Pokorny I 872 (to *reu-g- ‘to vomit’); Vries ANEW 443; Zalizniak II 188; Onions 749; Seebold 380; Bammesberger Nom. 133; Kluge-Seebold 669.
ON reykja ‘to smoke’, OE récan id., OFris rēka, rēza id., MLG roken id., OHG rouhhen id. Derived from *raukiz. Holthausen AEEW 259; Vries ANEW 443.

ON raun ‘trial, experiment, experience’, OFris rān ‘attempt, trial’. An archaic ablaut grade related to *rūnō. Bugge KZ XX (1872) 9–10; Torp-Falk 349 (to Gk đreuna ‘inquiry, search’); Pokorny I 337; Chemodanov SGGJa I 110; Vries ANEW 435.

Goth raupjan ‘to pluck’, OE rípan ‘to spoil, to plunder’, MLG rōpen ‘to reap, to pluck’. Despite the irregular *-p-, seems to be connected with *reufanan. Wood MLN XVI 310 (to Lat rubus ‘blackberry’); Lewy PBB XXXII 141 (to Lith ru Mbas ‘scar on the tree bark’); Zupitza Gutt. 35; Torp-Falk 352; Holthausen AEEW 259; Feist 395; Specht KZ LXVIII 125; Lehmann GED 282.

Goth raus ‘reed’, ON reyrr id. (masc.), MLG rōr id., OHG rōr id. Derived from *rūjanan. Uhlenbeck ANF XV 157 (to Skt roṣa- ‘anger’); Hoffmann BB XXV 106–108 (to Gk ὄροφος ‘pipe’); Hirt PBB XX 234 (to Slav *rogozъ ‘rush’); Torp-Falk 332 (same as Hirt); Bugge PBB XIII 334; Feist 395; Pokorny I 332; Machek 420 (together with Slav *rogozъ from pre-IE); Vries ANEW 443; Pisani Iu E 47–48; Lehmann GED 282 (from non-IE source); Kluge-Seebold 690 (to MLG rūschen ‘to sough’, MHG rūschen id.).

OHG rōz, hrōz ‘weeping, wailing’. Identical with Skt róda- id., cf. also Lith raudā ‘lamentation’. Derived from *reutanan I. Cf. also ON fem. raust ‘voice’ < *raustō. Torp-Falk 351; Mayrhofer III 77; Pokorny I 332, 867; Fraenkel 704.
ON reyta ‘to pluck, to pick’, MLG rétin ‘to make rot (of flax)’, MLG rōten id., MHG rœzen ‘to make rotten’. Causative of *reutanan II. Torp- Falk 350.

Goth razda ‘language, speech’, ON rǫdd ‘voice’ (partly < *razđiz), OHG fogal-rarta ‘bird’s song as an omen’. Cf. also OE fem., neut. reord ‘speech, tongue, language, voice’ < *rezđō ~ *rezđan. Probably, goes back to *redhdhē- similar to *˚er dhē- ‘to believe’. See *rēđanan. Schweizer KZ I 154; Grienberger Got. 175 (to Gk =όθος ‘sound, noise’); Torp-Falk 340; Holthausen AEEW 257–258; Feist 396 (to *rēđanan or to Skt rásati ‘to roar, to yell, to sound’); Pokorny I 852; Vries ANEW 457; Zalizniak II 194; Lehmann GED 283.

Goth razn ‘house’, ON rann id., OE ærn ‘place, habitation, house’, OFris fīa-ern ‘cattle shed’. Continues *rodh-sno- related to Slav *rodъ ‘kin’, Latv rads id. Aufrecht KZ I 360; Meringer IF XVI 142 (to *rastaz); Torp-Falk 340; Trautmann BSW 234; Holthausen AEEW 12; Feist 396; Pokorny I 339 (to *er H- ‘to rest’); Trubachev VJa VI/2 88 (against traditional comparisons of the Balto-Slavic word with Skt várdhati ‘to increase’); Vries ANEW 433; Zalizniak I 143; Onions 75, 760; Vasmer- Trubachev III 490–491; Lehmann GED 283.
Goth ga-razna ‘neighbor’, ON g-ranni id. Derived from *raznan. Feist 197; Vries ANEW 184.
MHG reben ‘to move, to stir’. Probably related to Lat rēpō ‘to crawl’, OPrus rīpaiti ‘to follow’. Trautmann BSW 246; Pokorny I 865, 853 (to MIr reb ‘game, perfidy’ < *rebhā).

ON rif ‘rib’, OE ribb id., OFris rib id., OS ribbi id., OHG ribbi id. Closely connected with Slav *rebro id. and identical with *(ko-)rebьje ‘rib, skeleton’. Torp-Falk 338; Holthausen AEEW 258, AWN 228; Schulze KZ XLII 233, Kl. Schr. 372; Pokorny I 30, 853; Chemodanov SGGJa I 80; Vries ANEW 444; Zalizniak I 145; Onions 766; Stang LS 44; Vasmer-Trubachev III 453; Trubachev ESSJa XI 49–50 (reconstructs *ko-rebrьje); Kluge-Seebold 688.

MHG regen ‘to rise’. Related to *er(e)·h- in Gk đrxomai ‘to come’, Alb aor. erdha ‘to come’, and probably also OIr imper. eirg ‘to go’. Sarauw KZ XXXVIII 160 (on Celtic); Torp-Falk 334; Pokorny I 327–328, 863; Frisk I 572; Orel AED 508.
ON regn-bogi ‘rainbow’, OE rezn-boza id., OFris rein-boga id., MLG regen-boge id., OHG regan-bogo id. Compound of *reznan ~ *reznaz and *buzōn. Torp-Falk 273; Carr Nom. comp. 51; Kluge-Seebold 674.

Goth rign ‘rain’, ON regn id., OE rezn id. (masc.), OFris rein id. (masc.), OS regan-, regin id. (masc.), OHG regan id. (masc.). Similarity with Lat rigō ‘to moisten, to irrigate’ and Alb rrjedh ‘to flow’ deserves attention (Hoffmann BB XXVI 136: also a wrong comparison with Gk βρέχω ‘to wet, to rain’; Walde-Hoffmann II 435). Gmc *reznan ~ *reznaz could be then a derivative in *-no-. Zupitza Gutt. 136; Torp- Falk 335–336; Trautmann apud Feist (from *re˚-no-, to ON raki ‘dampness, moisture’); Holthausen GRM VIII 368 (to Gk =όχθος ‘roar, thunder’), AEEW 256; Walde-Hofmann II 435; Feist 397; Pokorny I 857; Ernout-Meillet 573–574 (isolated character of Lat rigō); Chemodanov SSGJa I 55; Zalizniak II 172; Vries ANEW 432, 437 (follows Trautmann); Onions 737; Schulze Kl. Schr. 476; Lehmann GED 284; Kluge- Seebold 674; Orel AED 387.
ON regn-skúr ‘shower’, OE rezn-scúr id. Compound of *reznan ~ *reznaz and *skūrō. Carr Nom. comp. 78 (parallel formations).
Swed regn-orm ‘earthworm’, OE rezn-wyrm id., OS regan-wurm id., OHG regan-wurm id. Compound of *reznan ~ *reznaz and *wurmaz ~ *wurmiz. Carr Nom. comp. 51.
Goth rignjan ‘to rain’, ON regna, rigna ‘to rain’. Derived from *reznan ~ *reznaz. Torp-Falk 336; Holthausen AEEW 260; Vries ANEW 437, 445; Lehmann GED 284.
ON rétt-ligr ‘just, due’, OFris riuchte-lik ‘just, fair, legal’, MDu reht-līk ‘just, fair’, OHG rehtlīh ‘just, right (of faith)’. Derived from *rextaz. Heidermanns 442.

Goth raihts ‘straight’, ON réttr ‘upright, straight’, OE riht ‘straight, right’, OFris riucht ‘right, correct’, OS reht ‘good, true, right’, OHG reht ‘straight’. Close to Av rašta- ‘straight (way)’, Gk ὀρεκτός ‘stretched out’, Lat rēctus ‘straight, upright’, OIr recht ‘law, authority’ (Vani‘ek 228–229). Derived from *rekanan. Bartholomae 1516; Karsten NB II 327; Torp-Falk 333–334; Feist 393; Holthausen AEEW 260; Walde-Hofmann II 424; Hoops EG V 84–86; Gonda KZ LXXIII 161; Porzig Gliederung 122; Pokorny I 854–857; Vries ANEW 442; Frisk II 413; Onions 767; Benveniste IEL 309, 311; Seebold 373; Lehmann GED 281; Bammesberger Nom. 252; Heidermanns 441–442; Kluge-Seebold 672.
ON rétting ‘correction’, OE rihtunz ‘direction, guidance, correction’, OHG rehtunga ‘direction, justification’. Derived from *rextaz. Torp-Falk 334.
Goth ga-raihtei ‘justice, righteousness’, ON rétti ‘straight direction’, OS rihti ‘canon, rule’, OHG rehtī ‘order, justice’. Derived from *rextaz. Feist 197; Vries ANEW 442; Heidermanns 441.
ON van-rétti ‘inflicted wrong’, OHG in rihti ‘straight ahead, directly’. Derived from *rextaz. Heidermanns 441.

Goth ga-raihtjan ‘to direct, to make straight’, ON rétta ‘to make straight, to make straight’, OE rihtan ‘to right’, OFris riuchta ‘to direct, to speak the truth’, OS rihtian ‘to uplift’, OHG rihten ‘to direct’. Derived from *rextaz. Torp-Falk 334; Holthausen AEEW 260; Feist 197; Vries ANEW 442; Lehmann GED 281; Heidermanns 442; Kluge-Seebold 685.

ON réttr ‘right, law’, OE riht ‘straight object, perpendicular, right, law’, OFris riucht id., MLG richt id., OHG reht id. Substantivized adjective *rextaz. Derivationally cf. OIr recht ‘law’ < *rektu-. Torp-Falk 334; Holthausen AEEW 260; Porzig 122; Vries ANEW 442; Onions 767; Lehmann GED 281.

Goth rikan ‘to heap up, to rake up’, ME reken ‘to stoke a fire’, OFris reka ‘to gather’, MLG reken ‘to stoke a fire’, MHG rechen id. Connected with Toch AB räk- ‘to extend, to cover’, Skt ṛjyati ‘to stretch’, ṛñjati id., Lat regō ‘to direct in a straight way’, Gk ὀρέγω ‘to stretch’, OIr rigim ‘to stretch out’, Lith ρἅἤιυ, ρἅἤτι ‘to tighten, to elongate’ (Meringer IF XVII 145). Zupitza Gutt. 198; Torp-Falk 334; Feist 397–398; Holthausen AEEW 255; Walde- Hofmann II 426–427; Mayrhofer I 121; Pokorny I 854–856; Fraenkel 711, 726; Frisk II 412–413; Onions 737; Seebold 373–374; Lehmann GED 284–285; Kluge-Seebold 671; Adams TB 529–530.
OE recen ‘ready, prompt’, OFris rekon ‘clear, open’. Derived from *rekanan. Derivationally close to MIr rigin ‘stiff, rigid’ (Holthausen IF XX 329). Holthausen AEEW 256; Pokorny I 856; Onions 746.
ON naut-reki ‘herdsman’, OHG anut-rehho ‘drake’. Probably derived from *rekanan. Torp-Falk 333 (directly to Lat rex ‘king’, OIr rí id.); Bammesberger Nom. 178.

ON reka ‘shovel, spade’, OS reka ‘rake’, OHG masc. rehho id. Derived from *rekanan. Cf. *rakō. Zupitza Gutt. 198; Torp-Falk 334; Holthausen AEEW 251; Vries ANEW 440; Onions 737; Lehmann GED 284; Kluge-Seebold 671.

Goth riqis ‘darkness’, ON rǫkkr ‘twilight’. Continues IE *reg ⁄os: Skt rájas ‘dust, mist, darkness’, Gk đrebow ‘darkness of the underworld’, Arm erek ‘evening’ (Bopp apud Aufrecht KZ I 355). Cf. also Toch A orkäm, B orkmo ‘darkness’ < *H(o)rg ⁄-mon-. Kuhn KZ I 380; Grienberger PBB XIX 527–530; Zupitza Gutt. 18, 93; Torp-Falk 334; Feist 399–400; Mayrhofer III 34–35; Pokorny I 857; Frisk II 431–432; Vries ANEW 456; Seebold 374; Lehmann GED 286; Bammesberger Nom. 212; Adams TB 123.

Goth rimis ‘rest, calmness’. Close to Gk -remow in ἤρεμος ‘peaceful’, comp. ἠ-ρεμέσ-τερος (an es-stem as in Gmc), cf. further cognates in Skt rámate ‘to calm, to stop, to rest’, Lith re Mti to support’, rīmti ‘to be quiet’ (Nesselmann apud Aufrecht KZ I 358). Zimmer KZ XXIV 212; Torp-Falk 339 (to *ramō); Brugmann IF XV 103 (against isolating ἠ- in Gk); Trautmann BSW 243–244; Feist 398; ’op KZ LXXIV 228; Mayrhofer III 44–45; Pokorny I 864–865; Fraenkel 695, 718; Frisk I 643; Chantraine 416; Lehmann GED 285.
ON rim ‘rail’, EFris rim ‘rafter’. See *ramō. Torp-Falk 339; Vries ANEW 446.
ON rimi ‘strip of land’, OE rima, reoma ‘edge’. Related to *ramō. Torp-Falk 339; Holthausen AEEW 260; Onions 768.
Norw rind ‘stripe’, OE rind, rinde ‘bark’, OS rinda id., OHG rinta id. See *ranđō ~ *ranđiz. Torp-Falk 339; Holthausen AEEW 261; Vries ANEW 458; Onions 768.

Goth un-mana-riggws ‘untamed, wild (of a person)’, OFris adv. ring ‘quick, fast’, MLG ringe ‘small, easy’, OHG ringi ‘small’. Identical with Gk adv. =ίμφα ‘easy, light, deft, adroit, swift’ unless it continues *ϝρέμφα ~ Lith rangūs ‘agile, nimble’ (Schwyzer GG 275). Zupitza Gutt. 100; Grienberger Got. 228 (to OE reów ‘fierce, cruel’); Holthausen IF XX 328 (to Lat ruō ‘to hurry’); Feist 522; Pokorny I 332, 1155 (from *⁄ren·hu-); Frisk II 656–657; Heidermanns 445–446; Kluge-Seebold 316 (to Lith ”rangūs ‘rapid, actively working’).

Burg *rinks ‘warrior’, ON rekkr ‘straight, upright man’, OE rinc ‘man’, OS rink ‘man, hero, warrior’, OHG rinc, hrinc id. Related to *rankaz (Holthausen IF XXXII 337). Holthausen AEEW 261; Vries ANEW 441; Köbler 713; Lerchner 225; Heidermanns 437.

Goth rinnan ‘to run’, ON rinna, renna id., OE irnan id., OFris rinna id., OS rinnan id., OHG rinnan id. Continues *ren-w- < *re-nu- related to Hitt arnu- ‘to set in motion, to bring’, Skt ṛṇóti ‘to arise, to move’, Av 6 r 6 naoiti ‘to move’, Gk ὄρνυμαι ‘to begin’, Lat orior ‘to rise, to come forth’ (Kuhn KZ II 460). Persson Wurzelerw. 25, Beiträge 768–772 (to Slav *rinǫti ‘to thrust’, *roniti ‘to drop’); Bartholomae 183–184; Torp-Falk 17; Feist 398–399; Holthausen AEEW 261; Walde-Hofmann II 222–223; Martinet Gém. 69, 105; Kuiper Nasalpräsentia 94 (on similarity with Skt raṇvati, pres. subj. of ṛṇóti); Prokosch CGG 85 (to Skt ríṇvati ‘to cause to flow’); Jóhannesson IEW 63–67; Mayrhofer I 122; Pokorny I 326–332; Vries ANEW 441–442; Rix IF LXX 25–49; Onions 777; Frisk II 422–424; Chantraine 824; Seebold 375–376 (from *ṛ-n⁄- and in connection with *rei- ‘to flow’); Lühr MSS XXXV 73–92 (*-nn- < *-n H-); Lehmann GED 285–286; Kluge-Seebold 688.
Goth rinno ‘torrent, watercourse’, OHG rinna ‘cataract’. Derived from *rennanan. Feist 399; Seebold 375.

ON rjóäa ‘to redden, to besmear with blood’, OE reódan id., OHG riuten ‘to exterminate, to destroy’. See *rauđaz. Torp-Falk 351–352 (separate OHG); Holthausen AEEW 257; Pokorny I 869, 872–873; Vries ANEW 448; Seebold 377–378; Adams TB 538 (to Toch AB rutk- ‘to move, to remove’).

Goth ga-riuþs ‘respectable’, ON rjóär ‘ruddy, red’, OE reód ‘red, reddening’. Connected with *rauđaz, *reuđanan. Karsten NB I 31; Grienberger Got. 91–92 (for the semantic development cf. Slav *krasa ‘beauty’ ~ *krasьnъ ‘red’); Torp-Falk 351; Holthausen AEEW 257; Feist 199; Pokorny I 872–873; Vries ANEW 449; Onions 748; Seebold 378; Lehmann GED 148, 282; Bammesberger Nom. 240; Heidermanns 448.
ON rjóär ‘clearing’, OE ze-rieäre id. Derived from *reuđanan. Torp-Falk 352; Holthausen AEEW 259; Pokorny I 869; Vries ANEW 449; Kluge-Seebold 683.

ON rjúfa ‘to break, to rip up’, OE part. rofen ‘to break, to rend’. Related to IE *reup-: Skt rúpyati ‘to suffer racking pain’, Lat rumpō ‘to break’, Lith rumpū, rūpti ‘to become rough, uneven’. Zupitza Gutt. 35; Torp-Falk 352–353; Holthausen AEEW 257; Walde-Hofmann II 451; Jóhannesson IEW 707–711; Mayrhofer III 68–69; Pokorny I 868–871; Fraenkel 750; Vries ANEW 449; Onions 744; Seebold 378–379; Lehmann GED 72; Heidermanns 447–448.

ON rjúka ‘to smoke’, OE reócan ‘to reek, to smoke’, OFris riāka ‘to smoke’, MDu rieken, ruken id., OHG riohhan id. Despite the phonetic differences, related to Lith r~kti id., r~kas ‘smoke, fog’. See *raukiz. Zupitza Gutt. 164; Torp-Falk 349; Holthausen AEEW 257; Jóhannesson IEW 713; Pokorny I 871–872 (to Lith ráugti ‘to sour’); Fraenkel 747; Vries ANEW 449; Onions 749; Seebold 379–380; Lehmann GED 206; Kluge-Seebold 686.

ON rjómi ‘cream’, OE reóma ‘membrane, ligament’, OS riomo ‘thong, leather strap’, OHG riomo, riumo id. Cf. also MLG rōm, rōme ‘cream’, OHG roum id. (< *raumaz). Close to Lith raumuõ ‘muscle, lean meat’. Schwyzer IF XXI 180 (from *reuzmōn, to Av raogna- ‘butter’); Schröder PBB XLVII 164 (from *sreu- ‘to flow’); Torp-Falk 348 (from *rūjanan); Vendryes RC XLV 351 (to Lat rudēns ‘rope, line, cord’); Kluge Nom. Stamm. 46–47; Holthausen AEEW 255; Fraenkel 707 (Lith raumuõ < *raud-m-, to ra Udas ‘red-brown’); Vries ANEW 449; Onions 743, 767; Kluge-Seebold 664, 686–687.

Goth riurs ‘transient, mortal, perishable’, ON rÿrr ‘thin, poor’, OFris *riure ‘transient’. Derived from *rūjanan (Zimmer ZDADL XIX 450). Torp-Falk 348; Feist 400; Vries ANEW 455; Lehmann GED 286 (to *rūxwaz); Heidermanns 449.

Goth riurjan ‘to spoil, to corrupt’, ON rÿra ‘to make small, to depreciate’. Derived from *reuriz. Karsten NB II 447; Torp-Falk 348; Feist 400; Pokorny I 868; Vries ANEW 455; Lehmann GED 286; Heidermanns 449 (from *rei⁄-ro-, to Lat rīuus ‘stream’).

ON rjóta ‘to roar, to rattle’, OE reótan ‘to make a noise, to lament, to wail’, MLG rēten ‘to make a noise’, OHG riozan ‘to weep, to howl’. Related to Skt róditi ‘to weep, to roar’, Av raod- ‘to lament, to wail’, Lat rudō ‘to roar, to bellow’, Lith raudā ‘lamentation’, raudóti ‘to weep’, Slav *rydati ‘to cry’ (Brugmann II/1 458). Bartholomae 1492–1493; Torp-Falk 351; Trautmann BSW 239–240; Holthausen AEEW 258; Walde-Hofmann II 447; Jóhannesson IEW 705–707; Mayrhofer III 77; Pokorny I 867; Fraenkel 704; Vries ANEW 449; Seebold 380–381; Vasmer- Trubachev III 526–527.
ON part. rotinn ‘rotten’. Of unknown origin. Torp-Falk 350 (to Lat rūdus ‘rude mass, piece of brass’); Pokorny I 869.
ON ráä-bani ‘accessory to a murder’, OE ræd-bana id. Compound of *rēđan ~ *rēđaz and *banōn. Carr Nom. comp. 55.
ON ráä-gjafi‘counsellor’, OE ræd-zifa id., OFris rēd-ieva ‘judge’, OS rād-gebo ‘ruler’, OHG rāt-gebo ‘counsellor’. Compound of *rēđan ~ *rēđaz and *zebōn. Carr Nom. comp. 77 (parallel formations).

ON ráä-lauss ‘shiftless’, OE ræd-leás ‘ill-advised, unwise, inconsiderate’, OFris rēd-lās ‘without legal advice’, MDu ræd-loos ‘perplexed’, MHG rāt-lōs ‘ill-advised’. Compound of *rēđan ~ *rēđaz and *lausaz. Carr Nom. comp. 90 (parallel formations).

Burg *reþs ‘advice’, ON ráä ‘counsel, advice’, OE ræd id., OFris rēd id., OS rād id., OHG rāt id. Derived from *rēđanan. Torp-Falk 337; Holthausen AEEW 252; Pokorny I 60; Vries ANEW 430–431; Zalizniak II 171; Onions 748; Seebold 366; Lehmann GED 148; Köbler 713; Kluge-Seebold 668.

Goth ga-redan ‘to intend, to have a plan for’, ON ráäa ‘to advise’, OE rædan ‘to counsel, to ask advice’, OFris rēda ‘to advise, to help’, OS rādan ‘to take care of’, OHG rātan ‘to advise, to consider’. Related to Skt rādhnóti ‘to achieve, to prepare’, Av rādaiti ‘to prepare’, OIr imm-rádaim ‘to think over, to consider’, Lith ródyti ‘to show’, Slav *raditi ‘to think over, to care for’. Bartholomae 1520–1521; Torp-Falk 336–337; Pedersen Kelt. Gr. I 591; Trautmann BSW 235; Feist 199; Holthausen AEEW 252; Jóhannesson IEW 31–34; Mayrhofer III 54; Pokorny I 59–60; Fraenkel 741–742; Vries ANEW 431; Onions 743; Seebold 365–367; Vasmer- Trubachev III 430; Lehmann GED 148; Kluge-Seebold 668.
ON ráä-snotr ‘clever with advice’, OE ræd-snotor ‘wise in counsel’. Compound of *rēđan ~ *rēđiz and *snutraz. Carr Nom. comp. 61.

Goth adv. ga-redaba ‘respectably, commendably’, ON harä-ráär ‘hard in counsel, tyrannical’, OE ze-rád ‘considered, instructed, learned’, OHG ein-rāti ‘secret, isolated’. Derived from *rēđan ~ *rēđaz. Holthausen AEEW 252; Feist 198–199; Schubert Erw. 62; Seebold 366; Matzel KZ CV 129; Heidermanns 439–440.
ON ræäi ‘rule, management’, OE ræde ‘reading, lesson’ (fem.), OFris rēde ‘advice’, OS gi-rādi id., OHG gi-rāti ‘advice, secret’. Derived from *rēđan ~ *rēđaz. Holthausen AEEW 253; Vries ANEW 456.

ON ráf ‘roof ’, OHG rāvo, rāfo ‘beam, girder’. Related to Slav *rîpьjь ‘burdock’ (Fick BB VI 240). Torp-Falk 338; Holthausen AWN 222; Pokorny I 866; Vries ANEW 431; Vasmer-Trubachev III 471; Abaev III 364 (to Osset æræf ‘girder’); Trubachev VJa XXIV/1 133 (to Slav *stropъ ‘beam, girder’ < * sropъ < *˚râpo-); Lerchner 221.
OHG rām ‘dirt, soot’. Cf. also OE rómiz ‘blackened, sooty’. Identical with Skt rāmá- ‘black’. Pokorny I 853; Dybo VSJa 27; Kluge-Seebold 664.
ON rás ‘race, running’, OE masc. ræs id., MLG rās ‘strong current’. Related to Gk ἐρωή ‘quick motion, rush, force’. Torp-Falk 340; Holthausen AEEW 254; Pokorny I 336; Vries ANEW 434; Onions 735.

ON rása ‘to race, to run’, OE ræsan ‘to rush, to move violently’, MLG rāsen ‘to race, to rage’, OHG rāsen id. Derived from *rēsō ~ *rēsan. Torp-Falk 340; Holthausen AEEW 254; Vries ANEW 434, 456; Kluge-Seebold 668.
ON -riäi ‘rider’ (in compounds), OE tot-rida ‘swing (?)’. Derived from *rīđanan. Holthausen AEEW 259; Seebold 368.

ON rif ‘reef (a part of sail)’, MLG reffid. Related to Lith ripā ‘disk used by children to play with’, Latv ripa ‘ring, round disk’. On the other hand, cf. *rīfanan. Torp-Falk 345; Pokorny I 858; Fraenkel 737; Kluge-Seebold 674.
ON ript ‘a kind of cloth’, OE rift, rifte ‘veil, curtain, cloak’ (neut.), OHG bein-reft ‘trousers’ (masc.). Derived from *rīfanan. Torp-Falk 345; Holthausen AEEW 260; Vries ANEW 447.
Goth ur-rists ‘rising up, resurrection’, OE ærist id., OHG ur-rist id. Derived from *rīsanan. Holthausen AEEW 262; Feist 527; Bammesberger Nom. 139.
ON rista ‘to slice’, MLG risten id. Derived from *rīstanan. Torp-Falk 346; Vries ANEW 448 (to Skt ríṣyati ‘to be hurt, to be injured’).

ON ríäa ‘to ride’, OE rídan id., OFris rīda id., OS pret. pl. umbiridun ‘to ride around’, OHG rītan ‘to ride’. Related to OIr rédid ‘to ride’, MIr ríadaim id., W rhwydd-hau ‘to hurry’, Lith riedū, riedėti ‘to roll (also of a wagon)’, Slav *ridati ‘to move’. Torp-Falk 343; Holthausen AEEW 259; Kluge 594–595; Jóhannesson IEW 702–704; Porzig 120; Pokorny I 861; Fraenkel 729; Vries ANEW 444; Onions 767; Seebold 367–368; Lehmann GED 281; Goriacheva Etim. 1986–1987 156–157 (Slavic reconstruction); Kluge-Seebold 678.

ON rífa ‘to tear’, OFris rīva id. Related to Gk ἐρείπω ‘to throw, to dash down’ (Fick KZ XIX 263–264). Torp-Falk 344; Jóhannesson IEW 698–701; Pokorny I 858; Vries ANEW 445; Frisk II 552; Seebold 368; Kluge- Seebold 676, 686.

ON rífr ‘munificent, abundant’, OE rífe ‘rife, abundant’, MLG rīve ‘wasteful, easily available’. An alternative reconstruction is *rībaz. Derived from *rīfanan. Torp-Falk 345; Holthausen AEEW 260; Pokorny i 858; vries ANEW 445; Onions 767; Matzel KZ CV 95; Heidermanns 442–443.

WFris riuwe ‘to string’, MLG rigen id., MHG rīhan ‘to plait, to turn’. Related to Skt rikháti ‘to scratch’, Gk ἐρείκω ‘to bruise, to pound’. Mayrhofer III 58; Pokorny I 858; Frisk II 551–552; Kluge-Seebold 676.
ON rík-dómr ‘power, might’, OE ríce-dóm ‘power, rule, dominion’, OFris rīke-dōm ‘power, might’, OS rīki-dōm ‘richness, fortune’, OHG rīh-tuom id. Derived from *rīkjaz. Heidermanns 450.

Goth reiki ‘realm, authority’, ON ríki ‘power, might, empire’, OE ríce id., OFris rīke id., OS rīki id., OHG rīhhi id. Substantivized *rīkjaz or direct borrowing from Celtic, cf. MIr ríge ‘kingdom’ < *rē·ƒom. Torp-Falk 342; Feist 396; Holthausen AEEW 258; Trier NGWG 1943 535–537 (directly to *re·- ‘to fence’); Jóhannesson IEW 1136; Pokorny I 856; Vries ANEW 446; Zalizniak I 145; Vendryes R-25; Seebold 369; Lehmann GED 283; Bammesberger Nom. 67; Heidermanns 451; Kluge-Seebold 675– 676.
ON ríkja ‘to reign, to rule’, OHG gi-rīhhen id. Derived from *rīkjaz. Torp-Falk 342; Heidermanns 450.

Goth reikeis ‘noble, distinguished, princely’, ON ríkr ‘mighty, powerful’, OE ríce id., OFris rīk id., OS rīki id., OHG rīhhi ‘rich, mighty’. Derived from *rīkz. Torp-Falk 342; Feist 396–397; Holthausen AEEW 258; Pokorny I 856; Vries ANEW 446; Onions 766; Ross-Thomson IF LXXXI 176–179; Normier KZ XCI 187; Lehmann GED 293; Seebold 369; Heidermanns 450–451; Kluge-Seebold 676.

Goth reiks ‘ruler, lord, prince’. Borrowed from Celtic, cf. OIr ríg (Arbois de Jubainville MSL VII 288–290). Schweizer KZ I 559 (related to Skt rājan- ‘king’); Torp-Falk 342; Feist 396–397; Vries ANEW 446; Pokorny I 856; Evans BBCS XXIX 230–255; Onions 766; Ross-Thomson IF LXXXI 176–179; Lehmann GED 283; Bammesberger Nom. 199; Schmidt Zfcelt Ph XLI 382–384; Griepentrog Wurzelnomina 350–366.

ON rím ‘computation’, OE rím ‘number’, OS un-rīm ‘uncountable number’, OHG masc. rīm ‘row, number’. Identical with (or, more plausibly, borrowed from) OIr rím ‘number’, W rhif id. If *rīman continues an IE source, it is further connected with Toch B yärm ‘measure’, Gk ἀραρίσκω ‘to join together, to fasten’, ἀριθμός ‘number’ (Persson Beiträge 741–742). Torp-Falk 342; Holthausen AEEW 260; Pokorny I 60; Frisk I 139; Vries ANEW 446; Zalizniak II 171; Seebold 370 (on OHG str. pret. gireim); Kluge-Seebold 677.

OE repan ‘to reap’. A variant of *rīfanan? Cf. also WGmc adj. *rīpaz ~ *rīpiz: OE rípe ‘ripe, mature’, OS rīpi id., OHG rīf, rīfiid. Torp-Falk 344; Holthausen AEEW 261; Pokorny I 858 (to *rei- ‘to tear’); Onions 769; Seebold 371; Matzel KZ CV 95; Heidermanns 443–444; Kluge-Seebold 676.

Burg *ripa ‘slope, bank’, ON rípr ‘crag’, EFris rip, ripe ‘border, edge, bank, shore’, MHG rīf ‘bank, shore’. Connected with *rīfanan, *rīpanan. Despite the phonetic difficulties, it is tempting to compare it with Lat rīpa ‘bank’, W rhiw ‘slope, hill’ (in pln.). Loth RC XLIII 140; Torp-Falk 344– 345; Vries ANEW 447; Köbler 713.

Goth ur-reisan ‘to rise’, ON rísa id., OE rísan id., OFris rīsa id., OS rīsan id., OHG rīsan ‘to toss, to fall’. From *rīđ-s- related to *rīđanan. Torp- Falk 345; Wiedemann BB XXVIII 72 (to Lith rÿtas ‘morning’); Uhlenbeck PBB XXX 319 (to Lat rīuus ‘small stream of water, brook’); Il’inskii IORJa S XXIII/ 2 240–241 (to Slav *rîxati ‘to roam’); Feist 527–528; Holthausen AEEW 261; Jóhannesson IEW 63–67; Pokorny I 326–332 (to *er- ~ *or-); Vries ANEW 447 (to Skt riṇāti ‘to stream, to release’, Gk ὀρίνω ‘to stir, to raise’); Onions 769; Seebold 371–372; Kluge-Seebold 677.
ON rísta ‘to cut, to slash’. Related to *rītanan, *rīpanan. Torp-Falk 346 (to Skt ríṣyati ‘to be hurt, to be injured’); Pokorny I 859; Vries ANEW 448.

OSwed rita ‘to scratch’, MLG rīten ‘to rend, to tear’, OHG rīzan id. Related to *rīpanan. Persson Wurzelerw. 12, 102–103 (to Lat irrītō ‘to stir, to provoke’), Beiträge 773 (to Lat rīma ‘cleft, crack’ < *reid-mā); Torp-Falk 343.

OE ríä ‘small stream’, OS rīth ‘torrens’. Structurally close to Skt rītí- ‘stream’, W rhid ‘semen’, OIr riathor ‘torrens’, further to *(e)rei- ‘to move, to flow’ (Pedersen Kelt. Gr. I 67–68). Torp-Falk 341; Pokorny I 330.

ON róa ‘to row’, OE rówan id. Identical with OIr raid id., further related to Lith iriū, īrti id. Torp- Falk 346; Holthausen AEEW 264; Jóhannesson IEW 67–68; Pokorny I 338; Fraenkel 187; Vries ANEW 450; Onions 775; Seebold 381–382; Kluge- Seebold 691.

MLG rōve ‘beet’, OHG ruoba id. Close to Lith rópë id., Lat rāpum id., Gk =άπυς id., =άφυς id. An old cultural loanword (Hoops Waldbäume 350). Slav *rîpa id. goes back to the same source but, in view of its vocalism (hardly resulting from ablaut), must have been borrowed separately. Torp-Falk 347; Petersson GL 32; Trautmann BSW 237; Walde-Hofmann II 418; Pokorny I 852; Fraenkel 743; Frisk II 645; Gindin Etim. 55–63 (from Thrac *rãph- ‘beet’).

Goth rodjan ‘to talk, to speak’, ON rœäa ‘to speak’, OFris rēda id. Identical with OIr no-ráidiu ‘to say’, Lith ródyti ‘to show, to point to’, Slav *raditi ‘to care for’. Connected with *rēđanan. Torp-Falk 347; Feist 400; Trautmann BSW 235 Porzig 122 (from Celtic); Pokorny I 59; Chemodanov SGGJa I 68, 100; Fraenkel 741–742; Vries ANEW 457; Schulze Kl. Schr. 476; Scardigli Goten 227 (on G reden < *raþjanan semantically influenced by *rōđjanan); Vasmer- Trubachev III 430; Lehmann GED 286–287.

Gep *roda ‘rod’, ON róäa ‘rood, holyrood, crucifixion’, OE ród ‘pole, rod, cross’, OFris rōd, rōde ‘gallows’, OS rōda ‘pole, cross, crucifixion’, OHG ruota ‘branch, pole’. Of unknown origin. Curtius Gr. Et. 352 (to Lat rudis ‘stirring spoon’); Lewy KZ XL 422 (to Lat ratis ‘raft, float’); Torp-Falk 347 (to OCS ratište ‘lance shaft’); Holthausen AEEW 262; Pokorny I 866; Vries ANEW 450; Zalizniak II 180; Onions 772; Köbler 715; Kluge-Seebold 697 (to Lat rētae ‘trees standing on the bank’).

ON staf-róf ‘alphabet’, OE secz-róf ‘host of men (?)’, OHG ruoba ‘number, sum’ (fem.). Related to IE *rep-: Gk ἐρέπτομαι ‘to feed on’, Alb rjep ‘to strip off, to tear off’, Lat rapiō ‘to seize and carry off, to snatch’, Lith rėpiu, rėpti ‘to grasp’. For the semantic development cf. Lat numerus ‘number’ ~ IE *nem- ‘to take’. Holthausen AEEW 263; Pokorny I 865; Vries ANEW 450, 541 (to *rainō); Onions 749.

MLG rōke ‘care’, OHG ruoh id. Derivationally close to Gk ἀρωγή ‘help’. Related to *rekanan. Meringer IF XVII 144–145 (semantic development); Torp-Falk 346–347; Pokorny I 857; Seebold 374 (to Gmc *rēkanan > OHG part. fer-rāchen ‘to indulge in vice’); Lehmann GED 280.

ON rœkja ‘to take care of, to heed’, OE récan id., OS rōkian id., OHG bi-ruohhen id. Derived from *rōkaz. Zupitza Gutt. 198; Torp-Falk 347; Holthausen AEEW 262; Vries ANEW 457; Onions 746; Seebold 374; Lehmann GED 280, 284, 287; Heidermanns 452; Kluge-Seebold 317.
ON rœkr ‘legitimate’, OHG un-bi-ruoh ‘unheeded’. Derived from *rōkjanan. Heidermanns 452.

OE rót ‘glad, cheerful’. Identical with Slav *radъ id. (Uhlenbeck PBB XXII 536) Torp-Falk 347; Trautmann BSW 235; Holthausen AEEW 263; Pokorny I 853; Chemodanov SGGJa I 85; Vasmer-Trubachev III 429; Heidermanns 453.

ON róär ‘rowing’, OE róäer ‘rudder, oar’, OFris rōder id., MLG rōder id., OHG ruodar id. Derived from *rōanan. Cf. a similar derivational structure with a different ablaut grade in Skt arítra- ‘oar’. Torp- Falk 346; Kluge Nom. Stamm. 695; Holthausen AEEW 264; Pokorny I 338; Vries ANEW 450; Zalizniak II 203; Onions 775; Seebold 382; Bammesberger Nom. 87; Kluge-Seebold 695.
ON rœäri ‘rudder’, OE pl. ze-réäru ‘rudder, helm’. Derived from *rōþraz ~ *rōþran. Vries ANEW 457.
ON rór ‘calm, composed’, OE rów ‘quiet, calm, mild’. See *rōwō. Karsten NB I 197–198; Torp-Falk 346; Holthausen AEEW 264; Pokorny I 338; Vries ANEW 451; Heidermanns 454.

ON ró ‘rest, calm, quietness’’, OE rów ‘quiet, rest’, MLG rōwe id., OHG ruowa id. Another ablaut grade is attested in MLG rāwe id., OHG rāwa id. < *rēwō. Etymologically identical with Lith rovā ‘boulder left behind on a meadow after the flood’ and Gk ἐρωή (πολέμοιο) ‘calm of the battle’ (Fick KZ XXII 375; Schmidt KZ II 375–376). Torp-Falk 346; Holthausen AEEW 264; Pokorny I 338; Fraenkel 708 (Lith rovā—to ráuju, ráuti ‘to scuffle’); Vries ANEW 449; Zalizniak I 149; Frisk I 573; Lehmann GED 281; Heidermanns 454; Kluge- Seebold 695.
ON ruä ‘clearing’, OFris rothe id., OHG rod id. Derived from *reuđanan. Torp-Falk 352; Vries ANEW 452; Kluge-Seebold 690.
ON ryä ‘rust’, masc. ryär id., MHG rot id. Close to Slav *rъdja id. See *rauđaz. Torp-Falk 351; Vries ANEW 454.

ON roäa ‘to gleam red’, OHG rotēn ‘to turn red’. Derived from *ruđō(n). Cf. structural parallels in Lat rubēre ‘to be red’ and Slav *rudîti ‘to become red’ (< *roudh-). Torp-Falk 351; Holthausen AEEW 264; Walde- Hofmann II 444–445; Vries ANEW 450.
ON ryäja ‘to clear’, OE ryddan ‘to strip’. Derived from *reuđanan. Torp-Falk 352; Holthausen AEEW 265; Vries ANEW 454.
ON roäi ‘redness’, OE rudu id., MLG rode id. Related to *rauđaz. Holthausen AEEW 264; Vries ANEW 450; Lehmann GED 282; Bammesberger Nom. 110.

ON roära ‘blood’. Based on the unattested *ruđraz ‘red’ identical with Toch A rtär, B ratre, Gk ἐρυθρός id., Lat ruber id., Slav *rъdrъ id. Torp-Falk 351; Holthausen AEEW 265; Schwentner KZ LXXIII 110–111; Walde-Hofmann II 444–445; Pokorny I 872–873; Vries ANEW 450; Frisk I 567–568; Vasmer- Trubachev III 453; Schindler KZ LXXXI 71; Risch Festschr. Szemerényi 708–709; Heidermanns 454; Adams TB 526.

ON rugr ‘rye’, OE ryze id., OFris rogga id., OS roggo id., OHG roggo id. Identical with Lith rug‹s id., Slav *rъžь id. (Meyer BB XX 121; Fick BB XXIX 241; Hirt BB XXII 235). It is not clear if this group of words is related to Thrac βρίζα id. < *⁄rughƒā (indicative of the initial *⁄-). Hoops Waldbäume 447–461; Zupitza Gutt. 179; Torp-Falk 350; Charpentier ANF XLVI 63–73 (from Balt); Trautmann BSW 246; Holthausen AEEW 265, AWN 233; Porzig Gliederung 143; Senn KZ LXXI 186; Detschew Thr. 87–88; Pokorny I 1183; Chemodanov SGGJa I 82–83; Gindin Etim. 65 (from Thrac); Fraenkel 745–746; Vries ANEW 453; Zalizniak II 225; Onions 778–779; Stang LS 46; Vasmer-Trubachev III 493–494; Lühr Expr. 291; Kluge- Seebold 690.
ON rugla ‘to confound’, MHG rugelen ‘to stir’. Related to *rukkaz ~ *rukkōn. Torp-Falk 335; Pokorny I 869.
Norw dial. rok ‘current’, MLG roke ‘smell’, MHG ruch id. Derived from *reukanan. Torp-Falk 349.

ON rokkr ‘jerkin’, OE rocc ‘upper garment’, OFris rokk ‘skirt’, OS rok ‘overcoat’, OHG (h)roc id. Related to OIr rucht ‘tunica’ < *ruktu-, MW rhuch(en) ‘coat’. Torp-Falk 350; Holthausen AEEW 262; Martinet Gém. 192; Pokorny I 874; Chemodanov SGGJa I 99; Zalizniak I 134; Kluge-Seebold 689–690.

ON rokkr ‘distaff’, MLG rocken id., OHG roc, rocko id. Related to Skt luñcati ‘to tear, to pluck’, Gk ὀρύσσω ‘to dig up’, Lat runcō ‘to weed out, to root up’. Zupitza Gutt. 216 (to OIr rucht ‘tunica’); Holthausen AWN 231 (to *raxō(n)); Torp-Falk 349–350 (to OE renze ‘spider (?)’ and further to Gk ἀράχνη id., Lat arānea id.); Torp NNEO 541 (to *wranzaz); Walde- Hofmann II 453; Martinet Gém. 196; Mayrhofer III 105; Pokorny I 869; Vries ANEW 451; Frisk II 430–431; Onions 770; Kluge-Seebold 690 (from Rom *rotica derived from Lat rotō ‘to turn’).
ON rykkr ‘hasty pull’, MDu ruc ‘start, jolt’, OHG ruc ‘movement’. Related to *rukkjanan. Torp-Falk 335; Kluge-Seebold 694.

ON rykkja ‘to pull roughly and hastily’, MLG rucken ‘to rock’, OHG fram-rucken ‘to go away’. Related to *rukkaz ~ *rukkōn. Torp- Falk 335; Holthausen AEEW 262; Martinet Gém. 77; Vries ANEW 455; Onions 770; Kluge-Seebold 694.

ON rumpr ‘rump, buttocks’, MHG rump ‘body, beehive’. Identical with Lith ru Mbas ‘edge, border’, further related to Lith rambūs ‘lazy, dull’, Slav *rǫbъ ‘cut, edge’. Torp-Falk 336; Trautmann BSW 236; Pokorny I 864–865; Fraenkel 696.

Goth runs ‘race, running, course’, ON runnr ‘bush, grove’, runi ‘flux’ (n-stem), OE ryne ‘course, run’, OFris rene ‘blood trickling’, OHG run ‘walk, running’. Derived from *rennanan. Kuhn KZ I 380; Torp-Falk 17; Holthausen AEEW 265; Feist 401; Seebold 376; Lehmann GED 285, 288.

Goth ga-runs ‘street, marketplace’, OHG runs ‘river-bed, stream’ (masc., fem.). Derived from *run(n)iz. Based on an s-stem reflected in Skt neut. árṇas- ‘undulating, surging’. Feist 200; Mayrhofer I 51; Seebold 376.
ON rypta ‘to belch’, OHG roffezzen id. Cf. also ON repta id. < *rapatjan. Related to *raupjanan. Torp- Falk 337 (to Norw dial. rapa ‘to belch’); Pokorny I 871.
ON ruppa ‘to pluck’, ME ryppen id., MLG roppen id., OHG ropfōn id. Intensive of *raupjanan. Martinet Gém. 169; Vries ANEW 454; Lehmann GED 282.

Norw dial. ryskje ‘hair-grass’ (neut.), rusk id. (< *ruskō), OE rysc, rysce ‘rush, bulrush’, MLG rusch ‘reed, bulrush’. Related to Lith rūzginti ‘to gnarl, to murmur, to move, to touch’, ruzgūs ‘surly, sulky’. Torp-Falk 341 (to IE *rezg-: Lith rēgzti ‘to knit’, Skt rájju- ‘rope’); Pokorny I 868; Fraenkel 753.

Norw dial. rust ‘rust’, OE rust ‘rust’, OS rost id., OHG rost id. Etymologically connected with *rauđaz. Close to Skt neut. loṣṭa- ‘rust of iron’, Lith rūstas ‘brown-red’ (Johansson IF VIII 162–163). Torp- Falk 351; Holthausen AEEW 264; Mayrhofer III 118; Pokorny I 872; Fraenkel 752; Vries ANEW 452; Onions 778; Seebold 378; Bammesberger Nom. 77; Kluge-Seebold 692.
ON sbst. rot ‘concussion of the brain from a blow’, E rot, MDu rot ‘rotten’. Derived from *reutanan II. Vries ANEW 452.
ON roäa ‘to huddle together’, OFris rothia ‘to grub up, to clear’, MLG roden id., OHG rodōn ‘to stub, to root out’. Derived from *reuđanan. Vries ANEW 450.

OE rúh ‘rough’, MLG rūch id., OHG rūh id. Related to Skt luñcati ‘to tear, to pluck’, Lat runcō ‘to weed out, to root up’, Lith runkū, rūkti ‘to be wrinkled’. Zupitza Gutt. 137; Torp-Falk 350; Holthausen AEEW 264; Walde-Hofmann II 452; Mayrhofer III 105; Pokorny I 869–870; Fraenkel 747–748; Vries ANEW 454; Onions 774.
ON rÿja ‘rag’, OE rÿhe ‘rug, rough covering’, OS rūwi ‘rough skin’, OHG rūhī id. Derived from *rūxwaz. Torp-Falk 350; Holthausen AEEW 265; Lehmann GED 286.

ON rÿja ‘to pluck the wool offsheep’. Related to Toch AB ruwā- ‘to pull out’, Skt rávati ‘to shatter, to batter’, Lat ruō ‘to fall down, to rush down’, Lith ráuju, ráuti ‘to scuffle, to tear out’, Slav *rъvǫ, *ruti ‘to tear’ (Osthoff Morph. Unt. IV 28–29). Persson Beiträge 287–289; Torp-Falk 348; Trautmann BSW 247; Walde- Hofmann II 453–454; Pokorny I 868; Fraenkel 708–709; Adams TB 537; Dybo BSA 33–34.

Goth rum ‘room, space’, ON rúm id., OE rúm id., OFris rūm id., OS rūm ‘distance’, OHG rūm ‘room, space’. Based on *rūmaz. Torp-Falk 353; Holthausen AEEW 264; Feist 400; Pokorny I 874; Vries ANEW 453; Zalizniak II 171; Onions 773; Lehmann GED 287; Heidermanns 455; Kluge-Seebold 670.

Goth rums ‘roomy, spacious’, ON rúmr ‘roomy, ample, spacious’, OE rúm ‘roomy, spacious, ample’, OFris rūm ‘spacious’, MDu ruum ‘wide, broad’, OHG rūmi id. A form in *-mo- related to Av ravah- ‘open space’, Lat rūs ‘countryside’, OIr róe ‘field, open land’ (Fick I 118, 529; Osthoff Morph. Unt. IV 126). Further cf. Toch AB ru- ‘to open’. Sütterlin IF XLV 308 (from *rūzmaz, to Skt loká- ‘open space, place, world’); Bartholomae 1513; Karsten NB II 237–238, 456; Torp-Falk 353; Holthausen AEEW 264; Walde-Hofmann II 454; Feist 401; Pokorny I 874; Vries ANEW 453; Lehmann GED 287; Bammesberger Nom. 244–245; Heidermanns 455–456; Kluge-Seebold 670.

ON rÿma ‘to make room’, OE rÿman ‘to make roomy, to extend’, OFris rēma ‘to clear’, OS rūmian id., OHG rūmen ‘to give away, to weaken’. Derived from *rūman ~ *rūmaz. Holthausen AEEW 265; Vries ANEW 455; Lehmann GED 287; Heidermanns 456.
Goth Ruma ‘Rome’, ON Rúma-borg id., OHG Rūm-liuti ‘Romans’.
ON rúna-stafr ‘Runic letter’, OE rún-stæf id., OHG rūn-stab ‘letter, character’. Compound of *rūnō and *stabaz. Carr Nom. comp. 51–52.
Goth ga-runi ‘counsel, council’, ON rÿni ‘scrutiny, contemplation’, OE rÿne ‘mystery’, OS gi-rūni id., OHG ga-rūni id. Derived from *rūnō. Torp- Falk 348–349; Feist 199–200; Lehmann GED 287.

Goth runa ‘mystery, secret’, ON rún ‘secret, mystery, rune’, OE rún ‘whisper, mystery, secret, rune’, OS rūna ‘confidential talk, advice’, OHG rūna ‘mystery’. Directly related to (Vendryes R-53–54) or borrowed from Celtic (Marstrander NTS I 175–177): OIr rún ‘secret, mystery’, W rhin ‘secret, mystery, charm’, Bret rin ‘secret, wisdom’. Further might be connected with Gk ἐρέω, eǫromai ‘to ask, to seek’, ἐρευνάω ‘to find out’ (Feist 401). Cf. also *raunō. Torbiörnson Festschr. Noreen 255–257 (to Gk Οὔρανος ~ Skt Váruṇa-); Torp-Falk s

Goth sa ‘this, that’, ON sá id., OE se, sió id. Identical with IE *so id.: Skt sá ‘that, he’, Av hā, hō, Gk ὁ, OLat acc. sum ‘eum’. Corresponding feminine and neuter forms are *sō and *þat (the latter could be generalized): Goth so, þata, ON sú, þat, OE þæt, OFris thet, OS that, OHG daz. Brugmann Dem. 20–32, Grundriß II/2 313–321; Bartholomae 1718–1727; Torp-Falk 420; Holthausen AEEW 286; Feist 402; Walde-Hofmann II 628–629; Mayrhofer III 410; Pokorny I 978–979, 1086–1087; Vries ANEW 348 (to *rūjanan > OE rÿan ‘to roar, to rage’); Holthausen AEEW 264; Dumezil Mythes 24 (follows Torbiörnson); Arntz RK 294–296; Pokorny I 337, 867 (to *reu- ‘to roar’); Chemodanov SGGJa I 96; Vries ANEW 453–454; Zalizniak I 147; Frisk I 467–468; Onions 777; Polomé GPG 65 (to Hitt ḫurtaliƒa- ‘to chant a magical formula’); Lehmann GED 287–288; van Windekens MSS XLIX 152–153 (to Hitt ḫarwaši- ‘hidden, secret’); Morris PBB CVII 344–358 (separates *rūnō ‘mystery’ from *rūnō ‘rune’); Bammesberger Nom. 115; Kluge-Seebold 670, 696.
ON rúni ‘counsellor, friend’, OE ze-rúna ‘counsellor’, OHG gi-rūno id. Derived from *rūnō. Torp-Falk 348; Vries ANEW 454.
Norw dial. rūsa ‘weel, bownet, weir-basket’, MLG rūse id., OHG rūsa ‘fish-trap’. Related to *rausan ~ *rauzan. Torp-Falk 332; Pokorny I 332.
ON rÿta ‘to squeal (of boars and pigs)’, MDu ruten, ruyten ‘to grunt’, OHG rūzen id. Derived from *reutanan I. Vries ANEW 456. 459; Frisk II 342–343; Onions 914; Lehmann GED 289.

Goth saþs ‘satisfied, sated, full’, ON saär, saddr ‘sated’, OE sæd ‘sated, weary, filled’, OS sad ‘full’, OHG sat ‘full, sated’. Continues IE *s 6 tós, a participle in *-to- identical with Gk ἄ-ατος ‘insatiable’ < *–-s 6 to- based on *sā- ‘to satisfy’: Lat satis ‘enough, sufficient’, OIr sáith ‘satisfaction’, Lith sotūs ‘satisfied, replete’. Karsten NB II 306; Torp- Falk 421; Trautmann BSW 250; Holthausen AEEW 267; Walde-Hofmann II 481–482; Feist 411–412; Wissmann Postv. 67–68; Pokorny I 876; Fraenkel 857; Vries ANEW 459; Frisk I 2; Onions 781; Szemerényi KZ XCIII 103–107 (separates Goth saþs < *sađiz similar to Lat satis ‘repleteness’); Lehmann GED 296; Bammesberger Nom. 252; Heidermanns 458–459; Kluge-Seebold 705.
ON seäja ‘to satiate’, OE seddan ‘to satisfy’, MHG seten id. Derived from *sađaz. Torp-Falk 421; Holthausen AEEW 288; Vries ANEW 466; Lehmann GED 296.
ON sǫäul-bogi ‘saddle-bow’, OE sadol-boga id., MLG sadel-boge id., OHG satul-bogo id. Compound of *sađulaz and *buzōn. Carr Nom. comp. 79 (parallel formations).

ON sǫäull ‘saddle’, OE sadol id., OFris sadel id., MLG sadel id., OHG satul, satil id. Derivation from *setjanan is phonetically difficult. Probably an old loanword from Slav *sedъlo ‘saddle’. Lidén PBB XV 515 (continues *sodlo- < *sod-tlo-); Saussure MSL VI 247 (against reconstructing *sod-tlo); Torp-Falk 427; Holthausen AEEW 266; Pokorny I 886 (from “East IE”); Vries ANEW 578; Zalizniak I 135; Martynov Jazyk 25–26 (Gmc < Slav); Onions 781; Seebold 397 (reconstructs *satulaz); Vasmer- Trubachev III 589–590; Lehmann GED 306; Kluge-Seebold 705.

OS af-seffian ‘to perceive’, OHG in-sebben id. Related to Lat sapiō ‘to taste’, OIr saer ‘artful; artist, artisan’. Meyer Got. 962 (to Gk ἵψία ‘amusement, plaything’); Wood JEGP II 218 (to Skt sápati ‘to foster, to seek’); Torp-Falk 431; Holthausen AEEW 288; Walde-Hofmann II 477; Jóhannesson IEW 763–764; Pokorny I 880; Chemodanov SGGJa I 103; Vendryes S-6; Seebold 383; Lehmann GED 302.
ON sefi‘mind, affection’, OE sefa ‘understanding, mind’, OS sebo ‘sense, feeling’. Derived from *safjanan. Torp-Falk 431 (reconstruct *sebōn); Holthausen AEEW 288; Vries ANEW 467; Seebold 383.

ON segja ‘to say, to tell’, OE seczan id., OFris sedza, sega id., OS seggian id., OHG sagēn id. Related to *sazō(n). Torp-Falk 425; Holthausen AEEW 288; Pokorny I 898; Vries ANEW 467; Onions 792; Kluge- Seebold 699; Boutkan OFED s.v.
ON segi, sigi ‘slice, bit, clot’. Close to OIr seche ‘hide, skin’ (Lidén BB XXI 95). Connected with *sazō. Vries ANEW 467.
ON sǫgn ‘tale, report’, OE sæzen ‘saying, statement’. Derived from *sazjanan ~ *sazēnan. Holthausen AEEW 267; Vries ANEW 578.

ON sǫg ‘saw’, OE sazu id., MLG sage id., OHG saga id. (cf. also sega id.). Related to Lat secō ‘to cut’, MIr tescaid id., Lith Âisėkti ‘to incise, to cut’, Slav *sîkti ‘to cut’. Meillet MSL XVII 195; Pedersen Kelt. Gr. II 612; Torp- Falk 424; Trautmann BSW 255; Holthausen AEEW 269; Walde- Hofmann II 504–505; Pokorny I 895; Fraenkel 773; Vries ANEW 578; Zalizniak I 147; Onions 791–792; Vasmer-Trubachev III 593; Kluge- Seebold 699.
ON saga ‘to saw’, E saw id., MLG sagen id., MHG sagen id. Derived from *sazō. Vries ANEW 459.

ON saga ‘tale, story, saga’, OE sazu ‘saying, story, tale’, OFris sege id., MLG sage id., OHG saga ‘tale, message’. Derivationally close to Lith pã-saka ‘story’. An abstract noun related to IE *sek⁄-: Gk ἐνέπω ‘to tell’, Lat īnseque ‘announced’, MW hebu ‘to speak’, Lith sekū, sēkti ‘to tell’. Torp-Falk 426; Trautmann BSW 254; Holthausen AEEW 269; Walde-Hofmann I 702; Pokorny I 898; Chemodanov SGGJa I 75; Fraenkel 773; Zalizniak II 180; Vries ANEW 459; Frisk I 464; Onions 792; Kluge-Seebold 699.
ON sann-sǫgull ‘truthful’, OE leás-sazol ‘mendacious’. Derived from *sazjanan ~ *sazēnan. Karsten NB II 285; Holthausen AEEW 268; Vries ANEW 578; Heidermanns 459.

ON seggr ‘man’, OE secz id. Identical with Lat socius ‘fellow, companion’ (Curtius Gr. Et. 460). Related to Skt sákhā ‘friend, companion’, Av haxayid. Uhlenbeck IF XIII 218, KZ XX 559; Zupitza Gutt. 72; Bartholomae 1744; Hirt Urgerm. II 40; Torp-Falk 424; Holthausen AEEW 288; Walde- Hofmann II 551–552; Mayrhofer III 413; Pokorny I 896–897; Vries ANEW 467; Bammesberger Nom. 67.

ON sax ‘a kind of sword’, OE seax ‘knife, a short sword’, OFris sax ‘sword’, OS sahs id., OHG sahs ‘small sword, knife’. Derivationally close to Lat saxum ‘rock’. Further related to *sazō. Uhlenbeck IF XVII 99 (to Slav *soxa ‘wooden plough’); Zupitza Gutt. 137 (same as Uhlenbeck); Persson Beiträge 304; Torp-Falk 424; Holthausen AEEW 288; Walde-Hofmann II 484; Pokorny I 895 (from *se˚- ‘to cut’); Vries ANEW 465–466; Zalizniak I 143; Onions 791–792; Kluge-Seebold 698; Boutkan OFED s.v.
ON pl. Saxar ‘Saxons’, OE pl. Seaxe id., OS Sahso ‘Saxon’, OHG Sahso id. Derived from *saxsan. Holthausen AEEW 288; Vries ANEW 466.

Goth adv. un-sahþaba (leg. unsahtaba) ‘indisputably, accordingly’, ON sáttr ‘reconciled, agreeing’. Derived from *sakanan, cf. *saxtiz. Karsten NB II 331; Zupitza Gutt. 209; Torp-Falk 423; Feist 407, 523; Pokorny I 876–877; Chemodanov SGGJa I 87; Vries ANEW 463–464; Lehmann GED 292–293; Heidermanns 459–460.

Goth ga-sahts ‘reproach, refutation’, OE seht ‘settlement, agreement’ (fem., masc.). Derived from *sakanan. Torp-Falk 424; Wood MLN XXIV 49 (Goth in-sahts ‘account, report’, OE in-siht ‘account, narrative’, to *sazō); Holthausen AEEW 288; Feist 200, 294; Seebold 384; Lehmann GED 206; Bammesberger Nom. 145.
Goth sai ‘here, see’, OHG sē id. Form of *sa. Torp-Falk 421; Pokorny I 978–979.

ON seiär ‘band’, OE sáda ‘cord, halter, snare’, OHG seid ‘band, string’ (neut.), seito id. Related to Skt sétu- ‘binding, fetter, bond, bridge’, Av haētu- ‘dam’, Lat saeta ‘mane, (horse) hair’, Lith saĩtas ‘string, band’, si‚tas ‘cord’, Slav *sîtь ‘net’. Meillet MSL XIV 348; Bartholomae 1728; Torp-Falk 438; Trautmann BSW 252– 253; Holthausen AEEW 266; Mayrhofer III 501; Pokorny I 892; Fraenkel 756; Vries ANEW 468; Vasmer- Trubachev III 614; Lehmann GED 207; Hamp IF LXXXVII 72–75; Kluge- Seebold 700.

ON seigr ‘tough’, OE onsæze ‘falling upon, assailing, attacking’, MLG sēge ‘with watery eyes’, OHG anaseigi ‘hostile’. Derived from *sīz(w)anan. Karsten NB II 445; Torp-Falk 440; Holthausen AEEW 267; Vries ANEW 468; Seebold 389 (reconstructs *saiziz); Matzel Festschr. Schröder 94, KZ CV 96; Heidermanns 460–461.

ON sár ‘large cask’, OE sá ‘tub, pail, vessel’. Identical with Lith saĩkas ‘measure of capacity’. Further related to Toch B sik- ‘to set foot’, Gk ἵκω ‘to come, to attain’, Lith síekiu, síekti ‘to reach, to measure capacity’ (Fick GGA 1891 207). Zupitza Gutt. 137; Torp- Falk 422; Pokorny I 893 (to IE *seik- ‘to be enough’); Fraenkel 755, 781; Vries ANEW 463; Frisk I 719–720; Adams TB 691.

Goth in-sailjan ‘to let down’, ON refl. seilask ‘to stretch out one’s hands’, OE sælan ‘to fasten with a cord’, OFris sēla ‘to make ropes, to bind with a rope’, MLG sēlen id., MHG seilen id. Derived from *sailō ~ *sailan. Torp- Falk 438; Holthausen AEEW 267; Feist 294; Lehmann GED 206; Kluge-Seebold 755.

ON seil ‘string’, OE sál ‘rope, cord, line’, OFris sēl ‘string’, OS sēl id., OHG seil id. Derivative in *-l- related to Hitt išḫiƒa- ‘to bind’, Skt syáti ‘to bind, to tie, to fetter’, Latv sīet id. Structurally close to Slav *silo ‘noose, snare’ with a different ablaut grade. Lith āt-sailë ‘connecting rod attached to the axle’ is an independent formation. Torp-Falk 438; Trautmann BSW 253; Meillet MSL XIV 348; Holthausen AEEW 269; Mayrhofer III 549–550; Pokorny I 891–892; Fraenkel 755; Vries ANEW 468; Zalizniak II 175; Vasmer-Trubachev III 621–622; Lehmann GED 206; Hamp RRL XXXII 107; Kluge-Seebold 755.

Burg *saims ‘liquid, clot’, ON seimr ‘honey-comb’, OS sēm ‘nectar, honey’, OHG seim id. Probably related to Gk αἶμα ‘blood’ (Fick apud Loewenthal PBB XLIX 416). Torp-Falk 422; Vries ANEW 468–469; Frisk I 39; Chantraine I 34; Köbler 713; Heidermanns 462; Kluge-Seebold 755 (to

ON seinn ‘slow, slack’, OE sæne ‘slow, dull, sluggish’, MHG seine ‘slow, slack’. Related to Lith at-sainūs ‘careless’ (with a secondary u-stem?). Cf. *sinēnan. Karsten NB II 365; Torp- Falk 439; Feist 405; Holthausen AEEW 267–268; Pokorny I 891; Fraenkel 771; Vries ANEW 469; Schubert Erw. 70; Lehmann GED 291–292; Matzel KZ CV 97; Heidermanns 462–463.
Goth sainjan ‘to delay’, ON seina id., OE á-sánian ‘to languish, to grow weak’. Derived from *sainiz. Torp-Falk 439; Holthausen AEEW 270; Feist 405; Vries ANEW 469; Lehmann GED 291–292.

OE sápe ‘soap’, sáp ‘amber, resin’, MLG sēpe id., OHG seifa id. Derived from *sīpanan. Torp-Falk 440; Schrader-Nehring II 284 (to Lat sēbum ‘tallow, suet, grease’); Holthausen AEEW 270; Vries ANEW 462–463 (to Norw sīpa ‘to cry, to blubber’, OE sypian ‘to take in moisture’); Onions 842; Kluge-Seebold 754–755.
ON pl. sár-bœtr ‘compensation for a wound’, OE sár-bót id. Compound of *sairan and *bōtiz. Carr Nom. comp. 82 (parallel formations).
ON sár-ligr ‘sore’, OE sár-líc ‘sad, mournful, lamentable’, OFris sēr-lik ‘painful’, OHG sēr-līh id. Derived from *sairaz. Heidermanns 464.

Goth sair ‘pain’, ON sár ‘sore, wound’, OE sár ‘pain, suffering, soreness’, OFris sēr ‘pain, wound’, OS sēr ‘pain, sorrow’, OHG sēr ‘pain, suffering’ (also masc.). Substantivized *sairaz. Grienberger Got. 578–579 (to ON seiär ‘spell, charm’); Hirt PBB XXIII 354 (to Gk ξηρός ‘dry’); Torp-Falk 422; Holthausen AEEW 270; Feist 405; Pokorny I 877; Vries ANEW 463; Zalizniak II 171; Onions 847; Lehmann GED 292; Heidermanns 464.

ON sárr ‘sore, wounded’, OE sár ‘sore, painful, grievous’, OFris adv. sēre ‘very’, OS sēr ‘grievous’, OHG sēr ‘sad, gloomy’. May be related to OIr saeth ‘illness’, W hoed id. < *sai-tu-, Lat saeuus ‘fierce’ < *sai-⁄o-, Latv sievs ‘sharp, biting’. Brugmann II/1 498 (to Lat saucius ‘wounded, hurt’ < *saƒ-ūcios); Grienberger Got. 178–179 (to ON seiär ‘spell, charm’); Karsten NB II 259– 260; Torp-Falk 422; Feist 405–406; Holthausen AEEW 270; Walde- Hofmann II 462–463; Pokorny I 877; Vries ANEW 463; Onions 847; Vendryes S-6–7; Lehmann GED 292; Heidermanns 463–464; Kluge-Seebold 754.
ON særa ‘to wound’, OFris sēra id., OS sērian ‘to injure’, OHG sēren ‘to cause worry, to sadden’. Derived from *sairan. Torp-Falk 422; Vries ANEW 576; Heidermanns 464.

ON seiär ‘spell, charm’. Close to MW hut ‘praestigiae, illusio’. Based on ON síäa ‘to practise sorcery’ < *sīđanan. Derivationally close to W hud ‘magician’ < *soito-. Perhaps further connected with *saiđaz ~ *saiþaz. Torp- Falk 440; Pokorny I 892; Chemodanov SGGJa I 67; Fraenkel 756 (Lith saĩtas ‘emblem, divination, talisman’ represents a metaphoric usage of saĩtas ‘bucket rope’).

Goth saiwala ‘soul’, OE sáwel id., OFris sēle id., OS sēola, sēla id., OHG sēola, sēla id.
