Derived from *saiwiz ~ *saiwaz (Grimm DG 99), probably because of a Germanic belief in souls born out of and returning to sacred lakes (Weisweiler IF LVII 25–55). Schweizer KZ I 152; Helten PBB XX 508–510; Kögel GGA 1897 655 (to Gk αἰόλος ‘rapid’ < *sai Wolow); Zupitza Gutt. 76; Grienberger Got. 179 (to Lat saeuus ‘fierce’); Persson BB XIX 276–278 (to Slav *sila ‘strength’); Bréal MSL XVI 131–132 (original adj. related to *sēljanan); Torp-Falk 422–423; Walde IF XII 382 (reconstructs Gmc *saiwlō, to Lat saeuus); Neckel Walhall 49 (reconstructs the original meaning as ‘breath’ ~ ‘life’); Holthausen AEEW 270; Feist 406; Porzig Gliederung 125; Pokorny I 877; Zalizniak II 180; Koivulehto NM LXVIII 113–118; Mezger KZ LXXXII 285–287 (from *swai-walō ‘one’s own will’); Onions 848; Lehmann GED 292 (against Feist); Kluge-Seebold 753.
ON sjó-borg ‘sea-town’, OE sæ-burz id., OFris sē-burch ‘seadyke’. Compound of *saiwiz ~ *saiwaz and *burzz. Carr Nom. comp. 78 (parallel formations).
ON sjó-dÿr ‘sea animal’, OE sæ-déor ‘sea-beast’. Compound of *saiwiz ~ *saiwaz and *đeuzan. Carr Nom. comp. 55.
ON sjó-fiskr ‘sea-fish’, OE sæ-fisc id., MLG sē-visch id. Compound of *saiwiz ~ *saiwaz and *fiskaz. Carr Nom. comp. 78 (parallel formations).
ON toponym Sjóland, OE sæ-land ‘maritime district’, OFris sē-land id., Du zee-land id., OHG sēo-lant id. Compound of *saiwiz ~ *saiwaz and *lanđan. Carr Nom. comp. 78 (parallel formations).
ON sjó-maär ‘seaman’, OE sæ-mann id., MLG sē-man id., G Seemann id. Compound of *saiwiz ~ *saiwaz and *mannz. Carr Nom. comp. 78 (parallel formations).
ON sjó-vegr ‘way on the sea’, OE sæ-wez ‘sea-way’. Compound of *saiwiz ~ *saiwaz and *wezaz. Carr Nom. comp. 55.

Goth saiws ‘sea, lake, marshland’, ON sær, sjár, sjór ‘sea, lake’ (gen. sg. sævar ~ sæs), OE sæ id. (masc., fem.), OFris sē id., OS sēo id., OHG sē, sēo id. Probably, connected with Lat saeuus ‘fierce’ and Latv sievs, sīvs ‘sharp’ (Kuhn KZ LXXI 149). Schweizer KZ I 152; Fick II 260 (to Lat saeuus ‘fierce’); Persson UUÅ 1891 6–7 (to Lith sÿvas ‘juice’, Gk ἀιονάω ‘to moisten, to foment’); Grienberger Got. 179 (< *saizwiz, to *sīz(w)anan); Zupitza Gutt. 68–69; Collinder Festschr. Pipping 85 (from Uralic); Loewenthal ANF XXXII 292–293 (to *skīnanan); Torp- Falk 440; Holthausen AEEW 266; Walde-Hofmann II 462–463; Feist Idg. 26–27 (a pre-Germanic loanword), 406– 407; Schrader-Krahe Idg. 37 (same as Feist); Weisweiler IF LVII 25–55 (same as Feist); Pokorny I 877; Vries ANEW 575; Zalizniak II 188; Onions 804; Ebbinghaus GL XI 15–16 (follows Grienberger); Koivulehto NM LXVIII 113–118; Meid Festschr. Kronasser 91–93; Lehmann GED 292; Kluge-Seebold 753; Boutkan OFED s.v.

Goth sakan ‘to dispute, to argue’, OE sacan ‘to fight, to strive, to disagree’, OFris seka ‘to argue’, OS sakan ‘to rebuke’, OHG sahhan ‘to argue, to litigate’. Related to Hitt šak- ‘to know’, Gk ἡγέομαι ‘to go before, to lead the way’, Lat sāgiō ‘to feel, to notice’, OIr saigid ‘to seek, to aspire’. See *sōkjanan (Schweizer KZ I 566). Zupitza Gutt. 164; Torp- Falk 423; Mezger KZ LXI 259–262 (same as Schweizer); Wood IF XIII 119 (to OIr saigim ‘to say’); Bezzenberger BB XVI 255 (same as Wood); Feist 407; Holthausen AEEW 266; Walde- Hofmann II 464–465; Pokorny I 877–878; Frisk I 621–622; Seebold 383–385; Martynov Jazyk 26–27 (from Slav *so‘iti ‘to accuse’); Lehmann GED 292–293; Springer IEur. 39–44; Kluge- Seebold 698.

Goth sakjo ‘dispute, quarrel’, OE sæcc ‘strife, contest, conflict’, OHG secka ‘strife’. Derived from *sakō. Zupitza Gutt. 164; Holthausen AEEW 267; Feist 407; Vries ANEW 469; Onions 782; Seebold 384; Lehmann GED 293; Bammesberger Nom. 113; Kluge-Seebold 698.

Goth sakkus ‘sack’, ON sekkr id., OE sæcc ‘sack-cloth’ (a-stem), OS sakk ‘sack’, OHG sac id. (astem). An old borrowing from Lat saccus id. (Gaebeler Zd Ph XLIII 3). Torp- Falk 423; Holthausen AEEW 268; Feist 407; Martinet Gém. 64, 106, 193; Corazza Lat. 21–22, 43; Zalizniak II 203; Onions 780; Lehmann GED 293; Kluge-Seebold 698–699.

ON sǫk ‘charge, guilt, crime, suit’, OE sacu ‘strife, contention, distress’, OFris seke ‘thing’, OS saka ‘charge, accusation, case’, OHG sahha ‘case’. Derived from *sakanan. Zupitza Gutt. 72, 164; Torp-Falk 423; Holthausen AEEW 266; Pokorny I 878; Vries ANEW 578; Zalizniak II 218; Onions 782; Seebold 384; Lehmann GED 293; Bammesberger Nom. 109; Kluge-Seebold 698.
ON saka ‘to blame, to hurt’, OE sacian ‘to strive, to brawl’. Derived from *sakanan. Holthausen AEEW 266; Vries ANEW 460.

ON saki ‘loss’ (unless a mistake for sakni id.), OE and-saca ‘denire, opposer, enemy’, OFris wither-saka ‘adversary’, OS and-saco id., OHG widar-sahho id. Derived from *sakanan. Holthausen AEEW 266; Vries ANEW 460.

OE sealf, sealfe ‘salve, ointment’, OS salba id., OHG salba id. Related to Toch A ßälyp, B ßalype ‘fat, butter, oil’, Skt sarpí- ‘melted butter’, Gk đlpow: đlaion, στέαρ, εὐθηνία; đlfow: βούτυρον (Hes.), Alb gjalpë ‘butter’ (Schmidt KZ II 316). Torp-Falk 437; Holthausen AEEW 286; Mayrhofer III 446; Pokorny I 901; Zalizniak I 148; Frisk I 503; Onions 785; Chantraine 342–343; Lehmann GED 293; Kluge- Seebold 701; Orel AED 129; Adams TB 652–653.
Goth salbon ‘to anoint’, OE sealfian id., OS salbōn id., OHG salbōn id. Derived from *salbō. Torp-Falk 437; Holthausen AEEW 286; Feist 407–408; Pokorny I 901; Lehmann GED 293; Kluge-Seebold 701.
Run Swed prop. saligasti R, OE sele-zist ‘guest in a hall’, OLFrank prop. Sali-gast. Compound of *saliz ~ *salaz and *zastiz. Friesen UUÅ 1924/4 98 (ON < WGmc); Vries ANEW 461.

ON selja ‘sallow’, OE masc. sealh, salh id., OHG salaha id. Related to Lat salix id., MIr sail id. Zupitza Gutt. 210; Holthausen AEEW 286; Walde-Hofmann II 469; Porzig Gliederung 101–102; Pokorny I 879; Chemodanov SGGJa I 101; Vries ANEW 469; Zalizniak II 218; Onions 784; Benveniste IEL 108; Kluge-Seebold 702.

Goth saliþwa ‘inn, dwelling’, OE sælä ‘dwelling, abode’, OS selitha id., OHG selida id. Derived from *saliz ~ *salaz. Close to Slav *selitva ‘dwelling’. Brugmann Grundriß II/1 449–450; Torp-Falk 436; Holthausen AEEW 267; Feist 408; Pokorny I 898; Lehmann GED 293–294.
ON sal-vǫrär ‘porter, janitor’, OE seleweard ‘hall-warder’. Compound of *saliz ~ *salaz and *warđaz. Carr Nom. comp. 82 (parallel formations).

ON salr ‘hall’, OE sele ‘hall, house, dwelling’, sæl ‘hall’, NFris seal id., OS seli id., OHG sal ‘house, hall’. Related to Lith salā ‘island’, Slav *selo ‘field’. Grienberger Got. 180 (to Lat soleō ‘to look after, to care for’); Torp-Falk 435–436; Meringer IF XVI 182, XVIII 241–242 (follows Grienberger and adds Lat solum ‘floor’); Trautmann BSW 248; Holthausen AEEW 267, 289; Feist 408; Pokorny I 898; Chemodanov SGGJa I 82; Fraenkel 758 (Lith salā is derived from sálti ‘to flow’); Vries ANEW 461; Zalizniak II 224; Fraenkel 758; Vasmer-Trubachev III 596; Lehmann GED 294; Kluge- Seebold 698.

Goth saljan ‘to sacrifice’, ON selja ‘to hand over, to sell’ (irreg.), OE sellan ‘to give, to furnish, to deliver’, OFris sella ‘to hand over, to sell’, OS gisellian id., OHG sellen id. Causative of unattested str. *selanan further related to Gk ἵλεῖν ‘to take’, OIr selaim ‘to take’ (Osthoff PBB XIII 457–458). Meillet Etudes 419 (to Slav *sъlati ‘to send’); Collitz Prät. 69–70 (to Gk ἅλλομαι ‘to spring, to leap’); Torp-Falk 435; Holthausen AEEW 289; Feist 408–409; Pokorny I 899; Vries ANEW 461, 469 (to Goth saljan ‘to stay’, see *saliz ~ *salaz); Frisk I 487–488; Onions 808; Stang LS 56; Benveniste IEL 108–109; Mezger KZ LXXXIII 124–149; Lehmann GED 294.
ON sala ‘sale’, OE masc. sala id., OHG sale ‘transfer of property’. See *saljanan. Torp-Falk 435; Holthausen AEEW 269; Vries ANEW 460; Onions 783; Lehmann GED 294; Kluge- Seebold 701.
ON salt-fat ‘salt-vat’, OE sealt-fæt ‘vessel for salt, salt-cellar’, OS salt-fat ‘salt-vat’, OHG salz-faz id. Compound of *saltan and *fatan. Carr Nom. comp. 78 (parallel formations).

Goth salt ‘salt’, ON salt id., OE sealt id., OS salt id., OHG salz id. Closely related to Lith saldūs ‘sweet’, Slav *soldъkъ id. Further connected to other continuants of IE *sal- ‘salt’: Toch A sāle, B sālyiye, Gk ëlw, Arm a∑, Lat sāl, Slav *solь. Schmidt Plur. 182–183; Uhlenbeck PBB XX 564 (to *salwaz); Torp-Falk 436; Trautmann BSW 249, 298; Holthausen AEEW 286; Walde- Hofmann II 465–466; Feist 409; Fraenkel 759–760, KZ XLII 234; Jóhannesson IEW 765; Pokorny I 879; Vries ANEW 461; Zalizniak I 143; Frisk I 78–79; Onions 784; Seebold 385; Vasmer-Trubachev III 713, 715; Lehmann GED 294; Kluge-Seebold 702; Boutkan OFED s.v.

Goth saltan ‘to salt’, OE sealtan id., MLG solten id. (wk., with str. part.), OHG salzan id. Related to *saltan. Identical with Lat sallō ‘to salt’, OIr saillim id. Pedersen Kelt. Gr. I 114; Torp- Falk 436; Feist 409; Holthausen AEEW 286; Walde-Hofmann II 465; Pokorny I 879; Vries ANEW 461; Onions 784; Seebold 385–386; Lehmann GED 294; Kluge-Seebold 702.
ON salt-steinn ‘saltstone’, OE sealt-stán ‘pillar of salt, rock salt’, OHG salz-stein ‘pillar of salt’. Compound of *saltan and *stainaz. Carr Nom. comp. 78 (parallel formations).
ON salta ‘to salt’, OHG salzōn id. Derived from *saltan. Torp-Falk 436; Heidermanns 466.

ON saltr ‘salty’, OE sealt id., OFris salt id., MLG solt ‘salty (of water)’. Derived from *saltan. Originally identical with the u-stem: Lith saldūs ‘sweet’, Slav *soldъ-kъ id. Karsten NB I 48–49; Torp-Falk 436; Feist 409; Holthausen AEEW 286; Pokorny I 878–879; Fraenkel 759; Vries ANEW 461; Onions 784; Seebold 385; Vasmer- Trubachev III 713; Lehmann GED 294; Heidermanns 466; Boutkan OFED s.v.

ON sǫlr ‘yellow’, OE salu ‘dusky, dark’, MDu salu ‘dirty, pale yellow’, OHG salo ‘dark, black’. Related to Slav *solvъ ‘cream-colored, sallow’. Karsten NB II 196–197; Loewenthal KZ LII 222 (to Lat salmō ‘salmon’); Schwentner FB 21–24, KZ LXXIII 117; Uhlenbeck PBB XX 264, XXII 536; Kock ANF X 306; Torp-Falk 437; Naumann ANNS 104; Holthausen AEEW 269, AWN 298; Schulze Kl. Schr. 118–119 (to *sal-u- ‘salt’); Pokorny I 879; Vries ANEW 578; Stang LS 52; Onions 784; Vasmer-Trubachev III 712; Lehmann GED 294; Heidermanns 466– 467.

Goth sama-kuns ‘of the same kin’, ON sam-kynja ‘of the same kind’. Compound of *samōn and *kunjan (see *kunjaz). Structurally similar to Gk ὁμό-γνιος ‘of the same origin’. Torp- Falk 433; Feist 410; Frisk I 307; Lehmann GED 294–295.

Goth sama-leiks ‘equal, identical’, ON sam-líkr ‘like, similar’, OHG sama-līh id. Compound of *samōn and *līkaz. Holthausen AEEW 269; Feist 410; Lehmann GED 142, 295. *samanē adv: Goth samana ‘together’, ON saman id., OE samen id., OFris samin, semin id., OS saman id., OHG saman id. Identical with Skt samanā id. (Wackernagel Festschr. Geiger 229) and OIr samain ‘festival of Tara’, bech-samain ‘beeswarm’ (Stokes KZ XL 245, Urkelt. 293). Further connected with *samōn. Torp- Falk 433; Holthausen AEEW 270; Feist 410; Wackernagel-Debrunner II/2 205; Pokorny I 905; Vries ANEW 461–462; Mayrhofer III 435 (doubts the direct comparison with Germanic); Lehmann GED 295; Kluge-Seebold 703, 917.
ON samning ‘agreement, treaty’, OS samanunga ‘congregation, community’, OHG samanunga id. Derived from *samanē. Vries ANEW 462.

ON samna, safna ‘to gather, to collect’, OE samnian id., OFris samenia, somnia id., OS samnōn id., OHG samanōn id. Derived from *samanē. Torp-Falk 433; Holthausen AEEW 270; Vries ANEW 462; Kluge-Seebold 703.
ON samnaär ‘gathering, host’, OFris samnath id. Derived from *samanē. Vries ANEW 462.

Goth samaþ ‘together’, OE samod id., OS samad id., OHG samant, samit id. Etymologically close to Gk ὁμόσε ‘together’ < *ὁμοτε (Brugmann KVG 455). Cf. also Skt smát, sumát id. (Mayrhofer III 548) and OIr samud ‘assembly’ < *sama-t-u- (Stokes Urkelt. 293). Further connected with *samōn. Torp-Falk 433; Holthausen AEEW 270; Feist 410; Pokorny I 902–905; Frisk II 390; Lehmann GED 295; Kluge-Seebold 703.
ON sam-vist ‘living together’, OE sam-wist ‘living together, cohabitation, matrimony’, OHG samawist ‘consortium’. Compound of *samōn and *westiz I. Torp-Falk 433.

Goth lustu-sama ‘longed for’, ON samr ‘agreeing, willing’, OS frithu-sam ‘peaceful’, OHG lioht-sam ‘obvious’. See *samōn. Karsten NB II 453; Torp-Falk 433; Feist 409; Holthausen AEEW 269–270; Pokorny I 902–904; Vries ANEW 461–462; Dishington IESt IV 436, 439–440; Heidermanns 467–468.
ON mann-semi ‘valour’, OHG arbeit-samī ‘hardship, difficulty’. Derived from *samaz. Heidermanns 467.

Goth samjan ‘to seek to please’, ON semja ‘to arrange, to shape, to compose’. Derived from *samaz. Wood MP II 475 (to Gk ἥμερος ‘gentle’); Torp-Falk 433; Feist 410; Vries ANEW 470; Lehmann GED 295 (to *samōn); Heidermanns 467.
ON veg-sama ‘to honor, to glorify’, OHG gireht-samōn ‘to be said justly’. Derived from *samaz. Vries ANEW 461–462; Heidermanns 468.

Goth sama ‘same’, ON sami, samr id., OE adv. swá same ‘in the same way’, OS sama, samo ‘same’, OHG samo id. Identical with Skt samá- ‘equal, like, the same, even’, Av hama- ‘equal, the same’, Gk ὁμός ‘one and the same’, OIr -som ‘that one’ (Bugge KZ XX (1872) 31). Cf. also *samaz. Bartholomae 1773; Karsten NB II 453; Schmidt KZ XXXII 372; Torp-Falk 432–433; Feist 409; Holthausen AEEW 269–270, AWN 237; Porzig Gliederung 128, 199; Mayrhofer III 437 (separates Gk from Indo-Iranian); Pokorny I 902–904; Vries ANEW 461– 462; Frisk II 390; Onions 785; Lehmann GED 294–295; Dishington IESt IV 436, 439–440; Bammesberger Nom. 238; Heidermanns 468; Kluge-Seebold 917.

OE séfte ‘gentle, mild’, OS adv. sāftor ‘simply, easily’, OHG samfti ‘simple, easy’. Identical with Skt voc. santya (address to Agni) (Hoffmann MSS XXIII 29–38). Torp-Falk 433; Holthausen AEEW 306; Mayrhofer III 429; Pokorny I 904; Onions 843 (to *samaz); Rosengren 22–29; Heidermanns 468– 469.
ON sand-korn ‘grain of sand’, OE sand-corn id., G Sandkorn id. Compound of *sanđaz ~ *sanđan and *kurnan. Carr Nom. comp. 78 (parallel formations).

ON sandr ‘sand’, OE sand id., OFris sand, sond id., OS sand id., OHG sant id. From an earlier *samđaz. Close to Gk ἄμαθος ‘sand’. Probably derived from *sem- ‘to pour’: Gk ἀμάομαι ‘to gather’, OIr to-ess ‘to pour out’, Lith semiū, sémti ‘to scoop’. Pedersen Kelt. Gr. II 624; Torp-Falk 430–431; Holthausen AEEW 270; Kuiper Festschr. Kretschmer 218; Pokorny I 146 (to Gk ψάμμος ‘sand’), 901–902; Fraenkel 774–775; Vries ANEW 462; Zalizniak II 171; Frisk I 84; Onions 786; Kluge-Seebold 703.

Goth sandjan ‘to send’, ON senda id., OE sendan id., OFris senda id., OS sendian id., OHG senten id. Causative of *senþanan ‘to go’. Torp- Falk 430; Holthausen AEEW 289; Feist 410–411; Vries ANEW 470; Onions 809; Seebold 395; Lehmann GED 295–296; Kluge-Seebold 758.

OE senzan ‘to singe’, OFris sanga, senga id., MLG sengen id., OHG bi-sengen id. Cf. also Icel sangr ‘singed’. Identical with Slav *sǫ‘iti ‘to dry, to boil’. The underlying str. vb. is not attested. On the other hand, if *sanzjanan was originally connected with sacrificial practices, it might be connected with *senzwanan – cf. similar cases in Skt hávate ‘to invoke, to address’ ~ juhóti ‘to pour’ (Hirt Idg. Gr. II 189), Slav *pîti ‘to sing’ ~ *piti ‘to drink’ (Trubachev VSJa-P 135–138). Zupitza Gutt. 205; Torp- Falk 429; Holthausen AEEW 289; Pokorny I 907; Onions 829; Vasmer- Trubachev III 814; Kluge-Seebold 758.

Goth saggws ‘song’ (i-stem), ON sǫngr id., OE sanz ‘sound, singing’, OFris song ‘song’, OS sang id., OHG sang id. (masc., neut.). Structurally close to Gk ὀμφή ‘voice’. Derived from *senzwanan. Bloch BSL XXXI 62 (adds Pkt saághai ‘to say, to teach’); Loth RC XLI 377; Torp-Falk 429; Holthausen AEEW 270; Feist 403; Pokorny I 907; Vries ANEW 578; Zalizniak II 188; Frisk II 392–393; Chantraine 801; Onions 846; Seebold 393; Lehmann GED 302; Bammesberger Nom. 53.
Goth saggqs ‘setting (of the sun), Occident’, ON sǫkk ‘sinking’. Derived from *senkwanan. Close to Slav *ob-sǫga ‘frost, sweat’ (Anikin Etim. 1983 48–49). Feist 403; Vries ANEW 576; Seebold 394.

Goth saggqjan ‘to plunge, to immerse’, ON sǫkkva ‘to sink’, OE sencan id., OFris sanza, senza id., OS bi-senkian id., OHG senken id. Causative of *senkwanan. Torp-Falk 429; Holthausen AEEW 289; Feist 403; Vries ANEW 576–577; Seebold 394; Kluge- Seebold 758.
ON sannr ‘truth, justice’, OE sóä id., OS sōth id. Derived from *sanþz. Torp-Falk 430.
ON senna ‘to chatter, to tell’, OE séäan ‘to declare true, to affirm’, OFris sannia id. Derived from *sanþz. Torp-Falk 430; Holthausen AEEW 306; Vries ANEW 470; Lehmann GED 329; Heidermanns 469.
ON sanna ‘to prove, to affirm’, OE ze-sóäian ‘to prove the truth, to bear witness’, OHG sandōn ‘to prove, to affirm’. Derived from *sanþz. Torp-Falk 430; Vries ANEW 462; Heidermanns 470.
ON sann-ligr ‘just, fair, fit’, OE sóä-líc ‘true, genuine, unfeigned’, OS sōth-līk ‘true’. Derived from *sanþz. Heidermanns 469.
ON pl. sann-yräi ‘true words’, OE sóä-word ‘true word’, OS sōth-word id. Compound of *sanþz and *wurđan. Carr Nom. comp. 87 (parallel formations).

Burg *sanþs ‘true, truthful’, ON sannr, saär ‘true’, OE sóä id., OS sōth id. An old active participle similar to OLith sant”, Slav *sy-jь. Further cf. Lat sōns ‘guilty, criminal’. See *esti. Torp- Falk 430; Wissmann KZ LX 285; Holthausen AEEW 307; Walde- Hofmann II 560; Porzig Gliederung 112, 194; Pokorny I 341; Chemodanov SGGJa I 87; Fraenkel 124; Vries ANEW 462; Onions 846; Meid Wortbild. 150, 172; Watkins Festschr. Lane 186–194; Seebold 177, Sprache XV 14–45; Vasmer-Trubachev III 817; Lehmann GED 329; Bammesberger Nom. 202; Köbler 713; Heidermanns 469–470.

ON safi‘sap’, OE sæp id., MLG sap id., OHG saf, sapf id. Despite phonetic difficulties, related to Lat sapa ‘must, new wine boiled thick’ (Curtius Gr. Et. 512). Torp-Falk 431; Solmsen KZ XXXIV 62 (Gmc < Lat); Holthausen AEEW 268; Walde- Hofmann II 476–477; Pokorny I 880; Onions 787; Kluge-Seebold 699.

ON serkr ‘shirt’, OE serc, syrc id. Borrowed from Lat sarica ‘silk’. Miklosich 316 (from Slav *sorka ‘shirt’); Förstemann KZ XIX 355 (to Lith šaıkas ‘piece of clothing, fisherman’s coat’); Solmsen KZ XXXII 275 (borrowed from Slav *sorka ‘shirt’); Pedersen KZ XXXIX 370 (ON < Russ sorok ‘shirt’); Schneider ZDADL LXVI 252 (to Skt sráj- ‘wreath, garland’); Torp-Falk 435; Holthausen AEEW 292; Detschew Thr. 179–180 (to Thrac ζειρά ‘article of clothing’); Vries ANEW 471 (ON < OE); Onions 789.

Goth pl. tant. sarwa ‘arms, armament’, OE searo ‘device, craft, art, skill, machine’ (neut., fem.), OS saro ‘arms, armament’, OHG saro id. Related to Av -haurvō ‘defending, protecting’, Lat seruō ‘to save, to preserve’ (Meyer Goth. 154) and, probably, Gk ἭΗρα as a *‘protecting deity’. Bugge KZ XX 32 (to Gk eǫrv ‘to join’, Lat serō ‘to connect, to set in order’); Schulze Qu. 325; Grienberger Got. 180–181; Schröder IF XVII 464–465 (follows Bugge); Uhlenbeck PBB XXX 306; Torp-Falk 435; Holthausen AEEW 287; Walde-Hofmann II 525–526; Feist 411; Pokorny I 910, 911; Zalizniak I 146; Frisk I 642; Lehmann GED 296.
ON sǫrvi ‘stone necklace, armor’, OHG gi-sarawi ‘arms, armament’. Derived from *sarwan. Torp-Falk 435.
Goth gasateins ‘foundation’, OE seten ‘set, shoot, plantation’, OFris seten ‘negotiation, assertion’. Derived from *satjanan. Holthausen AEEW 291; Feist 200; Lehmann GED 296.

Goth satjan ‘to set’, ON setja id., OE settan id., OFris setta id., OS settian id., OHG sezzen id. Causative of *setjanan, cf. Skt sādáyati ‘to seat’, OIr sáidim ‘to sit’, Slav *saditi ‘to set, to plant’. Meillet MSL IX 144; Pedersen Kelt. Gr. I 71; Torp-Falk 426; Holthausen AEEW 291; Feist 411; Mayrhofer III 472; Pokorny I 884–887; Vries ANEW 471; Onions 90, 813; Seebold 397; Vasmer-Trubachev III 544; Lehmann GED 296; Kluge-Seebold 760.

Goth saups ‘sacrifice’, ON sauär ‘sheep’. Derived from *seuþanan. Schrader-Nehring II 136–137; Torp- Falk 443; Feist 413; Vries ANEW 464; Onions 807; Seebold 400–401; Lehmann GED 297–298; Bammesberger Nom. 133.
Norw dial. søygja ‘to suckle’, OS sōgian id., OHG sougen id. Causative of *sūzanan. Torp-Falk 443.
Goth bi-sauljan ‘to spot’, Sw dial. saula ‘to soil’. Derived from *saulō. Feist 94–95; Lehmann GED 72.

Norw dial. saula ‘dirt’. Identical with Slav rvn. Sula < *soulā (or < Balt *saulā). Further related to Skt súrā ‘spirituous liquor’, Av hurā ‘intoxicating drink, kumys’, Lith sulā ‘birch sap’, Latv sula id., OPrus sulo ‘curdled milk’. Bartholomae 1837; Trautmann BSW 291–292; Mayrhofer III 487; Pokorny I 912–913; Fraenkel 940; Vasmer- Trubachev III 799–800; Lehmann GED 72.

ON saumr ‘seam’, OE seám id., OFris sām id., MLG sōm id., OHG soum id. Derivationally close to Skt syūman- ‘band’, Gk ὑμήν ‘thin skin, membrane’, OPrus schumeno ‘twine, waxed end’ although the inlaut vocalism was changed in Germanic. Further related to *siwjanan. Torp-Falk 442; Holthausen AEEW 287; Mayrhofer III 477–478; Pokorny I 915–916; Vries ANEW 464; Zalizniak I 135; Frisk II 964; Onions 804; Bammesberger Nom. 71; Kluge-Seebold 70–707.
ON seyma ‘to stud’, MLG sœmen id. Derived from *saumaz. Vries ANEW 471.
ON saup ‘sup’, MDu soop, sope ‘broth’ (fem.), OHG souf id. (masc.). Derived from *sūpanan. Vries ANEW 464.

OGutn sauþr ‘spring’, OE seáä ‘pit, hole, well, lake’, OFris sāth ‘spring’, MLG sōd ‘spring, seething’, MHG sōt id. Derived from *seuþanan. Torp-Falk 443; Holthausen AEEW 287; Onions 807; Lehmann GED 297–298.

Norw dial. søyr ‘withered, rotten (of wood)’, OE seár ‘dry, barren’, MLG sōr id. From IE *sausós id., cf. Gk αὖος id., Lith sa Usas id., Slav *suxъ id. Karsten NB II 454; Torp-Falk 447; Trautmann BSW 250–251; Holthausen AEEW 287; Pokorny I 880– 881; Fraenkel 766; Frisk I 188–189; Onions 811; Vasmer-Trubachev III 813; Bammesberger Nom. 238; Heidermanns 471; Kluge-Seebold 769; Lubotsky KZ XCVIII 1–10 (reconstructs *Hsousó-, based on *Hsus-, part. perf. of *Hes- ‘to burn, to glow’).

ON seyrask ‘to starve’, Norw søyra ‘to make dry’, OE seárian ‘to wither, to pine away’, MLG sœren ‘to make dry’, OHG ir-sōrēn ‘to become thin, meagre’. Derived from *sauzaz. Structurally close to Slav *sušiti ‘to dry’. Holthausen AEEW 287; Vries ANEW 471.
OE seáw ‘juice, moisture’, OS sou id., OHG sou id. Identical with Skt neut. sava- ‘juice of flowers’. Further related to Skt sunóti ‘to squeeze’. Torp- Falk 441; Pokorny I 912.

Goth un-sibjis ‘unlawful’, OE sibb ‘related’, OFris sib id., MLG sibbe id., OHG sibbi id. Cf. *sebjō. Torp-Falk 432; Feist 417, 523; Holthausen AEEW 291; Pokorny I 882–883; Lehmann GED 300; Heidermanns 472–473.

Goth sibja ‘kinship’, ON sif, usually pl. sifjar ‘sib, affinity’, OE sibb ‘relationship, peace’, OFris sibbe ‘kinship’, OS sibbia id., OHG sibba ‘peace, alliance’. Etymologically close to Skt sabhā ‘assembly, social gathering, meeting’, Slav *sobь ‘peculiarity’, OPrus subs ‘self’ (Solmsen UGLV 200–201). Kuhn KZ IV 370–372; Lidén SVSU VI 54; Edgerton KZ XLVI 178–180 (separates Skt sabhā as sa(m)- ‘together’ and bhā- ‘to speak’); Torp-Falk 432; Feist 417; Holthausen AEEW 432; Specht KZ LXVIII 46; Mayrhofer III 433–434 (same as Edgerton); Pokorny I 883; Chemodanov SGGJa I 105; Vries ANEW 473; Zalizniak I 148; Onions 825; Vasmer-Trubachev III 162; Lehmann GED 300; Bammesberger Nom. 113–114 (to IE *sep- ‘to respect’); Heidermanns 472 (substantivized *sebjaz); Kluge- Seebold 765; Orel JSL V/1 144–149 (separates Slav *svoboda ‘freedom’ from this root).

Goth ga-sibjon ‘to become reconciled’, ON af-sifja ‘to cut off from one’s sib, to alienate from one’s family’, OE sibbian ‘to reconcile’, OHG sibbōn ‘to form an alliance’. Derived from *sebjō. Torp-Falk 432; Holthausen AEEW 291; Feist 200; Lehmann GED 300; Heidermanns 472.
ON guä-sefi‘godfather’, OS sibbio ‘blood relative’, OHG *sibbo ‘relative’. See *sebjaz, *sebjō. Torp-Falk 432; Vries ANEW 467; Heidermanns 472.
ON sbst. sjóli ‘king, prince’, MHG un-sivel ‘unfriendly’. Derived from *sebjō. Vries ANEW 479.

Goth sibun ‘seven’, ON sjau id., OE seofon id., OFris sigun, soven id., OS sibun id., OHG sibun id. Continues *sepṃ < IE *septṃ id.: Toch A ßpät, B ßukt, Skt saptá-, Av hapta, Gk ἵπτά, Alb shtatë < PAlb *septati-, Lat septem, OIr secht n-, Lith septynī, Slav *sedmь. Kock PBB XV 252 (on the history of ON sjau); Kluge Zd Wf I 363–365; Bartholomae 1765; Pedersen Kelt. Gr. I 72; Torp-Falk 431; Trautmann BSW 257; Holthausen AEEW 295; Walde-Hofmann II 517– 518; Feist 417; Pipping GHÅ XI/3 26 (analogy with *ahtau in ON); Sturtevant MLN XL 26; Hamp Word VIII 136– 139 (*sebun results from restructuring of *sebunđōn ~ *sebunþōn); Mayrhofer III 431; Pokorny I 909; Fraenkel 776; Szemerényi Numerals 104–105; Vries ANEW 478; Frisk I 545; Onions 813; Vasmer-Trubachev III 599–600; Lehmann GED 300–301; Ross-Berns Numerals 586–588; Kluge-Seebold 762; Orel AED 436; Adams TB 657; Burlak IFTJa 260.
ON sjaund ‘period of seven (nights)’. Identical with Skt saptatí- ‘seven decades’, Alb shtatë ‘seven’ < PAlb *septati- < *septṃti-. Torp-Falk 431; Pokorny I 909; Orel AED 436.

ON sjaundi ‘seventh’, OE seofoäa id., OFris sigunda id., OS sibondo, sivoäo id., OHG sibunto id. Related to Toch A ßäptänt, B ßuktante id., Skt saptátha- id., Lith septiñtas id. Derived from *sebun. Torp-Falk 431; Holthausen AEEW 295; Feist 417; Pokorny I 909; Vries ANEW 478; Szemerényi Numerals 35 (with dissimilation, from *septṃtos, cf. Skt saptátha- ‘seventh’); Onions 813; Ross-Berns Numerals 629; Adams TB 658.
ON siäugr ‘well-bred, well-behaved’, OHG sitīg ‘being in the habit, modest’. Derived from *seđuz. Torp-Falk 428.

Goth sidon sis ‘to practice’ (in þo sido þus ‘ταῦτα μελέτα’), ON siäa ‘to mend, to improve one’s life and manners’, OE sidian ‘to arrange, to set right’, OS gi-sidōn ‘to add, to prepare’, OHG sitōn ‘to plan, to carry out’. Derived from *seđuz. Holthausen AEEW 292; Feist 418; Vries ANEW 472; Lehmann GED 301.
ON siä-lauss ‘ill-mannered’, OHG situ-lōs id. Compound of *seđuz and *lausaz. Carr Nom. comp. 91 (parallel formations).
ON siä-samr ‘wellconducted’, OHG situ-sam ‘clever’. Compound of *seđuz and *samaz. Carr Nom. comp. 91 (parallel formations).

Goth sidus ‘custom, habit’, ON siär id., OE sidu id., OFris side id., OS sidu id., OHG situ id. Related to Skt svadhā ‘particularity, custom’, Gk đyow id. < *s⁄e-dh- (Curtius Gr. Et. 251) with unclear anlaut *s⁄- ~ *s-. Feist PBB XV 548–550 (on the anlaut); Johansson IF II 8; Torp-Falk 428; Wood MLN XVIII 13–14 (to *sīþiz); Holthausen AEEW 292; Feist 418; Trier Lehm 41; Wissmann MSS VI 129 (*-i- in the stem contradicts Feist’s etymology); Mayrhofer III 559; Pokorny I 883; Vries ANEW 472; Zalizniak I 152; Frisk I 449; Schiefer MSS XX 49; Lehmann GED 301 (analogical modification of the vowel); Bammesberger Nom. 159 (reconstructs *siđuz from IE *sī- ‘to bind’); Kluge-Seebold 765.

Goth sigis ‘victory’, ON sigr id., sig id., OE size, sizor id., OFris sī id., OS sigi- (in cmpn.), OHG sigu id. Structurally identical with Skt sáhas- ‘force, victory’, Av hazah- ‘force, power’ (Aufrecht KZ I 355). Related to Skt sáhate ‘to overcome, to conquer, to win’, sáhas ‘force, victory’, Gk đxv ‘to have’, MIr seg ‘strength’. Zupitza Gutt. 205; Bartholomae 1799; Torp-Falk 426; Holthausen AEEW 293; Feist 419; Mayrhofer III 450–451; Pokorny I 888–889; Zalizniak II 224; Vries ANEW 474; Frisk I 602–604; Benveniste IEL 135; Lehmann GED 302; Bammesberger Nom. 136, 212; Kluge-Seebold 762.
ON sigra ‘to vanquish, to overcome’, OE sizorian id. Derived from *sezez ~ *sezaz. Torp-Falk 426; Holthausen AEEW 293; Vries ANEW 474; Lehmann GED 302.
ON sigär ‘sickle’, OE sigäe id., MLG segede id. Related to *sazō. Torp-Falk 424; Holthausen AEEW 293; Chemodanov SGGJa I 88; Vries ANEW 473; Onions 804.

ON segl ‘sail’, OE sezl id. (also masc.), OFris seil id., OS segal id., OHG segal id. (masc.). The original meaning must have been *‘piece of cloth’, cf. OIr séol ‘kerchief, sail’ borrowed from Germanic. Probably, from IE *sek-lo-, to *sek- ‘to cut, to cut off’. Much ZDADL XXXVI 50 (to Gk ὅπλον ‘tackle, tool’); Zupitza Gutt. 76, 138; Torp-Falk 426; Holthausen AEEW 288; Pokorny I 895; Vries ANEW 467; Zalizniak II 171; Onions 782; Kluge- Seebold 753.
ON sigla ‘to sail’, OE sezlian, sezlan id., MLG segelen id., MHG sigelen id. Derived from *sezlan. Torp-Falk 426; Holthausen AEEW 288; Vries ANEW 474.

Goth siuns ‘sight, form, face, image’, ON sjón, sÿn ‘sight, vision’, OE sín ‘sight, vision’, OFris siune ‘face, sight, appearance’, OS siun ‘sight’. Derived from *sexwanan. Torp-Falk 425; Holthausen AEEW 292; Feist 426; Pokorny I 898; Vries ANEW 479, 573; Zalizniak II 228; Schubert Erw. 58; Seebold 388, Festschr. Meid 351–355; Lehmann GED 33, 307; Bammesberger Nom. 147; Heidermanns 474.

Goth ana-siuns ‘visible’, ON sÿnn id. Identical with *sezwniz I. Karsten NB II 3–4; Torp-Faalk 425; Holthausen AEEW 292; Feist 426; Pokorny I 898; Vries ANEW 573; Seebold 388; Lehmann GED 307; Heidermanns 473–474.
ON sÿni ‘sight’, OS gisiuni ‘face’, OHG gi-siuni ‘face, sight’. Derived from *sezwniz I. Vries ANEW 573.

Goth saihs ‘six’, ON sex id., OE six, syx id., OFris sex id., OS sehs id., OHG sehs id. Continues IE *s(⁄)e˚s id.: Toch A ßäk, B ßkas, Skt ßá†, Av xšvaš, Arm vec', Gk ἑξ, Lat sex, OIr sé, Lith šešī, Slav *šestь. Saussure MSL VII 73–74; Zupitza Gutt. 191; Bartholomae 561; Pedersen Kelt. Gr. I 78; Torp-Falk 425; Trautmann BSW 144; Holthausen AEEW 293; Walde-Hofmann II 528– 529; Feist 404; Mayrhofer III 407; Pokorny I 1044; Fraenkel 976; Vries ANEW 471; Frisk I 527–528; Onions 830; Vasmer-Trubachev IV 433–434; Lehmann GED 290–291; Ross-Berns Numerals 585; Kluge-Seebold 753; Adams TB 664; Burlak IFTJa 259.

ON sétt ‘body of six’. Identical with Skt ßaßtí- ‘sixty’, Av xšvaštiid., Alb gjashtë ‘six’, Slav *šestь id. (Jucquois Muséon LXXVIII 440). See *sexs. Bartholomae 561; Torp-Falk 425; Mayrhofer III 408; Pokorny I 1044; Vries ANEW 471; Vasmer- Trubachev IV 433–434; Orel AED 130.

Goth saihsta ‘sixth’, ON sétti id. (< *sextōn), ODan sæxte id., OE sixta id., OFris sexta id., OS sehsto id., OHG sehsto, sehto id. Derived from *sexs. Cf. Toch A ßkäṣṭ, B ßkaste, Skt ßaṣṭhá- id., Gk ἑκτος id., Lat sextus id., OIr sessed id., Lith “‚štas id., Slav *šestъ. Torp-Falk 425; Trautmann BSW 143; Meillet BSL XXIX 33–34; Holthausen AEEW 293; Walde-Hofmann II 528; Feist 404; Mayrhofer III 408–409; Pokorny I 1044; Fraenkel 976; Vries ANEW 471; Frisk I 527; Vasmer-Trubachev IV 433; Ross-Berns Numerals 628–629; Adams TB 664.

Goth saiƕan ‘to see’, ON sjá id., OE seón id., OFris sia id., OS sehan id., OHG sehan id. Related to Hitt šakuwāi- ‘to observe’, Skt sácate ‘to accompany, to stand by, to follow’, Gk ἑπομαι ‘to follow’, Alb shoh ‘to see’ < PAlb *sāk-ska, Lat sequor ‘to follow’, Lith sekū, sēkti id. (Grimm Gesch. 409). Aufrecht KZ I 352; Loewe KZ XXXIX 312 (Alb shoh < Gmc); Uhlenbeck PBB XXIX 336 (to *sazō(n)); Hirt IF XXXVII 231–233 (to *skawwōjanan); Zupitza Gutt. 68; Torp-Falk 425, 572; Walde-Hofmann II 519–520; Feist 404–405; Holthausen AEEW 290; Jóhannesson IEW 779–780; Mayrhofer III 417–418; Pokorny I 896–899; Fraenkel 773; Vries ANEW 477–478; Kammenhuber KZ LXXVII 62 (on the Hittite parallel); Frisk I 544–545; Onions 806; Seebold 387–388; Lehmann GED 291 (doubts the identity of *sek ⁄- ‘to see’ and *sek ⁄- ‘to follow’); Kluge-Seebold 754; Orel AED 425–426.

Goth sik ‘self’, ON sik id., MLG sik id., OHG sih id. Continues *se ·e. For the structure cf. *meke. Based on the reflexive pronoun *s(⁄)e-. Brugmann Grundriß II/2 390, 395–402; Torp-Falk 421; Feist 420; Pokorny I 882–884; Vries ANEW 474; Seebold 73–79; Lehmann GED 303; Kluge- Seebold 761.

Goth silba ‘self’, ON sjálfr id., OE self, selfa id., OFris self id., OS self id., OHG selb id. Venet sselboi sselboi is identical with OHG selbselbo (Sommer IF XLII 128–129). The frequently posited connection with *s(⁄)e- is not so obvious. IE *sel-bho- could be derived from *sol(⁄)o- ‘whole, all’ and probably influenced by *se-bh- (see *sebjō). Fick I 795 (suffixal derivative of the pronominal *se-, see *seke); Grienberger Got. 185–186 (to OIr selb ‘property’); Torp-Falk 437; Krahe IF XLVII 325; Holthausen AEEW 289; Feist 421; Porzig Gliederung 128; Pokorny I 884; Vries ANEW 478; Pellegrini- Prosdocimi I 451–452, II 167–168; Onions 807–808; Lehmann GED 303; Kluge-Seebold 756.

Goth silda-leiks ‘wonderful’, OE seld-líc ‘strange, wonderful’, OS seld-līk ‘remarkable’. Derived from unattested *selđaz, see *selđanē. Torp-Falk 437; Feist 421; Onions 807; Lehmann GED 303; Kluge-Seebold 757.

ON sjaldan ‘seldom’, OE seldan id., OFris sielden id., MLG selden id., OHG seltan id. Derived from unattested *selđan ~ *selđaz. Derivative of *sel- ‘to jump’: Gk ἅλλομαι, Lat saliō, Lith sálti ‘to flow’. For the formation cf. Lat salitus. Lidén Studien 55 (from *se- ‘separate’); Torp-Falk 437; Walde-Hoffmann II 557 (to Lat sōlus ‘alone’); Holthausen AEEW 289; Vries ANEW 478; Onions 807; Lehmann GED 303; Kluge-Seebold 757.

ON sjald-sénn ‘seldom seen’, OE seld-síne id., MDu selt-siene ‘rare’, OHG selt-sāni ‘seldom seen’. Compound of *selđaz (see *selđana) and *sezwniz II. Holthausen AEEW 289; Carr Nom. comp. 62; Lehmann GED 303; Kluge-Seebold 757.

ON selr ‘seal’, OE seolh id., MLG sel id., OHG selah id. A Wanderwort of unknown origin. Holthausen IF XXV 147 (to Gk ἑλκω ‘to draw, to drag’), AEEW 290; Fick III 328 (to Gk σέλαχος ‘cartilaginous fish’); Torp-Falk 436 (to OIr selige ‘tortoise’ < *selekƒo-); Pokorny I 901 (from *selk- ‘to pull’); Vries ANEW 469–470; Zalizniak I 135; Onions 804; Schindler Sprache XII 65–66 (from Finno-Ugric *šülke, cf. Finn dial. hylki); Kluge-Seebold 753.
MHG selken ‘to trickle, to drip’. Identical with Skt sṛjáti ‘to send off, to discharge’, Av har 6 zaiti id. Bartholomae 1792–1793; Torp- Falk 436; Mayrhofer III 497–498; Pokorny I 900–901.

OE sealma ‘couch’, OFris bed-selma ‘bedstead’, OS selmo ‘bed’. Despite the difference of the anlaut, related to Gk σέλμα ‘upper planking of a ship’ < *s⁄elm–, Lith šelmuõ ‘eaves, beam’, Slav *σελμἡ ‘beam’ (Kretschmer KZ XXXI 422: reconstructs an improbable *˚s-). Further see *swelljō (Schmidt Voc. II 78). Pedersen IF V 59; Torp-Falk 437; Holthausen AEEW 286; Pokorny I 898–899 (also adduces Lith súolas ‘bench’, sīlë ‘trough’); Frisk II 691–692; Vasmer- Trubachev III 668–669.

Goth simle ‘once, formerly’, OE simble ‘always’, OS simla, simbla id., OHG adj. simbal ‘continuous’. Cf. ON simul ‘ever’. An old instr. sg. (Scherer Gesch. 595) or gen. pl. Related to Gk ὁμαλός ‘like, smooth’, Lat similis ‘once’, OIr samlith ‘once’, further to *sem- ‘one’. Helten PBB XXVIII 564 (from gen. pl. *semlōn đazōn); Brugmann Grundriß II/2 695 (follows Helten); Wackernagel KZ XXX 316 (to *mēlan I); Torp-Falk 432; Kluge Urgerm. 250 (on semantic development in Gothic; from *sem-blē related to Gk ē-πλός ‘simple, easy’); Holthausen AEEW 294; Ritter ANF CXIX 180 (from *sem- and *mel-, to *mēlan I), CXXII 99; Walde-Hofmann II 538–539; Feist 422; Horn Spr. 115 (same as Ritter); Pokorny I 903; Vries ANEW 476; Frisk II 384; Lehmann GED 304; Bammesberger Nom. 248.

ON sí-grœnn ‘evergreen’, OE sbst. sin-zréne ‘house-leek’, MDu sbst. sinde-grœn id., OHG sin-gruoni ‘evergreen’. Probably, compound of *senaz and *zrōniz. Björkman Zd Wf II 229–230; Kluge-Seebold 764 (the first component from IE *sem- ‘one’).

Goth superl. sinista ‘oldest’, Gmc-Lat sbst. sinistus ‘sacerdos apud Burgundios omnium maximus’ (Amm. Marcell. 28, 5.14). Identical with Skt sána-, Av hana-, Gk ἑνος, Arm hin, Lat senex, senis, OIr sen, Lith s‚nas. Brugmann PBB XVIII 313–314; Bartholomae 1769; Pedersen Kelt. Gr. I 36; Torp-Falk 428; Feist 422; Trautmann BSW 256; Feist 423; Walde-Hofmann II 513–514; Mayrhofer III 426; Pokorny I 907–908; Vries ANEW 476; Fraenkel 774–775; Scardigli Goten 25; Frisk I 522–523; Porzig Festschr. Debrunner 343– 349; Lehmann GED 304–305; Heidermanns 478–479.

ON sindr ‘dross, slag’, OE sinder id. (neut., masc.), OS sinder ‘scale, slag’, OHG sintar id. (masc.). Identical with Slav *s≤dra ‘thickened liquid’ (Lidén SVSU VI/1 39). Further related to *sanđaz ~ *sanđan. Torp-Falk 431; Holthausen AEEW 294, AWN 244; Pokorny I 906; Vries ANEW 476; Zalizniak I 143; Stang LS 46; Vasmer-Trubachev III 825; Kluge-Seebold 764–765.

Goth siggwan ‘to sing’, ON syngva, syngja id., OE sinzan ‘to sing, to recite, to narrate’, OFris siunga ‘to sing’, OS singan id., OHG singan id. See *sanzwaz. Zupitza Gutt. 100; Torp- Falk 429; Feist 419; Holthausen AEEW 294; Kluge 709–710; Benveniste BSL XXXIII 136 (attributes IE *seng ⁄h- to the religious lexicon); Jóhannesson IEW 786; Pokorny I 906–907; Vries ANEW 573; Onions 829; Seebold 392–393; Lehmann GED 302; Kluge-Seebold 764.

Goth sineigs ‘old’, OFranc prop. Sinigus. Derived from *senaz. Similar to Skt sanaká- ‘old’, Lat senex id. if the latter is from *sene-k(o)-s (Szemerényi AION-L III 10; the reconstruction *sene H-s proposed by Martinet BSL LI 42–56 has to be dropped in any case). Schweizer KZ I 478 (to Lat sinister ‘left’); Förstemann AND I 1337; Feist 422–423; Mayrhofder III 426; Lehmann GED 304–305; Bammesberger Nom. 255.
ON sǫkk ‘sinking, hollow, pit’, OE sinc ‘treasure, gold, silver’, OS sinc ‘gold, treasure’. Derived from *senkwanan. Holthausen AEEW 294; Vries ANEW 576.

Goth sigqan ‘to sink’, ON sǫkkva id., OE sincan id., OFris sinka id., OS sincan id., OHG sinkan id. Identical with Slav *sęgti ‘to reach, to grab’ (Anikin Etim. 1983 48–57: for the semantic development cf. Slav *ob-sęgati ‘to sink, to settle’). Shmidt Kritik 63–64 (to Lith senkū, sēkti ‘to sink, to dry up’, Slav *sęknǫti ‘to dry up’); Meillet MSL VIII 288 (to Arm ankanim ‘to fall’); Zupitza Gutt. 93; Torp-Falk 428; Trautmann BSW 256– 257; Feist 420; Holthausen AEEW 294; Jóhannesson IEW 785–786; Pokorny I 906; Chemodanov SGGJa I 111; Fraenkel 772–773; Vries ANEW 576; Onions 829; Seebold 393–394 (to *sīxwanan); Vasmer-Trubachev III 826; Lehmann GED 303 (against Schmidt); Kluge-Seebold 764.

OE sinnan ‘to care for, to mind, to heed’, OFris sinna ‘to think’, OHG fir-sinnan ‘to recover, to come back to one’s senses’. Related to Av hant- ‘to arrive’, Lat sentiō ‘to perceive’ (Vani‘ek 296; Fick I 562). Pedersen IF II 316; Bartholomae 1771; Torp-Falk 430; Holthausen AEEW 295; Walde- Hofmann II 515–516; Pokorny I 908; Seebold 394–395; Eichmann KZ LXXXVII 269–271 (reconstructs *sennanan < *sen H-, cf. Hitt sanḫ- ‘to seek’); Lehmann GED 296, 305.

ON sinni ‘walk, fellowship’, OE ze-síä ‘company, fellowship’, OS gisīthi id., OHG gi-sindi id. Cf. also Goth ga-sinþja ‘fellow traveller’. Derived from *senþanan. Feist 200; Vries ANEW 477; Lehmann GED 305; Kluge-Seebold 319.

Goth sinþs ‘time, instance’ (in iterative numerals), ON neut. sinn ‘time’, OE síä ‘going, journey, travel’, OS sīä ‘way’, OHG sind ‘direction, way’. Identical with OIr sét ‘way’, W hynt id., MBret hent id. Further connected with *senþanan. Torp-Falk 430; Holthausen AEEW 296; Feist 423–424; Pokorny I 908; Vries ANEW 477; Zalizniak II 172; Seebold 394; Lehmann GED 305.
ON sinna ‘to journey, to travel’, OE síäian id., OS sīäōn id., OHG sindōn id. Derived from *senþanan. Torp-Falk 430; Holthausen AEEW 296; Vries ANEW 477; Lehmann GED 305.
Goth ga-sinþa ‘companion’, ON sinni id., OS gi-sīd id., OHG gi-sind id. Derived from *senþanan. Feist 200;Vries ANEW 477; Lehmann GED 305; Kluge-Seebold 319.

ON seräa ‘to fornicate’, OE seoräan ‘to violate’, OHG sertan ‘to have sexual intercourse’. Of unknown origin. Brugmann IF XXXII 323 (to Gk eǫrv ‘to fasten together in rows’); Wood MP V 283 (to Skt sísarti ‘to stretch, to extend’); Torp-Falk 435 (to W serth ‘obscene’); Jóhannesson IEW 787–788; Pokorny I 911, 1023; Vries ANEW 470; Seebold 395–396.

ON sess ‘seat’, OE sess ‘seat, bench’. Derived from *setjanan. Cf. Lat part. sessus < *sed-to-. Torp-Falk 427; Holthausen AEEW 291; Walde- Hofmann II 507–508; Vries ANEW 471; Seebold 397; Bammesberger Nom. 78.

ON set ‘aisle, wing (along the hall)’, sjǫt ‘residence, seat’ (u-stem), OE set ‘seat, stall’, MDu set ‘podex’, OHG sez ‘seat’. Derived from *setjanan. Cf. W sedd ‘seat’. Torp-Falk 426–427; Holthausen AEEW 291; Pokorny I 886; Vries ANEW 471, 479; Zalizniak I 143; Seebold 396; Lehmann GED 306; Bammesberger Nom. 59.

Goth sitan ‘to sit’ (no *-j- in pres.), ON sitja id., OE sittan id., OFris sitta id., OS sittian id., OHG sizzen id. Related to Skt sīdati ‘to sit down, to be seated’, Av had- ‘to sit’, Gk ἑζομαι id., Lat sedeō id., OIr saidid ‘to sit’, Lith sėdu, sėsti, Slav *s≤dǫ, *sîsti. Brugmann Grundriß II/3 190–192 (analogical loss of *-j- in Goth); Bartholomae 1753–1754; Torp-Falk 426; Trautmann BSW 258; Feist 424–425; Holthausen AEEW 296; Karstien KZ LXV 145–153; Walde- Hofmann II 507–508; Jóhannesson IEW 781–782; Mayrhofer III 472–474; Pokorny I 884–886; Fraenkel 769, 777; Vries ANEW 477; Frisk I 445–446; Onions 830; Seebold 396–397; Vasmer- Trubachev III 613; Lehmann GED 306; Kluge-Seebold 765–766.

Goth sitls ‘seat’, OE setl, seld id. (neut., masc.), MLG setel id., OHG sezzal ‘chair, seat’. Identical with Arm et∑ ‘place’, loc. •llä: καθέδρα, Hes., Lat sella ‘seat of the chair’, Gaul sedlon ‘seat’, Slav *sedlo ‘village’. Derived from *setjanan. On the other hand cf. OS sethal ‘seat, throne’, OHG sedal id. < *seþlaz. Torp-Falk 427; Kluge Nom. Stamm. 47–48; Holthausen AEEW 291; Walde- Hofmann II 511; Feist 425; Pokorny I 886; Vries ANEW 479; Zalizniak II 172; Trubachev Etim. 46 (*seþlaz directly to Slav *sedlo); Onions 813; Seebold 397; Vasmer-Trubachev III 596; Lehmann GED 306 (from *sed-tlo-); Hilmarsson Festschr. Rix 213 (analogically restored *d of the original *setlo- < *sed-tlo-; *seþlaz < *sed-tlo-); Bammesberger Nom. 75; Kluge- Seebold 760.
ON seti ‘sitter’, OE anz-seta ‘carbuncle, pimple’, MLG -sete ‘sitter’, OHG stuol-sezzo ‘architriclinus’. Derived from *setjanan. Holthausen AEEW 291; Vries ANEW 471.
ON setr ‘seat, residence’. Close to Gk ἑδρα id. Further see *setjanan. Torp-Falk 427; Mayrhofer III 423; Pokorny I 884–886; Frisk I 443; Vries ANEW 471.
ON sjóär ‘money-bag’, OE seód ‘money-bag, purse’. Derived from *siwjanan (Johansson Festschr. Kuhn 272– 279). Torp-Falk 442; Holthausen AEEW 290; Vries ANEW 479.
ON sÿsl ‘business’, OE súsl ‘torment’. Related to *seukaz. Torp-Falk 443.
ON sjúk-dómr ‘illness’, MLG sēke-dōm id., OHG sioh-tuom id. Derived from *seukaz. Carr Nom. comp. 87 (parallel formations); Heidermanns 480.

Goth siuks ‘ill, sick’, ON sjúkr id., OE séoc id., OFris siāk id., OS siok id., OHG sioh id. Cf. also str. *seukanan > Goth siukan ‘to be sick’. Two possibilities have to be further explored: connection with *sūkanan (Lid NTS VII 170–173) and comparison with Lith sáugoti ‘to watch over’ (Fick III 442) revealing a semantic relation similar to that of Lith siıgti ‘to be sick’ and sérgëti ‘to guard, to watch over’. Karsten NB I 31; Zupitza Gutt. 165 (same as Fick), KZ XXXVI 66 (to Lith sūkti ‘to turn’); Scheftelowitz BB XXVIII 707 (to Arm k'akem ‘to dissolve’); Torp-Falk 442; Feist 426; Holthausen KZ XLVII 311–312 (to Lat sūgillō ‘to thrash’), AEEW 290; Jóhannesson IEW 776–777; Pokorny I 915 (to MIr socht ‘silence, depression’); Vries ANEW 479; Onions 825; Seebold 398–399, IF LXXVI 327; Lehmann GED 307; Bammesberger Nom. 240; Heidermanns 480–481; Kluge-Seebold 762; Boutkan OFED s.v.
Goth siukei ‘illness, sickness’, ON hug-sÿki ‘anxiety’, MLG suke ‘pestilence, epidemic’, OHG siuhhī ‘illness’. Derived from *seukaz. Feist 426; Lehmann GED 307; Heidermanns 480; Kluge-Seebold 760.

Burg *seudan‚ to boil, to sacrifice’, ON sjóäa ‘to cook’, OE séoäan ‘to seethe’, OFris siātha id., MLG seden id., OHG siodan id. Close to Lith siau‘iū, sia Usti ‘to winnow’ (Berneker IF X 160). Johansson PBB XV 237 (to Goth suþjan ‘to tickle’); Charpentier KZ XL 427 (to Av hāvayeiti ‘to stew’); Torp-Falk 443; Holthausen AEEW 290; Jóhannesson IEW 776; Pokorny I 914; Vries ANEW 478; Onions 807; Seebold 400–401; Peeters IF XCIV 294–295; Lehmann GED 297–298; Köbler 713;.
Goth sis ‘oneself’, ON sér id. A form of dative derived from IE *seid. See *seke. Feist 420; Pokorny I 882; Vries ANEW 470.

Goth saian ‘to sow’, ON sá id., OE sáwan id., OFris part. esēn id., OS pret. obar-seu ‘darübersäen’, OHG part. in-sāniu ‘incultu’. Related to Lat serō id. < *sisō, perf. sēvī, Lith sėju, sėti id., Slav *sîjǫ, *sîti id. Möller PBB XI 51–54; Jacobsohn KZ XLVII 92–94 (treats Goth -ai- as an original diphthong); Streitberg GE 75–76; Torp-Falk 421; Trautmann BSW 253–254; Feist 403– 404; Holthausen AEEW 270; Walde- Hofmann II 522; Kluge 618–619; Jóhannesson IEW 768–770; Pokorny I 889–890; Chemodanov SGGJa I 62; Fraenkel 778–779; Vries ANEW 459; Onions 849; Seebold 386–387; Vasmer- Trubachev III 615; Gamkrelidze- Ivanov 688–689 (add Hitt šai- ‘to press down, to sow’); Lehmann GED 290; Kluge-Seebold 699.
ON sáä ‘seed, crop, corn’, MLG sāt id. A morphological variant of *sēđiz. Torp-Falk 421; Holthausen AEEW 267; Pokorny I 890; Vries ANEW 459; Zalizniak II 188; Kluge- Seebold 698.
ON sáä-tími ‘sowing season’, OE sæd-tíma id. Compound of *sēđiz and *tīmōn. Carr Nom. comp. 82 (parallel formations).

Goth mana-seþs ‘mankind’, OE sæd ‘seed, fruit, growth, sowing’ (neut.), OFris sēd ‘crop, sowing’, OS sād id., OHG smala-sāt ‘small vegetables’. Similar to W had ‘seed’, Bret had id., Corn has. Continues *sē-ti-. Related to *sēanan. Torp-Falk 421; Holthausen AEEW 267; Feist 344; Pokorny I 890; Zalizniak II 188; Onions 806; Seebold 386; Bammesberger Nom. 146; Ramat Gedenkschr. Güntert 75; Kluge-Seebold 698.

ON sáld ‘sieve’. Identical with Lith sėkla ‘seed’ and Dac dies-sathel ‘Verbascum sinuatum’ (= Gk φλόμος) < *di⁄es-sētlo-. Cf. a synonymic Dac διέσεμα < *di⁄es-sēm–. Cf. also W hidl ‘sieve’. Derived from *sēanan. Zupitza Gutt. 137; Torp-Falk 421; Detschew Thr. 546–547 (Dac -sathel ~ Lith sótis ‘satiety, satiation’); Pokorny I 889; Fraenkel 772; Vries ANEW 460.
ON sæl-ligr ‘healthy, wealthy, blissful’, OE ze-sæl-líc ‘happy’. Derived from *sēliz. Heidermanns 476.
ON sæld ‘bliss’, OE sælä ‘happiness, joy’, OS sāläa id., OHG sālida id. Derived from *sēliz. Torp-Falk 436; Holthausen AEEW 267; Vries ANEW 575; Heidermanns 476.

Goth sels ‘good, kind’, ON sæll ‘happy, blessed’, OE un-sæle ‘evil, wicked’. Probably related to *saljanan. Fick I 564 (to Skt sárva- ‘entire, whole, intact’ < *sol⁄o-); Froehde BB IX 119 (to Lat sōlor ‘comfort’, Gk ἵλημι ‘to be gracious’); Karsten NB II 445–446; Wood PBB XXIV 531 (to *sēanan); Schroeder ZDADL XLII 63 (from *sēdlo-); Meringer IF XVI 151 (same as Schroeder); Torp- Falk 436; Feist 416; Holthausen AEEW 267, AWN 297 (to Osc sivom ‘omnino’); Wood AJPhil XXIV 40–41 (to *sēanan); Pokorny I 979–980 (to *sol⁄o- ‘whole, hale’); Vries ANEW 575; Mezger KZ LXXXIII 124–149; Onions 827; Klingenschmidt MSS XXVIII 75–88 (to Gk φλλάσκομαι ‘to appease’, Arm a∑ac'em ‘to ask’); Darms Vṛddhi 85–86, 466–467; Lehmann GED 299–230; Matzel Festschr. Schröder 101, KZ CV 111; Heidermanns 476–477; Kluge-Seebold 756–757.

Goth selei ‘usefulness, goodness, kindness’, ON ár-sæli ‘blessing on the year, plenty’. Cf. also OE un-sæl ‘unhappiness, improper season’ (masc.). Derived from *sēliz. Feist 416; Vries ANEW 636; Lehmann GED 299; Heidermanns 476.
ON sæla ‘to bless’, OE sælan ‘to happen, to betide’. Derived from *sēliz. Holthausen AEEW 267; Vries ANEW 575; Heidermanns 476.

ON sámr ‘swarthy, blackish’. Identical with Skt kßāmá- ‘singed, dried up’. Torp-Falk 434; Mayrhofer I 288 (against the comparison with Skt); Pokorny I 624; Vries ANEW 462; Darms MSS XXXV 28; Heidermanns 477.

OE sám-wyrcan ‘to do a thing incompletely’, OS sām-quik ‘half-dead’, OHG sāmi-heil ‘spoiled, weakened’. Identical with Skt sāmí ‘half’, Gk ἡμι- id., Lat sēmi- id. (Curtius Gr. Et. 325). Torp-Falk 434; Holthausen AEEW 267, 269; Walde-Hofmann II 512–513; Feist 405 (to *saimaz); Mayrhofer III 459; Pokorny I 905–906; Frisk I 636; Naert ANF LXXII 30–34; Darms MSS XXXV 10–11, Vṛddhi 108–108; Heidermanns 477–478.

OS sāmo ‘seed’, OHG sāmo id. Close to Lat sēmen id., OPrus semen id., Lith sėmenys ‘flax seed’, Slav *sîmę ‘seed’ and also Dac διέσεμα ‘Verbascum sinuatum’ < *di⁄e(s)-sēm– as opposed to diessathel id. < *di⁄es-sētlo- (see *sēđlan). Further related to *sēanan (Kuhn KZ LXXI 141– 142). Torp-Falk 422; Trautmann BSW 253–254; Walde-Hofmann II 512; Pokorny I 890; Dybo VSJa 26; Fraenkel 774; Seebold 386; Vasmer-Trubachev III 600–601; Bammesberger Nom. 184; Kluge-Seebold 703.
ON pl. sætr ‘mountain pastures’, G dial. Säß id. Derived from *setjanan. Kluge-Seebold 533.

Goth anda-sets ‘detestable, horrible’, ON neut. sætt ‘endurable’, OE andsæte ‘odious, hateful’, MDu ont-set id., MHG ant-sæze ‘brave’. Derived from *setjanan. Karsten NB I 101; Torp- Falk 427–428; Holthausen AEEW 268; Feist 47; Pokorny I 886; Seebold 397; Lehmann GED 35; Bammesberger Nom. 261; Matzel Festschr. Schröder 103, 117, KZ CV 114; Heidermanns 479–480.
ON sæti ‘seat’, OE ze-sæte ‘back of the head’, OHG gi-sāzi ‘seat, district’. Derived from *setjanan. Holthausen AEEW 268; Vries ANEW 576; Onions 804; Seebold 397.
ON sát ‘ambush, sitting in ambush’, OE sæt ‘ambush’. Derived from *setjanan. Torp-Falk 427; Holthausen AEEW 268; Vries ANEW 463; Seebold 397; Lehmann GED 306; Bammesberger Nom. 111.
ON sáta ‘truss of hay’, OE sæte ‘house’, OFris sēte ‘bail’, OHG hūssāza ‘home’. Derived from *setjanan. Holthausen AEEW 268; Vries ANEW 463.

ON sef ‘sedge’, OE sife ‘sieve’, MLG seve id., OHG sib id. An isolated derivative *si-bhorelated to *sēanan. Zupitza Gutt. 16; Torp-Falk 440; Holthausen AEEW 293; Pokorny I 889–890, 894 (from *seip- ~ *seib-); Zalizniak II 189; Onions 826.
ON sig ‘rope which is let down’, OE size ‘fall, setting of the sun’ (masc.). Derived from *sīz(w)anan. Holthausen AEEW 293; Vries ANEW 473.
Goth ana-silan to cease, to grow calm’. Identical with Lat sileō ‘to be silent’. Grienberger Goten 23; Torp- Falk 440–441; Walde-Hofmann II 535–536; Feist 44; Pokorny I 891; Lehmann GED 33.

ON sili, seli ‘strap belonging to the harness’, OFris sil-rāp ‘rope’, MLG sele id., OHG silo id. A zero-grade variant of *sailan ~ *sailō. Torp-Falk 438; Vries ANEW 469; Lehmann GED 206; Kluge-Seebold 763.

Goth silubr ‘silver’, ON silfr id., OE seolfor, siolufr id., OFris selover id., OS siluvar id., OHG silabar id. (with *-a-). The HG vocalism may well be more archaic. A North European Wanderwort of uncertain origin, cf. OPrus sirablan id., Lith sidãbras id., Latv sidrabs id., Slav *sьrebro id. (Schulze Kl. Schr. 115). Further explanations are more than dubious (Ivanov Met. 104: to Lyc rvn. Σίβρος: ἀργύρεος ποταμός, Kartv *werc 1 xl- ‘silver’, Basque zillar id.). Meillet BSL XXIV 138 (to Lyc Σίβρος ἀργύρεος ποταμός); Uhlenbeck PBB XX 43 (originally, to Akk ßarpu ‘silver’); Ipsen Festschr. Streitberg 229–230; Loewenthal PBB XLIX 71 (via Thracian); Mikkola Bu S 41 (to Gk σίδηρος ‘iron’); Torp-Falk 441; Holthausen AEEW 295; Feist 421– 422; Porzig Gliederung 143; Fraenkel 780–781; Vries ANEW 475; Zalizniak I 143; Onions 827; Benveniste IEL 48, 154–155; Stang LS 47; Tovar IEur. 271–272 (to Basque zillar, zidar, zirar ‘silver’); Vasmer-Trubachev III 606; Trubachev VJa 1985/5 5–6 (from residual Indo-Aryan in Eastern Europe); Gamkrelidze-Ivanov 713 (from Kartvelian); Lehmann GED 303–304; Kluge- Seebold 763.
ON silfr-smiär ‘silver-smith’, OE seolfor-smiä id., MDu silver-smit id., OHG silabar-smid id. Compound of *silubran and *smiþaz. Carr Nom. comp. 78 (parallel formations).
Goth silubreins ‘of silver’, OE seolfren id., OFris selvirn id., OS silubrin id., OHG silberīn id. Derived from *silubran. Holthausen AEEW 292; Feist 421; Lehmann GED 303–304.

ON sin ‘sinew’, OE seonu id., OFris sine, sini, sin id., OS sinewa id., OHG senawa id. Related to Av hinu- ‘band, chain, manacle’, OIr sin, sín ‘chain’, Latv pa-sainis ‘string’. Bartholomae 1814; Torp-Falk 428 (to Skt snāvan- ‘sinew’); Holthausen AEEW 295; Pokorny I 892; Vries ANEW 476; Zalizniak II 180; Onions 828; Bammesberger Nom. 118; Kluge-Seebold 754.

Swed- Norw dial. sina ‘to cease, to be exhausted’, MHG senen ‘to yearn’. Related to *sainiz. Particularly close to Lat sinō ‘to leave’. Karsten MSN III 412; Walde- Hofmann II 545–546; Pokorny I 890–891; Lehmann GED 291.
Norw syl ‘awl’, MLG sūwele id., MHG siule id. Derived from *siwjanan. Torp-Falk 442.

Goth siujan ‘to sew’, ON sÿja ‘to sew, to tie the planks of a ship together’, OE seówan, seowian ‘to sew’, OFris sīa id., OS siuwian id., OHG siuwen id. Related to Skt sīvyati ‘to sew, to join’, Osset xuyun ~ xuin ‘to sew’, Lat suō id., Lith siuvū, siūti id., Slav *šiti id. Persson Beiträge 854; Torp-Falk 441; Trautmann BSW 261–263; Holthausen AEEW 296; Walde-Hofmann II 631– 632; Feist 425; Mayrhofer III 477–478; Pokorny I 915–916; Fraenkel 789–790; Vries ANEW 572; Onions 814; Vasmer- Trubachev IV 443–444; Lehmann GED 306–307; Kluge-Seebold 706–707.

Goth si ‘she’, OFris se id., OS siu id., OHG siu, sī id. Related to Skt encl. part. sīm, Av acc. hīm ‘him’ (Wackernagel KZ XXIV 605–606), Gk ǫ id., OIr sí id., Lith -si id. (Schmidt KZ XXXVI 395–396). Ahrens KZ VIII 344 (on Gk ǫ); Brugmann Grundriß II/2 321; Bartholomae 1814; Feist 416; Mayrhofer III 475, 551; Pokorny I 978–9979; Frisk I 702; Lehmann GED 300; Kluge-Seebold 761–762; Boutkan OFED s.v.

ON síär ‘long, hanging’, OE síd ‘wide, broad, ample’, OFris adv. sīde ‘deep’, MLG sīt, sīde ‘low, quiet’, OHG adv. sīto ‘slack, loose’. Related to Lat sētius ‘late’, sērus id., OIr sír ‘long (of time)’. Torp-Falk 439; Holthausen AEEW 292; Walde-Hofmann II 526– 527; Pokorny I 891; Vries ANEW 473; Onions 825; Heidermanns 475–476; Kluge-Seebold 756.

ON síäa ‘side’, OE síde id., OFris sīde id., OHG sīta id. Derived from *sīđaz. Torp-Falk 439; Holthausen AEEW 292; Pokorny I 891; Vries ANEW 472; Zalizniak I 156; Onions 825; Heidermanns 475; Kluge-Seebold 756.

ON síga ‘to sink down, to slide’, OE sízan ‘to sink, to decline, to fall’, OFris pres. sīga id., OS pret. sg. sēg id., OHG sīgan id. A variant of *sīxwanan. Wood MLN XVIII 14 (Gk ἵκω ‘to come’); Torp-Falk 439; Holthausen AEEW 293; Jóhannesson IEW 772–773; Pokorny I 893; Vries ANEW 473; Seebold 388–389 (against Torp- Falk); Kluge-Seebold 755.

ON sía ‘to filter’ (originally str.vb.), OE seón id., OFris sīa id., MDu sien ‘to drip’, OHG sīhan ‘to strain’. Related to Skt siñcáti ‘to pour out, to sprinkle’, Av hin‘aiti id., Gk ὦξαι: διηθῆσαι (Hes.), Slav *sьcǫ, *sьcati ‘to urinate’. Zupitza Gutt. 68; Torp-Falk 439; Holthausen AEEW 295; Mayrhofer III 464–465; Pokorny I 893; Vries ANEW 472; Seebold 389–391; Vasmer-Trubachev III 815–816; Kluge- Seebold 755.
ON sía ‘sieve, strainer’, OHG sīha id. Derived from *sīxwanan. Torp-Falk 439; Seebold 390; Vries ANEW 472.

ON sík ‘ditch, trench’, OE síc ‘brook, water-course’, MLG sīk ‘marshy wold’ (masc.), OHG seih ‘urine’ (masc.). Related to Lat siat ‘urinates’. Zupitza Gutt. 68; Torp-Falk 439; Holthausen AEEW 291; Walde-Hofmann II 531; Pokorny I 893 (to *seik ⁄- ‘to flow out’); Vries ANEW 474–475; Zalizniak II 218.

ON síkr ‘a kind of salmon, Coregonus lavaretus’. Of unknown origin. Borrowed from the same source as Russ sig id., Lith sykis id., Latv sĩga, sĩka id. (Finn siika is from Gmc). Torp-Falk 439; Būga II 561 (from a non-IE language of the Eastern Baltic).

ON síma ‘cord’ (neut.), OE síma id., OFris sīm, sīma id., OS sīmo id. Close to Toch B ßim ‘roof’, Skt sīmán- ‘parting of the hair’, Gk íμάς ‘leather strap’ (suggesting *ímvn) and further derived from *sēi- ‘to bind’ (Skt syáti, Lith si‚ti). Torp-Falk 437; Trautmann BSW 253; Holthausen AEEW 294; Mayrhofer III 475 (against this etymology); Pokorny I 891–892; Vries ANEW 476; Fraenkel 783; Frisk I 724–725; Lehmann GED 206; Bammesberger Nom. 184; Adams TB 656–657.
Goth nom. pl. seinaigairnai (leg. seina-gairnai) ‘selfish’, ON síngjarn ‘covetous, avaricious’. Compound of *sīnaz and *zernaz. Streitberg GE 163; Feist 415; Carr Nom. comp. 67; Lehmann GED 299.

Goth seins ‘his, her’, ON sinn id., OE sín id., OFris sīn id., OS sīn id., OHG sīn id. Derived with *-nofrom IE loc. *sei, to IE refl. *s(⁄)e-. Cf. in particular Venet veina- ‘one’s own’ < *s⁄ei-no- (Krahe ZONF XI 86, Festschr. Hirt II 565–566). Bugge KZ XX (1872) 41–42; Torp-Falk 421; Holthausen AEEW 294; Mezger Word IV 100; Feist 415; Porzig Gliederung 129; Pokorny I 882–884; Vries ANEW 477; Lehmann GED 299; Kluge-Seebold 755.

OFris part. bi-seppen ‘bloodshot (of an eye)’, MDu sīpen ‘to drip’, MHG sīfen ‘to flow (of blood)’. Related to Gk eǫbv ‘to drop, to let fall in drops’. Torp-Falk 440; Holthausen AEEW 296 (OE sípian ‘to fall’ < *sīpōjanan); Pokorny I 894; Frisk I 450–451 (eǫbv as “Reimwort zu λείβω”); Seebold 391. *sīþiz adv: Goth þana-seiþs ‘further, thereafter’, ON síär ‘less’, OE síä ‘later, since’, OS sīä ‘later’, OHG sīd ‘later, since’. Compared with Lat sētius ‘inferior, lower, worse’ (Bugge BB III 106–107) and MIr sith- ‘long, lasting’ continuing IE *sēi-t-. May be related to *sīđaz. Franck ZDADL XLVI 168–175; Karsten NB II 303–304; Torp-Falk 438; Holthausen AEEW 296; Walde-Hofmann II 526– 527; Feist 489; Pokorny I 889–891; Vries ANEW 472; Onions 828; Lehmann GED 299; Lühr MSS XXXVII 121–130; Heidermanns 475; Kluge-Seebold 755– 756.

Goth skaban ‘to shear, to shave’, ON skafa ‘to scrape, to shave’, OE sceafan id., OS pres. scaban id., OHG skaban id. Related to Gk σκάπτω ‘to dig, to hack’, Lith skapiū, skõpti ‘to cut’, Slav *skopiti ‘to castrate’. Bickell KZ XIV 433 (to Lat scabō ‘to shave, to scratch’); Zupitza Gutt. 150; Meillet MSL XIV 339; Solmsen KZ XXXVII 584; Torp- Falk 451–452; Feist 426; Holthausen AEEW 272; Jóhannesson IEW 824– 826; Pokorny I 931; Fraenkel 812; Vries ANEW 480; Frisk II 718–720; Onions 817; Seebold 401–402; Vasmer- Trubachev III 650; Lehmann GED 307; Kluge-Seebold 707–708.
ON skafl‘snow-drift’, G dial. Schabel ‘scraper’. Derived from *skabanan. Vries ANEW 480.
ON skafa ‘scraper’, OE scéafa ‘plane’, OHG scaba id. Derived from *skabanan. Holthausen AEEW 272; Vries ANEW 480; Seebold 401.
Norw skadd ‘snap’, OE sceadd ‘shad’, G dial. schad, schade id. Of unknown origin. Torp-Falk 449 (to Lat scateō ‘to bubble, to gush’); Holthausen AEEW 272; Pokorny I 950; Vries ANEW 479.

Goth skadus ‘shadow’, OE sceadu id. (fem.), MLG schade id., OHG scato id. Related to Gk σκότος ‘darkness’, OIr scáth ‘shadow, mirror’. Zupitza Gutt. 150; Torp-Falk 449; Holthausen AEEW 272; Feist 427; Pokorny I 957; Zalizniak II 219; Frisk II 739–740; Onions 815; Lehmann GED 307–308; Bammesberger Nom. 85; Kluge-Seebold 713.
Goth ufar-skadwjan ‘to overshadow’, OE sceadwian ‘to cover with shadow’. Derived from *skađuz ~ *skađwaz. Torp-Falk 449; Feist 510; Lehmann GED 307–308.

Burg *skafts ‘shaft’, ON skapt ‘shaft, missile’, OE sceaft ṅstick, pole, shaft’, OFris skaft ‘shaft’, OS skaft ‘spear’, OHG scaft ‘shaft, spear’. Related to Gk σκῆπτρον ‘staff’, Dor σκᾶπτον id., σκᾶπος: κλάδος, ka‹ ἄνεμος ποιός (Hes.), Lat scāpus ‘shaft, stem’, Latv šk Eps ‘spear’. Probably, to *skabanan. Zupitza Gutt. 152, 213; Torp-Falk 452; Holthausen AEEW 272; Walde-Hofmann II 490; Pokorny I 932–933; Vries ANEW 483; Zalizniak II 189; Frisk II 728–729; Onions 815; Lehmann GED 298; Köbler 713; Kluge- Seebold 710.

Goth ga-skafts ‘creation, creature’, OE (ze-)sceaft id. (fem., masc.), OS gi-scaft id., OHG gi-scaft id. Derived from *skapjanan. Torp-Falk 450; Holthausen AEEW 272; Feist 200; Seebold 407; Lehmann GED 148; Bammesberger Nom. 145; Kluge-Seebold 317.
ON skepti ‘shaft’, OHG scefti ‘spear’. Derived from *skaftan ~ *skaftaz. Vries ANEW 490.
ON skepta ‘to make shafts (to arrow-heads)’, LG scheften id., OHG scaften ‘provide with a shaft’. Derived from *skaftaz ~ *skaftan. Vries ANEW 490.
ON skegg ‘beard’, OE sceacza ‘hair of the head’ (masc.). Derived from *skazōn. Lindroth ANF XX 367; Zupitza Gutt. 154; Torp-Falk 448; Holthausen AEEW 272; Vries ANEW 487.

ON skagi ‘low cape, ness’, OE sceaza ‘small wood, copse, thicket’. Probably derived from *skexanan. Torp- Falk 448; Holthausen AEEW 272; Vries ANEW 480 (to OIr der-scaigim ‘to distinguish oneself’); Onions 815.

ON skeifr ‘askew, oblique’, OE sceáf-fót ‘splay-footed’, OS skēf ‘crooked’, G dial. scheib id. Probably related to Lith skybas ‘wedge-like piece of land’, Latv “˚ībs ‘crooked’. Karsten NB I 60; Zupitza Gutt. 43, 154; Persson Beiträge 85; Torp-Falk 464; Holthausen AEEW 271, ANW 249 (ON < MLG); Pokorny I 922; Fraenkel 804; Vries ANEW 488; Knapp PBB XCVI 210– 211; Lühr-Matzel KZ XLIX 257–258; Heidermanns 481–482; Kluge-Seebold 720.

Goth skaidan ‘to separate, to divide’, OE sceádan id., OFris skētha id., OS skēdan id., OHG sceidan id. Continues zero grade of IE *skēi-t- (Brugmann IF XXXII 179–181). Related to *skīđanan. Zupitza Gutt. 150; Torp-Falk 463; Feist 427; Holthausen AEEW 272; Prokosch CGG 176–180; Pokorny I 921; Onions 818; Seebold 402–404; Lehmann GED 308; Kluge- Seebold 715.
ON skeina ‘to scratch, to wound slightly’, OE scænan ‘to break’, OFris skēnia id., OHG ir-skeinen id. Related to *skinō. Holthausen AEEW 270; Vries ANEW 488.
ON skeiä ‘race, course’, OS skēth ‘difference’. Derived from *skaiđanan ~ *skaiþanan. Torp-Falk 463.

Burg *skaiþi ‘sheath’, ON skeiä ‘slay, weaver’s rod’, pl. skeiäar ‘scabbard’ (in pl. also root stem and i-stem), OE sceáä ‘sheath’, OFris skēthe id., OHG skeida id. Derived from *skaiđanan ~ *skaiþanan. Torp-Falk 464; Holthausen AEEW 2774; Pokorny I 921; Vries ANEW 487; Zalizniak II 210; Onions 818; Seebold 403; Köbler 713; Kluge-Seebold 715.
ON skeiäa ‘to sheath (a sword)’, ME scéthen id. Derived from *skaiþō. Structurally close to Lith skaitÿti ‘to count’. Fraenkel 792; Vries ANEW 488.

ON skaka ‘to shake’, OE sceacan ‘to shake, to quiver’, OFris pres. skeka ‘to run away’, OS pret. sg. skōk ‘to leave’. Related to Skt khájati ‘to stir, to churn’, W ysgogi ‘to stir’. Zupitza Gutt. 154; Torp-Falk 447; Holthausen AEEW 271; Jóhannesson IEW 822–824; Mayrhofer I 296; Pokorny I 923 (from *skeg-); Vries ANEW 480–481; Onions 815; Lerchner 227; Seebold 404–405.

ON skǫkull ‘pole of a cart’, OE sceacel ‘shackle’, EFris schakel ‘wooden semi-circle’ (fem.), LG schakel ‘chain link’ (fem.). Derived from *skakanan. Torp-Falk 447; Holthausen AEEW 271; Vries ANEW 511; Onions 814.

Goth skal ‘to owe, to have to’, ON skal ‘must, to be obliged’, OE sceal id., OFris skel id., OS scal id., OHG scal id. Related to Lith skeliū, skelėti ‘to owe’, OPrus skellānts ‘indebted’ (Siebs KZ XXXVII 320). Zupitza Gutt. 159 (to Lat scelus ‘crime’); Meringer IF XVIII 229–230 (to Lith skélti ‘to split’); Torp- Falk 459; Feist 435–436; Holthausen AEEW 284; Jóhannesson IEW 850; Pokorny I 927; Chemodanov SGGJa I 76; Fraenkel 799–800; Vries ANEW 507; Onions 815; Benveniste IEL 153–155; Seebold 405–406; Lehmann GED 313–314; Kluge-Seebold 770.
ON skelfa ‘to make shake, to make tremble’, OE á-scielfan ‘to destroy’. Derived from *skelbanan. Holthausen AEEW 277; Vries ANEW 488–489; Seebold 412.
ON skálgi ‘a kind of fish’, OE scealza id. Derived from *skelzaz ~ *skelxaz. Holthausen AEEW 273; Vries ANEW 481 (to *skaljō).
ON skel-fiskr ‘shellfish’, OE scil-fisc id., MLG schell-visch ‘haddock’, early G Schell-fisch id. Compound of *skaljō and *fiskaz. Carr Nom. comp. 78 (parallel formations).

ON skella ‘to make slam’, OE á-scilian ‘to shell’, MLG schellen ‘to divide, to separate’. Derived from *skaljō. Holthausen AEEW 277; (to MHG schel ‘springing’, OHG scelo ‘draught horse’); Vries ANEW 489.

Goth skalja ‘tile’, ON skel ‘shell’, OE scill id., MLG schelle ‘shell, scale’. Derived from *skalō. Close to Alb halë ‘fish bone, splinter; pine’ (Persson KZ XXXIII 285) < PAlb *skalā. Zupitza Gutt. 151; Torp-Falk 458; Holthausen AEEW 277; Feist 427–428; Pokorny I 924; Vries ANEW 488 (directly to IE *skel- ‘to split’); Zalizniak II 218; Onions 819; Lehmann GED 308; Kluge-Seebold 710; Orel AED 141.

Goth skalks ‘servant’, ON skálkr ‘rogue, slave’, OE scealc ‘servant, man’, OFris skalk ‘slave’, OS skalk ‘servant’, OHG scalc id. Etymologically identical with Slav *xolkъ ‘bachelor’ (with a difference of voiced/unvoiced inlaut consonants). The latter is clearly connected with Slav *xolpъ ‘servant’ and *xolstъ ‘unmarried’, thus implying the reconstruction of IE *skel-/*skol- (Brückner KZ LI 235: from *skel- ‘to cut’, treating the servant as originally *‘maimed’). Arbois de Jubainville MSL VII 291–292 (from Celtic, cf. OIr scoloc ‘vassal, novice’); Friesen Gemin. (to Norw skalk ‘bread stump’ < IE *skel- ‘to cut’ which might strengthen Brückner’setymology); Grienberger Got. 187 (to *skala); Siebs KZ XXXVII 313 (to Goth kalkjo ‘whore’); Brugmann IF XIX 381 (from *s˚el- ‘to jump’); Much Wu S I 47 (same as Friesen); Marstrander NTS I 142 (from non-IE Caucasian xkalax, xkalak ‘servant’); Güntert Labyrinth 40–42 (to *skel- ‘to cut’, treating the ‘servant’ as *‘stone mason’); Torp-Falk 460 (to *skalkaz II); Holthausen AEEW 273; Feist 428; Vries ANEW 482; Zalizniak I 135; Knobloch Muttersprache LXXXIX 45– 46; Lehmann GED 308–309 (šfrom the designation of a subjugated people”); Trubachev ESSJa VIII 64–65; Kluge- Seebold 710.

ON skálkr ‘sword’, EFris schalk ‘small plank preventing the surface from being penetrated by a nail’, MLG schalc ‘support on which the rafter is resting’. Any connection with *skalmō? Torp-Falk 460; Vries ANEW 482 (to
ON skelkja ‘to mock’, MHG be-schelken id. Related to *skalkaz I. Vries ANEW 489.
ON sbst. skellr ‘loud splash’, OE sbst. scelle ‘concisium’, MLG schal(l) ‘sound’, OHG scal id. Derived from *skellanan. Holthausen AEEW 277; Seebold 413; Matzel KZ CV 102–103.
ON skella ‘to clash, to knock’, OE scellan ‘to make a noise, to sound’, OHG gi-skellen ‘to make sound, to ring’. Causative of *skellanan. Holthausen AEEW 277; Seebold 413.

ON skálm ‘short sword’, EFris schalm ‘thin stripe of wood’, Du schalm id. Identical with Thrac σκάλμη: μάχαιρα Θρ&κία (Hes.), Gk σκαλμός ‘pin to which the oar is fastened’ (Fick Spracheinh. 419). Torp-Falk 458; Detschew Thr. 454; Pokorny I 923–924; Vries ANEW 511; Frisk II 716.

OE scealu ‘shell, husk’, MLG schale ‘bark’, OHG scala ‘shell, hull’. Derived from *skeljanan ~ *skelōjanan. Cf. *skēlō with another ablaut grade. Identical with Alb halë ‘fish bone’, Lith skalā ‘splinter’, further cf. Slav *skala ‘rock’ with *-ō-. Zupitza Gutt. 151, 154; Güntert Labyrinth 40–41; Torp-Falk 458; Holthausen AEEW 273; Pokorny I 924–925; Fraenkel 792–793; Zalizniak II 218; Onions 793; Lehmann GED 308; Orel AED 141.
ON skamm-laus ‘shameless’, OE sceam-leás id., MLG schame-lōs id., OHG scama-lōs id. Compound of *skamō and *lausaz. Carr Nom. comp. 60.

Goth skaman sik ‘to be ashamed’, ON skamma ‘to shame, to disgrace’, OE sceamian ‘to be ashamed’, OFris skamia id., MLG schamen id., OHG scamēn, scamōn id. Derived from *skamō. Zupitza Gutt. 195; Torp-Falk 452; Holthausen AEEW 271; Feist 428; Vries ANEW 482; Onions 816; Lehmann GED 309.
ON skemmd ‘shame, disgrace’, MLG schemede id. Derived from *skamēnan ~ *skamōjanan. Vries ANEW 489.

ON skammr ‘short’, OE sceam, scamm id., OHG scam id. Of unknown origin. Probably related to Av kamna- ‘little’ < *kombh-no-. Bartholomae 440; Karsten NB II 232–233, 456; Zupitza Gutt. 152; Torp-Falk 453; Holthausen AEEW 271; Martinet Gém. 175; Pokorny I 929; Vries ANEW 482; Onions 794; Heidermanns 484.
ON skemma ‘to shorten’, OHG skemmen id. Derived from *skam(m)az. Torp-Falk 453; Heidermanns 484.

ON skǫm, skǫmm ‘shame’, OE sceamu id., OFris skome id., OS skama id., OHG scama id. Substantivization of *skam(m)az without gemination. Johansson KZ XXX 428–430 (from *˚em- ‘to cover’); Torp-Falk 452 (< *skem- ‘to cover’); Holthausen AEEW 271; Feist 428–429; Pokorny I 557; Vries ANEW 512; Zalizniak II 225; Onions 816; Pisani Gedenkschr. Güntert 285–286 (to Umbr eskamitu ‘male pudenda’); Lehmann GED 309 (to Gk σκαμβός ‘crooked’); Kluge-Seebold 711.

Goth skanda ‘shame’, OE skeand id., OFris skande id., MLG schande id., OHG scanta id. Could continue sbst. *skanđaz ‘ashamed’ (OHG scant). Probably connected with Gk σκινθός ‘diver’, Lith skęstū, sk.sti ‘to sink, to drown’, OPrus auskiēndlai ‘to set, to go down’. Fick KZ XXII 111; Persson Beiträge 155; Zupitza Gutt. 195; Torp-Falk 452–453 (to *˚em- ‘to cover’); Trautmann BSW 265; Feist 429 (to *skamō); Holthausen AEEW 271; Pokorny I 930; Fraenkel 803; Zalizniak II 227; Onions 816; Lehmann GED 309; Bammesberger Nom. 116; Heidermanns 483; Kluge-Seebold 711.

ON skakkr ‘slew, wry, distorted’. Related to Gk σκάζω ‘to limp’ < *σκ–γωō, MIr scingim ‘to spring’. Karsten NB I 45; Zupitza Gutt. 23, 151; Torp- Falk 450; Holthausen AEEW 271; Hoops EG V 94; Sommer Festschr. Debrunner 425–427; Pokorny I 930; Vries ANEW 481; Frisk II 714; Onions 816; Heidermanns 485; Kluge-Seebold 717.
Norw skank ‘shank’, OE sceanca id., LG schanke id., OHG scanc ‘rack, cupboard’. Derived from *skankaz. Torp-Falk 450; Holthausen AEEW 271; Vries ANEW 481; Onions 816; Heidermanns 485.

ON skekkja ‘to set askew, to displace’, OE scencan ‘to pour out’, OFris skenka id., OS skenkian id., OHG skenken ‘to give to drink, to pour, to mix’. Derived from *skankaz. Torp- Falk 450; Holthausen AEEW 275; Pokorny I 930; Vries ANEW 488; Heidermanns 485; Kluge-Seebold 717.

ON skap ‘shape, form’, OE ze-sceap ‘creation, decree’, OS skap ‘vessel’, MHG ge-schaf ‘creature’. With irregular *-p- < IE *-b-, to be compared with Lat scabō ‘to scrape’, Lith skambū, skābti ‘to pick’. For the semantic development cf. various reflexes of IE *te˚þ-. Zupitza Gutt. 150; Persson Beiträge 142, 784; Torp-Falk 450; Trautmann BSW 262; Holthausen AEEW 272; Walde- Hofmann II 484–485; Feist 200–201; Pokorny I 930–933; Fraenkel 791; Vries ANEW 483; Onions 816; Seebold 407; Lehmann GED 148–149.

OFris schep ‘cupboard’, OS skapo ‘vessel for liquids’, OHG scaffo id. Cf. also ON skeppa ‘bushel’ < *skappjōn. Despite the differences in consonantism, close to Gk σκάφη ‘tub’, σκάφος ‘ship’. Further see *skabanan. Torp-Falk 451; Pokorny I 932; Frisk II 718.

Goth ga-skapjan ‘to create’, ON skepja ‘to shape, to form’, OE scippan ‘to shape, to form, to create’, OFris skeppa id., OS skeppian id., OHG skepfen id. Derived from *skap(j)an ~ *skapōn (in its original meaning *‘shape’?). Kuhn KZ I 139 (related to Skr skabh-, skambh- ‘to support’); Grimm KZ I 81; Torp-Falk 450–451; Feist 200–201; Holthausen AEEW 277–278; Kluge 631–632; Jóhannesson IEW 824–826; Pokorny I 930–933; Vries ANEW 483, 489; Onions 816; Seebold 406–408 (to Lith skõpti ‘to hollow, to chisel’, Slav *skopiti ‘to castrate’); Lehmann GED 148; Kluge-Seebold 709.
ON skapa ‘to shape, to form, to make’, OHG gi-skaffōn ‘to form, to build’. Derived from *skap(j)an ~ *skapōn. Cf. *skapjanan. Torp-Falk 451; Seebold 407; Kluge-Seebold 709.

ON skarfr ‘cormorant’, OE scræf id., OHG scarbo id. (n-stem). Identical with *skarbaz II? Suolahti DVN 393–397; Torp-Falk 457 (to *skrafōjanan); Holthausen AEEW 282; Vries ANEW 484; Kluge-Seebold 712. *skarbaz II sb.m.: ON skarfr ‘plank, board’, MLG scharf ‘broken fragment’. Derived from *skerfanan. Torp-Falk 457; Vries ANEW 484; Seebold 415.

ON skarä ‘notch, chink’, OE sceard ‘tile, sherd’, OFris skerd ‘cut, notch’, MLG schart ‘crack, chink’, MHG schart, scharte ‘notch, gap’ (fem.). Substantivized *skarđaz. Torp-Falk 454; Holthausen AEEW 273–274; Vries ANEW 484; Onions 816–817; Kluge- Seebold 713.

ON skarär ‘diminished’, OE sceard ‘notched, hacked, gashed’, OFris skerd ‘nicked’, OS skard ‘chopped up, wounded’, OHG lidu-scart ‘mutilated’. Probably related to OIr scert ‘part’, Lat scortum ‘hide, skin’. Further see *skeranan. Karsten NB II 333; Torp-Falk 454 (substantivized participle of *sker-); Holthausen AEEW 273; Walde-Hofmann II 497–498; Pokorny I 940; Vries ANEW 484; Onions 817; Bammesberger Nom. 251; Heidermanns 485–486; Kluge- Seebold 713.
ON skeräa ‘to diminish’, OE part. ze-scyrded ‘destroyed’, OHG skerten ‘to damage, to cut’. Derived from *skarđaz. Vries ANEW 490–491; Heidermanns 486.
ON sker ‘isolated rock in the sea’, MLG scher ‘rock, cliff’, MHG scher id. Derived from *skeranan. Zupitza Gutt. 154; Vries ANEW 490.

Goth us-skarjan ‘to tear out, to sober’, OE scirian ‘to separate, to divide, to allot’, OS skerian ‘to separate, to distribute, to allot’, OHG skerien id. Causative of *skeranan. Grienberger Got. 233; Torp-Falk 453–454; Holthausen AEEW 278; Feist 534; Pokorny I 939; Lehmann GED 383.

Norw skere ‘plowshare’, OE scear id. (< *skaraz), OFris skere id. (fem.), MLG schar, schare id. (neut., fem.), OHG scaro id. Derived from *skeranan. Derivationally close to Lith skarā ‘rag’, Slav *skora ‘hide, skin’ (Fick III 453). Persson Beiträge I 176; Torp-Falk 454; Būga III 950–951.

ON skarn ‘dung, dirt’, OE scearn ‘dung, filth’, OFris skern ‘dung’, MLG scharn id. Related to Hitt šakkar ‘dirt’, Gk σκῶρ id. Further to *skeranan. Petersson SVSL I 95 (*-rn- reflects heteroclysis similar to Gk and Hitt); Zupitza Gutt. 155; Torp-Falk 456; Holthausen AEEW 274; Pokorny I 947–948; Vries ANEW 484; Frisk II 746; Lerchner 228; Kluge-Seebold 720.

ON skǫr ‘crowd, multitude, army’, MLG schare ‘crowd, share’, OHG scara ‘crowd, army’. Derived from *skeranan. Zupitza Gutt. 154; Torp-Falk 453; Holthausen AEEW 274; Pokorny I 939; Zalizniak II 183; Seebold 414; Bammesberger Nom. 108; Kluge- Seebold 712.
ON skǫr ‘hair, rim, edge’, OE scearu ‘shearing, cutting’. Derived from *skeranan. Torp-Falk 453.

ON skarpr ‘sharp’, OE scearp id., OFris skerp id., OS skarp id., OHG skarpf ‘sharp, rough’. Identical with Latv skarbs ‘sharp, strict, rough’. For the labial extension of *sker- ‘to cut’ cf. also MIr cerb ‘sharp’, Toch B kärpye ‘rough’, Slav *š‘ьrbъ ‘incomplete, deficient’. Note ON part. skorpinn ‘shrivelled’ reflecting str. *skerpanan. Schröder IF XVII 459–465; Karsten NB I 67, II 228; Zupitza Gutt. 155; Torp-Falk 456; Holthausen AEEW 274; Pokorny I 943; Kuhn Festschr. Wagner 107–113; Vries ANEW 485; Onions 817; Vasmer- Trubachev IV 503–504; Seebold 416; Lühr Expr. 266; Heidermanns 487; Kluge-Seebold 712.
OESc skærpa ‘to press, to tuck’, OS gi-skerpian ‘to sharpen’, MHG scherpfen id. Derived from *skarpaz. Heidermanns 487.

ON skart ‘show, finery’, MHG scharz ‘jump, spring’. Derived from a poorly attested str. *skertanan > MHG scherzen ‘to spring, to jump’. The latter is related to Skt kūrdati ‘to spring’, Gk κραδάω ‘to swing’ (a secondary denominative based on *κέρδω). Torp-Falk 455; Pokorny I 934; Vries ANEW 485–486; Frisk II 1–2.
Norw skarra ‘to make a scratching sound’, MLG scharren ‘to scratch’, MHG scharren id. Derived from *skerzanan. Torp-Falk 457.

Goth skatts ‘money, coin’, ON skattr ‘tribute’, OE sceatt ‘property, goods, wealth’, OFris sket ‘money, cattle’, OS skatt ‘piece of money, wealth’, OHG scaz ‘denarius, money, wealth’. A very difficult word. Grienberger Got. 188 (with the original meaning ‘coin, piece of money’, from Gmc *ska(b)- ‘to cut’); Schröder KZ XLVIII 266–272 (reconstructs *skwattaz); Wood MP XVIII 86 (from *s∞h 6 t⁄o- ‘covered with pelt’); Marstranderapud Knutsson Zfslav Ph XV 129 (from a Celtic ethn. Σκόττοι); Velten JEGP XXIX 497 (semantic influence of Lat pecūnia); Torp-Falk 448–449; Lewy KZ LXII 263–264 (from Osset sk’ætt ‘cattle-shed’); Stender- Petersen 313–315 (Gmc > Slav *skotъ ‘cattle, property, money’); Feist 429; Mladenov 585 (related to Slav *skotъ ‘cattle’); Holthausen PBB LXVI 267 (to Lat scateō ‘to bubble, to gush’), AEEW 274; Martinet Gém. 64, 150, 200; Jakobson IJSLP 1/2 271 (from Slav *skotъ), SR IV 605–606 (Slav *skotъ < *skoptъ, to *skopiti ‘to castrate’); Vries ANEW 486 (Oriental Wanderwort); Zalizniak I 135; Martynov Jazyk 28– 29 (same as Jakobson); Onions 795; Benveniste IEL 48, 154–155; Abaev III 123–124 (Gmc < Slav < Osset < Iran *fšu-kata-); Lehmann GED 309; Kluge- Seebold 713.

Goth skaþjan ‘to injure, to harm’, ON skeäja ‘to scathe, to hurt’, OE sceääan id. Derived from *skaþōn. Bugge IF I 447 (adds Arm xat'arem ‘to destroy’); Torp-Falk 449; Feist 429–430; Holthausen AEEW 274; Pokorny I 950; Vries ANEW 487; Vendryes S-46–47; Seebold 408; Lehmann GED 309–310.

ON skaäa ‘to scathe, to hurt’, OE sceaäian ‘to hurt, to harm’, OFris skathia, skethia id., OS scathōn id., OHG scadōn id. Derived from *skaþōn. Torp-Falk 449; Holthausen AEEW 274; Pokorny I 950; Vries ANEW 479; Onions 795; Lehmann GED 310.

ON skaäi ‘harm, damage’, OE sceaäa ‘scathe, harm, injury’, OFris skatha ‘harm, loss’, OS skatho ‘harm’, OHG scado id. Cf. also Goth neut. skaþis ‘harm, injustice’. Despite the phonetic discrepancies, related to Gk *σκῆθος in é-σκηθής ‘unharmed’, OIr scathaim ‘to scathe, to harm’ (Osthoff PBB XIII 459). Torp-Falk 449; Holthausen AEEW 274–275 (the first element of Sca(n)dinavia identified with *skaþōn); Feist 429–430; Jóhannesson IEW 824; Pokorny I 950; Vries ANEW 480; Zalizniak II 224; Frisk I 164; Onions 795; Lehmann GED 309–310; Bammesberger Nom. 179; Kluge-Seebold 709.
Goth skaþuls ‘harmful’, OHG skadal ‘wicked, spoiled’. Derived from *skaþōn, *skaþjanan. Feist 429; Seebold 408; Lehmann GED 310; Heidermanns 488.

Goth skauda-raip ‘thong, strap’, ON pl. skauäir ‘horse’s sheath’, MLG schode ‘horse’s sheath’ (neut.), MHG schōte ‘pod’. Related to Gk σκῦτος ‘skin, leather’, Lat scūtum ‘shield’. Torp- Falk 468; Walde-Hofmann II 503; Feist 430; Pokorny I 951–953; Vries ANEW 486 (to ON skjóäa ‘small skin-bag’); Frisk II 744–745; Lehmann GED 310; Kluge- Seebold 741.

ON skauf ‘sheaf’, OE sceáf ‘sheaf, bundle’, OFris scāf ‘bundle, sheaf’, OS skōf id., OHG scoub ‘bundle of straw’. Connected with Slav *‘ubъ ‘hairlock, bundle’ < *(s)keubho-. Zupitza Gutt. 156; Torp- Falk 470; Holthausen AEEW 272; Pokorny I 956; Zalizniak II 172; Vries ANEW 486–487; Onions 817; Stang LS 17; Trubachev ESSJa IV 126; Kluge- Seebold 713–714.

Goth skauns ‘graceful, goodlooking’, OE scíne ‘beautiful’, OFris skēne id., OS scōni ‘brilliant, beautiful’, OHG scōni ‘beautiful’. Further related to *skawaz. Cf. probably NPhryg prop. Jeunh unless it continues *jen W` = Gk fem. ξένη ‘host, stranger’ (Orel Phrygian 438). Karsten NB II 178; Torp-Falk 465; Feist 431; Holthausen AEEW 277; Wissmann NP 40; Pokorny I 588; Onions 818; Lehmann GED 310–311; Heidermanns 488–489 (reconstructs
Goth guda-skaunei ‘godly form, θεοῦ μορφή’, OS skōnī ‘splendor, beauty’, OHG scōnī id. Derived from *skauniz. Feist 431; Lehmann GED 310.

Goth neut. or masc. skaut(s) ‘hem, edge, fringe’, ON skaut ‘corner of a square cloth, sheet’, OE sceát ‘corner, angle, nook’, OFris skāt ‘coattail, skirt’, MLG schōt id., MHG schōz ‘fold, apron’. Derived from *skeutanan. Meyer Goth. 1036 (to Lat cauda ‘tail’); Torp-Falk 468; Walde-Hoffmann I 185; Holthausen AEEW 274; Feist 431; Pokorny I 956; Vries ANEW 487; Zalizniak II 183; Onions 818–819; Lehmann GED 311; Kluge-Seebold 741.
ON skeyta ‘to convey a piece of land to another, to join together’, MLG schœten ‘to splice, to piece together’. Derived from *skautan ~ *skautaz. Vries ANEW 491.
ON skauti ‘kerchief, a square piece of wood’, OE sceáta ‘corner, angle, cloth’, MLG schote ‘pot’, MHG fem. scōze ‘fold, apron’. See *skautan ~ *skautaz. Holthausen AEEW 274; Vries ANEW 487.

Goth us-skaus ‘sober’ (leg. un-skaus), ON opin-skár ‘made public’. Identical with Gk (yuo-)σκόος ‘sacrificing priest’. Psilander Festschr. Lidén 229; Torp-Falk 465; Feist 534–535; Sturtevant Ph Q VII 78; Pokorny I 588; Vries ANEW 107; Frisk I 695.

OE sceáwian ‘to look, to observe’, OFris skawia id., OS skawōn id., OHG scouwōn id. Derived from *skawaz. Related to Gk κοέω ‘to notice’, Lat caueō ‘to beware’ (< *coueō), Slav *‘ujǫ, *‘uti ‘to feel, to perceive’ without initial *s-. Cf. OE háwian ‘to view, to look’ < *xē(u)w- < IE *kēu-. Thurneysen KZ XXVIII 155; Solmsen KZ XXXVII 1–3; Torp-Falk 465; Brückner 183 (to Slav *xovati ‘to hide, to keep’); Walde-Hofmann 186–187; Vries ANEW 486–487; Pokorny I 587–588; Frisk I 890–891; Onions 823; Trubachev ESSJa IV 134–136; Lehmann GED 310, 378; Kluge-Seebold 714.
MLG skēn ‘to happen (to smb.)’, OHG gi-skehan id. Related to OIr scuchid ‘to leave, to go away’ < *skoketi, Slav *sko‘iti ‘to spring, to hop’. Torp-Falk 448; Pokorny I 922–923; Kluge-Seebold 318.

ON skjálfa ‘to shake, to shiver, to quiver’, OE scelfan id. Of unknown origin. Zupitza Gutt. 154 (to Slav *kolîbati ‘to shake’); Torp-Falk 461; Jóhannesson IEW 850–851; Pokorny I 928; Vries ANEW 495; Seebold 411– 412.
ON skjálf ‘shelf, seat’, OE scilfe ‘shelf, ledge’. Related to *skelbanan. Torp-Falk 461 (to *skel-p- ‘to split’); Pokorny I 926.
