ON skilda ‘to shield’, OE scildan ‘to shield, to protect’, MHG schilten ‘to shield’. Derived from *skelđuz ~ *skelđiz. Holthausen AEEW 278; Vries ANEW 492.
ON skjald-borg ‘wall of shields, testudo’, OE scild-burz id., OHG scilt-burg id. Compound of *skelđuz ~ *skelđiz and *burzz. Carr Nom. comp. 52.

Goth skildus ‘shield’, ON skjǫldr id., OE scild id. (< *skelđaz), OFris skeld id., MLG schilt id., OHG skilt id. Derivative of *skeljanan ~ *skelōjanan (Kögel IF IV 319: to Lith skīltis ‘slice’, skīlti ‘to split off’). Aufrecht KZ I 360–361; Hoffmann BB XVIII 286–287 (to Skt khe†a- ‘shield’); Charpentier IF XXVIII 185 (against Hoffmann); Uhlenbeck KZ XX 560; Zupitza Gutt. 151; Torp-Falk 458; Feist 432; Pokorny I 924; Vries ANEW 486–487; Zalizniak II 203; Onions 820; Lehmann GED 311; Kluge- Seebold 721.

ON skjálgr ‘wry, oblique’, OE sceolh ‘oblique, wary’, MLG schēle ‘squinting’, OHG skelah ‘squinting, oblique’. An Erweiterung related to Gk σκολιός ‘curved, bent’, Lat scelus ‘evil deed, sin, crime’, Arm šel ‘crooked’, Alb ᾶαλἅ ‘lame’ (Johansson PBB XIV 296–298). Karsten NB II 291–292; Zupitza Gutt. 75; Torp-Falk 460; Holthausen AEEW 275; Walde-Hofmann II 492; Kluge 627 (identical with MDu schelu ‘crooked’, MHG schelb id. < *skelxwaz); Pokorny I 550, 928; Vries ANEW 495; Frisk II 723–724; Heidermanns 493–494; Kluge-Seebold 714– 715; Orel AED 49.
ON skelgjask ‘to come askew’, MLG schelen id., OHG skilihen id. Derived from *skelzaz ~ *skelxaz. Holthausen AEEW 276; Vries ANEW 489.

ON skilja ‘to part, to divide’, OE scilian ‘to separate’, OFris skilla ‘to battle’, MLG schelen, schillen ‘to split, to separate’. Related to Hitt iškalla- ‘to tear apart, to slit open’, Gk σκάλλω ‘to hoe, to harrow’ < *σκ¬ωō, Lith skeliū, skélti ‘to split’. Zupitza Gutt. 151; Torp-Falk 458; Holthausen AEEW 277; Pokorny I 924; Fraenkel 801; Vries ANEW 492; Frisk II 715–716; Tischler HEG I 397–398; Lehmann GED 311–312.
ON skelkr ‘fear’, MHG schelch ‘male deer’. Distantly related to *skelbanan. Pokorny I 929 (to MHG schel ‘springing’, OHG scelo ‘draught horse’); Vries ANEW 489.

ON skjalla ‘to clatter, to slam’, OE scillan ‘to cause to sound’, OHG skellan ‘to sound’. Related to Slav *skoliti ‘to howl, to whine’. Torp-Falk 459; Holthausen AEEW 277; Jóhannesson IEW 247; Pokorny I 550; Vries ANEW 495; Seebold 412–413; Vasmer- Trubachev III 662; Kluge-Seebold 711, 716.

ON skjallr ‘loud, clashing, shrill’, OE scill ‘sonorous, sounding’, OHG ab-skelli ‘absurd, discordant’. Derived from *skellanan. Karsten NB I 35–36; Torp-Falk 459; Holthausen AEEW 277; Vries ANEW 495; Seebold 413 (reconstructs *skalliz); Matzel Festschr. Schröder 98, Kratylos XXXIV 135, KZ CV 102–103; Heidermanns 494.

Goth skilliggs ‘shilling, golden solidus’, ON skillingr ‘gold coin, aureus’, OE scillinz ‘shilling’, OFris scilling id., OS skilling id., OHG skilling id. Derived from *skellanan (Diefenbach VW II 249). Persson KZ XXXIII 286 (to *skaljō); Schröder KZ XLVIII 254–256, ZDADL XLVIII 254 (to *skelđuz ~ *skelđiz similar to or influenced by the Romance usage of Lat scūtum ‘shield’ as a name of a coin); Torp-Falk 459; Brøndal Substrat. 147–149 (from Rom *scella < *silicula ‘little coin’); Marstrander Corr. 25 (originally, shield-like clipeoli on the Roman gravestones); Holthausen AEEW 278–279; Feist 433; Vries ANEW 492; Onions 820; Lehmann GED 312 (from *skel- ‘to split, to cut’); Kluge-Seebold 721.

ON skinn ‘skin (of a little animal)’, MHG scint ‘fruit shell’. Probably related to Bret skant ‘dandruff’ < *sk–to-. Zupitza Gutt. 156; Torp-Falk 449–450; Pokorny I 929; Chemodanov SGGJa I 99; Vries ANEW 493.

ON skera ‘to cut, to slaughter’, OE sceran ‘to cut, to shear’, OFris skera id., MLG skeren id., OHG skeran id. Connected with Gk σκείρω ‘to cut off’, κείρω ‘to cut, to shear’, Alb shqerr ‘to tear (cloth)’, OIr scaraim ‘to separate’, Lith skiriū, skīrti id. (Zupitza Gutt. 154–155). Grimm KZ I 83; Torp-Falk 453–455; Trautmann BSW 266–267; Holthausen AEEW 278; Trier PBB LXIX 425–432; Jóhannesson IEW 835–842; Pokorny I 938–940; Fraenkel 808; Vries ANEW 490; Frisk I 810– 811; Onions 818; Vendryes S-33–34; Seebold 413–414; Lehmann GED 383; Orel AED 433; Kluge-Seebold 717– 718.
OE sceorfan ‘to gnaw, to bite’. Related to Latv šķērpêt ‘to cut the turf with a plough’. Zupitza Gutt. 155; Torp-Falk 456; Holthausen AEEW 276; Pokorny I 938–947; Seebold 414–415.

OS of-skerran ‘to scratch’, OHG scerran id. Related to Skt káßati id., Lat carrō id., Lith karšiū, kaıšti ‘to card, to comb (of wool)’ < *(s)ker-s-. Torp-Falk 457; Walde-Hofmann II I 173–174; Pokorny I 532; Fraenkel 224.

Goth af-skiuban ‘to reject’, OSwed skiuva ‘to shove’, OE sceófan, scúfan id., OFris pres. skūva id., MLG schuven id., OHG skioban id. Related to Lith skumbū, skūbti ‘to hurry up’, Slav *skubti ‘to pluck’. Torp-Falk 470; Trautmann BSW 263; Feist 9; Holthausen AEEW 284; Jóhannesson IEW 822; Pokorny I 955; Fraenkel 820; Vries ANEW 506; Onions 823; Seebold 416–417; Stang LS 50; Lehmann GED 6; Kluge-Seebold 719.

ON skjól ‘shelter, cover’, EFris schūl ‘protection, rescue’, MLG schūl ‘hiding place’. Related to *skuwwōn. Zupitza Gutt. 153; Torp-Falk 466; Pokorny I 951; Vries ANEW 495; Hilmarsson TIES V 153– 154 (to Toch B oßßale ‘north’ < *-ske⁄elo-).
ON skÿla ‘to screen, to shelter’, MHG schūlen ‘to be hidden’. Derived from *skeulan ~ *skūlan. Torp-Falk 466.
ON skjómi ‘flickering light’, OE sceám ‘white horse’. Derived from *skeu-: Skt skunóti ‘to rummage’. Holthausen AEEW 273; Pokorny I 954; Vries ANEW 496 (to

ON skjór ‘magpie’, MLG schare id. Identical with Slav *š‘urъ ‘rat’. Bruinier KZ XXXIV 366 (to ON skjarr ‘shy, timid’); Friesen SVSU VII/2 40 (to *skēwjanan); Holthausen AWN 254 (to *skauniz); Pokorny I 598; Vries ANEW 496.

Crim. Goth schieten ‘to shoot’, ON skjóta id., OE sceótan id., OFris skiata id., OS skiotan ‘telo sequi’, OHG skiozan ‘to shoot, to throw’. Connected with Alb h(j)edh ‘to throw, to shoot’ < PAlb *skeuda. Zupitza Gutt. 156; Torp- Falk 467–468; Holthausen AEEW 276; Feist 414; Pokorny I 955–956 (to Skt códati ‘to incite, to animate’); Vries ANEW 496; Onions 822; Seebold 417–418 (to Lith šáuju, šáuti ‘to shoot’); Lehmann GED 298; Kluge-Seebold 720; Orel AED 143–144.
ON skjótr ‘swift’, OE sceót ‘ready, quick’. Derived from *skeutanan. Karsten NB II 453–454; Torp-Falk 468; Holthausen AEEW 276; Wissmann NP 56; Vries ANEW 496; Bammesberger Nom. 240; Heidermanns 497.
ON skjótr ‘vehicle, horse’, OHG scioz ‘side of a fork’. Substantivized *skeutaz. Vries ANEW 496.

ON comp. skári ‘better, advisable’, OHG schæhe ‘squinting’ (secondary ja-stem). Derived from *skexanan. Karsten NB I 63–64, 72–73, II 48; Torp-Falk 448; Pokorny I 930; Vries ANEW 108; Matzel KZ CV 115; Heidermanns 490–491.

Norw dial. skaak ‘fork shaft’, LG schake ‘chain link’. Probably related to MIr ail-cheng ‘rack (for weapons)’, Lith kéngë ‘hook’. Torp-Falk 447 (to Lat cingō ‘to go round in a circle, to surround’); Holthausen AEEW 271; Pokorny I 537–538; Vries ANEW 511; Onions 814.

ON skál ‘bowl’ (pl. from *skēliz), OS skāla id., OHG scāla id. Connected with *skalō. Güntert Labyrinth 40–41; Torp-Falk 458; Pokorny I 925 (to IE *skel- ‘to cut’); Vries ANEW 481; Zalizniak II 194; Onions 793; Lehmann GED 308; Kluge-Seebold 710.

OE sceáp ‘sheep’, OFris skēp id., OS skāp id., OHG scāf id. Of unknown origin. Probably belongs to a group of Wanderwörter similar to Alb c(j)ap, sqap ‘he-goat’ (> Rum flap id.), Slav *capъ id., presumably irradiating from Iranian (NPers ‘apiš ‘one year old goat’). Fick BB V 169 (to Skt chāga- ‘he-goat’); Suolahti ANT 121–124 (same as Fick); Torp-Falk 451 (to NPers ‘apiš); Holthausen IF XXXIX 74 (to *skapjanan, cf. Dan kreatur ‘cattle’), AEEW Holthausen AEEW 273; Partridge 615 (non-IE, to Eg s.t id.); Zalizniak I 143; Martynov Jazyk 27–28 (from Slav *skopъ ‘castrated ram’); Onions 818; Davis IF XCVI 118–135 (to IE *skebh- ‘to cut with a sharp instrument’); Kluge-Seebold 709.

ON pl. skæri ‘scissors’, OE sceár id., OFris skēre id., OS skāra id., OHG scāra ‘thongs, scissors’. Derived from *skeranan. Torp-Falk 454; Holthausen AEEW 273; Pokorny I 940; Vries ANEW 510; Zalizniak II 218; Seebold 414; Kluge-Seebold 717.

Goth skewjan ‘to go (forth)’, ON skæva ‘to stride’. Cf. also EFris schōjen ‘to go, to hurry’, Du schooien id. < *skaujanan. Related to *s˚eu-: Lith šáuju, šáuti ‘to shove, to shoot’, Slav *sujǫ, *sovati ‘to push, to throw’. Grimm KZ I 83; Pipping Xenia 169; Torp NNEO 606 (to Lith šuõliais ‘galloping’); Zupitza Gutt. 195 (same as Torp); Torp-Falk 466; Trautmann BSW 300; Feist 432 (to IE *skek⁄- ‘to jump’); Jóhannesson IEW 205 (to *skūrō ~ *skūraz), 822 (to *skakanan); Pokorny I 954–955; Vasmer- Trubachev III 705; Vries ANEW 511; Lehmann GED 311.

OHG skiteri, sketari ‘loose, thin’. Close to Skt chidrá- ‘pierced’, Gk σκιδαρόν: ἀραιόν (Hes.), Latv šķidrs ‘thin’. Further see *skīđan. Torp-Falk 463–464; Trautmann BSW 263–264; Mayrhofer I 407; Pokorny I 920; Fraenkel 805–806; Hiersche TA 102– 103; Euler IGG 115; Heidermanns 498; Kluge-Seebold 746.

ON skipta ‘to divide, to part, to share’, OE sciftan ‘to divide, to separate into shares’, OFris skifta ‘to determine’, MLG schiften ‘to divide, to exchange’. Connected with *skipōjanan. Torp-Falk 463; Holthausen AEEW 278; Vries ANEW 493; Onions 820.

ON skima ‘to look all around’, OE scimian ‘to grow dark’, MDu schemen ‘to give shadow’, MHG schimmen ‘to gleam, to glisten’. Related to *skīmōn. Cf. an expressive derivative *skimrōjanan > OE scimrian ‘to shine, to glisten’, MLG schēmeren ‘to be shaded or shadowy’, G schimmern id. Holthausen AEEW 279; Vries ANEW 492; Onions 820.
ON skin ‘shining’, OHG gescin ‘appearance’. Derived from *skīnanan. Torp-Falk 462; Vries ANEW 492; Seebold 410.
OE scinu ‘shin’, MLG schene id., OHG scina id. Related to *skījan. Torp-Falk 463; Holthausen AEEW 279; Onions 820; Kluge-Seebold 720.
ON skip-ferä ‘seavoyage’, OE scip-fird ‘naval expedition’, MLG schip-vart ‘navigation’, OHG scif-fart id. Compound of *skipan and *farđiz. Carr Nom. comp. 78 (parallel formations).
ON skip-maär ‘sailor’, OE scip-mann id., OFris scip-mann id., MLG schip-man id., OHG scif-man id. Compound of *skipan and *mannz. Carr Nom. comp. 78 (parallel formations).

Goth skip ‘ship’, ON skip id., OE scip id., OFris skip id., OS skip id., OHG scif id. Originally, *‘structure, framework’ as demonstrated by the derivative *skipōjanan, cf. semantically Slav *sǫdъ ‘vessel, ship’ < *˚om-dhē-. Related to Lith ski‚bti ‘to unstitch, to rip, to make a hole, to cut (bread)’, Latv šķibît ‘to cut’ (Trier Zd Ph LXX 348–349: ‘ship’ < ‘skin-covered framework’). Bréal MSL VII 147 (to Gk σκάφος ‘hull of a ship’); Uhlenbeck PBB XXVII 131 (‘ship’ < ‘hollowed log’, to Gk σκίπων ‘staff’); Torp-Falk 463; Holthausen IF XXXIX 70 (to Lat cibus ‘dish, food’), AEEW 279; Feist 433–434; Pokorny I 922; Chemodanov SGGJa I 72; Fraenkel 805; Vries ANEW 493; Zalizniak I 143; Onions 821; Lehmann GED 312; Kluge- Seebold 720.
ON skip-stjóri ‘shipper, captain’, OE scip-steóra ‘steersman, pilot’. Compound of *skipan and *steurōn (see *steurō ~ *steur(j)an). Carr Nom. comp. 82 (parallel formations).

ON skipa ‘to order, to arrange, to establish’, OFris skipia ‘to set in order, to array’, MLG schippen id. Based on *skipan in its unattested original meaning *‘structure, framework’. Torp- Falk 463; Pokorny I 895 (to Lith skiebū, ski‚bti ‘to unstitch, to rip, to make a hole, to cut (bread)’, Slav *š‘ipati ‘to pinch’); Vries ANEW 493; Fraenkel 805; Onions 820; Lehmann GED 312.
ON skÿ ‘cloud’, OE sceó id., OS skio, skeo ‘light cloudiness’. Probably related to *skīnanan. Zupitza Gutt. 153; Torp-Falk 462; Holthausen AEEW 279; Pokorny I 951 (to *skeulan ~

ON skífa ‘slice’, ME schífe id., OFris skīve id., OS skīva id., OHG skība ‘ball, disk, slice’. Related to *skaibaz. Derivationally close to Gk sk,pvn ‘staff, cane’. Further connected with Gk σκοῖπος: ἡἐξοχή τῶν ξύλων, ἐφ’ ὧν efisi ofl κέραμοι (Hes.), Lat scīpiō ‘staff’. Torp-Falk 464–465; Walde-Hofmann II 496; Pokorny I 922; Vries ANEW 491; Frisk II 733; Onions 818; Kluge- Seebold 715.

Crim. Goth sched-iit ‘light’, ON skíä ‘firewood, ski’, skíäi ‘sheath’, OE scíd ‘shingle, a piece of wood split thin’, OFris skīd id., OHG scīt ‘log, wooden handle’. Related to OIr scíath ‘shield’ and Slav *š‘itъ id. Within Germanic, cf. *skaiđanan ~ *skaiþanan. Grienberger Got. XXX 129; Loewe IF XIII 12 (Crim. Goth < Iranian, cf. Av xšaēta- ‘glamor, radiance’); Torp-Falk 463–464; Holthausen AEEW 276; Feist 414; Pokorny I 921; Vries ANEW 491; Zalizniak I 143; Onions 820, 831; Seebold 403; Lehmann GED 298, 308; Kluge-Seebold 716.

ON skí ‘jugglery’. Metaphor based on the original meaning *shadow’, *‘shining’ or the like. Derivationally close to Toch B skiyo ‘shade, shadow’, Gk σκιά id., Alb hije id., Latv sejs ‘shadow, mirror reflection, image’ (Sturtevant SSN IX 155). See *skīnanan. Pokorny I 917– 918; Vries ANEW 491; Frisk II 730– 731; Orel AED 147; Adams TB 706; Burlak IFTJa 262.

Goth skeima ‘torch, lantern’, ON skími ‘gleam of light’, OE scíma ‘splendor, brightness, light’, OS scīmo ‘light, shine, shadow’, OHG scīmo ‘brilliance, shine’. Derived from *skīnanan. Close to OIr scíam ‘beauty’. Zupitza Gutt. 195; Torp-Falk 462; Holthausen AEEW 279; Feist 431; Pokorny I 917; Vries ANEW 492; Zalizniak I 154; Seebold 410; Lehmann GED 311; Bammesberger Nom. 184; Kluge- Seebold 717.

Goth skeinan ‘to shine’, ON skína id., OE scínan id., OFris skīna id., OS skīnan id., OHG skīnan id. Etymologically connected with Toch B skiyo ‘shadow’, Skt chāyā ‘shadow, reflection’, Gk σκιά ‘shadow’, Alb hije, he id., Latv seja ‘shadow, face’, Slav *sijati ‘to shine’. Zupitza Gutt. 195; Torp-Falk 461–462; Trautmann BSW 304; Feist 432; Holthausen AEEW 279; Jóhannesson IEW 809–810; Mayrhofer I 407; Pokorny I 917; Vries ANEW 492; Zalizniak I 154; Frisk II 730–731; Onions 820; Stang LS 47–48 (to Slav *sьjati ‘to shine’); Seebold 409–410; Vasmer-Trubachev III 629; Lehmann GED 311; Kluge-Seebold 715; Orel AED 147; Burlak IFTJa 262.

Goth skeirs ‘clear’, ON skírr ‘clear, bright, pure’, OE scír ‘bright, clear’, OFris skīre id., OS skīr ‘clear, unmixed’, OHG skīri ‘pure, clear, brilliant’. From here *skīrō (OE scír ‘office, business, district’, OHG scīra ‘business, office’) is derived. Identical with Alb hir ‘grace, favor, mercy’ < PAlb *skīra, Slav *š‘irъ ‘clean, true’. Probably further related to *skīnanan. Grimm KZ I 83; Karsten NB II 447; Brückner KZ LI 226 (to Slav *širokъ ‘wide’); Zupitza Gutt. 195; Torp-Falk 462 (Slav < Gmc); Feist 432; Holthausen IF XIV 341–342 (*skīrō to Lat cūra ‘care, charge’ < *koisā), AEEW 280; Pokorny I 917–918; Vries ANEW 494; Onions 818, 821; Stang LS 58; Seebold 410; Vasmer-Trubachev IV 507–508; Lehmann GED 311; Heidermanns 492; Orel AED 148–149; Kluge- Seebold 720.
Goth ga-skeirjan ‘to make clear’, ON skíra ‘to cleanse’, OE scíran ‘to make clear, to tell’. Derived from *skīriz. Torp-Falk 462.

ON skíta ‘to defecate’, OE scítan id., WFris skite id., MLG schiten id., OHG bi-skīzan ‘to make dirty, to defecate’. Related to Skt chinátti ‘to cut off, to split’, Gk σχίζω id., Lat scindō ‘to cut, to tear’, Lith skíedžiu, skíesti ‘to separate, to divide’. Torp-Falk 463; Trautmann BSW 263–264; Holthausen AEEW 280; Walde-Hofmann II 493– 494; Jóhannesson IEW 813–815; Mayrhofer I 407; Pokorny I 919–921; Fraenkel 805–806; Vries ANEW 494; Frisk II 838–840; Onions 821; Seebold 410–411; Lehmann GED 308; Kluge- Seebold 715–716.
ON skítr ‘excrements’, MLG schīt id. Derived from *skītanan. Torp-Falk 463.
ON skóf ‘singed crusts (at the bottom of a pan)’, MLG schōve ‘scale’, OHG scuoba id. Derived from *skabanan. Torp-Falk 452; Lehmann GED 307.
ON skó-þvengr ‘shoe-string’, OE scóh-äwanz id. Compound of *skōxaz and *þwanziz. Carr Nom. comp. 82 (parallel formations).

Goth skohs ‘shoe’, ON skór id., OE scóh id., OFris skōch id., OS skōh id., OHG scuoh id. Derived from *skexanan (in its original meaning unattested in Gmc). Derivationally close to Toch B skāk ‘balcony’. Osthoff Zfcelt Ph VI 398–400 (to MW eskit ‘shoe’ < *pedskūto-); Zupitza Gutt. 153 (from *skeu- ‘to cover’); Persson Beiträge 186 (same as Zupitza); Petersson Glotta VIII 72 (to Gk σύκχοι: ὑποδήματα Φρύγια, Hes.); Torp- Falk 465; Wood JEGP XIII 506 (to *skankaz); Holthausen AEEW 280; Feist 434; Pokorny I 951–953; Vries ANEW 499; Onions 822; Zalizniak I 135; Onions 822; Lehmann GED 312 (from
Goth ga-skohi ‘sandals, shoes’, OE ze-scÿ ‘pair of shoes’, OS ziscōhi ‘shoe’, OHG gi-scuohi id. Derived from *skōxaz. Feist 201; Lehmann GED 312.
ON skúa ‘to shoe’, OE scózan id., MLG schōien, schōgen id., OHG scuohen id. Derived from *skōxaz. Holthausen AEEW 280; Vries ANEW 506.

Goth skohsl ‘evil spirit, demon’. Identical with OIr scál ‘supernatural being, phantom’ < *skōk-sloderived from *skek- ‘to jump’ (O’Brien Ériu XI 89–90). Grimm DM 455 (‘wood-spirit’ related to ON skógr ‘wood’); Kauffmann PBB XVIII 154–155 (same as Grimm); Hellquist ANF VII 45 (from *skakanan); Feist 432 (to *skēwjanan); Meid KZ LXXVIII 153–155; Vendryes S-30; Lacy APS XXXII 114–120; Lehmann GED 312–313.
MLG schōpe ‘vessel for baling’, MHG schuofe id. Connected with *skapan ~ *skapōn. Onions 799.
ON skœra ‘quarrel’, MHG schuor ‘shearing’. Derived from *skeranan. Pokorny I 938–939; Vries ANEW 511.

ON skrafa ‘to prate, to chat’, MDu schraven ‘to scrape’. Cf. also Norw skravla ‘to crunch, to gnash’, G dial. schräbbeln ‘to make a noise’ < *skraf-l-. Related to *skerfanan. The innovative root structure may result from the re-interpretation of zero grade forms like *skurfaz. Pokorny I 569 (to *xrabnaz); Vries ANEW 501.
ON skreiä ‘step, pace, movement’, OE scrád ‘moving body (?), vessel (?)’. Derived from *skrīđanan. Holthausen AEEW 282; Vries ANEW 502; Seebold 421.

ON skreiäask ‘to creep, to slink’, MHG schreiten ‘to stride, to walk’, OHG screiten ‘to spread wide’. Derived from *skrīđanan. Vries ANEW 502; Seebold 421. *skraiþaz adj.; ON ǫr-skreiär ‘sliding swiftly (of a ship)’, MHG schreit ‘broad, expanded’. Derived from *skrīđanan. Karsten NB I 79; Matzel Festschr. Schröder 95, KZ CV 96; Heidermanns 500.

Norw dial. skrakal ‘fragile, ailing’, LG sbst. schräkel ‘creature crippled while growing’, MHG sbst. walt-schreckel ‘faun, spirit of the woods’. Related to *skrenkwanan? Torp- Falk 471 (to Skt kharjati ‘to creak’).
Norw skrella ‘to cry’, ME schrillen ‘to shrill’. Derived from *skrellanan. Torp-Falk 474.
Icel skrölta ‘to cry’, OE scrallettan ‘to make a loud sound’. Derived from *skrellanan. Torp-Falk 474.

ON skráma ‘scar’, MLG schramme ‘scratch’, MHG schramme id. Related to MIr screm ‘surface, skin’, Lith krãmas ‘tetter, scab’, Slav *kroma ‘edge’ (Mikkola IF XXIII 121). Zupitza Gutt. 157; Torp-Falk 471; Pokorny I 945; Vries ANEW 501; Petleva Etim. 1974 22–25; Kluge- Seebold 741.
Norw skramp ‘thin man, thin horse’, ME shrimp ‘dwarf’, MHG schrimpf ‘scratch’. Derived from *skrempanan. Torp-Falk 474; Vries ANEW 503.

ON skrapa ‘to scrape, to clatter’, OE scrapian ‘to scratch’, MDu schrapen id., MHG schraffen ‘to fleece’. Derived from *skrepanan. Zupitza Gutt. 28, 157; Torp-Falk 456; Holthausen AEEW 282; Vries ANEW 501; Onions 801 (to *skarpaz); Stang LS 49; Seebold 425; Kluge-Seebold 742.

ON skrati, skratti ‘wizard, warlock, goblin’, OHG scraz ‘pixie, goblin’. Related to Norw dial. skrata ‘to laugh loudly’, Swed skratta ‘to laugh’ of imitative origin. Torp-Falk 472–473; Pokorny I 933 (to Lith skrentū, skręsti ‘to be covered with a crust’); Vries ANEW 501–502 (to Norw skranta ‘to become thin’); Dukova Etim. 1982 61–63 (to Slav
OHG scrōtan ‘to cut off’. See *skrauđaz. Torp-Falk 476; Pokorny I 947.

OE fem. scréad(e) ‘shred’, OFris skrēd ‘hair-cutting, clipping coin’, MLG schrōt ‘cut offpiece’, OHG scrōt ‘shred’. Derived from *skrauđanan. Identical with Lat dial. scrōtum ‘scrotum’ < *scrautum, MW ysgryd ‘skeleton’ < *skrouto-. Torp-Falk 476; Holthausen AEEW 282; Walde-Hofmann II 501; Pokorny I 947; Onions 823; Kluge- Seebold 743.

ON sbst. skrá ‘dry skin, scroll’, MLG schrā ‘barren, thin’. Related to Gk χρόος ‘surface of the body, skin’ < *ghro⁄os (Fick III 339). Torp-Falk 472 (reconstruct *skraxō; to Norw dial. skraaen ‘dry’); Torp NNEO 616 (to Skt kṛ≤á- ‘emaciated, lean, weak’); Pokorny I 933; Vries ANEW 500–501; Frisk II 1120– 1121.

Norw dial. skrikka ‘to spring, to jump’, MLG schricken, schrecken ‘to move hands and feet, to dance’. Of imitative origin. Might be eventually connected with Gk σκαίρω ‘to spring’. Torp-Falk 472; Pokorny I 934; Frisk II 714–715.

Norw skrella ‘to sound’. Of imitative origin. Cf. *skrattōn. Torp-Falk 474. *skrempanan ~ *skrempēnan str.⁄ wk.vb.: ON skreppa ‘to slip’, MLG schrimpen ‘to shrink, to shrivel’, MHG schrimpfen id. Related to Lith skrembū, skrēbti ‘to get a thin crust, to stiffen’, Slav *skrebǫ, *skrebti ‘to scratch’ (Zupitza Gutt. 28). See *skrepanan. Torp-Falk 474; Trautmann BSW 267; Jóhannesson IEW 843–845; Pokorny I 948–949; Fraenkel 815–816; Vries ANEW 503; Seebold 423 (to Lith skirbstū, skiıbti ‘to shrink, to shrivel’).

OSwed part. skrunken ‘to shrink, to shrivel’, Norw skrøkka ‘to shrivel up’, OE scrincan ‘to wither away, to become weak’, MDu schrinken ‘to move together’. Similar to *skre-n-t- in Lith skrentū, skręsti ‘to be covered with a crust’. Torp-Falk 473; Holthausen AEEW 283; Jóhannesson IEW 831–832; Pokorny I 936 (to *xrenkwanan > ON hrøkkva ‘to fall back, to recoil’); Seebold 424–425.

OE screpan ‘to scrape, to scratch’, MDu schrepen ‘to shove’. Related to Lith skrembū, skrēbti ‘to get a thin crust, to stiffen’, Latv skrabt ‘to scratch’, Slav *skrebǫ, *skrebti ‘to scratch’. Zupitza Gutt. 157; Torp-Falk 456; Trautmann BSW 267; Holthausen AEEW 282; Pokorny I 938–947; Seebold 425; Vasmer-Trubachev III 656.

ON skrækja ‘to screech, to shriek’, MLG schrēken ‘to laugh loudly’. A variant of *skrīkjanan. Cf. also ME shreame ‘to scream’, WFris skrieme ‘to weep’, WFlem schreemen id. Pokorny I 569 (to Skt kharjati ‘to creak’); Vries ANEW 505; Onions 801.

ON skriär ‘creeping or sliding motion’, OE scriäe ‘course’, OHG scrit ‘step’. Derived from *skrīđanan. Torp-Falk 475; Holthausen AEEW 283; Pokorny I 937; Vries ANEW 503; Zalizniak I 149; Seebold 421; Bammesberger Nom. 129.

ON skript ‘picture, drawing, tapestry’, OE scrift ‘penalty, penance’ (masc.), OFris scrift ‘writing’, MDu schrift id., OHG scrift id. Derived from *skrībanan. Holthausen AEEW 283; Onions 823–824; Seebold 420.
ON skriäall ‘transient’, OE wíd-scriäol ‘wide-wandering, rambling’. Derived from *skrīđanan. Karsten II 276; Holthausen AEEW 284; Heidermanns 502.

OSwed skríva ‘to write’, OE scrífan ‘to assign, to decree, to allot’, OFris skrīva ‘to write’, OS skrīban id., OHG skrīban id. Hardly borrowed from but, probably, semantically influenced by Lat scrībō ‘to write’. Of unknown origin. Holthausen AEEW 283; Jóhannesson IEW 1166–1167; Pokorny I 946–947; Onions 824; Seebold 419–420; Kluge-Seebold 742.

ON skríäa ‘to creep, to crawl’, OE scríäan ‘to go, to wander’, OFris pres. scrīda ‘to walk’, OS skrīdan ‘to glide, to stride’, OHG bi-scrītan ‘to glide’. Related to Lith skriedžiū, skri‚sti ‘to make a circle, to move around’. Torp-Falk 475; Holthausen AEEW 283; Mezger GR XVII 94–98; Jóhannesson IEW 831–835; Pokorny I 937; Fraenkel 817; Vries ANEW 503 (to *xrīþō); Zalizniak I 149; Onions 801; Seebold 421–422; Kluge-Seebold 742.
Icel skríkja ‘to cry’, ME schrīken id., OS scrīkon id. Of imitative origin. Cf. *skrēkjanan. Zupitza Gutt. 158; Vries ANEW 503 (to Icel hrikta ‘to creek’).

Goth dis-skreitan ‘to tear (asunder)’, G dial. schreissen, schritzen id. Close to MIr scrissid ‘to cut’ < *skrid-t- continuing IE *skrei-. Grienberger Got. 57 (to Lat scindō ‘to cut, to tear’); Zupitza KZ XXXVI 243; Feist 120; Pokorny I 938–947; Seebold 420–421; Lehmann GED 91 (to IE *skrei- ‘out, to separate’).
Dan dial. skrub ‘brushwood’, OE scrybb ‘underwood, shrubbery’, NFris skrobb ‘broom, brushwood’. Related to *skrubbōjanan. Būga RFV LXX 252 (to Lith skra Ubis ‘fear’); Torp- Falk 476; Onions 824.
Swed skrubba ‘to rub, to scrub’, MLG schrobben, schrubben ‘to scratch, to rub’. Related to Lat scrūpus ‘rough, sharp stone’. Torp-Falk 476; Walde-Hofmann II 501–502; Pokorny I 947; Vries ANEW 504.
ON skrúä, skrúäi ‘shroud, gear’, OE scrúd ‘dress, clothing, attire’. Derived from *skrauđanan. Zupitza Gutt. 157; Torp-Falk 476; Holthausen AEEW 284; Vries ANEW 504–505 (ON < OE); Onions 824.
ON skrÿäa ‘to clothe, to dress, to adorn’, OE scrÿdan ‘to put on (clothes), to clothe’. Derived from *skrūđan. Holthausen AEEW 284; Onions 824; Vries ANEW 505.

OFris schedda ‘to tremble’, OS skuddian id., OHG scutten id. Probably related to Gk σκύζομαι ‘to be angry’ < *skud-j-, Lith skundū, skūsti ‘to be nervous, to be tired’. Bezzenberger- Fick BB VI 240; Pokorny I 955; Fraenkel 823; Frisk II 740–741; Onions 824.

Goth skuft ‘hair’, ON skopt id., OHG scuft ‘tuft of hair, hair-lock’ (fem.). Based on *skuppaz. Grienberger Got. 190–191 (from *skeubanan ~ *skūbanan); Zupitza Gutt. 47; Torp-Falk 470 (to ON skúfr ‘tassel’); Feist 435; Pokorny I 956; Vries ANEW 499 (to Slav *‘upъ ‘forelock’); Zalizniak I 143; Lehmann GED 313; Kluge-Seebold 740.
ON skyldr ‘obligatory, due’, OHG scult ‘accused’. Participle of *skala. Torp-Falk 460; Vries ANEW 509; Seebold 405; Lehmann GED 313; Bammesberger Nom. 251.

ON skyld ‘tax, due’, OE scyld ‘guilt, sin, debt’, OFris skelde ‘debt’, OS skuld id., OHG sculd ‘accusation, guilt’. Derived from *skala. Torp-Falk 460; Pokorny I 927; Holthausen AEEW 285; Vries ANEW 506–507; Vries ANEW 509; Zalizniak I 151; Onions 815; Seebold 405; Lehmann GED 313; Bammesberger Nom. 142; Kluge- Seebold 744.

ON skylda ‘to bind in duty, to oblige’, OE scildan ‘to shield, to protect’, OFris ur-skelda ‘to forfeit’, MLG schulden ‘to accuse’, OHG sculdōn id. Derived from *skulđiz. Holthausen AEEW 285; Vries ANEW 509.

Swed skuldra ‘shoulder’, OE sculdor id. (masc.), OFris skulder id., MLG schulder id., OHG scultra, scultirra id. A zero-grade derivative of *skelđuz ~ *skelđiz. Torp-Falk 460 (to Gk κωλή ‘thighbone with the flesh on it, ham’); Holthausen AEEW 285; Pokorny I 925 (reconstructs *sk¬-dhrā ‘shoulder-bone as a digging tool’); Zalizniak II 181; Onions 823; Kluge-Seebold 744.
Swed skolla ‘piece of tin, thin plate’, MLG schulle ‘piece of earth’, OHG scollo ‘earth lump’. Related to *skeulan ~ *skūlan. Torp-Falk 459; Kluge-Seebold 739–740.
Goth skula ‘debtor’, OS skolo id., OHG skolo id. Derived from *skala. Feist 435; Onions 815; Benveniste 153; Seebold 405; Benveniste IEL 153; Lehmann GED 313; Bammesberger Nom. 177.
ON skolpr ‘turner’s chisel’, MLG scholpe ‘mussel shell’, G dial. Schölp ‘clod, lump’. Probably related to *skeljanan ~ *skelōjanan. Pokorny I 926; Vries ANEW 498 (to *skaljō).
Norw dial. skum ‘foam’, MLG schūm id., OHG scūm id. Derived from *skeu-: Skt skunóti ‘to cover’. Torp-Falk 466; Pokorny I 951.
ON skynda ‘to hasten’, OE scyndan id., OS skundian ‘to lure’, OHG scunten id. Of unknown origin. Torp-Falk 469; Pokorny I 957.
ON skunda ‘to speed, to hasten’, OS far-skundian ‘to incite, to instigate’. See *skunđjanan. Torp-Falk 469; Mayrhofer III 508; Pokorny I 957; Vries ANEW 507 (to

ON skokkr ‘chest, trunk, ship’s hulk’, NFris skonk ‘cross-beam under the end of the roof’, LG schunk ‘second layer of beams in a square structure’. Cf. also OS skokka ‘swinging movement’ (fem.), OHG skokka ‘swinging’ (fem.). Related to *skankaz. Ljunggren ANF LIV 17–44 (to ON skukka ‘crease’); Torp-Falk 467; Holthausen AWN 254 (reconstructs *skukkaz, to Skt kó≤a- ‘cask, coop’); Pokorny I 953–954; Vries ANEW 497.

ON skop ‘railing, mocking’, MDu scop ‘mockery, derision’, OHG scopf ‘story, anecdote, mockery’. Cf. also OE scop ‘poet’ (masc.). Probably derived from *skupōjan ‘to spring’ > ON skopa ‘to mock’ (Wissmann SDAW Jahrg. 1954/2). Zupitza Gutt. 154; Torp-Falk 469; Holthausen AEEW 281; Vries ANEW 498; Onions 799; Bammesberger Nom. 98.

MHG schopf ‘tuft, forelock’. Related to Slav *‘ubъ ~ *‘upъ id. < *(s)keub(h)o- ~ *(s)keupo- (Ehrismann PBB XX 54–56). See *skaufan ~ *skaufaz. Torp-Falk 469; Berneker I 160–161; Pokorny I 956; Trubachev ESSJa IV 126.
ON skoppa ‘to spin like a top’, MDu schoppen ‘to mock’. Derived from *skupan. Martinet Gém. 168, 173; Torp-Falk 469; Vries ANEW 499.
ON skurär ‘cutting, carving’, OFris skerd id. Continues *skṛtiderived from *skeranan. Zupitza Gutt. 155; Torp-Falk 455; Vries ANEW 507.
Swed skorf ‘scurf’, OE scorf id., MLG schorf id., OHG scorf id. Derived from *skerfanan. Torp-Falk 457.

ON skora ‘to cut, to incise’, OE scorian ‘to reject, to refuse’, MLG schuren ‘to rub’. Derived from *skurō(n). Holthausen AEEW 281; Pokorny I 954; Vries ANEW 499 (directly to *skurōn); Lehmann GED 405.
ON skora ‘score, incision’, OE scoru ‘score, 20 pieces’, MLG schore ‘fissure’ (masc.). Derived from *skeranan. Holthausen AEEW 281 (OE < ON); Vries ANEW 499.

Goth winþi-skauro ‘winnowing fork’, Swed dial. skurä ‘shovel’, OHG scora id. Related to Gk σκῦρος ‘chippings of stone’, Lith skiaur E ‘boat full of holes used to keep fish’ (Persson Beiträge I 374– 376). Hirt Ablaut 113 (to Lat secūris ‘axe’); Torp-Falk 470–471; Feist 566; Pokorny I 954; Frisk II 743–744; Lehmann GED 405 (to IE *skeu- ‘to cut, to separate’).

ON sbst. skortr, skort ‘shortage, want’, OE scort ‘short’, OHG scurz id. Probably related to OIr scerdid ‘to scrape off’, Lith skérdžiu, skérdëti ‘to get fissures, to crack’. Karsten NB II 203; Zupitza Gutt. 155; Torp-Falk 455; Holthausen AEEW 281; Kluge Vorg. 338 (from Lat *excurtus ‘short’); Pokorny I 941; Fraenkel 801; Vries ANEW 500; Onions 822; Lühr-Matzel KZ XCIX 259; Heidermanns 503; Kluge-Seebold 718; Orel AED 425 (Alb shkurt ‘short’ < Gmc).
ON skorta ‘to be short of, to lack’, MDu schorten ‘to be absent, to mangle, to lack’. Derived from *skurtaz. Zupitza Gutt. 157; Torp-Falk 455; Heidermanns 503.

ON skyrta ‘shirt, robe’ (if not from OE), OE scyrte ‘short garment, skirt’, MLG schorte ‘apron’. Derived from *skurtaz. Holthausen AEEW 286; Onions 821, 832; Vries ANEW 510; Heidermanns 503; Kluge-Seebold 745; Orel AED 425 (Alb shkurte ‘shirt’ < Gmc).

ON skot ‘shot, shooting’, OE scot ‘rapid movement’, OFris scot ‘throw, shot’, OHG scoz ‘catapult, projectile’. Derived from *skeutanan. Torp-Falk 468; Holthausen AEEW 276; Vries ANEW 500; Onions 822–823; Seebold 418; Bammesberger Nom. 57.
ON pl. neut. skotyräi ‘scoffs, taunts’, MHG schoz-wort id. Compound of *skutan and *wurđan. Jóhannesson IEW 206 (*skuta- compared with Gk κυδάζω ‘to revile, to abuse’); Vries ANEW 500.
ON skutill ‘harpoon, bolt’, OE scytel ‘dart’. Derived from *skutan. Torp-Falk 468; Holthausen AEEW 286; Vries ANEW 508; Onions 803, 825; Seebold 418.
ON skozkr ‘nimble, alert’, MDu schotsch ‘wrong, strange’. Derived from *skutan. Vries ANEW 500.
ON skutr ‘stern’, OE scyte ‘shooting’, OFris skete id., MLG schöte id., OHG scuz ‘throwing, shooting’. Derived from *skeutanan. Holthausen AEEW 286; Vries ANEW 508.

ON skyti ‘marksman’, OE scytta ‘shooter, archer’, OFris sketta id., MLG schutte id., OHG skuzzo id. Derived from *skeutanan or *skutan. Holthausen AEEW 286; Vries ANEW 510; Seebold 418; Kluge-Seebold 746.
ON skytja ‘little nook’, MLG schütte ‘bulkhead’. Derived from *skutan. Vries ANEW 510.
ON skota ‘to shove’, OE scotian ‘to move rapidly’, OS skotōn ‘to shoot, to throw’, OHG scozzōn ‘to shoot, to throw’. Derived from *skutan. Holthausen AEEW 281; Vries ANEW 500; Seebold 418.
ON aust-skota ‘scoop’, MHG dial. schoss ‘bread shovel’. Derived from *skeutanan. Vries ANEW 500.
Norw dial. skygg ‘timid’, MLG schūwe id. May be related to *skawōjanan. On the other hand, a direct derivation from *skuwwōn cannot be outruled. Torp-Falk 467; Onions 825; Kluge-Seebold 718.
ON skyggva, skyggja ‘to overshadow’, OHG pres. scuit ‘adumbrat’. Derived from *skuwwōn. Torp- Falk 466.

Goth skuggwa ‘mirror’, ON skuggi ‘shade, shadow’, OE scúwa id., OHG scūwo ‘look’. Related to Skt skunāti ‘to cover’, Arm c'iw ‘roof’, OIr cúl ‘back’ continuing IE *(s)∞eu 6- ‘to cover’ (van Helten PBB XXX 247: separates Goth). Tucker Notes 37 (to Lat scūtum ‘shield’); Uhlenbeck TNTL XXV 294 (to *skawōjanan); Torp-Falk 465; Holthausen AEEW 284; Feist 435; Mayrhofer III 508; Pokorny I 951 (to *skeulan ~ *skūlan); Vries ANEW 506; Zalizniak I 154; Onions 832; Lindeman Verschärfung 17.7; Vendryes C-28–29; Lehmann GED 313.
ON skúfa ‘to shove, to push’, OE scúfan id., OFris skūva id., MLG schuven id. Derived from *skeubanan ~ *skūbanan. Holthausen AEEW 284; Vries ANEW 506; Onions 823.
Swed skofvel ‘shovel’, OE scofl id., OS wind-skūfla id., OHG scūvala, schūvel id. Derived from *skeubanan ~ *skūbanan. Torp-Falk 470.
MLG schūl ‘asylum’. Cf. also OFris skūl id. < *skūlō. Identical with Gk σκῦλον ‘weapons stripped offa slain enemy, spoils’. Pokorny I 951; Dybo VSJa 26; Frisk II 742–743.

Icel skúrr ‘shelter’, OFris skūre ‘shelter, screen’ (neut.), OS skūr id., MHG schūr ‘shelter, screen’. Related to Lith skūrā ‘skin, bark’, Lat ob-scūrus ‘dark, dusky’. Torp-Falk 466; Walde- Hofmann II 196–197; Pokorny I 951; Fraenkel 822; Vries ANEW 507 (to

Goth skura ‘storm, shower’, ON skúr ‘shower’, OE scúr id., OFris skūr ‘fit of illness; shower (of rain)’, OS skūr ‘blows in battle’, OHG scūr ‘shower’ (< *skūriz). May be connected with Lat caurus ‘northwest wind’, Arm c'urt ‘cold, shower’ < *s˚ūrdo-, Lith šiaur‹s ‘North wind’, Slav *sîverъ ‘north, north wind’ (Schmidt KSB VI 149; Mikkola IF XVI 100). Torp-Falk 466–467 (to *skēwjanan); Wood MLN XXI 228; Trautmann BSW 303–304; Meillet Etudes 410; Būga RFV LXVII 245; Holthausen AEEW 285; Walde- Hofmann I 190; Feist 436; Pokorny I 597; Chemodanov SSGJa I 55–56; Vries ANEW 507; Fraenkel 978; Zalizniak II 197; Szemerényi KZ LXX 65; Onions 823; Vasmer-Trubachev III 588–589; Lehmann GED 314; Kluge-Seebold 714.
ON skúta ‘small craft, cutter’, MDu schūte ‘flat-bottomed boat’. Derived from *skeutanan (Ohlmarks apud Vries 507). Vries ANEW 507–508; Onions 800.
ON skvakka ‘to splash’, E dial. squack ‘to shout loudly’. Of imitative origin. See *kwakōjanan. Vries ANEW 508.
Norw skvelpa ‘to rinse’. Probably related to Lith skalbiū, ska÷lbti ‘to do laundry’. Torp-Falk 477; Fraenkel 793.
ON skola ‘to wash’, MLG scholen ‘to stream, to flow’. Probably related to Lith skaláuju, skaláuti ‘to wash’ (Zupitza Gutt. 47). Torp-Falk 477; Fraenkel 793; Vries ANEW 497.
Icel skolpa ‘to rinse’, LG schulpen ‘to splash’. Derived from *skwelpanan. Torp-Falk 477; Vries ANEW 498 (to *sk(w)ulōjanan).
Swed dial. slabba ‘to botch’, MDu slabben ‘to smirch’. Related to *slēpanan. Torp-Falk 537; Pokorny I 656.

Norw dial. slad ‘valley’, OE slæd ‘flat piece of grass, valley’. Probably related to Lith sl Ednas ‘flat’. Hendriksen IF LVI 28 (to Slav *slîdъ ‘trace’ < *sloidhos); Torp-Falk 535; Holthausen IF XX 326 (to Lat lāma ‘slough, bog, fen’), AEEW 297; Fraenkel 828; Vries ANEW 516–517 (to Slav *ledъ ‘ice’); Onions 833; Levickij IF CIII 221 (to IE *slei- ‘to slide’).
ON slag ‘blow, stroke’, MLG slach ‘lock, bolt’. Derived from *slaxanan. Vries ANEW 512.
ON slag ‘wetness’, MLG slagge ‘rainy weather’. Probably related to Lith šlãkas ‘drop, spot’ < *(s)˚loko-. Torp-Falk 534; Pokorny I 957; Fraenkel 998; Vries ANEW 512–513; Kluge-Seebold 723.

Goth slahs ‘blow’, ON slagr id., OE sleze id., OFris slei id., OS slegi id., OHG slag id. Derived from *slaxanan. Torp-Falk 534; Holthausen AEEW 298; Feist 436; Vries ANEW 513; Zalizniak II 189; Seebold 426; Lehmann GED 314; Bammesberger Nom. 135.
ON sleggja ‘sledgehammer’, OE slecz id., Du slegge id. Derived from *slaziz. Torp-Falk 534; Holthausen AEEW 298; Vries ANEW 514; Onions 834; Lerchner 233; Bammesberger Nom. 114.
ON slaga ‘to tack, to cruise’, OFris slagia ‘to beat’, OLG slagōn id., OHG slagōn ‘to strike together’. Based on *slazan, *slaziz. Vries ANEW 513.

Goth slahan ‘to strike’, ON slá id., OE sleán id., OFris slā id., OS slahan id., OHG slahan ‘to strike, to smite’. Related to MIr slacc ‘sword’, slachta ‘struck’ (Stokes KZ XLI 388). Svanberg APS III 260 (to *slenzwanan); Zupitza Gutt. 138; Torp-Falk 533; Feist 436; Holthausen AEEW 298; Jóhannesson IEW 920–92; Pokorny I 959; Vries ANEW 512; Vendryes S-124; Onions 834; Seebold 425–427; Lehmann GED 314; Levickij IF CIII 222 (to IE *slei- ‘to strike, to cut’); Kluge-Seebold 722–724.
ON slá ‘bar, bolt, crossbeam’, OE slæ, sleahe ‘weaver’s reed’, MLG slage ‘beating tool’. Derived from *slaxanan. Torp-Falk 533; Holthausen AEEW 298; Vries ANEW 512.

ON slátta ‘mowing’, OFris mon-slachta ‘manslaughter’, OS man-slahta ‘murder’, OHG slachta ‘massacre’. Derived from *slaxanan. Torp-Falk 533; Vries ANEW 514; Onions 833; Seebold 427; Lehmann GED 314; Kluge-Seebold 722.
ON sláttr ‘mowing, smiting’, OE sliht, sleaht ‘striking, slaughter’. Derived from *slaxanan. Torp-Falk 533.
Dan slaa-torn ‘sloe’, OE sláh-äorn id., MLG slē-dorn id. Compound of *slaixwōn ~ *slaixwaz and *þurnuz ~ *þurnaz. Torp-Falk 532.

Dan slaa ‘sloe-thorn’, OE sláh id., MLG slē id., OHG slēha id. Related to OIr lí ‘color’, Lat līueō ‘to be blue’ (Schrader- Nehring I 149, II 181). Torp-Falk 532 (to Slav *sliva ‘prune’); Holthausen AEEW 297; Walde-Hofmann I 816; Pokorny I 965; Onions 835; Kluge- Seebold 725–726.

ON sleipr ‘slippery’, MDu sleep ‘rising at an angle’, OHG sleif ‘smooth, slippery’. Derived from *slīpanan. Karsten NB I 60–61; Torp-Falk 539; Kluge 655–656; Jóhannesson IEW 729–731; Pokorny I 663 (to Gk ὀλιβρός ‘slippery’); Vries ANEW 514 (to ON sleppa ‘to slip, to slide’); Seebold 429– 430; Heidermanns 505.
ON sleita ‘backsliding’, MLG slēt ‘wooden pole’, MHG sleize ‘splinter’. Derived from *slītanan. Vries ANEW 514 (to Lith sklaidÿti ‘to disperse’).

ON slær, sljór ‘blunt, dull’, OE sláw ‘sluggish, slow’, WFris sleau ‘slow’, OS slēu ‘despondent, cowardly’, OHG slēo ‘blunt, withered, tepid’. Without reliable external connections. A semantic interaction with *slakwaz cannot be excluded. Friesen SVSU VII/2 19 (reconstructs *slaiwjaz); Brugmann Grundriß II/1 202 (to Lat laeuus ‘left’, Skt sreváyant- ‘causing miscarriage’); Karsten NB II 417–418; Torp-Falk 532–533; Holthausen AEEW 297–298 (follows Brugmann); Vries ANEW 518; Onions 836; Bammesberger Nom. 244; Heidermanns 505–506.
ON sljóva ‘to blunt’, OE á-sláwian ‘to be heavy’, OHG slēwōn ‘to become limp, to become blunt’. Derived from *slaiwaz. Holthausen AEEW 297; Vries ANEW 516; Heidermanns 505.

ON slakr ‘slack’, OE slæc ‘slack, inactive, lazy, slow’, OS slak ‘despondent, cowardly’, OHG slah. Derived from *slekwanan (see *slakwjanan). Karsten NB I 2; Zupitza Gutt. 165; Torp-Falk 533; Holthausen AEEW 296–297; Pokorny I 959–960; Vries ANEW 513 (to Russ pereslîga ‘defect in a fabric’); Onions 833; Lühr Expr. 229 (to Gk λάγνος ‘lecherous, lustful’); Heidermanns 506; Kluge-Seebold 723.

ON slǫkkva ‘to extinguish’, OE ze-sleccan ‘to make slack’, OS sleckian ‘to deaden, to weaken’. Causative of str. *slekwanan > ON part. slokinn ‘to extinguish, to run out’. Related to Lith slėgiu, slėgti ‘to press, to squeeze’. Cf. also a similar secondary suffix in Lat langueō ‘to be faint’ (Persson Beiträge 131). Torp-Falk 533; Holthausen AEEW 298; Pipping Xenia 146; Pokorny I 962; Fraenkel 828; Vries ANEW 518; Heidermanns 506.
Norw dial. slampa ‘to be negligent’, MHG slampen ‘to sag’. Close to Lith slambū, slābti ‘to weaken’. See *slabbōjanan, *slēpanan. Torp-Falk 537; Fraenkel 825–826.
ON slǫngva ‘to sling’, MLG slengen ‘to hurl, to fling’, OHG slangen ‘to throw’. Derived from *slenzwanan. Torp-Falk 536; Vries ANEW 518; Seebold 433.
Swed dial. slank ‘long and slender’, MLG slank ‘flexible, slack’, MHG slanc ‘slender’. Derived from *slenkanan. Zubaty LF XX 406 (to Slav

Goth af-slaupjan ‘to put off, to make slip off’, OE slípan ‘to slip, to glide’, OFris slēpa ‘to transfer’, OS slōpian ‘to let loose’, OHG sloufen ‘to join, to connect’. Causative of *sleupanan ~ *slūpanan. Torp-Falk 542; Holthausen AEEW 299; Feist 9; Seebold 436.

E sleet, LG pl. slöten ‘hail’, MHG slōz id. Close to Slav *sludъ ‘crust of ice’ (Petersson BSl. 62). Torp-Falk 541; Lewy IF XXXII 158 (to Lat lutum); Onions 834; Shirokov VBK 204 (to Alb ledh ‘mud, clay’, OPrus laydis ‘clay’); Vasmer-Trubachev III 676–677.

Goth fra-slindan ‘to devour, to swallow up’, OS pret. sg. farsland id., OHG slintan id. Of unknown etymology. Grienberger Got. 74 (to Lith sklendžiū, skl.sti ‘to fly, to hover’); Torp-Falk 536–537; Feist 164–165; Johansson PBB XIV 326 (to *slīđanan); Mayrhofer III 558 (to Skt srédhati ‘to fail, to err’); Pokorny I 960–961; Seebold 432 (to Lith lendū, l⁄sti ‘to creep’); Lehmann GED 125; Kluge- Seebold 728.

ON slyngva, slyngja ‘to throw, to sling’, OE slinzan ‘to wind, to twist’, MLG slingen, slengen ‘to wind’, OHG slingan ‘to decay’. A variant of *lenz(w)anan? On the other hand, may be related to Lith slenkū, sliñkti ‘to crawl’ (see *slenkanan). Zupitza Gutt. 69–70 (to Lat līquis ‘crooked’); Torp-Falk 536; Holthausen AEEW 299; Jóhannesson IEW 930–931; Pokorny I 961; Vries ANEW 518; Onions 835; Seebold 432–433; Kluge-Seebold 727–728.

OSwed pret. slank ‘to creep’, OE slincan ‘to crawl’, WFris slinke ‘to reduce, to decrease’, MLG slinken ‘to decrease’. Despite the irregular correspondence of velars, can be related to Lith slenkū, sliñkti ‘to crawl’ (Zupitza Gutt. 69–70). On the other hand, cf. a certain semantic affinity with *slenzwanan. Torp-Falk 535–536; Trautmann BSW 269; Holthausen AEEW 299; Senn Sprache V 182–186 (Lith < Gmc); Pokorny I 959–960; Fraenkel 832–833; Seebold 433; Kluge-Seebold 724.

Goth sliupan ‘to slip, to slide’, OE slúpan ‘to slip, to glide’, WFris slūpe id., MLG slūpen id., OHG ir-sliofan ‘to come (out)’. No reliable external parallels except for Toch AB lup- ‘to rub lightly, to smear’ (Duchesne- Guillemin BSL XLI 152). Corssen KB 430 (to Lat lūbricus ‘slippery’); Fick I 577 (same as Corssen); Zupitza Gutt. 28; Torp-Falk 542; Feist 438 (to Lat lūbricus ‘slippery’, Lith slūbnas ‘languid, dull, weak’); Holthausen AEEW 300; Pokorny I 963–964; Chemodanov SGGJa I 91; Onions 836; Seebold 435–436; Lehmann GED 315; Kluge- Seebold 727; Adams TB 558.

OFris slūta ‘to close’, OS part. ut-bi-slotenun ‘seclusis’, OHG bi-sliozan ‘to close’. A variant of *lūtanan? Fierlinger KZ XXVII 191 (to Lat claudō ‘to limp, to be lame’); Johansson PBB XIV 289–291 (same as Fierlinger); Torp-Falk 541; Pokorny I 604–605; Seebold 436–437 (follows Fierlinger); Kluge-Seebold 727.

Goth slepan ‘to sleep’, OE slápan id., OFris slēpa id., OS slāpan id., OHG slāfan id. Related to Lith slãbnas ‘weak’, slobstū, slõbti ‘to weaken’, Slav *slabъ id. (Zupitza Gutt. 27–28; Hirt PBB XXIII 292, Idg. Gr. II 130). Fick Glotta II 128 (to Gk λήγω ‘to stay, to abate’); Brugmann IF VI 96 (to Lat labō ‘to totter, to be ready to fall’); Persson Beiträge 489; Torp-Falk 537; Trautmann BSW 270; Schwentner KZ LXVI 260; Feist 437–438; Holthausen AEEW 297; Pokorny I 655–657; Fraenkel 825–826; Onions 834; Stang LS 50; Seebold 434–435; Vasmer-Trubachev III 664; Lehmann GED 315; Kluge-Seebold 723.
Goth sleps ‘sleep’, OE slæp id., OFris slēp id., OS slāp id., OHG slāf id. Related to *slēpanan. Torp-Falk 537; Holthausen AEEW 297; Feist 437; Onions 834; Seebold 434; Lehmann GED 315.
ON sleäi ‘sledge’, OS slido id., OHG slito id. Derived from *slīđanan. Torp-Falk 539; Vries ANEW 514; Onions 834; Seebold 428; Kluge- Seebold 728.
Norw slidra ‘to slide, to slip’, OE slidrian, slidorian id., LG slidderen id., G schlittern id. An emphatic derivative of *slīđanan. Torp-Falk 539; Holthausen AEEW 298; Heidermanns 512.

Goth slaihts ‘smooth, even’, ON sléttr ‘plain, flat, even, smooth’, OE adv. eorä-slihtes ‘in an earth-destroying manner’, OFris sliucht ‘simple’, OS sliht ‘decorated, ornamented’, OHG sleht ‘smooth, mild’. Derived from *slīkanan (Zupitza BB XXV 97). Karsten NB II 318; Froehde BB III 5 (to Gk Hom adv. λίγδην ‘grazing’); Feist 436–437; Holthausen AEEW 299; Kluge 638–639; Pokorny I 664; Vries ANEW 515; Onions 834–835; Vendryes S-133; Seebold 428–429; Lehmann GED 314; Bammesberger Nom. 249; Heidermanns 512–513; Kluge-Seebold 725.
ON slétta ‘to make plain, to level’, OHG slihten ‘to smooth, to quiet’. Derived from *slixtaz. Vries ANEW 515; Heidermanns 513.

MLG slim ‘bent, crooked’, OHG slimb id. Related to Latv slīps ‘slanting, sloped’ without nasal (Lewy KZ XX 561). Further cf. *slīpanan. Torp- Falk 538; Pokorny I 657; Onions 835; Lühr Expr. 148–150; Heidermanns 513.

OE slipor ‘slippery’, MLG slipper id., OHG sleffar id. Close to Gk ὀλιβρόν: ὀλισθηρόν, λεῖον, ἐπισφαλές (Hes.). Derived from *slīpanan. Torp- Falk 539; Holthausen AEEW 299; Pokorny I 663; Austin Language XVII 87; Frisk II 376; Heidermanns 514.
ON slit ‘tear, slit’, OE lah-slit ‘fine payable for the breach’, OFris slit ‘slit’, OHG gi-sliz ‘split’. Derived from *slītanan. Vries ANEW 516; Seebold 430.
ON slita ‘tearing, splitting’, MLG slete ‘wear, wear and tear’. See *slitan. Vries ANEW 516.

OE slídan ‘to slide’, MLG slīden id. (stem?), OHG bi-slītan ‘to make slippery, to lubricate’. Related to Skt srédhati ‘to fail, to err’, Gk ὀλισθάνω ‘to slip’ (based on *ὀλιθ-), Lith slÿstu, slÿsti ‘to slide, to slip’ (Uhlenbeck PBB XXVI 294–295). Torp-Falk 539; Holthausen AEEW 298; Mayrhofer III 558; Pokorny I 960–961; Fraenkel 833; Frisk II 377; Onions 834; Seebold 427–428; Kluge-Seebold 728.
MLG sliken ‘to creep’, OHG slīhhan id. Related to OIr sligim ‘to smear’, Slav *slьzъkъ ‘smooth’, ‘slime’ (Scheftelowitz KZ LIV 248). Pokorny I 662–664; Seebold 428–429; Kluge-Seebold 726.
ON slíkr ‘sleek, smooth’, MLG sbst. slīk ‘mud’, MHG sbst. slīch id. Related to *slīkanan. Zupitza Gutt. 199; Trautmann BSW 270; Pokorny I 664; Vries ANEW 515; Vasmer-Trubachev III 671–672.

ON slím ‘slime’, OE slím id. (masc. or neut.?), OFris slīm id., MLG slīm id. (masc., neut.), OHG slīm id. (masc.). Related to Gk λειμών ‘moist place, meadow’, Lat līmus ‘mud, slime’, OIr slím ‘slime’. Cf. also *slīkaz, *slīkanan. Pedersen Kelt. Gr. I 84; Torp-Falk 538; Holthausen AEEW 299; Walde- Hofmann I 804–805; Pokorny I 663; Vries ANEW 516; Frisk II 97–99; Onions 835; Kluge-Seebold 726.
OFris slīpa ‘to sharpen’, MLG slīpen ‘to grind, to smoothen’ (str. and wk.), OHG slīfan ‘to smoothen, to whet’. Related to *slimbaz, *slipraz. Torp-Falk 539; Pokorny I 663.

ON slíta ‘to slit, to break’, OE slítan ‘to slit, to tear’, OFris slīta ‘to wear out’, OS slītan ‘to split’, OHG slīzan ‘to cut, to tear’. Of unknown origin. Johansson PBB XIV 316 (to Lat laedō ‘to hurt, to wound’); Torp-Falk 538 (to Lith skleidžiū, skleĩsti ‘to spread’); Holthausen AEEW 299; Jóhannesson IEW 845–850; Pokorny I 923–927; Vries ANEW 516; Onions 835; Seebold 430–431; Kluge-Seebold 726.

Goth sleiþs (or sleideis) ‘dangerous, fierce’, ON slíär ‘fearful’, OE slíäe ‘dire, hard, cruel’, OS slīthi ‘cruel, wicked’. Related to *laiþaz (Saussure Mémoire 75: to Gk ἀλιταίνω ‘to sin’; Grienberger Got. 192–193). Persson UUÅ 1891 48 (to Skt srédhati ‘to fail, to err’); Karsten NB II 448; Torp-Falk 539; Feist 437; Holthausen AEEW 300; Wissmann NP 75–77; Pokorny I 672; Vries ANEW 515; Lehmann GED 314–315; Griepentrog KZ CIV 111; Heidermanns 509.

ON slÿ ‘water-cotton’, OE slíw ‘a kind of fish, tench’, MLG slī ‘slimy water plant’, OHG slīo ‘a kind of fish, tench’. Probably connected with *slīman (Persson Wurzelerw. 110). Torp-Falk 538; Holthausen AEEW 300; Vries ANEW 517– 518 (to Gk λινεύς ‘a kind of sea fish’, Lith lÿnas ‘tench’); Kluge-Seebold 726.
ON slœäa ‘to trail’, early Du slooien ‘to tow, to carry’. Related to *slađan. Torp-Falk 535; Vries ANEW 518.
ON pl. slœäur ‘gown that trails along the ground’, MDu slooye ‘train (of dress)’. Related to *slōđjanan. Vries ANEW 518.
ON sloära ‘to trail oneself along’, Du slodderen ‘to sway, to rock back and forth’, MHG slotern ‘to shake’. An expressive derivative of *slōđjanan. Torp-Falk 542; Pokorny I 963; Vries ANEW 517.
ON slok ‘water gutter’, OE sloca ‘bit, morsel’, MLG sloke ‘throat’. Derived from *slūkanan. Torp-Falk 540; Holthausen AEEW 300; Pokorny I 964; Vries ANEW 517.

Norw dial. slum ‘slack, flabby’. Related to Goth slawan ‘to be silent’? Grienberger Got. 192 (Goth slawan to Gk λήγω ‘to stay, to abate’, Lat langueō ‘to be faint’); Torp-Falk 540; Pokorny I 962–963; Lehmann GED 314.
ODan slum ‘slumber’, OE sluma id. Substantivized *slumaz. Holthausen AEEW 300; Pokorny I 962; Vries ANEW 517.
Norw dial. sluma ‘to weaken’, MLG slömen ‘to sleep’, late MHG slumen id. Derived from *slumaz. Torp-Falk 540; Pokorny I 962.
Norw slump ‘accident, chance’, MLG slump id. Related to *slampōjanan. Torp-Falk 537.
ON adv. slund-samliga ‘flabbily’, OS slund ‘gullet, throat’, OHG slunt id. Derived from *slenđanan. Lehmann GED 125.
ON sloppr ‘a kind of gown’, OE slop ‘loose upper garment’, ME sloppe id. Derived from *sleupanan ~ *slūpanan. Torp-Falk 542; Holthausen AEEW 300; Martinet Gém. 192; Vries ANEW 517; Seebold 436.
ON vind-slot ‘abatement’, MLG slot ‘door-bolt, lock’, OHG sloz ‘lock, bolt’. Derived from *sleutanan ~ *slūtanan. Pokorny I 963; Onions 836; Seebold 436.
ON slota ‘to hang down, to droop’, G dial. schlossen ‘to become slack’. Connected with *sluđrōjanan? Pokorny I 963; Vries ANEW 517.

Norw slō ‘fleshy kernel in a horn or a hoof’, ME slúghe ‘stripped hide’, MLG slū ‘shell, hull’. Close to Slav *lyža ‘pole, keel, ski’ < *lūg(h)ƒā. Torp-Falk 541; Pokorny I 964 (to Lith šlia Užti ‘to crawl’); Vasmer- Trubachev II 540.
Norw slūka ‘to swallow’, MLG slūken id., MHG schlūchen ‘to intertwine’. Related to Gk λύζω ‘to swallow’, OIr sluccim id. Torp-Falk 540; Torp-Falk 540; Pokorny I 964; Frisk II 142; Seebold 435.
Norw dial. slūre ‘idler’, MHG slūr id. Cf. *sluđrōjanan, *slutōjanan. Torp-Falk 540.
ON smeittr ‘enamelled’, OE smæte ‘pure, refined’. Derived from *smītanan. Holthausen AEEW 301; Vries ANEW 519.
Norw smeita ‘to strike’, MHG smeizen ‘to throw, to defecate’. Derived from *smait(j)az. Vries ANEW 519 (to *smēxiz).

Goth smakka ‘fig’, OE smæcc ‘taste’, OFris smaka id., MLG smak id., OHG smac id. Related to Lith smagūs ‘merry, cheerful’ (Zupitza Gutt. 165–166). Torp-Falk 526 (to Lith smaguriaĩ ‘tidbits’); Holthausen AEEW 300–301; Pokorny I 967; Fraenkel 838; Illich-Svitych EIRJa 71–73 (on Slav *smoky ‘fig’); Onions 837; Kluge-Seebold 731.
Swed smaka ‘to taste’, OFris smakia id. Derived from *smak(k)ōn ~ *smak(k)az. Torp-Falk 526. *smakōjanan ~

Swed smacka ‘to smack’, OE smacian id., MLG smacken id. Related to Lith smagiū, smõgti ‘to flog, to fling, to hurl’, Slav *smagati id. (Zupitza Gutt. 165). Torp-Falk 526; Trautmann BSW 270; Fraenkel 848.

Goth smals ‘small’, ON smalr id., OE smæl id., OFris smel ‘slim, thin’, OS smal id., OHG smal id. Probably related to Gk μῆλον ‘small cattle’, Lat malus ‘bad’, Slav *malъ ‘small’ (Solmsen KZ XXXVII 17). See *mēlan III. Karsten NB II 266–267; Berneker II 13; Solmsen KZ XXXVII 18; Torp-Falk 528; Holthausen AEEW 301; Walde- Hofmann II 19–20; Feist 439; Pokorny I 724; Vries ANEW 519; Frisk II 226– 227; Onions 837; Vasmer-Trubachev II 564; Lehmann GED 315; Bammesberger Nom. 249; Heidermanns 517–518; Kluge-Seebold 730.

MHG smelhe ‘small, little’. Related to Lett sma÷lks ‘little, small’, sma÷lce ‘new shoot on the tree’ and, with a different vocalism, to Lith smulkūs ‘little, small’. Further connected with *smalaz. Torp-Falk 528; Fraenkel 839–840, 849–850.
Norw smele, smylve ‘Aira flexuosa’, MHG smelha id. Substantivized *smalxjaz. Torp-Falk 528.
ON smali ‘small cattle’, MHG smal-nōz id. Connected with *smalaz. Vries ANEW 519; Lehmann GED 316.

Norw smolt ‘melted fat’, OE smolt ‘lard’, MLG smalt, smolt ‘fat’, OHG smalz ‘melted fat, lard’. Derived from *smeltanan. Cf. also *maltan. Torp-Falk 529; Holthausen AEEW 303; Seebold 439; Lehmann GED 144.
OE sméah ‘subtle’. Derived from *smeuzanan ~ *smūzanan. Formally identical with Latv smaugs ‘slender’. Torp- Falk 531–532; Holthausen AEEW 301; Pokorny I 745; Heidermanns 518–519.

ON smeygja ‘to make slip’, OE smeázan ‘to consider, to meditate’, MHG sich smougen ‘to cling’. Derived from *smeuzanan ~ *smūzanan. Torp-Falk 531–532; Holthausen AEEW 301; Vries ANEW 520; Seebold 440.
OSwed smælta ‘to melt’, WFris smelte id., OS smeltan id., OHG smelzan id. A variant of *meltanan. Torp-Falk 528–529; Pokorny I 716– 719; Seebold 438–439; Lehmann GED 144; Kluge-Seebold 731.
Dan smelt ‘kind of fish, Osmerus eperlanus’, OE smelt ‘smelt’, Du smelt ‘Ammodytes tobianus’. Derived from *smeltanan. Holthausen AEEW 301; Vries ANEW 519–520.

OE smerian ‘to laugh, to scorn’, OHG smierōn ‘to smile’. Connected with Toch AB smi- ‘to smile’, Skt smáyate ‘to smile, to blush’, smera- ‘smiling’, Latv smite ‘to laugh’, Slav *smîjati s≤ id. Pedersen IF V 41; Trautmann BSW 270–271; Schwentner KZ LXIII 262–263; Schwyzer IF XLV 265; Holthausen AEEW 302; Mayrhofer III 548; Pokorny I 967–968; Onions 838; Vasmer-Trubachev III 688; Adams TB 721; Burlak IFTJa 262.

OE smeortan ‘to smart’, MDu smerten ‘to be painful’, OHG smerzan ‘to wound, to cause pain’. Probably related to Lith smīrdžiu, smirdėti ‘to stink’, Slav *smьrdîti id. Persson Ger. 68 (to Lat mordeō ‘to bite’); Osthoff Etym. 94–95 (same as Persson); Torp-Falk 527; Trautmann BSW 271; Holthausen AEEW 302; Pokorny I 735– 737; Fraenkel 847–848; Onions 838; Seebold 439; Vasmer-Trubachev III 685; Kluge-Seebold 731–732.

ON smjǫr ‘grease, butter’, OE smeoru ‘fat, grease’, OFris smere ‘pus’, OS smero ‘fat, butter’, OHG smer, smero id. Related to Toch B ßmare ‘oil’, OIr smiur, smir ‘marrow’. Tucker Notes 35 (to Lith smársas ‘fat’); Feist 438; Torp-Falk 527; Holthausen AEEW 302; Walde- Pokorny II 690 (to Gk σμάω ‘to rub, to anoint’); Pokorny I 971 (to Gk σμύρις ‘emery-powder’); Vries ANEW 520; Vendryes S-142; Zalizniak I 146; Onions 838; Lehmann GED 315; Heidermanns 520; Kluge-Seebold 731; Adams TB 668.
ON smyrsl ‘ointment’, OE smirels id. (masc.). Derived from *smerwjanan. Marstrander NTS I 244– 245 (to Lat medulla ‘marrow’ < *(s)meuslā); Holthausen AEEW 302; Vries ANEW 521.

ON smyrva, smyrja ‘to anoint’, OE smirwan ‘to smear’, MLG smeren id., OHG smirwen id. Derived from *smerwan ~ *smerwōn. Torp-Falk 527; Holthausen AEEW 302; Pokorny I 956; Vries ANEW 521; Onions 838; Kluge- Seebold 732.

ON smjúga ‘to creep (through a hole), to pierce’, OE smúzan ‘to creep, to crawl’, MHG smiugen id. Related to Lith smunkū, smūkti ‘to glide off’, Slav *smykati ‘to whisk, to crawl’ (Fick KZ XX 366). Zupitza Gutt. 138–139 (to Lat mucrō ‘sharp point, edge’); Torp-Falk 531–532; Trautmann BSW 271; Holthausen AEEW 303; Jóhannesson IEW 660–662; Pokorny I 744–745; Fraenkel 849; Vries ANEW 520; Onions 839; Stang LS 51; Seebold 439–440; Vasmer-Trubachev III 694– 695; Kluge-Seebold 732.

OE smeócan ‘to smoke’, Du smieken ‘to burn, to smoke’. Related to Lith smáugiu, smáugti ‘to strangle’, Slav *smuga ‘black spot, stripe’? Cf. further, with a different velar, Gk σμύχω ‘to burn in a slow fire’ and Slav *smaga ‘fire, flame’. Zupitza Gutt. 166; Torp- Falk 531; Holthausen AEEW 301; Kluge 662–663; Pokorny I 971; Fraenkel 841; Frisk II 752; Onions 839; Seebold 440–441; Vasmer-Trubachev III 682–683, 693.

ON smyrill ‘merlin, a kind of hawk’, MHG smirle id. Related to Lat merula id. and Celt *mesalkā or *misalkā > W mwyalch (see *amslōn). Torp-Falk 527; Vries ANEW 521 (to MHG smerle ‘loach’, Gk σμαρίς ‘small sea-fish, Smaris vulgaris’).

ON smár ‘little, small’, OS smā-wort ‘contemptuous word’, OHG smāh ‘small’. Any connection with *smalaz or *smikraz? Curtius Gr. Et. 693 (to Gk sm—κρός ‘little, short’); Persson UUÅ 1891 10–11 (to Gk μακεδνός ‘tall, taper’, Lat macer ‘soft, tender’); Hirt Ablaut 37; Karsten NB II 251–252; Torp-Falk 526; Pokorny I 966; Vries ANEW 519; Heidermanns 519; Kluge-Seebold 729.
ON for-smá ‘to despise’, OFris for-smāia ‘to spurn’, OHG bi-smāhōn ‘to show contempt’. Derived from *smēxaz. Torp-Falk 526; Vries ANEW 519; Heidermanns 519.

OE smicer ‘fine, fair, beautiful’, OHG smeckar ‘delicate, fine’. Despite the irregular *-k-, somehow related to Gk sm—κρός, m—κρός ‘little, small’. Zupitza Gutt. 199; Torp-Falk 530; Holthausen AEEW 302; Pokorny I 966–967 (to Lith šmižti ‘to remain small’); Frisk II 236–237; Heidermanns 521.
Norw dial. smita ‘to smear in a thin layer’, OE smittian ‘to smear’, MLG smitten ‘to make dirty’, MHG bi-smizzen ‘to smear’. Related to *smītanan. Torp-Falk 530.
ON smiä-belgr ‘bellows’, OE smiä-belz id. Compound of *smiþaz and *balziz. Carr Nom. comp. 82 (parallel formations).

ON smiär ‘smith, craftsman’ (also u- and i-stems), OE smiä ‘smith’, OFris smith id., OS smith id., OHG smid id. Cf. also Goth aiza-smiþa ‘copper-smith’ < *-smiþōn. Derivative in *-to- based on IE *smei- ‘to spread, to smear’, cf. Gk σμάω id., Gmc *smītanan. Semantically, this development is close to that of Slav *kaliti ‘to temper (of metals)’ derived from *kalъ ‘dirt’. This shift implies an intermediate stage ‘to dip the metal into water or liquid clay’ (Trubachev ESSJa IX 124). Potebnia RFV IV 209 (to Slav *mîdь ‘copper’); Brugmann IF VI 93 (to Gk σμίλη ‘knife, chisel’); Torp-Falk 529; Holthausen AEEW 302; Feist 31–32; Pokorny I 966, 968; Chemodanov SGGJa I 108; Vries ANEW 520; Zalizniak II 203; Onions 838; Lehmann GED 23 (to *maitanan); Kluge-Seebold 732.

ON smiäja ‘smithy’, OE smiääe id., OFris smithe id., MLG smede id., OHG smitta id. Derived from *smiþaz. Torp-Falk 529; Holthausen AEEW 302; Vries ANEW 520; Zalizniak I 156; Onions 838; Lehmann GED 23.
Goth ga-smiþon ‘to forge’, OE smiäian id., MLG smeden id., OHG smidōn id. Derived from *smiþaz. Torp-Falk 529; Holthausen AEEW 302; Feist 201; Vries ANEW 520; Onions 838.

Goth ga-smeitan ‘to smear’, OE smítan id., OFris smīta id., MLG smiten id., OHG bi-smīzan id. Related to Arm mic ‘dirt’, Slav *smîdъ ‘dark brown’ (Scheftelowitz BB XXIX 30) and further to Gk σμήω ‘to smear’. Pott EF I 253 (to Lat mittō ‘to send’); Bartholomae Zd Wf VI 354–355 (to Av ha-mīsta- ‘thrown down’); Torp-Falk 530; Feist 95–96; Holthausen AEEW 302; Pokorny I 966; Chantraine 1027; Onions 838; Vasmer-Trubachev III 684; Seebold 437–438 (follows Pott); Lehmann GED 72–73; Kluge-Seebold 731.
ON smíäi ‘smith’s work’, MLG smīde id., OHG gi-smīdi ‘metal’. Related to *smiþaz. Torp-Falk 529; Vries ANEW 520.
ON smíä ‘craft, smith’s work’, OHG smīda ‘metal, piece of metal (to be worked on)’. Related to *smiþaz. Torp-Falk 529; Vries ANEW 520.
ON smuga ‘narrow cleft to creep through, hole’, OE æ-smozu ‘snake’s skin’. Derived from *smeuzanan ~ *smūzanan. Holthausen AEEW 303; Vries ANEW 520.

ON smokkr ‘smock (with an opening in front)’, OE smocc ‘smock’, MLG smuck ‘shirt’, OHG smoccho ‘underwear’ (< *smukkōn). Of unknown origin. Torp-Falk 532 (to *smeuzanan ~ *smūzanan); Holthausen AEEW 303; Martinet Gém. 192; Pokorny I 745 (to IE *(s)meug- ~ *(s)meuk- ‘to glide’); Vries ANEW 520; Zalizniak II 179; Onions 839; Seebold 440.
Norw dial. smolla ‘to smile’, MHG smollen id. Of imitative origin. Torp-Falk 531.
OFris snabba ‘mouth’, MLG snabbe ‘beak’. Close to Lith snãpas id. Torp-Falk 520; Fraenkel 851– 852.
ON sneiäingr ‘zigzag path’, OE snædinz ‘meal’ (fem.). Derived from *snaiđiz. Holthausen AEEW 303; Vries ANEW 523.
ON sneiä ‘slice’, OE snæd ‘cut, slice, morsel, bit’, OFris snēde ‘slice’. Derived from *snīþanan. Torp-Falk 522; Holthausen AEEW 303; Vries ANEW 523; Seebold 443.
ON sneiäa ‘to cut into slices, to walk zigzag’, OE snædan ‘to slice’, OHG sneiten id. Derived from *snīþanan. Torp-Falk 522; Holthausen AEEW 303; Vries ANEW 523; Seebold 443.
ON snæ-hvítr ‘snow-white’, OE snáw-hwít id., OFris snie-wyt id., MLG snē-wīt id., MHG snēwīz id. Compound of *snaizwaz and *xwītaz. Carr Nom. comp. 60.

Goth snaiws ‘snow’ (aor i-stem), ON snær, snjór (gen.sg. snævar ~ snæs) id., OE snáw id., OFris snē id., OS snēo id., OHG snēo id. Etymologically connected with Toch B ≤iñcatstse ‘snowy’, Prakrit siṇeha- < *sneha- id., Av snaēža- ‘to snow’, Gk acc. νίφα ‘snow’, Thrac (?) νίβα: χιόνα. καί κρήνην (Hes.), Lat nix id., Ir snigid ‘to rain, to snow’, Lith sni‚gas ‘snow’, Latv snīegs, Slav *snîgъ id. (Kuhn KZ I 138). See *snīzwanan. Schweizer KZ I 479 (to Skt snāti ‘to bathe’); Hirt Urgerm. II 42 (on the suffixation); Zupitza Gutt. 100; Bartholomae 1626; Pedersen Kelt. Gr. I 85; Torp-Falk 522; Trautmann BSW 272–273; Holthausen AEEW 304; Walde-Hofmann II 169–170; Feist 440; Detschew Thr. 331; Porzig Gliederung 141; Pokorny I 974; Chemodanov SSGJa I 56; Fraenkel 853; Vries ANEW 527; Zalizniak II 189; Frisk II 298–299; Onions 841; Seebold 442, KZ LXXX 122–123; Vasmer-Trubachev III 697; Lehmann GED 316; Bammesberger Nom. 52; Kluge-Seebold 735; Adams TB 629–630.
ON snjóva ‘to snow’, ME snówen id., MDu sneeuwen, snouwen, snīwen id. Derived from *snaizwaz. Vries ANEW 525.

ON sneisa ‘to šspit” a sausage, to coil a sausage up’, OE snæsan ‘to spit, to run through with a pointed weapon’, MHG sneisen ‘to thread, to line up’. Derived from *snaisō. Holthausen AEEW 304; Vries ANEW 523.

ON sneis ‘skewer’, OE snæs, snás ‘spit, skewer’, OFris snēs ‘twenty pieces’, MLG snēse ‘branch, measure of 7–20 fishes’, MHG sneise ‘row, string on which items are put in a line’. Probably derived from *snīþanan. Torp-Falk 522–523; Holthausen AEEW 304; Pokorny I 974; Vries ANEW 523.
OHG snahhan ‘to crawl’. Related to OIr snaighim id. Zupitza Gutt. 166; Torp-Falk 518; Walde-Pokorny II 697–692; Pokorny I 974; Onions 840; Seebold 441–442.

ON snekkja ‘a kind of swift-sailing ship’, OE snacc ‘swift-sailing vessel’, OHG snagga ‘ship with a prow’. Derived from *snē-: Skt snāti ‘to bathe’, Gk νέω ‘to swim’, Lat nō id., OIr snáim id. Brugmann IF XX 221–223; Torp- Falk 519 (to Norw dial. snaga ‘to stick out’); Holthausen AEEW 303; Walde-Hofmann II 172; Pokorny I 971–972; Vries ANEW 523; Frisk II 310–311.

ON snákr ‘snake’, OE snaca id., MLG snake id. (fem.). Derived from *snakanan. Zupitza Gutt. 166; Torp-Falk 518; Holthausen AEEW 303; Vries ANEW 522; Onions 840; Lerchner 196; Seebold 441; Levickij IF CIII 220; Kluge-Seebold 734.
MHG snal ‘swift movement’. Related to *snellaz (and confirming this reconstruction of the latter). Heidermanns 525.
ON snellask ‘to strap forcefully’, MDu snellen ‘to move fast’, MHG snellen ‘to click, to snap’. Derived from *snallaz. Heidermanns 525.
ON snapa ‘to snuffle’, Du snappen ‘to snatch’, MHG snappen id. Of unknown origin. Cf. probably *snabbōn. Torp- Falk 520; Vries ANEW 522; Kluge- Seebold 734.
Norw dial. snar ‘intertwining, knot’, MLG snar ‘string’. Substantivized *snarxaz. Torp-Falk 521.

ON snarr ‘hard-twisted (of a rope), swift, keen’, OE snear ‘nimble, strong’, MDu snare ‘nimble, skillful’. Derived from *snerxanan. Karsten NB I 49–50; Torp-Falk 521; Vries ANEW 523 (to *snarxōn); Heidermanns 522.

ON snara ‘snare’, OE snearu ‘noose, snare’, MLG snare ‘string’, OHG snaraha id. Related to Gk νάρκη ‘numbness, deadness’. Derived from *snerxanan. Torp-Falk 521; Jóhannesson IEW 917–919; Pokorny I 977–978; Vries ANEW 522; Zalizniak I 156; Onions 840; Seebold 444.

ON snarpr ‘rough to the touch, sharp’. Related to Arm snerb ‘narrow’ (Schröder IF XVII 461). Derived from *snerpanan. Fick I 575 (to Gk νορβά: καλή, Hes., νορβεῖ: ἐνταμεῖται, Hes., Arm snerb ‘narrow’); Zupitza Gutt. 28; Torp-Falk 521; Pokorny I 976; Vries ANEW 524; Lehmann GED 46.
Goth at-snarpjan ‘to grasp, to touch’, ON snerpa ‘to whet’. Derived from *snarpaz or from *snerpanan. Feist 61; Vries ANEW 524; Lehmann GED 46.
ON snata ‘spear’, OHG snazo ‘pike’. Probably related to Slav *snaditi ‘to fix together’, *snastь ‘instrument, tool, weapons’. Vries ANEW 523 (to ON snót ‘gentlewoman’); Vasmer- Trubachev III 696–697.
Norw dial. snatra ‘to sniff, to crinkle’, MLG snateren ‘to chatter’. Of imitative origin. Cf. *snawwōjanan ~ *snuwwōjanan. Torp-Falk 519.

ON snauär ‘poor, stripped, bereft’, MLG snōde ‘contemptible, worthless’, MHG snœde ‘contemptible, servile’. Derived from *sneuþanan. Zupitza BB XXV 95 (to Skt kṣṇāuti ‘to whet’); Karsten NB II 395–397; Torp-Falk 524–525; Kluge 648–649; Pokorny I 585; Vries ANEW 523; Seebold 445 (reconstructs *snauđaz); Heidermanns 523.
ON sneyäa ‘to bereave’, OE be-snyäian ‘to deprive’. Derived from *sneuþanan. Torp-Falk 524; Holthausen AEEW 305; Seebold 445.

ON snøggr ‘short, smooth, sudden, brief’, MLG snouw, snaw ‘rapid, shrewd, skillful’. Derived from *snew(w)anan. Karsten NB II 399–401; Torp-Falk 524; Vries ANEW 527; Lindeman Verschärfung 146–147; Heidermanns 524; Dybo BSA 27 (with oxytonic shortening).
Norw dial. snugga ‘to sniff, to scent’, MHG snouwen ‘to sniff, to wheeze’. Derived from *snawwaz. Torp-Falk 524.

ON snigill ‘snail’, OE snezel id., OS snegil id., OHG snegil id. Derived from *snezzōn. Torp-Falk 519; Holthausen AEEW 304; Chemodanov SGGJa I 74; Vries ANEW 524; Zalizniak I 135; Onions 839–840; Kluge-Seebold 735.

MLG snigge ‘snail’, OHG sneggo id. Related to Lith snãkë id. connected with IE *snā- ‘to flow, to swim’. For the suffix cf., probably, the Greek present νήχω < *snāghō. Pokorny I 971–972; Fraenkel 851 (Lith < G dial. schnak ‘snail’); Vries ANEW 524; Kluge- Seebold 735.

ON snjallr ‘swift, good, excellent, valliant’, OE snell ‘quick, active, strong’, OFris snell ‘quick, fast’, OS snell ‘valiant, pugnacious, spright’, OHG snel ‘brave, deft, lively’. Of unknown origin. Karsten NB II 371; Holthausen AEEW 304; Vries ANEW 525; Ritter Anglia XXXIII 471 (reconstructs *sniđlaz, to *snīþanan); Bammesberger Nom. 248 (follows Ritter); Heidermanns 524–525 (reconstructs *snellaz); Kluge-Seebold 736.
ON snilli ‘prowess, skill, eloquence’, OHG snellī ‘agility, courage’. Derived from *snellaz. Vries ANEW 524–525; Heidermanns 525.
OHG in-snerahan ‘to bind’. An isolated archaism, cf. *snarxōn. Of unknown origin. Torp-Falk 521; Pokorny I 976–977; Onions 840; Seebold 444.

Icel part. snorkinn ‘wrinkled’, OE pret. sg. ze-snerc ‘to shrink’. Cf. *snerpanan. Zupitza Gutt. 166 (to Lith snargl‹s ‘mucus’); Torp-Falk 520; Holthausen AEEW 304; Jóhannesson IEW 917–919; Pokorny I 975–977; Vries ANEW 524; Seebold 444–445.
Norw snerpa ‘to shrink’, OHG snerfan id. Of unknown origin. Torp-Falk 521; Feist 61; Jóhannesson IEW 917–919; Pokorny I 975–977; Vries ANEW 523; Seebold 445.

ON snimma ‘early’, OE adv. sneóme ‘swiftly, rapidly’, OS sniumi ‘rapid, fast’, OHG sniumi id. Derived from *snew(w)anan (Kluge Nom. Stamm. 93). Torp-Falk 524; Holthausen AEEW 304; Vries ANEW 523–524; Schubert Erw. 72; Seebold 446; Lehmann GED 317; Heidermanns 525– 526; Kluge-Seebold 727.
Goth sniumjan ‘to hurry’, MLG ge-sniumen id., OHG sniumen ‘to expedite’. Derived from *sneumaz. Torp-Falk 524; Feist 440; Lehmann GED 317; Heidermanns 526.
ON part. snoäinn ‘bald’, late MHG be-schnotten ‘to cut, to trim’. Of unknown origin. Cf. *snīþanan? Torp-Falk 524; Holthausen AEEW 305; Vries ANEW 525; Pokorny I 585; Seebold 445.

Goth sniwan ‘to come upon, to hasten’, OE sneówan ‘to proceed, to hasten’. Cf. also ON str. snúa ‘to wind’ < *snōwanan. Related to Slav *snovǫ, *snuti ‘to warp’. Persson Beiträge 818–819; Uhlenbeck PBB XXX 309; Schwyzer Festschr. Kretschmer 244–251; Torp-Falk 523; Feist 441; Holthausen AEEW 304, AWN 270; Pokorny I 973, 977; Vries ANEW 526; Stang LS 51–52; Seebold 446– 447; Bammesberger KZ XC 358–261; Vasmer-Trubachev III 699; Lindeman Verschärfung 15.13; Lehmann GED 317; Kluge-Seebold 727.
ON sniä ‘slice’, OE ze-snid ‘killing, slaughter’. Derived from *snīþanan. Holthausen AEEW 304; Vries ANEW 524; Seebold 443; Bammesberger Nom. 56.
ON sniäill ‘pruning knife’, MHG snittel ‘little cut’. Derived from *sniđan. Vries ANEW 524; Seebold 444.
ON snepill ‘snip, flap’, eyra-snepill ‘earlobe’, MLG sneppel ‘mouthful, corner, end’. Derived from *snīpanan. Torp-Falk 523; Vries ANEW 524.

ON snÿr ‘it snows’, OE sníwan ‘to snow’, OHG sniwit ‘it snows’. See *snaizwaz. Close to Av snaēžaid., Gk νείφει id., Ir snigid ‘to rain’, Lith snīgti ‘to snow’, snéigëti id. Bartholomae 1626; Torp-Falk 522; Trautmann BSW 273–274; Jóhannesson IEW 913; Pokorny I 974; Fraenkel 853; Vries ANEW 527; Frisk II 298–299; Onions 841; Seebold 442–443; Lehmann GED 316; Kluge-Seebold 736.

OE snícan ‘to crawl’. Cf. also Norw dial. sníkja ‘to cadge, to live on others’. Identical with Latv snīgt ‘to hand, to pass, to reach’. Torp-Falk 522; Holthausen AEEW 304; Pokorny I 974; Vries ANEW 524.
Norw dial. snīpa ‘to snap’. Probably connected with Skt snāyati ‘to wrap around’, Gk νήθω ‘to spin’, Lat neō id. < *snēi-. Torp-Falk 523 (to Gk σκνίπτω ‘to pinch, to nip’); Pokorny I 973.
ON mÿri-snípa ‘moor-snipe’, ME snípe ‘snipe’, OS sneppa id., OHG snepfa id. Derived from *snīpanan. Torp- Falk 523; Vries ANEW 525; Kluge- Seebold 736.

Goth sneiþan ‘to harvest, to reap’, ON sníäa ‘to cut, to slice’, OE sníäan ‘to cut, to make an incision’, OFris snītha ‘to cut’, OS snīäan id., OHG snīdan id. No external cognates. Lewy KZ XX 561 (to Slav *snîtъ ‘block, log’); Torp-Falk 522; Stokes KZ XXXVIII 471 (to OIr snéid ‘small, short’); Lewy KZ XL 561 (to Slav *snîtъ ‘log, branch’); Holthausen AEEW 304; Feist 440; Jóhannesson IEW 913–914; Pokorny I 974; Vries ANEW 524; Seebold 443– 444 (to OIr snadat ‘to cut off’ with a different vocalism); Lehmann GED 316– 317; Kluge-Seebold 735–736.
ON snœfr ‘tight, narrow, tough’, G dial. schnueper ‘swift, lively’. A variant of *nōbriz. Karsten NB II 448; Torp-Falk 292, 569; Pokorny I 973– 974; Heidermanns 526–527.
OSwed snoä ‘string, cord’, OE snód ‘snood, fillet, head-dress’. Related to OIr gloss snáthe ‘filum’, Latv snāte ‘linen cover’. Torp-Falk 523; Pokorny I 973.
Norw dial. snōk ‘muzzle, snout’, MLG snōk ‘pike’. Of imitative origin? Cf. *snabbōn. Vries ANEW 522, 525 (to *snakōn ~ *snēkaz).
Norw snópa ‘to snatch’, EFris snōpen ‘to regale oneself with’, Du snoepen id. Connected with *snappēnan ~ *snappōjanan. Torp-Falk 520; Vries ANEW 525.

Goth snorjo ‘net, basket’, ON neut. snœri ‘twisted rope’, OE snér ‘string (of a musical instrument)’. Derived from *snōrō. Holthausen AEEW 305; Feist 441; Vries ANEW 528; Lehmann GED 317; Kluge-Seebold 738.

Norw snōr ‘cord, string’, MLG snōr id., OHG snuor id. An ablaut innovation related to Toch B ßñor ‘sinew’, Av snāvar 6 id., Gk νεῦρον id., Lat neruus id. < *neuro-, Arm neard id. (Johansson PBB XIV 379). Wood IF XVIII 24–25 (to *(s)ner- ‘to turn’); Persson Beiträge 820–822 (same as Wood); Bartholomae 1629; Torp-Falk 523; Walde-Hofmann II 165; Benveniste Origines 21, 111; Pokorny I 976; Frisk II 308–309; Burlak IFTJa 259.
ON snubba ‘to snub, to chide’, E snub. Cf. *snuppōjanan, *snuppōn. Martinet Gém. 170, 174; Vries ANEW 526.
Norw snukka ‘to sniff, to snort’, MLG snucken ‘to sob’. Cf. *snuþjanan. Torp-Falk 524.
Norw dial. snuppa ‘to cut off’, LG snuppen ‘to clean’, G dial. schnupfen id. Related to *snuppōn? Vries ANEW 526 (to *snuppōn).
ON snopa, snoppa ‘muzzle, snout’, MLG snoppe ‘snuff’, MHG snupfe id. Of imitative origin. Cf. *snabbōn. Vries ANEW 525 (to ON sneypa ‘to outrage, to dishonor’).

Goth snutrs ‘wise, clever’, ON snotr id., OE snotor ‘prudent, wise’, OHG snottar ‘clever, intelligent’. Derived from *snew(w)anan. Meyer KZ V 368 (to Gk νοῦς ‘mind’); Karsten NB II 253; Grienberger Got. 195; Hirt Ablaut 120 (to Lat sentiō ‘to perceive’); Schade AW II 940–941 (to *snūtjanan, cf. Lat sapiēns ‘wise’ ~ sapiō ‘to taste’); Uhlenbeck Altind. Wb. 333 (to Skt sāndra- ‘viscid, compact, strong’); Torp-Falk 524; Schwyzer Festschr. Kretschmer 244–251 (same as Meyer); Feist 441–442; Holthausen AEEW 305, 425; Vries ANEW 526 (follows Schade); Lehmann GED 317; Bammesberger Nom. 247; Heidermanns 527–528.
Goth snutrei ‘intelligence, wisdom’, OE snytru ‘prudence, wisdom’. Derived from *snutraz. Holthausen AEEW 305; Feist 441; Lehmann GED 317–318; Heidermanns 528.

Norw snott ‘snot’, OE snot id., OFris snotte ‘snuffof a candle, snot’, MLG snotte ‘catarrh’, OHG snuz id. (masc.). Of expressive origin. Torp- Falk 525; Holthausen AEEW 304; Onions 841; Kluge-Seebold 737.
ON snuära ‘to snuff, to scent’, MLG snoderen ‘to blow one’s nose’, MHG snuderen ‘to sniff, to snort’. Derived from *snuþjanan. Torp- Falk 525; Vries ANEW 526.
ON snyäja ‘to sniff, to sniffle’, OE snyäian ‘to go as a dog with its nose to the ground (?)’. Of imitative origin. Cf. *snuttan. Torp-Falk 525; Holthausen AEEW 305; Vries ANEW 526; Lehmann GED 317.

Crim. Goth schuos ‘daughter-in-law’ (leg. *schnos), ON snǫr, snor id., OE snoru id., OFris snore id., MLG snore id., OHG snura id., snur id. (i-stem). Together with Skt snuṣā id. and Slav *snъxa id. represents a morphological modification of *snusos id.: Arm nu, Gk νυός id., Lat nurus id. (Aufrecht KZ I 119). Tomaschek Taurien 63 (Crim. Goth form *swōs from *s⁄e-); Meillet MSL XIII 121; Pedersen IF V 34; Kluge Zd Wf VII 169–170; Torp-Falk 526; Holthausen Anz. ZDADL XXIV 33 (conjecture for Crim. Gothic), AEEW 305; Meillet MSL XIII 211; Walde-Hofmann II 190; Feist 414; Mohr ZDADL LXXVI 185 (ON < WGmc); Mayrhofer III 535; Pokorny I 978; Trubachev Rod. 131–133 (on G Schnur ‘daughter-in-law’ ~ ‘string, cord’); Vries ANEW 528; Zalizniak II 197; Frisk II 328; Vasmer-Trubachev III 700; Lehmann GED 298–299; Kluge- Seebold 738–739.
Norw dial. snūve ‘snuff’, MLG snūf, snūve id. Derived from the poorly attested *snūbanan ‘to snore’ > MHG snūben. Cf. *snuþjanan. Torp- Falk 525.
ON snúär ‘twist, twirl’, OE snúd ‘swiftness, quickness’. Derived from *snew(w)anan. Torp-Falk 523; Holthausen AEEW 305; Dybo VSJa 33; Vries ANEW 526; Lehmann GED 317.
Norw snūt ‘snout’, ME snúte id., MLG snūt id. Related to *snuttan. Torp-Falk 525; Onions 841.

ON snÿta ‘to blow the nose’, OE snÿtan id., MLG snuten id., OHG snūzen id. Derived from *snūtaz ~ *snūtō. Torp-Falk 525; Holthausen AEEW 305; Vries ANEW 527 (to MIr snuad ‘flow’); Onions 841; Heidermanns 528; Kluge-Seebold 737–738.

Goth un-and-soks ‘indisputable, irrefutable’, OESc söker ‘guilty, punishable’. Derived from *sōkjanan. Karsten NB II 454; Lindqvist ANF XXV 283; Feist 517; Matzel Festschr. Schröder 105, KZ CV 120; Heidermanns 528.

Goth sokjan ‘to seek, to quest’, ON sœkja ‘to seek, to advance, to catch’, OE sécan ‘to seek’, OFris sēka id., OS sōkian id., OHG suohhen id. Related to Hitt šak- ‘to know’, Gk ἡγέομαι ‘to lead’, Lat sāgiō ‘to perceive’, OIr saigim ‘to seek out’. Factitive of *sakanan. Zupitza Gutt. 216; Torp-Falk 423; Mezger KZ LXII 259–260 (rejects the Greek forms); Holthausen AEEW 306; Walde-Hofmann II 464–465; Feist 442; Pokorny I 877–878; Chemodanov SGGJa I 103; Vries ANEW 577; Frisk I 621–622; Onions 806; Seebold 384; Justus Perspectives 296–299 (on Hittite); Gamkrelidze-Ivanov 805 (on the ritual connotations of *sāg-); Lehmann GED 318; Kluge-Seebold 807; Boutkan OFED s.v.

Goth sokns ‘controversy, controversial question’, ON sókn ‘attack, prosecution’, OE sócn ‘seeking, search’. Cf. OHG suohnī ‘examination, interrogation’. Derived from *sōkjanan. Zupitza Gutt. 19; Holthausen AEEW 306; Feist 442; Vries ANEW 529; Onions 843; Seebold 384; Lehmann GED 293; Bammesberger Nom. 148.

ON sœmr ‘becoming, fit’, OS sōmi ‘suitable’, MHG süeme ‘pleasant, charming’. Related to OIr sám ‘quiet, peaceful, pleasant’ (Möller PBB VII 509). Karsten NB I 113; Torp-Falk 434; Feist 409; Holthausen AEEW 306–307; Vries ANEW 577; Vendryes S-20; Darms Vṛddhi 167–176; Lehmann GED 295; Bammesberger Nom. 257; Heidermanns 529.
ON sœma ‘to honor’, OE séman ‘to bring to an agreement, to settle’, MHG süemen ‘to decorate’. Derived from *sōmiz. Torp-Falk 434; Holthausen AEEW 306; Vries ANEW 577; Heidermanns 529.
ON sómi ‘honor’, OE sóm ‘agreement, concord’. Substantivized *sōmiz. Holthausen AEEW 307; Lehmann GED 295.

ON són ‘atonement, sacrifice’, MLG sōne, swōne ‘atonement’, OHG suona ‘reconciliation, atonement’. Related to Lat sānus ‘healthy’, fem. sāna. Torp- Falk 556; Walde-Hofmann II 476 (šunsicher”); Pokorny I 880.

ON sót ‘soot’, OE sót id., MLG sōt id. Derived from *setjanan. Similar formations with ō-grade are also attested in OIr suide ‘soot’ < *sōdƒā and in Balto-Slavic: Lith pl. súodžiai ‘soot’, Slav *sadja id. (Hirt IF XXXII 311). Cf. further Slav *sadъ ‘garden’ < *sōdo-. Torp-Falk 428; Trautmann BSW 273; Holthausen AEEW 307; Pokorny I 886; Fraenkel 942; Vries ANEW 531; Zalizniak I 143; Onions 846; Stang LS 56; Vasmer-Trubachev III 544; Darms Vṛddhi 296–298; Lehmann GED 306; Bammesberger Nom. 63.
Goth soþ ‘satiation, satisfaction’. Close to Lith sótis id. Further related to *sađaz. Torp-Falk 421; Pokorny I 876–877 (postverbal to *sōþjanan); Fraenkel 857.
Goth ga-soþjan ‘to satisfy, to satiate’, OE sédan ‘to satisfy’. Derived from *sōþan. Torp-Falk 421; Pokorny I 876.

Goth sauil ‘sun’, ON sól id. Related to Skt súvar- id., Av hvar 6 id., Gk Hom ἠέλιος id., Lat sōl id., W haul id., OCorn heuul id., Bret heol id. (< *sā⁄el-), Lith sáulë id., Latv sa Ule id., Slav *sъlnьce id. (Curtius KZ I 31). Petersson SVSL I 4 (relation to *sunnan); Bartholomae 1847–1848; Pedersen Kelt. Gr. I 62, 282; Persson Beiträge 578–579; Torp-Falk 441; Solmsen KZ XXXVIII 454; Trautmann BSW 251; Walde- Hofmann II 553–554; Feist 412; Mayrhofer III 566–567; Pokorny I 881; Fraenkel 765–766; Vries ANEW 529; Zalizniak II 194; Frisk I 631– 632; Vasmer-Trubachev III 710–711; Lehmann GED 297; Bammesberger Nom. 206.
ON spaä ‘meat stew’, OE sped ‘phlegm, rheum’. Probably from *spa-tó- related to Gk σπάω ‘to pull’, σπάτος ‘skin’. Holthausen AEEW 309; Walde-Hofmann II 568–569; Pokorny I 982; Vries ANEW 531.

Icel spaäi ‘spade’, OE spada id., OFris spada id., OS spado id., early G spade id. Etymologically close to Hitt išpatar ‘spit, skewer’, Gk σπάθη ‘blade, paddle’ (Kammenhuber MIO III 354–355). Torp-Falk 507; Holthausen AEEW 307; Pokorny I 980; Frisk II 755; Onions 849; Kluge- Seebold 775.

Swed dial. spaik ‘spoke’, OE spáca id. (masc.), OFris *spāke id., OS spēka id., OHG speihha id. Related to *spīkaz ~ *spīkō. Zupitza Gutt. 167; Holthausen AEEW 307; Vries ANEW 535; Onions 856; Kluge-Seebold 776. *spaiskulđran ~

Goth spaiskuldr ‘spittle’, OS spēkaldra id., OHG speihhaltra id. Inlaut -slost in WGmc as a result of deetymologization. Vocalism is not clear. An old compound of dubious origin. Probably, the first element is *spaiwa-, to *spīwanan, while the second part is derived from *skulđr(j)ō. No reliable etymology. Grienberger Got. 195–196 (reconstructs *spaiskōjanan as a source); Scheftelowitz Zf Ind VI 96 (to Skt picchā ‘scum’); Schulze ASNSL CXL 179 (reconstructs *spaiskalđrō); Torp-Falk 514; Feist 442–443; Kluge Nom. Stamm. 51 (explains -s- in Gothic as a scribal error); Ebbinghaus Beitr. Gesch. LXXXI 116–117 (the second component to *sk(w)ulōjanan); Lehmann GED 318; Kluge-Seebold 776 (derived from *spaiw-l-ō(n) > *spaiklō(n) > OHG speihhila ‘spittle’).

MLG spalden ‘to split’, OHG spaltan id. Related to Skt spha†ati ‘to burst’. Further cf. *spelđan, *spelđō. Zupitza Gutt. 47; Torp-Falk 511; Endzelin KZ LII 120 (to Latv spiltavas ‘chaff’); Mayrhofer III 540 (against comparison with Skt); Pokorny I 985–986; Vries ANEW 536; Seebold 448–449; Kluge-Seebold 773.
ON spǫlr ‘rail, bar, latticework’, ME spale id., MHG spale ‘stair’. Connected with *spalđanan. Torp-Falk 511; Pokorny I 985–986; Vries ANEW 540.

OSwed spana ‘to lure, to entice’, OE spanan id., OFris pres. spona ‘to induce, to lead, to tempt’, OS spanan ‘to drive on, to lure’, OHG spanan ‘to lure, to entice’. Continues *sp 6-nō further related to Gk σπάω ‘to pull’. Persson SVSU X 386–421; Torp-Falk 507; Holthausen AEEW 308; Pokorny I 982; Vries ANEW 532; Frisk II 759–761; Seebold 449–450; Kluge- Seebold 9.
ON spengja ‘to spangle’, MDu spengen ‘to keep within limits’, MHG spengen ‘to fix with a clasp’. Derived from *spanzō(n). Vries ANEW 533.

ON spǫng ‘spangle’ (root stem and i-stem), OE spanz ‘clasp, fastening’, MLG spange id., OHG spanga ‘crosspiece, bar’. Somehow connected with *spananan. Zupitza Gutt. 210; Torp- Falk 508; Holthausen AEEW 308; Pokorny I 982; Vries ANEW 540 (to Gk σφηκόω ‘to bind tightly’ < *‘to be like a wasp’); Zalizniak II 210; Onions 849; Kluge-Seebold 774 (to *spannanan).
ON spaning ‘temptation’, OHG spanunga ‘encouragement’. Derived from *spananan. Vries ANEW 532.

ON spenja ‘to attract’, OE spennan ‘to allure’, OHG spennen ‘to lure, to entice’. Derived from *spananan. Torp-Falk 507; Holthausen AEEW 309; Jóhannesson IEW 889; Pokorny I 982; Vries ANEW 533–534; Seebold 449.
