ON speni ‘nipple, teat’, OE spanu id., MHG spen id. (< *spaniz). Related to Lith spen‹s id., OPrus spenis id., OIr sine ‘teat’ < *spenƒo- (Persson Beiträge 411). Torp-Falk 508; Trautmann BSW 275; Holthausen AEEW 308; Porzig Gliederung 163; Pokorny I 990; Chemodanov SGGJa I 61; Fraenkel 865; Vries ANEW 533; Kluge-Seebold 774.

ON spann ‘pail’, spǫnn id. (fem.), OE ze-spann ‘joining, fastening together’, OFris twi-spon ‘clasp’, OHG gispan ‘clasp, chain’. Derived from *spannanan. Persson Beiträge 413, 588 (< *spanđan, to Slav *spǫdъ ‘pail’); Torp- Falk 507; Holthausen AEEW 308; Trubachev KSIS XXV 103 (against the comparison with Slav *spǫdъ); Seebold 450.

OE spannan ‘to join, to attach, to span’, OS part. ur-spannane ‘to slacken, to unbend’, MLG spannen ‘to span’, OHG spannan id. Related to *spananan. Torp-Falk 507; Holthausen AEEW 308; Pokorny I 982; Abaev AION IV 31–32 (to Osset æfsonz ~ æfsoj ‘yoke’ < *span-ti-); Seebold 450; Kluge-Seebold 774.
ON spenzl, spennsl ‘clasp’, OE spennels ‘clasp’. Derived from *spannjanan. Holthausen AEEW 309; Vries ANEW 534.
ON spenna ‘to span’, MLG spennen ‘to stretch’, MHG spennen id. Causative of *spannanan. Torp-Falk 507; Vries ANEW 534.

ON spǫnn ‘span’, OE spann id., OFris sponne id., MLG spanne id., OHG spanna ‘span, cubit’. Derived from *spannanan. Torp-Falk 507; Holthausen AEEW 308; Jóhannesson IEW 889–890; Pokorny I 982 (to Lat sponda ‘frame (of a bedstead)’, Slav *spǫdъ ‘bushel’); Vries ANEW 532, 540; Zalizniak II 194; Onions 849; Seebold 450; Kluge-Seebold 774.
ON adv. spar-liga ‘sparingly’, OHG spara-līhho ‘poor’. Derived from *sparaz. Heidermanns 531–532.

ON sparr ‘sparing’, OE spær ‘spare, frugal’, MDu spaer ‘thrifty’, OHG spar id. Identical with Skt sphirá- ‘fat’, Slav *sporъ ‘plentiful’. Karsten NB I 50; Meillet Etudes 404; Torp-Falk 510; Lindqvist ANF XXV 244–245; Holthausen AEEW 308, AWN 273; Lewy IF XXXII 159; Mayrhofer III 541; Pokorny I 983; Vries ANEW 532; Onions 850; Vasmer-Trubachev III 738; Heidermanns 531–532 (derived from

ON spara ‘to spare’, OE sparian id., OFris sparia ‘to receive, to protect’, OS sparōn ‘to spare’, OHG sparēn id. Derived from *sparaz. Torp-Falk 510; Holthausen AEEW 308; Vries ANEW 532; Onions 850; Heidermanns 531–532; Kluge-Seebold 774.

OE spearca ‘spark’, MLG sparke id. Related to Skt sphūrjati ‘to break forth’, Av spar 6 ga- ‘sprout’, Gk σφαραγέομαι ‘to burst with a noise, to crackle’, Lith spragū, spragėti ‘to crack’. Bartholomae 1613; Torp-Falk 515 (to Latv spīrkts ‘hot coals under the ashes’); Holthausen AEEW 308; Walde- Hofmann II 566–567; Pokorny I 997; Frisk II 828; Onions 850.
ON sperra ‘to raise the spars in the house’, MDu sperren ‘to bar, to block’. Derived from *sparrōn. Torp-Falk 510; Vries ANEW 534.
ON sparri ‘spar, gag’, ME sparre ‘beam’, OS sparro id., OHG sparro id. Related to *speru. Fick I 149 (to *spurnanan); Torp-Falk 510; Pokorny I 990; Vries ANEW 532–533; Kluge- Seebold 774.
ON spǫrr-haukr ‘sparrow-hawk’, OE spear-hafoc id. Compound of *sparwaz ~ *sparwōn and *xabukaz. Carr Nom. comp. 55.

Goth sparwa ‘sparrow’, ON spǫrr id., OE spearwa id., OHG sparo, spar id. Related to other IE names of birds with minor morphonological discrepancies: Toch A ßpār ‘a kind of bird’, Gk σπαράσιον: ὄρνεον ἐμφερḫς στρουθ“ (Hes.), from *spar W–tjon (Schmidt KZ II 318: adds OPrus spurglis ‘sparrow’; Hoffmann BB XXI 140), W frau ‘crow’, Bret frāo id. < *sprawo-. Förstemann KZ III 51; Diefenbach VW II 295 (to Lith sparvā ‘gadfly’); Meyer Goth. (to Gk στρουθός ‘sparrow’); Brugmann IF XIII 160 (to *spurnanan); Pedersen Kelt. Gr. I 81; Torp-Falk 510; Holthausen AEEW 308–309; Feist 443; Pokorny I 991 (to Gk σπέργουλος ‘a kind of bird’); Vries ANEW 540; Zalizniak II 179; Onions 850; Lehmann GED 318–319; Kluge- Seebold 777; Adams TB 666.
ON spá ‘prophesy’, OHG speha ‘looking out’. See *spexōjanan ~ *spaxōjanan. Torp-Falk 506.

ON spá ‘to prophesy, to foretell’, MLG speen ‘to peer’, OHG spehōn to explore, to inquire’. Related to Toch AB päk- ‘to intend, to want’, Av spasyeiti ‘to see’, Gk σκέπτομαι id. < *spekjomai, Lat speciō id. Bartholomae 1614; Torp-Falk 506; Walde-Hofmann II 570–571; Pokorny I 984; Vries ANEW 531; Frisk II 725–726; Heidermanns 534–535; Kluge-Seebold 773; Adams TB 368–369.
ON spjald ‘square tablet’, OE speld ‘splinter, torch’. Related to *spalđanan. Torp-Falk 511; Holthausen AEEW 309; Pokorny I 985–987; Vries ANEW 536; Seebold 448; Lehmann GED 320; Kluge-Seebold 773.
Goth spilda ‘tablet’, ON flagspilda ‘slice, cut’. See *spelđan. Torp- Falk 511; Feist 445; Pokorny I 985–987; Seebold 448; Lehmann GED 320; Kluge-Seebold 773.

ON pl. spelkur ‘splint’, OE spelc, spilc id. Cf. also Du spalk ‘skewer’. Probably related to Lith spilgstū, spi÷lgti ‘to lose plant’, pa-spīlgęs ‘with weak stalks (of corn)’. Zupitza Gutt. 167; Torp-Falk 511; Holthausen AEEW 309; Pokorny I 536; Fraenkel 870, Vries ANEW 536.

Goth spill ‘myth, tale’, ON spjall ‘saying’, OE spell ‘story, narrative’, OS spel id., OHG spel id. Related to Arm afia-spel ‘fable’, Alb fjalë ‘word, speech, tale’ < *speli-. Froehde BB XIX 241– 243 (to Lat ap-pellō ‘to speak, to address’); Wood MLN XXVI 167 (to Gk ψελλός ‘faltering in speech’); Torp-Falk 511; Holthausen AEEW 309; Feist 445; Pokorny I 985; Vries ANEW 536; Zalizniak I 143; Onions 852; Meid Germ. Rek. 109 (from Celtic: OIr scél ‘report, account’ < *sk⁄etlom); Lehmann GED 320; Kluge-Seebold 94; Orel AED 98.

Goth spillon ‘to proclaim the gospel, to tell’, ON spjalla ‘to talk’, OE spellian ‘to talk, to announce’, MLG spellen ‘to talk’, MHG spellen ‘to tell, to talk’. Derived from *spellan. Torp- Falk 512; Holthausen AEEW 309; Feist 445; Vries ANEW 536; Onions 852; Lehmann GED 320.
Goth spilla ‘promulgator, teller’, ON spjalli ‘friend’, OHG wār-spello ‘fortune-teller, prophet’. Derived from *spellan. Feist 445; Vries ANEW 536.
ON spjall ‘mischief, damage, flaw’, OE spild ‘destruction, ruin’. Related to *spalđanan. Holthausen AEEW 310; Torp-Falk 511; Pokorny I 986; Vries ANEW 536; Heidermanns 535.

ON spilla ‘to spoil, to destroy’, OE spildan ‘to waste, to destroy’, OS spildian ‘to kill’, OHG spilden ‘to waste’. Derived from *spelþan ~ *spelþaz. Torp-Falk 511; Holthausen AEEW 310; Vries ANEW 535 (to *spelđan ~

Goth spinnan ‘to spin’, ON spinna id., OE spinnan id., OFris spinna id., MLG spinnen id., OHG spinnan id. Related to Toch A pänw-, B pänn- ‘to stretch, to reach for’ < *(s)pen-⁄-, Lith spéndžiu, spësti ‘spannen’ and further, perhaps, to Gk πένομαι ‘to work’, Lith pinū, pīnti ‘to weave’, Slav *pьnǫ, *p=ti ‘to stretch’. Brugmann IF I 173 (from *spen-⁄-ō); Pedersen KZ XXXIX 414; Persson Beiträge I 411–413; Torp-Falk 508; Lidén IF XIX 322–323; Trautmann BSW 219; Feist 445–446; Holthausen AEEW 310–311; Jóhannesson IEW 892; Pokorny I 988; Fraenkel 865–866; Vries ANEW 535; Frisk II 504–506; Onions 853–854; Seebold 452–453; Vasmer-Trubachev III 292; Lehmann GED 320 (reconstructs *(s)pen-d-); Kluge- Seebold 780 (to Lith spréndžiu, sprësti ‘to stretch’, Slav *pręsti ‘to spin’); Adams TB 370.
OSwed spinnil ‘spider’, OE spinel ‘spindle’, OS spinnila id., OHG spinnila, spinnil id. Derived from *spennanan. Torp-Falk 508.

ON spjǫr ‘spear’, OE spere id., OFris spere id., OS sper id., OHG sper id. Related to Lat sparus ‘short spear’. Torp-Falk 510; Holthausen AEEW 309; Walde-Hofmann II 568; Pokorny I 990–991; Chemodanov SGGJa I 87; Vries ANEW 536; Zalizniak II 225; Onions 851; Kluge-Seebold 775.

ON spjót ‘spear, lance’, OS evur-spiot ‘spear for hunting wild boars’, OHG spioz ‘spear’ (masc.). Related to Gk σπεύδω ‘to set going, to urge on’, Lith σπáυδἤιυ, spáusti ‘to press’ (Uhlenbeck PBB XXXV 178: or from *spreutaz ‘spear, pole’ > OE spreót, MDu spriet). Torp-Falk 514; Pokorny I 998; Vries ANEW 536 (to *speru); Kluge- Seebold 779 (< *spreutan).

Goth comp. spediza ‘later’, MLG spāde ‘late’, OHG spāti ‘related to evening, late’. Continues *spē-tí-, from IE *spē(i)-: Hitt išpai- ‘to eat to satiety’, Skt sphāyate ‘to grow fat, to increase’, Lith spėju, spėti ‘to get full, to be fast enough, to be able’, Slav *spîjǫ, *spîti ‘to succeed, to become ripe’. The strong verb is represented in OE spówan ‘to succeed’. Schulze KZ XXVII 426; Torp-Falk 505–506, 514; Persson Beiträge 400–402; Trautmann BSW 274–275; Forrer apud Feist 444 (to Hitt išpant- ‘night’); Feist 444; Holthausen AEEW 312; Mayrhofer III 541–542; Pokorny I 983–984; Fraenkel 866; Onions 852; Vries ANEW 531; Vasmer-Trubachev III 734; Tischler HEG I 408–409; Seebold 455; Lehmann GED 319; Puhvel II 429–431; Matzel KZ C 164–165, CV 132; Heidermanns 533–534; Kluge-Seebold 775, 785.
ON ó-spá-ligr ‘prophetic’, OS spā-līk ‘intelligent, wise’. Derived from *spēxaz. Heidermanns 534.

ON spár ‘prophetic’, OS spāh ‘intelligent, wise’, OHG spāhi id. Derived from *spexōjanan ~ *spaxōjanan. Karsten NB II 385; Torp-Falk 506; Kluge 681–682; Wissmann NP 41, 110; Pokorny I 984; Vries ANEW 532; Matzel Festschr. Schröder 103, KZ CV 114; Darms Vṛddhi 89; Heidermanns 534–535.
ON hug-spæi ‘prophecy’, OHG spāhī. ‘wisdom’ Derived from *spēxaz. Heidermanns 534.

ON spánn ‘chip, shaving’, spónn ‘spoon’, OE spón ‘chip’, OFris spān, spōn ‘flat golden breast ornament, splinter’, MLG spān, spōn ‘splinter’, OHG spān id. Probably connected with Skt sphyá- ‘shoulder-blade’, Gk σφήν ‘wedge’ (Kuhn KZ IV 15). Solmsen Beiträge 129 (to *spē- ‘to cut’); Torp-Falk 505; Holthausen AEEW 311; Mayrhofer III 547; Pokorny I 980; Vries ANEW 532; Zalizniak II 203; Frisk II 830–831; Onions 856; Kluge- Seebold 773.

Swed hack-spick ‘woodpecker’, OHG speh id. Probably related to Lat pīcus id., Skt piká- ‘Indian cuckoo’ (Fick I 148). Torp-Falk 513; Walde- Hofmann II 299–300; Mayrhofer I 268; Pokorny I 999; Kluge-Seebold 775.
ON spettr, spætr ‘woodpecker’, MLG specht id., OHG speht id. Derived from *spixaz. Zupitza Gutt. 210; Torp-Falk 513; Vries ANEW 534; Kluge-Seebold 775.

ON spik ‘blubber’, OE spic ‘bacon, lard’, OFris spek id., OS spekk id., OHG spec id. Identical with Skt nom. sg. sphik, dual sphijau ‘buttock, hip’. Zupitza Gutt. 167; Torp-Falk 512; Holthausen AEEW 310; Mayrhofer III 542–543; Pokorny I 983; Vries ANEW 534–535; Zalizniak II 225; Onions 851; Lehmann GED 319 (to *spēi-, see *spēđjaz); Kluge-Seebold 775 (to Lat pinguis ‘fat’).

ON spiki ‘a kind of bird, tit’, E spink id. Reminiscent of Gk σπίγγος ‘finch’, σπίζα id. (Bugge BB III 108). However, the latter is in an obvious connection with σπίζω ‘to twitter (of birds)’ and may also be related to σπιγνόν: μικρόν. Pokorny I 982; Vries ANEW 535 (to *spikan ~ *spikaz); Frisk II 767.

ON spil ‘tablet, narrow piece of wood’, MLG spīle ‘thin pointed stick’, MHG spīl ‘tip, point’. Related to Gk σπίλος ‘rock, cliff’, Latv spīle ‘wooden nail’. Torp-Falk 512; Holthausen AEEW 310; Pokorny I 981; Vries ANEW 535; Frisk II 767–768.
Norw spit ‘iron pole’, MLG spit ‘spear’, OHG spiz id. See *spitaz. Torp-Falk 513; Heidermanns 536.

Norw spit ‘point, nail’, MLG spit ‘spit’, OHG spiz id. Unclear. Cf. *spīkaz ~ *spīkō. Torp-Falk 513; Holthausen AEEW 311 (OE spitu ‘spit’ < *spitō); Pokorny I 981–982 (to Lat spīca ‘point, top’, spīna ‘thorn’); Onions 855; Lühr-Matzel KZ XCIX 264; Heidermanns 536.
ON spÿja ‘vomit’, OE spiwe id. (masc.), MLG spiē id., OHG spīwa id. Derived from *spīwanan. Holthausen AEEW 311; Vries ANEW 539.

ON spík ‘spike, sprig’, OE spíc ‘pointed piece of land’. Similar to but phonetically different from Lat spīcus ‘pin’. Holthausen AEEW 310; Pokorny I 981; Vries ANEW 535 (to Lith speigliaĩ ‘prickles’); Kluge-Seebold 776.

ON spíra ‘spar’, OE spír ‘spire’, MLG spīr ‘tip, point’, late MHG spīr ‘stalk, stem’. Related to Skt sphyá- ‘splinter’. Cf. in particular Osset æfsīr ~ æfseræ ‘ear of corn’ (Abaev I 483). Torp-Falk 512; Holthausen AEEW 311; Pokorny I 981; Vries ANEW 535–536; Onions 854; Kluge-Seebold 779.

Goth speiwan ‘to spit’, ON spÿja ‘to spew, to spit up’ (also wk.), OE spíwan id., OFris spīa id., OS pret. pl. spiwun id., OHG spīwan id. Related to Lat spuō ‘to spit’, Lith spiáunu, spiáuti id. and their irregular cognates (Gk πτύω id. and the like). Torp-Falk 513–514; Persson Beiträge 270; Trautmann BSW 276; Walde KZ XXXIV 479; Holthausen AEEW 311; Walde-Hofmann II 580– 581; Feist 444–445; Jóhannesson IEW 904–905; Pokorny I 999; Vries ANEW 539; Fraenkel 866–867; Frisk II 617–618; Onions 852; Seebold 450– 452; Lehmann GED 319–320; Kluge- Seebold 776.
Norw splint ‘wooden nail, wedge’, MLG splinte ‘splint’. Derived from *splītanan. Torp-Falk 518.
OFris splīta ‘to split’, MLG splīten id., MHG splīzen id. Probably related to OIr sliss ‘splinter’. Torp- Falk 518; Pokorny I 1000; Onions 855; Seebold 454–455; Kluge-Seebold 781.
Icel spóla ‘shuttle’, MLG spōle id., OHG spuola id. Related to *spaluz. Torp-Falk 511.
Norw dial. sprāla ‘to wriggle’, OHG spratalōn id. Of imitative origin. See *spratlōjan. Torp-Falk 516; Vries ANEW 537 (to *spurđz).
OSwed sprēda ‘to spread’, OE sprædan id., MLG sprēden id., OHG spreiten id. Causative of *sprīđanan. Torp-Falk 517; Holthausen AEEW 312; Vries ANEW 538; Kluge-Seebold 783.

ON sprengja ‘to make burst’, OE sprenzan ‘to scatter, to sprinkle’, OFris sprendza ‘to sprinkle’, MLG sprengen id., OHG sprengen ‘to drip, to pour’. Causative of *sprenzanan. Torp-Falk 516; Holthausen AEEW 312; Vries ANEW 538; Seebold 457; Kluge-Seebold 783.
ON spretta ‘to make spring up, to rip up’, ME sprenten ‘to send, to hurl’, OHG sprenzen ‘to support’. Causative of *sprentanan. Vries ANEW 538.
Norw dial. spratla ‘to flounder, to fidget’, OS spartalōn id., OHG sprazzalōn id. Of imitative origin. Torp-Falk 516.

OE sprecan ‘to speak’, OFris spreka id., OS sprekan, OHG sprehhan id. Related to W ffraeth ‘funny, odd’ (Fick I 149, 337) and further to Skt sphūrjati ‘to thunder, to rumble’, Gk σφαραγέομαι id., Lith spragū, spragėti ‘to splatter, to bicker’. Bezzenberger BB XVII 214; Persson Wurzelerw. 17, Beiträge 417–418; Zupitza Gutt. 28, 166; Torp- Falk 515; Holthausen AEEW 313; Mayrhofer III 545–546; Pokorny I 996–998; Chemodanov SGGJa I 100; Vries ANEW 537 (on ON spraka ‘to crackle’ < *sprakōjanan); Frisk II 828; Onions 851; Seebold 455–457; Kluge- Seebold 782.
Norw dial. sprekla ‘spot on the skin’ (fem.), MHG spreckel id. Derived from *sprekan ~ *sprakan. Torp- Falk 515 (to Slav *pręga ‘buckle’).

ON sprek ‘stick’, OE spræc ‘shoot’. Related to Gk ἀσπάραγος ‘asparagus’, Lith spūrgas ‘knot on a tree’. Zupitza Gutt. 166; Torp- Falk 515; Trautmann BSW 277; Holthausen AEEW 312; Pokorny I 997; Fraenkel 885–886; Vries ANEW 538; Frisk I 175.

ON springa ‘to burst, to split’, OE sprinzan ‘to spring’, OFris springa id., OS pret. sg. sprang id., OHG springan ‘to jump up’. Identical with Lith sprengiū, spreñgti ‘to push in’, Slav *pręgti ‘to spin, to stretch’. Torp-Falk 516; Holthausen AEEW 313; Jóhannesson IEW 899–900; Pokorny I 992, 998; Vries ANEW 538; Fraenkel 879–880; Onions 857–858; Stang LS 43; Seebold 457–458 (to Gk σπέρχω ‘to set in rapid motion, to haste’); Vasmer-Trubachev III 393; Kluge-Seebold 783.

ON af-springr ‘progeny, offspring’, OE sprinz ‘source of water, springing, rising’, OFris spedel-spring ‘flow of spittle’, OS aha-spring ‘source’, OHG ur-spring id. Derived from *sprenzanan. Seebold 457.

ON spretta ‘to spurt out, to start, to spring’, OE ze-sprant ‘to articulate, to pronounce’, MHG sprinzen ‘to break forth, to shine’. Related to Slav *prędati ‘to spring, to throw’ (Schmidt Voc. II 231). On the other hand, cf. *sprenzanan. Torp-Falk 516– 517; Trautmann KZ L 66, BSW 278; Persson Beiträge 873; Holthausen AEEW 313; Jóhannesson IEW 900–901; Pokorny I 995–996; Vries ANEW 538 (to *sparrōn); Seebold 458–459.

OE á-spreótan ‘to sprout’, sprútan id., OFris sprūta id., OS pres. ūt-sprūtan ‘to pop out’, OHG spriuzen. Related to Lith sprūstu, sprūsti ‘to slip out, to flee’, spráudžiu, spráusti ‘to press, to squeeze’. Persson Wurzelerw. 17; Torp-Falk 517–518; Holthausen AEEW 314; Pokorny I 886, 993–995; Fraenkel 878–879, 883– 884; Onions 858; Seebold 459–460; Lehmann GED 320–321; Kluge-Seebold 783.
ON sprækr ‘sprouting, sprightly’, OE ze-spræce ‘eloquent, affable’, OHG wāh-sprāhhi id. Derived from *sprekanan. Torp-Falk 516; Holthausen AEEW 312; Vries ANEW 539.
OHG sprītan ‘to extend’. Related to OIr srédim ‘to throw’. Torp-Falk 517; Holthausen AEEW 312; Pokorny I 994; Onions 857; Heidermanns 137; Kluge-Seebold 783.
ON sprunga ‘crack in the skin, scratch’, MLG sprunk ‘jump, bound’, OHG sprung id. Derived from *sprenzanan. Holthausen AEEW 314; Vries ANEW 539.

ON sproti ‘sprout, twig’, OE sprota ‘sprout’, MLG sprote id., OHG sprozzo id. Derived from *spreutanan ~ *sprūtanan. Torp-Falk 518; Holthausen AEEW 313; Pokorny I 994 (to W ffrwst ‘haste’, Lith spriáusti ‘to squeeze in’); Vries ANEW 538; Zalizniak I 154; Onions 857; Seebold 459; Kluge- Seebold 784.
ON spor-hundr ‘bloodhound’, OS spuri-hunt id., OHG spuri-hunt id. Compound of *spuran and *xunđaz. Carr Nom. comp. 84 (parallel formations).

ON spor ‘track’, OE spor id., MLG spor id., OHG spor id. Related to (or derived from?) *spurnanan. Torp- Falk 509; Holthausen AEEW 312; Pokorny I 993; Vries ANEW 537; Zalizniak I 143; Seebold 454; Bammesberger Nom. 58; Kluge-Seebold 784.

ON sporär ‘fish tail’, MHG sporte id. Related to Lith spūrū, spūrti ‘to be fringy’, spūrdu, spurdėti ‘to flounder, to fidget, to struggle’, Latv spurstu, spurt ‘to rove’. Torp-Falk 509; Pokorny I 995; Dybo VSJa 33; Fraenkel 885–886; Vries ANEW 537 (to

Goth spaurds ‘race-track, stadium course’, OE spyrd ‘race-track, course’, OHG spurt ‘stadium course’ (istem). Identical with Skt spṛdh- ‘battle’, Av sp 6 r 6 d- ‘zeal’ derived from *sperdh-: Skt spárdhate ‘to rival, to contend’. Grimm DG II 57 (to Skt sphuráti ‘to bound, to spurn, to dart’); Grienberger Got. 196; Bartholomae 1623; Torp-Falk 514– 515; Holthausen AEEW 314; Feist 443–444; Mayrhofer III 537; Pokorny I 995–996; Lehmann GED 319; Bammesberger Nom. 197; Griepentrog Wurzelnomina 366–378.

ON spyrja ‘to track, to ask’, OE spyrian ‘to track, to inquire’, OFris spera ‘to ask’, OHG gi-spurren ‘to trace, to find’. Derived from *spuran. Torp-Falk 509; Holthausen AEEW 314; Vries ANEW 539; Onions 852; Kluge-Seebold 784.

OE spornan ‘to strike with the foot’, OFris spurna id. (may be wk.), OS pres. bi-spurnan ‘to trample’, OHG fir-spurnan ‘to push, to move’. Related to Lat spernō ‘to scorn’ and further to Skt sphuráti ‘to bound, to spurt, to dart’, Gk ἀσπαίρω ‘to pant, to struggle’, Lith spiriū, spīrti ‘to defy, to affront’. Probably based on an original present in *-n-ō. Torp-Falk 509; Reichelt KZ XXXIX 56; Persson Beiträge 167, 417; Holthausen AEEW 312; Walde- Hofmann II 572–573; Jóhannesson IEW 894–895; Mayrhofer III 544; Pokorny I 992–993; Fraenkel 873–874; Vries ANEW 534; Frisk I 166–167; Onions 858; Seebold 453–454; Kluge-Seebold 782.
ON spyrna ‘to spurn’, OE spyrnan ‘to step, to trample’, OHG spurnen ‘to trample’. Derived from *spurnanan. Torp-Falk 509–510; Holthausen AEEW 314; Vries ANEW 534, 539.
ON sporna ‘to spurn, to kick’, OHG spornōn ‘to spurn’. Derived from *spurnanan. Vries ANEW 537.
ON spora ‘to spurn, to kick’, MDu sporen ‘to follow a trace’. Derived from *spuran. Mayrhofer III 544; Pokorny I 993; Fraenkel 873–874; Vries ANEW 537.
ON spori ‘spur’, OE spora id., MLG spore id., OHG sporo id. Related to *spuran. Torp-Falk 509; Holthausen AEEW 312; Pokorny I 993; Vries ANEW 537; Zalizniak I 155; Onions 858; Kluge-Seebold 782.
Norw spytta ‘to spit’, spütten id., G dial. spützen id. Derived from *sputtōn ~ *sputtan. Torp-Falk 513; Holthausen AEEW 311; Vries ANEW 539; Lehmann GED 320 (to *spīwanan).

ON spotti ‘bit, small piece’, EFris spot ‘spot’, MDu spot id. Related to *spīwanan. Goes back to part. *sp(ƒ)utom, cf. Lat spūtum. Torp-Falk 513–514; Walde-Hofmann II 4; Walde-Hofmann II 580–581; Pokorny I 999; Vries ANEW 537 (to
ON spott ‘mock, scoff’, OFris spot id., OS spott id. (masc.), OHG spot id. (masc.). Probably related to *sputtōn ~ *sputtan. Torp-Falk 514; Vries ANEW 537; Kluge-Seebold 782.
ON spotta ‘to mock’, MLG spotten id., OHG spottōn id. Derived from *spuþþan ‘mockery’. Torp-Falk 514; Vries ANEW 537.
ON spÿta ‘to spit’, ME spúten id., Du spuiten ‘to spatter, to splash’, MHG spūtzen ‘to spit’. A variant of *sputtjanan. Torp-Falk 513; Vries ANEW 539; Lehmann GED 320.

Swed dial. staver ‘pole in the fence’. Derived from *stabiz ~ *stabaz. Structurally identical with Lith stãbaras ‘dry branch’, Slav *stoborъ ‘column-like pole’. Torp-Falk 483; Trautmann BSW 280; Pokorny I 1013; Fraenkel 890–891; Stang LS 54.

Goth stafs ‘element, στοιχεῖον’, ON stafr ‘staff, post, stave, letter’, OE stæf ‘staff, stick, character’, OFris stef ‘staff’, OS staf id., OHG stab ‘staff, cane, prop, rod’. Close to MIr sab ‘shaft’ < *st 6 bhā, Lith stãbas ‘post’. Further connected with Skt stabhnāti ‘to fix, to support’. Kuhn KZ I 139; Krogmann IF LVIII 268–272; Torp- Falk 483; Lindquist SNF IX/1 13; Trautmann BSW 280; Būga RFV LXXV 147; Holthausen AEEW 314; Feist 446; Mayrhofer III 511; Pokorny I 1012– 1013; Fraenkel 891; Vries ANEW 541; Zalizniak II 189; Onions 860–861; Stang LS 54; Vasmer-Trubachev III 762–763; Gutenbrunner Zd Ph LXXXVI 325–352 (to *stebnō ~ *stemnō); Lehmann GED 321; Kluge-Seebold 785.
ON stefna ‘appointed meeting, date, term’, OE stefn, stemn ‘turn, time’ (masc.), MLG stevene ‘arrival day’. Derived from *stabiz ~ *stabaz. Torp- Falk 483.

ON stafa ‘to make staves, to put letters together’, OE stafian ‘to direct, to dictate’, OFris stavia ‘to make staves, to put letters together’, MLG staven id., OHG stabōn ‘to give an oath at court’. Derived from *stabiz ~ *stabaz. Holthausen AEEW 316; Vries ANEW 540.

ON staär ‘restive (of a horse)’. Identical with Skt sthitá- ‘standing’, Gk στατός, Lat status. See *stađiz. Karsten NB II 302; Torp-Falk 478; Walde-Hofmann II 596–598; Mayrhofer III 526–527; Pokorny I 1006; Vries ANEW 540; Frisk I 739; Heidermanns 540–541.
ON staä-fastr ‘steadfast’, OE stede-fæst id., Fris steed-fest, MLG stede-vast id. Compound of *stađiz and *fastaz. Carr Nom. comp. 61; Onions 865–866.
ON stǫäugr ‘steady, stable’, MLG stedich id., OHG stetīg id. Derived from *stađaz or *stađiz. Vries ANEW 559. *stađizōjanan ~
OSwed staþga ‘to determine’, MLG stadigen id., G be-stätigen ‘to confirm’. Derived from *stađizaz ~ *stađuzaz. Vries ANEW 540.
ON staä-lauss ‘timid, unsteady’, OE stede-leás ‘unsteady’, MHG stæte-lōs id. Compound of *stađiz and *lausaz. Carr Nom. comp. 61.

Goth staþs ‘place’, ON staär ‘place, abode’, OE stede ‘place, position, state’, OFris sted ‘place, town’, OS stedi id., OHG stat fem. ‘place’. Related to *stanđanan. Together with Skt sthíti- ‘location, rank’, Gk στάσις ‘station’, Lat statim ‘standing firmly’ continues IE *st 6 tis. Schweizer KZ I 150; Holthausen AEEW 318; Walde-Hofmann II 596– 598; Feist 451; Mayrhofer III 526; Pokorny I 1004–1005; Vries ANEW 540; Zalizniak II 177; Frisk I 739; Onions 865; Seebold 461; Lehmann GED 323–324; Bammesberger Nom. 135; Kluge-Seebold 785–786.
ON stǫä ‘berth, harbor’, OFris stathe ‘place’, MLG stade ‘opportunity, help’, OHG stata ‘possibility’. Derived from *stanđanan. Torp-Falk 478; Vries ANEW 540; Onions 865; Seebold 461.

ON stag ‘mast-rope’, OE stæz ‘rope supporting a mast’, MLG stach ‘mast-rope’. Related to Skt stákati ‘to resist, to strive again’. On the other hand, cf. Hitt ištagga(i)- ‘bowstring’. Torp- Falk 480; Holthausen AEEW 315; Mayrhofer III 509–510; Pokorny I 1011; Vries ANEW 541–542; Onions 865; Puhvel II 451.
ON steggi ‘male bird’, OE stacza ‘stag’. Probably related to *stazan. Pokorny I 1014 (to Gk στόχος ‘pillar (of brick)’); Vries ANEW 545; Onions 861.

ON stál ‘steel’ (< WGmc?), MLG stāl id., OHG stahal id. Cf. also OE stíle, OS stehli < *staxljan. Identical with Av staxra- ‘strong, hard’, further related to Skt stákati ‘to resist, to strive again’, Umbr stakaz ‘statūtus’. Zupitza Gutt. 139; Bartholomae 1591; Torp-Falk 480; Holthausen AEEW 321; Mayrhofer III 509–510; Pokorny I 1011; Zalizniak II 218; Onions 866; Kluge- Seebold 786.
ON stæla ‘to steel’, OE stílan id., MLG stēlen id., MHG stahelen id. Derived from *staxlan. Holthausen AEEW 321; Vries ANEW 558.

Goth staiga ‘path, way’, ON steig- (in pln.), OHG steiga ‘steep road’. Close to Gk στοῖχος ‘row, line’, στίχος id., Alb shteg ‘path, way’, Lith adv. staigā ‘sudden’, Latv staiga ‘alley’, Slav *stьza id. (Fraenkel Wu S XII 191–192). For further connections see *stīzanan. Torp- Falk 492; Trautmann BSW 285; Feist 447; Pokorny I 1017–1018; Fraenkel 892; Zalizniak II 204; Frisk II 784; Seebold 466; Vasmer-Trubachev III 752; Lehmann GED 321; Bammesberger Nom. 107; Kluge-Seebold 791; Orel AED 437.
ON adv. steigur-liga ‘prudishly’, OE stæzer ‘steep, upright’, MLG stēger id. Derived from *stīzanan. Torp- Falk 492; Vries ANEW 545; Heidermanns 541.
ON stein-bogi ‘stone arch’, OE stán-boza id. Compound of *stainaz and *buzōn. Carr Nom. comp. 55.
ON stein-borg ‘stone castle’, OE stán-burz ‘town built with stone’. Compound of *stainaz and *burzz. Carr Nom. comp. 82 (parallel formations).
Goth stainahs ‘stony’, OE stániz id., OHG steinag id. OHG rvn. Steinaha can hardly be connected as it goes back to *staina-axwō. Derived from *stainaz. Feist 447; Lehmann GED 321.
ON stein-hús ‘stone house’, OFris stēn-hūs id., MLG stēn-hūs id., MHG stein-hūs id. Compound of *stainaz and *xūsan. Carr Nom. comp. 78 (parallel formations).
ON stein-ofn ‘stone oven’, OHG stein-ovan ‘stone pot’. Compound of *stainaz and *ufnaz. Carr Nom. comp. 85 (parallel formations).

Goth stains ‘stone’, ON steinn id., OE stán id., OFris stēn id., OS stēn id., OHG stein id. Related to Slav *stîna ‘(stone) wall’ (Meillet Etudes 446; Schulze KZ XXVII 427) and, further, to IE *stāi- ‘to thicken’: Skt styāyate ‘to coagulate, to harden’. Schrader-Nehring II 50 (Slav < Gmc); Hirt PBB XXIII 336 (same as Schrader-Nehring); Torp- Falk 490; Holthausen AEEW 316–317, AWN 280; Feist 447–448; Pokorny I 1011; Vries ANEW 546; Zalizniak I 135; Onions 871–872; Stang LS 55; Vasmer-Trubachev III 754; Lehmann GED 321–322; Kluge-Seebold 791–792.
ON steina ‘to stain, to paint’, OE stænan ‘to stone’, MLG stēnen id., OHG steinōn id. Derived from *stainaz. Holthausen AEEW 315; Lehmann GED 321.
ON stakr ‘single, odd’, MLG stak ‘stiff, immobilized by an injury’. Related to *stakōn. Karsten NB II 443; Lühr Expr. 228; Heidermanns 542.
ON stekkr ‘enclosure (for sheep)’, EFris stek, stekke ‘enclosure’. Derived from *stakōn. Vries ANEW 546.
ON stakkr ‘short blouse’, OFris stack ‘small coat’. See *stakōn. Pokorny I 1011; Vries ANEW 542; Heidermanns 542– 543 (to Lith stokā ‘shortage, lack’).
ON staka ‘to punt, to push’, MLG staken ‘to make a palisade’. Derived from *stakōn. Vries ANEW 542.

ON hvat-staki ‘a kind of weapon’, OE staca ‘stake’, MLG stake ‘pole’, MHG stache id. Probably related to Lith stãgaras ‘dry stalk’, Slav *stožerъ ‘stack-pole’, *stogъ ‘stack, rick’ (Zupitza Gutt. 167–168). Derived from *stekanan. Torp-Falk 479; Trautmann BSW 288; Feist 448 (on Goth staks ‘mark, stigma’ < *stakiz); Holthausen AEEW 314, AWN 278; Pokorny I 1014; Chemodanov SGGJa I 80; Vries ANEW 542; Fraenkel 891–892; Onions 861 (to *stek(k)ōn); Stang LS 54; Seebold 468; Vasmer- Trubachev III 763; Lehmann GED 322 (to Lat tīgnum ‘beam’); Heidermanns 542.
ON stakra ‘to push, to stagger’, E dial. stacker ‘to reel, to sway’, LG stackern ‘to walk ponderously’. An emphatic derivative of *stakōjanan. Vries ANEW 542.
Goth and-stald ‘purveyance, supply’, OE ze-steald ‘settled place’. Continues *stol-tó-, cf. *stallaz (Lehmann Language XVIII 129). Torp-Falk 489; Holthausen AEEW 317; Feist 50; Seebold 462.
Goth and-staldan ‘to purvey, to supply’, OE pret. sg. steóld ‘to possess’. See *stalđan. Torp-Falk 489; Holthausen AEEW 317; Feist 50; Pokorny I 1019–1020; Seebold 461– 462; Lehmann GED 37.

ON stallr ‘shelf, pedestal, stall’, OE steall ‘position, state, place’, OFris stall ‘position, standpoint’, MLG stal id., OHG stal ‘position, standing’. Related to Skt sthala- ‘elevation, dry land, firm earth’, Gk στόλος ‘equipment, journey, armament’ based on *stel-: Skt sthálati ‘to stand’, Gk στέλλω ‘to set, to put in order’. Sievers IF IV 337–338 (from *st 6 tló-); Torp-Falk 487; Holthausen AEEW 318; Mayrhofer III 525; Pokorny I 1019; Vries ANEW 542; Zalizniak I 135; Frisk II 786–788; Onions 862 (from *stađlaz); Lehmann GED 327; Heidermanns 543; Kluge- Seebold 786.
ON stalli ‘altar’, OE zestealla ‘companion’, OHG gi-stallo ‘helper’. Derived from *stallaz. Vries ANEW 542.
Norw dial. stelpa ‘to check, to balk’, Du stelpen ‘to stanch, to assuage’. Related to *stulpōn. Torp- Falk 489; Vries ANEW 546.

Goth stamms ‘stammering’, ON stamr id., OE stam id., OHG stam id. A descriptive stem similar to Latv stuomîtiês ‘to stutter, to stammer’. Karsten NB I 51–52, II 230–231; Grienberger Goth. 198 (to IE *stā- ‘to stand, to put’); Torp-Falk 483; Feist 449; Holthausen AEEW 316; Wissmann KZ LXII 141–142; Martinet Gém. 175; Pokorny I 1021; Vries ANEW 543; Onions 862; Lehmann GED 322–323; Heidermanns 544–545; Kluge-Seebold 787.
ON stamma ‘to stammer’, OHG stammēn id. Derived from *stam(m)az. Torp-Falk 483.

ON stemma ‘to stem, to stop’, OE for-stemman ‘to prevent, to hinder’, OS stemmian ‘to lift’, OHG stemmen id. Probably derived from *stam(m)az. Torp-Falk 483–484; Holthausen AEEW 319; Pokorny I 1021; Vries ANEW 546; Onions 867; Lehmann GED 322; Heidermanns 545; Kluge- Seebold 792.

ON stafn ‘stem (of a ship)’, OE stefna ‘prow, stern’ (n-stem), OFris stevene id. (fem.), OS stamn id., MLG stam id., OHG stam ‘stem’. Identical with Gk στάμνος ‘(earthen) jar’ based on *στᾶμα or *σταμήν close to Toch A ἑτāμ, B stām ‘tree’ < *stam–, OIr taman ‘tree trunk’. Torp NNEO 705 (separates *stabnaz and *stamnaz); Torp- Falk 484; Holthausen AEEW 318; Pokorny I 1013; Vendryes T-25 (differently on OIr taman); Vries ANEW 540– 541; Frisk II 777; Onions 867; Lehmann GED 327; Kluge-Seebold 786–787, 794; Adams TB 708–709; Burlak IFTJa 259– 260.

ON stefna ‘to stand in a certain direction’, OE stefnan ‘to regulate, to direct’, LG stevenen ‘to steer’. Derived from *stamnaz ~ *stamniz. Holthausen AEEW 319; Vries ANEW 545 (sees two separate words here).

OS stamp ‘pestle, mortar’, OHG stampf id. Identical with Lith sta Mbas ‘cabbage-stalk, stock’. Denominative related to Gk στέμβω ‘to shake about, to agitate’, Lith stembstū, ste Mbti ‘to stem’. Torp-Falk 484; Trautmann BSW 284; Holthausen AEEW 316; Pokorny I 1011; Fraenkel 895; Vries ANEW 543; Frisk II 788; Onions 862 (nasalized cognate of *stapiz).

ON stappa ‘to stamp’, MLG stampen ‘to tap, to pound’, OHG stampfōn id. Derived from *stampaz. Kuhn KZ I 139; Zupitza Gutt. 28; Torp- Falk 484; Porzig Gliederung 137; Vries ANEW 543; Onions 862; Seebold 463; Kluge-Seebold 787.
Norw dial. stamra ‘to stammer’, OE stamerian id., OS stamurōn id. Derived from *stam(m)az. Holthausen AEEW 316; Vries ANEW 556.

Goth standan ‘to stand’, ON standa id., OE standan id., OFris stonda id., OS standan id., OHG stantan id. Based on a secondarily built nasal present. Derived from *stēnan. Torp-Falk 477; Brugmann IF XV 126–128; Sturtevant GR VII 467–476, Language IV 3 (after Hrozny, to Hitt ištandāi- ‘to hesitate, to delay’); Feist 449–450; Holthausen AEEW 317; Kluge 743–744; Jóhannesson IEW 854– 858; Pokorny I 1004–1005; Kammenhuber KZ LXXVII 32–35 (follows Sturtevant); Vries ANEW 543; Onions 862–863; Seebold 460–461; Peeters IF LXXXI 33–35; Lehmann GED 323; Kluge-Seebold 787.

ON stǫng ‘pole’, OS stanga id., OHG stanga id. Derived from *stenzanan. Torp-Falk 482; Pokorny I 1015; Vries ANEW 559; Zalizniak II 210; Onions 863; Seebold 470; Lehmann GED 383; Lühr Expr. 470–471; Bammesberger Nom. 107; Kluge-Seebold 787.
ON stǫkkr ‘stir, disturbance’, OS stank ‘scent’, OHG stanc id. Derived from *stenkwanan II. Vries ANEW 559; Seebold 471; Lehmann GED 325.

Goth ga-stagqjan ‘to strike against’, ON stǫkkva ‘to cause to spring, to make start’, OE stencan ‘to scatter’. Causative of *stenkwanan I. Torp- Falk 481; Holthausen AEEW 319; Feist 201; Vries ANEW 558; Seebold 471; Lehmann GED 325.

OE stepe, stæpe ‘step’, OFris stap id., OHG stapf id. Despite the difference in consonantism, related to Slav *stopa ‘sole, foot’. Torp-Falk 482; Holthausen AEEW 320; Pokorny I 1011–1012; Onions 867; Seebold 462; Vasmer-Trubachev III 766–767.
ON stapi ‘steeple-formed single rock’, OE stapa ‘one who steps’. See *stapiz. Torp-Falk 482; Holthausen AEEW 317; Vries ANEW 543.
OE stæppan ‘to step’, OFris stapa, steppa ‘to stride, to walk’, OS pret. sg. stōp ‘to step’. See *stapiz. Torp-Falk 482; Holthausen AEEW 320; Onions 867; Seebold 462–463.

ON stǫpull ‘tower, steeple’, OE stapol ‘post, pillar’, OFris stapul ‘rung anvil, crown of a tooth’, MLG stapel ‘pillar, steeple’, OHG stapfal ‘sockle, footstand’. Derived from *stapōn. Alternatively, might reflect IE *steb-, an irregular variant of a better attested *stebh-: Skt stabhnāti ‘to fix, to support’, Lith stãbas ‘post’ and the like. See *stabiz ~ *stabaz. Kuhn KZ I 139; Torp-Falk 482; Holthausen AEEW 317; Mayrhofer III 511; Pokorny I 1011–1012; Fraenkel 891; Vries ANEW 559; Zalizniak I 135; Onions 863; Kluge- Seebold 788.

OESc star-blinder ‘quite blind’, OE stær-blind id., OFris stareblind id., MLG star-blint id., OHG stara-blint id. Derived from *starēnan and *blenđaz. Holthausen AEEW 315; Heidermanns 545–546; Kluge-Seebold 788.

ON stari, starri ‘blackbird, starling’, OE stær ‘starling’, MLG star id., OHG staro, star id. For IE parallels see *sternaz. Torp-Falk 486; Holthausen AEEW 315; Pokorny I 1036; Vries ANEW 544; Zalizniak II 179; Onions 864; Kluge-Seebold 788.

ON stara ‘to stare, to gaze’, OE starian id., OHG starēn, starōn id. Related to *starraz. Torp-Falk 485– 486; Feist 50–51; Holthausen AEEW 315; Pokorny I 1022; Vries ANEW 543; Onions 864; Heidermanns 546.

ON sterkr ‘strong’, OE stearc ‘stiff, hard, strong’, OFris stark ‘strong’, OS stark id., OHG starc, starah id. Cf. also Goth *starks in prop. Starcedius. Derived from *sterkanan. Kirchhoff KZ I 39; Karsten NB II 376–377; Torp-Falk 486–487; Feist 201–202; Holthausen AEEW 318; Pokorny I 1023; Vries ANEW 546; Onions 864; Lehmann GED 149; Heidermanns 546–547; Kluge-Seebold 788.

ON starr ‘blunt’, MLG star ‘rigid, repelling’, MHG sterre ‘rigid, stiff’. Related to Toch B ścīre ‘hard, harsh’, Gk στερεός ‘stiff, hard’, Lith stęrū, stérti ‘to congeal, to petrify’, and probably Slav *starъ ‘old’. Karsten NB II 258; Brugmann IF XXXIII 302–303 (on gemination); Meillet Etudes 404; Torp- Falk 485–486; Trautmann BSW 282; Pokorny I 1022; Fraenkel 902–903; Vries ANEW 544; Frisk II 790–791; Vasmer-Trubachev III 747; Heidermanns 547; Adams TB 639–640.
Norw dial. starra, sterra ‘to ruffle, to bristle’, MLG starren ‘to goggle’, MHG starren ‘to be stiff’. See *starēnan. Torp-Falk 486; Vries ANEW 544.
ON sterta ‘to crease, to pleat’, ME sterten ‘to drive up, to start’, MHG sterzen ‘to stalk, to grow stiff’. Related to *stertaz II. Torp-Falk 487; Pokorny I 1023.

Goth dat. sg. staþa ‘shore’, Norw dial. stad ‘bank’, OE stæä ‘shore’, OFris sted id., OS stath id., OHG stad id. Continues IE *st 6-to-, similar to other derivatives of *stā- ‘to stand’, cf. Lat status, part. of sistō id., Lith statūs ‘standing, still, straight’, stãtas ‘row’. See *stađaz, *stanđanan, *stēnan. Torp- Falk 477; Holthausen AEEW 316; Walde-Hofmann II 596–599; Feist 450; Pokorny I 1006; Fraenkel 897–898; Zalizniak II 183; Onions 861; Seebold 460; Lehmann GED 324.

ON steäja ‘to stop’, OE stæääan ‘to make staid, to stay’, MLG steden ‘to establish’. Identical with Lith statÿti ‘to put’ (Bugge BB III 120). Further see *stanđanan. Holthausen AEEW 316; Pokorny I 1006; Fraenkel 897–898; Vries ANEW 545; Seebold 461.
Goth lukarna-staþa ‘candlestick’, ON staäi ‘stack, rick’, MLG stade ‘place where wheat is stored’. Derived from *stēnan, *stanđanan. Torp-Falk 478.

ON stǫäull ‘milkingshed’, stál ‘stack’, OE staäol ‘foundation’, OFris stathul ‘barn’, OS staäal id., OHG stadal id. Derivative in *-tlo- of IE *stā- ‘to stand’, cf. Lat ob-staculum ‘hinderance, obstacle’, Lith stãklës ‘loom’. See *stanđanan, *stēnan. Torp-Falk 478; Trautmann BSW 283; Holthausen AEEW 317; Pokorny I 1007; Fraenkel 893; Vries ANEW 542, 559; Onions 860.

ON staup ‘knobby lump, cup, beaker’, OE steáp ‘cup, drinking vessel’, MLG stōp id., OHG stouf id. Substantivized *staupaz. Zupitza Gutt. 28; Torp-Falk 497; Holthausen AEEW 318; Pokorny I 1034; Zalizniak II 172; Vries ANEW 544; Onions 872; Kluge-Seebold 790.
OE steáp ‘high, lofty’, OFris stāp id. Connected with *stūpanan. Torp- Falk 496–497; Holthausen AEEW 318; Pokorny I 1034; Onions 866; Seebold 474.

ON steypa ‘to cast down, to overthrow’, OE stípan ‘to raise, to elevate’, OFris stēpa ‘to help, to contribute’. Factitive of *stūpanan. Torp- Falk 496; Holthausen AEEW 321; Vries ANEW 547; Onions 866.

ON staurr ‘pale, stake’. Identical with Gk σταυρός id. Cf. also a denominative Lat īn-staurō ‘to restore’ (Curtius Gr. Et. 212). For further connections see *steurō ~ *steur(j)an. Torp- Falk 493; Walde-Hofmann I 705–706; Pokorny I 1009; Vries ANEW 544; Frisk II 778; Lehmann GED 326.

Goth stautan ‘to strike, to hit’, OFris stēta ‘to punch, to kick’, OS stōtan ‘to strike’, OHG stōzan id. Probably connected with Lat studeō ‘to strive, to study’, Alb shtyj ~ sht#j ‘to thrust’ < *stūdnƒō. Torp-Falk 495; Walde- Hofmann II 608; Feist 451; Pokorny I 1032–1034; Seebold 3–464; Lehmann GED 324; Orel AED 444; Kluge- Seebold 799.
ON steytr ‘capsize’, OFris stēt ‘thrust’, MLG stōt ‘blow’, OHG stōz id. Derived from *stautanan. Torp-Falk 495; Vries ANEW 547; Seebold 463; Lehmann GED 324; Bammesberger Nom. 133.
ON stauta ‘to beat, to strike’, OHG stōzōn ‘to goad’. Cf. also ON steyta ‘to push’ < *stautjanan. Derived from *stautanan. Jóhannesson IEW 862– 866; Vries ANEW 544, 547; Seebold 463; Lehmann GED 324.

ON stefna ‘to give notice, to summon’, OE stefnian ‘to summon, to cite’, MLG stevenen ‘to summon’. Derived from *stebnō ~ *stemnō. Reflexes of this verb were influenced by those of *stamnjanan. Holthausen AEEW 319; Vries ANEW 545.

Goth stibna ‘voice’, OE stefn, stemn id., OFris stifne, stemme id., OS stemna id., OHG stimna, stimma id. Of unknown origin. Noreen Abriß 140 (to Av staman- ‘muzzle (of a dog)’, Gk στόμα ‘mouth’, W safn ‘jaw’); Torp-Falk 484 (against Noreen); Petersson IF XX 368, XXIII 386– 387; Falk-Torp ND 1197 (to *stabaz); Schmidt Kritik 133 (against Noreen); Grienberger Got. 199 (to Gk στένω ‘to sigh, to moan’); Pedersen Kelt. Gr. I 78; Sturtevant Language IV 123 (to Hitt ištaman- ‘ear’); Holthausen AEEW 319; Feist 452; Zalizniak II 181; Onions 868; Wennerberg Sprache XVIII 28–29 (follows Noreen); Lehmann GED 325; Kluge-Seebold 796–797.

OFris steka ‘to prick, to sting’, OS stekan id., OHG stehhan id. An alternative reconstruction is *stikanan. The latter may be related to Gk στίζω ‘to stick’, Lat īnstīgō ‘to sting, to prick’. Zupitza Gutt. 168; Torp-Falk 480; Walde-Hofmann I 706–707; Pokorny I 1016–1017; Vries ANEW 549; Frisk II 797–798; Seebold 467–468; Heidermanns 542; Kluge-Seebold 790.

ON stjaki ‘punt-pole, stake’, stikki ‘short measured poem’, OE sticca ‘stick’, OFris stekk id., OS stekko id., OHG stehho ‘pole’. Ablaut variant of *stakōn. However, an alternative reconstruction and etymology cannot be excluded: *stik(k)ōn to Gk στίζω ‘to stick’, Lat īnstīgō ‘to urge, to stimulate’. Torp- Falk 491; Holthausen AEEW 320; Walde-Hofmann I 706; Pokorny I 1016–1017; Vries ANEW 549; Zalizniak II 225; Frisk II 797–798; Onions 869; Heidermanns 542; Kluge-Seebold 790 (to Lith stãgaras ‘stock’).
ON stjaka ‘to punt’, MLG steken ‘to prick, to sting’, OHG stehhōn id. Probably related to *stek(k)ōn. Vries ANEW 549.

Goth stilan ‘to steal’, ON stela id., OE stelan id., OFris stela id., OS stelan id., OHG stelan id. Probably, related to IE *stel(H)- ‘to stretch’, hence, ‘to cover’ > ‘to steal’. Prellwitz KZ XLII 88–90 (to Lat stēlliō ‘newt, stellion, lizard with spotted back’); Osthoff PBB XIII 460–461 (from *steranan influenced by *xelanan); Torp-Falk 489; Feist 453–454 (non-IE); Holthausen AEEW 319; Jóhannesson IEW 878; Pokorny I 1018–1019, 1028 (to Gk στερέω ‘to rob’); Chemodanov SGGJa I 110; Vries ANEW 546; Onions 866; Seebold 468–469; Lehmann GED 326 (a pre-Gmc word); Kluge-Seebold 791.

ON sbst. stelkr, stjalkr ‘a kind of bird, Tringa islandica’, OE adj. stealc ‘steep’. Related to Lith stelgiū, stefigti ‘to stare’? Torp-Falk 489; Holthausen AEEW 317; Pokorny I 1020; Vries ANEW 546; Fraenkel 900.
ON stelling ‘mast-step’, MLG stellinge ‘rack’. Derived from *stelljanan. Vries ANEW 546.

ON stilla ‘to moderate, to still, to soothe’, OE stillan ‘to become still, to make still’, OS stillian ‘to calm down’, OHG stillen id. Derived from *stelljaz. Torp-Falk 488; Holthausen AEEW 323; Vries ANEW 548; Heidermanns 550; Kluge-Seebold 796.

Burg *stiljis ‘quiet’, OE stille ‘still, quiet’, OFris stille id., OS stilli id., OHG stilli ‘motionless’. Probably derived from *stelanan in its original meaning *‘to stretch’. Curtius Gr. Et. 212, 216 (to Lat stolidus ‘slow, dull, obtuse’); Torp-Falk 488; Persson Beiträge 424; Holthausen AEEW 323 (to Skt sthāṇú- ‘motionless, firm’); Vries ANEW 548; Pokorny I 1019; Onions 869 (to *stallaz); Köbler 713; Rosengren 72–74; Heidermanns 550–551; Kluge-Seebold 796 (to OIr tuilid ‘to sleep’, Lith tīlti ‘to be silent’).
Norw dial. stilta ‘stilt, crutch’, ME stilte id., MLG stelte id., OHG stelza id. Related to *stultaz. Torp-Falk 489; Onions 869.

ON stjǫlr ‘hinder part’, OE stela ‘stalk’. Cf. MLG stal, stale ‘chair leg’. Related to Gk στέλος ‘beam’, Arm ste∑n ‘trunk, stalk’. Torp-Falk 488; Holthausen AEEW 329; Pokorny I 1019; Vries ANEW 549; Frisk II 786– 787.

OE wk. sténan ‘to groan’, MLG str./wk. stenen id. Related to Skt stánati ‘to thunder’, Gk στένω ‘to moan, to sigh’, Lith stenū, stenėti ‘to moan’, Slav *stenati id. Torp-Falk 481; Trautmann BSW 286; Holthausen AEEW 319; Mayrhofer III 510; Pokorny I 1021; Fraenkel 901; Frisk II 789–790; Seebold 469–470; Vasmer-Trubachev III 754; Kluge-Seebold 798.

ON stinga ‘to sting’, OE stinzan ‘to thrust, to prick, to sting’, OHG stingen ‘fleotomare’. Cf. also Goth us-staggan ‘to pluck out’ (Grienberger Got. 233–234). Identical with Lith sténgiu, sténgti ‘to exert oneself, to be able’, Slav *stęgati ‘to flog, to lash’. Brugmann IF XXXII 180; Zupitza Gutt. 101; Torp- Falk 481–482; Feist 535; Holthausen AEEW 323; Jóhannesson IEW 867–868; Pokorny I 1015; Vries ANEW 548; Fraenkel 901–902; Zalizniak II 210; Onions 870; Seebold 470–471 (to Gk στίζω ‘to tattoo’); Lehmann GED 383; Kluge-Seebold 787.

Goth stigqan ‘to clash, to collide’, ON stǫkkva ‘to leap, to spring’, OE stincan id. Etymology unknown. Probably related to Lat stinguō ‘to extinguish’ (Rozwadowski IF IV 411). Bugge BB III 120 (to Gk τέγγω ‘to wet, to moisten’); Sabler KZ XXXI 282 (to Lith sténgiu, sténgti ‘to exert oneself’); Osthoff Etym. I 365 (to Skt stambha- ‘post’); Zupitza Gutt. 94 (to Skt táṅgati ‘to go, to stumble, to tremble’); Brugmann KVG 515 (to Skt tujáti ‘to move quickly, to urge’); Grienberger Got. 199 (follows Rozwadowski); Pedersen Kelt. Gr. I 79 (to W sangu ‘to trample’); Torp-Falk 481; Walde-Hofmann II 593; Feist 452–453; Holthausen AEEW 323; Jóhannesson IEW 862–866; Pokorny I 1032–1034; Vries ANEW 558 (to Gk στίζω ‘to tattoo’); Fraenkel 901–902; Seebold 471–472; Lehmann GED 325; Kluge- Seebold 797.

Norw stokka ‘to stink’, OE stincan ‘to smell, to stink’, WFris stjonke id., MLG stinken id., OHG stinkan id. Historically identical with *stenkwanan I (Wood MLN XXV 74). Holthausen AEEW 323; Hoffner RHA XXI 36 (to Hitt išta(n)ḫ- ‘to taste’); Onions 870; Seebold 471; Lehmann GED 325.

ON stinnr, stiär ‘stiff, unbending, strong’, OE stíä ‘hard, stiff’, OFris stīth ‘strong’, MLG stīde ‘heavy, stiff’. Originally, a participle of *stēnan (Heidermanns 552). Karsten NB II 328–329; Torp-Falk 481 (to Gk sb. στενός ‘narrow, straight’); Holthausen AEEW 323; Pokorny I 1021–1022; Vries ANEW 548; Lerchner 242.

OE steorfan ‘to die’, OFris sterva id., OS sterban id., OHG sterban id. Related to OIr ussarb ‘death’ < *ud-sterbhā, Slav *stьrbati ~ *storbati ‘to stiffen, to die’. Walther KZ XII 411 (to Lat torpeō ‘to be stiff’); Persson Beiträge 435, 438 (to Gk στέρφος ‘hide, skin’, Lat tergum ‘back’); Torp-Falk 487; Holthausen AEEW 320; Jóhannesson IEW 873–878; Pokorny I 1022–1027; Vries ANEW 549; Onions 864 (to *sterđaz); Seebold 472–473; Vasmer- Trubachev III 756; Kluge-Seebold 793.

ON stjarfi‘epilepsy’, OE steorfa ‘mortality, pestilence, flesh of dead animals’, OS man-sterbo ‘epidemic’, OHG sterbo ‘pest’. Derived from *sterbanan. Torp-Falk 487; Holthausen AEEW 320; Vries ANEW 549; Seebold 472.
ON stirđr ‘stiff, rigid’. Identical with W serth id. Torp-Falk 487; Pokorny I 1023; Vries ANEW 547.

ON part. blóä-storkinn ‘to coagulate’, OHG ki-storchenen ‘dabatis’. Corresponds to Slav *stergti ‘to guard’ (the latter is hardly connected with Lith sérgiu, sérgëti ‘to protect, to save’) and probably Hitt ištark- ‘to ail’. Cf. *sterbanan. Torp- Falk 486; Jóhannesson IEW 873–878; Pokorny I 1022–1027; Vries ANEW 551; Seebold 473–474; Puhvel II 475– 477; Kluge-Seebold 788.

OE stearn ‘starling’, LG stern ‘sea-swallow’. Cf. also ON þerna id. A localism related to *staraz ~ *starōn and directly compared with Lat sturnus ‘starling’ < *stṛnos or *stornos and OPrus starnite (v.l. stamite) ‘gull’. Torp-Falk 486; Holthausen AEEW 318; Specht 49; Walde-Hofmann II 610; Pokorny I 1036.

Goth stairno ‘star’, ON stjarna id., MLG sterne id., OHG masc. sterno id. Cf. also, with gemination, OE steorra ‘star’, OFris stera id., OS sterro id., OHG sterro id. Related to Hitt ḫašter- id., Toch A pl. śre-ñ, B ≤cirye ‘stars’, Skt instr. pl. stṛbhiъ ‘star’, Av acc. sg. stâr 6 m id., Arm ast∑id., Gk ἀστήρ id., Lat stēlla id. < *stērlā, W seren id., MIr ser id. (Benfey apud Kuhn KZ I 540). Bartholomae 1598– 1599; Ipsen IF XLI 179–180; Torp-Falk 485; Krogmann KZ LXIII 256–259; Walde-Hofmann II 587–588; Holthausen AEEW 320; Feist 448; Porzig Gliederung 155; Mayrhofer III 512; Pokorny I 1027–1028; Vries ANEW 549; Zalizniak II 222; Frisk I 170–171; Onions 863–864; Watkins Sprache XX 41–50; Tischler HEG I 204–206; Puhvel III 338–339; Lehmann GED 322; Kluge-Seebold 794; Adams TB 640; Burlak IFTJa 258.

Goth fem. stairo ‘barren’, OHG sbst. stero ‘ram’. Related to Toch B śari ‘calf, heifer (?)’, Skt starī‘sterile cow’, Arm sterú ‘infertile’, Gk στεῖρα id., Alb shtjerrë ‘lamb, young cow, heifer’. Torp- Falk 486; Schulze Kl. Schr. 60; Feist 448; Mayrhofer III 513; Pokorny I 1031; Frisk II 783; Lehmann GED 322; Bammesberger Nom. 177; Kluge- Seebold 794; Orel AED 440; Adams TB 622.

ON stertr ‘tail’, OE steort id., OFris stert id., MLG stert id., OHG sterz id. Despite the phonetical irregularities, reminds of Gk στόρθη: τό ὀξύ τοῦ δόρατος, καί ἐπιδορατίς (Hes.). At the same time, cf. *sterđaz and *stertaz II. Lidén SVSU VI/1 17 (to IE *sterd-), Studien 17 (to Lat tardō ‘to tarry, to hinder’); Torp-Falk 487; Holthausen AEEW 320; Pokorny I 1023 (to Gk τόρδυλον ‘hartwort, Tordylium officinale’); Vries ANEW 547; Frisk II 802; Onions 864; Kluge- Seebold 794 (to *starraz).
ON upp-stertr ‘haughty, stately’. A variant of *sterđaz. Torp- Falk 487; Pokorny I 1023.
ON stirtla ‘to hobble, to stagger’, OE steartlian ‘to kick with the foot, to stumble’. Related to *stertaz II. Torp-Falk 487; Holthausen AEEW 318; Vries ANEW 549.

MLG stūven ‘to be dispersed’, OHG stioban id. Of unknown origin. Cf. also unclear WGmc *staumaz ‘steam’ (?< *staubmaz): OE steám, WFris steam, Du stoom. Wood JEGP II 225–226; Uhlenbeck PBB XXVI 308–309; Much ZDW II 286 (to Gk τύφω ‘to produce smoke’); Torp-Falk 497; Holthausen AEEW 318; Feist 457; Onions 866; Lehmann GED 328; Kluge- Seebold 789.
ON stjúp-barn ‘stepchild’, OE steóp-bearn ‘orphan’. Compound of *steupaz and *barnan. Carr Nom. comp. 82 (parallel formations).
ON stjúp-dóttir ‘stepdaughter’, OE steóp-dohtor id., OFris stiāp-dochter id., OS stēf-dohtor id., OHG stiof-tohter id. Compound of *steupaz and *đuxtēr. Carr Nom. comp. 52.
ON stjúp-faäir ‘stepfather’, OE steóp-fæder id., OFris stiāp-feder id., OS stēf-fader id., OHG stiof-fater id. Compound of *steupaz and *fađēr. Carr Nom. comp. 52.
ON stjúp-móäir ‘stepmother’, OE steóp-módor id., OFris stiāp-mōder id., OS stēf-mōder id., OHG stiof-muotar id. Compound of *steupaz and *mōđēr. Carr Nom. comp. 52.
ON stjúp-sonr ‘stepson’, OE steóp-sunu id., OFris stiāp-sunu id., OS stēf-sun id., OHG stiof-sun id. Compound of *steupaz and *sūnuz. Carr Nom. comp. 52.

ON stjúp-móäir ‘stepmother’, stjúpr ‘stepson’, OE steóp-cild ‘stepchild’, OFris stiāp-feder ‘stepfather’, MDu stiep-kind ‘stepchild’, OHG stiof-kind id. Used predominantly in compounds. Related to *stubbaz ~ *stubbōn (Holthausen PBB LXVI 274). Torp-Falk 497; Holthausen AEEW 320; Carr Nom. comp. 52; Pokorny I 1034; Vries ANEW 549; Onions 867; Heidermanns 554; Kluge-Seebold 795.
ON stÿri-maär ‘skipper, captain’, OE steór-mann ‘steersman’, MLG stūrmann id., MHG stiur-man id. Compound of *steur(j)an ~ *steurō and *mannz. Carr Nom. comp. 78–79 (parallel formations).

Goth stiur ‘calf, young steer’, ON stjórr ‘steer, young bull’, OE steór id., MLG stēr id., OHG stior id. Identical with Av staora- ‘cattle’, MPers stōr ‘draught animal, horse’ (Petersson SVSL I 40–41; Bartholomae IF VII 57). Unrelated to *þeuraz. Försterman KZ I 495 (to IE *tauros and Skr sthūrá- ‘strong’); Kuhn KZ I 515; Schulze KZ XXIX 271 (to Skt stávira- ‘thick’); Kock KZ XXXVI 581; Bartholomae 1590–1591; Torp- Falk 479; Lommel KZ XLVI 53–54 (on Iranian parallels); Holthausen AEEW 320; Feist 454; Porzig Gliederung 199; Pokorny I 1009–1010; Zalizniak II 226; Schulze Kl. Schr. 483 (identical with Skt sthávira- ‘wide, thick’); Onions 866; Lehmann GED 326; Kluge-Seebold 796.

ON stÿri ‘helm, rudder’, OE steór ‘guidance, direction’, ‘rudder, helm’, OFris stiure id., MLG stiure id., stūre id., OHG stiura ‘steering-oar, post, support’. Originally, a substantivized adj. *steuraz preserved in Goth adv. us-stiuriba ‘dissolutely’ and related to Skt sthūrá-, sthávira- ‘thick’, Arm stvar id. Brugmann IF VI 98; Torp- Falk 493; Holthausen AEEW 320; Feist 535; Mayrhofer III 530–531; Pokorny I 1009–1010; Vries ANEW 557; Onions 866; Lehmann GED 326; Kluge-Seebold 794.

Goth stiurjan ‘to establish’, ON stÿra ‘to steer’, OE stíeran id., OFris stiura id., MLG stūren id., OHG stiurren id. Derived from *steur(j)an ~ *steurō. Torp-Falk 494; Holthausen AEEW 321; Feist 454–455; Vries ANEW 557; Onions 866; Lehmann GED 326; Kluge-Seebold 794.
ON stjórn ‘steering’, OFris stiārne id. Derived from *steurō ~ *steur(j)an. Onions 868.

Norw stà ‘to stand’, OFris stān id., OS stān id., OHG stān, stēn id. Related to Toch B 3 sg. pres. ste ‘to be’, Skt tíṣṭhati ‘to stand’, Av hišta- id., Gk ἵστημι ‘to put’, Lat sistō id., stō ‘to stand’, OIr at-táu- ‘to be’, Lith stóju, stóti ‘to stand (up)’, Slav *stati ‘to stand, to become’. Streitberg UGG 310; Wilmanns ZDADL XXXIII 424–430; Brugmann IF XV 126–128; Bartholomae 1600–1605; Torp-Falk 477; Trautmann BSW 281; Walde-Hofmann II 596–599; Feist 449–450; Kluge 743, Urgerm. 160; Hirt Urgerm. II 162; Jóhannesson IEW 854– 858; Mayrhofer III 526–527; Pokorny I 1004–1010; Prokosch CGG 223; Fraenkel 914; Vries ANEW 543; Frisk I 739; Vasmer-Trubachev III 748, 769; Seebold 464–465; Lehmann GED 323; Kluge-Seebold 791.
ON stig ‘step’, OE stiz-ráp ‘stirrup’, Du steg ‘footbridge’, OHG steg id. See *stizō(n). Holthausen AEEW 322; Vries ANEW 547. *stiza-raipan ~
ON stig-reip ‘stirrup’, OE stizráp id., MLG stege-rēp id., OHG stege-reif id. Compound of *stizan and *raipan ~ *raipaz. Holthausen AEEW 322; Carr Nom. comp. 52; Onions 870.
ON stigr ‘path, footway’, MLG stech id., OHG steg ‘footbridge, little bridge’. A variant of *stīzaz. Torp- Falk 492.

ON upp-stiga ‘ascent’, OHG stega ‘step, ladder’. See *staizō, *stīzanan. Particularly close to Latv stiga ‘path’, Slav *stьza id. Related to *stīzanan. Torp-Falk 492; Trautmann BSW 286; Pokorny I 1017; Vries ANEW 547; Seebold 466; Vasmer-Trubachev III 752; Lehmann GED 321.
ON stétt ‘pavement, stepping-stones’, EFris stigt ‘stile’. Derived from *stīzanan. Structurally close to OIr techt ‘walk’. Torp-Falk 491; Pokorny I 1017; Vries ANEW 547.

ON stía ‘sty, kennel’, svín-stí ‘pig-sty’ (neut.), OE stiz ‘sty’ (neut.), OFris stei id., MLG stege ‘enclosure’, OHG stīga ‘barn for sheep or goats’. Of uncertain origin. Torp-Falk 490; Holthausen AEEW 322; Pokorny I 1011 (to *stainaz); Zalizniak II 183; Onions 879; Kluge-Seebold 791.

Goth stiks ‘point’, OE stice ‘prick, puncture’, OFris stek, steke ‘point’, OS stiki ‘prick, stab’, OHG stih ‘prick, sting, stitch’. Connected with *stekanan (see on the reconstruction of the root vowel there). Zupitza Gutt. 168; Torp- Falk 491; Holthausen AEEW 321; Feist 453; Pokorny I 1016–1017; Onions 870; Lehmann GED 325–326; Kluge-Seebold 795.
ON stika ‘to drive piles’, OE stician ‘to stick, to pierce, to prick’. Derived from *stikiz. Torp-Falk 491; Holthausen AEEW 321; Vries ANEW 548.

Goth stikls ‘beaker, chalice’, ON stikill ‘pointed end of the horn’, OHG stehhal ‘goblet’. Derived from *stikiz (Fick KZ XX 360–361). Uhlenbeck PBB XXII 191 (from Slav *stьklo ‘glass’ which itself, however, is from Gmc); Torp-Falk 491; Feist 453; Vries ANEW 544, 548 (follows Fick, for the semantics compares *staupaz ~ *staupan and *staupaz); Onions 869; Seebold 468 (to *stīzanan); Lehmann GED 325; Heidermanns 555; Kluge-Seebold 795.
OS stil ‘stalk, shaft’, OHG stil id. Identical with Lat stilus ‘stake, pale, a pointed instrument for freeing plants from worms’. Torp-Falk 490; Walde- Hofmann II 592; Pokorny I 1015.
ON stífla ‘dam’, MLG stīvel ‘support, prop’. Derived from *stīfaz. Heidermanns 549; Kluge-Seebold 791.

Burg *stiffs ‘stiff’, OE stíf id., OFris stīf ‘resolute’, MLG stīf ‘stiff’, MHG stīf ‘rigid’. Related to Lat ob-stīpus ‘bent aside or backwards’, further cf. Lat stīpō ‘to press’, stīpes ‘stem, stick’, Lith stimpū, stīpti ‘to stiffen’ (Zupitza Gutt. 45). Torp-Falk 492; Holthausen AEEW 322; Walde-Hofmann II 593; Pokorny I 1015–1016; Fraenkel 908; Vries ANEW 547; Onions 869; Köbler 713; Heidermanns 549–550; Kluge-Seebold 791.

Goth steigan ‘to climb’, ON stíga ‘to step (up)’, OE stízan ‘to go, to ascend’, OFris stīga ‘to climb’, OS stīgan id., OHG stīgan id. Identical with Skt (Dhātup.) stighnoti ‘to step, to mount’, Gk στείχω id., OIr tíagaim ‘to stride’, Lith steigiū, steĩgti ‘to desire’, Slav *stignǫti ‘to arrive’. Zupitza Gutt. 180; Torp- Falk 491; Trautmann BSW 285–286; Feist 452; Holthausen AEEW 322; Jóhannesson IEW 861–862; Mayrhofer III 514–515; Pokorny I 1017–1018; Fraenkel 899–900; Vries ANEW 547; Vendryes T-57–60; Frisk II 783–785; Onions 879; Seebold 466–467; Vasmer- Trubachev III 760; Lehmann GED 324–325; Kluge-Seebold 791.

ON stígr ‘path, way’ (rare, also u-stem), OE stíz id. (fem.), OHG stīg ‘path’. Identical with W mor-dwy ‘sea travel’. Cf., with zero vocalism, Gk στίχος ‘row’, Latv stiga ‘path’, Slav *stьza id. See *stīzanan. Torp-Falk 492; Holthausen AEEW 322; Pokorny I 1017–1018; Vries ANEW 547; Zalizniak II 204; Frisk II 784–785; Seebold 466; Lehmann GED 321; Bammesberger Nom. 60; Kluge-Seebold 791.

Crim. Goth. stega ‘twenty’, OFris stīge ‘twenty items’, G Stiege ‘ladder’, dial. steige. Derived from *stīzanan (Siebs PBB XLVI 171: compares directly with Gk στίχος ‘row’). Grienberger Zd Ph XXX 132–133 (to Goth -tigjus); Feist 451–452 (from non- Indo-European); Lehmann GED 324.
Norw dial. stīl ‘thin stick’, MLG stīl ‘pillar’. A variant of *stilaz. Torp-Falk 490.
Norw stīm ‘school (of fish)’, MHG stīm ‘commotion, hubbub’. Identical with Skt stīmá- ‘lazy, passive’ derived from styāyate ‘to become hard’. Torp- Falk 490; Pokorny I 1010–1011; Dybo VSJa 27.
EFris stīr ‘stiff’, G stier ‘motionless’. Related to Lith styrū, st‹rti ‘to stiffen’, Lat stīria ‘frozen drop, ice-drop’. Torp-Falk 490; Walde- Hofmann II 595; Pokorny I 1010–1011; Fraenkel 910.
ON stira ‘to stare, to goggle’, EFris stīren ‘to become rigid’, MLG stīren id., G stieren id. Derived from *stīraz. Torp-Falk 490; Vries ANEW 548–549.
ON stóä-hross ‘stud-horse, stallion’, OE stód-hors id., OHG stuot-ros id. Compound of *stōđan and *xrussan. Carr Nom. comp. 78 (parallel formations).
ON stóä-merr ‘brood-mare’, OE stód-mere id. Compound of *stōđan and *marxjōn. Carr Nom. comp. 82 (parallel formations).

ON stóä ‘stud, herd (of horses)’, OE stód id., OS stōd ‘pen (for horses)’ (fem.), OHG stuot ‘herd (of horses)’ (fem.). Together with Slav *stado continues dialectal IE *stādhom, derived from IE *stā- ‘to stand’, see *stanđanan, *stēnan. Torp-Falk 479 (Slav < Gmc); Holthausen AEEW 323, AWN 283; Pokorny I 1004–1007; Chemodanov SGGJa I 83; Vries ANEW 550; Zalizniak II 175; Onions 877; Stang LS 54; Vasmer-Trubachev III 743; Lehmann GED 33; Kluge-Seebold 806.

ON gagn-stœär ‘contrary’. Close to Av stāiti- ‘standing’, Lith stã‘ias id., Latv stātis id., Slav *statь ‘row, way, composure’. Continues *stā-ti-, cf. *stēnan. Bartholomae 1605; Karsten NB II 186–187; Pokorny I 1006; Trautmann BSW 283; Fraenkel 891; Vries ANEW 558; Vasmer-Trubachev III 748; Lehmann GED 324; Matzel Festschr. Schröder 105, KZ CV 120, 132–133; Heidermanns 556.
Goth ana-stodjan ‘to begin’, ON stœäa ‘to establish’. Formally derived from *stōđan or *stōđiz. Feist 44–45; Vries ANEW 558.

Goth stols ‘seat, chair, throne’, ON stóll ‘chair, stool’, OE stól id., OFris stōl id., OS stōl id., OHG stuol id. Close to Lith pa-stõlas ‘stand’ < *stālo-, cf. also Slav *stolъ ‘throne, table, chair’. Further to IE *stā- ‘to stand, to put’, see *stēnan. Torp-Falk 488; Holthausen AEEW 324; Feist 455–456; Pokorny I 1007; Fraenkel 547–548; Vries ANEW 551; Zalizniak I 135; Onions 872; Stang LS 55; Seebold 464; Vasmer- Trubachev III 764–765; Lehmann GED 327; Bammesberger Nom. 76; Kluge- Seebold 804.

Goth stoma ‘basis, substance, ὑπόστασις’, OHG gi-stuomi ‘quiet’. Close to Skt neut. sthāman- ‘location’, Gk στήμων ‘warp in weaving’, Lat stāmen id., Lith stomuõ ‘stature’, Slav *stamъ ‘permanent, stubborn’. Further based on IE *stā- ‘to stand’. See *stēnan. Schröder ZDADL XLII 68 (from *stōbma-); Trautmann BSW 282; Walde- Hofmann II 586; Feist 456; Mayrhofer III 526; Pokorny I 1007–1008; Fraenkel 912; Frisk II 796; Vasmer- Trubachev III 744–745; Lehmann GED 327; Bammesberger Nom. 184.

ON stórr ‘great, potent’, OE stór ‘great, strong, violent’, OFris stōr ‘big, weighty’, OLG stōri ‘famous’. Identical with Lith stóras ‘thick’, Slav *starъ ‘old’ (Meillet Etudes 404). Cf. *starraz. Karsten NB II 251; Torp-Falk 479; Persson Beiträge 707, 711; Trautmann BSW 282; Feist 50–51; Holthausen AEEW 324, AWN 284; Pokorny I 1008; Fraenkel 913; Vries ANEW 551–552; Vasmer-Trubachev III 747; Lehmann GED 37–38; Stang LS 55; Heidermanns 557–558.
ON stœra ‘to swell’, OHG stuorren ‘to support’. Derived from *stōraz. Structurally close to Slav *stariti sę ‘to become old’. Heidermanns 558.

Goth stojan ‘to judge’, OE stówian ‘to hold back, to restrain’, MLG stōwen ‘to heap up’, OHG stouwen ‘to complain’. Derived from *stōwō (Brugmann Grundriß II/I 202, 207). However, can be structurally close to Slav *staviti ‘to put, to place’, Lith stóviu, stovėti ‘to stand, to stand still’ (ZubatŸ BB XVIII 247). Grienberger Got. 198; Persson Beiträge 563, 713; Mikkola Festschr. Streitberg 269–270; Torp-Falk 493; Trautmann BSW 273; Holthausen AEEW 324; Feist 455; Pokorny I 1008; Fraenkel 915–916; Vasmer-Trubachev III 742; Lehmann GED 327; Kluge- Seebold 790.

ON eld-stó ‘stove’, OE stów ‘place’, OFris stō id. Derivationally close to Gk στοά, Aeol στωΐα ‘roof colonnade, storehouse, court’ < *stv Wιά, Lith stovā ‘place, stand’, Latv stāvs ‘height, shape’, Slav *stavъ ‘stand’. Further connected with *stā- ‘to stand’, see *stēnan. Brugmann Grundriß I 322; Helten PBB XXX 246 (to Gmc *þauwaz ‘custom’: OE þeáw); Torp-Falk 493; Trautmann BSW 283; Holthausen AEEW 324; Pokorny I 1008; Fraenkel 915; Vries ANEW 549; Frisk II 800; Seebold 465; Vasmer-Trubachev III 742; Lehmann GED 324.

Norw strak ‘tight’, OE stræc ‘strict, hard’, MLG strak ‘tight, taut’, OHG fram-strah ‘rigid’. Substantivized in WGmc: OE stræc ‘strictness, rigor’, MLG strak ‘penalty’, OHG strach id. Identical with Slav *strogъ ‘strict’. Hirt PBB XXIII 306–307 (eventually, to *ster- ‘to stretch’); Torp-Falk 497; Persson Beiträge 432; Holthausen AEEW 324; Wissmann NP 191; Pokorny I 1023; Vasmer-Trubachev III 779–780; Lühr Expr. 230–231; Heidermanns 559.
Norw strekkja ‘to stretch’, OE streccan ‘to hold out, to extend’, OFris strekka ‘to stretch’, MLG stricken id., OHG strecken id. Derived from *strak(k)az. Hirt Idg. Gr. I 332 (to

Norw dial. sbst. stramb ‘strong smell’, MLG stram ‘strong, powerful’. Close to OPrus strambo ‘stubble’, Lith stra Mpas ‘club, truncheon’ (Persson Beiträge 440). Torp-Falk 499; Pokorny I 1025–1026; Fraenkel 919, IF LIX 95.

ON strǫnd ‘border, edge, coast, strand’ (traces of i-stem and root stem), OE neut. strand ‘strand, shore’, MLG strand id. Unless a chain of internal borrowings (ON > OE > MLG), connected with *strenđō. Jensen NTF X 20 (to Lat frōns ‘forehead’); Torp-Falk 499; Holthausen AEEW 325; Schröder NGWG 1941 294–296 (WGmc < ON); Pokorny I 1030; Vries ANEW 555 (to OIr srath ‘bottom of the valley’); Zalizniak II 210; Onions 873; Kluge-Seebold 800.
ON strang-ligr ‘strong, severe’, OE strenz-líc ‘strong, firm’, OHG streng-līh id. Derived from *stranzaz. Heidermanns 560.

ON strangr ‘strong’, OE stranz id., OS strang id., OHG strang, strengi id. Related to Latv strangs ‘brave, fresh’. Further can be analyzed as a deverbative (see *stranziz). Karsten NB II 377–378; Zupitza Gutt. 180; Torp-Falk 498; Holthausen AEEW 325; Pokorny I 1036; Vries ANEW 552; Onions 876; Lühr Expr. 161; Heidermanns 560–561; Kluge-Seebold 801–802.

ON strengr ‘string, cord, rope’, OE strenz id., MLG strank id., OHG strang id. Related to Lat stringō ‘to draw tight, to bind’ and, further, to *stranzaz. Zupitza Gutt. 180; Persson Beiträge 866; Torp-Falk 498–499; Holthausen AEEW 325; Walde-Hofmann II 604–605; Pokorny I 1036; Vries ANEW 552; Onions 876; Heidermanns 560; Kluge-Seebold 800.
ON strengja ‘to string, to fasten’, OHG gi-strengen ‘to strengthen’. Derived from *stranzaz. Torp-Falk 498; Vries ANEW 552; Heidermanns 560.

ON straumr ‘stream, current’, OE streám id., OFris strām id., OS strōm id., OHG stroum id. From IE *sroumo-. Etymologically connected with Gk =εῦμα id., Thrac Στρυμών (rivername), Alb rrymë ‘flow (of water)’ < *srūmā, OIr srúaim ‘river’, Lith sraumuõ ‘stream, brook’, Latv strāume ‘river, stream’, Slav *strumy, gen. *strumene ‘brook’. These forms are further derived from IE *sreu- ‘to flow’. Tomaschek Thr. II/2 99; Pedersen Kelt. Gr. I 82; Būga RFV LXXV 147; Torp-Falk 502; Kluge Nom. Stamm. 46–47; Trautmann BSW 279–280; Holthausen AEEW 325; Detschew Thr. 483–485; Pokorny I 1003; Fraenkel 887; Vries ANEW 552; Zalizniak I 135; Frisk II 650–651; Onions 874; Vasmer-Trubachev III 783–784; Bammesberger Nom. 71; Orel AED 392; Kluge-Seebold 803.
Norw strøypa ‘to pinch, to squeeze’, ME stripe ‘to strip’, MDu stroopen id., OHG stroufen ‘to pluck, to gather’. Related to Lith strūbas ‘closely cut’. Pokorny I 1027; Onions 876.

Goth fem. straua ‘funeral feast’, ON strá ‘straw’, OE streáw id., OFris strē id., OS strō id., OHG strou, strō id. Etymologically connected with Lat struō ‘to build up’, OBret pret. strovis ‘to spread out’ derived from IE *ster H- id. The original meaning (‘something spread out’) is revealed by the derivative *strawjanan. Grimm Kl. Schr. III 135 (on the Gothic word); Niederle Star. II 136–139 (Goth < Slav *strava); Kluge IF IV 309 (separates streáw- in OE streáw-berize ‘strawberry’ as continuing *srazwa- ~ Lat frāgum ‘strawberry plant’); Torp-Falk 502; Holthausen AEEW 325; Walde- Hofmann II 607–608; Feist 456–457; Rooth Strawa 37–52; Pokorny I 1031; Vries ANEW 552; Zalizniak I 146; Onions 874; Lehmann GED 327–328; Kluge-Seebold 802.

Goth straujan ‘to spread, to strew’, ON strá ‘to strew’ (< *strawēnan, new form based on pret.), OE streáwian id., OFris strēwa id., OS prêt. strōidun id., OHG strewen, strouwen id. Derived from *strawan. Torp-Falk 502; Holthausen AEEW 326; Feist 456; Pokorny I 1031; Vries ANEW 552; Onions 875; Lehmann GED 327; Kluge- Seebold 802.
ON strind ‘earth, land’. Related to OIr trét ‘herd’, without initial *s-. Torp-Falk 499; Pokorny I 1030; Fraenkel 1118 (Lith trénta ‘place, area’ < G dial. trent ‘area, step’); Zalizniak II 210.

ON strjúka ‘to stroke, to rub, to wipe’. Related to Toch A sruk- ‘to kill’, B sruk- ‘to die’, Gk στρεύγομαι ‘to be squeezed out in drops’, Slav *strъgati ‘to plane’ (Schmidt Voc. I 161). Zupitza Gutt. 28, 168; Torp- Falk 503; Jóhannesson IEW 878–881; Pokorny I 1028–1029; Vries ANEW 554; Frisk II 807–808; Seebold 478– 479; Vasmer-Trubachev III 779; Adams TB 722–723.

OE stræl ‘arrow’, OS strāla id., OHG strāl id., strāla id. Identical with Lith strëlā ‘arrow’ (if not from Slavic), Slav *strîla ‘arrow’ (Kuhn KZ I 540). Further connections are uncertain. Persson Beiträge 449; Torp-Falk 500; Trautmann BSW 289; Holthausen AEEW 324; Pokorny I 1028; Fraenkel 920–921; Zalizniak II 175; Stang LS 55; Vasmer-Trubachev III 774; Kluge- Seebold 800.
Norw strika ‘to stop’. Identical with Lat strigō ‘to hold up, to halt, to stop’ (Persson Beiträge 458–460). Walde-Hofmann II 604; Pokorny I 1036; Dybo BSA 173.

Goth striks ‘stroke, hook, serif’, OHG strih ‘line, stroke, cut’. Related to *strīk(w)anan. Cf. Lat striga ‘row of grain or hay cut down, a swath, windrow’. Torp-Falk 501; Feist 457; Walde-Hofmann II 603; Pokorny I 1028–1029; Zalizniak II 197; Seebold 476; Lehmann GED 328; Bammesberger Nom. 120.
Swed dial. strena ‘stripe on the skin’, MDu strene ‘strand, skein’, OHG streno id. Of unknown origin. Torp-Falk 500; Pokorny I 1028.

ON stríä ‘woe, grief, strife, combat’, OFris strīd ‘quarrel, fight’, OS strīd id., OHG strīt id. Derived from *strīđanan. Persson Beiträge 442 (to Slav *trizna ‘wake, contest’); Meillet Etudes 448; Vries ANEW 553; Seebold 475.

OSwed stríäa ‘to quarrel’, OE strídan ‘to get by force, to pillage, to rob’, OFris strīda ‘to quarrel’, MLG striden id., OHG strītan id. Historically identical with *strīđanan > OE strídan ‘to stride’, MLG strīden ‘to set the legs wide apart’ in its metaphoric meaning. Uhlenbeck PBB XX 328–329 (to Lat līra ‘earth thrown up between two furrows, a ridge’); Torp-Falk 501; Holthausen AEEW 326; Jóhannesson IEW 873–878; Pokorny I 1026; Vries ANEW 553; Onions 875; Seebold 475–476; Kluge- Seebold 801 (to Lith strainūs ‘decorated, furbished’).
ON stríär ‘strong, hard, stubborn’, OHG ein-strīti ‘tenacious, rebellious’. Derived from *strīđanan. Vries ANEW 553; Heidermanns 561.
ON stríäa ‘to harm, to punish’, OFris strīda ‘to quarrel, to dispute’, OS strīdian id. Derived from *strīđaz. Torp-Falk 501; Heidermanns 561.

ON strÿkva, strÿkja ‘to flog, to punish’ (wk.), OE strícan ‘to make a stroke’, OFris strīka ‘to strike’, MLG striken id., OHG strīhhan ‘to smear, to whet’. Connected with Lat stringō ‘to stroke, to touch’, Slav *strigǫ, *strigti ‘to cut (hair), to shear’. Zupitza Gutt. 94; Torp-Falk 500; Meillet MSL XIV 350; Trautmann BSW 289; Holthausen AEEW 326; Walde-Hofmann II 604–605; Jóhannesson IEW 878–881; Pokorny I 1028–1029; Vries ANEW 554; Onions 876; Seebold 476–477; Vasmer-Trubachev III 778; Lehmann GED 328; Kluge-Seebold 801.
Norw strīpa ‘stripe’, MLG strīpe id., MHG strīfe id. Close to OIr sríab id. < *streibā. Torp-Falk 501; Pokorny I 1029.
Norw strop ‘measure’, EFris strump ‘stump’, MLG strump id., MHG strumpf id. Identical with Latv strumps ‘short’. Torp-Falk 504; Pokorny I 1027.
Icel strokkr ‘small wooden vessel’, MLG strunk ‘stock of a large plant’, MHG strunc ‘tirsus’. Derived from *strawjanan? Torp-Falk 503.
Swed strutta ‘to strut’, ME strut ‘to abound’, OS strotōn ‘to be talkative’, MHG strozzen ‘to abound’. Of imitative origin. Pokorny I 1027; Kluge-Seebold 803 (to *þrūtanan).

ON strútr ‘a kind of hood’, ME strút ‘swelling’, MHG strūz ‘resistance, quarrel’. Related to *strut(t)ōjanan. Torp-Falk 503; Pokorny I 1027; Vries ANEW 553 (to Lith strustīs ‘stripes of bast in the sieve’).

ON stubbr ‘stump’, stubbi id., ME stubbe id., MLG stubbe id. Related to Lith stūburas ‘backbone, spine’, Latv stuburs ‘stump’. See *stūfaz. Torp-Falk 496; Trautmann BSW 290; Holthausen AEEW 328; Martinet Gém. 196; Pokorny I 1035; Fraenkel 928; Vries ANEW 555; Onions 877; Kluge-Seebold 804.
Goth stubjus ‘dust’, MLG stubbe id., OHG stubbi id. Derived from *steubanan ~ *stūbanan. Torp-Falk 497.
ON stuäill ‘prop, stud’, MHG studel ‘post, door-post’. Cf. OS tanstuthili ‘row of teeth’. Derived from *stuđō. Torp-Falk 495; Vries ANEW 555.
ON styäja ‘to steady, to prop’, OHG gi-studen ‘to establish’. Derived from *stuđō. Persson Wurzelerw. 144; Vries ANEW 557; Kluge- Seebold 806.

ON stoä ‘post, prop’, OE stod ‘post’. Related to Latv stūte ‘post, prop’. Or derived from *stanđanan with an unexpected vocalism? Torp-Falk 495; Holthausen AEEW 323; Pokorny I 1009; Vries ANEW 550; Onions 877; Seebold 461; Kluge-Seebold 806.

ON stofa ‘sitting-room’, OE stofa ‘bathroom’ (masc.), MLG stove ‘heated room’, OHG stuba ‘bathroom, a heated room’. Borrowed from Rom *extūfa, cf. Ital stufa ‘bath(room)’, Fr étuve id. Meyer-Lübke REW 270 (to *steubanan ~ *stūbanan); Schuchardt Zd Wf. I 66–67 (against Meyer-Lübke); Van Wijk IF XXIV 35; Holthausen AEEW 323; Martynov Slav.-germ. 122–123 (from Slav *jьstъba ‘hut, room’); Onions 872; Kluge-Seebold 804.

ON styggr ‘shy’, MDu stugge ‘stubborn, stiff, unfriendly’. Related to Skt stúkā ‘tuft of wool, of hair’, Lith stūkis ‘stump’, stūkas ‘short’. Karsten NB II 446; Torp-Falk 494–495; Jóhannesson IEW 862–863; Pokorny I 1032; Polomé Language XXV 187–188 (to *stūraz); Mayrhofer III 516; Pokorny I 1032; Fraenkel 930; Vries ANEW 557; Heidermanns 490 (ON < *skewwaz, adds G scheu ‘shy’).

ON stokkr ‘trunk, block, log of wood’, OE stocc ‘stock, trunk, log’, OFris stokk ‘stick, stump’, OS stokk ‘stock, trunk, log’, OHG stoc id. With an irregularity in the inlaut geminate, related to Skt stúkā ‘tuft of wool, of hair’. See *stuzjaz. Zupitza Gutt. 28; Torp-Falk 494; Holthausen AEEW 323; Martinet Gém. 87, 196; Mayrhofer III 516; Pokorny I 1033 (to IE *steu-g-), 1035; Vries ANEW 550; Zalizniak I 136; Onions 871; Lühr Expr. 231; Heidermanns 563; Kluge-Seebold 797 (to Skt tujáti ‘to strike, to hit’).

ON stykki ‘piece’, OE stycce id., OS stukki id., OHG stucki id. Derived from *stukkaz. Torp-Falk 494; Holthausen AEEW 328; Martinet Gém. 197; Pokorny I 1033; Vries ANEW 557; Zalizniak I 145; Onions 871; Kluge- Seebold 804.
ON stokka ‘to build, to raise’, ME stocken ‘to put together, MLG stocken id., OHG samastocken id. Derived from *stukkaz. Vries ANEW 550.

ON stólpi ‘post, pillar’, ME stolpe id., MLG stolpe id. Close to Lith dial. stu÷lbas id., Slav *stъlbъ ‘pole, post’ (Förstemann KZ XIX 355). Tamm UUÅ 1881 31 (ON < Slav *stъlpъ); Torp-Falk 489; Trautmann BSW 290– 291; Specht KZ LXVIII 123; Stender- Petersen Slav.-germ. 279–281 (Lith < Gmc); Pokorny I 1020; Chemodanov SGGJa I 82; Fraenkel 930; Vries ANEW 551; Stang LS 56; Vasmer-Trubachev III 765; Kluge-Seebold 805.

ON stund ‘period of time, while, hour’ (partly *stunđiz), OE stund id., OFris stunde id., OS stunda id., OHG stunta id. Derived from *stēnan, *stanđanan. Torp-Falk 477; Holthausen AEEW 327; Pokorny I 1005; Vries ANEW 556; Zalizniak II 194; Meier ASNSL CCII 428–431 (from Rom *extunda ‘short period of time’: OSard istunda, Catal estona, Cors stonda); Heidermanns 552; Kluge-Seebold 805 (follows Meier).
ON stunda ‘to proceed, to strive’, OE á-stundian ‘to astound, to grieve’. Derived from *stunđō. Holthausen AEEW 328.

ON stynja ‘to groan’, OE stunian ‘to crash, to resound’, MLG stonen ‘to moan’. Derived from *stenanan. Cf. also derivatives: ON stynr ‘groan’, OE ze-stun ‘noise, stun’. Torp-Falk 481; Holthausen AEEW 328; Pokorny I 1021; Vries ANEW 557.

ON stuttr ‘short, stunted, scant’, OE stunt ‘foolish, stupid’, NFris stönt ‘short’, MHG stunz id. Probably derived from *stautanan. Kock ANF XI 317 (ON stuttr by analogy, instead of *stottr); Karsten NB I 13–14; Torp-Falk 482; Holthausen AEEW 328; Pokorny I 1033–1034; Vries ANEW 556–557; Vries ANEW 556–557 (to Skt tudáti ‘to push, to strike’); Onions 878; Lühr Expr. 102; Heidermanns 564–565.

ON stytta ‘to shorten’, OE styntan ‘to make dull, to become dull’. Derived from *stuntaz. Ehrismann PBB XVIII 216 (to Lat tundō ‘to beat, to strike’, also with nasalization); Torp-Falk 482; Holthausen AEEW 328; Vries ANEW 558; Heidermanns 565.

ON storä ‘young wood, plantation, earth (grown with brushwood)’. Close to Gk στόρθη: τό ὀξύ τοῦ δόρατος, καί ἐπιδορατίς (Hes.) < *stordhā (Lidén SVSU VI/1 17). See *stertaz I. Pokorny I 1024; Vries ANEW 551; Frisk II 802.

OE styrian ‘to stir, to move’, OS far-sturian ‘to subvert’, OHG ir-sturen ‘to shake’. Related to Skt turáti ‘to hurry, to press forward’, tūryate ‘to run’. Torp-Falk 505; Holthausen AEEW 328; Mayrhofer I 514; Pokorny I 1101; Onions 870.
ON styrr ‘tumult, stir’, OE ze-styr ‘movement’. Derived from *sturjanan. Vries ANEW 558.

ON styrja ‘sturgeon’, OE styria id., MLG stor, store id., OHG sturo, sturio id. Based on *sturjanan ‘to stir, to move’ (Falk ANF XLI 134). This derivation is motivated by one of the most striking features of sturgeon’s behaviour—its movements during the spawning period’. Hirt IF XXII 69 (to Slav *osetrъ ‘sturgeon’, Lith eršk‚tas id.); Torp- Falk 505; Schrader-Nehring II 484–485; Holthausen AEEW 328; Vries ANEW 557; Zalizniak II 177; Witczak KZ CIV 106 (from *sṛHƒon-, cf. Lat sariō ‘salmon trout’, from Gaulish); Kluge-Seebold 798–799.

ON storkr ‘stork’, OE storc id., MLG stork id., OHG storah id. The original meaning must have been ‘stiff bird’, ‘bird standing without moving’. Derived from *sterkanan. Kirchhoff KZ I 39; Fick I 57 (to Gk τόργος ‘vulture’); Zupitza Gutt. 216; Torp-Falk 487; Holthausen AEEW 324; Pokorny I 1023; Vries ANEW 551; Zalizniak I 136; Onions 872; Seebold 473–474; Witczak KZ CIV 107 (from *sṛ·os, cf. Skt sṛjayá- ‘a kind of bird’, Rum barz> ‘stork’ < Dacian); Kluge-Seebold 799.

Goth ga-staurknan ‘to wither’, ON storkna ‘to coagulate’, OHG part. er-storchenet ‘numb’. Derived from *sturkaz (in its unattested original meaning). See also *sterkanan. Torp- Falk 486; Feist 201–202; Pokorny I 1023; Jóhannesson IEW 873–878; Vries ANEW 551; Onions 864; Seebold 473; Lehmann GED 149; Kluge-Seebold 788.

Burg *storms ‘storm’, ON stormr id., OE storm id., OS storm id., OHG sturm id. Derived from *sturjanan. Torp-Falk 505; Holthausen AEEW 324; Pokorny I 1101; Vries ANEW 551 (to Lat turma ‘troop, squadron, crowd’); Zalizniak I 136; Onions 872; Witczak KZ CIV 107 (from *sṛmos, cf. Skt sárma- ‘going, running, flowing’, Gk ὁρμή ‘rapid motion forward’); Köbler 713; Kluge- Seebold 805–806.
ON styrma ‘to blow up for a gale, to storm’, OE styrman ‘to storm’, MLG stormen id., OHG sturmen id. Derived from *sturmaz. Torp-Falk 505; Holthausen AEEW 328; Vries ANEW 558.
Goth and-staurran ‘to confront, to glare at’, OHG storrēn ‘to tower’. Related to *starraz. Torp-Falk 486; Feist 50–51; Lehmann GED 37; Heidermanns 547; Kluge-Seebold 788.

ON stúfr ‘stump’, MLG stūve id. Related to Skt stūpa- ‘shock of hair’, Gk στύπος ‘stem, stump, block’, Latv stūps ‘fag-end’ (Fick I 145, III 496–497). Zupitza Gutt. 35; Torp-Falk 496; Mayrhofer III 516; Pokorny I 1035; Vries ANEW 555; Frisk II 813–814; Onions 877.
ON stÿfa ‘to cut off, to chop off’, MLG stūven ‘to deaden’. Derived from *stūfaz. Vries ANEW 557.
ON stúka ‘sleeve’, MLG stūke ‘tree stump’, OHG stūhha ‘wide sleeve, kerchief’. Connected with *stūfaz? Torp-Falk 494; Kluge-Seebold 789.

ON stúpa ‘to stoop’ (hap. leg.), MDu stupen ‘to stick out’. A secondary strong verb related to *staupaz. Etymologically connected with Skt tupáti ‘to hurt’, túmpati id., pra-stumpati ‘to butt’, Gk τύπτω ‘to strike’, Lat stupeō ‘to stiffen’, Slav *tъpati ‘to stamp’ despite Gmc *-pof unknown origin. Torp-Falk 496; Walde-Hofmann II 609; Jóhannesson IEW 862–866; Mayrhofer I 512–513; Pokorny I 1034; Vries ANEW 556; Frisk II 945–946; Onions 872; Seebold 474.

Norw stūr ‘surly, sad’, MLG stūr ‘stiff, strict’, OHG stūri ‘strong, well-built’. Identical with Skt sthūrá- ‘big, strong, thick’, Osset stur ‘big’, Latv stūrs ‘stubborn’. Karsten NB II 250–251; Torp-Falk 493–494; Mayrhofer III 530–531; Pokorny I 1009; Dybo VSJa 26; Vries ANEW 556; Schrijver LL 355 (no expected shortening of pretonic *ū in Gmc); Heidermanns 566; Kluge- Seebold 805.
ON stútr ‘bull; a stumpy thing’, MLG stūt ‘thick part of the thigh, rump’. Derived from *stautanan. Torp- Falk 495; Vries ANEW 556.
ON syfja ‘to become sleepy’, EFris suffen id. Derived from *swefanan. Torp-Falk 548; Vries ANEW 572.
ON sufl‘garnish’, OE sufel id., OS sūval id., OHG sūfil id. Related to Skt sūpa- ‘broth, soup’. See *sūpōn. Torp-Falk 445; Holthausen AEEW 329; Mayrhofer III 494–495; Pokorny I 913; Vries ANEW 559.
ON syfla ‘to furnish with garnish’, OE syflan id. Derived from *suflan. Holthausen AEEW 339; Vries ANEW 572.
ON sog ‘inlet’, MHG suc ‘juice’. Derived from *sūzanan. Torp- Falk 443; Vries ANEW 529.

Goth sugil ‘sun, name of srune’, OE sizel, sezl id. A variant of *sōwelan ~ *sōwelō (Schmidt KZ XXVI 9) with a difficult inlaut. On the other hand, cf. OE swezle ‘bright as the sun’, swezel ‘heaven, sun’, OS swigli ‘light’ < *swezlaz. Sievers PBB VI 565–566 (phonologically, Goth *sōjil = sauil); Grienberger PBB XXI 216–217 (same as Sievers), Got. 182; Wood MLN XVI 306; Marstrander NTS I 149 (against connection to *sōwelan ~ *sōwelō); Holthausen ASNSL CXIII 43–44 (to *sīz(w)anan), AEEW 293, 334; Uhlenbeck PBB XXX 311; Feist 457–458; Pokorny I 881–882; Vries ANEW 529; Lehmann Phon. 48 (*-z- < *-H⁄-), GED 328; Heidermanns 575.

Norw dial. sugga ‘sow’ (with secondary gemination), OE sugu id., OS suga id., G dial. suge id. Close to OIr socc ‘pig’, W hwch id., Corn hoch id., Bret houc’h id. < *sukku-. Further connected to *sūz. Torp-Falk 443; Holthausen AEEW 329; Pokorny I 1038; Zalizniak II 210; Onions 849.

Goth sauhts ‘sickness, disease’, ON sótt id., OE út-siht ‘diarrhea’, OFris secht ‘disease’, OS suht id., OHG suht, suft id. Derived from *seukaz. Zupitza Gutt. 165; Torp-Falk 442; Holthausen AEEW 293; Feist 412; Pokorny I 915; Zalizniak I 151; Vries ANEW 531; Seebold 399; Lehmann GED 296– 297; Bammesberger Nom. 140; Kluge- Seebold 807; Boutkan OFED s.v.

OE sol ‘mire, miry place’, OFris sol ‘mud’, MLG sol id., OHG sol id. Cf. also ON place name Soleyjar. Related to Skt súrā ‘spirituous liquor’, Gk ὕλη ‘sediment, mud, slime’, Lith sulā ‘juice, birch juice’. Trautmann BSW 291; Holthausen AEEW 306; Fraenkel 940; Mayrhofer III 487; Pokorny I 913; Vries ANEW 529; Frisk II 962– 963; Kluge-Seebold 769–770.

Goth ga-suljan ‘to lay the foundation’, ON sylla ‘to furnish with a sill’. Related to *swelljō. Kretschmer KZ XXXI 417 (to Gk ὕλη ‘forest’); Grienberger Got. 92 (to Lat solum ‘bottom, ground, base’); Torp-Falk 446; Feist 202; Vries ANEW 573.
Norw dial. sylt ‘saline swamp’, MLG sulte ‘saline, meat jelly’, OHG sulza ‘saltwater’. Related to *saltan. Torp- Falk 437.

Goth sums ‘some, any’, ON sumr id., OE sum id., OFris sum id., OS sum id., OHG sum id. Related to Toch A sam, B sām ‘same’, Skt samá- ‘equal, like, the same, even’, Av hama- ‘every, each’, Gk ëma ‘together’. Bartholomae 1773– 1774; Torp-Falk 434; Holthausen AEEW 329; Feist 458; Mayrhofer III 436–437; Pokorny I 903; Vries ANEW 561; Frisk I 83; Onions 845; Lehmann GED 328–329; Bammesberger Nom. 227; Burlak IFTJa 260–261.
ON sumar-dagr ‘summer day’, late ME summer-day id., MLG somer-dach id., MHG sumer-tac id. Compound of *sumeraz and *đazaz. Carr Nom. comp. 79 (parallel formations).
ON sumar-langr ‘summer long’, OE sumer-lanz ‘long as in summer’, OS sumar-lang id., MHG sumer-lanc id. Compound of *sumeraz and *lanzaz. Carr Nom. comp. 90 (parallel formations).
ON sumar-tíä ‘summer time’, ME sumer-tid id., MLG somertīt id., MHG sumer-zīt id. Compound of *sumeraz and *tīđiz. Carr Nom. comp. 79 (parallel formations)
