ON sumarr ‘summer’ (also neut. sumar), OE sumer id., OFris sumur id., OS sumar id., OHG sumar id. Close to Arm amafin ‘summer’ < *semerom. Further related to Skt sámā ‘half-year, year, season’, Av ham- ‘summer’, Arm am ‘year’, W haf ‘summer’. Bartholomae 1773; Torp-Falk 445; Holthausen AEEW 329; Mayrhofer III 437–438; Pokorny I 905; Vries ANEW 560–561; Zalizniak II 204; Onions 885; Kluge- Seebold 770.
ON sumrungr ‘one summer old animal’, OE symerinz-wyrt ‘a kind of plant, Malva crispa’. Derived from *sumeraz. Vries ANEW 561.
ON sumra ‘to come (of summer)’, ME someren id., MLG someren id., MHG sumeren id. Derived from *sumeraz. Vries ANEW 561.

ON sum(b)l ‘banquet, symposium’, OE symbel ‘feast, banquet’ (< *sumljan), OS sumbal ‘meal’. Continues *sṃ-lo- ‘joint meal’ related to *samaz, *samōn. Torp-Falk 434 (from *semōjan > OHG gi-semōn ‘to banquet’, to Gk ψωμός ‘morsel, bit’, ψώω ‘to rub, to grind’); Holthausen AEEW 340; Jóhannesson IEW 764; Pokorny I 903–904; Vries ANEW 561; Frisk II 390.

ON sund ‘swimming’, OE sund ‘swimming, sea’, MLG sunt ‘strait’ (masc.). Continues *sumđan derived from *swemmanan. Torp-Falk 548–549; Holthausen AEEW 329–330; Vries ANEW 561; Onions 848; Seebold 492; Bammesberger Nom. 77; Kluge-Seebold 809.

OE ze-sund ‘sound, healthy’, OFris sund id., OS gi-sund id., OHG gisunt id. Related to *sanþz. On the other hand, may be structurally identical with Hitt ašuwant- ‘good’ < *esu-⁄–t- (Seebold Lu R 505). Torp-Falk 547; (to *swenþaz); Onions 848; Heidermanns 586; Kluge-Seebold 321 (to IE *s·hu-nt-).

ON syn ‘denial, protest’, OE syn, synn ‘misdeed, sin’, OFris sinne id., OS sundia id., OHG sunta id. Related to *sunjaz, *sanþz. van Helten PBB XXX 249 (to Gk ἄτη ‘bewilderment, infatuation’); Fick KZ XX 367–369 (from *sunjaz); Schrader KZ XX 467–469 (to Gk ἀάω ‘to hurt, to damage’); Schröder KZ LVI 106–116 (to *skanđō); Torp-Falk 430; Rossell UUÅ 1942/7 107; Holthausen AEEW 340; Feist 459; Pokorny I 341; Vries ANEW 573; Zalizniak I 149; Onions 828 (related to Lat sōns ‘guilty’); Seebold Sprache XV 14–45; Lehmann GED 329–330; Kluge-Seebold 809.

Goth adv. sundro ‘asunder, alone, apart’, ON sundr ‘asunder’, OE sundor ‘alone, apart’, OFris prep. sunder id., OS sundar id., OHG suntar ‘remote, separate’. Continues a form with a comparative suffix *-ter-, cf. Skt sanutár- ‘away, off, aside’, Gk ἄτερ ‘without, far from’, W hanner ‘half’ (Bugge BB III 120: reconstructs *σἢ-τερ-, to *sem- ‘one’). Schmidt KZ XXV 92 (same as Bugge), XXXII 360 (identical with Gk ἅτερος ‘other, different’ < *σἢ-τερο-); Bartholomae BB XV 16; Brugmann Grundriß II/1 324; Grienberger Got. 202 (same as Bugge); Meringer IF XVIII 223 (to *s–t- ‘being’); Torp-Falk 444; Holthausen AEEW 330; Feist 458–459; Mayrhofer III 427; Pokorny I 907; Vries ANEW 561; Mayrhofer III 427; Frisk I 178; Chantraine 381–382; Onions 886; Lehmann GED 329; Kluge- Seebold 770–771.
Swed söndra ‘to sunder’, OE syndrian ‘to sunder, to separate’. Derived from *sunđraz. Torp- Falk 444.

ON sundra ‘to break asunder’, OE sundrian ‘to sunder, to separate’, MLG sonderen id., OHG suntarōn id. Derived from *sunđraz. Torp- Falk 444; Holthausen AEEW 330; Vries ANEW 561; Onions 886; Kluge- Seebold 771.

Goth fem. sunja ‘truth’. Replaces the expected but unattested *sunđjaz < *s–tƒó-. The latter is identical with Skt satyá- ‘true, real’, Av haiyya- ‘true’ (Kluge PBB X 144). A zero grade form related to *sanþaz. Curtius Gr. Et. 197–198 (to Gk ἐτεός ‘true’); Brugmann KVG 592 (to Lat sōns ‘guilty, criminal’); Meillet MSL XV 219–220 (to Gk ὅσιος ‘holy’); Meringer IF XVIII 223–224 (same as Brugmann); Bartholomae 1760–1761; Torp-Falk 430; Helten PBB XXX 249; Feist 459; Frisk Wahrheit 4–5; Mayrhofer III 422; Pokorny I 341; Onions 846; Chantraine 380–381 (to Gk ἐτεός ‘true’); Seebold Sprache XV 14–45 (interprets sunja as sb. ‘truth’, same as Frisk); Lehmann GED 329– 330; Bammesberger Nom. 202; Kluge- Seebold 809.

Goth sunno, dat. sg. sunnin ‘sun’, ON sunna id., OE sunne id., OS sunno id. (masc.), sunna id., OHG sunna id. Reflects an old n-stem of the heteroclytic stem also attested in Av gen. x vēng id. as opposed to nom. x var 6. In other Indo- European languages the l-stem is dominating: Skt súvar- id., Gk Hom ἠέλιος id., Lat sōl and the like. Bartholomae KZ XXVIII 12; Schulze KZ XXVII 428–429 (< *sul-n); Brugmann Grundriß II/1 303, 310 (reconstructs nom. *sunōn, gen. *sun-n-ez, with later generalization of *-nn-); Schere Gest. 45–51; Schwentner Zd Ph LXVI 1; Torp-Falk 444; Holthausen ASNSL CXIII 43–44 (from *senþanan), AEEW 330; Pisani AAL XI 782 (fem. in Goth); Walde-Hofmann II 553–554; Feist 460; Mayrhofer III 566–567; Pokorny I 881; Zalizniak II 177; Vries ANEW 561–562; Frisk I 631–632; Onions 885; Lehmann GED 330; Bammesberger Nom. 180, 206; Kluge-Seebold 771.
ON sunnan ‘from the South’, OE súäan id., OS sūthon id., OHG sundana ‘in the South’. Derived from *sunþiz. Torp-Falk 444; Holthausen AEEW 330; Vries ANEW 562.

ON suär ‘South’, OE adv. súä ‘southwards’, OFris sūth id., sūther id., OS adv. sūth ‘in the South’, sūthar-liuti ‘Southern people’, OHG adv. sund ‘in the South’, adj. sundar ‘southern’. Derived from *sunnōn (Brugmann IF XVIII 423– 426). Derivationally close to Toch A swāñce, B swāñco ‘ray, sunbeam’ < *sun-t- (Hilmarsson apud Adams TB 725). Schröder PBB XLVII 345 (to *swenþaz); Wood Language III 185 (from IE *s–to-); Torp-Falk 444; Holthausen AEEW 330; Huisman KZ LXXI 101 (to Gk ὑπέρ ‘over’); Pokorny I 881; Vries ANEW 559; Onions 848; Lehmann GED 330; Kluge-Seebold 808.
ON syäri ‘more southern’, OFris sūther ‘southern’. Derived from *sunþiz. Vries ANEW 572.
ON suärœnn ‘southern’, OE súäerne id., OFris sūthern id., OS sūärōni id., OHG sundrōni id. Derived from *sunþrjaz. Holthausen AEEW 330; Vries ANEW 559.

ON sonardreyri ‘pig blood’, OE sunor ‘herd of swine’, OHG swanur ‘herd’. Derived from *swīnan, *sūz. Torp-Falk 546; Holthausen AEEW 330; Fowkes Language XXII 347 (to Skt suváti ‘to set in motion, to vivify’); Vries ANEW 530 (to *saumaz).

Goth sunus ‘son’, ON sunr, sonr id., OE sunu id., OFris sunu id., OS sunu id., OHG sunu, sun id. Despite the difference in vocalism, continues (with oxytonic shortening) IE *sūnus id.: Skt sūnú-, Av hunu-, Lith sūnūs, Slav *synъ. Cf. also Toch B dim. soá≤ke id. Bartholomae 1831; Torp-Falk 441; Trautmann BSW 292; Holthausen AEEW 330; Feist 460–461; Porzig Gliederung 166; Mayrhofer III 494; Pokorny I 913; Dybo VSJa 24 (pretonic shortening of *ū in Gmc); Fraenkel 941–942; Vries ANEW 530; Zalizniak I 152; Onions 845; Benveniste IEL 191; Vasmer-Trubachev III 817–818; Lehmann GED 330–331; Bammesberger Nom. 161; Kluge-Seebold 769; Schrijver LL 354 (follows Dybo); Adams TB 702.
Goth supon ‘to salt, to season’, OHG soffōn ‘to spice, to enbalm’. Derived from *supōn. Feist 461; Lehmann GED 331.
ON sopi ‘mouthful’, OE sopa ‘sup, draught’ Derived from *sūpanan. Torp-Falk 445; Holthausen AEEW 307; Vries ANEW 530; Seebold 400; Lehmann GED 331.
ON soppa ‘sop’, OE soppe id., MLG soppe ‘soup’, MHG soppe ‘soup’. Related to *supōn. Torp-Falk 445; Holthausen AEEW 307; Onions 846 (to *sūpanan); Lehmann GED 331.
ON sorg-fullr ‘sorrowful’, OE sorh-full ‘full of care, solicitous’. Compound of *surzō and *fullaz. Carr Nom. comp. 60.

Goth saurgan ‘to be worried, to care’, ON syrgja ‘to sorrow, to mourn’, OE sorzian ‘to care, to be anxious’, OFris sorgia ‘to sorrow, to mourn’, OS sorgōn id., OHG sorgēn id. Derived from *surzō. Torp-Falk 446; Holthausen AEEW 307; Feist 413; Vries ANEW 530, 574; Onions 847; Lehmann GED 297.

Goth saurga ‘care, sorrow’, ON sorg ‘sorrow, care’ (i-stem), OE sorz ‘sorrow’, OS sorga id., OHG sorga id. Deverbative identical with Slav *sorgъ ‘strict, severe’ based on *s(⁄)ergh- attested in Toch A särk-, B sark- ‘sickness’, Lith sergū, siıgti ‘to be ill’, OIr serg ‘illness’ (Fick III 329). Kirchhoff KZ I 39; Schmidt Voc. II 76; Zupitza Gutt. 179, BB XV 104–105; Torp-Falk 446 (to Skt sūrkßati ‘to be sad’); Trautmann BSW 258; Holthausen AEEW 307; Feist 413; Pokorny I 1051 (to Skt sūrkßati ‘to take care of’); Fraenkel 787; Vries ANEW 531; Zalizniak II 198; Onions 847; Vasmer- Trubachev III 722; Hamp IF LXXIX 154–155; Lehmann GED 297; Lindeman IF XCVIII 52 (< *surgh-); Bammesberger Nom. 110; Kluge-Seebold 772; Burlak IFTJa 261.

ON soä ‘meat broth’, OE ze-sod ‘cooking, boiling’, OFris soth ‘broth’, OS soth id., OHG sod id. (masc.). Derived from *seuþanan. Torp-Falk 443; Holthausen AEEW 306; Vries ANEW 528; Seebold 401; Bammesberger Nom. 57.
ON súä ‘clinching of a ship’s boards’, MHG sūte ‘seam’. Derived from *siwjanan. Structurally close to Lat part. sūtus ‘sewn’. Torp-Falk 441; Walde- Hofmann II 631; Pokorny I 916; Vries ANEW 559.

ON súga ‘to suck’, OE súzan id., OS sūgan id., OHG sūgan id. A variant of *sūkanan. Zupitza Gutt. 139; Torp-Falk 443; Holthausen AEEW 329; Jóhannesson IEW 773–775; Pokorny I 912–913; Vries ANEW 560 (to Lat sūcus ‘juice, moisture, sap’, Lith sunkiū, suñkti ‘to squeeze out’); Onions 882; Seebold 398; Kluge-Seebold 706.
ON sÿsl, sÿsla ‘business, work’, OE súsl ‘torment’. Related to *seukaz. Holthausen AEEW 330; Lehmann GED 297.

OE súcan ‘to suck’, MDu suken id. Related to Lat sūgō id., OIr súgim id. (Brugmann II/3 128). Zupitza Gutt. 169; Torp-Falk 443; Holthausen AEEW 329; Walde- Hofmann II 622; Pokorny I 912–913; Onions 882; Seebold 399.

ON súl ‘pillar’, OE sÿl id., OFris sēle id., OS sūl id., OHG sūl id. Cf. also Goth sauls id. with a short *5. Identical with Gk ὕλη ‘wood’ (Persson Beiträge 383). Further probably connected to Lat solum ‘floor’, Gk ὑλία ‘sole’ (Hes.). Related to *swelljō. Torp-Falk 446 (to Gk ξυλόν ‘wood, timber’); Holthausen AEEW 339; Walde-Hofmann II 554; Feist 412–413; Pokorny I 899 (to *selmōn), 1046; Dybo VSJa 26; Vries ANEW 560; Zalizniak II 197; Frisk II 962–963; Lehmann GED 297; Kluge- Seebold 706.

ON súpa ‘to drink, to sup’, OE súpan id., OFris sūpa id., MLG sūpen id., OHG sūfan id. See *sūpō(n). The origin of the word is not clear. Torp- Falk 444–445; Holthausen AEEW 330; Jóhannesson IEW 773–775; Pokorny I 912–913; Vries ANEW 562; Onions 886; Seebold 399–400; Lehmann GED 331 (to IE *seu- ‘to rain, to flow’); Kluge- Seebold 706.
ON súpa ‘soup’, MHG sūf ‘drink’. Looks similar to Skt sūpa- ‘soup, broth’ but the unvoiced -p- creates a problem. Torp-Falk 445; Mayrhofer III 494; Pokorny I 913; Vries ANEW 562.

ON súr-eygr ‘bleareyed’, OE súr-íze id., OS sūr-ōgi id., OHG sūr-ougi id. Compound of *sūraz and *auzjaz. Persson UUÅ 1891 9 (to ON saurr ‘mud, dirt’ and *sūzan); Holthausen AEEW 330; Carr Nom. comp. 66; Vries ANEW 562.

ON súrr ‘acid, sour’, OE súr ‘sour’, MLG sūr id., OHG sūr id. Related to Lith sūras ‘salty’, Slav *syrъ ‘wet, raw’. Cf. also Illyr rvn. Syr-apus (Krahe Glotta XX 188–189). Karsten NB II 249– 250, 451; Torp-Falk 446; Trautmann BSW 293–294; Būga RFV LXXV 148; Holthausen AEEW 330; Pokorny I 1039; Dybo VSJa 26; Fraenkel 944– 945; Vries ANEW 562; Onions 848; Seebold Festschr. Matzel 124–125; Vasmer-Trubachev III 819; Heidermanns 568–569; Kluge-Seebold 706.

ON sÿr ‘sow’, OE sú id., OS sū id. (i-stem), OHG sū id. (i-stem). Related to Toch B suwo id., Av hū id., Gk ὗς id., Alb thi (Försterman KZ I 493). Hamel APS II 31–42; Kretschmer Glotta XIII 132–138; Bartholomae 1817; Torp- Falk 443; Holthausen AEEW 329; Pokorny I 1038; Vries ANEW 574; Zalizniak II 210; Frisk II 973–974; Onions 849; Griepentrog Wurzelnomina 373–393; Kluge-Seebold 705–706; Orel AED 477; Adams TB 698.

Norw svabba ‘to splash’, E swab ‘to swab, to mop’, MLG swabben. Related to Lat supō ‘to throw, to scatter’, Slav *sypati ‘to pour, to strew’. Trautmann BSW 293; Walde-Hofmann I 356; Pokorny I 1049; Onions 891.

ON svefja ‘to lull to sleep’, OE swebban ‘to send to sleep, to lull, to kill’, OS an-swebbian ‘to fall asleep’, OHG in-swebben ‘to lull to sleep’. Causative of *swefanan. Structurally close to Skt svāpáyati ‘to make sleep’, Lat sōpiō ‘to make sleep’. Torp-Falk 548; Holthausen AEEW 334; Walde-Hofmann II 561; Mayrhofer III 561; Pokorny I 1048; Vries ANEW 566; Seebold 483.
ON sveifla ‘to swing’, EFris sweifeln ‘to sway’, MHG sweibeln id. Related to *swīfanan. Vries ANEW 566.
ON sveigja ‘to bend, to bow’, ME sweigh ‘to sweep, to swing’. Related to Lith svaigiū, svaĩgti ‘to waver, to become dizzy’ (Wood MLN XVI 21). Pokorny I 1042; Fraenkel 947; Vries ANEW 566; Onions 892.
ON sveimr ‘soaring, stir’, MHG sweim ‘soaring, rambling’. Derived from *swīmanan. Torp-Falk 553; Vries ANEW 566.
ON sveima ‘to wander, to soar’, MLG sweimen ‘to sway’, MHG sweimen ‘to swing, to hover, to hang’. Derived from *swaimaz, *swīmanan. Holthausen AEEW 331; Vries ANEW 566–567.

ON sveinn ‘boy, servant, attendant’, OE swán ‘herd, swine-herd’, OS swēn id., OHG swein ‘servant, shepherd’. Derived from *swīnan. Torp- Falk 543 (from *s⁄e- ‘(his) own’, cf. Lith svaĩnis ‘brother-in-law’); Holthausen AEEW 332; Pokorny I 884; Vries ANEW 567; Zalizniak I 136; Onions 891.

ON sveipa ‘to sweep, to stroke, to wrap’ (relic str. forms), OE swápan ‘to sweep’, OS pret. sg. for-suuēp ‘to drive out’, OHG sweifan ‘to wrestle’. Probably related to Av xšviwra- ‘rapid’, xšaēwayat-aštra- ‘swinging the whip’. Bartholomae 563; Torp-Falk 555; Holthausen AEEW 332; Jóhannesson IEW 794–797; Pokorny I 1041–1042; Vries ANEW 567; Seebold 479–480; Lehmann GED 252–253; Kluge-Seebold 749. *swaipaz I sb.m.: ON sveipr ‘fold, hood’, OHG sweif ‘cloak’. Substantivized *swaipaz II or direct derivative of *swaipanan. Torp-Falk 555; Vries ANEW 567; Seebold 479; Lehmann GED 252– 253.
ON auä-sveipr ‘pliable, yielding’, MHG wīt-sweif ‘wandering around’. Derived from *swaipanan. Karsten NB I 112; Matzel Festschr. Schröder 107, KZ CV 128; Heidermanns 571.
ON sveipa ‘to sweep, to stroke, to wrap’, ME swépen ‘to sweep’, OHG gi-sweifen id. Causative of *swaipanan. Torp-Falk 555.

ON sveiti ‘sweat’ (< *swaitjan), OE swát id., OFris swēt ‘sweat, perspiration’, OS swēt id., OHG sweiz ‘sweat, blood of animals’. Identical with Skt svéda- ‘sweat’, Av x vaēda- id. and further related to Arm k'irtn id., Gk Ion ἶδος id., Lat sūdor id., Alb djersë id. < PAlb *swidertjā, Latv sviêdri id. Connected with *switjanan. Bartholomae 1860; Torp-Falk 554; Trautmann BSW 295; Holthausen AEEW 333; Walde-Hofmann II 623–624; Mayrhofer III 570; Pokorny I 1043; Vries ANEW 567; Zalizniak II 179; Frisk I 709–710; Onions 893; Kluge-Seebold 749; Orel AED 69.

ON sveitask ‘to sweat’, OE swætan id., MLG swēten id., OHG sweizen ‘to fry, to melt’. Causativeiterative connected with *swaitaz, *switjanan. Derivationally close to Skt svedayati ‘to make sweat’. Torp-Falk 554; Holthausen AEEW 332; Pokorny I 1043; Vries ANEW 567; Onions 893.
ON svelgr ‘swirl, whirlpool’, OE ze-swelze ‘abyss, gulf’ (neut.). Derived from *swelzanan. Torp-Falk 552; Seebold 489; Bammesberger Nom. 134.

ON svala ‘swallow’, OE swealwe id., OFris swale id., OS swala id., OHG swalawa id. Derived from *swelzanan. Saussureapud Vries (to Gk ēλκυών ‘mythical bird, identified with the kingfisher, Alcedo ispida’); Wood MLN XXI 228 (to Latv svelpju ‘to whistle’); Solmsen Afsl Ph XXIV 575–576 (to Slav *solovьjь ‘nightingale’); Torp-Falk 552–553; Holthausen AEEW 333; Vries ANEW 563; Zalizniak I 156; Onions 891; Kluge-Seebold 747.

Goth swaleiks ‘such’, ON slíkr id., OE swelc, swilc id., OFris sulik, selik id., OS sulik id., OHG sulih id. Derived from *swō(t). Zupitza Gutt. 169; Torp-Falk 543; Holthausen AEEW 334; Feist 463; Vries ANEW 515–516; Onions 882; Lehmann GED 332; Kluge-Seebold 769.
ON svella ‘to make swell’, OHG bi-swellen ‘to block’. Derived from *swellanan. Torp-Falk 551; Vries ANEW 567; Seebold 490.
ON svelta ‘to put to death’, OE sweltan ‘to die’. Causative of *sweltanan. Holthausen AEEW 335; Seebold 491.

Goth swamms ‘sponge’, ON svǫppr ‘ball, sponge’ (u-stem), OE swamm ‘mushroom’, MLG swam, swamp ‘sponge’, OHG swam, swamb ‘fungus, mushroom’. Despite the difference in the inlaut consonantism, to be compared with Gk σομφός ‘swampy, porous’ (Kuhn KZ IV 17; Curtius Gr. Et. 380). Luft KZ XXXVI 147 (to Lat fungus ‘toadstool, mushroom’ < *sg⁄hongo-); Kluge Glotta II 55 (same as Luft); Pedersen MPKJ I 165–167 (to Lith gu Mbas ‘sponge’, Slav *gǫba id.); Torp-Falk 549; Holthausen AEEW 332; Feist 463; Pokorny I 1052; Chemodanov SGGJa I 109; Vries ANEW 530; Frisk II 753–754; Lehmann GED 332; Kluge-Seebold 747.

ON svanr ‘swan’, OE swan id., OS swan id., OHG swan, swano id. Identical with Skt svaná- ‘sound’, Lat sonus id. (Suolahti DVN 408). Further related to Skt svánati ‘to sound’, Lat sonō id. Suolahti DVN 408; Torp-Falk 546; Holthausen AEEW 332; Walde- Hofmann II 559; Porzig Gliederung 180; Pokorny I 1046–1047; Vries ANEW 564; Onions 892; Bammesberger Nom. 51; Kluge-Seebold 747.

ON svangr ‘thin, slender, slim’, MLG swank ‘unstable, wavering’, MHG swanc id. Derived from *swenzwanan. Torp-Falk 547; Kluge- Seebold 748. *swanzwjanan wk.vb: Goth af-swaggwjan ‘to cause to waver’, OE swenzan ‘to cause to swing’, OFris swenga ‘to water’, MLG swengen ‘to rock, to waver’, OHG swenken ‘to beat, to whip’. Causative of *swenzwanan. Johansson Beitr. Gesch. XV 237 (to Skt váñcati ‘to move crookedly’); Torp-Falk 547; Holthausen AEEW 335; Feist 10; Onions 894; Seebold 493; Lehmann GED 7; Kluge-Seebold 750.
Norw dial. svokk ‘arch of the foot’, OE swenc ‘trial affliction’, MLG swanc ‘turn’, MHG swanc ‘swinging movement’. Derived from *swenkanan. Torp-Falk 546–547.

ON svar ‘answer’, OE and-swaru id., OFris ond-ser id.. Derived from *swarjanan. Structurally close to Slav *svarъ ‘quarrel, fight’, *svara id. Osthoff BB XXIV 211–213; Torp- Falk 549; Holthausen AEEW 333; Pokorny I 1049; Vries ANEW 565; Onions 39; Seebold 481; Lehmann GED 333; Bammesberger Nom. 63.

ON svarf ‘file-dust’, OHG pl. swarpun ‘gurgitibus’. Derived from *swerbanan. Formally identical with Slav *svorbъ ‘scab, rash’. Torp-Falk 551; Trautmann BSW 295; Seebold 495; Vasmer-Trubachev III 583–584.
ON svarfa ‘to sweep, to swerve’, EFris swarven ‘to move around’, LG swarven id. Derived from *swerbanan. Vries ANEW 565.

ON svǫrär ‘skin’, OE sweard, swearä ‘skin, hide’, OFris swarde ‘scalp’, MLG swarde id., MHG swart ‘scalp, bacon crust’. Derived from *swerđanan. Torp-Falk 550; Holthausen AEEW 333; Pokorny I 1050 (to OHG str. sweran ‘to ache, to pain’); Vries ANEW 572 (to Latv šerve ‘thick skin’); Onions 892; Kluge- Seebold 748.

Goth swaran ‘to swear’ (secondary absence of j-present), ON sverja id., OE swerian id., OFris swera id., OS swerian id., OHG swerren id. Probably connected with OESc dat. sg. sverunneí ‘arbiter’ < *s⁄er-sōn-, Slav *svara ‘quarrel’. Grienberger Got. 204 (to IE *⁄er- ‘to speak’); Torp-Falk 549; Feist 463–464; Holthausen AEEW 336, AWN 290; Kluge 695–696; Krahe Su V 77; Jóhannesson IEW 803; Pokorny I 1049; Vries ANEW 568; Onions 892; Seebold 480–482; Benveniste IEL 434, 475; Vasmer-Trubachev III 569; Lehmann GED 333; Kluge-Seebold 752.

Norw svarmr ‘swarm’, OE swearm id., MLG swarm id., OHG swarm id. Derived from *swarjanan? Torp-Falk 549 (from *swarbmaz, cf. G dial. swurbel id., or to Lith surmā ‘pipe, reed-pipe’ < *s⁄er- ‘to sound’); Holthausen AEEW 333; Pokorny I 1049– 1050 (to IE *s⁄er- ‘to sound’); Vries ANEW 565; Onions 892; Kluge- Seebold 748.
ON svara ‘to answer’, OE and-swarian id., OFris andswara ‘to take responsibility’. Derived from *swaran ~ *swarō. Holthausen AEEW 333; Pokorny I 1049; Vries ANEW 565; Benveniste IEL 434.
Goth ufar-swara ‘perjurer’, ON mein-svari id., OE áä-swara ‘oath’, OFris swara ‘juror’. Derived from *swarjanan. Feist 510; Seebold 481; Bammesberger Nom. 177–178.

Goth swarts ‘black’, ON svartr id., OE sweart ‘black, dark’, OFris swart ‘black’, OS swart ‘black, dark’, OHG swarz ‘black’. Related to Lat sordēs ‘dirt’, from *sordus < *s⁄ordo- ‘dirty’ (Schmidt KZ XIX 270). Further cf. Balkanic rvn. Βαρδάριος < *(s)⁄ordo-⁄ori- ‘black water’ (Georgiev Isseldov. 114). Curtius KZ I 268; Niedermann IF X 230; Persson Beiträge 167; Osthoff Etym. 92–97 (to Lat sorbus ‘sorb-tree’ < *s⁄ord- ⁄o-); Torp-Falk 550; Schwentner FB 4–14; Feist 464; Holthausen AEEW 334; Walde-Hofmann II 562–563; Szemerényi Kratylos II 122; Pokorny I 1052; Chemodanov SGGJa I 91; Vries ANEW 565; Onions 892; Lehmann GED 333; Heidermanns 574–575; Kluge- Seebold 748.
OHG ir-swarzēn ‘to become black’. Derived from *swartaz. Structurally close to Lat sordēre ‘to be dirty’. Walde-Hofmann II 562; Vries ANEW 530; Heidermanns 574.

ON svaä ‘slide, a slippery place’, OE swaäu ‘track, trace’, swæä id., OFris swethe ‘boundary, track, trace’, MLG swad ‘row of mown grass’, MHG masc. swade id. Of unknown origin. Torp-Falk 545 (part. of *swējanan > MLG swāien ‘to swing’); Holthausen AEEW 333; Pokorny I 1041; Onions 892.

Goth swibls ‘sulfur’, OE swefel id., OS sweval id., OHG swebal, sweval id. Derived from IE *s⁄ep- ‘to throw, to pour’: Slav *sъpǫ, *suti, Lith supū, sūpti ‘to rock, to swing’, Lat supō. For the semantic development cf. Slav *sîra ‘sulphur’ ~ Lat serum ‘whey’ < IE *ser- ‘to flow’ (Trubachev Etim. 1968 38–40). Slotty IF LI 147 (a Mediterranean Wanderwort); Zupitza BB XXV 93–94; Grienberger Got. 205 (derived from *swefanan: sulfur vapors cause sleep); Much ZDADL XLII 165– 166 (reconstructs *s⁄elk ⁄lo- > *swelblaz); Hirt Idg. Gr. I 309 (related to Lat sulp(h)ur); Brüch Glotta XXI 72–75 (from *swezwlaz, follows Hirt); Torp-Falk 548 (reconstruct Gmc *swelplaz); Trautmann BSW 293; Walde-Pokorny II 533; Holthausen AEEW 334; Walde- Hofmann II 628; Feist 466–467; Pokorny I 909–910, 1046 (same as Hirt); Fraenkel 943–944; Zalizniak I 136; Vasmer-Trubachev III 818; Lehmann GED 335; Kluge-Seebold 749; Adams TB 689–690 (to Toch AB sälp- ‘to be set alight’).

ON svefn ‘sleep’, OE neut. swefen id., OS sweban id. Derived from *swefanan. Continues IE *s⁄opnos ~ *s⁄epnos ~ *supnos id.: Toch A ßpäá, B ßpane, Skt svápna-, Av x vafna-, Gk ὕπνος, Alb gjumë, Lat somnus, OIr súan, Lith σάπνασ, Slav *sъnъ (Bugge KZ XX 40–41). Meillet Etudes 383; Bartholomae 1863–1864; Torp-Falk 548; Pedersen Kelt. Gr. I 94; Trautmann BSW 292; Holthausen AEEW 334; Walde-Hofmann II 557–558; Porzig Gliederung 179; Mayrhofer III 561–562; Pokorny I 1048; Fraenkel 762; Vries ANEW 566; Zalizniak II 172; Frisk II 970–971; Seebold 482; Vasmer- Trubachev III 716–717; Bammesberger Nom. 73; Orel AED 138; Adams TB 666.

ON sofa ‘to sleep’, OE swefan id. Related to Hitt šup- ‘to sleep’, Skt svápiti id., Av -ap- id., Lat sopor ‘sleep’, Slav *sъpati id. See *swebnaz. Bartholomae 1862–1863; Torp-Falk 548; Holthausen AEEW 334, AWN 271, 297; Walde-Hofmann II 561; Jóhannesson IEW 800; Mayrhofer III 561; Pokorny I 1048; Vries ANEW 528; Seebold 482–483; Vasmer-Trubachev III 732– 733.
ON sofna ‘to fall asleep’, OE swefnian ‘to appear in a dream, to dream’. Derived from *swebnaz. Structurally close to Slav *sъnǫti ‘to fall asleep’. Vries ANEW 528; Vasmer- Trubachev III 733.
Goth swiglon ‘to play the flute’, OHG swegalōn id. Derived from *swezlō. Fröhde BB III 15, XIV 111; Persson Beiträge 355; Torp-Falk 554; Feist 467; Lehmann GED 335.

OE swezel ‘music (?)’, OHG swegala, swegel ‘pipe’. An onomatopoeic formation. Hardly related to Lat sībilus, sīfilus ‘hissing, whistling’ (cf. sūbulō ‘flutist’ < Etr ≤uplu). Torp-Falk 554; Holthausen AEEW 334; Walde- Hofmann II 531–532, 620; Pokorny I 1040–1041; Lehmann GED 335.

Goth swaihro ‘mother-inlaw’, ON sværa id., OE swér id., swehor id., OFris swiār id. Cf. also *swezrō > OE swezer id., MDu sweger id., OHG swigar id. Transformation of IE *s⁄e˚rū id.: Skt śvaśrū-, Gk ἵκυρά, Arm skesur, Alb vjehërr, Lat socrus, W chwegr, Slav *svekry. Torp-Falk 544; Schulze Kl. Schr. 60–62; Holthausen AEEW 334, 336; Walde- Hofmann II 550–551; Feist 462–463; Mayrhofer III 400–401; Pokorny I 1043–1044; Trubachev Rod. 118–120; Vries ANEW 571; Zalizniak II 181; Frisk I 478–479; Vasmer-Trubachev III 572; Benveniste IEL 201; Lehmann GED 332; Bammesberger Nom. 179; Kluge- Seebold 751; Orel AED 510–511.

Goth swaihra ‘father-inlaw’ (n-stem), OSwed svér id., svær, OE swehor, swéor id., MDu sweer id., OHG swehur id. Related to Skt śváśura- id., Gk ἵκυρός id., Alb vjehërr id., Lat socer id., Lith “‚šuras id., Slav *svekrъ id. continuing IE *s⁄e˚uros. Zupitza Gutt. 191; Torp- Falk 544; Trautmann BSW 295; Holthausen AEEW 335; Schulze Kl. Schr. 69, KZ XL 400–401; Walde- Hofmann II 550–551; Feist 462–463; Mayrhofer III 400–401; Pokorny I 1043–1044; Fraenkel 977; Zalizniak II 179; Frisk I 478–479; Benveniste IEL 201; Vasmer-Trubachev III 571–572; Lehmann GED 332; Kluge-Seebold 747; Orel AED 510–511.

Goth swikns ‘pure, innocent’, ON sykn ‘innocent’. Continues *s⁄e-·no-, a compound of refl. *s⁄e- and a form of *·en 6- ‘to give birth’ as in Gk νεογνός ‘newly born’, Lat mali-gnus ‘wicked, ill-disposed’, Gaul -cnos in proper names (e.g. Truticnos), Venet volti-xnos. Bugge KZ XX (1872) 34 (from *su- ‘good’ and *ƒa·no- as in Gk ēγνός ‘pure, chaste, holy’); Bezzenberger BB IV 354 (to Gk σιγαλόεις ‘shining’); Karsten NB II 211–212; Wood MLN XXV 75 (to *swīk(w)anan); Fröhde KZ XXIII 311– 312 (to Gk σεμνός ‘holy’, σέβομαι ‘to revere’); Zupitza Gutt. 199; Zubaty BB XVII 325–326 (to Slav *svîžь ‘fresh’); Torp-Falk 554; Mikkola IF XXIII 126 (same as Zubaty); Feist 467; Holthausen AEEW 336; Walde-Hofmann II 16; Mezger Language XIX 261–262 (from *s⁄e- and *·nō- ‘to recognize, to know’); Jóhannesson IEW 791 (to IE *su- ‘good’); Pokorny I 375; Vries ANEW 572; Lehmann GED 335–336; Mayrhofer Diachronica IV 249; Lühr Expr. 339; Heidermanns 582 (reconstructs *swiknaz).
ON sykn ‘absolution’, OE swicn ‘clearance from a criminal charge’. Substantivized *sweknaz. Torp- Falk 554; Holthausen AEEW 336; Heidermanns 582.

OE swelan ‘to burn’, OHG pres. er-swilit ‘obcaluit’. Identical with Skt svarati ‘to shine’, cf. also Lith sv”lū, svīlti ‘to singe, to smoulder’. Torp- Falk 551; Holthausen AEEW 334; Mayrhofer III 563; Pokorny I 1045; Fraenkel 954; Vries ANEW 564; Seebold 480; Kluge-Seebold 750; Dybo BSA 21.

ON svelgja ‘to swallow’, OE swelzan id., MLG swelgen id., OHG fir-swelgan id. Unclear. Kirchhoff KZ I 39; Specht KZ LXVI 25–26; Torp-Falk 552; Holthausen AEEW 334; Jóhannesson IEW 805; Pokorny I 1045; Vries ANEW 567; Onions 891; Seebold 488–489; Kluge- Seebold 750.
ON svell ‘lump of ice’, OE ze-swell ‘swelling, tumor’, MDu geswelle id. Derived from *swellanan. Vries ANEW 567.

ON svella ‘to swell’, OE swellan id., OFris pres. swella id., OS swellan id., OHG swellan id. Derived from *swelanan? Curtius Gr. Et. 372 (to Lat salum ‘open sea, high sea’); Sommer Gr. 112 (to Gk ἅλις ‘enough’); Pokrowskij KZ XXXV 230–231 (to Lat īnsolēscō ‘to become unusual’); Torp-Falk 551; Feist 513; Holthausen AEEW 334–335; Walde-Hofmann I 704–705 (to Lat īnsolēns ‘insolent’); Jóhannesson IEW 806; Pokorny I 1045; Vries ANEW 567; Onions 893; Fraenkel II 758 (to Lith sálti ‘to flow’); Seebold 489–490; Lehmann GED 373; Kluge-Seebold 750.

ON syll, svill ‘threshold, sill’, OE syll ‘sill, support’, MLG sul, sulle id. (masc.), OHG swelli ‘sockle, foot-support’ (neut.). Connected with *sūlō ~ *sūliz. Torp-Falk 552; Holthausen AEEW 340; Pokorny I 1046; Vries ANEW 573 (to Gk σελίς ‘cross-beam, junction’); Zalizniak II 211; Onions 827.

Goth swiltan ‘to die’, ON svelta ‘to starve, to die’, OE sweltan ‘to die’, OS sweltan id., OHG swelzan ‘to burn slowly, to smoulder’ (hap. leg.). Derived from *swelanan. Pedersen KZ XXXIX 429 (to Arm k'a∑c' ‘hunger’ < *s⁄¬d-sko-); Torp-Falk 552; Feist 468; Holthausen AEEW 335; Jóhannesson IEW 806–807; Pokorny I 1045; Vries ANEW 567–568; Onions 893; Seebold 490–491; Lehmann GED 336.

ON svimma, svima ‘to swim’, OE swimman id., OFris swimma id., MLG swümmen id., OHG swimman id. Secondary ablaut type based on *swīmanan. Torp-Falk 548–549; Holthausen AEEW 337; Walde-Pokorny 2 254 (to Lith sūmdyti ‘to hunt’); Martinet Gém. 71; Jóhannesson IEW 801; Pokorny I 1046 (to OIr do-sennaim ‘to hunt’ < *-s⁄emdhō); Fraenkel 788 (against Walde-Pokorny); Vries ANEW 570; Onions 893–894; Seebold 491–492; Lehmann GED 337; Kluge-Seebold 751.

OE swinzan ‘to swinge, to flog, to give a blow’, OFris swinga ‘to water’, MLG swingen ‘to swing’, OHG swingan ‘to flog’. Related to Skt svájate ‘to embrace, to clasp’ (Wood MP V 285). Reichelt KZ XXXIX 78 (to Lith sūkti ‘to turn’); Zupitza Gutt. 70; Torp- Falk 547; Holthausen AEEW 338; Pokorny I 1047–1048; Onions 894 (to *senkwanan); Seebold 493; Kluge- Seebold 752.
OE swincan ‘to toil, to labor’. Related to OIr seng ‘slender, slim’ < *s⁄engo-. Torp-Falk 546; Pokorny I 1047.
ON svinn-ligr ‘sensible’, OE swíä-líc ‘great, violent’. Derived from *swenþaz. Heidermanns 577.

Goth swinþs ‘strong’, ON svinnr, sviär ‘quick, swift’, OE swíä ‘strong’, OFris adv. swīthe ‘very, hard’, OS swīth ‘strong’, MHG swint, swinde id. Related to *sunđaz. An alternative explanation based on Slav *svętъ ‘holy’ is less probable. Grienberger Got. 206; Persson Beiträge 189, 587 (to Lith siun‘iū, siûsti ‘to send’); Johansson Festschr. Bugge 28 (to Av spentō ‘holy’, Slav *svętъ id.); Fröhde BB XIV 109–110 (to Lat sonticus ‘hazardous’); Persson Beiträge 189 (to Skt suváti ‘to set in motion’); Torp-Falk 547; Feist 468–469; Holthausen AEEW 330, 338; Kluge 262 (from *s·hu-, to *se·h- ‘to capture’), 264; Specht 128 (to Lith sveĩkas ‘healthy’); Hoops EG V 82–83; Pokorny I 1048; Vries ANEW 570; Onions 848; Piirainen 48–49; Seebold Sprache XXIX 32–33; Lehmann GED 336; Bammesberger Nom. 251 (from *sṃ-tó- ‘whole’); Heidermanns 577–578, 586; Kluge- Seebold 319.
Goth swinþei ‘strength’, MHG swinde id. Derived from *swenþaz. Feist 468; Lehmann GED 336.
Goth ga-swinþjan ‘to strengthen’, OE swíäan id. Derived from *swenþaz. Holthausen AEEW 338; Feist 468; Lehmann GED 336; Heidermanns 577.

Goth af-swairban ‘to wipe away’, ON sverfa ‘to file, to whirl around’, OE sweorfan ‘to rub, to scour, to file’, OFris pres. swerva ‘to crawl’, OS pret. sg. swarf ‘to wipe away’, OHG swerban ‘to wipe, to make dry’. Related to Slav *svьrbîti ‘to itch’. Kirchhoff KZ I 39; Persson UUÅ 1891 55 (to Gk σύρφος, Hes.); Much ZDADL XLII 169 (to the unattested W chwerfu ‘whirling, turning’); Zupitza Gutt. 57; Torp-Falk 550–551; Feist 10–11; Holthausen AEEW 335; Jóhannesson IEW 805; Pokorny I 1050–1051; Vries ANEW 568; Stang LS 57; Seebold 494–495; Vasmer-Trubachev III 573; Lehmann GED 7.
ON sverä-berendr ‘warrior’, OE sweord-berende ‘sword-bearing’. Compound of *swerđan and *beranđz, participle of *beranan. Carr Nom. comp. 61.
ON sverä-fetill ‘sword-strap’, MHG swert-vezzel id. Compound of *swerđan and *fatilaz. Carr Nom. comp. 52.

ON sverä ‘sword’, OE sweord id., OFris swerd id., OS swerd id., OHG swert id. Etymologically close to Slav *svьrdьlъ ‘drill’ < *s⁄ṛdh-ilo- (Schrader-Nehring I 160). Krogmann KZ LIX 204 (to OHG sweran ‘to hurt, to pain’); Lidén PBB XV 518 (to Lat sorbus ‘sorb-tree’); Sperber Wu S VI 39 (to *swarđuz ~ *swarđō); Pipping SNF XVI/2 38 (same as Lidén); Torp-Falk 550 (to Gk ἄορ ‘sword’); Krogmann KZ LIX 204 (to OHG sweran ‘to ache, to pain’); Walde-Pokorny I 265; Holthausen AEEW 335; Pokorny I 1050; Vries ANEW 568; Zalizniak I 143; Onions 894; Trubachev Rem. 155, 363, 375; Vasmer-Trubachev III 573–574; Stang LS 57; Kluge-Seebold 751.

ON svíri ‘neck, nape’, OE sweora, swira id. Related to Alb z-verk ‘occiput’ < PAlb *-swerka. Zupitza Gutt. 50; Holthausen AEEW 337 (to Lat sūrus ‘branch’); Pokorny I 1050 (to Skt sváru- ‘pole’); Vries ANEW 571 (to OE sweor ‘column, pillar’, MHG swir ‘riverside post’); Huld KZ CVII/1 165–171 (Alb < Goth *swairhs); Orel AED 526– 527.

Goth swistar ‘sister’, ON systir id., OE sweostor id., OFris swester id., OS swestar id., OHG swester id. Continues IE *s⁄esor- id.: Toch A ßar, B ßer, Skt svásar-, Arm k'oyr, Gk đor: θυγάτηρ, ἀνεψιός (Hes.), Lat soror, OIr siur, Lith sesuõ, Slav *sestra. Generalized Gmc *-str- < *-sr- in oblique cases (Brugmann Grundriß I 776, II/1 333). Pedersen Kelt. Gr. I 73; Torp-Falk 544; Trautmann BSW 258; Holthausen AEEW 335–336; Walde-Hofmann II 563; Feist 469; Porzig Gliederung 79; Mayrhofer III 565; Pokorny I 1051; Fraenkel 777– 778; Vries ANEW 574; Zalizniak I 157; Frisk I 530–531; Onions 830; Szemerényi Kinship 313–318; Benveniste IEL 173; Vasmer-Trubachev III 612; Lehmann GED 336–337; Bammesberger Nom. 208; Kluge-Seebold 751; Adams TB 661; Burlak IFTJa 259.
OSwed swiri ‘cousin’, OFris swire ‘status of cousin’, OE ze-swiria ‘nephew, sister’s son’ (masc. n-stem). Derived from *swēsaz. Holthausen AEEW 338; Pokorny I 882; Lehmann GED 336.

ON Sváfa-land ‘land of Swabians’, OE pl. Swæfe ‘Swabians’, OHG pl. Swāba id. Cf. also Lat-Germ Suēbi, Suēvi. Based on the possessive *s⁄e-. Holthausen AEEW 331; Pokorny I 882–883; Vries ANEW 563; Orel JSL V/1 144–149 (against the connection with Slav *svoboda ‘freedom’).

OFris swāger ‘brotherin-law’, MLG swāger id., OHG swāger, swāgur id. Vṛddhi of *swex(u)raz, structurally identical with Skt śvāśura- ‘of the father-in-law’ (Persson IF II 201). Torp-Falk 544; Schulze Kl. Schr. 64–65; Mayrhofer III 400; Pokorny I 1043– 1044; Lehmann GED 332; Bammesberger IF LXXIII 133–135 (OFris swāger < OHG).

ON svæla ‘to suffocate with smoke’, OE swælan ‘to burn’. Derived from *swelanan. Torp-Falk 551; Holthausen AEEW 331; Vries ANEW 571 (to ON svalr ‘cool, fresh’); Seebold 488; Kluge-Seebold 750; Dybo BSA 21.

Goth swers ‘valued, honored’, ON svárr ‘heavy’, OE swær id., OFris swēr id., OS swār id., OHG swār id. Related to Lith sveriū, sveıti ‘to weigh, to balance’, svarūs ‘heavy’, Latv svars ‘weight’. Cf. also Lat sērius ‘grave, earnest’ (Uhlenbeck PBB XXX 312). Karsten NB II 364–365; Hirt Idg. Gr. I 331 (to *g ⁄er- ‘heavy’); Torp-Falk 550; Feist 466; Holthausen AEEW 331; Walde-Hofmann II 521; Pokorny I 1150–1151; Fraenkel 951; Vries ANEW 565; Lehmann GED 334–335; Heidermanns 578–579; Kluge-Seebold 750.
Goth sweran ‘to honor’, OFris swēria ‘to be too heavy’, OHG ana-ir-swārēn ‘to get worse’. Derived from *swēraz. Feist 465–466; Lehmann GED 334; Heidermanns 579.
Goth sweriþa ‘honor’, OHG swārida ‘load, weight’. Derived from *swēraz. Feist 466; Lehmann GED 334; Heidermanns 579.
Goth un-swerei ‘dishonor’, OHG swārī ‘heaviness, load’. Derived from *swēraz. Feist 466; Heidermanns 579.
ON svás-ligr ‘lovely, delightful’, OE swæs-líc ‘kindly, pleasant’, OFris swēs-lik ‘kinsmanlike’, OS swēs-līk ‘native’, OHG swās-līh ‘own, private’. Derived from *swēsaz. Heidermanns 580.

Goth swes ‘one’s own’, ON sváss id., OE swæs id., OFris swēs ‘related’, OS swās ‘own, nice, cosy’, OHG swās ‘private, intimate, homely’. Derived from IE *s⁄e-: Skt svá- ‘own’, Av hva- id., Gk ὅς id., Lat suus id., OPrus swais, Slav *svojь and the like (Diefenbach VW II 263). Bartholomae 1845–1846; Torp-Falk 543; Uhlenbeck PBB XXVII 132 (from *s⁄ēdh-to-); Trautmann BSW 251–252; Walde-Hofmann II 626; Feist 466; Holthausen AEEW 331–332; Mayrhofer III 566; Pokorny I 882; Vries ANEW 565–566; Frisk I 431; Vasmer- Trubachev III 583; Benveniste IEL 269; Lehmann GED 335; Heidermanns 579– 580.
MHG swifte ‘silent’. Derived from *swībanan. Derivationally close to Messap σίπτα: σιώπα. Μεσσάπιοι, Hes. < *s⁄ipto- (Krahe IF XLVII 327). Torp-Falk 556; Kluge 78; Pokorny I 1052; Heidermanns 581.
ON svik ‘treason, fraud’, OE swic ‘deception, illusion’. Cf. also OHG swīh id. < *swīkaz. Derived from *swīk(w)anan. Torp-Falk 553.

ON svikall ‘treacherous’, OE swicol ‘deceitful, false’, OHG bi-swihhil ‘insidious’. Derived from *swīk(w)anan. Karsten NB II 276, 284; Holthausen AEEW 336; Vries ANEW 569; Seebold 486; Heidermanns 582–583.
ON svina ‘to subside’, OHG swinōn ‘flaccere’. Derived from *swīnanan. Jóhannesson IEW 807– 808; Vries ANEW 570; Seebold 484.
ON svipr ‘swoop, sudden loss’, MHG umbe-swif ‘turn, turning, ambit’. Derived from *swaipanan. Torp- Falk 555; Vries ANEW 571.
ON svipa ‘whip’, OE swipe id., MLG swepe id. Related to *swaipanan. Torp-Falk 555; Holthausen AEEW 338; Vries ANEW 570; Seebold 480.
OHG swizzen ‘to sweat’. Identical with Skt svidyate id. Related to *swaitaz. Torp-Falk 554; Mayrhofer III 570; Pokorny I 1043; Kluge-Seebold 752.

ON svífa ‘to rove, to turn, to drift’, OE swífan ‘to move, to sweep’, OFris pres. swīva ‘to be uncertain’, MHG pret. sg. sweif ‘to swing’. Cf. also Goth sweiban ‘to cease’. Related to *swaipanan, *swaizjanan. Persson BB XIX 263–266 (to Gk σιωπή ‘silence’); Holthausen AEEW 337; Torp-Falk 555–556; Feist 465; Jóhannesson IEW 808 (to Gk σιγή ‘silence’); Pokorny I 1042 (to Latv svipstis ‘coward’); Vries ANEW 569; Onions 893; Seebold 484–485; Lehmann GED 334; Kluge- Seebold 749.

ON svíkva, svíkja ‘to betray’, OE swícan ‘to move about, to wander’, OFris swīka ‘to remove’, OS swīkan ‘to desert, to leave in the lurch’, OHG swīhhan ‘to turn away, to decline’. Related to Lith svaigiū, svaĩgti ‘to be dizzy’, Slav *svigati ‘to run after, to hurry’. Torp-Falk 553–554; Holthausen AEEW 336; Schneider KZ LXVI 253 (to Toch B waike ‘lie’); Jóhanesson IEW 794–797 (to *swaipanan); Pokorny I 1042; Fraenkel 947; Trubachev Slav. jaz. V 173; Vries ANEW 569; Fraenkel 953, 1248; Seebold 486–487; Bammesberger Nom. 56; Adams TB 611–612.
Norw svīma ‘to move back and forth’, OHG ūz-gi-swīman ‘to emerge’. Probably from *swīp-m-anan related to *swaipanan. Torp-Falk 553; Pokorny I 1041.
ON svimi ‘dizziness, giddiness’, OE swíma id., OFris swīma id., MLG swīm id. Derived from *swīmanan. Torp-Falk 553; Holthausen AEEW 337; Pokorny I 1041 (W chwyf ‘motion, unrest’); Vries ANEW 570.
ON svín-hiräir ‘swine-herd’, OE swín-hyrde id., OS swīnhirdi id., OHG swīn-hirte id. Compound of *swīnan and *xerđjaz. Carr Nom. comp. 79 (parallel formations).

Goth swein ‘pig, swine’, ON svín id., OE swín id., OFris swīn id., OS swīn id., OHG swīn id. Identical with Lat suīnus ‘of pig’, Latv svīns ‘dirty’, Slav *svinъ ‘of pig’ < *s⁄īno-. The latter is derived from IE *sū- ‘pig’. See *sūz. Torp-Falk 442; Trautmann BSW 294; Holthausen AEEW 337–338; Schulze Kl. Schr. 850; Walde-Hofmann II 636; Feist 465; Porzig Gliederung 145; Pokorny I 1038; Vries ANEW 570; Zalizniak I 144; Onions 894; Benveniste IEL 28; Vasmer-Trubachev III 578; Lehmann GED 334; Kluge-Seebold 749.
WFris swine ‘to fade’, OHG swīnan id. Of unknown origin. Torp-Falk 553; Pokorny I 1052; Vries ANEW 570; Seebold 483–484; Lehmann GED 334 (to *swīfanan).
ON sœfa ‘to kill, to sacrifice’. Identical with Skt svāpáyati ‘to make sleep’. Derived from *swefanan. Mayrhofer III 561; Vries ANEW 576.

ON sœgr ‘tumult, confusion’, OE swéz ‘noise, din’. Related to Gk ἠχή ‘noise’ < *s⁄āgh-, Lith svagiū, svagėti ‘to sound’ (Fick GGA 1894 237; Zupitza Gutt. 181). Bezzenberger BB XXVII 152; Torp-Falk 545; Holthausen AEEW 338; Pokorny I 1110; Fraenkel 946–947; Vries ANEW 577; Frisk I 646– 647; Lehmann GED 333.
Goth ga-swogjan ‘to sigh’, OE swézan ‘to sound’, LG swögen ‘to sigh’. Derived from *swōziz. Holthausen AEEW 339; Feist 202–203; Onions 847; Lehmann GED 333–334.
ON sópa ‘to sweep’, E swoop. Related to *swabbōjanan. Pokorny I 1049; Vries ANEW 530.

Goth swa ‘so, thus’, ON svá, só id., OE swá, swæ‘as, that’, OFris sā, sō ‘so, thus’, OS sō id., OHG sō id. Continues *s⁄ōd (Brugmann Grundriß II/3 1008). Related to Gk particle (instr.) ὥς ‘as, like’ < *s⁄ō-s, OLat adv. (abl.) suād ‘thus’. Brugmann Dem. 31–32 (to Av *xva˜t- ‘as, like’); Torp-Falk 543; Walde- Pokorny II 611; Holthausen AEEW
ON sœti ‘sweetness’, OS suoti id., OHG suozī id. Derived from *swōtuz. Heidermanns 585.

ON sœtr ‘sweet’, OE swót, swéte id., OFris swēte id., OS swōti id., OHG suozi id. Zero grade in Goth sutis ‘gentle, yielding’ < *sutjaz. Related to Skt svādú- ‘savory, sweet’, Gk ἡδύς ‘sweet’, Lat suāuis id. Karsten NB II 363; Torp-Falk 556; Ehrlich apud Feist (Goth suts < *su-di-, to *su- ‘good’ and *dei- ‘to shine’); Streitberg IF XXVII 157–158 (on short *u in Goth); Feist 461–462; Holthausen AEEW 339; Armknecht passim; Walde-Hofmann II t
ON teäja ‘to dung, to manure’, E ted ‘to spread (hay)’, OHG zetten ‘to spread’. Derived from *tađōn. Torp-Falk 150; Vries ANEW 584; Kluge-Seebold 909.

ON taäa ‘manured meadow’, OHG zata ‘tousled hair’. Continues *d 6-tó-, participle of *dā-: Skt dāti ‘to cut, to divide’, part. ditá- ‘cut out, divided’, Gk δαίομαι ‘to divide’, δατέομαι id. Torp-Falk 150; Pokorny I 175–178 (specifically to Gk δατέομαι); Vries ANEW 579; Frisk I 341–342, 351–352.

ON tafn ‘sacrifice’. Identical with Lat damnum ‘harm, damage’ < *dapnom, cf. also daps ‘meal’, Arm tawn ‘feast’ < *dapni-. Pedersen KZ XXXII 247 (heteroclytic stem related to *tibran); Devoto Festschr. Hirt II 546; Torp-Falk 155; Walde-Hofmann I 323–324; Pokorny I 176; Vries ANEW 579; Benveniste IEL 61, 485. 611–612; Mayrhofer III 567–569; Pokorny I 1039–1040; Vries ANEW 577; Frisk I 623; Onions 893; Rosengren 75–84; Seebold Festschr. Matzel 121–124; Lehmann GED 331; Bammesberger Nom. 263; Heidermanns 584– 585; Kluge-Seebold 810.
ON sullr ‘boil’, OFris swoll ‘swelling’. Derived from *swellanan. Torp-Falk 551; Vries ANEW 560; Seebold 490.
ON sultr ‘hunger’, OE swylt ‘death, destruction’. Zero-grade derivative of *sweltanan (Loewe IF XIII 8). Torp-Falk 552; Holthausen AEEW 339; Vries ANEW 560; Lehmann GED 336.
ON soppr ‘ball’, OHG sumpf ‘swamp’. Related to *swampaz. Torp-Falk 549.
Norw dial. tag, tagged ‘towering hill top’, MLG tagge ‘edge, prong’. Close to Skt daśā ‘thread on the edge of the fabric, fringe’. Torp-Falk 153; Pokorny I 191.

Goth tagl ‘hair’, ON tagl ‘horse’s tail’, OE tæzl ‘tail’, MLG tagel ‘rope’s end’, OHG zagal ‘tail’. Derived from *tazzaz. Derivationally close to Ir dúal ‘hairlock’ < *do˚lo-. Schweizer KZ I 155 (to Skt dṛhyati ‘to be strong’); Zupitza Gutt. 192; Torp-Falk 152; Holthausen AEEW 341; Feist 470; Pokorny I 191; Chemodanov SGGJa I 99; Vries ANEW 580; Zalizniak II 172; Onions 900; Gutenbrunner BNF III 23–24 (to Illyr Docleates); Lehmann GED 338; Bammesberger Nom. 75–76; Kluge- Seebold 902.

Goth tagr ‘tear’, OE teazor id. Accentual and morphological variant of *taxru(z). Zupitza Gutt. 192; Torp-Falk 154; Holthausen AEEW 343; Feist 470; Porzig Gliederung 202; Pokorny I 179; Lehmann GED 338; Kluge-Seebold 903.
Goth tahjan ‘to tear’, Icel tæja ‘to card, to comb’. Related to *tēzō. Zupitza Gutt. 192; Torp-Falk 152; Feist 470–471; Lehmann GED 338.
ON tárug-hlÿra ‘with tearful cheeks’, OE teáriz-hleór ‘having the cheeks wet with tears’. Compound of *taxrizaz ~ *taxruzaz (see *taxr(uz)) and *xleuzaz (see *xleuzan). Carr Nom. comp. 67.

ON tár ‘tear’, OE teár id., OFris tār id., OHG zahar id. (i-stem). Related to Gk δάκρυ id., OIr dér id., W deigr id. Curtius Gr. Et. 133; Torp-Falk 154; Holthausen AEEW 344; Feist 470; Sapir Language XV 180–187; Porzig Gliederung 202; Pokorny I 179; Vries ANEW 582; Zalizniak II 189; Frisk I 344; Onions 906; Kluge-Seebold 903.
ON teigr ‘strip of land’. Structurally similar to Skt deśá- ‘place, region’. Derived from *tīxanan. Torp- Falk 163; Mayrhofer II 65; Pokorny I 188; Vries ANEW 585; Bammesberger Nom. 49.

ON tá ‘toe’ (root stem), OE táhe, tá id., OFris tāne id., MLG tē, tēn, tēwe id., OHG zēha id. Probably derived from *tīxanan. Zupitza Gutt. 70; Torp- Falk 163; Holthausen AEEW 342; Pokorny I 188–189; Chemodanov SGGJa I 89; Vries ANEW 578; Zalizniak II 211; Onions 928; Kluge-Seebold 905; Boutkan OFED s.v.

Goth taikn ‘token’, ON teikn ‘token, sign’, OE tácn id., OFris tēken id., OS tēkan id., OHG zeihhan id. A phonetically irregular derivative of *tīxanan (Noreen Abriß 165). Karsten Festschr. Kluge 65–69; Torp-Falk 162; Feist 472; Holthausen AEEW 341; Pokorny I 188–189; Vries ANEW 585; Zalizniak II 190; Onions 929; Lehmann GED 340; Bammesberger Nom. 72, 147; Seebold KZ CIV 36–40; Kluge-Seebold 905; Boutkan OFED s.v.

Goth taiknjan ‘to show, to exhibit’, OE tæcnan ‘to show, to appoint’, OHG zeihhanen, zeihnen ‘to sign, to signal’. Derived from *taiknan. Feist 472; Vries ANEW 585; Onions 929; Lehmann GED 340; Kluge-Seebold 905.
ON teikna ‘to mark, to denote, to beckon’, OE tácnian ‘to make a mark, to indicate, to mark’’, OFris tēkna ‘to sign, to signal’, MLG tēkenen id. Derived from *taiknan. Torp-Falk 162.

OE tácor ‘husband’s brother’, OFris tāker id., OHG zeihhur id. Related to Skt devár- id., Afgh lēwar- id., Arm taigr id., Gk δ`ήρ id., Lat lēvir id., Lith dieverīs id., Slav *dîverь id. (Curtius Gr. Et. 221). Gmc reflects the thematization of the oblique cases (gen. sg. *dai⁄res) but the inlaut -k- remains unexplained. Lidén Studien 36 (-k- influenced by an unattested form related to Lith laigõnas ‘brother-in-law’); Torp-Falk 151; Holthausen AEEW 341; Walde-Hofmann I 787–788; Mayrhofer II 64; Pokorny I 179; Fraenkel 94; Zalizniak I 136; Frisk I 338–339.

Goth tains ‘branch, twig’, ON teinn ‘twig, sprout’, OE tán id., OS tēn ‘staff’, OHG zein ‘shaft, staff’. Continues *d 6 i-no- based on IE *dāi- ‘to divide’, cf. Skt dāti ‘to cut, to divide, to mow’, Gk pass. δαίεται ‘to be torn’ (Kroes GRM XXXVI 346). Fick I 459 (to Gk δόναξ ‘reed’, Latv pl. dōnis ‘reed’); Grienberger Got. 207 (to Skt dīyati ‘to fly, to soar’, Gk δίεμαι ‘to hasten’); Bugge BB XXIV 446 (follows Fick); Wiedemann BB XXVIII 54 (same as Grienberger); Torp-Falk 151; Holthausen AEEW 342; Feist 473; Mayrhofer II 31; Pokorny I 175–176; Vries ANEW 585–586; Zalizniak I 136; Frisk I 341–342; Onions 582; Kluge- Seebold 903.

Goth tainjo ‘basket’, ON teina id., OHG zeina id. Derived from *tainaz. Torp-Falk 151; Schulze KZ LXII 258 (same motivation as in Gk σχοινοῖσιν ὑφασμένος, Anth. Palatina VI 247); Kluge Nom. Stamm. 43–44; Vries ANEW 585; Lehmann GED 340; Kluge- Seebold 903.
OHG zeisan ‘to pluck’. Related to Gk δαίομαι ‘to divide’. Cf. *tainaz. Torp-Falk 164; Pokorny I 175–179; Frisk I 341–342; Seebold 498.
Swed dial. teisa ‘to pluck, to pull to pieces’, OE tæsan ‘to tear to pieces, to pull to pieces’, MLG tēsen ‘to twitch, to scratch’. Derived from *taisanan. Torp-Falk 164; Holthausen AEEW 342.

Burg *taits ‘serene’, ON teitr ‘glad, cheerful’, OE tát- (in prop.), MDu teet ‘fine’, OHG zeiz ‘nice, delicate’. Related to Lith dīdis ‘big’, didūs ‘sublime’ (Solmsen IF XIV 433; Bezzenberger BB XXVII 161). Saussure MSL VI 75 (to Lat lūdus ‘play’, with “Sabinian” l- < d-); Karsten NB I 61; Torp-Falk 162; Holthausen AEEW 343; Pokorny I 183 (to Gk δῆλος ‘visible, clear’, Lith dailūs ‘pretty’); Vries ANEW 586; Fraenkel 93; Köbler 714; Heidermanns 587–588.
ON teita ‘to gladden, to cheer’, OE tætan id. Derived from *taitaz. Holthausen AEEW 342; Vries ANEW 586; Heidermanns 588.
ON prop. Teiti, OHG zeizo ‘little boy’. Derived from *taitaz. Vries ANEW 586; Heidermanns 588.
ON tak ‘hold, chattels, seizure’, Scot tack ‘lease’. Derived from *takanan. Vries ANEW 580; Seebold 499.

ON taka ‘to take, to seize’, OE tacan ‘to take’, MLG tacken id. Despite the phonetic difference in the anlaut, to be compared with Toch B tek-, tak- ‘to touch’, Gk aor. part. τεταγών ‘seizing’, Lat tangō ‘to touch’. Lewy PBB XXXII 148 (to Lith dag‹s ‘thistle’); Zupitza Gutt. 169, KZ XXXVII 390 (to Gk δάκτυλος ‘finger’); Torp-Falk 151– 152; Holthausen AEEW 341 (OE < ON); Walde-Hofmann II 647–648; Feist 475; Jóhannesson IEW 479–480; Pokorny I 183; Chemodanov SGGJa I 111; Vries ANEW 580; Frisk I 345 (follows Zupitza), 884; Onions 900; Seebold 498–499; Lehmann GED 342–343; Mottausch KZ CVI 151–160.

ON tal ‘talk, conversation, number’, OE tæl ‘tale, number, series’, OS tal, gi-tal ‘number, series, reckoning’. Cf. also *talō(n). Related to Gk δόλος ‘wile, bait, cunning’, Lat dolus ‘guile, deceit’ (Fick BB II 209). Torp-Falk 158–159; Wood MLN XXVI 167 (to Skt dálati ‘to burst, to crack’, Lat dolō ‘to hew’); Rosenhagen ZDADL LVII 189– 190; Walde-Hofmann I 366–367; Holthausen AEEW 341, ANW 299 (to Arm to∑ ‘row’); Pokorny I 193; Vries ANEW 580; Zalizniak II 183; Frisk I 407–408; Lehmann GED 341; Kluge-Seebold 902.

Goth un-tals ‘undisciplined, disobedient’, OE ze-tal ‘quick, ready, active’, OS gi-tal ‘deft, adroit’, OHG zal ‘quick, fast’. Related to *talan, *talō(n). Torp-Falk 158–159; Feist 158–159; Holthausen AEEW 341; Pokorny I 193; Lehmann GED 341; Heidermanns 588–589.
ON talg ‘tallow’, MDu talch id. Probably related to *tulzuz (Holthausen ANW 305). Torp- Falk 160; Pokorny I 196 (to MIr delt ‘dew’); Vries ANEW 594; Lerchner 246; Heidermanns 607.

ON telja ‘to tell, to count’, OE tellan ‘to recount, to tell’, OFris tella ‘to tell’, OS tellian id., OHG zellen ‘to count’. Derived from *talan. Torp-Falk 159; Holthausen AEEW 344; Pokorny I 193; Vries ANEW 586; Onions 908; Lehmann GED 341; Kluge- Seebold 902.
ON tálma ‘to hinder’, MLG talmen ‘to dawdle’. Unclear. Pokorny I 196 (to Slav *dьliti); Vries ANEW 581.

ON tala ‘to talk, to speak’, OE talian ‘to suppose, to consider, to reckon’, OFris talia ‘to count’, OS talōn ‘to calculate’, OHG zalōn ‘to count’. Derived from *talan. Torp-Falk 159; Holthausen AEEW 342; Vries ANEW 581.

ON tala ‘number’, OE talu ‘tale, story, account’, OFris tele ‘sum, pledge’, OS tala ‘number’, OHG zala ‘crowd, calculation, number’. A variant of *talan. Torp-Falk 158–159; Holthausen AEEW 342; Vries ANEW 581; Zalizniak II 183; Onions 900; Lehmann GED 341; Kluge-Seebold 902; Boutkan OFED s.v.

ON tamr ‘tame’, OE tam id., OFris tam id., OS tam id., OHG zam id. Related to Skt dāmyati ‘to be tame, to tame’, arim-dama- ‘bridling (an enemy)’, Gk δαμάω ‘to tame’, Lat domō id., MIr damnaim ‘to subdue’. Further connected with IE *dom- ‘house’. Torp-Falk 156; Feist 203; Holthausen AEEW 342; Wissmann NP 44–45, Postv. 42–46; Walde-Hofmann I 367–368; Mayrhofer II 19, 35; Pokorny I 199–200; Vries ANEW 581; Frisk I 346; Onions 901; Rosengren 31; Schubert Erw. 73–74; Lehmann GED 149–150; Bammesberger Nom. 239; Heidermanns 589–560; Kluge-Seebold 902.

Goth ga-tamjan ‘to tame, to subdue’, ON temja ‘to tame’, OE temian id., OHG zemmen id. Derived from *tamaz. Structurally similar to Skt damāyáti ‘to subdue, to overpower’. Torp-Falk 156; Holthausen AEEW 345; Feist 203; Mayrhofer II 19; Vries ANEW 586; Lehmann GED 149; Heidermanns 590.

Goth tandjan ‘to light (a lamp), to kindle’, ON tenda ‘to kindle’ (hap. leg.), OE tendan, on-tendan id. Cf. OHG zunten ‘to put on fire’ < *tunđjanan. Related to *tenþanan. Torp-Falk 154; Holthausen AEEW 345; Feist 474; Vries ANEW 586; Onions 925; Seebold 502; Hamp SCelt XII–XIII 1–2 (to W ennyn ‘to light’); Lehmann GED 341–342; Kluge-Seebold 916.
ON tandri ‘fire’, OHG zantero, zantaro ‘hot ashes’. Derived from *tanđjanan, *tenþanan. Torp-Falk 155; Vries ANEW 581; Seebold 502.

ON tengja ‘to fasten, to tie together’, OE tengan ‘to press, to hasten’, MLG tengen ‘to begin’. Derived from *tanxuz. Fick III 152; Torp-Falk 152; Holthausen AEEW 345; Pokorny I 201; Vries ANEW 586; Heidermanns 591.

ON sam-tengr ‘bandaged’, OE ze-tenze ‘near, close’, OS bi-tengi ‘adhering’, OHG gi-zengi ‘reaching’. Derived from *tanzjanan. Karsten NB II 375; Torp-Falk 152; Holthausen AEEW 345; Pokorny I 201; Vries ANEW 581; Matzel KZ CV 103; Heidermanns 590–591.

ON tǫng ‘tongs’ (i-stem and root stem), OE tanz, tanze id., OFris tange id., OS tanga id., OHG zanga id. Related to *tanzjanan. Torp-Falk 152; Mikkola UG I 72 (to Slav *dǫga ‘arc’); Holthausen AEEW 342–343; Pokorny I 201; Vries ANEW 604; Zalizniak II 211; Onions 930; Lehmann GED 338; Kluge-Seebold 903 (to Alb danë ‘tongs’).

ON tangi ‘spit of land, pointed end by which the blade is driven into the handle’, MLG tange ‘sandbank between two marshes’. Historically identical with *tanzō. Related to *tanzjanan. Torp-Falk 168; Vries ANEW 581.

ON sbst. tá ‘path, walk’, OE tóh ‘tough, tenacious’, MLG tā, tēie id., OHG zāh ‘hard, firm’. Probably connected with Gk δασύς ‘thick’, Lat dēnsus id. Karsten NB II 374–375; Torp- Falk 152; Holthausen AEEW 350; Pokorny I 191, 201–202; Vries ANEW 579; Onions 933; Lühr Expr. 170; Heidermanns 591– 592; Kluge-Seebold 902.
ON tann-lauss ‘toothless’, late OE tóä-leás id., late OHG zandlōs id. Compound of *tanþz and *lausaz. Carr Nom. comp. 90 (parallel formations).
ON tanna ‘to dent’, Du dial. tanden ‘to thrust teeth into’. Derived from *tanþz. Vries ANEW 581.

ON tǫnn ‘tooth’ (< *tanþu-), OE tóä id., OFris tōth id., OS tand id. (i-stem), OHG zand, zant, zan id. (i-stem). Related to Skt dánt- id., Gk ὀδών, ὀδούς id., Arm atamn id., OIr dét, Lith dantīs id. Cf. also *tunþuz. Torp-Falk 154; Holthausen AEEW 351; Mayrhofer II 16–17; Pokorny I 289; Benveniste BSL XXXII 74–76; Chemodanov SGGJa I 103; Fraenkel 82; Vries ANEW 604; Zalizniak II 211; Frisk II 352–353; Chantraine 776; Onions 931; Seebold 180; Lehmann GED 349; Bammesberger Nom. 202; Griepentrog Wurzelnomina 479–481; Kluge-Seebold 902–903.
ON teppa ‘to close, to stop, to shut in’, MLG teppen ‘to plug’, MHG zepfen ‘to tap’. Derived from *tappōn. Vries ANEW 586.
ON tapa ‘to lose, to destroy’, MLG tappen ‘to touch lightly’. Based on *tappōn. Torp-Falk 155; Pokorny I 155; Vries ANEW 582; Heidermanns 595.

ON tappi ‘tap’, OE tæppa id., MLG tappe id., OHG zapfo id. Connected with Skt dépati ‘to push, to strike’, Gk δέφω ‘to knead’? Petersson LUÅ XIV/31 60 (to *teppaz); Torp-Falk 155; Holthausen AEEW 341; Pokorny I 203; Vries ANEW 582; Frisk I 372–373; Onions 903; Kluge-Seebold 903.
OHG zart ‘delicate’. Cf. MLG tert-lik ‘pampered’. Identical with MPers dart ‘harassed’. Derived from *teranan (Grimm DW 403). Feist 120 (against Grimm); Torp-Falk 158; Lehmann GED 92.

Swed dial. targa ‘to pull with teeth’, Norw dial. terga ‘to tease’, OE tirgan ‘to irritate, to provoke’, MLG tergen, targen ‘to drag, to pull’, G zergen ‘to pull’. Related to Lith dīrginti ‘to move, to shake’, Slav *dьrgati ‘to pull’. Torp-Falk 158; Holthausen AEEW 347; Pokorny I 210; Fraenkel 96; Stang LS 19.

Gep *targa ‘rim, edge’, ON targa ‘target, a small round shield’, OE tarze ‘small shield’, LG targe ‘shield’, OHG zarga ‘framing’. Probably, connected with *tarzjanan (Wood MLN XIII 292). Zupitza Gutt. 181; Torp-Falk 158 (to Slav *po-dorgъ ‘edge’); Holthausen AEEW 343 (OE < ON); Pokorny I 213 (to Gk δράσσομαι ‘to grasp’); Chemo-danov SGGJa I 73; Vries ANEW 582; Zalizniak II 178; Stang LS 17 (to Lith δαὁἤασ ‘garden’); Köbler 715; Kluge-Seebold 903.

Goth ga-tarhjan ‘to distinguish, to note’. Identical with Skt darśáyati ‘to cause to see’. Further connected with Skt perf. dadárśa ‘to see’, Gk δέρκομαι id., OIr pret. ad-con-darc id. See *turxtaz. Meillet MSL XV 349–351 (on *-x- in this position); Torp-Falk 157–158; Feist 203; Mayrhofer II 23; Pokorny I 213; Frisk I 368; Lehmann GED 150.
ON tara ‘war, fight, battle’, OE taru ‘tear, break’, MHG zar ‘war, fight’. Derived from *teranan. Holthausen AEEW 343; Jóhannesson IEW 483 (to Lith dardėti ‘to creak’); Vries ANEW 582.
ON taska ‘trunk, chest, pouch, pocket’, MLG tasche ‘bag’. Of unknown origin. Torp-Falk 160 (to *tasōjanan).
Norw dial. tasa ‘to ravel out’, LG tasen ‘to pluck’. Of unknown origin. Torp-Falk 160.
Norw dial. taspa ‘to drag, to trail’, MHG zaspen id. An emphatic derivative of *tasōjanan. Torp- Falk 160.

Goth un-ga-tass ‘irregular, undisciplined, idle’, MDu on-ge-thes ‘restless’. The negative form *un-tassaz is identical with Gk ἄ-δαστος ‘undivided’, further related to Gk δατέομαι ‘to divide’ (Fick Glotta II 128). See *tađōn. Schwyzer GG I 676; Feist 520; Pokorny I 177; Frisk I 351–352; Chantraine 254; Lehmann GED 377.

ON taufr ‘sorcery’, OE téafor ‘coloring pigment’, OFris tāver ‘magic’, MLG tover, tober id., OHG zoubar id. (also masc.). Derived from *tawjanan? Reminds of *tafnan and *tibran. Loewenthal ANF XXXII 285 (to unattested Slav *dъpati); Torp-Falk 151; Holthausen AEEW 343; Pokorny I 193; Vries ANEW 583; Zalizniak II 172; Kluge- Seebold 904.
ON teygja ‘to stretch out, to draw’, OE tízan ‘to tie’. Causative of *teuxanan. Torp-Falk 167; Holthausen AEEW 347; Pokorny I 220; Vries ANEW 587; Onions 922; Lehmann GED 346.
ON taug ‘string, rope’, OE teáz ‘band, tie’. Derived from *teuxanan. Torp-Falk 167; Holthausen AEEW 343; Vries ANEW 583 (to *tēzō); Onions 923; Seebold 504; Lehmann GED 346; Kluge-Seebold 817.

Icel tóa ‘she-fox’, MLG tō ‘bitch’, OHG zōha id. Derived from *teuxanan. For a similar semantic development cf. Skt lopā≤á- ‘fox’, Gk ἀλώπηξ id. based on *⁄el- ‘to tear (into parts)’ (Gamkrelidze- Ivanov II 513). Torp-Falk 151.

ON taumr ‘bridle, rein’, OE teám ‘tie, offspring, family, children’, OFris tām ‘bridle, descendants, offspring’, MLG tōm ‘bridle, rein’, OHG zoum id. Derived from *teuxanan (Grassmann KZ XII 133; Saussure Recueil 418). Zupitza Gutt. 75; Torp-Falk 167; Holthausen AEEW 344; Pokorny I 221; Vries ANEW 584 (against Grassmann); Marstrander NVA 1925/1 22–23; Zalizniak I 136; Onions 906; Seebold 504; Lehmann GED 346; Bammesberger Nom. 71; Kluge-Seebold 904.
ON teyma ‘to lead by the rein’, MLG tömen ‘to bridle’. Derived from *tauxmaz. Vries ANEW 587.

Goth taujan ‘to do, to make’, Run pret. tawido ‘to make’, MLG touwen ‘to prepare, to make’, OHG zowen ‘to prepare’. Derived from *tawō, with the semantic evolution from a concrete to an abstract meaning, cf. Slav *kresati ‘to kindle (light)’ ~ Lat creō ‘to create’ (Trubachev ESSJa XII 124–125). Osthoff IF V 282–283 (to Skt dūrá- ‘far, distant’, Gk δηρός ‘long’); Psilander KZ XLV 281–284 (derived from *tēwō); Lorentz IF V 342–343 (to Gk δοῦλος ‘slave’); Marstrander NTS III 124 (from *tawjanan, to *aujan); Torp-Falk 166; Holthausen AEEW 343; Feist 474–475; Pokorny I 218; Vries ANEW 583; Onions 905; Lehmann GED 342; Kluge- Seebold 904.
ON pl. gǫtvar ‘armor, hoard’, OE pl. zeatwe ‘arms, garments’, OHG gizouwa ‘tool, implement’. Of unknown origin. Zupitza Gutt. 70; Vries ANEW 199; Lehmann GED 342.

Goth pl. -tigjus ‘set of ten’, ON -tigr id., OE -tiz id., OFris -tich id., OS -tig id., OHG -zug id. Continues poorly attested IE *de˚ú-, a derivational variant of *de˚ṃ, see *texun. Schulze Lat. 145–146 (identical with decu- in Lat decu-ria ‘division consisting of ten, a company of ten, tithing’); Torp-Falk 153; Holthausen AEEW 347; Feist 150; Pokorny I 191–192; Vries ANEW 588 (reconstructs *de˚-u-); Onions 952; Lehmann GED 114, 344; Bammesberger GD 3–8 (from *de˚št-); Ross-Berns Numerals 602–609; Kluge-Seebold 911.

ON té ‘disposal’, OE teohh ‘association, company, band’, MLG teche ‘succession, association’, OHG zehha ‘collective contribution’. Related to IE *de˚- ‘to take, to receive’: Gk δοκέω ‘to seem, to think’, Lat decet ‘to be seemly, to be appropriate’. Grienberger Got. 209 (to *tēwō); Uhlenbeck IF XXV 146 (ON té to OE tíä ‘grant, concession’); Holthausen AEEW 349, AWN 301 (to *tīxanan); Walde-Hofmann I 330–331; Pokorny I 189–190; Vries ANEW 584; Frisk I 404–406; Lehmann GED 343; Kluge-Seebold 904. *texanđōn ~ *texunđōn ~

Goth taihunda ‘tenth’, ON tíundi id., OE teóäa id., OFris sbst. tegotha ‘tax, tithe’, OS tegotho, tehando ‘tenth’, OHG zehanto id. Derived from *texun. Cf. Toch A śkänt, B ≤kante ‘tenth’, Gk δέκατος id., Lith deši Mtas id. Torp- Falk 153; Holthausen AEEW 345; Feist 471–472; Pokorny I 191–192; Frisk I 359; Fraenkel 91; Vries ANEW 590; Lehmann GED 339; Ross-Berns Numerals 631–633; Adams TB 637; Burlak IFTJa 257; Boutkan OFED s.v.
Goth taihswa ‘right hand, right side’, OLFr tesewa id., OHG zesawa id. Substantivized *texswōn ~ *texswaz. Feist 471; Lehmann GED 338; Heidermanns 594.

Goth taihswa ‘right’, MDu tesuwe id., OHG zeso id. Particularly close to Gk δεξιός id. < *deji WÒw, Gaul Dexsiva Dea, OIr dess id. < *de˚s⁄o- (Porzig Gliederung 138). Further related to Skt dákṣina-, Alb djathtë (based on *djath < PAlb *detsa), Lat dexter, Lith d‚šinas, Slav *desnъ. Zupitza Gutt. 192; Brugmann Grundriß II/1 202 (suff. *-⁄ounder the influence of *lai⁄os ‘left’); Torp-Falk 154; Walde-Hofmann I 346–347; Feist 471; Specht KZ LXII 217–219; Hoops EG V 81–82; Pokorny I 189–191; Fraenkel 91; Frisk I 366–367; Benveniste IEL 211; Lehmann GED 338; Heidermanns 593–594; Orel AED 67–68.

Goth taihun ‘ten’, ON tíu id., OE tín id., OFris tiān id., OS tehan id., OHG zehan id. Continues IE *de˚ṃ id.: Toch A śäk, B ≤ak, Skt dáśa, Gk δέκα, Arm tasn, Lat decem, OIr deich n-. Zupitza Gutt. 192; Torp-Falk 153; Sturtevant MLN XL 25; Holthausen AEEW 347; Walde-Hofmann I 327–328; Feist 471; Porzig Gliederung 198; Mayrhofer II 26; Pokorny I 191–192; Vries ANEW 590; Frisk I 359–360; Onions 909; Lehmann GED 339; Ross- Berns Numerals 590–593; Kluge-Seebold 905; Adams TB 619; Burlak IFTJa 257–258; Boutkan OFED s.v.

ON tjald ‘tent, hangings’, OE teld ‘tent’, MLG telt, telde id., OHG zelt id. Related to Gk δέλτος ‘writing tablet’ < *‘plank, board’ (Fick I 456). Bréal MSL VII 446 (from Rom *tenda ‘tent’); Torp-Falk 159; Holthausen AEEW 344; Jóhannesson IEW 492; Pokorny I 195 (to IE *del- ‘to split’); Vries ANEW 591; Zalizniak I 144; Frisk I 361–362; Seebold 501; Chantraine I 260; Kluge-Seebold 907.

ON tjálgr ‘prong, fork’, OE telza ‘branch, bough’, MLG telge, telch ‘narrow branch’ (also neut.), OHG zelga ‘field (one of three)’ (fem.). Probably related to Lith dafigis ‘scythe’ < *delg(h)- ‘to cut off’. Torp- Falk 160; Trautmann BSW 44; Holthausen AEEW 344; Pokorny I 196; Fraenkel 81; Vries ANEW 591; Kluge- Seebold 906.

Goth ga-timan ‘to match, to fit in’, MDu be-temen ‘to allow oneself, to befit’, OHG zeman ‘to fit, to befit, to become’. Identical with Toch AB tsäm- ‘to grow’, Gk δέμω ‘to build’ (Fick I 66, 454) although šdie abweichende Bedeutung... macht die ursprüngliche Identität etwas fraglich” (Frisk I 364). Grienberger Got. 93–94 (to IE *dē(i)- ‘to bind’); Torp-Falk 156; Feist 205; Benveniste BSL LI 14–29; Pokorny I 198–199; Seebold 501–502; Lehmann GED 150–151; Kluge-Seebold 910; Adams TB 735–736.

ON timbr ‘timber’, OE timber ‘timber, building’, OFris timber id., OS timbar ‘timber, building’, OHG zimbar id. From *dem-ro- further related to *temanan. Bezzenberger BB XXVII 152; Torp- Falk 156; Holthausen AEEW 348; Pokorny I 198; Vries ANEW 588; Zalizniak I 144; Onions 924; Benveniste 242, 250; Lehmann GED 345; Kluge-Seebold 911.

Goth timrjan ‘to build, to construct’, ON timbra ‘to build of timber’, OE timbran, timbrian ‘to build, to construct’, OFris timbria, timmeria ‘to build of timber’, OS timbrōn id., OHG zimbarōn id. Derived from *temran. Torp-Falk 156; Holthausen AEEW 348; Feist 478; Vries ANEW 588; Benveniste IEL 242, 250–251; Kluge- Seebold 911.
Goth timrja ‘carpenter’, OS timbro ‘builder’, OHG zimbaro id. Derived from *temran (Schulze Kl. Schr. 863). Feist 478; Onions 924; Lehmann GED 345.

ON tindr ‘spike, tooth’, OE tind ‘tine, prong, tooth’, MLG tind, tinde ‘tine, sharp projecting point’, OHG zint id. Cf. also *tenđjō id. > MLG tinne, OHG zinna. Derived from *tunþuz. Torp-Falk 154; Holthausen AEEW 349; Feist 483; Pokorny I 289 (to OIr dind ‘hill’); Vries ANEW 589; Onions 925; Darms Vṛddhi 402–406; Kluge-Seebold 912.

OE tinneä ‘burned’, MHG 1 sg. pres. zinne ‘to start burning’. Of unknown origin. Thurneysen Idg. Anz. XXXIII 32 (to OIr and- ‘to kindle’); Torp-Falk 154–155; Vendryes Mélanges Andler 383–388; Feist 474; Onions 925; Seebold 502; Lehmann GED 341.
Norw dial. tipp ‘tip’, ME tip id., MLG tip id., MHG zipf id. Related to *tappōn. Torp-Falk 164.

Goth dis-tairan ‘to tear asunder’, OE teran ‘to tear, to rend’, OHG zeran ‘to quarrel, to have a row’. Related to Toch AB tsär- ‘to be separated, to separate’, Skt dṛṇāti ‘to burst, to tear’, Gk δέρω ‘to flay’, Lith derū, διὁτι ‘to tear’, Slav *derǫ, dьrati id. Goth tarmjan ‘to break forth’ may be based on a derivative noun *tar-ma- close to Gk δέρμα ‘skin’ (Grienberger 207). Bugge IF V 175 (Goth tarmjan < Arm patarem ‘to break’); Torp- Falk 156; Feist 120, 474; Holthausen AEEW 346; Trier Holz 122 (to *terwōn); Mayrhofer II 59; Pokorny I 206–211; Fraenkel 96–97; Frisk I 368–370; Onions 906; Seebold 502–503; Lehmann GED 91–92, 342; Kluge-Seebold 908; Adams TB 736.
OE teors ‘reed, penis’, MLG ters ‘penis’, OHG zers id. May be close to MIr dorr ‘thorn’ < *dorso-. Torp-Falk 158 (to *ter- ‘to split’); Holthausen AEEW 346; Pokorny I 210; Zalizniak I 136.

ON tjara ‘tar’, OE tierwe id., OFris tera id., MLG tere, ter id., G dial. Zehr id. Close to Lith dervā ‘pine-wood, tar’, Latv daıva ‘tar’, Slav *dervo ‘tree’. Further connected with IE *deru-: Hitt taru ‘wood’, Skt dāru id., Gk δόρυ ‘wood, trunk, spear’. Hermann Lautg. 282 (connects with Goth ethn. Tervingi); Torp-Falk 157; Holthausen AEEW 346–347; Pokorny I 214– 216; Chemodanov SGGJa I 72; Fraenkel 90–91; Vries ANEW 591; Frisk I 411– 412; Onions 903; Lehmann GED 343; Kluge-Seebold 819.

OE teter ‘tetter, a cutaneous disease’. Related to Skt dadrú- ‘skin disease, leprosy’, Lith dedervin E ‘tetter’, Latv dedere ‘eczema’ (Būga Aist. 143). Persson Beiträge 780; Krogmann KZ LXV 142; Torp-Falk 157; Holthausen AEEW 346; Mayrhofer II 14; Pokorny I 209; Fraenkel 85; Onions 913.

Goth tiuhan ‘to lead, to bring’, ON part. toginn ‘to draw’, OE teón id., OFris tia id., OS tiohan id., OHG ziohan id. Etymologically related to Lat dūcō ‘to pull, to lead’, W dygaf ‘to bring’, Alb nduk ‘to pluck out, to pull out, to tear’ < PAlb *en-duka. Zupitza Gutt. 210; Torp- Falk 166; Feist 478–479; Holthausen AEEW 345; Walde-Hofmann I 377–378; Jóhannesson IEW 474–476; Pokorny I 220; Ernout-Meillet 186 (West European pastoral term); Chemodanov SGGJa I 86, 91; Vries ANEW 594; Benveniste IEL 125; Seebold 503–505; Lehmann GED 346; Kluge-Seebold 910; Orel AED 288–289.

ON tjóär ‘tether’, OFris tiāder id., MLG tuder id., OHG ziotar id. (masc., neut.). Derivative in *-đra- < *-troof *teuxanan. Lidén SVSU VI/1 42 (to Skt neut. dóraka- ‘rope’); Torp-Falk 165; Holthausen IF XX 324, AEEW 347; Pokorny I 221; Vries ANEW 592; Onions 913; Heidermanns 607; Kluge- Seebold 840, 913.
ON tjóära ‘to tether’, E tether id., MLG tüdern id. Derived from *teuxđran. Vries ANEW 592.
ON tjón ‘damage, loss’, OE teóna ‘damage, harm, hurt’, OS tiono ‘evil’. Of unknown origin. Torp-Falk 165; Holthausen AEEW 346; Pokorny I 179 (to Skt dunóti ‘to burn’); Onions 907; Vries ANEW 592.

ON tÿna ‘to lose, to destroy’, OE tínan ‘to vex, to annoy, to irritate’, OFris tiona, tiuna ‘to damage’, OS gi-tiunian ‘to inflict injustice’. Derived from *teunō(n). Torp-Falk 165; Holthausen AEEW 347; Vries ANEW 603.
ON tág ‘willow twig’, MHG zāch ‘wick’ (masc.), zāhe id. Related to *taxjanan. Torp-Falk 152; Lehmann GED 338 (to IE *den˚- ‘to bite’).

Norw dial. taag ‘slow and enduring’, EFris tāge ‘tough’, MLG tēge id. Related to Skt jihmá- ‘crooked’ (with assimilation from *dižhmá-), Gk δοχμός id. < *d 6·h-mo-. Torp-Falk 152 (to *tanxuz); Pokorny I 181.

Goth tekan ‘to touch’, OFris bi-tech ‘to carry off’. An ablaut form of *takanan. Directly related to Toch B täk- ‘to touch, to feel with the hand’ < *dēg- (Adams TB 289). Zupitza Gutt. 169; Torp-Falk 151–152; Feist 475; Pokorny I 183; Seebold 500–501; Lehmann GED 342–343.
ON tæla ‘to entice, to betray’, OE tælan ‘to blame, to rebuke, to slander’. Derived from *tēlō. Holthausen AEEW 341; Vries ANEW 604.

ON tál ‘bait, allurement’, OE tæl ‘evil speaking, calumny’, OHG zāla ‘plot, jeopardy’. Related to *talan. Semantically, *tēlō is particularly close to Gk δόλος ‘cunning’, Lat dolus ‘fraud, malice’ (Fick BB II 209). Torp-Falk 159; Holthausen AEEW 341; Walde- Hofmann I 366; Pokorny I 193; Vries ANEW 580–581; Zalizniak I 148; Frisk I 407–408; Lehmann GED 341.

Goth adv. ga-temiba ‘appropriately, congruently’, MLG be-tame ‘befitting, appropriate’, OHG gi-zāmi ‘suitable’. Derived from *temanan. Torp- Falk 156; Feist 204–205; Pokorny I 198; Seebold 501–502; Bammesberger Nom. 261; Matzel Festschr. Schröder 99, KZ CV 109; Heidermanns 594–595.

Goth tewa ‘order’, Lang zāwa ‘(unlawful) association’, OHG zouwa ‘dye’ (< *‘preparation’). Derived from *tawjanan (Psilander KZ XLV 281–284). Fick III 153 (to Lat decet ‘to be seemly, to be appropriate’); Grienberger Got. 209 (to *texan ~ *texōn); Brugmann Grundriß I 609 (to Gk δεῖπνον ‘meal’); Zupitza Gutt. 70; Torp-Falk 153 (< *tēzwō); Feist 476; Pokorny I 218; Vries ANEW 584; Lehmann GED 343; Bammesberger Nom. 120–121.

Goth aibr ‘sacrifice, offering’ (leg. tibr, Grimm DM 36), OE tiber, tifer id., OHG zebar id. Cf. also ON tífurr ‘god (?), offering (?)’ in Vǫl. 31 (if not from OE). Continues IE *dip-ro- (Schmidt Plur. 199–200) attested also in Arm tvar ‘male sheep’ (Lidén Arm. St. 8–10; Sievers PBB X 509). Torp-Falk 155 (to Gk δείπνον ‘meal’); Holthausen AEEW 346; Feist 477; Pokorny I 222; Vries ANEW 590; Hamp JIES I 322 (from *di-bher- connected with *bher- ‘to bring’); Must Festschr. Hill III 155–162; Lehmann GED 344; Kluge- Seebold 848 (from IE *ati-bher-, to *bher- ‘to bear’).

ON tifa ‘to move the feet quickly, to trip’, MHG zipfen ‘to trip’. Of imitative origin. Siebs KZ XXXVII 308 (to Gk δέφω ‘to soften by working with the hand’); Wood MLN XXI 227 (to Gk διφάω ‘to search after’); Holthausen PBB LXVI 272 (to OE tife ‘bitch’); Vries ANEW 587 (to OHG zabalōn ‘to flutter’).

Swed dial. ticka ‘goat having a kid’, OHG ziga ‘she-goat’. Close to Lacon d¤za: aǫj, Λάκωνες, Hes. (Fick KZ XLII 148: erroneously adds Thrac -diza in pln. < IE *dhei·h-). Janzén Bu Z 21–31; Torp-Falk 163; Hirt Idg. Gr. I 39 (taboo development of *ghid-); Meillet MSL XV 356 (contra Hirt); Detschew Thr. 132; Pokorny I 222; Onions 953; Kluge-Seebold 909.

ON tigi ‘charge, suspicion’, MHG zic ‘charge, accusation’. Derived from *tīxanan. Identical with Gk δίκη ‘custom, usage’. Pokorny I 188–189; Vries ANEW 587; Frisk I 393–394 (an independent formation in Gk δίκη and Skt Epic διἡā ‘trend’).

OE tiht ‘charge, accusation’, OFris tiht id., MLG ticht id., OHG bi-ziht id. Identical with Skt diṣṭi- ‘direction, prescription’, Av ā-dišti- ‘intention, purpose, intention’. Further related to *tīxanan. Bartholomae 321; Torp-Falk 163; Holthausen AEEW 348; Mayrhofer II 44; Pokorny I 188; Lehmann GED 150; Bammesberger Nom. 139.
Norw dial. tikka ‘to tick’, LG tikken id., MHG zicken ‘to nudge, to touch’. Of imitative origin. Torp-Falk 162–163; Pokorny I 187–188 (to *tikkōn).
ME tyke ‘tick’, MLG teke id., OHG zehho id. Related to Arm tiz ‘bug’, OIr dega ‘stag-beetle’. Torp-Falk 163; Pokorny I 187–188; Onions 922; Kluge-Seebold 905.

Goth til ‘opportunity, occasion’, OE till ‘fixed point, station’, MLG til, tel ‘point of time, aim’, OHG zil ‘end, limit, goal’. Substantivized *tilaz. The form of acc. is used as a preposition ‘till’. Torp-Falk 161; Holthausen AEEW 348; Feist 477; Pokorny I 3; Onions 924; Lehmann GED 344–345; Kluge- Seebold 910.

Goth ga-tils ‘appropriate’, ON tilr ‘useful, good’, OE til ‘good, apt, excellent’, OFris til ‘good’. Continues *di-loderived from *dei- ‘to shine’: Skt dīdeti, Gk impf. δέατο ‘seemed’. Cf. *tinan. Persson Wurzelerw. 115 (related to *talan); Karsten NB II 264–265; Pedersen KZ XXXIX 372 (follows Persson); Torp- Falk 161; Feist 477 (to Lat diēs ‘day’); Holthausen AEEW 348; Wissmann Postv. 72; Pokorny I 3 (to W addas ‘suitable’, eddyl ‘aim’ < *ad-ilo-); Vries ANEW 588; Schubert Erw. 84–85; Lehmann GED 344–345; Hamp IF XC 70 (same as Pokorny); Bammesberger Nom. 249; Heidermanns 596–597; Kluge-Seebold 910.

Goth ga-tilon ‘to reach, to attain’, OE tilian ‘to strive, to pursue’, OFris tilia ‘to testify, to build on’, OLFr tilōn ‘to toil, to labor’. Derived from *tilan. Torp-Falk 161; Holthausen AEEW 348; Feist 477; Onions 924; Lehmann GED 344; Heidermanns 597; Kluge- Seebold 910.

ON tin ‘tin’, OE tin id., OFris tin id., MLG tin id., OHG zin id. A lightcolored metal called *di-no- and derived from *dei-: Skt dīdeti ‘to shine, to be bright’, Gk impf. δέατο ‘seemed’ (Wood MLN XV 329). Torp-Falk 164 (from Celtic, cf. Bret sten < *stagno- id.); Holthausen AEEW 349; Mayrhofer II 45; Pokorny I 183–185; Vries ANEW 589; Zalizniak I 144; Frisk I 354; Onions 925.

ON titra ‘to twinkle, to shake, to shiver’, OHG zittarōn ‘to tremble’. Of imitative origin. Torp- Falk 164 (to Skt drāti ‘to run, to hasten’, Gk διδράσκω ‘to run away’); Vries ANEW 590; Kluge-Seebold 913.
Norw dial. titta ‘tit’, OE titt id. (masc.), MLG titte id., MHG zitze id. A descriptive stem. Torp-Falk 163–164.

ON tíä ‘time’, OE tíd id., OFris tīd id., OS tīd id., OHG zīt id. Etymologically close to Arm ti ‘age’, Alb ditë ‘day’ < *dī-ti- (Persson KZ XXXIII 287). Cf. also Skt dīti ‘brightness, splendor’? Torp-Falk 160–161; Kluge Zd Wf VIII 145–146; Holthausen AEEW 346; Mayrhofer II 44–45; Pokorny I 176; Vries ANEW 587; Zalizniak II 190; Onions 923; Bammesberger Nom. 146; Kluge-Seebold 906; Orel AED 66–67.
ON tíäa ‘to long for, to wish’, MLG tīden ‘to desire’. Derived from *tīđiz. Torp-Falk 161; Vries ANEW 587.

Goth ga-teihan ‘to announce, to declare’, ON 1 sg. pres. té ‘to indicate’, OE teón ‘to accuse’, OFris pres. ur-ti(g)a ‘to refuse, to object’, OS pres. af-tīhan ‘to deny, to refuse’, OHG zīhan ‘to accuse’. Connected with Hitt tekkuššāi- ‘to show’, Skt dídeṣṭi ‘to show’, diśáti id., Gk δείκνυμι id., Lat dīcō ‘to say’. Torp-Falk 163; Feist 204; Holthausen AEEW 349; Walde-Hofmann I 348–349; Jóhannesson IEW 471–472; Mayrhofer II 43; Pokorny I 188; Chemodanov SGGJa I 87; Vries ANEW 590–591; Frisk I 355–356; Seebold 498–500; Lehmann GED 150; Kluge-Seebold 905–906.
ON tík ‘bitch’, MLG tīke id. Probably related to *tikkōn. Torp-Falk 162.
ON adv. tíma-liga ‘timely, early’, OE adv. tím-líce ‘in good time, soon’. Derived from *tīmōn. Onions 924.
ON tíma ‘to happen, to befall’, OE ze-tímian id. Derived from *tīmōn. Torp-Falk 161; Holthausen AEEW 349; Vries ANEW 588.
ON tími ‘time’, OE tíma id., G dial. zīme ‘opportunity’. Continues *dī-men- connected with *tīđiz. Torp- Falk 161; Holthausen AEEW 348; Vries ANEW 588; Zalizniak I 155; Onions 924; Kluge-Seebold 906.

Goth sin-teins ‘daily’. Identical with Lith dienā id., Latv dīena id., OPrus acc. deinan id. < *deinā. Further connected with Slav *dьnь id., Alb gdhij ~ gdhîj ‘to stay awake at night’ < PAlb *ka-dinja, Lat nūn-dinum ‘market held every ninth day’, OIr tré-denus ‘for three days’. Brugmann Grundriß II/1 264, 298; Walde-Hofmann II 188; Feist 423; Pokorny I 183–187; Fraenkel 93; Vries ANEW 586; Lehmann GED 305; Orel AED 112.

ON tírr ‘glory, renown’, OE tír ‘glory, honor’, OS tīr id. Cf. also OFris tīre ‘jewelry’. Probably derived from IE *dei(6)-: Skt dīdeti ‘to shine, to be bright’ (see *tilaz). Torp-Falk 161; Holthausen AEEW 349; Mayrhofer II 45; Pokorny I 186 (to Lith dyrėti ‘to look out’); Vries ANEW 589; Van Windekens I 506 (to Toch AB tiri ‘way, manner’); Heidermanns 596; Kluge-Seebold 910.

Goth tyz ‘name of t-rune’, ON theon. Tÿr, OE theon. Tíz, Tíw id., OHG theon. *Ziu id. Related to Skt déva- ‘god’, Av daēvō ‘demon’, Lat deus ‘god’, Lith di‚vas id. continuing IE *dei⁄o- (Bremer IF III 301). Sievers PBB IX 203; Grienberger PBB XXI 217, ANF XV 5; Bartholomae 667–670; Torp- Falk 162; Holthausen AEEW 350; Walde-Hofmann I 345–346; Feist 486–487; Mayrhofer II 63–64; Pokorny I 185; Fraenkel 93–94; Zalizniak I 138; Vries ANEW 603; Lehmann GED 352; Watkins Gedenkschr. Güntert 102 (to OIr in Dagdae < Celt *dagodē⁄os ‘good god’); RudnyÆkyj Gedenkschr. Güntert 111 (to Slav *divo ‘wonder’, *divъ ‘negative spirit, unfavorable god’); Bammesberger Nom. 94.

OE tó ‘to’, OFris tō id., OS tō id., OHG zuo id. Cf. also WGmc forms with a different vocalism: OE te ‘to’, OFris te id., OS te id., OHG zi id. Close to Lat dōin dōnec ‘up to’, Latv da id., Slav *do id. Schmidt KZ XXVI 24 (Goth du ‘to’ < *tō); Torp-Falk 164; Holthausen AEEW 343, 350; Walde-Hofmann I 325–326; Pokorny I 181–183; Fraenkel 78; Onions 927; Lehmann GED 96; Kluge- Seebold 915.
ON tómr ‘empty’, OE tóm id., OS tōm ‘single’. Derived from *temanan. Karsten NB II 457; Torp-Falk 164– 165; Holthausen AEEW 351; Vries ANEW 594–595; Heidermanns 598.
ON tœma ‘to empty’, OFris tēma ‘to empty’, OS tōmian ‘to free’. Derived from *tōmaz. Torp-Falk 165; Vries ANEW 604; Heidermanns 598.
ON tó ‘tow’, OE tow-hús ‘spinning-house’. Derived from *tawjanan. Torp-Falk 166; Holthausen AEEW 351; Vries ANEW 593; Kluge-Seebold 817.

ON pl. tól ‘tools’, OE tól ‘tool, implement, instrument’. Derived from *tawjanan or directly from *tōwan. Loewenthal ANF XXXV 238 (to Skt dāmán- ‘giver, donor’), Wu S X 168 (to Gk δημός ‘fat’); Wood MP XXIV 218 (to Gk δῆμος ‘land’); Holthausen AEEW 350; Vries ANEW 594 (to *temran); Onions 931; Lehmann GED 342.
ON pl. trefr ‘fringes’, MHG trābe id. Related to Gk δρέπω ‘to pluck’, Skt drāpí- ‘mantle, garment’. Torp- Falk 170; Mayrhofer II 77; Pokorny I 211; Vries ANEW 597; Frisk I 417.

ON treäja ‘to tread’, OE treddan ‘to tread, to trample’, MLG tredden ‘to tread upon’, OHG tretten ‘to stamp on’. Derived from *tređanan. Holthausen AEEW 352; Vries ANEW 596; Seebold 506; Lehmann GED 348.
ON trǫä ‘pen, a piece of fallow land’, OS trada ‘step, trace’, OHG trata ‘way’. Derived from *tređanan. Torp-Falk 170; Vries ANEW 600; Seebold 505; Bammesberger Nom. 109.

ON pl. tramar ‘fiends, demons’, OE trem ‘step’, MLG trame ‘stair (of a ladder)’. Structurally close to Gk δρόμος ‘course, race, running’, cf. further δραμεῖν ‘to run’, Skt drámati ‘to run about’. Holthausen AEEW 352; Mayrhofer II 72; Pokorny I 204; Chemodanov SGGJa I 108; Vries ANEW 596; Frisk I 419.
Norw dial. trappa ‘to stomp’, MLG trappen id. Cf. also OE treppan ‘to tread’ < *trappjanan. Related to Lith drebū, drebėti ‘to tremble’. Torp- Falk 170; Pokorny I 204.
OSwed trø ‘corn-measure’, OE tríz, tréz ‘tray, trough’. Derived from *trewan. Holthausen AEEW 353; Onions 938.
ON traust-lauss ‘without protection, helpless’, MLG trōstlōs ‘without comfort’, OHG trōst-lōs id. Compound of *traustaz and *lausaz. Carr Nom. comp. 91 (parallel formations).
ON traust-ligr ‘reliable, safe’, OHG trōst-līh ‘solacing’. Derived from *traustaz. Heidermanns 600.
ON traust ‘trust, shelter, confidence’, OFris trāst ‘comfort’, MLG trōst id., OHG trōst id. Substantivized *traustaz. Torp-Falk 171; Kluge-Seebold 838.

ON traustr ‘reliable, sure, strong’, MLG ge-trōst ‘confident’, OHG gi-trōst id. Continues *drou-st Ho- ‘firm’, cf. Pers durušt- ‘hard, strong’ < *dru-st Ho- (Osthoff Etym. 122–133, 145–146). Further related to *trewan (Benveniste Word X 257–259). Karsten NB II 334; Torp-Falk 171; Braune PBB XLIII 383–390; Pokorny I 216; Vries ANEW 596; Onions 946; Schubert Erw. 33; Benveniste IEL 85; Lehmann GED 346– 347; Heidermanns 599–601; Kluge- Seebold 838.
Goth trausti ‘pact, covenant’, OHG gi-trōsti ‘consolation, comfort’. Derived from *traustaz. Benveniste IEL 85; Heidermanns 600.
ON treysta ‘to make trusty, to make strong’, OS trōstian ‘to console, to comfort’, OHG trōsten ‘to make confident, to console’. Derived from *traustaz. Torp-Falk 171; Vries ANEW 598; Heidermanns 600.

Norw treda ‘to tread’, OE tredan id., OFris treda id., MLG treden id., OHG tretan id. A secondary transformation of *truđanan into a thematic e-grade (Meillet BSL XX 25). Torp- Falk 169–170; Holthausen AEEW 352; Vries ANEW 598; Onions 939; Seebold 505–506; Lehmann GED 348; Kluge- Seebold 835.

ON irreg. trega ‘to grieve’, OS tregan ‘to suffer’. Of unknown origin. Torp-Falk 169; Persson Beiträge 46–47; Būga II 244 (to Lith drīžti ‘to become weak’); Holthausen AEEW 352; Fraenkel 97, 106 (to Lith drežėti ‘to be unwilling’); Jóhannesson IEW 496; Pokorny I 226–227 (follows Būga); Vries ANEW 597; Seebold 506; Heidermanns 601–602.

ON tregi ‘difficulty, reluctance, grief’, OE treza ‘pain, grief, vexation’, OS trego ‘pain’. Cf. also Goth trigo ‘grief, regret’. Derived from *trezanan. Torp-Falk 169; Holthausen AEEW 352; Feist 480; Scherer GBS 26; Chemodanov SGGJa I 77; Zalizniak II 178; Lehmann GED 347.
Goth ana-trimpan ‘to step up close to’. Related to *trappōjanan. Based on the unattested str. *trep(p)anan. Torp-Falk 170.

Goth triu ‘stick, tree trunk’, ON tré ‘tree, wood’, OE treów id., OFris trē id., OS trio ‘tree, beam’. A thematic derivative of *deru-: *drū- ‘tree, wood’: Hitt taru- ‘wood’, Skt dāru ‘piece of wood, wood’, Av dāuru- id., Gk δόρυ ‘wood, beam, spear’, W derwen ‘oak’, Lith dervā ‘(resinous) pine-tree’, Slav *dervo ‘tree, wood’ (Hirt IF I 477–478). Structurally similar to *knewan. Grassman Rig. 595 (‘hard’ > ‘tree’); Osthoff Etym. 98–180 (on the original meaning); Bartholomae 738–739; Torp-Falk 157; Specht KZ LXVI 58–59 (follows Grassmann); Holthausen AEEW 353; Feist 480–481; Mayrhofer II 36; Pokorny I 214–215; Fraenkel 90; Zalizniak I 146; Vries ANEW 597; Frisk I 411–412; Onions 939; Friedrich Trees 140–149 (against Grassmann); Lindeman Verschärfung 16.18; Benveniste IEL 87–88; Anttila Schw. 91; Lehmann GED 347–348.
