Goth triweins ‘wooden’, Norw treen ‘hard’, OE treówen ‘of tree, wooden’. Derived from *trewan. Structurally similar to Gk δρύινος ‘of oak’, Thrac Zulmus-δριηνός, name of Asclepios, Slav *dervînъ ‘wooden’. Holthausen AEEW 353; Feist 481; Detschew Thr. 195–196; Frisk I 421; Benveniste IEL 87; Lehmann GED 347.

Goth triggws ‘faithful, true’, ON tryggr id., OE treówe id., OFris triūwe id., OS triuwi id., OHG gi-triuwi. Based on *trewan (Osthoff Etym. 98–180). Karsten NB I 82; Torp-Falk 171; Holthausen AEEW 353; Feist 480; Sturtevant MLN XXVIII 161; Pokorny I 215–216; Vries ANEW 599; Onions 946; Benveniste IEL 85; Friedrich Trees 142; Lindeman Verschärfung 148, 188; Lehmann Phon. 36–46 (-gg- < *-⁄H-), GED 347; Bammesberger Nom. 257; Heidermanns 603–604; Kluge-Seebold 835.
ON tryggä ‘faith, trust’, OE treówä ‘truth, faith, honor’, OHG gi-triuwida ‘trust’. Derived from *trewwjanan. Torp-Falk 171; Holthausen AEEW 353; Vries ANEW 599; Onions 947; Heidermanns 603.

ON tryggja, tryggva ‘to make firm and trusty’, OE treówan ‘to trust’, OS part. gi-triuwid ‘allied, in alliance’. Derived from *trewwaz. Holthausen AEEW 353; Vries ANEW 599; Heidermanns 604; Kluge-Seebold 833.

Goth triggwa ‘covenant, testament’, OE tréow ‘truth, faith’, OFris triūwe ‘fidelity, loyalty’, OS treuwa id., OHG triuwa id. Substantivized *trewwaz. Holthausen AEEW 353; Feist 480; Pokorny I 215–216; Zalizniak I 149; Onions 945; Lehmann GED 347; Heidermanns 603; Kluge-Seebold 835.
ON prop. Tryggvi, OHG gi-triuwo ‘abider’. Derived from *trewwaz. Heidermanns 603.
ON troä ‘treading, trampling’, OE trod ‘track’. Derived from *truđanan. Torp-Falk 170; Holthausen AEEW 353; Seebold 506; Lehmann GED 348; Bammesberger Nom. 58.

Goth trudan ‘to tread, to trample’, ON troäa id. Continues IE *dru-t-, with *dreu- ‘to run’ preserved in Skt drávati ‘to run, to hasten’ (Osthoff Etym. I 372). Stokes IF XII 188; Meillet BSL XX/64 25 (from aor.-pres. *dṛtō); Torp-Falk 169–170; Feist 481; Jóhannesson IEW 483–485; Mayrhofer II 73; Pokorny I 204–206; Vries ANEW 598; Onions 939; Seebold 505–506; Lehmann GED 348; Kluge-Seebold 835.

ON trog ‘trough’, OE troz id., OFris trog id., MLG troch id., OHG trog id. Originally, *‘wooden’ as suggested by its etymology—from *dru-kó-, to IE *deru- ‘tree’. Cf. *traujan, *trewan. Torp-Falk 157; Holthausen AEEW 354; Pokorny I 216; Chemodanov SGGJa I 98; Vries ANEW 598; Zalizniak II 173; Onions 945; Kluge- Seebold 837.
ON trygill ‘little trough’, OHG trugil id. Derived from *truzan ~ *truzaz. Vries ANEW 599.

ON troll, trǫll ‘giant, fiend, demon, werewolf’, MHG troll, trolle ‘fiend’ (masc., probably from ON). Of unknown origin. Any connection with *trewan? Sievers IF IV 339 (from *truđlan, to *truđanan); Torp-Falk 172 (< *truzlan); Elqvist apud Vries (to EFris trul, trulle ‘fat person’, G Troll id.); Pokorny I 205; Vries ANEW 598–599; Motz KZ LXXXIX 180–185; Kluge-Seebold 837.
ON trylla ‘to enchant, to turn into a troll’, MHG trüllen ‘to flutter’. Derived from *trullan. Torp-Falk 172; Vries ANEW 599–600.

OE trum ‘strong, firm’. Identical with Skt drumá- ‘tree’, Gk Hom pl. δρυμά ‘copse, thicket’, Thrac pln. Dinδρύμη. Further connected with *trewan (Osthoff Etym. 109–111). Torp-Falk 171; Holthausen AEEW 354; Detschew Thr. 137; Mayrhofer II 79; Pokorny I 216; Frisk I 420; Heidermanns 605.
Norw dial. trumpa ‘to hit, to push’, MHG trumpfen ‘to run’. Based on *trempanan. Torp-Falk 170.

ON tros ‘droppings, rubbish, twigs and leaves used for fuel’, OFris tros ‘tree stump’. Derived from *trewan. Torp- Falk 171 (to ON tjǫrn ‘pool, small lake’), NNEO 816 (to Norw trysja ‘to rattle); Johansson PBB XV 238 (to Latv dīrst ‘cacare’); Pokorny I 216; Vries ANEW 599.

ON trúär ‘juggler’, OE trúä ‘trumpeter, actor’. Identical with OIr drúth ‘fool’. Holthausen KZ LXXII 203 (to *truđanan), AEEW 354; Bugge Indf. 64 (ON < OE); Fischer Lehnw. 19 (ON < OIr); Hammerich ANF XXXVIII 36 (to Dan trygle, LG troggelen ‘to beg’); Pokorny I 216; Vries ANEW 599.

ON trú ‘trust, belief, faith’, OE trúw ‘faith’, OHG trūwa ‘belief, trust’. Related to *trewwaz. Torp-Falk 171; Holthausen AEEW 354; Pokorny I 216; Vries ANEW 599; Zalizniak II 184; Benveniste IEL 85; Lehmann GED 346.

Goth trauan ‘to trust’ (with a different vocalism), ON trúa ‘to trust, to believe’, OE trúwian ‘to trust, to confide’, OS trūōn ‘to trust’, OHG trūēn ‘to hope’. Derived from *trūwō. Torp- Falk 171; Holthausen AEEW 354; Feist 479; Vries ANEW 599; Stang LS 20 (directly to OPrus druwīt ‘to believe’); Lehmann GED 346–347; Kluge-Seebold 833.

ON toddi ‘bit, piece, bunch, tuft’, EFris todde ‘bundle’ (fem.), E tod ‘bush’, Du todde ‘scrab, rag’, OHG zotto ‘bush’. Of unknown origin. Torp- Falk 167; Martinet Gém. 87, 197; Pokorny I 178 (to *za-tassaz); Vries ANEW 593; Onions 928; Kluge-Seebold 915.

ON tog ‘line, rope’, OE sceafttoz ‘strap attached to the shaft of a missile’, OHG bi-zog ‘cover’ (masc.). Derived from *teuxanan. Torp-Falk 166; Holthausen AEEW 350; Vries ANEW 594; Seebold 504; Lehmann GED 346; Bammesberger Nom. 57.

ON tygill ‘string, strap, thong’, OE tyzel ‘strap, trace’, MLG togel ‘rein’, OHG zugil id. Derived from *teuxanan. Löwenthal Afsl Ph XXXVII 389 (to Slav *kodъlo ‘kin’); Torp-Falk 167; Holthausen AEEW 358; Vries ANEW 602; Seebold 504; Kluge-Seebold 916.

ON toga ‘to draw’, OE tozian id., OFris togia ‘to pull strongly’, MLG togen ‘to tow, to pull’, OHG zogōn ‘to rock’. Derived from *tuzan. Torp- Falk 166; Holthausen AEEW 350; Vries ANEW 594; Onions 934; Seebold 504; Lehmann GED 346.

Goth us-tauhts ‘completion’, OE tyht ‘way, manner, motion, move’ (masc.), OFris tocht ‘upbringing, discipline’, OS athum-zuht ‘breath’, OHG zuht ‘breeding, upbringing’. Derived from *teuxanan. Torp-Falk 166; Holthausen AEEW 358; Feist 535; Seebold 504; Bammesberger Nom. 140; Kluge- Seebold 915.

Goth tulgus ‘firm, steadfast’, OE adv. tulze ‘strongly, firmly’, OS adv. tulgo ‘very’. Etymologically identical with Hitt daluki- ‘long’, Skt dīrghá- id., Av dar 6 ga- id., Gk δολιχός id., Slav *dъlgъ id. (Diefenbach apud Schweizer KZ I 558). Bartholomae 693–694; Torp-Falk 168; Feist 482–483; Holthausen AEEW 355; Mayrhofer II 47; Pokorny I 197; Frisk I 406–407; Szemerényi KZ XCIII 107–110 (to IE *del·h- ‘to capture, to cling’: Av dar 6 z- ‘to fasten’, Brit *dalg- ‘to hold’); Lehmann GED 349; Bammesberger Nom. 262; Heidermanns 607.
ON tumba ‘to tumble’, OE tumbian ‘to tumble, to dance’, MLG dumben ‘to behave stupidly’, OHG tumbōn, tumbēn id. Of imitative origin. Holthausen AEEW 355; Onions 948.

ON topt, tupt, tomt ‘place marked out for a house, a square piece of ground with walls but without roof’. Structurally close to Gk δάπεδον ‘floor’ < *dá-pedo- (Bugge PBB XXI 42). Further related to *temran and *fōtz ~ *fōtuz. Torp-Falk 156; Pokorny I 198; Vries ANEW 595; Frisk I 347–348.

ON tundr ‘tinder’, OE tynder id., MLG tunder id., OHG zuntara id. Related to *tanđjanan. Torp-Falk 155; Holthausen AEEW 358; Pokorny I 198; Vries ANEW 600; Zalizniak II 184; Onions 925; Lehmann GED 341; Kluge-Seebold 916.

Goth tuggl ‘star, constellation’, ON tungl ‘moon’, OE tunzol ‘heavenly body, star, planet, constellation’, OS tungal ‘constellation’ (in cmpn.), OHG himil-zungal id. Despite the tradition of deriving it from a dissimilated *tulzlan based on *tulzuz, the old explanation from *tunzōn (Grimm DM II 663) seems preferable. Grienberger Got. 210–211 (to Lith dangūs ‘sky’); Torp-Falk 168; Krogmann KZ LXIII 258–260 (to *tanđjanan); Holthausen AEEW 355; Feist 481–482; Pokorny I 197; Vries ANEW 601; Zalizniak I 144; Lehmann GED 348.

Goth tuggo ‘tongue’, ON tunga id., OE tunze id., OFris tunge id., OS tunga id., OHG zunga id. Related to other highly variative Indo-European forms: Toch A pl. käntu, B kantwo id. (< PToch *tänkwo), Skt jihvā id., Av hizū id., Arm lezu, Lat lingua id., OIr tengae, Lith liežūvis id., Slav *jêzykъ id. Zupitza Gutt. 101, 205; Bartholomae 1815; Torp-Falk 168; Holthausen AEEW 355; Walde- Hofmann I 806–807; Feist 482; Porzig Gliederung 129; Mayrhofer I 436–437; Pokorny I 223; Chemodanov SGGJa I 88; Fraenkel 369–370; Vries ANEW 600; Zalizniak I 156; Vendryes T- 50–51; Mayrhofer I 436–437; Onions 930; Winter JIES X 167–186; Lehmann GED 349; Bammesberger Nom. 175; Kluge-Seebold 917; Huld Festschr. Puhvel I 81 (*zw > *z dissimilated under the influence of *-u- in the first syllable); Adams TB 139.
ON prn. Rata-toskr (= *‘squirrel’), OE tusc ‘canine tooth, molar, tusk’, OFris tusk ‘tooth’. Derived from *tunþuz. Torp-Falk 154; Holthausen AEEW 355; Pokorny I 289; Vries ANEW 596.

Goth tunþus ‘tooth’. Zero grade of *(e)dont-, see *tanþz. Close to Skt gen. datáъ, Lat dēns id., OIr dét id. reflecting the same ablaut grade. Schmidt KZ XXXII 329 (on connection with *IE *ed- ‘to eat’); Torp-Falk 154; Walde- Hofmann I 340–341; Feist 483; Porzig Gliederung 124, 155; Mayrhofer II 16; Pokorny I 289; Chemodanov SGGJa I 103; Zalizniak II 211; Lehmann GED 349; Bammesberger Nom. 202.

ON toppr ‘tuft, lock of hair’, OE topp ‘top’, OFris topp ‘tuft’, Du top ‘crest, summit’, OHG zopf ‘plaited hair’. Identical with Slav *dybъ ‘standing erect’, with a lengthening according to Winter’s law, cf. further *dybati ‘to stick up, to protrude’ (Berneker I 249) based on *dъbъ < *dubo- (Trubachev ESSJa V 198). Petersson SVSL I 70 (to OHG zumpfo ‘penis’); Torp-Falk 168; Bloomfield Germanica 95 (on *-pp-); Holthausen AWN 306 (same as Berneker), AEEW 351; Martinet Gém. 188, 197; Pokorny I 177 (to IE *dāp- ‘to divide’); Vries ANEW 595; Zalizniak I 136; Onions 931; Trubachev ESSJa V 197– 198; Kluge-Seebold 914; Dybo BSA 142–143.
Norw dial. tuppa ‘to pick, to twitch’, early Du toppen id., G zupfen id. Derived from *tuppaz. Torp- Falk 168.

ON torf ‘turf’, OFris turf id., MLG turf id. Close to Skt darbhá- ‘tuft of grass’, Slav *dьrba ‘plowed field’. Torp-Falk 158; Mayrhofer II 23; Pokorny I 212; Vries ANEW 595; Zalizniak I 184; Griepentrog Wurzelnomina 403–404; Kluge- Seebold 828–829.
ON tyrfa ‘to cover with turf’, OE ze-tyrfan ‘to hit, to strike’, MDu turven, torven ‘to extract peat’. Derived from *turban ~ *turbaz, *turbō(n). Holthausen AEEW 358; Vries ANEW 603.
ON torfa ‘piece of peat’, OE turf ‘turf’. See *turban ~ *turbaz. Torp- Falk 158; Holthausen AEEW 355; Vries ANEW 595; Zalizniak I 184; Onions 949.

OE tord ‘turd, dung’, MDu tort, torde id. Probably from part. *dṛtó-, to Latv dīrst ‘cacare’ (Persson BB XIX 283). Torp-Falk 156–157 Holthausen AEEW 351; Specht KZ LXVI 203 (to Gk δαρδαίνω ‘to stain’, Hes.); Pokorny I 207; Onions 949.
ON torä-yfill ‘dung-beetle’, OE tord-wifel id., ODu tortwevel id. A compound of *turđan and *webilaz. Carr Nom. comp. 52; Vries ANEW 595; Onions 949; Zalizniak I 137.

OE torht ‘bright, splendid’, OS torht id., OHG zoraht id. Identical with Skt dṛṣṭá- ‘seen, visible’, OIr an-dracht ‘ugly’ < *dṛ˚-tó-. Further related to *tarxjanan. Zupitza Gutt. 17; Torp-Falk 157–158; Holthausen AEEW 351; Mayrhofer II 61; Pokorny I 213; Lehmann GED 150; Bammesberger Nom. 250; Heidermanns 607–608.

OE torn ‘grievous, distressing, bitter’, OS torn ‘bitter, painful’, MHG zorn ‘furious’. Derived from *teranan. Structurally close to Skt dīrṇa- ‘torn, rent’, W darn ‘piece, part’, Slav *dьrnъ ‘turf ’ < *dṛHnó-. Torp-Falk 157; Holthausen AEEW 351; Mayrhofer II 47; Lehmann GED 150; Lühr Expr. 330; Heidermanns 608–609; Kluge-Seebold 915.

Goth af-taurnan ‘to tear off’, Du tornen ‘to depart’. Related to Hitt tarna- ‘to leave’, Skt dṛṇāti ‘to burst, to tear’ (Feist 12). Formally derived from *turnaz. Torp-Falk 156; Mayrhofer II 59; Lehmann GED 8.
Icel tota ‘toe (of a boot)’, Norw tot ‘mouth’, OE tot ‘projection’, MLG tote ‘top, point’. Of unknown origin. Holthausen AEEW 351; Vries ANEW 596.

ON tún ‘hedged plot, enclosure’, OE tún ‘enclosure, court, yard’, OFris tūn ‘fence’, OS tūn id., OHG zūn id. (< *tūniz). Borrowed from Celtic, cf. Gaul Δοῦνον, OIr dún ‘castle’, OW din id. Torp NNEO 816 (to Gk δύναμις ‘power, might’); Wood MLN XVI 18 (to *teuxanan); Torp-Falk 165; Holthausen AEEW 355; Pokorny I 263; Vries ANEW 600 (related to OIr dún); Zalizniak II 190; Onions 934; Martynov Jazyk 31–34 (from Slav *tynъ ‘fence’); Lerchner 164; Bammesberger Nom. 73; Kluge-Seebold 904.
ON sam-tÿnis ‘abutting, adjoining’, OE ze-tÿne ‘court’, MHG ge-ziune ‘fencing’. Derived from *tūnan ~ *tūnaz. Holthausen AEEW 358; Vries ANEW 603.

Norw dial. tút ‘muzzle, snout’, EFris tūte ‘pipe, snout’, MLG tūte ‘blow-horn’. Related to *tutō(n) ~ tutaz. Torp-Falk 167; Onions 933. *twalifa-wentruz ~ *twalifa-wentrjaz adj.: Goth twalib-wintrus ‘twelve years old’, OE twelf-wintre id., OFris twelef-wintre id., MLG twelef-wintre id. Compound of *twalifa and *wentruz ~ *wentrjaz (see *wentruz). Carr Nom. comp. 65.

Goth twalif ‘twelve’, ON tólf id. (< *twaluf-), OE twelf id., OFris twelef id., OS twelif id., OHG zwelif id. A structure similar to that of *ainlifa. Structurally close to Lith dvÿlika id. Torp-Falk 172; Holthausen AEEW 356; Feist 485; Porzig Gliederung 146; Fraenkel 108; Vries ANEW 594; Onions 951; Lehmann GED 350; Ross-Berns Numerals 596–597; Kluge-Seebold 920.
ON tólfti ‘twelfth’, OE twelfta id., OFris twilifta id., MDu twalefde id., OHG zwelifto id. Derived from *twalifa. Holthausen AEEW 356; Vries ANEW 594; Onions 951; Ross-Berns Numerals 634.
ON tylft ‘body of twelve’, OFrank tualepti id. Derived from *twalifa. Vries ANEW 602.

Dan tvige ‘branch’, OE twiz ‘branch, twig’, MLG twīch id., OHG zwīg id. Related to *twō(u). Close to Slav *dvigъ ‘branch’ (Trubachev Etim. 4–6). Torp-Falk 173; Holthausen AEEW 357; Onions 951; Ross-Berns Numerals 659; Kluge-Seebold 918.
Norw tvisl ‘unstable person’, OE twisla ‘fork (of a river, road)’ (n-stem), MLG twil ‘branch split into a fork’. Derived from *twiz. Pokorny I 230; Ross-Berns Numerals 662.
Norw twisla ‘to vacillate’, OE twislian ‘to fork, to branch’, MHG zwiselen ‘to fork’. Derived from *twislaz ~ *twizlaz. Pokorny I 230; Ross-Berns Numerals 662.

Norw tvist ‘flaw in a fabric’, OE twist ‘branch, fork (?)’, mæst-twist ‘rope to support a mast’, OFris twist ‘dissension’, OS twist id., MHG zwist id. Structurally close to Skt dviṣṭha- ‘staying in two places, ambiguous’. Further connected with *twiz. Mayrhofer II 86; Pokorny I 232; Vries ANEW 602; Lehmann GED 352; Ross-Berns Numerals 660.

ON tvisvar ‘twice’, OE twiwa id., OFris twi(r)a id., OS twio id., OHG zwirōr id. Derived from *twiz. Bezzenberger BB VII 77; Torp-Falk 173; Holthausen AEEW 357; Kluge Nom. Stamm. 260; Loewe KZ XLVII 99–102; Lehmann GED 352.

MHG zwir, zwier ‘twice’. Continues IE *d⁄is- ‘twofold, twice’: Skt dviъ, Av biš, Gk δίς, Lat bis. See *twō(u). Bartho-lomae 966; Walde-Hofmann I 107; Mayrhofer II 86; Pokorny I 231–232; Frisk I 398–399; Lehmann GED 352; Kluge-Seebold 919–920.

ON adj. tvinnr ‘twofold, double’, OE twin id., MLG sbst. twern ‘two-cored thread’, MHG zwirn id. Derived from *twiz. Structurally close to Lat bīnī ‘two by two, pair’ < *d⁄isno-. Torp-Falk 173; Holthausen AEEW 357; Walde-Hofmann I 106; Pokorny I 231; Chemodanov SGGJa I 86; Vries ANEW 602; Onions 952; Ross-Berns Numerals 645–646; Kluge-Seebold 920.
ON tvinna ‘to twine, to twist thread’, MLG twernen id., OHG zwirnēn id. Derived from *twiznaz. Torp-Falk 173–174; Vries ANEW 602.

Goth tweifls ‘doubt’, OFris twīfil id., MLG twīfel id., OHG zwīfal id. Etymologically identical with Gk di-πλόος ‘double, twofold’, Lat duplus id., Umbr dupla, OIr díabul id., cf. also Av bifra- ‘comparison’ continuing *d⁄i-plo- ~ *d⁄eiplo- (Schmidt KZ XVI 431; Pedersen Kelt. Gr. I 237). Grienberger Got. 211; Zupitza Gutt. 16; Bartholomae 965; Torp-Falk 173; Solmsen PBB XXVII 358–360 (derivative in -l- of a form similar to Lith dvejópas ‘twofold’); Thurneysen Zfcelt Ph XIII 107; Walde-Hofmann I 383–384; Feist 485–486; Pokorny I 230, 802; Zalizniak I 136; Frisk I 121–122; Lehmann GED 351; Ross-Berns Numerals 663; Kluge-Seebold 918.
Goth tweifljan ‘to cause doubt’, OS twīflian, twīflōn id., MHG zwīvelōn id. Derived from *twīflaz. Feist 485; Lehmann GED 351.

Goth pl. masc. tweihnai ‘two each’, ON tvénn ‘double’, OE tweóne ‘double, not simple’, OFris twīne ‘two, two each, double’. Derived from *twīxōn. Brugmann Grundriß II/2 8–11, 64, 78; Torp-Falk 173; Holthausen AEEW 356; Feist 486; Pokorny I 231; Onions 952; Lehmann GED 351; Ross-Berns Numerals 643.

OE tweó ‘doubt’, OS tweho id., OHG zweho id. Close to Skt dvika- ‘consisting of two’ (Brugmann Distr. 36–37). Further see *twīflaz. The original numeric meaning of *twīxōn is preserved in its derivative *twīxnaz. Holthausen AEEW 356; Feist 486; Mayrhofer II 84; Pokorny I 231; Lehmann GED 351. *twō(u) num: masc., fem., neut. Goth twai, ## þ

ON þefja ‘to stir, to thicken’, EFris dafen ‘to hit, to pound’, OHG bi-debben ‘to suppress’. Close to Lith tapa U, tapÿti ‘to paint, to color, to smear’, further related to Lith tepū, tēpti ‘to smear, to anoint’, Slav *tepǫ, *tepti ‘to hit, to beat’. Trautmann BSW 319; Pokorny I 1056; Fraenkel 1057, 1081 Vries ANEW 606–607.

Goth þahan ‘to keep secret, to be silent’, ON þegja ‘to be silent’, OS thagian id., OHG dagēn id. Identical with Lat taceō id., Gk τήκω ‘to melt’ (for the semantic mechanism see Trubachev PIEJa 100–105). Fick I 439; Prellwitz BB XXI 162 (adduces OIr tachtaid ‘to suffocate’); Meillet MSL XV 351–352; Zupitza Gutt. 210; Torp-Falk 177; Walde- Hofmann II 641–642; Feist 487–488; Porzig Gliederung 107; Pokorny I 1055; Chemodanov SGGJa I 91; Vries ANEW 607; Frisk II 891; Lehmann GED 353.

Norw svin-toks ‘badger’, MLG das id., OHG dahs id. Originally, *‘builder’ connected with *þexsanan (Schrader-Nehring I 180). Torp-Falk 178; Sommer IF XXXI 359–360 (against connection with *þexsanan); Pokorny I 1059; Kluge-Seebold 159.

ON þak ‘thatch, roof ’, OE äæc id., MLG dack id., OHG dah id. Connected with Gk τέγος, τέγη ‘roof, AEEW 357; Walde-Hofmann I 381–383, Feist 484–485; Sturtevant JEGP XXVII 371–382; Mayrhofer II 82; Pokorny I 228–232; Vries ANEW 601; Szemerényi Numerals 24; Frisk I 424–425; Onions 951–952; Lehmann GED 350; Ross- Berns Numerals 565–571 (compounds with *enaz in WGmc); Kluge-Seebold 918; Orel AED 79; Adams TB 598–599. house’, OIr tech ‘house’, Lat toga ‘covering, garment’, Lith stógas ‘roof ’ and is eventually derived from *(s)teg- ‘to cover’. Torp-Falk 176; Trautmann BSW 288; Holthausen AEEW 359; Walde-Hofmann II 654–655; Pokorny I 1013–1014; Fraenkel 911; Vries ANEW 605; Zalizniak II 184; Frisk II 780–781; Onions 914; Kluge-Seebold 159.

ON þekja ‘to cover, to thatch’, OE äeccan ‘to cover’, OFris thekka id., OS thekkian id., OHG decken id. Derived from *þakan ‘roof ’. Torp-Falk 176; Holthausen AEEW 361; Pokorny I 1014 (to OIr -tuigiur ‘to cover’ < *togeƒō); Vries ANEW 607; Onions 914; Kluge- Seebold 165.
ON þekja ‘roof ’, OFris thekke id., OHG decka id. Derived from *þakan. Torp-Falk 176; Pokorny I 1014; Vries ANEW 607.

ON á þamba ‘with full belly, swollen’. Identical with Slav *tǫpъ ‘blunt’ (Fick I 443; Lidén IF XIX 362– 365) and Toch A tampe ‘force, ability’. Torp-Falk 181; Pokorny I 1065; Vries ANEW 605; Bammesberger Nom. 238; Heidermanns 611–612; Adams TB 254.

Goth þan ‘but, then’, ON þá ‘then’, OE äan id., OFris than id., OS than id., OHG dan id. Continues demonstrative *þa- (see *þat) with a particle *na. Similar to *xwana ~ *xwannai. Cf. also the univerbation with an unclear *đē in Goth þan-de ‘while’, OE äen-de-n id. (< *þandin), OHG danta ‘because, that’. Skutsch Glotta I 305–306 (to Lat tum ‘then’); Brugmann Grundriß II/2 736; Hirt IF VI 69 (*đē to -dō in Lat quandō ‘when’); Torp-Falk 174–175; Dal NTS IX 208, 215; Holthausen AEEW 360, 362; Feist 489–490; Walde-Hoffmann II 398 (*đē from IE *dhē- ‘to put’); Pokorny I 1086–1087; Vries ANEW 605; Lehmann GED 354–355.
ON þanan ‘thence’, OE äanan id., OS thanana id., OHG danana id. Derived from *þana. Torp- Falk 175; Holthausen AEEW 361; Vries ANEW 605; Lehmann GED 354.

ON þengill ‘prince, king’, OE äenzel ‘prince’. Derived from *þenxanan. Sütterlin BB XVII 165 (to Gk τάσσω ‘to draw up, to form, to array’); Holthausen AEEW 362; Pokorny I 1068 (to OS ā-thengian ‘to take out, to carry out’); Vries ANEW 608; Lehmann GED 359.

Goth þaho ‘clay’, ON þá ‘thawed ground’, OE äóhe, äó ‘clay’, MLG dā id., OHG dāha ‘clay vessel, clay’. Derived from *þenxanan. Torp- Falk 179; Holthausen AEEW 366; Feist 488; Trier Lehm 16–23; Pokorny I 1068; Vries ANEW 609; Zalizniak II 228; Lehmann GED 353 (from a non-IE source); Kluge-Seebold 827–828.

Goth uf-þanjan ‘to stretch out, to strive for’, ON þenja ‘to stretch, to extend’, OE äennan id., OS thennian id., OHG dennen id. Related to Skt tanóti ‘to expand, to extend, to spread’, Gk τείνω ‘to stretch’, Lat teneō ‘to hold’, Lith tīnstu, tīnti ‘to swell’. Torp- Falk 178; Holthausen AEEW 362; Walde-Hofmann II 664–665; Feist 513–514; Mayrhofer I 475; Pokorny I 1065–1066; Fraenkel 1099; Vries ANEW 609; Frisk II 863–865; Lehmann GED 374; Kluge-Seebold 167.

Goth þagks ‘thanks, favor’, ON fem. þǫkk ‘gratitude’, OE äanc ‘favor, grace, satisfaction, thanks’, OFris thank ‘thanks, favor’, OS thank id., OHG danc id. Connected with *þankjanan. Torp- Falk 179; Holthausen AEEW 360; Feist 487; Vries ANEW 631; Zalizniak II 190; Onions 914; Lehmann GED 353; Heidermanns 612; Kluge-Seebold 162.

Goth þagkjan ‘to think, to plan’, ON þekkja ‘to perceive, to know, to comply’, OE äencan ‘to think’, OFris thenza, thanka id., OS thenkian id., OHG denken id. Structurally and etymologically close to Lat tongeō ‘to know’ (Vani‘ek 99). Further cf. Toch B cänk- ‘to please’. Aufrecht KZ I 353; Arbois de Jubainville MSL XI 330 (to OIr tongu ‘to swear’); Wood CPh III 85–86 (to Gk τεταγών ‘grasping’); Zupitza Gutt. 216; Torp-Falk 179; Holthausen AEEW 362; Walde-Hofmann II 690; Feist 487; Pokorny I 1088; Chemodanov SGGJa I 68, 91; Vries ANEW 607; Onions 917; Lehmann GED 353; Kluge-Seebold 170–171; Adams TB 253.
ON þekkr ‘agreeable, pleasant, pliable’, MDu ge-denke ‘mindful’, OHG in-denki ‘favorite’. Derived from *þankjanan. Karsten NB II 446; Heidermanns 613.

ON þakka ‘to thank’, OE äancian id., OFris thonkia id., OS thankōn id., OHG gi-dancōn id. Derived from *þankaz. Torp-Falk 179; Holthausen AEEW 360; Vries ANEW 605; Lehmann GED 353; Peeters IF XCIV 297.

Goth þar ‘there’, ON þar id., OE äær id., OFris thēr id., OS thār id., OHG dār id. Derived from *þa- (see *þat) with a locative -r. Cf. also OS tharod ‘there’, OHG thar-ōt id. with the second part related to Skt utá ‘and, also’, Gk *ὑτε in Gk ἠύτε ‘as, like as’, pron. οὖτος. Torp-Falk 175; Holthausen AEEW 360; Feist 490 (to Skt tár-hi ‘then, at that time’); Pokorny I 74, 1087; Frisk I 646; Vries ANEW 605; Onions 916; Lehmann GED 355; Kluge-Seebold 159.

Goth ga-þarban ‘to abstain’, OS tharbōn ‘to be needy’, OHG darbēn ‘to miss, to lack’. Derived from *þarfa. Kirchhoff KZ I 39; Torp-Falk 182; Feist 490; Seebold 509; Lehmann GED 355; Kluge-Seebold 162–163 (from *þarbō).
ON þerfi-ligr ‘useful, convenient’, OE äearf-líc ‘necessary, useful’. Derived from *þarfaz ~ *þarfōn. Vries ANEW 609.

ON þarfa ‘to need, to want’, OE äearfian ‘to be in need’, OFris thervia ‘to get lost’, OS tharbon ‘to miss, to lack’. Derived from *þarfaz ~ *þarfōn. Torp-Falk 182; Holthausen AEEW 361; Vries ANEW 606; Lehmann GED 355; Heidermanns 613.

Goth þarf ‘to need’, ON þarf id., OE äearf id., OFris thor, thorf, thurf id., OS tharf id., OHG darf id. Related to Skt tṛpyati, tṛpṇóti ‘to satisfy oneself, to be pleased’, Gk τέρπομαι ‘to enjoy’, Lith tarpstū, taıpti ‘to thrive, to prosper’, OPrus enterpo ‘to fit’ (Kluge KZ XXV 311–312; Saussure MSL VII 83–84: semantic connection between ‘taking away’ as in Skt pa≤u-tṛp- ‘stealing cattle’ and ‘enjoying’). Grienberger Got. 213–214; Kluge KZ XXV 311–312; Wood MLN XX 102– 104; Collitz MLN XX 105; Uhlenbeck TNTL XXV 299–300; Meringer IF XVIII 225–226; Torp-Falk 182; Trautmann BSW 314; Sverdrup NTS II 83 (on the semantic development in Gmc); Feist 491–492; Holthausen AEEW 372; Jóhannesson IEW 444; Mayrhofer I 524; Pokorny I 1077–1078; Fraenkel 1062–1063; Vries ANEW 627; Frisk II 881–882; Seebold 509–510; Lehmann GED 356–357; Kluge-Seebold 201.

Goth þarfs ‘necessary’, ala-þarba ‘being in want of everything’, ON þarfr ‘useful’, þarfi‘needy’, OE äearfa ‘destitute, needing’, OHG darbo ‘missing, lacking’. Derived from *þarfa. Karsten NB I 51; Torp-Falk 182; Holthausen AEEW 361; Feist 490; Pokorny I 1078; Vries ANEW 606; Seebold 509; Lehmann GED 25, 355; Bammesberger Nom. 238; Heidermanns 613–614.

Goth þarba ‘want, need’, ON þǫrf ‘need, necessity’, OE äearf ‘need’, OFris therve id., OS tharf id., OHG darba ‘fast, lent’. Identical with Lith tarpā ‘thriving, growth’. Further related to *þarfa. Torp-Falk 182; Trautmann BSW 314; Holthausen AEEW 361; Feist 490; Pokorny I 1078; Fraenkel 1062–1063; Vries ANEW 631–632; Zalizniak II 175; Seebold 509; Bammesberger Nom. 107; Kluge-Seebold 163.

ON þarmr ‘gut’, OE äearm id., OFris therm id., MLG darm id., OHG darm id. Identical with Gk τόρμος ‘hole’, τράμις: τό τρῆμα τῆς ἑδρας, τινὲς đnteron (Hes.) and further connected with τείρω ‘to rub’, τερέω ‘to drill, to bore’. Torp-Falk 182; Holthausen AEEW 361; Jóhannesson IEW 442 (to *ter- ‘to cross over’); Pokorny I 1071–1072; Vries ANEW 606; Zalizniak I 136; Frisk II 913; Kluge-Seebold 163.

ON þerra ‘to dry, to wipe’, OE äirran ‘to dry, to wipe’, MDu dorren ‘to make dry’, OHG derren id. Causative of *þersanan. Structurally identical with Skt tarßáyati ‘to cause to become dry’, Lat torreō ‘to parch, to roast’. Torp- Falk 183; Walde-Hofmann II 694; Mayrhofer I 525; Pokorny I 1078– 1079; Vries ANEW 609; Seebold 515; Lehmann GED 151; Kluge-Seebold 190.

Norw dial. tarre ‘malt drying device’, MLG dare ‘dryer’, OHG darra ‘dryer, drying device’. See *þersanan. Structurally similar to Arm t'afi‘pole for drying of grapes, roost’, Gk ταρσός ‘frame of wicker-work, crate, flat basket, for drying cheeses on’ (Lidén Arm. St. 45–46). Torp-Falk 183; Pokorny I 1078–1079; Frisk II 858; Kluge- Seebold 163.

Goth þata ‘that’, ON þat id., OE äæt id., OFris thet id., OS that id., OHG daz id. The auslaut in Goth is not clear (a particle?), see Kieckers HG 140. Related to IE *tod id.: Skt tát, Av ta∆, Gk acc. τόν, Lat is-tud, Slav *to. Cf. the univerbation of this stem with *uxwe in Goth þauh ‘or, but’, ON þó ‘though, yet, nevertheless’, OE äeáh ‘yet, still, however, nevertheless’, OFris thach, thāch id., OS thōh id., OHG doh id. (with a difficult auslaut in Goth). Bartholomae 613–623; Torp-Falk 174; Holthausen AEEW 360–361; Walde-Hofmann I 721; Feist 490–491 (Goth þauh to Skt tú ‘now, but’); Mayrhofer I 465; Pokorny I 1086– 1087; Vries ANEW 606, 615; Frisk II 907; Onions 914, 919; Lehmann GED 356; Kluge-Seebold 186–187.

Goth þaþro-h ‘from there’, ON þaära ‘there’, OE äæder ‘thither’. Suffixal derivative of *þa- (see *þata). Identical with Skt tátra, tatrā ‘there, thither’. The suffix is close to that of Lat intrā ‘within’. Torp-Falk 174; Holthausen AEEW 359; Walde-Hofmann I 712; Feist 491; Mayrhofer I 471; Pokorny I 1087; Vries ANEW 605; Onions 918; Lehmann GED 356.

ON þeysa ‘to make to spurt out, to gush forth’, MHG dōsen ‘to rage’. Probably based on IE *teu(H)-: Skt tavīti ‘to be strong’, Gk σῶς ‘safe and sound, alive’. On the other hand, a comparison with Av taošayeiti ‘to untie, to unbind’ of the same derivational structure might be not less attractive. Torp- Falk 188; Pokorny I 1084–1086; Vries ANEW 610 (to ON þjóstr ‘anger, fury’).
ON þeyta ‘to make sound’, OE á-äeótan ‘to sound’. Causative of *þeutanan. Holthausen AEEW 365; Vries ANEW 610.
ON þána ‘to melt’, OE ze-äawenian ‘to moisten’. Related to *þawjanan. Torp-Falk 175.
ON þeyr ‘thaw’, E thaw id., Du dooi id. Derived from *þawjanan. Torp-Falk 175; Vries ANEW 610.

ON þeyja ‘to thaw’, MLG douwen id., OHG douwen id. A comparison with Osset tajyn ~ tajun ‘to thaw, to melt’, Slav *tajati id. is not precise but yet, very probable. Meillet MSL IX 154–155, XXIII 50–51; Torp- Falk 175; Holthausen AEEW 361; Pokorny I 1054; Vries ANEW 609–610; Onions 914; Abaev III 222–223; Kluge- Seebold 817.

ON þefa ‘to smell, to sniff’, OE äefian ‘to pant, to be agitated’. Related to Skt tápati ‘to warm, to burn’, Lat tepeō ‘to be tepid’, OIr té ‘hot’, Slav *teplъ ‘warm’. Torp-Falk 180; Trautmann BSW 319; Walde-Hofmann II 667–668; Pokorny I 1069–1070.

ON þiggja ‘to receive, to accept’, OE äiczan id. Related to OIr techtaid ‘to have, to possess’, Lith tenkū, tēkti ‘to suffice, to suit’. Torp-Falk 176; Holthausen AEEW 364; Jóhannesson IEW 433; Pokorny I 1058; Fraenkel 1077; Vries ANEW 610; Seebold 510–511.
ON þegn-gildi ‘wergild for a thane’, OE äezen-zilde id. Compound of *þeznaz and *zelđjan ~ *zelđjōn. Carr Nom. comp. 82 (parallel formations).

ON þegn ‘thane, freeman’, OE äezen ‘servant’, OS thegan ‘man’, OHG degan ‘warrior, hero, servant’. Close to Gk τέκνον ‘child’ < IE *tekno- (Zupitza Gutt. 76, 140). Cf. further Skt tákman- ‘offspring’ also derived from IE *tek- ‘to give birth’. Zupitza Gutt. 76; Torp-Falk 177; Holthausen AEEW 362; Feist 498; Mayrhofer I 466; Pokorny I 1057; Chemodanov SGGJa I 108; Vries ANEW 607; Zalizniak I 137; Frisk II 867; Onions 914; Bammesberger Nom. 73; Kluge-Seebold 166–167.

ON sbst. þjónn ‘servant, attendant’, OE sbst. äeówen ‘female servant’. Derived from *þezwaz. Structurally close to Lith tek~nas ‘runner, messenger’. Holthausen AEEW 363; Fraenkel 1077; Vries ANEW 614.

ON þjóna ‘to serve’, OFris thiānia id., OS thionōn id., OHG dionōn id. Derived from *þezwanaz with analogical changes in ON. Torp- Falk 177; van Wijk IF XXIV 37 (ON < OS); Vries ANEW 614; Kluge-Seebold 179.
ON þjónusta, þjónasta ‘service, attendance’, OE þeónest ‘service’ (unless from OHG), OFris thianest id., OS thionost id., OHG dionōst id. Derived from *þezwanōjan. Torp-Falk 177.

Goth þius ‘boy, house-servant’, ON -þér (in prop.), þÿ ‘serf, bondsman’ (neut.), OE äeów ‘servant, slave’, OS theo- (in cmpn.), OHG adj. teo ‘unfree’. Identical with Skt táku- ‘rushing, quick’, takvá- ‘quick’ < *tek ⁄⁄ós derived from IE *tek ⁄- ‘to run’ (Solmsen KZ XXXIV 2). Zupitza Gutt. 76–77 (to *þeznaz); Brugmann IF XIX 381–382 (semantic parallel in Gk τρόχις ‘runner, servant’ from τρέχω ‘to run’); Torp-Falk 177; Holthausen AEEW 362; Feist 497– 498; Mayrhofer I 466–467; Pokorny I 1059; Vries ANEW 609; Zalizniak II 179; Seebold KZ LXXXI 125, 130; Scardigli Goten 31; Peeters IF LXXXI 29– 30 (doubts the connection with *tek⁄- on phonetic grounds); Hamp IF LXXXVIII 93–94 (contra Peeters); Lehmann GED 362; Bammesberger Nom. 69–70; Heidermanns 616; Kluge-Seebold 170.
ON þerna ‘servant, maid’, OS thiorna ‘virgin’, OHG diorna id. Derived from *þezwaz. Mezger MLN LVII 432–433; Peters IF LXXXI 29–30; Hamp IF LXXXVIII 93–94; Kluge-Seebold 183.
Goth ana-þiwan ‘to subdue, to subjugate’, ON þjá ‘to enslave, to constrain’, OE äeówian ‘to serve, to enslave’. Derived from *þezwaz. Holthausen AEEW 363; Feist 45; Vries ANEW 611; Lehmann GED 34.

Goth þiwi ‘maidservant’, OE äéowe id., OS thiwi, thiu id., OHG diu id. Derived from *þezwaz (Hirt Urgerm. II 62: from *tek⁄ī). Holthausen AEEW 363; Feist 499; Pokorny I 1057; Vries ANEW 629; Zalizniak II 181; Lehmann GED 362; Bammesberger Nom. 114.

MHG dehsan ‘to swingle (flax)’ (str. pret.). Related to Hitt takš- ‘to tie, to join’, Toch B tāks- ‘to chop up, to grind up’, Skt tákṣati ‘to fashion, to create, to do carpentry’, Av taš- ‘to cut (out)’, Lat texō ‘to weave’, Lith taša U, tašÿti ‘to chop off, to do carpentry’, Slav *tesati ‘to hew’. Bartholomae 644–645; Torp- Falk 177; Trautmann BSW 319–320; Walde-Hofmann II 678–679; Mayrhofer I 468; Pokorny I 1058–1059; Fraenkel 1065; Seebold 511; Adams TB 286.

ON þexla ‘axe’, MLG desele id., OHG dehsala id. Structurally close to Lat tēlum ‘dart, spear, javelin’, OIr tál ‘axe’ < *tō˚slo-, Slav *teslo ~ *tesla id. Related to *þexsanan. Torp-Falk 178; Walde-Hofmann II 656; Pokorny I 1058; Vries ANEW 609; Seebold 511; Vasmer-Trubachev IV 51; Kluge- Seebold 165.

ON þistill ‘thistle’, OE äistel id., EFris dīssel id., OS thistil id., OHG distil id. (also fem. distila). Derivationally identical with Lat textilis ‘woven’. Further related to *þexsanan. Torp-Falk 184; Holthausen AEEW 366; Walde- Hofmann II 678–679; Pokorny I 1016 (to IE *steig- ‘to prick’); Fraenkel 1065; Vries ANEW 611; Zalizniak II 176; Onions 918; Kluge-Seebold 185.
ON þykt ‘density, thickness’, OHG dickida id. Derived from *þekwuz. Vries ANEW 630; Heidermanns 618.
ON þjokkva ‘to make thick, to thicken’, OHG dickōn id. Derived from *þekwuz. Vries ANEW 614; Heidermanns 618.

ON þjokkr, þykkr ‘thick’, OE äicce id., OS thikki id., OHG dicki id. Related to OIr tiug id. < *tegu-. Karsten NB II 411–412; Zupitza Gutt. 95, 99; Torp-Falk 176; Holthausen AEEW 364; Pokorny I 1057; Chemodanov SGGJa I 100; Vries ANEW 614, 630; Onions 916; Bammesberger Nom. 263; Heidermanns 617–618, KZ XCIX 282; Kluge-Seebold 178–179.

ON þel ‘nap on woollen fabrics’, OE äel ‘thin piece of wood, plate, plank’, OHG dil ‘board’ (masc.). Structurally close to Slav *tъlo ‘floor, ground’. Further cf. Skt neut. tala- ‘plane, surface, level, palm’, Lat tellūs ‘earth’, OIr talam id., OPrus talus ‘floor’. See *þeljōn. Pedersen Kelt. Gr. I 132, 380; Torp-Falk 183–184; Trautmann BSW 321; Holthausen AEEW 362; Walde- Hofmann II 655–656; Pokorny I 1061; Vries ANEW 608; Kluge-Seebold 179; Vasmer-Trubachev IV 65.
ON þili ‘plank’, OS thili ‘board, plank’, OHG dili id. (masc.). See *þeljōn. Vries ANEW 610; Kluge- Seebold 179.
ON þilja ‘to cover with planks’, OE äilian ‘to plank’, OHG gi-dillōn id. Derived from *þeljan, *þeljōn. Holthausen AEEW 365; Vries ANEW 610.

ON þilja ‘board, plank’, OE äille ‘boarding, flooring, floor’, OFris thille ‘plank floor’, OHG dilla ‘board, floor’. Close to Gk τηλία ‘a kind of board’. See *þelan. Torp-Falk 184; Holthausen AEEW 365; Walde-Hofmann II 655– 656; Pokorny I 1061; Vries ANEW 610; Zalizniak I 156; Frisk II 892; Onions 247; Kluge-Seebold 179.

MDu demster ‘dark, gloomy’, MLG vinster ‘dark’ (with dissimilation), OHG dinstar id. From *tem 6 sroclose to Skt pl. támisrāъ ‘dark night’, Av tāyraēšu ‘in the darkness’, Lat pl. tenebrae ‘darkness’ (Trautmann apud Walde- Hofmann), further connected with Skt támas- ‘darkness’, Lith témsta, témti ‘to become dark’, Slav *tьma ‘darkness’ < *tem- ‘dark’ (Szemerényi IBK XV 200). Bartholomae 650; Torp-Falk 180–181; Schwentner PBB XLV 453–456; Trautmann BSW 322–323; Walde- Hofmann II 664; Mayrhofer I 479; Pokorny I 1063–1064; Fraenkel 1080; Vasmer-Trubachev IV 133–134; Heidermanns 618–619.

OS thimm ‘dark’, OHG sbst. demar ‘darkness’. Close to Skt tamasá- ‘dark-colored’, Av t 6 maŋha- ‘dark’, Lith tamsā ‘darkness’. Further see *þemstraz (Weihe PBB XXX 56). Bartholomae 648; Torp-Falk 180; Schwentner PBB XLV 454–455; Schulze Kl. Schr. 126–127; Mayrhofer I 478; Pokorny I 1063– 1064; Lühr MSS XXXV 81; Bammesberger Nom. 252–253; Heidermanns 619.

ON þind ‘diaphragm’. Identical with OIr tét ‘chord’ < *t–tā. Substantivized feminine of the participle *t–to-, cf. Skt tatá- ‘stretched’, Gk τατός id., Lat tentus ‘held (fast)’. Torp-Falk 180; Walde-Hofmann II 666; Pokorny I 1065– 1066; Vries ANEW 610; Frisk II 864.
ON þing-hús ‘house in which a meeting is held’, OS thing-hūs ‘law-court’, OHG ding-hūs id. Compound of *þenzaz ~ *þenxaz and *xūsan. Carr Nom. comp. 79 (šnot Germanic as the Thing met in the open”).
ON þing-maär ‘member of the assembly’, OFris thing-mon ‘advocate’, MLG dink-man id., OHG dingman id. Compound of *þenzaz ~ *þenxaz and *mannz. Carr Nom. comp. 79 (parallel formations).

Goth þeihs ‘occasion, time’, ON þing ‘assembly, thing’, OE äinz ‘thing, meeting’, OFris thing ‘assembly; legal case; thing’, OS thing id., OHG ding id. Probably connected with *þenxanan (Grienberger Got. 214). Kluge QF XXXII 21 (to Lat tempus ‘time’, presumably, from *tenk⁄o-); Osthoff IF VIII 40–41 (to Gk τόπος ‘place’); Zupitza Gutt. 140 (to Lith tenkū, tēkti ‘to hold out, to suffice’); Sütterlin BB XVII 165 (to Gk τάσσω ‘to arrange’); Torp- Falk 176; Holthausen AEEW 365; Feist 494; Pokorny I 1067 (šnur germanisch”); Vries ANEW 610–611; Zalizniak II 224; Lehmann GED 360 (to IE *ten-k- ‘to extend, to span’); Kluge-Seebold 181– 182 (to MIr téchtae ‘legality’ < *tenktƒo-).
ON þing-staär ‘place where the assembly is held’, OE äinz-stede id., OHG ding-stat id. (fem.). Compound of *þenzaz ~ *þenxaz and *stađiz. Carr Nom. comp. 52.

ON þinga ‘to hold a meeting’, OE äinzian ‘to intercede, to ask favor, to settle, to speak’, OFris thingia ‘to convey a court examination’, OS thingōn id., OHG dingōn ‘to analyze (in court), to address’. Derived from *þenzaz ~ *þenxaz. Holthausen AEEW 366; Vries ANEW 611.

Goth þeihan ‘to succeed, to make progress, to thrive’, OE äeón ‘to thrive, to flourish, to prosper’, OS thīhan ‘to prosper’, OHG dīhan ‘to become powerful, to grow’. Related to Skt tanákti ‘to cause coagulation’, OIr cotécim ‘to coagulate’, Lith tenkū, tēkti ‘to hold out, to suffice, to reach’ (Grienberger Got. 214). Zupitza Gutt. 140; Torp-Falk 179–180, 184; Feist 493– 494; Holthausen AEEW 366; Trier Lehm 16–18, 20–23; Benveniste Word XIX 253–254; Mayrhofer I 473; Pokorny I 1068; Fraenkel 1077; Onions 923; Seebold 512–514; Lehmann GED 359; Kluge-Seebold 304.
Icel þél ‘sour milk’. Related to *þanxōn. Besides the difference in the ablaut grade, identical with Skt neut. takrá- ‘buttermilk’ < *t–klo-. Torp- Falk 179; Pokorny I 1068.

ON þísl ‘cart-pole’, OE äísl, äíxl ‘beam, pole of a wagon, pole’, MLG dīsel ‘pole (of the harness)’, OHG dīhsala id. Related to *þunzuz. Torp- Falk 179; Holthausen AEEW 366; Pokorny I 1067; Vries ANEW 611; Zalizniak I 148; Kluge-Seebold 167.

ON þéttr ‘tight, heavy’, OE äíht ‘tight, firm, strong’, MLG dichte ‘thick’, MHG dīchte id. Derived from *þenxanan. Identical with OIr técht ‘thick, glutionous’ < *tenk-to-. Further cf. Av taxma- ‘strong, firm’, Lith tánkus ‘thick’, Slav *tǫ‘a ‘cloud’. See *þunzuz. Bartholomae 626–627; Karsten NB II 447; Torp-Falk 179; Feist 488; Holthausen AEEW 365; Kluge 141–142; Trier Lehm 16–23; Pokorny I 1068; Fraenkel 1056; Vries ANEW 609; Onions 923; Lehmann GED 359; Heidermanns 619–620; Kluge-Seebold 178–179.

Goth at-þinsan ‘to draw, to pull up to’, OS pres. uer-thinse ‘subtrahat’, OHG dinsan ‘to draw, to drag, to pull’. Belongs to IE *tens- ‘to extend’: Skt taṃsayati ‘to draw to and fro’, Lith tęsiū, t.sti ‘to tug, to extend’ (Walde- Hofmann II 666). Schmidt Voc. I 71; Persson Beiträge 341–342; Torp-Falk 180; Trautmann BSW 318–319; Feist 62; Mayrhofer I 465; Pokorny I 1068– 1069; Fraenkel 1083–1084; Seebold 514–515; Lehmann GED 47; Kluge- Seebold 63.

ON þjarfr ‘unleavened, fresh (of water)’, OE äeorf id., OFris therf ‘violent’, OS therbi ‘unleavened’, OHG derb id. Probably related to Lat torpeō ‘to be stiff’, Lith tirpstū, tiıpti ‘to stiffen’, Slav *tьrpîti id. (Fick I 444; Persson Beiträge 437–438). Karsten NB I 25; Torp-Falk 183 (to Gk τρόφις ‘wellfed, stout, large’); Trautmann BSW 325; Holthausen AEEW 363; Walde- Hofmann II 692; Pokorny I 1024; Fraenkel 1100–1101; Vries ANEW 612; Heidermanns 620; Kluge-Seebold 172.

Goth þairh ‘through’, OE äurh, äerh, äorh id., OFris thruc, thriuc id., OS thurh id., OHG duruh, thuruch id. Cf. also OHG derh ‘perforated’. Based on IE *ter- as in Skt tiráъ ‘across, over, apart’, Av tarō ‘aside, over’, OIr tar ‘across, above’ and the like (Thumb KZ XVI 198–201) followed by *-xwe < *k ⁄e. Bartholomae 641–642; Torp-Falk 181; Holthausen AEEW 364; Feist 488; Pokorny I 1074–1076; Mayrhofer I 503–504; Onions 920; Vendryes T- 28–29, 125–126; Lehmann GED 354; Kluge-Seebold 200–201.

ON þjarka ‘to quarrel, to wrangle’, OE á-äercian ‘to despise’. Related to Skt tárjati ‘to threaten, to scold’, Gk τάρβος ‘alarm, terror’, ταρβέω ‘to be frightened’ (Kuhn KZ XIII 454). Zupitza Gutt. 96; Holthausen AEEW 364; Mayrhofer I 485; Pokorny I 1077; Vries ANEW 612 Frisk II 855–856.

Goth part. ga-þaursans ‘to dry up’. Related to Skt tṛßyati ‘to be thirsty’, Gk τέρσομαι ‘to be dry’, Lat torreō ‘to dry’. Torp-Falk 183; Walde- Hofmann II 694; Feist 206; Mayrhofer I 525; Pokorny I 1078–1079; Frisk II 882; Seebold 515; Lehmann GED 151, 358.

ON þiäurr ‘partridge’. Close to Skt tittirá- ‘partridge’, Gk τέτραξ ‘grouse’, τέταρος ‘pheasant’, Latv teteris ‘grouse’, Slav *tetervь id. (Fick I 58, 441). Originally, of imitative origin (Persson UUÅ 1891 196). Torp-Falk 184; Mayrhofer I 500; Pokorny I 1079; Vries ANEW 610 Frisk II 886.

Goth þiubs ‘thief’, ON þjófr id., OE äeóf id., OFris thiāf id., OS thiof id., OHG diob id. Related to Lith taupÿti ‘to keep, to save’, Latv ta Upît id. (Trautmann Zd Wf VII 267–269). Meyer NGWG 1905 185–186 (to Gk τυφλός ‘blind’); Grienberger Got. 215 (to Gk τύπτω ‘to strike’); Torp NNEO 792 (to ON þópta ‘rowing bench’); Torp-Falk 187; Bartholomae IF IX 270 (to Av t 6 viš ‘coarseness’); Holthausen AEEW 363; Feist 497; Pokorny I 1085 (to Lith t~pti ‘to cower, to kneel down’); Fraenkel 1067; Zalizniak I 137; Frisk II 950; Vries ANEW 613 (contra Torp); Onions 916; Fraenkel 74; Lehmann GED 362 (Germanic innovation); Kluge-Seebold 179.
ON þÿfä ‘theft’, OE äífä id., OFris thiūfthe, thiūvethe id., OS thiubda id., MHG dēfte id. Derived from *þeubaz. Holthausen AEEW 365; Vries ANEW 629–630; Onions 915.
Goth þiubi ‘theft’, ON þÿfi ‘stolen goods’, OE äífe-feoh id. Cf. also OHG fem. diuba ‘theft’. Derived from *þeubaz. Holthausen AEEW 365; Feist 497; Lehmann GED 362; Bammesberger Nom. 67.
ON þjóä-konungr ‘sovereign’, OE äeód-cyninz id., OS thiod-kuning id. Compound of *þeuđō and *kuninzaz ~ *kununzaz. Carr Nom. comp. 53.
ON þjóä-land ‘empire’, OE äeód-land ‘region, country’. Compound of *þeuđō and *lanđan. Carr Nom. comp. 82 (parallel formations).

Goth þiudans ‘king’, ON þjóäann id., OE äeóden id., OS thiodan ‘ruler’. Derived from *þeuđō. Structurally close to Illyr PN Teutana, Gaul Toutonos (Krahe Glotta XVII 93–94, IF XLVII 327). Holthausen AEEW 363; Feist 496–497; Porzig Gliederung 130; Pokorny I 1084; Vries ANEW 613; Meid IF LXII 260, 279; Benveniste IEL 75, 90, 246–247; Lehmann GED 361; Bammesberger Nom. 82.
ON þjóä-vegr ‘highway’, OE äeód-wez id., MLG dēt-wech id. Compound of *þeuđō and *wezaz. Probably, a calque of Lat via publica. Carr Nom. comp. 79 (internal Germanic loans).

Goth adv. þiudisko ‘like the heathen’, ON þÿäerskr, þÿäverskr ‘German’ (phonetically irregular; a loan?), OE äéodisc ‘gentile, belonging to a people’, OS thiudisc ‘pertaining to people’, MDu dutsch ‘Dutch’, OHG diutisc ‘German’. Derived from *þeuđō. Braune PBB XLIII 436–445 (development of meaning), XLV 145; Weisgerber RMP LXXXVI 97–126 (connected with Teuton); Weisweiler DWG 102–103; Feist 497; Vries ANEW 629; Onions 295; Lehmann GED 362; Must IF XCVII 103–121; Kluge-Seebold 175. *þeuđjanan I wk.vb.: ON þÿäa ‘to explain, to interpret, to signify’, OE ze- äiédan ‘to translate’, OFris bi-thīoda ‘to explain, to translate’, MLG dūden ‘to interpret’, MHG diuten id. Derived from *þeuđō. Torp-Falk 185; Holthausen AEEW 364; Vries ANEW 629; Lehmann GED 361; Kluge-Seebold 174.

Goth þiuþjan ‘to bless, to pronounce good upon’, ON þÿäa ‘to rejoice’, OE ze-äiedan ‘to participate’, MLG dieden ‘to help’, OHG untar-diuten ‘to be subject to’. Derived from *þeuđjaz. Torp-Falk 186; Holthausen AEEW 364; Feist 498; Vries ANEW 629; Lehmann GED 362–363.

ON þÿär ‘kind, meek, amiable’, OE ze-þÿde ‘good’, MDu ge-diede ‘obliging, benevolent’. Derived from *þeuđō (Johansson PBB XV 238). Related to OIr túath ‘left’, with a taboo denotation similar to that of Gk ἀριστερός. Cf. also Goth þiuþ ‘good’. Karsten NB II 447; Torp-Falk 186; Feist 498; Holthausen AEEW 363–365; Walde-Hofmann II 721; Sturtevant JEGP XXXVI 176–177; Trier SG I 108; Pokorny I 1079–1080; Vries ANEW 629; Schubert Erw. 56; Lehmann GED 362–363 (related to OE äeáw ‘custom, usage’, OFris thāw id., OS thau id.); Heidermanns 621–623.

Goth þiuda ‘people, nation’, ON þjóä id. (partly < *þiuđiz), OE äéod id., OFris thiād id., OS thiod, thioda id., OHG diot id. (masc.), prop. Diota. Identical with Hitt tuzzi- ‘army’, Thrac prop. Tiouta, Touta, Illyr prop. Τεύτα, Osc touto ‘citizenry, state’, Umbr acc. tutas id., OIr túath ‘tribe, people’, W tud ‘people, country’, OLith tautā ‘people, nation’, Latv tāuta id., OPrus tauto ‘land’. Schweizer KZ I 560; Torp-Falk 185; Krahe Glotta XXII 123; Trautmann BSW 314–315; Holthausen AEEW 362–363; Feist 496; Detschew Thr. 507; Porzig Gliederung 200; Pokorny I 1084; Chemodanov SGGJa I 64; Fraenkel 1069–1070; Vries ANEW 613; Zalizniak II 198; Benveniste IEL 296; Lehmann GED 361; Bammesberger Nom. 116; Kluge-Seebold 175.
Goth *þiuh-broks ‘knee breeches’ (reconstructed from late Lat tubrucus, Rum tureci), OHG dioh-bruoh ‘knee-breeches’. Compound of *þeuxan and *brōkz. Carr Nom. comp. 45; Gamillscheg RG I 374, II 258.

ON þjó ‘thigh’, OE äeóh id., OFris thiāch id., MLG dēch id., OHG dioh id. Derivationally close to Lith pl. taukaĩ ‘fat’, Latv adj. tāuks ‘fat’, Slav *tukъ ‘fat, animal fat’ and further related to IE *teu H- ‘to swell’. Weinhold KZ I 247; Zupitza Gutt. 140; Persson Beiträge 554; Torp-Falk 186; Trautmann BSW 314; Holthausen AEEW 363; Pokorny I 1080–1081; Fraenkel 1066; Vries ANEW 612–613; Zalizniak I 144; Onions 917; Vasmer-Trubachev IV 116.

ON þjórr ‘bull’. Related to Gk ταῦρος ‘bull’, Lat taurus id., OIr tarb id. (with a metathesis), Lith ta Uras ‘aurochs’, Slav *turъ id. An IE loanword from Sem *∆awru- (Schmidt Urheimat 6; Lewy Fremdw. 4; Illich-Svitych PIEJa 3) hardly connected with *steuraz. Brugmann IF VI 98, Grundriß II/1 353 (vocalism influenced by *steuraz); Ipsen Festschr. Streitberg 227–228; Specht 35; Walde- Hofmann II 650–652; Porzig Gliederung 199; Pokorny I 1083; Vries ANEW 614; Frisk II 860–861.

ON þjóta ‘to howl, to emit a whistling sound’, OE äeótan, äútan ‘to howl’, OHG diozan ‘to roar’. Of unknown—probably, imitative—origin. Bezzenberger GGA 1875 281 (to Lat tussis ‘cough’); Persson UUÅ 1891 90 (to Lat tundō ‘to beat, to strike’); Froehde BB I 208 (same as Bezzenberger); Torp- Falk 186–187 (to IE *teu(6)- ‘to swell’); Holthausen AEEW 363; Jóhannesson IEW 427–431; Pokorny I 1097 (to Lith tūtúoti ‘to blow’); Vries ANEW 615; Seebold 516 (to Lat tumultus ‘uproar, bustle’); Lehmann GED 369 (to IE *teud- ‘to kick’).
ON þægr ‘acceptable, agreeable’, OE *ze-äæze ‘pleasant’. Derived from *þezjanan. Holthausen AEEW 359; Vries ANEW 631; Matzel KZ CV 114.

ON þáttr ‘single strand of rope’, OHG tāht ‘wick’. Related to *þexsanan. Scheftelowitz BB XIX 14; Torp-Falk 177; Walde-Hofmann II 678–679; Porzig Gliederung 168; Pokorny I 1058–1059; Vries ANEW 606; Kluge- Seebold 187.

Goth þeins ‘thine’, ON þín, þinn id., OE äín id., OFris thīn id., OS thīn id., OHG dīn id. An original Germanic formation based on *þu. Torp-Falk 185; Holthausen AEEW 365; Feist 495; Pokorny I 1098; Vries ANEW 610; Lehmann GED 360; Kluge-Seebold 168.

ON þrefa ‘to wrangle, to dispute’, OE ärafian ‘to urge, to press’. Identical with Slav *tropiti ‘to make a path’, further to Toch A trap-, B trāpp- ‘to trip, to stumble’, Lith trapinėti ‘to trample’, OPruss trapt ‘to walk’, Slav *tropa ‘path’. Wood PBB XXIV 533 (to Lat trepit ‘to turn’); Trautmann BSW 329; Holthausen AEEW 368; Pokorny I 1037 (to Lat strepō ‘to rattle, to rustle’); Fraenkel 1111; Vries ANEW 620; Vasmer-Trubachev IV 105; Adams TB 313–314.
ON þrapt ‘quarrel’, OE äræft ‘quarrel, dispute’. Derived from *þrafjōjanan. Torp-Falk 191.
Goth þragjan ‘to run’, OE äræzan id. Derived from *þrazō. Zupitza Gutt. 140; Torp-Falk 190 (to Slav *tъrkъ ‘running’); Holthausen AEEW 367; Feist 500–501; Onions 919; Lehmann GED 364.

OE äráz ‘time, season’. Close to W tro ‘turn, time’ < *trogho-. Further related to Lat trahō ‘to pull’ < *draghō (Trautmann BB XXX 330). Torp- Falk 190; Holthausen AEEW 368; Walde-Hofmann II 698–699; Pokorny I 1089; Lehmann GED 364.

ON þræll ‘slave, serf’, OHG dregil ‘servant’. Derived from *þrazjanan. Torp-Falk 190; Palmer TPS 1954 21 (to Proto-Gk *τροκ⁄εωō ‘to plough’); Jóhannesson IEW 456 (to Lat trahō ‘to draw’); Vries ANEW 625 (< *þranxilaz); Onions 919.
ON þrekr ‘strength, courage’, OE ärece ‘force, oppression’. Related to OIr tracht ‘strength’. Zupitza Gutt. 217; Torp-Falk 189; Holthausen AEEW 369; Pokorny I 1090; Vries ANEW 620.

ON þramma ‘to lumber along, to walk heavily’, MLG drammen ‘to make noise’. Derived from *þremmanan. Zupitza KZ XXXVI 243 (to OIr tromm ‘heavy’); Torp-Falk 191; Vries ANEW 619; Meid IF LXIX 248; Pokorny I 1092–1093; Lehmann GED 364.

ON þrǫmr ‘brim, edge, verge’, MHG drām ‘piece, splinter’. Derived from *þremmanan. Torp-Falk 181 (to Gk τέρμα ‘end, boundary’, Lat termen ‘boundary’); Jóhannesson IEW 442; Vries ANEW 625 (to *þruman ~ *þrumō).

ON þrǫngr ‘narrow, close, tight’, MLG dranc ‘narrow’, MHG mir ist drange nāch ‘I am compelled to do smth.’. Derived from *þrenzwanan ~ *þrenxwanan. Close to Lith trankūs ‘uneven (of a road), hard’. Karsten NB II 408–409; Torp- Falk 190; Fraenkel 1111; Vries ANEW 625; Onions 920; Matzel KZ CV 103–104; Heidermanns 624.
ON þrøngsl ‘narrows, straits’, LG drangsal ‘crowd, throng’. Derived from *þranzwjanan. Vries ANEW 625.
ON þrǫngva ‘to press’, MLG drengen id., MHG drengen id. Causative of *þrenzwanan ~ *þrenxwanan. Torp-Falk 190; Vries ANEW 625; Lehmann GED 365.
ON prǫng ‘crowd’, OE äranz, ze-äranz id., MLG ge-drang id., OHG gi-drang id. Substantivized *þranzwaz. Holthausen AEEW 368; Onions 920; Bammesberger Nom. 107 (reconstructs *þranzō).

ON lík-þrár ‘leprous’, OE äróh ‘rancid’, MDu dra ‘sickening’, MHG adv. drāhe ‘odorous’. Related to Lith tręštū, trēšti ‘to rot’. Torp-Falk 190; Holthausen AEEW 370; Pokorny I 1031– 1032; Fraenkel 1121; Vries ANEW 619; Hille Festschr. Sundby 131–144; Heidermanns 624–625.

Goth þrasa-balþei ‘quarrelsomeness’, OE äræs ‘fringe, border’. Since the gender in OE is unknown, one can also reconstruct *þrasaz or *þrasan. Related to Skt trásati ‘to tremble’, Av frat 6 r 6 saiti ‘to be afraid’, Gk τρέω ‘to tremble’. Torp-Falk 191–192; Feist 501; Mayrhofer I 531–532; Pokorny I 1095; Frisk II 929–930; Chantraine 1131– 1132; Lehmann GED 364–365.

ON þrasa ‘to talk big, to make a bold show’, OS thrāsian id., OHG drāsen ‘to neigh, to breathe hard’. See *þrasō. Related to Skt trásati ‘to tremble, to quiver’, Av frat 6 r 6 saiti ‘to be afraid’, Gk τρέω ‘to tremble’, Lat terreō ‘to frighten’. Torp-Falk 191–192; Walde-Hofmann II 674–675; Mayrhofer I 531–532; Pokorny I 1095; Vries ANEW 620; Frisk II 929–930; Lehmann GED 364–365.

ON þrǫstr ‘thrush’. A derivative *þrustlōn is found in WGmc: OE ärostle ‘throstle’, OS throsla, OHG drōscala. With irregular phonetic correspondences, close to Gk στροῦθος id., Lat turdus id. < *tṛzdo-, MIr truid id., Lith strãzdas id., OPrus tresde id., Slav *drozdъ id. Cf. also *þrauskō(n) > OE äræsce id., OHG drōsca id. Torp-Falk 192; Trautmann BSW 327; Holthausen AEEW 368, 370; Walde-Hofmann II 718; Specht 229, 245; Pokorny I 1096; Chemodanov SGGJa I 59; Fraenkel 920; Vries ANEW 625; Frisk II 811; Onions 920–921; Hamp KZ XCV 81; Kluge-Seebold 196.

ON þraut ‘struggle, exertion, labor’, OE äréat ‘violence, compulsion, force’, MLG drōt ‘threat’, MHG drōz ‘vexation, inconvenience’. Derived from *þreutanan. Structurally close to Slav *trudъ ‘difficulty, labor’. Torp-Falk 194; Holthausen AEEW 368; Pokorny I 1095–1096; Vries ANEW 620; Zalizniak II 190; Onions 919; Lehmann GED 384.

ON þreyta ‘to strive, to struggle’, OE ärítan ‘to weary, to urge’, MDu droten ‘to threaten’, OHG drōzen ‘to make unfaithful’. Causative of *þreutanan. Torp-Falk 195; Holthausen AEEW 369; Vries ANEW 621–622; Seebold 523.
ON þrá ‘hard struggle, obstinacy’, OS thrā-werk ‘suffering, torment’. Substantivized *þrawaz. Torp-Falk 193; Pokorny I 1073; Vries ANEW 618– 619; Zalizniak I 149; Heidermanns 625.

ON þrár ‘stubborn, obstinate’. Related to Gk τρύω ‘to wear down’, Lith trūniū, trūnėti ‘to rot’, Slav *tryti ‘to rub’. Karsten NB I 64–65, 73; Torp-Falk 193; Pokorny I 1072–1073; Vries ANEW 619 (< *þranxaz); Frisk II 938; Heidermanns 625–626.
OESc þra ‘to oppose’, OE äreáwian ‘to reprove, to rebuke’. Derived from *þrawaz. Torp-Falk 193; Holthausen AEEW 368; Vries ANEW 619; Heidermanns 625.

Goth þreis ‘three’, ON þrír id., OE ärí id., OFris thrē id., OS thrīa id., OHG drī id. Related to IE *treƒes id.: Lyc tri-, Toch A tre, B trey, Skt tráyaъ, Av yrāyō, Arm erek', Gk τρεῖς, Lat trēs, OIr tri, Lith τρίσ, Slav *trije. Bartholomae 801– 802; Torp-Falk 192; Trautmann BSW 327–328; Holthausen AEEW 369; Walde-Hofmann II 702–703; Feist 502; Mayrhofer I 531; Pokorny I 1090– 1092; Fraenkel 1125; Vries ANEW 622; Frisk II 921–922; Onions 919; Lindeman Verschärfung 11.2; Lehmann GED 365– 366; Ross-Berns Numerals 575–579; Kluge-Seebold 193; Adams TB 319– 320; Burlak IFTJa 246.

ON þrekkr ‘dirt, filth’, OFris threkk id., MLG dreck id., late OHG mūs-drec ‘mouse excrements’. Related to Gk στεργάνος: κόπρων (Hes.), Lat stercus ‘dung’, W troeth ‘soapy water, urine’, Lith tręšiū, tr.šti ‘to fertilize, to manure’ (Persson SVS X 454). Zupitza KZ XXXVI 65; Sommer IF XI 91 (to Lat troia ‘sow’ < *trogƒa); Torp-Falk 189 (reconstruct *þrakjaz); Walde-Hofmann II 589; Martinet Gém. 190; Benveniste Origines 9; Pokorny I 1031–1032; Fraenkel 1120–1121; Vries ANEW 620; Frisk II 790; Kluge-Seebold 193.

OS thrimman ‘to jump, to spring’. Related to Toch A träm- ‘to be angry’, Gk τρέμω ‘to tremble’, Alb tremb ‘to frighten’ < PAlb *trema, Lat tremō ‘to shake, to quake’, Lith tremiū, tre Mti ‘to throw down’ (Bugge BB III 121). Zupitza KZ XXXVI 243 (to OIr tromm ‘heavy’); Torp-Falk 191; Walde- Hofmann II 701; Pokorny I 1092–1093; Fraenkel 1117; Frisk II 922–923; Meid IF LXIX 248; Orel AED 463.

Goth þreihan ‘to press, to throng’, ON þryngva, þryngja ‘to press’, OE ärinzan ‘to press, to throng’, OFris ur-thringa ‘to press, to drive’, OS thringan ‘to penetrate’, OHG dringan ‘to press, to drive’. Identical with Lith trenkiū, treñkti ‘to push roughly, to fling’ (Trautmann BSW 328–329). Pedersen IF II 290; Wood MP V 267 (to OS thregian ‘to threaten’); Zupitza Gutt. 70, 140; Uhlenbeck PBB XXX 316; Torp-Falk 190; Holthausen AEEW 369; Feist 501; Pokorny I 1093; Fraenkel 1118; Vries ANEW 622, 624; Onions 920; Seebold 520–521; Lehmann GED 365; Kluge-Seebold 194.

ON þrep ‘shelf in a wall’. Identical with OW treb ‘dwelling’, OBret treb id., MIr treb ‘house, estate’. Further cf. Lat trabs ‘beam, rafter’, Osk trííbúm ‘house, building’, OW treb ‘house’, Lith trobā id. Persson Beiträge 138; Torp- Falk 190–191; Trautmann BSW 330; Walde-Hofmann II 696–697; Pokorny I 1090; Chemodanov SGGJa I 65; Fraenkel 1127–1128; Vries ANEW 621; Lehmann GED 358.

ON þreskǫldr, þreskjǫldr, þrepskjǫldr ‘threshold’, OE äerscwold, ärexwold id., MLG dreskeleffid. (fem.). Cf. a structurally similar form in OHG driscubli ‘limen, threshold’. Probably, a compound of *þreskanan (Meringer IF XIX 425) and *alđuz (see *alđōn). A difficult word. Petersson IF XXIV 261 (to Russ treska ‘cod’); Hellquist SEO 1237; Torp-Falk 192 (reconstruct *þreskwaþluz ~ *þreskūþljan); Holthausen AEEW 364; Vries ANEW 621 (to *þrepan); Trubachev Etim. 43 (from *þreskađla- with a suffix *-đla-); Onions 919; Seebold 522 (reconstructs *þreskuđlaz); Kluge-Seebold 837 (reconstructs *þreskublan).

Goth þriskan ‘to thresh’, OSwed þryskia id., OE äerscan ‘to strike, to beat, to flog’, WFris terskje id., MLG derschen id., OHG dreskan id. Related to Toch AB trāsk- ‘to chew’, Gk τιτρώσκω ‘to wound, to kill’, Lith treškū, treškėti ‘to crackle, to crash’, Slav *trîskъ ‘crack, crash’ (Fick BB II 209; Schmidt Voc. II 73). Prellwitz KZ XLII 385–386; Persson Beiträge 662, 776; Zupitza Gutt. 91; Torp-Falk 192; Scheftelowitz KZ LVI 171; Trautmann BSW 329; Feist 503 (to Gk τρίβω ‘to rub’); Holthausen AEEW 364; Schneider IF LVIII 50; Pokorny I 1090; Fraenkel 1120; Vries ANEW 622; Frisk II 905; Onions 919; Seebold 522–523; Vasmer- Trubachev IV 100; Lehmann GED 366 (to IE *ter- ‘to rub’); Kluge-Seebold 193.

Goth us-þriutan ‘to bother, to persecute’, ON þrjóta ‘to fail, to come to an end’, OE äreótan ‘to weary’, OS part. a-throtan ‘pertaesum, otiosum’, OHG bi-driozan ‘to irritate, to annoy’. Related to Lat trūdō ‘to push’, Alb tredh ‘to castrate’ < PAlb *treuda. Torp-Falk 194–195; Uhlenbeck PBB XXX 321; Feist 535–536; Holthausen AEEW 369; Walde-Hofmann II 710; Jóhannesson IEW 459; Pokorny I 1095; Vries ANEW 622–623; Onions 919; Lerchner 65; Seebold 523–524; Lehmann GED 383– 384; Kluge-Seebold 855; Orel AED 463.
ON þrjótr ‘defaulter, bad debtor, knave, defiance’, OHG ur-drioz ‘annoyance, nausea’. Derived from *þreutanan. Seebold 523.

ON prop. (< part.) ėrainn, OE äráwan ‘to turn, to twist’. Weak verb in WGmc: OS thraian ‘to turn’, OHG drāen id. Related to Gk τείρω ‘to oppress, to weaken’, Lat terō ‘to rub’, Lith trinū, trīnti id., Slav *tьrǫ, *terti id. Torp-Falk 189; Holthausen AEEW 368; Walde-Hofmann II 672–673; Jóhannesson IEW 440–442; Pokorny I 1072; Fraenkel 1124–1125; Frisk II 865; Onions 920; Seebold 518–519; Kluge-Seebold 193.

ON þráär ‘thread’ (u-stem and root stem), OE äræd id., OFris thrēd id., OS thrād id. (i-stem), OHG drāt id. Derived from *þrēanan. Structurally close to Gk τρητός ‘perforated, with a hole in it’. Torp-Falk 189; Holthausen AEEW 377; Pokorny I 1072; Vries ANEW 619; Zalizniak II 211; Frisk II 885; Onions 919; Seebold 519; Lehmann GED 366; Kluge-Seebold 192.

Goth þridja ‘third’, ON þriäi id., OE äridda, äirda id., OFris thredda, thirda id., OS thrida id., OHG dritto id. Related to Toch A trit, B trite id., Skt tṛtīya- id., Av yrityō, Lat tertius, Lith tr‚‘ias, Slav *tretьjь. See *þrejez. Bartholomae 807–808; Torp-Falk 192; Trautmann BSW 328; Holthausen AEEW 369; Feist 502–503; Walde-Hofmann II 703; Porzig Gliederung 203; Mayrhofer I 522; Pokorny I 1090–1091; Fraenkel 1114– 1115; Vries ANEW 622; Szemerényi Num. 81–96; Onions 917; Peeters IF LXXXVIII 202–203; Lehmann GED 365; Ross-Berns Numerals 626; Kluge- Seebold 194; Adams TB 316.
ON þrennr ‘triple’, OFris thrīna id., OE ärinen ‘three-fold’. Derived from *þrejez. Cf. *twīxnaz. Holthausen AEEW 369; Lehmann GED 365; Ross- Berns Numerals 643.

OE äríste ‘bold’, OS thrīst, thrīsti id. Identical with Lat trīstis ‘unfriendly, strict, sullen’ < *trīk-sti- (Pedersen IF V 56). Torp-Falk 193; Holthausen AEEW 370; Walde- Hofmann II 706–707; Pokorny I 1092; Lerchner 66–67; Heidermanns 626–627 (to *þrenzwanan ~ *þrenxwanan).

Goth þroþjan ‘to train, to practice’. Identical with Lith trõtyti ‘to lack, to lose, to spoil’, Slav *tratiti ‘to spend’, cf. also Lith trótinti ‘to irritate, to tease’. Torp-Falk 193; Feist 503; Pokorny I 1072; Fraenkel 1129; Lehmann GED 366 (to *þrēđuz).
ON þróttr ‘strength, might’, OE äroht ‘oppression, hardship’. Related to *þrakjaz. Zupitza Gutt. 217; Torp-Falk 189; Holthausen AEEW 370; Vries ANEW 623–624.

Norw trykkja ‘to press’, OE äryccan ‘to press, to crush, to oppress’, OHG drucken ‘to press, to push’. Related to *þrūzanan? Torp-Falk 194; Holthausen AEEW 371; Martinet Gém. 168; Sturtevant JEGP XXXIII 96; Vries ANEW 620; Kluge-Seebold 196.
ON þrymill ‘hard knot in the flesh’, MLG drömel ‘small field’. Derived from *þrumōn ~ *þruman. Vries ANEW 624.

ON þrymr ‘noise, alarm’, OE ärymm ‘host, crowd, multitude’. Probably related to Lat turma ‘crowd’. Torp-Falk 197; Holthausen AEEW 371; Walde-Hofmann II 719; Pokorny I 1100; Chemodanov SGGJa I 87; Vries ANEW 624.

ON þruma ‘land’, OE tunze-ärum ‘ligament of the tung’, MDu drom, drum ‘end’, OHG drum ‘end-piece, remnant, splinter’. Derived from *þremmanan. Noreen Abriß 102 (to Lat termen ‘boundary’, Gk τέρμα ‘end, boundary’); Holthausen AEEW 370; Vries ANEW 624; Onions 920–921 (to *þramuz); Kluge-Seebold 839.
ON þrot ‘lack, want’, OE æ- ärot ‘disgust’, OS far-throt ‘annoyance’. Derived from *þreutanan. Holthausen AEEW 370; Vries ANEW 623; Seebold 523; Bammesberger Nom. 57.

ON þroti ‘swelling, tumour’, OE ärotu ‘throat’, OFris throt-bolla ‘larynx’, OS strota ‘throat’, OHG drozza id., MHG strozze id. Derived from *þrūtanan. Holthausen AEEW 370; Pokorny I 1027; Vries ANEW 623; Onions 920; Kluge-Seebold 196.

ON þrúga ‘to press’. Related to W trychu ‘to cut’, Lith trūkstu, trūkti ‘to tear into pieces, to burst’ (Persson Beiträge 173, 447–448). Zupitza Gutt. 140; Pokorny I 1074; Fraenkel 1130–1131; Kluge-Seebold 196.

ON þró ‘trough’ (pl. þrœr), OE ärúh ‘trough, pipe, chest, box’ (masc., fem., neut.), MLG drū ‘trap’, OHG drūh ‘fetter, sling, trap’. Derived from *þrūzanan. Torp-Falk 194; Holthausen AEEW 370; Vries ANEW 623; Kluge- Seebold 839.
ON þrÿsta ‘to thrust’, OHG drūsten id. Related to *þrūtanan. Vries ANEW 624; Onions 921.

ON part. þrútinn ‘swollen’, OE part. á-äruten id. Identical with Lat trūdō ‘to push’, Alb ndrydh ‘to press, to squeeze’ < PAlb *en-trūda. Torp-Falk 194; Holthausen AEEW 370; Walde-Hofmann II 710–711; Onions 920; Vries ANEW 624; Seebold 524; Lehmann GED 366; Orel AEW 288.

Goth þruts-fill ‘leprosy’, OE ärust-fell id. (with metathesis). Compound of an s-stem *þrūtez (see *þrūtanan) and *fellan. Grienberger Got. 218–219; Torp-Falk 194; Holthausen AEEW 370; Feist 503–504; Carr Nom. comp. 66; Lehmann GED 366.
ON þrúä-hamarr ‘master hammer’, OE ärÿä ‘force, power, strength’. Related to *þrūtanan. Torp- Falk 193; Holthausen AEEW 371; Vries ANEW 624.

Goth þu ‘thou’, ON þú id., OE äú id., OFris thu id., OS thu id., OHG dū id. Corresponds to IE *tu id.: Toch A tu, B tuwe, Skt tvám, Av tvēm, Arm du, Alb ti, Gk σύ, Lat tū, OIr tú, Lith tū, Slav *ty. Bartholomae 660–662; Torp-Falk 185; Holthausen AEEW 371; Walde- Hofmann II 712; Feist 504; Mayrhofer I 538–539; Pokorny I 1097; Fraenkel 1133–1134; Vries ANEW 625–626; Vendryes T-155–157; Frisk II 817; Onions 918; Lehmann GED 366–367; Kluge-Seebold 197; Orel AED 455– 456; Adams TB 302; Burlak IFTJa 245.

ON þópta ‘thwart, rowing bench’, OE äoft(e) ‘rower’s bench’, MLG ducht id., OHG dofta id. From *tup-toconnected with Lith tupiū, t~pti ‘to squat’. Torp-Falk 187; Holthausen AEEW 366; Pokorny I 1085; Fraenkel 1141– 1142; Vries ANEW 616; Zalizniak II 181; Onions 921–922; Kluge-Seebold 197–198.

Goth þulan ‘to endure, to bear’, ON þola id., OE äolian ‘to suffer’, OFris tholia id., OS tholian, tholon id., OHG dolēn id. Related to Toch AB täl- ‘to uphold, to raise’, Skt tulayati ‘to weigh, to compare’, Lat perf. tulī ‘to bear’, Lith tylū, tīlti ‘to become silent’, Slav *toliti ‘to make silent’. Pedersen KZ XXXVIII 354, 371; Torp-Falk 188; Holthausen AEEW 366; Walde-Hofmann II 688– 689; Feist 504–505; Mayrhofer I 516; Pokorny I 1060–1061; Fraenkel 1095; Vries ANEW 615; Onions 918; Lehmann GED 367; Kluge-Seebold 305; Adams TB 296–297; Burlak IFTJa 245.

ON þulr ‘cultic speaker, sage’, OE äyle ‘orator, spokesman’. Derived from *þulēnan. Torp-Falk 188 (to ON þjóstr ‘anger, fury’); Blankenstein IF XXIII 134 (to Slav *tъlkъ ‘sense’); Loewenthal ANF XXXV 236 (to Lat tullius ‘flow, shower’); Holthausen AEEW 374; Jóhannesson IEW 450 (to Latv tū4īgs ‘clumsy’); Pokorny I 1060–1061; Vries ANEW 626; Ivanov Etim. 1978 174–175 (to Hitt tuliƒa- ‘meeting’); Bammesberger Nom. 133.

ON þollr ‘fir-tree, wooden peg’, OE äoll ‘peg in the side of a boat to keep the oar in place’, OFris tholl ‘peg’, MLG dolle ‘peg in the side of a boat to keep the oar in place’ (fem.). Related to Gk τύλος ‘bolt, knob’, Alb tul ‘meat, flesh, pulp’, Lith tulīs ‘peg in the axle preventing the wheel from falling out’. Torp-Falk 185–186; Holthausen AEEW 367; Johannesson IEW 428; Pokorny I 1081; Fraenkel 1137; Vries ANEW 615; Frisk II 942–943; Onions 918; Orel Zf Balk XXIII 147, AED 468; Kluge- Seebold 188.

ON þungr ‘heavy’. Related to Toch AB täṅk- ‘to check, to stop’, Av yang- ‘to pull’, Arm t'anjr ‘thick’ (< *t–ghƒuor *t–·hu-), Lith tingūs ‘sluggish, lazy’, Slav *têžь-kъ ‘heavy’, *tǫgъ ‘tough’. Zupitza BB XXV 89, Gutt. 181; Bartholomae 784–785; Karsten NB I 17–18; Torp- Falk 180; Lewy IF XXXII 161; Trautmann BSW 318; Pokorny I 1067; Fraenkel 1098; Vries ANEW 626–627; Stang LS 58; Vasmer-Trubachev IV 140; Hamp LP XXVIII 77; Heidermanns 629–630; Adams TB 290.
Goth þuhtus ‘conscience’, ON þóttr ‘thought, mind’, OE äóht id., MHG an-dūht ‘thought’. Derived from *þankjanan. Holthausen AEEW 366; Feist 504; Vries ANEW 618; Onions 919; Bammesberger Nom. 158.

Goth þugkjan ‘to believe, to have an opinion’ (irreg.), ON þykkja ‘to seem’, OE äyncan id., OFris thinka id., OS thunkian id., OHG dunken id. Derived from *þunkōn. Aufrecht KZ I 353; Torp-Falk 179; Holthausen AEEW 374; Feist 504; Pokorny I 1088; Ernout-Meillet 695; Onions 917; Vries ANEW 630; Lehmann GED 367; Kluge- Seebold 200.

ON þokki ‘thought, consent, mood’, OE æf-äunca ‘offense’. Derived from *þankjanan. Derivationally close to W tank ‘peace’ < *t–gā (Loth RC XLI 225) and Toch A tunk, B tankw ‘love’ < *t–g-⁄– (Pedersen REIE III 209–210). Vries ANEW 615; Adams TB 277.
ON þynna ‘to make thin’, OHG dunnen id. Derived from þunnuz. Vries ANEW 630; Heidermanns 630.

ON þunnvangi ‘temple (anat.)’, OE äunwanz(e) id., OS tinnong id., OHG dun-wengi. Compound of *þunnuz and *wanzan ~ *wanzōn. Torp-Falk 178; Holthau-sen AEEW 372; Carr Nom. comp. 58; Jóhannesson IEW 438 (to

ON þunnr, þuär ‘thin’, OE äynne id., OFris thenne, OS thunni id., OHG dunni id. Derived from IE *tenu- ‘thin’: Skt tanú-, Lat tenuis, OIr tanae, Slav *tъnъ-kъ. See *þanjanan. Karsten NB II 359–360, 407; Torp-Falk 178; Holthausen AEEW 374; Wissmann NP 87; Walde-Hofmann II 666; Mayrhofer I 474–475; Pokorny I 1069; Vries ANEW 627; Onions 917; Peeters IF XL 207; Lehmann GED 374; Bammesberger Nom. 263; Heidermanns KZ XCIX 282–283, 630; Kluge-Seebold 200.

ON theon. ėórr, OE äunor ‘thunder’, OFris thuner id., OS thunar id., OHG donar id. Related to Skt stánati ‘to thunder’, Gk στένω ‘to moan, to sigh’, Lat tonō id., Slav *stenati id. Torp-Falk 178; Holthausen AEEW 372; Walde- Hofmann II 690–691; Mayrhofer I 510; Pokorny I 1021; Vries ANEW 618; Zalizniak I 137; Frisk II 789–790; Onions 921; Szemerényi IBK XV 202– 203 (to OIr torann ‘thunder’); Lehmann GED 360; Kluge-Seebold 189.
Norw dial. tōra ‘to thunder’, OE äunrian id., MLG donern id., OHG donarōn id. Derived from *þunraz. Torp-Falk 178.
Goth ga-þaurbs ‘self-controlling, forgoing, ἐγκρατής’, ON þurfi‘wanting, in need of’. Derived from *þarfa. Feist 206, 491–492; Matzel Festschr. Schröder 98; Heidermanns 630.

ON þora ‘to dare’. A weak verb based on the unattested *þuraz, cf. *þurōn > ON þori ‘greater part, main part’. Etymologically connected with Skt turá- ‘strong, powerful, rich’. Mayrhofer I 514; Pokorny I 1083.

Goth þaurfts ‘necessary’. Identical with Skt tṛptá-, participle of tṛpyati ‘to satisfy oneself, to be pleased’. Further see *þarfa. Torp-Falk 182; Feist 491–492; Mayrhofer I 524; Bammesberger Nom. 250.

Goth þaurfts ‘need, necessity’, ON þyrft id., OS thurft id., OHG durft id. Derivationally identical with Skt tṛpti- ‘satisfaction, contentment’. See *þarfa. Kirchhoff KZ I 39; Torp-Falk 182; Feist 501–492; Mayrhofer I 523; Pokorny I 1078; Vries ANEW 627; Zalizniak I 151; Seebold 509; Lehmann GED 357; Bammesberger Nom. 141.

ON þurs ‘giant’, OE äyrs id., OHG duris ‘devil, evil spirit’. Derived from *þurēnan. Harder ASNSL XCIV 90 (to Skt turá- ‘strong’); Focke Saeculum II 584 (to Etr turs); Torp-Falk 197; Holthausen AEEW 375; Pokorny I 1100; Vries ANEW 627; Zalizniak I 137.
ON þyrnir ‘thorn’, OE äyrne ‘thorn-bush’. Derived from *þurnuz ~ *þurnaz. Holthausen AEEW 375; Vries ANEW 630; Lehmann GED 357.
Goth þaurneins ‘thorny’, OE äyrnen ‘of thorns’. Derived from *þurnuz ~ *þurnaz. Holthausen AEEW 375; Feist 492; Lehmann GED 357.

Goth þaurnus ‘thorn-plant’, ON þorn ‘thorn’, OE äorn id., OFris thorn id., OS thorn id., OHG dorn id. Etymologically identical with Skt tṛṇa- ‘grass, herb, blade, straw’, Slav *tьrnъ ‘thorn’ (Meillet Etudes 447). Torp-Falk 182; Trautmann b BSW 324; Holthausen AEEW 367; Feist 492; Mayrhofer I 522; Pokorny I 1031; Vries ANEW 617; Zalizniak II 204; Onions 918; Vasmer-Trubachev IV 48–49; Lehmann GED 357; Kluge- Seebold 190.

Goth þaurp ‘land, field, livedon property’, ON þorp ‘hamlet, village’, OE masc. äorp ‘estate, village’, OFris thorp, therp ‘village, estate’, OS thorp id., OHG dorf id. Connected with *þrepan. Kirchhoff KZ I 39; Bugge BB III 112; Meringer IF XVIII 215–218 (to Slav *terbiti ‘to purge, to cleanse’); Zupitza Gutt. 29; Kretschmer Glotta XXIV 9–10; Torp- Falk 187–188; Holthausen AEEW 367; Feist 492; Pokorny I 1090; Schulze Kl. Schr. 524; Vries 617–618; Vries ANEW 617–618; Zalizniak II 173; Onions 918; Lehmann GED 357; Kluge-Seebold 190.

Goth þaursjan ‘to be thirsty’, OE part. äyrred ‘dried up’. Identical with Skt tṛßyati id. Further connected with *þersanan and *þurzuz. Torp-Falk 183; Holthausen AEEW 375; Feist 493; Mayrhofer I 525; Pokorny I 1078–1079; Lehmann GED 358; Heidermanns 633.
ON þorskr ‘codfish’, MLG dorsch id. Derived from *þurzuz. Pedersen IF V 72 (to Russ treska id.); Torp-Falk 183; Vries ANEW 618; Kluge-Seebold 190 (MLG < ON).
Goth þaurstei ‘thirst’, ON þorsti id. See *þurstuz. Torp-Falk 183.

Swed törst ‘thirst’, OE äurst id., OS thurst id., OHG durst id. Based on the unattested *þurstaz ‘thirsty’ (Heidermanns 632). See *þersanan. Torp-Falk 183; Holthausen AEEW 372; Feist 493; Pokorny I 1079; Vries ANEW 618; Zalizniak II 198; Onions 917; Seebold 515; Lehmann GED 358; Kluge-Seebold 202.

ON þyrsta ‘to thirst’, OE äyrstan id., OS thurstian id., OHG dursten id. Derived from *þurstuz ~ *þurstiz. Torp-Falk 183; Holthausen AEEW 375; Vries ANEW 630; Lehmann GED 358; Heidermanns 632 (reconstructs
Goth ga-þaursnan ‘to dry up’, ON þorna ‘to become dry’. Derived from *þurzuz. Torp-Falk 183; Feist 493; Pokorny I 1078; Seebold 515; Lehmann GED 358; Heidermanns 633.

Goth þaursus ‘dry’ (with s < z under the influence of *þersanan), ON þurr ‘dry’, OE äyrre id., MLG dorre ‘arid, dry’, OHG durri ‘dry’. Identical with Skt tṛßú- ‘greedy, desirous, rapid’, Av taršu- ‘dry’, OLat torrus ‘dried up’, OIr tur ‘dry’. See *þersanan. Bartholomae 644; Karsten NB II 360; Torp-Falk 183; Feist 493; Holthausen AEEW 375; Walde-Hofmann II 694; Mayrhofer I 525; Pokorny I 1078–1079; Vries ANEW 627; Seebold 515; Lehmann GED 358; Bammesberger Nom. 262; Heidermanns 632–633; Kluge-Seebold 201.
ON þyss ‘uproar, tumult’, OE äys ‘storm’. Connected with *þausjanan. Torp-Falk 188; Holthausen AEEW 375 (to Skt tavāъ ‘strong’); Vries ANEW 630.
ON þot ‘rush’, OE ze-äot ‘howling’, äéot id. Derived from *þeutanan. Holthausen AEEW 367; Vries ANEW 618.
Goth þut-haurn ‘trumpet’, ON þytr ‘noise, whistling sound’, MHG duz id. Derived from *þeutanan. Torp-Falk 187; Feist 506; Vries ANEW 615; Lehmann GED 369 (to IE *teud- ‘to kick’); Seebold 516.
ON þúfa ‘mound’, OE äúf ‘tuft’. Related to Lat tūber ‘hump, swelling’ (Persson Beiträge 482). Torp-Falk 187; Holthausen AEEW 371; Walde-Hofmann II 712– 713; Pokorny I 1080; Vries ANEW 626.
ON þumalfingr ‘thumb’, OE äÿmel ‘thimble’. Short -u- must be secondary in ON. Derived from *þūmōn. Torp-Falk 185; Holthausen AEEW 374; Vries ANEW 626.
ON þumlungr ‘thumb (of a glove)’, Fris tommeling ‘thumbstall’, MLG dūmelink id. Derived from *þūmalaz ~ *þūmilaz. Torp-Falk 185; Vries ANEW 626.

OSwed þumi ‘thumb’ (with a short *-u-), OE äúma id., OFris thūma id., MLG dūm, dūme id., OS thūmo id., OHG dūmo id. Etymologically close to Av tūma- ‘strong’, Lat tūmor ‘swelling, tumor’, tumeō ‘to be swollen’, W tyfu ‘to grow’, Lith tumėti ‘to thicken’. Persson Beiträge 191, 480; Bartholomae 638– 639; Torp-Falk 185; Trautmann BSW 332; Holthausen AEEW 372; Walde- Hofmann II 715; Pokorny I 1082; Dybo VSJa 21 (pretonic shortening of *ū in OSwed); Fraenkel 1139; Zalizniak I 155; Vries ANEW 626; Onions 921; Schrijver LL 354 (follows Dybo); Kluge-Seebold 163.

Goth þusundi ‘thousand’, ON þúsund ‘host, large number, thousand’, OE äúsend ‘thousand’, OFris thūsend id., OHG dūsunt id. Fem. of a root stem. Related to Lith túkštantis id., Slav *tys=tja id. Goes back to a compound of *tūs-˚áti- ‘strong hundred’ (Brugmann Grundriß II/2 48; Meillet MSL XIV 372). Hirt IF VI 344–346; Bugge PBB XIII 327; Torp-Falk 186; Sobolevskii RFV XIII 144 (Gmc < Slav); Trautmann BSW 332; Holthausen AEEW 372–373; Feist 505–506; Porzig Gliederung 142; Pokorny I 1083; Chemodanov SGGJa I 84; Fraenkel 1135–1136; Vries ANEW 628; Onions 919; Stang LS 59; Lehmann GED 367–368; Vasmer-Trubachev IV 133; Pijnenburg KZ CII 99–105 (reconstructs *tūt-s–t-ī); Bammesberger Nom. 215 (from IE *tū-s-–t-); Ross-Berns Numerals 621; Kluge-Seebold 818.

Goth þwahan ‘to wash’, ON þvá id., OE äweán id., OS thwahan id., OHG dwahan id. Compared with OPr twaxtan ‘bathbrush’ < *t⁄akstom (Schmidt apud Feist). The latter, however, could be connected with IE *t⁄ak- ‘to tie together’ which leaves the Germanic verb isolated. Fick I 64 (to Skt tóśate ‘to drip, to stream’); Grienberger Got. 219 (to Lith tvaskėti ‘to strike’); Wood JEGP II 227–228 (to *teu- ‘to swell’); Zupitza Gutt. 140; Torp-Falk 196; Feist 506; Holthausen AEEW 373; Kuiper Nasalpräsentia 125–126 (to Skt tujáti ‘to move quickly, to urge, to incite’); Jóhannesson IEW 451; Pokorny I 1098; Chemodanov SGGJa I 77; Vries ANEW 628; Seebold 525; Lehmann GED 369; Kluge-Seebold 918.

Goth þwahl ‘washing, bath’, ON þvál ‘a kind of soap’, OE äwéal ‘washing’, OHG dwahal ‘washing, bath’. Derived from *þwaxanan. Torp-Falk 196; Holthausen AEEW 373; Feist 507; Pokorny I 1098; Vries ANEW 628; Zalizniak II 173; Seebold 525; Lehmann GED 369; Bammesberger Nom. 76; Kluge-Seebold 918.
ON þvengr ‘thong, latchet’, OE äwanz ‘thong’. Derived from *þwenzanan. Torp-Falk 196; Holthausen AEEW 373; Vries ANEW 628; Onions 918; Seebold 527.
Norw tvinga ‘to force, to constrain’ (str./wk.), OFris thwinga id., OS thwingan ‘to oppress’, OHG dwingan ‘to force, to constrain’. Related to IE forms with a different velar: Skt tvanakti

OE ze-äweran ‘to stir, to twirl’, OHG gi-dweran ‘to stir up’. Related to Skt turáti ‘to hurry, to press forward’, (Brahm.) tvárate ‘to hurry’. Persson Beiträge 122; Torp-Falk 196– 197; Holthausen AEEW 373; Mayrhofer I 514, 539; Pokorny I 1100–1101; Seebold 528; Kluge-Seebold 661.

Goth þwairhs ‘angry’, ON þverr ‘athwart, across, transverse’, OE äweorh ‘crooked, cross’, OFris adv. thweres ‘crosswise, transverse’, OS thwerh ‘stupid’, OHG dwerah ‘crosswise, transverse’. Related to Skt tarkú- ‘spindle’, Gk ἄτρακτος id., Lat torqueō ‘to twist’ (Noreen Abriß 224; Much PBB XVII 92). Grienberger Got. 219 (derived from u

Goth uf ‘under’, ON of ‘over’, OE ufe-weard ‘upper’, OHG oba ‘up, over’. Continues *upo. Related to Skt úpa ‘towards, near to, at’, Av upâ ‘near to’, Gk ὑπό ‘under’, OIr fo ‘under’. Cf. also *uppa. Bartholomae 388–389; Torp- Falk 31; Holthausen AEEW 376; Feist 509; Mayrhofer I 105; Pokorny I 1106–1107; Vries ANEW 416; Frisk II 971–972; Lehmann GED 370–371; Kluge-Seebold 595.

ON ofan ‘from above, down’, OE ufan ‘from above, down’, OFris uva ‘up, above’, OS ovana id., OHG oben, obana id. Derived from *uba. Holthausen AEEW 376; Vries ANEW 417; Kluge-Seebold 595. *þweranan); Karsten NB I 5–6, 25; Hoffmann BB XXV 106 (to Gk σαρκάζω ‘to pluck (grass), to tear (fish)’); Osthoff PBB XIII 461–463 (to Gk πραπίς ‘diaphragm’); Zupitza Gutt. 71; Torp-Falk 197; Walde-Hofmann II 692–693; Feist 507; Holthausen AEEW 373; Mayrhofer I 485; Pokorny I 1077; Vries ANEW 628; Frisk I 180; Onions 921; Lehmann GED 369; Bammesberger Nom. 254 (to Gk σάρξ ‘meat’ < *t⁄ṛk-); Heidermanns 634–635; Kluge-Seebold 919.
Goth þwairhei ‘anger, ire, quarrel’, OHG duruh dwerahī ‘sideways’. Derived from *þwerxwaz. Feist 507; Heidermanns 634.
ON þyrill ‘whisk’, OE äwirel ‘stick for whipping milk’, OHG dwiril ‘whisk’. Derived from *þweranan. Torp-Falk 197; Holthausen AEEW 374; Jóhannesson IEW 453–455; Vries ANEW 630; Kluge-Seebold 661.
OSwed thvīna ‘to dwindle’, OE äwínan id. Of unknown origin. Torp-Falk 197; Pokorny I 1054.

Goth ufaro ‘over, above’, ON ofarr ‘up, above’, OE ufera ‘upper, higher’, OHG obaro id. Comparative of *uba. Close to Skt úpara- ‘situated below’, Av upara- ‘upper’. Bartholomae 393; Holthausen AEEW 412; Pokorny I 1106–1107; Vries ANEW 417; Kluge-Seebold 595.

ON ofra ‘to brandish, to wave in the air’, OE oferian ‘to exalt’, MLG overen ‘to remain’, OHG obarōn ‘to delay, to remove’. Derived from *ubaraz ~ *ubarōn. Torp-Falk 31; Holthausen AEEW 240; Vries ANEW 417.

Goth ubizwa ‘portico’, ON ups ‘eaves’, OE efes ‘eaves, brim, brink’, OFris ōse ‘gutters’, MLG ovese id., OHG obasa ‘portico, entrance hall’. Derived from *uba. Hirt PBB XXIII 326; Johansson PBB XV 239; Zupitza Gutt. 29; Torp-Falk 32; Holthausen AEEW 243; Feist 508–509; Pokorny I 1107; Vries ANEW 635; Zalizniak II 228; Onions 298–299; Lehmann GED 370 (directly to Skt upás- ‘lap’).
Goth ubil ‘evil’, OE yfel id., OFris evel id., OS ubil id., OS uvil id., OHG ubil id. Substantivized *ubelaz. Holthausen AEEW 412; Feist 508; Heidermanns 637.

Goth ubils ‘bad, wicked, evil’, OE yfel ‘evil, ill, bad’, OFris evel id., OS ubil ‘bad, wicked, evil’, MDu evel id., OHG ubil id. Structurally close to OIr fel ‘bad’ < *up-elo- (Stokes KZ XXXVI 274–275). Further derived from *uba (Johansson PBB XV 238–239; Grienberger Got. 220). Uhlenbeck PBB XXX 317 (to Skt úpala- ‘stone’); Torp- Falk 31–32; Feist 508; Holthausen AEEW 412; Pokorny Festschr. Streitberg 292–293 (to OIr ad-bal ‘strong’); Onions 332; Amira Recht II 126; Watkins Verbalfl. 30 (to Hitt ḫu⁄ap(p)a- ‘bad’); Lehmann GED 370; Heidermanns 637–638; Kluge-Seebold 845.

ON inn-yfli ‘intestines, bowels’, OE in-ylfe ‘gut, bowel’, OHG inn-ovili id. Derived from *ubelaz. Heinertz SVSL VII 72 (from *en-nabali ‘inside the navel’); Holthausen AEEW 188; Vries ANEW 287 (suffix *-ublja-).

Goth ufar ‘over’, ON yfir ‘over, above’, OE ofer id., OFris over ‘over’, OS ovar id., OHG prep. ubar id., adv. ubari, ubiri id. Continues IE *uperi ‘above, over’: Skt upári, Av upairi, Gk ὑπέρ. Cf. also *uba. Bartholomae 394–395; Torp-Falk 31; Holthausen AEEW 240; Feist 509; Mayrhofer I 106; Pokorny I 1105–1106; Vries ANEW 676; Frisk II 966–967; Onions 637; Lehmann GED 371; Kluge-Seebold 844.

ON ofn ‘oven’, OE ofen id., OFris oven id., MLG oven id., OHG ovan id. A phonetic variant of *uxwnaz ~ *uzwnaz, with *k ⁄ > *x(w) > *f. On the other hand, a comparison with Hitt ḫappena- ‘baking kiln, fire-pit’ remains attractive. Meringer IF XXI 295–296 (from Proto-Gk *ὑπνός); Holthausen AEEW 240; Pokorny I 88; Vries ANEW 417; Zalizniak II 227; Onions 637; Stang LS 64 (directly to OPrus wumpnis ‘oven’); Lehmann GED 49; Puhvel III 121–122; Kluge-Seebold 597.

Goth ufta ‘often’, ON opt id., OE oft id., OFris ofta id., OS adv. ofto id., OHG adv. ofto id. Derived from *uba (Wood JEGP II 214). Uhlenbeck PBB XXVII 133 (to Skt uptá- ‘scattered, sown’); Torp-Falk 31; Meringer IF XVIII 210 (to Lat opus ‘work’); Grienberger Zd Ph XXXVII 548 (same as Wood); Holthausen AEEW 240; Feist 513; Vries ANEW 419; Onions 625; Lehmann GED 373; Kluge-Seebold 598.

Goth auhns ‘oven’, OSwed oghn id. Related to Gk φλπνός id. despite phonetic difficulties (Myc i-po-no implies the original *p and thus makes the variant *ufnaz more archaic). Further connections are not certain (Skt ukhá- ‘vessel, boiler’?, see Kretschmer Einleitung 147). Fick III 29 (reconstructs *⁄ek⁄nós for Greek); Meillet MSL IX 137 (separates forms with *-xw- /*-zw- from those with *-f-); Torp-Falk 29; Svedrup Festschr. Torp 108 (separates *uxwnaz ~ *uzwnaz from *ufnaz); Meringer IF XXI 295–296 (from proto- Gk *ὑπνός); Feist 65–66 (a Wanderwort); Fraenkel KZ LXIII 202 (flpnÒw < *Ík WnÒw); Mayrhofer I 98; Pokorny I 88; Szemerényi PBB LXXXII 25–26 (Gmc < Gk); Zalizniak II 227; Frisk I 732–733; Onions 637; Lehmann GED 49 (follows Svedrup).

Goth auhsa ‘ox’ (gen. pl. auhsne), ON uxi, oxi id. (pl. yxn, ǫxn), OE oxa id., OFris oxa id., OS ohso id., OHG ohso id. Related to Toch A ops-, B okso ‘cow, ox’, Skt ukßán- ‘bull’, Av uxšan- id., W ych ‘ox’, MW pl. ychen (Försterman KZ I 496). Bartholomae 383; Torp- Falk 29; Sturtevant Language VI 253; Güntert Festschr. Panzer 10 (from Turkic, cf. Turk öküz ‘ox’); Holthausen AEEW 243; Feist 66; Porzig Gliederung 199; Mayrhofer I 98; Pokorny I 1118; Vries ANEW 422, 680; Zalizniak I 221; Onions 638; Lehmann GED 49; Bammesberger Nom. 176; Kluge-Seebold 597; Adams TB 111.
