Goth -uh ‘and’. The same particle also appears in other Germanic languages in a constraint form. Related to Skt ca id., Av ‘a, Lat que id., Gk te id.—but phonetic details of the anlaut are not clear (should we posit IE *e k ⁄e?). Lidén ANF IV 99–101 (from *–-k ⁄e); Hirt PBB XVIII 299 (same as Lidén); Brugmann IF XXXIII 173–180, Dem. 65 (to Lat -ce in ecce ‘here, behold’); Bartholomae 563–575; Streitberg GE 71; Loewe PBB LI 266; Walde-Hofmann II 401–402; Feist 5141 Mayrhofer I 365; Pokorny I 635–636; Frisk II 862–863; Onions 919; Lehmann GED 374.

ON umb, um ‘around’, OE ymb, ymbe ‘about, by’, OFris umbe ‘around’, OS umbi id., OHG umbi id. Together with Skt abhí ‘to, towards’, Av aiwi ‘to’ (if not from *ebhi ~ *obhi), Gaul ambi- ‘around’, OIr imb- id., continues IE *ṃbhi. Other IE forms go back to *ambhi. Maybe connected with *bi. Bartholomae 87–88; Torp-Falk 32; Holthausen AEEW 412; Mayrhofer I 41; Pokorny I 34; Vries ANEW 633; Kluge- Seebold 846.
ON und ‘under’, OFris und, ont id., OS und ‘moreover’. See *unđera. Pedersen Kelt. Gr. I 45 (to Skt ádhi ‘above, over’, OIr ind-); Torp-Falk 30; Feist 517–518; Vries ANEW 634.

Goth und ‘unto, until’, ON und ‘up to’, OE und ‘till’, óä ‘until’, OFris und id., OS und id., OHG unt ‘to, until’. Cf. the univerbation *unđa tē > Goth unte ‘until’, OS unti, unt id., OHG unzi, unz id. where the particle *tē is related to Lat dē ‘from’, OIr dí id. Zero grade of IE *anti ~ *anta, cf. Lith iñt ‘to’. See also *anđa. Solmsen KZ XXIX 333 (*unđa tē to Gk đnte ‘until’); Torp- Falk 30 (to Arm 6 nd ‘under’); Kluge PBB XXXV 573–574 (reconstructs *unđa þe); Walde-Hofmann I 325–326; Holthausen AEEW 243; Feist 517–518, 524; Pokorny I 49–50, 181–182; Onions 962; Lehmann GED 376, 378.
ON undan ‘from underneath’, MLG unden, undene ‘from beneath, down’, OHG untana ‘from beneath’. Derived from *unđa I. Vries ANEW 634.
OHG unti, unt ‘and’. Related to Skt átha ‘then, now’, Toch B entwe ‘also’. Mayrhofer I 28 (separates Skt a- as a pronominal element); Pokorny I 50; Kluge-Seebold 847.

OE under ‘between’, OFris under id., OS undar id., OHG untar id. Related to Skt antár ‘within, in, between’, Av antar 6 ‘between’, Lat inter id., OIr éter id. Bartholomae 131–132; Torp-Falk 30; Holthausen AEEW 376; Walde- Hofmann I 708–709; Mayrhofer I 35; Pokorny I 313; Boutkan OFED s.v.

Goth undar ‘under’, ON undir id., OE under id., OFris under id., OS undar id., OHG untar id. Continues IE *–dhero-: Skt ádhara- ‘lower, inferior’, Av adarō id., Lat īnferus id. Comparative form of *unđa I. Bartholomae 57–58; Torp-Falk 30; Holthausen AEEW 376; Walde-Hofmann I 698; Feist 518; Mayrhofer I 30–31; Pokorny I 771; Vries ANEW 634; Onions 958; Lehmann GED 376; Kluge- Seebold 849; Boutkan OFED s.v.
Goth undaurnimats ‘midday meal’, OE undern-mete ‘breakfast’. Compound of *unđurniz and *matiz. Holthausen AEEW 376; Feist 518; Carr Nom. comp. 45; Lehmann GED 376–377; Kluge-Seebold 849.

Goth undaurni-mats ‘midday meal’, ON undorn ‘mid-afternoon, mid-forenoon, meal’, OE undern ‘nine in the morning’, OS undorn ‘time before noon’, OHG untorn, untarn ‘midday’. Derived from *unđer (Osthoff Morph. Unt. VI 359). Structurally close to Lat internus ‘internal’ (Brugmann IF XXXIII 304). Meyer Goth. 560 (to Skt antá ‘within, in, between’, Lat inter ‘between’); Torp-Falk 30–31; Gaebeler Zd Ph XLIII 57 (on Goth -au-); Holthausen AEEW 376; Walde-Hofmann I 710; Feist 518; Vries ANEW 634; Pokorny I 313; Schmidt IF LXIII 294–298; Lehmann GED 376–377; Kluge-Seebold 849.

Goth uhtwo ‘dawn’, ON ótta ‘last part of the night’, OE masc. úht ‘time just before the daybreak’, OS ūhta ‘dawn’, OHG ūhta id. Continues *–k ⁄t⁄-, derivative of *nek ⁄t- ‘night’. Cf. especially Skt aktú- ‘last part of the night, the darkness just before dawn’ and probably Gk ἀκτίς ‘ray’ (Osthoff IF V 284; Schmidt Plur. 212–214). Further related to *naxtz (Hirt Idg. Gr. II 17, 159). Torp-Falk 30; Holthausen AEEW 376; Feist 515; Frisk I 61; Mayrhofer I 15; Pokorny I 762– 763; Vries ANEW 421–422; Zalizniak II 219; Frisk I 61; Lehmann GED 374–375; Markey Festschr. Gimbutas 299–321.

ON unna ‘to love, to grant’ (< *unnēnan), OE unnan ‘to grant’, OS unnan id., OHG gi-unnan id. Present form *anna (ON ann, OE onn, on, OHG an) is an innovation based on older pl. *unnum < *unz-nu-m probably related to Gk pros-ηνής ‘friendly, well-disposed’ (Brugmann Btrg. Gesch. XXXIX 89). On the other hand, cf. Hitt ašš- ‘to be favored, to be dear’ < *ans- (Puhvel 204–206). Torp-Falk 14; Holthausen AEEW 376; Feist 53; Jóhannesson IEW 30–31; Pokorny I 47; Vries ANEW 635; Seebold 79–80; Kluge-Seebold 331.

Goth unsar ‘our’, ON várr id., OE úser id., OHG unsēr id. Derived from *uns- with a comparative suffix (Brugmann Grundriß II/2 404), see *wez ~ *wīz. Torp-Falk 29–30; Holthausen AEEW 377; Feist 523; Pokorny I 758; Onions 636; Lehmann GED 378.

ON unnr, uär ‘wave’, OE ÿä id., OS ūthia id., OHG unda id. Similar to but not phonetically identical with Lat unda ‘wave’, Lith vanduõ ‘water’. Cf. *watnan ~ *watar. Torp- Falk 30; Holthausen AEEW 413; Walde-Hofmann II 816–818; Pokorny I 78–80; Vries ANEW 635; Zalizniak II 214.

ON opinn ‘open’, OE open id., OFris epen id., OS opan, OHG offan. Derived, with a suffix of strong past participle, from *uppa. Torp-Falk 31; Holthausen AEEW 241; Vries ANEW 419; Onions 628; Szemerényi Festschr. Knobloch 469–472 (from adv. *upo-nē ‘from above’); Peeters IF XCIV 297–298 (reconstructs *upnaz); Heidermanns 639–640; Kluge-Seebold 597–598.
ON opna ‘to open’, OE openian id., OFris epenia id., OS opanon id., OHG offanōn id. Derived from *upanaz ~ *upenaz. Vries ANEW 419; Heidermanns 640.

ON upp ‘up’, OE up id., OFris up, op id., OS ūp, upp id., OHG uppa id. Cf. also Goth iup ‘up’ < *eup(p)a. A geminated form of *uba (Johansson PBB XV 239–241: from *up-nó). Cf. also, with lengthening, OS ūp id., OHG ūf id. Torp-Falk 31; Holthausen AEEW 377; Feist 298; Martinet Gém. 176; Pokorny I 1107; Vries ANEW 635; Onions 962; Lehmann GED 371; Kluge-Seebold 62.
ON yppa ‘to lift, to hold up’, OE yppan ‘to bring up, to disclose’, MLG uppen ‘to raise’, OHG ūfen ‘to reveal’. Derived from *uppa. Holthausen AEEW 413; Vries ANEW 679.
ON uppi ‘up, upon, above’, OE uppe ‘above, up’, OFris uppa ‘up’, OS uppa id. Related to *uppa. Holthausen AEEW 377; Vries ANEW 635; Onions 962.

Goth aurti-gards ‘garden’, Sw örtagàrd id., OE ort-zeard ‘orchard’. Semicalque combining Rom *ortus ‘garden’ and Gmc *zarđaz. Torp- Falk 397 (reconstructs *urtiz ‘herb’); Feist 68, GGE 12–13 (the first element to *wurtiz); Carr Nom. comp. 70 (parallel formations); Holthausen AEEW 242; Corazza Lat. 23–24; Lehmann GED 51 (independent borrowings). ## ū

ON orri ‘heathcock, moorfowl’, Norw orre ‘aurochs’. Cf. also OHG orre-hano ‘pheasant’. Identical with Av aršan- ‘man’, Gk ἄρσην, đrshn ‘male, masculine’. Cf. also Skt ṛßa-bhá- ‘bull’. Bartholomae 203–204; Torp-Falk 32; Pokorny I 336; Vries ANEW 420; Frisk I 152–153.

ON usli ‘conflagration, embers’, OE ysel, ysle ‘spark, ash, ember’, MLG ösele ‘hot ashes’, MHG usele id. Probably derived from IE *eus-, cf. Lat ūrō ‘to burn’, Gk εὕω ‘to singe’. Torp-Falk 33; Holthausen AEEW 413; Walde-Hofmann II 841–842; Pokorny I 348; Vries ANEW 636; Frisk I 3 Frisk I 596–597.
Swed yster ‘very active’, OHG ustar ‘greedy’. Probably, derived from IE *eus- ‘to burn’. Torp-Falk 33; Pokorny I 348, 1170–1171.

ON otr ‘otter’, OE oter id., MLG otter id., OHG ottar id. Identical with Skt udrá- ‘aquatic animal’, Av udrō id., Gk ὕδρος ‘water snake’, cf. also Lith ūdra ‘otter’, Slav. *vydra id. (Försterman KZ I 494) with long vocalism explained by Winter’s law; further connected with IE *⁄edōr ‘water’. Meillet MSL XIV 342; Bartholomae 387; Torp-Falk 384; Trautmann BSW 334; Holthausen AEEW 243; Mayrhofer I 104; Pokorny I 79; Vries ANEW 421; Zalizniak I 137; Fraenkel II 1157–1158; Frisk II 957; Vasmer-Trubachev I 367; Klugeū

OE úder ‘udder’, OFris ūder id., OS ūder id., OHG masc. ūtar id. Related to Skt ūdhar ‘udder, bosom, breast’, Gk οὖθαρ ‘udder’, Lat ūber id. Torp-Falk 29, 561; Holthausen AEEW 375; Walde-Hofmann II 738–739; Mayrhofer I 115; Pokorny I 347; Seebold 606; Dybo BSA 143–144.

ON ugla ‘owl’, OE úle id., MLG ule id., OHG ūwila id. (< *uwwilōn). Deminutive of *uwwōn. Zupitza Gutt. 17; Torp-Falk 32; Holthausen AEEW 376; Pokorny I 1103; Vries ANEW 632; Zalizniak I 156; Onions 638; Kluge- Seebold 237.
Swiss huwe, hue ‘owl’. An isolated archaism. Originally, of imitative origin. Cf. *ūfaz ~ *ūfōn. Pokorny I 1103.

Goth uz, us ‘out of’, ON ór, ur id., OE or- id., OFris ur-, or- id., OS ur-, or- id., OHG ur id. Probably, from *ut-sfurther related to *ūt (Schmidt Plur. 219). Brugmann KVG 463, 468 (same as Schmidt); Uhlenbeck TNTL XXV 302 (to IE *au- ‘away from’); Jacobsohn KZ XLIX 195; Holthausen AEEW 241; Feist 528; Pokorny I 1103–1104; Vries ANEW 419; Lehmann GED 380; Kluge- Seebold 850.
ON odd-hvass ‘sharppointed’, OHG ort-was id. Compound of *uzđaz and *xwassaz. Carr Nom. comp. 62.

ON oddr ‘sharp point (of a weapon)’, OE ord id., OFris ord id., OS ord ‘sharp point, sharp edge’, OHG ort id. Identical with Alb usht ‘ear (of grain)’ < *us-to-; further cf. Lith usnīs ‘thistle’. Torp-Falk 33, 561; Holthausen AEEW 242; Pokorny I 237, 1172; Vries ANEW 415; Zalizniak II 173; Onions 623; Kluge-Seebold 604; Orel AED 490. Zalizniak II 184; Frisk II 442–443; Onions 954; Kluge-Seebold 237–238.
ON úfr ‘a kind of owl’, OE úf ‘owl’, OHG ūvo id. Related to Lav Upis ‘owl’, Slav *vypь ‘bittern’ (Persson Beiträge 495). Zupitza Gutt. 17; Torp-Falk 32; Būga RFV LXV 323;
ON úr ‘metal slag’, LG ūr ‘soil containing iron’. Identical with OIr úr ‘earth, clay’. Torp-Falk 6; Vries ANEW 635.

ON úr ‘drizzling rain’. Related to OPrus wurs ‘pool, pond’, with a different ablaut grade Lith jáura ‘marsh, swamp’. Further see *warōn ~ *waraz (Bugge KZ XX 29–30). Torp-Falk 32; Pokorny I 80–81; Fraenkel 198.
ON úrigr ‘wet’, OE úriz-feäera ‘wet-feathered’. Derived from *ūran II. Holthausen AEEW 377; Pokorny I 80.

Goth uraz ‘name of the urune’, ON úrr ‘aurochs’, OE úr ‘a kind of ox, bison’ (a-stem), MLG ūr ‘aurochs’, OHG ūro id. (n-stem). Connected with *ūran II (Grienberger PBB XXI 209), cf. the same semantic development in IE *⁄ṛsen- ‘male’ from *⁄ers- ‘rain’. Försterman KZ I 499 (related to Lat ūrus); Froehde BB II 337 (identical with Skt usrá- ‘bull’, which is, however, metaphorically derived from usrá- ‘early in the morning’); Torp-Falk 32; Holthausen AEEW 377; Feist 527; Pokorny I 80; Vries ANEW 636; Lehmann GED 379; Kluge-Seebold 62 (to *⁄ṛsen- ‘male’).
Goth ut ‘out’, ON út id., OE út w
ON vafla ‘to hover about’, G wabelen ‘to move busily’. See *wab(a)rōjanan. Vries ANEW 638 (to ON váfa ‘to swing, to vibrate’); Heidermanns 642.

ON vafra ‘to hover about’, Du wapperen ‘to move back and forth’, MHG waberen ‘to move back and forth, to work busily’. Connected with *wabōn. Johansson GGA 1890 767– 768 (to Lat uapor ‘steam’), BB XVIII 31; id., OFris ūt id., OS ūt id., OHG ūz id. Related to Skt út ‘up, upon, from’, OIr ud-, od- ‘out’. Further connections are dubious. Torp-Falk 29; Wood JEGP II 218–219 (to *watnan ~ *watar); Güntert IF XL 189 (to Gk οὐδ-αμός ‘none, no one’); Holthausen AEEW 377; Feist 537; Mayrhofer I 101; Pokorny I 1103– 1104; Vries ANEW 636; Onions 636; Lehmann GED 384; Kluge-Seebold 66.
Goth uta ‘outside’, ON úti id., OE úte id., OFris ūte id., OS ūta, ūte id., OHG ūze id. Derived from *ūt. Torp- Falk 29; Holthausen AEEW 377; Feist 537; Vries ANEW 636; Lehmann GED 384.

Goth utana ‘outside’, ON útan ‘from outside’, OE útan id., OS ūtan id., OHG ūzan, ūzana ‘putside’. Derived from *ūt. Holthausen AEEW 377; Feist 537; Vries ANEW 636; Lehmann GED 384; Kluge-Seebold 67–68.
ON útarr ‘farther out’, OE útor ‘beyond, outside’, OFris ūter id., OS far-ūtar id., OHG prep. ūzar ‘out, outside’. Derived from *ūt. Holthausen AEEW 377; Vries ANEW 636; Kluge- Seebold 68.
ON ÿta ‘to push out, to launch’, OE ÿtan ‘to put out, to expel’. Derived from *ūt. Holthausen AEEW 413; Vries ANEW 680. Vries ANEW 638; Onions 995; Heidermanns 642; Kluge-Seebold 869.

ON vefr ‘web (in the loom), cloth’, OE webb ‘web, woven stuff’, OFris webb ‘cloth’, OS webbi id., OHG webbi id. Derived from *webanan. Torp-Falk 391–392; Holthausen AEEW 387; Pokorny I 1114; Zalizniak II 214; Onions 997; Bammesberger Nom. 66.
ON vefja ‘to wrap, to fold’, OE webbian ‘to weave, to contrive’. Derived from *webanan. Holthausen AEEW 387; Vries ANEW 650; Seebold 541.
ON vafi‘doubt, uncertainty’. Probably derived from *webanan. Onions 994.

OE wæsp, wæps ‘wasp’, OS wepsia id., OHG wefsa, wefs id. Close to Lat uespa id., OBret guohi id., OPrus wobse id., Lith vapsā id., Slav *osa id. Derived from *webanan. Pedersen Kelt. Gr. I 75; Torp-Falk 392; Trautmann BSW 342; Holthausen AEEW 379; Walde-Hofmann II 770; Pokorny I 1179; Chemodanov SGGJa I 59; Fraenkel 1196–1197; Onions 993; Vasmer-Trubachev III 156; Kluge- Seebold 887.

Burg *waþs ‘ford’, ON vaä id., OE wæd ‘ford, shallow water, sea’, MLG wat ‘place that can be used as a ford’, OHG wat ‘ford’. Related to Lat uadum id., uādō ‘to wade’. Connected with *wađanan. At the same time, may at least partly reflect *wađan ‘water’ identical with Slav *voda id. (Dybo BSA 145–146). Torp-Falk 385–386; Holthausen AEEW 378; Walde-Hofmann II 724; Pokorny I 1109; Vries ANEW 637; Zalizniak I 144; Kluge-Seebold 877.

ON vaäa ‘to wade’, OE wadan ‘to go, to pass, to proceed’, OFris wada ‘to wade’, MLG waden id., OHG watan id. Related to Arm gam ‘to come’, Lat uādō ‘to go, to walk’. Zupitza Gutt. 33; Torp-Falk 385; Holthausen AEEW 378; Walde-Hofmann II 723–724; Jóhannesson IEW 103–104; Pokorny I 1109; Chemodanov SGGJa I 91; Vries ANEW 637; Onions 988; Seebold 530–531; Kluge-Seebold 877; Boutkan OFED s.v.

ON vađr ‘fishing line’, Swed dial. neut. vad ‘dragnet’, OE wadu id., MLG wade id., OHG wata ‘big net’. Derived from *wađanan, cf. Lith bradīnë ‘drag-net’, Slav *brodьnь id. connected with OLith bradyti ‘to wander’, Slav *broditi ‘to wade’. Hardly to be directly compared with Lith deverb. vãdas ‘drag-net’ with a similar semantic motivation. Holthausen AEEW 378; Pokorny I 76; Fraenkel 1177; Vries ANEW 638; Kluge-Seebold 876–877.

Goth wadi ‘pledge, guarantee’, ON veä ‘pledge’, OE wedd id., OFris wed id., OS weddi id., OHG wetti id. Etymologically connected with Lat uas ‘bail, security’, Lith vãdas ‘guarantee, surety’ (Kienle Wu S XVI 76–78). Further cf. *weđanan (Osten-Sacken IF XXXIII 266). Breal MSL VII 441–443 (from MLat uadium ‘bail’); Torp-Falk 386; Holthausen AEEW 388; Walde- Hofmann II 735–736; Feist 539–540; Walde-Hoffmann II 735–736; Pokorny I 1109; Chemodanov SGGJa I 67; Fraenkel 1177; Vries ANEW 649; Zalizniak I 146; Fraenkel 1177; Otrébski KZ LXXXI 227; Amira Recht II 110–111; Lehmann GED 152, 386; Bammesberger Nom. 66; Kluge-Seebold 887.

Goth ga-wadjon ‘to betroth, to pledge’, ON veäja ‘to lay a wager, to bet, to appeal’, OE weddian ‘to engage, to betroth’, OFris weddia ‘to pay a fine, to bet’, MLG wedden ‘to pay a fine, to bet’, MHG wetten ‘to pledge’. Derived from *wađjan. Torp-Falk 386; Holthausen AEEW 388; Feist 539; Vries ANEW 649; Onions 997; Lehmann GED 152.
ON vangsni ‘plowshare’ (< *vagsni), OHG waganso id. Derived from *wazjaz (Brugmann Grundriß II/1 282). Torp-Falk 384; Pokorny I 1180; Chemodanov SGGJa I 63; Vries ANEW 643–644; Kluge-Seebold 870.

Goth wagjan ‘to shake, to move to and fro’, OE weczan ‘to wag, to move, to shake’, OS weggian ‘to move’, OHG weggen id. Causative of *wezanan. Structurally similar to Gk ὀχέω ‘to hold fast, to carry, to let ride’, Slav *voziti ‘to carry, to drive’. Torp-Falk 383; Holthausen AEEW 388; Feist 541; Pokorny I 1120; Frisk II 455–456; Seebold 543; Vasmer-Trubachev I 334; Lehmann GED 387.

ON veggr ‘wedge’ (partly, to *waziz), OE wecz ‘wedge’, OS weggi id., OHG weggi id. Identical with Lith vãgis ‘hook, wedge’ (Bugge BB III 121; Fick BB XII 162). Further cf. Lat uōmis ‘plowshare’, Gk ὄφνις: ὕννις, ἄρατρον (Hes.), OPrus wagnis ‘plowshare’. Bezzenberger-Fick BB XII 168; Meringer IF XVII 132; Torp-Falk 384; Trautmann BSW 337; Holthausen AEEW 388; Pokorny I 1180; Fraenkel 1179; Vries ANEW 650; Zalizniak II 214; Onions 997; Stang LS 60; Lindeman Verschärfung 10.10; Bammesberger Nom. 66.

Crim. Goth waghen ‘wagon’, ON vagn id., OE wæzn id., OFris wein id., OHG wagan id. Derived from *wezanan or directly from *wazōn (in which case cf. the semantics of Slav *kolyba ‘cradle’ ~ ‘wagon’, see Orel SSlav 1985/5 79–84) and derivationally similar to Toch A wkäá, B yakne ‘way, manner’, OIr fén ‘a kind of vehicle’. Zupitza Gutt. 205; Torp-Falk 383; Holthausen AEEW 380; Feist 540– 541; Porzig Gliederung 120; Pokorny I 1120; Chemodanov SGGJa I 97; Vries ANEW 639; Zalizniak I 137; Onions 988; Seebold 543; Lehmann GED 387; Bammesberger Nom. 73; Kluge-Seebold 870; Adams TB 481–482.
ON vaga ‘to wag, to waddle’, OE wazian ‘to move’, OS wagian id., OHG wagōn id. Derived from *wezanan. Holthausen AEEW 382; Pokorny I 1120; Vries ANEW 638; Onions 988; Seebold 543.
ON pl. vagar ‘a kind of bier’, OHG waga ‘movement’. Derived from *wezanan. Torp-Falk 383; Vries ANEW 672–673; Seebold 543.
ON vagga ‘cradle’ (with expressive gemination), OS waga id., OHG waga id. Derived from *wezanan. Torp- Falk 383; Seebold 543; Kluge-Seebold 889.

ON vax ‘wax’, OE weax id., OFris wax id., OS wahs id., OHG wahs id. Etymologically connected with Lith vãškas id., Slav *voskъ id. (Osthoff Etym. 19–21). Further connection with *waxsanan cannot be excluded, cf. the designation of wax as *sila ‘power, might’ in Slavic folklore (Orel Simp. 38–44). Lidén SVSU VI/1 28 (further to IE *⁄eg- ‘to weave’); Zupitza Gutt. 33, 192; Torp- Falk 381 (Balt, Slav < Gmc); Trautmann BSW 343; Holthausen AEEW 387; Pokorny I 1180; Chemodanov SGGJa I 83; Fraenkel 1207; Vries ANEW 648; Zalizniak I 144; Onions 995; Stang LS 61; Vasmer-Trubachev I 357; Kluge- Seebold 869.

ON vaxa ‘to grow, to increase’, OE weaxan ‘to grow’, OFris waxa id., OS wahsan id., OHG wahsan id. Related to Toch B auks- ‘to sprout, to grow up’, Skt perf. vavákṣa ‘to grow’, Av vaxš- id., Gk ađjv ‘to increase’. Bartholomae 1337–1338; Torp-Falk 382; Holthausen AEEW 387; Jóhannesson IEW 10; Mayrhofer I 98; Pokorny I 84–85; Schulze Kl. Schr. 863; Vries ANEW 648; Onions 995; Frisk I 187– 188; Seebold 532–533; Lehmann GED 387; Kluge-Seebold 869; Adams TB 131.
Goth wahsjan ‘to grow’, Norw vexa id. (wk., originally str. pres.). Derived from *waxsanan. Zupitza Gutt. 160; Torp-Falk 382; Feist 541; Vries ANEW 648, 658; Seebold 532–533.
ON vexa ‘to wax, to cover with wax’, OFris wexa id., OHG wahsen id. Derived from *waxsan. Identical with Slav *voš‘iti ‘to wax’. Vries ANEW 658.
Goth us-wahsts ‘growth’, OHG gi-wahst id. Derived from *waxsanan. Torp-Falk 382; Feist 541; Seebold 532; Bammesberger Nom. 144.
Goth wahstus ‘growth’, ON vǫxtr ‘size, growth’, OHG wahst ‘growth’ (fem.). Derived from *waxsanan. Torp-Falk 382; Feist 541; Pokorny I 84–85; Vries ANEW 676; Onions 988; Seebold 532; Lehmann GED 387.

ON váttr ‘witness’, OHG gi-waht ‘remembrance, opinion’ (masc., fem.). The corresponding strong verb is preserved in WGmc: MLG wagen ‘to mention’, MHG part. gi-wagen. Related to Toch A wak, B wek ‘voice’, Skt vāc- ‘speech, saying, call’, Av vaxša- ‘speech, word’, Gk đpow ‘word’, Lat uōx ‘voice’. Bartholomae 11339; Torp-Falk 381; Walde-Hofmann II 723–825; Jóhannesson IEW 121–122; Mayrhofer III 180; Pokorny I 1136; Vries ANEW 648; Frisk I 545; Seebold 531; Burlak IFTJa 255.
Goth wahtwo ‘watch, guard’, OS wahta id., OHG wahta id. (ōand ōn-stems). Related to *wakanan (see *wakēnan). Torp-Falk 380; Seebold 535.

Goth wai ‘woe’, ON vei id., OE wá id., OS wē id., OHG wē id. There are similar interjections in numerous IE and non-IE languages, cf. Lat uae, MIr fae, Lith vaĩ. Neisser BB XXX 303 (to Skt uvé ‘oh alas’, Av vayōi and the like); Torp-Falk 379; Holthausen AEEW 378; Walde-Hofmann II 724; Feist 541; Pokorny I 1111; Fraenkel 1179; Vries ANEW 650; Lehmann GED 387–388.

Goth bi-waibjan ‘to surround’, ON veifa ‘to wave, to vibrate’, OE wæfan ‘to wrap up, to clothe’, MDu weiven ‘to be in swinging movement’, OHG zi-weiben ‘to scatter’. Identical with Skt vepáyati ‘to make tremble’, Lith vaipÿtis ‘to grimace, to wince’, further related to Skt vépate ‘to tremble, to be agitated’, Lith viepiū, vi‚pti ‘to gape about’ (Persson Beiträge 235). Torp-Falk 412 (to Lat uibrō ‘to vibrate’); Feist 97–98; Mayrhofer III 260; Pokorny I 1131–1133; Fraenkel 1182, 1242–1243; Vries ANEW 651; Onions 997; Lehmann GED 74.
Goth wai-deþs ‘evil deed’, OE weá-dæd id., MHG wē-tāt ‘pain’. Compound of *wai and *đēđiz. Lehmann GED 388.

OE wáz ‘wall’, OFris wāch id., OS wēg id. A substantivized adjective structurally close to Lith vi‚kas ‘strength, life’, Slav *vîkъ ‘age’ (Bugge BB III 113) and derived from *wīxanan ~ *wīzanan. Cf. also *waizō > ON veig ‘strong beverage, strength’ and *waixjōn > Goth waihjo ‘battle’. Wood MP XI 337 (*waizō to Class. Skt vīcī- ‘wave’); Torp-Falk 408; Trautmann BSW 339; Feist 542; Pokorny I 1128–1129; Chemodanov SGGJa I 97; Fraenkel 1239; Vries ANEW 651 (to *wajjuz); Vasmer-Trubachev I 286; Lehmann GED 388; Boutkan OFED s.v.
ON veik-ligr ‘weakly’, OE wác-líc ‘poor, mean’. Derived from *waikaz. Heidermanns 644.

ON veikr ‘weak, weakly, sick’, OE wác ‘yielding, pliant, weak, poor’, OS wēk ‘weak, despondent’, OHG weih ‘soft, weak’. Derived from *wīkanan. Karsten NB I 61–62, II 455; Torp-Falk 407; Holthausen AEEW 378; Trier Lehm 44–51; Pokorny I 1130; Vries ANEW 640, 651; Onions 996; Rosengren 86–91; Seebold 546; Bammesberger Nom. 237; Heidermanns 644– 645; Kluge-Seebold 880.
ON veikjask ‘to grow ill’, OE wæcan ‘to weaken’, OLFr ge-weiken ‘to soften’, OHG weihhen ‘to break, to bend, to soften’. Derived from *waikaz. Torp-Falk 407–408; Holthausen AEEW 378; Heidermanns 644.

Goth wainahs ‘miserable, woeful’, MLG wēnic id., OHG wēnag id. Derived from the unattested *wainaz probably based on *wai. Grienberger Got. 235 (to Latv vaĩna ‘guilt’); Torp-Falk 379; Feist 544; Lehmann GED 389.

ON veina ‘to wail’, OE wánian ‘to deplore, to lament’, OFris wēnia id., OS wēnon id., OHG weinōn id. Derived from *wainaz (see *wainazaz ~ *wainaxaz). Torp-Falk 379; Holthausen AEEW 383; Vries ANEW 651; Lehmann GED 275 (to *kwainōjanan); Kluge-Seebold 882.
Goth waips ‘wreath’, MHG umbe-weif ‘cloth wrapped around the skirt’. Derived from *wīpanan. Feist 544; Vries ANEW 652; Onions 1003; Seebold 546; Lehmann GED 400; Bammesberger Nom. 52.
ON veipa ‘woman’s hood’, OE wápe ‘cloth’. Derived from *waipaz. Holthausen AEEW 384; Vries ANEW 652.
Norw veis ‘stalk’. Cf. also ON vísir ‘index, sprout’. Identical with Slav *vîxa ‘straw bundle’. Stang LS 63; Vasmer-Trubachev I 308.

ON veisa ‘pool, stagnant pond’, OE wáse ‘ooze, mud, slime’, OFris wāse id. Cf. also WGmc *wisō(n) > MLG wese ‘meadow’, OHG wisa id. Related to Skt vißá- ‘liquid’, Gk ïός ‘poison’, Lat uīrus ‘liquid’. Holthausen AEEW 384; Walde-Hofmann II 800; Pokorny I 1134; Vries ANEW 652; Frisk I 730; Onions 628. *waita pret.-pres.: Goth wait ‘to know’, ON veit id., OFris wēt id., OE wát id., OS wēt id., OHG weiz id. Continues IE perf. *⁄oida, of *⁄eid-: Skt perf. véda id., Gk aor. εἶδον ‘to see’, perf. οἶδα, Arm gitem ‘to know’, Lat uideō ‘to see’, OPrus waist ‘to know’, Slav perf. *vîdî ‘to know’. Torp-Falk 409; Trautmann BSW 338; Holthausen AEEW 400; Walde- Hofmann II 784–785; Feist 569; Jóhannesson IEW 116–118; Mayrhofer III 256–257; Pokorny I 1125–1126; Vries ANEW 669; Schulze Kl. Schr. 219; Frisk II 357; Onions 1009; Seebold 533–535; Vasmer-Trubachev I 283; Lehmann Language XIX 25, GED 379, 406–407; Kluge-Seebold 894–895.

ON veita ‘to grant, to give a grant’, OFris wēta ‘to claim, to testify’, OHG weizen ‘to show, to prove’. Causative of *waita. Structurally close to Skt vedáyati ‘to announce’. Torp-Falk 410; Vries ANEW 652; Seebold 534.
ON veiäi-maär ‘huntsman’, MHG weide-man id. Compound of *waiþiz ~ *waiþ(j)ō and *mannz. Carr Nom. comp. 85 (parallel formations).

Burg *waiþs ‘hunt’, ON veiär ‘hunting’, OE wáä ‘wandering, hunting’, OS weiäa ‘pasture’, OHG weida ‘hunting; pasture, food’. Continues *⁄oití- ~ *⁄oitā derived from *⁄ei-: Skt. véti ‘to have in view, to approach’, Gk ἵεμαι ‘to move forward, to hurry’, Lith vejū, vÿti ‘to hunt, to drive’ (Grimm KZ I 82). Uhlenbeck KZ XX 560 (to OIr fíad ‘game’); Meringer IF XVIII 237, 255; Torp-Falk 379; Trautmann BSW 345–346; Holthausen AEEW 384; Mayrhofer III 255; Pokorny I 1123–1124; Chemodanov SGGJa I 70; Fraenkel 1267; Vries ANEW 650–651; Zalizniak II 219; Frisk I 711; Campanile BBCS XXVI 306 (to OIr fiadach ‘hunting’); Vasmer- Trubachev III 294; Köbler 714; Kluge-Seebold 881 (separates OHG weida ‘hunting’ from weida ‘pasture’; the latter to Av vāstra- ‘pasture’).
ON veiäa ‘to catch, to hunt’, OE wæäan ‘to hunt’, MLG weiden ‘to hunt with a falcon’, OHG weidōn ‘to graze’. Derived from *waiþiz ~ *waiþ(j)ō. Holthausen AEEW 382; Vries ANEW 650.

ON vá ‘woe, calamity’, OE weá ‘woe, misery, evil’ (masc.), OS wē id., OHG wēwa id. Derived from *wai. Torp-Falk 379; Weisweiler IF XLI 333; Holthausen AEEW 385; Pokorny I 1111; Vries ANEW 637; Zalizniak II 184.

Goth baurgs-waddjus ‘city wall’, ON veggr ‘wall’. Deverbative of IE *⁄ei- ‘to turn, to bend’, cf. Skt váyati ‘to weave’, Lat uīeō ‘to bind, to weave’, Lith vejū, vÿti ‘to wind, to turn’, Slav *viti id. Kögel PBB IX 542–543 (to *waizaz); Kauffmann Zd Ph XXXIX 291; Torp- Falk 406–407; Uhlenbeck PBB XXX 324 (same as Kögel); Trautmann BSW 346; Holthausen AEEW 382 (against Kögel); Walde-Hofmann II 786–787; Feist 538–539; Mayrhofer III 147; Pokorny I 1120–1122; Chemodanov SGGJa I 73; Fraenkel 1267; Vries ANEW 650; Lindeman Verschärfung 118–119 (same as Kögel); Vasmer- Trubachev I 322; Lehmann GED 386; Bammesberger Nom. 161.

Goth wakan ‘to be awake’, ON vaka id., ON vakinn ‘awake’, OE wacian ‘to watch, to wake’, wacan ‘to be born’, OFris wakia, waka ‘to be awake’, OS wakon id., OHG wahhēn id. Residual traces of str. *wakanan are registered (cf. for example OE pret. wóc). Related to Skt vāja- ‘strength, vigor, speed, contest’, Lat uegeō ‘to be lively’ (Prokosch CGG 174). Zupitza Gutt. 199; Holthausen AEEW 378; Feist 547–548; Pokorny I 1117– 1118; Mayrhofer III 182–183; Vries ANEW 639; Onions 989; Seebold 535; Lehmann GED 392; Kluge-Seebold 869.

Goth us-wakjan ‘to awake’, ON vekja ‘to waken, to rouse from sleep’, OE weccan ‘to wake, to rouse’, OS wekkian ‘to wake’, OHG wecken id. Derived from *wakanan (see *wakēnan). Structurally close to Skt vājáyati ‘to make fire’, Lat uegeō (Watkins HSCP LXXVII 195–201). Torp-Falk 380; Holthausen AEEW 387; Feist 547; Mayrhofer III 182; Pokorny I 1117; Vries ANEW 652; Seebold 535; Lehmann GED 392; Kluge-Seebold 878.

Goth ga-waknan ‘to awake’, ON vakna id., OE wæcnan ‘to waken, to arise, to spring’. Based on the residually attested str. *wakanan (see *wakēnan). Schweizer KZ I 154; Torp-Falk 380; Holthausen AEEW 378; Feist 547; Walde-Hofmann II 741; Jóhannesson IEW 123–124; Pokorny I 1117; Vries ANEW 639; Onions 989; Seebold 535–536; Lehmann GED 392.

ON vaka ‘being awake, vigil, eve’, OE wacu ‘waking, wake, watch’, MLG wake ‘wake, vigil’, OHG naht-wahha id. Derived from *wakanan (see *wakēnan). Holthausen AEEW 378; Pokorny I 1117–1118; Vries ANEW 639; Zalizniak II 181; Onions 989; Seebold 535.

ON vakr ‘watchful, alert, nimble’, OE wacor ‘watchful, vigilant’, MLG wacker ‘watchful, lively’, OHG ēr-wackar ‘awake early’. Derived from *wakanan (see *wakēnan). Formally similar to Skt vájra- ‘thunderbolt’, Av vazra- ‘club’. Försterman KZ I 240; Karsten NB II 258; Zupitza Gutt. 199; Bartholomae 1392; Torp-Falk 380; Kluge Nom. Stamm. 97; Holthausen AEEW 378; Mayrhofer III 126; Pokorny I 1117–1118; Vries ANEW 640; Seebold 535; Wissmann AWNP 67; Bammesberger Nom. 248; Heidermanns 645; Kluge- Seebold 870.
ON vekra ‘to freshen up, to rouse’, OFris wekkria ‘to increase’, MDu wackeren id. A derivative of *wakraz or, more probably, an emphatic derivative of *wakanan (see *wakēnan). Vries ANEW 652.

Icel vökull ‘watchful’, OE wacol id., OHG wahhal id. Related to *wakanan (see *wakēnan). Karsten NB II 286; Torp-Falk 380 (to Lat uigil ‘watchful’); Holthausen AEEW 378; Pokorny I 1117–1118; Vries ANEW 673; Seebold 535; Lehmann GED 392; Heidermanns 645–646.

ON vǫkr ‘moist’, E dial. wack id., MDu wac id. Related to Gk ὑγρός id. (Osthoff IF IV 278). Fick II 266; Karsten NB I 33; Torp-Falk 381; Pokorny I 1118; Vries ANEW 673; Frisk II 955–956; Heidermanns 646.
ON vǫk ‘hole, opening in the ice’, MLG wake id. Substantivized *wakwaz. Torp-Falk 381; Pokorny I 1118; Vries ANEW 673; Heidermanns 646; Kluge-Seebold 872.
ON val ‘choice’, OHG wala id. Derived from *waljanan. Torp-Falk 400; Vries ANEW 640; Lehmann GED 392; Kluge- Seebold 871.
ON val-dreyri ‘the blood of the slain’, OE wæl-dreór id. Compound of *walaz II and *đrauzaz ~ *drauzjōn. Carr Nom. comp. 55.
ON val-fall ‘the fall of the slain’, OE wæl-feall id. Compound of *walaz II and *fallan ~ *fallaz. Carr Nom. comp. 55.
ON val-kyrja ‘Valkyrie, chooser of the slain’, OE wælcyrze id. Compound of *walaz II and *kuzjōn (from *keusanan). Torp-Falk 402; Carr Nom. comp. 55.
ON valr ‘round, oval’. Identical with Latv uôls id., further to Slav sbst. *valъ ‘wave’. Pokorny I 1142; Dybo VSJa 25 (pretonic shortening in Gmc).

ON valr ‘the slain’ (a- or i-stem), OE wæl ‘the slain, slaughter, carnage’ (< neut.), OS wal-dād ‘murder’, OHG wal ‘battlefield, blood bath’ (masc., fem. *waliz, or neut.). Related to OIr fuil ‘blood’, MIr fuili ‘bleeding wound’, W gweli ‘wound’, Corn goly id., MBret goulyow id. Kögel PBB XVI 511 (to Lat uulnus ‘wound’); Berneker IF IX 360 (to Czech váleti ‘to battle’); Mikkola BB XXI 223 (to Lith v Elës ‘spirit of the dead’); Torp-Falk 402; Holthausen AEEW 380; Pokorny I 1144; Vries ANEW 642; Zalizniak II 190; Lehmann GED 404; Kluge-Seebold 873.
ON vald ‘power, authority’, OE weald id. Derived from *walđanan. Torp-Falk 404; Holthausen AEEW 385; Vries ANEW 640; Seebold 536; Lehmann GED 392.

Goth waldan ‘to rule (a household), to govern’, ON valda ‘to wield, to rule, to cause’ (str. ~ wk.), OE wealdan ‘to have power, to control’, OFris walda ‘to rule, to possess’, OS waldan ‘to wield’, OHG waltan ‘to possess, to reign’. Connected with Lith véldu, veldėti ‘to possess’, valda U, valdÿti ‘to rule’, Slav *voldîti ‘to possess, to rule’. Torp-Falk 404 (Slav < Gmc); Meillet MSL VIII 290; Trautmann BSW 341; Feist 548; Holthausen AEEW 385; Stender- Petersen 213–215 (Balto-Slavic < Gmc); Porzig Gliederung 142; Jóhannesson IEW 106–107; Pokorny I 1111–1112; Chemodanov SGGJa I 66; Fraenkel 1188, 1217–1218; Vries ANEW 640; Onions 1006; Stang LS 61–62; Seebold 536– 537; Benveniste IEL 274; Vasmer- Trubachev I 340–341; Lehmann GED 392; Kluge-Seebold 874; Boutkan OFED s.v.
ON valdr ‘causing something’, OE weald ‘powerful, mighty’, MHG ge-walt ‘enormous’. Derived from *walđanan. Karsten NB I 74; Heidermanns 647–648.
ON vǫldugr ‘mighty, powerful’, OFris weldich id., OS gi-weldig id., OHG gi-waltīg id. Derived from *walđanan. Vries ANEW 673.

ON auä-veldr ‘easy to cope with’, OE earfoä-wielde ‘difficult to rule’, OFris un-welde ‘involuntary’. Derived from *walđanan. Karsten NB I 112; Seebold 536; Matzel Festschr. Schröder 106, KZ CV 127; Heidermanns 648.
ON veldi ‘power’, OE zewild id., MLG ge-welde id. Derived from *walđan. Holthausen AEEW 393; Vries ANEW 653.
ON ein-valdi ‘monarch’, OS ala-waldo ‘sovereign king’, MHG alwalte id. Derived from *walđanan. Torp- Falk 404; Vries ANEW 641.

Icel volgr ‘lukewarm’, OE wealz ‘nauseous’. Unclear. Cf. *welkaz. Karsten NB II 293; Zupitza Gutt. 142; Torp-Falk 402–403; Holthausen AEEW 385; Pokorny I 1143–1145 (to *⁄elk-/ *⁄elg- ‘wet’); Heidermanns 649 (to ON ylr ‘warmth’, ylja ‘to warm’).

Goth us-walugjan ‘to make swirl around’, OHG walagōn ‘to roll about’. Distantly related to Skt válati ‘to turn’, Arm gelum ‘to turn’, Gk εφιλέω id. Streitberg Bibel I 343 (Goth -u- explained by svarabhakti); Hamel Neophilologus X 72 (Goth based on *walugs derived from *waluz); Feist 536; Specht KZ LIX 221 (Goth -ugj-an close to Gk -ussv < *-υκωō); Pokorny I 1144; Frisk I 457–458; Lehmann GED 384.

ON valr ‘hawk’, OE wealh-hafoc ‘gerfalcon’, OHG walh-habuh ‘falcon’. Plausibly, identical with *walxaz II, lit. ‘foreign bird, Welsh bird’ (Fischer Lehnw. 13). Holthausen AEEW 386; Pokorny KZ XLVI 151 (to *⁄el- ‘to tear’); Vries ANEW 642.

Burg adj. *walhs ‘Celtic, foreign’, ON pl. valir ‘(Celtic) inhabitants of Northern France’, OE wealh ‘foreigner, slave, Celt (esp. of Britain)’, MLG wale ‘Welsh’, OHG pl. Walaha ‘Roman, Rutulian’. Borrowed from Celt-Lat Volcae, name of a Celtic people (Streitberg UGG 136). As to *walxa-xnutz (> ON val-hnot ‘walnut’, OE wealh-hnutu id., MLG wal-nut id.), it is a calque of Rom *nux gallica id. > Fr noix gauge (Weisgerber IF LXII 33–61). Zupitza Gutt. 142; Torp-Falk 403; Holthausen AEEW 386; Vries ANEW 641; Zalizniak II 191; Onions 990, 999; Benveniste 293; Köbler 714; Kluge-Seebold 873, 884–885.

ON valskr ‘foreign, of Northern France’, OE wílisc ‘foreign, Welsh’, MLG welsch ‘Romance’, OHG walahisc ‘Welsh, Romance’. Derived from *walxaz II. Torp-Falk 403; Holthausen AEEW 393; Vries ANEW 642; Onions 999; Kluge-Seebold 884–885.

Goth waljan ‘to choose’, ON velja id., OHG wellen id. Derived from *wel(j)anan. Torp-Falk 400; Feist 548–549; Pokorny I 1137; Vries ANEW 653; Onions 1007; Seebold 551; Lehmann GED 392; Kluge-Seebold 871.

ON valk ‘tossing to and fro’, OE ze-wealc ‘rolling, motion, attack’, MDu walc ‘felted wad’. Close to Lat ualgus ‘bow-legged’, Latv valgs ‘cord’. Derived from *walkanan. Torp-Falk 402; Holthausen AEEW 385; Walde- Hofmann II 728–729; Pokorny I 1144; Vries ANEW 641; Seebold 537.

OE wealcan ‘to roll, to toss’, MDu walken ‘to roll’, OHG part. gi-walchenemo ‘concreto’. Related to Skt válgati ‘to leap, to bound, to wave’, Lat ualgus ‘having the calves of the legs bent outwards, bow-legged’ (Fick I 541), Latv valgs ‘cord’. Persson Wurzelerw. 131, Beiträge 85, 543; Torp-Falk 402; Holthausen AEEW 385; Mayrhofer III 164 (against the comparison with Skt); Pokorny I 1140–1144; Vries ANEW 641; Seebold 537; Kluge-Seebold 872–873.
ON valka ‘to roll’, OE wealcian ‘to roll up, to muffle up’. Derived from *walkanan. Torp-Falk 402; Holthausen AEEW 385; Jóhannesson IEW 160–164; Vries ANEW 641; Onions 989; Seebold 537.
ON vella ‘boiling heat, ebullition’, OE will ‘spring, well, fountain’, OFris walla ‘boiling’, MDu welle id. Derived from *wellanan II. Torp-Falk 401; Holthausen AEEW 393; Vries ANEW 653.
ON vella ‘to boil, to well over’, OE willan ‘to boil’, MLG wellen id., MHG wellen id. Derived from *wellanan II. Torp-Falk 401; Vries ANEW 653; Seebold 538; Kluge-Seebold 873.

ODan valm, volm ‘boiling’, OE wilm ‘fount, stream, boiling water’, MHG walm ‘seething’. Derived from *wellanan II. Close to Skt ūrmí- ‘wave’, Av var 6 mi- id. < *⁄¬Hmi-. Torp-Falk 401; Holthausen AEEW 393–394.

ON vala ‘knuckle-bone’, OE walu ‘ridge, bank (?)’, LG wāle ‘weal’. Related to Gk εφιλέω, Hom eǫlv ‘to squeeze’, Lith valÿti ‘to cleanse’, Slav *valъ ‘wave’. For the semantic development cf. Latv valnis ‘edge, brim, rim’. Further see *wellanan I. Holthausen AEEW 392; Pokorny I 1138; Frisk I 457; Onions 989.

ON valtr ‘reeling, easily upset’, OE seonu-wealt ‘circular, round’. Derived from *weltanan ~ *waltanan. Karsten I 75; Torp-Falk 403; Wissmann NP 25; Vries ANEW 642; Heidermanns 651; Kluge-Seebold 874.

Goth waltjan ‘to beat into, to roll’, ON velta ‘to roll’, OE wæltan, wiltan id., OHG welzen id. Causative of *weltanan ~ *waltanan. Torp-Falk 403; Feist 549; Vries ANEW 653; Onions 999; Seebold 554; Lehmann GED 392–393.
ON vǫlt ‘roller’, OE wealte id., MLG wolte id., OHG walza ‘sling, trap’. Substantivized *waltaz. Holthausen AEEW 386; Vries ANEW 673–674; Kluge-Seebold 874.
ME welde, wolde ‘weld, Reseda luteola’, MDu wolde id. Derived from *walþuz. Torp-Falk 403 (to Lat lūtum ‘weld’ < *⁄lūto-); Onions 998.

ON vǫllr ‘field’, OE weald ‘high land covered with wood’, OFris wald ‘forest’, OS wald id., OHG wald id. Related to Gk λάσιος ‘shaggy, woolly’, OIr folt ‘hair’, OPrus wolti ‘ear of grain’, Lith váltis ‘ear of oats’, Slav *voltь ‘ear of grain’ (Solmsen KZ XLII 214). Fick II 263; Lewy KZ XL 422 (to Lat saltus ‘pass, mountain forest’ < *(s)⁄altus); Torp-Falk 403 (to Lat uallis ‘valley’); Trautmann BSW 341; Holthausen KZ XLVI 178 (follows Lewy), AEEW 385; Pokorny I 1139; Fraenkel 1192–1193; Vries ANEW 673; Zalizniak II 204; Frisk II 88; Onions 1011; Vasmer-Trubachev I 344; Bammesberger Nom. 156 (to *welþjaz); Kluge-Seebold 872.

Goth walus ‘staff’, ON vǫlr ‘round stick, staff’, OE wyrt-walu ‘root’, OFris walu-bera ‘pilgrim’. Identical with Lith ap-valūs ‘round’. Related to *wellanan I. Cf. other words for ‘stick, staff’ connected with IE *⁄el- ‘to turn’: Lat uallus ‘pole, stake’, OPrus walis ‘pull rod’. Torp-Falk 400; Walde-Hofmann II 730; Feist 549; Holthausen AEEW 382; Pokorny I 1142; Fraenkel 1193; Vries ANEW 673; Zalizniak I 152; Lehmann GED 393; Boutkan OFED s.v.
Goth af-walwjan ‘to roll away’, OE wilwan ‘to roll’. Derived from *waluz. Torp-Falk 401; Holthausen AEEW 394; Feist 13; Pokorny I 1142; Onions 990; Lehmann GED 9.

Goth wamba ‘belly, womb’, ON vǫmb id., OE wamb id., OFris wamme id., MLG wamme id., OHG wambo id. (masc.). A descriptive stem similar to Lith bámba ‘navel’, Latv bamba ‘ball’, Slav *bǫba ‘grain, swelling’. Kuhn KZ I 138; Pedersen BB XX 238 (to Skt gabhá- ‘vulva’); Uhlenbeck PBB XXII 192 (follows Pedersen); Wood MLN XV 99 (to Skt vapā ‘omentum, skin investing the intestines’); Torp-Falk 392 (to Bret gwamm ‘woman’); Holthausen AEEW 382; Feist 549; Schwentner IF LXI 228–234; Fraenkel 33; Vries ANEW 674; Zalizniak I 148; Onions 1011; Lehmann GED 393; Windekens KZ XCVI 93–94 (to Gk νύμφη ‘pregnant woman’); Trubachev ESSJa II 229– 230; Heidermanns 612 (phonetically influenced by *þambaz); Kluge-Seebold 874.
ON vamm-lauss ‘spotless’, OS wam-lōs ‘free from blame’. Compound of *wamman and *lausaz. Carr Nom. comp. 91 (parallel formations).

Goth wamm ‘stain, flaw’, ON vamm ‘blemish’, OE wamm ‘spot, mark, stain’, OFris wam id., OS wam ‘mistake, ailment’. Substantivized *wammaz. Torp-Falk 392; Holthausen AEEW 382; Feist 549–550; Martinet Gém. 197; Vries ANEW 642–643; Lehmann GED 393; Heidermanns 652.

Goth ga-wamms ‘unclean, stained’, OE wamm ‘foul, evil, wicked’, OS wam ‘bad, evil, nefarious’. Derived from *⁄on-mo- related to *⁄–mo- in Skt vāma- ‘left’ and vāmá- ‘lovely, dear’. Uhlenbeck PBB XXX 324 (<*⁄om-⁄o- or *⁄om-no-, to *⁄em(H)- ‘to vomit’: Skt vámiti, Gk ἐμέω, Lat uomō); Torp-Falk 392; Feist 549–550; Holthausen AEEW 382; Weisweiler IF XLI 46 (supports Uhlenbeck); Pokorny I 1146 (sceptical of Uhlenbeck’s etymology); Vries ANEW 642–643; Schubert Erw. 75; Lehmann GED 393; Heidermanns 652–653.

Goth ana-wammjan ‘to stain’, OE wemman ‘to spot, to mar, to spoil’, OFris wemma ‘to damage’, OHG bi-wemmen ‘to dishonor’. Derived from *wammaz. Holthausen AEEW 389; Feist 549; Lehmann GED 393; Heidermanns 652.
ON van-heill ‘disabled, ill’, OE wan-hál ‘weak, sick, maimed’, OHG wana-heil ‘disabled, weak’. Compound of *wanaz I and *xailaz. Carr Nom. comp. 66.
ON vakr, vákr ‘a kind of hawk’. Identical with Lith vãnagas ‘hawk’ (Lidén MASO I 55–67). Fraenkel 1194; Vries ANEW 640.
Goth wan ‘want, lack’, OE wan id. Substantivized neut. of *wanaz I. Feist 550; Heidermanns 653.

Goth wans ‘lacking, wanting’, ON vanr id., OE wan ‘wanting, absent’, OFris won-gare ‘deficient clothing’, OS wan ‘deficient, absent’, OHG wan id. Related to Skt ūná- ‘defective, wanting’, Av unō id., Lat uānus ‘empty, vain’ (Pedersen CDL 34). Bartholomae 1407; Karsten NB II 233–234; Torp- Falk 378; Endzelin KZ LII 119–120 (to Lith vāns-kariai ‘unhatched eggs’); Feist 550; Holthausen AEEW 382; Walde- Hofmann II 731–732; Mayrhofer I 115; Pokorny I 345–346; Vries ANEW 644; Onions 991; Hoffmann MSS XX 34–35 (< *⁄6 nos); Schubert Erw. 30; Lehmann GED 394; Bammesberger Nom. 246; Heidermanns 653–654; Kluge-Seebold 871.

ON vanr ‘accustomed’. Related to Toch A wañi, wīna ‘pleasure’, Skt vanóti ‘to demand, to like’, Lat uenus ‘love’ and other continuants of IE *⁄en- ‘to wish, to love’, cf. *wunēnan. Karsten NB I 69–70; Torp-Falk 387; Pokorny I 1146–1147; Vries ANEW 644; Onions 996; Heidermanns 654 (derived from

ON vandr ‘difficult’, OS wand ‘fluctuating, variable’. Derived from *wenđanan. Karsten NB II 338–339; Torp-Falk 390; Wissmann NP 16; Vries ANEW 643; Meid Wortbild. 229; Seebold 555; Matzel KZ CV 104; Heidermanns 654–655.

East Gmc Wandali ‘Vandals’ (Saxo), ON pln. Vendill, OE pl. Wendelas ‘Vandals’. Cf. also OHG wentilsēo ‘sea’. Derived from *wanđaz. Erdmann SVSU I/1 79 (to *wanđuz); Hald CM IV 62–78 (to *wenđanan); Holthausen AEEW 389; Vries ANEW 653– 654 (to *wanđ- ‘water’).

Goth wandjan ‘to turn’, ON venda id., OE wendan id., OFris wenda id., OS wendian ‘to direct’, OHG wenten ‘to turn’. Causative of *wenđanan. Torp-Falk 390; Holthausen AEEW 389; Feist 550; Vries ANEW 653; Onions 1000; Seebold 555; Lehmann GED 393; Kluge-Seebold 885.
ON vanda ‘to object, to be fastidious’, OE wandian ‘to turn aside’, MDu wanden ‘to turn into’, OHG part. wantōnti ‘mutual’. Derived from *wanđaz. Holthausen AEEW 383; Vries ANEW 643.
ON vǫndull ‘wisp of hay’, OFris wandel ‘exchange’, OHG wandel ‘change of mind’. Derived from *wanđaz. ON could be etymologically separate from WGmc and related to *wanđuz. Torp-Falk 390; Seebold 555.

Goth wandus ‘rod’, ON vǫndr ‘wand, switch, roller’. Derived from *wenđanan. Scheftelowitz BB XXIX 43 (to Arm vandak ‘network’); Uhlenbeck PBB XXII 192 (to Skt aor. vádhīm ‘to slay, to kill’); Torp-Falk 390; Feist 550; Endzelin KZ LII 110–111 (to Lith vánta ‘bunch of green birch twigs’); Fraenkel 1196, 1223–1224; Vries ANEW 674; Onions 991; Seebold 555; Lehmann GED 393–394; Kluge-Seebold 874.

Norw vond ‘a kind of shrew’, OE wand ‘mole’. Probably related to Gk ǫonyow ‘root of a hair, young hair’ < *⁄i-⁄ondho- and especially close to MIr find ‘hair (on the head)’ < *⁄endhu- (Lidén IF XIX 345–347). Torp-Falk 390 (to *wenđanan); Pokorny I 1148; Frisk I 729–730.

Goth *wanan ‘to decrease’ (based on wanains ‘lack, deficiency’), OE wanian ‘to make less’, OFris wonia id., OS wanon id., OHG part. giwanēt ‘reduced, decreased’. Derived from *wanaz I. Torp-Falk 378; Holthausen AEEW 383; Feist 550; Heidermanns 653.

Goth waggs ‘paradise’, ON vangr ‘garden, a green home-field’, OE wanz ‘field, plain, land, earth’, OS wang ‘garden, a green home-field’, OHG holzwang ‘wooded field’. Together with Alb vang ‘fallow’ continues IE *⁄onkó- (Lehmann GED 387). See *wanxaz. Zupitza Gutt. 181; Torp-Falk 389; Holthausen AEEW 383; Feist 540; Pokorny I 1148– 1149 (to *⁄e-n-g- ~ *⁄e-n-gh- ‘to be bent’); Vries ANEW 643; Zalizniak I 137; Stang LS 60; Bammesberger Nom. 50.

ON vangi ‘cheek’, OE wanze id., OS wanga id., OHG wanga id. Etymologically identical with *wanzaz. Scheftelowitz BB XXIX 41 (to Arm gang ‘skull, head’, Gk ἄμφην ‘neck’); Zupitza Gutt. 142; Torp-Falk 390; Holthausen AEEW 383; Pokorny I 1149; Vries ANEW 643; Zalizniak II 178; Lehmann GED 386; Heidermanns 656; Kluge-Seebold 874.

Goth un-wahs ‘blameless’, OE wóh ‘bent, crooked, twisted’. Related to Skt váñcati ‘to move crookedly’, Lat conuexus ‘curved’. Torp-Falk 389; Weisweiler IF XLI 339–341 (to *waiwō(n)); Walde-Hofmann I 268–269; Feist 525; Holthausen AEEW 404; Mayrhofer III 127; Pokorny I 1134–1135; Vries ANEW 637 (against Weisweiler); Lehmann GED 379; Heidermanns 655–656.

Icel vinstr ‘fourth stomach of ruminants’, OHG wanast, wenist ‘abdomen, belly’. Identical with Skt vaniṣṭhú- ‘rectum’ (Johansson IF XIV 324: from *⁄oned-sthu-). Torp-Falk 389; Lidén KZ LXI 20 (to Gk ἤνυστρον ‘fourth stomach of ruminants’); Pokorny I 1105; Kluge-Seebold 875.
Dan dial. vann ‘swelling’, OE wenn ‘wen’, MLG wene id. Connected with *wunđaz. Holthausen AEEW 389; Pokorny I 1109.

ON venja ‘to accustom’, OE wenian id., OS gi-wennian ‘to get used’, OHG gi-wennen id. Derived from *wanaz II. Torp-Falk 387; Rooth UUÅ 1924/8 93–106; Vries ANEW 654; Onions 996; Heidermanns 654; Kluge- Seebold 323.
ON vakka ‘to stray, to hover about’, OS wankon ‘to sway’, OHG wancōn id. Derived from *wenkjanan. Trautmann BSW 350–351; Pokorny I 1149; Vries ANEW 639; Kluge-Seebold 874.

ON vanask ‘to wane, to fail’, OE wanian ‘to lessen, to diminish’, OHG wanōn ‘to spoil, to take away’. Derived from *wanaz I. Cf. also ON vansa ‘to do too little’ < *wan(e)sōjanan. Holthausen AEEW 383; Vries ANEW 643– 644; Onions 991; Heidermanns 653.
ON vani ‘shortage, lack’, OE wana ‘want, lack’. Related to *wanaz I. Torp-Falk 378; Holthausen AEEW 383; Vries ANEW 644; Heidermanns 653.
ON vani ‘custom’, OE ze-wuna id., OS gi-wono id. Cf. also OHG fem. gi-wona id. Derived from *wanaz II. Torp-Falk 387.
ON vǫttr ‘glove’, LG want id. (fem.). Unclear. Torp-Falk 391 (to *wenđanan); Vries ANEW 676; Kluge- Seebold 875.
ON vappa ‘to waddle’, LG wappen ‘to move up and down’, ME wappen ‘to develop’. Of imitative origin. Martinet Gém. 170; Vries ANEW 644.
OSwed vappel ‘amount of slime’, OE wapol ‘foam’, OFris wapul- ‘pool, puddle’. Derived from *wappōjanan. Boutkan OFED s.v.
ON var-ligr ‘safe, requiring wariness’, OE wær-líc ‘cautious, prudent’, OHG gi-wara-līh ‘attentive, careful’. Derived from *waraz. Heidermanns 658.

Goth wars ‘wary, on guard’, ON varr ‘aware’, OE wær ‘aware, prepared’, OS war ‘aware, careful’, OHG giwar ‘aware’. Corresponds to Gk *Worow in ἐπίουρος ‘warder’, Hom οὖρος ‘watcher, warder’, OIr coair, coir ‘equitable, appropriate’ < *kom-⁄ari-. Further connected with Lat uereor ‘to respect’, Latv véru, vērt ‘to watch’. Karsten NB I 51; Torp- Falk 393; Feist 551; Holthausen AEEW 380; Wissmann Postv. 44–45; Walde- Hofmann II 757–758; Pokorny I 1164; Vries ANEW 647; Frisk I 539; Onions 992; Lehmann GED 395; Bammesberger Nom. 239; Heidermanns 657–658; Kluge- Seebold 321.
ON varä-hús ‘watchhouse’, MLG warde-hūs id., OHG wart-hūs id. Compound of *warđaz II and *xūsan. Carr Nom. comp. 85 (parallel formations).
ON varä-maär ‘watchman, warder’, OE weard-mann id., MLG wart-man id., OHG wart-man id. Compound of *warđaz II and *mannz. Carr Nom. comp. 79 (parallel formations).

ON á-varär ‘dear (to the gods)’, OE adv. for-weard ‘onwards, continually’, OFris bek-ward ‘back, rear’, OS adv. for-ward ‘forward, onwards’, OHG ab-wart ‘absent’. Derived from *werþanan. Structurally identical with Slav *vortъ ‘turn’. Holthausen AEEW 386; Kluge 777–778; Vasmer-Trubachev I 354; Heidermanns 658–659.

Goth daura-wards ‘doorkeeper’, ON vǫrär ‘warden, warder’ (ustem), OE weard ‘guard, warder’, OS ward id., OHG wart id. Participle *⁄or-tóderived from an unattested verb, cf. *waraz. Torp-Falk 393; Holthausen AEEW 386; Feist 117; Pokorny I 1164; Vries ANEW 675; Zalizniak II 204; Lehmann GED 394; Kluge-Seebold 875.

Goth fra-wardjan ‘to corrupt, to destroy’, OE wirdan ‘to injure, to hurt, to annoy’, OS a-werdian ‘to spoil, to destroy’, OHG fir-werten id. Derived from *werþanan. Cf. similar causatives in Skt vartáyati ‘to set in motion’, Slav *vortiti ‘to return’. Torp-Falk 398; Holthausen AEEW 394; Feist 166; Mayrhofer III 154–155; Fraenkel 1204; Seebold 560; Lehmann GED 127.

Goth daura-warda ‘female doorkeeper’, ON varäa ‘beacon, pile of stones used as a road sign’, OE weard ‘ward, guard, watch’, OS warda ‘guard, post’, OHG warta ‘watch, guard’. Related to *warđaz II. Torp-Falk 393; Holthausen AEEW 386; Feist 117; Vries ANEW 645; Onions 992; Lehmann GED 394.

ON varäa ‘to warrant, to guard’, OE weardian ‘to guard, to keep, to defend’, OFris wardia ‘to wait, to observe’, OS wardōn ‘to protect, to beware’, OHG wartēn ‘to wait, to stay awake, to peer’. Derived from *warđaz II or *warđō(n). Torp-Falk 393; Holthausen AEEW 386; Vries ANEW 645; Onions 992; Kluge-Seebold 875.
ON varg-tré ‘gallows’, OE wearz-treów id., OS warag-trēo id. Compound of *warzaz and *trewan. Carr Nom. comp. 53; Gamkrelidze- Ivanov II 487.

Goth launa-wargs ‘unthankful’, ON vargr ‘wolf, outlaw’, OE wearz ‘villain, felon, criminal’, OS warg ‘criminal’, OHG warg ‘evil-doer’. Identical with Lith vaıgas ‘need’, Latv vargs ‘miserable’, OPrus wargs ‘wicked’, Slav *vorgъ ‘enemy’ (Kauffmann PBB XVIII 175; Miklosich 395). Further related to *werzanan (Schrader-Nehring II 490–491). The original meaning was ‘strangler’, cf. similar development of words for ‘wolf’ (Ivanov SBJa Karp. 181–213); cf. also Phryg davo- ‘wolf’ < IE *dhau- ‘to squeeze, to strangle’ (Kretschmer Einleitung 211). Mikkola Wu S II 218 (same as Kauffmann); Zupitza Gutt. 16; Torp- Falk 396; Trautmann BSW 342; Holthausen AEEW 36; Feist 325; Pokorny I 1154; Fraenkel 1198–1199; Zalizniak I 137; Vries ANEW 645–646; Benveniste 135; Martynov Jazyk 34–35 (from Slav *vorgъ ‘enemy’); Vasmer-Trubachev I 352; Lehmann GED 153, 229 (to Lith veržiū, veıžti ‘to tie up’); Bammesberger Nom. 50; Orel Phrygian 29–30.
Goth wargiþa ‘judgment, condemnation’, OE wirgä, wirzäu ‘condemnation, curse’. Derived from *warzaz (Amira Recht II 134). Holthausen AEEW 394; Feist 551; Benveniste IEL 135; Lehmann GED 394.

Goth ga-wargjan ‘to outlaw, to condemn’, OE wirzan ‘to curse, to do evil’, OS gi-waragean ‘to punish (as a criminal)’. Derived from *warzaz. Structurally close to Slav *voržiti ‘to practise witchcraft’. Torp-Falk 397; Holthausen AEEW 394; Feist 210; Vasmer- Trubachev I 353; Benveniste IEL 135; Lehmann GED 153.
Goth warei ‘slyness, wariness, πανουργία’, OHG gi-werī ‘circumspection, attentiveness’. Derived from *waraz. Torp-Falk 393; Feist 551; Lehmann GED 395; Heidermanns 657.
OE masc. wermód ‘wormwood (bitter plant Artemisia Absinthium)’, OS wermōda id., OHG wermuota, werimuota id. Derived from *warjanan and *mōđō (see *mōđaz). Holthausen AEEW 391; Onions 1013.
ON ver ‘fishing-place’, OE wer ‘dam, weir’ (masc.), OS werr ‘dam for fishing’, OHG wer ‘dam, weir’. Derived from *warjanan. Torp-Falk 395; Holthausen AEEW 391; Vries ANEW 654; Lehmann GED 394.
ON var-nagli ‘bung for the hole in the boat’s bottom’, MHG wer-nagel ‘dagger handle’. Compound of *warjan and *nazlaz. Vries ANEW 646.

Goth warjan ‘to forbid, to hinder’, ON verja ‘to defend’, OE werian ‘to hinder, to check, to restrain’, OFris wera ‘to hinder, to forbid’, OS werian id., OHG werien, werren id. Related to Skt vṛṇóti ‘to cover, to surround’, Lat aperiō ‘to uncover’, Lith veriū, vérti ‘to open, to string, to thread’, Slav *vьrǫ, *verti ‘to lock, to stick’. Persson Beiträge 500; Torp-Falk 394; Trautmann BSW 351–352; Holthausen AEEW 391; Walde-Hofmann I 56–57; Feist 551– 552; Porzig Gliederung 155; Mayrhofer III 245–246; Pokorny I 1160; Fraenkel 1229–1230; Vries ANEW 656; Onions 998; Vasmer-Trubachev I 293; Lehmann GED 394; Kluge-Seebold 879.

Burg *warja ‘dweller’ (n-stem), ON pl. skip-verjar ‘crew’, OE pl. burh-ware ‘inhabitants of a city’. For HG cf. ethnonyms Angri-varii, Boju-varii. Derived from *warjanan. Torp-Falk 395; Holthausen AEEW 384; Vries ANEW 655; Köbler 714.

ON varmr ‘warm’, OE wearm id., OFris warm id., OS warm id., OHG warm id. Derivative of *⁄er-: Hitt war- ‘to burn’, Arm vafiem ‘to burn’, Lith vérdu, vīrti ‘to boil, to cook’, Slav *vьrîti ‘to boil’ (Fick BB II 203). Karsten NB II 245; Torp-Falk 395; Trautmann BSW 361; Holthausen AEEW 387; Benveniste BSL XXXIII 136–137; Pokorny I 1166; Chemodanov SGGJa I 83; Fraenkel 1263; Vries ANEW 646; Onions 992–993; Vasmer-Trubachev I 362; Schubert Erw. 75; Georgiev Lu E 200; Lehmann GED 394 (against the connection with IE *g ⁄herm- ‘warm’); Bammesberger Nom. 244; Heidermanns 659– 660; Kluge-Seebold 875.

Goth warmjan ‘to warm’, ON verma id., OE wirman id., OS warmian id., OHG ir-warmen id. Derived from *warmaz. Zupitza Gutt. 33; Holthausen AEEW 394; Feist 552; Vries ANEW 656; Onions 993; Lehmann GED 394–395; Heidermanns 660.
Burg warns ‘defence’, ON vǫrn ‘defense’, OE wearn ‘hindrance, obstacle’, OHG furi-warna ‘preparation’. Related to *warjanan. Holthausen AEEW 387; Vries ANEW 675; Köbler 714.

ON varna ‘to warn off, to abstain’, OE warenian, warnian ‘to take head, to beware’, OFris warna ‘to refuse, to deny’, MLG warnen ‘to make attentive’, OHG warnōn ‘to equip, to instruct’. Derived from *warnō. Torp- Falk 394; Holthausen AEEW 387; Vries ANEW 646; Heidermanns 658.

ON vǫr ‘place of landing’, OE waru ‘embankment’, MLG ware ‘dam’. Identical with Lith vãras ‘pole (in a fence)’, pa-varā ‘net rope’, Slav *vorъ, *vora ‘fence’ (Holthausen Zfslav Ph XXII 147). Torp-Falk 395; Trautmann BSW 352– 353; Holthausen AEEW 384; Fraenkel 1197–1198; Vries ANEW 674 (to Av vār ‘castle’, Slav *za-vorъ ‘bolt’).

ON vara ‘to beware, to warn’, OE warian ‘to beware, to guard’, OFris waria ‘to protect’, OS waron ‘to protect, to mark, to note’, OHG biwarōn ‘to protect’. Derived from *warō(n). Torp-Falk 393; Holthausen AEEW 38; Holthausen AEEW 384; Pokorny I 1164; Vries ANEW 645; Onions 992; Heidermanns 658; Kluge-Seebold 871.

ON vara ‘ware, article of merchandise’, masc. vari ‘wariness, caution’, OE waru ‘watchful care, care, observance’, OFris ware ‘care, attention; keeping, possession’, OS wara ‘care, attention’, OHG wara ‘attention, product, article’. Derived from *waraz. Similar to Gk *Worä on which ὀράω ‘to see’ is based. The reconstruction of a separate *wazō(n) ‘article of merchandise’ with further links to Hitt wašiƒa- ‘to buy’ can hardly be accepted. Persson UUÅ 1891 61; Wadstein Zd Ph XXVIII 529 (to Skt úrā ‘sheep’, Lat ueruex ‘wether’); Torp-Falk 393; Holthausen AEEW 384; Pokorny I 1164; Vries ANEW 645–646; Frisk II 409–410; Onions 992; Lehmann GED 395; Bammesberger Nom. 108; Kluge-Seebold 871, 8.

ON vari ‘liquid, water’, OE wær ‘sea’. Related to Toch A wär, B war ‘water’, Skt vāri ‘water, rain’, Av vār- ‘rain’, W gwer ‘tallow’. Cf. also *ūraz. Bartholomae 1410; Torp-Falk 395; Holthausen AEEW 380; Jóhannesson IEW 138 (to *warzuz); Mayrhofer III 194; Pokorny I 80; Vries ANEW 646; Dybo VSJa 9–23, BSA 33 (identical with Indo-Iranian *vār-, with shortening in an oxytonic stem); Adams TB 577– 578; Burlak IFTJa 256 (derives Toch from *⁄odōr).

ON varp ‘casting, cast (of a net)’, OE wearp ‘warp’, OS warp id., OHG warf id. Derived from *werpanan. Torp-Falk 398; Holthausen AEEW 387; Pokorny I 1153; Vries ANEW 647; Zalizniak I 144; Trubachev Slav. jaz. V 171, Rem. 111 (against direct comparison with Slav *vorba ‘reel’); Stang LS 64; Seebold 558; Kluge-Seebold 886.
ON verpill ‘die; barrel, cask’, OE wyrpel ‘strap on the falcon’s leg’, MLG worpel ‘die’, OHG wurfil id. Derived from *werpanan. Torp- Falk 398; Holthausen AEEW 412; Vries ANEW 657; Kluge-Seebold 899.

ON varta ‘wart’, OE weart, wearte id., OFris warte id., OS warta ‘nipple’, OHG warza, werza ‘wart, nipple’. Related to Latv ap-virde ‘abscess, boil’, Slav *verdъ ‘wound, harm’ (Schmidt Vok. II 74). Wood JEGP XIII 507 (to Lat uarix ‘dilated vein’, uerrūca ‘height, wart’); Torp-Falk 397; Holthausen AEEW 387; Pokorny I 1151; Vries ANEW 647; Zalizniak II 181; Onions 993; Vasmer- Trubachev I 295; Kluge-Seebold 876.
ON vǫrr ‘lip’. Identical with OPrus warsus id. Further related to *werō (Wood MLN XXIX 71). Torp-Falk 399; Pokorny I 1151; Chemodanov SGGJa I 73; Stang LS 61; Vries ANEW 676; Lehmann GED 390.
Swed vase ‘straw bundle’, ME wase ‘bundle’, MLG wase id. Derived from *wasjanan ~ *wazjanan. Cf. also ON neut. ver ‘case’ < *wazan. Torp-Falk 404.

Goth wasjan ‘to clothe’, ON verja id., OE werian id., OHG werien id. Related to IE *⁄es- ‘to clothe’: Hitt ⁄ašš- ‘to clothe’, ⁄ešš- ‘to be dressed’, Toch AB wäs- ‘to be dressed in, to wear’, Skt váste ‘to wear’, caus. vāsáyati, Av vastē id., Arm zgenum ‘to get dressed’, Gk Att đnnumi id. Bartholomae 1394; Torp-Falk 404; Holthausen AEEW 391; Feist 552–553; Porzig Gliederung 155; Mayrhofer III 175–176; Pokorny I 1172; Vries ANEW 655–656; Frisk I 521–522; Onions 996; Lehmann GED 395; Adams TB 597; Burlak IFTJa 256.

OE wæscan ‘to wash’, WFris waskje id., OS pret. sg. wōsk id., OHG wascan id. ON vaska id. < *waskōjanan might indicate the Proto-Gmc status of this verb, unless ON is from WGmc. Derived from *watnan ~ *watar. Torp- Falk 384; Holthausen AEEW 384; Krogmann NDJb LXXXI 13–21; Pokorny I 80; Onions 993; Seebold 539; Darms Vṛddhi 13–24 (from *m⁄6 dsko-, to IE *me⁄6- ‘to wash’); Kluge- Seebold 876.

Goth wasti ‘dress, clothing’. Identical with Gk γεστία: đndusiw (Hes.) and further Lat uestis ‘clothes, clothing’. Cf. *westrō. Derived from *wasjanan ~ *wazjanan. Torp-Falk 404; Walde- Hofmann II 775–776; Frisk I 521–522.

Goth wato ‘water’ (reflects *watōn and *watnan), ON vatn id., OE wæter id., OFris weter, water id., OS watar id., OHG wazzar id. An original r/n-heteroclysis. Related to Hitt ⁄adar id., Skt loc. udán(i) id., Arm get ‘river’, Gk ὕδωρ ‘water’, Phryg βέδυ id., Alb ujë id., Umbr u t u r, OIr uisce id., Lith vanduõ id., and probably Slav *voda id. (but see *wađan). Kuhn KZ I 373; Schmidt Plur. 109, 202–203; Torp-Falk 384; Trautmann BSW 337; Holthausen AEEW 381–382; Feist 553–554; Benveniste Origines 20; Detschew Thr. 46; Porzig Gliederung 144; Mayrhofer I 103–104; Pokorny I 78–80; Chemodanov SGGJa I 79; Vries ANEW 648; Zalizniak II 222; Fraenkel 1194–1195; Frisk II 957–959; Onions 994; Vasmer- Trubachev I 330; Lehmann GED 395– 396; Bammesberger Nom. 204–205; Kluge-Seebold 876; Orel AED 483–484.

ON vél ‘artifice, craft, defice, machine’, OFris wedel ‘frond’, OS wethil ‘fan’, OHG wadil, wadal, wedil id. Derived from *wējanan I (Sievers PBB XVI 244). Bugge KZ XX 30–31 (to Lat adūlō ‘to fawn upon’); Pokorny I 83; Vries ANEW 653; Kluge- Seebold 878.

ON vǫävi ‘muscle’, OS watho ‘calf ’, OHG wado, wad id. Related to Lat uātrax ‘with crooked feet, club-footed’ (Lidén KZ XLI 396). Torp- Falk 385 (to Umbr vatuva ‘part of a sacrificial animal (?)’); Pokorny I 1113; Vries ANEW 672; Kluge-Seebold 870.
ON verja ‘outer garment’, OHG gi-wera ‘clothing’. Derived from *wasjanan ~ *wazjanan. Vries ANEW 655.

ON vefa ‘to weave’, OE wefan id., MLG weven id., OHG weban id. From IE *⁄ebh-: Toch A wäp-, B wāp- id., Skt ubhnāti ‘to lace up, to cover’, Gk ὑφή ‘web’, Alb vej ‘to weave’ < PAlb *webnja. Torp-Falk 391; Holthausen AEEW 388; Jóhannesson IEW 130–131; Porzig Gliederung 178, 186; Mayrhofer I 107; Pokorny I 1114; Chemodanov SGGJa I 109; Vries ANEW 649–650; Zalizniak I 137; Frisk II 977; Onions 996–997; Seebold 540–541; Kluge-Seebold 877; Orel AED 498; Adams TB 586.

ON tord-yfill ‘dung-beetle’, OE wifel ‘beetle’, OS gold-wivil ‘glowworm’, OHG wibil ‘beetle, chafer’. Derived from *webanan. Cf. probably a similar derivative in Lith vãbalas ‘beetle’ (Brugmann IF XIII 158), Slav *vebьlica > Russ dial. veblica ‘worm’ (Zubaty Afslav Ph XVI 416). Torp-Falk 391 (to ON váfa ‘to soar’); Trautmann BSW 336; Holthausen AEEW 395; Chemodanov SGGJa I 74; Fraenkel 1176; Zalizniak I 137; Onions 998; Vasmer-Trubachev I 282; Kluge-Seebold 889.

ON í-viä-gjarn ‘wicked, evil’, OE in-wid ‘fraud, guile, deceit’, OS in-wid id., OHG in-wit ‘treachery’. Derived from *weđanan, cf. Goth in-widan ‘to deny (guilt)’ (Sturtevant JEGP XXXIII 89). OE in-widda ‘guileful, deceitful’ is derived from this word and is hardly related to *wenđaz II (despite Lehmann GED 207). Holthausen AWN 144 (from Lat inuidia ‘envy’); Feist 295; Vries ANEW 287.

Goth ga-widan ‘to bind’, OHG wetan id. Related to Hitt weda-, wete- ‘to build’, OIr air-com-fed- ‘to damage’ (Petersson IF XXIII 387; Feist 211–212). Torp-Falk 386; Feist 211– 212; Lagercrantz KZ XXXV 273 (to Gk ἐθμοί: δεσμοί. πλόκαμοι); Mayrhofer III 222; Pokorny I 1116–1117; Seebold 542; Lehmann GED 154; Kluge- Seebold 890.
ON veär-dagr ‘fine day’, OE weder-dæz id., MLG weder-dage id., MHG weter-tac id. Compound of *weđran and *đazaz. Carr Nom. comp. 53.
ON veär-leikr ‘weather condition’, Norw dial. ve-leik ‘flash of lightning’, MDu weder-lijc id., MHG weter-leich id. Derived from *weđran. Carr Nom. comp. 56.

ON veär ‘wind, weather’, OE weder id., OFris weder id., OS wedar id., OHG wetar ‘weather, air’. Identical with Slav *vedro ‘good weather’ (Schmidt Plur. 202). Brugmann IF XVIII 435 (to Slav *vedro or *vîtrъ ‘wind’); Torp-Falk 385; Holthausen AEEW 388; Pokorny I 82, 84; Vries ANEW 649 (alternatively, to Slav *vîtrъ ‘wind’); Zalizniak I 150; Onions 996; Vasmer-Trubachev I 284; Stang LS 61; Kluge-Seebold 887.

ON viära ‘to be such and such (of weather)’, OE wederian ‘to be (good or bad) weather’, MHG witeren id. Derived from *weđran. Hirt Ablaut 133 (to Skt api-vatáti ‘to understand’); Holthausen AEEW 393; Vries ANEW 660; Onions 996.
ON veptr ‘woof’, OE fem. wift ‘implement used in weaving’, MHG wift ‘thin thread’. Derived from *webanan. Torp-Falk 391; Holthausen AEEW 395; Onions 998; Seebold 541.
ON veg-farandi ‘wayfaring’, OE wez-farende id. Compound of *wezaz and *faranđz, participle of *faranan. Carr Nom. comp. 61; Onions 995.
ON vega-lauss ‘out of the way’, OE wez-leás ‘without a road, impassable’, OHG wega-lōs id. Compound of *wezaz and *lausaz. Carr Nom. comp. 61.

Goth ga-wigan ‘to move, to shake’, ON vega ‘to move, to carry, to lift, to weigh’, OE wezan ‘to move, to bear, to carry’, OFris pres. wega ‘to weigh, to bring’, OS wegan ‘to weigh’, OHG wegan ‘to move, to weigh’. Connected with Skt váhati ‘to drive, to ride’, Av 3 pl. vaz 6 nti id., Gk Pamph Wεχέτω, đxesfin: ërmasin Hes., Alb vjedh ‘to steal’, Lat uehō ‘to bear, to carry’, Lith vežū, vēžti ‘to carry (in a vehicle)’, Slav *vezǫ, *vezti ‘to carry, to drive’. Bartholomae 1386–1389; Torp-Falk 382–383; Trautmann BSW 356; Feist 212; Holthausen 388; Walde-Hofmann II 742–743; Porzig Gliederung 195; Jóhannesson IEW 125–128; Mayrhofer III 177–178; Pokorny I 1118–1120; Fraenkel 1236; Vries ANEW 650; Frisk I 604; Onions 998; Vasmer-Trubachev I 286; Seebold 542–544; Lehmann GED 154, 387; Kluge-Seebold 870; Orel AED 510.
ON veg-nest ‘provisions for a journey’, OE wez-nest id., OHG wega-nest id. Compound of *wezaz and *nestan. Carr Nom. comp. 53.

Goth wigs ‘way’, ON vegr ‘way, road’ (also an u-stem), OE wez ‘way’, OFris wei id., OS weg id., OHG weg id. Derived from *wezanan. Torp- Falk 382; Meillet Mélanges Andler 249–255; Holthausen AEEW 388; Feist 563; Pokorny I 1118–1120; Vries ANEW 650; Zalizniak II 204; Onions 995; Seebold 543; Lehmann GED 402– 403; Bammesberger Nom. 61–62; Kluge- Seebold 878.

ON vigg ‘horse’, OE wicz ‘steed’, OFris widze id., OS wigg id. Identical with Skt neut. vahyá- ‘stretcher, sofa’. Derived from *wezanan (Kahle IF XIV 170). Torp-Falk 383; Holthausen AEEW 392; Porzig PBB LVIII 89; Mayrhofer III 178; Pokorny I 1120; Vries ANEW 661; Bammesberger Nom. 66.

Goth waihts ‘thing’ (traces of a root stem in pl.), ON vættr ‘being’, OE wiht ‘creature, being, thing’, OS wiht ‘tutelary spirit, being, demon, thing’ (masc.), OHG wiht ‘being, thing’ (also neut.). Identical with Slav *vektь ‘thing’. Prusik KZ XXXV 597 (to IE *⁄ek⁄- ‘to speak’); Wood MLN XXIII 148 (to Lith veikūs ‘rapid’); Hirt PBB XXIII 337 (Slav < Gmc); Loewenthal ANF XXXV 229 (to IE *⁄e˚- ‘to wish’); Torp-Falk 381–382; Holthausen PBB XLVI 146 (to Lat uegeō ‘to be lively’), AEW 395–396; Feist 543; Pokorny I 1136; Vries ANEW 672; Zalizniak II 211; Onions 1006; Vasmer-Trubachev I 308; Stang LS 63; Lehmann GED 388–389; Bammesberger Nom. 143 (combines *wextiz I and II); Kluge-Seebold 888.

ON vætt ‘weight’, OE wiht id., OFris wicht id., MDu wicht, wichte id. Derived from *wezanan. Close to Lat masc. uectis ‘lever’. Torp-Falk 382– 383; Walde-Hofmann II 741; Chemodanov SGGJa I 88; Vries ANEW 672; Onions 998; Seebold 543; Kluge- Seebold 322.
Goth waila-deþs ‘good deed’, OE wel-dæd id., OHG wola-tāt id. Compound of *welō(n) ~ *walō(n) and *đēđiz. Feist 543; Lehmann GED 136.
ON vil ‘self-will’, OE will ‘will’, OS will id. Related to *wel(j)anan. Holthausen AEEW 396; Vries ANEW 663; Onions 1006.

OE weliz ‘willow’. Cf. also OS wilgia id. < *wel(i)zjōn. Close to Gk ἵλίκη id., further to ἑλιξ ‘twisted, curved’ < *⁄elik-. Holthausen AEEW 389; Pokorny I 1141; Chemodanov SGGJa I 107; Frisk I 494–496; Onions 1007.

Goth wiljan ‘to will’, ON vilja id. (irreg.), OE pres. wile id., OFris pres. wel, weli id., OS pres. wili id., OHG pres. wili id. All paradigms are irregular. Originally, belonged to mi-conjugation. Related to Skt vṛṇāti ‘to choose, to select, to prefer’, Av var- ‘to choose, to select’, Lat uolō ‘to want’, Lith vélmi, vélti id., Slav *velîti ‘to order’. Helten PBB XXXV 297–299; Zupitza Gutt. 33; Bartholomae 1360–1362; Torp-Falk 399–400; Trautmann BSW 348; Feist 563–564; Holthausen AEEW 396; Walde-Hofmann II 828–830; Prokosch CGG 224–225; Mezger KZ LXXV 225–233; Jóhannesson IEW 158–159; Mayrhofer III 244–245; Pokorny I 1137; Fraenkel 1220; Vries ANEW 663; Krause HG 251; Onions 1006–1007; Vasmer-Trubachev I 288; Seebold 551; Lehmann GED 403; Kluge-Seebold 897.

Goth wilja ‘will’, ON vili id., OE willa id., OFris willa id., OS willio id., OHG willo ‘desire, wish’. Derived from *wel(j)anan. Steinthal KZ I 570; Mohr ZDADL LXXVI 153 (ON < WGmc); Torp-Falk 400; Holthausen AEEW 396; Feist 563; Pokorny I 1137; Vries ANEW 663; Zalizniak I 155; Onions 1006; Seebold 551; Lehmann GED 403; Bammesberger Nom. 182; Kluge-Seebold 891.

MLG welk ‘flabby, arid’, OHG welc, welh ‘wet, soft’. Identical with Latv velgs ‘wetness’, further cf. Lith vīlgau, vīlgyti ‘to moisten’, Slav *volga ‘liquid’. Zupitza Gutt. 169; Torp-Falk 402; Trautmann BSW 358–359; Pokorny I 1145–1146; Fraenkel 1251; Vasmer- Trubachev I 340.

OSwed vælla ‘to roll’, OHG wellan id. Related to Skt válate ‘to turn, to move’, Gk εφιλέω ‘to shut in, to hinder, to press’, Lith veliū, vélti ‘to roll’, Slav *valiti ‘to make fall’. Persson Beiträge 539, 543–544; Trautmann BSW 349; Torp-Falk 400; Pokorny I 1141; Fraenkel 1221; Frisk I 456–457; Seebold 553; Kluge-Seebold 884.

ON vella ‘to boil’, OE weallan ‘to well, to bubble forth’, OFris bi-wellen ‘to stain’, OS bi-wellan id., OHG bi-wellan ‘to stain, to disgrace’. Probably, historically identical with *wellanan I. Torp-Falk 401; Holthausen AEEW 386; Jóhannesson IEW 160–164; Pokorny I 1140–1144; Vries ANEW 653; Onions 999; Seebold 538–539, 553; Kluge-Seebold 873 (to Lith vilnīs ‘wave’).

ON vella ‘seething, a seething mass’, OE wiella ‘well, spring, fountain’, OFris walla ‘seething, spring’, MLG welle id., OHG wella ‘wave, stream’. Derived from *wellanan II. Holthausen AEEW 393; Onions 999; Stang LS 63–64 (to Lith vilnīs ‘wave’, Slav *vьlna id.); Lehmann GED 411 (to *wellanan I).

Goth waila ‘well’, ON vel, val id., OE wel id., OFris wel id., OS wela, wala id., OHG wola, wela, wala id. Derived from *wel(j)anan. Cf. comp. *welizōn ~ *walizōn > Goth walisa ‘beloved, genuine’, ON comp. vildri, vildari ‘more pleasant, better’, OHG adv. *waliro ‘in a more excellent way’. Karsten NB II 183–184 (comp. < *⁄eltƒo-); Brugmann IF XV 99–100 (to Skt vélā ‘limit, boundary, end’); Persson Beiträge 514–516 (to Skt véti ‘to have in view, to approach’); Loewe PBB LI 254–256; Streitberg GE 69; Torp-Falk 399–400; Holthausen AEEW 388; Feist 543, 548; Pokorny I 1137; Vries ANEW 652, 663; Onions 999; Lehmann GED 389, 392; Bammesberger Btrg. Gesch. CII 1–4 (Goth -s- < -z- according to Thurneysen’s rule); Heidermanns 650, 667; Kluge-Seebold 896.

ON velta ‘to roll’, OHG walzan id. Related to *wellanan I. Torp-Falk 403 (to OIr fillim ‘flecto’ ? < *⁄eld-); Jóhannesson IEW 160–164; Pokorny I 1140–1144; Vries ANEW 653; Seebold 554; Kluge- Seebold 874 (to Skt válate ‘to turn, to move’, Slav *valiti ‘to make fall’).
ON villi-bráä ‘venison’, MLG neut. wilt-brāt id., MHG neut. wilt-brāt id. Compound of *welþjaz and *brēđō(n). Carr Nom. comp. 87 (parallel formations); Kluge-Seebold 890.
ON villi-dÿr ‘wild animal’, OE wild-déor id., late OHG wildtior id. Compound of *welþjaz and *đeuzan. Carr Nom. comp. 87 (parallel formations).

Goth wilþeis ‘wild’, ON villr ‘wild, bewildered’, OE wilde ‘wild’, OFris wilde id., OS wildi id., OHG wildi ‘wild, savage’. Related to W gwyllt ‘untamed’, Corn gwyls id. < *⁄el-ti- or *⁄el-to-. Bugge KZ XX (1872) 27 (to OBakt vareta ‘mad’); Zupitza Gutt. 17; Torp-Falk 403–404; Lidén BB XXI 106–107 (to Russ vil’at’ ‘to wag’); Ehrlich apud Feist (to Gk θέλγω ‘to enchant, to bewitch’, Lith žvelgiū, žvefigti ‘to look’); Fay JEGP XVII 423 (to *walþuz); Feist 564; Holthausen AEEW 396; Pokorny I 1139–1140; Trier Venus 48–50; Vries ANEW 664 (against Fay); Onions 1006; Lehmann GED 403–404; Heidermanns 668–669; Kluge-Seebold 890.
Goth wilwan ‘to rob’. Identical with Lat uellō ‘to pluck, to pull’. Torp-Falk 402; Walde-Hofmann II 744–745 (Lat uellō < *⁄elsō or *⁄eldō); Pokorny I 1145.
ON vimpill ‘a kind of hood, veil’, OE wimpel ‘article of woman’s dresss’, OFris wimpel ‘veil’, OS wimpal id., OHG wimpal id. Of unknown origin. Holthausen AEEW 396; Onions 1007.

Goth us-windan ‘to twine, to plait’, ON vinda ‘to wring, to twist’, OE windan ‘to twist, to roll’, OFris winda id., OS windan id., OHG wintan id. Related to Toch AB wänt- ‘to cover, to envelop’, Umbr imper. aha-uendu ‘avertito’. Torp-Falk 390; Osthoff Zd Ph XXIV 215 (to IE *⁄ēi-); Nehring IF IV 401 (to Slav *vęzati ‘to tie’); Feist 98; Holthausen AEEW 397; Jóhannesson IEW 134–137; Pokorny I 1116–1117; Solta Arm. 142–143; Vries ANEW 665; Onions 1008; Seebold 554–556; Lehmann GED 74; Kluge-Seebold 892; Adams TB 592.

Goth winds ‘wind’, ON vindr id. (partly from *wenđiz), OE wind id., OFris wind id., OS wind id., OHG wint id. Corresponds to Hitt ḫu⁄ant- id., Toch A want, B yente id., Lat uentus id., W gwynt id. Derived from *wējanan. Torp- Falk 378; Holthausen AEEW 397; Walde-Hofmann II 751–752; Feist 565; Pokorny I 82 (reconstructs *ē in *⁄ēntos based on part. *⁄e-nt- ‘blowing’); Porzig Gliederung 187, 197; Jóhannesson IEW 107–108; Kammenhuber KZ LXXVII 67–68; Vries ANEW 665; Zalizniak II 191; Onions 1007; Seebold 540; Tischler HEG I 328–331; Lehmann GED 404; Puhvel III 428–429; Bammesberger Nom. 93–94; Kluge-Seebold 892; Adams TB 505.

Goth in-winds ‘unjust, wicked, wrong’, ON vindr ‘awry’. Derived from *wenđanan. Karsten NB I 27; Meringer IF XVIII 295 (on the prefix in- in Goth); Torp-Falk 390; Weisweiler IF XLI 350–351; Holthausen AEEW 189; Feist 296; Vries ANEW 665; Seebold 555; Lehmann GED 207; Lühr Expr. 607; Bammesberger Nom. 241; Heidermanns 669.
ON pl. vindingar ‘windings’, Langob wintingas id., OS winding ‘band’, OHG winting id. Derived from *wenđanan. Vries ANEW 665.
ON vinda ‘bank of yarn’, MLG winde ‘winding device’, OHG winta id. Derived from *wenđanan. Vries ANEW 664.

ON vindr ‘Slav, Western Slav’, OE pl. Winedas id., OHG pl. Winida id. Cf. also Lat Venedī, Venethī. Any connection with *weniz? Schrader- Nehring II 415 (to Slav *vęti‘i, the name of an East Slavic tribe); Holthausen AEEW 397; Vries ANEW 665; Onions 1000.

ON vinstri ‘left’ (< *wenistrōn), OE winestra id., OFris winister id., OS winistar id., OHG winistar id. Derived from *weniz as a taboo motivated name of the ‘friendly’ left side (Hoops EG V 88). For the suffix cf. Gk ἀρίστερος ‘left’, Lat sinister id. Torp-Falk 387; Holthausen AEEW 398; Huisman KZ LXXI 105 (to Icel vinstur, Gk ἤνυστρον ‘fourth stomach of ruminating animals’); Vries ANEW 666–667; Lehmann GED 363.

ON vinr ‘friend’, OE wine id., OFris wine id., OS wini id., OHG wini id. Related to Toch A wañi, wīna ‘pleasure’, Skt vanóti ‘to demand, to like’, Lat uenus ‘love’ and other continuants of IE *⁄en- ‘to wish, to love’, cf. *wunēnan. Particularly close to OIr fine ‘family, kin’ < *⁄enƒā (Zimmer KZ XXIV 212). Torp- Falk 387; Holthausen AEEW 397; Walde-Hofmann II 752–753; Mayrhofer III 141–142; Pokorny I 1146– 1147; Vries ANEW 666; Zalizniak I 149; Lehmann GED 379; Bammesberger Nom. 136; Adams TB 601–602.

Goth winja ‘pasture’, ON vin ‘meadow, pasture’, MLG winne ‘pasture’, OHG winne id. Derivationally identical with OIr fine ‘kinship, family’. Related to *weniz (Lidén BSB 27; Trier Venus 117–118). Torp-Falk 387; Feist 565–566; Pokorny I 1147; Vries ANEW 664; Zalizniak I 149; Lehmann GED 404; Kluge-Seebold 897.
OHG winken ‘to move sidewards, to stagger, to wink’. Close to Skt váṅgati ‘to walk’, Lith véngiu, véngti ‘to avoid’. Torp-Falk 389; Mayrhofer III 124; Pokorny I 1148–1149; Fraenkel 1223, 1256–1257.

Goth winnan ‘to suffer’, ON vinna ‘to labor, to work, to win’, OE winnan ‘to labor, to toil’, OFris winna ‘to gain’, OS winnan ‘to work, to battle’, OHG winnan ‘to suffer, to work’. Related to Hitt ⁄en- ‘futuere’, Skt vanóti ‘to demand, to strive for, to obtain’, Lat uenus ‘love’. See *weniz. Nordling SNF XX/6 21 (from *⁄en⁄-); Torp-Falk 388; Holthausen AEEW 398; Meringer IF XVI 180–181; Walde-Hofmann II 752–753; Feist 566; Jóhannesson IEW 132–133; Mayrhofer III 141–142; Pokorny I 404; Trier Venus passim; Vries ANEW 666; Onions 1007; Seebold 556–557; Lehmann GED 404; Kluge-Seebold 322.

Goth winno ‘suffering’, winna ‘passion, suffering’, ON vinna ‘work, labor’, OE mód-ze-winna ‘care, anxiety’ (masc.), OHG winna ‘quarrel’. Derived from *wennanan. Torp-Falk 388; Feist 566; Vries ANEW 666; Seebold 557; Lehmann GED 404.

Goth wintrus ‘winter’, ON vetr id. (pl. from a root stem), OE winter ‘winter, year’, OFris winter ‘winter’, OS wintar id. (< *wentraz), OHG wintar id. (< *wentraz). Unclear. Bugge KZ XIX (1870) 434 (to IE *·heim- ‘winter’); Lidén PBB XV 522 (to *watnan ~ *watar; ‘winter as a wet season’); Uhlenbeck PBB XXX 326 (to OIr find ‘white’); Petersson SVSL I 14; Wood Language VIII 214 (to *wenđaz I); Torp-Falk 384; Holthausen AEEW 398; Feist 566; Pokorny I 78–79; Vries ANEW 658; Zalizniak II 212; Krause HG 161 (same as Uhlenbeck); Onions 1008; Mathiassen NTS XXII 91–98 (to Slav *vędnǫti ‘to wane’); Lehmann GED 404–405; Bammesberger Nom. 161; Kluge-Seebold 893.
Goth dis-winþjan ‘to crush’, OE windwian ‘to winnow’, OHG wintōn id. Related to *wenđaz I. Holthausen AEEW 397; Feist 121; Lehmann GED 92.

Goth ana-wairþs ‘future’, ON ofan-verär ‘upper, uppermost’, OFris adv. ūta-werdes ‘outwards’, OS adv. forwerd ‘forwards, henceforth’, OHG abwert ‘absent’. Derived from *werþanan. Torp-Falk 398; Feist 45–46, 181; Vries ANEW 655; Seebold 560; Lehmann GED 152–153; Heidermanns 672–673; Kluge-Seebold 875–876.
ON au-visli ‘damage, hurt, injury’, OE æf-werdelsa ‘famage, detriment, loss’ (with metathesis), OHG wertisala ‘corruption’. See *warđjanan. Falk ANF V 120; Vries ANEW 21.
Goth ga-ga-wairþjan ‘to reconcile’, OHG ant-wirten ‘to address’. Derived from *werđaz. Feist 181; Heidermanns 673.

Goth wairdus ‘host’, OFris hūs-werda ‘master of the house’ (n-stem), OS werd id. (a-stem), OHG wirt id. Probably derived from *warjanan. Grienberger Got. 236 (to Lith veriū, vérti ‘to open’); Messing Neophilologus II 280–282 (to *werþaz); Schrader Idg. (from *⁄er-tu- ‘protection’); Torp-Falk 394; Feist 544–545 (to IE *⁄er- ‘to be attentive’); Porzig Gliederung 133; Pokorny I 1161, 1165–1166 (to *⁄er- ‘to be friendly’); Zalizniak II 204; Seebold 475 (to OIr ferthigis ‘steward’); Lehmann GED 390; Bammesberger Nom. 158; Kluge-Seebold 894.

MHG er-wergen ‘to strangle’. Identical with Alb z-vjerdh ‘to wean’, Lith veržiū, veıžti ‘to squeeze, to bind’, Slav *vьrzǫ, *verzti ‘to bind’. Torp-Falk 396; Trautmann BSW 355; Holthausen AEEW 411; Pokorny I 1120–1121; Fraenkel 1230–1231; Onions 1013; Vasmer-Trubachev I 298; Orel AED 527; Kluge-Seebold 899.

ON virgill ‘halter’, OS wurgil ‘cord, rope’. Derived from *werzanan (Persson Beiträge 506). Torp-Falk 397; Vries ANEW 667; Puhvel III 401 (to Hitt ḫurkil- ‘severe sexual offence’ < *‘hanging matter’); Kluge- Seebold 899.

Goth wairilo ‘lip’, OE weler id. (with metathesis). Derived from *werō. Wood MLN XXIX 71 (to Lat uarus ‘facial eczema’); Lewy IF XXXII 165 (to Slav *vys-okъ ‘high’); Holthausen AEEW 389; Feist 545; Lehmann GED 390.
ON verk-dagr ‘workday’, OE weorc-dæz id., MLG werk-dag id., MHG werk-tac id. Compound of *werkan and *đazaz. Carr Nom. comp. 79 (parallel formations).
ON verk-hús ‘workshop, officina’, OE weorc-hús ‘workshop’, MDu werc-huus id., OHG werc-hūs id. Compound of *werkan and *xūsan. Carr Nom. comp. 79 (parallel formations).
ON verk-maär ‘workman, laborer’, OE weorc-mann id., MLG werk-man id., MHG werc-man id. Compound of *werkan and *mannz. Carr Nom. comp. 79 (parallel formations); Onions 1012.

ON verk ‘work’, OE weorc id., OFris werk id., OS werk id., OHG werah, werc id. Etymologically related to Gk đrgon id. and Av var 6 za- id. continuing IE *⁄ερἵ- ‘to work’. Bartholomae 1378; Torp-Falk 396; Holthausen AEEW 390; Mladenov 667 (to Slav *vьrgǫ, *vergti ‘to throw’); Kuryłowicz Apophonie 151 (Vṛddhi formation); Pokorny I 1168; Vries ANEW 656; Zalizniak I 144; Frisk I 548–549; Onions 1012; Anttila Schw. 157; Bammesberger Nom. 61; Kluge-Seebold 886.
ON virki ‘work’, OS gi-wirki id. Derived from *werkan. Vries ANEW 667.
ON virkja ‘to pain’, OE wyrcan ‘to work, to labor’. Derived from *werkan. Holthausen AEEW 394; Vries ANEW 667.
ON verka ‘to work’, OFris werkia id., MLG werken id., OHG wercōn ‘to build, to work’. Derived from *werkan. Vries ANEW 656.
ON verki ‘doer, offender’, MHG ge-werke ‘comrade’. Derived from *werkan. Vries ANEW 656.

OFris were ‘lip’. Related to Lat uarus ‘pimple’, Lith vīras ‘pustule on pigs’ (Bezzenberger-Fick BB VI 240; Wood MLN XXIX 71), Toch B weru ‘blister’. Cf. *warzuz. Torp-Falk 399; Trautmann BSW 360; Walde-Hofmann II 734; Feist 545 (contra Wood); Pokorny I 1151–1152; Fraenkel 1258–1259; Vries ANEW 676 (to *warjanan); Lehmann GED 390; Adams TB 609.

Goth wairpan ‘to cast, to throw’, ON verpa id., OE weorpan id., OFris werpa id., OS werpan id., OHG werfan id. Related to Lith vīrbu, virbėti ‘to shiver, to move’. Diefenbach VW I 201 (to Lith verpiū, veıpti ‘to spin’); Zupitza Gutt. 30; Potebnia RFV IV 164 (to Slav *vorpъ ‘raid’); Persson Beiträge 502–503; Kluge PBB IX 193 (to Skt vṛṇákti ‘to turn, to twist’); Meyer KZ VI 176 (to Gk =άπτω ‘to throw’); Zupitza Gutt. 30; Torp-Falk 398; Feist 545; Holthausen AEEW 390; Kluge 855, PBB IX 193–196; Jóhannesson IEW 141–149; Pokorny I 1153 (to IE *⁄er- ‘to turn’); Fraenkel 1259; Vries ANEW 656–657; Onions 993; Seebold 557–559; Lehmann GED 390; Kluge-Seebold 886.

ON litverpr ‘one quickly changing color’, OE zeweorp ‘throwing, tossing’, OFris werp ‘throw’, MDu werp id., OHG gi-werf ‘contribution’. Derived from *werpanan. Holthausen AEEW 390; Vries ANEW 657; Seebold 558.

Goth wairs ‘worse’, ON verr id., OE wirs id., OS wirs id., MHG wirs id. Close to OIr ferr ‘better’, further to Skt várßīyas- ‘higher’, Lith viršūs ‘top, peak’, Slav *vьrxъ ‘top’. Comp. *wersizōn ~ *werzizōn > Goth wairsiza ‘worse’, ON verri id., OE wirsa id., OFris wirra id., OS wirsa id., OHG wirsiro id. Superl. *wersistaz ~ *wersistōn (ON verstr ‘worst’, OE wirrest id., OFris wersta id., OS wirsista id., OHG wirsisto id.) is structurally identical with Skt várßißtha- ‘the highest’. Grimm DG III 658 (to Slav *gorьjь ‘worse’); Diefenbach VW I 191 (same as Grimm); Osthoff Morph. Unt. VI 289–291 (to *wersan ‘confusion’); Torp- Falk 398–399 (to OS werran ‘to entangle’, OHG werran ‘to confuse’ < *werzanan); Uhlenbeck PBB XXX 323; Torp-Falk 399; Trautmann BSW 362; Holthausen AEEW 394; Feist 546; Sturtevant AJPhil LVI 141; Mayrhofer III 160–161; Pokorny I 1151–1152; Fraenkel 1262–1263; Vries ANEW 657; Onions 1013; Vasmer-Trubachev I 301–302; Lehmann GED 391; Bammesberger Nom. 236; Heidermanns 675–676.
ON virtr ‘sweet-wort, new beer’, OHG wirz ‘wort’. An ablaut variant of *wurtiz. Holthausen AEEW 412; Vries ANEW 668.
ON verä-ligr ‘valuable’, OE weorä-líc id., OHG werd-līh ‘dignified’. Derived from *werþaz. Heidermanns 674.

Goth wairþ ‘price’ (neut. or masc.), ON verä ‘worth, price’, OE weorä ‘worth, value’, OFris werth id., OS werth ‘payment, money’, OHG werd ‘worth’. Substantivized *werþaz. Holthausen AEEW 390; Feist 546; Pokorny I 1157; Zalizniak II 173; Lehmann GED 391; Heidermanns 674; Kluge-Seebold 886.

Goth wairþan ‘to become’, ON veräa id., OE weoräan id., OFris wertha id., OS weräan id., OHG werdan id. Related to Toch A wart- ‘to throw’, Skt vártate ‘to turn, to roll’, Av var 6 t- ‘to turn’, Lat uertō id., OIr ad-ferta ‘aversatur’, Lith ver‘iū, veısti ‘to turn, to spin’, Slav *vьrtjǫ, *vьrtîti id.
