Bartholomae 1368; Torp-Falk 397–398; Holthausen AEEW 390; Walde- Hofmann II 763–765; Feist 547; Jóhannesson IEW 141–149; Mayrhofer III 154–155; Pokorny I 1156–1157; Fraenkel 1228–1229; Vries ANEW 655; Onions 1013; Vasmer-Trubachev I 301; Seebold 559–560; Lehmann GED 391; Kluge-Seebold 885.

Goth wairþs ‘worth, qualified’, ON verär ‘worth’, OE weorä id., OFris werth id., OS werth id., OHG werd ‘worth, dignified’. Derived from *werþanan (Jóhannesson IEW 145). Structurally close to W gwerth ‘price’. Bartholomae 176 (to Av a-var 6 tā ‘thing of value’); Arbois de Jubainville MSL VII 294 (from Celtic); Karsten NB I 325; Brugmann IF XIII 88 (to *werđaz); Arbois de Jubainville MSL VII 294 (from Celt *wertas > W gwerth ‘price’); Torp-Falk 394; Feist 547; Wissmann Postv. 77; Holthausen AEEW 390; Pokorny I 1157; Vries ANEW 655; Onions 1013; Lehmann GED 391; Bammesberger Nom. 241; Heidermanns 674–675.
Goth wairþida ‘worthiness’, OHG wirdida ‘dignity’. Derived from *werþjanan. Feist 547; Lehmann GED 391; Heidermanns 674.
ON viräa ‘to estimate, to rate, to tax’, OHG wirden ‘to revere, to praise’. Derived from *werþan. Torp- Falk 394; Vries ANEW 667; Heidermanns 675.

Goth wairþon ‘to estimate, to price’, OE weoräian ‘to set a value, to honor’, OS gi-werthon ‘to fulfill’, OHG werdōn ‘to value, to revere’. Derived from *werþan, *werþaz. Torp-Falk 394; Holthausen AEEW 390; Feist 547; Vries ANEW 655; Lehmann GED 391; Heidermanns 675.

ON vesall ‘bereft, poor, destitute’, MHG wesel ‘weak’. Related to Latv vęsęls ‘healthy, whole, unharmed’, Slav *veselъ ‘merry’. For the semantic development (suggesting taboo in Gmc) cf. *đapraz. Torp-Falk 379, 413 (to *wīsanan); Weisweiler IF XLI 332–333; Holthausen AEEW 399; Pokorny I 1123; Jóhannesson IEW 111; Krahe IF LVII 113 (adds Illyr prop. Veselia Felicetas); Mezger KZ LXXXIII 124–149, LXXXV 296 (a compound with the second element close to MHG sal, *saljanan); Vries ANEW 657; Vasmer-Trubachev I 303; Heidermanns 680–681.

Goth wisan ‘to be, to exist, to remain’, ON vesa, vera ‘to be, to exist’, OE wesan id., OFris wesa id., OS wesan id., OHG wesan id. Related to Hitt ḫ⁄eš- ‘to live, to be alive’, Toch B wäs- ‘to dwell, to abide’, Skt vásati ‘to dwell, to live, to stay’, Av van˘ haiti ‘to pause’, Arm gom ‘to be’, Gk aor. ἄ-εσα ‘to remain’, MIr fóaid ‘to remain, to stay’ (Meyer KZ XXII 530–532). The paradigm of this word is suppletive and highly regular. Present is based on IE *es- ‘to be’: 1 sg. *esmi (Goth im, ON em, OE eom); 3 pl. *senđi (Goth sind, OE sind, OFris sind, OS sind, OHG sint) identical with Hitt ašanzi, Skt sánti, Av h 6 nti, Gk Dor ἐντί, Lat sunt, Slav *sǫtь. Brugmann KVG 495; Zupitza Gutt. 34; Bartholomae 1394; Torp-Falk 405; Feist 567–568; Holthausen AEEW 391; Walde-Hofmann II 628–629; Jóhannesson IEW 165–166; Mayrhofer III 171–172; Pokorny I 1170–1171; Vries ANEW 657; Frisk I 25, 463–464; Seebold 561–562; Lehmann GED 304, 405–406; Puhvel III 420–429; Kluge- Seebold 886–887; Adams TB 597–598; Burlak IFTJa 256.

Goth wisan ‘to feast’, OHG fir-wesen ‘to spend’. Related to Hitt ⁄ešiƒa- ‘to graze’, Av vāstr 6 m ‘meadow, grass’, Lat uēscor ‘to live on’, OIr feis id. Wood Postcons. 54 (together with Lat uēscor < *gh⁄ēs-); Bartholomae 1414; Torp-Falk 405; Walde-Hofmann II 769; Feist 568; Pokorny I 1171; Rosén Festschrift Collinder 378–387 (identical with *wesanan ‘to be’); Seebold 562–563; Lehmann GED 406.
ON vesl ‘a kind of cloak’. Related to *wasjanan ~ *wazjanan. Structurally close to Toch A wsāl ‘garment’. Torp-Falk 404; Pokorny I 1172; Vries ANEW 657 (< *was(t)ilan); Lehmann GED 395.
ON vera ‘dwelling’, OE ze-wesa ‘support’. Derived from *wesanan I. Holthausen AEEW 391; Vries ANEW 654.

OE adv. west ‘West, westwards’, OFris west ‘West’, MLG west id., OHG adv. nord-west ‘in the north-west’. Continues *⁄es-to- close in derivation and original meaning to Skt vástu- ‘spending the night’, MW gwest ‘place of rest’, further to *⁄es- (see *wesanan I). Brugmann IF XIII 157–160 (identifies *⁄es- of *westan and of *⁄esperos); Kretschmer Glotta XXVII 232 (connected with wisi- in Uisigothae); Torp-Falk 405; Trautmann BSW 348; Schröder GRM XVII 421–427 (to Gk ὕστερος); Holthausen AEEW 391; Pokorny I 73 (to Skt avá˛ ‘downwards’), 1173–1174; Vries ANEW 658; Kluge-Seebold 887.

ON vestan ‘from the West’, OE westan id., OS westan, westana id., OHG sbst. westan ‘West’. Derived from *westan. For the formation, cf. Lith ūkanas ‘cloudy’ vs. vãkaras ‘evening’. Torp- Falk 405–406; Holthausen AEEW 392; Fraenkel 1159, 1186; Vries ANEW 658; Kluge-Seebold 887. *westiz I sb.f.: Goth wists ‘essence, nature’, ON vist ‘abode, dwelling, domicile’, OE æt-wist ‘substance, existence’, OHG wist ‘substance’. Identical with OIr feiss ‘staying, resting’ < *⁄esti-. Derived from *wesanan I. Torp-Falk 405; Feist 568; Pokorny I 1171; Vries ANEW 669; Zalizniak I 151; Lehmann GED 405; Seebold 561; Bammesberger Nom. 143.

ON vist ‘food, provisions’, OE wist id., OS wist id., OHG wist id. Derived from *wesanan II. Cf. in particular OIr feis ‘food’ < *⁄es-t-ā. Holthausen AEEW 400; Vries ANEW 669 (to Lat uēscor ‘to eat’); Seebold 562; Lehmann GED 406.
MHG wester ‘baptismal dress’. Identical with Skt neut. vástra- ‘garment’, Gk γέστρα (sc. Wέστρα): đndusiw, στολή, flmãtia (Hes.). Further connected with *wasjanan ~ *wazjanan. Frisk I 521.
Burg sbst. *wistr ‘West’, ON adv. vestr ‘westwards’, OS adv. westar id., OFris adv. wester id., OHG westar ‘western’. Derived from *westan. Torp- Falk 405; Köbler 715.
ON vestrœnn ‘western’, OE westerne id., OS westrōni id., OHG westrōni id. Derived from *westraz. Holthausen AEEW 392; Vries ANEW 658.

ON hreysi-visla ‘weasel’, OE wesle id., MLG wesele id., OHG wisula, wisala id. Somehow may be connected with a difficult Gk αἰέλουρος id. < *a- Wisel- (Schrader KZ X 462) or, more probably, to *wesalaz (for the semantic development cf. Slav *laska ‘caress, weasel’). Riegler Wu S IV 420 (to Lat uīsiō ‘seeing, vision’); Wiedemann BB XXVII 207 (to Slav *veselъ ‘merry’); Suolahti Germanica 112 (derived from OHG uuiessa ‘polecat’); Torp-Falk 413; Holthausen AEEW 390; Machek Zfsl Ph XXIII 121 (to a mistakenly reconstructed Slav *vьlasъka ‘Mustela vulgaris’); Pokorny I 1141; Vries ANEW 255; Frisk I 36; Onions 996; Kluge-Seebold 890.

Goth wit ‘we (two)’, ON vit id., OE wit id., OS wit id. Identical with Lith vēdu id. < *⁄e-d⁄ō (Grimm DGn 343). Cf. further Skt gen.-dat.-acc. vām ‘both of us’, Av acc. vā ‘we two’, Slav *vî id. Meillet MSL XII 226–228, XIII 208– 209; Streitberg GE 60; Bartholomae 1310–1312; Torp-Falk 406; Holthausen AEEW 400; Prokosch CGG 284; Feist 569; Mayrhofer III 188; Pokorny I 1114; Fraenkel 1212; Vries ANEW 669; Onions 996; Lehmann GED 406.

Goth wiþrus ‘lamb’, ON veär ‘wether’ (traces of ustem), OE weäer id., NFris wether id., OS wethar id., OHG widar id. Like many other words for (young) small cattle (Skt vatsá- ‘calf’, Alb viç id. < PAlb *wetuša, Lat uitulus id.), derived from *⁄et- ‘year’: Hitt ⁄itt-, Gk đtow, Alb vit, vjet (Johansson UUÅ 1927/1 85). Torp-Falk 385; Holthausen AEEW 392; Walde- Hofmann II 807–808; Feist 571; Mayrhofer III 133; Pokorny I 1175; Vries ANEW 649; Zalizniak II 205; Frisk I 583–584; Onions 1000; Hamp IF LXVI 52; Lehmann GED 408; Bammesberger Nom. 162; Orel AED 506–507, 509; Kluge-Seebold 888.

Goth weis ‘we’, ON vér id., OE wé id., OFris wī id., OS wī id., OHG wir id. Final *-z is secondary (due to the analogy with pl. forms). Related to Hitt ⁄ēš id., Toch A was, B wes id., Skt vayám id., Av vaēm id. Acc. Goth uns, ON oss, OE ús, OFris ūs, OS ūs, OHG uns-ih goes back to *uns- < IE *–s-, related to Toch A näß, B ñi≤‘we’, Skt acc.-gen.-dat. na˛ id., Av nō, nē id., Alb ne, na id., Lat nōs id., OIr ni id., Slav *ny id. Bartholomae 13357; Torp-Falk 406; Jensen IF XLVIII 122; Holthausen AEEW 385; Walde-Hofmann II 175–176; Feist 560; Mayrhofer III 147; Pokorny I 758; Vries ANEW 654; Onions 995; Schmidt Pron. 167–176 (forms with short vowels influenced by dual); Lehmann GED 378, 400; Orel AED 289; Kluge-Seebold 893; Burlak IFTJa 255.

ON váä ‘cloth, piece of stuff’ (ō- and i-stem), OE wæd, wæde ‘article of dress’, OFris wēd ‘garment’, OS wād id., OHG wāt id. Derived from *weđanan. Torp-Falk 386; Holthausen AEEW 378; Pokorny I 75–76 (to Lith áudžiau, áusti ‘to weave’); Vries ANEW 637; Zalizniak II 219; Onions 997; Bammesberger Nom. 136; Kluge-Seebold 876.
ON væäa ‘to clothe’, OE wædian id., OS wādian id., OHG wāten id. Derived from *wēđiz. Torp- Falk 386; Holthausen AEEW 378; Vries ANEW 671.

Goth wegs ‘storm, wave’ (dat. pl. wegim is from an i-stem), ON vágr ‘sea, wave’, OE wæz ‘movement, wave, water’ (< *wēzaz and *wēziz), OFris wēg ‘wave’, OS wāg id., OHG wāg ‘stream, sea’. Derived from *wezanan (Wissmann NP 1, 14). Cf. Skt vāhá- ‘driving, current’. Meillet Festschr. Andler 251 (originally athematic); Torp- Falk 383; Holthausen AEEW 379; Feist 556; Pokorny I 1119–1120; Vries ANEW 639; Zalizniak II 191; Seebold 543; Bammesberger Nom. 63; Kluge- Seebold 896.

ON vægr ‘balancing (of scales), lenient’, MLG wēger ‘bent, favorable’ (< comp.), OHG un-wāgi ‘unimportant’. Derived from *wezanan. Karsten NB I 100; Torp-Falk 383; Pokorny I 1118– 1120; Vries ANEW 671; Seebold 543; Matzel Festschr. Schröder 102, 117, KZ CV 114; Heidermanns 661–662.

ON vág ‘scales, balance’ (i- and root stems), OE wæz, wæze ‘weight, balance’, OS wāga ‘lanx’, OHG wāga ‘balance’. Derived from *wezanan or substantivized *wēziz. Torp-Falk 383; Holthausen AEEW 379; Pokorny I 1119– 1120; Vries ANEW 638; Zalizniak II 212; Onions 1000; Kluge-Seebold 869.
ON vægja ‘to give way, to yield’, OS wāzian ‘to move’. Derived from *wezanan. Vries ANEW 671.

ON vægi ‘weight, moment’, OE ze-wæze ‘weight, measure’, OHG giwāgi ‘weight’. Derived from *wezanan. Derivationally close to Slav *vîža ‘tower’ < *‘house on wheels’ (Potebnia RFV III 177; Meringer ZÖG LIV 392). Holthausen AEEW 379; Vries ANEW 671; Vasmer-Trubachev I 285.

Goth waian ‘to blow’, OE pres. wæweä id., OFris waja id., MDu waien id. Related to Skt vāti id., Av vāiti id., Gk ἄημι id., Slav *vîjati id. Bartholomae 1406; Torp-Falk 378–379; Trautmann BSW 345; Feist 541–542; Holthausen AEEW 385; Kluge 845, Festschr. Viëtor 106–108; Mayrhofer III 186; Pokorny I 83; Frisk I 26–27; Vasmer-Trubachev I 310; Seebold 539–540; Lehmann GED 388; Kluge- Seebold 879.
OSwed via ‘to blow’, OHG wāen id. Derived from *wējanan I. Seebold 539.
ON vænd ‘expectance’, OFris wēnthe ‘hope’, OHG wānida ‘opinion’. Derived from *wēnjanan. Vries ANEW 671.

Goth wens ‘expectation, hope’, ON ván id., OE wén ‘supposition, opinion’, OFris wēn ‘idea, opinion’, OS wān ‘expectation, hope’ (masc.), OHG wān ‘guess, delusion, hope’ (masc.). Long grade of a root otherwise attested in *weniz. Related to Skt vanóti ‘to demand, to strive for, to like’, Lat uenus ‘love’, OIr fine ‘kinship, kin, family’. Derivationally close to Toch A wañi, B wīna ‘pleasure’. Meillet MSL IX 55 (to Lat uēnor ‘to hunt’); Torp-Falk 387; Walde-Hofmann II 752–753; Feist 561; Holthausen AEEW 404; Mayrhofer III 141–142; Pokorny I 1147; Vries ANEW 643; Zalizniak II 191; Onions 998; Lehmann GED 400–401; Bammesberger Nom. 136; Heidermanns 670; Kluge-Seebold 871; Adams TB 601–602.

ON vænn ‘promising, likely’, OE ze-wéne ‘perhaps’, OS ana-wāni ‘suspicious’, OHG ana-wāni id. Exocentric derivative of *wēniz I. Karsten NB I 97–98; Torp-Falk 387; Holthausen AEEW 404; Vries ANEW 671; Matzel KZ CV 111; Lloyd-Springer I 238; Heidermanns 670.

Goth wenjan ‘to hope’, ON væna id., OE wénan ‘to think, to suppose’, OFris wāna id., OS wānian ‘to fancy, to imagine’, OHG wānen ‘to suppose’. Derived from *wēniz I. Torp- Falk 387–388; Feist 561; Vries ANEW 671; Onions 998.
ON vápn-lauss ‘weaponless’, OE wæpen-leás ‘unarmed’, MLG wāpen-lōs ‘weaponless’. Compound of *wēpnan and *lausaz. Carr Nom. comp. 90 (parallel formations).

Goth wepn ‘weapon’, ON vápn id., OE wæpen id., OFris wēpin id., OS wāpan id., OHG wāfan id. Related to Toch B yepe ‘weapon, knife’ < *⁄ēb-en- (Van Windekens SL XXV 125–128). Zupitza Gutt. 19; Torp-Falk 391; Krogmann KZ LXV 143–144 (to Skt aor. vádhīm ‘to slay, to kill’); Holthausen AEEW 380; Feist 561; Vries ANEW 644; Zalizniak I 145; Onions 996; Lehmann GED 401; Kluge-Seebold 870; Adams TB 506.

ON væringi ‘Varangian, Scandinavian’, OE wer-zenza ‘stranger who seeks protection’, Langob ware-gang id. Compound of *wēraz and *zanzjōn. Stender-Petersen APS VI 26 (originally, ‘member of a trade companionship’); Holthausen AEEW 381; Carr Nom. comp. 53; Vries ANEW 671–672.

ON vár ‘spring’. Related to Skt vasantá- id., Av van˘ ri id., Gk đar id., Lat uēr id., Lith ωάσαρα ‘summer’, Slav *vesna ‘spring’ and influenced phonetically by *jēran (Porzig Gliederung 110–111). Walde-Hofmann II 755; Pokorny I 1174; Vries ANEW 644–645; Frisk I 432–433; Huld JIES XVIII 420 (adds Toch A wsār ‘heap of grain’, B ysāre ‘grain’).

Burg *wers ‘true’, OFris wēr id., OS wār id., OHG wār id. Related to Lat uērus id., OIr fír id., W gwīr id. See *wērō. Pedersen Kelt. Gr. I 50; Torp- Falk 395; Wissmann NP 117 (from Italic or Celt); Holthausen AEEW 381; Walde-Hofmann II 768; Pokorny I 1166; Seebold IF LXXVIII 146–162; Lehmann GED 350; Köbler 714; Heidermanns 671–672; Kluge-Seebold 871.
ON værr ‘comfortable, tranquil’, OHG wāri ‘true’. Derived from *wēraz. Torp-Falk 395; Vries ANEW 672; Sevilla Rodríguez Festschr. Adrados 455–458.

ON pl. várar ‘pledge, plight’, OE wær ‘covenant, agreement, pledge’, MLG wāre ‘truth’, OHG wāra ‘union, agreement’. Identical with Slav *vîra ‘belief, faith’ and maybe Gk Hom acc. sg. ἦρα ‘kindness’. Cf. *wēraz. Hirt PBB XXIII 337 (Slav < Gmc); Meillet Etudes 169; Torp-Falk 395; Holthausen AEEW 381; Wissmann Postv. 115–121 (isolates *wērō from *wēraz); Feist 484; Pokorny I 1165; Dybo VSJa 26; Vries ANEW 645; Zalizniak I 148; Frisk I 641; Vasmer-Trubachev I 292–293; Chantraine 415; Onions 992; Lehmann GED 350; Heidermanns 672.

Icel vás ‘aura refrigerans’, Du waas ‘haze, mist’. Close to Skt vāsa- ‘perfume, fragrance’, Lith vësā ‘coolness’ (Persson Beiträge 11–12). Further connected with *wējanan I. Holthausen IF LXII 152; Mayrhofer III 197–198 (against comparison with Skt); Pokorny I 83; Fraenkel 1232.

ON vátr ‘wet’, OE wæt id., OFris wēt id. A vṛddhi form related to *watnan ~ *watar. Identical with Hier. Luw wida- ‘watery’ (Watkins Fu W 373– 374). Karsten NB I 31; Torp-Falk 384; Holthausen AEEW 381; Pokorny I 80; Vries ANEW 648; Onions 1000; Darms Vṛddhi 13–24, 450; Bammesberger Nom. 255; Heidermanns 676–677.
ON væta ‘to wet, to make wet’, OE wætan ‘to moisten, to wet’, OFris wēta id. Derived from *wētaz. Torp-Falk 384; Holthausen AEEW 381; Heidermanns 677.
ON væta ‘wetness, sleet, rain’, OE wæte ‘wet, moisture’. Derived from *wētaz. Holthausen AEEW 381; Vries ANEW 672.
ON váta-drífa ‘fall of sleet’, OE wæta ‘wet, moisture’. Substantivized *wētaz. Holthausen AEEW 381; Heidermanns 677.

Goth widuwo ‘widow’, OE widuwe id., OFris widwe id., OS widowa id., OHG wituwa id. Etymologically connected with Skt vidhávā id., Av vidavā id., Lat uidua id., OIr fedb id., OPrus widdewū id., Slav *vьdova id. Bartholomae 1443; Torp-Falk 411; Trautmann BSW 357; Holthausen AEEW 393; Walde-Hofmann II 785–786; Feist 562; Mayrhofer III 211; Pokorny I 1128; Zalizniak I 156; Onions 1006; Vasmer- Trubachev I 281–282; Szemerényi Kinship 85; Lindeman Verschärfung 1.4; Lehmann GED 402; Bammesberger Nom. 179; Beekes KZ CV 181–182; Kluge- Seebold 895.

ON viär ‘tree, forest, wood’, OE widu, wudu ‘wood’, OS wido-hoppa ‘hoopoe’, OHG witu ‘wood’. Etymologically identical with OIr fid ‘tree, wood’, W gwydd. Cf. also Hom ἼΙδη < *⁄id⁄ā (Gindin apud Trubachev VJa XXXIII/2 26). Torp-Falk 411; Holthausen AEEW 393; Detschew Thr. 214; Pokorny I 1177; Vries ANEW 660; Zalizniak I 152; Onions 1012; Bammesberger Nom. 155.

ON á víxl ‘across’, OS wehsal ‘change, exchange’, OHG wehsal id. Related to Lat acc. uicem id. Zupitza Gutt. 210; Torp- Falk 407 (to *wīkanan); Walde-Hofmann II 781–782; Pokorny I 1131; Vries ANEW 671; Kluge-Seebold 877.
ON víxla ‘to cross, to put across’, OE wíxlan ‘to change’, OFris wixlia id., OS wehslōn id., OHG wehsalōn id. Derived from *wixslan ~ *wixslaz. Holthausen AEEW 402; Vries ANEW 670.

Goth wiko ‘sequence, τάξις’, ON vika ‘week, nautical mile’, OE wicu ‘week’, OFris wike id., OS wika id., OHG wehha id. Derived from *wīkanan (Grienberger Got. 241). Kluge DS 187 (the meaning ‘week’ developed under the influence of Lat uicem ‘exchange’); Torp-Falk 407; Holthausen AEEW 392; Feist 563 (against Grienberger); Pokorny I 1131 (to Lat uicem ‘exchange’); Vries ANEW 662; Zalizniak I 156; Onions 997; Lehmann GED 403; Kluge-Seebold 895.
Icel vippa ‘to seesaw’, ME wippen ‘to tremble’, MLG wippen ‘to rock, to swing’, OHG wipfōn ‘to wander’. Of imitative origin. Vries ANEW 667.
ON ver-ǫld ‘world’, OE weor-old id., OFris warld id., OLFrank wer-old id., OHG wer-alt id. Compound of *wiraz and *alđiz. Torp-Falk 20; Holthausen AEEW 390; Onions 1012; Kluge-Seebold 885.
ON ver-þjóä ‘mankind, men’, OE wer-äeód ‘nation, people’. Compound of *wiraz and *þeuđō. Carr Nom. comp. 53.

ON varg-úlfr ‘werewolf’, Norw var-ulf id., OE were-wulf id., MLG wer-wulf id., MHG wer-wolf id. Compound of *wiraz and *wulfaz with various taboo modifications in individual languages (e.g. with *wiraz replaced by *warzaz in ON). On the other hand, cf. an alternative reconstruction *wazi-wulfaz with the first component related to *wasjanan ~ *wazjanan. The Gmc word for werewolf will be then semantically identical with Slav *vьlko-dlakъ ‘werewolf’, with *dlakъ to *dlaka ‘animal hair, fur’. Vries ANEW 646; Onions 1000; Anikin BS 231–233.

Goth wair ‘man’, ON verr id., OE wer id., OFris wer- id., OS wer id., OHG wer id. Belongs to IE *⁄iros ‘man’: Lat uir, OIr fer, W gwr. Other forms reflect *⁄īros like Skt vīrá- id., Av vīra- id., Umbr ueiro id., Lith vÿras id. Bartholomae 1453; Hirt Idg. Gr. II 187 (*-i-: *-ī- originally in compounds); Torp-Falk 412; Trautmann BSW 360; Holthausen AEEW 391; Walde-Hofmann II 796–797; Feist 544; Porzig Gliederung 124; Mayrhofer III 238–239; Pokorny I 1177–1178; Dybo VSJa 24 (pretonic shortening of *ī in Gmc, Celt and Lat); Chemodanov SGGJa I 101; Fraenkel 1258; Vries ANEW 657; Zalizniak I 137; Schulze Kl. Schr. 398 (long *-ī- from IE *⁄eƒes ‘strength’); Lehmann GED 389–390; Bammesberger Nom. 74; Schrijver LL 354 (follows Dybo); Kluge-Seebold 885.

ON visk ‘wisp’, OE wisc id., MLG masc. wisch id., OHG masc. wisc id. Identical with Lat uirga ‘branch, twig’ < *⁄izgā. Kögel IF IV 317; Wood CPh VII 334 (to Lat uīscus ‘mistletoe’); Torp- Falk 413; Holthausen AEEW 399; Walde-Hofmann II 797–798; Pokorny I 1134; Vries ANEW 668; Onions 1003; Kluge-Seebold 894.

ON visna ‘to wither’, OE wisnian, weosnian ‘to wizen, to dry up’, OHG wesanēn ‘to wither’. Derived from *wīsanan. Torp-Falk 413; Holthausen AEEW 399; Onions 1010 (to Lat uiēscō ‘to shrink up, to shrivel’); Seebold 548; Kluge-Seebold 862.

Goth un-wiss ‘uncertain’, ON viss ‘certain’, OE ze-wis id., OFris wiss id., OS wiss id., OHG gi-wis ‘definite, certain’. Derived from *waita. Continues *⁄id-to- as in Skt vittá- ‘known’, Gk ἄιστος ‘unknown’, OIr ro.fess ‘it is known’, Slav *vîstъ ‘known’ (with secondary vocalism). Karsten NB II 309; Torp- Falk 410; Feist 171, 364; Holthausen AEEW 399; Feist 526; Pokorny I 1125–1126; Vries ANEW 669; Vasmer- Trubachev I 304; Seebold 534; Lehmann GED 130–131; Bammesberger Nom. 249; Heidermanns 681–682; Kluge- Seebold 322–323.
Goth miþ-wissei ‘awareness, consciousness’, OHG gi-wissī ‘attempt, proof’. Derived from *wissaz. Feist 364; Heidermanns 681.

OE wesend id., MDu wesent id., OHG wisant, wisunt id. ON vísundr ‘bison-ox’ is a LG loanword. Related to *waisōn due to the specific smell of the male animal. Charpentier MO VI 128–129 (to OPrus wis-sambrs ‘aurochs’); Schrader-Nehring II 60 (same as Charpentier); Torp-Falk 413 (to *wes(u)lōn and further to Lat uīrus ‘stench’); Būga IORJa S XVII/1 45–46; Petersson Heter. 39–40; Holthausen AEEW 391; Petersson KZ XLVII 131 (to Skt vißānā ‘horn’); Pokorny I 1134; Vries ANEW 669; Kluge-Seebold 894.
ON vit-lauss ‘witless’, OE wit-leás id., Fris wit-lās id., MLG witlōs id. Compound of *witan and *lausaz. Carr Nom. comp. 61.
ON vit ‘consciousness, sense, intelligence’, OHG gi-wiz ‘knowledge’. Derived from *waita. Seebold 534.

Goth witan ‘to keep watch over’, OHG gi-wizzēn ‘to become reasonable’. Derived from *waita. Structurally close to Lat uideō ‘to see’, Slav *vidîti id. Torp-Falk 409–410; Walde-Hofmann II 784–785; Feist 569; Vries ANEW 669; Seebold 534; Lehmann GED 407.
ON vitugr ‘clever’, OE witiz ‘having knowledge, sagacious, wise’, MLG wittich ‘clever’, OHG sbst. wīzago ‘prophet’. Derived from *witan. Torp-Falk 410; Holthausen AEEW 401; Vries ANEW 670.

Goth un-witi ‘foolishness, ignorance’, OE witt ‘right mind, wits’, OFris wit ‘joke, comprehension’, OS giwit ‘understanding’, OHG wizzi id. Cf. ON adj. ǫr-viti ‘out of one’s senses’. Derived from *waita. Close to Skt vidyā ‘knowledge’. Holthausen AEEW 401; Feist 526; Mayrhofer III 256; Pokorny I 1125–1127; Vries ANEW 669; Seebold 534; Lehmann GED 379, 407; Bammesberger Nom. 65.

Goth un-wita ‘ignorant, fool’, ON viti ‘signal, beacon’, OE wita ‘wise man’, OFris wita ‘witness’, OS mēn-giwito ‘false witness’. Derived from *witan. Torp-Falk 410; Holthausen AEEW 400; Feist 526; Vries ANEW 670; Seebold 534; Lehmann GED 379; Bammesberger Nom. 173.

Goth sbst. witoþ ‘law’, Run sbst. vitada-halaiban, OE witod ‘appointed, ordained’, OFris sbst. witat ‘wafer’, OS sbst. witut ‘law’, OHG sbst. wizzōd id. Participle of unattested wk.vb. *witōjanan. Derived from *waita. Wood MLN XXI 41 (to Skt neut. vidátha- ‘distribution’); Torp- Falk 410; Feist 570; Lehmann GED 407.

ON vitr ‘wise’, OE witer ‘knowing, wise’. Close to Gk ǫdriw ‘skillful, clever’. Further related to *waita. Karsten NB II 447; Torp-Falk 410; Holthausen AEEW 401; Pokorny I 1127; Vries ANEW 670; Frisk I 357; Seebold 534; Heidermanns 682.
ON mann-vitull ‘simple, dull-witted’, OE fore-witol ‘foreknowing’. Derived from *witan. Holthausen AEEW 401; Heidermanns 682–683.
ON viä-beina ‘collarbone’, OE wiäo-bán id., OFris widu-bēn id., MLG wede-bēn id. Compound of *wiþiz and *bainan. Holthausen AEEW 393; Carr Nom. comp. 53; Vries ANEW 659.

Goth kuna-wida ‘fetter, cord’, ON viä ‘withy’, MLG wede ‘fetter’, OHG wid ‘thin branch, fetter’. Related to Av vaēti- ‘willow’, Gk φιτέα id., Lith vytīs ‘willow branch’, Slav *vitь id. and other derivatives of *⁄ei- ‘to braid’. Bartholomae 1314; Torp-Falk 406; Trautmann BSW 347; Feist 315–316; Pokorny I 1122; Vries ANEW 659; Fraenkel 1268; Frisk I 743–744.

ON viäja ‘withy’, OE wiääe ‘thong, cord’, OFris withe ‘band, fetter’, MDu wide id., OHG witta ‘head-band’. See *wiþiz. Torp-Falk 406; Holthausen AEEW 402; Vries ANEW 659; Onions 1010; Lehmann GED 222.
Goth wiþra-wairþs ‘opposite’, OE wiäer-weard ‘contrary, adverse, hostile’, OHG widar-wert ‘adverse, hostile’. Derived from *wiþra(n) and *werþaz. Feist 571; Lehmann GED 34, 408.

Goth wiþra ‘against, opposite’, ON viär id., OE wiäer id., OFris wither id., OS withar id., OHG widar id. Comparative in *-ter- based on IE *⁄i-, cf. Skt vitarám ‘farther’, Av vītara- ‘further one, later one’. Bartholomae 1439; Torp-Falk 411; Holthausen AEEW 401; Feist 570–571; Mayrhofer III 207; Pokorny I 1176; Vries ANEW 660; Onions 1010; Lehmann GED 408; Kluge-Seebold 888.

Burg *wif ‘wife, woman’, ON víf ‘woman’, OE wíf id., OFris wīf id., OS wīf id., OHG wīb id. Of uncertain origin. Torp-Falk 412 (to *waibjanan); Tavernier-Vereecken RBPH XXXII/1 97–99 (to *webanan); Bezzenberger KZ XLI 282 (from *⁄ei˚-po-, cf. Skt viś-páti ‘lord of the house’, nṛ-pa- ‘prince, king’); Printz IF L 132–135; Holthausen AEEW 395; Pokorny I 1132; Vries ANEW 661; Zalizniak I 145; Onions 1006; Köbler 714; Kluge- Seebold 879–880 (to Lith vaĩkas ‘child’); Adams TB 238 (to Toch A kip, B kwīpe ‘shame’, with the semantic development ‘shame’ > ‘pudenda’ > ‘woman’).
ON koma aä vífandi ‘to arrive as by chance’, OHG weibōn ‘to waver’. Connected with *waibjanan. Vries ANEW 661; Lehmann GED 74.
ON víä-faämr ‘widefathoming’, OE wíd-fæäme ‘broad-bosomed’. Compound of *wīđaz and *faþmjaz (see *faþmaz). Carr Nom. comp. 67.

Burg *wids ‘wide’, ON víär id., OE wíd id., OFris wīd id., OS wīd id., OHG wīt id. Probably related to *wiđuz. The semantic relation of the latter to Lith vidūs ‘middle’ is similar to that of Lith m‚dis ‘wood’ to Skt mádhya- ‘middle’. Karsten NB II 304; Prellwitz KZ XLVIII 153–154 (to Skt part. vītá- ‘verfolgen’); Torp-Falk 412; Holthausen AEEW 393; Trier Lehm 50; Pokorny I 295; Vries ANEW 660; Onions 1005; Bammesberger Nom. 252; Köbler 714; Heidermanns 678–679; Kluge-Seebold 884.
ON vídd ‘width’, EFris wīdte id., MLG wīdde id. Derived from *wīđjanan. Vries ANEW 659.
ON víäa ‘to widen’, OHG wīten id. Derived from *wīđaz. Vries ANEW 659; Heidermanns 679.
ON víäa ‘widely’, OE wíde id., OS wīdo id., OHG wīto id. Derived from *wīđaz. Vries ANEW 659.
ON víg-harär ‘hardy in war’, OE wíz-heard ‘stout in fight, hardy’. Compound of *wīzan and *xarđuz. Carr Nom. comp. 61.
ON víg-ligr ‘martial, doughty’, OS un-wīg-līk ‘non-militant’, OHG wīg-līh ‘militant’. Derived from *wīzaz. Heidermanns 663.
ON víg-maär ‘warrior, champion’, OE wíz-mann ‘man of war, soldier’, OS wīg-man ‘warrior, champion’, OHG wīg-man id. Compound of *wīzan and *mannz. Carr Nom. comp. 79 (parallel formations).

ON víg ‘fight, battle’, OE wíz id. Derived from *wīxanan ~ *wīzanan. Structurally close to OIr fích ‘quarrel’. Torp-Falk 408; Holthausen AEEW 395; Vries ANEW 661; Onions 1006; Seebold 544–545; Bammesberger Nom. 60; Kluge-Seebold 881.
ON víg-sǫk ‘prosecution for manslaughter’, OS wīg-saka ‘fight’. Compound of *wīzan and *sakō. Carr Nom. comp. 85 (parallel formations).

Burg sbst. *wigs ‘fighter’, ON vígr ‘in fighting state, serviceable’, OE or-wíze ‘defenseless’, MHG wīge ‘militant’. Derived from *wīxanan ~ *wīzanan. Holthausen AEEW 426; Vries ANEW 662; Seebold 545; Matzel KZ CV 96; Köbler 714; Heidermanns 662–663 (OE derived from *wīzan).
ON vígsla ‘consecration, ordination’, MLG wīgelse id. Derived from *wīzjanan ~ *wīxjanan. Vries ANEW 662.
ON vígja ‘to consecrate’, OFris wīga id., OS wīhian id., OHG wīhen id. Derived from *wīxaz. Vries ANEW 661; Lehmann GED 398; Kluge-Seebold 881–882.
ON vé ‘temple’, OE wíh ‘idol’ (masc.), OS wīh ‘shrine’, OHG wīh id. Substantivized *wīxaz. Holthausen AEEW 398; Vries ANEW 648; Benveniste IEL 450; Lehmann GED 398; Heidermanns 663.

Goth weihan ‘to dispute, to fight’, ON vega ‘to fight, to fence’, OE wízan ‘to fight’, OHG ubarwehan ‘to overcome’. Forms with *-e- are secondary (Falk-Torp ND 1362). Connected with Gk ἐπί-εικτος ‘unconquerable’, OIr fichim ‘to fight’, Lat uincō ‘to conquer’, Lith veikiū, ωεϊκτι ‘to carry out, to do’ (Froehde BB XXI 207). Zupitza Gutt. 72, 142; Torp-Falk 408; Trautmann BSW 339; Holthausen AEEW 395; Walde-Hofmann II 791– 792; Feist 557; Jóhannesson IEW 113–114; Porzig Gliederung 195; Pokorny I 1128–1129; Chemodanov SGGJa I 66–67; Fraenkel 1213–1214; Vries ANEW 650; Frisk I 536; Seebold 544–545; Benveniste IEL 450; Lehmann GED 397–398; Kluge-Seebold 881.

Goth weihs ‘holy’, OE wízbedd ‘altar’, OS wīh-dag ‘holiday’, OHG wīh ‘holy’. Derived from *wīxanan ~ *wīzanan (for the development of meaning cf. Lith vi‚kas ‘life force’ from the same IE *⁄eik-). Karsten NB I 28–29; Zupitza Gutt. 142; Torp-Falk 408–409; Braune PBB XLIII 398–404; Schulze Kl. Schr. 573–574; Feist 557–558; Walde-Hoffmann II 782, 791–792; Holthausen AEEW 398; Baetke HG 55–57 (to Skt vinákti ‘to sift, to separate’, Lat uictima ‘sacrificial animal’); Trier Lehm 48–49; Porzig Gliederung 167; Pokorny I 1128; Chemodanov SGGJa I 87; Fraenkel 1239; Vries ANEW 649; Krause SUR 66–67 (to Lat uinciō ‘to bind, to tie up’); Benveniste 450–452; Scardigli Goten 219–221; Lacy JIES VII 287–296; Lehmann GED 398–399; Bammesberger Nom. 240; Heidermanns 663–664; Kluge-Seebold 882.
Goth weihan ‘to consecrate, to dedicate’, OE ful-wian ‘to baptize’. Derived from *wīxaz. Torp-Falk 409; Feist 557; Lehmann GED 398; Heidermanns 663.

Goth weihiþa ‘holiness’, OFris wītha ‘relic’, OS wīhitha ‘sanctity, sacrament’, OHG wīhida id. Derived from *wīxaz or *wīzjanan ~ *wīxjanan. Feist 557; Benveniste IEL 450; Lehmann GED 398; Heidermanns 663.
Goth weiha ‘priest’, ON theon. Véi Odin’s brother. Derived from *wīxaz. Feist 556–557; Benveniste IEL 450; Heidermanns 663.

Goth weihs ‘village’. Based on an s-stem found in Toch B īke ‘place, location’ < *⁄eikos- and Lat uīlla ‘house’ < *⁄ei˚s-lā (if not with *-sl- suffix). Further related to Skt víś- ‘settlement, tribe, race’, Av acc. vīs- ‘house, court’, Gk οἶκος ‘house’, Slav *vьsь ‘village’. Zupitza Gutt. 192; Bartholomae 1455– 1457; Torp- Falk 409; Walde-Hofmann II 790–791; Feist 558; Mayrhofer III 205–206; Pokorny I 1131; Vries ANEW 649; Frisk II 360–361; Onions 1005; Vasmer-Trubachev I 305; Benveniste IEL 252, 450–452; Lehmann GED 399; Adams TB 63.

ON víkva, víkja ‘to turn, to move’, OE wícan ‘to yield’, OFris wīka ‘to turn, to move’, OS pret. sg. wēc id., OHG wīhhan ‘to cede, to weaken’. Related to Skt vijáte ‘to heave, to speed, to flee’, Lith svíegiu, svíegti ‘to throw’. Torp-Falk 407; Holthausen AEEW 392; Jóhannesson IEW 114–116; Mayrhofer III 204–205; Pokorny I 1131; Vries ANEW 663; Fraenkel 953; Seebold 545–546; Bammesberger Nom. 237; Kluge-Seebold 880.
ON vík ‘bay, a small creek’ (traces of a cons. stem), OE wíc ‘bay’, MLG wīk id. Derived from *wīkanan. Torp-Falk 407; Holthausen AEEW 392; Vries ANEW 662.

OE víl ‘wile, device’. Close to MIr fell ‘deception’, Lith v‹lius ‘fooling, cunning, deceit’, further related to Lith viliū, vīlti ‘to deceive’. Torp-Falk 413; Pokorny I 1140; Fraenkel 1251, 1254– 1255; Vries ANEW 663 (to Skt vāyati ‘to vanish, to become exhausted’).
ON vín-belgr ‘wineskin’, OE wín-belz id. Compound of *wīnan and *balziz. Carr Nom. comp. 82 (parallel formations).

Goth weina-basi ‘grape’, ON vín-ber id., OE wín-beri(z)e id. (fem.), OS wīn-beri id., OHG wīn-beri id. Compound of *wīnan and *bazjan. Feist 559; Carr Nom. comp. 69 (parallel formations); Lehmann GED 400.

ON vín-byrli ‘cupbearer’, OE wín-byrele ‘vintner’. Compound of *wīnan and *burilōn. Carr Nom. comp. 82 (parallel formations). *wīna-đrunkanaz ~ *wīna-đrunkenaz adj.: ON vín-drukkinn ‘drunken with wine’, OE wín-druncen id., OHG wīntruncan id. Compound of *wīnan and *đrunkanaz ~ *đrunkenaz. Carr Nom. comp. 90 (parallel formations).
ON vín-fat ‘wine-vat’, OE wín-fæt id., MLG wīn-fat id., OHG wīn-faz id. Compound of *wīnan and *fatan I. Carr Nom. comp. 80 (parallel formations).

Goth weina-gards ‘vineyard’, ON vín-garär id., ON wín-zeard id., OS wīn-gardo id., OHG wīn-garto id. Compound of *wīnan and *zarđaz, zarđōn. Feist 559; Carr Nom. comp. 69 (parallel formations); Stender-Petersen Germ.-slav. 365–367 (on Slav *vino-gordъ id. < Gmc); Lehmann GED 400; Kluge-Seebold 892.
ON vín-hús ‘tavern, wine-house’, OE wín-hús id., MLG wīnhūs id., OHG wīn-hūs id. Compound of *wīnan and *xūsan. Carr Nom. comp. 80 (parallel formations).

Goth wein ‘wine’, ON vín id., OE wín id., OFris wīn id., OS wīn id., OHG wīn id. An old borrowing from Lat uīnum (Sturtevant Language X 6–8). Kuhn KZ I 191; Jellinek ZDADL LXVI 126; Holthausen AEEW 396; Feist 558; Vries ANEW 664 (ON < OE or MLG); Zalizniak I 145; Onions 1008; Merlingen Urh. Idg. 412; Lehmann GED 399; Kluge-Seebold 882.
Goth weina-triu ‘vine’, ON vín-tré id., OE wín-treów id. Compound of *wīnan and *trewan. Feist 559; Carr Nom. comp. 82 (parallel formations); Lehmann GED 399.

Goth weipan ‘to crown, to encircle’, OHG part. bi-wifen ‘unhappy’. Related to Latv viêbt ‘to wince, to grimace’, Lat uibrō ‘to move tremblingly’ (Trautmann Zd Wf VII 268–269). Torp-Falk 412; Walde-Hofmann II 780–781; Feist 559; Pokorny I 1131– 1132; Fraenkel 1236–1237; Seebold 546–547; Lehmann GED 400; Kluge- Seebold 890.

ON vírr ‘wire’, Swed dial. vir ‘wire’, OE wír id., MLG wīre id. Related to OIr fíar ‘crooked’, Lith vairūs ‘diverse, various’ (Osthoff Morph. Unt. IV 164). Noreen Abriß 31; Brate Zd Wf X 678 (from Celt); Torp-Falk 406; Holthausen AEEW 398–399; Walde-Hoffman II 799 (Lat pl. uiriae ‘armlets, bracelets’ < Celt); Pokorny I 1122 (to IE *⁄ei- ‘to wind’); Fraenkel 1182–1183; Vries ANEW 667; Onions 1008.

ON vís-dómr ‘knowledge, intelligence’, OE wís-dóm ‘wisdom, experience’, OFris wīs-dōm id., OS wīs-dōm id., OHG wīs-tuom id. Derived from *wīsaz. Carr Nom. comp. 58; Onions 1009; Heidermanns 665; Kluge-Seebold 884.
ON vís-ligr ‘certain’, OE wís-líc ‘wise, discreet’, OS wīs-līk ‘wise, intelligent’, OHG wīs-līh id. Derived from *wīsaz. Heidermanns 665.

ON part. visinn ‘wilted, withered’, OHG part. wesan ‘weak, tired’. Distantly related to Lat uiēscō ‘to wilt’, W gwyw ‘faded’, Lith vÿstu, vÿsti ‘to wither’. Pedersen Kelt. Gr. I 74, 252; Torp-Falk 413; Walde-Hofmann II 787–788; Jóhannesson IEW 111; Pokorny I 1123; Fraenkel 1265; Vries ANEW 668; Seebold 548; Kluge-Seebold 862.

Goth un-weis ‘ignorant’, ON víss ‘certain, wise’, OE wís ‘wise, discreet, judicious’, OFris wīs ‘wise, intelligent’, OS wīs ‘learned, clever, wise’, OHG wīs ‘wise, clever’. Continues *⁄eid-s-o- derived from the s-stem of IE *⁄eid- ‘to know’, cf. Skt jātá-vedas- ‘having whatever is born or created as his property; knowing [or known by] all created beings’. Derived from *waita. Karsten NB II 342; Osthoff Morph. Unt. IV 77 (< *⁄eid-to-, as in Lat uīsus, part. of uideō ‘to see’); Torp-Falk 410; Meillet Wu S XII 19; Feist 171; Holthausen AEEW 399; Trier ZDK XLVI 625–635; Pokorny I 1127; Fraenkel 1212–1213 (from *⁄eid-s-); Vries ANEW 668–669; Onions 1009; Seebold 533; Lehmann GED 130–131; Bammesberger Nom. 256; Heidermanns 664–665; Kluge-Seebold 883 (OHG wīsi ‘wise, understanding’ < *⁄eid-tƒosimilar to Slav *vîš‘ь ‘knowing’ < *⁄oidtƒo-).
Goth hindar-weisei ‘deceitfulness, guile’, ON vísi ‘knowledge, science’, OHG wīsī ‘wisdom’. Derived from *wīsaz. Feist 257; Vries ANEW 668; Heidermanns 665.

Goth fulla-weisjan ‘to make informed, to instruct thoroughly’, OFris wīsa ‘to show’, OS wīsian id., OHG wīsen ‘to call, to make known’. Derived from *wīsaz. Torp-Falk 410; Feist 171–172; Vries ANEW 668; Lehmann GED 153.
ON vísi ‘guide, leader, captain’, OE wísa ‘leader, director, captain’, OS balu-wīso ‘devil’, OHG wīso ‘leader’. See *wīsjanan. Holthausen AEEW 399; Vries ANEW 668; Kluge- Seebold 883.

ON vísa ‘strophe’, OE wíse ‘way, manner’, OS wīsa id., OHG wīsa id. Cf. also WGmc *wīsō > OE wís ‘manner, way’, OFris wīs id. Probably, from *⁄eid-s- or *⁄eid-t- further related to *waita. Heinertz SVSL VII 141–161 (to *wīsjanan); Torp-Falk 410; Holthausen AEEW 399; Pokorny I 1127; Vries ANEW 668; Zalizniak II 181; Onions 1009; Kluge-Seebold 883.

Goth ga-weison ‘to visit’, OS wīsōn id., OHG wīsōn id. Derived from *wīsaz. Loewe KZ XXXIX 307 (from Lat uīsō ‘to visit’); Brugmann Grundriß II/3 55 (-s- from desiderative IE *-s-); Güntert IF XXX 136; Torp-Falk 410–411; Feist 210; Pokorny I 1125; Onions 418; Lehmann GED 153.

ON vísa ‘to show, to direct’, OE wísian ‘to show the way, to guide, to direct’. Derived from *wīsaz. Historically identical with *wīsōjanan I. Torp- Falk 410; Holthausen AEEW 399; Heidermanns 665; Kluge-Seebold 883.
OESc ræt-visa ‘justice’, OHG wīsa ‘wisdom’. Derived from *wīsaz. Heidermanns 665.
ON vitka ‘to bewitch’, OE wítezian ‘to prophesy’, OFris wītgia id., OHG wīzagōn id. Derived from *wītazōn. Holthausen AEEW 400; Vries ANEW 670; Kluge-Seebold 883.
ON vitki ‘wizard’, OE wíteza ‘wizard, prophet’, OHG wīzago ‘prophet’. Substantivized adj. *wītazaz > OHG wīzag id. Derived from *waita. Holthausen AEEW 400; Vries ANEW 670; Kluge-Seebold 883.
Goth id-weit ‘reproach, disgrace’, OE ed-wít ‘reproach, disgrace’, OHG ita-wīz ‘disgrace’. Derived from *wītanan. Holthausen AEEW 88; Feist 290; Lehmann GED 203.

Goth fra-weitan ‘to avenge’, OSwed víta ‘to accuse, to blame’, OE wítan ‘to charge, to blame’, OFris wīta ‘to guard’, OS pres. wītan ‘to reproach’, OHG wīzan ‘to punish, to rebuke’. Derived from *waita. Torp- Falk 409; Feist 167; Holthausen AEEW 400; Jóhannesson IEW 116–118; Pokorny I 1125–1127; Vries ANEW 669; Onions 418, 952; Seebold 550–551; Lehmann GED 127; Kluge-Seebold 862.

ON víti ‘fine, sconce’, OE wíte ‘punishment’, OFris wīte id., OS wīti id., OHG wīzi id. Derived from *wītanan. Holthausen AEEW 400; Vries ANEW 670; Lehmann GED 127; Seebold 550, Sprache XIX 170–171 (reconstructs *⁄eƒH- ‘to pursue, to approach’).

ON víäir ‘willow’, MLG wīde id., OHG wīda id. Cf. also OE masc. wíäiz id. Related to various semantically similar derivatives of IE *⁄ei- ‘to turn, to weave’: Lat uītis ‘vine’, OIr féith ‘fibra’, Lith vytīs ‘willow rod’. Further connected with Gk ïτέα ‘willow’. Cf. also *wiþiz which may be the immediate source of derivation. Torp-Falk 406; Holthausen AEEW 401; Walde- Hofmann II 804; Pokorny I 1122; Fraenkel 1268; Vries ANEW 659; Zalizniak II 176; Frisk I 743; Kluge- Seebold 880–881.

ON lang-vé ‘a kind of bird’, MLG wie, wige ‘harrier’, OHG wīo id. Unclear. Neuß Tierb. 168–170 (to IE *⁄i- ‘two’ because of the forked tail); Kluge-Seebold 881 (to Lat avis ‘bird’ or to Skt śyená- ‘eagle’, Gk φικτῖνος ‘kite’).

Goth wlaiton ‘to look around’, ON leita ‘to look for’, OE wlátian ‘to gaze, to look’. Derived from *wlītanan. Torp-Falk 420; Holthausen AEEW 402; Feist 571–572; Pokorny I 1136–1137; Vries ANEW 352; Seebold 563; Lehmann GED 408.
Norw dial. leisp ‘lisping’, OE wlisp id., MDu lisp id., OHG lisp id. Of imitative origin. Torp-Falk 420; Holthausen AEEW 403; Pokorny I 1143; Heidermanns 684–685; Kluge- Seebold 521.

Goth wlits ‘face, appearance’, ON litr ‘color’ (u-stem), OE wlite ‘aspect, countenance’, OFris wlite ‘face, apearance’, OS wliti ‘image, appearance’. Derived from *wlītanan. Schweizer KZ I 154; Torp-Falk 420; Holthausen AEEW 403; Feist 571–572; Pokorny I 1136 (to Lat uultus ‘appearance, expression’); Vries ANEW 359; Zalizniak II 205; Hamp IF LXXXVII 79–81 (to OIr fili ‘seer’); Lehmann GED 408; Kluge- Seebold 45.

Goth anda-wleizn ‘face’ (with unclear auslaut), ON á-lit ‘appearance, view’, OE wlita ‘face’ (masc.), OHG ant-lizzi id. Derived from *wlītanan or directly from *wlitiz. Grienberger Goten 24; Holthausen AEEW 403; Feist 48; Pokorny I 1136; Vries ANEW 9; Zalizniak II 205; Seebold 563; Lehmann GED 35; Lloyd-Springer I 280–283; Kluge- Seebold 45.

ON líta ‘to see’, OE wlítan ‘to look, to gaze’. Of unknown origin. The unusual anlaut is an obstacle to a reasonable etymology. Zupitza Gutt. 34; Torp-Falk 420; Holthausen AEEW 403; Feist 571–572; Jóhannesson IEW 158; Pokorny I 1136–1137; Vries ANEW 358; Seebold 563–564 (to Lat uultus ‘face’, W gweled ‘to see’ < *⁄el-); Hamp IF LXXXVII 79–81 (contamination of *⁄el- ‘to see’ and *⁄eid- id.); Lehmann GED 408; Kluge-Seebold 45.

ON theon. Óäinn ‘Odin, the highest god of the Germanic pantheon’, OE Wóden id., OS Wōden id., OHG Wuotan id. Derived from *wōđaz. Holthausen AEEW 403; Vries ANEW 416; Onions 997; Benveniste 92, 247; Bammesberger Nom. 82.

Goth woþs ‘possessed’, ON óär ‘mad, frantic, furious’, OE wód ‘mad’. Related to Lat uātēs ‘seer, prophet’, OIr fáith id. Torp-Falk 414; Marstrander NTS VII 337–338; Feist 572–573; Holthausen AEEW 403; Walde-Hofmann II 737–738; Thieme Festschr. Weller 656–666; Pokorny I 1113; Chemodanov SGGJa I 103; Blum PBB LXXXII 189–195; Vries ANEW 416; Onions 997; Wissmann AWNP 67; Lehmann GED 409; Heidermanns 685–686.

ON óär ‘mind, wit, soul, sense’, OE ellen-wód ‘zeal’, MDu woet ‘madness’, OHG wuot ‘thrill, violent agitation’. Substantivized *wōđaz. Cf. also a structurally similar W gwawd ‘poem’. Torp-Falk 414; Holthausen AEEW 403; Vries ANEW 416; Lehmann GED 409; Kluge-Seebold 900.
ON œäi ‘rage, fury’, OHG wuotī ‘madness’. Derived from *wōđaz. Vries ANEW 684; Heidermanns 685.
ON œäa ‘to rage’, OE wédan ‘to be mad, to be furious’, OS wōdian ‘to rage’, OHG wuoten ‘to be insane, to rage’. Derived from *wōđaz. Holthausen AEEW 403; Vries ANEW 684; Heidermanns 686.

Goth wokrs ‘interest, τόκος’, ON okr ‘usury’, OE fem. wócor ‘increase, fruit, offspring’, OFris wōker ‘interest’, MLG woker id., OHG wuohhar ‘yield, gain’. A long-grade form morphologically similar to Skt ugrá- ‘powerful, mighty’, Av ugrō id. Further see *aukanan. Bartholomae 380; Torp- Falk 380; Holthausen AEEW 403; Feist 572; Mayrhofer I 98–99; Pokorny I 85; Zalizniak II 176; Lehmann GED 409; Kluge-Seebold 898.
ON ómr ‘sound, voice’, OE wóm ‘sound, noise’. Continues *wōpm-az derived from *wōpjanan (Rozwadowski Qu. Gr. I 422). Uhlenbeck PBB XXII 193, XXVII 135; Torp-Falk 414.
Norw øma ‘to squeak, to whisper’, OE wéman ‘to sound’. Derived from *wōmaz. Torp-Falk 414.

ON óp ‘crying, shouting’, OE wóp ‘cry, wailing’, OS wōp ‘crying, shouting’, OHG wuof id. Back formation of *wōpjanan I (Wissmann Postv. 46–47). Torp-Falk 414; Holthausen AEEW 405; Pokorny I 1109; Vries ANEW 419; Zalizniak II 173; Bammesberger Nom. 98.

OE wépan ‘to weep’, OFris wēpa ‘to shout’, OS wōpian ‘to bewail’, OHG wuofan ‘to weep’. See *wōpjanan II. Schweizer KZ I 154 (to Skt hváyati ‘to invoke, to call’); Schmidt KZ I 283–286; Persson Beiträge 494– 495; Froede BB XVII 319 (to Lat uāgiō ‘to cry, to squall’); Petersson PBB XXXVIII 322; Torp-Falk 414; Holthausen AEEW 404; Jóhannesson IEW 104; Pokorny I 1109; Vries ANEW 684; Onions 998; Vasmer-Trubachev I 263; Stang LS 60; Seebold 564–565; Lehmann GED 409.

Goth wopjan ‘to call’, ON œpa ‘to cry, to shout’, OHG wuofen ‘to weep’. Derived from *wōpjanan I. Structurally identical with Slav *vabiti ‘to call, to summon’ (Rozwadowski Qu. Gr. I 422). Zupitza Gutt. 46; Uhlenbeck PBB XXII 193, XXVII 135; Torp-Falk 414; Trautmann BSW 336–337; Feist 572; Porzig Gliederung 144; Jóhannesson IEW 104; Chemodanov SGGJa I 83; Vries ANEW 684; Seebold 564.

OE wériz ‘weary, tired’, OS wōrag, wōrig id., OHG wuorag ‘intoxicated’. Derived from *wōriz > Burg *woris ‘tired, intoxicated’ related to Gk ἄωρος, ὦρος ‘sleep’ (Benfey Wurzel- Lex. I 298). Holthausen AEEW 404; Pokorny I 72; Frisk I 205; Köbler 715.

Norw ōs ‘steam, smoke’, OE wós ‘moisture, juice’ MLG wōs ‘must, broth’. Cf. WGmc masc. *wasan > MLG wase ‘wet ground’. Related to Gk ἐαρόν: λουτῆρα, ἢ πρόχουν (Hes.). Torp-Falk 404 (to Latv vaša ‘floor wetness’); Pokorny I 1171– 1172; Frisk I 433.

OE wéste ‘waste, desert, empty’, OFris wōst ‘devastated’, OS wōsti ‘desert, barren’, OHG wuosti ‘empty, lonely, forlorn, desert’. Identical with Lat uāstus ‘desert, barren’, OIr fás ‘forlorn, uninhabited, desert, empty’. Torp-Falk 414; Holthausen AEEW 404; Walde- Hofmann II 737; Pokorny I 345–346; Chemodanov SGGJa I 103; Heidermanns 688; Kluge-Seebold 900.

Goth woþeis ‘pleasant, sweet, charming’, ON comp. œdri ‘higher’, OE wéäe ‘sweet, gentle, mild’, OS comp. wōthiera ‘more pleasant’, OHG wuodi id. Derived from *wōđaz. Grienberger Got. 244 (from *e⁄e-t-, cf. Goth prop. Eutha-ricus); Torp-Falk 414; Petersson PBB XXXVIII 323–324 (to Skt vāmá- ‘dear, fair, noble’); Wood Postcons. 62, 109 (to φώτιον: προσφιλές Hes.); Feist 572; Holthausen AEEW 404; Fowkes Goth. 54–55 (to Skt svādú- ‘savory, sweet’); Vries ANEW 684; Lehmann GED 409; Matzel KZ CV 132; Heidermanns 689.

Swed dial. vrà ‘averse, sulky, glum’, ME wráh ‘twisted, stubborn’. Identical with Gk =οίκος ‘bent, crooked’, Lith ráišas ‘lame’. Torp-Falk 418; Trautmann BSW 236; Pokorny I 1158–1159; Fraenkel 690, 730–731; Frisk II 656; Illich-Svitych IA 69; Huld 116 (to Alb rreth ‘hoop, rim (of a wheel)’).

Goth wraiqs ‘crooked, curved’, OFris wrāk id. Identical with Gk =αιβός ‘bent’ (Aufrecht KZ XII 400). Fay KZ XLV 113 (to Gk =ίζα ‘root’); Zupitza Gutt. 95; Torp-Falk 415; Feist 573; Pokorny I 1158; Vries ANEW 438; Frisk II 639; Lehmann GED 409–410; Heidermanns 689–690.

ON reini ‘stallion’, OS wrēnio id., OHG reino, wreinno id. Of unknown origin. Cf. OE wræne ‘lascivous’ that indicates the original adjectival meaning. Holthausen AEEW 406– 407; Specht 81 (close to Skt vṛßan- ‘man, male (of animals)’ but without -s-); Pokorny I 81; Vries ANEW 439.
ON reista ‘to turn, to twist’, OE wræstan ‘to wrest, to twist’. Derived from *wristiz. Torp-Falk 419; Holthausen AEEW 407; Onions 1014.
ON reita ‘to scratch’, OHG reizen ‘to irritate’. Derived from *wrītanan. Torp-Falk 418; Seebold 567.
ON reitr ‘furrow’, OHG reiz ‘line’. Derived from *wrītanan. Torp- Falk 418; Seebold 567.

ON reiär ‘angry’, OE wráä id., OS wrēth id., OHG reid ‘frizzy, curly’. Derived from *wrīþanan. Torp- Falk 418; Holthausen AEEW 407; Vries ANEW 437; Onions 1015; Bammesberger Nom. 237; Heidermanns 691–692.

ON reiäa ‘to anger’, OS wrēthian ‘to become angry’, OHG reiden ‘to make curly’. Derived from *wraiþaz. Karsten NB I 59–60; Torp- Falk 418; Holthausen AEEW 407; Pokorny I 1159–1160; Vries ANEW 437; Piirainen 55–56; Heidermanns 692.
ON reiäi ‘wrath’, OE wræä, wræäu id., MLG wrēde id. Derived from *wraiþaz. Holthausen AEEW 407; Vries ANEW 437.
Goth wraks ‘pursuer’, OE wræc ‘what is driven’ (gender unknown). Derived from *wrekanan. Torp-Falk 416; Holthausen AEEW 406; Feist 573; Seebold 569; Lehmann GED 410.
Goth wrakjan ‘to persecute’, OE wreccan ‘to raise’. Derived from *wrekanan. Torp-Falk 416; Holthausen AEEW 407; Feist 573; Vries ANEW 440– 441; Seebold 569; Lehmann GED 410.
Goth wrakja ‘pursuit’, OFris wretse id., OHG recke-gerna ‘vindictive’. Derived from *wrakō. Feist 573; Seebold 569; Bammesberger Nom. 114.
Goth wraka ‘pursuit’, OE wracu ‘pain, suffering, misery’. Derived from *wrekanan. Feist 573; Seebold 569; Bammesberger Nom. 109.

Gep *wranks ‘twisted’, ON rangr ‘crooked, wrong’, OE sbst. wranz ‘wrong’, MLG wrank ‘sour, sharp, bitter’. Derived from *wrenzanan. Torp-Falk 417; Holthausen AEEW 406; Vries ANEW 433; Onions 1015; Köbler 715; Heidermanns 692–693.

ON rǫng ‘timber(s)’, OE wranz(a) ‘hold of a ship’, MLG wrange ‘wale’. Derived from *wrenzanan. Cf. also ON rá ‘corner, nook’ < *wranxō. Mikkola IF XXIII 120–121 (to Slav *rǫka ‘hand, arm’); Holthausen AEEW 407; Vries ANEW 458.

ON rǫskr ‘valiant, bold, brave’, MLG rasch ‘strong, powerful’, OHG adv. rasco ‘energetically, lively’. Derived from *wreskwanan. Karsten NB II 296; Torp-Falk 341, 417; Schwentner PBB XLVIII 84–85; Wissmann NP 133; Kluge 582 (to *xraþaz); Vries ANEW 458; Onions 740; Piirainen 53; Heidermanns 693–694.
ON røskvi ‘efficiency’, OHG reskī ‘liveliness, fervor’. Derived from *wraskwaz. Heidermanns 693.

Goth wraton ‘to travel’, ON rata ‘to wander about’. Unclear. Scheftelowitz BB XXIX 44 (to Arm ga∑t' ‘to travel’); Wood MLN XVI 308; Torp-Falk 416 (to Skt vṛṇdá- ‘crowd, swarm’); Feist 573–574; Pokorny I 1153 (to IE *⁄er- ‘to turn’); Vries ANEW 434 (to Gk ῥαδινός ‘slender, taper’); Lehmann GED 410.
ON riga ‘to move’, OE wrizian ‘to turn’. Related to Gk =άσσω ‘to strike, to trample’. Pokorny I 1181; Frisk II 643–644; Kluge- Seebold 898.
ON rétta ‘to raise, to set up’, MLG wrechten ‘to drive’. Derived from *wrekanan. Vries ANEW 442.

Goth wrikan ‘to persecute’, ON reka ‘to drive, to chase’, OE wrecan ‘to drive, to press’, OFris wreka ‘to pursue, to follow’, OS wrekan ‘to repay, to punish’, OHG rehhan ‘to pursue, to punish’. Related to Skt vrájati ‘to stride, to go, to walk’, Lith veržiū, véržti ‘to bind, to squeeze’, Slav *vьrzǫ, *verzti ‘to bind’. Zupitza Gutt. 170; Torp-Falk 415–416; Trautmann BSW 342; Feist 574; Holthausen AEEW 407; Walde-Hofmann II 839–840; Jóhannesson IEW 172–173; Mayrhofer III 276–277; Pokorny I 1181; Fraenkel 1230–1231; Mezger KZ LXXVI 179; Vries ANEW 440; Fraenkel 1225; Onions 1014; Seebold 568–569; Lehmann GED 410; Kluge- Seebold 662; Adams TB 609–610 (to Toch B werke ‘chase, hunt, hunting’).
Burg sbst. *wriks ‘persecutor’, ON aptr-reka ‘driven back (by stress of weather)’, OHG wreh ‘banished’. Derived from *wrekanan. Köbler 715; Heidermanns 694.
MLG wrimpen ‘to grimace, to wince’. Identical with Gk =έμβω ‘to turn in circles’ (Persson Beiträge I 498). Torp-Falk 417; Pokorny I 1153; Frisk II 648–649.

OE wrinzan ‘to wring, to twist’, WFris wringe ‘to wring’, MLG wringen id., OHG ringan ‘to fight’. Identical with Lith rengiū, reñgti ‘to prepare, to set up’ (Lottner KZ XI 200). Aufrecht KZ XII 400; Zupitza Gutt. 170; Torp-Falk 416–417; Holthausen AEEW 408; Pokorny I 1155 (from *⁄reng ⁄h-); Fraenkel 719–720; Onions 1015; Seebold 570; Lehmann GED 411; Kluge-Seebold 688.

Goth ga-wrisqan ‘to bear fruit’, ON part. roskinn ‘to grow’. Unclear. Uhlenbeck PBB XXX 283 (to Skt vṛkßá- ‘tree’); Diefenbach VW I 241 (to W gwrysg(en) ‘branch’); Scheftelowitz KZ LVIII 132 (to Av var 6 da- ‘shrub’); Zupitza Gutt. 90; Torp-Falk 417; Feist 213; Pokorny I 1167; Vries ANEW 451; Seebold 571.

ON rækr ‘reprehensible, banished’, OFris in-wrēsze ‘driven in, penetrating’. Derived from *wrekanan. Karsten NB I 103; Torp-Falk 416; Wissmann NP 113; Seebold 569; Matzel Festschr. Schröder 100–101, 117, KZ CV 109; Heidermanns 694–695.
ON rækja ‘to refuse, to reject’. Derived from *wrekanan. Structurally close to Skt pra-vrājayati ‘to banish’. Vries ANEW 456; Pokorny I 1181.

ON klauf-rák ‘driving of cattle’, OE wræc ‘vengeance’, OFris wrēke id., OS wrāka id., OHG rāhha id. Cf. also Goth wrekei ‘persecution’. Derived from *wrekanan. Torp-Falk 416; Holthausen AEEW 406; Vries ANEW 432, 456; Seebold 569; Kluge-Seebold 662.

Goth wriþus ‘herd’ (leg. *wreþus), ODan vrath ‘herd of twelve pigs’, OE wræä, wræd ‘bandage, band, flock’. Close to Skt vrāta- ‘troop, swarm, flock’. Torp-Falk 416; Holthausen AEEW 406; Feist 574–575; Mayrhofer III 280; Pokorny I 1151; Chemodanov SGGJa I 106; Vries ANEW 431; Benveniste IEL 48; Lehmann GED 411.

ON risi ‘giant’, OHG riso id. Cf. a derivative in OS wrisi-līk ‘enormous’. Probably related to Gk =ίον ‘peak, headland’ < *⁄riso- (Bezzenberger apud Walde-Pokorny I 267; Lidén Festschr. Kuhn 144–145: to Thrac βρία ‘wall, town’ < *⁄riƒo-) unless the latter is from *⁄ṛsoand connected with Slav *vьrxъ ‘top’. Detschew Thr. 86; Vries ANEW 447 (to Slav *vьrxъ ‘top’); Pokorny I 1152; Frisk II 658.

ON rist ‘wrist, ankle’, OE wrist ‘wrist’, OFris wirst id., MLG wrist id., OHG masc., neut. rist id. Derived from *wrīþanan (Johansson IF XIX 117). Probably connected with Lith ríešas id. (Persson Beiträge 343). Torp-Falk 418–419; Holthausen AEEW 409; Pokorny I 1159 (reconstructs *wrixs-t-); Fraenkel 730; Vries ANEW 448; Zalizniak II 212; Onions 1015; Seebold 566 (< *wrixstiz, to OHG part. gi-rigan ‘woven’); Stang LS 45; Heidermanns 691; Kluge-Seebold 688.

ON rit ‘writing’, OE writ ‘writing, writ’. Derived from *wrītanan. Torp-Falk 418; Holthausen AEEW 409; Pokorny I 1163–1164; Vries ANEW 448; Zalizniak II 191; Onions 1015; Seebold 567; Lehmann GED 410–411; Bammesberger Nom. 130.
ON riäull ‘tuft’, MLG wrēdel ‘stick holding a bundle together’, OHG ridil ‘hairband’. Cf. also OE under-wrædel ‘belt’. Derived from *wrīþanan. Holthausen AEEW 406; Vries ANEW 444.
Goth writs ‘serif’, OHG riz ‘stroke, character’. Derived from *wrītanan. Torp-Falk 418; Feist 574; Seebold 567.

ON ríta ‘to scratch, to write’, OE wrítan ‘to cut (a figure), to draw, to write’, OFris part. -writen ‘to write’, OS wrītan ‘to tear, to write’, OHG rīzan ‘to tear, to scratch’. Of uncertain origin. Sommer HL 231 (to Lat rīma ‘cleft, crack’); Torp-Falk 343, 418; Schröder ZDADL LXI 57–58; Holthausen AEEW 409; Jóhannesson IEW 154; Pokorny I 1163–1164 (d-present compared with *⁄er- ‘to tear, to scratch’); Vries ANEW 448; Onions 1015; Seebold 566–567; Lehmann GED 410–411 (to IE *⁄er- ‘to tear, to scratch’); Kluge-Seebold 677.
ON rít ‘shield decorated with carvings’, OE wæter-write ‘waterclock’, OHG rīza ‘compass’. Derived from *wrītanan. Holthausen AEEW 409; Vries ANEW 448.

ON ríäa ‘to wind’, OE wríäan ‘to twist’, OHG rīdan ‘to turn’. Related to Lith rie‘iū, ri‚sti ‘to unbend’ (Lidén Anl. 4–6). Wood MP IV 495; Torp-Falk 418; Holthausen AEEW 409; Jóhannesson IEW 141–149; Pokorny I 1159–1160; Fraenkel 729–730; Vries ANEW 444; Onions 1015; Seebold 567–568.

Goth wrohs ‘complaint, accusation’, ON róg ‘quarrel, calumny’ (neut.), OFris ruogie ‘accusation, charge’, MLG wroge id., OHG ruog ‘quarrel’. Derived from *wrezōjanan. Lewy PBB XXXII 142 (to Slav *rîkti ‘to speak’); von Sabler KZ XXXI 283 (to Lith riejū, ríeti ‘to scold loudly’); Torp-Falk 419; Feist 575; Jóhannesson IEW 172 (to Av urvāxra- ‘heat’); Pokorny I 1162–1163; Vries ANEW 450; Lehmann GED 411; Kluge-Seebold 695.

Goth fra-wrohjan ‘to suspect, to accuse’, ON rœgja ‘to accuse’, OE wrézan ‘to accuse, to denounce’, OFris wrōgia ‘to accuse’, OS wrōgian id., OHG ruogen id. Derived from *wrōziz ~ *wrōzaz. Zupitza Gutt. 143; Lewy PBB XXXII 142 (to Lith rëkiū, r Ekti ‘to shout, to roar’, Slav *rekǫ, *rekti ‘to say’); Torp-Falk 419; Holthausen AEEW 409; Feist 575; Lehmann GED 411; Kluge-Seebold 695.

OE wrótan ‘to turn up with the snout, to root up’, OHG wk. ruozen ‘to plow’. Probably related to Lat rōdō ‘to gnaw’ (Schulze KZ LV 112). A possible connection with *wrōtiz has to be considered. Lidén KZ LVI 218– 220; Torp-Falk 419 (to Slav *verdъ ‘harm’); Holthausen AEEW 409; Walde- Hofmann II 439–440; Pokorny I 1163– 1164; Seebold 571; Kluge-Seebold 697.

ON rót ‘root’. Related to Lat rādīx id., Gk =άδιξ id., W gwridd id. Further see *wurtiz. Torp-Falk 397; Walde-Hofmann II 415; Pokorny I 1167; Vries ANEW 452; Frisk II 637–638; Onions 773; Griepentrog Wurzelnomina 458–460.
ON rót ‘rolling up’, OE wrót ‘snout, trunk’, LG wrōte id. Related to *wrōtanan. Holthausen AEEW 409; Pokorny I 1167; Vries ANEW 452; Onions 773 (to Lat rōdō ‘to gnaw’).
ON róta ‘to dig’, OFris wrōta id. Derived from *wrōtanan. Torp-Falk 419; Onions 773; Vries ANEW 452; Seebold 571.

Goth wulfs ‘wolf ’, ON ulfr id., OE wulf id., OFris wolf id., OS wulf id., OHG wolf id. Assimilated to *wulfaz from *wulxwaz, cf. *femfe. Related to Toch B walkwe id., Skt vṛka- id., Av v 6 hrkaid., Gk λύκος id., Alb ujk id., Lat lupus id. (borrowed from Sabinian or assimilated like in Gmc), Lith vifikas id., Slav *vьlkъ id. (Försterman KZ I 494). Zupitza Gutt. 16; Bartholomae 1418–1419; Torp-Falk 415; Trautmann BSW 359; Holthausen AEEW 410; Walde- Hofmann I 836–837; Feist 576; Mayrhofer III 240–241; Pokorny I 1178– 1179; Fraenkel 1251–1252; Vries ANEW 632–633; Zalizniak I 137; Frisk II 143–144; Onions 1011; Ivanov BLS 152–152 (parallelism between ON prn. Ulf-bjǫrn, OHG prn. Wulf-bero and Slav *vьlko-dlakъ ‘werewolf’); Vasmer-Trubachev I 338; Lehmann GED 411–412; Orel AED 484; Kluge-Seebold 896.

ON ylgr ‘she-wolf ’. Cf. also OHG wulpa id. (with *-b-). Feminine form of *wulfaz. Close to Skt vṛkī‘female wolf’. Zupitza Gutt. 16; Streitberg UGG 111– 112, 125 (ON -g- < *zw before -j- in gen. *⁄¬k ⁄ƒōs); Torp-Falk 415; Mayrhofer III 240, Festschr. Leroy 131; Pokorny I 1178–1179; Lehmann GED 411–412; Bammesberger Nom. 112.

OE wolcen ‘cloud’, OFris wolken ‘cloud’, OS wolkan id., OHG wolcan id. Related to *welkaz and further to OIr folc ‘stream’, Lith vīlgau, vīlgyti ‘to moisten’, Latv vafigs ‘damp, moist’, Slav *vьlgъkъ ‘wet’, *volga ‘wetness, liquid’. Structurally identical with Maced hydronym ἼΟλγανος < *⁄¬gano- (Tomaschek Thr. II/2 94) and Latv velgans, valgans ‘wet’. Zupitza Gutt. 169; Pedersen Kelt. Gr. I 59; Torp-Falk 402–403; Trautmann BSW 337, 358; Holthausen AEEW 405; Detschew Thr. 340; Pokorny I 1145–1146; Fraenkel 1251; Onions 999; Vasmer-Trubachev I 337, 340; Kluge-Seebold 896–897 (to Slav *ob-volkъ ‘cloud’).

Goth wulla ‘wool’, ON ull id., OE wull id., OFris wolle id., MLG wulle id., OHG wolla id. Related to Hitt ḫulana/i- id., Skt ūrṇā id., Av var 6 nā, Gk λῆνος id., Lat lāna id., W gwlan id., Lith vīlna id., Slav *vьlna id. Bartholomae 1372–1373; Torp-Falk 401; Trautmann BSW 359; Holthausen AEEW 410; Walde-Hofmann I 756–757; Feist 576–577; Martinet Gém. 71; Mayrhofer I 116; Pokorny I 1139; Fraenkel 1253; Vries ANEW 633; Zalizniak I 148; Frisk II 117–118; Onions 1012; Vasmer-Trubachev I 339–340; Tischler HEG I 278–279; Lehmann GED 412; Lindeman KZ CIII 22–24; Bammesberger Nom. 115; Kluge-Seebold 897.

Goth wulþus ‘splendor’, ON theonym Ullr. Identical with Lat uultus, uoltus ‘facial expression, appearance’ (Schweizer KZ I 154). On the other hand, cf. *welþjaz. Torp-Falk 401; Kretschmer Glotta XXX 144 (to Illyr Voltu- in prop.); Holthausen AEEW 410 (on OE wuldor ‘glory’); Walde-Hofmann II 831; Feist 577; Pokorny I 1136; Chemodanov SGGJa I 91; Vries ANEW 633; Lehmann GED 413; Bammesberger Nom. 159.

Goth wunds ‘wounded’, OE wund id., OS wund id., OHG wunt id. Participle *⁄–tó- of an unattested verb *wenanan. Probably related to W ym-wan ‘to battle’, Corn ym-wanas ‘percussit’. Cf. also Arm vandem ‘to destroy’. Connected with *wanjanan. Torp-Falk 388–389; Feist 577–578; Holthausen AEEW 410; Pokorny I 1108; Lerchner 259; Lehmann GED 413; Bammesberger Nom. 116 (from IE *g ⁄h–to-); Heidermanns 696–697; Kluge-Seebold 898.

ON und ‘wound’, OE wund id., OFris wunde id., OS wunda id., OHG wunta id. Substantivized *wunđaz. Holthausen AEEW 410; Pokorny I 1108; Vries ANEW 634; Zalizniak II 198; Onions 1013–1014; Lehmann GED 413; Kluge-Seebold 898.
Goth ga-wundon ‘to wound’, ON unda id., OE wundian id., OFris wundia id., OHG wuntōn id. Derived from *wunđiz ~ *wunđō. Feist 577; Vries ANEW 634; Heidermanns 697.
ON pl. undr-sjónir ‘wonderful sight’, OE wundor-séon id., OHG wuntar-siun id. Compound of *wunđran and *sezwniz. Carr Nom. comp. 53.

ON undr ‘wonder’, OE wundor id., OFris wonder id., OS wundar id., OHG wuntar id. Continues IE *⁄–ro-, a suffixal derivative of IE *⁄en- ‘to wish, to love’. See *wunēnan. Loewenthal ANF XXXV 239 (to *wunđiz ~ *wunđō); Torp-Falk 415; Holthausen AEEW 410; Pokorny I 1146–1147; Vries ANEW 634; Zalizniak I 145; Onions 1011; Kluge-Seebold 898 (to *wenđanan).

Goth part. un-wunands ‘worried, upset’, ON una ‘to be satisfied’, OE wunian ‘to remain, to dwell’, OFris wonia, wunia ‘to live to stay’, OS wunōn, wonōn ‘ to stay, to be accustomed’, OHG wonēn id. Related to Toch A wañi, B wīn ‘contentment’, Skt vanóti ‘to demand, to strive for, to like’, Av vanaiti ‘to conquer’, Lat uenus ‘love’. Bartholomae 1350– 1351; Torp-Falk 388; Feist 526–527; Holthausen AEEW 410; Walde- Hofmann II 752; Mayrhofer III 141; Pokorny I 1146–1147; Vries ANEW 634; Onions 1011; Lehmann GED 379; Heidermanns 696; Kluge-Seebold 896.

OE wynn ‘delight, pleasure’, OS wunnia ‘joy’, OHG wunna id. Connected with *wunēnan. Derivationally close to Lat uenia ‘complaisance, indulgence, kindness’ (Szemerényi IBK XV 200–202). Holthausen AEEW 411; Walde-Hofmann II 747; Kluge-Seebold 897 (to *wenjō).
ON ÿskja ‘to wish’, OE wÿscan id., MLG wünschen id., OHG wunsken id. Derived from *wunskō ~ *wunskaz. Torp-Falk 388; Holthausen AEEW 412; Vries ANEW 680; Onions 1009.

ON ósk ‘wish’, MLG wunsch ‘wish’, OHG wunsc id. Derivationally similar to Skt vāñchā ‘wish’, vāñchati ‘to desire, to wish’ with a different ablaut grade. Further connected with *wunēnan. Torp-Falk 388; Holthausen AEEW 411; Mayrhofer III 182– 183; Pokorny I 1147; Vries ANEW 421; Zalizniak II 176; Onions 1009; Kluge- Seebold 898.

Goth waurd ‘word’, ON orä id., OE word id., OFris word id., OS word id., OHG wort id. Identical with OPrus wīrds id. < *⁄ṛdho- (Fraenkel BS 43, 107). Etymologically close to Lat uerbum id., Lith vaıdas ‘name’. Schweizer KZ I 154 (related to Skt gṛṇāti ‘to call, to invoke’ and Gk γλῶσσα ‘tongue’); Torp-Falk 415; Holthausen AEEW 40 Holthausen AEEW 405; Walde- Hofmann II 756–757; Feist 554; Porzig Gliederung 208; Trier Lehm 76; Pokorny I 1163; Chemodanov SGGJa I 67; Fraenkel 1198; Vries ANEW 419; Zalizniak I 145; Onions 1012; Peeters IF XCIV 294; Lehmann Language XVIII 127, GED 396; Kluge-Seebold 897.
ON orä-víss ‘wise in words’, OE word-wís id., OS word-wīs id., MHG word-wīs id. Compound of *wurđan and *wīsaz. Carr Nom. comp. 61.
ON oräigr ‘wordy’, OE wordiz id. Derived from *wurđan. Lehmann GED 396.

ON Urär ‘fate, one of three Norns’, OE wyrd ‘fate, fortune, chance’, OS wurd ‘fate’, OHG wurt id. Derived from *werþanan. Bringleb Zd Ph LXVII 10–11; Torp-Falk 398; Holthausen AEEW 411; Pokorny I 1157; Vries ANEW 635–636; Zalizniak I 151; Onions 998; Seebold 560; Bammesberger Nom. 131.
Goth filu-waurdei ‘verbosity’, ON sann-yräi ‘truth’. Derived from *wurđan. Feist 554; Vries ANEW 679.

Goth ga-waurdi ‘conversation’, ON -yräi ‘speech’ (in cmpn.), OE ze-wyrde ‘speech’, OFris ond-warde ‘answer’, OS and-wurdi id., OHG ant-wurti id. Derived from *wurđan. Derivationally close to Lat pro-uerbium ‘proverb’. Holthausen AEEW 411; Feist 554; Chemodanov SGGJa I 86; Vries ANEW 679; Lehmann GED 396; Lloyd-Springer I 288–289; Kluge-Seebold 45.

Goth and-waurdjan ‘to answer’, ON yräa ‘to talk, to speak’, OE and-wyrdan ‘to answer’, OFris and-werda id., OS and-wurdian id., OHG ant-wurten ‘to arrange, to assign’. Derived from *wurđan. Holthausen AEEW 411; Feist 554; Vries ANEW 679.
ON oräa ‘to speak’, OE wordian id., MHG worten id. Derived from *wurđan. Holthausen AEEW 405; Vries ANEW 420.

ON orf ‘scythe handle’, OHG worb id. Related to Gk =απίς ‘rod’, =έπω ‘to bend’, Lith verpiū, ωεὁπτι ‘to spin’ (Fick II 247). Persson Wurzelerw. 53; Schrader KZ XXX 481; Torp-Falk 415; Trautmann BSW 353; Pokorny I 1156; Fraenkel 1227; Vries ANEW 420; Frisk II 642–643 (Gk =απίς is based on =απίζω ‘to strike (with a rod or a hand)’).

Swed dial. yrga ‘to lace up’, OE wyrzan ‘to strangle, to throttle’, OFris wergia ‘to kill’, OHG wurgen ‘to strangle’. Related to Lat urgeō ‘to press’ < *⁄ṛgeƒō, Lith veržiū ‘to bind’, Slav *vьrzǫ, *verzti id. (Persson Beiträge 506). Walde- Hofmann II 839–840; Pokorny I 1154–1155.
Goth waurstw ‘work, activity, ἐνέργεια, đrgon’. Derived from *wurkjanan. Identical with Av varštva- ‘faciendus’. Bartholomae 1380; Trubachev Etim. 1965 11.
Goth fra-waurhts ‘sinful’, OS far-warht id., MHG ver-worht id. Participle of *wurkjanan. Torp-Falk 396; Feist 166–167; Lehmann GED 127.

Goth fra-waurhts ‘sin’, ON pl. fyr-urtir ‘pretext, excuse’, OE ze-wyrht ‘deed, work’, OS far-wurht id., MHG wurht ‘cause’. Derived from *wurkjanan. Cf. *wurxtaz. Torp-Falk 396; Wessén ANF XLIV 106; Feist 166–167; Vries ANEW 139; Lehmann GED 127; Bammesberger Nom. 141.
Goth ga-waurki ‘undertaking, business’, ON yrki ‘work’, OE ze-wyrce id., MHG ge-würke id. Derived from *wurkjanan. Holthausen AEEW 411; Feist 555; Vries ANEW 679.

Goth waurkjan ‘to work, to do, to make’, ON yrkja ‘to work’, OE wyrcan id., OFris werka id., OS workian id., OHG wurken id. Derived from *werkan. Zupitza Gutt. 200; Torp-Falk 395–396; Holthausen AEEW 411; Feist 555; Vries ANEW 139, 679; Lehmann GED 396–397; Kluge-Seebold 893.
ON orm-garär ‘pit of serpents’, OE wyrm-zeard ‘enclosure full of snakes’, MHG wurm-garte ‘pit of serpents’. Compound of *wurmaz ~ *wurmiz and *zarđaz. Carr Nom. comp. 77 (parallel formations).

Goth waurms ‘snake’, ON ormr ‘serpent’, OE wyrm id., OFris worm id., OS wurm id., OHG wurm id. Related to Gk =ομος: σκώληξ ἐν ξύλοις (Hes.), Lat uermis ‘worm’, Lith vaımas ‘midge’ (Schweizer KZ I 154: mistakenly adds reflexes of *k ⁄ṛmi-; ZubatŸ IF VI 156), Alb rrime ‘rainworm’ < *⁄ṛmā. A rhyme formation parallel to *k⁄ṛmi- (Watkins apud Mayrhofer EWAi A I 394). Zupitza Gutt. 18; Torp- Falk 415; Trautmann BSW 342; Holthausen AEEW 412; Walde- Hofmann II 760; Feist 555–556; Porzig Gliederung 209; Pokorny I 1152; Chemodanov SGGJa I 60; Fraenkel 1201; Vries ANEW 420; Zalizniak II 191; Frisk II 662; Onions 1012–1013; Lehmann GED 397; Kluge-Seebold 899; Orel IF XCIII 116, AED 386.

Goth waurts ‘root’, ON urt ‘plant’, OE wyrt ‘plant, herb, root’, OS wurt id., OHG wurz ‘herb, plant, root’. Related to Gk =άδιξ ‘branch’, Alb rrënjë ~ rranjë ‘root’ < PAlb *wradnjā, Lat rādīx id., W gwraidd id. (zero grade). Bally MSL XII 328–329; Torp-Falk 397; Holthausen AEEW 412; Walde- Hofmann II 415; Feist 556; Pokorny I 1167; Frisk II 655–656; Vries ANEW 636; Zalizniak I 151; Onions 1013; Orel AED 384; Kluge-Seebold 899.

ON urä ‘heap of rocks at the foot of a mountain’, OE worä ‘enclosed place, enclosed homestead, court, hall, street’, OS wurth ‘trampled floor’. Related to *warjanan. Structurally close to Toch A wärt, B wartto ‘forest’, OIr fert ‘burial mound’. Torp-Falk 395; Holthausen AEEW 406; Pokorny I 1162; Vries ANEW 635; Onions 1013; Lehmann GED 394; Adams TB 580.
